text: “You are a good man and a true friend,” Carton said in a changed voice. “Forgive me if I mention that you are moved. I could not see my own father weep and remain here indifferent. And if you were my father, I could not honor your grief more deeply. As it is, you are spared that sorrow.”
source_text: “You are a good man and a true friend,” said Carton, in an altered voice. “Forgive me if I notice that you are affected. I could not see my father weep, and sit by, careless. And I could not respect your sorrow more, if you were my father. You are free from that misfortune, however.”

text: “I make the promise,” said the Doctor, “without any reservation. I believe your purpose to be, as you have declared it, pure and honest. I believe your intention is to strengthen, and not to weaken, the bond between me and my other, far more cherished self. If she should ever tell me that you are necessary to her perfect happiness, I will give her to you. If there were—Charles Darnay, if there were—”
source_text: “I give the promise,” said the Doctor, “without any condition. I believe your object to be, purely and truthfully, as you have stated it. I believe your intention is to perpetuate, and not to weaken, the ties between me and my other and far dearer self. If she should ever tell me that you are essential to her perfect happiness, I will give her to you. If there were--Charles Darnay, if there were--”

text: “See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, lifting his hand toward the moon. “I have watched her from my prison window when I could not endure her light. I have watched her when it tortured me to think of her shining on what I had lost, so that I struck my head against the prison walls. I have watched her in such a dull, stupefied state that I have thought of nothing except the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of upright lines with which I might cross them.” He added, in his inward, meditative way, as he gazed at the moon, “It was twenty each way, I remember, and the twentieth was hard to fit in.”
source_text: “See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, raising his hand towards the moon. “I have looked at her from my prison-window, when I could not bear her light. I have looked at her when it has been such torture to me to think of her shining upon what I had lost, that I have beaten my head against my prison-walls. I have looked at her, in a state so dull and lethargic, that I have thought of nothing but the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of perpendicular lines with which I could intersect them.” He added in his inward and pondering manner, as he looked at the moon, “It was twenty either way, I remember, and the twentieth was difficult to squeeze in.”

text: He was carried onward, and other carriages swept past in rapid succession; the Minister, the State-Projector, the Farmer-General, the Doctor, the Lawyer, the Ecclesiastic, the Grand Opera, the Comedy, the whole Fancy Ball in one brilliant unbroken stream, all flashed by. The rats had come out of their holes to watch, and there they remained for hours; soldiers and police passed again and again between them and the spectacle, setting up a barrier behind which they crouched and through which they peered. The father had long since lifted his bundle and gone off with it, when the women who had kept watch over the bundle as it lay on the fountain’s base sat there observing the water run and the Fancy Ball roll past—when the one woman who had stood out, knitting, still knitted on with the constancy of Fate. The fountain’s water ran, the swift river ran, the day ran into evening, so much life in the city ran into death by rule; time and tide waited for no man, the rats were once more sleeping huddled together in their dark burrows, the Fancy Ball shone at supper, and all things went their appointed way.
source_text: He was driven on, and other carriages came whirling by in quick succession; the Minister, the State-Projector, the Farmer-General, the Doctor, the Lawyer, the Ecclesiastic, the Grand Opera, the Comedy, the whole Fancy Ball in a bright continuous flow, came whirling by. The rats had crept out of their holes to look on, and they remained looking on for hours; soldiers and police often passing between them and the spectacle, and making a barrier behind which they slunk, and through which they peeped. The father had long ago taken up his bundle and bidden himself away with it, when the women who had tended the bundle while it lay on the base of the fountain, sat there watching the running of the water and the rolling of the Fancy Ball--when the one woman who had stood conspicuous, knitting, still knitted on with the steadfastness of Fate. The water of the fountain ran, the swift river ran, the day ran into evening, so much life in the city ran into death according to rule, time and tide waited for no man, the rats were sleeping close together in their dark holes again, the Fancy Ball was lighted up at supper, all things ran their course.

text: Lucie and her father! Lucie, with her arms reaching out to him, and with that familiar expression of deep sincerity now so gathered and heightened that it seemed as if it had been impressed upon her face solely to lend strength and force to this single moment of her life.
source_text: Lucie and her father! Lucie with her arms stretched out to him, and with that old look of earnestness so concentrated and intensified, that it seemed as though it had been stamped upon her face expressly to give force and power to it in this one passage of her life.

text: “Tut! Nonsense, sir!--And, my dear Charles,” said Mr. Lorry, glancing once more at the House, “you must remember that, at this moment, getting anything out of Paris is next to impossible, no matter what it may be. Papers and valuable things were brought to us here this very day—I speak in strict confidence; it is not business-like even to whisper it to you—by the strangest bearers you can imagine, each one of whom had his head hanging by a single hair as he went through the Barriers. At another time our parcels would pass to and fro as freely as in practical Old England; but now, everything is at a standstill.”
source_text: “Tut! Nonsense, sir!--And, my dear Charles,” said Mr. Lorry, glancing at the House again, “you are to remember, that getting things out of Paris at this present time, no matter what things, is next to an impossibility. Papers and precious matters were this very day brought to us here (I speak in strict confidence; it is not business-like to whisper it, even to you), by the strangest bearers you can imagine, every one of whom had his head hanging on by a single hair as he passed the Barriers. At another time, our parcels would come and go, as easily as in business-like Old England; but now, everything is stopped.”

text: “Upon my soul, I’m not sure it wasn’t yours. You were always driving and wrenching and pushing and slipping by, to such a restless pitch that I had no chance for my life except in rust and rest. It is, though, a dismal thing to talk of one’s own past at daybreak. Turn me another way before I go.”
source_text: “Upon my soul, I am not sure that it was not yours. You were always driving and riving and shouldering and passing, to that restless degree that I had no chance for my life but in rust and repose. It’s a gloomy thing, however, to talk about one’s own past, with the day breaking. Turn me in some other direction before I go.”

text: To this lodging he immediately moved Lucie and her child, together with Miss Pross, giving them all the comfort he could, and far more than he had for himself. He left Jerry with them, as a sort of figure set in a doorway that might endure a good deal of knocking on the head, and went back to his own affairs. He carried to them a troubled and sorrowful spirit, and the day dragged slowly and heavily on for him.
source_text: To this lodging he at once removed Lucie and her child, and Miss Pross: giving them what comfort he could, and much more than he had himself. He left Jerry with them, as a figure to fill a doorway that would bear considerable knocking on the head, and returned to his own occupations. A disturbed and doleful mind he brought to bear upon them, and slowly and heavily the day lagged on with him.

text: It was as late as seven o’clock when he woke, refreshed, and once more went out into the streets. As he walked toward Saint Antoine, he paused before a shop window that held a mirror, and there slightly set right the untidy arrangement of his loose cravat, his coat collar, and his wild hair. When he had done this, he went straight on to Defarge’s, and entered.
source_text: It was as late as seven o’clock when he awoke refreshed, and went out into the streets again. As he passed along towards Saint Antoine, he stopped at a shop-window where there was a mirror, and slightly altered the disordered arrangement of his loose cravat, and his coat-collar, and his wild hair. This done, he went on direct to Defarge’s, and went in.

text: “Well! Some whisper one thing, some another; they can talk of nothing else, and even the fountain seems to drip its refrain. At last, on Sunday night, when the whole village is asleep, soldiers come winding down from the prison, and their guns clatter on the stones of the narrow street. Workmen dig, workmen hammer, soldiers laugh and sing; and in the morning, by the fountain, there stands a gallows forty feet high, poisoning the water.”
source_text: “Well! Some whisper this, some whisper that; they speak of nothing else; even the fountain appears to fall to that tune. At length, on Sunday night when all the village is asleep, come soldiers, winding down from the prison, and their guns ring on the stones of the little street. Workmen dig, workmen hammer, soldiers laugh and sing; in the morning, by the fountain, there is raised a gallows forty feet high, poisoning the water.”

text: “I laid that hidden truth before him. I beat upon this breast with both my hands, even as I beat it now, and said to him, ‘Monsieur Dufort, I was reared among the fishermen of the seashore, and that humble household so cruelly wronged by the two de Villiers brothers, as the Bastille record sets forth, is my own house. Monsieur Dufort, that sister of the youth lying mortally stricken upon the ground was my sister; that husband was my sister’s husband; that child unborn was their child; that brother was my brother; that father was my father; those who died are my dead, and that call to answer for such deeds has come down upon me!’ Ask him whether it was so.”
source_text: “I communicate to him that secret. I smite this bosom with these two hands as I smite it now, and I tell him, ‘Defarge, I was brought up among the fishermen of the sea-shore, and that peasant family so injured by the two Evrémonde brothers, as that Bastille paper describes, is my family. Defarge, that sister of the mortally wounded boy upon the ground was my sister, that husband was my sister’s husband, that unborn child was their child, that brother was my brother, that father was my father, those dead are my dead, and that summons to answer for those things descends to me!’ Ask him, is that so.”

text: “And I am very happy this evening, dear father. I am profoundly happy in the love that Heaven has so kindly blessed—my love for Edmund, and Edmund’s love for me. But if my life were not still to be devoted to you, or if my marriage were ordered so that it must separate us, even by the breadth of a few of these streets, I should feel more unhappy and more full of self-reproach now than I can say. Even as it is—”
source_text: “And I am very happy to-night, dear father. I am deeply happy in the love that Heaven has so blessed--my love for Charles, and Charles’s love for me. But, if my life were not to be still consecrated to you, or if my marriage were so arranged as that it would part us, even by the length of a few of these streets, I should be more unhappy and self-reproachful now than I can tell you. Even as it is--”

text: Night after night, day after day, the house’s inhabitants had strained to catch the sounds from that corner, and each time the heavy tread came, their courage sank within them. For those footfalls had grown in their imagination to seem like the tramp of a multitude, driven beneath a crimson banner and a cry that the nation was imperiled, and, by some ancient and dreadful spell that had endured too long, transformed into savage beasts.
source_text: Many a night and many a day had its inmates listened to the echoes in the corner, with hearts that failed them when they heard the thronging feet. For, the footsteps had become to their minds as the footsteps of a people, tumultuous under a red flag and with their country declared in danger, changed into wild beasts, by terrible enchantment long persisted in.

text: “It was not the finishing of it, I daresay? I mean, when you first began it, it was trying enough; though I have no complaint to make of Dr. Hartwell, save that he is unworthy of so dear a daughter, which is no reproach to him, for no man could reasonably have been expected to be worthy of such a blessing in any case. But it is indeed hard, past bearing, to have hosts and swarms of people come flocking after him — him I could have pardoned — and carry off little Bee’s heart from me.”
source_text: “It wasn’t ending it, I suppose? I say, when you began it, it was hard enough; not that I have any fault to find with Doctor Manette, except that he is not worthy of such a daughter, which is no imputation on him, for it was not to be expected that anybody should be, under any circumstances. But it really is doubly and trebly hard to have crowds and multitudes of people turning up after him (I could have forgiven him), to take Ladybird’s affections away from me.”

text: At first, his thoughts turned back to Defarge, and he considered seeking out the wine-shop once more and asking its master’s advice as to the safest place to lodge in the city’s disturbed condition. But the very reflection that brought Defarge to mind set him aside again; he lived in the most turbulent quarter, and no doubt possessed influence there, being deeply involved in its perilous activities.
source_text: At first, his mind reverted to Defarge, and he thought of finding out the wine-shop again and taking counsel with its master in reference to the safest dwelling-place in the distracted state of the city. But, the same consideration that suggested him, repudiated him; he lived in the most violent Quarter, and doubtless was influential there, and deep in its dangerous workings.

text: Night falls heavy and dark. He stirs more now; he is beginning to come to himself and to speak clearly. He still believes they are together. He asks him, by his name, what he is holding in his hand. O pity us, gracious Heaven, and aid us! Look out, look out, and see whether we are being pursued.
source_text: The night comes on dark. He moves more; he is beginning to revive, and to speak intelligibly; he thinks they are still together; he asks him, by his name, what he has in his hand. O pity us, kind Heaven, and help us! Look out, look out, and see if we are pursued.

text: She stayed on for a week. Toward the end, I was able to catch a few syllables of what she said to me by bending my ear close to her lips. She asked where she was, and I told her; who I was, and I told her that too. It was no use asking her for her family name. She gave a faint shake of her head on the pillow and kept her secret, as the boy had done.
source_text: “She lingered for a week. Towards the last, I could understand some few syllables that she said to me, by placing my ear close to her lips. She asked me where she was, and I told her; who I was, and I told her. It was in vain that I asked her for her family name. She faintly shook her head upon the pillow, and kept her secret, as the boy had done.

text: I managed, though only with great difficulty and after many attempts, to make the patient swallow the dose I had meant to give. Since I planned to repeat it later and needed to observe its effect, I took a seat beside the bed. A shy, subdued woman was in attendance— the man’s wife from downstairs— and she had drawn back into a corner. The house was damp and falling apart, poorly furnished, plainly only recently occupied and meant for temporary use. Thick, old draperies had been hung over the windows to muffle the sound of the screams. They went on in their steady order, with the cry, “My husband, my father, and my brother!” the count up to twelve, and then “Hush!” The agitation was so extreme that I had not removed the bandages binding the arms, though I had inspected them to make sure they were not causing pain. The one slight sign of hope in the case was that when I laid my hand on the sufferer’s breast, it had a soothing effect enough to calm the figure for several minutes at a time. It did nothing to stop the cries; no clockwork could have been more exact.
source_text: “I made the patient swallow, with great difficulty, and after many efforts, the dose that I desired to give. As I intended to repeat it after a while, and as it was necessary to watch its influence, I then sat down by the side of the bed. There was a timid and suppressed woman in attendance (wife of the man down-stairs), who had retreated into a corner. The house was damp and decayed, indifferently furnished--evidently, recently occupied and temporarily used. Some thick old hangings had been nailed up before the windows, to deaden the sound of the shrieks. They continued to be uttered in their regular succession, with the cry, ‘My husband, my father, and my brother!’ the counting up to twelve, and ‘Hush!’ The frenzy was so violent, that I had not unfastened the bandages restraining the arms; but, I had looked to them, to see that they were not painful. The only spark of encouragement in the case, was, that my hand upon the sufferer’s breast had this much soothing influence, that for minutes at a time it tranquillised the figure. It had no effect upon the cries; no pendulum could be more regular.

text: The dark gathered close, and then there came the tolling of church bells and the far-off roll of military drums in the Palace Yard, while the women sat knitting, knitting. Darkness hemmed them in. Another darkness was drawing on as inevitably, when the church bells, now sounding so pleasantly from a hundred light steeples across France, should be changed to roaring cannon; when the military drums should beat to smother a miserable voice, that night still all powerful as the voice of Dominion and Abundance, Liberty and Life. So much was closing in upon the women who sat knitting, knitting, that they themselves seemed to be closing in about a building not yet raised, where they were to sit knitting, knitting, counting falling heads.
source_text: Darkness closed around, and then came the ringing of church bells and the distant beating of the military drums in the Palace Courtyard, as the women sat knitting, knitting. Darkness encompassed them. Another darkness was closing in as surely, when the church bells, then ringing pleasantly in many an airy steeple over France, should be melted into thundering cannon; when the military drums should be beating to drown a wretched voice, that night all potent as the voice of Power and Plenty, Freedom and Life. So much was closing in about the women who sat knitting, knitting, that they their very selves were closing in around a structure yet unbuilt, where they were to sit knitting, knitting, counting dropping heads.

text: The people directly behind Madame Defarge, explaining the reason for her satisfaction to those behind them, and those in turn to others, and so on, soon set the neighbouring streets ringing with applause. In the same way, during two or three hours of slow, lingering speech and the sifting of many bushels of words, Madame Defarge’s frequent signs of impatience were carried, with astonishing speed, to a distance; and the message traveled all the more easily because certain men, who by some marvelous feat of nimbleness had climbed the outer framework to peer in at the windows, knew Madame Defarge well, and served as a kind of telegraph between her and the crowd outside the building.
source_text: The people immediately behind Madame Defarge, explaining the cause of her satisfaction to those behind them, and those again explaining to others, and those to others, the neighbouring streets resounded with the clapping of hands. Similarly, during two or three hours of drawl, and the winnowing of many bushels of words, Madame Defarge’s frequent expressions of impatience were taken up, with marvellous quickness, at a distance: the more readily, because certain men who had by some wonderful exercise of agility climbed up the external architecture to look in from the windows, knew Madame Defarge well, and acted as a telegraph between her and the crowd outside the building.

text: Tellson’s Bank was the object of a run on the mail. As the bank passenger—with one arm threaded through the leather strap, which did its best to keep him from crashing against the next passenger and driving him back into his corner whenever the coach took a violent jolt—sat nodding in his seat with half-closed eyes, the small coach windows and the coach lamp faintly shining through them, along with the bulky bundle of the passenger opposite, turned into the bank, and brisk business was done there. The jingle of the harness became the clink of coin, and more drafts were paid in five minutes than even Tellson’s, with all its foreign and domestic connections, would settle in three times that long. Then the underground strong rooms at Tellson’s, with whatever of their precious stores and secrets the passenger knew of—and it was no small knowledge—opened out before him; and he entered them with the great keys and the dimly burning candle, and found them secure, firm, sound, and motionless, exactly as he had last seen them.
source_text: Tellson’s Bank had a run upon it in the mail. As the bank passenger--with an arm drawn through the leathern strap, which did what lay in it to keep him from pounding against the next passenger, and driving him into his corner, whenever the coach got a special jolt--nodded in his place, with half-shut eyes, the little coach-windows, and the coach-lamp dimly gleaming through them, and the bulky bundle of opposite passenger, became the bank, and did a great stroke of business. The rattle of the harness was the chink of money, and more drafts were honoured in five minutes than even Tellson’s, with all its foreign and home connection, ever paid in thrice the time. Then the strong-rooms underground, at Tellson’s, with such of their valuable stores and secrets as were known to the passenger (and it was not a little that he knew about them), opened before him, and he went in among them with the great keys and the feebly-burning candle, and found them safe, and strong, and sound, and still, just as he had last seen them.

text: There were three rooms on the floor, and, since the doors between them had been thrown open so that the air might move freely through all of them, Mr. Lorry, smiling at the fanciful likeness he saw everywhere, went from one to the next. The first was the finest room, containing Lucie’s birds, flowers, books, writing desk, sewing table, and box of water-colours; the second was the Doctor’s consulting room, which also served as the dining room; the third, dappled now and then by the rustling plane tree in the yard, was the Doctor’s bedroom, and there, in a corner, stood the unused shoemaker’s bench and tray of tools, much as they had stood on the fifth floor of the bleak house near the wine-shop in the Saint Antoine district of Paris.
source_text: There were three rooms on a floor, and, the doors by which they communicated being put open that the air might pass freely through them all, Mr. Lorry, smilingly observant of that fanciful resemblance which he detected all around him, walked from one to another. The first was the best room, and in it were Lucie’s birds, and flowers, and books, and desk, and work-table, and box of water-colours; the second was the Doctor’s consulting-room, used also as the dining-room; the third, changingly speckled by the rustle of the plane-tree in the yard, was the Doctor’s bedroom, and there, in a corner, stood the disused shoemaker’s bench and tray of tools, much as it had stood on the fifth floor of the dismal house by the wine-shop, in the suburb of Saint Antoine in Paris.

text: By then, there was only one adventurous traveler left to be congratulated, since the other two had already been dropped at their separate stopping places by the road. The musty interior of the coach, with its wet, dirty straw, its foul odor, and its darkness, was not unlike a larger dog kennel. Mr. Lorry, the passenger, as he shook himself free of it in clinging chains of straw, a tangle of shaggy wraps, a flapping hat, and muddy legs, was rather like a larger kind of dog.
source_text: By that time, there was only one adventurous traveller left be congratulated: for the two others had been set down at their respective roadside destinations. The mildewy inside of the coach, with its damp and dirty straw, its disagreeable smell, and its obscurity, was rather like a larger dog-kennel. Mr. Lorry, the passenger, shaking himself out of it in chains of straw, a tangle of shaggy wrapper, flapping hat, and muddy legs, was rather like a larger sort of dog.

text: “No. That was a different matter altogether. It rose before the troubled reach of my sight, yet it never stirred. The image my thoughts went after was another child, and one more substantial than the first. Of her outward look I know nothing beyond this—that she was like her mother. The other possessed that same resemblance as well, as you do, yet she was not the same. Do you follow me, Clara? Hardly, I think? I fear one must have lived alone in a prison to make sense of such tangled distinctions.”
source_text: “No. That was another thing. It stood before my disturbed sense of sight, but it never moved. The phantom that my mind pursued, was another and more real child. Of her outward appearance I know no more than that she was like her mother. The other had that likeness too--as you have--but was not the same. Can you follow me, Lucie? Hardly, I think? I doubt you must have been a solitary prisoner to understand these perplexed distinctions.”

text: Though he spoke the final words with a lapse into his customary manner, there was sincerity and respect alike in his tone and in the pressure of his hand, which Mr. Lorry, having never seen the gentler side of him, was wholly unprepared for. He offered him his hand, and Carton softly pressed it.
source_text: Though he said the last words, with a slip into his usual manner, there was a true feeling and respect both in his tone and in his touch, that Mr. Lorry, who had never seen the better side of him, was wholly unprepared for. He gave him his hand, and Carton gently pressed it.

text: The wretched little shop of the wood-sawyer was so cramped that its entire front afforded very poor room for such a proclamation. Still, he had found someone to write it up for him, though Death had been crammed in there with the greatest awkwardness. On the roof of his house he displayed his pike and cap, as any proper citizen ought, and in the window he had placed his saw, labeled as his “Little Sainte Guillotine”—for by then the great sharp female had been popularly canonized. The shop was closed, and he was away, which was a comfort to Lucie and left her entirely alone.
source_text: The miserable shop of the wood-sawyer was so small, that its whole surface furnished very indifferent space for this legend. He had got somebody to scrawl it up for him, however, who had squeezed Death in with most inappropriate difficulty. On his house-top, he displayed pike and cap, as a good citizen must, and in a window he had stationed his saw inscribed as his “Little Sainte Guillotine”--for the great sharp female was by that time popularly canonised. His shop was shut and he was not there, which was a relief to Lucie, and left her quite alone.

text: “‘You see, gentlemen,’ said I, with my hands still resting upon her breast, ‘how ill I serve you in the condition to which you have brought me! Had I foreseen what sight awaited me, I might have come properly prepared. As it is, we shall lose precious time. In this desolate place, there are no medicines to be had.’
source_text: “‘See, gentlemen,’ said I, still keeping my hands upon her breast, ‘how useless I am, as you have brought me! If I had known what I was coming to see, I could have come provided. As it is, time must be lost. There are no medicines to be obtained in this lonely place.’

text: Up the broad flight of shallow steps, Monsieur the Marquis, with a flambeau going before him, passed from his carriage, disturbing the darkness just enough to draw a loud protest from an owl in the roof of the great stable building standing off among the trees. Everything else was so still that the torch borne up the steps, and the other torch held at the great door, burned as though they were in some enclosed chamber of state rather than in the open night air. No sound was heard save the owl’s cry and the water of a fountain dropping into its stone basin; for it was one of those dark nights that seem to hold their breath for hours together, then give a long, low sigh, and fall breathless once more.
source_text: Up the broad flight of shallow steps, Monsieur the Marquis, flambeau preceded, went from his carriage, sufficiently disturbing the darkness to elicit loud remonstrance from an owl in the roof of the great pile of stable building away among the trees. All else was so quiet, that the flambeau carried up the steps, and the other flambeau held at the great door, burnt as if they were in a close room of state, instead of being in the open night-air. Other sound than the owl’s voice there was none, save the falling of a fountain into its stone basin; for, it was one of those dark nights that hold their breath by the hour together, and then heave a long low sigh, and hold their breath again.

text: It was not until night had fully fallen that the men and women returned to the children, lamenting and without bread. Then the poor bakers’ shops were crowded with long lines of people patiently waiting to buy inferior loaves; and while they stood there with empty, sinking stomachs, they passed the time by greeting one another over the day’s victories and winning them again in talk. Little by little, those ragged queues grew shorter and wore thin, until they disappeared; then faint lights began to glimmer in the upper windows, and small fires were lit in the streets, where neighbors cooked together and later ate their suppers at their doors.
source_text: Not before dark night did the men and women come back to the children, wailing and breadless. Then, the miserable bakers’ shops were beset by long files of them, patiently waiting to buy bad bread; and while they waited with stomachs faint and empty, they beguiled the time by embracing one another on the triumphs of the day, and achieving them again in gossip. Gradually, these strings of ragged people shortened and frayed away; and then poor lights began to shine in high windows, and slender fires were made in the streets, at which neighbours cooked in common, afterwards supping at their doors.

text: Taking his arm through his own, he led him down Ludgate Hill into Fleet Street, and then, by way of a covered passage, into a tavern. There they were shown into a small room, where Charles Darnay quickly began to recover his strength with a plain, hearty dinner and good wine, while Carton sat opposite him at the same table, his own bottle of port set before him, and wearing his usual half-insolent air.
source_text: Drawing his arm through his own, he took him down Ludgate-hill to Fleet-street, and so, up a covered way, into a tavern. Here, they were shown into a little room, where Charles Darnay was soon recruiting his strength with a good plain dinner and good wine: while Carton sat opposite to him at the same table, with his separate bottle of port before him, and his fully half-insolent manner upon him.

text: What breeze had carried this haste to the gray-haired road mender, who was already at work on the hilltop beyond the village, with his midday meal—little enough to carry—wrapped in a bundle too poor to tempt even a crow, lying on a pile of stones? Had the birds, after carrying a few grains of it away, dropped one above him as they scattered random seeds? Whatever the cause, the road mender raced, on that hot morning, as if his life depended on it, down the hill, the dust rising to his knees, and did not stop until he reached the fountain.
source_text: What winds conveyed this hurry to the grizzled mender of roads, already at work on the hill-top beyond the village, with his day’s dinner (not much to carry) lying in a bundle that it was worth no crow’s while to peck at, on a heap of stones? Had the birds, carrying some grains of it to a distance, dropped one over him as they sow chance seeds? Whether or no, the mender of roads ran, on the sultry morning, as if for his life, down the hill, knee-high in dust, and never stopped till he got to the fountain.

text: It is true that the five had no spectators to prolong their business; for when he and Doctor Lenoir came forth from the gate, a great throng was gathered there, in which he fancied he saw every face that had been in Court—save two, for which he searched in vain. As he appeared, the multitude closed about him again, now weeping, now embracing, now shouting, and often all at once, until even the tide of the river by whose bank this wild spectacle had been enacted seemed to lose its reason, and to run as crazily as the people on the shore.
source_text: The five had had, it is true, no audience to lengthen their proceedings, for when he and Doctor Manette emerged from the gate, there was a great crowd about it, in which there seemed to be every face he had seen in Court--except two, for which he looked in vain. On his coming out, the concourse made at him anew, weeping, embracing, and shouting, all by turns and all together, until the very tide of the river on the bank of which the mad scene was acted, seemed to run mad, like the people on the shore.

text: “If we ever make it back to our own country,” said Miss Pross, “you can count on me to tell Mrs. Cruncher as much as I can remember and make sense of from what you have said so impressively; and at the very least, you may be sure I shall testify that you are entirely sincere in this dreadful hour. Now, please, let us think! My respected Mr. Cruncher, let us think!”
source_text: “If we ever get back to our native land,” said Miss Pross, “you may rely upon my telling Mrs. Cruncher as much as I may be able to remember and understand of what you have so impressively said; and at all events you may be sure that I shall bear witness to your being thoroughly in earnest at this dreadful time. Now, pray let us think! My esteemed Mr. Cruncher, let us think!”

text: In the docile manner of one long trained to submit under compulsion, he ate and drank what they set before him, and dressed himself in the cloak and the other wrappings they provided for him to wear. When his daughter slipped her arm through his, he answered at once, and took her hand in both of his, holding it fast.
source_text: In the submissive way of one long accustomed to obey under coercion, he ate and drank what they gave him to eat and drink, and put on the cloak and other wrappings, that they gave him to wear. He readily responded to his daughter’s drawing her arm through his, and took--and kept--her hand in both his own.

text: He shut the lattice once more with a fluttering heart, drew in the window and the curtain, and hurried to Lucinda, telling her that her father had been aided by the people and had gone in search of her husband. He found her child and Miss Prentice with her; but it never entered his mind to wonder at their being there until long afterward, when he sat beside them in such stillness as the night could know.
source_text: He closed the lattice again with a fluttering heart, closed the window and the curtain, hastened to Lucie, and told her that her father was assisted by the people, and gone in search of her husband. He found her child and Miss Pross with her; but, it never occurred to him to be surprised by their appearance until a long time afterwards, when he sat watching them in such quiet as the night knew.

text: That night—it was the fourteenth of August—he remained up late and wrote two earnest letters; one to Lucie, in which he explained the urgent duty that compelled him to go to Paris, and set forth at length the reasons for believing he could incur no personal danger there; the other to the Doctor, committing Lucie and their beloved child to his care, and touching on the same matters with the strongest assurances. To each, he said that he would send letters as proof of his safety immediately after reaching Paris.
source_text: That night--it was the fourteenth of August--he sat up late, and wrote two fervent letters; one was to Lucie, explaining the strong obligation he was under to go to Paris, and showing her, at length, the reasons that he had, for feeling confident that he could become involved in no personal danger there; the other was to the Doctor, confiding Lucie and their dear child to his care, and dwelling on the same topics with the strongest assurances. To both, he wrote that he would despatch letters in proof of his safety, immediately after his arrival.

text: Monseigneur departed, and the three stalwart men, releasing themselves from the guilt of having taken his rich pay by proving more than eager to cut his throat on the altar of the rising Republic, one and indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, his house had first been sequestrated and then confiscated. So swiftly did all things move, and so relentlessly did decree succeed decree, that by the third night of the autumn month of September the patriotic agents of the law were in possession of Monseigneur’s house, had marked it with the tricolour, and were drinking brandy in its state apartments.
source_text: Monseigneur gone, and the three strong men absolving themselves from the sin of having drawn his high wages, by being more than ready and willing to cut his throat on the altar of the dawning Republic one and indivisible of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, Monseigneur’s house had been first sequestrated, and then confiscated. For, all things moved so fast, and decree followed decree with that fierce precipitation, that now upon the third night of the autumn month of September, patriot emissaries of the law were in possession of Monseigneur’s house, and had marked it with the tri-colour, and were drinking brandy in its state apartments.

text: Now, which among the many countenances that disclosed themselves to him was truly the face of the buried man, the darkness of the night gave no sign; yet they were all the faces of one whose years had reached some five and forty, differing chiefly in the passions they wore, and in the ghastly ruin of their wasted condition. Pride, scorn, defiance, obstinacy, surrender, and lamentation came one after another; so too did the hollowing of cheek, the corpse-like tint, the gauntness of hand and frame. But in the main the visage was one and the same, and every head was prematurely whitened. A hundred times the half-sleeping traveler addressed this apparition:
source_text: Now, which of the multitude of faces that showed themselves before him was the true face of the buried person, the shadows of the night did not indicate; but they were all the faces of a man of five-and-forty by years, and they differed principally in the passions they expressed, and in the ghastliness of their worn and wasted state. Pride, contempt, defiance, stubbornness, submission, lamentation, succeeded one another; so did varieties of sunken cheek, cadaverous colour, emaciated hands and figures. But the face was in the main one face, and every head was prematurely white. A hundred times the dozing passenger inquired of this spectre:

text: “Mr. Vale,” he continued, in the manner of a man truly studying a hand of cards: “Creature of the prisons, messenger for Republican committees, by turns warder and captive, always informer and secret spy, the more useful here because he is English, since an Englishman arouses less suspicion in such characters than a Frenchman, presents himself to those who employ him under an assumed name. A capital card. Mr. Vale, now in the service of the republican French government, was once in the service of the aristocratic English government, the foe of France and liberty. An admirable card. The conclusion is plain enough in this country of distrust, that Mr. Vale, still drawing wages from the aristocratic English government, is the agent of Pitt, the perfidious enemy of the Republic crouched within its very breast, the English traitor and manufacturer of every evil so often discussed and so hard to discover. A card that cannot easily be trumped. Do you take my meaning, Mr. Vale?”
source_text: “Mr. Barsad,” he went on, in the tone of one who really was looking over a hand at cards: “Sheep of the prisons, emissary of Republican committees, now turnkey, now prisoner, always spy and secret informer, so much the more valuable here for being English that an Englishman is less open to suspicion of subornation in those characters than a Frenchman, represents himself to his employers under a false name. That’s a very good card. Mr. Barsad, now in the employ of the republican French government, was formerly in the employ of the aristocratic English government, the enemy of France and freedom. That’s an excellent card. Inference clear as day in this region of suspicion, that Mr. Barsad, still in the pay of the aristocratic English government, is the spy of Pitt, the treacherous foe of the Republic crouching in its bosom, the English traitor and agent of all mischief so much spoken of and so difficult to find. That’s a card not to be beaten. Have you followed my hand, Mr. Barsad?”

text: “All the village,” continued the road-mender, standing on tiptoe and speaking very softly, “falls silent; all the village murmurs by the fountain; all the village sleeps; all the village dreams of that poor wretch, shut within the bolts and bars of the prison on the rock, never to come out again except to die. In the morning, with my tools on my shoulder and my mouth chewing my piece of black bread as I walk, I go round by the prison on my way to work. There I see him, high up behind the bars of a tall iron cage, bloody and dust-covered just as he was last night, staring out. He has no hand free to wave to me; I do not dare call to him; he looks at me like a dead man.”
source_text: “All the village,” pursued the mender of roads, on tiptoe and in a low voice, “withdraws; all the village whispers by the fountain; all the village sleeps; all the village dreams of that unhappy one, within the locks and bars of the prison on the crag, and never to come out of it, except to perish. In the morning, with my tools upon my shoulder, eating my morsel of black bread as I go, I make a circuit by the prison, on my way to my work. There I see him, high up, behind the bars of a lofty iron cage, bloody and dusty as last night, looking through. He has no hand free, to wave to me; I dare not call to him; he regards me like a dead man.”

text: So when Sunday arrived, the road mender was not in the least delighted, despite his protestations to the contrary, to learn that the lady was to go with the gentleman and himself to Versailles. It was no less upsetting to see the lady knitting all the way there in a public coach; and even more upsetting still to find her afterward among the throng, in the afternoon, her knitting still in hand as the people waited to catch sight of the King’s and Queen’s carriage.
source_text: Therefore, when Sunday came, the mender of roads was not enchanted (though he said he was) to find that madame was to accompany monsieur and himself to Versailles. It was additionally disconcerting to have madame knitting all the way there, in a public conveyance; it was additionally disconcerting yet, to have madame in the crowd in the afternoon, still with her knitting in her hands as the crowd waited to see the carriage of the King and Queen.

text: Madame Vauvert and her husband, monsieur, made their way back in friendly fashion to the heart of Saint-Cloud, while a tiny figure in a blue cap labored on through the blackness and the dust, and along the long, tiring miles of road, slowly bending toward that quarter of the sky where the château of Monsieur the Comte, now gone to his grave, seemed to listen with the trees. So much leisure had the carved stone faces now for listening to the trees and the fountain, that the few village scarecrows who, in search of herbs to eat and bits of dead wood to burn, came within sight of the broad stone court and the stair to the terrace, fancied in their famine-struck minds that the faces had altered. A rumor scarcely lived in the village—had about as frail and shadowy a being there as the people themselves—that when the blade had found its mark, those faces changed from pride to rage and pain; and that when the swinging body was drawn up forty feet above the fountain, they changed yet again, wearing thenceforth and forever a hard, vindictive look of retribution. On the stone face above the great chamber window where the killing had been done, two neat dents were shown in the carved nose, dents everybody knew, though no one had noticed them before; and on those rare occasions when two or three ragged peasants stepped out of the crowd to snatch a hurried glimpse of Monsieur the Comte turned to stone, a thin finger would scarcely have pointed for a moment before they all darted off among the moss and leaves, like the luckier hares that could still make a living there.
source_text: Madame Defarge and monsieur her husband returned amicably to the bosom of Saint Antoine, while a speck in a blue cap toiled through the darkness, and through the dust, and down the weary miles of avenue by the wayside, slowly tending towards that point of the compass where the chateau of Monsieur the Marquis, now in his grave, listened to the whispering trees. Such ample leisure had the stone faces, now, for listening to the trees and to the fountain, that the few village scarecrows who, in their quest for herbs to eat and fragments of dead stick to burn, strayed within sight of the great stone courtyard and terrace staircase, had it borne in upon their starved fancy that the expression of the faces was altered. A rumour just lived in the village--had a faint and bare existence there, as its people had--that when the knife struck home, the faces changed, from faces of pride to faces of anger and pain; also, that when that dangling figure was hauled up forty feet above the fountain, they changed again, and bore a cruel look of being avenged, which they would henceforth bear for ever. In the stone face over the great window of the bed-chamber where the murder was done, two fine dints were pointed out in the sculptured nose, which everybody recognised, and which nobody had seen of old; and on the scarce occasions when two or three ragged peasants emerged from the crowd to take a hurried peep at Monsieur the Marquis petrified, a skinny finger would not have pointed to it for a minute, before they all started away among the moss and leaves, like the more fortunate hares who could find a living there.

text: “I think,” he whispered to Mrs. Baines, after a troubled pause of thought, “I think it would be wiser not to address him at present, nor in any way stir him. I must step into Harwood’s; so I shall go there immediately and return in a little while. Then we will carry him out for a drive into the country, dine there, and everything will be right.”
source_text: “I think,” he whispered to Miss Pross, after anxious consideration, “I think we had best not speak to him just now, or at all disturb him. I must look in at Tellson’s; so I will go there at once and come back presently. Then, we will take him a ride into the country, and dine there, and all will be well.”

text: When the newly married pair returned home, the first to appear with his congratulations was Sydney Carton. They had not been in the house many hours before he came to present himself. He was no better in his habits, his appearance, or his manner; yet there was about him a certain rough honesty of devotion, something in the set of him that was new to Charles Darnay’s observation.
source_text: When the newly-married pair came home, the first person who appeared, to offer his congratulations, was Sydney Carton. They had not been at home many hours, when he presented himself. He was not improved in habits, or in looks, or in manner; but there was a certain rugged air of fidelity about him, which was new to the observation of Charles Darnay.

text: She rested her head against her father’s breast, as once, long ago, she had cradled his poor head upon her own. He was content in the service he had rendered her, repaid for all he had endured, and proud of his own courage. “You must not be weak, my darling,” he said gently. “Do not tremble so. I have saved him.”
source_text: She laid her head upon her father’s breast, as she had laid his poor head on her own breast, long, long ago. He was happy in the return he had made her, he was recompensed for his suffering, he was proud of his strength. “You must not be weak, my darling,” he remonstrated; “don’t tremble so. I have saved him.”

text: The Farmer-General was a magnificent man. Thirty horses stood in his stables, twenty-four male servants filled his halls, and six ladies-in-waiting attended his wife. Though he affected to do nothing but pillage and forage wherever he could, the Farmer-General—whatever his marriage might have done for public virtue—was at least the most substantial of the figures who appeared at Monseigneur’s hotel that day.
source_text: A sumptuous man was the Farmer-General. Thirty horses stood in his stables, twenty-four male domestics sat in his halls, six body-women waited on his wife. As one who pretended to do nothing but plunder and forage where he could, the Farmer-General--howsoever his matrimonial relations conduced to social morality--was at least the greatest reality among the personages who attended at the hotel of Monseigneur that day.

text: Customers came in, and the little group was dispersed. The English customer paid for what he had taken, puzzled over his change, and, as a stranger, asked to be directed toward the National Palace. Madame Defarge escorted him to the door and laid her arm on his as she indicated the road. The English customer then had his reflections, that it might be a worthy deed to seize that arm, raise it, and strike sharply and deep beneath it.
source_text: Customers entered, and the group was broken up. The English customer paid for what he had had, perplexedly counted his change, and asked, as a stranger, to be directed towards the National Palace. Madame Defarge took him to the door, and put her arm on his, in pointing out the road. The English customer was not without his reflections then, that it might be a good deed to seize that arm, lift it, and strike under it sharp and deep.

text: Silence in the court! Yesterday Charles Darnay had pleaded Not Guilty to an indictment that charged him, with endless legal clatter, with being a false traitor to our serene, illustrious, excellent, and so on, prince, our Lord the King, because he had, on various occasions and by various means, helped Lewis, the King of France, in his wars against our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so on; that is to say, by traveling back and forth between the territories of our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so on, and those of the said French Lewis, and by wickedly, falsely, traitorously, and in other bad-adverbial ways, revealing to the said French Lewis what forces our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so on, had prepared to send to Canada and North America. This much Jerry, whose head grew more and more prickly as the legal words bristled it, understood with great satisfaction, and thus came, by a roundabout path, to the realization that the aforesaid, and repeatedly aforesaid, Charles Darnay was standing there before him on his trial; that the jury were being sworn in; and that Mr. Attorney-General was getting ready to begin.
source_text: Silence in the court! Charles Darnay had yesterday pleaded Not Guilty to an indictment denouncing him (with infinite jingle and jangle) for that he was a false traitor to our serene, illustrious, excellent, and so forth, prince, our Lord the King, by reason of his having, on divers occasions, and by divers means and ways, assisted Lewis, the French King, in his wars against our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so forth; that was to say, by coming and going, between the dominions of our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so forth, and those of the said French Lewis, and wickedly, falsely, traitorously, and otherwise evil-adverbiously, revealing to the said French Lewis what forces our said serene, illustrious, excellent, and so forth, had in preparation to send to Canada and North America. This much, Jerry, with his head becoming more and more spiky as the law terms bristled it, made out with huge satisfaction, and so arrived circuitously at the understanding that the aforesaid, and over and over again aforesaid, Charles Darnay, stood there before him upon his trial; that the jury were swearing in; and that Mr. Attorney-General was making ready to speak.

text: Everyone within reach had left off either their work or their laziness and hurried to the place to drink the wine. The rough, uneven stones of the street, jutting out in every direction and seeming almost made for the express purpose of crippling any living thing that came near, had checked it into little pools; each pool was ringed by its own pushing cluster or crowd, according to its size. Some men knelt and cupped their two hands together to sip, or tried to help women, who leaned over their shoulders, drink before the wine slipped away between their fingers. Others, men and women alike, dipped into the puddles with tiny mugs of broken pottery, or even with women’s handkerchiefs from their heads, which were wrung dry into infants’ mouths; others built little banks of mud to hold the wine back as it flowed; others, prompted by watchers at upper windows, darted hither and thither to catch small streams that had broken off and taken new courses; others gave themselves to the soaked, wine-stained splinters of the cask, licking, and even chewing, the wetter fragments softened by the wine with greedy pleasure. There was no drain to carry the wine away, and not only was every drop taken up, but so much mud with it as well, that one might have thought a scavenger had passed through the street, if anyone who knew the place could have believed in so miraculous a sight.
source_text: All the people within reach had suspended their business, or their idleness, to run to the spot and drink the wine. The rough, irregular stones of the street, pointing every way, and designed, one might have thought, expressly to lame all living creatures that approached them, had dammed it into little pools; these were surrounded, each by its own jostling group or crowd, according to its size. Some men kneeled down, made scoops of their two hands joined, and sipped, or tried to help women, who bent over their shoulders, to sip, before the wine had all run out between their fingers. Others, men and women, dipped in the puddles with little mugs of mutilated earthenware, or even with handkerchiefs from women’s heads, which were squeezed dry into infants’ mouths; others made small mud-embankments, to stem the wine as it ran; others, directed by lookers-on up at high windows, darted here and there, to cut off little streams of wine that started away in new directions; others devoted themselves to the sodden and lee-dyed pieces of the cask, licking, and even champing the moister wine-rotted fragments with eager relish. There was no drainage to carry off the wine, and not only did it all get taken up, but so much mud got taken up along with it, that there might have been a scavenger in the street, if anybody acquainted with it could have believed in such a miraculous presence.

text: “Foolish, pig-like woman!” said Madame Defarge with a frown. “I will not accept an answer from you. I insist on seeing her. Either tell her that I demand to see her, or step away from the door and let me pass to her!” She said this, making an angry, explanatory sweep of her right arm.
source_text: “Woman imbecile and pig-like!” said Madame Defarge, frowning. “I take no answer from you. I demand to see her. Either tell her that I demand to see her, or stand out of the way of the door and let me go to her!” This, with an angry explanatory wave of her right arm.

text: “Then, I suppose,” said Mr. Gilling, “I have been treated most indifferently, and should certainly have been consulted in the choosing of my own model. But enough of that! Now, my dear Helena,” and he drew his arm tenderly about her waist, “I can hear them stirring in the adjoining room, and Miss Prout and I, being two highly proper persons of business, are unwilling to miss the last chance of saying what we know you would gladly hear. You leave your excellent father, my dear, in hands as watchful and affectionate as your own; he shall be cared for with every imaginable solicitude; for the next two weeks, while you are in Kent and the neighbourhood, even Hargrove’s shall be allowed to go to ruin (in a manner of speaking) before him. And when, at the end of those weeks, he comes to join you and your beloved bridegroom upon your further two weeks’ excursion into Cumberland, you shall be able to say that we have sent him to you in the best of health and the happiest of spirits. Hush, I hear Some One’s footstep at the door. Let me give my dear child an old bachelor’s blessing, before Some One comes to reclaim what is his own.”
source_text: “Then, I think,” said Mr. Lorry, “that I was very unhandsomely dealt with, and that I ought to have had a voice in the selection of my pattern. Enough! Now, my dear Lucie,” drawing his arm soothingly round her waist, “I hear them moving in the next room, and Miss Pross and I, as two formal folks of business, are anxious not to lose the final opportunity of saying something to you that you wish to hear. You leave your good father, my dear, in hands as earnest and as loving as your own; he shall be taken every conceivable care of; during the next fortnight, while you are in Warwickshire and thereabouts, even Tellson’s shall go to the wall (comparatively speaking) before him. And when, at the fortnight’s end, he comes to join you and your beloved husband, on your other fortnight’s trip in Wales, you shall say that we have sent him to you in the best health and in the happiest frame. Now, I hear Somebody’s step coming to the door. Let me kiss my dear girl with an old-fashioned bachelor blessing, before Somebody comes to claim his own.”

text: The wine was red wine, and it had stained the ground of the narrow street in the Saint Antoine suburb of Paris where it had been spilled. It had stained many hands as well, and many faces, and many bare feet, and many wooden shoes. The hands of the man sawing the wood left red marks on the billets; and the forehead of the woman nursing her baby was marked by the stain of the old rag she had wound around her head again. Those who had been greedy with the staves of the cask had gained a savage-looking smear around their mouths; and one lank joker, so begrimed that his head seemed more out of a long filthy nightcap-bag than in it, wrote on a wall with his finger dipped in the muddy dregs of the wine—BLOOD.
source_text: The wine was red wine, and had stained the ground of the narrow street in the suburb of Saint Antoine, in Paris, where it was spilled. It had stained many hands, too, and many faces, and many naked feet, and many wooden shoes. The hands of the man who sawed the wood, left red marks on the billets; and the forehead of the woman who nursed her baby, was stained with the stain of the old rag she wound about her head again. Those who had been greedy with the staves of the cask, had acquired a tigerish smear about the mouth; and one tall joker so besmirched, his head more out of a long squalid bag of a nightcap than in it, scrawled upon a wall with his finger dipped in muddy wine-lees--BLOOD.

text: The Concord bed-chamber was always allotted to a mail passenger, and as mail passengers were always so thickly muffled from head to foot, the room had the curious distinction, in the eyes of the Royal George household, that though only one sort of man was seen to enter it, every sort and condition of man seemed to emerge from it. Accordingly, another waiter, two porters, several maidservants, and the landlady herself happened to be lingering at various points of the passage between the Concord and the coffee-room when a gentleman of sixty, neatly attired in a brown suit of clothes, somewhat worn but carefully preserved, with broad square cuffs and large flap pockets, came along on his way to breakfast.
source_text: The Concord bed-chamber being always assigned to a passenger by the mail, and passengers by the mail being always heavily wrapped up from head to foot, the room had the odd interest for the establishment of the Royal George, that although but one kind of man was seen to go into it, all kinds and varieties of men came out of it. Consequently, another drawer, and two porters, and several maids and the landlady, were all loitering by accident at various points of the road between the Concord and the coffee-room, when a gentleman of sixty, formally dressed in a brown suit of clothes, pretty well worn, but very well kept, with large square cuffs and large flaps to the pockets, passed along on his way to his breakfast.

text: “All labor ceases; everyone is gathered there; no one drives the cattle out, and the cattle remain among the rest. At noon comes the beat of drums. In the night the soldiers have entered the prison, and he stands amid a great many soldiers. He is bound as before, and in his mouth there is a gag—fastened so with a tight cord that it makes him seem almost to be smiling.” He indicated it by pressing his two thumbs into his cheeks, drawing them from the corners of his mouth toward his ears. “On the summit of the gallows the knife is fixed, blade upward, its point lifted into the air. He is hung there forty feet above the ground—and left to hang, poisoning the water.”
source_text: “All work is stopped, all assemble there, nobody leads the cows out, the cows are there with the rest. At midday, the roll of drums. Soldiers have marched into the prison in the night, and he is in the midst of many soldiers. He is bound as before, and in his mouth there is a gag--tied so, with a tight string, making him look almost as if he laughed.” He suggested it, by creasing his face with his two thumbs, from the corners of his mouth to his ears. “On the top of the gallows is fixed the knife, blade upwards, with its point in the air. He is hanged there forty feet high--and is left hanging, poisoning the water.”

text: I noticed this within the first minute of studying the patient; in her restless struggles she had turned onto her face at the edge of the bed, drawn the end of the scarf into her mouth, and was in danger of suffocating. My first move was to reach out and free her breathing; and as I shifted the scarf aside, the embroidery in the corner caught my eye.
source_text: “I saw this, within the first minute of my contemplation of the patient; for, in her restless strivings she had turned over on her face on the edge of the bed, had drawn the end of the scarf into her mouth, and was in danger of suffocation. My first act was to put out my hand to relieve her breathing; and in moving the scarf aside, the embroidery in the corner caught my sight.

text: The panther then settled himself full length upon a sofa beside the drinking-table, while the hyena took his own paper-littered desk on the farther side of it, with the bottles and glasses at his elbow. Both made free use of the drinking-table, though in quite different fashions; the panther mostly lounging back with his hands in his sash, gazing into the fire, or now and then idly turning over some lighter paper; the hyena, with knit brows and a face all fixed attention, so absorbed in his labor that his eyes scarcely followed the hand he put out for his glass—which often groped for a minute or more before it found the waiting rim to his lips. Two or three times the question in hand grew so tangled that the hyena judged it necessary to rise and soak his towels afresh. From these excursions to the pitcher and basin he came back with such uncouth changes in his wet headgear as no language could properly convey; and these were rendered the more comical by the solemn anxiety with which he bore them.
source_text: The lion then composed himself on his back on a sofa on one side of the drinking-table, while the jackal sat at his own paper-bestrewn table proper, on the other side of it, with the bottles and glasses ready to his hand. Both resorted to the drinking-table without stint, but each in a different way; the lion for the most part reclining with his hands in his waistband, looking at the fire, or occasionally flirting with some lighter document; the jackal, with knitted brows and intent face, so deep in his task, that his eyes did not even follow the hand he stretched out for his glass--which often groped about, for a minute or more, before it found the glass for his lips. Two or three times, the matter in hand became so knotty, that the jackal found it imperative on him to get up, and steep his towels anew. From these pilgrimages to the jug and basin, he returned with such eccentricities of damp headgear as no words can describe; which were made the more ludicrous by his anxious gravity.

text: The village fountain flowed unseen and unheard, and the fountain at the chateau fell unseen and unheard as well—both dissolving, like the minutes dropping from the spring of Time—through three dark hours. Then the grey water of both began to turn ghostlike in the light, and the eyes of the stone faces at the chateau were opened.
source_text: The fountain in the village flowed unseen and unheard, and the fountain at the chateau dropped unseen and unheard--both melting away, like the minutes that were falling from the spring of Time--through three dark hours. Then, the grey water of both began to be ghostly in the light, and the eyes of the stone faces of the chateau were opened.

text: Giving a promise here, a smile there, a soft word to one fortunate dependent and a careless gesture to another, the great lord moved graciously through his apartments toward the farthest reaches of the Circle of Truth. There he turned about, and came back again, and so in proper time found himself enclosed in his sanctuary by the chocolate-colored attendants, and was seen no more.
source_text: Bestowing a word of promise here and a smile there, a whisper on one happy slave and a wave of the hand on another, Monseigneur affably passed through his rooms to the remote region of the Circumference of Truth. There, Monseigneur turned, and came back again, and so in due course of time got himself shut up in his sanctuary by the chocolate sprites, and was seen no more.

text: Miss Pross, yielding herself to his decision, the plan was arranged with scrupulous care. Having ample time for his accustomed and orderly toilette, Mr. Lorry appeared at the breakfast hour in his customary white linen, and with his customary trim leg. The Doctor was called in the usual manner, and came down to breakfast.
source_text: Miss Pross, submitting herself to his judgment, the scheme was worked out with care. Having abundance of time for his usual methodical toilette, Mr. Lorry presented himself at the breakfast-hour in his usual white linen, and with his usual neat leg. The Doctor was summoned in the usual way, and came to breakfast.

text: The traveller made his way slowly, he who journeyed toward Paris from England in the autumn of the year seventeen hundred and ninety-two. He would have found delay enough in bad roads, bad conveyances, and bad horses, even had the fallen and unhappy King of France still sat upon his throne in all his splendour; but the altered times presented hindrances of another sort besides these. At every town gate and village tollhouse stood their bands of citizen-patriots, national muskets in a most ready and explosive condition, stopping all who came and went, questioning them closely, examining their papers, searching for their names in lists of their own, sending them back, or onward, or into custody, as their whim or caprice dictated, in the service of the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.
source_text: The traveller fared slowly on his way, who fared towards Paris from England in the autumn of the year one thousand seven hundred and ninety-two. More than enough of bad roads, bad equipages, and bad horses, he would have encountered to delay him, though the fallen and unfortunate King of France had been upon his throne in all his glory; but, the changed times were fraught with other obstacles than these. Every town-gate and village taxing-house had its band of citizen-patriots, with their national muskets in a most explosive state of readiness, who stopped all comers and goers, cross-questioned them, inspected their papers, looked for their names in lists of their own, turned them back, or sent them on, or stopped them and laid them in hold, as their capricious judgment or fancy deemed best for the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.

text: “No, dear Doctor Vale. As you have been, so am I, a willing exile from France; as you were forced away by its confusions, its cruelties, and its sorrows, so have I been driven from it; as you have laboured to make a life apart from it by your own hands, and to look for better days, so do I. I ask only to share your lot, to dwell beneath your roof, to live beside you, and to be true to you until I die. I would not usurp from Elise her dear privilege of being your child, your companion, your friend; only to strengthen it, if it may be, and draw her still more tenderly and closely to you.”
source_text: “No, dear Doctor Manette. Like you, a voluntary exile from France; like you, driven from it by its distractions, oppressions, and miseries; like you, striving to live away from it by my own exertions, and trusting in a happier future; I look only to sharing your fortunes, sharing your life and home, and being faithful to you to the death. Not to divide with Lucie her privilege as your child, companion, and friend; but to come in aid of it, and bind her closer to you, if such a thing can be.”

text: “Gentlemen,” said her husband, whose keen eye had been steadily upon her, “good day. The room, fitted up in bachelor style, which you wished to inspect, and were asking for when I went out, is on the fifth floor. The stairway entrance opens into the little courtyard just here on the left,” he said, indicating the spot with his hand, “close to the window of my shop. But now that I think of it, one of you has already been there and can lead the way. Gentlemen, farewell!”
source_text: “Gentlemen,” said her husband, who had kept his bright eye observantly upon her, “good day. The chamber, furnished bachelor-fashion, that you wished to see, and were inquiring for when I stepped out, is on the fifth floor. The doorway of the staircase gives on the little courtyard close to the left here,” pointing with his hand, “near to the window of my establishment. But, now that I remember, one of you has already been there, and can show the way. Gentlemen, adieu!”
