text: Young Jerry, walking beside his father with the stool tucked under his arm along the bright, crowded length of Fleet Street, was a very different Young Jerry from the boy of the night before, who had fled home through darkness and emptiness from his grim pursuer. With the coming of day his cunning had returned, and with the passing of night his fears had vanished—in which respects, it is not unlikely, he had plenty of companions in Fleet Street and the City of London that fine morning.
source_text: Young Jerry, walking with the stool under his arm at his father’s side along sunny and crowded Fleet-street, was a very different Young Jerry from him of the previous night, running home through darkness and solitude from his grim pursuer. His cunning was fresh with the day, and his qualms were gone with the night--in which particulars it is not improbable that he had compeers in Fleet-street and the City of London, that fine morning.

text: Mr. Wrench did not take part in the final games, but lingered instead in the churchyard, where he might confer and commiserate with the undertakers. The place exerted a calming effect upon him. He fetched a pipe from a nearby alehouse, and, smoking it, stood at the railings gazing in and giving the ground his deliberate consideration.
source_text: Mr. Cruncher did not assist at the closing sports, but had remained behind in the churchyard, to confer and condole with the undertakers. The place had a soothing influence on him. He procured a pipe from a neighbouring public-house, and smoked it, looking in at the railings and maturely considering the spot.

text: “There’s no time to lose. I’m fully prepared, but there are precautions to take that could not be taken until he was actually called before the Tribunal. He has not yet received the notice, but I know he will soon be summoned for tomorrow and taken to the Conciergerie; I have advance warning. You are not afraid?”
source_text: “There is no time to lose. I am well prepared, but there are precautions to be taken, that could not be taken until he was actually summoned before the Tribunal. He has not received the notice yet, but I know that he will presently be summoned for to-morrow, and removed to the Conciergerie; I have timely information. You are not afraid?”

text: The man slept on, heedless of hailstorms and breaks of brightness, of sunlight warming his face and shadow falling across it, of the dull pellets of ice striking his body and the diamonds the sun turned them into, until the sun sank low in the west and the sky burned with color. Then the road mender, having gathered up his tools and made all ready to go down into the village, woke him.
source_text: The man slept on, indifferent to showers of hail and intervals of brightness, to sunshine on his face and shadow, to the paltering lumps of dull ice on his body and the diamonds into which the sun changed them, until the sun was low in the west, and the sky was glowing. Then, the mender of roads having got his tools together and all things ready to go down into the village, roused him.

text: The carriage came to a halt as soon as the driver could draw up his horses, and the same voice called me by name. I answered. By then the carriage had gone so far ahead of me that two gentlemen had time to open the door and step out before I reached it.
source_text: “The carriage stopped as soon as the driver could rein in his horses, and the same voice called to me by my name. I answered. The carriage was then so far in advance of me that two gentlemen had time to open the door and alight before I came up with it.

text: But when they reached the town of Montreuil—which they did at dusk, as the streets were thronged with people—he could not hide from himself that the state of things was most alarming. An ill-omened crowd assembled to watch him alight at the posting-house, and many voices cried aloud, “Down with the exile!”
source_text: But when they came to the town of Beauvais--which they did at eventide, when the streets were filled with people--he could not conceal from himself that the aspect of affairs was very alarming. An ominous crowd gathered to see him dismount of the posting-yard, and many voices called out loudly, “Down with the emigrant!”

text: He knew very well that, in his horror of the act that had brought to a close the evil doings and evil name of the old family house, in his bitter suspicions of his uncle, and in the disgust with which his conscience looked upon the crumbling structure he was expected to support, he had behaved imperfectly. He knew very well that, in his love for Lucie, his giving up of his social position, though by no means a new idea in his own mind, had been rushed and incomplete. He knew that he ought to have worked it out methodically and kept watch over it, and that he had meant to do so, and that it had never been done.
source_text: He knew very well, that in his horror of the deed which had culminated the bad deeds and bad reputation of the old family house, in his resentful suspicions of his uncle, and in the aversion with which his conscience regarded the crumbling fabric that he was supposed to uphold, he had acted imperfectly. He knew very well, that in his love for Lucie, his renunciation of his social place, though by no means new to his own mind, had been hurried and incomplete. He knew that he ought to have systematically worked it out and supervised it, and that he had meant to do it, and that it had never been done.

text: “You weren’t. And if you had been, I shouldn’t have taken the liberty with you. Here now! Your mother is a fine woman, young Jerry, going off to pray against your father’s prosperity. You’ve got a dutiful mother, you have, my son. You’ve got a religious mother, you have, my boy: going and throwing herself down, and praying that the bread and butter may be snatched from the mouth of her only child.”
source_text: “You weren’t. And if you were, I won’t be took the liberty with. Here! your mother’s a nice woman, young Jerry, going a praying agin your father’s prosperity. You’ve got a dutiful mother, you have, my son. You’ve got a religious mother, you have, my boy: going and flopping herself down, and praying that the bread-and-butter may be snatched out of the mouth of her only child.”

text: On a night in mid-July, seventeen hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came home late from Tellson’s and seated himself with Lucie and her husband by the darkened window. It was a hot, restless night, and the three of them were all reminded of that old Sunday evening when they had watched the lightning from the same spot.
source_text: On a night in mid-July, one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came in late, from Tellson’s, and sat himself down by Lucie and her husband in the dark window. It was a hot, wild night, and they were all three reminded of the old Sunday night when they had looked at the lightning from the same place.

text: “Monseigneur, only the good God knows; I do not. I do not ask for that. All I beg is that a little stone or piece of wood, bearing my husband’s name, be put above him, so people may know where he is buried. Otherwise the spot will soon be forgotten, and it will never be found again when I too am dead of the same sickness, and I shall be laid beneath some other poor mound of grass. Monseigneur, there are so many of them, and they multiply so quickly, and there is such misery. Monseigneur! Monseigneur!”
source_text: “Monseigneur, the good God knows; but I don’t ask it. My petition is, that a morsel of stone or wood, with my husband’s name, may be placed over him to show where he lies. Otherwise, the place will be quickly forgotten, it will never be found when I am dead of the same malady, I shall be laid under some other heap of poor grass. Monseigneur, they are so many, they increase so fast, there is so much want. Monseigneur! Monseigneur!”

text: Most likely, Monsieur Valcour endured a long vigil there, with the distant château serving him for fire and taper, and the pounding at his door, together with the pealing of festive bells, supplying him with music; not to speak of an unlucky lantern hung across the road before his posting-house gate, which the village seemed in no small hurry to remove in his behalf. A cruel suspense, indeed, to spend an entire summer night upon the edge of the black sea, prepared for that plunge into it on which Monsieur Valcour had made up his mind! Yet the kindly dawn at last came on, the village rushlights burned themselves low and out, the crowd fortunately broke away, and Monsieur Valcour descended, for the time being, with his life still in his keeping.
source_text: Probably, Monsieur Gabelle passed a long night up there, with the distant chateau for fire and candle, and the beating at his door, combined with the joy-ringing, for music; not to mention his having an ill-omened lamp slung across the road before his posting-house gate, which the village showed a lively inclination to displace in his favour. A trying suspense, to be passing a whole summer night on the brink of the black ocean, ready to take that plunge into it upon which Monsieur Gabelle had resolved! But, the friendly dawn appearing at last, and the rush-candles of the village guttering out, the people happily dispersed, and Monsieur Gabelle came down bringing his life with him for that while.

text: There was not the slightest hint of pity, grief, or fellow feeling in that reply. The speaker appeared only to admit that it was troublesome to have a creature of that different sort dying there, and that it would have been better had he perished in the ordinary, unnoticed manner of his verminous kind. He was wholly incapable of any compassionate thought for the boy, or for the fate awaiting him.
source_text: “There was no touch of pity, sorrow, or kindred humanity, in this answer. The speaker seemed to acknowledge that it was inconvenient to have that different order of creature dying there, and that it would have been better if he had died in the usual obscure routine of his vermin kind. He was quite incapable of any compassionate feeling about the boy, or about his fate.

text: When the performance ended, the tremor in the air swelled into a small storm, and the dainty little bells went chiming downstairs. Before long, only one man remained of the whole crowd, and he, with his hat tucked under his arm and his snuff-box in his hand, moved slowly among the mirrors on his way out.
source_text: The show being over, the flutter in the air became quite a little storm, and the precious little bells went ringing downstairs. There was soon but one person left of all the crowd, and he, with his hat under his arm and his snuff-box in his hand, slowly passed among the mirrors on his way out.

text: “I had no chance to ask her anything until I had told the brothers that she was sinking rapidly and could not survive another day. Up to that time, though no one ever came before her consciousness except the woman and myself, one or the other of them had always kept jealous watch behind the curtain at the head of the bed whenever I was present. But once I said as much, they appeared indifferent to whatever exchange I might have with her, as though—I could not help thinking—I were dying as well.
source_text: “I had no opportunity of asking her any question, until I had told the brothers she was sinking fast, and could not live another day. Until then, though no one was ever presented to her consciousness save the woman and myself, one or other of them had always jealously sat behind the curtain at the head of the bed when I was there. But when it came to that, they seemed careless what communication I might hold with her; as if--the thought passed through my mind--I were dying too.

text: It was a long, mournful sound, like a sigh—almost like a sob. It drew Mr. Lorry’s gaze to Carton’s face, turned toward the fire. A light, or a shadow—the old gentleman could not have told which—slipped across it as quickly as a change that sweeps over a hillside on a wild, bright day, and he raised his foot to push back one of the small blazing logs, which was rolling forward. He was wearing the white riding-coat and top-boots then in fashion, and the firelight striking those pale surfaces made him look very pale indeed, with his long brown hair, left uncut and hanging loose around him. His disregard of the fire was unusual enough to draw a warning from Mr. Lorry; his boot was still on the hot embers of the burning log when it gave way beneath his weight.
source_text: It was a long, grieving sound, like a sigh--almost like a sob. It attracted Mr. Lorry’s eyes to Carton’s face, which was turned to the fire. A light, or a shade (the old gentleman could not have said which), passed from it as swiftly as a change will sweep over a hill-side on a wild bright day, and he lifted his foot to put back one of the little flaming logs, which was tumbling forward. He wore the white riding-coat and top-boots, then in vogue, and the light of the fire touching their light surfaces made him look very pale, with his long brown hair, all untrimmed, hanging loose about him. His indifference to fire was sufficiently remarkable to elicit a word of remonstrance from Mr. Lorry; his boot was still upon the hot embers of the flaming log, when it had broken under the weight of his foot.

text: When Mr. Lorry had finished his breakfast, he went out for a walk along the beach. The little narrow, crooked town of Dover tucked itself away from the shore and thrust its head into the chalk cliffs, like a sea-bird that had lost its way. The beach was a wilderness of sand and stones heaped and hurled about in confusion, and the sea did as it pleased, and what it pleased was ruin. It roared against the town, and roared against the cliffs, and was forever battering the coast to pieces. The air among the houses had so strong a savour of fish that one might have fancied sick fish came up to be dipped in it, as sick people went down to be dipped in the sea. A little fishing was carried on in the port, and much wandering about at night, and gazing out to sea: especially at those times when the tide was making, and close upon flood. Small shopkeepers, who appeared to do no business at all, sometimes somehow grew rich all at once, and it was notable that no one in the neighbourhood could abide a lamplighter.
source_text: When Mr. Lorry had finished his breakfast, he went out for a stroll on the beach. The little narrow, crooked town of Dover hid itself away from the beach, and ran its head into the chalk cliffs, like a marine ostrich. The beach was a desert of heaps of sea and stones tumbling wildly about, and the sea did what it liked, and what it liked was destruction. It thundered at the town, and thundered at the cliffs, and brought the coast down, madly. The air among the houses was of so strong a piscatory flavour that one might have supposed sick fish went up to be dipped in it, as sick people went down to be dipped in the sea. A little fishing was done in the port, and a quantity of strolling about by night, and looking seaward: particularly at those times when the tide made, and was near flood. Small tradesmen, who did no business whatever, sometimes unaccountably realised large fortunes, and it was remarkable that nobody in the neighbourhood could endure a lamplighter.

text: As he jogged back with the note he had been charged to carry to the night watchman in his little shelter at the gate of Mellor’s Bank, near Temple Bar, from whom it was to pass to higher powers inside, the night’s dimness assumed for him whatever forms the errand suggested, and for the mare whatever shapes sprang from her own particular anxieties. They appeared to be many indeed, for she started at every darkness that crossed the road.
source_text: While he trotted back with the message he was to deliver to the night watchman in his box at the door of Tellson’s Bank, by Temple Bar, who was to deliver it to greater authorities within, the shadows of the night took such shapes to him as arose out of the message, and took such shapes to the mare as arose out of _her_ private topics of uneasiness. They seemed to be numerous, for she shied at every shadow on the road.

text: The friends of the man who had been acquitted had already gone away, having believed—because he had said as much himself—that he would not be freed that night. Most of the lights in the corridors had been put out, and the iron gates were being shut with clanging, grinding sounds. The bleak place stood empty, until the next morning’s business of gallows, pillory, whipping-post, and branding iron should fill it again. Lucie Manette, walking between her father and Mr. Darnay, came out into the open air. A hackney coach was summoned, and father and daughter drove off in it.
source_text: The friends of the acquitted prisoner had dispersed, under the impression--which he himself had originated--that he would not be released that night. The lights were nearly all extinguished in the passages, the iron gates were being closed with a jar and a rattle, and the dismal place was deserted until to-morrow morning’s interest of gallows, pillory, whipping-post, and branding-iron, should repeople it. Walking between her father and Mr. Darnay, Lucie Manette passed into the open air. A hackney-coach was called, and the father and daughter departed in it.

Having relieved his noble breast of its burden, he would have withdrawn himself with becoming modesty, but that the wigged gentleman with the papers before him, seated not far from Mr. Lorry, begged leave to put a few questions to him. text: “Don’t you go wearing your wits out over that fellow,” snapped the argumentative Mr. Snagg; “you’ll have your hands full enough minding that gentleman. And see here! Once again!”—Mr. Snagg could not keep himself from making rather a showy display of his generosity—“I’d take you by the throat and throttle you for half a guinea.”
source_text: “Never you trouble your head about this man,” retorted the contentious Mr. Cruncher; “you’ll have trouble enough with giving your attention to that gentleman. And look here! Once more!”--Mr. Cruncher could not be restrained from making rather an ostentatious parade of his liberality--“I’d catch hold of your throat and choke you for half a guinea.”

text: Mr. Stryver forced his way through the law like some mighty engine driving onward through muddy water, and he drew his useful friend after him like a boat being towed behind. And as the boat thus favored is commonly in a sorry condition, and for the most part half submerged, so Sydney led a drowned sort of life. But habit, easy and powerful, and in his case, unhappily far stronger than any stirring sense of merit or shame, fixed that as the life he was to live; and he thought no more of escaping from his position as a lion’s jackal than any true jackal might be imagined to think of rising into a lion. Stryver was prosperous; he had married a ruddy widow with property and three sons, who had nothing notably distinguished about them except the straight hair on their round little heads.
source_text: Mr. Stryver shouldered his way through the law, like some great engine forcing itself through turbid water, and dragged his useful friend in his wake, like a boat towed astern. As the boat so favoured is usually in a rough plight, and mostly under water, so, Sydney had a swamped life of it. But, easy and strong custom, unhappily so much easier and stronger in him than any stimulating sense of desert or disgrace, made it the life he was to lead; and he no more thought of emerging from his state of lion’s jackal, than any real jackal may be supposed to think of rising to be a lion. Stryver was rich; had married a florid widow with property and three boys, who had nothing particularly shining about them but the straight hair of their dumpling heads.

text: “The highest good of which I am now capable, Miss Delaney, I have come to seek and make real. Let me bear away through the remainder of my mistaken life the memory that I unclosed my heart to you, the very last in all the world; and that, even at this hour, there was yet something in me which you might grieve over and compassionately pity.”
source_text: “The utmost good that I am capable of now, Miss Manette, I have come here to realise. Let me carry through the rest of my misdirected life, the remembrance that I opened my heart to you, last of all the world; and that there was something left in me at this time which you could deplore and pity.”

text: Saint Antoine had been, that morning, a great dusky mass of scarecrows surging to and fro, with frequent flashes of light above the tossing heads, where steel blades and bayonets gleamed in the sun. A tremendous roar rose from the throat of Saint Antoine, and a forest of bare arms fought upward in the air like withered branches of trees in a winter wind: all the fingers convulsively clutching at every weapon, or at any object that might pass for one, flung up from the depths below, no matter how distant.
source_text: Saint Antoine had been, that morning, a vast dusky mass of scarecrows heaving to and fro, with frequent gleams of light above the billowy heads, where steel blades and bayonets shone in the sun. A tremendous roar arose from the throat of Saint Antoine, and a forest of naked arms struggled in the air like shrivelled branches of trees in a winter wind: all the fingers convulsively clutching at every weapon or semblance of a weapon that was thrown up from the depths below, no matter how far off.

text: The chateau awoke later, as befitted its rank, but it awakened by degrees and with certainty. First the solitary boar-spears and hunting knives were reddened as of old; then they flashed keenly in the morning sun; now doors and windows stood wide open, horses in their stalls turned their heads toward the light and freshness flowing through the entrances, leaves shone and stirred at the iron-barred windows, and dogs strained hard against their chains, rearing up in impatience to be set free.
source_text: The chateau awoke later, as became its quality, but awoke gradually and surely. First, the lonely boar-spears and knives of the chase had been reddened as of old; then, had gleamed trenchant in the morning sunshine; now, doors and windows were thrown open, horses in their stables looked round over their shoulders at the light and freshness pouring in at doorways, leaves sparkled and rustled at iron-grated windows, dogs pulled hard at their chains, and reared impatient to be loosed.

text: “I’m going,” said Mr. Stryver, resting his arms in a confidential manner on the desk; and although it was a large double one, there did not seem to be half enough desk to hold him. “I’m going to propose marriage to your charming little friend, Miss Manette, Mr. Lorry.”
source_text: “I am going,” said Mr. Stryver, leaning his arms confidentially on the desk: whereupon, although it was a large double one, there appeared to be not half desk enough for him: “I am going to make an offer of myself in marriage to your agreeable little friend, Miss Manette, Mr. Lorry.”

text: “In the name of this company of fellow sufferers,” said a gentleman of polished bearing and manner, stepping a little in advance, “I have the honour to bid you welcome to La Force, and to offer you my sympathy in the misfortune that has conducted you hither. May it end speedily and well! It might elsewhere seem an impertinence, but it is not so here, to inquire your name and station?”
source_text: “In the name of the assembled companions in misfortune,” said a gentleman of courtly appearance and address, coming forward, “I have the honour of giving you welcome to La Force, and of condoling with you on the calamity that has brought you among us. May it soon terminate happily! It would be an impertinence elsewhere, but it is not so here, to ask your name and condition?”

text: These pursuits carried her through to the month of December, in which her father moved among the terrors with a clear and steady mind. One afternoon, when snow was falling lightly, she came to the familiar corner. It was a day of fierce celebration and a holiday. As she had walked along, she had noticed the houses adorned with little pikes and with small red caps set on top of them; they were also hung with tricolored ribbons; and there was the usual slogan, with tricolored letters favored above all: Republic One and Indivisible. Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death!
source_text: These occupations brought her round to the December month, wherein her father walked among the terrors with a steady head. On a lightly-snowing afternoon she arrived at the usual corner. It was a day of some wild rejoicing, and a festival. She had seen the houses, as she came along, decorated with little pikes, and with little red caps stuck upon them; also, with tricoloured ribbons; also, with the standard inscription (tricoloured letters were the favourite), Republic One and Indivisible. Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death!

text: Such a heart Madame Defarge carried beneath her rough cloak. Worn with seeming neglect, it was in its way a fitting garment, and her dark hair showed richly under her coarse red cap. Hidden in her breast was a loaded pistol. Hidden at her waist was a sharpened dagger. So equipped, and moving with the assured step of one such as she, with the lithe ease of a woman who had once, in girlhood, often gone barefoot and bare-legged over the brown seashore sand, Madame Defarge made her way through the streets.
source_text: Such a heart Madame Defarge carried under her rough robe. Carelessly worn, it was a becoming robe enough, in a certain weird way, and her dark hair looked rich under her coarse red cap. Lying hidden in her bosom, was a loaded pistol. Lying hidden at her waist, was a sharpened dagger. Thus accoutred, and walking with the confident tread of such a character, and with the supple freedom of a woman who had habitually walked in her girlhood, bare-foot and bare-legged, on the brown sea-sand, Madame Defarge took her way along the streets.

text: The valet had already drawn her back from the door, the carriage had broken into a lively trot, the postilions had urged the horses faster, and she was left far behind, while Monseigneur, once more attended by the Furies, was swiftly covering the league or two that still lay between him and his château.
source_text: The valet had put her away from the door, the carriage had broken into a brisk trot, the postilions had quickened the pace, she was left far behind, and Monseigneur, again escorted by the Furies, was rapidly diminishing the league or two of distance that remained between him and his chateau.

text: “My last request of all is this: and with it, I shall rid you of a visitor with whom, I know well, you have nothing in common, and between whom and you there lies an impassable distance. It is needless to say so, I am aware, yet it rises from my heart. For you, and for anyone dear to you, I would do anything. If my life were of a better sort, one in which sacrifice was possible, I would gladly make any sacrifice for you and for those you love. Try to remember me, in some quiet moment, as earnest and truthful in this one respect. The time will come, and it will not be long delayed, when new bonds will gather round you—bonds that will hold you even more tenderly and firmly to the home you so adorn—the dearest bonds that will ever brighten and bless your life. O Miss Manette, when the little image of a happy father’s face looks up into yours, when you see your own radiant beauty rising again at your feet, think sometimes that there is a man who would give his life to keep a life you love beside you!”
source_text: “My last supplication of all, is this; and with it, I will relieve you of a visitor with whom I well know you have nothing in unison, and between whom and you there is an impassable space. It is useless to say it, I know, but it rises out of my soul. For you, and for any dear to you, I would do anything. If my career were of that better kind that there was any opportunity or capacity of sacrifice in it, I would embrace any sacrifice for you and for those dear to you. Try to hold me in your mind, at some quiet times, as ardent and sincere in this one thing. The time will come, the time will not be long in coming, when new ties will be formed about you--ties that will bind you yet more tenderly and strongly to the home you so adorn--the dearest ties that will ever grace and gladden you. O Miss Manette, when the little picture of a happy father’s face looks up in yours, when you see your own bright beauty springing up anew at your feet, think now and then that there is a man who would give his life, to keep a life you love beside you!”

text: To the eyes of Mr. Jeremiah Cruncher, perched on his stool in Fleet Street with his grisly urchin at his side, there was each day displayed a vast multitude and variety of moving objects. Who, indeed, could sit on anything in Fleet Street during the busy hours and not be half blinded and half stunned by two great processions, one forever drifting westward with the sun, the other forever pressing eastward from the sun, both forever moving toward the plains beyond the reach of red and purple where the sun goes down!
source_text: To the eyes of Mr. Jeremiah Cruncher, sitting on his stool in Fleet-street with his grisly urchin beside him, a vast number and variety of objects in movement were every day presented. Who could sit upon anything in Fleet-street during the busy hours of the day, and not be dazed and deafened by two immense processions, one ever tending westward with the sun, the other ever tending eastward from the sun, both ever tending to the plains beyond the range of red and purple where the sun goes down!

text: He looked out upon a crowd of men and women—too few in number, or too far away, to fill the courtyard, no more than forty or fifty altogether. The people who possessed the house had admitted them at the gate, and they had hurried in to their work at the grindstone; it had plainly been placed there for their use, in a convenient and secluded spot.
source_text: Looked out upon a throng of men and women: not enough in number, or near enough, to fill the courtyard: not more than forty or fifty in all. The people in possession of the house had let them in at the gate, and they had rushed in to work at the grindstone; it had evidently been set up there for their purpose, as in a convenient and retired spot.

text: There was no fish for breakfast, and scarcely anything besides. Mr. Tallow was low in spirits and sharp in temper, and kept an iron pot-cover close at hand, ready to fling at Mrs. Tallow should he detect in her the least inclination to say Grace. He was brushed and washed at the customary hour, and then departed with his son to follow his professed occupation.
source_text: There was no fish for breakfast, and not much of anything else. Mr. Cruncher was out of spirits, and out of temper, and kept an iron pot-lid by him as a projectile for the correction of Mrs. Cruncher, in case he should observe any symptoms of her saying Grace. He was brushed and washed at the usual hour, and set off with his son to pursue his ostensible calling.

text: “I think, sir,” said the spy, in the humblest manner, turning to Mr. Lorry, “I may appeal to a gentleman of your years and benevolence to ask this other gentleman, so much younger than yourself, whether he can in any circumstances think it consistent with his position to play that Ace he has mentioned. I freely admit that _I_ am a spy, and that it is accounted a disgraceful calling—though someone must needs follow it; but this gentleman is no spy, and why should he lower himself so far as to make one of himself?”
source_text: “I think, sir,” said the spy, in the meanest manner, as he turned to Mr. Lorry, “I may appeal to a gentleman of your years and benevolence, to put it to this other gentleman, so much your junior, whether he can under any circumstances reconcile it to his station to play that Ace of which he has spoken. I admit that _I_ am a spy, and that it is considered a discreditable station--though it must be filled by somebody; but this gentleman is no spy, and why should he so demean himself as to make himself one?”

text: It could hardly have been called a trade, despite his own fond account of himself as “an honest tradesman.” His whole equipment was a wooden stool, fashioned from a broken-backed chair cut down to serve the purpose, and this stool young Jerry, walking beside his father, carried each morning to the spot beneath the banking-house window nearest Temple Bar. There, with the first handful of straw that could be gathered from any passing vehicle to shield the odd-job-man’s feet from cold and wet, it became his day-long camp. In this station, Mr. Cruncher was as well known to Fleet Street and the Temple as Temple Bar itself, and was nearly as fixed a feature of the place.
source_text: It could scarcely be called a trade, in spite of his favourite description of himself as “a honest tradesman.” His stock consisted of a wooden stool, made out of a broken-backed chair cut down, which stool, young Jerry, walking at his father’s side, carried every morning to beneath the banking-house window that was nearest Temple Bar: where, with the addition of the first handful of straw that could be gleaned from any passing vehicle to keep the cold and wet from the odd-job-man’s feet, it formed the encampment for the day. On this post of his, Mr. Cruncher was as well known to Fleet-street and the Temple, as the Bar itself,--and was almost as in-looking.

text: They had spoken little, though they had been there a long while. While there was still enough daylight to work and read, she had neither taken up her usual task nor read to him. Many times before she had busied herself with both, sitting beside him under the tree; but this time was unlike any other, and nothing could ever make it the same.
source_text: They had said little, though they had been there a long time. When it was yet light enough to work and read, she had neither engaged herself in her usual work, nor had she read to him. She had employed herself in both ways, at his side under the tree, many and many a time; but, this time was not quite like any other, and nothing could make it so.

text: There was much pity for her as she was taken away, and much sympathy for her father. The recollection of the days of his imprisonment had plainly been a deep distress to him. He had shown keen inward agitation when questioned, and that thoughtful, brooding expression which made him look old had rested on him ever since, like a heavy cloud. As he went out, the jury, who had turned back and paused for a moment, spoke through their foreman.
source_text: There was much commiseration for her as she was removed, and much sympathy with her father. It had evidently been a great distress to him, to have the days of his imprisonment recalled. He had shown strong internal agitation when he was questioned, and that pondering or brooding look which made him old, had been upon him, like a heavy cloud, ever since. As he passed out, the jury, who had turned back and paused a moment, spoke, through their foreman.

text: Lucien d’Évreux, known as Valen, was denounced by the public prosecutor as an émigré, one whose life, by the decree against all émigrés, had been forfeited to the Republic under pain of death. It availed him nothing that the edict had been issued only after his return to France. There he stood, and there stood the decree; he had been arrested in France, and his head was required.
source_text: Charles Evrémonde, called Darnay, was accused by the public prosecutor as an emigrant, whose life was forfeit to the Republic, under the decree which banished all emigrants on pain of Death. It was nothing that the decree bore date since his return to France. There he was, and there was the decree; he had been taken in France, and his head was demanded.

text: Not only so; the village, made half-mad by famine, fire, and the ringing of bells, and remembering that Monsieur Gabelle had something to do with collecting rent and taxes—though in those latter days he had gathered but a small portion of the taxes, and no rent at all—grew eager for a meeting with him and, gathering about his house, called upon him to come out for a personal conference. Thereupon Monsieur Gabelle securely barred his door and withdrew to hold counsel with himself. The outcome of that private consultation was that Gabelle once more retired to the roof of his house behind his row of chimneys; and this time he was resolved, if his door should be forced open—being a small Southern man of a retaliatory turn—to fling himself headlong over the parapet and smash one or two men beneath him.
source_text: Not only that; but the village, light-headed with famine, fire, and bell-ringing, and bethinking itself that Monsieur Gabelle had to do with the collection of rent and taxes--though it was but a small instalment of taxes, and no rent at all, that Gabelle had got in those latter days--became impatient for an interview with him, and, surrounding his house, summoned him to come forth for personal conference. Whereupon, Monsieur Gabelle did heavily bar his door, and retire to hold counsel with himself. The result of that conference was, that Gabelle again withdrew himself to his housetop behind his stack of chimneys; this time resolved, if his door were broken in (he was a small Southern man of retaliative temperament), to pitch himself head foremost over the parapet, and crush a man or two below.

text: They lay down on some straw in a loft until midnight, and then set out again when the town had fallen asleep. Of all the strange alterations that had come over familiar things and made this desperate journey seem unreal, none was stranger than the apparent scarcity of sleep. After long, solitary spurts over wretched roads, they would come upon a cluster of poor cottages, not sunk in darkness but blazing with lights, and there, in the middle of the night, would find the people moving in a spectral fashion, hand in hand around a shrivelled Tree of Liberty, or gathered together in a body to sing a Liberty song. Happily, there was sleep in Beauvais that night to relieve them of this, and they went on once more into desolation and loneliness: their harness jingling through the untimely cold and rain, past barren fields that had borne no harvest that year, broken here and there by the blackened shells of burned houses, and by the sudden springing from concealment, and quick pulling up in their path, of patriotic patrols keeping watch on all the roads.
source_text: They rested on some straw in a loft until the middle of the night, and then rode forward again when all the town was asleep. Among the many wild changes observable on familiar things which made this wild ride unreal, not the least was the seeming rarity of sleep. After long and lonely spurring over dreary roads, they would come to a cluster of poor cottages, not steeped in darkness, but all glittering with lights, and would find the people, in a ghostly manner in the dead of the night, circling hand in hand round a shrivelled tree of Liberty, or all drawn up together singing a Liberty song. Happily, however, there was sleep in Beauvais that night to help them out of it and they passed on once more into solitude and loneliness: jingling through the untimely cold and wet, among impoverished fields that had yielded no fruits of the earth that year, diversified by the blackened remains of burnt houses, and by the sudden emergence from ambuscade, and sharp reining up across their way, of patriot patrols on the watch on all the roads.

text: This constant vigilance did not merely halt him twenty times on the road when he was traveling by coach, but delayed him twenty times a day besides, by following after him and bringing him back, riding ahead of him and stopping him in advance, riding alongside him and keeping him under guard. For days he had been making his way through France alone, when at last he went to bed, exhausted, in a small town on the main road, still far from Paris.
source_text: This universal watchfulness not only stopped him on the highway twenty times in a stage, but retarded his progress twenty times in a day, by riding after him and taking him back, riding before him and stopping him by anticipation, riding with him and keeping him in charge. He had been days upon his journey in France alone, when he went to bed tired out, in a little town on the high road, still a long way from Paris.

text: When the reading of this document ended, a dreadful sound arose. It was a sound of craving and eagerness, with nothing articulate in it but blood. The narrative awakened the most revengeful passions of the age, and there was not a head in the nation that must not have bowed before it.
source_text: A terrible sound arose when the reading of this document was done. A sound of craving and eagerness that had nothing articulate in it but blood. The narrative called up the most revengeful passions of the time, and there was not a head in the nation but must have dropped before it.

text: “Have no fear, Miss Manette, that I shall ever renew this conversation even by the lightest word. I will speak of it no more. Short of being dead, nothing could be more certain than that. In the hour of my death, I shall hold one blessed remembrance sacred—and thank and bless you for it—that my final avowal of myself was made to you, and that my name, my faults, and my sorrows were borne so gently in your heart. May all else be bright and peaceful!”
source_text: “Be under no apprehension, Miss Manette, of my ever resuming this conversation by so much as a passing word. I will never refer to it again. If I were dead, that could not be surer than it is henceforth. In the hour of my death, I shall hold sacred the one good remembrance--and shall thank and bless you for it--that my last avowal of myself was made to you, and that my name, and faults, and miseries were gently carried in your heart. May it otherwise be light and happy!”

text: From what quarter they had been sent, by whose hands they had last been borne, where their beginning lay, and by what means they came twitching and quivering askew, score upon score, above the crowd like some species of lightning, no eye in the press could have said; yet muskets were being dealt out, along with cartridges, powder, and shot, bars of iron and timber, knives, axes, pikes, every implement that frantic invention could contrive or uncover. Those who could seize nothing else set themselves, with hands already bleeding, to wrench stones and bricks from the very fabric of the walls. Every pulse and every heart in Saint-Étienne burned in a fever of the highest pitch. Every living thing there counted life as nothing, and was maddened by a fierce readiness to cast it away.
source_text: Who gave them out, whence they last came, where they began, through what agency they crookedly quivered and jerked, scores at a time, over the heads of the crowd, like a kind of lightning, no eye in the throng could have told; but, muskets were being distributed--so were cartridges, powder, and ball, bars of iron and wood, knives, axes, pikes, every weapon that distracted ingenuity could discover or devise. People who could lay hold of nothing else, set themselves with bleeding hands to force stones and bricks out of their places in walls. Every pulse and heart in Saint Antoine was on high-fever strain and at high-fever heat. Every living creature there held life as of no account, and was demented with a passionate readiness to sacrifice it.

text: Because he had willingly given up a title that displeased him, and a position that displeased him as well, and had left his country—before the Tribunal’s present use of the word emigrant had come into being—to make his living in England by his own labor rather than by burdening the already overtaxed people of France.
source_text: Because he had voluntarily relinquished a title that was distasteful to him, and a station that was distasteful to him, and had left his country--he submitted before the word emigrant in the present acceptation by the Tribunal was in use--to live by his own industry in England, rather than on the industry of the overladen people of France.

text: No sooner had they settled into their new dwelling, and her father resumed the accustomed round of his occupations, than she set the small household in order as scrupulously as though her husband had stood beside her. Every thing was given its fixed place and its fixed hour. Little Claire she instructed with the same regularity as if they were all still together in their English home. The faint contrivances by which she deluded herself into seeming to believe that their reunion could not be far off—the slight arrangements made in expectation of his swift return, the keeping aside of his chair and his books—these, together with the nightly prayer for one beloved captive in particular among the many unhappy souls in prison and under the shadow of death, were almost the only open consolations of her oppressed heart.
source_text: As soon as they were established in their new residence, and her father had entered on the routine of his avocations, she arranged the little household as exactly as if her husband had been there. Everything had its appointed place and its appointed time. Little Lucie she taught, as regularly, as if they had all been united in their English home. The slight devices with which she cheated herself into the show of a belief that they would soon be reunited--the little preparations for his speedy return, the setting aside of his chair and his books--these, and the solemn prayer at night for one dear prisoner especially, among the many unhappy souls in prison and the shadow of death--were almost the only outspoken reliefs of her heavy mind.

text: Earlier in the day, the summer light poured brilliantly into the corner; but when the streets grew hot, it fell into shadow there, though not so deep a shadow that you could not look past it into a blaze of brightness beyond. It was a cool place, quiet yet pleasant, a marvelous setting for echoes, and almost a refuge from the seething streets.
source_text: The summer light struck into the corner brilliantly in the earlier part of the day; but, when the streets grew hot, the corner was in shadow, though not in shadow so remote but that you could see beyond it into a glare of brightness. It was a cool spot, staid but cheerful, a wonderful place for echoes, and a very harbour from the raging streets.

text: He had never seen the machine that was to end his life. How far it stood above the ground, how many steps led up to it, where he would be placed, how he would be touched, whether the hands that touched him would be stained red, which way his face would be turned, whether he would be the first or perhaps the last: these and many such questions, in no way summoned by his own will, thrust themselves upon him again and again, without number. Nor were they born of fear: he was aware of no fear. Rather, they sprang from a strange, haunting longing to know what he ought to do when the moment arrived; a longing monstrously out of proportion to the few rapid moments it concerned, a wondering that seemed less his own than that of some other spirit dwelling within him.
source_text: He had never seen the instrument that was to terminate his life. How high it was from the ground, how many steps it had, where he would be stood, how he would be touched, whether the touching hands would be dyed red, which way his face would be turned, whether he would be the first, or might be the last: these and many similar questions, in nowise directed by his will, obtruded themselves over and over again, countless times. Neither were they connected with fear: he was conscious of no fear. Rather, they originated in a strange besetting desire to know what to do when the time came; a desire gigantically disproportionate to the few swift moments to which it referred; a wondering that was more like the wondering of some other spirit within his, than his own.

text: A second man rose and went out. Madame Defarge placed wine before the road mender called Jacques, who lifted his blue cap to the company and drank. In the breast of his blouse he carried some rough dark bread; he ate it by mouthfuls at intervals, and sat there munching and drinking close to Madame Defarge’s counter. A third man rose and went out.
source_text: A second man got up and went out. Madame Defarge set wine before the mender of roads called Jacques, who doffed his blue cap to the company, and drank. In the breast of his blouse he carried some coarse dark bread; he ate of this between whiles, and sat munching and drinking near Madame Defarge’s counter. A third man got up and went out.

text: Miss Pross was a charming figure, though wild, flushed, and severe, as she took off her darling’s bonnet when she came upstairs, dusted it with the corners of her handkerchief, blew the dust from it, laid aside the mantle in readiness, and smoothed her abundant hair with as much pride as she could have felt in her own, had she been the vainest and most beautiful of women. Her darling was a charming sight as well, clasping her and thanking her, and protesting at all the trouble she took on her behalf—though that she ventured only in play, for otherwise Miss Pross, deeply wounded, would have gone off to her own room and wept. The Doctor was a charming sight too, watching them and telling Miss Pross how she spoiled Lucie, in a tone and with an expression that showed as much spoiling in him as in Miss Pross, and more still if more were possible. Mr. Lorry was a charming sight also, smiling on the whole scene from beneath his small wig and blessing his old age for having been brought at last to a home. But no, hundreds came to see the sights, and Mr. Lorry looked in vain for Miss Pross’s prophecy to come true.
source_text: Miss Pross was a pleasant sight, albeit wild, and red, and grim, taking off her darling’s bonnet when she came up-stairs, and touching it up with the ends of her handkerchief, and blowing the dust off it, and folding her mantle ready for laying by, and smoothing her rich hair with as much pride as she could possibly have taken in her own hair if she had been the vainest and handsomest of women. Her darling was a pleasant sight too, embracing her and thanking her, and protesting against her taking so much trouble for her--which last she only dared to do playfully, or Miss Pross, sorely hurt, would have retired to her own chamber and cried. The Doctor was a pleasant sight too, looking on at them, and telling Miss Pross how she spoilt Lucie, in accents and with eyes that had as much spoiling in them as Miss Pross had, and would have had more if it were possible. Mr. Lorry was a pleasant sight too, beaming at all this in his little wig, and thanking his bachelor stars for having lighted him in his declining years to a Home. But, no Hundreds of people came to see the sights, and Mr. Lorry looked in vain for the fulfilment of Miss Pross’s prediction.

text: Mr. Lorry came quietly forward, leaving the daughter by the door. After he had stood for a minute or two beside Defarge, the shoemaker raised his eyes. He showed no surprise at the presence of another figure; yet the unsteady fingers of one hand wandered to his lips as he looked at him—his lips and nails being of the same dull leaden hue—and then the hand fell again to his work, and he bent once more over the shoe. The glance and the gesture had taken but a moment.
source_text: Mr. Lorry came silently forward, leaving the daughter by the door. When he had stood, for a minute or two, by the side of Defarge, the shoemaker looked up. He showed no surprise at seeing another figure, but the unsteady fingers of one of his hands strayed to his lips as he looked at it (his lips and his nails were of the same pale lead-colour), and then the hand dropped to his work, and he once more bent over the shoe. The look and the action had occupied but an instant.

text: He had raised a hand against the light between his eyes, and even the bones of it seemed to show through. So he sat, his gaze fixed and vacant, with his work broken off for the moment. He never looked at the figure before him without first looking down to one side of himself, then the other, as though he had lost the habit of joining place to sound; nor did he ever begin to speak without first drifting in this way, and then forgetting to speak.
source_text: He had put up a hand between his eyes and the light, and the very bones of it seemed transparent. So he sat, with a steadfastly vacant gaze, pausing in his work. He never looked at the figure before him, without first looking down on this side of himself, then on that, as if he had lost the habit of associating place with sound; he never spoke, without first wandering in this manner, and forgetting to speak.

text: “I am greatly gratified,” said Mr. Lorry, “though, upon my honour, I never meant to make those little keepsakes invisible to anyone. Dear me! This is the sort of occasion that sets a man pondering over all he has lost. Dear, dear, dear! To think that there might have been a Mrs. Lorry, at any time for nearly these fifty years!”
source_text: “I am highly gratified,” said Mr. Lorry, “though, upon my honour, I had no intention of rendering those trifling articles of remembrance invisible to any one. Dear me! This is an occasion that makes a man speculate on all he has lost. Dear, dear, dear! To think that there might have been a Mrs. Lorry, any time these fifty years almost!”

text: Such a staircase, with its accompaniments, in the older and more crowded quarters of Paris, would be bad enough now; but at that time it was truly vile to senses unused and unseasoned to it. Every tiny dwelling within the great foul nest of a tall house—that is, the room or rooms behind every door opening on the common stair—left its own heap of refuse upon its own landing, and besides cast other refuse from its windows. The unchecked and hopeless mass of decay thus bred would have poisoned the air even had poverty and want not burdened it with their unseen taints; the two evil sources together made it nearly intolerable. Through such an atmosphere, by a steep, dark shaft of filth and poison, the way lay. Yielding to his own troubled mind, and to the agitation of his young companion, which grew greater at every moment, Mr. Jarvis Lorry twice paused to rest. Each pause was made beside a melancholy grating, where whatever poor air remained unspoiled seemed to escape, and all that was corrupt and sickly seemed to creep in. Through the rusted bars there came, rather tastes than sights, of the confused neighborhood; and nothing within view, nearer or lower than the summits of the two great towers of Notre-Dame, bore any sign of healthy life or wholesome hope.
source_text: Such a staircase, with its accessories, in the older and more crowded parts of Paris, would be bad enough now; but, at that time, it was vile indeed to unaccustomed and unhardened senses. Every little habitation within the great foul nest of one high building--that is to say, the room or rooms within every door that opened on the general staircase--left its own heap of refuse on its own landing, besides flinging other refuse from its own windows. The uncontrollable and hopeless mass of decomposition so engendered, would have polluted the air, even if poverty and deprivation had not loaded it with their intangible impurities; the two bad sources combined made it almost insupportable. Through such an atmosphere, by a steep dark shaft of dirt and poison, the way lay. Yielding to his own disturbance of mind, and to his young companion’s agitation, which became greater every instant, Mr. Jarvis Lorry twice stopped to rest. Each of these stoppages was made at a doleful grating, by which any languishing good airs that were left uncorrupted, seemed to escape, and all spoilt and sickly vapours seemed to crawl in. Through the rusted bars, tastes, rather than glimpses, were caught of the jumbled neighbourhood; and nothing within range, nearer or lower than the summits of the two great towers of Notre-Dame, had any promise on it of healthy life or wholesome aspirations.

text: Because the corner lay well away from idle or inquisitive eyes, and because the arrangements had been so few and simple, the Doctor, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross found themselves quite alone. It was only when they passed into the pleasant shade of the cool old hall that Mr. Lorry noticed how greatly the Doctor had changed, as though the golden arm raised there had dealt him a poisoned wound.
source_text: The corner being out of the way of the idle and curious, and the preparations having been very simple and few, the Doctor, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross, were left quite alone. It was when they turned into the welcome shade of the cool old hall, that Mr. Lorry observed a great change to have come over the Doctor; as if the golden arm uplifted there, had struck him a poisoned blow.

text: After a few weary attempts to fall asleep again, which the man cleverly defeated by poking the fire steadily for five minutes, he rose, flung on his hat, and went out. He turned into the Temple, and, after restoring himself by walking twice along the pavements of King’s Bench-walk and Paper-buildings, turned into Stryver’s chambers.
source_text: After a few dull efforts to get to sleep again, which the man dexterously combated by stirring the fire continuously for five minutes, he got up, tossed his hat on, and walked out. He turned into the Temple, and, having revived himself by twice pacing the pavements of King’s Bench-walk and Paper-buildings, turned into the Stryver chambers.

text: With the dead man disposed of, and the crowd forced to seek some other diversion, another brighter wit—or perhaps the same one—hit upon the idea of accusing random passers-by of being Old Bailey spies, and taking vengeance on them. In pursuit of this fancy, dozens of harmless people, who had never come within sight of the Old Bailey in their lives, were chased, roughly seized, and ill-used. From this it was an easy and natural step to the amusement of smashing windows, and then to the plundering of public-houses. At length, after several hours, when various summer-houses had been pulled down and some area railings torn up to furnish weapons for the more pugnacious, a rumour spread that the Guards were on their way. At that, the mob slowly drifted apart; and perhaps the Guards did come, and perhaps they did not, for this was the ordinary course of a crowd.
source_text: The dead man disposed of, and the crowd being under the necessity of providing some other entertainment for itself, another brighter genius (or perhaps the same) conceived the humour of impeaching casual passers-by, as Old Bailey spies, and wreaking vengeance on them. Chase was given to some scores of inoffensive persons who had never been near the Old Bailey in their lives, in the realisation of this fancy, and they were roughly hustled and maltreated. The transition to the sport of window-breaking, and thence to the plundering of public-houses, was easy and natural. At last, after several hours, when sundry summer-houses had been pulled down, and some area-railings had been torn up, to arm the more belligerent spirits, a rumour got about that the Guards were coming. Before this rumour, the crowd gradually melted away, and perhaps the Guards came, and perhaps they never came, and this was the usual progress of a mob.

text: Concerning this, his rightful and inalienable birthright, the mounted courier was no less well endowed than the King himself, the Prime Minister, or the wealthiest trader in London. Thus the three travelers, penned together within the cramped confines of one creaking old mail coach, remained enigmas to one another, as wholly inscrutable as though each had been rolling along in his own coach and six, or even his own coach and sixty, with a whole county stretching between him and the next.
source_text: As to this, his natural and not to be alienated inheritance, the messenger on horseback had exactly the same possessions as the King, the first Minister of State, or the richest merchant in London. So with the three passengers shut up in the narrow compass of one lumbering old mail coach; they were mysteries to one another, as complete as if each had been in his own coach and six, or his own coach and sixty, with the breadth of a county between him and the next.

text: “But might it not—mind, I ask only for information, as a matter-of-fact man of business who deals solely in such tangible things as guineas, shillings, and banknotes—might not keeping the thing also mean keeping the idea? If the thing were gone, my dear Manette, might not the fear go with it as well? In short, would it not be yielding to the uneasiness to keep the forge?”
source_text: “But may not--mind! I ask for information, as a plodding man of business who only deals with such material objects as guineas, shillings, and bank-notes--may not the retention of the thing involve the retention of the idea? If the thing were gone, my dear Manette, might not the fear go with it? In short, is it not a concession to the misgiving, to keep the forge?”

text: The President demanded the name of that citizen. The accused replied that the citizen was his first witness. He also spoke with assurance of the citizen’s letter, which had been seized from him at the Barrier, though he had no doubt it would be among the papers now laid before the President.
source_text: The President required the name of that citizen. The accused explained that the citizen was his first witness. He also referred with confidence to the citizen’s letter, which had been taken from him at the Barrier, but which he did not doubt would be found among the papers then before the President.

text: “My dear, there is an upper window in the prison, to which Charles can sometimes gain access at three in the afternoon. If he can contrive to reach it—which depends on many uncertainties and accidents—he believes he may catch sight of you in the street, provided you take your place in a certain spot that I can show you. But you will not be able to see him, my poor child, and even if you could, it would be unsafe for you to give any sign that you knew him.”
source_text: “My dear, there is an upper window in the prison, to which Charles can sometimes gain access at three in the afternoon. When he can get to it--which depends on many uncertainties and incidents--he might see you in the street, he thinks, if you stood in a certain place that I can show you. But you will not be able to see him, my poor child, and even if you could, it would be unsafe for you to make a sign of recognition.”

text: Mr. Halden had but just ended his supper, and was seated before the pleasant glow of one or two small logs upon the hearth—perhaps gazing into their bright flame for the likeness of that younger old gentleman from Hargrove’s, who, many years before, had stared into the scarlet coals at the King’s Arms in Dover. He turned at their entrance, and betrayed by his look the astonishment with which he beheld an unknown face.
source_text: Mr. Lorry had just finished his dinner, and was sitting before a cheery little log or two of fire--perhaps looking into their blaze for the picture of that younger elderly gentleman from Tellson’s, who had looked into the red coals at the Royal George at Dover, now a good many years ago. He turned his head as they entered, and showed the surprise with which he saw a stranger.

text: “Let us be reasonable,” said the spy, “and let us be fair. To show you how mistaken you are, and how groundless your supposition is, I will put before you a certificate of Cly’s burial, which I happened to have kept in my pocket-book,” and with an eager hand he drew it out and unfolded it, “all this time. There it is. Oh, look at it, look at it! You may take it in your own hand; it is no forgery.”
source_text: “Let us be reasonable,” said the spy, “and let us be fair. To show you how mistaken you are, and what an unfounded assumption yours is, I will lay before you a certificate of Cly’s burial, which I happened to have carried in my pocket-book,” with a hurried hand he produced and opened it, “ever since. There it is. Oh, look at it, look at it! You may take it in your hand; it’s no forgery.”

text: It was afterwards recalled that, as he stooped and touched her cheek with his lips, he breathed a few words. The child, who stood nearest to him, repeated them later, and in her turn told them to her grandchildren when she had grown into a handsome old woman, saying that she had heard him murmur, “A life you love.”
source_text: It was remembered afterwards that when he bent down and touched her face with his lips, he murmured some words. The child, who was nearest to him, told them afterwards, and told her grandchildren when she was a handsome old lady, that she heard him say, “A life you love.”

text: Leisurely, our four horses are unyoked; leisurely, the coach remains in the little street, stripped of horses and seeming to have no prospect of moving again; leisurely, the fresh horses appear, one after another, into sight; leisurely, the new postilions come behind them, sucking at and plaiting the lashes of their whips; leisurely, the old postilions count out their money, make false sums, and reach dissatisfied conclusions. Meanwhile, our overburdened hearts are beating at a pace that would far outstrip the swiftest gallop of the swiftest horses ever born.
source_text: Leisurely, our four horses are taken out; leisurely, the coach stands in the little street, bereft of horses, and with no likelihood upon it of ever moving again; leisurely, the new horses come into visible existence, one by one; leisurely, the new postilions follow, sucking and plaiting the lashes of their whips; leisurely, the old postilions count their money, make wrong additions, and arrive at dissatisfied results. All the time, our overfraught hearts are beating at a rate that would far outstrip the fastest gallop of the fastest horses ever foaled.

text: Soon Madame Defarge’s hands stopped beating, and searched at her waist where Miss Pross had her arms locked around her. “It’s under my arm,” Miss Pross said in a muffled voice. “You shall not get it out. I am stronger than you, thank Heaven for that. I will hold you here until one of us faints or dies!”
source_text: Soon, Madame Defarge’s hands ceased to strike, and felt at her encircled waist. “It is under my arm,” said Miss Pross, in smothered tones, “you shall not draw it. I am stronger than you, I bless Heaven for it. I hold you till one or other of us faints or dies!”

text: He had been speaking throughout the day, upon a variety of matters, and with a spirit more animated than was common with him. “Pray, Doctor Belleau,” said Mr. Valois, as they sat beneath the plane-tree—and said it in the natural course of the discourse then engaging them, which chanced to be the ancient edifices of London—“have you seen much of the Tower?”
source_text: He had been talking all day, on many subjects, and with unusual vivacity. “Pray, Doctor Manette,” said Mr. Darnay, as they sat under the plane-tree--and he said it in the natural pursuit of the topic in hand, which happened to be the old buildings of London--“have you seen much of the Tower?”

text: No human wit could have deciphered the secrets of his mind from the frightened blankness of his face. Whether he understood what had passed, whether he remembered what had been said to him, whether he even knew that he was free, were questions no shrewdness could resolve. They attempted to address him; but he was so bewildered, and so slow in answering, that they were alarmed by the depth of his confusion and agreed, for the time, to trouble him no further. At moments he would clasp his head in his hands with a wild, bewildered gesture never before seen in him; yet he took a certain comfort from the mere sound of his daughter’s voice, and always turned toward it when she spoke.
source_text: No human intelligence could have read the mysteries of his mind, in the scared blank wonder of his face. Whether he knew what had happened, whether he recollected what they had said to him, whether he knew that he was free, were questions which no sagacity could have solved. They tried speaking to him; but, he was so confused, and so very slow to answer, that they took fright at his bewilderment, and agreed for the time to tamper with him no more. He had a wild, lost manner of occasionally clasping his head in his hands, that had not been seen in him before; yet, he had some pleasure in the mere sound of his daughter’s voice, and invariably turned to it when she spoke.

text: “Then be so kind,” said Miss Manette, “as to leave us here. You can see how composed he has grown, and surely you cannot now be afraid to leave him with me. Why should you be? If you will only lock the door, to keep us from being disturbed, I have no doubt that when you come back you will find him as quiet as you leave him. In any case, I will look after him until your return, and then we shall take him away at once.”
source_text: “Then be so kind,” urged Miss Manette, “as to leave us here. You see how composed he has become, and you cannot be afraid to leave him with me now. Why should you be? If you will lock the door to secure us from interruption, I do not doubt that you will find him, when you come back, as quiet as you leave him. In any case, I will take care of him until you return, and then we will remove him straight.”

text: Mr. Stryver, having firmly decided on that noble conferring of good fortune upon the Doctor’s daughter, determined to let her know of her happiness before he departed from town for the Long Vacation. After some inward discussion of the matter, he concluded that it would be best to settle all the preliminaries at once, and that they could afterwards decide at their leisure whether he should offer her his hand a week or two before Michaelmas Term, or during the short Christmas vacation between that and Hilary.
source_text: Mr. Stryver having made up his mind to that magnanimous bestowal of good fortune on the Doctor’s daughter, resolved to make her happiness known to her before he left town for the Long Vacation. After some mental debating of the point, he came to the conclusion that it would be as well to get all the preliminaries done with, and they could then arrange at their leisure whether he should give her his hand a week or two before Michaelmas Term, or in the little Christmas vacation between it and Hilary.

text: “I saw him then, gentlemen,” began the road mender, “a year ago this very summer, beneath the Marquis’s carriage, hanging from the chain. This is how it happened. I had left my work on the road, the sun was setting, the Marquis’s carriage was slowly climbing the hill, and he was hanging by the chain—like this.”
source_text: “I saw him then, messieurs,” began the mender of roads, “a year ago this running summer, underneath the carriage of the Marquis, hanging by the chain. Behold the manner of it. I leaving my work on the road, the sun going to bed, the carriage of the Marquis slowly ascending the hill, he hanging by the chain--like this.”

text: There was no customer in the shop save Jacques the Third, with his twitching fingers and hoarse, raucous voice. This was the man he had once seen among the jury, and he now stood at the small counter, taking his drink while he talked with the Defarges, husband and wife. The Vengeance took her part in the exchange as freely as though she were one of the house itself.
source_text: There happened to be no customer in the shop but Jacques Three, of the restless fingers and the croaking voice. This man, whom he had seen upon the Jury, stood drinking at the little counter, in conversation with the Defarges, man and wife. The Vengeance assisted in the conversation, like a regular member of the establishment.

text: But not for long. Before long, the chateau began to reveal itself in a strange light of its own, as though it were turning luminous. Then a wavering glow moved behind the front of the building, tracing out the transparent spaces and showing where the balustrades, arches, and windows stood. Then it climbed higher, widening and brightening. Soon, from a score of the great windows, flames broke out, and the stone faces seemed to wake and glare out through the fire.
source_text: But, not for long. Presently, the chateau began to make itself strangely visible by some light of its own, as though it were growing luminous. Then, a flickering streak played behind the architecture of the front, picking out transparent places, and showing where balustrades, arches, and windows were. Then it soared higher, and grew broader and brighter. Soon, from a score of the great windows, flames burst forth, and the stone faces awakened, stared out of fire.

text: Beside the lone one, two more passengers were trudging up the hill alongside the mail-coach. All three were muffled to the cheekbones and over their ears, and all wore jack-boots. None of the three could have told, from anything visible, what either of the other two looked like; each, to the eyes of the mind as much as to the eyes of the body, was hidden from his companions by nearly as many wrappings. In those days, travellers were very cautious about becoming confidential at short notice, since anyone on the road might prove to be a robber, or in collusion with robbers. And as for the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could furnish someone in “the Captain’s” pay, from the landlord down to the most insignificant stable-hand, that seemed the likeliest possibility of all. So the guard on the Dover mail reflected, on that Friday night in November of 1775, as he laboured up Shooter’s Hill, standing on his own special perch behind the coach, stamping his feet and keeping both eye and hand on the arm-chest in front of him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay atop six or eight loaded horse-pistols, all resting on a base of cutlass.
source_text: Two other passengers, besides the one, were plodding up the hill by the side of the mail. All three were wrapped to the cheekbones and over the ears, and wore jack-boots. Not one of the three could have said, from anything he saw, what either of the other two was like; and each was hidden under almost as many wrappers from the eyes of the mind, as from the eyes of the body, of his two companions. In those days, travellers were very shy of being confidential on a short notice, for anybody on the road might be a robber or in league with robbers. As to the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could produce somebody in “the Captain’s” pay, ranging from the landlord to the lowest stable non-descript, it was the likeliest thing upon the cards. So the guard of the Dover mail thought to himself, that Friday night in November, one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five, lumbering up Shooter’s Hill, as he stood on his own particular perch behind the mail, beating his feet, and keeping an eye and a hand on the arm-chest before him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay at the top of six or eight loaded horse-pistols, deposited on a substratum of cutlass.

text: Without troubling himself to glance at the group again, Monsieur the Marquis leaned back in his seat and was already being driven away, with the easy air of a gentleman who had by chance broken some common object, paid for it, and could well afford the cost, when his composure was abruptly shattered by a coin that flew into the carriage and rang upon its floor.
source_text: Without deigning to look at the assemblage a second time, Monsieur the Marquis leaned back in his seat, and was just being driven away with the air of a gentleman who had accidentally broke some common thing, and had paid for it, and could afford to pay for it; when his ease was suddenly disturbed by a coin flying into his carriage, and ringing on its floor.

text: “‘For his sake, Doctor,’ she said, tears falling as she pointed to him, ‘I would do anything I could to make such poor amends as lie in my power. Otherwise he will never prosper in his inheritance. I have a foreboding that if no other innocent atonement is made for this, it will one day be demanded of him. Whatever I have left that I may call my own—it is little more than the value of a few jewels—I will make it the first duty of his life to give, together with the pity and grief of his dead mother, to this wronged family, if the sister can be found.’
source_text: “‘For his sake, Doctor,’ she said, pointing to him in tears, ‘I would do all I can to make what poor amends I can. He will never prosper in his inheritance otherwise. I have a presentiment that if no other innocent atonement is made for this, it will one day be required of him. What I have left to call my own--it is little beyond the worth of a few jewels--I will make it the first charge of his life to bestow, with the compassion and lamenting of his dead mother, on this injured family, if the sister can be discovered.’

text: The gentleman from Tellson’s had no choice but to drain his glass with a look of dogged despair, adjust his odd little flaxen wig over his ears, and follow the waiter to Miss Manette’s room. It was a large, dim chamber, furnished in a funereal style with black horsehair and burdened by heavy dark tables. These had been rubbed with oil again and again, until the two tall candles on the table in the center of the room were dismally mirrored in every surface, as though they themselves were entombed in deep coffins of black mahogany, and no real light could be expected from them until they were drawn out.
source_text: The gentleman from Tellson’s had nothing left for it but to empty his glass with an air of stolid desperation, settle his odd little flaxen wig at the ears, and follow the waiter to Miss Manette’s apartment. It was a large, dark room, furnished in a funereal manner with black horsehair, and loaded with heavy dark tables. These had been oiled and oiled, until the two tall candles on the table in the middle of the room were gloomily reflected on every leaf; as if _they_ were buried, in deep graves of black mahogany, and no light to speak of could be expected from them until they were dug out.

text: With a furious rattling and clanging, and a kind of reckless disregard that is hard to imagine in these times, the carriage hurtled through the streets and swung round the corners, while women shrieked ahead of it and men seized one another and dragged children aside to escape its path. At last, as it swept around a corner beside a fountain, one of the wheels gave a small, dreadful jolt, and a cry rose from several voices, while the horses reared and struggled.
source_text: With a wild rattle and clatter, and an inhuman abandonment of consideration not easy to be understood in these days, the carriage dashed through streets and swept round corners, with women screaming before it, and men clutching each other and clutching children out of its way. At last, swooping at a street corner by a fountain, one of its wheels came to a sickening little jolt, and there was a loud cry from a number of voices, and the horses reared and plunged.
