text: The spy’s polished bearing, so oddly at odds with his deliberately coarse attire, and perhaps with the habit of his ordinary conduct as well, met so abrupt a rebuff in the impenetrable face of Carton—who was a riddle to men far wiser and more upright than himself—that his assurance wavered and then deserted him altogether. While he hesitated, Carton, taking up again his former look of attention to the cards, said:
source_text: The smooth manner of the spy, curiously in dissonance with his ostentatiously rough dress, and probably with his usual demeanour, received such a check from the inscrutability of Carton,--who was a mystery to wiser and honester men than he,--that it faltered here and failed him. While he was at a loss, Carton said, resuming his former air of contemplating cards:

text: “Only one thing more,” said Mr. Lorry, emphasizing the words as a sober means of compelling her attention: “he has been discovered under another name; his own, long since forgotten, or long concealed. It would now be worse than useless to ask which of these is true; worse than useless to try to learn whether he has been overlooked for years, or whether he has always been purposely kept a prisoner. It would be worse than useless to make any inquiry now, because it would be dangerous. It is better not to speak of the matter anywhere, or in any way, and to remove him—for the present at least—out of France. Even I, secure as an Englishman, and even Tellson’s, important as they are to French credit, avoid mentioning it. I carry about me not a scrap of writing that openly refers to it. This is altogether a secret service. My credentials, my entries, and my memoranda are all contained in the one line, ‘Recalled to Life,’ which may mean anything. But what is the matter! She does not heed a word! Miss Manette!”
source_text: “Only one thing more,” said Mr. Lorry, laying stress upon it as a wholesome means of enforcing her attention: “he has been found under another name; his own, long forgotten or long concealed. It would be worse than useless now to inquire which; worse than useless to seek to know whether he has been for years overlooked, or always designedly held prisoner. It would be worse than useless now to make any inquiries, because it would be dangerous. Better not to mention the subject, anywhere or in any way, and to remove him--for a while at all events--out of France. Even I, safe as an Englishman, and even Tellson’s, important as they are to French credit, avoid all naming of the matter. I carry about me, not a scrap of writing openly referring to it. This is a secret service altogether. My credentials, entries, and memoranda, are all comprehended in the one line, ‘Recalled to Life;’ which may mean anything. But what is the matter! She doesn’t notice a word! Miss Manette!”

text: A scant few French leagues of his way had been traversed, when Valerius Darnet began to understand that, for him, these country roads held no hope of turning back until he should have been proclaimed a worthy citizen in Paris. Whatever now might chance, he must still press onward to the end of his journey. No petty hamlet shut itself behind him, no ordinary barrier fell across the road at his back, but he felt that yet another iron gate had been locked in the long chain between himself and England. So universal was the vigilance that hemmed him in, that had he been snared in a net, or carried toward his fate in a cage, he could scarcely have known a more utter loss of liberty.
source_text: A very few French leagues of his journey were accomplished, when Charles Darnay began to perceive that for him along these country roads there was no hope of return until he should have been declared a good citizen at Paris. Whatever might befall now, he must on to his journey’s end. Not a mean village closed upon him, not a common barrier dropped across the road behind him, but he knew it to be another iron door in the series that was barred between him and England. The universal watchfulness so encompassed him, that if he had been taken in a net, or were being forwarded to his destination in a cage, he could not have felt his freedom more completely gone.

text: As the Nightfall lowers itself from its height to accomplish the deed, the tumbrils begin to spill their burden. The attendants of Saint Guillotine stand vested and prepared. Crash!--a head is lifted high, and the knitting-women, who but a moment before had scarcely raised their eyes to regard it when it could still think and speak, count One.
source_text: As The Vengeance descends from her elevation to do it, the tumbrils begin to discharge their loads. The ministers of Sainte Guillotine are robed and ready. Crash!--A head is held up, and the knitting-women who scarcely lifted their eyes to look at it a moment ago when it could think and speak, count One.

text: But he had never before seen his friend as he was now; he had never known him in anything like this present state of mind. For the first time, the Doctor now felt that his own suffering had become a source of strength and power. For the first time, he understood that in that fierce fire he had slowly forged the iron that could burst open the prison door of his daughter’s husband and set him free. “It has all worked toward a good end, my friend; it was not all mere loss and destruction. As my dear child helped restore me to myself, I shall now help restore the dearest part of herself to her; with Heaven’s help, I will do it!” So said Doctor Manette. And when Jarvis Lorry saw the brightened eyes, the determined face, the calm, strong look and bearing of the man whose life had always seemed to him to have been halted, like a clock, for so many years, and then started again with an energy that had lain dormant through its long stillness, he believed him.
source_text: But, he had never seen his friend in his present aspect: he had never at all known him in his present character. For the first time the Doctor felt, now, that his suffering was strength and power. For the first time he felt that in that sharp fire, he had slowly forged the iron which could break the prison door of his daughter’s husband, and deliver him. “It all tended to a good end, my friend; it was not mere waste and ruin. As my beloved child was helpful in restoring me to myself, I will be helpful now in restoring the dearest part of herself to her; by the aid of Heaven I will do it!” Thus, Doctor Manette. And when Jarvis Lorry saw the kindled eyes, the resolute face, the calm strong look and bearing of the man whose life always seemed to him to have been stopped, like a clock, for so many years, and then set going again with an energy which had lain dormant during the cessation of its usefulness, he believed.

text: The man drew a little farther off as soon as he could, and the road mender waved himself with his blue cap, feeling the air thick and stifling. If he needed a King and Queen to bring him back to life, he was lucky enough to have the cure right there before him; for before long the big-faced King and the fair-faced Queen arrived in their gilded coach, accompanied by the bright Bull’s Eye of their court, a sparkling crowd of laughing ladies and elegant lords. Amid jewels and silks and powder and splendor, among figures posed in graceful contempt and handsome, disdainful faces of both sexes, the road mender soaked himself until he was almost drunk with the sight of it all, so that he shouted, Long live the King, Long live the Queen, Long live everybody and everything! as though he had never in his life heard of the ever-present Jacques. Then came gardens, courtyards, terraces, fountains, grassy banks, more King and Queen, more Bull’s Eye, more lords and ladies, more cries of Long live them all! until he was actually weeping with feeling. Throughout the whole spectacle, which lasted some three hours, he had enough shouting, weeping, and sentimental company to last him, while Defarge kept hold of his collar the entire time, as though to stop him from hurling himself at the objects of his sudden worship and ripping them apart.
source_text: The man moved a little further away, as soon as he could, and the mender of roads fanned himself with his blue cap: feeling it mightily close and oppressive. If he needed a King and Queen to restore him, he was fortunate in having his remedy at hand; for, soon the large-faced King and the fair-faced Queen came in their golden coach, attended by the shining Bull’s Eye of their Court, a glittering multitude of laughing ladies and fine lords; and in jewels and silks and powder and splendour and elegantly spurning figures and handsomely disdainful faces of both sexes, the mender of roads bathed himself, so much to his temporary intoxication, that he cried Long live the King, Long live the Queen, Long live everybody and everything! as if he had never heard of ubiquitous Jacques in his time. Then, there were gardens, courtyards, terraces, fountains, green banks, more King and Queen, more Bull’s Eye, more lords and ladies, more Long live they all! until he absolutely wept with sentiment. During the whole of this scene, which lasted some three hours, he had plenty of shouting and weeping and sentimental company, and throughout Defarge held him by the collar, as if to restrain him from flying at the objects of his brief devotion and tearing them to pieces.

text: After buying a few small groceries and a measure of oil for the lamp, Miss Pross remembered the wine they needed. After peering into several wine shops, she stopped at the sign of the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, not far from the National Palace, formerly—and twice formerly—the Tuileries, where the look of the place rather caught her fancy. It seemed quieter than any other establishment of the same kind they had passed, and though it was decorated with red patriotic caps, it was not as red as the others. Putting the question to Mr. Cruncher and finding him to agree with her, Miss Pross made for the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, with her cavalier in attendance.
source_text: Having purchased a few small articles of grocery, and a measure of oil for the lamp, Miss Pross bethought herself of the wine they wanted. After peeping into several wine-shops, she stopped at the sign of the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, not far from the National Palace, once (and twice) the Tuileries, where the aspect of things rather took her fancy. It had a quieter look than any other place of the same description they had passed, and, though red with patriotic caps, was not so red as the rest. Sounding Mr. Cruncher, and finding him of her opinion, Miss Pross resorted to the Good Republican Brutus of Antiquity, attended by her cavalier.

text: Lucie shivered as he dropped two more billets into his basket, but while the wood-sawyer was at work, it was impossible to be there and not come under his eye. After that, in order to win his favor, she always addressed him first and often gave him a little drink money, which he was quick to accept.
source_text: Lucie shuddered as he threw two more billets into his basket, but it was impossible to be there while the wood-sawyer was at work, and not be in his sight. Thenceforth, to secure his good will, she always spoke to him first, and often gave him drink-money, which he readily received.

text: As she crossed the bridge, she let the door key slip into the river. Reaching the cathedral a few minutes ahead of her escort, and waiting there, she thought: what if the key had already been caught in a net, what if someone recognized it, what if the door were opened and the remains found, what if she were arrested at the gate, carried off to prison, and accused of murder? While these restless thoughts kept fluttering through her mind, the escort arrived, received her, and led her away.
source_text: In crossing the bridge, she dropped the door key in the river. Arriving at the cathedral some few minutes before her escort, and waiting there, she thought, what if the key were already taken in a net, what if it were identified, what if the door were opened and the remains discovered, what if she were stopped at the gate, sent to prison, and charged with murder! In the midst of these fluttering thoughts, the escort appeared, took her in, and took her away.

text: Doctor Moreau did not come back until the morning of the fourth day after he had been gone. So much of that terrible interval as could be hidden from Clara’s knowledge was so carefully concealed from her, that it was not until long afterward, when France and she were parted by great distances, that she learned eleven hundred unarmed captives of every age and both sexes had been slain by the mob; that four days and nights had been made black by that crime of terror; and that the very air about her had been fouled by the dead. She knew only that there had been an assault upon the prisons, that all political prisoners had stood in peril, and that some had been dragged forth by the crowd and butchered.
source_text: Doctor Manette did not return until the morning of the fourth day of his absence. So much of what had happened in that dreadful time as could be kept from the knowledge of Lucie was so well concealed from her, that not until long afterwards, when France and she were far apart, did she know that eleven hundred defenceless prisoners of both sexes and all ages had been killed by the populace; that four days and nights had been darkened by this deed of horror; and that the air around her had been tainted by the slain. She only knew that there had been an attack upon the prisons, that all political prisoners had been in danger, and that some had been dragged out by the crowd and murdered.

text: The dash with which the carriage left the village and climbed the rise beyond was soon brought under by the steepness of the hill. Little by little, it sank to a walking pace, swaying and jolting upward through the many sweet smells of a summer night. The postilions, with a thousand gauzy gnats wheeling about them in place of the Furies, calmly repaired the points on their whip lashes; the valet walked beside the horses; the courier could be heard trotting ahead into the dim distance.
source_text: The burst with which the carriage started out of the village and up the rise beyond, was soon checked by the steepness of the hill. Gradually, it subsided to a foot pace, swinging and lumbering upward among the many sweet scents of a summer night. The postilions, with a thousand gossamer gnats circling about them in lieu of the Furies, quietly mended the points to the lashes of their whips; the valet walked by the horses; the courier was audible, trotting on ahead into the dull distance.

text: These lines are traced, with painful labor, by the blunted iron tip with which I write, upon fragments of soot and charcoal scraped from the hearth and mingled with my own blood, in the final month of the tenth year of my imprisonment. All hope has wholly deserted my heart. By dreadful admonitions I have marked in myself, I know my reason cannot long remain entire; yet I do most solemnly avow that, at this hour, I am in full possession of my senses—that my recollection is exact and particular—and that I record the truth, as I shall answer for these my final words, whether they are ever read by mortal eyes or never, before the Eternal Judgment Seat.
source_text: “These words are formed by the rusty iron point with which I write with difficulty in scrapings of soot and charcoal from the chimney, mixed with blood, in the last month of the tenth year of my captivity. Hope has quite departed from my breast. I know from terrible warnings I have noted in myself that my reason will not long remain unimpaired, but I solemnly declare that I am at this time in the possession of my right mind--that my memory is exact and circumstantial--and that I write the truth as I shall answer for these my last recorded words, whether they be ever read by men or not, at the Eternal Judgment-seat.

text: In a city ruled by the axe, he stood alone at night, with a natural grief rising in him for the sixty-three who had been executed that day, and for tomorrow’s victims already waiting in the prisons for their fate, and for the tomorrows after that, and after that still again, the train of association that carried the words back to him, like a rusty old ship’s anchor hauled up from the depths, might have been easily traced. He did not try to follow it; instead, he repeated the words and went on.
source_text: In a city dominated by the axe, alone at night, with natural sorrow rising in him for the sixty-three who had been that day put to death, and for to-morrow’s victims then awaiting their doom in the prisons, and still of to-morrow’s and to-morrow’s, the chain of association that brought the words home, like a rusty old ship’s anchor from the deep, might have been easily found. He did not seek it, but repeated them and went on.

text: The undertaker gentlemen who had the charge of the affair offered a small remonstrance against these alterations in the rites; but, as the river lay uncomfortably close, and as more than one voice observed how effectually a plunge in cold water might restore obstinate members of the craft to prudence, the remonstrance was but weak and short-lived. So the new-ordered procession set forth, with a chimney-sweep at the reins of the hearse, instructed by the ordinary driver, who sat beside him under strict observation for that very end; while a pie-man, likewise accompanied by his own minister of state, guided the mourning coach. Before they had proceeded far down the Strand, a bear-keeper, one of the well-known street figures of the day, was enlisted as an extra embellishment; and his bear, a black creature, rough and most unkempt, lent a singularly funerary dignity to that section of the procession in which it marched.
source_text: The officiating undertakers made some protest against these changes in the ceremonies; but, the river being alarmingly near, and several voices remarking on the efficacy of cold immersion in bringing refractory members of the profession to reason, the protest was faint and brief. The remodelled procession started, with a chimney-sweep driving the hearse--advised by the regular driver, who was perched beside him, under close inspection, for the purpose--and with a pieman, also attended by his cabinet minister, driving the mourning coach. A bear-leader, a popular street character of the time, was impressed as an additional ornament, before the cavalcade had gone far down the Strand; and his bear, who was black and very mangy, gave quite an Undertaking air to that part of the procession in which he walked.

text: “That is so, yes, that is so. Courage! Duty! You have duty before you; useful duty. Miss Manette, your mother followed this path with you. And when she died—I believe heartbroken—after never once relaxing her fruitless search for your father, she left you, at two years old, to grow up blooming, beautiful, and happy, with no dark cloud hanging over you in the doubt whether your father had worn away his heart in prison, or lingered there through many long years and wasted away.”
source_text: “That’s right, that’s right. Courage! Business! You have business before you; useful business. Miss Manette, your mother took this course with you. And when she died--I believe broken-hearted--having never slackened her unavailing search for your father, she left you, at two years old, to grow to be blooming, beautiful, and happy, without the dark cloud upon you of living in uncertainty whether your father soon wore his heart out in prison, or wasted there through many lingering years.”

text: They began by fishing with a spade. After a while, the respected father seemed to be setting some contrivance in order, a thing like a monstrous corkscrew. Whatever tools they used, they used them laboriously, and kept at it with all their might, until the dreadful clang of the church clock so alarmed Little Ned that he fled away, his hair standing as bristly as his father’s.
source_text: They fished with a spade, at first. Presently the honoured parent appeared to be adjusting some instrument like a great corkscrew. Whatever tools they worked with, they worked hard, until the awful striking of the church clock so terrified Young Jerry, that he made off, with his hair as stiff as his father’s.

text: No more delicate nook than the one where the physician had his lodging was to be found in all London. It led nowhere through; and from the front windows of his rooms there opened a charming little prospect of street, with an air of retirement peculiarly its own. In those days, north of the Oxford Road, there were few houses indeed; woodland trees prospered, wild blossoms grew, and hawthorn was in flower in the fields that are now gone. By reason of this, rural breezes moved through Soho with a brisk and fearless freedom, instead of drooping into the parish like homeless beggars without a settlement; and here and there, not far distant, there stood a kindly south wall on which the peaches came to perfection in their season.
source_text: A quainter corner than the corner where the Doctor lived, was not to be found in London. There was no way through it, and the front windows of the Doctor’s lodgings commanded a pleasant little vista of street that had a congenial air of retirement on it. There were few buildings then, north of the Oxford-road, and forest-trees flourished, and wild flowers grew, and the hawthorn blossomed, in the now vanished fields. As a consequence, country airs circulated in Soho with vigorous freedom, instead of languishing into the parish like stray paupers without a settlement; and there was many a good south wall, not far off, on which the peaches ripened in their season.

text: “But I do, and you must take my word for it. Well, if you could endure such a worthless fellow, a man of so dubious a reputation, to be coming and going at odd hours, I should beg permission to come and go here as a privileged person; to be regarded as a useless piece of furniture—and I would add, were it not for the resemblance I have noticed between you and me, an unornamental one—kept out of kindness for the sake of its old services, and passed over without remark. I doubt whether I should abuse the privilege. It is a hundred to one that I should make use of it even four times in a year. Still, I dare say, it would be enough for me simply to know that I had it.”
source_text: “But I do, and you must take my word for it. Well! If you could endure to have such a worthless fellow, and a fellow of such indifferent reputation, coming and going at odd times, I should ask that I might be permitted to come and go as a privileged person here; that I might be regarded as an useless (and I would add, if it were not for the resemblance I detected between you and me, an unornamental) piece of furniture, tolerated for its old service, and taken no notice of. I doubt if I should abuse the permission. It is a hundred to one if I should avail myself of it four times in a year. It would satisfy me, I dare say, to know that I had it.”

text: As Carton came in, took his seat, and asked—in very indifferent French—for a small measure of wine, Madame Defarge let her eyes wander over him carelessly, then more sharply, then sharper still, and finally came up to him herself to ask what he had ordered.
source_text: As Carton walked in, took his seat and asked (in very indifferent French) for a small measure of wine, Madame Defarge cast a careless glance at him, and then a keener, and then a keener, and then advanced to him herself, and asked him what it was he had ordered.

text: He was interrupted by the sudden arrival of another man, to whom the others immediately gave way. At the sight of him, the wretched creature threw himself against his shoulder, sobbing and weeping, and pointing toward the fountain, where several women were bent over the still bundle, moving softly around it. Yet they were as silent as the men.
source_text: He was arrested by the quick arrival of another man, for whom the rest made way. On seeing him, the miserable creature fell upon his shoulder, sobbing and crying, and pointing to the fountain, where some women were stooping over the motionless bundle, and moving gently about it. They were as silent, however, as the men.

text: That night, the final night of the year, as it drew near nine o’clock, a man in black came to my gate, insisted on seeing me, and quietly followed my servant, Ernest Defarge, a young fellow, up the stairs. When my servant entered the room where I sat with my wife—O my wife, beloved of my heart! My fair young English wife!—we saw that the man, who had been thought to remain at the gate, was standing in silence just behind him.
source_text: “That night, the last night of the year, towards nine o’clock, a man in a black dress rang at my gate, demanded to see me, and softly followed my servant, Ernest Defarge, a youth, up-stairs. When my servant came into the room where I sat with my wife--O my wife, beloved of my heart! My fair young English wife!--we saw the man, who was supposed to be at the gate, standing silent behind him.

text: “The wives and mothers we have been accustomed to seeing since we were as small as this child—indeed, much smaller—have they not been little regarded? Have we not often seen their husbands and fathers cast into prison and kept away from them? All our lives long, have we not watched our sister women endure, in their own persons and in their children, poverty, nakedness, hunger, thirst, sickness, misery, oppression, and every kind of neglect?”
source_text: “The wives and mothers we have been used to see, since we were as little as this child, and much less, have not been greatly considered? We have known _their_ husbands and fathers laid in prison and kept from them, often enough? All our lives, we have seen our sister-women suffer, in themselves and in their children, poverty, nakedness, hunger, thirst, sickness, misery, oppression and neglect of all kinds?”

text: He had not come back earlier, he said, for the simple reason that he possessed no means of subsistence in France except those he had already relinquished; whereas in England he maintained himself by teaching the French tongue and French letters. He had returned when he did only in obedience to the urgent written appeal of a Frenchman, who declared that his life was imperilled by that absence. He had come back to preserve a fellow-citizen’s life, and to give his evidence, at whatever risk to himself, in the service of truth. Was such conduct a crime in the Republic’s eyes?
source_text: He had not returned sooner, he replied, simply because he had no means of living in France, save those he had resigned; whereas, in England, he lived by giving instruction in the French language and literature. He had returned when he did, on the pressing and written entreaty of a French citizen, who represented that his life was endangered by his absence. He had come back, to save a citizen’s life, and to bear his testimony, at whatever personal hazard, to the truth. Was that criminal in the eyes of the Republic?

text: The messenger rode back at an easy trot, pausing often at alehouses along the way to drink, yet seeming determined to keep his own counsel and to wear his hat pulled low over his eyes. Those eyes suited that fashion well: black on the surface, with no depth of color or expression, and set much too close together, as if they feared being detected in some misdeed if they kept too far apart. They gave him a sinister look beneath an old cocked hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and above a huge muffler for his chin and throat that fell almost to his knees. Whenever he stopped to drink, he lifted the muffler with his left hand only long enough to pour the liquor in with his right; once he had done so, he drew it up again.
source_text: The messenger rode back at an easy trot, stopping pretty often at ale-houses by the way to drink, but evincing a tendency to keep his own counsel, and to keep his hat cocked over his eyes. He had eyes that assorted very well with that decoration, being of a surface black, with no depth in the colour or form, and much too near together--as if they were afraid of being found out in something, singly, if they kept too far apart. They had a sinister expression, under an old cocked-hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and over a great muffler for the chin and throat, which descended nearly to the wearer’s knees. When he stopped for drink, he moved this muffler with his left hand, only while he poured his liquor in with his right; as soon as that was done, he muffled again.

text: Few children were in sight, and there were no dogs at all. As for the men and women, the prospect made plain the choice before them in this world: life reduced to the barest terms that could still sustain it, down in the little village beneath the mill; or else captivity and death in the governing prison on the crag.
source_text: Few children were to be seen, and no dogs. As to the men and women, their choice on earth was stated in the prospect--Life on the lowest terms that could sustain it, down in the little village under the mill; or captivity and Death in the dominant prison on the crag.

text: He had naturally held down much, and some revulsion might have been expected from him now that there was no longer any need for such restraint. Yet it was the old, frightened, desolate expression that troubled Mr. Lorry; and as he absentmindedly clasped his head and wandered drearily off into his own room when they went upstairs, Mr. Lorry was reminded of Defarge, the wine-shop keeper, and of the ride by starlight.
source_text: He had naturally repressed much, and some revulsion might have been expected in him when the occasion for repression was gone. But, it was the old scared lost look that troubled Mr. Lorry; and through his absent manner of clasping his head and drearily wandering away into his own room when they got up-stairs, Mr. Lorry was reminded of Defarge the wine-shop keeper, and the starlight ride.

text: “The other patient was lodged in a back room reached by a second staircase, in a place that was something like a loft above a stable. Part of it had a low plastered ceiling; the rest rose open to the ridge of the tiled roof, with beams crossing it overhead. In that section were stored hay and straw, bundles of firewood, and a heap of apples buried in sand. I had to go through that part to reach the other room. My memory is exact and unshaken. I test it with these particulars, and I see them all now, in this cell of mine in the Bastille, near the close of the tenth year of my imprisonment, just as I saw them that night.
source_text: “The other patient lay in a back room across a second staircase, which was a species of loft over a stable. There was a low plastered ceiling to a part of it; the rest was open, to the ridge of the tiled roof, and there were beams across. Hay and straw were stored in that portion of the place, fagots for firing, and a heap of apples in sand. I had to pass through that part, to get at the other. My memory is circumstantial and unshaken. I try it with these details, and I see them all, in this my cell in the Bastille, near the close of the tenth year of my captivity, as I saw them all that night.

text: The lady was young, graceful, and beautiful, though seeming to bear upon her countenance no promise of long years. She was in deep agitation. She announced herself to me as the wife of the Marquis de Valcour. I joined the title by which the boy had spoken of the elder brother with the initial worked into the scarf, and had little trouble in concluding that I had very recently seen that nobleman.
source_text: “The lady was young, engaging, and handsome, but not marked for long life. She was in great agitation. She presented herself to me as the wife of the Marquis St. Evrémonde. I connected the title by which the boy had addressed the elder brother, with the initial letter embroidered on the scarf, and had no difficulty in arriving at the conclusion that I had seen that nobleman very lately.

text: “‘Marquis,’ said the boy, turning toward him with his eyes wide open and his right hand lifted, ‘when the time comes for all these things to be accounted for, I call upon you and yours, down to the very last of your wicked line, to answer for them. I set this cross of blood upon you as a sign that I do so. When the time comes for all these things to be accounted for, I call upon your brother, the worst of that wicked line, to answer for them on his own. I set this cross of blood upon him as a sign that I do so.’
source_text: “‘Marquis,’ said the boy, turned to him with his eyes opened wide, and his right hand raised, ‘in the days when all these things are to be answered for, I summon you and yours, to the last of your bad race, to answer for them. I mark this cross of blood upon you, as a sign that I do it. In the days when all these things are to be answered for, I summon your brother, the worst of the bad race, to answer for them separately. I mark this cross of blood upon him, as a sign that I do it.’

text: Who could it be with Mr. Lorry—the owner of the riding-coat draped over the chair—who must not be seen? From whom had he just arrived, so that he came out agitated and astonished to gather his favorite into his arms? To whom did he seem to repeat her broken words when, lifting his voice and turning his head toward the door of the room he had come from, he said, “Taken to the Conciergerie, and ordered to appear tomorrow?”
source_text: Who could that be with Mr. Lorry--the owner of the riding-coat upon the chair--who must not be seen? From whom newly arrived, did he come out, agitated and surprised, to take his favourite in his arms? To whom did he appear to repeat her faltering words, when, raising his voice and turning his head towards the door of the room from which he had issued, he said: “Removed to the Conciergerie, and summoned for to-morrow?”

text: “What now? Do you belong in the madhouse?” said the wine-shop keeper, crossing the road and wiping out the jest with a handful of mud he had gathered for the purpose and flung over it. “Why do you write in the public streets? Is there—tell me—can you find no other place in which to write such words?”
source_text: “What now? Are you a subject for the mad hospital?” said the wine-shop keeper, crossing the road, and obliterating the jest with a handful of mud, picked up for the purpose, and smeared over it. “Why do you write in the public streets? Is there--tell me thou--is there no other place to write such words in?”

text: “I was very occupied that day, and could not finish my letter that night. I rose long before my usual hour the next morning in order to complete it. It was the last day of the year. The letter lay before me, just finished, when I was told that a lady was waiting and wished to see me.
source_text: “I was much engaged that day, and could not complete my letter that night. I rose long before my usual time next morning to finish it. It was the last day of the year. The letter was lying before me just completed, when I was told that a lady waited, who wished to see me.

text: The discreet Mr. Barrow said, in an exemplary tone of the voice he would have recommended under the circumstances, “How do you do, Mr. Arden? How do you do, sir?” and gave his hand. There was a peculiarity in the manner of that handshake, such as might always have been observed in any clerk of Fenwick’s who shook hands with a customer when the House pervaded the atmosphere. He shook in a self-renouncing way, as one who shook for Fenwick and Company.
source_text: The discreet Mr. Lorry said, in a sample tone of the voice he would recommend under the circumstances, “How do you do, Mr. Stryver? How do you do, sir?” and shook hands. There was a peculiarity in his manner of shaking hands, always to be seen in any clerk at Tellson’s who shook hands with a customer when the House pervaded the air. He shook in a self-abnegating way, as one who shook for Tellson and Co.

text: “I was beginning to fancy,” said Mr. Harbury, as he thrust his chestnut wig a little farther back upon his head, “that I should be forced to spend the night at Dallison’s. The day has been so crowded with affairs that we have scarcely known where to begin, or what course to take. There is such a disquiet in Paris, that it has, in truth, brought a perfect tide of confidence upon us! Our friends there appear quite unable to commit their property to our keeping quickly enough. Indeed, with some of them, there is fairly a fever for dispatching it to England.”
source_text: “I began to think,” said Mr. Lorry, pushing his brown wig back, “that I should have to pass the night at Tellson’s. We have been so full of business all day, that we have not known what to do first, or which way to turn. There is such an uneasiness in Paris, that we have actually a run of confidence upon us! Our customers over there, seem not to be able to confide their property to us fast enough. There is positively a mania among some of them for sending it to England.”

text: But that was not the change in the village, nor in many another like it. For scores of years past, Monseigneur had squeezed it and wrung it dry, and had rarely honored it with his presence except for the sports of the chase—at one time hunting men, at another hunting animals, whose preservation Monseigneur encouraged by setting aside edifying tracts of barbarous, barren wilderness. No. The change lay in the sight of strange low-born faces, not in the vanishing of Monseigneur’s high-born, finely carved, and otherwise refined and refining features.
source_text: But, this was not the change on the village, and on many a village like it. For scores of years gone by, Monseigneur had squeezed it and wrung it, and had seldom graced it with his presence except for the pleasures of the chase--now, found in hunting the people; now, found in hunting the beasts, for whose preservation Monseigneur made edifying spaces of barbarous and barren wilderness. No. The change consisted in the appearance of strange faces of low caste, rather than in the disappearance of the high caste, chiselled, and otherwise beautified and beautifying features of Monseigneur.

text: The response came from the man who had spoken second. “Doctor, your patients are people of consequence. As for the nature of the matter, our confidence in your skill assures us that you will determine it for yourself far better than we could explain it. Enough. Will you kindly step into the carriage?”
source_text: “The reply to this was made by him who had spoken second. ‘Doctor, your clients are people of condition. As to the nature of the case, our confidence in your skill assures us that you will ascertain it for yourself better than we can describe it. Enough. Will you please to enter the carriage?’

text: “I see that child who lay against her breast and bore my name, now a man making his way upward along the path of life that once was mine. I see him advancing so nobly that my name is made famous there by the brightness of his. I see the stains I cast upon it slowly fading away. I see him, first among righteous judges and honored men, bringing to this place a boy who bears my name, with the brow I know and the golden hair—then still lovely to behold, and with no trace of this day’s ruin—and I hear him, in a tender, wavering voice, tell the child my story.
source_text: “I see that child who lay upon her bosom and who bore my name, a man winning his way up in that path of life which once was mine. I see him winning it so well, that my name is made illustrious there by the light of his. I see the blots I threw upon it, faded away. I see him, fore-most of just judges and honoured men, bringing a boy of my name, with a forehead that I know and golden hair, to this place--then fair to look upon, with not a trace of this day’s disfigurement--and I hear him tell the child my story, with a tender and a faltering voice.

text: The time had come for Saint Antoine to carry out his monstrous scheme of hanging men aloft like lanterns, to prove what he could do and what he was. Saint Antoine’s blood was up, while the blood of tyranny and rule by the iron hand was flowing down—down the steps of the Hôtel de Ville, where the governor’s body lay—down to the sole of Madame Defarge’s shoe, where she had stamped on the body to hold it steady for mutilation. “Lower the lamp there!” cried Saint Antoine, after casting about with a fierce look for some fresh way of killing; “there is one of his soldiers to stand guard!” The hanging sentinel was set in place, and the sea swept onward.
source_text: The hour was come, when Saint Antoine was to execute his horrible idea of hoisting up men for lamps to show what he could be and do. Saint Antoine’s blood was up, and the blood of tyranny and domination by the iron hand was down--down on the steps of the Hotel de Ville where the governor’s body lay--down on the sole of the shoe of Madame Defarge where she had trodden on the body to steady it for mutilation. “Lower the lamp yonder!” cried Saint Antoine, after glaring round for a new means of death; “here is one of his soldiers to be left on guard!” The swinging sentinel was posted, and the sea rushed on.

text: While Sydney Carton and the Sheep of the prisons were in the adjoining dark room, speaking so softly that no sound could be caught, Mr. Lorry regarded Jerry with marked doubt and mistrust. The honest tradesman’s way of meeting that look did little to inspire confidence; he shifted from one leg to the other as though he had fifty of them and meant to try each in turn; he studied his fingernails with a suspicious minuteness of attention; and whenever Mr. Lorry’s glance met his, he was seized by that peculiar little cough that calls for a hand held before the mouth, a complaint seldom, if ever, associated with complete openness of character.
source_text: While Sydney Carton and the Sheep of the prisons were in the adjoining dark room, speaking so low that not a sound was heard, Mr. Lorry looked at Jerry in considerable doubt and mistrust. That honest tradesman’s manner of receiving the look, did not inspire confidence; he changed the leg on which he rested, as often as if he had fifty of those limbs, and were trying them all; he examined his finger-nails with a very questionable closeness of attention; and whenever Mr. Lorry’s eye caught his, he was taken with that peculiar kind of short cough requiring the hollow of a hand before it, which is seldom, if ever, known to be an infirmity attendant on perfect openness of character.

text: They were not of one mind, and desired to withdraw. My Lord, perhaps with General Washington in his thoughts, expressed some astonishment that they had not yet agreed, but intimated his satisfaction that they should retire under guard, and then withdrew himself. The hearing had occupied the whole day, and the lamps in the court were now being kindled. It began to be whispered that the jury would be absent for a long time. The spectators straggled away in search of refreshment, and the prisoner retired to the rear of the dock and seated himself.
source_text: They were not agreed, and wished to retire. My Lord (perhaps with George Washington on his mind) showed some surprise that they were not agreed, but signified his pleasure that they should retire under watch and ward, and retired himself. The trial had lasted all day, and the lamps in the court were now being lighted. It began to be rumoured that the jury would be out a long while. The spectators dropped off to get refreshment, and the prisoner withdrew to the back of the dock, and sat down.

text: The question was put to the wood-chopper, who answered yes in great haste, and took the chance to declare himself the warmest of patriots, and, in truth, the most wretched of patriots, should anything keep him from the delight of smoking his after-dinner pipe while he watched the comical national barber at his work. He was so loud and earnest in this profession, that he might have been suspected—perhaps was suspected, by the dark eyes that regarded him scornfully from within Madame Lafleur’s head—of carrying about with him, every hour of the day, his private little terrors for his own safety.
source_text: The question was addressed to the wood-sawyer, who hurriedly replied in the affirmative: seizing the occasion to add that he was the most ardent of Republicans, and that he would be in effect the most desolate of Republicans, if anything prevented him from enjoying the pleasure of smoking his afternoon pipe in the contemplation of the droll national barber. He was so very demonstrative herein, that he might have been suspected (perhaps was, by the dark eyes that looked contemptuously at him out of Madame Defarge’s head) of having his small individual fears for his own personal safety, every hour in the day.

text: By the decree of the Republic, One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, every dwelling was required to bear, upon its door or doorpost, the names of all who lived within, written plainly in letters of prescribed size at a prescribed and commodious height above the earth. Thus the name of Mr. Tobin Cruncher stood duly adorned upon the post below; and, as the afternoon lengthened into shadow, the gentleman himself emerged from his watch over the painter whom Doctor Manville had engaged to inscribe, among the rest, the name of Charles Evrard, known as Darnay.
source_text: It was an ordinance of the Republic One and Indivisible of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, that on the door or doorpost of every house, the name of every inmate must be legibly inscribed in letters of a certain size, at a certain convenient height from the ground. Mr. Jerry Cruncher’s name, therefore, duly embellished the doorpost down below; and, as the afternoon shadows deepened, the owner of that name himself appeared, from overlooking a painter whom Doctor Manette had employed to add to the list the name of Charles Evrémonde, called Darnay.

text: The spirited horse, checked at once by the whip’s flat refusal, made a determined scramble forward, and the other three went after him. Once again the Dover coach labored onward, while the jack-boots of its passengers squelched along beside it. They had halted when the coach halted, and they kept as near to it as though they were chained. Had any one of the three been bold enough to suggest that another should walk a little ahead into the fog and blackness, he would very likely have earned himself a bullet on the spot as a footpad.
source_text: The emphatic horse, cut short by the whip in a most decided negative, made a decided scramble for it, and the three other horses followed suit. Once more, the Dover mail struggled on, with the jack-boots of its passengers squashing along by its side. They had stopped when the coach stopped, and they kept close company with it. If any one of the three had had the hardihood to propose to another to walk on a little ahead into the mist and darkness, he would have put himself in a fair way of getting shot instantly as a highwayman.

text: Mr. Harbury at once undertook the matter, and the conference came to a close. They spent the day in the country, and the Doctor was completely himself again. During the three days that followed, he continued in perfect health, and on the fourteenth day he departed to rejoin Helena and her husband. Mr. Harbury had already explained to him the precaution adopted to account for his silence; he had written to Helena accordingly, and she entertained no suspicion whatever.
source_text: Mr. Lorry readily engaged for that, and the conference was ended. They passed the day in the country, and the Doctor was quite restored. On the three following days he remained perfectly well, and on the fourteenth day he went away to join Lucie and her husband. The precaution that had been taken to account for his silence, Mr. Lorry had previously explained to him, and he had written to Lucie in accordance with it, and she had no suspicions.

text: A thin, high peal of laughter, with the mingled voices of men, women, and children, rang down the street while the wine played out its brief enchantment. There was little violence in the sport, and a great deal of merriment. It carried with it a strange fellowship, a visible tendency in all to seek another, so that the luckier, and those of easier spirits, fell into capricious embraces, the drinking of toasts, hand-clasping, and even the joining of hands in dances, a dozen at a time. When the wine was spent, and the places where it had flowed most freely were combed by fingers into a pattern like a gridiron, these demonstrations died away as abruptly as they had begun. The man who had left his saw standing in the firewood he was cutting set it going once more; the woman who had set aside on a doorstep the small pot of hot ashes by which she had been trying to ease the ache in her own pinched hands and feet, or in those of her child, went back to it; men with bare arms, tangled hair, and hollow faces, who had come up into the winter light from the cellars, turned away to descend again; and over the whole scene there gathered a melancholy that seemed more native to it than the sun.
source_text: A shrill sound of laughter and of amused voices--voices of men, women, and children--resounded in the street while this wine game lasted. There was little roughness in the sport, and much playfulness. There was a special companionship in it, an observable inclination on the part of every one to join some other one, which led, especially among the luckier or lighter-hearted, to frolicsome embraces, drinking of healths, shaking of hands, and even joining of hands and dancing, a dozen together. When the wine was gone, and the places where it had been most abundant were raked into a gridiron-pattern by fingers, these demonstrations ceased, as suddenly as they had broken out. The man who had left his saw sticking in the firewood he was cutting, set it in motion again; the women who had left on a door-step the little pot of hot ashes, at which she had been trying to soften the pain in her own starved fingers and toes, or in those of her child, returned to it; men with bare arms, matted locks, and cadaverous faces, who had emerged into the winter light from cellars, moved away, to descend again; and a gloom gathered on the scene that appeared more natural to it than sunshine.

text: “Regarding any future means of making a living, miss,” Mr. Cruncher replied, “I hope so. As for any present use of this blessed old head of mine, I think not. Would you do me the favor, miss, of noting down two promises and vows that I wish to record in this crisis?”
source_text: “Respectin’ a future spear o’ life, miss,” returned Mr. Cruncher, “I hope so. Respectin’ any present use o’ this here blessed old head o’ mine, I think not. Would you do me the favour, miss, to take notice o’ two promises and wows wot it is my wishes fur to record in this here crisis?”

text: “But this is my father’s story, sir, and I’m beginning to think”—the oddly weathered brow was fixed on him with great attention—“that when I was left an orphan, because my mother outlived my father by only two years, it was you who brought me to England. I’m almost certain it was you.”
source_text: “But this is my father’s story, sir; and I begin to think”--the curiously roughened forehead was very intent upon him--“that when I was left an orphan through my mother’s surviving my father only two years, it was you who brought me to England. I am almost sure it was you.”

text: But, amid that sea of countenances where every stern and frantic visage seemed alive with its own fierce passion, there were two clusters of faces—seven in each—that stood in such stark opposition to all the rest as never any wave that bore its most dreadful wreckage. Seven faces of captives, flung suddenly free by the storm that had broken their sepulchre, were lifted high above the throng: all aghast, all bewildered, all stricken with wonder, as though the Judgment Day had arrived and the jubilant shapes about them were doomed souls. And there were seven others, borne yet higher still, seven faces of the dead, whose languid lids and half-disclosed eyes seemed to await that same Last Day. Unmoved faces, and yet not wholly without expression, but holding it in suspense, not cancellation; faces, rather, caught in a dreadful pause, as if they had still to lift the fallen lids of those eyes and let the pale lips bear their witness: “THOU DIDST IT!”
source_text: But, in the ocean of faces where every fierce and furious expression was in vivid life, there were two groups of faces--each seven in number--so fixedly contrasting with the rest, that never did sea roll which bore more memorable wrecks with it. Seven faces of prisoners, suddenly released by the storm that had burst their tomb, were carried high overhead: all scared, all lost, all wondering and amazed, as if the Last Day were come, and those who rejoiced around them were lost spirits. Other seven faces there were, carried higher, seven dead faces, whose drooping eyelids and half-seen eyes awaited the Last Day. Impassive faces, yet with a suspended--not an abolished--expression on them; faces, rather, in a fearful pause, as having yet to raise the dropped lids of the eyes, and bear witness with the bloodless lips, “THOU DIDST IT!”

text: “Yes—but your weakness is that, at times, you need to see both your victim and your chance in order to keep yourself going. Learn to keep yourself going without that. When the moment arrives, release a tiger and a devil; but until then, keep the tiger and the devil chained—unseen—yet always at hand.”
source_text: “Yes! But it is your weakness that you sometimes need to see your victim and your opportunity, to sustain you. Sustain yourself without that. When the time comes, let loose a tiger and a devil; but wait for the time with the tiger and the devil chained--not shown--yet always ready.”

text: Cyril Valcour, known as Marlan. Freed yesterday. Charged anew and seized again yesterday. The bill of accusation placed in his hands last night. An object of suspicion and denunciation, an enemy of the Republic, a noble, one sprung from a house of tyrants, one of a proscribed bloodline, for having once turned their now-abolished privileges to the shameless oppression of the people. Cyril Valcour, known as Marlan, by virtue of such proscription, utterly Dead in Law.
source_text: Charles Evrémonde, called Darnay. Released yesterday. Reaccused and retaken yesterday. Indictment delivered to him last night. Suspected and Denounced enemy of the Republic, Aristocrat, one of a family of tyrants, one of a race proscribed, for that they had used their abolished privileges to the infamous oppression of the people. Charles Evrémonde, called Darnay, in right of such proscription, absolutely Dead in Law.

text: All eyes turned toward the jury. It was the same set of steadfast patriots and loyal republicans as yesterday, as the day before, and as it would be tomorrow and the day after. Among them, one man stood out in eager prominence, with a hungry-looking face and fingers that kept drifting to his mouth, a figure whose appearance delighted the spectators. A bloodthirsty, cannibal-faced, murderous juryman—the Jacques Three of St. Antoine. The whole jury seemed like a panel of dogs called together to judge a deer.
source_text: Every eye was turned to the jury. The same determined patriots and good republicans as yesterday and the day before, and to-morrow and the day after. Eager and prominent among them, one man with a craving face, and his fingers perpetually hovering about his lips, whose appearance gave great satisfaction to the spectators. A life-thirsting, cannibal-looking, bloody-minded juryman, the Jacques Three of St. Antoine. The whole jury, as a jury of dogs empannelled to try the deer.

text: Had he ever served as a spy himself? No; he rejected the filthy insinuation with disdain. Then what did he live on? His estate. And where was this estate? He could not exactly say where it lay. What was it? That was no one else’s concern. Had he come by it through inheritance? Yes, he had. From whom? A remote connection. How remote? Very. Had he ever been confined in prison? Certainly not. Never in a debtors’ prison? He failed to see what that had to do with the matter. Never in a debtors’ prison?—Come now, once more. Never? Yes. How many times? Two or three. Not five or six? Possibly. What was his profession? A gentleman. Ever been kicked? It was possible. Often? No. Ever kicked down a staircase? Certainly not; on one occasion he received a blow at the top of a flight, and descended the stairs of his own free will. Had he been kicked then for cheating at dice? Something of that kind had been alleged by the drunken scoundrel who laid hands on him, but it was false. Would he swear it was false? He would swear it most positively. Had he ever lived by cheating at cards? Never. Had he ever lived by play? No more than other gentlemen did. Had he ever borrowed money from the prisoner? Yes. Had he ever paid it back? No. Was not this supposed intimacy with the prisoner, in truth, a very small one, imposed upon the prisoner in coaches, inns, and packets? No. Was he sure he had seen the prisoner with those lists? Perfectly sure. Knew nothing more of the lists? Nothing. Had he not obtained them himself, for example? No. Did he expect to gain anything by this testimony? No. Was he not in regular government pay and employment, set to lay traps? Oh, certainly not. Or to do anything at all? Oh, certainly not. Would he swear to that? Again and again. Had he any motive except pure patriotism? None whatever.
source_text: Had he ever been a spy himself? No, he scorned the base insinuation. What did he live upon? His property. Where was his property? He didn’t precisely remember where it was. What was it? No business of anybody’s. Had he inherited it? Yes, he had. From whom? Distant relation. Very distant? Rather. Ever been in prison? Certainly not. Never in a debtors’ prison? Didn’t see what that had to do with it. Never in a debtors’ prison?--Come, once again. Never? Yes. How many times? Two or three times. Not five or six? Perhaps. Of what profession? Gentleman. Ever been kicked? Might have been. Frequently? No. Ever kicked downstairs? Decidedly not; once received a kick on the top of a staircase, and fell downstairs of his own accord. Kicked on that occasion for cheating at dice? Something to that effect was said by the intoxicated liar who committed the assault, but it was not true. Swear it was not true? Positively. Ever live by cheating at play? Never. Ever live by play? Not more than other gentlemen do. Ever borrow money of the prisoner? Yes. Ever pay him? No. Was not this intimacy with the prisoner, in reality a very slight one, forced upon the prisoner in coaches, inns, and packets? No. Sure he saw the prisoner with these lists? Certain. Knew no more about the lists? No. Had not procured them himself, for instance? No. Expect to get anything by this evidence? No. Not in regular government pay and employment, to lay traps? Oh dear no. Or to do anything? Oh dear no. Swear that? Over and over again. No motives but motives of sheer patriotism? None whatever.

text: Of all those cries and the thousand wild confusions, “The Prisoners!” was the shout most eagerly caught up by the sea that poured in, as though there were endless multitudes, no less than endless time and space. When the leading waves had swept by, carrying the jail officers with them, and menacing them all with sudden death if any hidden corner still remained unrevealed, Defarge placed his strong hand upon the breast of one of these men—a grey-headed fellow holding a lighted torch—and drew him away from the others, setting him between himself and the wall.
source_text: Of all these cries, and ten thousand incoherences, “The Prisoners!” was the cry most taken up by the sea that rushed in, as if there were an eternity of people, as well as of time and space. When the foremost billows rolled past, bearing the prison officers with them, and threatening them all with instant death if any secret nook remained undisclosed, Defarge laid his strong hand on the breast of one of these men--a man with a grey head, who had a lighted torch in his hand--separated him from the rest, and got him between himself and the wall.

text: The Sheep of the prisons turned from him to Sydney Carton and said, with greater firmness, “This has come to a head. I am going on duty soon, and cannot outstay my time. You told me you had a proposal; what is it? Mind, there is no use in expecting too much from me. Ask me to do something in my own office that would put my head in any extra danger, and I would sooner trust my life to the chance of a refusal than to the chance of my consenting. In short, that is the choice I should make. You talk of desperation. We are all desperate here. Remember that! If I choose, I may denounce you, and I can swear my way through stone walls, and so can others. Now, what do you want with me?”
source_text: The Sheep of the prisons turned from him to Sydney Carton, and said, with more decision, “It has come to a point. I go on duty soon, and can’t overstay my time. You told me you had a proposal; what is it? Now, it is of no use asking too much of me. Ask me to do anything in my office, putting my head in great extra danger, and I had better trust my life to the chances of a refusal than the chances of consent. In short, I should make that choice. You talk of desperation. We are all desperate here. Remember! I may denounce you if I think proper, and I can swear my way through stone walls, and so can others. Now, what do you want with me?”

text: He paced from one end of his sumptuous bedroom to the other, once more seeing in his mind the scattered fragments of the day’s journey that rose there unbidden: the slow climb up the hill at sunset, the sinking sun, the descent, the mill, the prison perched on the crag, the little village in the hollow, the peasants at the fountain, and the road mender in his blue cap, pointing out the chain beneath the carriage. The sight of that fountain brought back the fountain in Paris, the small bundle lying on the step, the women bending over it, and the tall man with his arms raised, crying, “Dead!”
source_text: He moved from end to end of his voluptuous bedroom, looking again at the scraps of the day’s journey that came unbidden into his mind; the slow toil up the hill at sunset, the setting sun, the descent, the mill, the prison on the crag, the little village in the hollow, the peasants at the fountain, and the mender of roads with his blue cap pointing out the chain under the carriage. That fountain suggested the Paris fountain, the little bundle lying on the step, the women bending over it, and the tall man with his arms up, crying, “Dead!”

text: Dress was the one sure charm, the single talisman used to keep everything in its proper place. Everyone was attired as for a Fancy Ball that was never meant to end. From the Palace of the Tuileries, through Monseigneur and the entire Court, through the Chambers, the Tribunals of Justice, and all of society except the scarecrows, the Fancy Ball came down to the Common Executioner: who, in obedience to the charm, was expected to conduct his duties “frizzled, powdered, in a gold-laced coat, pumps, and white silk stockings.” At the gallows and the wheel—the axe was a rarity—Monsieur Paris, as it was the episcopal fashion among his brother Professors in the provinces, Monsieur Orleans, and the rest to call him, held office in this elegant attire. And who among the guests at Monseigneur’s reception in that year of our Lord seventeen hundred and eighty could possibly have doubted that a system founded on a frizzled hangman, powdered, gold-laced, pumped, and white-silk-stockinged, would outlast the stars themselves?
source_text: Dress was the one unfailing talisman and charm used for keeping all things in their places. Everybody was dressed for a Fancy Ball that was never to leave off. From the Palace of the Tuileries, through Monseigneur and the whole Court, through the Chambers, the Tribunals of Justice, and all society (except the scarecrows), the Fancy Ball descended to the Common Executioner: who, in pursuance of the charm, was required to officiate “frizzled, powdered, in a gold-laced coat, pumps, and white silk stockings.” At the gallows and the wheel--the axe was a rarity--Monsieur Paris, as it was the episcopal mode among his brother Professors of the provinces, Monsieur Orleans, and the rest, to call him, presided in this dainty dress. And who among the company at Monseigneur’s reception in that seventeen hundred and eightieth year of our Lord, could possibly doubt, that a system rooted in a frizzled hangman, powdered, gold-laced, pumped, and white-silk stockinged, would see the very stars out!

text: “Without it, may I not still save you, Mr. Vale? May I not draw you back—pardon me once more!—to a wiser path? Is there no means by which I can repay your trust? I know it is a trust,” she said with modesty, after a brief pause, and with tears that were wholly sincere; “I know you would speak thus to no other. Can I not make some good use of it for your own sake, Mr. Vale?”
source_text: “Without it, can I not save you, Mr. Carton? Can I not recall you--forgive me again!--to a better course? Can I in no way repay your confidence? I know this is a confidence,” she modestly said, after a little hesitation, and in earnest tears, “I know you would say this to no one else. Can I turn it to no good account for yourself, Mr. Carton?”

text: “Ah well!” said Miss Priss, briskly smothering a sigh as she looked at her cherished one’s bright hair in the glow of the hearth, “then we must learn to wait and be patient: that is the whole of it. We must keep a brave front and strike below, as my brother Silas used to say. Now, Mr. Granger!--Don’t you stir, little Lady!”
source_text: “Heigh-ho-hum!” said Miss Pross, cheerfully repressing a sigh as she glanced at her darling’s golden hair in the light of the fire, “then we must have patience and wait: that’s all. We must hold up our heads and fight low, as my brother Solomon used to say. Now, Mr. Cruncher!--Don’t you move, Ladybird!”

text: “I should not think so. It may be the temper of his mind to require, at all times, some singular employment. This may arise partly from its own nature, and partly from suffering. The less it is engaged with what is sound and wholesome, the more liable it will be to wander into a less wholesome course. He may have watched himself, and come to perceive it.”
source_text: “I think not. It may be the character of his mind, to be always in singular need of occupation. That may be, in part, natural to it; in part, the result of affliction. The less it was occupied with healthy things, the more it would be in danger of turning in the unhealthy direction. He may have observed himself, and made the discovery.”

text: As the road-mender went on with his dusty task, and the hail-clouds drifted off to uncover bright bands and streaks of sky, answered by silver flashes over the land, the little man, who now wore a red cap instead of a blue one, seemed spellbound by the man lying on the mound of stones. He kept glancing so constantly at him that he worked almost without thinking, and, it would have seemed, with very poor results. The bronze-coloured face, the thick black hair and beard, the rough red wool cap, the coarse patched clothing of homespun cloth and the hairy skins of animals, the strong body worn thin by hard living, and the hard, desperate set of the mouth in sleep filled the road-mender with awe. The traveller had come a great distance, and his feet were sore, his ankles raw and bleeding; his heavy shoes, packed with leaves and grass, had been burdensome over the many long leagues, and his clothes were rubbed through to holes, just as he himself was worn to sores. Bending down beside him, the road-mender tried to catch a glimpse of hidden weapons in his breast or elsewhere; but it was useless, for the man slept with his arms folded over him, set as firmly as his lips. Fortified towns, with their palisades, guardrooms, gates, ditches, and drawbridges, seemed to the road-mender nothing more than air compared with this figure. And when he raised his eyes from the sleeper to the horizon and looked about him, his small imagination saw other such figures, unchecked by any barrier, moving toward centers all across France.
source_text: As the road-mender plied his dusty labour, and the hail-clouds, rolling away, revealed bright bars and streaks of sky which were responded to by silver gleams upon the landscape, the little man (who wore a red cap now, in place of his blue one) seemed fascinated by the figure on the heap of stones. His eyes were so often turned towards it, that he used his tools mechanically, and, one would have said, to very poor account. The bronze face, the shaggy black hair and beard, the coarse woollen red cap, the rough medley dress of home-spun stuff and hairy skins of beasts, the powerful frame attenuated by spare living, and the sullen and desperate compression of the lips in sleep, inspired the mender of roads with awe. The traveller had travelled far, and his feet were footsore, and his ankles chafed and bleeding; his great shoes, stuffed with leaves and grass, had been heavy to drag over the many long leagues, and his clothes were chafed into holes, as he himself was into sores. Stooping down beside him, the road-mender tried to get a peep at secret weapons in his breast or where not; but, in vain, for he slept with his arms crossed upon him, and set as resolutely as his lips. Fortified towns with their stockades, guard-houses, gates, trenches, and drawbridges, seemed to the mender of roads, to be so much air as against this figure. And when he lifted his eyes from it to the horizon and looked around, he saw in his small fancy similar figures, stopped by no obstacle, tending to centres all over France.

text: Madame Defarge, his wife, was seated in the shop behind the counter when he entered. She was a stout woman of about his own age, with a vigilant eye that seemed seldom to settle on anything, a broad hand thickly ringed, a firm face, powerful features, and an air of perfect composure. There was something in Madame Defarge’s character that suggested she was not often at fault in those reckonings over which she presided, especially when her own advantage was concerned. Sensitive to the cold, she was wrapped in fur, and a bright shawl was wound about her head, though not so closely as to hide her large earrings. Her knitting lay before her, but she had set it aside to pick her teeth with a toothpick. In this posture, with her right elbow resting on her left hand, Madame Defarge said nothing when her husband came in, but gave one small cough. This, together with the slight lifting of her darkly marked eyebrows above the toothpick, told her husband that he had better look about the shop among the customers for any new face that might have come in while he had stepped across the way.
source_text: Madame Defarge, his wife, sat in the shop behind the counter as he came in. Madame Defarge was a stout woman of about his own age, with a watchful eye that seldom seemed to look at anything, a large hand heavily ringed, a steady face, strong features, and great composure of manner. There was a character about Madame Defarge, from which one might have predicated that she did not often make mistakes against herself in any of the reckonings over which she presided. Madame Defarge being sensitive to cold, was wrapped in fur, and had a quantity of bright shawl twined about her head, though not to the concealment of her large earrings. Her knitting was before her, but she had laid it down to pick her teeth with a toothpick. Thus engaged, with her right elbow supported by her left hand, Madame Defarge said nothing when her lord came in, but coughed just one grain of cough. This, in combination with the lifting of her darkly defined eyebrows over her toothpick by the breadth of a line, suggested to her husband that he would do well to look round the shop among the customers, for any new customer who had dropped in while he stepped over the way.

text: The valet having come and gone, Monsieur the Marquis paced to and fro in his loose chamber robe, gently preparing himself for sleep on that hot, still night. Moving softly about the room, his slippered feet making no sound on the floor, he went like a polished tiger—like some bewitched marquis of the irredeemably wicked kind in a tale, whose periodic change into tiger shape was either just ending or just beginning.
source_text: The valet come and gone, Monsieur the Marquis walked to and fro in his loose chamber-robe, to prepare himself gently for sleep, that hot still night. Rustling about the room, his softly-slippered feet making no noise on the floor, he moved like a refined tiger:--looked like some enchanted marquis of the impenitently wicked sort, in story, whose periodical change into tiger form was either just going off, or just coming on.

text: “But what did I know? The deed had not yet been done, nor did he place his confidence in me. Observe! Even in those circumstances, I do not give my testimony. Monsieur the Marquis points me out with his finger, standing beside our little fountain, and says, ‘To me! Bring that rascal!’ Faith, gentlemen, I offer nothing.”
source_text: “But what did I know? The deed was not then accomplished, neither did he confide in me. Observe! Under those circumstances even, I do not offer my testimony. Monsieur the Marquis indicates me with his finger, standing near our little fountain, and says, ‘To me! Bring that rascal!’ My faith, messieurs, I offer nothing.”

text: It was only the question that rose in the first shock of his bewilderment; the reply lay plain enough before him. If the effect had not been wrought by some real, adequate, and answering cause, then how had he, Edward Carbury, come to be there at all? How had he come to sink asleep, still dressed, upon the couch in Dr. Moreau’s consulting-room, and afterward find himself, in the pale early morning, standing before the good doctor’s bedroom door and turning these matters over in his mind?
source_text: It was but the inquiry of his first confusion and astonishment, the answer being obvious. If the impression were not produced by a real corresponding and sufficient cause, how came he, Jarvis Lorry, there? How came he to have fallen asleep, in his clothes, on the sofa in Doctor Manette’s consulting-room, and to be debating these points outside the Doctor’s bedroom door in the early morning?

text: In the first shock and terror of her plight, Miss Pross kept as far from the body as she could, and ran down the stairs to summon help that could not be of use. Happily, she remembered in time what the consequences of such a step must be, and checked herself, turning back. It was dreadful to enter the room again; yet she did enter, and even came near enough to take the bonnet and the other things she needed to wear. She put them on in the staircase, first shutting and locking the door and carrying off the key. Then she sat for a few moments on the stairs to catch her breath and to cry, and afterward rose and hurried away.
source_text: In the first fright and horror of her situation, Miss Pross passed the body as far from it as she could, and ran down the stairs to call for fruitless help. Happily, she bethought herself of the consequences of what she did, in time to check herself and go back. It was dreadful to go in at the door again; but, she did go in, and even went near it, to get the bonnet and other things that she must wear. These she put on, out on the staircase, first shutting and locking the door and taking away the key. She then sat down on the stairs a few moments to breathe and to cry, and then got up and hurried away.

text: Yet there was this consolation: every soul assembled in the great inn of Lord Valmont was flawlessly attired. Had Judgment Day itself been proclaimed a day of ceremony, every one of them would have stood there in perpetual propriety. Such curling and powdering, such towers of hair so artfully raised, such dainty complexions preserved and repaired by cunning hands, such brave swords to delight the eye, and such refined honour to the nostrils, could surely sustain the world forever and a day. The most elegant men of the highest breeding wore little dangling ornaments that made a faint music as they moved with leisure; those golden chains seemed like precious tiny bells. And with that chiming, and the whisper of silk and brocade and fine linen, there went a stirring in the air that seemed to blow poor Jacques, and his ravenous hunger, a long way off.
source_text: But, the comfort was, that all the company at the grand hotel of Monseigneur were perfectly dressed. If the Day of Judgment had only been ascertained to be a dress day, everybody there would have been eternally correct. Such frizzling and powdering and sticking up of hair, such delicate complexions artificially preserved and mended, such gallant swords to look at, and such delicate honour to the sense of smell, would surely keep anything going, for ever and ever. The exquisite gentlemen of the finest breeding wore little pendent trinkets that chinked as they languidly moved; these golden fetters rang like precious little bells; and what with that ringing, and with the rustle of silk and brocade and fine linen, there was a flutter in the air that fanned Saint Antoine and his devouring hunger far away.

text: Miss Pross remembered afterward, and remembered to the end of her life, that as she laid her hands upon Sydney’s arm and looked up into his face, begging him not to injure Solomon, there was a resolute strength in the arm and a kind of inspiration in the eyes which not only belied his easy manner, but altered and ennobled the man. At the time, she was too much taken up with anxiety for the brother who so ill deserved her love, and with Sydney’s kindly assurances, to give full heed to what she saw.
source_text: Miss Pross recalled soon afterwards, and to the end of her life remembered, that as she pressed her hands on Sydney’s arm and looked up in his face, imploring him to do no hurt to Solomon, there was a braced purpose in the arm and a kind of inspiration in the eyes, which not only contradicted his light manner, but changed and raised the man. She was too much occupied then with fears for the brother who so little deserved her affection, and with Sydney’s friendly reassurances, adequately to heed what she observed.

text: By then, Lucie had sunk into a stupor on the floor at his feet, still holding fast to his hand. Miss Pross had laid the child on his own bed, and her head had slowly drooped onto the pillow beside her lovely little charge. Oh, the long, long night, with the poor wife’s moans! And oh, the long, long night, with no sign of her father’s return and no news at all!
source_text: Lucie had, by that time, fallen into a stupor on the floor at his feet, clinging to his hand.
