Miss Pross had laid the child down on his own bed, and her head had gradually fallen on the pillow beside her pretty charge. O the long, long night, with the moans of the poor wife! And O the long, long night, with no return of her father and no tidings!

text: “My dear Charles,” said Mr. Lorry, with cheerful assurance, “you are naming some of the very reasons for my going, not for my staying away. It is safe enough for me; no one is likely to trouble an old man who is close upon fourscore, when there are so many people there far more worth troubling than I am. As for its being a disordered city, if it were not disordered there would be no need to send someone from our House here to our House there, who knows the city and the business of old, and is in Tellson’s confidence. As for the doubtful traveling, the long road, and the winter weather, if I were not ready to endure a few inconveniences for Tellson’s sake, after all these years, who should be?”
source_text: “My dear Charles,” said Mr. Lorry, with cheerful confidence, “you touch some of the reasons for my going: not for my staying away. It is safe enough for me; nobody will care to interfere with an old fellow of hard upon fourscore when there are so many people there much better worth interfering with. As to its being a disorganised city, if it were not a disorganised city there would be no occasion to send somebody from our House here to our House there, who knows the city and the business, of old, and is in Tellson’s confidence. As to the uncertain travelling, the long journey, and the winter weather, if I were not prepared to submit myself to a few inconveniences for the sake of Tellson’s, after all these years, who ought to be?”

text: The night grew darker. The trees that surrounded the old chateau and set its lonely place apart stirred in the rising wind, as if they were threatening the great mass of the building, huge and shadowy in the gloom. Up the two flights of terrace steps the rain rushed wildly and struck the massive door, like a swift messenger calling to those inside; restless gusts of wind swept through the hall among the old spears and knives, and went moaning up the stairs, shaking the curtains of the bed in which the last Marquis had slept. From east, west, north, and south, through the woods, four rough, heavy-footed figures crushed the tall grass and snapped branches, moving cautiously until they gathered in the courtyard. Four lights flashed there, then drifted off in separate directions, and once more everything was black.
source_text: The night deepened. The trees environing the old chateau, keeping its solitary state apart, moved in a rising wind, as though they threatened the pile of building massive and dark in the gloom. Up the two terrace flights of steps the rain ran wildly, and beat at the great door, like a swift messenger rousing those within; uneasy rushes of wind went through the hall, among the old spears and knives, and passed lamenting up the stairs, and shook the curtains of the bed where the last Marquis had slept. East, West, North, and South, through the woods, four heavy-treading, unkempt figures crushed the high grass and cracked the branches, striding on cautiously to come together in the courtyard. Four lights broke out there, and moved away in different directions, and all was black again.

text: “Oh, Carton, Carton, dear Carton!” cried little Lucie, rising at once and flinging her arms around him with passionate tenderness, in a sudden outburst of grief. “Now that you have come, I am sure you will do something to help mamma, something to save papa! Oh, look at her, dear Carton! Of all the people who love her, can you bear to see her like this?”
source_text: “Oh, Carton, Carton, dear Carton!” cried little Lucie, springing up and throwing her arms passionately round him, in a burst of grief. “Now that you have come, I think you will do something to help mamma, something to save papa! O, look at her, dear Carton! Can you, of all the people who love her, bear to see her so?”

text: “It would be madness if I asked you to run away; but am I asking that? If I ask you to go out through that door, then tell me it is madness and stay here. Change that neckcloth for mine, that coat for mine. While you do, let me take this ribbon from your hair and loosen it, letting your hair fall free, like mine!”
source_text: “It would be madness if I asked you to escape; but do I? When I ask you to pass out at that door, tell me it is madness and remain here. Change that cravat for this of mine, that coat for this of mine. While you do it, let me take this ribbon from your hair, and shake out your hair like this of mine!”

text: As the prisoner of so many years gazed in turn with fixed attention at Mr. Lorry and at Defarge, certain long-erased traces of an active, searching intelligence in the center of his brow gradually struggled up through the black haze that had settled over him. They were shadowed over again, grew dimmer, and vanished; yet they had been there. And so precisely was that same expression mirrored in the fair young face of the woman who had edged along the wall to a place where she could see him, and now stood looking at him—with hands that at first had been lifted only in startled pity, if not to keep him away and shut out the sight of him, but that were now reaching toward him, trembling with the desire to draw that ghostly face to her warm young breast and love it back to life and hope—so exactly was the expression repeated there, though in stronger lines, that it seemed as if it had flashed like a wandering light from him to her.
source_text: As the captive of many years sat looking fixedly, by turns, at Mr. Lorry and at Defarge, some long obliterated marks of an actively intent intelligence in the middle of the forehead, gradually forced themselves through the black mist that had fallen on him. They were overclouded again, they were fainter, they were gone; but they had been there. And so exactly was the expression repeated on the fair young face of her who had crept along the wall to a point where she could see him, and where she now stood looking at him, with hands which at first had been only raised in frightened compassion, if not even to keep him off and shut out the sight of him, but which were now extending towards him, trembling with eagerness to lay the spectral face upon her warm young breast, and love it back to life and hope--so exactly was the expression repeated (though in stronger characters) on her fair young face, that it looked as though it had passed like a moving light, from him to her.

text: “It will mean nothing to you. Such fancies, I think, are only striking while they are our own invention; they cannot be handed on. I have sometimes sat here alone in the evening, listening, until I have persuaded myself that the echoes were those of all the footsteps that are yet to come into our lives.”
source_text: “It will seem nothing to you. Such whims are only impressive as we originate them, I think; they are not to be communicated. I have sometimes sat alone here of an evening, listening, until I have made the echoes out to be the echoes of all the footsteps that are coming by-and-bye into our lives.”

text: Among the riders in the tumbrils, some take in these sights, and all they see on that last road, with a blank, unblinking gaze; others look on with a slow, reluctant curiosity about the ways of life and of men. Some sit with their heads hanging, sunk in mute despair; and there are others so conscious of their appearance that they send out toward the crowd the same looks they have learned from theatres and paintings. A few shut their eyes and think, or try to gather their wandering thoughts into order. Only one, and he a wretched fellow with a mad look, is so broken and so stupefied by terror that he sings and attempts to dance. Not one of them, by glance or movement, makes any appeal to the pity of the people.
source_text: Of the riders in the tumbrils, some observe these things, and all things on their last roadside, with an impassive stare; others, with a lingering interest in the ways of life and men. Some, seated with drooping heads, are sunk in silent despair; again, there are some so heedful of their looks that they cast upon the multitude such glances as they have seen in theatres, and in pictures. Several close their eyes, and think, or try to get their straying thoughts together. Only one, and he a miserable creature, of a crazed aspect, is so shattered and made drunk by horror, that he sings, and tries to dance. Not one of the whole number appeals by look or gesture, to the pity of the people.

text: There was nothing in this act to draw my special notice, for I had often seen ordinary people struck more often than dogs. But the other of the two, being angered as well, struck the man in the same way with his arm; and the appearance and manner of the brothers were then so exactly alike that I first realized they were twin brothers.
source_text: “There was nothing in this action to attract my particular attention, for I had seen common people struck more commonly than dogs. But, the other of the two, being angry likewise, struck the man in like manner with his arm; the look and bearing of the brothers were then so exactly alike, that I then first perceived them to be twin brothers.

text: Another year, and then another, had slipped away, until Monsieur Étienne Darnel was settled in England as an instructor of the French tongue, well read in French books and letters. In a later time he might have borne the title of Professor; in that day he was only called a Tutor. He read with young men who could spare a little leisure and curiosity for the study of a living language spoken across the world, and he led them into the riches of its thought and imagination. He could also write of those matters in clear English, and render them into clear English as well. Such teachers were not easy to find in those days; princes who had once been, and kings who were yet to be, had not entered the class of instructors, and no shattered aristocrat had yet fallen out of Tellson’s ledgers to become a cook or carpenter. As a tutor, making the scholar’s path both agreeable and profitable, and as a graceful translator who brought more than dictionary knowledge to his task, the young Monsieur Darnel was quickly noticed and favored. He knew, besides, the condition of his native land, and that knowledge was daily growing in consequence. Thus, with patient resolve and unwearied labor, he prospered.
source_text: More months, to the number of twelve, had come and gone, and Mr. Charles Darnay was established in England as a higher teacher of the French language who was conversant with French literature. In this age, he would have been a Professor; in that age, he was a Tutor. He read with young men who could find any leisure and interest for the study of a living tongue spoken all over the world, and he cultivated a taste for its stores of knowledge and fancy. He could write of them, besides, in sound English, and render them into sound English. Such masters were not at that time easily found; Princes that had been, and Kings that were to be, were not yet of the Teacher class, and no ruined nobility had dropped out of Tellson’s ledgers, to turn cooks and carpenters. As a tutor, whose attainments made the student’s way unusually pleasant and profitable, and as an elegant translator who brought something to his work besides mere dictionary knowledge, young Mr. Darnay soon became known and encouraged. He was well acquainted, more-over, with the circumstances of his country, and those were of ever-growing interest. So, with great perseverance and untiring industry, he prospered.

text: “I have imagined my daughter as wholly forgetful of me—or, rather, as entirely ignorant of me, unaware that I ever existed. Year after year, I have counted the years of her life. I have seen her married to a man who knew nothing of what became of me. I have utterly vanished from the memory of the living, and in the generation that followed, my place was simply an empty space.”
source_text: “I have pictured my daughter, to myself, as perfectly forgetful of me--rather, altogether ignorant of me, and unconscious of me. I have cast up the years of her age, year after year. I have seen her married to a man who knew nothing of my fate. I have altogether perished from the remembrance of the living, and in the next generation my place was a blank.”

text: “No!” replied the spy. “I surrender. I admit that we were so detested by that riotous crowd that I barely escaped from England without being ducked to death, and that Cly was so hunted up and down that he would never have made his escape at all but for that pretence. Though how this man knows it was a pretence is a marvel of marvels to me.”
source_text: “No!” returned the spy. “I throw up. I confess that we were so unpopular with the outrageous mob, that I only got away from England at the risk of being ducked to death, and that Cly was so ferreted up and down, that he never would have got away at all but for that sham. Though how this man knows it was a sham, is a wonder of wonders to me.”

text: In the cell there stood a chair, a table, and a straw mattress. While the gaoler made his round of these objects and of the four walls before departing, a stray thought crossed the mind of the prisoner, who leaned against the opposite wall, that the man was so unnaturally swollen in face and figure as to resemble a drowned body filled with water. After the gaoler had gone, he mused in the same absent way, “Now I am left here, as though I were dead.” Then, pausing to look down at the mattress, he drew back from it with a sickened feeling and thought, “And here, in these crawling things, is the first state of the body after death.”
source_text: There were in the cell, a chair, a table, and a straw mattress. As the gaoler made a general inspection of these objects, and of the four walls, before going out, a wandering fancy wandered through the mind of the prisoner leaning against the wall opposite to him, that this gaoler was so unwholesomely bloated, both in face and person, as to look like a man who had been drowned and filled with water. When the gaoler was gone, he thought in the same wandering way, “Now am I left, as if I were dead.” Stopping then, to look down at the mattress, he turned from it with a sick feeling, and thought, “And here in these crawling creatures is the first condition of the body after death.”

text: At last it came about that he was obliged to exchange the tool he held for the knife he used at his trade. It lay upon the side opposite to that on which she was standing. He had picked it up, and was bending once more to his work, when his eye fell upon the hem of her gown. He lifted his gaze, and saw her face. The two onlookers sprang forward, but she restrained them with a slight gesture of her hand. She had no dread of his turning the knife against her, though they had.
source_text: It happened, at length, that he had occasion to change the instrument in his hand, for his shoemaker’s knife. It lay on that side of him which was not the side on which she stood. He had taken it up, and was stooping to work again, when his eyes caught the skirt of her dress. He raised them, and saw her face. The two spectators started forward, but she stayed them with a motion of her hand. She had no fear of his striking at her with the knife, though they had.

text: He followed his guide into a guard-room redolent of common wine and tobacco, where certain soldiers and patriots, asleep and awake, drunk and sober, and in every neutral state between slumber and watchfulness, drunkenness and sobriety, stood or lay about. The light in the guard-house, half supplied by the fading oil-lamps of the night and half by the overcast day, was itself in a like uncertain condition. A few registers lay open upon a desk, over which an officer of a rough, dark countenance was presiding.
source_text: He accompanied his conductor into a guard-room, smelling of common wine and tobacco, where certain soldiers and patriots, asleep and awake, drunk and sober, and in various neutral states between sleeping and waking, drunkenness and sobriety, were standing and lying about. The light in the guard-house, half derived from the waning oil-lamps of the night, and half from the overcast day, was in a correspondingly uncertain condition. Some registers were lying open on a desk, and an officer of a coarse, dark aspect, presided over these.

text: The spectators saw in the two figures a young lady of scarcely more than twenty, and a gentleman who was plainly her father: a man of remarkable appearance, owing to the complete whiteness of his hair and to a certain indefinable intensity in his face—not an active intensity, but one of meditation and inward converse. When this look rested on him, he seemed old; but when it was disturbed and dispersed, as it was now in an instant when he spoke to his daughter, he appeared a handsome man, still not beyond the prime of life.
source_text: The spectators saw in the two figures, a young lady of little more than twenty, and a gentleman who was evidently her father; a man of a very remarkable appearance in respect of the absolute whiteness of his hair, and a certain indescribable intensity of face: not of an active kind, but pondering and self-communing. When this expression was upon him, he looked as if he were old; but when it was stirred and broken up--as it was now, in a moment, on his speaking to his daughter--he became a handsome man, not past the prime of life.

text: A trading boat, its sail the muted color of a dead leaf, then drifted into view, passed him, and vanished. As the quiet wake it left upon the water faded, the prayer that had risen from his heart for a merciful judgment of all his poor blindnesses and mistakes ended with the words, “I am the resurrection and the life.”
source_text: A trading-boat, with a sail of the softened colour of a dead leaf, then glided into his view, floated by him, and died away. As its silent track in the water disappeared, the prayer that had broken up out of his heart for a merciful consideration of all his poor blindnesses and errors, ended in the words, “I am the resurrection and the life.”

text: “Ay. Between ourselves, ma’am,” returned Mr. Weller, “there’s the main principle of it; and for that very reason, if you cast your eye over any of those impudent newspapers, you’ll always find as the poor invalids has been comforted with a bit of cheese. That’s the standing regulation now, Mrs. Green, from one end of the country to t’other. But, all the same,” said the beadle, pausing to open his parcel, “these are matters of office, ma’am; not for common talk; except, as I might observe, among parochial functionaries like ourselves. This here is the port wine, ma’am, as the guardians ordered for the sick ward; real, fresh, true port wine; drawn from the cask only this morning; bright as crystal, and not a grain of lees in it!”
source_text: “Yes. Betwixt you and me, ma’am,” returned Mr. Bumble, “that’s the great principle; and that’s the reason why, if you look at any cases that get into them owdacious newspapers, you’ll always observe that sick families have been relieved with slices of cheese. That’s the rule now, Mrs. Corney, all over the country. But, however,” said the beadle, stopping to unpack his bundle, “these are official secrets, ma’am; not to be spoken of; except, as I may say, among the porochial officers, such as ourselves. This is the port wine, ma’am, that the board ordered for the infirmary; real, fresh, genuine port wine; only out of the cask this forenoon; clear as a bell, and no sediment!”

text: “She was hanging round me all day, and all night too, when I was laid up on my back; and you, like the blackhearted wolf you are, kept clear of us,” said Sikes. “We were poor all the while too, and I fancy, one way or another, it’s fretted and worn on her; and being shut up here so long has made her uneasy—eh?”
source_text: “She was hanging about me all day, and night too, when I was stretched on my back; and you, like a blackhearted wolf as you are, kept yourself aloof,” said Sikes. “We was poor too, all the time, and I think, one way or other, it’s worried and fretted her; and that being shut up here so long has made her restless—eh?”

text: She could not have been more than seventeen. So delicately and exquisitely fashioned; so gentle, so mild, so pure and beautiful; it seemed as though the earth were not her proper element, nor its coarse inhabitants suitable companions for her. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and seemed written on her noble head, appeared scarcely to belong to her years, or even to this world; and yet the shifting sweetness and good temper of her expression, the countless lights that played across her face and left no shadow there; above all, the smile—the bright, contented, happy smile—seemed made for home, and for the peace and happiness of the hearth.
source_text: She was not past seventeen. Cast in so slight and exquisite a mould; so mild and gentle; so pure and beautiful; that earth seemed not her element, nor its rough creatures her fit companions. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and was stamped upon her noble head, seemed scarcely of her age, or of the world; and yet the changing expression of sweetness and good humour, the thousand lights that played about the face, and left no shadow there; above all, the smile, the cheerful, happy smile, were made for Home, and fireside peace and happiness.

text: Oliver was seized with such dread when he recognized the place that, for a moment, he forgot the pain of his wound and thought only of escape. Escape! He could scarcely keep his feet: and even if he had been in full possession of all the best strength of his slight, youthful frame, where could he have fled? He pressed against the garden gate; it was unfastened and swung open on its hinges. He staggered across the lawn, mounted the steps, knocked faintly at the door, and, his strength wholly giving way, sank down against one of the pillars of the little portico.
source_text: Oliver felt such fear come over him when he recognised the place, that, for the instant, he forgot the agony of his wound, and thought only of flight. Flight! He could scarcely stand: and if he were in full possession of all the best powers of his slight and youthful frame, whither could he fly? He pushed against the garden-gate; it was unlocked, and swung open on its hinges. He tottered across the lawn; climbed the steps; knocked faintly at the door; and, his whole strength failing him, sunk down against one of the pillars of the little portico.

text: “But what am I to do?” Rose said. “How can I make any use of this message if you are not here? Go back—why would you long to rejoin companions you have described in such dreadful terms? If you will repeat this account to the gentleman I can call in a moment from the adjoining room, you may be sent at once to some secure place, and all within half an hour.”
source_text: “But what can I do?” said Rose. “To what use can I turn this communication without you? Back! Why do you wish to return to companions you paint in such terrible colors? If you repeat this information to a gentleman whom I can summon in an instant from the next room, you can be consigned to some place of safety without half an hour’s delay.”

text: These thoughts ran through Fagin’s mind during the brief time he sat alone in the housebreaker’s room; and, with them foremost, he had seized the opportunity, afterward given him, to sound the girl by the broken hints he let fall at parting. There was no look of surprise, no feigned failure to catch his meaning. The girl plainly understood him. Her parting glance showed that.
source_text: These things passed through the mind of Fagin, during the short time he sat alone, in the housebreaker’s room; and with them uppermost in his thoughts, he had taken the opportunity afterwards afforded him, of sounding the girl in the broken hints he threw out at parting. There was no expression of surprise, no assumption of an inability to understand his meaning. The girl clearly comprehended it. Her glance at parting showed _that_.

text: “It’s all over, Bill!” cried Toby; “leave the child, and show them a clean pair of heels.” With this farewell counsel, Mr. Crackit, preferring the risk of being shot by his companion to the certainty of capture by his pursuers, fairly wheeled about and fled at full speed. Sikes ground his teeth, cast one glance around him, flung over the motionless figure of Oliver the cape in which he had been hastily wrapped, and ran along the face of the hedge, as though to draw the eyes of those behind him from the spot where the boy lay; then, after a brief pause before another hedge that crossed it at right angles, he flung his pistol high into the air, cleared the obstacle in a single bound, and was gone.
source_text: “It’s all up, Bill!” cried Toby; “drop the kid, and show ’em your heels.” With this parting advice, Mr. Crackit, preferring the chance of being shot by his friend, to the certainty of being taken by his enemies, fairly turned tail, and darted off at full speed. Sikes clenched his teeth; took one look around; threw over the prostrate form of Oliver, the cape in which he had been hurriedly muffled; ran along the front of the hedge, as if to distract the attention of those behind, from the spot where the boy lay; paused, for a second, before another hedge which met it at right angles; and whirling his pistol high into the air, cleared it at a bound, and was gone.

text: For a considerable while after it had been introduced into this doleful and disordered world by the parish doctor, it remained very much a question whether the infant would live long enough to receive any name whatever; and in that event it is more than likely that these recollections would never have come to light at all; or, if they had, that being confined to no more than two pages, they would have enjoyed the priceless distinction of forming the briefest and most scrupulously exact specimen of biography ever offered in the writings of any nation or century.
source_text: For a long time after it was ushered into this world of sorrow and trouble, by the parish surgeon, it remained a matter of considerable doubt whether the child would survive to bear any name at all; in which case it is somewhat more than probable that these memoirs would never have appeared; or, if they had, that being comprised within a couple of pages, they would have possessed the inestimable merit of being the most concise and faithful specimen of biography, extant in the literature of any age or country.

text: Oliver needed no urging to set out, and in scarcely five minutes they were on their way to Craven Street. When they reached the house, Rose left Oliver in the coach, saying she was going in first to prepare the old gentleman for his visitor; and, sending her card up by the servant, she asked to see Mr. Brownlow on very urgent business. The servant soon returned and begged that she would come upstairs, and, following him into an upper room, Miss Maylie was introduced to an elderly gentleman of kindly appearance, dressed in a bottle-green coat. Not far from him sat another old gentleman, in nankeen breeches and gaiters, who did not seem especially benevolent, and who sat with his hands clasped over the top of a thick stick, his chin resting on it.
source_text: Oliver needed no prompting to despatch, and in little more than five minutes they were on their way to Craven Street. When they arrived there, Rose left Oliver in the coach, under pretence of preparing the old gentleman to receive him; and sending up her card by the servant, requested to see Mr. Brownlow on very pressing business. The servant soon returned, to beg that she would walk upstairs; and following him into an upper room, Miss Maylie was presented to an elderly gentleman of benevolent appearance, in a bottle-green coat. At no great distance from whom, was seated another old gentleman, in nankeen breeches and gaiters; who did not look particularly benevolent, and who was sitting with his hands clasped on the top of a thick stick, and his chin propped thereupon.

text: “Pray, my dear young lady, do not pursue that point,” said Mr. Fairchild, checking Clara just as she was about to speak. “The promise shall be observed. I do not believe it will, in the least, hinder our proceedings. Yet before we can determine on any exact plan, it will be needful first to see the girl; to learn from her whether she will be willing to point out this Vane, on the understanding that he is to be dealt with by ourselves, and not by the law; or, if she cannot, or will not do so, to obtain from her such an account of his haunts, and such a description of his person, as may enable us to identify him. She cannot be seen until next Sunday night; and this is Tuesday. I should therefore propose that, in the interval, we remain altogether quiet, and keep the whole matter secret even from Arthur himself.”
source_text: “Do not discuss the point, my dear young lady, pray,” said Mr. Brownlow, interrupting Rose as she was about to speak. “The promise shall be kept. I don’t think it will, in the slightest degree, interfere with our proceedings. But, before we can resolve upon any precise course of action, it will be necessary to see the girl; to ascertain from her whether she will point out this Monks, on the understanding that he is to be dealt with by us, and not by the law; or, if she will not, or cannot do that, to procure from her such an account of his haunts and description of his person, as will enable us to identify him. She cannot be seen until next Sunday night; this is Tuesday. I would suggest that in the meantime, we remain perfectly quiet, and keep these matters secret even from Oliver himself.”

text: “Well,” said the doctor, “you are a strange fellow. But of course they will have you in parliament at the election before Christmas, and these abrupt turnings and alterations are no bad preparation for a political career. There is something to that. Good training is always desirable, whether the contest be for office, cup, or sweepstakes.”
source_text: “Well,” said the doctor, “you are a queer fellow. But of course they will get you into parliament at the election before Christmas, and these sudden shiftings and changes are no bad preparation for political life. There’s something in that. Good training is always desirable, whether the race be for place, cup, or sweepstakes.”

text: The chamber where the boys took their meals was a vast stone dining-hall, with a great copper vat at one end, from which the master, wearing a special apron and helped by one or two women, served out the gruel at dinner-time. Each boy received one basin of this nourishing compound, and no more—save upon occasions of extraordinary public merriment, when he was allowed, in addition, two ounces and a quarter of bread. The dishes were never in want of cleansing. The boys scoured them with their spoons until they gleamed again; and when they had completed this task, which was not of long duration, the spoons being almost as large as the basins, they would sit fixedly regarding the copper with such ravenous looks as though they could have devoured the very stones that made it up; while, in the meantime, they busied themselves most diligently with sucking their fingers, in hopes of gathering up any scattered drops of gruel that might have fallen upon them. Boys, as a rule, possess excellent appetites. Young Elias Fenn and his companions endured the miseries of a slow famine for three months; and at length they grew so fierce and famished that one lad, tall for his years and unaccustomed to such treatment—for his father had once kept a little pie-shop—hinted darkly to his fellows that unless he received another bowl of gruel _per diem_, he feared he might one night accidentally consume the boy who slept beside him, a feeble child of delicate years. He had a strange, hungry look in his eye; and they believed him entirely. A consultation was called; lots were drawn to decide who should go up to the master after supper that very evening and beg for more; and the lot fell to Elias Fenn.
source_text: The room in which the boys were fed, was a large stone hall, with a copper at one end: out of which the master, dressed in an apron for the purpose, and assisted by one or two women, ladled the gruel at mealtimes. Of this festive composition each boy had one porringer, and no more—except on occasions of great public rejoicing, when he had two ounces and a quarter of bread besides. The bowls never wanted washing. The boys polished them with their spoons till they shone again; and when they had performed this operation (which never took very long, the spoons being nearly as large as the bowls), they would sit staring at the copper, with such eager eyes, as if they could have devoured the very bricks of which it was composed; employing themselves, meanwhile, in sucking their fingers most assiduously, with the view of catching up any stray splashes of gruel that might have been cast thereon. Boys have generally excellent appetites. Oliver Twist and his companions suffered the tortures of slow starvation for three months: at last they got so voracious and wild with hunger, that one boy, who was tall for his age, and hadn’t been used to that sort of thing (for his father had kept a small cook-shop), hinted darkly to his companions, that unless he had another basin of gruel _per diem_, he was afraid he might some night happen to eat the boy who slept next him, who happened to be a weakly youth of tender age. He had a wild, hungry eye; and they implicitly believed him. A council was held; lots were cast who should walk up to the master after supper that evening, and ask for more; and it fell to Oliver Twist.

text: “Good heavens above, and protect us! Wash your hands, and let me comb and part your hair properly for you, child,” said Mrs. Bedwin. “Mercy me! If we had known he was going to ask for you, we’d have put a clean collar on you and dressed you up as neat as a pin!”
source_text: “Bless us, and save us! Wash your hands, and let me part your hair nicely for you, child,” said Mrs. Bedwin. “Dear heart alive! If we had known he would have asked for you, we would have put you a clean collar on, and made you as smart as sixpence!”

When his master paused at the margin of a pool and turned to summon him, he came to a dead stop.
source_text: The animal looked up into his master’s face while these preparations were making; whether his instinct apprehended something of their purpose, or the robber’s sidelong look at him was sterner than ordinary, he skulked a little farther in the rear than usual, and cowered as he came more slowly along. When his master halted at the brink of a pool, and looked round to call him, he stopped outright.

text: It was market-morning. The ground lay almost ankle-deep in slime and ordure; and above it there hung a dense vapour, forever rising from the steaming bodies of the cattle and blending with the mist that seemed to settle upon the chimney-roofs. Every pen in the middle of the wide yard, together with as many makeshift enclosures as could be forced into the remaining space, was crammed with sheep; while along the gutter-side, fastened to posts, stood long ranks of bullocks and oxen, three and four deep. Peasants, meat-sellers, cattle-drivers, hucksters, boys, pilferers, loiterers, and vagabonds of the lowest stamp were all jostled together in one confused multitude; the shrill whistling of the drovers, the barking of dogs, the roaring and struggling of the oxen, the bleating of sheep, the grunting and squealing of pigs, the cries of the hawkers, the shouts, curses, and disputes on every hand; the clanging of bells and the din of voices pouring from every tavern; the crowding, thrusting, driving, beating, whooping, and yelling; the frightful and jarring tumult that echoed from every part of the market; and the unwashed, unshaven, wretched, and begrimed figures perpetually hurrying to and fro, and breaking through and back into the press—gave to the whole a stunning, bewildering character that wholly overpowered the senses.
source_text: It was market-morning. The ground was covered, nearly ankle-deep, with filth and mire; a thick steam, perpetually rising from the reeking bodies of the cattle, and mingling with the fog, which seemed to rest upon the chimney-tops, hung heavily above. All the pens in the centre of the large area, and as many temporary pens as could be crowded into the vacant space, were filled with sheep; tied up to posts by the gutter side were long lines of beasts and oxen, three or four deep. Countrymen, butchers, drovers, hawkers, boys, thieves, idlers, and vagabonds of every low grade, were mingled together in a mass; the whistling of drovers, the barking dogs, the bellowing and plunging of the oxen, the bleating of sheep, the grunting and squeaking of pigs, the cries of hawkers, the shouts, oaths, and quarrelling on all sides; the ringing of bells and roar of voices, that issued from every public-house; the crowding, pushing, driving, beating, whooping and yelling; the hideous and discordant dim that resounded from every corner of the market; and the unwashed, unshaven, squalid, and dirty figures constantly running to and fro, and bursting in and out of the throng; rendered it a stunning and bewildering scene, which quite confounded the senses.

text: Scarce had the grave interview come to an end, and Oliver been settled once more to repose, when the physician, having first dried his eyes and reproached them for their sudden weakness, descended the stairs to confer with Mr. Hargrove. Finding the parlours empty, it occurred to him that he might with greater advantage begin the business in the kitchen; so to the kitchen he went.
source_text: The momentous interview was no sooner concluded, and Oliver composed to rest again, than the doctor, after wiping his eyes, and condemning them for being weak all at once, betook himself downstairs to open upon Mr. Giles. And finding nobody about the parlours, it occurred to him, that he could perhaps originate the proceedings with better effect in the kitchen; so into the kitchen he went.

text: After a long bout of bargaining and quarrelling, Fagin drove the required advance down from five pounds to three pounds four and sixpence, insisting with many grave protestations that this would leave him no more than eighteenpence to keep house on; while Mr. Sikes, in a surly tone, observed that if he could not get any more he must go home with him. Meanwhile the Dodger and Master Bates put the provisions away in the cupboard. The Jew then took leave of his affectionate friend and set off homeward, accompanied by Nancy and the boys; Mr. Sikes, in the meantime, threw himself upon the bed and settled down to sleep away the hours until the young lady’s return.
source_text: After a great deal of haggling and squabbling, Fagin beat down the amount of the required advance from five pounds to three pounds four and sixpence: protesting with many solemn asseverations that that would only leave him eighteen-pence to keep house with; Mr. Sikes sullenly remarking that if he couldn’t get any more he must accompany him home; with the Dodger and Master Bates put the eatables in the cupboard. The Jew then, taking leave of his affectionate friend, returned homeward, attended by Nancy and the boys: Mr. Sikes, meanwhile, flinging himself on the bed, and composing himself to sleep away the time until the young lady’s return.

text: What could Mr. Bumble do? He cast a downcast glance around him and slunk away; and when he reached the door, the suppressed tittering of the paupers burst into a shrill peal of uncontrollable delight. This alone was wanting. In their eyes he was degraded; he had lost all caste and station before the very paupers; he had fallen from the full height and dignity of his beadleship to the lowest depths of the most abused and henpecked domesticity.
source_text: What could Mr. Bumble do? He looked dejectedly round, and slunk away; and, as he reached the door, the titterings of the paupers broke into a shrill chuckle of irrepressible delight. It wanted but this. He was degraded in their eyes; he had lost caste and station before the very paupers; he had fallen from all the height and pomp of beadleship, to the lowest depth of the most snubbed hen-peckery.

text: In fact, as he flung himself back at one agile dive into his former seat, Mr. Brownlow returned, accompanied by Oliver, whom Mr. Grimwig welcomed with great cordiality; and had the delight of that instant been the sole reward for all her anxiety and devotion on Oliver’s behalf, Rose Maylie would have been richly recompensed.
source_text: In fact, as he threw himself at one dexterous dive into his former seat, Mr. Brownlow returned, accompanied by Oliver, whom Mr. Grimwig received very graciously; and if the gratification of that moment had been the only reward for all her anxiety and care in Oliver’s behalf, Rose Maylie would have been well repaid.

text: The Jew was clearly too well acquainted with the route he was taking to be at all confused, whether by the darkness of the night or by the twists and turns of the way. He moved quickly through several alleys and streets, and at last turned into one that was lit only by a single lamp at its far end. He knocked at the door of a house in this street; after exchanging a few muttered words with the person who opened it, he went upstairs.
source_text: The Jew was evidently too familiar with the ground he traversed to be at all bewildered, either by the darkness of the night, or the intricacies of the way. He hurried through several alleys and streets, and at length turned into one, lighted only by a single lamp at the farther end. At the door of a house in this street, he knocked; having exchanged a few muttered words with the person who opened it, he walked upstairs.

text: The wide sky looked as though it were ablaze. Sheets of fire rose into the air, throwing off showers of sparks and tumbling one over another, lighting the air for miles around and driving clouds of smoke toward the place where he stood. The shouts grew louder as more voices joined the uproar, and he could hear the cry of Fire! mixed with the pealing of an alarm bell, the crash of falling heavy objects, and the crackle of flames as they wound around some fresh obstruction and shot upward as if fed and revived. The clamor swelled as he watched. There were people there—men and women—movement, confusion, stir. It was like a new kind of life to him. He sprang forward at once, headlong and straight, crashing through brier and brush, vaulting gate and fence as wildly as his dog, which raced ahead of him with loud, ringing barks.
source_text: The broad sky seemed on fire. Rising into the air with showers of sparks, and rolling one above the other, were sheets of flame, lighting the atmosphere for miles round, and driving clouds of smoke in the direction where he stood. The shouts grew louder as new voices swelled the roar, and he could hear the cry of Fire! mingled with the ringing of an alarm-bell, the fall of heavy bodies, and the crackling of flames as they twined round some new obstacle, and shot aloft as though refreshed by food. The noise increased as he looked. There were people there—men and women—light, bustle. It was like new life to him. He darted onward—straight, headlong—dashing through brier and brake, and leaping gate and fence as madly as his dog, who careered with loud and sounding bark before him.

text: At six the next morning, Mr. Beadle, having laid aside his peaked hat for one of a rounder fashion, and wrapped himself in a blue greatcoat with a cape attached, mounted the outside of the coach, in company with the offenders whose parish settlement was in question; and with them, in the proper course of time, he reached London.
source_text: At six o’clock next morning, Mr. Bumble: having exchanged his cocked hat for a round one, and encased his person in a blue great-coat with a cape to it: took his place on the outside of the coach, accompanied by the criminals whose settlement was disputed; with whom, in due course of time, he arrived in London.

text: There was no fire in the room; yet a man was crouched there, bent by habit, over the empty stove. An old woman had also pulled a low stool up to the cold hearth and was sitting beside him. In another corner stood a few ragged children; and in a shallow recess opposite the door, something lay on the floor under an old blanket. Oliver shivered as his eyes fell on it, and without meaning to, he edged nearer to his master; for though it was covered, the boy knew it was a corpse.
source_text: There was no fire in the room; but a man was crouching, mechanically, over the empty stove. An old woman, too, had drawn a low stool to the cold hearth, and was sitting beside him. There were some ragged children in another corner; and in a small recess, opposite the door, there lay upon the ground, something covered with an old blanket. Oliver shuddered as he cast his eyes toward the place, and crept involuntarily closer to his master; for though it was covered up, the boy felt that it was a corpse.

text: Oliver, who could scarcely keep his feet, managed somehow to get upright, and was immediately dragged along the streets by the collar of his jacket at a brisk pace. The gentleman walked beside the officer with them; and as many of the crowd as could manage it went a little ahead, turning now and then to stare back at Oliver. The boys shouted in triumph, and so they went on.
source_text: Oliver, who could hardly stand, made a shift to raise himself on his feet, and was at once lugged along the streets by the jacket-collar, at a rapid pace. The gentleman walked on with them by the officer’s side; and as many of the crowd as could achieve the feat, got a little ahead, and stared back at Oliver from time to time. The boys shouted in triumph; and on they went.

text: “You can’t mean that’s the boy who had the fever, I trust?” said Mr. Grimwig, drawing back a little farther. “Hold on a moment! Don’t say a word! Stop—” he went on suddenly, all fear of the fever vanishing in the joy of his discovery; “that’s the boy who had the orange! If that isn’t the very boy, sir, who had the orange and dropped this bit of peel on the stairs, I’ll eat my head—and his as well.”
source_text: “You don’t mean to say that’s the boy who had the fever, I hope?” said Mr. Grimwig, recoiling a little more. “Wait a minute! Don’t speak! Stop—” continued Mr. Grimwig, abruptly, losing all dread of the fever in his triumph at the discovery; “that’s the boy who had the orange! If that’s not the boy, sir, who had the orange, and threw this bit of peel upon the staircase, I’ll eat my head, and his too.”

text: “Ah! I daresay he will,” the lady answered peevishly, “out of our food and our drink. I can see no saving in parish children, I cannot; for they always cost more to keep than they’re worth. But men always think they know best. There now! Off downstairs, little bag of bones.” With that, the undertaker’s wife opened a side door and thrust Oliver down a steep staircase into a stone cell, damp and dark, serving as the anteroom to the coal cellar, and called the “kitchen”; there sat a slovenly girl, with shoes worn down at the heels and blue worsted stockings sadly in need of repair.
source_text: “Ah! I dare say he will,” replied the lady pettishly, “on our victuals and our drink. I see no saving in parish children, not I; for they always cost more to keep, than they’re worth. However, men always think they know best. There! Get downstairs, little bag o’ bones.” With this, the undertaker’s wife opened a side door, and pushed Oliver down a steep flight of stairs into a stone cell, damp and dark: forming the ante-room to the coal-cellar, and denominated “kitchen”; wherein sat a slatternly girl, in shoes down at heel, and blue worsted stockings very much out of repair.

text: “I shan’t be gone ten minutes, sir,” said Oliver, eagerly. After buttoning the bank-note safely into the pocket of his coat and tucking the books carefully beneath his arm, he made a respectful bow and left the room. Mrs. Bedwin accompanied him to the street door, giving him plenty of instructions about the shortest route, the bookseller’s name, and the name of the street; all of which Oliver declared he understood perfectly. After adding many final cautions to be sure he did not catch cold, the old lady at last allowed him to go.
source_text: “I won’t be ten minutes, sir,” said Oliver, eagerly. Having buttoned up the bank-note in his jacket pocket, and placed the books carefully under his arm, he made a respectful bow, and left the room. Mrs. Bedwin followed him to the street-door, giving him many directions about the nearest way, and the name of the bookseller, and the name of the street: all of which Oliver said he clearly understood. Having superadded many injunctions to be sure and not take cold, the old lady at length permitted him to depart.

text: “The more I reflect on it,” said the doctor, “the more clearly I see that it will lead to endless trouble and difficulty if we place these men in possession of the boy’s true story. I am quite sure it will not be believed; and even if they can do him no harm in the end, still the mere bringing it forward, and making public all the doubts that will be raised about it, must seriously interfere with your charitable plan of rescuing him from misery.”
source_text: “The more I think of it,” said the doctor, “the more I see that it will occasion endless trouble and difficulty if we put these men in possession of the boy’s real story. I am certain it will not be believed; and even if they can do nothing to him in the end, still the dragging it forward, and giving publicity to all the doubts that will be cast upon it, must interfere, materially, with your benevolent plan of rescuing him from misery.”

text: Yet the full depth of his abasement had not been reached. Having made his way through the house, and being, for the first time, of opinion that the poor laws were indeed harsh in their dealings, and that husbands who deserted their wives, leaving them a burden upon the parish, ought in equity to be subjected to no penalty whatever, but rather to be honoured as deserving men who had endured much, Mr. Binns arrived at a chamber where certain of the female paupers were commonly set to washing the parish linen; and from within there now came the sound of voices in discourse.
source_text: But, the measure of his degradation was not yet full. After making a tour of the house, and thinking, for the first time, that the poor-laws really were too hard on people; and that men who ran away from their wives, leaving them chargeable to the parish, ought, in justice to be visited with no punishment at all, but rather rewarded as meritorious individuals who had suffered much; Mr. Bumble came to a room where some of the female paupers were usually employed in washing the parish linen: when the sound of voices in conversation, now proceeded.

source_text: This was addressed to a bluff old fellow, in a striped waistcoat, who was standing by the bar. He bent over Oliver, and repeated the inquiry; but finding him really incapable of understanding the question; and knowing that his not replying would only infuriate the magistrate the more, and add to the severity of his sentence; he hazarded a guess.

text: “It must be a piercing thing, if it can work its way through your heart,” said Mr. Sikes. “Give him something to drink, Nancy. Burn my body, be quick! It’s enough to make a man sick, seeing his thin old carcass trembling like that, like some ugly ghost just dragged up from the grave.”
source_text: “It must be a piercer, if it finds its way through your heart,” said Mr. Sikes. “Give him something to drink, Nancy. Burn my body, make haste! It’s enough to turn a man ill, to see his lean old carcase shivering in that way, like a ugly ghost just rose from the grave.”

text: “Assuredly,” answered the smaller of the two; “and whatever Mr. Bell says, it is not for us to gainsay him. No, no, I understand my position well enough! Heaven be praised, I understand my position.” In truth, the little fellow did appear to understand it, and to know very thoroughly that it was anything but an enviable one; for his teeth were actually rattling in his head as he spoke.
source_text: “Certainly,” replied the shorter man; “and whatever Mr. Giles says, it isn’t our place to contradict him. No, no, I know my sitiwation! Thank my stars, I know my sitiwation.” To tell the truth, the little man _did_ seem to know his situation, and to know perfectly well that it was by no means a desirable one; for his teeth chattered in his head as he spoke.

text: The same faithful friend, with Mr. Lascelles’s aid, had prudently closed every avenue by which tidings of the dreadful events so lately passed might reach them. “It is very true,” he observed, “that they must hear of them before long; but it may be wiser to let that hour come later than now, and it surely could not come at a more unfortunate moment.” Thus they went forward in silence, each occupied with thoughts of the errand that had united them, and none inclined to voice the reflections that pressed alike upon all.
source_text: The same kind friend had, with Mr. Losberne’s assistance, cautiously stopped all channels of communication through which they could receive intelligence of the dreadful occurrences that had so recently taken place. “It was quite true,” he said, “that they must know them before long, but it might be at a better time than the present, and it could not be at a worse.” So, they travelled on in silence: each busied with reflections on the object which had brought them together: and no one disposed to give utterance to the thoughts which crowded upon all.

text: “She charged me to keep it safe,” the woman replied with a groan, “and trusted me, since I was the only woman near her. I stole it in my heart the moment she first showed it to me hanging about her neck; and perhaps the child’s death lies on me as well! They would have treated him more kindly if they had known everything!”
source_text: “She charged me to keep it safe,” replied the woman with a groan, “and trusted me as the only woman about her. I stole it in my heart when she first showed it me hanging round her neck; and the child’s death, perhaps, is on me besides! They would have treated him better, if they had known it all!”

text: So Oliver remained quite still, partly from a wish to obey the kind old lady in everything, and partly, to say the truth, because he was utterly worn out by all he had already told. Before long he sank into a light doze, from which he was roused by the glow of a candle. Brought close to the bedside, it revealed a gentleman holding a very large gold watch that ticked loudly in his hand, who felt Oliver’s pulse and declared that he was much better.
source_text: So, Oliver kept very still; partly because he was anxious to obey the kind old lady in all things; and partly, to tell the truth, because he was completely exhausted with what he had already said. He soon fell into a gentle doze, from which he was awakened by the light of a candle: which, being brought near the bed, showed him a gentleman with a very large and loud-ticking gold watch in his hand, who felt his pulse, and said he was a great deal better.

text: Though the magistrates who preside in such a place wield a swift and arbitrary power over the liberty, reputation, character, and even the very lives of Her Majesty’s subjects, particularly those of the poorer sort; and though within those walls there are daily enough monstrous proceedings to make the angels weep themselves blind, the public is admitted only through the columns of the daily press. Mr. Fang was therefore not a little angered to see a stranger forcing an entrance in so disrespectful and disorderly a fashion.
source_text: Although the presiding Genii in such an office as this, exercise a summary and arbitrary power over the liberties, the good name, the character, almost the lives, of Her Majesty’s subjects, especially of the poorer class; and although, within such walls, enough fantastic tricks are daily played to make the angels blind with weeping; they are closed to the public, save through the medium of the daily press. Mr. Fang was consequently not a little indignant to see an unbidden guest enter in such irreverent disorder.

text: “My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking like a gentleman, “is Brownlow. Allow me to ask the name of the magistrate who is ready to offer an unprovoked and gratuitous insult to a respectable man, while sheltering himself behind the bench.” As he said this, Mr. Brownlow glanced about the office, as though looking for someone who could give him the information he wanted.
source_text: “My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking _like_ a gentleman, “my name, sir, is Brownlow. Permit me to inquire the name of the magistrate who offers a gratuitous and unprovoked insult to a respectable person, under the protection of the bench.” Saying this, Mr. Brownlow looked around the office as if in search of some person who would afford him the required information.

text: “I can’t say,” answered Vane, his teeth set hard and his face whitening at the bare thought. “I’d do something in the prison that would get me clapped in irons; and if I were put on trial with you, I’d turn on you with them in the court itself, and batter your brains out before all the people. I should have such power,” muttered the ruffian, lifting his sinewy arm, “that I could crush your skull as though a loaded wagon had rolled straight across it.”
source_text: “I don’t know,” replied Sikes, clenching his teeth and turning white at the mere suggestion. “I’d do something in the jail that ’ud get me put in irons; and if I was tried along with you, I’d fall upon you with them in the open court, and beat your brains out afore the people. I should have such strength,” muttered the robber, poising his brawny arm, “that I could smash your head as if a loaded waggon had gone over it.”

text: Such was the gallant pledge with which Mr. Blenkinsop was wont to garnish and lend authority to almost every statement he advanced; and in his case the offer had a singular air, because, even granting, for argument’s sake, the possibility that science might one day be carried so far as to allow a gentleman, should the whim seize him, to dine upon his own head, Mr. Blenkinsop’s own head was of so extraordinary a girth that the most hopeful of mortals could scarcely expect to make any sensible progress with it at a single meal—to say nothing of the liberal sprinkling of powder with which it was encumbered.
source_text: This was the handsome offer with which Mr. Grimwig backed and confirmed nearly every assertion he made; and it was the more singular in his case, because, even admitting for the sake of argument, the possibility of scientific improvements being brought to that pass which will enable a gentleman to eat his own head in the event of his being so disposed, Mr. Grimwig’s head was such a particularly large one, that the most sanguine man alive could hardly entertain a hope of being able to get through it at a sitting—to put entirely out of the question, a very thick coating of powder.

text: Soon after the young couple were married, the worthy doctor returned to Chertsey, where, deprived of the company of his old friends, he would have been discontented had his nature allowed him such a feeling, and would have grown downright peevish if he had known how. For two or three months he contented himself with dropping hints that he feared the air was beginning to disagree with him; then, discovering that the place was in truth no longer what it had once been to him, he handed over his practice to his assistant, took a bachelor’s cottage outside the village where his young friend was rector, and at once recovered his spirits. There he set himself to gardening, planting, fishing, carpentry, and a variety of other pursuits of a like sort, all entered upon with his usual impetuosity. In each of them he has since become known throughout the neighborhood as a most learned and authoritative expert.
source_text: Soon after the marriage of the young people, the worthy doctor returned to Chertsey, where, bereft of the presence of his old friends, he would have been discontented if his temperament had admitted of such a feeling; and would have turned quite peevish if he had known how. For two or three months, he contented himself with hinting that he feared the air began to disagree with him; then, finding that the place really no longer was, to him, what it had been, he settled his business on his assistant, took a bachelor’s cottage outside the village of which his young friend was pastor, and instantaneously recovered. Here he took to gardening, planting, fishing, carpentering, and various other pursuits of a similar kind: all undertaken with his characteristic impetuosity. In each and all he has since become famous throughout the neighborhood, as a most profound authority.

text: With a footstep as soft and gentle as her voice, the speaker glided away. She soon returned with the order that the wounded man was to be carried, with due care, upstairs to Mr. Giles’s room; and that Brittles was to saddle the pony and set off at once for Chertsey, where he was to send back, with all possible speed, a constable and a doctor.
source_text: With a footstep as soft and gentle as the voice, the speaker tripped away. She soon returned, with the direction that the wounded person was to be carried, carefully, upstairs to Mr. Giles’s room; and that Brittles was to saddle the pony and betake himself instantly to Chertsey: from which place, he was to despatch, with all speed, a constable and doctor.

text: Now, though it was quite natural that the guardians, above all men in the world, should be thrown into a fine condition of righteous amazement and horror at the least symptom of insensibility in anybody whatsoever, they were, in this instance, somewhat mistaken. The plain truth was that Elias, instead of having too little feeling, had rather too much; and was in fair danger of being driven, for life, into a state of savage stolidity and gloom by the harsh treatment he had endured. He received the tidings of his removal in perfect silence; and, his luggage having been placed in his hand—which was no great burden, since it consisted of nothing more than a brown paper parcel, about half a foot square by three inches deep—he drew his cap down over his eyes, and, once more fastening himself upon Mr. Bumble’s coat sleeve, was conducted by that worthy person to another scene of misery.
source_text: Now, although it was very natural that the board, of all people in the world, should feel in a great state of virtuous astonishment and horror at the smallest tokens of want of feeling on the part of anybody, they were rather out, in this particular instance. The simple fact was, that Oliver, instead of possessing too little feeling, possessed rather too much; and was in a fair way of being reduced, for life, to a state of brutal stupidity and sullenness by the ill usage he had received. He heard the news of his destination, in perfect silence; and, having had his luggage put into his hand—which was not very difficult to carry, inasmuch as it was all comprised within the limits of a brown paper parcel, about half a foot square by three inches deep—he pulled his cap over his eyes; and once more attaching himself to Mr. Bumble’s coat cuff, was led away by that dignitary to a new scene of suffering.

text: After holding the first bottle up to the light and giving it a good shake to test its quality, Mr. Bumble set both bottles on top of a chest of drawers, folded the handkerchief they had been wrapped in, carefully tucked it into his pocket, and picked up his hat as though he were about to leave.
source_text: Having held the first bottle up to the light, and shaken it well to test its excellence, Mr. Bumble placed them both on top of a chest of drawers; folded the handkerchief in which they had been wrapped; put it carefully in his pocket; and took up his hat, as if to go.

text: In short, after further inquiry and a good deal more talk, a neighboring magistrate was easily persuaded to accept the joint bail of Mrs. Maylie and Mr. Losberne for Oliver’s appearance, should he ever be required; and Blathers and Duff, after being rewarded with a couple of guineas, returned to town with divided opinions about the purpose of their journey: the latter gentleman, on mature reflection and after considering all the circumstances, being inclined to believe that the attempted burglary had begun with the Family Pet; while the former was just as ready to grant the whole credit for it to the great Mr. Conkey Chickweed.
source_text: In short, after some more examination, and a great deal more conversation, a neighbouring magistrate was readily induced to take the joint bail of Mrs. Maylie and Mr. Losberne for Oliver’s appearance if he should ever be called upon; and Blathers and Duff, being rewarded with a couple of guineas, returned to town with divided opinions on the subject of their expedition: the latter gentleman on a mature consideration of all the circumstances, inclining to the belief that the burglarious attempt had originated with the Family Pet; and the former being equally disposed to concede the full merit of it to the great Mr. Conkey Chickweed.

text: At this point in Mr. Bumble’s speech, Oliver, catching only enough to understand that some reference was being made to his mother, began kicking again, with such fury that all other sounds were drowned out. At that moment Sowerberry came back. Once Oliver’s misconduct had been explained to him, with all the embellishments the ladies considered most likely to stir his anger, he unlocked the cellar door in an instant, and hauled his mutinous apprentice out by the collar.
source_text: At this point of Mr. Bumble’s discourse, Oliver, just hearing enough to know that some allusion was being made to his mother, recommenced kicking, with a violence that rendered every other sound inaudible. Sowerberry returned at this juncture. Oliver’s offence having been explained to him, with such exaggerations as the ladies thought best calculated to rouse his ire, he unlocked the cellar-door in a twinkling, and dragged his rebellious apprentice out, by the collar.

text: “I trust I may find some fair reason for it,” returned Dr. Henshaw; “though, to speak truth, I much doubt I shall. Only yesterday morning you had resolved, in great haste, to remain here, and to escort your mother, like a proper son, down to the coast. Before the clock had struck noon, you were pleased to inform me that you would do me the honour of accompanying me as far as I might travel, upon your way to London. And now, at night, you beg me, with a deal of secrecy, to set out before the ladies are awake; the end of it being that young Felix here is kept captive at his breakfast, when he ought rather to be roaming the meadows in search of botanical marvels of every description. A sad business, is it not, Felix?”
source_text: “I hope I may have good cause to do so,” replied Mr. Losberne; “though I confess I don’t think I shall. But yesterday morning you had made up your mind, in a great hurry, to stay here, and to accompany your mother, like a dutiful son, to the sea-side. Before noon, you announce that you are going to do me the honour of accompanying me as far as I go, on your road to London. And at night, you urge me, with great mystery, to start before the ladies are stirring; the consequence of which is, that young Oliver here is pinned down to his breakfast when he ought to be ranging the meadows after botanical phenomena of all kinds. Too bad, isn’t it, Oliver?”

text: “I dare say a married gentleman,” replied Mr. Wattle, lifting his hand to screen his eyes, and regarding the stranger from head to toe with obvious bewilderment, “is no more disinclined than a bachelor to turn an honest shilling when occasion offers. Parish officers are not so handsomely remunerated that they can afford to reject a small gratuity, provided it is tendered in a civil and becoming fashion.”
source_text: “I suppose, a married man,” replied Mr. Bumble, shading his eyes with his hand, and surveying the stranger, from head to foot, in evident perplexity, “is not more averse to turning an honest penny when he can, than a single one. Porochial officers are not so well paid that they can afford to refuse any little extra fee, when it comes to them in a civil and proper manner.”

text: When the month of probation had ended, Oliver was formally bound apprentice. It happened, conveniently enough for the trade, to be a particularly unhealthy season. In commercial language, coffins were in request; and within a few weeks Oliver gained a vast amount of experience. Mr. Sowerberry’s clever venture succeeded beyond even his rosiest expectations. The oldest people in the place could recall no time when measles had spread so widely, or proved so deadly to infant life; and many were the sad little processions over which Oliver presided, wearing a hatband that hung to his knees, to the unspeakable admiration and deep feeling of all the mothers in the town. As Oliver went with his master on most of his visits among grown-up mourners as well, that he might learn the composure of manner and perfect steadiness of nerve required in a finished undertaker, he found many chances to observe the admirable resignation and courage with which some strong-minded persons endure their afflictions and losses.
source_text: The month’s trial over, Oliver was formally apprenticed. It was a nice sickly season just at this time. In commercial phrase, coffins were looking up; and, in the course of a few weeks, Oliver acquired a great deal of experience. The success of Mr. Sowerberry’s ingenious speculation, exceeded even his most sanguine hopes. The oldest inhabitants recollected no period at which measles had been so prevalent, or so fatal to infant existence; and many were the mournful processions which little Oliver headed, in a hat-band reaching down to his knees, to the indescribable admiration and emotion of all the mothers in the town. As Oliver accompanied his master in most of his adult expeditions too, in order that he might acquire that equanimity of demeanour and full command of nerve which was essential to a finished undertaker, he had many opportunities of observing the beautiful resignation and fortitude with which some strong-minded people bear their trials and losses.

text: He crouched upon his stony pallet and fell to brooding over what had gone before. On the day they seized him, stones and other missiles from the mob had found him, and his head was swathed in a linen bandage. His auburn hair drooped over a face drained of blood; his beard was ragged and knotted; his eyes burned with a dreadful brightness; his unwashed skin crackled under the fever that consumed him. Eight—nine—then. If it were no mere device to scare him, and those were the true hours stepping one upon another’s heels, where should he be when they came back again? Eleven! Another had sounded before the echo of the last had died away. At eight, he would walk alone in the mourners’ line behind his own coffin; at eleven—
source_text: He cowered down upon his stone bed, and thought of the past. He had been wounded with some missiles from the crowd on the day of his capture, and his head was bandaged with a linen cloth. His red hair hung down upon his bloodless face; his beard was torn, and twisted into knots; his eyes shone with a terrible light; his unwashed flesh crackled with the fever that burnt him up. Eight—nine—then. If it was not a trick to frighten him, and those were the real hours treading on each other’s heels, where would he be, when they came round again! Eleven! Another struck, before the voice of the previous hour had ceased to vibrate. At eight, he would be the only mourner in his own funeral train; at eleven—

text: There was, after all, not quite the need for haste that Mr. Sowerberry had expected; for when they reached that lonely corner of the churchyard where the nettles grew and the parish graves were dug, the clergyman had not yet come. The clerk, who was warming himself by the fire in the vestry-room, seemed to think it quite likely that he might be delayed for another hour or so. Accordingly, they set the bier down at the edge of the grave, and the two mourners waited quietly in the wet clay while a thin cold rain fell steadily. Meanwhile, the ragged boys drawn into the churchyard by the sight of it made a great noise at hide-and-seek among the tombstones, or changed their sport by leaping back and forth over the coffin. Mr. Sowerberry and Bumble, being on friendly terms with the clerk, sat with him by the fire and read the newspaper.
source_text: There was not so great a necessity for hurrying as Mr. Sowerberry had anticipated, however; for when they reached the obscure corner of the churchyard in which the nettles grew, and where the parish graves were made, the clergyman had not arrived; and the clerk, who was sitting by the vestry-room fire, seemed to think it by no means improbable that it might be an hour or so, before he came. So, they put the bier on the brink of the grave; and the two mourners waited patiently in the damp clay, with a cold rain drizzling down, while the ragged boys whom the spectacle had attracted into the churchyard played a noisy game at hide-and-seek among the tombstones, or varied their amusements by jumping backwards and forwards over the coffin. Mr. Sowerberry and Bumble, being personal friends of the clerk, sat by the fire with him, and read the paper.

text: Oliver was surprised to observe that, as Mrs. Maylie uttered these words, she seemed by one exertion to check her lamentations; and, drawing herself up as she spoke, became calm and resolute. He was even more astonished to find that this composure endured; and that, amid all the care and watching which followed, Mrs. Maylie was always prepared and collected, fulfilling every duty that had fallen to her with steadiness, and, to all outward seeming, even cheerfulness. But he was young, and knew nothing of what strong minds can bear in seasons of trial. How should he, when those who possess them so seldom know themselves?
source_text: Oliver was surprised to see that as Mrs. Maylie said these words, she checked her lamentations as though by one effort; and drawing herself up as she spoke, became composed and firm. He was still more astonished to find that this firmness lasted; and that, under all the care and watching which ensued, Mrs. Maylie was ever ready and collected: performing all the duties which had devolved upon her, steadily, and, to all external appearances, even cheerfully. But he was young, and did not know what strong minds are capable of, under trying circumstances. How should he, when their possessors so seldom know themselves?

text: He was haggard and spent; yet over his countenance lay the same easy, contented calm it had always worn, and through the grime, the beard, and the bristles there still glimmered, undiminished, the self-satisfied leer of dapper Ned Sly. Meanwhile the Jew, in a fever of impatience, followed every mouthful he swallowed, striding to and fro across the room in a tumult of restlessness. It was all useless. Ned went on eating with the most complete appearance of indifference, until at last he could swallow no more; then, sending the Artful away, he shut the door, compounded himself a glass of spirits and water, and settled down to speak.
source_text: He looked tired and worn, but there was the same complacent repose upon his features that they always wore: and through dirt, and beard, and whisker, there still shone, unimpaired, the self-satisfied smirk of flash Toby Crackit. Then the Jew, in an agony of impatience, watched every morsel he put into his mouth; pacing up and down the room, meanwhile, in irrepressible excitement. It was all of no use. Toby continued to eat with the utmost outward indifference, until he could eat no more; then, ordering the Dodger out, he closed the door, mixed a glass of spirits and water, and composed himself for talking.

text: “Very good,” replied Mr. Brownlow, smiling, “but no doubt they will manage that for themselves in time, and if we interfere to get ahead of them, it seems to me we should be acting very much like Don Quixote, and against our own interests—or at least against Oliver’s, which amounts to the same thing.”
source_text: “Very good,” replied Mr. Brownlow, smiling; “but no doubt they will bring that about for themselves in the fulness of time, and if we step in to forestall them, it seems to me that we shall be performing a very Quixotic act, in direct opposition to our own interest—or at least to Oliver’s, which is the same thing.”

text: If Noah had still been dressed in his charity-boy’s clothes, there might have been some reason for the Jew to open his eyes so widely; but as he had laid aside the coat and badge, and wore instead a short smock-frock over his leathers, there appeared to be no special cause why his presence should attract so much notice in a public-house.
source_text: If Noah had been attired in his charity-boy’s dress, there might have been some reason for the Jew opening his eyes so wide; but as he had discarded the coat and badge, and wore a short smock-frock over his leathers, there seemed no particular reason for his appearance exciting so much attention in a public-house.

text: Mr. Chitling was older than the Dodger, perhaps by several years; he may have been about eighteen. Yet there was something in the way he carried himself toward that young gentleman that suggested he was aware of a slight inferiority in matters of wit and professional skill. He had small, restless eyes and a face pitted with marks; he wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His clothes, in truth, were in a poor state; but he made his excuse to the company by saying that his “time” had only run out an hour earlier, and that, after wearing the regimentals for the past six weeks, he had not had a chance to give any attention to his own wardrobe. Mr. Chitling went on, with evident annoyance, to declare that the new method of fumigating clothes up there was infernally unconstitutional, because it burned holes in them, and there was no redress against the county. He said the same complaint applied to the approved way of cutting the hair, which he considered flatly unlawful. He ended by saying that he had not taken a drop of anything for forty-two long, hard, moral days of labor, and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”
source_text: Mr. Chitling was older in years than the Dodger: having perhaps numbered eighteen winters; but there was a degree of deference in his deportment towards that young gentleman which seemed to indicate that he felt himself conscious of a slight inferiority in point of genius and professional aquirements.
