He had small twinkling eyes, and a pock-marked face; wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His wardrobe was, in truth, rather out of repair; but he excused himself to the company by stating that his “time” was only out an hour before; and that, in consequence of having worn the regimentals for six weeks past, he had not been able to bestow any attention on his private clothes. Mr. Chitling added, with strong marks of irritation, that the new way of fumigating clothes up yonder was infernal unconstitutional, for it burnt holes in them, and there was no remedy against the county. The same remark he considered to apply to the regulation mode of cutting the hair: which he held to be decidedly unlawful. Mr. Chitling wound up his observations by stating that he had not touched a drop of anything for forty-two moral long hard-working days; and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

text: “I can’t help it,” cried Tom, “I really can’t help it! To watch him tearing off at that rate, and whipping round the turns, and banging up against the posts, and then off again as though he were made of iron like they were, and I with the strap in my pocket, shouting after him—oh, it’s too much!” The lively fancy of young Stokes drew the picture before him in colours too sharp to bear. When he came to this outburst, he once more tumbled against the doorstep and laughed more uproariously than before.
source_text: “I can’t help it,” said Charley, “I can’t help it! To see him splitting away at that pace, and cutting round the corners, and knocking up again’ the posts, and starting on again as if he was made of iron as well as them, and me with the wipe in my pocket, singing out arter him—oh, my eye!” The vivid imagination of Master Bates presented the scene before him in too strong colours. As he arrived at this apostrophe, he again rolled upon the door-step, and laughed louder than before.

text: In answer to this confused questioning, the old man told the deeply distressed sister that Oliver had fallen ill in the office and had therefore been released, after a witness had shown that the robbery was in fact committed by another boy, who was not in custody; and that the prosecutor had taken him away, unconscious, to his own home. Of that home, the only thing the informant knew was that it was somewhere in Pentonville, having heard that name given in the directions to the coachman.
source_text: In reply to this incoherent questioning, the old man informed the deeply affected sister that Oliver had been taken ill in the office, and discharged in consequence of a witness having proved the robbery to have been committed by another boy, not in custody; and that the prosecutor had carried him away, in an insensible condition, to his own residence: of and concerning which, all the informant knew was, that it was somewhere in Pentonville, he having heard that word mentioned in the directions to the coachman.

text: Oliver looked for a moment at the madman’s struggles, for such he supposed him to be, and then ran into the house for help. After seeing him safely carried into the hotel, he set off homeward again, running as quickly as he could to recover the lost time, and thinking back with much astonishment and some dread on the extraordinary behavior of the man from whom he had just parted.
source_text: Oliver gazed, for a moment, at the struggles of the madman (for such he supposed him to be); and then darted into the house for help. Having seen him safely carried into the hotel, he turned his face homewards, running as fast as he could, to make up for lost time: and recalling with a great deal of astonishment and some fear, the extraordinary behaviour of the person from whom he had just parted.

text: In the dim back room of a shabby public house in the dirtiest part of Little Saffron Hill—a dark, dismal hole where a harsh gas lamp burned all day in winter and no sunbeam ever reached in summer—there sat, bent over a small pewter measure and a tiny glass, both strongly tainted with the smell of liquor, a man in a velveteen coat, drab breeches, half-boots, and stockings, whom even in that weak light no practiced police officer would have hesitated to identify as Mr. William Sikes. At his feet lay a white-coated, red-eyed dog, who spent his time now blinking with both eyes at once at his master, and now licking a deep fresh cut at one corner of his mouth, the mark of some recent struggle.
source_text: In the obscure parlour of a low public-house, in the filthiest part of Little Saffron Hill; a dark and gloomy den, where a flaring gas-light burnt all day in the winter-time; and where no ray of sun ever shone in the summer: there sat, brooding over a little pewter measure and a small glass, strongly impregnated with the smell of liquor, a man in a velveteen coat, drab shorts, half-boots and stockings, whom even by that dim light no experienced agent of the police would have hesitated to recognise as Mr. William Sikes. At his feet, sat a white-coated, red-eyed dog; who occupied himself, alternately, in winking at his master with both eyes at the same time; and in licking a large, fresh cut on one side of his mouth, which appeared to be the result of some recent conflict.

text: The condemned man sat on his bed, swaying from side to side, his face closer to that of a trapped animal than to a human being. His thoughts were plainly drifting back to his former life, for he kept muttering to himself, seeming aware of the others only as figures within his own troubled vision.
source_text: The condemned criminal was seated on his bed, rocking himself from side to side, with a countenance more like that of a snared beast than the face of a man. His mind was evidently wandering to his old life, for he continued to mutter, without appearing conscious of their presence otherwise than as a part of his vision.

text: It was fortunate for her that the possession of money kept him so busily engaged next day with eating and drinking, and had besides so wholesome an effect in smoothing the rough edges of his temper, that he had neither leisure nor inclination to be very sharp in observing her conduct and manner. To Fagin’s keen and watchful eye, her abstracted, uneasy bearing would have plainly betrayed one who stood upon the brink of some bold and hazardous undertaking, resolved on only after no ordinary struggle; and he would most likely have taken alarm at once. But Mr. Sikes, wanting all fineness of discrimination, and troubled by no subtler doubts than those that showed themselves in a sullen coarseness toward everybody, and being, moreover, in a singularly good humour, as has already been noted, perceived nothing strange in her demeanour; and indeed concerned himself so little about her, that even had her agitation been much more visible than it was, it would have been very unlikely to stir his suspicions.
source_text: It was fortunate for her that the possession of money occasioned him so much employment next day in the way of eating and drinking; and withal had so beneficial an effect in smoothing down the asperities of his temper; that he had neither time nor inclination to be very critical upon her behaviour and deportment. That she had all the abstracted and nervous manner of one who is on the eve of some bold and hazardous step, which it has required no common struggle to resolve upon, would have been obvious to the lynx-eyed Fagin, who would most probably have taken the alarm at once; but Mr. Sikes lacking the niceties of discrimination, and being troubled with no more subtle misgivings than those which resolve themselves into a dogged roughness of behaviour towards everybody; and being, furthermore, in an unusually amiable condition, as has been already observed; saw nothing unusual in her demeanor, and indeed, troubled himself so little about her, that, had her agitation been far more perceptible than it was, it would have been very unlikely to have awakened his suspicions.

text: Fagin walked toward his home, absorbed in the thoughts that stirred within his mind. He had formed the notion—not from what had just occurred, though that had served to strengthen it, but little by little—that Nancy, wearied of the housebreaker’s violence, had taken a fancy to some new acquaintance. Her changed demeanour, her repeated going out alone, her relative indifference to the concerns of the gang for which she had once shown such zeal, and, added to all this, her eager impatience to leave the house that night at a particular hour, all supported the supposition, and made it, in his judgment at least, almost a certainty. The object of this new attachment was not among his followers. With such an ally as Nancy, he would be a highly valuable addition, and must, Fagin reasoned, be secured without delay.
source_text: Fagin walked towards his home, intent upon the thoughts that were working within his brain. He had conceived the idea—not from what had just passed though that had tended to confirm him, but slowly and by degrees—that Nancy, wearied of the housebreaker’s brutality, had conceived an attachment for some new friend. Her altered manner, her repeated absences from home alone, her comparative indifference to the interests of the gang for which she had once been so zealous, and, added to these, her desperate impatience to leave home that night at a particular hour, all favoured the supposition, and rendered it, to him at least, almost matter of certainty. The object of this new liking was not among his myrmidons. He would be a valuable acquisition with such an assistant as Nancy, and must (thus Fagin argued) be secured without delay.

text: Indeed, they were. Shame at the collapse of his cunning design; loathing for the girl who had ventured to trifle with strangers; and a total disbelief in the truth of her refusal to surrender him; sharp disappointment at being robbed of his vengeance on Slyke; the terror of discovery, of ruin, and of death; and a fierce, consuming fury kindled by them all—these were the turbulent impulses that, one after another in swift and unbroken succession, rushed through the mind of Rendel, while every evil notion and darkest intent worked deep within his breast.
source_text: Indeed they were. Mortification at the overthrow of his notable scheme; hatred of the girl who had dared to palter with strangers; and utter distrust of the sincerity of her refusal to yield him up; bitter disappointment at the loss of his revenge on Sikes; the fear of detection, and ruin, and death; and a fierce and deadly rage kindled by all; these were the passionate considerations which, following close upon each other with rapid and ceaseless whirl, shot through the brain of Fagin, as every evil thought and blackest purpose lay working at his heart.

text: They went on for some time through the most crowded and thickly settled part of the town; and then, turning into a narrow street, fouler and more wretched than any they had yet seen, they halted to look for the house that was the aim of their search. The buildings on either side were tall and roomy, but very old, and occupied by the poorest class of people: their neglected aspect would have shown that plainly enough, even without the further evidence of the sallow, miserable-looking men and women who, with arms folded and bodies bent nearly double, now and then slunk past. Many of the houses had shop fronts; but these were shut up tight and crumbling to decay, with only the upper rooms still lived in. Some, shaken and made unsafe by age and ruin, were kept from toppling into the street by great timber props set against the walls and planted firmly in the roadway; yet even these broken-down dens seemed to have been taken over by homeless wretches for the night, for many of the rough boards that served as door and window had been torn aside to make an opening broad enough for a person to crawl through. The gutter was foul and motionless. Even the rats, seen here and there rotting in its filth, looked ghastly with hunger.
source_text: They walked on, for some time, through the most crowded and densely inhabited part of the town; and then, striking down a narrow street more dirty and miserable than any they had yet passed through, paused to look for the house which was the object of their search. The houses on either side were high and large, but very old, and tenanted by people of the poorest class: as their neglected appearance would have sufficiently denoted, without the concurrent testimony afforded by the squalid looks of the few men and women who, with folded arms and bodies half doubled, occasionally skulked along. A great many of the tenements had shop-fronts; but these were fast closed, and mouldering away; only the upper rooms being inhabited. Some houses which had become insecure from age and decay, were prevented from falling into the street, by huge beams of wood reared against the walls, and firmly planted in the road; but even these crazy dens seemed to have been selected as the nightly haunts of some houseless wretches, for many of the rough boards which supplied the place of door and window, were wrenched from their positions, to afford an aperture wide enough for the passage of a human body. The kennel was stagnant and filthy. The very rats, which here and there lay putrefying in its rottenness, were hideous with famine.

text: There was such peace and beauty in the scene; such brightness and cheer in the sunlit landscape; such lively music in the summer birds’ songs; such freedom in the swift flight of the rook, wheeling high overhead; such life and gladness in everything, that when the boy lifted his aching eyes and looked around him, the thought came to him at once that this was no time for death. Rose surely could not die while even the humblest things were so bright and happy; graves belonged to the cold, cheerless winter, not to sunlight and sweetness. He almost believed that shrouds were for the old and withered, and that they never enclosed the young, graceful form in their dreadful folds.
source_text: There was such peace and beauty in the scene; so much of brightness and mirth in the sunny landscape; such blithesome music in the songs of the summer birds; such freedom in the rapid flight of the rook, careering overhead; so much of life and joyousness in all; that, when the boy raised his aching eyes, and looked about, the thought instinctively occurred to him, that this was not a time for death; that Rose could surely never die when humbler things were all so glad and gay; that graves were for cold and cheerless winter: not for sunlight and fragrance. He almost thought that shrouds were for the old and shrunken; and that they never wrapped the young and graceful form in their ghastly folds.

text: “It was about half past two,” said Mr. Giles, “though I couldn’t swear it wasn’t a little nearer three, when I woke up and, turning over in bed, as you might say”—here Mr. Giles turned round in his chair and drew the corner of the tablecloth over him to imitate bedclothes—“I thought I heard a noise.”
source_text: “It was about half-past two,” said Mr. Giles, “or I wouldn’t swear that it mightn’t have been a little nearer three, when I woke up, and, turning round in my bed, as it might be so, (here Mr. Giles turned round in his chair, and pulled the corner of the table-cloth over him to imitate bed-clothes,) I fancied I heerd a noise.”

text: “Good!” returned Mr. Brownlow. “And since I can plainly see on the faces around me a desire to ask how it came about that I was not there to confirm Oliver’s story, and why I had so abruptly left the country, let me beg that no questions be put to me until I think fit to anticipate them by telling my own history. Believe me, I ask this for a sound reason; otherwise I might raise hopes that are never meant to be fulfilled, and only add to difficulties and disappointments already more than sufficient. Come now! Supper has been announced, and young Oliver, who is by himself in the next room, must by this time have begun to think we are tired of his company, and have plotted some dark scheme to turn him out upon the world.”
source_text: “Good!” rejoined Mr. Brownlow. “And as I see on the faces about me, a disposition to inquire how it happened that I was not in the way to corroborate Oliver’s tale, and had so suddenly left the kingdom, let me stipulate that I shall be asked no questions until such time as I may deem it expedient to forestall them by telling my own story. Believe me, I make this request with good reason, for I might otherwise excite hopes destined never to be realised, and only increase difficulties and disappointments already quite numerous enough. Come! Supper has been announced, and young Oliver, who is all alone in the next room, will have begun to think, by this time, that we have wearied of his company, and entered into some dark conspiracy to thrust him forth upon the world.”

text: There was neither knocker nor bell-pull at the standing open door before which Mr. Toller and his apprentice paused; and so, feeling his way warily along the dim passage, and telling Ned to keep close behind him and take no fright, the undertaker ascended to the head of the first staircase. There, blundering against a door upon the landing, he tapped upon it with his knuckles.
source_text: There was neither knocker nor bell-handle at the open door where Oliver and his master stopped; so, groping his way cautiously through the dark passage, and bidding Oliver keep close to him and not be afraid, the undertaker mounted to the top of the first flight of stairs. Stumbling against a door on the landing, he rapped at it with his knuckles.

text: In an upper room of one of these houses—a separate dwelling of moderate size, ruined in every other respect, but firmly secured at both door and window, and with a rear that overlooked the ditch in the manner already described—three men were gathered. They sat for some time in deep and sullen silence, casting now and then at one another glances full of perplexity and expectation. One was Toby Crackit; another, Mr. Chitling; the third, a robber of about fifty, whose nose had been nearly smashed in some old fray, and whose countenance was marked by a terrible scar, probably gained in the same encounter. This man had been transported and had returned; his name was Kags.
source_text: In an upper room of one of these houses—a detached house of fair size, ruinous in other respects, but strongly defended at door and window: of which house the back commanded the ditch in manner already described—there were assembled three men, who, regarding each other every now and then with looks expressive of perplexity and expectation, sat for some time in profound and gloomy silence. One of these was Toby Crackit, another Mr. Chitling, and the third a robber of fifty years, whose nose had been almost beaten in, in some old scuffle, and whose face bore a frightful scar which might probably be traced to the same occasion. This man was a returned transport, and his name was Kags.

text: For a long while Oliver remained awake, thinking it not unlikely that Nancy might take the chance to whisper some further advice to him; but the girl sat brooding by the fire, still and silent, stirring only now and then to mend the light. At last, worn out by watching and anxiety, he fell asleep.
source_text: For a long time Oliver lay awake, thinking it not impossible that Nancy might seek that opportunity of whispering some further advice; but the girl sat brooding over the fire, without moving, save now and then to trim the light. Weary with watching and anxiety, he at length fell asleep.

text: They were both wrapped in old, shabby outer clothes, which may have served the double purpose of shielding their bodies from the rain and keeping them out of sight. The husband carried a lantern, though no light had yet been lit in it, and walked a few paces ahead, as if, the road being muddy, to let his wife place her feet in his heavy tracks. They moved along in complete silence; from time to time Mr. Bumble slackened his pace and looked back, as though to make sure his wife was still behind him. Then, finding her close at his heels, he quickened his step and went on, at a much greater speed, toward their destination.
source_text: They were both wrapped in old and shabby outer garments, which might, perhaps, serve the double purpose of protecting their persons from the rain, and sheltering them from observation. The husband carried a lantern, from which, however, no light yet shone; and trudged on, a few paces in front, as though—the way being dirty—to give his wife the benefit of treading in his heavy footprints. They went on, in profound silence; every now and then, Mr. Bumble relaxed his pace, and turned his head as if to make sure that his helpmate was following; then, discovering that she was close at his heels, he mended his rate of walking, and proceeded, at a considerable increase of speed, towards their place of destination.

text: It was a family hotel on a quiet yet elegant street near Hyde Park. The bright lamp burning above the entrance led her to it just as the clock struck eleven. She had lingered for a few steps, as if uncertain whether to go on, but the sound settled her mind, and she entered the hall. The porter’s chair was empty. She glanced about with a doubtful air, then moved toward the stairs.
source_text: It was a family hotel in a quiet but handsome street near Hyde Park. As the brilliant light of the lamp which burnt before its door, guided her to the spot, the clock struck eleven. She had loitered for a few paces as though irresolute, and making up her mind to advance; but the sound determined her, and she stepped into the hall. The porter’s seat was vacant. She looked round with an air of incertitude, and advanced towards the stairs.

text: The hour had not yet reached two minutes past, when a young lady, accompanied by a grey-haired gentleman, got down from a hackney carriage a short distance from the bridge, and, having sent the vehicle away, walked directly toward it. They had scarcely stepped onto the pavement when the girl started and at once moved toward them.
source_text: The hour had not struck two minutes, when a young lady, accompanied by a grey-haired gentleman, alighted from a hackney-carriage within a short distance of the bridge, and, having dismissed the vehicle, walked straight towards it. They had scarcely set foot upon its pavement, when the girl started, and immediately made towards them.

text: The man’s face was lean and very pale, his hair and beard grizzled, his eyes inflamed with bloodshot veins. The old woman’s face was wrinkled, her two remaining teeth jutting over her lower lip, and her eyes were bright and sharp. Oliver was afraid to look at either her or the man. They seemed so much like the rats he had seen outside.
source_text: The man’s face was thin and very pale; his hair and beard were grizzly; his eyes were bloodshot. The old woman’s face was wrinkled; her two remaining teeth protruded over her under lip; and her eyes were bright and piercing. Oliver was afraid to look at either her or the man. They seemed so like the rats he had seen outside.

text: But if Oliver, under these influences, had kept silent as they traveled toward the place of his birth along a road he had never seen before, how completely his memories rushed back to earlier days, and what a flood of feelings was stirred in his breast, when they turned into the road he had once walked on foot: a poor, homeless, wandering boy, with no friend to aid him and no roof to cover his head.
source_text: But if Oliver, under these influences, had remained silent while they journeyed towards his birth-place by a road he had never seen, how the whole current of his recollections ran back to old times, and what a crowd of emotions were wakened up in his breast, when they turned into that which he had traversed on foot: a poor houseless, wandering boy, without a friend to help him, or a roof to shelter his head.

text: The truth was that Mr. Giles, at first, could not bring himself to confess that he had shot only a boy. So many praises had been showered upon his bravery that he could not, for the life of him, resist delaying the explanation for a few delicious minutes, during which he basked at the very height of a short-lived fame for unflinching courage.
source_text: The fact was, that Mr. Giles had not, at first, been able to bring his mind to the avowal, that he had only shot a boy. Such commendations had been bestowed upon his bravery, that he could not, for the life of him, help postponing the explanation for a few delicious minutes; during which he had flourished, in the very zenith of a brief reputation for undaunted courage.

text: At this moment, the Artful one, affecting an exactness in readiness for what might follow, requested that the turnkey would be pleased to acquaint him with “the names of them two chaps as was sittin’ on the bench.” This so delighted the company, that they laughed nearly as freely as young Tom might have done had the question reached his ears.
source_text: At this point, the Dodger, with a show of being very particular with a view to proceedings to be had thereafter, desired the jailer to communicate “the names of them two files as was on the bench.” Which so tickled the spectators, that they laughed almost as heartily as Master Bates could have done if he had heard the request.

text: With these precautions duly observed, Mr. Giles kept a firm hold of the tinker’s arm—so, as he humorously observed, he might prevent him from taking to his heels—and gave the order for the door to be opened. Brittles obeyed; and the party, peering fearfully over one another’s shoulders, saw nothing more alarming than poor little Oliver Twist, speechless and spent, who lifted his heavy eyes and silently appealed to their pity.
source_text: These precautions having been taken, Mr. Giles held on fast by the tinker’s arm (to prevent his running away, as he pleasantly said), and gave the word of command to open the door. Brittles obeyed; the group, peeping timorously over each other’s shoulders, beheld no more formidable object than poor little Oliver Twist, speechless and exhausted, who raised his heavy eyes, and mutely solicited their compassion.

text: Moving in front of them, he looked into the room. Signaling them to follow, he shut the door once they had entered, and softly drew back the bed curtains. There, instead of the stubborn, dark-faced scoundrel they had expected to see, lay only a child: wasted by pain and exhaustion, and sunk in profound sleep. His injured arm, bandaged and splinted, rested across his breast; his head lay upon the other arm, half concealed by his long hair, which spilled across the pillow.
source_text: Stepping before them, he looked into the room. Motioning them to advance, he closed the door when they had entered; and gently drew back the curtains of the bed. Upon it, in lieu of the dogged, black-visaged ruffian they had expected to behold, there lay a mere child: worn with pain and exhaustion, and sunk into a deep sleep. His wounded arm, bound and splintered up, was crossed upon his breast; his head reclined upon the other arm, which was half hidden by his long hair, as it streamed over the pillow.

text: These candid admissions softened Mr. Giles, who at once confessed that he was afraid too; whereupon the three of them turned round together and ran back again in the fullest accord, until Mr. Giles, who was the least winded of the company and burdened besides with a pitchfork, most considerately insisted on stopping to apologize for the rashness of his words.
source_text: These frank admissions softened Mr. Giles, who at once owned that _he_ was afraid; upon which, they all three faced about, and ran back again with the completest unanimity, until Mr. Giles (who had the shortest wind of the party, as was encumbered with a pitchfork) most handsomely insisted on stopping, to make an apology for his hastiness of speech.

text: “Always the same!” the Jew muttered under his breath as he made his way home. “The worst of women is that the smallest trifle will rouse some feeling long since dead; and the best of them is that it never stays. Ha! ha! The man against the child, for a bag of gold!”
source_text: “Always the way!” muttered the Jew to himself as he turned homeward. “The worst of these women is, that a very little thing serves to call up some long-forgotten feeling; and, the best of them is, that it never lasts. Ha! ha! The man against the child, for a bag of gold!”

text: I would still wish to linger awhile with some of those among whom I have so long walked, and to share their happiness by striving to set it down. I would show Rose Maylie in all the bloom and grace of early womanhood, casting over the quiet path of her life a tender and gentle light, which rested on all who went beside her and entered into their hearts. I would depict her as the life and delight of the fireside circle and the cheerful summer gathering; I would follow her through the hot fields at noon, and hear the low music of her sweet voice in the moonlit walk; I would watch her abroad in all her goodness and charity, and at home in the unwearied and smiling performance of domestic duties; I would show her and the child of her dead sister happy in the love they bore one another, spending long hours together in imagining the friends they had so mournfully lost; I would call up once more those glad little faces that clustered about her knee, and listen to their merry chatter; I would remember the sound of that clear laugh, and bring back the tear of sympathy that sparkled in the soft blue eye. These, and a thousand glances and smiles, and turns of thought and speech—I would gladly recall them all.
source_text: I would fain linger yet with a few of those among whom I have so long moved, and share their happiness by endeavouring to depict it. I would show Rose Maylie in all the bloom and grace of early womanhood, shedding on her secluded path in life soft and gentle light, that fell on all who trod it with her, and shone into their hearts. I would paint her the life and joy of the fire-side circle and the lively summer group; I would follow her through the sultry fields at noon, and hear the low tones of her sweet voice in the moonlit evening walk; I would watch her in all her goodness and charity abroad, and the smiling untiring discharge of domestic duties at home; I would paint her and her dead sister’s child happy in their love for one another, and passing whole hours together in picturing the friends whom they had so sadly lost; I would summon before me, once again, those joyous little faces that clustered round her knee, and listen to their merry prattle; I would recall the tones of that clear laugh, and conjure up the sympathising tear that glistened in the soft blue eye. These, and a thousand looks and smiles, and turns of thought and speech—I would fain recall them every one.

text: “And always will!” exclaimed the young man. “The torment of mind I have endured these last two days forces from me a confession to you of a feeling which, as you well know, is neither of yesterday nor of sudden growth. Upon Elinor, dear, tender girl, my heart is fixed as steadfastly as ever man’s heart was fixed upon woman. I have no thought, no aim, no hope in life beyond her; and if you oppose me in this great matter, you hold my peace and happiness in your hands and scatter them to the air. Mother, think more kindly of this, and of me, and do not make light of the bliss which you appear to value so little.”
source_text: “And ever will!” said the young man. “The mental agony I have suffered, during the last two days, wrings from me the avowal to you of a passion which, as you well know, is not one of yesterday, nor one I have lightly formed. On Rose, sweet, gentle girl! my heart is set, as firmly as ever heart of man was set on woman. I have no thought, no view, no hope in life, beyond her; and if you oppose me in this great stake, you take my peace and happiness in your hands, and cast them to the wind. Mother, think better of this, and of me, and do not disregard the happiness of which you seem to think so little.”

text: The girl did as she was bid. Harker, clasping her fingers in his own, sank back upon the pillow, and fixed his gaze upon her countenance. His eyes drooped; then opened again; then closed; and once more were lifted. Uneasily he changed his posture; and after sinking for a minute or two into a fitful drowse, and as often starting up with a glance of sudden dread, and sweeping the room with vacant eyes, he was abruptly seized, as he seemed in the very act of rising, with a deep and leaden slumber. His hold upon her hand loosened; the arm he had half raised dropped heavily at his side; and he lay as one sunk in a trance too deep for waking.
source_text: The girl obeyed. Sikes, locking her hand in his, fell back upon the pillow: turning his eyes upon her face. They closed; opened again; closed once more; again opened. He shifted his position restlessly; and, after dozing again, and again, for two or three minutes, and as often springing up with a look of terror, and gazing vacantly about him, was suddenly stricken, as it were, while in the very attitude of rising, into a deep and heavy sleep. The grasp of his hand relaxed; the upraised arm fell languidly by his side; and he lay like one in a profound trance.

text: “Many years after this,” he said, “the mother of the man—Julian Merrow’s mother—came to me. He had deserted her at eighteen; had stripped her of her jewels and her money; had gambled, squandered, forged, and escaped to London, where for two years he had kept company with the vilest of the city’s refuse. She was wasting away under a cruel and incurable malady, and longed to see him before she died. Searches were begun, and every inquiry pursued. For a long while they came to nothing; but at last they were rewarded, and he returned with her to France.”
source_text: “Years after this,” he said, “this man’s—Edward Leeford’s—mother came to me. He had left her, when only eighteen; robbed her of jewels and money; gambled, squandered, forged, and fled to London: where for two years he had associated with the lowest outcasts. She was sinking under a painful and incurable disease, and wished to recover him before she died. Inquiries were set on foot, and strict searches made. They were unavailing for a long time, but ultimately successful; and he went back with her to France.”

text: The night was extremely dark. A damp mist rose from the river and the marshy ground around it, and drifted over the bleak fields. It was bitterly cold as well; everything was dismal and black. Not a word was spoken, for the driver had become drowsy, and Sikes was in no mood to draw him into conversation. Oliver sat crouched in a corner of the cart, bewildered with fear and dread, imagining strange shapes in the bare trees, whose branches swayed grimly back and forth, as though in some wild delight at the scene’s desolation.
source_text: The night was very dark. A damp mist rose from the river, and the marshy ground about; and spread itself over the dreary fields. It was piercing cold, too; all was gloomy and black. Not a word was spoken; for the driver had grown sleepy; and Sikes was in no mood to lead him into conversation. Oliver sat huddled together, in a corner of the cart; bewildered with alarm and apprehension; and figuring strange objects in the gaunt trees, whose branches waved grimly to and fro, as if in some fantastic joy at the desolation of the scene.

text: “By me,” said Mr. Brownlow. “I told you before long I should interest you. By me, I mean—I could see that your crafty companion kept my name back, though for all he knew it might have sounded entirely unfamiliar to you. When I rescued him, and he was afterward lying ill and recovering in my house, the likeness he bore to the portrait I have mentioned astonished me. Even when I first laid eyes on him, filthy and wretched as he was, there was still something in his face that seemed to me like the sudden sight of an old friend in the midst of a vivid dream. I need hardly tell you that he was spirited away before I had learned his story—”
source_text: “By me,” said Mr. Brownlow. “I told you I should interest you before long. I say by me—I see that your cunning associate suppressed my name, although for aught he knew, it would be quite strange to your ears. When he was rescued by me, then, and lay recovering from sickness in my house, his strong resemblance to this picture I have spoken of, struck me with astonishment. Even when I first saw him in all his dirt and misery, there was a lingering expression in his face that came upon me like a glimpse of some old friend flashing on one in a vivid dream. I need not tell you he was snared away before I knew his history—”

text: The room was lit by two gas lamps, their glare kept from showing outside by barred shutters and tightly drawn curtains of faded red. The ceiling had been blackened so that the flames would not stain it with smoke, and the place was so thick with tobacco smoke that at first it was hardly possible to make out anything at all. Gradually, though, as some of the haze drifted out through the open door, one could distinguish a crowd of heads, as confused and indistinct as the noises that reached the ear; and as the eye grew used to the scene, the observer slowly became aware of a large company of men and women gathered round a long table. At its head sat a chairman, holding the hammer of office in his hand, while a professional gentleman with a bluish nose and his face wrapped up for a toothache kept the jingling piano going in a distant corner.
source_text: The room was illuminated by two gas-lights; the glare of which was prevented by the barred shutters, and closely-drawn curtains of faded red, from being visible outside. The ceiling was blackened, to prevent its colour from being injured by the flaring of the lamps; and the place was so full of dense tobacco smoke, that at first it was scarcely possible to discern anything more. By degrees, however, as some of it cleared away through the open door, an assemblage of heads, as confused as the noises that greeted the ear, might be made out; and as the eye grew more accustomed to the scene, the spectator gradually became aware of the presence of a numerous company, male and female, crowded round a long table: at the upper end of which, sat a chairman with a hammer of office in his hand; while a professional gentleman with a bluish nose, and his face tied up for the benefit of a toothache, presided at a jingling piano in a remote corner.

text: “Not yet, not yet,” said the young man, holding her back as she began to rise. “My hopes, my desires, my future, my feelings—every thought in my life, except my love for you, has changed. What I offer you now is not a place in a noisy crowd, not a share in a world of spite and slander, where honest faces blush for nothing but true disgrace and shame; but a home—a heart and a home—yes, dearest Rose, and those, and those only, are all I have to give.”
source_text: “Not yet, not yet,” said the young man, detaining her as she rose. “My hopes, my wishes, prospects, feeling: every thought in life except my love for you: have undergone a change. I offer you, now, no distinction among a bustling crowd; no mingling with a world of malice and detraction, where the blood is called into honest cheeks by aught but real disgrace and shame; but a home—a heart and home—yes, dearest Rose, and those, and those alone, are all I have to offer.”

text: Mr. Fenwick perceived, with great satisfaction, that this homage to his abilities was no empty civility, but that he had in truth impressed his new associate with a due sense of his crafty powers—a conviction it was of the first importance to plant at the very beginning of their acquaintance. To confirm an impression so profitable and so agreeable, he pursued the advantage by setting forth, with considerable particularity, the scale and compass of his undertakings; mingling fact with invention as best suited his ends, and employing both with such dexterity that Mr. Haskett’s admiration plainly deepened, while it acquired, at the same time, a salutary touch of apprehension, which it was very desirable to inspire.
source_text: Mr. Fagin saw, with delight, that this tribute to his powers was no mere compliment, but that he had really impressed his recruit with a sense of his wily genius, which it was most important that he should entertain in the outset of their acquaintance. To strengthen an impression so desirable and useful, he followed up the blow by acquainting him, in some detail, with the magnitude and extent of his operations; blending truth and fiction together, as best served his purpose; and bringing both to bear, with so much art, that Mr. Bolter’s respect visibly increased, and became tempered, at the same time, with a degree of wholesome fear, which it was highly desirable to awaken.

text: By the time they had turned into Bethnal Green Road, dawn had fairly set in. Most of the lamps were already out; a few country wagons still laboured slowly onward toward London; and now and then a mud-spattered stage-coach rattled past at speed, the driver giving, as he went by, a warning crack of the whip to some heavy waggoner who, by keeping to the wrong side of the road, had nearly delayed his arrival at the office by a quarter of a minute. The public houses, their gaslights still shining within, were already open. Little by little, other shops began to be unshuttered, and a few isolated figures appeared upon the road. Then came scattered parties of labourers bound for their work; then men and women with fish-baskets balanced on their heads; donkey-carts heaped with vegetables; chaise-carts crowded with live stock or whole carcasses of meat; milk-women carrying pails; and an unbroken stream of people, trudging out with all manner of supplies toward the eastern suburbs of the town. As they drew nearer the City, the noise and movement increased by degrees; and when they threaded the streets between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had risen to a deafening roar of bustle and confusion. The light was as full as it was likely to be until night returned, and the busy morning life of half the population of London had begun.
source_text: By the time they had turned into the Bethnal Green Road, the day had fairly begun to break. Many of the lamps were already extinguished; a few country waggons were slowly toiling on, towards London; now and then, a stage-coach, covered with mud, rattled briskly by: the driver bestowing, as he passed, an admonitory lash upon the heavy waggoner who, by keeping on the wrong side of the road, had endangered his arriving at the office, a quarter of a minute after his time. The public-houses, with gas-lights burning inside, were already open. By degrees, other shops began to be unclosed, and a few scattered people were met with. Then, came straggling groups of labourers going to their work; then, men and women with fish-baskets on their heads; donkey-carts laden with vegetables; chaise-carts filled with live-stock or whole carcasses of meat; milk-women with pails; an unbroken concourse of people, trudging out with various supplies to the eastern suburbs of the town. As they approached the City, the noise and traffic gradually increased; when they threaded the streets between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had swelled into a roar of sound and bustle. It was as light as it was likely to be, till night came on again, and the busy morning of half the London population had begun.

text: Persuaded in some measure by these arguments, but far more constrained by his dread of Fagin, Mr. Bolter at last, though with very ill grace, agreed to undertake the errand. At Fagin’s direction, he promptly exchanged his own clothes for a waggoner’s frock, velveteen breeches, and leather leggings, all of which the Jew had ready at hand. He was likewise supplied with a felt hat thickly set with turnpike tickets, and a carter’s whip. So accoutred, he was to stroll into the office, as some country fellow from Covent Garden market might be imagined to do out of mere curiosity; and since he was as clumsy, ungainly, and raw-boned a creature as one could wish to see, Mr. Fagin had no doubt that he would perform the part to perfection.
source_text: Persuaded, in part, by these representations, but overborne in a much greater degree by his fear of Fagin, Mr. Bolter at length consented, with a very bad grace, to undertake the expedition. By Fagin’s directions, he immediately substituted for his own attire, a waggoner’s frock, velveteen breeches, and leather leggings: all of which articles the Jew had at hand. He was likewise furnished with a felt hat well garnished with turnpike tickets; and a carter’s whip. Thus equipped, he was to saunter into the office, as some country fellow from Covent Garden market might be supposed to do for the gratification of his curiousity; and as he was as awkward, ungainly, and raw-boned a fellow as need be, Mr. Fagin had no fear but that he would look the part to perfection.

text: The day wore away. Day? There had been no day; it had vanished almost as soon as it had arrived—and then night returned, a night so long and yet so brief; long in its awful silence, and brief in its passing hours. At one moment he raged and blasphemed; at another he howled and tore at his hair. Respectable men of his own faith had come to pray at his side, but he had driven them off with curses. They tried again in their kindness, and he struck them away.
source_text: The day passed off. Day? There was no day; it was gone as soon as come—and night came on again; night so long, and yet so short; long in its dreadful silence, and short in its fleeting hours. At one time he raved and blasphemed; and at another howled and tore his hair. Venerable men of his own persuasion had come to pray beside him, but he had driven them away with curses. They renewed their charitable efforts, and he beat them off.

text: Whether some subtle twitch of the old man’s reddish brows, and the slow narrowing of his sunken eyes, admonished Miss Nell that she had grown overfree with her confidences, is of small account. Enough that she checked herself all at once, and, with a cluster of smiling courtesies to Mr. Brace, turned the talk upon other things. Some ten minutes later, Mr. Beldon was overtaken by a paroxysm of coughing; whereupon Nell drew her shawl about her, and said the hour had come for her departure. Mr. Crowe, discovering that a portion of her route chanced to lie his own, signified his purpose of escorting her; so the two set off together, while at a little distance behind them came the dog, who slipped from a back yard the instant his master was no longer in sight.
source_text: Now, whether a peculiar contraction of the Jew’s red eye-brows, and a half closing of his deeply-set eyes, warned Miss Nancy that she was disposed to be too communicative, is not a matter of much importance. The fact is all we need care for here; and the fact is, that she suddenly checked herself, and with several gracious smiles upon Mr. Sikes, turned the conversation to other matters. In about ten minutes’ time, Mr. Fagin was seized with a fit of coughing; upon which Nancy pulled her shawl over her shoulders, and declared it was time to go. Mr. Sikes, finding that he was walking a short part of her way himself, expressed his intention of accompanying her; they went away together, followed, at a little distant, by the dog, who slunk out of a back-yard as soon as his master was out of sight.

text: He did so; and, by the light of the lamps, he saw the girl’s receding figure already some way before him. He moved on as close as he deemed safe, keeping to the opposite side of the street, that he might watch her movements the better. She glanced anxiously back two or three times, and once paused to allow two men who were following closely behind her to go past. As she went on, she seemed to regain confidence, and to walk with a firmer, more resolute step. The spy maintained the same distance between them, and followed, his eye fixed upon her.
source_text: He did so; and, by the light of the lamps, saw the girl’s retreating figure, already at some distance before him. He advanced as near as he considered prudent, and kept on the opposite side of the street, the better to observe her motions. She looked nervously round, twice or thrice, and once stopped to let two men who were following close behind her, pass on. She seemed to gather courage as she advanced, and to walk with a steadier and firmer step. The spy preserved the same relative distance between them, and followed: with his eye upon her.

text: “Yes, Edward. I owe it to myself, that I, a girl friendless and portionless, with a stain upon my name, should not give your acquaintances cause to believe that I had basely surrendered to your first passion, and fixed myself like a weight upon all your hopes and designs. I owe it to you and to those who are yours, to save you from setting yourself, in the ardor of your generous heart, against this great hindrance to your advancement in the world.”
source_text: “Yes, Harry. I owe it to myself, that I, a friendless, portionless, girl, with a blight upon my name, should not give your friends reason to suspect that I had sordidly yielded to your first passion, and fastened myself, a clog, on all your hopes and projects. I owe it to you and yours, to prevent you from opposing, in the warmth of your generous nature, this great obstacle to your progress in the world.”

text: The offense had been committed within the district, indeed quite near a very notorious metropolitan police office. The crowd had only the satisfaction of following Oliver through two or three streets, and down a place called Mutton Hill, before he was taken by a back entrance beneath a low archway, and up a filthy court, into this dispenser of summary justice. They came at last into a small paved yard; and there they met a stout man, with a tuft of whiskers on his face and a bunch of keys in his hand.
source_text: The offence had been committed within the district, and indeed in the immediate neighborhood of, a very notorious metropolitan police office. The crowd had only the satisfaction of accompanying Oliver through two or three streets, and down a place called Mutton Hill, when he was led beneath a low archway, and up a dirty court, into this dispensary of summary justice, by the back way. It was a small paved yard into which they turned; and here they encountered a stout man with a bunch of whiskers on his face, and a bunch of keys in his hand.

text: The gentleman’s attention was quickly caught; for he had not gone three steps before he wheeled about in irritation, and demanded to know what that young whelp was yelping at, and why Mr. Bumble did not oblige him with something that would make the whole succession of vocal outcries so styled an involuntary affair.
source_text: The gentleman’s notice was very soon attracted; for he had not walked three paces, when he turned angrily round, and inquired what that young cur was howling for, and why Mr. Bumble did not favour him with something which would render the series of vocular exclamations so designated, an involuntary process?

text: The exertion was too fierce to endure for any length of time. When at last they were all spent, and had no more strength left for blows and tearing hands, they dragged the struggling boy, who still cried out undismayed, down into the dark cellar, and there fastened him in. This accomplished, Mrs. Grimsby dropped wearily into a chair and broke into tears.
source_text: This was rather too violent exercise to last long. When they were all wearied out, and could tear and beat no longer, they dragged Oliver, struggling and shouting, but nothing daunted, into the dust-cellar, and there locked him up. This being done, Mrs. Sowerberry sunk into a chair, and burst into tears.

text: But the elderly gentleman was not alone in lifting the alarm. The Artful Jack and young Tom Bates, unwilling to draw the gaze of the crowd by dashing openly down the street, had contented themselves with slipping into the first doorway that offered shelter round the corner. No sooner had they caught the outcry, and seen the boy racing past, than, understanding at once how affairs must stand, they came out with admirable haste; and, crying “Stop thief!” with the rest, they entered the chase in the character of zealous and respectable citizens.
source_text: But the old gentleman was not the only person who raised the hue-and-cry. The Dodger and Master Bates, unwilling to attract public attention by running down the open street, had merely retired into the very first doorway round the corner. They no sooner heard the cry, and saw Oliver running, than, guessing exactly how the matter stood, they issued forth with great promptitude; and, shouting “Stop thief!” too, joined in the pursuit like good citizens.

text: Brittles gave in on these conditions; and the party, somewhat reassured by the discovery—made when the shutters were thrown open—that it was now broad daylight, made their way upstairs, the dogs leading the way. The two women, unwilling to remain below, came last. On Mr. Giles’s advice, they all spoke very loudly, to let any ill-minded person outside know that they were strong in numbers; and, by a stroke of policy conceived by the same ingenious gentleman, the dogs’ tails were smartly pinched in the hall, so that they might bark ferociously.
source_text: Brittles capitulated on these terms; and the party being somewhat re-assured by the discovery (made on throwing open the shutters) that it was now broad day, took their way upstairs; with the dogs in front. The two women, who were afraid to stay below, brought up the rear. By the advice of Mr. Giles, they all talked very loud, to warn any evil-disposed person outside, that they were strong in numbers; and by a master-stoke of policy, originating in the brain of the same ingenious gentleman, the dogs’ tails were well pinched, in the hall, to make them bark savagely.

text: The room stayed empty for a quarter of an hour or longer; the girl came back with that same ghostly, silent step; and soon after, the two men were heard going downstairs. Monks went straight out into the street, and the Jew crept up the stairs once more to fetch the money. When he came back, the girl was arranging her shawl and bonnet, as though she were getting ready to leave.
source_text: The room remained deserted for a quarter of an hour or more; the girl glided back with the same unearthly tread; and, immediately afterwards, the two men were heard descending. Monks went at once into the street; and the Jew crawled upstairs again for the money. When he returned, the girl was adjusting her shawl and bonnet, as if preparing to be gone.

text: Oliver was only too pleased to make himself useful; too glad to have some faces to look at, poor as they were; too anxious to win over those around him whenever he could do so honestly; to raise any objection to this plan. So he at once declared himself ready; and, kneeling on the floor while the Dodger sat on the table so that he could place one foot in his lap, he set to work at what Mr. Dawkins called “japanning his trotter-cases.” In plain English, that meant cleaning his boots.
source_text: Oliver was but too glad to make himself useful; too happy to have some faces, however bad, to look upon; too desirous to conciliate those about him when he could honestly do so; to throw any objection in the way of this proposal. So he at once expressed his readiness; and, kneeling on the floor, while the Dodger sat upon the table so that he could take his foot in his laps, he applied himself to a process which Mr. Dawkins designated as “japanning his trotter-cases.” The phrase, rendered into plain English, signifieth, cleaning his boots.

text: A faint stir in the court brought him back to himself. Glancing about, he perceived the jurors had drawn together to settle upon their verdict. Then his gaze drifted to the gallery, where he saw people rising upon one another’s shoulders to catch sight of his face: some hurriedly pressing spectacles to their eyes, others bending to whisper with looks of open loathing to their neighbors. A few appeared scarcely to heed him at all, attending only to the jury with an uneasy impatience that the matter should be delayed no longer. Yet on no countenance—not even among the women, of whom there were many—could he discover the least spark of pity for himself, nor any emotion save that consuming desire to see him condemned.
source_text: A slight bustle in the court, recalled him to himself. Looking round, he saw that the jurymen had turned together, to consider their verdict. As his eyes wandered to the gallery, he could see the people rising above each other to see his face: some hastily applying their glasses to their eyes: and others whispering their neighbours with looks expressive of abhorrence. A few there were, who seemed unmindful of him, and looked only to the jury, in impatient wonder how they could delay. But in no one face—not even among the women, of whom there were many there—could he read the faintest sympathy with himself, or any feeling but one of all-absorbing interest that he should be condemned.

text: Cursing his lateness under his breath, Sikes shoved Oliver ahead of him, and they went into a low, dark room, where a smoky fire burned among two or three broken chairs, a table, and an ancient couch. On this couch, lying stretched out with his feet much higher than his head, a man was smoking a long clay pipe. He wore a neatly tailored snuff-colored coat with large brass buttons, an orange neckerchief, a rough, gaudy waistcoat with a shawl-like pattern, and drab breeches. This was Mr. Crackit. He had not much hair, either on his head or face; but what there was had a reddish tint and was forced into long corkscrew curls, through which he now and then ran some very dirty fingers, adorned with large cheap rings. He was a little above medium height and seemed somewhat weak in the legs; yet this in no way lessened his admiration for his top-boots, which he regarded, from their lofty position, with evident pleasure.
source_text: Muttering a curse upon his tardiness, Sikes pushed Oliver before him; and they entered a low dark room with a smoky fire, two or three broken chairs, a table, and a very old couch: on which, with his legs much higher than his head, a man was reposing at full length, smoking a long clay pipe. He was dressed in a smartly-cut snuff-coloured coat, with large brass buttons; an orange neckerchief; a coarse, staring, shawl-pattern waistcoat; and drab breeches. Mr. Crackit (for he it was) had no very great quantity of hair, either upon his head or face; but what he had, was of a reddish dye, and tortured into long corkscrew curls, through which he occasionally thrust some very dirty fingers, ornamented with large common rings. He was a trifle above the middle size, and apparently rather weak in the legs; but this circumstance by no means detracted from his own admiration of his top-boots, which he contemplated, in their elevated situation, with lively satisfaction.

text: “You speak rather bluntly, my friend, but you seem an honest, open-hearted man,” said the old gentleman, turning his spectacles toward the man who was competing for Oliver’s reward, whose wicked face was as plain a proof of cruelty as any stamped receipt. Yet the magistrate was half blind and half childish, so it could hardly be expected that he should clearly see what others were really like.
source_text: “You’re a rough speaker, my friend, but you look an honest, open-hearted man,” said the old gentleman: turning his spectacles in the direction of the candidate for Oliver’s premium, whose villainous countenance was a regular stamped receipt for cruelty. But the magistrate was half blind and half childish, so he couldn’t reasonably be expected to discern what other people did.

text: Mr. Grimwig wasted no time in putting this admonition into practice upon the blushing girl; and the contagion of the example was quickly taken up by the doctor and by Mr. Hardcastle as well. Certain persons have gone so far as to declare that Edward Vale was seen to have first contrived it in a dim room beside the others; but the most respectable accounts reject this as sheer scandal, he being young and in holy orders.
source_text: Mr. Grimwig lost no time in carrying this notice into effect upon the blushing girl; and the example, being contagious, was followed both by the doctor and Mr. Brownlow: some people affirm that Harry Maylie had been observed to set it, originally, in a dark room adjoining; but the best authorities consider this downright scandal: he being young and a clergyman.

text: “This,” said Mr. Ashcombe, speaking low, yet with no abatement of force, “this is the youth who, having been accidentally hurt by a spring-gun in some boyish trespass upon Mr. What’s-his-name’s grounds yonder at the rear, came to the house this morning for help, and was straightway seized and ill-used by that very ingenious gentleman there with the candle in his hand: a person who has put his life in no small peril, as I can professionally attest.”
source_text: “This,” said Mr. Losberne, speaking softly, but with great vehemence notwithstanding, “this is the lad, who, being accidently wounded by a spring-gun in some boyish trespass on Mr. What-d’ ye-call-him’s grounds, at the back here, comes to the house for assistance this morning, and is immediately laid hold of and maltreated, by that ingenious gentleman with the candle in his hand: who has placed his life in considerable danger, as I can professionally certify.”

text: “I’ll take care to get myself among them as can,” replied Jonas. “They’ll find some use for us one way or another. Why, you yourself are worth fifty women; I never saw such a precious sly and deceitful creature as you can be when I choose to let you.”
source_text: “I shall look out to get into company with them as can,” replied Noah. “They’ll be able to make us useful some way or another. Why, you yourself are worth fifty women; I never see such a precious sly and deceitful creetur as yer can be when I let yer.”

text: Thomas looked up with an air of simple innocence into Mr. Sowerby’s countenance at this rather self-contradicting form of speech; but that worthy foreclosed all possibility of reply by conducting him straightway into the room beyond, whose door stood open. It was a spacious chamber, lit by a broad window. At a desk in the farther part sat two elderly gentlemen with powdered heads: one was absorbed in a newspaper, while the other, through a pair of tortoiseshell spectacles, was examining a small sheet of parchment spread before him. Mr. Crumpson was stationed before the desk on one side, and Mr. Gadsby, with his face still only half-washed, on the other; while two or three burly fellows in top-boots were idling about the room.
source_text: Oliver stared innocently in Mr. Bumble’s face at this somewhat contradictory style of address; but that gentleman prevented his offering any remark thereupon, by leading him at once into an adjoining room: the door of which was open. It was a large room, with a great window. Behind a desk, sat two old gentleman with powdered heads: one of whom was reading the newspaper; while the other was perusing, with the aid of a pair of tortoise-shell spectacles, a small piece of parchment which lay before him. Mr. Limbkins was standing in front of the desk on one side; and Mr. Gamfield, with a partially washed face, on the other; while two or three bluff-looking men, in top-boots, were lounging about.

text: It was a heavy, airless, grey summer evening. The clouds, which had hung menacingly since morning, had gathered themselves into one thick, sluggish body of vapour, and were already letting fall great drops of rain, as though they forewarned a fierce thunder-storm, when Mr. and Mrs. Pumble, quitting the town’s chief thoroughfare, bent their steps toward a straggling little cluster of dilapidated cottages, lying a mile and a half, or thereabout, beyond it, and standing upon a low, unhealthy marsh, close beside the river.
source_text: It was a dull, close, overcast summer evening. The clouds, which had been threatening all day, spread out in a dense and sluggish mass of vapour, already yielded large drops of rain, and seemed to presage a violent thunder-storm, when Mr. and Mrs. Bumble, turning out of the main street of the town, directed their course towards a scattered little colony of ruinous houses, distant from it some mile and a-half, or thereabouts, and erected on a low unwholesome swamp, bordering upon the river.

text: He found himself pressed in among a crowd, mostly women, who were packed together in a filthy, squalid room, at the far end of which stood a raised platform shut off from the rest by a railing, with a prisoners’ dock on the left against the wall, a witnesses’ box in the center, and a magistrates’ desk on the right; the last and most formidable of these places was screened by a partition that hid the bench from ordinary view and left the common people to imagine, if they could, the full majesty of justice.
source_text: He found himself jostled among a crowd of people, chiefly women, who were huddled together in a dirty frowsy room, at the upper end of which was a raised platform railed off from the rest, with a dock for the prisoners on the left hand against the wall, a box for the witnesses in the middle, and a desk for the magistrates on the right; the awful locality last named, being screened off by a partition which concealed the bench from the common gaze, and left the vulgar to imagine (if they could) the full majesty of justice.

text: “No,” replied the woman; “if he”—she pointed to Monks—“has been coward enough to own it, as I see he has, and you have questioned these old hags until at last you found the right ones, I have nothing more to say. I did sell them, and they are where you will never get them. What then?”
source_text: “No,” replied the woman; “if he”—she pointed to Monks—“has been coward enough to confess, as I see he has, and you have sounded all these hags till you have found the right ones, I have nothing more to say. I _did_ sell them, and they’re where you’ll never get them. What then?”

text: Mrs. Maylie went to live with her son and daughter-in-law, so that, through the peaceful remainder of her days, she might enjoy the highest happiness that age and goodness can know: the sight of the joy of those upon whom the warmest love and the tenderest devotion of a life well lived had been steadily and faithfully bestowed.
source_text: Mrs. Maylie took up her abode with her son and daughter-in-law, to enjoy, during the tranquil remainder of her days, the greatest felicity that age and worth can know—the contemplation of the happiness of those on whom the warmest affections and tenderest cares of a well-spent life, have been unceasingly bestowed.

text: “When, indeed, ma’am!” Mr. Bumble answered. “Here is one man who, taking into account his wife and large family, is given a quartern loaf and a fine pound of cheese, and full weight too. And is he thankful, ma’am? Is he thankful? Not worth a farthing of copper, not a bit of it! What does he do, ma’am, but ask for a few coals—if only enough to fill a pocket handkerchief, he says! Coals! What would he do with coals? Toast his cheese over them, and then come back for more. That’s how it is with these people, ma’am; give them an apronful of coals today, and they’ll be back for another the day after tomorrow, as brazen as alabaster.”
source_text: “When, indeed, ma’am!” rejoined Mr. Bumble. “Why here’s one man that, in consideration of his wife and large family, has a quartern loaf and a good pound of cheese, full weight. Is he grateful, ma’am? Is he grateful? Not a copper farthing’s worth of it! What does he do, ma’am, but ask for a few coals; if it’s only a pocket handkerchief full, he says! Coals! What would he do with coals? Toast his cheese with ’em and then come back for more. That’s the way with these people, ma’am; give ’em a apron full of coals today, and they’ll come back for another, the day after tomorrow, as brazen as alabaster.”

text: “No matter for the moment. We will speak of it again. You have a friend in me, Nance—a steadfast friend. I have the means here, ready and within reach, quiet and secret. If you want revenge on those who treat you like a dog—like a dog, worse than his dog, for he indulges his dog sometimes—come to me. I tell you, come to me. He is only the hound of a day, but you have known me long, Nance.”
source_text: “No matter just now. We’ll talk of this again. You have a friend in me, Nance; a staunch friend. I have the means at hand, quiet and close. If you want revenge on those that treat you like a dog—like a dog! worse than his dog, for he humours him sometimes—come to me. I say, come to me. He is the mere hound of a day, but you know me of old, Nance.”

text: It may have been that her tears had eased her, or that she had suddenly felt the whole desolation of her plight; but she turned away, and hastened back at almost the same reckless speed, now in the opposite direction, partly to make up for the time she had lost, and partly to keep pace with the tumult of her own thoughts, until at length she reached the place where she had left the housebreaker.
source_text: It might be that her tears relieved her, or that she felt the full hopelessness of her condition; but she turned back; and hurrying with nearly as great rapidity in the contrary direction; partly to recover lost time, and partly to keep pace with the violent current of her own thoughts: soon reached the dwelling where she had left the housebreaker.

text: Julian cast a rapid, expectant glance round him in search of the Artful one; but though there were several women who might very well have passed for that celebrated rogue’s mother or sister, and not a few men who could have passably claimed the likeness of his father, there was not a soul to be seen who answered in the least to the description he had been given of Master Vale. He remained in painful doubt and agitation until the women, having been sent on for trial, swept out with much bravado; and then his uneasiness was at once put an end to by the entrance of another prisoner, whom he knew instantly could be none other than the very person for whom he had come.
source_text: Noah looked eagerly about him for the Dodger; but although there were several women who would have done very well for that distinguished character’s mother or sister, and more than one man who might be supposed to bear a strong resemblance to his father, nobody at all answering the description given him of Mr. Dawkins was to be seen. He waited in a state of much suspense and uncertainty until the women, being committed for trial, went flaunting out; and then was quickly relieved by the appearance of another prisoner who he felt at once could be no other than the object of his visit.

text: “Stand still a moment, and I’ll get you one,” answered the voice. The speaker’s footsteps were then heard retreating; and, in another minute, the figure of Mr. John Dawkins, otherwise the Artful Dodger, came into view. In his right hand he carried a tallow candle fixed in the split end of a stick.
source_text: “Stand still a moment, and I’ll get you one,” replied the voice. The receding footsteps of the speaker were heard; and, in another minute, the form of Mr. John Dawkins, otherwise the Artful Dodger, appeared. He bore in his right hand a tallow candle stuck in the end of a cleft stick.

text: One lovely night, when they had taken a longer stroll than was customary with them—for the day had been unusually warm, and there was a splendid moon overhead, while a light breeze had risen, delightfully refreshing—Rose had been in unusually high spirits as well, and they had gone on in cheerful talk until they had passed far beyond their usual limit. Mrs. Maylie, becoming fatigued, they returned home more slowly. The young lady, after merely laying aside her simple bonnet, went as usual to the piano. After wandering listlessly over the keys for a few minutes, she fell into a soft and deeply solemn strain; and while she played, they heard a sound as though she were weeping.
source_text: One beautiful night, when they had taken a longer walk than was customary with them: for the day had been unusually warm, and there was a brilliant moon, and a light wind had sprung up, which was unusually refreshing. Rose had been in high spirits, too, and they had walked on, in merry conversation, until they had far exceeded their ordinary bounds. Mrs. Maylie being fatigued, they returned more slowly home. The young lady merely throwing off her simple bonnet, sat down to the piano as usual. After running abstractedly over the keys for a few minutes, she fell into a low and very solemn air; and as she played it, they heard a sound as if she were weeping.

text: “It is all sold as quickly as ever it can be made,” said the man. “There are fourteen water-mills, six steam-engines, and a galvanic battery at work on it always, and they cannot make it fast enough, though the men labour so hard they drop away, and the widows are pensioned at once, with twenty pounds a year for each child, and a premium of fifty for twins. One penny a square! Two half-pence is as good, and four farthings are received with delight. One penny a square! Wine-stains, fruit-stains, beer-stains, water-stains, paint-stains, pitch-stains, mud-stains, blood-stains! Here is a stain on the hat of a gentleman in company, which I will take out clean before he can order me a pint of ale.”
source_text: “It’s all bought up as fast as it can be made,” said the fellow. “There are fourteen water-mills, six steam-engines, and a galvanic battery, always a-working upon it, and they can’t make it fast enough, though the men work so hard that they die off, and the widows is pensioned directly, with twenty pound a-year for each of the children, and a premium of fifty for twins. One penny a square! Two half-pence is all the same, and four farthings is received with joy. One penny a square! Wine-stains, fruit-stains, beer-stains, water-stains, paint-stains, pitch-stains, mud-stains, blood-stains! Here is a stain upon the hat of a gentleman in company, that I’ll take clean out, before he can order me a pint of ale.”

text: A few minutes after that conversation, the sound of footsteps on the creaking stairs roused the merry old gentleman, who was sitting by the fire with a saveloy and a small loaf in his hand, a pocket-knife in his right, and a pewter pot standing on the trivet. A roguish smile spread over his white face as he turned round; then, peering keenly from beneath his thick red eyebrows, he bent his ear toward the door and listened.
source_text: The noise of footsteps on the creaking stairs, a few minutes after the occurrence of this conversation, roused the merry old gentleman as he sat over the fire with a saveloy and a small loaf in his hand; a pocket-knife in his right; and a pewter pot on the trivet. There was a rascally smile on his white face as he turned round, and looking sharply out from under his thick red eyebrows, bent his ear towards the door, and listened.

text: After a time she rose and, with weak and unsteady steps, went up the street. The astonished listener stood motionless at his post for several minutes afterward, and, after satisfying himself with many wary glances around that he was once more alone, he crept slowly out of his hiding-place and returned, stealthily and in the shadow of the wall, by the same route he had taken in coming down.
source_text: After a time she arose, and with feeble and tottering steps ascended the street. The astonished listener remained motionless on his post for some minutes afterwards, and having ascertained, with many cautious glances round him, that he was again alone, crept slowly from his hiding-place, and returned, stealthily and in the shade of the wall, in the same manner as he had descended.

text: At these words, the matron sank back into her chair and, once again resting her elbow on the table, fell to thinking of her lonely lot. The little teapot and the single cup had stirred painful memories of Mr. Corney, who had not been dead more than five-and-twenty years, and she was overcome.
source_text: With these words, the matron dropped into her chair, and, once more resting her elbow on the table, thought of her solitary fate. The small teapot, and the single cup, had awakened in her mind sad recollections of Mr. Corney (who had not been dead more than five-and-twenty years); and she was overpowered.

text: “I beg your pardon, Mr. Harry,” said Giles, giving his disordered face a last rub with the handkerchief; “but if you’d let the postboy say that, I should be very much obliged to you. It wouldn’t be proper for the maid-servants to see me in such a state, sir; I should never have any authority with them again if they did.”
source_text: “I beg your pardon, Mr. Harry,” said Giles: giving a final polish to his ruffled countenance with the handkerchief; “but if you would leave the postboy to say that, I should be very much obliged to you. It wouldn’t be proper for the maids to see me in this state, sir; I should never have any more authority with them if they did.”

text: Though Mr. Losberne greeted a proposal that meant a delay of a full five days with many a grimace, he was forced to admit that no better plan suggested itself to him at the moment; and since both Rose and Mrs. Maylie warmly supported Mr. Brownlow, that gentleman’s suggestion was agreed to without dissent.
source_text: Although Mr. Losberne received with many wry faces a proposal involving a delay of five whole days, he was fain to admit that no better course occurred to him just then; and as both Rose and Mrs. Maylie sided very strongly with Mr. Brownlow, that gentleman’s proposition was carried unanimously.

text: “You must do more than that,” said Mr. Ashcombe. “You must make amends to an innocent and unoffending child, for such he is, though born of a guilty and most wretched attachment. You have not forgotten the terms of the will. Carry them out so far as your brother is concerned, and then go whither you will. In this world you need never meet again.”
source_text: “You must do more than that,” said Mr. Brownlow. “Make restitution to an innocent and unoffending child, for such he is, although the offspring of a guilty and most miserable love. You have not forgotten the provisions of the will. Carry them into execution so far as your brother is concerned, and then go where you please. In this world you need meet no more.”

text: “With a little persuading,” thought Fagin, “what more likely than that she would agree to poison him? Women have done such things before, and worse, to gain the same end. There would be the dangerous villain—the man I hate—out of the way; someone else fixed in his place; and my control over the girl, with this crime known to me as my hold upon her, beyond all limit.”
