source_text: “With a little persuasion,” thought Fagin, “what more likely than that she would consent to poison him? Women have done such things, and worse, to secure the same object before now. There would be the dangerous villain: the man I hate: gone; another secured in his place; and my influence over the girl, with a knowledge of this crime to back it, unlimited.”

text: “Your having interfered on behalf of this dear boy before,” said Rose; “your coming here, at such peril, to tell me what you have heard; your manner, which convinces me that you speak truly; your clear remorse and your shame—all persuade me that you may yet be reclaimed. Oh!” cried the earnest girl, clasping her hands as the tears ran down her cheeks, “do not turn a deaf ear to the entreaties of one of your own sex—the first, I do believe, who ever appealed to you in the voice of pity and compassion. Hear me, and let me save you yet for better things.”
source_text: “Your having interfered in this dear boy’s behalf before,” said Rose; “your coming here, at so great a risk, to tell me what you have heard; your manner, which convinces me of the truth of what you say; your evident contrition, and sense of shame; all lead me to believe that you might yet be reclaimed. Oh!” said the earnest girl, folding her hands as the tears coursed down her face, “do not turn a deaf ear to the entreaties of one of your own sex; the first—the first, I do believe, who ever appealed to you in the voice of pity and compassion. Do hear my words, and let me save you yet, for better things.”

text: And Mr. Sykes had spoken truly. By means of alternate menaces, entreaties, and inducements, the lady in question was at length persuaded to accept the errand. She was not, it must be owned, restrained by the same scruples as her obliging companion; for, having lately taken up her abode in the vicinity of Field Place from the distant though fashionable quarter of Limehouse, she entertained no equal fear of being known by any among her numerous acquaintances.
source_text: And Mr. Sikes was right. By dint of alternate threats, promises, and bribes, the lady in question was ultimately prevailed upon to undertake the commission. She was not, indeed, withheld by the same considerations as her agreeable friend; for, having recently removed into the neighborhood of Field Lane from the remote but genteel suburb of Ratcliffe, she was not under the same apprehension of being recognised by any of her numerous acquaintances.

text: To reach this spot, the visitor must thread his way through a labyrinth of cramped, narrow, and muddy streets, crowded with the roughest and poorest people of the riverfront, and given over to the trade they are likely to foster. The shops are piled high with the cheapest and coarsest provisions; the most common and least refined garments hang at the shopkeeper’s door, and spill from the parapets and windows above. Bumping against idle labourers of the lowest sort, ballast-heavers, coal-whippers, bold women, ragged children, and the very dregs and drift of the river, he struggles onward, his senses offended by the sights and smells that pour from the narrow lanes branching off to either side, and his ears stunned by the grinding of heavy wagons hauling vast loads of goods from the warehouses that rise at every turn. At last, reaching streets still more remote and less frequented than those he has already traversed, he passes beneath shuddering house-fronts jutting out over the pavement, ruined walls that seem ready to fall as he goes by, chimneys half-crushed, half-reluctant to topple, windows protected by rusty iron bars that age and grime have nearly corroded away, and every possible token of decay and neglect.
source_text: To reach this place, the visitor has to penetrate through a maze of close, narrow, and muddy streets, thronged by the roughest and poorest of waterside people, and devoted to the traffic they may be supposed to occasion. The cheapest and least delicate provisions are heaped in the shops; the coarsest and commonest articles of wearing apparel dangle at the salesman’s door, and stream from the house-parapet and windows. Jostling with unemployed labourers of the lowest class, ballast-heavers, coal-whippers, brazen women, ragged children, and the raff and refuse of the river, he makes his way with difficulty along, assailed by offensive sights and smells from the narrow alleys which branch off on the right and left, and deafened by the clash of ponderous waggons that bear great piles of merchandise from the stacks of warehouses that rise from every corner. Arriving, at length, in streets remoter and less-frequented than those through which he has passed, he walks beneath tottering house-fronts projecting over the pavement, dismantled walls that seem to totter as he passes, chimneys half crushed half hesitating to fall, windows guarded by rusty iron bars that time and dirt have almost eaten away, every imaginable sign of desolation and neglect.

text: In some villages, large painted boards had been set up to warn all who begged within the district that they would be sent to jail. This alarmed Oliver greatly, and made him thankful to leave such places with all possible speed. In others, he would linger about the inn yards and gaze sorrowfully at every person who went by; a conduct which usually ended with the landlady telling one of the post-boys loafing nearby to turn the strange boy out of the place, for she was certain he had come to steal something. If he begged at a farmer’s house, they were ten to one to threaten to set the dog on him; and if he showed his face in a shop, they would begin talking about the beadle—which made Oliver’s heart leap into his mouth, very often the only thing he had there for many hours at a time.
source_text: In some villages, large painted boards were fixed up: warning all persons who begged within the district, that they would be sent to jail. This frightened Oliver very much, and made him glad to get out of those villages with all possible expedition. In others, he would stand about the inn-yards, and look mournfully at every one who passed: a proceeding which generally terminated in the landlady’s ordering one of the post-boys who were lounging about, to drive that strange boy out of the place, for she was sure he had come to steal something. If he begged at a farmer’s house, ten to one but they threatened to set the dog on him; and when he showed his nose in a shop, they talked about the beadle—which brought Oliver’s heart into his mouth,—very often the only thing he had there, for many hours together.

text: Inside the altar of the old village church stands a white marble tablet, bearing only a single word: “AGNES.” There is no coffin in that tomb; and may many, many years pass before any other name is placed beside it! But if the spirits of the dead ever return to earth, to visit the places made sacred by the love—the love beyond the grave—of those who knew them in life, I believe the shade of Agnes sometimes lingers around that solemn corner. I believe it no less because that corner is in a church, and because she was frail and fallible.
source_text: Within the altar of the old village church there stands a white marble tablet, which bears as yet but one word: “AGNES.” There is no coffin in that tomb; and may it be many, many years, before another name is placed above it! But, if the spirits of the Dead ever come back to earth, to visit spots hallowed by the love—the love beyond the grave—of those whom they knew in life, I believe that the shade of Agnes sometimes hovers round that solemn nook. I believe it none the less because that nook is in a Church, and she was weak and erring.

text: And so the night dragged slowly onward. Oliver lay awake for some time, counting the small rings of light that the reflection from the rushlight shade cast upon the ceiling, or following with his weary eyes the intricate pattern of the wallpaper. The darkness and deep stillness of the room were very solemn, and as they brought to the boy’s mind the thought that death had hovered there for many days and nights, and might still fill it with the gloom and terror of his dreadful presence, he turned his face to the pillow and prayed fervently to Heaven.
source_text: And thus the night crept slowly on. Oliver lay awake for some time, counting the little circles of light which the reflection of the rushlight-shade threw upon the ceiling; or tracing with his languid eyes the intricate pattern of the paper on the wall. The darkness and the deep stillness of the room were very solemn; as they brought into the boy’s mind the thought that death had been hovering there, for many days and nights, and might yet fill it with the gloom and dread of his awful presence, he turned his face upon the pillow, and fervently prayed to Heaven.

text: “Lord save us!” the old lady cried, giving a start. “Don’t talk like that, child. You’re weak and high-strung after your illness. Let me turn your chair round to the other side, and then you won’t see it. There!” she added, doing as she said; “at any rate, you can’t see it now.”
source_text: “Lord save us!” exclaimed the old lady, starting; “don’t talk in that way, child. You’re weak and nervous after your illness. Let me wheel your chair round to the other side; and then you won’t see it. There!” said the old lady, suiting the action to the word; “you don’t see it now, at all events.”

text: He went on from street to street, and so on again, until mere motion had somewhat cooled the first violence of his sorrow; and then the strange turning of his spirits left him thirsty. Many public-houses offered themselves to his eye; yet he at last stood still before one in a narrow lane, whose parlour, from a hurried glance through the blinds, appeared empty but for a single customer. At that very instant the rain began to fall in torrents. The circumstance decided him. Mr. Bumble went in; and, calling for drink as he crossed the bar, made his way into the room which he had been observing from the roadway.
source_text: He walked up one street, and down another, until exercise had abated the first passion of his grief; and then the revulsion of feeling made him thirsty. He passed a great many public-houses; but, at length paused before one in a by-way, whose parlour, as he gathered from a hasty peep over the blinds, was deserted, save by one solitary customer. It began to rain, heavily, at the moment. This determined him. Mr. Bumble stepped in; and ordering something to drink, as he passed the bar, entered the apartment into which he had looked from the street.

text: Mr. Barrow sat in the parish parlour, his eyes fixed in a sullen reverie upon the cheerless grate, from which, though it was summertime, no warmer light was returned than the faint reflection of a few wan sunbeams cast back from its cold, polished face. From the ceiling there hung a paper cage for flies, toward which he now and then lifted his glance in despondent reflection; and as the foolish creatures drifted about the showy mesh, Mr. Barrow would draw a long sigh, while a deeper cloud seemed to settle upon his brow. Mr. Barrow was in thought; perhaps the insects recalled to him some bitter chapter in his own earlier life.
source_text: Mr. Bumble sat in the workhouse parlour, with his eyes moodily fixed on the cheerless grate, whence, as it was summer time, no brighter gleam proceeded, than the reflection of certain sickly rays of the sun, which were sent back from its cold and shining surface. A paper fly-cage dangled from the ceiling, to which he occasionally raised his eyes in gloomy thought; and, as the heedless insects hovered round the gaudy net-work, Mr. Bumble would heave a deep sigh, while a more gloomy shadow overspread his countenance. Mr. Bumble was meditating; it might be that the insects brought to mind, some painful passage in his own past life.

text: “I’ve thought of everything,” said the Jew with animation. “I’ve—I’ve kept a sharp watch on him, my dears, very close—very close. Once he comes to believe he is one of us; once his mind is filled with the notion that he has been a thief; and he is ours! Ours for life. Oho! It could not have turned out better!” The old man folded his arms across his chest, and, drawing his head and shoulders down into a crouch, fairly hugged himself with delight.
source_text: “I’ve thought of it all,” said the Jew with energy. “I’ve—I’ve had my eye upon him, my dears, close—close. Once let him feel that he is one of us; once fill his mind with the idea that he has been a thief; and he’s ours! Ours for his life. Oho! It couldn’t have come about better!” The old man crossed his arms upon his breast; and, drawing his head and shoulders into a heap, literally hugged himself for joy.

text: That when the Artful One, and his accomplished companion Master Tomkins, took part in the hue-and-cry that was set up at poor Oliver’s heels, in consequence of their carrying off by stealth Mr. Brownlow’s personal property, as has already been related, they were governed by a most creditable and proper regard for their own safety; and since the liberty of the subject and the freedom of the individual are among the first and proudest boasts of a right-minded Englishman, it is scarcely needful, I trust, to remind the reader that this proceeding must serve to raise them in the esteem of all public and patriotic men, in nearly as high a degree as this striking evidence of their concern for their own preservation tends to confirm and strengthen the little system of laws which certain deep and judicious philosophers have set forth as the secret springs of all Nature’s acts and doings: those same philosophers, with great sagacity, reducing the amiable lady’s conduct to rules and theories; and, by a very graceful compliment to her lofty wisdom and understanding, wholly passing over all thought of heart, or generous impulse and feeling. For these are matters altogether beneath a female who, by universal acknowledgment, is far above the many small foibles and weaknesses of her sex.
source_text: That when the Dodger, and his accomplished friend Master Bates, joined in the hue-and-cry which was raised at Oliver’s heels, in consequence of their executing an illegal conveyance of Mr. Brownlow’s personal property, as has been already described, they were actuated by a very laudable and becoming regard for themselves; and forasmuch as the freedom of the subject and the liberty of the individual are among the first and proudest boasts of a true-hearted Englishman, so, I need hardly beg the reader to observe, that this action should tend to exalt them in the opinion of all public and patriotic men, in almost as great a degree as this strong proof of their anxiety for their own preservation and safety goes to corroborate and confirm the little code of laws which certain profound and sound-judging philosophers have laid down as the main-springs of all Nature’s deeds and actions: the said philosophers very wisely reducing the good lady’s proceedings to matters of maxim and theory: and, by a very neat and pretty compliment to her exalted wisdom and understanding, putting entirely out of sight any considerations of heart, or generous impulse and feeling. For, these are matters totally beneath a female who is acknowledged by universal admission to be far above the numerous little foibles and weaknesses of her sex.

text: He threw a dark glance and a threatening gesture of his hand back toward the place where he had left the bolder villain, and then went on his way, his bony hands busy among the folds of his ragged garment, which he twisted hard in his grip, as if each movement of his fingers were crushing some hated enemy.
source_text: He cast back a dark look, and a threatening motion of the hand, towards the spot where he had left the bolder villain; and went on his way: busying his bony hands in the folds of his tattered garment, which he wrenched tightly in his grasp, as though there were a hated enemy crushed with every motion of his fingers.

text: When these matters had been settled, he was instructed in the needful signs and tokens by which he might know the dexterous young rogue, and was conducted by Charlie Bates through dark, crooked lanes to a point very near Bow Street. After describing the exact position of the office, and fortifying his description with ample directions as to how he should go straight along the passage, and at what moment, on entering the side, he was to take off his hat as he stepped into the room, Charley bade him make haste on by himself, and promised to await his return at the very place where they had parted.
source_text: These arrangements completed, he was informed of the necessary signs and tokens by which to recognise the Artful Dodger, and was conveyed by Master Bates through dark and winding ways to within a very short distance of Bow Street. Having described the precise situation of the office, and accompanied it with copious directions how he was to walk straight up the passage, and when he got into the side, and pull off his hat as he went into the room, Charley Bates bade him hurry on alone, and promised to bide his return on the spot of their parting.

text: “I mean only this: when I left you last, I left you with a steadfast resolve to cast down every imagined barrier between us; determined that, if my world could not be yours, I would make yours mine; that no pride of birth should sneer at you, for I would turn away from it. And this I have done. Those who drew back from me because of it have also drawn back from you, and have thus proved you right in that at least. Such power and patronage, such kinsfolk of influence and degree, as smiled on me then, now look on me coldly; but there are fair fields and rustling trees in England’s richest county, and by one village church—mine, Rose, my own!—there stands a humble dwelling that can make me prouder than all the hopes I have surrendered, multiplied a thousand times. This is my rank and station now, and here I lay it down!”
source_text: “I mean but this—that when I left you last, I left you with a firm determination to level all fancied barriers between yourself and me; resolved that if my world could not be yours, I would make yours mine; that no pride of birth should curl the lip at you, for I would turn from it. This I have done. Those who have shrunk from me because of this, have shrunk from you, and proved you so far right. Such power and patronage: such relatives of influence and rank: as smiled upon me then, look coldly now; but there are smiling fields and waving trees in England’s richest county; and by one village church—mine, Rose, my own!—there stands a rustic dwelling which you can make me prouder of, than all the hopes I have renounced, measured a thousandfold. This is my rank and station now, and here I lay it down!”

text: She hurriedly put on her bonnet and shawl, glancing around her in terror from time to time, as though, in spite of the sleeping draught, she expected at any moment to feel Sikes’s heavy hand on her shoulder. Then she bent quietly over the bed and kissed the robber on the lips; and then, opening and closing the room door with the lightest touch, she hastened out of the house.
source_text: She hastily dressed herself in her bonnet and shawl: looking fearfully round, from time to time, as if, despite the sleeping draught, she expected every moment to feel the pressure of Sikes’s heavy hand upon her shoulder; then, stooping softly over the bed, she kissed the robber’s lips; and then opening and closing the room-door with noiseless touch, hurried from the house.

text: Of all the dreadful cries that ever struck mortal ears, none could surpass the shout of the enraged mob. Some called on those nearest at hand to set fire to the house; others bellowed to the officers to shoot him dead. Yet none among them displayed such savage fury as the man on horseback, who flung himself from the saddle and forced his way through the crowd as though parting water, crying beneath the window, in a voice louder than all the rest, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”
source_text: Of all the terrific yells that ever fell on mortal ears, none could exceed the cry of the infuriated throng. Some shouted to those who were nearest to set the house on fire; others roared to the officers to shoot him dead. Among them all, none showed such fury as the man on horseback, who, throwing himself out of the saddle, and bursting through the crowd as if he were parting water, cried, beneath the window, in a voice that rose above all others, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”

text: Mrs. Sowerberry’s generosity toward Oliver had shown itself in showering upon him all the dirty scraps and leavings that no one else would eat; and so there was much meekness and self-sacrifice in her choosing to remain under Mr. Bumble’s harsh accusation, of which, to do her justice, she was entirely innocent in thought, word, and deed.
source_text: The liberality of Mrs. Sowerberry to Oliver, had consisted of a profuse bestowal upon him of all the dirty odds and ends which nobody else would eat; so there was a great deal of meekness and self-devotion in her voluntarily remaining under Mr. Bumble’s heavy accusation, of which, to do her justice, she was wholly innocent, in thought, word, or deed.

text: For a while, Mr. Bumble led Oliver on in silence, making no comment; for the beadle held his head very high, as a beadle ought to do. And since it was a windy day, little Oliver was almost entirely hidden by the tails of Mr. Bumble’s coat, which flapped open and showed to excellent effect his turned-up waistcoat and drab plush knee-breeches. As they came close to their destination, however, Mr. Bumble judged it proper to glance down and make sure the boy was in presentable order for inspection by his new master: this he did at once, with an air of gracious authority that was both suitable and becoming.
source_text: For some time, Mr. Bumble drew Oliver along, without notice or remark; for the beadle carried his head very erect, as a beadle always should: and, it being a windy day, little Oliver was completely enshrouded by the skirts of Mr. Bumble’s coat as they blew open, and disclosed to great advantage his flapped waistcoat and drab plush knee-breeches. As they drew near to their destination, however, Mr. Bumble thought it expedient to look down, and see that the boy was in good order for inspection by his new master: which he accordingly did, with a fit and becoming air of gracious patronage.

text: The members of this board were very wise, far-seeing, philosophical men; and when they turned their attention to the workhouse, they immediately discovered what ordinary people would never have guessed—the poor actually liked it! It had become a sort of public amusement for the lower classes; a tavern where nothing had to be paid; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all year long; a brick-and-mortar paradise, where there was nothing but leisure and no labor at all. “Ha!” said the board, with a knowing air; “we are the people to put this right; we’ll put a stop to it in no time.” So they laid down the rule that every poor person should be given the choice (for they would force no one, certainly not they) of being slowly starved inside the house, or quickly starved outside it. With this in mind, they made a contract with the waterworks to provide an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn mill to furnish, at intervals, tiny amounts of oatmeal; and they served three meals a day of weak gruel, with an onion twice a week and half a roll on Sundays. They also introduced many other sensible and benevolent rules, concerning the women, which it is not necessary to repeat; graciously undertook to separate poor married couples, on account of the enormous cost of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of making a man keep his family, as had previously been done, took his family away from him and turned him into a bachelor! It is impossible to say how many claimants for relief under these last two arrangements might have appeared in every rank of society, had it not been linked to the workhouse; but the board were shrewd men and had foreseen this problem. Relief could not be had apart from the workhouse and the gruel; and that terrified people.
source_text: The members of this board were very sage, deep, philosophical men; and when they came to turn their attention to the workhouse, they found out at once, what ordinary folks would never have discovered—the poor people liked it! It was a regular place of public entertainment for the poorer classes; a tavern where there was nothing to pay; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all the year round; a brick and mortar elysium, where it was all play and no work. “Oho!” said the board, looking very knowing; “we are the fellows to set this to rights; we’ll stop it all, in no time.” So, they established the rule, that all poor people should have the alternative (for they would compel nobody, not they), of being starved by a gradual process in the house, or by a quick one out of it. With this view, they contracted with the water-works to lay on an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn-factory to supply periodically small quantities of oatmeal; and issued three meals of thin gruel a day, with an onion twice a week, and half a roll of Sundays. They made a great many other wise and humane regulations, having reference to the ladies, which it is not necessary to repeat; kindly undertook to divorce poor married people, in consequence of the great expense of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of compelling a man to support his family, as they had theretofore done, took his family away from him, and made him a bachelor! There is no saying how many applicants for relief, under these last two heads, might have started up in all classes of society, if it had not been coupled with the workhouse; but the board were long-headed men, and had provided for this difficulty. The relief was inseparable from the workhouse and the gruel; and that frightened people.

text: He could imagine no ill purpose in sending him to Sikes that would not be just as well served by keeping him with Fagin; and after long reflection, he came to the conclusion that he had been chosen to do some common drudgery for the housebreaker, until another boy, better fitted to the Jew’s design, could be found. He was too familiar with misery, and had endured too much where he was, to lament any change very bitterly. He sat absorbed in thought for several minutes; then, with a heavy sigh, he snuffed the candle and, taking up the book the Jew had left with him, began to read.
source_text: He could think of no bad object to be attained by sending him to Sikes, which would not be equally well answered by his remaining with Fagin; and after meditating for a long time, concluded that he had been selected to perform some ordinary menial offices for the housebreaker, until another boy, better suited for his purpose could be engaged. He was too well accustomed to suffering, and had suffered too much where he was, to bewail the prospect of change very severely. He remained lost in thought for some minutes; and then, with a heavy sigh, snuffed the candle, and, taking up the book which the Jew had left with him, began to read.

text: When Oliver awoke the next morning, he was much astonished to discover that a new pair of shoes, with stout thick soles, had been set beside his bed, while his old ones had been taken away. At first, the sight delighted him, for he hoped it might be a sign that his release was near; but these thoughts were soon destroyed when he sat down to breakfast with the Jew, who informed him, in a manner and tone that only deepened his uneasiness, that he was to be taken that night to the dwelling of Bill Sikes.
source_text: When Oliver awoke in the morning, he was a good deal surprised to find that a new pair of shoes, with strong thick soles, had been placed at his bedside; and that his old shoes had been removed. At first, he was pleased with the discovery: hoping that it might be the forerunner of his release; but such thoughts were quickly dispelled, on his sitting down to breakfast along with the Jew, who told him, in a tone and manner which increased his alarm, that he was to be taken to the residence of Bill Sikes that night.

text: “You have endured more, and yet remained, through all, the kindest and most gracious creature that ever spread gladness over all who knew her,” said Mrs. Vale, folding her in a tender embrace. “Come, come, my love; remember who it is that waits to draw you to his heart, poor child! There—look here, look, my dear!”
source_text: “You have borne more, and have been, through all, the best and gentlest creature that ever shed happiness on every one she knew,” said Mrs. Maylie, embracing her tenderly. “Come, come, my love, remember who this is who waits to clasp you in his arms, poor child! See here—look, look, my dear!”

text: When she came into the richer part of the town, the streets were for the most part empty; and there her breathless haste awakened a yet livelier wonder in the few idlers she swept by. Some quickened their steps behind her, as if to discover whither she could be bound at so extraordinary a pace; and a handful turned to meet her, then glanced after her, astonished at the unbroken swiftness of her course. But one after another they dropped away; and by the time she drew near the place she sought, she was alone.
source_text: When she reached the more wealthy quarter of the town, the streets were comparatively deserted; and here her headlong progress excited a still greater curiosity in the stragglers whom she hurried past. Some quickened their pace behind, as though to see whither she was hastening at such an unusual rate; and a few made head upon her, and looked back, surprised at her undiminished speed; but they fell off one by one; and when she neared her place of destination, she was alone.

text: Since Mr. Barrow had insisted that they should not come into London before dark, it was close upon eleven when they reached the turnpike at Islington. They passed the Angel and turned into St. John’s Road; hurried down the narrow lane that ends at Sadler’s Wells Theatre; threaded Exmouth Street and Coppice Row; slipped through the little alley beside the workhouse; crossed the storied ground once known as Hockley-in-the-Hole; and then gained Little Saffron Hill, and so Saffron Hill the Great: along which the Artful one darted onward at a swift pace, bidding Oliver keep hard upon his heels.
source_text: As John Dawkins objected to their entering London before nightfall, it was nearly eleven o’clock when they reached the turnpike at Islington. They crossed from the Angel into St. John’s Road; struck down the small street which terminates at Sadler’s Wells Theatre; through Exmouth Street and Coppice Row; down the little court by the side of the workhouse; across the classic ground which once bore the name of Hockley-in-the-Hole; thence into Little Saffron Hill; and so into Saffron Hill the Great: along which the Dodger scudded at a rapid pace, directing Oliver to follow close at his heels.

text: “I want to go back,” the girl said. “I have to go back, because—how can I say such things to a gentle lady like you?—because among the men I’ve spoken to you about, there is one: the most reckless of them all. I can’t leave him, no, not even if it would save me from the life I’m living now.”
source_text: “I wish to go back,” said the girl. “I must go back, because—how can I tell such things to an innocent lady like you?—because among the men I have told you of, there is one: the most desperate among them all; that I can’t leave: no, not even to be saved from the life I am leading now.”

text: “You’re a sharp little fellow, my dear,” said the merry old gentleman, tapping Oliver kindly on the head. “I never set eyes on a quicker-witted lad. There’s a shilling for you. If you keep on in this fashion, you’ll grow into the greatest man of the day. And now come along, and I’ll show you how the marks are taken out of the handkerchiefs.”
source_text: “You’re a clever boy, my dear,” said the playful old gentleman, patting Oliver on the head approvingly. “I never saw a sharper lad. Here’s a shilling for you. If you go on, in this way, you’ll be the greatest man of the time. And now come here, and I’ll show you how to take the marks out of the handkerchiefs.”

text: Once these preliminaries were adjusted, Mr. Sikes set to drinking brandy at a furious pace, while he brandished the crowbar in a most alarming fashion, all the while bellowing out most unmusical snatches of song, thickly interspersed with wild curses. At length, in a burst of professional zeal, he insisted on producing his box of housebreaking tools; but he had scarcely staggered in with it, and opened it to explain the use and qualities of the several instruments it held, together with the special merits of their workmanship, when he toppled over the box onto the floor and fell asleep where he lay.
source_text: These preliminaries adjusted, Mr. Sikes proceeded to drink brandy at a furious rate, and to flourish the crowbar in an alarming manner; yelling forth, at the same time, most unmusical snatches of song, mingled with wild execrations. At length, in a fit of professional enthusiasm, he insisted upon producing his box of housebreaking tools: which he had no sooner stumbled in with, and opened for the purpose of explaining the nature and properties of the various implements it contained, and the peculiar beauties of their construction, than he fell over the box upon the floor, and went to sleep where he fell.

text: Monks drew the small packet from the breast of his coat, where he had hastily thrust it; and fastening it to a lump of lead, which had once belonged to some pulley and now lay upon the floor, let it sink into the current. It dropped with a clean, direct plunge, true as an arrow; split the water with scarcely a sound; and was lost from sight.
source_text: Monks drew the little packet from his breast, where he had hurriedly thrust it; and tying it to a leaden weight, which had formed a part of some pulley, and was lying on the floor, dropped it into the stream. It fell straight, and true as a die; clove the water with a scarcely audible splash; and was gone.

text: When the girl reached the open street, she sank down on a doorstep and seemed, for a few moments, utterly bewildered, unable to go on. Then, all at once, she sprang up and hurried away in a direction quite opposite to that in which Sikes was waiting for her return. She quickened her pace until it broke into a frantic run. When she had quite exhausted herself, she stopped to catch her breath, and then, as though she had suddenly remembered something and bitterly regretted her inability to do what she had resolved upon, she wrung her hands and burst into tears.
source_text: When the girl got into the open street, she sat down upon a doorstep; and seemed, for a few moments, wholly bewildered and unable to pursue her way. Suddenly she arose; and hurrying on, in a direction quite opposite to that in which Sikes was awaiting her return, quickened her pace, until it gradually resolved into a violent run. After completely exhausting herself, she stopped to take breath: and, as if suddenly recollecting herself, and deploring her inability to do something she was bent upon, wrung her hands, and burst into tears.

text: It happened one morning, while Oliver’s affairs were in this fortunate and comfortable condition, that Mr. Gamfield, the chimney-sweep, made his way down the High Street, deeply absorbed in thinking how he might raise the money to pay certain overdue rent, for which his landlord had begun to press him rather hard. Even at his most hopeful, Mr. Gamfield could not see his resources as coming within a full five pounds of the sum required; and in a kind of numerical despair, he was by turns beating his brains and his donkey, when, as he passed the workhouse, his eyes fell upon the notice fixed to the gate.
source_text: It chanced one morning, while Oliver’s affairs were in this auspicious and comfortable state, that Mr. Gamfield, chimney-sweep, went his way down the High Street, deeply cogitating in his mind his ways and means of paying certain arrears of rent, for which his landlord had become rather pressing. Mr. Gamfield’s most sanguine estimate of his finances could not raise them within full five pounds of the desired amount; and, in a species of arithmetical desperation, he was alternately cudgelling his brains and his donkey, when passing the workhouse, his eyes encountered the bill on the gate.

text: There is a species of slumber that sometimes descends upon us, which, while it keeps the body in thrall, does not wholly release the mind from a perception of the things around it, nor permit it to wander at will. If that condition, marked by an overwhelming drowsiness, a complete sinking of strength, and an utter incapacity either to govern our thoughts or to command the least motion, may be named sleep, then sleep it is; and yet we remain aware of all that passes near us. Should we dream in such a moment, the words actually uttered, or the sounds truly existing at the instant, fit themselves with astonishing ease into our visions, until fact and fancy are so curiously mingled that it is afterward nearly impossible to distinguish one from the other. Nor is this the most remarkable circumstance attending such a state. It is a certain truth that, though sight and touch be for a while suspended, the dreams of the sleeper, and the phantasmal scenes that move before him, will be affected—indeed powerfully affected—by the mere silent nearness of some outward object, which may not have been beside him when he closed his eyes, and of whose presence he had no waking knowledge.
source_text: There is a kind of sleep that steals upon us sometimes, which, while it holds the body prisoner, does not free the mind from a sense of things about it, and enable it to ramble at its pleasure. So far as an overpowering heaviness, a prostration of strength, and an utter inability to control our thoughts or power of motion, can be called sleep, this is it; and yet, we have a consciousness of all that is going on about us, and, if we dream at such a time, words which are really spoken, or sounds which really exist at the moment, accommodate themselves with surprising readiness to our visions, until reality and imagination become so strangely blended that it is afterwards almost matter of impossibility to separate the two. Nor is this, the most striking phenomenon incidental to such a state. It is an undoubted fact, that although our senses of touch and sight be for the time dead, yet our sleeping thoughts, and the visionary scenes that pass before us, will be influenced and materially influenced, by the _mere silent presence_ of some external object; which may not have been near us when we closed our eyes: and of whose vicinity we have had no waking consciousness.

text: This was by no means a place of doubtful reputation; for it had long been known as the haunt of none but low ruffians, who, under various pretences of earning their bread by honest labour, lived chiefly by robbery and crime. It was nothing more than a cluster of wretched hovels: some thrown up hastily of loose brick; others fashioned from old, worm-eaten ship’s timber; all crowded together without order or design, and set, for the most part, within a few feet of the river’s edge. A few leaky boats, drawn up on the mud and secured to the low wall that bordered it; with an oar or a coil of rope here and there, at first seemed to suggest that the inmates of these miserable dwellings followed some business upon the river. But a single glance at the broken and useless state of the things so displayed would have made any passer-by suspect, without much trouble, that they were placed there less for use than to keep up appearances.
source_text: This was far from being a place of doubtful character; for it had long been known as the residence of none but low ruffians, who, under various pretences of living by their labour, subsisted chiefly on plunder and crime. It was a collection of mere hovels: some, hastily built with loose bricks: others, of old worm-eaten ship-timber: jumbled together without any attempt at order or arrangement, and planted, for the most part, within a few feet of the river’s bank. A few leaky boats drawn up on the mud, and made fast to the dwarf wall which skirted it: and here and there an oar or coil of rope: appeared, at first, to indicate that the inhabitants of these miserable cottages pursued some avocation on the river; but a glance at the shattered and useless condition of the articles thus displayed, would have led a passer-by, without much difficulty, to the conjecture that they were disposed there, rather for the preservation of appearances, than with any view to their being actually employed.

text: Only after the two boys had hurried at great speed through a bewildering labyrinth of cramped streets and courts did they dare to stop beneath a low, shadowed archway. There, remaining silent just long enough to catch his breath, Master Bates gave an exclamation of pleasure and amusement; then, suddenly overcome, he broke into such an uncontrollable burst of laughter that he threw himself upon a doorstep and rolled upon it in ecstasy of mirth.
source_text: It was not until the two boys had scoured, with great rapidity, through a most intricate maze of narrow streets and courts, that they ventured to halt beneath a low and dark archway. Having remained silent here, just long enough to recover breath to speak, Master Bates uttered an exclamation of amusement and delight; and, bursting into an uncontrollable fit of laughter, flung himself upon a doorstep, and rolled thereon in a transport of mirth.

text: It was Smithfield they were crossing, though for all Oliver knew it might just as well have been Grosvenor Square. The night was dark and fog-laden. The shop lights could scarcely force their way through the dense mist, which grew thicker with every moment and wrapped the streets and houses in shadow, making the unfamiliar place seem even more unfamiliar to Oliver’s eyes and his uncertainty all the more bleak and oppressive.
source_text: It was Smithfield that they were crossing, although it might have been Grosvenor Square, for anything Oliver knew to the contrary. The night was dark and foggy. The lights in the shops could scarecely struggle through the heavy mist, which thickened every moment and shrouded the streets and houses in gloom; rendering the strange place still stranger in Oliver’s eyes; and making his uncertainty the more dismal and depressing.

text: “For what reason,” said the gentleman in a gentler voice, “for what end could you have led us to this strange place? Why would you not have allowed me to speak with you up there, where there is light and some movement of life, rather than bringing us down into this dark and miserable hole?”
source_text: “Why, for what,” said the gentleman in a kinder tone, “for what purpose can you have brought us to this strange place? Why not have let me speak to you, above there, where it is light, and there is something stirring, instead of bringing us to this dark and dismal hole?”

text: Oliver had not been inside the workhouse for a quarter of an hour, and had barely finished eating a second piece of bread, when Mr. Bumble, after leaving him in the charge of an old woman, came back; and, telling him it was board night, informed him that the board had ordered him to present himself at once.
source_text: Oliver had not been within the walls of the workhouse a quarter of an hour, and had scarcely completed the demolition of a second slice of bread, when Mr. Bumble, who had handed him over to the care of an old woman, returned; and, telling him it was a board night, informed him that the board had said he was to appear before it forthwith.

text: “Then what the devil is to be done?” cried the fiery physician, once they had come back to the two ladies. “Are we to move a vote of thanks for all these rogues, men and women alike, and request them to accept a hundred pounds apiece, or thereabouts, as some paltry token of our regard, and a small acknowledgment of the kindness they have shown to Felix?”
source_text: “Then what the devil is to be done?” said the impetuous doctor, when they had rejoined the two ladies. “Are we to pass a vote of thanks to all these vagabonds, male and female, and beg them to accept a hundred pounds, or so, apiece, as a trifling mark of our esteem, and some slight acknowledgment of their kindness to Oliver?”

text: How Mr. Harcourt passed from day to day, storing the mind of his adopted boy with knowledge, and growing more and more fond of him as his character unfolded itself, and revealed the flourishing seeds of all he would fain have him become; how he discerned in him fresh lineaments of his earliest friend, and these recalled to his own breast memories long past, sad indeed, yet gentle and consoling; how the two children of misfortune, taught by suffering, remembered its lesson in kindness to others, in tenderness for one another, and in grateful praise to Him who had watched over and preserved them—these are matters there is no need to tell. I have said that they were truly happy; and without deep affection and a humane heart, and thankfulness to that Being whose law is Mercy, and whose chief attribute is goodness toward all that lives and breathes, happiness can never be won.
source_text: How Mr. Brownlow went on, from day to day, filling the mind of his adopted child with stores of knowledge, and becoming attached to him, more and more, as his nature developed itself, and showed the thriving seeds of all he wished him to become—how he traced in him new traits of his early friend, that awakened in his own bosom old remembrances, melancholy and yet sweet and soothing—how the two orphans, tried by adversity, remembered its lessons in mercy to others, and mutual love, and fervent thanks to Him who had protected and preserved them—these are all matters which need not to be told. I have said that they were truly happy; and without strong affection and humanity of heart, and gratitude to that Being whose code is Mercy, and whose great attribute is Benevolence to all things that breathe, happiness can never be attained.

text: Mr. Harrow knew the habits and usages of that particular breed of humankind to which Lena belonged well enough to feel fairly sure that it would be somewhat perilous to carry on any further conversation with her, for the present. Desiring to turn the company’s thoughts aside, he addressed himself to Arthur.
source_text: Mr. Fagin was sufficiently well acquainted with the manners and customs of that particular species of humanity to which Nancy belonged, to feel tolerably certain that it would be rather unsafe to prolong any conversation with her, at present. With the view of diverting the attention of the company, he turned to Oliver.

text: “A few—only a very few—will be enough, Clara,” said the young man, bringing his chair nearer to hers. “What I have to say has already, I think, occurred to you; the dearest hopes of my heart are not strangers to you, though from my own lips you have never heard them spoken.”
source_text: “A few—a very few—will suffice, Rose,” said the young man, drawing his chair towards her. “What I shall have to say, has already presented itself to your mind; the most cherished hopes of my heart are not unknown to you, though from my lips you have not heard them stated.”

text: After receiving from both an assurance that she might do so in safety, she began, in so subdued a voice that it was often hard for the hearer even to catch the meaning of her words, to identify, by name and situation, the public-house from which she had been tracked that night. By the way she would pause from time to time, it seemed as though the gentleman were rapidly taking down the particulars she imparted. When she had fully made clear the situation of the place, the best point from which to keep watch without drawing notice, and the night and hour at which the man called Vane was most commonly seen there, she fell silent for a few moments, as if striving to bring his countenance and manner more distinctly before her mind.
source_text: After receiving an assurance from both, that she might safely do so, she proceeded in a voice so low that it was often difficult for the listener to discover even the purport of what she said, to describe, by name and situation, the public-house whence she had been followed that night. From the manner in which she occasionally paused, it appeared as if the gentleman were making some hasty notes of the information she communicated. When she had thoroughly explained the localities of the place, the best position from which to watch it without exciting observation, and the night and hour on which Monks was most in the habit of frequenting it, she seemed to consider for a few moments, for the purpose of recalling his features and appearances more forcibly to her recollection.

text: They had intended to stay in London no more than three days before setting out for several weeks to some far-off stretch of the coast. It was now midnight of the first day. What course could she resolve on, that might be carried through in forty-eight hours? Or how was she to delay the journey without awakening suspicion?
source_text: They purposed remaining in London only three days, prior to departing for some weeks to a distant part of the coast. It was now midnight of the first day. What course of action could she determine upon, which could be adopted in eight-and-forty hours? Or how could she postpone the journey without exciting suspicion?

text: For several minutes Oliver’s sobs interrupted his speech; and just as he was about to tell how he had been reared at the farm and afterward conveyed to the workhouse by Mr. Bumble, there came at the street door a sharp little double rap, unusually impatient in its manner: whereupon the servant hastened up the stairs and announced Mr. Dallow.
source_text: Oliver’s sobs checked his utterance for some minutes; when he was on the point of beginning to relate how he had been brought up at the farm, and carried to the workhouse by Mr. Bumble, a peculiarly impatient little double-knock was heard at the street-door: and the servant, running upstairs, announced Mr. Grimwig.

text: There was another, and a darker, end to be served. Sikes knew too much, and his brutal sneers had stung Hester none the less keenly because the hurt they gave was concealed from sight. The girl must well have understood that, if she cast him off, she could never again be secure from his vengeance; and that it would certainly fall—bringing lameness perhaps, or even death—upon the object of her later caprice.
source_text: There was another, and a darker object, to be gained. Sikes knew too much, and his ruffian taunts had not galled Fagin the less, because the wounds were hidden. The girl must know, well, that if she shook him off, she could never be safe from his fury, and that it would be surely wreaked—to the maiming of limbs, or perhaps the loss of life—on the object of her more recent fancy.

text: Unadorned by any great violence of gesture, this might have seemed little enough of a compliment to the lady’s charms; but as Mr. Bumble accompanied the threat with many martial flourishes, she was deeply moved by this proof of his devotion, and declared, with much admiration, that he was indeed a dove.
source_text: Unembellished by any violence of gesticulation, this might have seemed no very high compliment to the lady’s charms; but, as Mr. Bumble accompanied the threat with many warlike gestures, she was much touched with this proof of his devotion, and protested, with great admiration, that he was indeed a dove.

text: “The short and the long of what you mean,” said Nancy, speaking with great emphasis and frowning a little at Oliver, as though to demand his full attention to what she said, “is that if he gets in your way in this job you’ve got in hand, you’ll stop him from ever telling tales afterward by shooting him through the head, and you’ll take your chance of being hanged for it, just as you do for a great many other things in the course of business, every month of your life.”
source_text: “The short and the long of what you mean,” said Nancy: speaking very emphatically, and slightly frowning at Oliver as if to bespeak his serious attention to her words: “is, that if you’re crossed by him in this job you have on hand, you’ll prevent his ever telling tales afterwards, by shooting him through the head, and will take your chance of swinging for it, as you do for a great many other things in the way of business, every month of your life.”

text: Mr. and Mrs. Bumble, stripped of their posts, were by degrees brought down to extreme poverty and wretchedness, and at last sank into paupers in that very workhouse where they had once ruled over others. Mr. Bumble has been heard to say that in this downfall and humiliation, he has not even the heart to feel grateful for being parted from his wife.
source_text: Mr. and Mrs. Bumble, deprived of their situations, were gradually reduced to great indigence and misery, and finally became paupers in that very same workhouse in which they had once lorded it over others. Mr. Bumble has been heard to say, that in this reverse and degradation, he has not even spirits to be thankful for being separated from his wife.

text: As the villain crossed his arms tightly over his breast and muttered imprecations against himself in the rage of defeated malice, Mr. Brownlow turned to the frightened group beside him and explained that the Jew, who had once been his accomplice and confidant, had been promised a substantial reward for keeping Oliver in his power, part of which was to be surrendered if he were rescued; and that a quarrel on this very matter had brought them to the country house, in order to identify him.
source_text: As the villain folded his arms tight together, and muttered curses on himself in the impotence of baffled malice, Mr. Brownlow turned to the terrified group beside him, and explained that the Jew, who had been his old accomplice and confidant, had a large reward for keeping Oliver ensnared: of which some part was to be given up, in the event of his being rescued: and that a dispute on this head had led to their visit to the country house for the purpose of identifying him.

text: It was a circular table; and as Mrs. Morley and Mr. Sniggs had been seated opposite one another, with no very wide interval between them, and both facing the hearth, it may readily be perceived that Mr. Sniggs, in edging away from the fire while still remaining by the table, widened the space betwixt himself and Mrs. Morley; a manoeuvre which some discreet readers will doubtless be inclined to applaud, and to account a deed of notable heroism on Mr. Sniggs’s part: he being, in a manner, solicited by season, situation, and chance, to breathe certain tender trifles, which, though they may sit prettily enough upon the mouths of the volatile and unthinking, appear vastly beneath the consequence of the judges of the realm, the members of parliament, the ministers of state, the lord mayors, and all other eminent public personages, but especially beneath the pomp and severity of a beadle: who, as every one knows, ought to be the most rigid and unbending of them all.
source_text: It was a round table; and as Mrs. Corney and Mr. Bumble had been sitting opposite each other, with no great space between them, and fronting the fire, it will be seen that Mr. Bumble, in receding from the fire, and still keeping at the table, increased the distance between himself and Mrs. Corney; which proceeding, some prudent readers will doubtless be disposed to admire, and to consider an act of great heroism on Mr. Bumble’s part: he being in some sort tempted by time, place, and opportunity, to give utterance to certain soft nothings, which however well they may become the lips of the light and thoughtless, do seem immeasurably beneath the dignity of judges of the land, members of parliament, ministers of state, lord mayors, and other great public functionaries, but more particularly beneath the stateliness and gravity of a beadle: who (as is well known) should be the sternest and most inflexible among them all.

text: “Though I must observe,” resumed the coffin-maker, taking up again the thread of his remarks which the beadle had so provokingly broken: “though I must observe, Mr. Sniggs, that I labor under one very serious disadvantage; namely, that the stout folk always die off the fastest. Those who have lived most comfortably, and paid the parish most regularly these many years, are the first to fade away once they come within doors; and I assure you, Mr. Sniggs, that three or four inches beyond one’s estimate makes a fearful gap in one’s gains—most especially when a man has a household to keep, sir.”
source_text: “Though I must say,” continued the undertaker, resuming the current of observations which the beadle had interrupted: “though I must say, Mr. Bumble, that I have to contend against one very great disadvantage: which is, that all the stout people go off the quickest. The people who have been better off, and have paid rates for many years, are the first to sink when they come into the house; and let me tell you, Mr. Bumble, that three or four inches over one’s calculation makes a great hole in one’s profits: especially when one has a family to provide for, sir.”

text: “They are the property of the old sir,” cried Adrian, twisting his hands together; “of the kind, generous old sir who took me into his home and had me cared for when I was all but dying of the fever. Oh, I beg you, send them back; send him back the books and the money. Keep me here all my days, if you will; only, pray, pray, send them back. He will believe I have stolen them—the old lady, every one of them who was so good to me—will think I stole them. Oh, have pity on me, and send them back!”
source_text: “They belong to the old gentleman,” said Oliver, wringing his hands; “to the good, kind, old gentleman who took me into his house, and had me nursed, when I was near dying of the fever. Oh, pray send them back; send him back the books and money. Keep me here all my life long; but pray, pray send them back. He’ll think I stole them; the old lady: all of them who were so kind to me: will think I stole them. Oh, do have mercy upon me, and send them back!”

text: She had picked up the same pen and set it down again fifty times, and had thought over and over about the first line of her letter without writing a single word, when Oliver, who had been wandering the streets with Mr. Giles for a bodyguard, rushed into the room with such breathless haste and such violent agitation that he seemed to announce some new cause for alarm.
source_text: She had taken up the same pen, and laid it down again fifty times, and had considered and reconsidered the first line of her letter without writing the first word, when Oliver, who had been walking in the streets, with Mr. Giles for a body-guard, entered the room in such breathless haste and violent agitation, as seemed to betoken some new cause of alarm.

text: Had the lady but persisted in her purpose, she would assuredly have screamed at this further presumption; but the effort was spared her by a brisk rapping at the door. Scarcely had the sound reached their ears, when Mr. Tumble sprang, with remarkable nimbleness, to the wine bottles, and set to polishing them with furious energy; while the matron, in a sharp tone, called out to know who stood without.
source_text: As the lady had stated her intention of screaming, of course she would have screamed at this additional boldness, but that the exertion was rendered unnecessary by a hasty knocking at the door: which was no sooner heard, than Mr. Bumble darted, with much agility, to the wine bottles, and began dusting them with great violence: while the matron sharply demanded who was there.

text: Mr. Bumble, who had not yet been let by his better half into any larger part of the secret than he had known at first, listened to this exchange with his neck stretched forward and his eyes wide open, which he shifted by turns from his wife to Monks in frank amazement, and was even more astonished, if that were possible, when the latter sharply asked what amount was needed to purchase the disclosure.
source_text: Mr. Bumble, who had not yet been admitted by his better half into any greater share of the secret than he had originally possessed, listened to this dialogue with outstretched neck and distended eyes: which he directed towards his wife and Monks, by turns, in undisguised astonishment; increased, if possible, when the latter sternly demanded, what sum was required for the disclosure.

text: “No, indeed,” replied his mother; “you already have, if I am not mistaken, too powerful a hold upon her affections. What I would say,” she went on, checking her son as he was on the point of speaking, “is this. Before you venture all upon this hazard; before you allow yourself to be lifted to the very summit of hope; consider for a few moments, my dear child, Rose’s history, and think what influence the knowledge of her uncertain birth may have upon her choice: devoted as she is to us, with all the fervor of her noble nature, and with that complete surrender of self which, in matters great or small, has ever been her distinguishing trait.”
source_text: “No, indeed,” replied his mother; “you have, or I mistake, too strong a hold on her affections already. What I would say,” resumed the old lady, stopping her son as he was about to speak, “is this. Before you stake your all on this chance; before you suffer yourself to be carried to the highest point of hope; reflect for a few moments, my dear child, on Rose’s history, and consider what effect the knowledge of her doubtful birth may have on her decision: devoted as she is to us, with all the intensity of her noble mind, and with that perfect sacrifice of self which, in all matters, great or trifling, has always been her characteristic.”

text: Before the echo of their footsteps had quite died away through the house, the girl had already slipped from her shoes; and, drawing the loose folds of her gown about her head, and wrapping her arms in it, stood at the door, listening with a breathless eagerness. The instant the sound was gone, she glided from the room; mounted the stairs with a softness and silence almost incredible; and vanished into the darkness above.
source_text: Before the sound of their footsteps had ceased to echo through the house, the girl had slipped off her shoes; and drawing her gown loosely over her head, and muffling her arms in it, stood at the door, listening with breathless interest. The moment the noise ceased, she glided from the room; ascended the stairs with incredible softness and silence; and was lost in the gloom above.

text: “Then I think it is necessary,” said the physician; “at any rate, I am certain you would bitterly repent not having done it, should you delay. He is quite still and comfortable at present. Allow me—Miss Clara, will you allow me? There is not the least occasion for fear, I give you my word of honour!”
source_text: “Then I think it is necessary,” said the doctor; “at all events, I am quite sure that you would deeply regret not having done so, if you postponed it. He is perfectly quiet and comfortable now. Allow me—Miss Rose, will you permit me? Not the slightest fear, I pledge you my honour!”

text: “Then, if your lot had been cast otherwise,” replied Rose, “if you had been only a little, though not too much, above me; if I could have been a comfort and support to you in some quiet scene of peace and retirement, and not a hindrance and reproach amid ambitious and brilliant company; I should have been spared this trial. I have every reason to be happy—very happy—now; but then, Harry, I confess I should have been happier.”
source_text: “Then, if your lot had been differently cast,” rejoined Rose; “if you had been even a little, but not so far, above me; if I could have been a help and comfort to you in any humble scene of peace and retirement, and not a blot and drawback in ambitious and distinguished crowds; I should have been spared this trial. I have every reason to be happy, very happy, now; but then, Harry, I own I should have been happier.”

text: Master Bates nodded his agreement, and would have said something, but the memory of Oliver’s escape came over him so abruptly that the smoke he was drawing in caught with a laugh, and rushed up into his head and down into his throat, setting off a coughing fit and a stamping fit that lasted about five minutes.
source_text: Master Bates nodded assent, and would have spoken, but the recollection of Oliver’s flight came so suddenly upon him, that the smoke he was inhaling got entangled with a laugh, and went up into his head, and down into his throat: and brought on a fit of coughing and stamping, about five minutes long.

At last he laid it down, as though abandoning all hope of deciphering it, and, leaning back in his chair, murmured:
source_text: Having replaced these trinkets, the Jew took out another: so small that it lay in the palm of his hand. There seemed to be some very minute inscription on it; for the Jew laid it flat upon the table, and shading it with his hand, pored over it, long and earnestly. At length he put it down, as if despairing of success; and, leaning back in his chair, muttered:

text: If she showed any sign of agitation when she appeared before Mr. Sikes, he did not notice it; for he only asked whether she had brought the money, and when she answered that she had, he gave a grunt of satisfaction and, laying his head back on the pillow, returned to the sleep her arrival had broken.
source_text: If she betrayed any agitation, when she presented herself to Mr. Sikes, he did not observe it; for merely inquiring if she had brought the money, and receiving a reply in the affirmative, he uttered a growl of satisfaction, and replacing his head upon the pillow, resumed the slumbers which her arrival had interrupted.

text: “You have set yourself outside its mercy,” said the man. “What lies behind you has been nothing but a barren desolation, with the heedless wasting of youth and of gifts so rare that the Giver of them bestows them once alone, and never gives them twice. Yet as for what lies before you, there is still hope. I do not pretend that we can bestow upon you quietness of soul and mind, for that must be won in the seeking; but a calm refuge, whether in England, or, if you dread to remain here, in some other land, is not only possible to us but the earnest object of our care. Before morning breaks, before this river has opened its eyes to the first pale light, you shall be so completely removed beyond the reach of those who knew you before that you will leave behind you no more trace than if you had vanished from the world this very instant. Come! I would not have you turn back to speak a single word with any old comrade, nor cast one last glance upon any familiar place, nor breathe again the air itself, which is poison and doom to you. Leave them all, while there is yet time and the chance to use it!”
source_text: “You put yourself beyond its pale,” said the gentleman. “The past has been a dreary waste with you, of youthful energies mis-spent, and such priceless treasures lavished, as the Creator bestows but once and never grants again, but, for the future, you may hope. I do not say that it is in our power to offer you peace of heart and mind, for that must come as you seek it; but a quiet asylum, either in England, or, if you fear to remain here, in some foreign country, it is not only within the compass of our ability but our most anxious wish to secure you. Before the dawn of morning, before this river wakes to the first glimpse of day-light, you shall be placed as entirely beyond the reach of your former associates, and leave as utter an absence of all trace behind you, as if you were to disappear from the earth this moment. Come! I would not have you go back to exchange one word with any old companion, or take one look at any old haunt, or breathe the very air which is pestilence and death to you. Quit them all, while there is time and opportunity!”

text: He ran quickly across the fields and along the narrow lanes that at times cut between them, now nearly hidden by the tall corn on either side, now coming out into an open field where the mowers and haymakers were hard at work. He never paused, except now and then for a few moments to catch his breath, until at last he reached the little market square of the market town, hot, breathless, and covered in dust.
source_text: Swiftly he ran across the fields, and down the little lanes which sometimes divided them: now almost hidden by the high corn on either side, and now emerging on an open field, where the mowers and haymakers were busy at their work: nor did he stop once, save now and then, for a few seconds, to recover breath, until he came, in a great heat, and covered with dust, on the little market-place of the market-town.

text: It was nine o’clock at night when the man, thoroughly spent, and the dog, limping and sore from exercise to which it was not used, turned down the hill by the church of the quiet village, and, trudging along the narrow street, made their way into a little public-house whose meagre light had led them there. A fire was burning in the tap-room, and a few country labourers were drinking before it.
source_text: It was nine o’clock at night, when the man, quite tired out, and the dog, limping and lame from the unaccustomed exercise, turned down the hill by the church of the quiet village, and plodding along the little street, crept into a small public-house, whose scanty light had guided them to the spot. There was a fire in the tap-room, and some country-labourers were drinking before it.

text: When he awoke, the table was spread with tea things, and Sikes was cramming sundry articles into the pockets of his great-coat, which hung over the back of a chair. Nancy was busily occupied in getting breakfast ready. It was not yet daylight; for the candle still burned, and all was dark without. A hard rain, too, was striking the window-panes; and the sky looked black and overcast.
source_text: When he awoke, the table was covered with tea-things, and Sikes was thrusting various articles into the pockets of his great-coat, which hung over the back of a chair. Nancy was busily engaged in preparing breakfast. It was not yet daylight; for the candle was still burning, and it was quite dark outside. A sharp rain, too, was beating against the window-panes; and the sky looked black and cloudy.

text: “Madam,” the girl exclaimed, dropping to her knees, “dear, gentle, angelic madam, you are the first who ever showered such words upon me; and had I heard them years ago, they might have turned me aside from a life of guilt and wretchedness. But now it is too late—too late indeed!”
source_text: “Lady,” cried the girl, sinking on her knees, “dear, sweet, angel lady, you _are_ the first that ever blessed me with such words as these, and if I had heard them years ago, they might have turned me from a life of sin and sorrow; but it is too late, it is too late!”

text: “Not to urge you to change your mind,” continued the young man, “but only to hear you repeat it, if you would. I was to lay whatever rank or fortune I might have at your feet, and if you still held to your former resolution, I pledged myself, by neither word nor action, to try to alter it.”
source_text: “Not to press you to alter your determination,” pursued the young man, “but to hear you repeat it, if you would. I was to lay whatever of station or fortune I might possess at your feet, and if you still adhered to your former determination, I pledged myself, by no word or act, to seek to change it.”

text: They crossed the lower chamber with slow and wary steps; for Valen started at each passing shade, while Mr. Pruitt, carrying his lantern a foot above the floor, moved not only with singular caution, but with a wonderfully light tread for so stout a gentleman, glancing anxiously this way and that for concealed trap-doors. The gate by which they had come in was silently unbarred and drawn open by Valen; and after a grave nod to their secretive companion, the wedded pair passed forth into the dripping blackness beyond.
source_text: They traversed the lower room, slowly, and with caution; for Monks started at every shadow; and Mr. Bumble, holding his lantern a foot above the ground, walked not only with remarkable care, but with a marvellously light step for a gentleman of his figure: looking nervously about him for hidden trap-doors. The gate at which they had entered, was softly unfastened and opened by Monks; merely exchanging a nod with their mysterious acquaintance, the married couple emerged into the wet and darkness outside.

text: “Bear this in mind! And do not make me suffer more for your sake at this moment. If I could aid you, I would; but I have no power to do so. They do not intend to hurt you; whatever they force you to do will be no fault of yours. Hush! Every word you speak strikes me like a blow. Give me your hand. Make haste! Your hand!”
source_text: “Remember this! And don’t let me suffer more for you, just now. If I could help you, I would; but I have not the power. They don’t mean to harm you; whatever they make you do, is no fault of yours. Hush! Every word from you is a blow for me. Give me your hand. Make haste! Your hand!”

text: Spiders had spun their webs in the corners where the walls met the ceilings; and now and then, when Oliver crept quietly into a room, the mice would dart across the floor and hurry back, frightened, to their holes. Apart from these, there was neither sight nor sound of any living creature; and often, when darkness came and he was wearied of wandering from room to room, he would huddle in the corner of the passage by the street door, so as to be as close to living people as he could, and would stay there, listening and counting the hours until the Jew or the boys came back.
source_text: Spiders had built their webs in the angles of the walls and ceilings; and sometimes, when Oliver walked softly into a room, the mice would scamper across the floor, and run back terrified to their holes. With these exceptions, there was neither sight nor sound of any living thing; and often, when it grew dark, and he was tired of wandering from room to room, he would crouch in the corner of the passage by the street-door, to be as near living people as he could; and would remain there, listening and counting the hours, until the Jew or the boys returned.

text: “It!” the woman replied, covering the other’s mouth with her hand. “That was the only thing she had. She wanted clothes to keep her warm and food to eat, but she had kept it hidden safely, against her breast. It was gold, I tell you—good gold, enough to have saved her life!”
source_text: “_It_!” replied the woman, laying her hand over the other’s mouth. “The only thing she had. She wanted clothes to keep her warm, and food to eat; but she had kept it safe, and had it in her bosom. It was gold, I tell you! Rich gold, that might have saved her life!”

text: Thus three months slipped by—three months which, in the life of the happiest and most favoured of mortals, might have been one unbroken season of bliss, and which, for Oliver, were indeed genuine happiness. With the purest and kindliest generosity on the one side, and the truest, warmest, most heartfelt gratitude on the other, it is no marvel that, by the end of so brief a period, Oliver Twist had grown wholly at home with the old lady and her niece, and that the ardent affection of his young and sensitive heart was returned by the pride they took in him, and by their own attachment to his person.
source_text: So three months glided away; three months which, in the life of the most blessed and favoured of mortals, might have been unmingled happiness, and which, in Oliver’s were true felicity. With the purest and most amiable generosity on one side; and the truest, warmest, soul-felt gratitude on the other; it is no wonder that, by the end of that short time, Oliver Twist had become completely domesticated with the old lady and her niece, and that the fervent attachment of his young and sensitive heart, was repaid by their pride in, and attachment to, himself.

text: He stood there, amid all that blaze of living light, one hand resting on the wooden rail before him, the other lifted to his ear, his head thrust forward in order to catch more clearly every word that fell from the presiding judge, who was delivering his charge to the jury. At times he turned his eyes sharply upon them, to watch the effect of the slightest particle of evidence in his favour; and when the arguments against him were set forth with dreadful plainness, he looked toward his counsel in silent entreaty, as though even then he might say something on his behalf. Beyond these signs of anxiety, he did not stir. He had scarcely moved since the trial began; and now that the judge had ceased speaking, he still remained in the same strained posture of close attention, his gaze fixed on him, as though he were still listening.
source_text: He stood there, in all this glare of living light, with one hand resting on the wooden slab before him, the other held to his ear, and his head thrust forward to enable him to catch with greater distinctness every word that fell from the presiding judge, who was delivering his charge to the jury. At times, he turned his eyes sharply upon them to observe the effect of the slightest featherweight in his favour; and when the points against him were stated with terrible distinctness, looked towards his counsel, in mute appeal that he would, even then, urge something in his behalf. Beyond these manifestations of anxiety, he stirred not hand or foot. He had scarcely moved since the trial began; and now that the judge ceased to speak, he still remained in the same strained attitude of close attention, with his gaze bent on him, as though he listened still.

text: “No. The people were poor and soon began to grow tired—at least the man did—of their fine humanity; so she left the child with them, giving them a small gift of money that would not last long, and promising more, which she never intended to send. Yet she did not depend entirely on their poverty and displeasure for the child’s misery. She told the story of the sister’s disgrace, changing it as suited her purpose; warned them to keep close watch on the child, since she came of bad blood; and told them she was illegitimate and bound to turn out badly sooner or later. The circumstances seemed to support all this; the people believed it; and there the child went on living a life miserable enough even to satisfy us, until a widow lady, living then at Chester, happened to see the girl, took pity on her, and brought her home. There must have been some cursed spell against us, I think; for despite all our efforts she stayed there and was happy.
