I lost sight of her two or three years ago, and did not see her again until a few months back.”
source_text: “No. The people were poor and began to sicken—at least the man did—of their fine humanity; so she left it with them, giving them a small present of money which would not last long, and promised more, which she never meant to send. She didn’t quite rely, however, on their discontent and poverty for the child’s unhappiness, but told the history of the sister’s shame, with such alterations as suited her; bade them take good heed of the child, for she came of bad blood; and told them she was illegitimate, and sure to go wrong at one time or other. The circumstances countenanced all this; the people believed it; and there the child dragged on an existence, miserable enough even to satisfy us, until a widow lady, residing, then, at Chester, saw the girl by chance, pitied her, and took her home. There was some cursed spell, I think, against us; for in spite of all our efforts she remained there and was happy. I lost sight of her, two or three years ago, and saw her no more until a few months back.”

text: “Why, she couldn’t very well slip out of the house unless he knew where she meant to go,” said Jonas; “and so the first time she went to visit the lady, she—ha! ha! ha! it fairly set me off laughing when she told it, it did—she handed him a dose of laudanum.”
source_text: “Why, that she couldn’t very easily get out of doors unless he knew where she was going to,” said Noah; “and so the first time she went to see the lady, she—ha! ha! ha! it made me laugh when she said it, that it did—she gave him a drink of laudanum.”

text: “Stay one moment longer,” Rose said, as the girl turned hastily toward the door. “Consider again your own situation, and the chance you have of escaping from it. You have a claim upon me—not only because you came of your own will to bring me this news, but because you are a woman almost beyond redemption, lost as you are. Will you go back to this band of robbers, and to that man, when a single word might save you? What spell is it that draws you back, and binds you to wickedness and misery? Oh, is there no string in your heart that I can touch? Is there nothing left to which I may appeal against this dreadful madness?”
source_text: “Stay another moment,” interposed Rose, as the girl moved hurriedly towards the door. “Think once again on your own condition, and the opportunity you have of escaping from it. You have a claim on me: not only as the voluntary bearer of this intelligence, but as a woman lost almost beyond redemption. Will you return to this gang of robbers, and to this man, when a word can save you? What fascination is it that can take you back, and make you cling to wickedness and misery? Oh! is there no chord in your heart that I can touch! Is there nothing left, to which I can appeal against this terrible infatuation!”

text: In compliance with this request, Nancy quickly spread the cloth; and after being absent for a few minutes, she returned with a pot of porter and a dish of sheep’s heads, which furnished Mr. Sikes with several merry jokes, turned on the odd coincidence that “jemmies” was both a familiar can name and also the name of a clever tool much employed in his trade. Indeed, the honest gentleman, perhaps enlivened by the immediate prospect of active business, was in high spirits and excellent humour; so much so, it may be noted, that he merrily drained the beer at a single draught, and, by a rough estimate, did not launch more than four-score oaths during the whole course of the meal.
source_text: In pursuance of this request, Nancy quickly laid the cloth; disappearing for a few minutes, she presently returned with a pot of porter and a dish of sheep’s heads: which gave occasion to several pleasant witticisms on the part of Mr. Sikes, founded upon the singular coincidence of “jemmies” being a can name, common to them, and also to an ingenious implement much used in his profession. Indeed, the worthy gentleman, stimulated perhaps by the immediate prospect of being on active service, was in great spirits and good humour; in proof whereof, it may be here remarked, that he humourously drank all the beer at a draught, and did not utter, on a rough calculation, more than four-score oaths during the whole progress of the meal.

text: Philip, speedily recovering from the fainting fit into which Mr. Harcourt’s sudden exclamation had thrown him, found the matter of the portrait carefully shunned, both by the old man and by Mrs. Garland, in the talk that followed; which, indeed, had no bearing on Philip’s past or future, but was kept to such subjects as might divert him without stirring his feelings. He was still too feeble to rise for breakfast; but when, on the next day, he came down into the steward’s room, his first impulse was to cast a hungry glance toward the wall, hoping once more to see the fair lady’s face. His hope, however, was vain; for the picture had been taken away.
source_text: Oliver soon recovering from the fainting-fit into which Mr. Brownlow’s abrupt exclamation had thrown him, the subject of the picture was carefully avoided, both by the old gentleman and Mrs. Bedwin, in the conversation that ensued: which indeed bore no reference to Oliver’s history or prospects, but was confined to such topics as might amuse without exciting him. He was still too weak to get up to breakfast; but, when he came down into the housekeeper’s room next day, his first act was to cast an eager glance at the wall, in the hope of again looking on the face of the beautiful lady. His expectations were disappointed, however, for the picture had been removed.

text: Dogs are not often disposed to avenge the wrongs done them by their masters; yet Mr. Vane’s hound, inheriting, as it were, the ill-humour of his keeper, and perhaps at that instant oppressed by a very lively consciousness of being ill-used, wasted no time in resentment, but at once clamped his teeth upon one of the worn half-boots. After administering to it a vigorous shake, he withdrew beneath a settle, still growling, and so narrowly escaped the pewter measure which Mr. Vane flung at his head.
source_text: Dogs are not generally apt to revenge injuries inflicted upon them by their masters; but Mr. Sikes’s dog, having faults of temper in common with his owner, and labouring, perhaps, at this moment, under a powerful sense of injury, made no more ado but at once fixed his teeth in one of the half-boots. Having given in a hearty shake, he retired, growling, under a form; just escaping the pewter measure which Mr. Sikes levelled at his head.

text: The girl moved nearer to the table, and, casting at Monks a look of seeming careless lightness, quickly averted her eyes; but as he turned toward Fagin, she glanced at him again, with such sharp, searching, purposeful intent that, had there been anyone present to witness the change, he could scarcely have believed both looks came from the same person.
source_text: The girl drew closer to the table, and glancing at Monks with an air of careless levity, withdrew her eyes; but as he turned towards Fagin, she stole another look; so keen and searching, and full of purpose, that if there had been any bystander to observe the change, he could hardly have believed the two looks to have proceeded from the same person.

text: “Ah!” said Mr. Weller, when the lady’s eyes had once more descended from their heavenward survey; “the only course left to us, so far as I can make out, is to keep him in the cellar for a day or two, until he has been somewhat reduced by hunger; and then to bring him forth again, and maintain him upon gruel for the whole term of his indenture. He comes of a very poor stock. Highly nervous constitutions, Mrs. Dobbins! Both the nurse and the surgeon declared that the woman who was his mother contrived to reach this place through trials and anguish that would have laid any respectable female low, weeks beforehand.”
source_text: “Ah!” said Mr. Bumble, when the lady brought her eyes down to earth again; “the only thing that can be done now, that I know of, is to leave him in the cellar for a day or so, till he’s a little starved down; and then to take him out, and keep him on gruel all through the apprenticeship. He comes of a bad family. Excitable natures, Mrs. Sowerberry! Both the nurse and doctor said, that that mother of his made her way here, against difficulties and pain that would have killed any well-disposed woman, weeks before.”

text: At length, after more than an hour had passed, Mr. Bumble, Sowerberry, and the clerk were seen hurrying toward the grave. Presently the clergyman appeared as well, drawing on his surplice as he came along. Mr. Bumble thereupon beat a boy or two, to preserve appearances; and the reverend gentleman, having read as much of the burial service as could be squeezed into four minutes, handed his surplice to the clerk and went away again.
source_text: At length, after a lapse of something more than an hour, Mr. Bumble, and Sowerberry, and the clerk, were seen running towards the grave. Immediately afterwards, the clergyman appeared: putting on his surplice as he came along. Mr. Bumble then thrashed a boy or two, to keep up appearances; and the reverend gentleman, having read as much of the burial service as could be compressed into four minutes, gave his surplice to the clerk, and walked away again.

text: Mr. Prittle made a bow. When he was bidden to take a seat, he laid his hat upon the floor and, drawing a chair toward him, signed to Vale to do likewise. That gentleman, who seemed not quite so well versed in polished company, or not quite so much at his ease in it—one of the two—settled himself after a series of uneasy movements in his legs and, with considerable confusion, thrust the head of his stick into his mouth.
source_text: Mr. Blathers made a bow. Being desired to sit down, he put his hat on the floor, and taking a chair, motioned to Duff to do the same. The latter gentleman, who did not appear quite so much accustomed to good society, or quite so much at his ease in it—one of the two—seated himself, after undergoing several muscular affections of the limbs, and the head of his stick into his mouth, with some embarrassment.

text: Inside there was no one but a wretched barefoot criminal, who had been arrested for playing the flute and, the offence against society having been fully proved, had been most properly committed by Mr. Fang to the House of Correction for a month; with the suitable and diverting observation that, as he had breath enough to spare, it would be better employed on the treadmill than in a musical instrument. He made no reply, being absorbed in mourning the loss of his flute, which had been seized for the county’s use; so Nancy went on to the next cell and knocked there.
source_text: There was nobody inside but a miserable shoeless criminal, who had been taken up for playing the flute, and who, the offence against society having been clearly proved, had been very properly committed by Mr. Fang to the House of Correction for one month; with the appropriate and amusing remark that since he had so much breath to spare, it would be more wholesomely expended on the treadmill than in a musical instrument. He made no answer: being occupied mentally bewailing the loss of the flute, which had been confiscated for the use of the county: so Nancy passed on to the next cell, and knocked there.

text: No one disputed the prophetic gentleman’s opinion. A spirited discussion followed. Oliver was immediately ordered into confinement, and by the next morning a notice had been pasted on the outer gate, promising a reward of five pounds to anyone who would take Oliver Twist off the parish’s hands. In other words, five pounds and Oliver Twist were offered to any man or woman in want of an apprentice for any trade, business, or calling.
source_text: Nobody controverted the prophetic gentleman’s opinion. An animated discussion took place. Oliver was ordered into instant confinement; and a bill was next morning pasted on the outside of the gate, offering a reward of five pounds to anybody who would take Oliver Twist off the hands of the parish. In other words, five pounds and Oliver Twist were offered to any man or woman who wanted an apprentice to any trade, business, or calling.

text: “Whence can he have come!” cried Toby. “No doubt he has visited the other dens, and, finding them occupied by strangers, has come hither instead, where he has been many a time before. But whence did he come at the first, and how is it that he is here alone, without the rest?”
source_text: “Where can he have come from!” exclaimed Toby. “He’s been to the other kens of course, and finding them filled with strangers come on here, where he’s been many a time and often. But where can he have come from first, and how comes he here alone without the other!”

text: “You are to say,” said Mr. Alderwood, fixing a steady look upon Mr. Harrow; “you are to say that you have returned those volumes, and that you have come to settle the four pounds ten I owe him. This is a five-pound note, so you will be good enough to bring me back ten shillings in change.”
source_text: “You are to say,” said Mr. Brownlow, glancing steadily at Grimwig; “you are to say that you have brought those books back; and that you have come to pay the four pound ten I owe him. This is a five-pound note, so you will have to bring me back, ten shillings change.”

text: “It is because I was your father’s earliest friend, young man,” said Mr. Brownlow; “it is because the hopes and desires of my youth were bound up with him, and with that fair creature of his blood and kin who went back to God while still young, and left me here a solitary, lonely man; it is because, when he was yet a boy, he knelt with me beside his only sister’s death-bed on the very morning that would—but Heaven decreed otherwise—have made her my young wife; it is because my scorched heart was thenceforth knit to him through all his trials and his errors, until he died; it is because old memories and associations are crowded in my heart, and even the sight of you awakens old thoughts of him; it is because of all these things that I am moved to deal gently with you now—yes, Edward Leeford, even now—and to blush for the unworthiness of one who bears his name.”
source_text: “It is because I was your father’s oldest friend, young man,” returned Mr. Brownlow; “it is because the hopes and wishes of young and happy years were bound up with him, and that fair creature of his blood and kindred who rejoined her God in youth, and left me here a solitary, lonely man: it is because he knelt with me beside his only sisters’s death-bed when he was yet a boy, on the morning that would—but Heaven willed otherwise—have made her my young wife; it is because my seared heart clung to him, from that time forth, through all his trials and errors, till he died; it is because old recollections and associations filled my heart, and even the sight of you brings with it old thoughts of him; it is because of all these things that I am moved to treat you gently now—yes, Edward Leeford, even now—and blush for your unworthiness who bear the name.”

text: Oliver got up the next morning in better spirits and went to his usual tasks with more hope and pleasure than he had felt for many days. The birds were once again put out in their old places to sing, and the sweetest wildflowers that could be found were gathered again to delight Rose with their beauty. The sadness that had seemed to the anxious boy’s troubled eyes to rest, for the past few days, over every object, lovely as they were, was lifted as if by magic. The dew seemed to glitter more brightly on the green leaves; the air to murmur through them with gentler music; and even the sky itself to appear bluer and clearer. Such is the power our own thoughts have, even over the way external things seem to us. Men who look upon nature and their fellow beings and declare that everything is dark and dismal are, in a sense, right; but the gloomy colors come from the jaundiced look of their own eyes and hearts. The true hues are finer, and require a clearer sight.
source_text: Oliver rose next morning, in better heart, and went about his usual occupations, with more hope and pleasure than he had known for many days. The birds were once more hung out, to sing, in their old places; and the sweetest wild flowers that could be found, were once more gathered to gladden Rose with their beauty. The melancholy which had seemed to the sad eyes of the anxious boy to hang, for days past, over every object, beautiful as all were, was dispelled by magic. The dew seemed to sparkle more brightly on the green leaves; the air to rustle among them with a sweeter music; and the sky itself to look more blue and bright. Such is the influence which the condition of our own thoughts, exercise, even over the appearance of external objects. Men who look on nature, and their fellow-men, and cry that all is dark and gloomy, are in the right; but the sombre colours are reflections from their own jaundiced eyes and hearts. The real hues are delicate, and need a clearer vision.

text: “You’ll make your fortune, Mr. Sowerberry,” said the beadle, dipping his thumb and forefinger into the snuff-box the undertaker held out to him, which happened to be a clever little model of a patented coffin. “I say you’ll make your fortune, Mr. Sowerberry,” Mr. Bumble repeated, giving the undertaker a friendly tap on the shoulder with his cane.
source_text: “You’ll make your fortune, Mr. Sowerberry,” said the beadle, as he thrust his thumb and forefinger into the proffered snuff-box of the undertaker: which was an ingenious little model of a patent coffin. “I say you’ll make your fortune, Mr. Sowerberry,” repeated Mr. Bumble, tapping the undertaker on the shoulder, in a friendly manner, with his cane.

text: Nor was Mr. Bumble’s melancholy the only thing likely to stir a pleasing sadness in the breast of any observer. There were other signs, too, and these were closely tied to his own person, which made it clear that a great change had come over the state of his affairs. The laced coat and the cocked hat—where had they gone? He still had on knee-breeches and dark cotton stockings on his legs; but they were not _the_ breeches. The coat was cut wide at the skirts, and in that way resembled _the_ coat, but, oh, how unlike it was! The grand cocked hat had given way to a plain round one. Mr. Bumble was no longer a beadle.
source_text: Nor was Mr. Bumble’s gloom the only thing calculated to awaken a pleasing melancholy in the bosom of a spectator. There were not wanting other appearances, and those closely connected with his own person, which announced that a great change had taken place in the position of his affairs. The laced coat, and the cocked hat; where were they? He still wore knee-breeches, and dark cotton stockings on his nether limbs; but they were not _the_ breeches. The coat was wide-skirted; and in that respect like _the_ coat, but, oh how different! The mighty cocked hat was replaced by a modest round one. Mr. Bumble was no longer a beadle.

text: It was near two hours before the dawn, at that season of the year when night may, in truth, be called dead; when the streets lie hushed and empty, when even noises seem themselves to sleep, and revelry and debauchery have reeled away to their dreams. It was at this still and breathless hour that Levi sat keeping watch in his old den, his face so drawn and ghastly, his eyes so inflamed and bloodshot, that he seemed less a living man than some loathsome apparition, fresh from the tomb and harried by an evil spirit.
source_text: It was nearly two hours before day-break; that time which in the autumn of the year, may be truly called the dead of night; when the streets are silent and deserted; when even sounds appear to slumber, and profligacy and riot have staggered home to dream; it was at this still and silent hour, that Fagin sat watching in his old lair, with face so distorted and pale, and eyes so red and blood-shot, that he looked less like a man, than like some hideous phantom, moist from the grave, and worried by an evil spirit.

text: Having said this, the good old lady hastened back into the parlour, and, sinking onto a sofa, broke into tears. The girl, who was not quite so impressionable, had meanwhile run upstairs, and now came back with a request that Mr. Bumble follow her at once, which he did.
source_text: Having said this, the worthy old lady hurried back into the parlour again; and seating herself on a sofa, burst into tears. The girl, who was not quite so susceptible, had run upstairs meanwhile; and now returned with a request that Mr. Bumble would follow her immediately: which he did.

text: So completely overcome by the idea of Mr. Chitling as the prey of Cupid, Master Bates flung himself so abruptly backward in his chair that he lost his balance and toppled headlong to the floor; where, the mishap in no degree diminishing his delight, he stretched at full length until his laughter had spent itself, after which he regained his seat and broke into a fresh fit of mirth.
source_text: Thoroughly overpowered with the notion of Mr. Chitling being the victim of the tender passion, Master Bates threw himself back in his chair with such violence, that he lost his balance, and pitched over upon the floor; where (the accident abating nothing of his merriment) he lay at full length until his laugh was over, when he resumed his former position, and began another laugh.

text: The dog, however. If anything were omitted in describing him, it would not be forgotten that the dog was missing, and had likely gone with him. This might cause his arrest as he made his way through the streets. He resolved to drown him, and went on, casting about for a pond, while he picked up a heavy stone and tied it to his handkerchief as he walked.
source_text: The dog, though. If any description of him were out, it would not be forgotten that the dog was missing, and had probably gone with him. This might lead to his apprehension as he passed along the streets. He resolved to drown him, and walked on, looking about for a pond: picking up a heavy stone and tying it to his handkerchief as he went.

text: Mr. Blemley had counted the teaspoons over again, had weighed the sugar-tongs afresh, examined the milk-jug with a keener eye, and satisfied himself to a hair’s breadth as to the exact state of the furniture, down even to the horsehair bottoms of the chairs; and had gone through each of these proceedings full six times over, before it began to dawn upon him that Mrs. Harrow ought now to be back. Thought breeds thought; and, as no sound announced Mrs. Harrow’s return, it occurred to Mr. Blemley that it would be an innocent and virtuous manner of filling up the interval, if he should further soothe his curiosity by casting a hasty look into the contents of Mrs. Harrow’s chest of drawers.
source_text: Mr. Bumble had re-counted the teaspoons, re-weighed the sugar-tongs, made a closer inspection of the milk-pot, and ascertained to a nicety the exact condition of the furniture, down to the very horse-hair seats of the chairs; and had repeated each process full half a dozen times; before he began to think that it was time for Mrs. Corney to return. Thinking begets thinking; as there were no sounds of Mrs. Corney’s approach, it occured to Mr. Bumble that it would be an innocent and virtuous way of spending the time, if he were further to allay his curiousity by a cursory glance at the interior of Mrs. Corney’s chest of drawers.

text: When breakfast had been cleared away, the merry old gentleman and the two boys engaged in a very strange and uncommon game, played in this manner. The merry old gentleman put a snuff-box into one pocket of his trousers, a note-case into the other, and a watch into the pocket of his waistcoat, with a guard-chain about his neck, while he stuck a sham diamond pin into his shirt. Then he buttoned his coat closely round him, and, placing his spectacle-case and handkerchief in his pockets, trotted to and fro across the room with a stick, imitating the way old gentlemen stroll about the streets at any hour of the day. Now and then he halted at the fireplace, and now and then at the door, pretending to peer with all his might into the shop-windows. At such moments he would keep glancing sharply about him, as though afraid of thieves, and would slap each of his pockets in turn to make sure that nothing was missing, all in so comical and natural a fashion that Oliver laughed until the tears came into his eyes. Meanwhile, the two boys kept close behind him, slipping out of his sight so quickly whenever he turned round that it was impossible to trace their movements. At last the Dodger trod on his toes, or perhaps accidentally ran against his boot, while Charley Bates stumbled up against him from behind; and in that single instant they removed from him, with astonishing quickness, the snuff-box, note-case, watch-guard, chain, shirt-pin, pocket-handkerchief, and even the spectacle-case. If the old gentleman felt a hand in any of his pockets, he called out which one it was; and then the game began again.
source_text: When the breakfast was cleared away; the merry old gentleman and the two boys played at a very curious and uncommon game, which was performed in this way. The merry old gentleman, placing a snuff-box in one pocket of his trousers, a note-case in the other, and a watch in his waistcoat pocket, with a guard-chain round his neck, and sticking a mock diamond pin in his shirt: buttoned his coat tight round him, and putting his spectacle-case and handkerchief in his pockets, trotted up and down the room with a stick, in imitation of the manner in which old gentlemen walk about the streets any hour in the day. Sometimes he stopped at the fire-place, and sometimes at the door, making believe that he was staring with all his might into shop-windows. At such times, he would look constantly round him, for fear of thieves, and would keep slapping all his pockets in turn, to see that he hadn’t lost anything, in such a very funny and natural manner, that Oliver laughed till the tears ran down his face. All this time, the two boys followed him closely about: getting out of his sight, so nimbly, every time he turned round, that it was impossible to follow their motions. At last, the Dodger trod upon his toes, or ran upon his boot accidently, while Charley Bates stumbled up against him behind; and in that one moment they took from him, with the most extraordinary rapidity, snuff-box, note-case, watch-guard, chain, shirt-pin, pocket-handkerchief, even the spectacle-case. If the old gentleman felt a hand in any one of his pockets, he cried out where it was; and then the game began all over again.

text: He encountered no further troubles on the road except those arising from the troublesome conduct of the two paupers, who went on shivering and grumbling about the cold in such a way, Mr. Bumble declared, that it made his teeth chatter in his head and left him quite ill at ease, though he was wearing a greatcoat.
source_text: He experienced no other crosses on the way, than those which originated in the perverse behaviour of the two paupers, who persisted in shivering, and complaining of the cold, in a manner which, Mr. Bumble declared, caused his teeth to chatter in his head, and made him feel quite uncomfortable; although he had a great-coat on.

text: “When they had been parted for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, having surrendered herself entirely to continental frivolities, had quite forgotten the young husband who was a full ten years her junior, and who, with his prospects ruined, remained at home in misery, he fell in with new acquaintances. This much, at any rate, you already know.”
source_text: “When they had been separated for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, wholly given up to continental frivolities, had utterly forgotten the young husband ten good years her junior, who, with prospects blighted, lingered on at home, he fell among new friends. This circumstance, at least, you know already.”

text: The two gentlemen watched Oliver’s serious face as he spoke, and, glancing from him to one another, appeared to be convinced of the truth of his words. Yet in no direction was there any sign of men fleeing in haste. The grass was long, but nowhere was it beaten down except where their own feet had crushed it. The sides and edges of the ditches were of wet clay, but in not a single place could they discover the print of a shoe, or the faintest trace to show that any feet had pressed the ground for many hours before.
source_text: The two gentlemen watched Oliver’s earnest face, as he spoke, and looking from him to each other, seemed to feel satisfied of the accuracy of what he said. Still, in no direction were there any appearances of the trampling of men in hurried flight. The grass was long; but it was trodden down nowhere, save where their own feet had crushed it. The sides and brinks of the ditches were of damp clay; but in no one place could they discern the print of men’s shoes, or the slightest mark which would indicate that any feet had pressed the ground for hours before.

text: Bill Sikes did no more than point to the empty measure. The Jew, comprehending the sign at once, withdrew to replenish it, after first exchanging a singular glance with Fagin, who lifted his eyes for an instant, as though he had been awaiting it, and answered with a shake of the head so slight that, to any attentive bystander, it would have been scarcely visible. Sikes perceived nothing of it, being then bent over to fasten the bootlace the dog had broken. Had he noticed that brief passing of signals, he might perhaps have judged that it promised him no good.
source_text: Bill Sikes merely pointed to the empty measure. The Jew, perfectly understanding the hint, retired to fill it: previously exchanging a remarkable look with Fagin, who raised his eyes for an instant, as if in expectation of it, and shook his head in reply; so slightly that the action would have been almost imperceptible to an observant third person. It was lost upon Sikes, who was stooping at the moment to tie the boot-lace which the dog had torn. Possibly, if he had observed the brief interchange of signals, he might have thought that it boded no good to him.

text: The tumult sank away, and he was asked whether he had anything to offer why sentence of death should not be pronounced against him. He had fallen back into his posture of strained attention, and fixed his eyes steadily on the one who addressed him while the question was put; but it had to be repeated twice before he appeared to take it in, and then he only murmured that he was an old man—an old man—and, sinking into a whisper, fell silent once more.
source_text: The noise subsided, and he was asked if he had anything to say why sentence of death should not be passed upon him. He had resumed his listening attitude, and looked intently at his questioner while the demand was made; but it was twice repeated before he seemed to hear it, and then he only muttered that he was an old man—an old man—and so, dropping into a whisper, was silent again.

text: “Now listen to me,” said the stranger, after shutting the door and window. “I came here today to look for you, and by one of those chances the devil sometimes sends his friends, you walked into the very room I was in just as you were most on my mind. I want some information from you. I’m not asking you to give it for nothing, slight as it may be. Take that first.”
source_text: “Now listen to me,” said the stranger, after closing the door and window. “I came down to this place, today, to find you out; and, by one of those chances which the devil throws in the way of his friends sometimes, you walked into the very room I was sitting in, while you were uppermost in my mind. I want some information from you. I don’t ask you to give it for nothing, slight as it is. Put up that, to begin with.”

text: Who could put into words the joy and comfort, the calm and gentle peace, that the sickly boy found in the mild air and amid the green hills and lush woods of an inland village? Who can say how sights of quiet and rest settle into the minds of those worn down by suffering in cramped, noisy places, and carry their own freshness deep into hearts grown stale with weariness? Men who have spent their lives in crowded, confined streets, in ceaseless labor, and who never once longed for change; men to whom habit had truly become second nature, and who had almost grown fond of every brick and stone marking the narrow limits of their daily rounds; even they, when death laid its hand upon them, have been known at last to crave one brief sight of Nature’s face. Removed far from the scenes of their old sorrows and pleasures, they seemed all at once to pass into another kind of existence. Day by day, as they dragged themselves out to some green, sunlit place, the sight of sky, hill and field, and shining water stirred up such memories within them that a foretaste of heaven itself eased their swift decline, and they went down into their graves as quietly as the sun, whose setting they had watched from the window of their lonely room only a few hours before, vanished from their dim, failing sight. The memories awakened by peaceful country scenes belong not to this world, nor to its concerns and ambitions. Their mild influence can teach us how to weave new garlands for the graves of those we loved; it can cleanse our thoughts and drive before it old hostility and hatred. Yet beneath all this there remains, in the least contemplative mind, a faint and half-formed sense of having known such feelings long ago, in some far-off time, which brings with it grave thoughts of times still farther ahead and bows down pride and worldliness beneath its weight.
source_text: Who can describe the pleasure and delight, the peace of mind and soft tranquillity, the sickly boy felt in the balmy air, and among the green hills and rich woods, of an inland village! Who can tell how scenes of peace and quietude sink into the minds of pain-worn dwellers in close and noisy places, and carry their own freshness, deep into their jaded hearts! Men who have lived in crowded, pent-up streets, through lives of toil, and who have never wished for change; men, to whom custom has indeed been second nature, and who have come almost to love each brick and stone that formed the narrow boundaries of their daily walks; even they, with the hand of death upon them, have been known to yearn at last for one short glimpse of Nature’s face; and, carried far from the scenes of their old pains and pleasures, have seemed to pass at once into a new state of being. Crawling forth, from day to day, to some green sunny spot, they have had such memories wakened up within them by the sight of the sky, and hill and plain, and glistening water, that a foretaste of heaven itself has soothed their quick decline, and they have sunk into their tombs, as peacefully as the sun whose setting they watched from their lonely chamber window but a few hours before, faded from their dim and feeble sight! The memories which peaceful country scenes call up, are not of this world, nor of its thoughts and hopes. Their gentle influence may teach us how to weave fresh garlands for the graves of those we loved: may purify our thoughts, and bear down before it old enmity and hatred; but beneath all this, there lingers, in the least reflective mind, a vague and half-formed consciousness of having held such feelings long before, in some remote and distant time, which calls up solemn thoughts of distant times to come, and bends down pride and worldliness beneath it.

text: “What are you at now? Abusing the lads, you grasping, greedy, in-sa-ti-a-ble old receiver?” said the fellow, as he settled himself with studied ease. “It’s a wonder they don’t put an end to you! I know I should, if I were in their place. Had I been your ’prentice, I’d have done it years ago, and—no, I couldn’t have disposed of you afterwards, for you’re good for nothing but to be kept, like some monstrous curiosity, in a glass jar; and I take it there isn’t a jar made big enough for your ugliness.”
source_text: “What are you up to? Ill-treating the boys, you covetous, avaricious, in-sa-ti-a-ble old fence?” said the man, seating himself deliberately. “I wonder they don’t murder you! I would if I was them. If I’d been your ’prentice, I’d have done it long ago, and—no, I couldn’t have sold you afterwards, for you’re fit for nothing but keeping as a curiousity of ugliness in a glass bottle, and I suppose they don’t blow glass bottles large enough.”

text: Julian turned back toward home, musing on the many acts of goodness he had received from the young woman, and longing that the hour might return when he could never tire of proving how grateful and how warmly attached he was. He had no ground for accusing himself of negligence, or of a want of feeling, for he had been faithful in her service; and yet a hundred small occasions came crowding upon him, in which he imagined he might have shown a greater ardour, a deeper concern, and wished heartily that he had done so. We should be wary in our dealings with those around us, when every death brings to some little circle of mourners thoughts of so much left undone, and so little performed—of so many things forgotten, and so many more that might still have been mended! There is no sorrow so piercing as that which cannot be helped; if we would be saved its anguish, let us bear this in mind, while there is yet time.
source_text: Oliver turned homeward, thinking on the many kindnesses he had received from the young lady, and wishing that the time could come again, that he might never cease showing her how grateful and attached he was. He had no cause for self-reproach on the score of neglect, or want of thought, for he had been devoted to her service; and yet a hundred little occasions rose up before him, on which he fancied he might have been more zealous, and more earnest, and wished he had been. We need be careful how we deal with those about us, when every death carries to some small circle of survivors, thoughts of so much omitted, and so little done—of so many things forgotten, and so many more which might have been repaired! There is no remorse so deep as that which is unavailing; if we would be spared its tortures, let us remember this, in time.

text: He was a comic sort of fellow, part peddler and part showman, who wandered the country on foot selling hones, strops, razors, wash balls, harness paste, medicine for dogs and horses, cheap perfumes, cosmetics, and the like, all packed in a case slung across his back. His arrival was the cue for all manner of rustic jokes among the countrymen, and the banter did not let up until he had eaten his supper and opened his box of wares, when he neatly managed to combine trade with entertainment.
source_text: This was an antic fellow, half pedlar and half mountebank, who travelled about the country on foot to vend hones, strops, razors, washballs, harness-paste, medicine for dogs and horses, cheap perfumery, cosmetics, and such-like wares, which he carried in a case slung to his back. His entrance was the signal for various homely jokes with the countrymen, which slackened not until he had made his supper, and opened his box of treasures, when he ingeniously contrived to unite business with amusement.

text: Alas, how few of nature’s faces are left untouched, left simply to delight us with their beauty! The world’s cares, sorrows, and longings alter them, just as they alter the heart; and only when those passions have fallen asleep and lost their power forever do the troubled clouds drift away, leaving the face of heaven clear. It is a common thing for the faces of the dead, even in that fixed and rigid stillness, to soften into the long-forgotten expression of sleeping infancy and settle into the very look of early life. They become so calm, so peaceful, that those who knew them in the happiness of childhood kneel beside the coffin in reverence and seem to behold an angel still on earth.
source_text: Alas! How few of Nature’s faces are left alone to gladden us with their beauty! The cares, and sorrows, and hungerings, of the world, change them as they change hearts; and it is only when those passions sleep, and have lost their hold for ever, that the troubled clouds pass off, and leave Heaven’s surface clear. It is a common thing for the countenances of the dead, even in that fixed and rigid state, to subside into the long-forgotten expression of sleeping infancy, and settle into the very look of early life; so calm, so peaceful, do they grow again, that those who knew them in their happy childhood, kneel by the coffin’s side in awe, and see the Angel even upon earth.

text: “Keep quiet, you varmint! Keep quiet!” said Mr. Sikes, suddenly breaking the silence. Whether his thoughts had been so deep that even the dog’s blinking disturbed them, or whether his feelings had been so stirred by reflection that he needed the relief of kicking an innocent creature to ease them, is a matter open to argument and consideration. Whatever the cause, the result was both a kick and a curse, administered to the dog at the same moment.
source_text: “Keep quiet, you warmint! Keep quiet!” said Mr. Sikes, suddenly breaking silence. Whether his meditations were so intense as to be disturbed by the dog’s winking, or whether his feelings were so wrought upon by his reflections that they required all the relief derivable from kicking an unoffending animal to allay them, is matter for argument and consideration. Whatever was the cause, the effect was a kick and a curse, bestowed upon the dog simultaneously.

text: There is something in an aroused woman, and especially in one who joins to all her other vehement passions the fierce instincts of recklessness and despair, that few men care to provoke. The Jew perceived that it would be useless to feign any further uncertainty as to the reality of Miss Nancy’s anger; and, shrinking back a few paces by instinct, he cast a look at Sikes that was half supplicating, half craven, as though to intimate that he was the proper person to carry on the discourse.
source_text: There is something about a roused woman: especially if she add to all her other strong passions, the fierce impulses of recklessness and despair; which few men like to provoke. The Jew saw that it would be hopeless to affect any further mistake regarding the reality of Miss Nancy’s rage; and, shrinking involuntarily back a few paces, cast a glance, half imploring and half cowardly, at Sikes: as if to hint that he was the fittest person to pursue the dialogue.

text: The old crone tottered along the passages and up the stairs, muttering vague answers to the reproaches of her companion; and, at last, being forced to stop for breath, she put the light into her hand and fell behind, following as she could, while her brisker superior made her way to the room where the sick woman lay.
source_text: The old crone tottered along the passages, and up the stairs, muttering some indistinct answers to the chidings of her companion; being at length compelled to pause for breath, she gave the light into her hand, and remained behind to follow as she might: while the more nimble superior made her way to the room where the sick woman lay.

text: “Not until you let me go—not until you let me go—Never—never!” the girl shrieked. Sikes watched her for a moment, waiting for his chance, and then, with a sudden grip that trapped her arms, hauled her, fighting and twisting against him, into a little room next door. There he settled himself on a bench, and, forcing her into a chair, held her there by main strength. She struggled and pleaded by turns until twelve o’clock struck, and then, tired out and spent, gave over the struggle at last. After warning her, with many oaths, not to make any further attempt to leave that night, Sikes left her to come round as she could and went back to Fagin.
source_text: “Not till you let me go—not till you let me go—Never—never!” screamed the girl. Sikes looked on, for a minute, watching his opportunity, and suddenly pinioning her hands dragged her, struggling and wrestling with him by the way, into a small room adjoining, where he sat himself on a bench, and thrusting her into a chair, held her down by force. She struggled and implored by turns until twelve o’clock had struck, and then, wearied and exhausted, ceased to contest the point any further. With a caution, backed by many oaths, to make no more efforts to go out that night, Sikes left her to recover at leisure and rejoined Fagin.

text: “To be sure it is,” replied Fagin; “and you can have a few good beats marked out in Camden Town and Battle Bridge, and places like that, where they’re always running errands; and you can knock down as many kinchins as you like, at any hour of the day. Ha! ha! ha!”
source_text: “To be sure it is,” replied Fagin; “and you can have a few good beats chalked out in Camden Town, and Battle Bridge, and neighborhoods like that, where they’re always going errands; and you can upset as many kinchins as you want, any hour in the day. Ha! ha! ha!”

text: Though Oliver had enough to keep his mind fixed on not losing sight of his guide, he could not help casting a few quick glances to either side as he went along. He had never seen a place more dirty or more miserable. The street was very narrow and full of mud, and the air was thick with foul smells.
source_text: Although Oliver had enough to occupy his attention in keeping sight of his leader, he could not help bestowing a few hasty glances on either side of the way, as he passed along. A dirtier or more wretched place he had never seen. The street was very narrow and muddy, and the air was impregnated with filthy odours.

text: Although Oliver had stirred from sleep, he was not yet fully awake. There is a sleepy condition, midway between slumber and waking, in which a person may dream more in five minutes, with the eyes half open and the mind only half aware of what is going on around him, than he could in five nights with the eyes tightly shut and the senses enclosed in complete unconsciousness. In such moments, a mortal learns just enough of his mind’s workings to gain some faint idea of its vast power, of its rising from the earth and defying time and space when released from the bonds of its bodily companion.
source_text: Although Oliver had roused himself from sleep, he was not thoroughly awake. There is a drowsy state, between sleeping and waking, when you dream more in five minutes with your eyes half open, and yourself half conscious of everything that is passing around you, than you would in five nights with your eyes fast closed, and your senses wrapt in perfect unconsciousness. At such time, a mortal knows just enough of what his mind is doing, to form some glimmering conception of its mighty powers, its bounding from earth and spurning time and space, when freed from the restraint of its corporeal associate.

text: An answer so unlike the one he had looked to draw forth, and was accustomed to hearing, quite disconcerted Mr. Weller. He recoiled from the keyhole; straightened himself to his full stature; and, in silent amazement, let his gaze pass from one to the other of the three persons standing by.
source_text: An answer so different from the one he had expected to elicit, and was in the habit of receiving, staggered Mr. Bumble not a little. He stepped back from the keyhole; drew himself up to his full height; and looked from one to another of the three by-standers, in mute astonishment.

text: There was a long silence. Every member of the respectable little company seemed sunk in private thought; not even the dog was excluded, for, by a certain spiteful licking of his lips, he appeared to be considering an assault on the legs of the first gentleman or lady he should meet in the street when he went out.
source_text: There was a long pause. Every member of the respectable coterie appeared plunged in his own reflections; not excepting the dog, who by a certain malicious licking of his lips seemed to be meditating an attack upon the legs of the first gentleman or lady he might encounter in the streets when he went out.

text: “No, sir, I do not,” the girl answered after a brief struggle. “I am bound to my old life. I hate it now—loathe it—but I cannot leave it. I must have gone too far to turn back; and yet I do not know, because if you had spoken to me like this some time ago, I should have laughed at it. But,” she said, glancing quickly around, “that fear is coming over me again. I must go home.”
source_text: “No sir, I do not,” replied the girl, after a short struggle. “I am chained to my old life. I loathe and hate it now, but I cannot leave it. I must have gone too far to turn back,—and yet I don’t know, for if you had spoken to me so, some time ago, I should have laughed it off. But,” she said, looking hastily round, “this fear comes over me again. I must go home.”

text: “Just a minute!” said the girl. “I wouldn’t be in such a rush if it were you coming out to be hanged the next time eight o’clock struck, Bill. I’d keep walking round and round the place till I dropped, if there were snow on the ground and I didn’t have a shawl to wrap myself in.”
source_text: “Wait a minute!” said the girl: “I wouldn’t hurry by, if it was you that was coming out to be hung, the next time eight o’clock struck, Bill. I’d walk round and round the place till I dropped, if the snow was on the ground, and I hadn’t a shawl to cover me.”

text: Fagin, with no deep feelings weighing on him, looked eagerly from one face to another while all this was going on; but evidently he did not find the one he was seeking. At last, when he succeeded in catching the eye of the man in the chair, he gave him a slight sign, and slipped out of the room as quietly as he had come in.
source_text: Fagin, troubled by no grave emotions, looked eagerly from face to face while these proceedings were in progress; but apparently without meeting that of which he was in search. Succeeding, at length, in catching the eye of the man who occupied the chair, he beckoned to him slightly, and left the room, as quietly as he had entered it.

text: They slept, or seemed to sleep, for a while; no one moved except Barney, who got up once or twice to toss more coals onto the fire. Oliver sank into a deep doze, imagining himself wandering through the gloomy lanes, or roaming the dark churchyard, or going over one or another of the scenes from the day before, when he was startled awake by Toby Crackit, who sprang to his feet and declared that it was half past one.
source_text: They slept, or appeared to sleep, for some time; nobody stirring but Barney, who rose once or twice to throw coals on the fire. Oliver fell into a heavy doze: imagining himself straying along the gloomy lanes, or wandering about the dark churchyard, or retracing some one or other of the scenes of the past day: when he was roused by Toby Crackit jumping up and declaring it was half-past one.

text: In short, the crafty old Jew had the boy caught in his snare. By subjecting him to solitude and darkness, he had already disposed him to prefer any company over the burden of his own sorrowful thoughts in such a dismal place; and now he was gradually working into his mind the poison he hoped would darken it and alter its nature forever.
source_text: In short, the wily old Jew had the boy in his toils. Having prepared his mind, by solitude and gloom, to prefer any society to the companionship of his own sad thoughts in such a dreary place, he was now slowly instilling into his soul the poison which he hoped would blacken it, and change its hue for ever.

text: Mr. Giles seemed to be weighing whether it would be proper to allow himself a respectful smile, when a gig drew up at the garden gate. Out of it sprang a stout gentleman, who hurried straight to the door and, by some unexplained means, got into the house with great speed, bursting into the room and nearly upsetting Mr. Giles and the breakfast table at the same time.
source_text: Mr. Giles was apparently considering the propriety of indulging in a respectful smile himself, when a gig drove up to the garden-gate: out of which there jumped a fat gentleman, who ran straight up to the door: and who, getting quickly into the house by some mysterious process, burst into the room, and nearly overturned Mr. Giles and the breakfast-table together.

text: It was a chill, black night. To the boy, the stars appeared more remote from the earth than ever he had known them; not a breath of wind stirred; and the gloomy shadows the trees cast upon the ground wore, by reason of their motionless gloom, an air of the grave, as though death itself had lain upon them. He gently closed the door again. Then, using the dying glimmer of the candle, he tied the few garments he possessed into a handkerchief, and seated himself upon a bench to await the coming of morning.
source_text: It was a cold, dark night. The stars seemed, to the boy’s eyes, farther from the earth than he had ever seen them before; there was no wind; and the sombre shadows thrown by the trees upon the ground, looked sepulchral and death-like, from being so still. He softly reclosed the door. Having availed himself of the expiring light of the candle to tie up in a handkerchief the few articles of wearing apparel he had, sat himself down upon a bench, to wait for morning.

text: Where was there a place, near at hand and yet little frequented, where he might find somewhat to eat and drink? Harrow. That seemed a likely refuge, close enough, and lying out of the common road. Thither he bent his steps,—now hurrying on at a run, now, with a strange wilfulness, dragging himself at a snail’s pace, or stopping wholly to idly strike at the hedges with a stick. Yet when he arrived, every soul he encountered—the very children lingering at the doorways—appeared to eye him with distrust. So he turned back again, lacking the heart to buy so much as a morsel or a drop, though many hours had passed since he had tasted food; and once more he loitered upon the Common, at a loss whither to go.
source_text: Where could he go, that was near and not too public, to get some meat and drink? Hendon. That was a good place, not far off, and out of most people’s way. Thither he directed his steps,—running sometimes, and sometimes, with a strange perversity, loitering at a snail’s pace, or stopping altogether and idly breaking the hedges with a stick. But when he got there, all the people he met—the very children at the doors—seemed to view him with suspicion. Back he turned again, without the courage to purchase bit or drop, though he had tasted no food for many hours; and once more he lingered on the Heath, uncertain where to go.

text: Minds such as his are prolific in contrivance. If, without wringing any confession from her, he kept her under observation, discovered the object of her changed attachment, and then menaced to lay the whole matter before Sykes—of whom she entertained no ordinary dread—unless she would lend herself to his purposes, might he not thus ensure her submission?
source_text: Such brains are fertile in expedients. If, without extracting a confession from herself, he laid a watch, discovered the object of her altered regard, and threatened to reveal the whole history to Sikes (of whom she stood in no common fear) unless she entered into his designs, could he not secure her compliance?

text: Night was drawing in quickly when he set out for home, carrying flowers he had gathered with particular care to brighten the sickroom. As he hurried along the road, he heard the sound of a vehicle racing up behind him at a furious speed. Turning, he saw it was a post-chaise, driven hard; and since the horses were galloping and the lane was narrow, he stepped aside and stood against a gate until it had gone by.
source_text: The night was fast closing in, when he returned homeward: laden with flowers which he had culled, with peculiar care, for the adornment of the sick chamber. As he walked briskly along the road, he heard behind him, the noise of some vehicle, approaching at a furious pace. Looking round, he saw that it was a post-chaise, driven at great speed; and as the horses were galloping, and the road was narrow, he stood leaning against a gate until it should have passed him.

text: It was late the next morning when Oliver woke from a deep, unbroken sleep. No one else was in the room except the old Jew, who was making coffee in a saucepan for breakfast and softly whistling to himself as he stirred it round and round with an iron spoon. Every now and then he would pause to listen for the slightest sound downstairs; and when he had made sure there was nothing to fear, he would begin whistling and stirring again, as before.
source_text: It was late next morning when Oliver awoke, from a sound, long sleep. There was no other person in the room but the old Jew, who was boiling some coffee in a saucepan for breakfast, and whistling softly to himself as he stirred it round and round, with an iron spoon. He would stop every now and then to listen when there was the least noise below: and when he had satisfied himself, he would go on whistling and stirring again, as before.

text: “No; I’ve changed my mind. Only women who have no pride in them run away from their husbands. There is one thing worse than being found dead in your husband’s house from the way he has treated you, and that is being found alive because you have gone off to somebody else’s house. I have been thinking it over all morning, and I have made up my mind. A wife who runs away is a burden to everyone, a burden to herself, and a disgrace—a whole load of misery greater than anything that comes from staying where you are, even if that does include the small matters of insult, blows, and starvation. Liddy, if you ever marry—God forbid that you ever should—you will find yourself in a dreadful position; but remember this: don’t give way. Hold your ground, and let them cut you to pieces. That is what I mean to do.”
source_text: “No; I’ve altered my mind. It is only women with no pride in them who run away from their husbands. There is one position worse than that of being found dead in your husband’s house from his ill usage, and that is, to be found alive through having gone away to the house of somebody else. I’ve thought of it all this morning, and I’ve chosen my course. A runaway wife is an encumbrance to everybody, a burden to herself and a byword—all of which make up a heap of misery greater than any that comes by staying at home—though this may include the trifling items of insult, beating, and starvation. Liddy, if ever you marry—God forbid that you ever should!—you’ll find yourself in a fearful situation; but mind this, don’t you flinch. Stand your ground, and be cut to pieces. That’s what I’m going to do.”

text: “Not even Death’s head himself shall wring it from me, mistress, if I mean to keep anything; and I’ll always be your friend,” Liddy replied with emphasis, while at the same moment she summoned a few more tears to her own eyes, not from any special need, but from an artistic instinct for keeping herself in harmony with the rest of the picture, as women seem apt to do in such moments. “I think God likes us to be good friends, don’t you?”
source_text: “Death’s head himself shan’t wring it from me, mistress, if I’ve a mind to keep anything; and I’ll always be your friend,” replied Liddy, emphatically, at the same time bringing a few more tears into her own eyes, not from any particular necessity, but from an artistic sense of making herself in keeping with the remainder of the picture, which seems to influence women at such times. “I think God likes us to be good friends, don’t you?”

text: It was very early the next morning, in that hour of sun and dew. The broken first notes of many birds widened through the fresh air, and the pale blue sky was here and there veiled by thin webs of cloud so insubstantial they did nothing to dim the day. Every light in the scene had a yellow cast, and every shadow was drawn out and slight. The climbing plants around the old manor-house bent under rows of heavy droplets, each one acting on the objects behind it like a tiny lens of great magnifying power.
source_text: It was very early the next morning—a time of sun and dew. The confused beginnings of many birds’ songs spread into the healthy air, and the wan blue of the heaven was here and there coated with thin webs of incorporeal cloud which were of no effect in obscuring day. All the lights in the scene were yellow as to colour, and all the shadows were attenuated as to form. The creeping plants about the old manor-house were bowed with rows of heavy water drops, which had upon objects behind them the effect of minute lenses of high magnifying power.

text: Bathsheba, for all her spirit, began to feel clear signs that she was by nature the weaker vessel. She struggled miserably against this femininity, which insisted on forcing in unsolicited emotions with ever-increasing force. While his reproaches poured over her, she had tried to escape disturbance by fastening her thoughts on the trees, the sky, or any trifling object within sight, but no cleverness could help her now.
source_text: Bathsheba, in spite of her mettle, began to feel unmistakable signs that she was inherently the weaker vessel. She strove miserably against this femininity which would insist upon supplying unbidden emotions in stronger and stronger current. She had tried to elude agitation by fixing her mind on the trees, sky, any trivial object before her eyes, whilst his reproaches fell, but ingenuity could not save her now.

text: We now direct our attention to the features on the left: flatness in relation to the river, verticality in relation to the wall behind it, and darkness in relation to both. These qualities formed the bulk of the scene. If anything could have been darker than the sky, it was the wall; and if anything could have been more dismal than the wall, it was the river beneath it. The blurred outline of the façade was broken here and there by chimneys, and across its surface the oblong forms of windows were faintly discernible, though only in the upper portion. Below, all the way to the water’s edge, the expanse remained unbroken by any opening or projection.
source_text: We turn our attention to the left-hand characteristics; which were flatness in respect of the river, verticality in respect of the wall behind it, and darkness as to both. These features made up the mass. If anything could be darker than the sky, it was the wall, and if any thing could be gloomier than the wall it was the river beneath. The indistinct summit of the façade was notched and pronged by chimneys here and there, and upon its face were faintly signified the oblong shapes of windows, though only in the upper part. Below, down to the water’s edge, the flat was unbroken by hole or projection.

text: “My judgment is, since you ask for it, that you are much to blame for playing your tricks upon a man such as Mr. Ashcombe, and doing it only for amusement. To lead a man on for whom you feel no real regard is no creditable thing. And even, Miss Rooke, if you had truly been disposed toward him, you might have allowed him to discover it by some token of genuine kindness, rather than by putting a valentine’s letter into his hands.”
source_text: “My opinion is (since you ask it) that you are greatly to blame for playing pranks upon a man like Mr. Boldwood, merely as a pastime. Leading on a man you don’t care for is not a praiseworthy action. And even, Miss Everdene, if you seriously inclined towards him, you might have let him find it out in some way of true loving-kindness, and not by sending him a valentine’s letter.”

text: Seven hundred and fifty pounds, in money at its noblest and most serviceable guise—that of needful sustenance for man and beast—was not to be hazarded, and have this great heap of grain reduced to less than half its worth, for the uncertainty of a woman’s temper. “Not while I have power to stop it!” said Edmund.
source_text: Seven hundred and fifty pounds in the divinest form that money can wear—that of necessary food for man and beast: should the risk be run of deteriorating this bulk of corn to less than half its value, because of the instability of a woman? “Never, if I can prevent it!” said Gabriel.

text: This climax of the whole series had been reached that night on the moor just named, and for the first time that season its irregularities had become shapeless masses without outlines or features; suggesting everything and declaring nothing, with no more individuality than being the outer boundary of something else—the lowest layer of a snowy firmament. From this wild heaven packed with falling flakes, the meadow and moor were for a moment given an extra covering, only to seem, for that very reason, even barer for a moment. The enormous arch of cloud overhead hung in an oddly low curve, like the roof of a great dark cavern slowly settling down toward its floor; for the instinctive impression was that the snow lining the sky and the snow crusting the earth would soon merge into a single body, with no intervening layer of air at all.
source_text: This climax of the series had been reached to-night on the aforesaid moor, and for the first time in the season its irregularities were forms without features; suggestive of anything, proclaiming nothing, and without more character than that of being the limit of something else—the lowest layer of a firmament of snow. From this chaotic skyful of crowding flakes the mead and moor momentarily received additional clothing, only to appear momentarily more naked thereby. The vast arch of cloud above was strangely low, and formed as it were the roof of a large dark cavern, gradually sinking in upon its floor; for the instinctive thought was that the snow lining the heavens and that encrusting the earth would soon unite into one mass without any intervening stratum of air at all.

text: “I know you don’t—I know that quite well,” said Adrian, with warm conviction plainly written upon his face, then letting it darken into gloom. “When a score of men are eager to speak softly to you, and to offer the admiration you merit without the caution you require, it is only natural that my own blunt, ill-matched blend of praise and reproach should give you little satisfaction. Fool though I may be, I am not vain enough to imagine otherwise!”
source_text: “I know you do not—I know it perfectly,” said Troy, with much hearty conviction on the exterior of his face: and altering the expression to moodiness; “when a dozen men are ready to speak tenderly to you, and give the admiration you deserve without adding the warning you need, it stands to reason that my poor rough-and-ready mixture of praise and blame cannot convey much pleasure. Fool as I may be, I am not so conceited as to suppose that!”

text: “I cannot think that he has any conscience whatever. And I must beg you, miss, to have nothing to do with him. Hear me this once—only this once! I do not say he is as wicked a man as I have imagined—I pray God he is not. But since we do not know precisely what he is, why should you not act as though he _might_ be wicked, merely for your own safety? Do not trust him, mistress; I implore you not to trust him.”
source_text: “I believe him to have no conscience at all. And I cannot help begging you, miss, to have nothing to do with him. Listen to me this once—only this once! I don’t say he’s such a bad man as I have fancied—I pray to God he is not. But since we don’t exactly know what he is, why not behave as if he _might_ be bad, simply for your own safety? Don’t trust him, mistress; I ask you not to trust him so.”

text: “Well, you need not trouble yourself about that any longer: perhaps you did not mean to be rude to me when you spoke your mind; indeed, I believe you did not,” said the shrewd woman, with a painfully innocent seriousness. “And I thank you for the help you have given here. But—but take care you do not speak to me again in that manner, or in any other, unless I speak to you first.”
source_text: “Well, you need not think more of that: perhaps you did not mean to be rude to me by speaking out your mind: indeed, I believe you did not,” said the shrewd woman, in painfully innocent earnest. “And I thank you for giving help here. But—but mind you don’t speak to me again in that way, or in any other, unless I speak to you.”

text: She moved away from Boldwood, and he paced to and fro by himself for almost a quarter of an hour. At length she came forth again, now clad in a new riding habit of myrtle green, shaped so closely to her waist as a rind clasps its fruit; and young Tom Coggan brought forward her mare, while Boldwood went to fetch his own horse from beneath the tree to which it had been fastened.
source_text: She left Boldwood’s side, and he walked up and down alone for nearly a quarter of an hour. Then she reappeared in her new riding-habit of myrtle green, which fitted her to the waist as a rind fits its fruit; and young Bob Coggan led on her mare, Boldwood fetching his own horse from the tree under which it had been tied.

text: All were given to musing on the plain conclusion, and while this lasted each fixed his gaze into the ashpit, which shone like a tropical desert beneath the vertical sun, drawing their eyes long and narrow, partly from the glare, partly from the depth of the matter under discussion.
source_text: A meditation on the obvious inference was indulged in by all, and during its continuance each directed his vision into the ashpit, which glowed like a desert in the tropics under a vertical sun, shaping their eyes long and liny, partly because of the light, partly from the depth of the subject discussed.

text: Gabriel had always known that once the fire was lit and the door shut, one of them had to be left open—the one chosen always being the side sheltered from the wind. He closed the windward slide, then turned to open the other; but on reflection the farmer decided first to sit down and leave both closed for a minute or two, until the hut’s temperature had risen a little. He sat down.
source_text: Gabriel had always known that when the fire was lighted and the door closed one of these must be kept open—that chosen being always on the side away from the wind. Closing the slide to windward, he turned to open the other; on second thoughts the farmer considered that he would first sit down leaving both closed for a minute or two, till the temperature of the hut was a little raised. He sat down.

text: The solemn, reserved yeoman folded the letter once more and tucked it into the frame of the mirror. As he did so, he caught sight of his own reflection—pale in expression and almost shadowlike in build. He noticed how tightly his mouth was drawn, and how his eyes stared back at him, wide and empty. Disturbed and displeased with himself for this nervous agitation, he went back to bed.
source_text: The solemn and reserved yeoman again closed the letter, and stuck it in the frame of the glass. In doing so he caught sight of his reflected features, wan in expression, and insubstantial in form. He saw how closely compressed was his mouth, and that his eyes were wide-spread and vacant. Feeling uneasy and dissatisfied with himself for this nervous excitability, he returned to bed.

text: The young hound, Martin’s son, might well have been the image of his dam, for there was little likeness between him and Martin himself. He was being trained in the craft of sheep-watching, that he might take his place with the flock when the other was gone; but as yet he had advanced no farther than the first plain rules, still unable to master the difference between doing a thing sufficiently well and doing it altogether too well. So zealous, and yet so perversely inclined, was this young hound—who had no particular name, and would answer with perfect cheerfulness to any friendly call—that if set behind the flock to drive them forward, he went about the task so heartily that, had he not been recalled or taught where to cease by the conduct of old Martin, he would have chased them over the whole shire with the greatest satisfaction.
source_text: The young dog, George’s son, might possibly have been the image of his mother, for there was not much resemblance between him and George. He was learning the sheep-keeping business, so as to follow on at the flock when the other should die, but had got no further than the rudiments as yet—still finding an insuperable difficulty in distinguishing between doing a thing well enough and doing it too well. So earnest and yet so wrong-headed was this young dog (he had no name in particular, and answered with perfect readiness to any pleasant interjection), that if sent behind the flock to help them on, he did it so thoroughly that he would have chased them across the whole county with the greatest pleasure if not called off or reminded when to stop by the example of old George.

text: Meanwhile Troy had added a few touches to his usual makeup for the part, so as to disguise himself more effectively; and though he had felt a faint uneasiness when he first went in, the transformation brought about by carefully “lining” his face with a wire made him safe from Bathsheba’s eyes and those of her men. Even so, he was relieved when it was over.
source_text: Meanwhile Troy had added a few touches to his ordinary make-up for the character, the more effectually to disguise himself, and though he had felt faint qualms on first entering, the metamorphosis effected by judiciously “lining” his face with a wire rendered him safe from the eyes of Bathsheba and her men. Nevertheless, he was relieved when it was got through.

text: To be fair, the men were not much at fault for the painful and demoralizing ending to the evening’s amusement. Sergeant Troy had so vehemently insisted, with his glass raised, that drinking should be the pledge of their fellowship, that those who wanted to refuse could hardly bring themselves to seem so ill-mannered in the circumstances. Since from childhood they had been wholly unused to any liquor stronger than cider or light ale, it was no surprise that, after about an hour, they had all given in with remarkable unanimity.
source_text: To be just, the men were not greatly to blame for this painful and demoralizing termination to the evening’s entertainment. Sergeant Troy had so strenuously insisted, glass in hand, that drinking should be the bond of their union, that those who wished to refuse hardly liked to be so unmannerly under the circumstances. Having from their youth up been entirely unaccustomed to any liquor stronger than cider or mild ale, it was no wonder that they had succumbed, one and all, with extraordinary uniformity, after the lapse of about an hour.

text: Adrian went on toward his own house. As he neared the doorway, his foot struck something that gave beneath it with a soft, leathery, swollen sound, like a boxing glove. It was a great toad, meekly making its way across the path. Vale lifted it up, thinking it might be kinder to destroy the poor thing and spare it suffering; but seeing that it was unhurt, he set it back among the grass. He understood what this plain sign from the Great Mother was meant to say. And before long there came another.
source_text: Gabriel proceeded towards his home. In approaching the door, his toe kicked something which felt and sounded soft, leathery, and distended, like a boxing-glove. It was a large toad humbly travelling across the path. Oak took it up, thinking it might be better to kill the creature to save it from pain; but finding it uninjured, he placed it again among the grass. He knew what this direct message from the Great Mother meant. And soon came another.

text: Yet one fortunate outcome of her general apathy was the long-delayed appointment of Oak as bailiff; though he had in effect been performing the duties for a long while already, the change, apart from the considerable rise in wages it brought him, was little more than a formality meant for the outside world.
source_text: However, one excellent result of her general apathy was the long-delayed installation of Oak as bailiff; but he having virtually exercised that function for a long time already, the change, beyond the substantial increase of wages it brought, was little more than a nominal one addressed to the outside world.

text: Troy then stepped into the center of the room, removed his cap, turned down the collar of his coat, and faced Boldwood squarely. Even then Boldwood did not realize that the man who had so long served as the embodiment of Heaven’s relentless irony toward him, who had once already burst in upon his happiness, lashed him, and torn his joy away, had returned to do the same a second time. Troy began to laugh, a hollow, automatic laugh; and then Boldwood knew him.
source_text: Troy next advanced into the middle of the room, took off his cap, turned down his coat-collar, and looked Boldwood in the face. Even then Boldwood did not recognize that the impersonator of Heaven’s persistent irony towards him, who had once before broken in upon his bliss, scourged him, and snatched his delight away, had come to do these things a second time. Troy began to laugh a mechanical laugh: Boldwood recognized him now.

text: “Why could Troy not have left my treasure in peace?” he demanded, with sudden heat. “When I meant him no harm, why must he thrust himself so obtrusively into your regard? Before he began to plague you, your own heart was set on me; and when I came to you again, as I should have done, your answer would have been yes. Can you deny it? I ask you, can you deny it?”
source_text: “Why did Troy not leave my treasure alone?” he asked, fiercely. “When I had no thought of injuring him, why did he force himself upon your notice! Before he worried you your inclination was to have me; when next I should have come to you your answer would have been Yes. Can you deny it—I ask, can you deny it?”

text: The maltster cleared his throat with an exaggerated emphasis, and, letting his gaze drift to the farthest corner of the ashpit, said in that slow manner which is excusable when a matter is so universally felt to be important that any oddity of speech must be endured in bringing it out, “Well, I don’t mind the year I was born in, but perhaps I can count up the places I’ve lived, and get at it that way. I stayed at Upper Longpuddle over there” (nodding northward) “till I was eleven. I stayed seven years at Kingsbere” (nodding eastward) “where I took to malting. From there I went to Norcombe, and malted there two-and-twenty years, and two-and-twenty years I was there turnip-hoeing and harvesting. Ah, I knew that old place, Norcombe, years before you were thought of, Master Oak” (Oak smiled in honest acceptance of the fact). “Then I malted at Durnover four years, and four years turnip-hoeing; and I was fourteen times eleven months at Millpond St. Jude’s” (nodding north-west-by-north). “Old Twills wouldn’t take me on for more than eleven months at a time, so as not to make me chargeable to the parish if I should be disabled.
