Then I was three years at Mellstock, and I’ve been here one-and-thirty years come Candlemas. How much is that?”
source_text: The maltster cleared his throat in an exaggerated form for emphasis, and elongating his gaze to the remotest point of the ashpit, said, in the slow speech justifiable when the importance of a subject is so generally felt that any mannerism must be tolerated in getting at it, “Well, I don’t mind the year I were born in, but perhaps I can reckon up the places I’ve lived at, and so get it that way. I bode at Upper Longpuddle across there” (nodding to the north) “till I were eleven. I bode seven at Kingsbere” (nodding to the east) “where I took to malting. I went therefrom to Norcombe, and malted there two-and-twenty years, and-two-and-twenty years I was there turnip-hoeing and harvesting. Ah, I knowed that old place, Norcombe, years afore you were thought of, Master Oak” (Oak smiled sincere belief in the fact). “Then I malted at Durnover four year, and four year turnip-hoeing; and I was fourteen times eleven months at Millpond St. Jude’s” (nodding north-west-by-north). “Old Twills wouldn’t hire me for more than eleven months at a time, to keep me from being chargeable to the parish if so be I was disabled. Then I was three year at Mellstock, and I’ve been here one-and-thirty year come Candlemas. How much is that?”

text: On a weekday morning, a small congregation, made up chiefly of women and girls, rose from its knees in the damp, mouldering nave of a church known as All Saints’, in the remote barrack-town already mentioned, at the close of a sermonless service. They were on the point of dispersing when a brisk tread, entering by the porch and advancing up the central aisle, caught their attention. The footsteps rang with a note unusual in a church: it was the jingle of spurs. Everyone turned to look. A young cavalry soldier in a scarlet uniform, bearing the three stripes of a sergeant on his sleeve, came striding up the aisle with an embarrassment made only more conspicuous by the force of his step and the resolve in his face not to betray it. By the time he had passed the scrutiny of these women, a faint colour had come into his cheek; yet, going on through the chancel arch, he did not pause until he had reached the altar rail. There, for a moment, he stood alone.
source_text: On a week-day morning a small congregation, consisting mainly of women and girls, rose from its knees in the mouldy nave of a church called All Saints’, in the distant barrack-town before-mentioned, at the end of a service without a sermon. They were about to disperse, when a smart footstep, entering the porch and coming up the central passage, arrested their attention. The step echoed with a ring unusual in a church; it was the clink of spurs. Everybody looked. A young cavalry soldier in a red uniform, with the three chevrons of a sergeant upon his sleeve, strode up the aisle, with an embarrassment which was only the more marked by the intense vigour of his step, and by the determination upon his face to show none. A slight flush had mounted his cheek by the time he had run the gauntlet between these women; but, passing on through the chancel arch, he never paused till he came close to the altar railing. Here for a moment he stood alone.

text: Amid these burly countrymen there drifted a womanly presence, the sole one of her kind that the chamber held. She was dressed with a neatness that was almost delicate. She passed among them as a light carriage among drays, was heard after them as a ballad after a homily, was felt in their midst like a fresh wind among furnaces. It had demanded a little resolution—much more, indeed, than she had at first supposed—to establish herself in such a place, for when she first entered the heavy talk had fallen silent, almost every countenance had swung toward her, and those already turned remained fixed there with a kind of stern attention.
source_text: Among these heavy yeomen a feminine figure glided, the single one of her sex that the room contained. She was prettily and even daintily dressed. She moved between them as a chaise between carts, was heard after them as a romance after sermons, was felt among them like a breeze among furnaces. It had required a little determination—far more than she had at first imagined—to take up a position here, for at her first entry the lumbering dialogues had ceased, nearly every face had been turned towards her, and those that were already turned rigidly fixed there.

text: “Mr. Boldwood, it is painful for me to say that I am so taken aback I scarcely know how to answer you with the proper respect and decorum, and can only just manage to speak my feelings—my meaning, I should say—that I am afraid I cannot marry you, however much I esteem you. You are too honorable and too dignified for me to be the wife to suit you, sir.”
source_text: “Mr. Boldwood, it is painful to have to say I am surprised, so that I don’t know how to answer you with propriety and respect—but am only just able to speak out my feeling—I mean my meaning; that I am afraid I can’t marry you, much as I respect you. You are too dignified for me to suit you, sir.”

text: “A fine time for one—a most excellent time,” said Josiah Poorman, drawing himself upright; for he, like certain others, had the habit of pausing a little in his toil upon such sweltering days for reasons preternaturally slight, among which the coming of Abel Bale on a week-day in his Sunday attire was of the first importance. “‘Twas a lame leg that let me read the _Pilgrim’s Progress_, and Mark Clarke learned All-Fours by means of a whitlow.”
source_text: “A good time for one—a’ excellent time,” said Joseph Poorgrass, straightening his back; for he, like some of the others, had a way of resting a while from his labour on such hot days for reasons preternaturally small; of which Cain Ball’s advent on a week-day in his Sunday-clothes was one of the first magnitude. “’Twas a bad leg allowed me to read the _Pilgrim’s Progress_, and Mark Clark learnt All-Fours in a whitlow.”

text: Then the dancing was fairly set afoot. As for the excellence of “Jack Tar’s Delight,” there could be, and never had been, any two minds on the matter. It had long been remarked in the musical societies of Mellstock and the hamlets about, that after three-quarters of an hour of resounding stamping, this air still retained, for heel and toe, a livelier provocation than most other dances do at their first beginning. “Jack Tar’s Delight” had also the further virtue of being so perfectly suited to the tambourine aforesaid—a no small instrument in the grasp of a player who knows the right thrummings, twitches, St. Vitus’s fits, and ghastly ecstasies demanded to bring out its sound in its utmost perfection.
source_text: So the dance began. As to the merits of “The Soldier’s Joy,” there cannot be, and never were, two opinions. It has been observed in the musical circles of Weatherbury and its vicinity that this melody, at the end of three-quarters of an hour of thunderous footing, still possesses more stimulative properties for the heel and toe than the majority of other dances at their first opening. “The Soldier’s Joy” has, too, an additional charm, in being so admirably adapted to the tambourine aforesaid—no mean instrument in the hands of a performer who understands the proper convulsions, spasms, St. Vitus’s dances, and fearful frenzies necessary when exhibiting its tones in their highest perfection.

text: There was a second performance in the evening, and the tent was brightly lit. Troy had played his part very quietly this time, venturing now and then to add a few words; and he was just bringing it to a close when, standing at the edge of the ring beside the front row of spectators, he noticed, scarcely a yard away, the eye of a man fixed sharply on his side profile. Troy quickly changed his position after recognizing in the watcher the rogue bailiff Pennyways, his wife’s declared enemy, who still lurked about the outskirts of Weatherbury.
source_text: There was a second performance in the evening, and the tent was lighted up. Troy had taken his part very quietly this time, venturing to introduce a few speeches on occasion; and was just concluding it when, whilst standing at the edge of the circle contiguous to the first row of spectators, he observed within a yard of him the eye of a man darted keenly into his side features. Troy hastily shifted his position, after having recognized in the scrutineer the knavish bailiff Pennyways, his wife’s sworn enemy, who still hung about the outskirts of Weatherbury.

text: Boldwood came out onto the main road and set off toward Casterbridge. He kept to a steady, even pace, crossing Yalbury Hill, then the flat stretch beyond, climbing Mellstock Hill, and by between eleven and twelve o’clock he had crossed the Moor and reached the town. The streets were almost empty now, and the flickering lamp flames threw light only on rows of grey shop shutters and narrow strips of white pavement, where his footsteps rang out as he went along. He turned to the right and stopped before an archway of massive stonework, shut off by a pair of iron-studded doors. This was the prison entrance, and a lamp fixed above it gave enough light for the unhappy traveler to find the bell-pull.
source_text: Boldwood passed into the high road and turned in the direction of Casterbridge. Here he walked at an even, steady pace over Yalbury Hill, along the dead level beyond, mounted Mellstock Hill, and between eleven and twelve o’clock crossed the Moor into the town. The streets were nearly deserted now, and the waving lamp-flames only lighted up rows of grey shop-shutters, and strips of white paving upon which his step echoed as his passed along. He turned to the right, and halted before an archway of heavy stonework, which was closed by an iron studded pair of doors. This was the entrance to the gaol, and over it a lamp was fixed, the light enabling the wretched traveller to find a bell-pull.

text: “Now for the truth of it,” she said, taking a seat. “The truth is that I have less than half a mile.” By deceiving herself with what she had known all along to be untrue, she had found the strength to cover more than half a mile—a distance she would have been unable to confront all at once. The ruse revealed that the woman had somehow, by a strange instinct, understood the paradoxical truth that blindness can act more forcefully than foresight, and that what is near-sighted may have more effect than what can see far ahead; that to deal a blow, what is needed is limitation, not breadth of vision.
source_text: “Now for the truth of the matter,” she said, sitting down. “The truth is, that I have less than half a mile.” Self-beguilement with what she had known all the time to be false had given her strength to come over half a mile that she would have been powerless to face in the lump. The artifice showed that the woman, by some mysterious intuition, had grasped the paradoxical truth that blindness may operate more vigorously than prescience, and the short-sighted effect more than the far-seeing; that limitation, and not comprehensiveness, is needed for striking a blow.

text: “Oh, it’s quite true enough. I may as well be hanged for a sheep as for a lamb—an old country saying, not worth much, but it suits a rough soldier—and so I’ll say what I think, without regard to whether it pleases you, and without expecting or meaning to win your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this way that your beauty may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant gazed down the meadow in reflective disapproval. “As a rule, perhaps one man falls in love with every ordinary woman. She can marry him; he is satisfied, and goes on to lead a useful life. But women such as you are desired by a hundred men at once—your eyes will cast scores and scores into hopeless admiration for you—you can marry only one of them. Of those, let us say twenty will try to drown the pain of rejected love in drink; twenty more will waste their lives in idleness, with no desire and no effort to make any mark in the world, because they have no ambition beyond their devotion to you; twenty more—the impressionable sort, myself perhaps among them—will be forever trailing after you, going wherever they can merely catch sight of you, doing desperate things. Men are such hopeless fools! The rest may manage, with greater or less success, to get over their passion. But all these men will be made unhappy. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are made unhappy too. There’s my story. That is why I say that a woman as lovely as you, Miss Everdene, is scarcely a blessing to her kind.”
source_text: “Oh, it is true enough. I may as well be hung for a sheep as a lamb (an old country saying, not of much account, but it will do for a rough soldier), and so I will speak my mind, regardless of your pleasure, and without hoping or intending to get your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this manner that your good looks may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant looked down the mead in critical abstraction. “Probably some one man on an average falls in love with each ordinary woman. She can marry him: he is content, and leads a useful life. Such women as you a hundred men always covet—your eyes will bewitch scores on scores into an unavailing fancy for you—you can only marry one of that many. Out of these say twenty will endeavour to drown the bitterness of despised love in drink; twenty more will mope away their lives without a wish or attempt to make a mark in the world, because they have no ambition apart from their attachment to you; twenty more—the susceptible person myself possibly among them—will be always draggling after you, getting where they may just see you, doing desperate things. Men are such constant fools! The rest may try to get over their passion with more or less success. But all these men will be saddened. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are saddened with them. There’s my tale. That’s why I say that a woman so charming as yourself, Miss Everdene, is hardly a blessing to her race.”

text: When the half-hour had run its course, a thin cloud of dust showed itself in the quarter looked for; and a little later a travelling carriage, conveying one of the two judges on the western circuit, laboured up the hill and drew rein upon the summit. The judge transferred himself to another vehicle while the broad-cheeked trumpeters sounded a flourish; and, the cars and javelin-men having been marshalled into a procession, the whole company moved on toward the town, save only the Weatherbury men, who, so soon as they had seen the judge depart, turned back again to their labour.
source_text: At the end of half-an-hour a faint dust was seen in the expected quarter, and shortly after a travelling-carriage, bringing one of the two judges on the Western Circuit, came up the hill and halted on the top. The judge changed carriages whilst a flourish was blown by the big-cheeked trumpeters, and a procession being formed of the vehicles and javelin-men, they all proceeded towards the town, excepting the Weatherbury men, who as soon as they had seen the judge move off returned home again to their work.

text: “Only a wife’s heart,” came to them like a breath around the ears of the three, and, turning, they beheld Elswyth standing among them. Then, as though to show at that very moment that her composure had been less instinct than resolve, she dropped without a word between them and became a disorderly mass of skirts upon the floor. The mere sense that no more than mortal endurance was demanded had in an instant brought her strength to a close.
source_text: “The heart of a wife merely,” floated in a whisper about the ears of the three, and turning they saw Bathsheba in the midst of them. Then, as if at that instant to prove that her fortitude had been more of will than of spontaneity, she silently sank down between them and was a shapeless heap of drapery on the floor. The simple consciousness that superhuman strain was no longer required had at once put a period to her power to continue it.

text: The maltster’s want of teeth seemed in no way to lessen his capacity as a mill. He had been without them for so many years that his toothlessness was felt less as a loss than his hardened gums as an attainment. Indeed, he appeared to draw near the grave as a hyperbolic curve draws near a straight line—by diminishing degrees as he advanced, until it became doubtful whether he would ever reach it at all.
source_text: The maltster’s lack of teeth appeared not to sensibly diminish his powers as a mill. He had been without them for so many years that toothlessness was felt less to be a defect than hard gums an acquisition. Indeed, he seemed to approach the grave as a hyperbolic curve approaches a straight line—less directly as he got nearer, till it was doubtful if he would ever reach it at all.

text: In three minutes, without stopping or altering a word, she had written a letter to Boldwood at his address beyond Casterbridge, telling him, in a manner at once gentle and resolute, that she had carefully considered the whole matter he had laid before her and so kindly allowed her time to decide on; and that her final answer was that she could not marry him. She had told Oak that she meant to wait until Boldwood came home before giving him her definitive reply. But Bathsheba found that she could not wait.
source_text: In three minutes, without pause or modification, she had written a letter to Boldwood, at his address beyond Casterbridge, saying mildly but firmly that she had well considered the whole subject he had brought before her and kindly given her time to decide upon; that her final decision was that she could not marry him. She had expressed to Oak an intention to wait till Boldwood came home before communicating to him her conclusive reply. But Bathsheba found that she could not wait.

text: Between this half-wooded, half-bare hill and the dim, motionless horizon that its summit faintly overlooked lay a strange expanse of unfathomable shadow, from which came sounds suggesting that whatever it concealed bore some diminished likeness to the forms visible here. The sparse grass, which covered the hill in patches, was stirred by winds of varying force and almost varying character—one that rubbed the blades heavily, another that cut across them with a sharp rasp, another that swept them as lightly as a broom. Instinctively, a person would stop and listen, and hear how the trees on the right and the trees on the left wailed or chanted to one another in the measured alternation of a cathedral choir; how hedges and other shapes downwind took up the sound, softening it to the gentlest sob; and how the rushing gust then plunged southward, never to be heard again.
source_text: Between this half-wooded half-naked hill, and the vague still horizon that its summit indistinctly commanded, was a mysterious sheet of fathomless shade—the sounds from which suggested that what it concealed bore some reduced resemblance to features here. The thin grasses, more or less coating the hill, were touched by the wind in breezes of differing powers, and almost of differing natures—one rubbing the blades heavily, another raking them piercingly, another brushing them like a soft broom. The instinctive act of humankind was to stand and listen, and learn how the trees on the right and the trees on the left wailed or chaunted to each other in the regular antiphonies of a cathedral choir; how hedges and other shapes to leeward then caught the note, lowering it to the tenderest sob; and how the hurrying gust then plunged into the south, to be heard no more.

text: “But there was a time when you turned to me, before I ever gave you a thought! I do not blame you, for even now I feel that the blind and chilly darkness I should have lived in had you not drawn me by that letter—your valentine, you call it—would have been worse than my knowledge of you, though that knowledge has brought me all this misery. Yet I say there was a time when I knew nothing of you, cared nothing for you, and still you drew me on. And if you tell me you gave me no encouragement, I cannot help but deny it.”
source_text: “But there was a time when you turned to me, before I thought of you! I don’t reproach you, for even now I feel that the ignorant and cold darkness that I should have lived in if you had not attracted me by that letter—valentine you call it—would have been worse than my knowledge of you, though it has brought this misery. But, I say, there was a time when I knew nothing of you, and cared nothing for you, and yet you drew me on. And if you say you gave me no encouragement, I cannot but contradict you.”

text: But Troy was never more resourceful than when he was completely at his wit’s end. He quickly pushed aside the curtain that separated his small dressing space from the manager and proprietor’s, where that gentleman now appeared as the person known as Tom King from the waist up, and as the said respectable manager from there to his toes.
source_text: But Troy was never more clever than when absolutely at his wit’s end. He hastily thrust aside the curtain dividing his own little dressing space from that of the manager and proprietor, who now appeared as the individual called Tom King as far down as his waist, and as the aforesaid respectable manager thence to his toes.

text: The road ran through water-meadows crossed by little brooks, their trembling surfaces braided down the middle and wrinkled at the edges; and where the current moved faster, the stream was speckled with white foam, drifting onward in calm disregard. On the higher ground, dry fallen leaves struck the earth as they rolled headlong on the wind’s shoulders, and the small birds in the hedges rustled their feathers and settled themselves snugly for the night, keeping their positions if Oak kept walking, but darting away if he paused to look. He went past Yalbury Wood, where the game birds were rising to roost, and heard the cracked-voiced cock pheasants calling “cu-uck, cuck,” and the hens giving their thin, wheezy whistles.
source_text: The road stretched through water-meadows traversed by little brooks, whose quivering surfaces were braided along their centres, and folded into creases at the sides; or, where the flow was more rapid, the stream was pied with spots of white froth, which rode on in undisturbed serenity. On the higher levels the dead and dry carcasses of leaves tapped the ground as they bowled along helter-skelter upon the shoulders of the wind, and little birds in the hedges were rustling their feathers and tucking themselves in comfortably for the night, retaining their places if Oak kept moving, but flying away if he stopped to look at them. He passed by Yalbury Wood where the game-birds were rising to their roosts, and heard the crack-voiced cock-pheasants “cu-uck, cuck,” and the wheezy whistle of the hens.

text: “And so I used to eat a lot of salt fish before I set out, and by the time I got there I was as dry as a lime basket—so utterly dry that the ale would go down—ah, it went down sweet! Happy times! Heavenly times! What splendid drunks I used to have in that house! You remember, Jacob? You used to go with me sometimes.”
source_text: “—And so I used to eat a lot of salt fish afore going, and then by the time I got there I were as dry as a lime-basket—so thorough dry that that ale would slip down—ah, ’twould slip down sweet! Happy times! Heavenly times! Such lovely drunks as I used to have at that house! You can mind, Jacob? You used to go wi’ me sometimes.”

text: Ruth spoke scarcely at all to her husband throughout that evening of their return from the market, and he was in no mood to be communicative. He showed that vexing union of an uneasy spirit and a tongue held fast in silence. The following day, being Sunday, went by in much the same fashion so far as their reserve was concerned, Ruth attending church both in the morning and again in the afternoon. It was the day before the Budmouth races. Toward evening Troy said abruptly—
source_text: Bathsheba said very little to her husband all that evening of their return from market, and he was not disposed to say much to her. He exhibited the unpleasant combination of a restless condition with a silent tongue. The next day, which was Sunday, passed nearly in the same manner as regarded their taciturnity, Bathsheba going to church both morning and afternoon. This was the day before the Budmouth races. In the evening Troy said, suddenly—

text: Gabriel’s features, in every part of their shape, so precisely kept to the mean between the beauty of St. John and the ugliness of Judas Iscariot, as shown in a window of the church he attended, that no single feature could be picked out and deemed either especially admirable or especially repellent. The dark-haired girl in the red jacket seemed to think the same, for she threw him only a careless glance and told her driver to go on. She might, on a very small scale, have given him a look of thanks, but she did not say it; more likely she felt none, for by helping her get through he had lost her the point, and we know how women receive a favour of that sort.
source_text: Gabriel’s features adhered throughout their form so exactly to the middle line between the beauty of St. John and the ugliness of Judas Iscariot, as represented in a window of the church he attended, that not a single lineament could be selected and called worthy either of distinction or notoriety. The red-jacketed and dark-haired maiden seemed to think so too, for she carelessly glanced over him, and told her man to drive on. She might have looked her thanks to Gabriel on a minute scale, but she did not speak them; more probably she felt none, for in gaining her a passage he had lost her her point, and we know how women take a favour of that kind.

text: Then a singular fire sprang into Larkyn’s eye, and his countenance colored with the restrained tumult of a thought too deep for words. Every gaze in the place had now turned upon him and upon the insensible Marian. He raised her from the ground in his arms, and, smoothing the gathered folds of her gown as a child might quiet the ruffled wings of a storm-driven bird, carried her over the paving stones to the White Hart Inn. There he bore her beneath the arch into a private chamber; and by the time he had laid down that precious burden—so reluctantly—upon a sofa, Marian had opened her eyes. Calling back at once all that had passed, she whispered, “I want to go home!”
source_text: Thereupon a strange fire lighted up Boldwood’s eye, and his face flushed with the suppressed excitement of an unutterable thought. Everybody’s glance was now centred upon him and the unconscious Bathsheba. He lifted her bodily off the ground, and smoothed down the folds of her dress as a child might have taken a storm-beaten bird and arranged its ruffled plumes, and bore her along the pavement to the King’s Arms Inn. Here he passed with her under the archway into a private room; and by the time he had deposited—so lothly—the precious burden upon a sofa, Bathsheba had opened her eyes. Remembering all that had occurred, she murmured, “I want to go home!”

text: “This is all my love for you has earned me! Ah! when I married you, your life was dearer to me than my own. I would have died for you—how truly I can say I would have died for you! And now you mock the folly of my marrying you. O! is it kind to fling my mistake in my face? Whatever opinion you may have of my prudence, you should not tell me of it so cruelly now that I am in your power.”
source_text: “This is all I get for loving you so well! Ah! when I married you your life was dearer to me than my own. I would have died for you—how truly I can say that I would have died for you! And now you sneer at my foolishness in marrying you. O! is it kind to me to throw my mistake in my face? Whatever opinion you may have of my wisdom, you should not tell me of it so mercilessly, now that I am in your power.”

text: Troy now took the whole scene in at a glance. She was leaning back, sipping a cup of tea held in her hand, and the man who had spoken was Boldwood, who had apparently just brought the cup to her. Bathsheba, in her careless mood, rested so idly against the canvas that it had been pressed into the outline of her shoulder, and she was, in effect, almost in Troy’s arms; while he was forced to draw his chest carefully back, lest she should feel its warmth through the fabric, as he looked in.
source_text: Troy took in the scene completely now. She was leaning back, sipping a cup of tea that she held in her hand, and the owner of the male voice was Boldwood, who had apparently just brought the cup to her, Bathsheba, being in a negligent mood, leant so idly against the canvas that it was pressed to the shape of her shoulder, and she was, in fact, as good as in Troy’s arms; and he was obliged to keep his breast carefully backward that she might not feel its warmth through the cloth as he gazed in.

text: He reached the race-course at two o’clock and stayed there, or in the town, until nine. Yet Fanny’s image, as it had come before him in the dim shadows of that Saturday evening, kept returning to his thoughts, reinforced by Bathsheba’s reproaches. He swore that he would not bet, and he kept his word; for when he left the town at nine that night, his money was reduced by only a few shillings.
source_text: He reached the race-course at two o’clock, and remained either there or in the town till nine. But Fanny’s image, as it had appeared to him in the sombre shadows of that Saturday evening, returned to his mind, backed up by Bathsheba’s reproaches. He vowed he would not bet, and he kept his vow, for on leaving the town at nine o’clock in the evening he had diminished his cash only to the extent of a few shillings.

text: The interior of the hut, as it then appeared, was snug and inviting; and the small crimson blaze, together with the candle, casting its kindly tint upon all within its reach, seemed to lend the very tools and implements an air of comfort and good cheer. In one corner stood the shepherd’s crook, while upon a shelf at the side were set out bottles and tins containing the plain medicines and preparations used in the care of sheep—chief among them spirit of wine, turpentine, tar, magnesia, ginger, and castor oil. Upon a triangular shelf that crossed the corner lay bread, bacon, cheese, and a mug for ale or cider, replenished from a flagon below. Close beside these provisions rested the flute, whose melancholy strains had lately been drawn forth by the solitary keeper to shorten a wearisome hour. The dwelling was aired by two circular openings, like the ports of a ship’s cabin, each fitted with a wooden slide.
source_text: The inside of the hut, as it now presented itself, was cosy and alluring, and the scarlet handful of fire in addition to the candle, reflecting its own genial colour upon whatever it could reach, flung associations of enjoyment even over utensils and tools. In the corner stood the sheep-crook, and along a shelf at one side were ranged bottles and canisters of the simple preparations pertaining to ovine surgery and physic; spirits of wine, turpentine, tar, magnesia, ginger, and castor-oil being the chief. On a triangular shelf across the corner stood bread, bacon, cheese, and a cup for ale or cider, which was supplied from a flagon beneath. Beside the provisions lay the flute, whose notes had lately been called forth by the lonely watcher to beguile a tedious hour. The house was ventilated by two round holes, like the lights of a ship’s cabin, with wood slides.

text: “Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “He invented an apple tree entirely from his own head, and it’s still called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know the sort, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted onto a Tom Putt, with a Rathe-ripe on top of that. It’s true he used to linger about in a public house with a woman in a way he really had no business doing, by rights, but there—he was a clever man, in the proper sense of the word.”
source_text: “Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “Invented a’ apple-tree out of his own head, which is called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know ’em, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted on a Tom Putt, and a Rathe-ripe upon top o’ that again. ’Tis trew ’a used to bide about in a public-house wi’ a ’ooman in a way he had no business to by rights, but there—’a were a clever man in the sense of the term.”

text: Then came the barley. The only way to shield it was by carefully thatching it throughout. Time passed, and the moon disappeared without returning. It was like the parting of an ambassador before war. The night had a worn, sickly appearance; and at last there came from all the heavens a complete dying away of the air, taking the form of a gentle breeze that might itself have been called death. And now, in the yard, nothing could be heard except the dull blows of the beetle as it drove in the spars, and, between them, the whispering of the thatch.
source_text: Next came the barley. This it was only possible to protect by systematic thatching. Time went on, and the moon vanished not to reappear. It was the farewell of the ambassador previous to war. The night had a haggard look, like a sick thing; and there came finally an utter expiration of air from the whole heaven in the form of a slow breeze, which might have been likened to a death. And now nothing was heard in the yard but the dull thuds of the beetle which drove in the spars, and the rustle of thatch in the intervals.

text: “Yes. Only we tone it down as much as we can, and call him Cainy. Ah, the poor widow-woman! She cried her heart out over it, nearly enough. She was reared by a very heathen father and mother, who never sent her to church or school, and it shows how the sins of the parents are visited upon the children, ma’am.”
source_text: “Yes. However, we soften it down as much as we can, and call him Cainy. Ah, pore widow-woman! she cried her heart out about it almost. She was brought up by a very heathen father and mother, who never sent her to church or school, and it shows how the sins of the parents are visited upon the children, mem.”

text: Then, before she knew it, her distress had broken from her; and she told him how she had once more fallen into the snare, what Clavering had asked of her, and how he waited for her answer. “The saddest cause of all for my consenting,” she said mournfully, “and the real cause why I think I must do so, for better or for worse, is this—there is that which I have not yet whispered to a living soul—I believe that if I do not promise him, he will lose his reason.”
source_text: Then out came her trouble before she was aware; and she told him how she had again got into the toils; what Boldwood had asked her, and how he was expecting her assent. “The most mournful reason of all for my agreeing to it,” she said sadly, “and the true reason why I think to do so for good or for evil, is this—it is a thing I have not breathed to a living soul as yet—I believe that if I don’t give my word, he’ll go out of his mind.”

text: “What does this mean?—though I suppose,” said Gabriel, looking up at Coggan as he passed the match over the ground near the turn. Coggan, who, like the panting horses, had lately begun to look tired, studied the mysterious marks once more. This time only three of them had the familiar horseshoe shape. Every fourth mark was a dot.
source_text: “What does this mean?—though I guess,” said Gabriel, looking up at Coggan as he moved the match over the ground about the turning. Coggan, who, no less than the panting horses, had latterly shown signs of weariness, again scrutinized the mystic characters. This time only three were of the regular horseshoe shape. Every fourth was a dot.

text: “Do not speak it—pray, do not! I cannot endure that you should feel so deeply and I remain altogether unmoved. And I fear they may observe us, Mr. Ashcombe. Will you not leave the matter where it is, now? I cannot think calmly. I did not know you meant to say this to me. Oh, it is cruel of me to have caused you such pain!” She was as frightened as she was distressed by his intensity.
source_text: “Don’t say it! don’t! I cannot bear you to feel so much, and me to feel nothing. And I am afraid they will notice us, Mr. Boldwood. Will you let the matter rest now? I cannot think collectedly. I did not know you were going to say this to me. Oh, I am wicked to have made you suffer so!” She was frightened as well as agitated at his vehemence.

text: The slow day was beginning to dawn. Its very place on the earth was one of those things that had taken on a fresh significance, and for no reason other than that the night’s encounter had happened there, Oak found himself once more in the plantation. While he lingered there in thought, he heard the tread of a horse at the bottom of the hill, and presently an auburn pony came into sight, carrying a girl as it climbed the path that passed the cattle-shed. It was the young woman from the previous night. At once Gabriel remembered the hat she had said the wind had blown away; perhaps she had returned to search for it. He quickly looked along the ditch, and after going about ten yards found the hat lying among the leaves. Gabriel took it up and went back to his hut. There he settled himself and watched through the loophole in the direction from which the rider was approaching.
source_text: The sluggish day began to break. Even its position terrestrially is one of the elements of a new interest, and for no particular reason save that the incident of the night had occurred there Oak went again into the plantation. Lingering and musing here, he heard the steps of a horse at the foot of the hill, and soon there appeared in view an auburn pony with a girl on its back, ascending by the path leading past the cattle-shed. She was the young woman of the night before. Gabriel instantly thought of the hat she had mentioned as having lost in the wind; possibly she had come to look for it. He hastily scanned the ditch and after walking about ten yards along it found the hat among the leaves. Gabriel took it in his hand and returned to his hut. Here he ensconced himself, and peeped through the loophole in the direction of the rider’s approach.

text: “I never heard a seasoned old married man of twenty years’ standing say ‘my wife’ in a more matter-of-fact tone than he did,” said Jacob Smallbury. “It might have been a little truer to nature if it had been spoken a bit more coldly, but that wasn’t to be expected just now.”
source_text: “I never heerd a skilful old married feller of twenty years’ standing pipe ‘my wife’ in a more used note than ’a did,” said Jacob Smallbury. “It might have been a little more true to nater if’t had been spoke a little chillier, but that wasn’t to be expected just now.”

text: Another week went by. The oat harvest had begun, and all the men were out in the fields beneath a one-colored Lammas sky, in the shimmering heat and the short shadows of noon. Inside, nothing could be heard but the hum of blue-bottle flies; outside, there was the sharpening of scythes and the rustle of the bearded oat heads brushing against one another as their upright amber-yellow stems came down heavily into each swath. Every drop of moisture not stored in the men’s bottles and flagons as cider was falling from their foreheads and cheeks as sweat. Everywhere else, there was drought.
source_text: Another week passed. The oat-harvest began, and all the men were a-field under a monochromatic Lammas sky, amid the trembling air and short shadows of noon. Indoors nothing was to be heard save the droning of blue-bottle flies; out-of-doors the whetting of scythes and the hiss of tressy oat-ears rubbing together as their perpendicular stalks of amber-yellow fell heavily to each swath. Every drop of moisture not in the men’s bottles and flagons in the form of cider was raining as perspiration from their foreheads and cheeks. Drought was everywhere else.

text: It was Sunday afternoon at the farmhouse, on the thirteenth of February. Dinner was finished, and Bathsheba, having no better company, had asked Liddy to come and keep her company. The mouldering old place was bleak in winter before the candles were lit and the shutters closed; the very air of it seemed as old as the walls themselves. Every corner behind the furniture had its own chill, since the fire was not set in this part of the house early in the day; and Bathsheba’s new piano, though in other days it had been old, looked especially crooked and out of true on the warped floor until evening cast shadows across its less obvious angles and concealed its ugliness. Liddy, like a little stream, though shallow, was always running; she was not weighty enough to burden the mind, and yet she was enough to engage it.
source_text: It was Sunday afternoon in the farmhouse, on the thirteenth of February. Dinner being over, Bathsheba, for want of a better companion, had asked Liddy to come and sit with her. The mouldy pile was dreary in winter-time before the candles were lighted and the shutters closed; the atmosphere of the place seemed as old as the walls; every nook behind the furniture had a temperature of its own, for the fire was not kindled in this part of the house early in the day; and Bathsheba’s new piano, which was an old one in other annals, looked particularly sloping and out of level on the warped floor before night threw a shade over its less prominent angles and hid the unpleasantness. Liddy, like a little brook, though shallow, was always rippling; her presence had not so much weight as to task thought, and yet enough to exercise it.

text: Soon after leaving Gabriel, the solitary, reserved man went back home to breakfast, troubled by pangs of shame and regret for having so openly revealed his feelings in those heated questions to a stranger. He once more set the letter on the mantelpiece and sat down to consider the circumstances surrounding it in the light of Gabriel’s information.
source_text: Soon parting from Gabriel, the lonely and reserved man returned to his house to breakfast—feeling twinges of shame and regret at having so far exposed his mood by those fevered questions to a stranger. He again placed the letter on the mantelpiece, and sat down to think of the circumstances attending it by the light of Gabriel’s information.

text: After obtaining twenty pounds from Bathsheba, he had contrived to add to it every farthing he could scrape together from his own resources, amounting to seven pounds ten. With this money, twenty-seven pounds ten in all, he had hurriedly driven away from the gate that morning to keep his appointment with Fanny Robin.
source_text: Twenty pounds having been secured from Bathsheba, he had managed to add to the sum every farthing he could muster on his own account, which had been seven pounds ten. With this money, twenty-seven pounds ten in all, he had hastily driven from the gate that morning to keep his appointment with Fanny Robin.

text: “And then I went into Griffin’s to have my boots hobbed, and after that I went to Riggs’s batty-cake shop, and asked them for a penn’orth of the cheapest and nicest stales, that were all but blue-mouldy, though not quite. And while I was chawing them down I walked on and saw a clock with a face as big as a baking-trindle—”
source_text: “And then I went into Griffin’s to hae my boots hobbed, and then I went to Riggs’s batty-cake shop, and asked ’em for a penneth of the cheapest and nicest stales, that were all but blue-mouldy, but not quite. And whilst I was chawing ’em down I walked on and seed a clock with a face as big as a baking trendle—”

text: He put out the dying lights, so that the barn should not be put in peril, closed the door on the men in their heavy, unknowing sleep, and went out once more into the solitary night. A warm wind, as though breathed from the open jaws of some dragon about to devour the earth, fanned him from the south, while straight before him in the north there rose a grim, misshapen mass of cloud, right against the wind. So strangely did it mount that one might have thought it was being hoisted by machinery from beneath. Meanwhile the pale little cloudlets had fled back to the southeast corner of the sky, as though in dread of the great cloud, like a young brood startled by the gaze of some monster.
source_text: He put out the expiring lights, that the barn might not be endangered, closed the door upon the men in their deep and oblivious sleep, and went again into the lone night. A hot breeze, as if breathed from the parted lips of some dragon about to swallow the globe, fanned him from the south, while directly opposite in the north rose a grim misshapen body of cloud, in the very teeth of the wind. So unnaturally did it rise that one could fancy it to be lifted by machinery from below. Meanwhile the faint cloudlets had flown back into the south-east corner of the sky, as if in terror of the large cloud, like a young brood gazed in upon by some monster.

text: Like some homeless wanderer, she lingered by the bank, as though soothed and captivated by the air of contentment that seemed to breathe from that little house and was so miserably absent from her own life. Gabriel appeared in an upper room, set his lamp upon the window-seat, and then knelt down to pray. The contrast between that picture and her own rebellious, troubled existence at this very moment was more than she could endure to gaze on any longer. It was not for her to seek peace with sorrow by such means. She must dance out her giddy, distracting measure to its final note, as she had begun it. With a heavy heart she turned again up the lane and went in through her own door.
source_text: Like a homeless wanderer she lingered by the bank, as if lulled and fascinated by the atmosphere of content which seemed to spread from that little dwelling, and was so sadly lacking in her own. Gabriel appeared in an upper room, placed his light in the window-bench, and then—knelt down to pray. The contrast of the picture with her rebellious and agitated existence at this same time was too much for her to bear to look upon longer. It was not for her to make a truce with trouble by any such means. She must tread her giddy distracting measure to its last note, as she had begun it. With a swollen heart she went again up the lane, and entered her own door.

text: “True, drink is a pleasant delight,” Jan said, as though repeating a truth so well worn in his mind that he scarcely noticed the words as they left his tongue; and, lifting the cup, Coggan slowly tipped his head back, his eyes shut, so that his eager soul might not be distracted for even a moment from its bliss by anything around him.
source_text: “True, drink is a pleasant delight,” said Jan, as one who repeated a truism so familiar to his brain that he hardly noticed its passage over his tongue; and, lifting the cup, Coggan tilted his head gradually backwards, with closed eyes, that his expectant soul might not be diverted for one instant from its bliss by irrelevant surroundings.

text: “But there was one gentleman who had enough discretion not to squander his time on me.” She put the matter in this shape lest Liddy should for an instant imagine her mistress was in the least offended. “A remarkably handsome man,” she went on, “tall and straight; I should say about forty. Have you any notion who he may have been?”
source_text: “But there was one man who had more sense than to waste his time upon me.” The information was put in this form that Liddy might not for a moment suppose her mistress was at all piqued. “A very good-looking man,” she continued, “upright; about forty, I should think. Do you know at all who he could be?”

text: “I’ve had a very wan companion these last four miles,” said Jasper, giving himself a shiver tempered by patience. “And, truth be told, it was beginning to weigh on me. I can assure you I haven’t set eyes on the colour of victual or drink since this morning’s breakfast, and that was no more than a scant mouthful taken out in the open.”
source_text: “I’ve had a very pale companion for the last four miles,” said Joseph, indulging in a shudder toned down by resignation. “And to speak the truth, ’twas beginning to tell upon me. I assure ye, I ha’n’t seed the colour of victuals or drink since breakfast time this morning, and that was no more than a dew-bit afield.”

text: She had spoken plainly, and she did not ask or expect any answer from Gabriel more satisfying than the one she had received. And yet, at the very center of her complicated heart, there was at that moment a small stab of disappointment for a reason she refused to admit even to herself. Oak had not once wished her free so that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the little sting. Not that she would have listened to any such notion. Oh no—for wasn’t she saying all the while that thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man for sentiment with her? And yet he might at least have hinted at that old love of his, and asked, in some lightly teasing way, whether he might mention it. It would have been touching and sweet, if nothing else; and then she would have shown how gentle and harmless a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to offer such cool advice—the very advice she had sought—kept our heroine unsettled all afternoon.
source_text: She had spoken frankly, and neither asked nor expected any reply from Gabriel more satisfactory than that she had obtained. Yet in the centremost parts of her complicated heart there existed at this minute a little pang of disappointment, for a reason she would not allow herself to recognize. Oak had not once wished her free that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the insect sting. Not that she would have listened to any such hypothesis. O no—for wasn’t she saying all the time that such thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man to speak sentiment to her? Yet he might have just hinted about that old love of his, and asked, in a playful off-hand way, if he might speak of it. It would have seemed pretty and sweet, if no more; and then she would have shown how kind and inoffensive a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to give such cool advice—the very advice she had asked for—it ruffled our heroine all the afternoon.

text: “I caught sight of the lady of the house,” went on the younger shepherd, “and a sodger, strolling together. And little by little they came nearer and nearer, till at last they went arm-in-crook, as proper as courting—haw-haw! as proper as courting—haw!—courting proper—” Here, his story and his breath failing him together, the teller lifted his eyes and swept them over the field as though some memory lay hidden in it. “Well, I seed our mistress and a soldier—a-ha-a-wk!”
source_text: “I saw our mistress,” continued the junior shepherd, “and a sojer, walking along. And bymeby they got closer and closer, and then they went arm-in-crook, like courting complete—hok-hok! like courting complete—hok!—courting complete—” Losing the thread of his narrative at this point simultaneously with his loss of breath, their informant looked up and down the field apparently for some clue to it. “Well, I see our mis’ess and a soldier—a-ha-a-wk!”

text: “‘O Lord, O Lord, O Lord, O Lord, O Lord’—that I know by heart. ‘Give us, give us, give us, give us, give us’—that I know too. ‘Grace that, grace that, grace that, grace that’—that I know.” Other phrases came after in the same fashion. The lad seemed to belong to the dunces’ class; the book in his hands was a psalter, and this was the manner in which he was mastering the collect. Even in the sharpest assaults of grief there seems always to remain some thin film of consciousness, left unoccupied and vulnerable to the smallest trifles, and Letitia was dimly diverted by the child’s method until he, too, moved on.
source_text: “‘O Lord, O Lord, O Lord, O Lord, O Lord’:—that I know out o’ book. ‘Give us, give us, give us, give us, give us’:—that I know. ‘Grace that, grace that, grace that, grace that’:—that I know.” Other words followed to the same effect. The boy was of the dunce class apparently; the book was a psalter, and this was his way of learning the collect. In the worst attacks of trouble there appears to be always a superficial film of consciousness which is left disengaged and open to the notice of trifles, and Bathsheba was faintly amused at the boy’s method, till he too passed on.

text: “Oak, don’t speak like that. You’ve grown rather cynical lately—what’s happened? It seems we’ve exchanged roles: I’ve become the young man full of hope, and you the old skeptic. Still, does a woman keep a promise not to marry, but simply to enter into an understanding that she will marry someday? You know women better than I do—tell me.”
source_text: “Oak, don’t talk like that. You have got quite cynical lately—how is it? We seem to have shifted our positions: I have become the young and hopeful man, and you the old and unbelieving one. However, does a woman keep a promise, not to marry, but to enter on an engagement to marry at some time? Now you know women better than I—tell me.”

text: “Keep it—pray do, Miss Vale—keep it!” cried the wayward creature of feeling. “The very thought of your having it makes it worth ten times the sum to me. A plainer one will serve my ends just as well, and as for the delight of knowing against whose heart my old one now rests—well, I shall say no more of that. It could not be placed in hands more worthy than those it has known until now.”
source_text: “Keep it—do, Miss Everdene—keep it!” said the erratic child of impulse. “The fact of your possessing it makes it worth ten times as much to me. A more plebeian one will answer my purpose just as well, and the pleasure of knowing whose heart my old one beats against—well, I won’t speak of that. It is in far worthier hands than ever it has been in before.”

text: There was a creak of machinery from behind, and some of the children turned to look. On the inner side of the tower’s west wall jutted a small canopy, with a quarter-jack and a little bell beneath it, the contrivance worked by the same clock mechanism that struck the great bell in the tower. Between the tower and the church stood a tight screen, whose door was kept closed during services and so concealed this grotesque piece of clockwork from view. But now the door was open, and many could see the jack come out, hammer the bell, and then retreat once more into its niche, while the sound carried through the entire church.
source_text: There was a creaking of machinery behind, and some of the young ones turned their heads. From the interior face of the west wall of the tower projected a little canopy with a quarter-jack and small bell beneath it, the automaton being driven by the same clock machinery that struck the large bell in the tower. Between the tower and the church was a close screen, the door of which was kept shut during services, hiding this grotesque clockwork from sight. At present, however, the door was open, and the egress of the jack, the blows on the bell, and the mannikin’s retreat into the nook again, were visible to many, and audible throughout the church.

text: At that moment hurried footsteps sounded in the entry, and the door was opened to admit Gabriel Oak, with the inn-maid following behind him and carrying a candle. He fixed a stern gaze upon the one long face and the two round ones of the seated men, which met his glance with the look of a fiddle and a pair of warming-pans. Joseph Poorgrass blinked, and drew back several inches into the background.
source_text: At that moment hasty steps were heard in the entry, and the door opened to admit the figure of Gabriel Oak, followed by the maid of the inn bearing a candle. He stared sternly at the one lengthy and two round faces of the sitters, which confronted him with the expressions of a fiddle and a couple of warming-pans. Joseph Poorgrass blinked, and shrank several inches into the background.

text: “‘A’s a stammering man, mem,” Henery Fray said under his breath, “and they turned him off because the only time ’a ever spoke plain was when ’a told the squire his soul belonged to himself, and other such iniquities. ’A can curse, mem, as well as you or I, but ’a can’t speak an ordinary sentence to save his life.”
source_text: “’A’s a stammering man, mem,” said Henery Fray in an undertone, “and they turned him away because the only time he ever did speak plain he said his soul was his own, and other iniquities, to the squire. ’A can cuss, mem, as well as you or I, but ’a can’t speak a common speech to save his life.”

text: Mr. Vale wore, for a watch, what might more properly have been called a little silver clock; that is to say, it was a watch in shape and purpose, and a clock only in its smallness. This piece of mechanism was older than his grandfather by several years, and possessed the singular habit of either running far too quickly or else refusing to run at all. The lesser hand, moreover, had a trick of sometimes slipping upon its pivot; so that while the minutes were shown with exactness, no one could be altogether sure to what hour they belonged. Vale cured the stopping by means of knocks and jolts, and escaped the inconvenience of the other two imperfections by perpetual reference to the sun and stars, together with such observations as he could make by pressing his face close against his neighbours’ panes, until he could distinguish the hour displayed in the green faces of the clocks within. It should be added that, his fob being hard of access by reason of its rather lofty position in the waistband of his trousers, which itself lay at a yet more remote elevation beneath his waistcoat, the watch had to be produced, of necessity, by throwing the body over to one side, compressing mouth and countenance into a mere crimson lump under the strain, and hauling the watch up by its chain as one might draw a bucket from a well.
source_text: Mr. Oak carried about him, by way of watch, what may be called a small silver clock; in other words, it was a watch as to shape and intention, and a small clock as to size. This instrument being several years older than Oak’s grandfather, had the peculiarity of going either too fast or not at all. The smaller of its hands, too, occasionally slipped round on the pivot, and thus, though the minutes were told with precision, nobody could be quite certain of the hour they belonged to. The stopping peculiarity of his watch Oak remedied by thumps and shakes, and he escaped any evil consequences from the other two defects by constant comparisons with and observations of the sun and stars, and by pressing his face close to the glass of his neighbours’ windows, till he could discern the hour marked by the green-faced timekeepers within. It may be mentioned that Oak’s fob being difficult of access, by reason of its somewhat high situation in the waistband of his trousers (which also lay at a remote height under his waistcoat), the watch was as a necessity pulled out by throwing the body to one side, compressing the mouth and face to a mere mass of ruddy flesh on account of the exertion required, and drawing up the watch by its chain, like a bucket from a well.

text: It was around seven o’clock on that dark, leaden morning when Gabriel climbed down from the final stack and cried out with relief, “It is done!” He was soaked, exhausted, and saddened, though not so much saddened as soaked and exhausted, for he was heartened by the feeling that he had succeeded in a worthy cause.
source_text: It was about seven o’clock in the dark leaden morning when Gabriel came down from the last stack, and thankfully exclaimed, “It is done!” He was drenched, weary, and sad, and yet not so sad as drenched and weary, for he was cheered by a sense of success in a good cause.

text: “Then curse him—curse him!” Boldwood said, his whispered rage bursting out. “When I would have given worlds just to touch your hand, you have let a rake come in here without right or ceremony and—kiss you! God’s mercy—kiss you!... Ah, a day will come in his life when he must repent and bitterly think of the pain he has caused another man; and then may he suffer, and long, and curse, and yearn—as I do now!”
source_text: “Then curse him; and curse him!” said Boldwood, breaking into a whispered fury. “Whilst I would have given worlds to touch your hand, you have let a rake come in without right or ceremony and—kiss you! Heaven’s mercy—kiss you!... Ah, a time of his life shall come when he will have to repent, and think wretchedly of the pain he has caused another man; and then may he ache, and wish, and curse, and yearn—as I do now!”

text: “That I cannot, indeed,” he said, slipping by Ash with the caution of a man who skirts an alms-dish when he has no mind to give. “If you keep along the road until you reach Havers’s Malt-house, where they’ve all gone to take their bite of dinner, I daresay one of them will put you on the way to a place. Good-night to you, shepherd.”
source_text: “That I can’t, indeed,” he said, moving past Oak as a Christian edges past an offertory-plate when he does not mean to contribute. “If you follow on the road till you come to Warren’s Malthouse, where they are all gone to have their snap of victuals, I daresay some of ’em will tell you of a place. Good-night to ye, shepherd.”

text: Clare lived apart and in a state of idle seclusion. A large part of his wheat, and the whole of his barley for that year, had been spoiled by the rain. It had sprouted, run together into tangled masses, and at last been flung to the pigs by the armful. The peculiar neglect that had brought about so much damage and waste became a matter of murmured discussion among all the neighbors; and it was learned from one of Clare’s laborers that forgetfulness had played no part in it, for the master had been warned of the peril to his grain as often and as urgently as a dependent might safely venture. The sight of the pigs turning away in loathing from the tainted ears seemed at last to rouse Clare, and one evening he sent for Gabriel. Whether the idea had been prompted by the recent favor shown to Bathsheba or not, the farmer at that meeting proposed that Gabriel should take charge of the Lower Holding as well as of Bathsheba’s, on account of the need Clare felt for such help, and because no more dependable man could be found. Gabriel’s evil star was certainly sinking swiftly.
source_text: Boldwood lived secluded and inactive. Much of his wheat and all his barley of that season had been spoilt by the rain. It sprouted, grew into intricate mats, and was ultimately thrown to the pigs in armfuls. The strange neglect which had produced this ruin and waste became the subject of whispered talk among all the people round; and it was elicited from one of Boldwood’s men that forgetfulness had nothing to do with it, for he had been reminded of the danger to his corn as many times and as persistently as inferiors dared to do. The sight of the pigs turning in disgust from the rotten ears seemed to arouse Boldwood, and he one evening sent for Oak. Whether it was suggested by Bathsheba’s recent act of promotion or not, the farmer proposed at the interview that Gabriel should undertake the superintendence of the Lower Farm as well as of Bathsheba’s, because of the necessity Boldwood felt for such aid, and the impossibility of discovering a more trustworthy man. Gabriel’s malignant star was assuredly setting fast.

text: She waited a minute—then two—thinking of Troy’s disappointment at her failure to keep the engagement she had promised, until once more she ran across the field, scrambled over the bank, and took up the original path. By now she was literally trembling and breathless at her boldness in such a reckless venture; her breathing came fast and uneven, and her eyes were bright with an uncommon light. Yet go on she must. She came to the edge of a pit set among the ferns. Troy was standing in the bottom of it, looking up at her.
source_text: She waited one minute—two minutes—thought of Troy’s disappointment at her non-fulfilment of a promised engagement, till she again ran along the field, clambered over the bank, and followed the original direction. She was now literally trembling and panting at this her temerity in such an errant undertaking; her breath came and went quickly, and her eyes shone with an infrequent light. Yet go she must. She reached the verge of a pit in the middle of the ferns. Troy stood in the bottom, looking up towards her.

text: The door was placed in a singular way. Its threshold stood three or four feet above the ground, and for a moment one could not account for so unusual a height, until the ruts directly below suggested that the opening served only for the passage of goods and people to and from the level of a vehicle waiting outside. In all, the door seemed to proclaim itself a kind of Traitor’s Gate, transported into another realm. That it was entered and left only at rare intervals was plain from the tufts of grass permitted to grow unmolested in the cracks of the sill.
source_text: The situation of the door was peculiar. The sill was three or four feet above the ground, and for a moment one was at a loss for an explanation of this exceptional altitude, till ruts immediately beneath suggested that the door was used solely for the passage of articles and persons to and from the level of a vehicle standing on the outside. Upon the whole, the door seemed to advertise itself as a species of Traitor’s Gate translated to another sphere. That entry and exit hereby was only at rare intervals became apparent on noting that tufts of grass were allowed to flourish undisturbed in the chinks of the sill.

text: The half-mile now rose before the sick and weary woman like a stubborn Juggernaut. It was the impassive king of her world. Here the road stretched across Durnover Moor, open to the roadway on either side. She looked out over the broad expanse, the lights, herself, sighed, and lay down against the guard-stone of the bridge.
source_text: The half-mile stood now before the sick and weary woman like a stolid Juggernaut. It was an impassive King of her world. The road here ran across Durnover Moor, open to the road on either side. She surveyed the wide space, the lights, herself, sighed, and lay down against a guard-stone of the bridge.

text: “I believe this,” she went on with a fearless candor; “and Heaven knows I say it in a spirit wholly unlike vanity, for it pains and distresses me to the very depths of my soul—I believe that man’s future lies in my hands. His whole career depends entirely on the way I deal with him. O Gabriel, I tremble at the burden of my responsibility, for it is dreadful!”
source_text: “I believe this,” she continued, with reckless frankness; “and Heaven knows I say it in a spirit the very reverse of vain, for I am grieved and troubled to my soul about it—I believe I hold that man’s future in my hand. His career depends entirely upon my treatment of him. O Gabriel, I tremble at my responsibility, for it is terrible!”

text: “Thank you, Coggan. But I can assure you this deep quiet is not what I wanted at all, nor what either of us would have wanted, if it hadn’t been for certain things that would make a cheerful wedding seem scarcely proper. Bathsheba is very anxious that the whole parish should not be in church staring at her—she’s shy and uneasy about it, in truth—so I’m arranging things this way to please her.”
source_text: “Thank you, Coggan. But I assure ’ee that this great hush is not what I wished for at all, or what either of us would have wished if it hadn’t been for certain things that would make a gay wedding seem hardly the thing. Bathsheba has a great wish that all the parish shall not be in church, looking at her—she’s shy-like and nervous about it, in fact—so I be doing this to humour her.”

text: In this predicament Troy immediately went out to scout the ground. It struck him that finding Pennyways—and, if possible, winning him over—would be a very prudent move. He had on a heavy beard borrowed from the show, and with this disguise he made his way about the fairground. By then it was nearly dark, and decent folk were already harnessing their carts and gigs to head home.
source_text: In this dilemma Troy at once went out to reconnoitre. It occurred to him that to find Pennyways, and make a friend of him if possible, would be a very wise act. He had put on a thick beard borrowed from the establishment, and in this he wandered about the fair-field. It was now almost dark, and respectable people were getting their carts and gigs ready to go home.

text: “No, no, now—that’s going too far!” cried the shrinking fellow, who had all at once grown as bold as brass. “I never said _sir_. I’ll swear I never called out, ‘Abel Mugford o’ Nethercombe, sir.’ No, no; fairness is fairness, and I never put the title on the bird, being well aware that no gentleman of standing would be bawling there at that hour o’ the night. ‘Abel Mugford of Nethercombe’—that was the whole of it, every syllable; and I’d never ha’ spoken so much, if it hadn’t been for old Gaffin’s barley-wine.... There now, it was a merciful thing the matter stopped where it did.”
source_text: “No, no, now—that’s too much!” said the timid man, becoming a man of brazen courage all of a sudden. “I didn’t say _sir_. I’ll take my oath I didn’t say ‘Joseph Poorgrass o’ Weatherbury, sir.’ No, no; what’s right is right, and I never said sir to the bird, knowing very well that no man of a gentleman’s rank would be hollering there at that time o’ night. ‘Joseph Poorgrass of Weatherbury,’—that’s every word I said, and I shouldn’t ha’ said that if ’t hadn’t been for Keeper Day’s metheglin.... There, ’twas a merciful thing it ended where it did.”

text: Finishing the last of his bread and ham, and taking a draught from the flask of cider he had wisely brought with him, he climbed into the desolate cart. There he made one half of the hay serve him for a couch, and, as far as the blackness would allow, drew the rest over him in place of coverings, so that he was hidden wholly beneath it, and, in body at least, felt as easy as ever he had done in his days. Yet the inward heaviness it was beyond a man like Dale, who brooded more deeply than those about him, altogether to dismiss while turning over this ill-starred chapter of his life. Thus, meditating on his troubles, both tender and rural, he sank at last to sleep, shepherds possessing, like mariners, the rare advantage of being able to call up the deity rather than wait to be summoned by him.
source_text: Eating his last slices of bread and ham, and drinking from the bottle of cider he had taken the precaution to bring with him, he got into the lonely waggon. Here he spread half of the hay as a bed, and, as well as he could in the darkness, pulled the other half over him by way of bed-clothes, covering himself entirely, and feeling, physically, as comfortable as ever he had been in his life. Inward melancholy it was impossible for a man like Oak, introspective far beyond his neighbours, to banish quite, whilst conning the present untoward page of his history. So, thinking of his misfortunes, amorous and pastoral, he fell asleep, shepherds enjoying, in common with sailors, the privilege of being able to summon the god instead of having to wait for him.

text: This arrangement consisted of a single raised bench in a very conspicuous quarter of the ring, its seat spread with scarlet cloth and its floor covered by a strip of carpet; and Evelina presently discovered, to her mortification, that she alone had been reserved for it, while all the other crowded spectators stood on their feet along the edge of the arena, where they enjoyed, for half the cost, a doubly advantageous view of the entertainment. Thus as many glances were directed toward her, enthroned by herself in this place of distinction against its crimson backdrop, as toward the ponies and the clown, who were engaged in their opening feats at the centre, for Turpin had not yet made his appearance. Once installed there, Evelina could do nothing but make the best of her predicament and remain; so she seated herself, disposing her skirts with a certain composure across the vacant space on either side, and lending the pavilion at once a new and feminine grace. Within a few minutes she perceived the stout red nape of Mr. Coggan’s neck among the throng standing immediately below her, and a little farther on the devout profile of Joseph Poorgrass.
source_text: This feature consisted of one raised bench in a very conspicuous part of the circle, covered with red cloth, and floored with a piece of carpet, and Bathsheba immediately found, to her confusion, that she was the single reserved individual in the tent, the rest of the crowded spectators, one and all, standing on their legs on the borders of the arena, where they got twice as good a view of the performance for half the money. Hence as many eyes were turned upon her, enthroned alone in this place of honour, against a scarlet background, as upon the ponies and clown who were engaged in preliminary exploits in the centre, Turpin not having yet appeared. Once there, Bathsheba was forced to make the best of it and remain: she sat down, spreading her skirts with some dignity over the unoccupied space on each side of her, and giving a new and feminine aspect to the pavilion. In a few minutes she noticed the fat red nape of Coggan’s neck among those standing just below her, and Joseph Poorgrass’s saintly profile a little further on.

text: In this respect he might, under a favorable aspect, have been counted among the happiest of his class. For one may very plausibly maintain that memory is less a gift than an affliction, and that anticipation, in its only tolerable guise—that of perfect confidence—is in practice unattainable; while, as hope and its lesser derivatives, patience, restlessness, determination, inquisitiveness, it becomes a perpetual alternation between delight and suffering.
source_text: On this account he might, in certain lights, have been regarded as one of the most fortunate of his order. For it may be argued with great plausibility that reminiscence is less an endowment than a disease, and that expectation in its only comfortable form—that of absolute faith—is practically an impossibility; whilst in the form of hope and the secondary compounds, patience, impatience, resolve, curiosity, it is a constant fluctuation between pleasure and pain.

text: Adrian was now so altered from his former self in aspect, speech, and action, that even he could feel the unlikeness in his own mind. The manner in which he set about this business of buying a grave was that of one wholly unversed in such matters. He could not force himself to think, to reckon, or to be sparing. He desired a thing at random, and went after it like a child in a nursery. “I want a good grave,” he said to the man who stood in a small office inside the yard. “I want as good a one as you can give me for twenty-seven pounds.”
source_text: Troy was so unlike himself now in look, word, and deed, that the want of likeness was perceptible even to his own consciousness. His method of engaging himself in this business of purchasing a tomb was that of an absolutely unpractised man. He could not bring himself to consider, calculate, or economize. He waywardly wished for something, and he set about obtaining it like a child in a nursery. “I want a good tomb,” he said to the man who stood in a little office within the yard. “I want as good a one as you can give me for twenty-seven pounds.”

text: Another invented answer had risen once more to his lips, but it was once more checked, and he regarded her with a look suddenly arrested. The truth was that, as she stood there now—excited, untamed, and as candid as daylight itself—her irresistible beauty justified so completely the very names he had flung at it that he was almost startled by his own audacity in calling them false. He murmured, almost involuntarily, “Ah, why?” and kept his eyes upon her.
source_text: A factitious reply had been again upon his lips, but it was again suspended, and he looked at her with an arrested eye. The truth was, that as she now stood—excited, wild, and honest as the day—her alluring beauty bore out so fully the epithets he had bestowed upon it that he was quite startled at his temerity in advancing them as false. He said mechanically, “Ah, why?” and continued to look at her.

text: Troy drifted southward. A mixed feeling, made up of disgust at the dull, wearisome routine of a farmer’s existence, dark thoughts of the woman who lay in the churchyard, remorse, and a broad distaste for his wife’s company, drove him to look for a home anywhere in the world except Weatherbury. The mournful details surrounding Fanny’s death rose before him in unmistakable images that seemed likely never to fade, and they made life in Bathsheba’s house unbearable. By three in the afternoon he found himself at the bottom of a slope more than a mile long, running up to the crest of a line of hills parallel to the coast and standing like a dull barrier between the inland basin of cultivated land and the rougher scenery by the sea. A road climbed the hill, almost perfectly straight and glaringly white, its two edges drawing gradually closer until, some two miles away, they seemed to join the sky at the summit. Along the whole stretch of this narrow, dismal ascent, not a trace of life could be seen in that bright afternoon. Troy labored upward with a weariness and heaviness greater than he had known for many a day, or for many a year before. The air was hot and close, and the summit seemed to move farther off the nearer he came to it.
source_text: Troy wandered along towards the south. A composite feeling, made up of disgust with the, to him, humdrum tediousness of a farmer’s life, gloomy images of her who lay in the churchyard, remorse, and a general averseness to his wife’s society, impelled him to seek a home in any place on earth save Weatherbury. The sad accessories of Fanny’s end confronted him as vivid pictures which threatened to be indelible, and made life in Bathsheba’s house intolerable. At three in the afternoon he found himself at the foot of a slope more than a mile in length, which ran to the ridge of a range of hills lying parallel with the shore, and forming a monotonous barrier between the basin of cultivated country inland and the wilder scenery of the coast. Up the hill stretched a road nearly straight and perfectly white, the two sides approaching each other in a gradual taper till they met the sky at the top about two miles off. Throughout the length of this narrow and irksome inclined plane not a sign of life was visible on this garish afternoon. Troy toiled up the road with a languor and depression greater than any he had experienced for many a day and year before. The air was warm and muggy, and the top seemed to recede as he approached.
