text: The great body of the sheep at the fair were South Downs and the old Wessex horned sort; Bathsheba’s and Farmer Boldwood’s flock belonged chiefly to the latter. These arrived about nine o’clock, their marked horns curving gracefully down on either side of their cheeks in exact, spiral turns, with a small pink-and-white ear tucked beneath each horn. In front of them and behind came other breeds, like leopards in the richness and fullness of their fleece, though of course without the spots. There were also a few Oxfordshire sheep, whose wool had begun to curl like a flaxen-haired child’s, though they were outdone in this by the delicate Leicesters, which were in turn less curly than the Cotswolds. Most striking of all, however, was a little flock of Exmoors, present there by chance that year. Their mottled faces and legs, dark, heavy horns, and locks of wool falling about their dusky foreheads broke pleasantly the sameness of the flocks in that part of the fair.
source_text: The great mass of sheep in the fair consisted of South Downs and the old Wessex horned breeds; to the latter class Bathsheba’s and Farmer Boldwood’s mainly belonged. These filed in about nine o’clock, their vermiculated horns lopping gracefully on each side of their cheeks in geometrically perfect spirals, a small pink and white ear nestling under each horn. Before and behind came other varieties, perfect leopards as to the full rich substance of their coats, and only lacking the spots. There were also a few of the Oxfordshire breed, whose wool was beginning to curl like a child’s flaxen hair, though surpassed in this respect by the effeminate Leicesters, which were in turn less curly than the Cotswolds. But the most picturesque by far was a small flock of Exmoors, which chanced to be there this year. Their pied faces and legs, dark and heavy horns, tresses of wool hanging round their swarthy foreheads, quite relieved the monotony of the flocks in that quarter.

text: It is a lofty, though perhaps a thankless, sort of love that cannot be turned aside by any dread of awakening dislike in the heart of the beloved, when it sets itself to oppose his or her faults. Mr. Ash resolved to address his lady. He meant to found his entreaty upon what he deemed her unjust behavior toward Farmer Halcombe, who was now away from home.
source_text: That is a noble though perhaps an unpromising love which not even the fear of breeding aversion in the bosom of the one beloved can deter from combating his or her errors. Oak determined to speak to his mistress. He would base his appeal on what he considered her unfair treatment of Farmer Boldwood, now absent from home.

text: “Fifty pounds for the hand of Clara. Very well. Twenty pounds not to wed Clara, but to wed Eleanor. Very well. And at the end: I am already Eleanor’s husband. Now, Mr. Harwood, you have brought upon yourself the grotesque destiny that ever overtakes a man who thrusts himself between husband and wife. And one thing more. Bad as I am, I am not the kind of villain who would turn the marriage, or the misery, of any woman into a mere bargain and commodity. Clara has been gone from me long since. I know not where she is. I have looked for her everywhere. One thing more still. You say you love Eleanor; and yet upon the faintest show of proof you are ready at once to believe her false. A curse on such love! And now that I have taught you your lesson, take back your money.”
source_text: “Fifty pounds to marry Fanny. Good. Twenty-one pounds not to marry Fanny, but Bathsheba. Good. Finale: already Bathsheba’s husband. Now, Boldwood, yours is the ridiculous fate which always attends interference between a man and his wife. And another word. Bad as I am, I am not such a villain as to make the marriage or misery of any woman a matter of huckster and sale. Fanny has long ago left me. I don’t know where she is. I have searched everywhere. Another word yet. You say you love Bathsheba; yet on the merest apparent evidence you instantly believe in her dishonour. A fig for such love! Now that I’ve taught you a lesson, take your money back again.”

text: He went trotting homeward at a sober pace, and it was then, for the first time, that the notion came upon him that Fanny had indeed been kept by sickness from fulfilling her promise. In this instance she could scarcely have erred. He was sorry now that he had not stayed in Casterbridge to make inquiry. On reaching home he gently unfastened the horse and went within, as has already been related, to meet the dreadful shock that there awaited him.
source_text: He trotted slowly homeward, and it was now that he was struck for the first time with a thought that Fanny had been really prevented by illness from keeping her promise. This time she could have made no mistake. He regretted that he had not remained in Casterbridge and made inquiries. Reaching home he quietly unharnessed the horse and came indoors, as we have seen, to the fearful shock that awaited him.

text: Poor Gabriel’s spirit was nourished with a rich contentment by having her above him, her eyes appraising his deft shears with critical attention, seeming at every closing as though they would catch a scrap of flesh, yet never doing so. Like Guildenstern, Oak was happy because he was not too happy. He had no desire to speak with her; it was enough that his radiant lady and he made a single company of their own, shut apart from all others in the world.
source_text: Poor Gabriel’s soul was fed with a luxury of content by having her over him, her eyes critically regarding his skilful shears, which apparently were going to gather up a piece of the flesh at every close, and yet never did so. Like Guildenstern, Oak was happy in that he was not over happy. He had no wish to converse with her: that his bright lady and himself formed one group, exclusively their own, and containing no others in the world, was enough.

text: “Rubbish!” cried Vale hotly. “There she is, rolling in money, with house and land, horses, and every comfort, while I scrape along from day to day like some penniless rover. And in any case, it’s useless prating now; it’s past, and I’m almost thankful for that. I was seen and known here this very afternoon. I should have gone back to her the day after the fair if you hadn’t been forever rattling on about the law, and that nonsense about securing a separation; and I mean to let the matter wait no longer. What possessed me to bolt in the first place, I can’t for the life of me tell. Foolish sentiment—that was all it was. But what man in his senses could have guessed that his wife would be so eager to be quit of his very name?”
source_text: “All nonsense!” said Troy, angrily. “There she is with plenty of money, and a house and farm, and horses, and comfort, and here am I living from hand to mouth—a needy adventurer. Besides, it is no use talking now; it is too late, and I am glad of it; I’ve been seen and recognized here this very afternoon. I should have gone back to her the day after the fair, if it hadn’t been for you talking about the law, and rubbish about getting a separation; and I don’t put it off any longer. What the deuce put it into my head to run away at all, I can’t think! Humbugging sentiment—that’s what it was. But what man on earth was to know that his wife would be in such a hurry to get rid of his name!”

text: “Not to-night,” said Mr. Clark, with visible self-restraint. “Thank ye all the same; but we’ll call at a more seemly hour. Still, we couldn’t let the day go by without some word of admiration. If ye could send a drop of som’at down to Warren’s, why, so be it. Here’s long life and happiness to neighbour Oak and his comely bride!”
source_text: “Not to-night,” said Mr. Clark, with evident self-denial. “Thank ye all the same; but we’ll call at a more seemly time. However, we couldn’t think of letting the day pass without a note of admiration of some sort. If ye could send a drop of som’at down to Warren’s, why so it is. Here’s long life and happiness to neighbour Oak and his comely bride!”

text: “I am willing to bear the punishment of it,” said Clara, with resolute quiet. “You know, Edward, there is one thing that will not leave my conscience—that I once did him a real injury, and did it out of mere carelessness. If I had never played that foolish prank upon him, he would never have wished to marry me. Oh, if I could but pay him a great sum in money for the wrong I did, and so wash the fault from my soul!... But there is the debt, and it can only be settled in one way, and I feel I am bound to settle it, if it can honestly be done, without a thought for my own future. If a spendthrift throws away his inheritance at the gaming-table, the debt is none the less a debt because it is troublesome to pay. I have been such a spendthrift myself; and the only point I ask is this: since my own scruples, and the fact that, in the eye of the law, my husband is merely absent, will prevent any man from marrying me until seven years are gone—am I free to entertain such an idea, even though it is a kind of penance, for penance it would be? I _abhor_ marriage in such a case, and the sort of women I should seem to rank among by doing it!”
source_text: “That I’m willing to pay the penalty of,” said Bathsheba, firmly. “You know, Gabriel, this is what I cannot get off my conscience—that I once seriously injured him in sheer idleness. If I had never played a trick upon him, he would never have wanted to marry me. Oh if I could only pay some heavy damages in money to him for the harm I did, and so get the sin off my soul that way!... Well, there’s the debt, which can only be discharged in one way, and I believe I am bound to do it if it honestly lies in my power, without any consideration of my own future at all. When a rake gambles away his expectations, the fact that it is an inconvenient debt doesn’t make him the less liable. I’ve been a rake, and the single point I ask you is, considering that my own scruples, and the fact that in the eye of the law my husband is only missing, will keep any man from marrying me until seven years have passed—am I free to entertain such an idea, even though ’tis a sort of penance—for it will be that? I _hate_ the act of marriage under such circumstances, and the class of women I should seem to belong to by doing it!”

text: “Yes; I suppose that’s the size o’t,” said Joseph Poorgrass, with a cheerful sigh, as they walked away; “and I wish him joy o’ her, though I was once or twice near saying today with holy Hosea, in my scripture manner, which comes as natural to me as breathing, ‘Ephraim is joined to idols: let him alone.’ But since it is as it is, why, it might have been worse, and I give thanks accordingly.”
source_text: “Yes; I suppose that’s the size o’t,” said Joseph Poorgrass with a cheerful sigh as they moved away; “and I wish him joy o’ her; though I were once or twice upon saying to-day with holy Hosea, in my scripture manner, which is my second nature, ‘Ephraim is joined to idols: let him alone.’ But since ’tis as ’tis, why, it might have been worse, and I feel my thanks accordingly.”

text: An uncanny silence fell, and every eye turned toward the newcomer. Those who had only just heard that he was in the vicinity recognized him at once; those who had not were left bewildered. No one noticed Bathsheba. She stood leaning on the staircase. Her brow had drawn down sharply; her whole face was pale, her lips slightly parted, her eyes fixed and motionless upon the visitor.
source_text: There was an unearthly silence, all looking towards the newcomer. Those who had just learnt that he was in the neighbourhood recognized him instantly; those who did not were perplexed. Nobody noted Bathsheba. She was leaning on the stairs. Her brow had heavily contracted; her whole face was pallid, her lips apart, her eyes rigidly staring at their visitor.

text: Then Nature, as though resentful, came quietly to the rescue. Great sheets of ivy climbed and spread until they entirely cloaked the walls, so that the house had the air of an old abbey; and it was found that the prospect from the front, beyond the chimneys of Mellstock, was among the finest in the shire. A nobleman of the neighborhood once declared that he would have surrendered a year’s income to possess, at his own threshold, the same prospect enjoyed by those within—and very likely those within would willingly have exchanged the view for his year’s income.
source_text: Then Nature, as if offended, lent a hand. Masses of ivy grew up, completely covering the walls, till the place looked like an abbey; and it was discovered that the view from the front, over the Casterbridge chimneys, was one of the most magnificent in the county. A neighbouring earl once said that he would give up a year’s rental to have at his own door the view enjoyed by the inmates from theirs—and very probably the inmates would have given up the view for his year’s rental.

text: “I hope nothing is amiss with mistress,” said Maryann, who, with several of the other women, was tying the bundles together, for oats were always bound in sheaves on this farm. “But I had an unlucky sign indoors this morning. I went to unlock the door and dropped the key, and it struck the stone floor and split in two. To break a key is a dreadful omen. I wish miss’ess were home.”
source_text: “I hope nothing is wrong about mistress,” said Maryann, who with some other women was tying the bundles (oats being always sheafed on this farm), “but an unlucky token came to me indoors this morning. I went to unlock the door and dropped the key, and it fell upon the stone floor and broke into two pieces. Breaking a key is a dreadful bodement. I wish mis’ess was home.”

text: Waking somewhat abruptly from a sleep whose length he had no means of knowing, Oak discovered that the wagon was moving. He was being jolted along the road at a fairly brisk pace for a vehicle without springs, and in a way that made him physically uncomfortable, his head bobbing on the wagon bed like a kettle-drumstick. He then made out voices in conversation coming from the front of the wagon. His uneasiness at this predicament—which would have been alarm had he been a prosperous man, but misfortune is a fine anodyne to personal fear—made him look out cautiously from the hay, and the first thing he saw was the stars overhead. Charles’s Wain was approaching a right angle with the Pole Star, and Gabriel judged that it must be about nine o’clock—in other words, that he had slept for two hours. This slight astronomical calculation was made without any deliberate effort, while he was stealthily turning in order, if possible, to discover whose hands he had fallen into.
source_text: On somewhat suddenly awaking, after a sleep of whose length he had no idea, Oak found that the waggon was in motion. He was being carried along the road at a rate rather considerable for a vehicle without springs, and under circumstances of physical uneasiness, his head being dandled up and down on the bed of the waggon like a kettledrum-stick. He then distinguished voices in conversation, coming from the forpart of the waggon. His concern at this dilemma (which would have been alarm, had he been a thriving man; but misfortune is a fine opiate to personal terror) led him to peer cautiously from the hay, and the first sight he beheld was the stars above him. Charles’s Wain was getting towards a right angle with the Pole star, and Gabriel concluded that it must be about nine o’clock—in other words, that he had slept two hours. This small astronomical calculation was made without any positive effort, and whilst he was stealthily turning to discover, if possible, into whose hands he had fallen.

text: At that instant, on the crest against the burning sky, a figure showed itself, like the black core of a wick in the heart of a candle-flame. Then it stirred and began to move busily from point to point, bearing square, gaunt frames through which the same shafts of light were pierced. A lesser shape crept after it on hands and knees. The taller was Elias Venn; the smaller, Tom; and the objects being carried were hurdles.
source_text: At this moment, on the ridge, up against the blazing sky, a figure was visible, like the black snuff in the midst of a candle-flame. Then it moved and began to bustle about vigorously from place to place, carrying square skeleton masses, which were riddled by the same rays. A small figure on all fours followed behind. The tall form was that of Gabriel Oak; the small one that of George; the articles in course of transit were hurdles.

text: By morning a troop of horse had ridden out of the town, and a sergeant with his men had gone searching the four streets for recruits. Toward evening, as the day wore down, Gabriel, still without a place, came near to wishing he had gone with them and taken the King’s shilling. Tired of lingering in the market square, and caring little what sort of labor he might set himself to, he resolved to present himself in some other role than that of a bailiff.
source_text: In the morning a regiment of cavalry had left the town, and a sergeant and his party had been beating up for recruits through the four streets. As the end of the day drew on, and he found himself not hired, Gabriel almost wished that he had joined them, and gone off to serve his country. Weary of standing in the market-place, and not much minding the kind of work he turned his hand to, he decided to offer himself in some other capacity than that of bailiff.

text: Her quiet, plain statement of the fact had more force than any tragic outcry, and it did something to bring the distorted impressions in every mind there into their proper shape. Oak, before he had even grasped more than the barest outline of what had happened, hastened from the room, saddled a horse, and rode off. It was not until he had gone more than a mile that it struck him he would have done better to send another man on this business and remain at the house himself. What had become of Boldwood? Someone should have been keeping watch on him. Had he gone mad—had there been a quarrel? And then how had Troy come there? Where had he appeared from? How had this astonishing return taken place when many believed him to be lying at the bottom of the sea? In some small degree Oak had already been prepared for Troy’s presence by a rumour of his return that reached him just before he entered Boldwood’s house; but before he had had time to think that over, this terrible event had come upon him. Yet it was too late now to send another messenger, and he rode on, so troubled by these questions that, when he was about three miles from Casterbridge, he did not notice a square-built man walking beneath the dark hedge in the same direction as himself.
source_text: Her statement of the fact in such quiet and simple words came with more force than a tragic declamation, and had somewhat the effect of setting the distorted images in each mind present into proper focus. Oak, almost before he had comprehended anything beyond the briefest abstract of the event, hurried out of the room, saddled a horse and rode away. Not till he had ridden more than a mile did it occur to him that he would have done better by sending some other man on this errand, remaining himself in the house. What had become of Boldwood? He should have been looked after. Was he mad—had there been a quarrel? Then how had Troy got there? Where had he come from? How did this remarkable reappearance effect itself when he was supposed by many to be at the bottom of the sea? Oak had in some slight measure been prepared for the presence of Troy by hearing a rumour of his return just before entering Boldwood’s house; but before he had weighed that information, this fatal event had been superimposed. However, it was too late now to think of sending another messenger, and he rode on, in the excitement of these self-inquiries not discerning, when about three miles from Casterbridge, a square-figured pedestrian passing along under the dark hedge in the same direction as his own.

text: A large hope had lately begun to take root in Boldwood, whose irrational devotion to Bathsheba could only be called a kind of loving madness, one that neither time nor circumstance, nor ill report nor good, could lessen or destroy. This heated hope had sprung up again like a mustard seed in the calm that followed the rash belief that Troy had been drowned. He tended it anxiously, and almost avoided really thinking about it, for fear that reality might expose the wildness of the dream. At last Bathsheba had been persuaded to dress in mourning, and the sight of her coming into church in that attire was, each week, in itself a fresh strengthening of his faith that a time was coming—perhaps very far away, yet surely drawing nearer—when his patience would be rewarded. How long he might have to wait he had not yet examined closely. What he chose to believe was that the stern lessons she had undergone had made Bathsheba far more mindful than before of other people’s feelings, and he trusted that if she ever proved willing in the future to marry any man at all, that man would be himself. There was a foundation of good feeling in her: her remorse for the wrong she had carelessly done him could now be relied upon much more than before her passion and disappointment. It would be possible to reach her through her kindness, and to propose some friendly, practical agreement between them to be carried out at a later time, while keeping entirely out of sight the passionate side of his wish. Such was Boldwood’s hope.
source_text: A great hope had latterly germinated in Boldwood, whose unreasoning devotion to Bathsheba could only be characterized as a fond madness which neither time nor circumstance, evil nor good report, could weaken or destroy. This fevered hope had grown up again like a grain of mustard-seed during the quiet which followed the hasty conjecture that Troy was drowned. He nourished it fearfully, and almost shunned the contemplation of it in earnest, lest facts should reveal the wildness of the dream. Bathsheba having at last been persuaded to wear mourning, her appearance as she entered the church in that guise was in itself a weekly addition to his faith that a time was coming—very far off perhaps, yet surely nearing—when his waiting on events should have its reward. How long he might have to wait he had not yet closely considered. What he would try to recognize was that the severe schooling she had been subjected to had made Bathsheba much more considerate than she had formerly been of the feelings of others, and he trusted that, should she be willing at any time in the future to marry any man at all, that man would be himself. There was a substratum of good feeling in her: her self-reproach for the injury she had thoughtlessly done him might be depended upon now to a much greater extent than before her infatuation and disappointment. It would be possible to approach her by the channel of her good nature, and to suggest a friendly businesslike compact between them for fulfilment at some future day, keeping the passionate side of his desire entirely out of her sight. Such was Boldwood’s hope.

text: “I haven’t been able to find out. There seems to be a good deal of feeling on his side, but I can’t answer for hers. I didn’t know a word about any such thing until yesterday, and then I only heard that she was going to the party at his house tonight. They say this is the first time she has ever been there. And they say she has not so much as spoken to him since they were at Greenhill Fair; but what can people believe of it? Still, she’s not fond of him—quite distant and quite indifferent, I know.”
source_text: “I haen’t been able to learn. There’s a deal of feeling on his side seemingly, but I don’t answer for her. I didn’t know a word about any such thing till yesterday, and all I heard then was that she was gwine to the party at his house to-night. This is the first time she has ever gone there, they say. And they say that she’ve not so much as spoke to him since they were at Greenhill Fair: but what can folk believe o’t? However, she’s not fond of him—quite offish and quite careless, I know.”

text: At the sight and sound of that act, which to her was intolerable, Eleanor sprang toward him. All the strong feelings that had been dispersed through her life since she first knew what feeling meant seemed now to gather into one throbbing pulse. The recoil from her earlier indignant mood, when she had brooded over wounded honor, over being forestalled, over maternity eclipsed by another, was sudden and complete. All that was forgotten in the simple yet still powerful attachment of wife to husband. She had longed then for her own completeness, and now she cried out against the breaking of the bond she had once lamented. She threw her arms about Adrian’s neck, and cried wildly from the deepest depth of her heart—
source_text: At the sight and sound of that, to her, unendurable act, Bathsheba sprang towards him. All the strong feelings which had been scattered over her existence since she knew what feeling was, seemed gathered together into one pulsation now. The revulsion from her indignant mood a little earlier, when she had meditated upon compromised honour, forestalment, eclipse in maternity by another, was violent and entire. All that was forgotten in the simple and still strong attachment of wife to husband. She had sighed for her self-completeness then, and now she cried aloud against the severance of the union she had deplored. She flung her arms round Troy’s neck, exclaiming wildly from the deepest deep of her heart—

text: So it stood at that moment. Serena’s eyes, half-veiled by the shade of her hand, pursued the climbing cloud of creatures across the unsearchable breadth of blue until at last they came to rest upon one of the cumbersome trees already mentioned. A movement not unlike those fabled beginnings of the world, in ages long gone, could be discerned. The busy multitude had swept over the sky in a thin, even mist, which now gathered itself into a darker core; this drifted onward to a branch and condensed yet more heavily, until it became a fixed black blot upon the brightness.
source_text: This was the case at present. Bathsheba’s eyes, shaded by one hand, were following the ascending multitude against the unexplorable stretch of blue till they ultimately halted by one of the unwieldy trees spoken of. A process somewhat analogous to that of alleged formations of the universe, time and times ago, was observable. The bustling swarm had swept the sky in a scattered and uniform haze, which now thickened to a nebulous centre: this glided on to a bough and grew still denser, till it formed a solid black spot upon the light.

text: The miles he had to cover, together with the other obstacles that came with the lateness of the hour and the darkness of the night, delayed the arrival of Mr. Aldritch, the surgeon; and more than three hours elapsed between the firing of the shot and his entrance into the house. Oak was further detained in Casterbridge by the need to report what had occurred to the authorities; and there he learned that Boldwood had likewise come into the town and surrendered himself.
source_text: The miles necessary to be traversed, and other hindrances incidental to the lateness of the hour and the darkness of the night, delayed the arrival of Mr. Aldritch, the surgeon; and more than three hours passed between the time at which the shot was fired and that of his entering the house. Oak was additionally detained in Casterbridge through having to give notice to the authorities of what had happened; and he then found that Boldwood had also entered the town, and delivered himself up.

text: Alas for her resolution! She felt she could not do it. Not for anything in the world could she now give him even the faintest hint of her distress, much less ask him outright for the truth about Fanny’s death. She must suspect, and wonder, and fret, and endure it all by herself.
source_text: Alas for her resolve! She felt she could not do it. Not for worlds now could she give a hint about her misery to him, much less ask him plainly for information on the cause of Fanny’s death. She must suspect, and guess, and chafe, and bear it all alone.

text: “Edmund Halbury,” said a blurred shape from the darkness, drawing near and falling in with them. “Dark as a stack of cinders to-night, isn’t it? I was all but off the board across the stream down yonder in the hollow—never did that in all my days before. Are any of you Master Venn’s hands?” He bent forward to scan their faces.
source_text: “William Smallbury,” said a dim figure in the shades, coming up and joining them. “Dark as a hedge, to-night, isn’t it? I all but missed the plank over the river ath’art there in the bottom—never did such a thing before in my life. Be ye any of Boldwood’s workfolk?” He peered into their faces.

text: “Pooh!” said the sergeant with contempt, then lifted his voice as though a fresh idea had suddenly struck him. “Friends,” he said, “we’ll send the women home! They ought to be in bed by now. Then we cockbirds can have a fine spree to ourselves! And if any of the men turn coward, let them find their winter’s work somewhere else.”
source_text: “Pooh!” said the sergeant contemptuously, and raised his voice as if lighted up by a new idea. “Friends,” he said, “we’ll send the women-folk home! ’Tis time they were in bed. Then we cockbirds will have a jolly carouse to ourselves! If any of the men show the white feather, let them look elsewhere for a winter’s work.”

text: Oak sat meditating for nearly an hour. During this time two black spiders, of the sort commonly found in thatched cottages, made their way along the ceiling and at last dropped to the floor. This reminded him that if there was any class of phenomena in this matter he understood thoroughly, it was the instincts of sheep. He left the room, ran across two or three fields toward the flock, climbed upon a hedge, and looked down among them.
source_text: Oak sat down meditating for nearly an hour. During this time two black spiders, of the kind common in thatched houses, promenaded the ceiling, ultimately dropping to the floor. This reminded him that if there was one class of manifestation on this matter that he thoroughly understood, it was the instincts of sheep. He left the room, ran across two or three fields towards the flock, got upon a hedge, and looked over among them.

text: Bathsheba had no idea that the dark, silent figure into which she had so carelessly cast a seed contained a furnace of tropical intensity. Had she understood Boldwood’s moods, her self-reproach would have been dreadful, and the stain upon her conscience impossible to erase. And had she realized the power she now held, for good or for ill, over this man, she would have shuddered at the weight of such responsibility. Fortunately for her at that moment, though disastrously for her later peace, she had not yet grasped what Boldwood truly was. No one understood him completely; although one could infer something of his wild possibilities from the old floodmarks still faintly visible, he had never been seen in the high tides that had made them.
source_text: Bathsheba was far from dreaming that the dark and silent shape upon which she had so carelessly thrown a seed was a hotbed of tropic intensity. Had she known Boldwood’s moods, her blame would have been fearful, and the stain upon her heart ineradicable. Moreover, had she known her present power for good or evil over this man, she would have trembled at her responsibility. Luckily for her present, unluckily for her future tranquillity, her understanding had not yet told her what Boldwood was. Nobody knew entirely; for though it was possible to form guesses concerning his wild capabilities from old floodmarks faintly visible, he had never been seen at the high tides which caused them.

text: It was the first day of June, and the sheep-shearing season had reached its height, the whole landscape, down to the scantiest pasture, glowing with health and color. Every green thing was young, every pore seemed open, and every stem was swollen with the swift flow of sap. God appeared plainly present in the country, while the devil had gone away with the rest of the world to town. Among the more curious sights of the vegetable kingdom in and around Weatherbury at this abundant season were the downy catkins of the later trees, fern-shoots like bishops’ croziers, the square-headed moschatel, the strange cuckoo-pint—like a man struck apoplectic in a niche of malachite—snow-white ladies’-smocks, the toothwort, suggestive of human flesh, the enchanter’s nightshade, and the black-petaled, mournful bells. And among the animal world were the altered figures of Mr. Jan Coggan, the chief shearer; the second and third shearers, itinerant by trade and needing no further naming; Henery Fray, the fourth shearer; Susan Tall’s husband, the fifth; Joseph Poorgrass, the sixth; young Cain Ball as assistant shearer; and Gabriel Oak as general overseer. None of them were dressed to any degree worth remarking, each seeming to have found, in point of clothing, a proper middle course between a high-caste and a low-caste Hindu. A certain sharpness of outline, and a rigid set to the face and features in general, showed that the day’s business was serious.
source_text: It was the first day of June, and the sheep-shearing season culminated, the landscape, even to the leanest pasture, being all health and colour. Every green was young, every pore was open, and every stalk was swollen with racing currents of juice. God was palpably present in the country, and the devil had gone with the world to town. Flossy catkins of the later kinds, fern-sprouts like bishops’ croziers, the square-headed moschatel, the odd cuckoo-pint,—like an apoplectic saint in a niche of malachite,—snow-white ladies’-smocks, the toothwort, approximating to human flesh, the enchanter’s night-shade, and the black-petaled doleful-bells, were among the quainter objects of the vegetable world in and about Weatherbury at this teeming time; and of the animal, the metamorphosed figures of Mr. Jan Coggan, the master-shearer; the second and third shearers, who travelled in the exercise of their calling, and do not require definition by name; Henery Fray the fourth shearer, Susan Tall’s husband the fifth, Joseph Poorgrass the sixth, young Cain Ball as assistant-shearer, and Gabriel Oak as general supervisor. None of these were clothed to any extent worth mentioning, each appearing to have hit in the matter of raiment the decent mean between a high and low caste Hindoo. An angularity of lineament, and a fixity of facial machinery in general, proclaimed that serious work was the order of the day.

text: “What is it? Oh, I can’t wear a ring!” she cried, seeing what he was holding. “And besides, I’d never let a soul know it was an engagement. Perhaps it’s improper? Besides, we’re not engaged in the ordinary sense, are we? Don’t press me, Mr. Boldwood—don’t!” In her distress at not being able to free her hand from his at once, she stamped her foot fiercely on the floor, and tears sprang to her eyes once more.
source_text: “What is it? Oh I cannot wear a ring!” she exclaimed, on seeing what he held; “besides, I wouldn’t have a soul know that it’s an engagement! Perhaps it is improper? Besides, we are not engaged in the usual sense, are we? Don’t insist, Mr. Boldwood—don’t!” In her trouble at not being able to get her hand away from him at once, she stamped passionately on the floor with one foot, and tears crowded to her eyes again.

text: She seemed so lovely and delicate that his former indifference toward the people of Mellstock gave way at once. He had not imagined that she could so suddenly exert this dominion over him, and in a moment. Was he to go on, and care for nothing? He could not persuade himself to do so. More than any prudent desire to remain unrecognized, there sprang up in him a feeling of shame at the thought that his comely young wife, who already held him in contempt, might despise him yet further on discovering him after so many years in so pitiful a plight. He positively colored at the notion, and was bitterly annoyed that his aversion to Mellstock folk should have led him to loiter about the countryside in this fashion.
source_text: She looked so charming and fair that his cool mood about Weatherbury people was changed. He had not expected her to exercise this power over him in the twinkling of an eye. Should he go on, and care nothing? He could not bring himself to do that. Beyond a politic wish to remain unknown, there suddenly arose in him now a sense of shame at the possibility that his attractive young wife, who already despised him, should despise him more by discovering him in so mean a condition after so long a time. He actually blushed at the thought, and was vexed beyond measure that his sentiments of dislike towards Weatherbury should have led him to dally about the country in this way.

text: His long habit of guarding his heart, year after year, with no outlet at all for feelings he could spare, had done its work. It has often been remarked that the causes of love lie chiefly within the lover himself, and Boldwood stood as living proof of the claim. There was no mother to receive his devotion, no sister to claim his tenderness, no trivial attachments to occupy his affection. He grew overfilled with the whole burden of it, which was true lover’s love.
source_text: The insulation of his heart by reserve during these many years, without a channel of any kind for disposable emotion, had worked its effect. It has been observed more than once that the causes of love are chiefly subjective, and Boldwood was a living testimony to the truth of the proposition. No mother existed to absorb his devotion, no sister for his tenderness, no idle ties for sense. He became surcharged with the compound, which was genuine lover’s love.

text: “Now, Cainy, shoulder your two and be off. Good morning, Mr. Boldwood.” The shepherd hoisted up the sixteen long legs and four little bodies that he himself had fetched, and disappeared with them toward the lambing field nearby—their bodies now in a smooth, thriving condition that made a pleasing contrast with their near-dead state only half an hour earlier.
source_text: “Now, Cainy, shoulder your two, and off. Good morning, Mr. Boldwood.” The shepherd lifted the sixteen large legs and four small bodies he had himself brought, and vanished with them in the direction of the lambing field hard by—their frames being now in a sleek and hopeful state, pleasantly contrasting with their death’s-door plight of half an hour before.

text: “Black Bess has only just begun, I suppose. Am I right in thinking you’d like to watch the performance, Mrs. Troy? Please forgive me if I’m mistaken; but if you do, I’d be very happy to get you a seat.” Seeing her hesitate, he went on, “I myself won’t stay for it: I’ve seen it already.”
source_text: “Black Bess just started off, I suppose. Am I right in supposing you would like to see the performance, Mrs. Troy? Please excuse my mistake, if it is one; but if you would like to, I’ll get a seat for you with pleasure.” Perceiving that she hesitated, he added, “I myself shall not stay to see it: I’ve seen it before.”

text: Malcolm took the key, without staying to hear the end of the scolding. Ten minutes later his solitary figure might have been seen dragging four great waterproof sheets across the yard, and before long two of those mounds of grain were snugly wrapped—two coverings to each. Two hundred pounds were thus made safe. Three wheat-stacks still stood bare, and there were no more sheets. Ash searched beneath the staddles and found a fork. He climbed the third heap of treasure and set to work, taking the course of slanting the upper sheaves one over another; and, besides this, packing the gaps with the straw from some loose sheaves.
source_text: Gabriel took the key, without waiting to hear the conclusion of the tirade. Ten minutes later his lonely figure might have been seen dragging four large water-proof coverings across the yard, and soon two of these heaps of treasure in grain were covered snug—two cloths to each. Two hundred pounds were secured. Three wheat-stacks remained open, and there were no more cloths. Oak looked under the staddles and found a fork. He mounted the third pile of wealth and began operating, adopting the plan of sloping the upper sheaves one over the other; and, in addition, filling the interstices with the material of some untied sheaves.

text: The airy command was like those crystal substances which, though colorless in themselves, take on the tint of the things around them. Here, in the stillness of Boldwood’s parlor, where anything not serious seemed out of place, and where the air had the feel of a Puritan Sunday stretching through the whole week, the letter and its pronouncement changed character—from the carelessness of their making to a grave solemnity, borrowed now from everything that surrounded them.
source_text: The pert injunction was like those crystal substances which, colourless themselves, assume the tone of objects about them. Here, in the quiet of Boldwood’s parlour, where everything that was not grave was extraneous, and where the atmosphere was that of a Puritan Sunday lasting all the week, the letter and its dictum changed their tenor from the thoughtlessness of their origin to a deep solemnity, imbibed from their accessories now.

text: At that moment Oak came in with the parson, and the whole party went upstairs together, Liddy Smallbury leading the way. When they stopped on the landing, there was a stillness like death. Liddy tapped at the door, and Bathsheba could be heard moving her dress softly across the room; then the key turned in the lock, and she opened it. Her expression was composed and almost fixed, like a bust of Melpomene that had been given a little life.
source_text: Oak entered with the parson at this moment, and they all went upstairs together, preceded by Liddy Smallbury. Everything was silent as the grave when they paused on the landing. Liddy knocked, and Bathsheba’s dress was heard rustling across the room: the key turned in the lock, and she opened the door. Her looks were calm and nearly rigid, like a slightly animated bust of Melpomene.

text: “Yes—” The woman paused once more. There was no need to say any further, and that she did so seemed to come from an unknowing wish to appear at ease by uttering something, a trait often seen in the guileless when they are trying to speak covertly. “You are not from Weatherbury?” she asked, timidly.
source_text: “Yes—” The woman again paused. There was no necessity for any continuance of speech, and the fact that she did add more seemed to proceed from an unconscious desire to show unconcern by making a remark, which is noticeable in the ingenuous when they are acting by stealth. “You are not a Weatherbury man?” she said, timorously.

text: These rather pitiful signs of a mind driven mad by anxiety and love were the topic of conversation in Warren’s malt-house when Oak came in from Casterbridge with news of the verdict. He arrived in the afternoon, and the look on his face, lit by the kiln’s glow, told the story plainly enough. Boldwood, as everyone had expected he would, had confessed his guilt and been condemned to death.
source_text: These somewhat pathetic evidences of a mind crazed with care and love were the subject of discourse in Warren’s malt-house when Oak entered from Casterbridge with tidings of the sentence. He came in the afternoon, and his face, as the kiln glow shone upon it, told the tale sufficiently well. Boldwood, as every one supposed he would do, had pleaded guilty, and had been sentenced to death.

text: Bathsabea drew from her desk a splendidly illuminated and embossed card in post-octavo, which had been purchased on the last market-day at the principal stationer’s in Casterbridge. In the midst of it lay a little oval space, left empty for the sender to fill with tender words more fitting to the occasion than any printer’s common phrases could ever be.
source_text: Bathsheba took from her desk a gorgeously illuminated and embossed design in post-octavo, which had been bought on the previous market-day at the chief stationer’s in Casterbridge. In the centre was a small oval enclosure; this was left blank, that the sender might insert tender words more appropriate to the special occasion than any generalities by a printer could possibly be.

text: “Thank you for the compliment,” said the sergeant cheerfully, taking Bathsheba by the hand and conducting her to the head of the dance. “For although I have bought my discharge from Her Most Gracious Majesty’s regiment of cavalry, the Eleventh Dragoon Guards, in order to attend to the new duties awaiting me here, I shall remain a soldier in heart and feeling as long as I live.”
source_text: “Thanks for the compliment,” said the sergeant gaily, taking Bathsheba by the hand and leading her to the top of the dance. “For though I have purchased my discharge from Her Most Gracious Majesty’s regiment of cavalry the 11th Dragoon Guards, to attend to the new duties awaiting me here, I shall continue a soldier in spirit and feeling as long as I live.”

text: “True,” said Jacob Smallbury, carrying his thoughts a little deeper into the matter. “‘Tis something to consider, that you might have been worse off; but even as you are, ’tis a very sore affliction for you, Joseph. For you see, shepherd, though ’tis all very well for a woman, by thunder, ’tis a cruel awkward thing for a man like him, poor fellow.”
source_text: “True,” said Jacob Smallbury, deepening his thoughts to a profounder view of the subject. “’Tis a thought to look at, that ye might have been worse; but even as you be, ’tis a very bad affliction for ’ee, Joseph. For ye see, shepherd, though ’tis very well for a woman, dang it all, ’tis awkward for a man like him, poor feller?”

text: Troy, in the humiliation he was now enduring, had no sense at all that there was anything ridiculous in these romantic gestures, born of a penitent reaction against his former indifference and proving so useless in the end. Drawing his peculiarities from both sides of the Channel, he revealed at moments like the present the rigidity of the Englishman, along with that French inability to see where feeling begins to shade into sentimentality.
source_text: Troy, in his prostration at this time, had no perception that in the futility of these romantic doings, dictated by a remorseful reaction from previous indifference, there was any element of absurdity. Deriving his idiosyncrasies from both sides of the Channel, he showed at such junctures as the present the inelasticity of the Englishman, together with that blindness to the line where sentiment verges on mawkishness, characteristic of the French.

text: “He wasn’t to blame, because it was quite true—true about his seeing someone else,” she said quickly. “And now I don’t want a single word from you on the matter—in fact, I forbid it. I only wanted you to know that one misunderstood part of my history before a time came when you could never have known it.—Would you like some more sheaves?”
source_text: “He was not to blame, for it was perfectly true about—about his seeing somebody else,” she quickly added. “And now I don’t wish for a single remark from you upon the subject—indeed, I forbid it. I only wanted you to know that misunderstood bit of my history before a time comes when you could never know it.—You want some more sheaves?”

text: Troy stretched out his hand to draw her toward him, but she quickly shrank away. This open fear of him seemed to anger Troy, and he caught her arm and jerked it sharply. Whether his grip pinched her, or whether it was only his touch, was never known; but as he seized her, she writhed and gave a quick, low scream.
source_text: Troy stretched out his hand to pull her towards him, when she quickly shrank back. This visible dread of him seemed to irritate Troy, and he seized her arm and pulled it sharply. Whether his grasp pinched her, or whether his mere touch was the cause, was never known, but at the moment of his seizure she writhed, and gave a quick, low scream.

text: So far as anyone could discover, the poor young dog still seemed to think that, because he was kept to chase sheep, the more he chased them the better. At the end of his meal on the dead lamb—an event that may well have given him extra strength and high spirits—he had gathered all the ewes into one corner, driven the frightened creatures through the hedge and across the upper field, and, by sheer persistence in harrying them, had forced them on with enough momentum to smash through part of the decayed fence and send them over the edge.
source_text: As far as could be learnt it appeared that the poor young dog, still under the impression that since he was kept for running after sheep, the more he ran after them the better, had at the end of his meal off the dead lamb, which may have given him additional energy and spirits, collected all the ewes into a corner, driven the timid creatures through the hedge, across the upper field, and by main force of worrying had given them momentum enough to break down a portion of the rotten railing, and so hurled them over the edge.

text: So the men came into the hall, the room chosen and fitted up for the gathering on account of its size. The younger men and girls had only just begun to dance. Bathsheba had been uncertain how she ought to behave, for she was hardly more than a slender young girl herself, and the burden of dignity weighed heavily on her. At times she felt she should not have come at all; then she reflected how cold and unkind that would have been, and at last decided on a middle course: to remain for about an hour and then slip away unnoticed, having from the outset resolved that she could on no account dance, sing, or take any active part in the evening's doings.
source_text: So the men entered the hall, which was the room selected and arranged for the gathering because of its size. The younger men and maids were at last just beginning to dance. Bathsheba had been perplexed how to act, for she was not much more than a slim young maid herself, and the weight of stateliness sat heavy upon her. Sometimes she thought she ought not to have come under any circumstances; then she considered what cold unkindness that would have been, and finally resolved upon the middle course of staying for about an hour only, and gliding off unobserved, having from the first made up her mind that she could on no account dance, sing, or take any active part in the proceedings.

text: As the clock over the South Street poor-house wanted five minutes of three, a blue spring wagon, edged with red and decked with branches and flowers, came along the end of the street and turned up toward this side of the building. While the bells were still faltering out a broken strain of “Malbrook,” Ephraim Dagg rang the bell, and was bidden to draw his wagon back against the tall door beneath the gable. The door then swung open, and a plain coffin of elm was slowly borne out and laid, by two men in fustian, along the midst of the vehicle.
source_text: As the clock over the South-street Alms-house pointed to five minutes to three, a blue spring waggon, picked out with red, and containing boughs and flowers, passed the end of the street, and up towards this side of the building. Whilst the chimes were yet stammering out a shattered form of “Malbrook,” Joseph Poorgrass rang the bell, and received directions to back his waggon against the high door under the gable. The door then opened, and a plain elm coffin was slowly thrust forth, and laid by two men in fustian along the middle of the vehicle.

text: “Whatever do you think,” said Susan Tall, “Gable Oak is coming it quite the dand. He goes about now in shining boots with hardly a hob on ’em, two or three times a week, and on Sundays a tall hat besides, and he scarce remembers the very name of a smockfrock. When I see folk strut enough to be carved up into bantam cocks, I stand dormant with wonder, and say no more!”
source_text: “Whatever d’ye think,” said Susan Tall, “Gable Oak is coming it quite the dand. He now wears shining boots with hardly a hob in ’em, two or three times a-week, and a tall hat a-Sundays, and ’a hardly knows the name of smockfrock. When I see people strut enough to be cut up into bantam cocks, I stand dormant with wonder, and says no more!”

text: “Liddy,” she said, her heart a little lighter now that youth and hope were beginning to come back to her; “you are to be my confidante for the present—someone must be, and I choose you. Well, I shall make my home here for a time. Will you have a fire lit, lay down a piece of carpet, and help me make the place snug? After that, I want you and Maryann to bring up that little stump bedstead from the small room, with the bed that goes with it, and a table, and a few other things.... What am I to do to make the long hours pass?”
source_text: “Liddy,” she said, with a lighter heart, for youth and hope had begun to reassert themselves; “you are to be my confidante for the present—somebody must be—and I choose you. Well, I shall take up my abode here for a while. Will you get a fire lighted, put down a piece of carpet, and help me to make the place comfortable. Afterwards, I want you and Maryann to bring up that little stump bedstead in the small room, and the bed belonging to it, and a table, and some other things.... What shall I do to pass the heavy time away?”

text: That day she almost resolved to ask his forgiveness the very next time they met. The greatest mischief in such a step was this: if he imagined she had been mocking him, an apology would only deepen the insult by seeming insincere; and if, on the other hand, he supposed she wished to entice his suit, it would appear as further proof of her own boldness.
source_text: She that day nearly formed the intention of begging his pardon on the very next occasion of their meeting. The worst features of this arrangement were that, if he thought she ridiculed him, an apology would increase the offence by being disbelieved; and if he thought she wanted him to woo her, it would read like additional evidence of her forwardness.

text: “Very well, then,” said the parson. “I will see that the funeral is held early tomorrow. Perhaps Mrs. Troy is right in thinking that we cannot be too considerate toward a dead fellow-creature. We must not forget that, although she may have sinned grievously in leaving her home, she is still our sister; and we may trust that God’s uncovenanted mercies are extended to her, and that she belongs to the flock of Christ.”
source_text: “Very well, then,” said the parson. “And I will arrange that the funeral shall take place early to-morrow. Perhaps Mrs. Troy is right in feeling that we cannot treat a dead fellow-creature too thoughtfully. We must remember that though she may have erred grievously in leaving her home, she is still our sister: and it is to be believed that God’s uncovenanted mercies are extended towards her, and that she is a member of the flock of Christ.”

text: The rain had entirely stopped, and the sun shone through the green, brown, and yellow leaves, which the raindrops had made to glitter and gleam like the varnished splendor of similar effects in the landscapes of Ruysdael and Hobbema, and alive with all those endless beauties born of the mingling of water and color with bright highlights. The air, made so clear by the heavy downpour, rendered the autumn tints of the middle distance as deep and vivid as those close at hand, and the distant fields, cut off by the angle of the tower, seemed to lie on the same plane as the tower itself.
source_text: The rain had quite ceased, and the sun was shining through the green, brown, and yellow leaves, now sparkling and varnished by the raindrops to the brightness of similar effects in the landscapes of Ruysdael and Hobbema, and full of all those infinite beauties that arise from the union of water and colour with high lights. The air was rendered so transparent by the heavy fall of rain that the autumn hues of the middle distance were as rich as those near at hand, and the remote fields intercepted by the angle of the tower appeared in the same plane as the tower itself.

text: “They are somewhat deadly. But now I shall be more diverting, and let you witness a freer display—dealing out cuts and thrusts, infantry and cavalry, faster than lightning, and just as indiscriminately, with only so much order as is needed to guide instinct without shackling it. You are my adversary, though with this difference from actual war: I shall miss you every time by a hair’s breadth, or perhaps two. See that you do not flinch, whatever happens.”
source_text: “They are rather deathly. Now I’ll be more interesting, and let you see some loose play—giving all the cuts and points, infantry and cavalry, quicker than lightning, and as promiscuously—with just enough rule to regulate instinct and yet not to fetter it. You are my antagonist, with this difference from real warfare, that I shall miss you every time by one hair’s breadth, or perhaps two. Mind you don’t flinch, whatever you do.”

It seemed strange indeed to these two people, who knew one another so thoroughly, that merely meeting in a new place and under new conditions should render them so awkward and self-conscious. In the fields, or in her own house, there had never been the least constraint; but now that Oak had become the host, their lives appeared to have slipped backward once more to the time when they had been strangers.
source_text: that formed Oak’s array of household possessions, which sent back a dancing reflection in reply. It was very odd to these two persons, who knew each other passing well, that the mere circumstance of their meeting in a new place and in a new way should make them so awkward and constrained. In the fields, or at her house, there had never been any embarrassment; but now that Oak had become the entertainer their lives seemed to be moved back again to the days when they were strangers.

text: The moment Cain mentioned Bath, they all dropped their hooks and forks and gathered about him. Unfortunately, the erratic crumb did nothing to improve his gift of storytelling, and another inconvenience was a sneeze, which jolted from his pocket his rather large watch, leaving it to swing before the young man like a pendulum.
source_text: Directly Cain mentioned Bath, they all threw down their hooks and forks and drew round him. Unfortunately the erratic crumb did not improve his narrative powers, and a supplementary hindrance was that of a sneeze, jerking from his pocket his rather large watch, which dangled in front of the young man pendulum-wise.

text: He had contrived an attire of a nicely balanced sort, neither over-nice nor slovenly, lying somewhere between a bargain-day finery and a Sunday picked in rain. He scoured his silver watch-chain with whiting till it shone, fitted fresh laces to his boots, inspected the brass eyelets, and then went to the farthest heart of the plantation for a new cane, which he sharply trimmed as he came back. From the bottom of his clothes-chest he drew a clean handkerchief, put on the pale waistcoat scattered all over with little sprigs of an elegant blossom that seemed to unite the graces of rose and lily without the failings of either, and then poured into his normally dry, sandy, and hopelessly curling hair every drop of oil he had. By the time he had done, it was darkened into a wonderfully strange hue, midway between guano and Roman cement, and clung to his head as mace clings about a nutmeg, or wet weed to a rock when the tide has gone out.
source_text: He had made a toilet of a nicely-adjusted kind—of a nature between the carefully neat and the carelessly ornate—of a degree between fine-market-day and wet-Sunday selection. He thoroughly cleaned his silver watch-chain with whiting, put new lacing straps to his boots, looked to the brass eyelet-holes, went to the inmost heart of the plantation for a new walking-stick, and trimmed it vigorously on his way back; took a new handkerchief from the bottom of his clothes-box, put on the light waistcoat patterned all over with sprigs of an elegant flower uniting the beauties of both rose and lily without the defects of either, and used all the hair-oil he possessed upon his usually dry, sandy, and inextricably curly hair, till he had deepened it to a splendidly novel colour, between that of guano and Roman cement, making it stick to his head like mace round a nutmeg, or wet seaweed round a boulder after the ebb.

text: When Bathsheba came back from a two months’ stay with her aged aunt at Norcombe, it gave the fervent and longing farmer a pretext for making direct inquiries after her—she being now perhaps in the ninth month of widowhood—and for trying to divine something of the temper of her feelings toward himself. This happened in the midst of haymaking, and Hale took care to place himself near Liddy, who was at work in the fields.
source_text: Bathsheba’s return from a two months’ visit to her old aunt at Norcombe afforded the impassioned and yearning farmer a pretext for inquiring directly after her—now possibly in the ninth month of her widowhood—and endeavouring to get a notion of her state of mind regarding him. This occurred in the middle of the haymaking, and Boldwood contrived to be near Liddy, who was assisting in the fields.

text: Bathsheba kept walking. The horse was now visible as it came down the hill, and the tedious procession had to be gone through again in reverse: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had been sensible enough to leave the mare with Gabriel and come back on foot himself. The rider drew closer. It was Tall.
source_text: Bathsheba continued walking. The horse was seen descending the hill, and the wearisome series had to be repeated in reverse order: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had had presence of mind enough to give the mare up to Gabriel, and return himself on foot. The rider neared them. It was Tall.

text: “Yes, that is the mischance of it,” said Gabriel, his voice full of distress. “And it is precisely because of that helplessness that I feel obliged to go. Good afternoon, ma’am,” he finished, with a plain eagerness to escape, and at once left the churchyard by a path she could by no means pretend to follow.
source_text: “Yes, that’s the ill fortune o’ it,” said Gabriel, in a distressed tone. “And it is because of that very helplessness that I feel bound to go. Good afternoon, ma’am” he concluded, in evident anxiety to get away, and at once went out of the churchyard by a path she could follow on no pretence whatever.

text: The pedestrian rose, seemingly with renewed resolve, and glanced about. The road looked familiar to her, and she moved slowly along, carefully searching the fence with her eyes. After a little while a faint white form came into view; it was another milestone. She ran her fingers over its face to make out the markings.
source_text: The pedestrian stood up, apparently with revived determination, and looked around. The road appeared to be familiar to her, and she carefully scanned the fence as she slowly walked along. Presently there became visible a dim white shape; it was another milestone. She drew her fingers across its face to feel the marks.

text: Outside the front of Boldwood’s house, a cluster of men stood in the darkness, facing the doorway, which from time to time opened and shut to let a guest or servant pass through. Each time it did, a golden bar of light briefly cut across the ground and then disappeared again, leaving nothing outside except the pale, glowworm glimmer of the lamp among the evergreens above the door.
source_text: Outside the front of Boldwood’s house a group of men stood in the dark, with their faces towards the door, which occasionally opened and closed for the passage of some guest or servant, when a golden rod of light would stripe the ground for the moment and vanish again, leaving nothing outside but the glowworm shine of the pale lamp amid the evergreens over the door.

text: “Very well, I am much obliged to you, Miss Vale,” said Martin at the threshold. “If I am not, I’ll make inquiries.” Martin was somewhat disconcerted by the extraordinary composure of her bearing. Assuredly no one, without some prior knowledge, would have supposed that Martin and the graceful woman before him had ever been anything but perfect strangers. Yet perhaps her manner was the natural consequence of that upward step in fortune which had carried her from a humble cottage to a spacious house and broad acres. The thing is not without precedent among the great. When, in the verses of later singers, Jupiter and his household are discovered to have removed from their narrow lodging on Olympus’s summit into the ample heavens above, their speech commonly acquires a corresponding haughtiness and distance.
source_text: “Quite well, I thank you, Miss Everdene,” said Shepherd Oak from the doorpost. “If I don’t, I’ll inquire.” Gabriel was rather staggered by the remarkable coolness of her manner. Certainly nobody without previous information would have dreamt that Oak and the handsome woman before whom he stood had ever been other than strangers. But perhaps her air was the inevitable result of the social rise which had advanced her from a cottage to a large house and fields. The case is not unexampled in high places. When, in the writings of the later poets, Jove and his family are found to have moved from their cramped quarters on the peak of Olympus into the wide sky above it, their words show a proportionate increase of arrogance and reserve.

text: “I am his lawful wife!” the voice went on, with a still bolder air and emphasis. The lady who uttered it called herself five-and-twenty, appeared to be thirty, was taken for thirty-five, and in fact had reached forty. She was one of those women who never, like some young brides, display any public tenderness for their husbands, perhaps because they have none to display.
source_text: “I be his lawful wife!” continued the voice with greater prominence of manner and tone. This lady called herself five-and-twenty, looked thirty, passed as thirty-five, and was forty. She was a woman who never, like some newly married, showed conjugal tenderness in public, perhaps because she had none to show.

text: After casting a look in this direction, a warning in that, and rebuking one of the younger hands who had let his last shorn sheep wander back among the flock without first marking it again with her initials, Clara returned to Gabriel just as he was setting down his luncheon in order to drag a terrified ewe to the shearing-station, where, with a nimble turn of the arm, he flung it on its back. He clipped away the wool about its head, and opened the throat and collar, while his mistress watched in silence.
source_text: Bathsheba, after throwing a glance here, a caution there, and lecturing one of the younger operators who had allowed his last finished sheep to go off among the flock without re-stamping it with her initials, came again to Gabriel, as he put down the luncheon to drag a frightened ewe to his shear-station, flinging it over upon its back with a dexterous twist of the arm. He lopped off the tresses about its head, and opened up the neck and collar, his mistress quietly looking on.

text: “Now light a candle, Liddy. Which seal shall we use? Here’s a unicorn’s head—there’s nothing there. What’s this? Two doves—no. It ought to be something remarkable, oughtn’t it, Liddy? Here’s one with a motto—I remember it’s a queer one, but I can’t make it out. We’ll try this, and if it won’t do, we’ll choose another.”
source_text: “Now light a candle, Liddy. Which seal shall we use? Here’s a unicorn’s head—there’s nothing in that. What’s this?—two doves—no. It ought to be something extraordinary, ought it not, Liddy? Here’s one with a motto—I remember it is some funny one, but I can’t read it. We’ll try this, and if it doesn’t do we’ll have another.”

text: Immediately after the incident in the tent, she rose to take her leave—now thoroughly alarmed, yet truly thankful for the protection of her old lover—though she still regretted Gabriel’s absence, whose company she would have preferred, as it was both more fitting and more agreeable, he being her own bailiff and servant. Still, nothing could be done about that; and on no account would she treat Boldwood cruelly, having already wronged him once before. So, with the moon up and the gig ready, she drove across the hilltop by the winding road that led downward—to a kind of dark oblivion, it seemed, for the moon and the hill it bathed in light appeared to stand on the same level, while all the world besides lay below in a great shaded hollow between them. Boldwood mounted his horse and kept close behind her. In this way they went down into the lowlands, while the voices of those left upon the hill sounded like voices from the sky, and the lights there looked like a camp in heaven. They soon passed the cheerful stragglers near the hill, crossed through Kingsbere, and reached the high road.
source_text: Immediately after the incident in the tent, she had risen to go—now absolutely alarmed and really grateful for her old lover’s protection—though regretting Gabriel’s absence, whose company she would have much preferred, as being more proper as well as more pleasant, since he was her own managing-man and servant. This, however, could not be helped; she would not, on any consideration, treat Boldwood harshly, having once already ill-used him, and the moon having risen, and the gig being ready, she drove across the hilltop in the wending way’s which led downwards—to oblivious obscurity, as it seemed, for the moon and the hill it flooded with light were in appearance on a level, the rest of the world lying as a vast shady concave between them. Boldwood mounted his horse, and followed in close attendance behind. Thus they descended into the lowlands, and the sounds of those left on the hill came like voices from the sky, and the lights were as those of a camp in heaven. They soon passed the merry stragglers in the immediate vicinity of the hill, traversed Kingsbere, and got upon the high road.

text: Through a partly opened door, the rasping of a scrubbing brush announced the charwoman, Maryann Money, a woman whose face was like a round disc, scored less by years than by the long, bewildered stare with which she had regarded distant things. To think of her was to feel good-humoured; to speak of her was to call up the image of a shrivelled Normandy pippin.
source_text: Through a partly-opened door the noise of a scrubbing-brush led up to the charwoman, Maryann Money, a person who for a face had a circular disc, furrowed less by age than by long gazes of perplexity at distant objects. To think of her was to get good-humoured; to speak of her was to raise the image of a dried Normandy pippin.

text: The pool on the grave had seeped down into the earth, leaving behind a hollow. The churned soil was spread across the grass and path in the form of the brown mud he had already noticed, and the marble headstone was marked with the same stains. Nearly all the flowers had been washed free of the ground, and they lay there with their roots turned upward, where the stream had flung them.
source_text: The pool upon the grave had soaked away into the ground, and in its place was a hollow. The disturbed earth was washed over the grass and pathway in the guise of the brown mud he had already seen, and it spotted the marble tombstone with the same stains. Nearly all the flowers were washed clean out of the ground, and they lay, roots upwards, on the spots whither they had been splashed by the stream.

text: “He’s the sort of man who is dependable in his own reckless way, you know. That is better than being like some people, reckless in a dependable way. I’m afraid that’s what I am. And promise me you’ll keep my secret—please, Liddy! Don’t let them know that I’ve been crying over him, because it would be terrible for me, and no help to him, poor dear!”
source_text: “He is a sort of steady man in a wild way, you know. That’s better than to be as some are, wild in a steady way. I am afraid that’s how I am. And promise me to keep my secret—do, Liddy! And do not let them know that I have been crying about him, because it will be dreadful for me, and no good to him, poor thing!”

text: Bathsheba let herself dwell on the possibility of escaping her situation through immediate death, and she thought that, though it was a dreadful and inconvenient means, its dreadfulness and inconvenience were at least bounded, unlike the limitless shame of living. Yet even this notion of self-annihilation was only a faint imitation of her rival’s act, and lacked the motives that had made that act noble in the rival’s case. She moved quickly to and fro across the room, as she usually did when her feelings were stirred, her clasped hands hanging before her, while she thought, and half spoke aloud in fragments: “Oh, I hate her—though I don’t mean that I truly hate her, for that would be sinful and cruel; and yet I do hate her a little! Yes, my body insists on hating her, whether my soul agrees or not!... If only she were alive, I could have been angry and harsh with some right on my side; but to be spiteful toward a poor dead woman turns back on me instead. O God, be merciful to me! I am wretched over all this!”
source_text: Bathsheba indulged in contemplations of escape from her position by immediate death, which, thought she, though it was an inconvenient and awful way, had limits to its inconvenience and awfulness that could not be overpassed; whilst the shames of life were measureless. Yet even this scheme of extinction by death was but tamely copying her rival’s method without the reasons which had glorified it in her rival’s case. She glided rapidly up and down the room, as was mostly her habit when excited, her hands hanging clasped in front of her, as she thought and in part expressed in broken words: “O, I hate her, yet I don’t mean that I hate her, for it is grievous and wicked; and yet I hate her a little! Yes, my flesh insists upon hating her, whether my spirit is willing or no!... If she had only lived, I could have been angry and cruel towards her with some justification; but to be vindictive towards a poor dead woman recoils upon myself. O God, have mercy! I am miserable at all this!”

text: Maryann, who had been too afraid to cry out while the robber stood there, now that she had seen him go, felt no fear at all. She hurriedly dressed, clattered down the broken staircase, which gave a hundred creaks beneath her feet, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and sounded the alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who was once more lodging in his house as he had at first, and the two of them went together to the paddock. There could be no question of it: the horse had been taken.
source_text: Maryann, who had been afraid to shout in the robber’s presence, having seen him depart had no fear. She hastily slipped on her clothes, stumped down the disjointed staircase with its hundred creaks, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and raised an alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who now again lodged in his house as at first, and together they went to the paddock. Beyond all doubt the horse was gone.

text: Henery went on in a milder temper: “I once put my mind to her about a few matters, as near as a battered body dared to such a wilful creature. You all know, neighbors, what sort of man I am, and how I can lay about with my strong words when my pride is on the boil wi’ scorn?”
source_text: Henery continued in a more complaisant mood: “I once hinted my mind to her on a few things, as nearly as a battered frame dared to do so to such a froward piece. You all know, neighbours, what a man I be, and how I come down with my powerful words when my pride is boiling wi’ scarn?”

text: Bathsheba was not aware of any deceit in this matter. In one sense she was a woman of the world, but it was the world of open daylight and green lawns, where cattle made up the passing crowd and the wind was the main murmur; where a quiet family of rabbits or hares lived on the far side of the party wall, where everyone in the parish counted as your neighbor, and where reckoning was mostly reserved for market days. She knew very little of the artificial tastes of polished fashionable society, and nothing at all of the studied self-indulgence of the bad. If her deepest thoughts on the subject had ever been clearly put into words, even by herself they never were, they would have amounted only to this: that she found impulse a pleasanter guide than judgment. Her love was whole as a child’s, and though as warm as summer, it was as fresh as spring. Her fault was that she made no effort to check feeling by any subtle, careful weighing of consequences. She could point out the steep and thorny road to others, yet “reck’d not her own rede.”
source_text: Bathsheba was not conscious of guile in this matter. Though in one sense a woman of the world, it was, after all, that world of daylight coteries and green carpets wherein cattle form the passing crowd and winds the busy hum; where a quiet family of rabbits or hares lives on the other side of your party-wall, where your neighbour is everybody in the tything, and where calculation is confined to market-days. Of the fabricated tastes of good fashionable society she knew but little, and of the formulated self-indulgence of bad, nothing at all. Had her utmost thoughts in this direction been distinctly worded (and by herself they never were), they would only have amounted to such a matter as that she felt her impulses to be pleasanter guides than her discretion. Her love was entire as a child’s, and though warm as summer it was fresh as spring. Her culpability lay in her making no attempt to control feeling by subtle and careful inquiry into consequences. She could show others the steep and thorny way, but “reck’d not her own rede.”

text: Bathsheba was for the moment freed from that fitful heaviness of the previous twenty-four hours, a burden that had drained the life of youth in her without replacing it with the calmer wisdom of older years, and she decided to go out and walk a little. So, after breakfast, she put on her bonnet and set off in the direction of the church. It was nine o’clock, and as the men had already gone back to work after their first meal, she was unlikely to meet many of them on the road. Knowing that Fanny had been buried in the quarter of the graveyard reserved for the reprobates, known in the parish as “behind church,” and hidden from the road, she could not resist the urge to go in and look at a place she, for reasons she could not name, was equally afraid to see. She had not been able to shake the feeling that some link existed between her rival and the light among the trees.
