source_text: Bathsheba was momentarily relieved of that wayward heaviness of the past twenty-four hours which had quenched the vitality of youth in her without substituting the philosophy of maturer years, and she resolved to go out and walk a little way. So when breakfast was over, she put on her bonnet, and took a direction towards the church. It was nine o’clock, and the men having returned to work again from their first meal, she was not likely to meet many of them in the road. Knowing that Fanny had been laid in the reprobates’ quarter of the graveyard, called in the parish “behind church,” which was invisible from the road, it was impossible to resist the impulse to enter and look upon a spot which, from nameless feelings, she at the same time dreaded to see. She had been unable to overcome an impression that some connection existed between her rival and the light through the trees.

text: It was a cloudy, close, and very dark night, and the glow from Troy’s lantern spread through the two ancient yews with a strange, almost magical effect, seeming to flicker upward to the black canopy of cloud above. He felt a large raindrop strike the back of his hand, and presently another found its way into one of the lantern’s holes, whereupon the candle spluttered and went out. Troy was tired, and as it was now not far from midnight, with the rain threatening to grow heavier, he decided to leave the final touches of his work until daylight returned. He groped his way along the wall and over the graves in the darkness until he found himself on the north side. There he went into the porch and, leaning back on the bench inside, fell asleep.
source_text: It was a cloudy, muggy, and very dark night, and the rays from Troy’s lantern spread into the two old yews with a strange illuminating power, flickering, as it seemed, up to the black ceiling of cloud above. He felt a large drop of rain upon the back of his hand, and presently one came and entered one of the holes of the lantern, whereupon the candle sputtered and went out. Troy was weary and it being now not far from midnight, and the rain threatening to increase, he resolved to leave the finishing touches of his labour until the day should break. He groped along the wall and over the graves in the dark till he found himself round at the north side. Here he entered the porch, and, reclining upon the bench within, fell asleep.

text: “I’m afraid I’ve cut a hole with my tongue that my heart will never be able to repair. Oh, these unbearable times: that a man should come to misfortune merely for honestly telling a woman she is beautiful! That was the most I said—you must admit it; and the least I could have said, which I freely admit is true of myself.”
source_text: “I am afraid I have made a hole with my tongue that my heart will never mend. O these intolerable times: that ill-luck should follow a man for honestly telling a woman she is beautiful! ’Twas the most I said—you must own that; and the least I could say—that I own myself.”

text: He stood there brooding—a wretched man. Where was he to go now? “Let him that is accursed remain accursed,” seemed the ruthless sentence written in this ruined proof of his newly awakened concern. A man who has used up his earliest strength moving in one direction has little spirit left for turning back. Troy had, since yesterday, begun to turn faintly in the opposite direction; but the least resistance had discouraged him. It would have been difficult enough to reverse himself even with the strongest encouragement from Providence; but to discover that Providence, instead of aiding him into a new path, or even appearing to wish that he might take one, actually mocked his first tremulous and crucial effort to do so, was more than human nature could endure.
source_text: He stood and meditated—a miserable man. Whither should he go? “He that is accursed, let him be accursed still,” was the pitiless anathema written in this spoliated effort of his new-born solicitousness. A man who has spent his primal strength in journeying in one direction has not much spirit left for reversing his course. Troy had, since yesterday, faintly reversed his; but the merest opposition had disheartened him. To turn about would have been hard enough under the greatest providential encouragement; but to find that Providence, far from helping him into a new course, or showing any wish that he might adopt one, actually jeered his first trembling and critical attempt in that kind, was more than nature could bear.

text: When Marlowe had retired for the night, he set the valentine in the angle of the mirror. Even with his face turned away from it, he remained aware of its presence. Never before in his life had any such thing befallen him. The same enchantment that led him to believe the act must have been meant for a purpose kept him from treating it as an insult. He looked once more at the address. The dim powers of the night endowed the handwriting with the living nearness of the unknown sender. Someone’s—some _woman’s_—hand had moved with quiet touch across the sheet that bore his name; her hidden eyes had followed every line as she traced it; her mind, while doing so, had pictured him. Why had she pictured him? Her mouth—were the lips scarlet or colorless, full or finely wrinkled?—must have shaped itself to some peculiar expression as the pen advanced, the corners of it stirring with all their natural softness: what expression had it been?
source_text: When Boldwood went to bed he placed the valentine in the corner of the looking-glass. He was conscious of its presence, even when his back was turned upon it. It was the first time in Boldwood’s life that such an event had occurred. The same fascination that caused him to think it an act which had a deliberate motive prevented him from regarding it as an impertinence. He looked again at the direction. The mysterious influences of night invested the writing with the presence of the unknown writer. Somebody’s—some _woman’s_—hand had travelled softly over the paper bearing his name; her unrevealed eyes had watched every curve as she formed it; her brain had seen him in imagination the while. Why should she have imagined him? Her mouth—were the lips red or pale, plump or creased?—had curved itself to a certain expression as the pen went on—the corners had moved with all their natural tremulousness: what had been the expression?

text: As soon as the tent was finished, the band broke into lively, stirring music, and an announcement was made aloud. Black Bess stood prominently outside as living proof, if any proof were needed, of the truth of the prophetic words spoken from the stage beneath which the crowd was to pass inside. The people were so persuaded by these sincere appeals to both feeling and reason that they quickly began streaming in thickly; among the first to be seen were Jan Coggan and Joseph Poorgrass, who were taking their holiday here that day.
source_text: As soon as the tent was completed the band struck up highly stimulating harmonies, and the announcement was publicly made, Black Bess standing in a conspicuous position on the outside, as a living proof, if proof were wanted, of the truth of the oracular utterances from the stage over which the people were to enter. These were so convinced by such genuine appeals to heart and understanding both that they soon began to crowd in abundantly, among the foremost being visible Jan Coggan and Joseph Poorgrass, who were holiday keeping here to-day.

text: This image of the present set in a frame four hundred years old did not yield that sharp opposition between antique and modern which the difference of date might suggest. Measured against towns, Radbourne was changeless. What the townsman calls Then is for the countryman only Now. In London, twenty or thirty years are already matters of antiquity; in Paris, ten, or even five; in Radbourne, three or four generations still belonged to the living moment, and nothing short of a century could leave a visible trace upon its aspect or its speech. Fifty years scarcely altered the cut of a gaiter or the edging of a smock-frock by so much as a hair’s breadth. Ten generations failed to change the turn of a single saying. In these Wessex corners the bustling stranger’s ancient times are merely old; his old times are still young; his present is yet to come.
source_text: This picture of to-day in its frame of four hundred years ago did not produce that marked contrast between ancient and modern which is implied by the contrast of date. In comparison with cities, Weatherbury was immutable. The citizen’s _Then_ is the rustic’s _Now_. In London, twenty or thirty-years ago are old times; in Paris ten years, or five; in Weatherbury three or four score years were included in the mere present, and nothing less than a century set a mark on its face or tone. Five decades hardly modified the cut of a gaiter, the embroidery of a smock-frock, by the breadth of a hair. Ten generations failed to alter the turn of a single phrase. In these Wessex nooks the busy outsider’s ancient times are only old; his old times are still new; his present is futurity.

text: “That is unfortunate,” said Farmer Oak, gazing sadly at a crack in the stone floor. “I am only an ordinary sort of man, and my one chance lay in being the first to come.... Well, there is no purpose in my waiting, for that was all I came for; so I shall take myself off home, Mrs. Hurst.”
source_text: “That’s unfortunate,” said Farmer Oak, contemplating a crack in the stone floor with sorrow. “I’m only an every-day sort of man, and my only chance was in being the first comer.... Well, there’s no use in my waiting, for that was all I came about: so I’ll take myself off home-along, Mrs. Hurst.”

text: When he reached Casterbridge, he left the horse and trap at an inn, then, five minutes before ten, returned to the bridge at the lower end of the town and took a seat on the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and Fanny did not appear. At that very moment, in fact, she was being dressed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last dressers the gentle girl had ever been privileged to have. The quarter hour passed, then the half hour. As Troy waited, a flood of memory came over him: this was the second time she had failed to keep a serious appointment with him. In anger, he swore it would be the last. By eleven o’clock, after he had sat there long enough to study the bridge stones until he knew every patch of lichen on their faces and to hear the ring of the ripples beneath them until the sound became unbearable, he sprang up, went back to the inn for his gig, and, in a bitter mood of indifference toward the past and carelessness about the future, drove on to the Budmouth races.
source_text: On reaching Casterbridge he left the horse and trap at an inn, and at five minutes before ten came back to the bridge at the lower end of the town, and sat himself upon the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and no Fanny appeared. In fact, at that moment she was being robed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last tiring-women the gentle creature had ever been honoured with. The quarter went, the half hour. A rush of recollection came upon Troy as he waited: this was the second time she had broken a serious engagement with him. In anger he vowed it should be the last, and at eleven o’clock, when he had lingered and watched the stone of the bridge till he knew every lichen upon their face and heard the chink of the ripples underneath till they oppressed him, he jumped from his seat, went to the inn for his gig, and in a bitter mood of indifference concerning the past, and recklessness about the future, drove on to Budmouth races.

text: He said at once, and with greater bitterness, “That may be true, in part; but ah, Miss Everdene, it is no reason. You are not the cold woman you would have me think you are. No, no! It is not because you have no feeling that you do not love me. You would have me believe that, of course—you would conceal from me that you have a heart as fiercely alive as mine. You have enough love, only it has been carried into a different current. I know where.”
source_text: He immediately said with more resentment: “That may be true, somewhat; but ah, Miss Everdene, it won’t do as a reason! You are not the cold woman you would have me believe. No, no! It isn’t because you have no feeling in you that you don’t love me. You naturally would have me think so—you would hide from me that you have a burning heart like mine. You have love enough, but it is turned into a new channel. I know where.”

text: “Ah, Baily, she’s a cut above you, and you must admit it: a higher sort of creature, made of finer stuff. Still, keep close to me, and neither this proud goddess, this splendid creature of a wife of mine—Juno, you know, was a goddess—or anyone else shall do you any harm. But I see this needs careful looking into. One thing and another, and I can tell my hands are full.”
source_text: “Ah, baily, she’s a notch above you, and you must own it: a higher class of animal—a finer tissue. However, stick to me, and neither this haughty goddess, dashing piece of womanhood, Juno-wife of mine (Juno was a goddess, you know), nor anybody else shall hurt you. But all this wants looking into, I perceive. What with one thing and another, I see that my work is well cut out for me.”

text: All understood this to be the end of some dismal speech that had been uttered only in thought during the shake of the head; meanwhile Henery still wore several signs of despair upon his face, as though to show they would be needed again the moment he resumed speaking.
source_text: This was recognized by all as the conclusion of some gloomy speech which had been expressed in thought alone during the shake of the head; Henery meanwhile retained several marks of despair upon his face, to imply that they would be required for use again directly he should go on speaking.

text: “I’ll tell ’ee how we could manage her,” said Coggan. “I’ll knock and ask to speak to Laban outside the door, with you standing back a bit. Then he’ll come out, and you can tell yer story. She’ll never guess what I want him for; and I’ll make up a few words about the farm-work, as a blind.”
source_text: “I’ll tell ’ee how we could tackle her,” said Coggan. “I’ll knock and ask to speak to Laban outside the door, you standing in the background. Then he’ll come out, and you can tell yer tale. She’ll never guess what I want en for; and I’ll make up a few words about the farm-work, as a blind.”

text: His house stood back from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, lay behind it, their lower parts hidden among laurel bushes. Through the blue door, opened halfway, one could then see the backs and tails of half a dozen warm, contented horses standing in their stalls; viewed in this way, they showed alternate patches of roan and bay, shaped like a Moorish arch, with the tail forming a streak down the center of each. Above them, and beyond the sight of anyone looking in from the outer light, the animals’ mouths could be heard busily maintaining that warmth and plumpness with oats and hay in generous measure. A restless, shadowy colt moved about a loose box at the far end, while the steady grinding of the feeders was at intervals broken by the rattle of a rope or the stamping of a hoof.
source_text: His house stood recessed from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, were behind, their lower portions being lost amid bushes of laurel. Inside the blue door, open half-way down, were to be seen at this time the backs and tails of half-a-dozen warm and contented horses standing in their stalls; and as thus viewed, they presented alternations of roan and bay, in shapes like a Moorish arch, the tail being a streak down the midst of each. Over these, and lost to the eye gazing in from the outer light, the mouths of the same animals could be heard busily sustaining the above-named warmth and plumpness by quantities of oats and hay. The restless and shadowy figure of a colt wandered about a loose-box at the end, whilst the steady grind of all the eaters was occasionally diversified by the rattle of a rope or the stamp of a foot.

text: “But it isn’t quite that, exactly. If a man alters his name and the rest of it, and contrives to deceive the world and even his own wife, he’s a fraud, and in the eyes of the law a sheer rogue, and by that reckoning a downright truant vagabond; and that is a matter fit to be punished.”
source_text: “But that’s not it, exactly. If a man changes his name and so forth, and takes steps to deceive the world and his own wife, he’s a cheat, and that in the eye of the law is ayless a rogue, and that is ayless a lammocken vagabond; and that’s a punishable situation.”

text: For a long while the woman kept walking. Her steps grew weaker, and she peered ahead with aching eyes at the bare road, which had now become blurred in the half-darkness of night. At last her forward movement sank into little more than a stagger, and she opened a gate where there was a haystack. She sat down beneath it and soon fell asleep.
source_text: For a considerable time the woman walked on. Her steps became feebler, and she strained her eyes to look afar upon the naked road, now indistinct amid the penumbræ of night. At length her onward walk dwindled to the merest totter, and she opened a gate within which was a haystack. Underneath this she sat down and presently slept.

text: “Ah, it is not the thing itself, but the manner of it, that wounds you,” he said with an air of indifference. “Yet I have at least the melancholy comfort of knowing that what I say, whether agreeable or not, is plain truth. Would you have had me gaze at you, and then assure my acquaintance that you were a perfectly ordinary woman, merely to spare you the awkwardness of being looked at should they draw near? Not I. I could not for the sake of anyone utter so absurd a falsehood about a beauty, nor by such means help even one woman in England into an excess of modesty.”
source_text: “Ah—it is not the fact but the method which offends you,” he said, carelessly. “But I have the sad satisfaction of knowing that my words, whether pleasing or offensive, are unmistakably true. Would you have had me look at you, and tell my acquaintance that you are quite a common-place woman, to save you the embarrassment of being stared at if they come near you? Not I. I couldn’t tell any such ridiculous lie about a beauty to encourage a single woman in England in too excessive a modesty.”

text: The following morning she was up earlier than was her wont, and ordered the horse to be saddled for her customary circuit of the farm. When she returned at half-past eight, the hour at which they ordinarily breakfasted, she learned that her husband had already risen, eaten his meal, and driven away to Casterbridge in the gig with Poppet.
source_text: The next morning she rose earlier than usual, and had the horse saddled for her ride round the farm in the customary way. When she came in at half-past eight—their usual hour for breakfasting—she was informed that her husband had risen, taken his breakfast, and driven off to Casterbridge with the gig and Poppet.

text: His judgment and his inclinations had long since ceased to act in concert, having parted, as it were, by mutual agreement many years before; and so it came about that, although his purposes were of the purest kind, some single act would sometimes furnish a shadowy backdrop against which they shone out with uncommon brightness. The corporal’s corrupt moods, being born of sudden impulse, and his better ones the fruit of deliberate reflection, the latter possessed only a quiet habit of being more often spoken of than actually observed.
source_text: His reason and his propensities had seldom any reciprocating influence, having separated by mutual consent long ago: thence it sometimes happened that, while his intentions were as honourable as could be wished, any particular deed formed a dark background which threw them into fine relief. The sergeant’s vicious phases being the offspring of impulse, and his virtuous phases of cool meditation, the latter had a modest tendency to be oftener heard of than seen.

text: “Yes—I know that,” she said, breathing fast like a robin, her face flushed and damp from her exertions, like a peony petal before the sun has dried the dew from it. “I didn’t know you had come to ask for me, or I would have come in from the garden at once. I ran after you to say that my aunt made a mistake in sending you away when you were courting me—”
source_text: “Yes—I know that,” she said panting like a robin, her face red and moist from her exertions, like a peony petal before the sun dries off the dew. “I didn’t know you had come to ask to have me, or I should have come in from the garden instantly. I ran after you to say—that my aunt made a mistake in sending you away from courting me—”

text: Through the whole of that day, Bathsheba and Liddy remained shut up in the attic as if under siege, though the precaution turned out to be needless so far as Troy was concerned, since he neither came near the place nor vexed them in any way. Bathsheba sat by the window until sunset, now and then making a pretence of reading, at other moments following every stir without much aim and hearkening, with little concern, to each passing sound.
source_text: All that day Bathsheba and Liddy lived in the attic in a state of barricade; a precaution which proved to be needless as against Troy, for he did not appear in the neighbourhood or trouble them at all. Bathsheba sat at the window till sunset, sometimes attempting to read, at other times watching every movement outside without much purpose, and listening without much interest to every sound.

text: Cheerful voices were heard that morning in the chambers above, reached by the chief stairway, whose steps of seasoned oak turned upward with a singular twist, the balusters, as massive as bed-posts, being carved and shaped in the odd manner of an older age, and the handrail itself as broad and sturdy as the coping of a wall, while the staircase seemed forever to wind upon itself, like one peering back over a shoulder. On mounting, the upper floors were found to have a strangely uneven surface, swelling into little ridges and falling away into hollows; and, as they were then without carpets, the wood of the boards could be seen to be worn into countless tiny worm-like channels. Every window answered the opening and shutting of every door with a sharp clash; every hurried movement sent a shiver through the place; and a creaking sound kept pace with any one who walked about the house, as though a ghost were attending him at every step.
source_text: Lively voices were heard this morning in the upper rooms, the main staircase to which was of hard oak, the balusters, heavy as bed-posts, being turned and moulded in the quaint fashion of their century, the handrail as stout as a parapet-top, and the stairs themselves continually twisting round like a person trying to look over his shoulder. Going up, the floors above were found to have a very irregular surface, rising to ridges, sinking into valleys; and being just then uncarpeted, the face of the boards was seen to be eaten into innumerable vermiculations. Every window replied by a clang to the opening and shutting of every door, a tremble followed every bustling movement, and a creak accompanied a walker about the house, like a spirit, wherever he went.

text: She watched him bend toward the turf, gather up the curling strand he had cut from her abundant hair, wind it about his fingers, undo a button at the breast of his coat, and with grave care place it within. She felt no strength left in her to resist or refuse him. He was altogether beyond her, and Clara seemed like one who, meeting a freshening wind, finds it rise so fiercely that it checks the breath. He came closer and said, “I must leave you now.”
source_text: She saw him stoop to the grass, pick up the winding lock which he had severed from her manifold tresses, twist it round his fingers, unfasten a button in the breast of his coat, and carefully put it inside. She felt powerless to withstand or deny him. He was altogether too much for her, and Bathsheba seemed as one who, facing a reviving wind, finds it blow so strongly that it stops the breath. He drew near and said, “I must be leaving you.”

text: At length the eighth day arrived. The cow had ceased to yield her milk for the season, and Miss Elinor Marsh came no more up the rise. Rowan had reached a state of being he could never, a little while earlier, have imagined for himself. He had taken to murmuring “Elinor” in private as a pleasure instead of whistling; had transferred his fancy to black hair, though since boyhood he had sworn by brown; and had so shut himself away that the place he occupied in the world’s regard was pitifully small. Love can be a latent strength within a real infirmity. Marriage turns a wandering thought into a prop, whose force ought to be, and fortunately often is, directly measured by the folly it displaces. Rowan began now to discern a gleam in this direction, and said within himself, “I’ll make her my wife, or, by heaven, I shall be good for nothing!”
source_text: At last the eighth day came. The cow had ceased to give milk for that year, and Bathsheba Everdene came up the hill no more. Gabriel had reached a pitch of existence he never could have anticipated a short time before. He liked saying “Bathsheba” as a private enjoyment instead of whistling; turned over his taste to black hair, though he had sworn by brown ever since he was a boy, isolated himself till the space he filled in the public eye was contemptibly small. Love is a possible strength in an actual weakness. Marriage transforms a distraction into a support, the power of which should be, and happily often is, in direct proportion to the degree of imbecility it supplants. Oak began now to see light in this direction, and said to himself, “I’ll make her my wife, or upon my soul I shall be good for nothing!”

text: Bathsheba actually sat down and cried most bitterly over this letter. She felt wronged and hurt that Gabriel’s hopeless love, which she had come to regard as her lifelong and unquestionable due, should be taken away in this manner, simply at his own whim. She was bewildered too at the thought of having to depend once more on her own efforts: it seemed to her that she could never again muster enough energy to go to market, haggle, and sell. Since Troy’s death, Oak had been attending all the sales and fairs for her, managing her affairs at the same time as his own. What was she to do now? Her life was growing into a kind of desolation.
source_text: Bathsheba actually sat and cried over this letter most bitterly. She was aggrieved and wounded that the possession of hopeless love from Gabriel, which she had grown to regard as her inalienable right for life, should have been withdrawn just at his own pleasure in this way. She was bewildered too by the prospect of having to rely on her own resources again: it seemed to herself that she never could again acquire energy sufficient to go to market, barter, and sell. Since Troy’s death Oak had attended all sales and fairs for her, transacting her business at the same time with his own. What should she do now? Her life was becoming a desolation.

text: “Well, let us say no more of it—no more. One thing is certain: you were almost mine, and now you are scarcely mine at all. All is altered, and altered by you alone, remember that. Once you meant nothing to me, and I was content; now you mean nothing to me again, and how unlike this second nothing is to the first! Would to God you had never raised me up, if only to cast me down!”
source_text: “Well, never mind arguing—never mind. One thing is sure: you were all but mine, and now you are not nearly mine. Everything is changed, and that by you alone, remember. You were nothing to me once, and I was contented; you are now nothing to me again, and how different the second nothing is from the first! Would to God you had never taken me up, since it was only to throw me down!”

text: Half an hour later Bathsheba, fully dressed and followed by Liddy, came into the upper end of the old hall and found that all her men had taken their places on a long bench and a settle at the far end. She seated herself at a table and opened the day-book, a pen in her hand, with a canvas money bag beside her. From this she tipped out a small pile of coins. Liddy settled herself at Bathsheba’s elbow and began to sew, now and then pausing to glance about, or, with the manner of one granted special privileges, lifting one of the half-sovereigns laid before her and examining it purely as an object of beauty, while carefully keeping her face from showing the least desire to have it as money.
source_text: Half-an-hour later Bathsheba, in finished dress, and followed by Liddy, entered the upper end of the old hall to find that her men had all deposited themselves on a long form and a settle at the lower extremity. She sat down at a table and opened the time-book, pen in her hand, with a canvas money-bag beside her. From this she poured a small heap of coin. Liddy chose a position at her elbow and began to sew, sometimes pausing and looking round, or, with the air of a privileged person, taking up one of the half-sovereigns lying before her and surveying it merely as a work of art, while strictly preventing her countenance from expressing any wish to possess it as money.

text: She restrained her feeling, looked him steadily and clearly in the face, and said in her low, resolute voice, “Mr. Boldwood, I promised you nothing. Would you have wished me to be a creature of clay when you paid me that furthest, highest compliment a man can offer a woman—by telling her that he loves her? I was bound to show some feeling, if I meant not to be an ungrateful shrew. Yet each of those pleasures belonged only to the day—the day to the pleasure. How was I to know that what is but a pastime to other men was death to you? Use your reason, I beg you, and think more kindly of me!”
source_text: She checked emotion, looked him quietly and clearly in the face, and said in her low, firm voice, “Mr. Boldwood, I promised you nothing. Would you have had me a woman of clay when you paid me that furthest, highest compliment a man can pay a woman—telling her he loves her? I was bound to show some feeling, if I would not be a graceless shrew. Yet each of those pleasures was just for the day—the day just for the pleasure. How was I to know that what is a pastime to all other men was death to you? Have reason, do, and think more kindly of me!”

text: Poorgrass, thus reassured, let out a wavering but respectable bit of feeling, the tune made up of the keynote and one other note, the latter being the one most heavily emphasized. It went so well that, after a few false starts, he recklessly launched into a second in the same breath:—
source_text: Poorgrass, thus assured, trilled forth a flickering yet commendable piece of sentiment, the tune of which consisted of the key-note and another, the latter being the sound chiefly dwelt upon. This was so successful that he rashly plunged into a second in the same breath, after a few false starts:—

text: Then he crossed to a fastened cupboard, and from a fastened drawer within it took a little round case, no larger than a pillbox, and was on the point of slipping it into his pocket. Yet he paused, detaining it just long enough to lift the lid and cast a fleeting look within. There lay a woman’s ring, encircled all around with small diamonds, and from its look it had plainly been but lately bought. His eyes rested for a long while upon its many glances, though that the thing itself had little hold upon his thoughts was evident from his bearing and countenance, which were those of a man whose mind was tracing, as he supposed, the future history of that jewel.
source_text: Then he went to a locked closet, and took from a locked drawer therein a small circular case the size of a pillbox, and was about to put it into his pocket. But he lingered to open the cover and take a momentary glance inside. It contained a woman’s finger-ring, set all the way round with small diamonds, and from its appearance had evidently been recently purchased. Boldwood’s eyes dwelt upon its many sparkles a long time, though that its material aspect concerned him little was plain from his manner and mien, which were those of a mind following out the presumed thread of that jewel’s future history.

text: “He isn’t bad at all.... My poor heart and miserable life, how weak I am!” she moaned, speaking lazily and aimlessly, quite forgetting that Liddy was there. “Oh, if I had never laid eyes on him! Loving is always misery for women. I shall never forgive God for making me a woman, and I’m beginning to pay dearly for the privilege of having a pretty face.” She stirred, then turned sharply to Liddy. “Mind this, Lydia Smallbury: if you ever repeat a single word of what I’ve said to you in this closed door, I will never trust you, or love you, or have you with me another moment—not one moment!”
source_text: “He is not bad at all.... My poor life and heart, how weak I am!” she moaned, in a relaxed, desultory way, heedless of Liddy’s presence. “Oh, how I wish I had never seen him! Loving is misery for women always. I shall never forgive God for making me a woman, and dearly am I beginning to pay for the honour of owning a pretty face.” She freshened and turned to Liddy suddenly. “Mind this, Lydia Smallbury, if you repeat anywhere a single word of what I have said to you inside this closed door, I’ll never trust you, or love you, or have you with me a moment longer—not a moment!”

text: One night, after Farmer Oak had come home, thinking there would be no more need of his services on the down, he called, as usual, to the dogs before shutting them in the outhouse until morning. Only one answered—the old dog George; the other was nowhere to be found, neither in the house, nor in the lane, nor in the garden. Gabriel then remembered that he had left the two dogs on the hill over a dead lamb—a sort of food he generally withheld from them unless other provisions were failing—and, supposing that the younger one had not yet finished his supper, he went indoors to the luxury of a bed, an indulgence he had lately enjoyed only on Sundays.
source_text: One night, when Farmer Oak had returned to his house, believing there would be no further necessity for his attendance on the down, he called as usual to the dogs, previously to shutting them up in the outhouse till next morning. Only one responded—old George; the other could not be found, either in the house, lane, or garden. Gabriel then remembered that he had left the two dogs on the hill eating a dead lamb (a kind of meat he usually kept from them, except when other food ran short), and concluding that the young one had not finished his meal, he went indoors to the luxury of a bed, which latterly he had only enjoyed on Sundays.

text: He stayed on and on until there was no longer any distinction between the sky to the east and the sky to the west, and the shy hares had begun to move with hesitant boldness around the fading hillocks. Gabriel may have been there for another half-hour when a dark figure came slowly past. “Good night, Gabriel,” the man said as he went by.
source_text: He lingered and lingered on, till there was no difference between the eastern and western expanses of sky, and the timid hares began to limp courageously round the dim hillocks. Gabriel might have been there an additional half-hour when a dark form walked slowly by. “Good-night, Gabriel,” the passer said.

text: She was standing behind a low armchair, from which she had only just risen; he was kneeling in it, leaning over its back toward her and holding her hand between both of his. His body kept shifting uneasily, and it was with what Keats so delicately calls a “too happy happiness.” This unusual stripping away by love of all dignity from a man in whom that dignity had always seemed the principal element was, in its troubling unfitness, painful to her, and it dulled much of the pleasure she took in the proof that she was adored.
source_text: She was standing behind a low arm-chair, from which she had just risen, and he was kneeling in it—inclining himself over its back towards her, and holding her hand in both his own. His body moved restlessly, and it was with what Keats daintily calls a too happy happiness. This unwonted abstraction by love of all dignity from a man of whom it had ever seemed the chief component, was, in its distressing incongruity, a pain to her which quenched much of the pleasure she derived from the proof that she was idolized.

text: He gave the blade a flourish, as though making a second introduction, and the next thing she knew the point and edge were flashing toward her left flank, just above the hip; then they seemed to vanish and reappear at her right, coming out, as it were, between her ribs, as though they had driven clean through her body. The third thing of which she became aware was the same sword, perfectly bright and unstained, held upright in Lucian’s hand in the position known, in the craft, as “recover swords.” It had all happened with the swiftness of lightning.
source_text: He flourished the sword by way of introduction number two, and the next thing of which she was conscious was that the point and blade of the sword were darting with a gleam towards her left side, just above her hip; then of their reappearance on her right side, emerging as it were from between her ribs, having apparently passed through her body. The third item of consciousness was that of seeing the same sword, perfectly clean and free from blood held vertically in Troy’s hand (in the position technically called “recover swords”). All was as quick as electricity.

text: “But you will only think—think of it kindly, and with forbearance—if you cannot bear with me as a husband! I am afraid I am too old for you; yet believe me, I would care for you more tenderly than many a man of your own years. I would shield and cherish you with all my power—I would indeed! You should have no burdens, be troubled by no domestic matters, and live in perfect comfort, Miss Everdene. The management of the dairy should be left to a man—I can well afford it—you should never have to look out of doors at haymaking time, nor give a thought to the weather at harvest. I am somewhat attached to the chaise, because it is the very one my poor father and mother used to drive; but if you dislike it, I will sell it, and you shall have a pony-carriage of your own. I cannot tell how far above every other thought and thing in the world you seem to me—no one knows—God alone knows how much you are to me!”
source_text: “But you will just think—in kindness and condescension think—if you cannot bear with me as a husband! I fear I am too old for you, but believe me I will take more care of you than would many a man of your own age. I will protect and cherish you with all my strength—I will indeed! You shall have no cares—be worried by no household affairs, and live quite at ease, Miss Everdene. The dairy superintendence shall be done by a man—I can afford it well—you shall never have so much as to look out of doors at haymaking time, or to think of weather in the harvest. I rather cling to the chaise, because it is the same my poor father and mother drove, but if you don’t like it I will sell it, and you shall have a pony-carriage of your own. I cannot say how far above every other idea and object on earth you seem to me—nobody knows—God only knows—how much you are to me!”

text: Marian’s quick intuition had long since discerned that the farmer’s steadfast attachment to her was yet unshaken, and her heart was touched with sorrow. The encounter had cast a heaviness over her evening; it recalled her own imprudence, and once more she found herself desiring, as she had desired many months before, some way of atoning for the wrong she had done. Thus her compassion for the man who clung so unweariedly to a love that could only deepen his hurt and his lifelong despondency led Marian into a heedless gentleness of bearing, which might almost have passed for tenderness, and breathed fresh life into the exquisite fancy of seven years’ service in the poor farmer’s mind.
source_text: The keen instincts of Bathsheba had perceived that the farmer’s staunch devotion to herself was still undiminished, and she sympathized deeply. The sight had quite depressed her this evening; had reminded her of her folly; she wished anew, as she had wished many months ago, for some means of making reparation for her fault. Hence her pity for the man who so persistently loved on to his own injury and permanent gloom had betrayed Bathsheba into an injudicious considerateness of manner, which appeared almost like tenderness, and gave new vigour to the exquisite dream of a Jacob’s seven years service in poor Boldwood’s mind.

text: Bathsheba was lonely and miserable now; not in truth more lonely than she had been before her marriage, but the loneliness of that earlier time was to the loneliness of the present as the solitude of a mountain is to that of a cave. And within the last day or two there had come these troubling thoughts about her husband’s past. Her wavering feeling that evening about Fanny’s temporary resting-place had sprung from a strange tangle of impulses in Bathsheba’s heart. Perhaps it would be more exactly called a deliberate revolt against her own prejudices, a recoil from a lower, uncharitable instinct that would have denied the dead woman every sympathy because, in life, she had come before Bathsheba in the affection of a man whom Bathsheba had by no means stopped loving, though at that moment her love was nearly sickened by the weight of a fresh uneasiness.
source_text: Bathsheba was lonely and miserable now; not lonelier actually than she had been before her marriage; but her loneliness then was to that of the present time as the solitude of a mountain is to the solitude of a cave. And within the last day or two had come these disquieting thoughts about her husband’s past. Her wayward sentiment that evening concerning Fanny’s temporary resting-place had been the result of a strange complication of impulses in Bathsheba’s bosom. Perhaps it would be more accurately described as a determined rebellion against her prejudices, a revulsion from a lower instinct of uncharitableness, which would have withheld all sympathy from the dead woman, because in life she had preceded Bathsheba in the attentions of a man whom Bathsheba had by no means ceased from loving, though her love was sick to death just now with the gravity of a further misgiving.

text: “Quite true,” she said; and though at first it put her out, she had too much understanding to reproach them long or in earnest for an attachment to her so uncommon and so precious. Then, with a charm of manner that became her well, she went on: “Well, I am truly obliged to you both for all this trouble; only I wish you had borrowed any horses rather than Mr. Alden’s.”
source_text: “True,” she said, and though vexed at first she had too much sense to blame them long or seriously for a devotion to her that was as valuable as it was rare. She added with a very pretty grace, “Well, I really thank you heartily for taking all this trouble; but I wish you had borrowed anybody’s horses but Mr. Boldwood’s.”

text: He came across to Bathsheba, who turned to meet him with an air of complete composure. He addressed her quietly, and she, by instinct, lowered her own voice to match his; before long, her speech had even taken on his inflection. She was by no means eager to seem secretly linked with him; yet a woman at an impressionable age is drawn toward the stronger presence not only in her choice of words, as is seen every day, but even in the finer shades of tone and feeling, when the influence is powerful.
source_text: He crossed over towards Bathsheba, who turned to greet him with a carriage of perfect ease. He spoke to her in low tones, and she instinctively modulated her own to the same pitch, and her voice ultimately even caught the inflection of his. She was far from having a wish to appear mysteriously connected with him; but woman at the impressionable age gravitates to the larger body not only in her choice of words, which is apparent every day, but even in her shades of tone and humour, when the influence is great.

text: “Well, I think this much, ma’am, as I told you years ago,” said Oak, “that his life is a complete blank whenever he is not hoping for ’ee; but I cannot imagine—I trust nothing so dreadful is bound up with it as you suppose. His natural manner has always been dark and peculiar, as you know. But since the matter is so sad and strange-like, why not give the conditional promise? I think I should.”
source_text: “Well, I think this much, ma’am, as I told you years ago,” said Oak, “that his life is a total blank whenever he isn’t hoping for ’ee; but I can’t suppose—I hope that nothing so dreadful hangs on to it as you fancy. His natural manner has always been dark and strange, you know. But since the case is so sad and odd-like, why don’t ye give the conditional promise? I think I would.”

text: When Ash heard these words, his desire to study her features grew keener; yet the hooded fall of the cloak denied him that sight, and his lofty vantage did no more to help him, so that he found himself compelled to invent their particulars out of his own imagination. Even in the plainest, most level act of looking, we shape and tint what our eyes present to us according to some inward need. Had Raphael, from the first, been granted a clear view of her face, his judgment of it, whether as truly beautiful or only passably so, would have answered to the divinity his soul then demanded, or was prepared at once to accept. Since for some time he had been conscious of a need for some fitting image to occupy a swelling emptiness within him, and since his situation gave his fancy the fullest license, he endowed her with beauty.
source_text: Oak, upon hearing these remarks, became more curious to observe her features, but this prospect being denied him by the hooding effect of the cloak, and by his aërial position, he felt himself drawing upon his fancy for their details. In making even horizontal and clear inspections we colour and mould according to the wants within us whatever our eyes bring in. Had Gabriel been able from the first to get a distinct view of her countenance, his estimate of it as very handsome or slightly so would have been as his soul required a divinity at the moment or was ready supplied with one. Having for some time known the want of a satisfactory form to fill an increasing void within him, his position moreover affording the widest scope for his fancy, he painted her a beauty.

text: Rest had once more brought color to her cheeks; and, at Gabriel’s request, she had that morning dressed her hair as she had worn it years before on Norcombe Hill, so that in his eyes she looked astonishingly like the girl of that enchanting dream, which, seeing that she was now only three or four-and-twenty, was perhaps not so very strange. In the church were Tall, Liddy, and the parson, and in a very short time the deed was done.
source_text: Repose had again incarnadined her cheeks; and having, at Gabriel’s request, arranged her hair this morning as she had worn it years ago on Norcombe Hill, she seemed in his eyes remarkably like a girl of that fascinating dream, which, considering that she was now only three or four-and-twenty, was perhaps not very wonderful. In the church were Tall, Liddy, and the parson, and in a remarkably short space of time the deed was done.

text: “Eli Poorman, don’t carry on so dismally! Parson Thirleby won’t take it amiss. He’s a liberal soul; for years he has supplied me with tracts, and I have read my share of them in the course of a long and shady life, yet he has never once been the sort to grudge the cost. Come, sit yourself down.”
source_text: “Joseph Poorgrass, don’t be so miserable! Pa’son Thirdly won’t mind. He’s a generous man; he’s found me in tracts for years, and I’ve consumed a good many in the course of a long and shady life; but he’s never been the man to cry out at the expense. Sit down.”

text: As soon as the light was strong enough to make things out, Troy rose from the bedcover and, feeling utterly indifferent to Bathsheba’s whereabouts and almost forgetting that she existed at all, went downstairs and out through the back door. He made his way to the churchyard, and there, after looking about for a time, he found a fresh, unused grave—the one dug the previous day for Fanny. Having noted its position, he hurried on toward Casterbridge, stopping only for a while to stand and think at the hill where he had last seen Fanny alive.
source_text: As soon as it grew light enough to distinguish objects, Troy arose from the coverlet of the bed, and in a mood of absolute indifference to Bathsheba’s whereabouts, and almost oblivious of her existence, he stalked downstairs and left the house by the back door. His walk was towards the churchyard, entering which he searched around till he found a newly dug unoccupied grave—the grave dug the day before for Fanny. The position of this having been marked, he hastened on to Casterbridge, only pausing and musing for a while at the hill whereon he had last seen Fanny alive.

text: “I know, I know. But my wish to increase your share comes from what I know of you. Oak, I have learned something of your secret: your concern for her is more than that of a steward for his employer. But you have acted like a man, and I, as a kind of fortunate rival—fortunate partly because of your generosity of spirit—would like to make it clear how deeply I value your friendship in what must have been a great anguish for you.”
source_text: “I know, I know. But the feeling I have about increasing your share is on account of what I know of you. Oak, I have learnt a little about your secret: your interest in her is more than that of bailiff for an employer. But you have behaved like a man, and I, as a sort of successful rival—successful partly through your goodness of heart—should like definitely to show my sense of your friendship under what must have been a great pain to you.”

text: That the girl’s thoughts should at once turn to her face and figure, the moment she saw Oak’s eyes upon the very page she was reading, was natural and almost inevitable. Had the self-consciousness been a trifle stronger it would have been vanity; had it been a trifle weaker, dignity. In country places, the glance of a man seems to have a tingling effect on a maiden’s face; she passed her hand over her own, as if Gabriel had been actually fretting its pink surface with his touch, and the free lightness of her earlier manner sank at once into a more subdued and chastened state. Yet it was the man who flushed, not the girl.
source_text: That the girl’s thoughts hovered about her face and form as soon as she caught Oak’s eyes conning the same page was natural, and almost certain. The self-consciousness shown would have been vanity if a little more pronounced, dignity if a little less. Rays of male vision seem to have a tickling effect upon virgin faces in rural districts; she brushed hers with her hand, as if Gabriel had been irritating its pink surface by actual touch, and the free air of her previous movements was reduced at the same time to a chastened phase of itself. Yet it was the man who blushed, the maid not at all.

text: As late autumn deepened and winter came on quickly, the leaves fell thick over the turf of the clearings and the moss in the woods. Bathsheba, who had lately been living in a state of feeling held in abeyance, though it was not exactly suspense, was now in a kind of stillness that was not quite peace. So long as she knew him to be alive, she could bear to think of his death calmly; but now that she might have lost him, she was sorry he was no longer hers. She kept the farm running, took in her profits without any real concern for them, and spent money on schemes simply because she had done so in earlier days—days that, though not far behind her, seemed infinitely remote from where she now stood. She looked back on that time across a great distance, as though she were already dead, with only the power of reflection left to her, and by it could sit, like the decaying country gentry in the poet’s tale, and brood over what a blessing life had once been.
source_text: The later autumn and the winter drew on apace, and the leaves lay thick upon the turf of the glades and the mosses of the woods. Bathsheba, having previously been living in a state of suspended feeling which was not suspense, now lived in a mood of quietude which was not precisely peacefulness. While she had known him to be alive she could have thought of his death with equanimity; but now that it might be she had lost him, she regretted that he was not hers still. She kept the farm going, raked in her profits without caring keenly about them, and expended money on ventures because she had done so in bygone days, which, though not long gone by, seemed infinitely removed from her present. She looked back upon that past over a great gulf, as if she were now a dead person, having the faculty of meditation still left in her, by means of which, like the mouldering gentlefolk of the poet’s story, she could sit and ponder what a gift life used to be.

text: “Well, I trust Providence won’t take offense at my conduct,” said Martin, lowering himself to the chair again. “I’ve had my tender moments of late, it’s true enough. I’ve been a bit on the bottle once already this month, and I missed church on Sunday, and I let slip a curse or two yesterday; so I don’t mean to press my luck too hard for the sake of my soul. The next world is a serious business, and not something to be thrown away carelessly.”
source_text: “Well, I hope Providence won’t be in a way with me for my doings,” said Joseph, again sitting down. “I’ve been troubled with weak moments lately, ’tis true. I’ve been drinky once this month already, and I did not go to church a-Sunday, and I dropped a curse or two yesterday; so I don’t want to go too far for my safety. Your next world is your next world, and not to be squandered offhand.”

text: That night the buildings were, as usual, inspected, and she went on to the farm paddock. There the only sounds breaking the stillness were the steady chewing of many mouths and the deep breathing of all but unseen noses, followed by snores and soft puffs like bellows slowly blowing. Then the chewing would begin again, and the lively imagination could help the eye make out a cluster of pink-white nostrils, cavern-shaped and very damp and clammy on the surface, not quite pleasant to touch until one had grown used to them; beneath, the mouths had a strong liking for fastening on any loose end of Bathsheba’s clothing that came within reach of their tongues. Above each of these, an even sharper glance might suggest a brown forehead and two staring, though not unfriendly, eyes, and above all a pair of whitish, crescent-shaped horns like two especially recent new moons, while an occasional dull “moo!” made it plain beyond question that these were the features and persons of Daisy, Whitefoot, Bonny-lass, Jolly-O, Spot, Twinkle-eye, and so on—the respectable Devon dairy cows belonging to the Bathsheba mentioned above.
source_text: This night the buildings were inspected as usual, and she went round to the farm paddock. Here the only sounds disturbing the stillness were steady munchings of many mouths, and stentorian breathings from all but invisible noses, ending in snores and puffs like the blowing of bellows slowly. Then the munching would recommence, when the lively imagination might assist the eye to discern a group of pink-white nostrils, shaped as caverns, and very clammy and humid on their surfaces, not exactly pleasant to the touch until one got used to them; the mouths beneath having a great partiality for closing upon any loose end of Bathsheba’s apparel which came within reach of their tongues. Above each of these a still keener vision suggested a brown forehead and two staring though not unfriendly eyes, and above all a pair of whitish crescent-shaped horns like two particularly new moons, an occasional stolid “moo!” proclaiming beyond the shade of a doubt that these phenomena were the features and persons of Daisy, Whitefoot, Bonny-lass, Jolly-O, Spot, Twinkle-eye, etc., etc.—the respectable dairy of Devon cows belonging to Bathsheba aforesaid.

text: He walked with her up the hill, describing in detail his coming occupation of the other farm. They said little of the feeling they held in common; fine phrases and tender declarations were likely needless between such long-tried friends. Theirs was that solid affection which springs, if it springs at all, only when two people drawn together first come to know one another’s rougher sides, and not the better until later, the romance taking shape in the crevices of a mass of plain, stubborn reality. Such companionship—_camaraderie_—usually born of shared work, is unhappily seldom added to love between man and woman, since men and women are brought together not in labor but only in pleasure. Yet where favorable fortune allows it to grow, the mingled bond proves itself the sole love strong as death—that love which many waters cannot quench, nor the floods drown, and beside which the passion commonly given that name is as fleeting as steam.
source_text: He accompanied her up the hill, explaining to her the details of his forthcoming tenure of the other farm. They spoke very little of their mutual feeling; pretty phrases and warm expressions being probably unnecessary between such tried friends. Theirs was that substantial affection which arises (if any arises at all) when the two who are thrown together begin first by knowing the rougher sides of each other’s character, and not the best till further on, the romance growing up in the interstices of a mass of hard prosaic reality. This good-fellowship—_camaraderie_—usually occurring through similarity of pursuits, is unfortunately seldom superadded to love between the sexes, because men and women associate, not in their labours, but in their pleasures merely. Where, however, happy circumstance permits its development, the compounded feeling proves itself to be the only love which is strong as death—that love which many waters cannot quench, nor the floods drown, beside which the passion usually called by the name is evanescent as steam.

text: The Weatherbury bees were tardy in swarming that year. It was late in June, and the day after her meeting with Troy in the hayfield, Bathsheba was standing in her garden, watching a swarm in the air and trying to guess where they would settle. They were not only late this year, but obstinate as well. In one season all the swarms might choose the lowest reachable twig—some part of a currant-bush or an espalier apple-tree; and the next, with equal unanimity, they would fly to the highest branch of some tall, lank costard or quarrenden, and there defy every intruder who did not arrive equipped with ladders and staves to capture them.
source_text: The Weatherbury bees were late in their swarming this year. It was in the latter part of June, and the day after the interview with Troy in the hayfield, that Bathsheba was standing in her garden, watching a swarm in the air and guessing their probable settling place. Not only were they late this year, but unruly. Sometimes throughout a whole season all the swarms would alight on the lowest attainable bough—such as part of a currant-bush or espalier apple-tree; next year they would, with just the same unanimity, make straight off to the uppermost member of some tall, gaunt costard, or quarrenden, and there defy all invaders who did not come armed with ladders and staves to take them.

text: Bathsheba cast down the brush, the crook, and the empty hive, gathered the skirt of her dress tightly about her ankles in a great hurry, and as best she could made her way down the ladder. By the time she reached the ground, Troy was there too, and he bent to lift up the hive.
source_text: Bathsheba flung down the brush, crook, and empty hive, pulled the skirt of her dress tightly round her ankles in a tremendous flurry, and as well as she could slid down the ladder. By the time she reached the bottom Troy was there also, and he stooped to pick up the hive.

text: She drew a breath, and then spoke in a voice of sorrow: “Alas, what am I to do? I do not love you, and I fear greatly that I shall never love you as a wife should love her husband. Yet if you, sir, know this, and I may still bring you happiness by nothing more than a promise to marry you at the end of six years, should my husband not return, then that is a high honour indeed for me. And if you hold dear such a proof of friendship from a woman who esteems herself less than she once did, and has but little affection left in her heart, why, I—I shall—”
source_text: She breathed; and then said mournfully: “Oh what shall I do? I don’t love you, and I much fear that I never shall love you as much as a woman ought to love a husband. If you, sir, know that, and I can yet give you happiness by a mere promise to marry at the end of six years, if my husband should not come back, it is a great honour to me. And if you value such an act of friendship from a woman who doesn’t esteem herself as she did, and has little love left, why I—I will—”

text: “Really and truly, I am glad to hear it!” said Farmer Oak, with one of his long, peculiar smiles, and a blush of pleasure. He stretched out his hand to take hers, which, after she had eased the pain in her side by pressing it there, was daintily laid across her bosom to quiet her hard-beating heart. The moment he caught at it, she drew it back behind her, and it slid through his fingers like an eel.
source_text: “Really and truly I am glad to hear that!” said Farmer Oak, smiling one of his long special smiles, and blushing with gladness. He held out his hand to take hers, which, when she had eased her side by pressing it there, was prettily extended upon her bosom to still her loud-beating heart. Directly he seized it she put it behind her, so that it slipped through his fingers like an eel.

text: It was a ploughboy. A little later he came abreast of her, and from his voice she believed him to be one of the lads from her own farm. Behind him came the heavy, lumbering tread of another, and, peering through the ferns, Bathsheba could just make out, in the pale light of dawn, a team of her own horses. They halted to drink at a pond on the far side of the road. She watched them splashing into the water, drinking, tossing their heads, drinking once more, the water trickling from their lips in silver threads. Then, with another splash and flounce, they came out of the pond and turned back once again toward the farm.
source_text: It was a ploughboy. Presently he came opposite, and she believed from his voice that he was one of the boys on her own farm. He was followed by a shambling tramp of heavy feet, and looking through the ferns Bathsheba could just discern in the wan light of daybreak a team of her own horses. They stopped to drink at a pond on the other side of the way. She watched them flouncing into the pool, drinking, tossing up their heads, drinking again, the water dribbling from their lips in silver threads. There was another flounce, and they came out of the pond, and turned back again towards the farm.

text: A woman came into view at the top of the hill. The ridge lay in a cutting, so she was quite close to the husband and wife before they could see her. Troy had turned back toward the gig to climb in again, and as he was setting his foot on the step, the woman went behind him.
source_text: A woman appeared on the brow of the hill. The ridge was in a cutting, so that she was very near the husband and wife before she became visible. Troy had turned towards the gig to remount, and whilst putting his foot on the step the woman passed behind him.

text: Furthermore, the hamlet named Brackenford, in which the greater part of the present narrative is set, would scarcely now be recognized by any traveller, unaided, in an actual place; though at the period, not so very remote, when the tale was first composed, there was enough living reality to correspond with the descriptions, alike of setting and of character, and to be found without much difficulty. The church still stands, by rare good fortune, untouched by restoration and sound as ever, together with a handful of the older dwellings; but the venerable malt-house, once so distinguishing a feature of the parish, was demolished some twenty years ago, as were most of the thatched cottages with their dormer windows that had long been the village’s homes. The game of prison-base, which not long since appeared to flourish endlessly before the weather-beaten stocks, may, for all I know, be wholly forgotten by the younger boys of the place. Fortune-telling with Bible and key, the treating of valentines as matters of grave concern, the shearing-feast, and the harvest-supper, have likewise almost vanished with the old houses; and with them, it is said, has disappeared much of the fondness for tippling for which the village was once so notorious. At the heart of the alteration lies the recent displacement of the settled cottager class, those who kept alive the local customs and caprices, by a body of labourers more or less itinerant, and thus a rupture has been made in the chain of local continuity more destructive than any other to the keeping of legend, rustic lore, close neighborly intercourse, and singular personal oddities. For such things can exist only where generation after generation remains rooted to the same patch of earth.
source_text: Moreover, the village called Weatherbury, wherein the scenes of the present story of the series are for the most part laid, would perhaps be hardly discernible by the explorer, without help, in any existing place nowadays; though at the time, comparatively recent, at which the tale was written, a sufficient reality to meet the descriptions, both of backgrounds and personages, might have been traced easily enough. The church remains, by great good fortune, unrestored and intact, and a few of the old houses; but the ancient malt-house, which was formerly so characteristic of the parish, has been pulled down these twenty years; also most of the thatched and dormered cottages that were once lifeholds. The game of prisoner’s base, which not so long ago seemed to enjoy a perennial vitality in front of the worn-out stocks, may, so far as I can say, be entirely unknown to the rising generation of schoolboys there. The practice of divination by Bible and key, the regarding of valentines as things of serious import, the shearing-supper, and the harvest-home, have, too, nearly disappeared in the wake of the old houses; and with them have gone, it is said, much of that love of fuddling to which the village at one time was notoriously prone. The change at the root of this has been the recent supplanting of the class of stationary cottagers, who carried on the local traditions and humours, by a population of more or less migratory labourers, which has led to a break of continuity in local history, more fatal than any other thing to the preservation of legend, folk-lore, close inter-social relations, and eccentric individualities. For these the indispensable conditions of existence are attachment to the soil of one particular spot by generation after generation.

text: All Troy’s strength came back in a sudden spasm, enough to prolong the struggle a little longer. Swimming with his right arm, he lifted his left to signal to them, beating the water and calling at the top of his lungs. Against the setting sun his white figure stood plainly out on the now darkened sea to the east of the boat, and the men caught sight of him at once. They checked their oars and turned the boat around, pulling toward him with all their strength, and within five or six minutes of his first cry, two of the sailors had hauled him aboard over the stern.
source_text: All Troy’s vigour spasmodically revived to prolong the struggle yet a little further. Swimming with his right arm, he held up his left to hail them, splashing upon the waves, and shouting with all his might. From the position of the setting sun his white form was distinctly visible upon the now deep-hued bosom of the sea to the east of the boat, and the men saw him at once. Backing their oars and putting the boat about, they pulled towards him with a will, and in five or six minutes from the time of his first halloo, two of the sailors hauled him in over the stern.

text: The longer Joseph Poorgrass stayed, the less his mind was burdened by the tasks laid upon him that afternoon. The minutes slipped away unnoticed, until the evening shadows had clearly begun to deepen, and the eyes of the three men were little more than bright points shining on the face of the darkness. Coggan’s repeater gave six from his pocket in its usual faint, subdued way.
source_text: The longer Joseph Poorgrass remained, the less his spirit was troubled by the duties which devolved upon him this afternoon. The minutes glided by uncounted, until the evening shades began perceptibly to deepen, and the eyes of the three were but sparkling points on the surface of darkness. Coggan’s repeater struck six from his pocket in the usual still small tones.

text: Mr. Barlow had not passed beyond his garden gate since his encounter with Miss Vale upon the road to Yelbury. There, in solitude and silence, he had brooded darkly upon the conduct of women, taking the accidents of the one woman he had ever closely observed for the very constitution of the whole sex. Little by little, however, a gentler spirit had come upon him; and it was this, more than anything else, that had sent him forth that night. He had come to ask pardon of Miss Vale, and to offer an apology for his violence, with something of shame in the recollection of it, having only then discovered that she had returned—so he supposed, merely from a visit to Lydia, the matter at Bath being wholly unknown to him.
source_text: Boldwood had not been outside his garden since his meeting with Bathsheba in the road to Yalbury. Silent and alone, he had remained in moody meditation on woman’s ways, deeming as essentials of the whole sex the accidents of the single one of their number he had ever closely beheld. By degrees a more charitable temper had pervaded him, and this was the reason of his sally to-night. He had come to apologize and beg forgiveness of Bathsheba with something like a sense of shame at his violence, having but just now learnt that she had returned—only from a visit to Liddy, as he supposed, the Bath escapade being quite unknown to him.

text: “I’ll punish him—by my soul, I will! I’ll face him, soldier or not, and I’ll horsewhip the untimely stripling for this reckless theft of my one delight. Were he a hundred men, I’d horsewhip him—” He suddenly lowered his voice, in a strange, unnatural way. “Bathsheba, sweet, lost coquette, forgive me! I have been blaming you, threatening you, and behaving like a churl to you, when he is the chief sinner. He stole your dear heart from you with his unfathomable lies!... It is a fortunate thing for him that he has gone back to his regiment—that he is away up the country, and not here! I hope he may not return here yet. I pray God he may not come into my sight, for I might be tempted beyond myself. Oh, Bathsheba, keep him away—yes, keep him away from me!”
source_text: “I’ll punish him—by my soul, that will I! I’ll meet him, soldier or no, and I’ll horsewhip the untimely stripling for this reckless theft of my one delight. If he were a hundred men I’d horsewhip him—” He dropped his voice suddenly and unnaturally. “Bathsheba, sweet, lost coquette, pardon me! I’ve been blaming you, threatening you, behaving like a churl to you, when he’s the greatest sinner. He stole your dear heart away with his unfathomable lies!... It is a fortunate thing for him that he’s gone back to his regiment—that he’s away up the country, and not here! I hope he may not return here just yet. I pray God he may not come into my sight, for I may be tempted beyond myself. Oh, Bathsheba, keep him away—yes, keep him away from me!”

text: It is more than likely that she secretly and without knowing it admired him a little for this stern loyalty, which had shown itself in his manner even more than in what he said. At any rate, she murmured something to the effect that he might remain if he wished. Then, more clearly, she said, “Will you leave me alone now? I do not command it as a mistress—I ask it as a woman, and I expect you not to be so discourteous as to refuse.”
source_text: It is more than probable that she privately and unconsciously respected him a little for this grim fidelity, which had been shown in his tone even more than in his words. At any rate she murmured something to the effect that he might stay if he wished. She said more distinctly, “Will you leave me alone now? I don’t order it as a mistress—I ask it as a woman, and I expect you not to be so uncourteous as to refuse.”

text: “‘Ye are to declare it,’ were the parson’s words,” Fairway went on. “And then a woman rose up beside me—putting her hand against me. ‘Well, I’ll be damned if there isn’t Mis’ess Yeobright a-standing up,’ I said to myself. Yes, neighbours, though I was in the temple of prayer, that is what I said. ’Tis against my conscience to curse and swear before company, and I hope any woman here will excuse it. Still, what I did say I did say, and it would be a lie if I denied it.”
source_text: “‘Ye are to declare it,’ was the parson’s words,” Fairway continued. “And then up stood a woman at my side—a-touching of me. ‘Well, be damned if there isn’t Mis’ess Yeobright a-standing up,’ I said to myself. Yes, neighbours, though I was in the temple of prayer that’s what I said. ’Tis against my conscience to curse and swear in company, and I hope any woman here will overlook it. Still what I did say I did say, and ’twould be a lie if I didn’t own it.”

text: “But, Adrian, do pray tell me what I am to do. To sit beside him hour after hour, to hear him condemn himself as though he had brought about her death, and to know that I, if any mortal soul, am the guilty one, drives me into a bleak and icy despair. I cannot tell what course to take. Ought I to confess to him, or ought I to hold my peace? That is the question I keep returning to. I long to tell him, and yet I am frightened. If the truth should ever come to him in another way, he must surely destroy me, for nothing else would answer to the depth of what he feels now. ‘Beware the wrath of the patient man’ rings in my ears each day as I watch him.”
source_text: “But, Damon, please pray tell me what I must do? To sit by him hour after hour, and hear him reproach himself as being the cause of her death, and to know that I am the sinner, if any human being is at all, drives me into cold despair. I don’t know what to do. Should I tell him or should I not tell him? I always am asking myself that. O, I want to tell him; and yet I am afraid. If he finds it out he must surely kill me, for nothing else will be in proportion to his feelings now. ‘Beware the fury of a patient man’ sounds day by day in my ears as I watch him.”

text: An hour and a half later he appeared once more at the garden gate. The house shutters were shut, and Christian Cantle, who had spent the whole day wheeling manure around the garden, had gone home. Going in, he saw that his mother, after waiting a long while for him, had already finished her meal.
source_text: It was an hour and a half later when he again appeared at the garden gate. The shutters of the house were closed, and Christian Cantle, who had been wheeling manure about the garden all day, had gone home. On entering he found that his mother, after waiting a long time for him, had finished her meal.

text: The solitary inn was still open, though there was hardly a soul within, its trade having been mostly with wayfarers on distant journeys, and these had now continued on their road. Venn entered the common room, ordered a mug of ale, and, with an air of carelessness, asked the maid whether Mr. Harlowe was at home.
source_text: The lonely inn was not yet closed, though scarcely an individual was there, the business done being chiefly with travellers who passed the inn on long journeys, and these had now gone on their way. Venn went to the public room, called for a mug of ale, and inquired of the maid in an indifferent tone if Mr. Wildeve was at home.

text: She then related the circumstances to him. All he had known of the matter before his return from Paris was that there had once been an attachment between Thomasin and Wildeve, which his mother had at first disapproved of, but had afterwards, under Thomasin’s persuasions, come to regard with somewhat more favour. When she went on, therefore, to explain everything, he was much surprised and deeply troubled.
source_text: She then told him the circumstances. All that he had known of the affair before he returned from Paris was that there had existed an attachment between Thomasin and Wildeve, which his mother had at first discountenanced, but had since, owing to the arguments of Thomasin, looked upon in a little more favourable light. When she, therefore, proceeded to explain all he was greatly surprised and troubled.

text: There she sat for twenty minutes or more before she could bring herself to go down to the door, her physical weariness having reduced her courage to nothing. To anyone other than a mother, it might have seemed somewhat humiliating that she, the older of the two women, should be the first to make advances. But Mrs. Yeobright had thought all this over carefully, and was concerned only with how best to make her visit seem to Eustacia not submissive, but sensible.
source_text: Here she sat for twenty minutes or more ere she could summon resolution to go down to the door, her courage being lowered to zero by her physical lassitude. To any other person than a mother it might have seemed a little humiliating that she, the elder of the two women, should be the first to make advances. But Mrs. Yeobright had well considered all that, and she only thought how best to make her visit appear to Eustacia not abject but wise.

text: When he arrived at the house, he went up to the room that was to serve as his study, and spent the evening unpacking his books from their boxes and placing them on the shelves. From another box he took out a lamp and a can of oil. He trimmed the lamp, set his table in order, and said, “Now I am ready to begin.”
source_text: On reaching the house he went up to the room which was to be made his study, and occupied himself during the evening in unpacking his books from the boxes and arranging them on shelves.
