From another box he drew a lamp and a can of oil. He trimmed the lamp, arranged his table, and said, “Now, I am ready to begin.”

text: “I can keep a secret,” said Venn softly. “You need not be afraid. I am the only man who knows of your meetings with him. There is only one more thing to say, and then I shall go. I heard you tell him that you hated living here—that Egdon Heath was a prison to you.”
source_text: “I can keep secrets,” said Venn gently. “You need not fear. I am the only man who knows of your meetings with him. There is but one thing more to speak of, and then I will be gone. I heard you say to him that you hated living here—that Egdon Heath was a jail to you.”

text: “I can’t say much by way of explaining, ma’am. All I know is that, as I was walking along the road this morning, near a mile beyond Anglebury, I heard something coming after me at a trot, light as a doe’s step; and when I turned, there she was, pale as death. ‘Oh, Diggory Venn!’ she said, ‘I thought it was you—will you help me? I am in trouble.’”
source_text: “I can’t explain much, ma’am. All I know is that, as I was going along the road this morning, about a mile out of Anglebury, I heard something trotting after me like a doe, and looking round there she was, white as death itself. ‘Oh, Diggory Venn!’ she said, ‘I thought ’twas you—will you help me? I am in trouble.’”

text: With the departure of those figures, the women’s abundant expressions faded from her memory; but the words of the other remained. Had there been anything in the voice of Mrs. Yeobright’s son—for Clym it was—that had startled her like a sudden sound? No; it had been merely all-embracing. Whatever emotions existed seemed possible in the speaker of that “good night.” Eustacia’s imagination supplied the rest, save for the answer to one puzzle. What could the tastes of that man be, who found friendliness and cheerfulness in these rough hills?
source_text: With the departure of the figures the profuse articulations of the women wasted away from her memory; but the accents of the other stayed on. Was there anything in the voice of Mrs. Yeobright’s son—for Clym it was—startling as a sound? No; it was simply comprehensive. All emotional things were possible to the speaker of that “good night.” Eustacia’s imagination supplied the rest—except the solution to one riddle. What _could_ the tastes of that man be who saw friendliness and geniality in these shaggy hills?

text: To invite by love the very trouble that will undo them is a familiar weakness in some ardent women. A conflict of affection, dread, and mortification had driven Celia into the keenest agitation. Her one urgent wish was to be gone. The other players showed no sign of quitting the room; and, whispering to the youth beside her that she would rather wait for them outside the cottage, she drifted toward the door with what stealth she could manage, opened it, and glided out.
source_text: To court their own discomfiture by love is a common instinct with certain perfervid women. Conflicting sensations of love, fear, and shame reduced Eustacia to a state of the utmost uneasiness. To escape was her great and immediate desire. The other mummers appeared to be in no hurry to leave; and murmuring to the lad who sat next to her that she preferred waiting for them outside the house, she moved to the door as imperceptibly as possible, opened it, and slipped out.

text: Upon a shelf in the fuelhouse burned three long rushlights, and in their wavering glow seven or eight young fellows were pacing to and fro, declaiming, and throwing one another into perplexity as they strove to master the performance. There, too, stood Martin and Job, the cutters of gorse and turf, watching the business; and with them was Abel Farrow, who leaned against the wall and called out the lines from recollection, slipping in among the appointed words such observations and stories as pleased him concerning the better times when he and his companions had been the chosen mummers of Egdon, as these lads now were.
source_text: On a ledge in the fuelhouse stood three tall rushlights and by the light of them seven or eight lads were marching about, haranguing, and confusing each other, in endeavours to perfect themselves in the play. Humphrey and Sam, the furze- and turf-cutters, were there looking on, so also was Timothy Fairway, who leant against the wall and prompted the boys from memory, interspersing among the set words remarks and anecdotes of the superior days when he and others were the Egdon mummers-elect that these lads were now.

text: “I do, and I do not,” he said with a mischievous air. “That is to say, I have my times and seasons. At one moment you are too tall, at another too idle; one time too melancholy, another too dark, and another I scarcely know what, except that you are not the whole world to me that you once were, my dear. But you are a pleasant lady to know and agreeable to meet, and I dare say as sweet as ever—almost.”
source_text: “I do, and I do not,” said he mischievously. “That is, I have my times and my seasons. One moment you are too tall, another moment you are too do-nothing, another too melancholy, another too dark, another I don’t know what, except—that you are not the whole world to me that you used to be, my dear. But you are a pleasant lady to know and nice to meet, and I dare say as sweet as ever—almost.”

text: He got up and went after the Grandfer, and the others too moved to the door. Presently an open fly came driving by, with Venn and Mrs. Venn, Yeobright, and one of Venn’s distinguished relations, who had come from Budmouth for the occasion, seated inside. The carriage had been engaged in the nearest town, no matter what the distance or expense, for in Venn’s judgment there was nothing on Egdon Heath grand enough for such an occasion when Thomasin was to be the bride; and the church was too far off for the wedding party to walk there.
source_text: He arose and followed the Grandfer, and the rest also went to the door. In a moment an open fly was driven past, in which sat Venn and Mrs. Venn, Yeobright, and a grand relative of Venn’s who had come from Budmouth for the occasion. The fly had been hired at the nearest town, regardless of distance and cost, there being nothing on Egdon Heath, in Venn’s opinion, dignified enough for such an event when such a woman as Thomasin was the bride; and the church was too remote for a walking bridal-party.

text: To long for what was hard to reach, and to grow tired of what lay ready to hand; to prize the distant and turn from the immediate—such had always been Wildeve’s nature. This is the true sign of the man of sentiment. Though Wildeve’s heated emotion had never developed into anything of real poetic range, it was of the familiar kind. He might have been called the Rousseau of Egdon.
source_text: To be yearning for the difficult, to be weary of that offered; to care for the remote, to dislike the near; it was Wildeve’s nature always. This is the true mark of the man of sentiment. Though Wildeve’s fevered feeling had not been elaborated to real poetical compass, it was of the standard sort. His might have been called the Rousseau of Egdon.

text: After a few more words they separated, the reddleman going on with his van, and the two women still standing together in the roadway. When the cart and its driver had gone far enough to be wholly beyond the reach of her voice, Mrs. Venn turned toward her niece.
source_text: And after a few further words they parted, the reddleman moving onwards with his van, and the two women remaining standing in the road. As soon as the vehicle and its driver had withdrawn so far as to be beyond all possible reach of her voice, Mrs. Yeobright turned to her niece.

text: “It is no use, Thomasin; it is no use. Your purpose is kind, but I do not wish you to argue with me. I have gone over all that can be said on both sides, again and again, many times. Clym and I have not separated in anger; we have parted in a worse manner than that. It is not a violent quarrel that would have broken my heart; it is the calm resistance and obstinate persistence in doing wrong that he has shown. O Thomasin, he was so good as a little boy—so gentle and so loving!”
source_text: “It is no use, Thomasin; it is no use. Your intention is good; but I will not trouble you to argue. I have gone through the whole that can be said on either side times, and many times. Clym and I have not parted in anger; we have parted in a worse way. It is not a passionate quarrel that would have broken my heart; it is the steady opposition and persistence in going wrong that he has shown. O Thomasin, he was so good as a little boy—so tender and kind!”

text: “For five and twenty years my uncle has served as the faithful steward of a wealthy widow, mistress of a fine house that looks straight upon the sea. The lady is grown aged and crippled now, and longs for some young companion to read to her and sing for her; yet, though she has advertised in the newspapers and tried no fewer than half a dozen, she cannot find one to suit her fancy for love nor money. She would be overjoyed to have you, and my uncle would contrive matters so that all should go smoothly.”
source_text: “My uncle has been for five and twenty years the trusty man of a rich widow-lady who has a beautiful house facing the sea. This lady has become old and lame, and she wants a young company-keeper to read and sing to her, but can’t get one to her mind to save her life, though she’ve advertised in the papers, and tried half a dozen. She would jump to get you, and Uncle would make it all easy.”

text: Eustacia regarded with curiosity the singular man who had spoken in this way. What a strange kind of love it was, wholly without that element of selfishness which is so often the chief ingredient of passion, and sometimes its only one! The reddleman’s unselfishness was so deserving of esteem that it passed beyond esteem by being scarcely understood; and she was almost inclined to think it ridiculous.
source_text: Eustacia looked curiously at the singular man who spoke thus. What a strange sort of love, to be entirely free from that quality of selfishness which is frequently the chief constituent of the passion, and sometimes its only one! The reddleman’s disinterestedness was so well deserving of respect that it overshot respect by being barely comprehended; and she almost thought it absurd.

text: She made no reply. The note of it was indeed grave and all-pervading. Complex utterances laid themselves upon the senses, and by ear alone one might almost survey the countenance of the place. The dim landscape yielded back acoustic images; they could tell where the stretches of heather commenced and ceased, where the gorse stood rank and stalky, where it had lately been clipped, in what quarter the fir thicket lay, and how near was the hollow where the holly bushes grew; for each of these varied forms had its own voice no less than its outline and hue.
source_text: She did not answer. Its tone was indeed solemn and pervasive. Compound utterances addressed themselves to their senses, and it was possible to view by ear the features of the neighbourhood. Acoustic pictures were returned from the darkened scenery; they could hear where the tracts of heather began and ended; where the furze was growing stalky and tall; where it had been recently cut; in what direction the fir-clump lay, and how near was the pit in which the hollies grew; for these differing features had their voices no less than their shapes and colours.

text: The old man often strained his eyes forward to look across the stretch he still had to cover. At last he made out, far ahead of him, a moving speck, which seemed to be a vehicle, and it was traveling in the same direction as his own journey. It was the only sign of life the scene offered, and it did nothing but make the surrounding loneliness more plain. It moved at a sluggish pace, and the old man was plainly drawing nearer to it.
source_text: The old man frequently stretched his eyes ahead to gaze over the tract that he had yet to traverse. At length he discerned, a long distance in front of him, a moving spot, which appeared to be a vehicle, and it proved to be going the same way as that in which he himself was journeying. It was the single atom of life that the scene contained, and it only served to render the general loneliness more evident. Its rate of advance was slow, and the old man gained upon it sensibly.

text: That declaration, however, was scarcely justified by what followed. Once more the instruments brought the air to its close; once more they struck up again with all the same ardor and emotion, as though it were only their opening phrase. It was now that sort of tune with no discernible first act, middle, or finish, which, among all the dances that crowd a gifted fiddler’s imagination, perhaps most nearly suggests the endless—the much-vaunted “Old Nick’s Reverie.” The wildness of bodily motion awakened by the wildness of the music could be faintly divined by those observers in the moonlight from the rare thuds of toe and heel against the door, whenever the circling had grown faster than usual.
source_text: This assertion, however, was hardly borne out by the event. Again the instruments ended the tune; again they recommenced with as much fire and pathos as if it were the first strain. The air was now that one without any particular beginning, middle, or end, which perhaps, among all the dances which throng an inspired fiddler’s fancy, best conveys the idea of the interminable—the celebrated “Devil’s Dream.” The fury of personal movement that was kindled by the fury of the notes could be approximately imagined by these outsiders under the moon, from the occasional kicks of toes and heels against the door, whenever the whirl round had been of more than customary velocity.

text: He picked up a green hazel stick he had been using as a walking staff, split the end, slipped in a small pebble, and, lantern in hand, went out onto the heath. By then Clym had lit a little fire and sent Susan Nunsuch off for a frying pan. Before she came back, Sam returned with three adders, one of them twisting and uncoiling briskly in the cleft of the stick, the other two hanging dead across it.
source_text: He took a green hazel which he had used as a walking stick, split it at the end, inserted a small pebble, and with the lantern in his hand went out into the heath. Clym had by this time lit a small fire, and despatched Susan Nunsuch for a frying pan. Before she had returned Sam came in with three adders, one briskly coiling and uncoiling in the cleft of the stick, and the other two hanging dead across it.

text: Throughout that afternoon, the anticipated arrival of the man occupying Eustacia’s thoughts set Blooms-End astir with preparations. Thomasin had been urged by her aunt, and by a natural feeling of loyalty to her cousin Clym, to exert herself for his sake with an eagerness uncommon to her during these bleakest days of her life. At the very moment when Eustacia was overhearing the rick-makers’ talk about Clym’s return, Thomasin was making her way up into a loft above her aunt’s fuel-house, where the stored apples were kept, in order to select the finest and largest of them for the approaching holiday season.
source_text: All that afternoon the expected arrival of the subject of Eustacia’s ruminations created a bustle of preparation at Blooms-End. Thomasin had been persuaded by her aunt, and by an instinctive impulse of loyalty towards her cousin Clym, to bestir herself on his account with an alacrity unusual in her during these most sorrowful days of her life. At the time that Eustacia was listening to the rick-makers’ conversation on Clym’s return, Thomasin was climbing into a loft over her aunt’s fuelhouse, where the store-apples were kept, to search out the best and largest of them for the coming holiday-time.

text: After listening for several minutes beneath the bank to their conversation, he turned away in a troubled, uncertain way and began to retreat as silently as he had arrived. It was plain that, on the whole, he did not think it wise to break in upon her talk with Wildeve unless he was ready to bear the full force of her displeasure.
source_text: After listening under the bank for some minutes to the talk he turned in a perplexed and doubting manner and began to withdraw as silently as he had come. That he did not, upon the whole, think it advisable to interrupt her conversation with Wildeve, without being prepared to bear the whole weight of her displeasure, was obvious.

text: Seeing that she made no answer, he looked at her more closely. She was nearly in tears. The thought of a future she was never to know, the renewed pang of her bitter disappointment, and the image of the neighbor’s mockery, now half-aroused by Wildeve’s words, had been too much for proud Eustacia’s composure.
source_text: Observing that she did not reply he regarded her more closely. She was almost weeping. Images of a future never to be enjoyed, the revived sense of her bitter disappointment, the picture of the neighbour’s suspended ridicule which was raised by Wildeve’s words, had been too much for proud Eustacia’s equanimity.

text: Eustacia stayed inside the bank, gazing at the fire and meaning to go back indoors, yet still lingering there. Had her circumstances not already disposed her to regard with indifference everything revered by gods and men, she would likely have left at once. But her condition was so utterly desperate that she could almost toy with it. Loss is less troubling than the question of whether we may perhaps have won after all; and now Eustacia, like others in such a state, could stand apart from herself, watch herself as though she were a detached observer, and wonder what a jest she made for Heaven, this woman Eustacia.
source_text: Eustacia remained within the bank looking at the fire, intending to go indoors, yet lingering still. Had she not by her situation been inclined to hold in indifference all things honoured of the gods and of men she would probably have come away. But her state was so hopeless that she could play with it. To have lost is less disturbing than to wonder if we may possibly have won; and Eustacia could now, like other people at such a stage, take a standing-point outside herself, observe herself as a disinterested spectator, and think what a sport for Heaven this woman Eustacia was.

text: “I should like to go with you,” said the old man doubtfully. “But I begin to fear my legs will scarcely bear me there on a night like this. I’m not as young as I used to be. If they are stopped in their flight, she is sure to come back to me, and I ought to be at the house to receive her. But, be it as it may, I can’t walk to the Quiet Woman, and that settles it. I’ll go straight home.”
source_text: “I would fain go with ’ee,” said the old man doubtfully. “But I begin to be afraid that my legs will hardly carry me there such a night as this. I am not so young as I was. If they are interrupted in their flight she will be sure to come back to me, and I ought to be at the house to receive her. But be it as ’twill I can’t walk to the Quiet Woman, and that’s an end on’t. I’ll go straight home.”

text: It was as though those tending the bonfire stood in some shining upper chamber of the world, cut off from and independent of the dark breadths below. The heath beneath had become a tremendous void, no longer seeming a continuation of the ground on which they stood; for eyes schooled to the glare could discern nothing of the depths beyond its reach. Now and then, it is true, some sharper-than-usual burst from the faggots would send quick shafts of light, like aides-de-camp, skimming down the slopes to some far-off bush, pool, or stretch of white sand, awakening each in turn to a like gleam, until everything was swallowed once more by darkness. Then the whole black spectacle below might have been Limbo seen from its edge by the sublime Florentine in his vision, and the wind’s low murmur in the hollows sounded like the complaints and supplications of the “souls of mighty worth” held there.
source_text: It seemed as if the bonfire-makers were standing in some radiant upper story of the world, detached from and independent of the dark stretches below. The heath down there was now a vast abyss, and no longer a continuation of what they stood on; for their eyes, adapted to the blaze, could see nothing of the deeps beyond its influence. Occasionally, it is true, a more vigorous flare than usual from their faggots sent darting lights like aides-de-camp down the inclines to some distant bush, pool, or patch of white sand, kindling these to replies of the same colour, till all was lost in darkness again. Then the whole black phenomenon beneath represented Limbo as viewed from the brink by the sublime Florentine in his vision, and the muttered articulations of the wind in the hollows were as complaints and petitions from the “souls of mighty worth” suspended therein.

text: Visiting the captain’s cottage was, for the lower-ranking people around there, always something of an ordeal. He could be sociable at times, but his temper was unpredictable, and no one could ever be sure how he might act from one moment to the next. Eustacia kept to herself and was a very private woman. Apart from the daughter of one of the cottagers, who served them, and a boy who tended the garden and stables, hardly anyone besides the household itself ever went inside. They were the only genteel family in the district except for the Yeobrights, and though they were by no means wealthy, they did not feel the need to wear a cordial expression toward every person, bird, and animal in the way their poorer neighbors did.
source_text: To call at the captain’s cottage was always more or less an undertaking for the inferior inhabitants. Though occasionally chatty, his moods were erratic, and nobody could be certain how he would behave at any particular moment. Eustacia was reserved, and lived very much to herself. Except the daughter of one of the cotters, who was their servant, and a lad who worked in the garden and stable, scarcely anyone but themselves ever entered the house. They were the only genteel people of the district except the Yeobrights, and though far from rich, they did not feel that necessity for preserving a friendly face towards every man, bird, and beast which influenced their poorer neighbours.

text: “Eustacia! Could I have forgotten that last autumn, on this very day of the month and in this very spot, you kindled just such a fire to summon me to come and meet you? Why else would there be a bonfire again near Captain Vye’s house, if it were not for the same intent?”
source_text: “Eustacia! could I forget that last autumn at this same day of the month and at this same place you lighted exactly such a fire as a signal for me to come and see you? Why should there have been a bonfire again by Captain Vye’s house if not for the same purpose?”

text: “No. But I would go with you if that could give you any pleasure; though, as things are, perhaps you already have too much of me. Still, for some reason I wish you did not want to leave. Yes, perhaps I am jealous; and who could have more reason to be jealous than I, a half-blind man, of a woman like you?”
source_text: “No. But I would come with you if it could give you any pleasure; though, as things stand, perhaps you have too much of me already. Still, I somehow wish that you did not want to go. Yes, perhaps I am jealous; and who could be jealous with more reason than I, a half-blind man, over such a woman as you?”

text: On the bright days of this season, and on those that had come before, certain brief activities would sometimes trouble, in their small way, the grand stillness of Egdon Heath. In contrast with the business of a town, a village, or even a farm, they would have seemed little more than the restlessness of stagnation itself, a faint stirring in the body of drowsiness. But here, with nothing to compare them to, enclosed by the steadfast hills, where even walking had the strangeness of ceremony and where any man might readily imagine himself to be Adam, they drew the gaze of every bird in sight, every reptile not yet asleep, and made the rabbits around them watch from the hillocks at a cautious distance.
source_text: On the fine days at this time of the year, and earlier, certain ephemeral operations were apt to disturb, in their trifling way, the majestic calm of Egdon Heath. They were activities which, beside those of a town, a village, or even a farm, would have appeared as the ferment of stagnation merely, a creeping of the flesh of somnolence. But here, away from comparisons, shut in by the stable hills, among which mere walking had the novelty of pageantry, and where any man could imagine himself to be Adam without the least difficulty, they attracted the attention of every bird within eyeshot, every reptile not yet asleep, and set the surrounding rabbits curiously watching from hillocks at a safe distance.

text: “I have been in that neighborhood for some little while, and I saw them go in,” said the reddleman. “Wildeve came up to the door to the minute, as if a clock had sent him. I hadn’t looked for that from him.” He did not go on to say what he might have said: that his being in that neighborhood was no chance matter; that, since Wildeve had resumed his claim to Thomasin, Venn, with the thorough persistence that belonged to his nature, had resolved to watch the affair through to its conclusion.
source_text: “I have been in that neighbourhood for some time, and I saw them go in,” said the reddleman. “Wildeve came up to the door, punctual as the clock. I didn’t expect it of him.” He did not add, as he might have added, that how he came to be in that neighbourhood was not by accident; that, since Wildeve’s resumption of his right to Thomasin, Venn, with the thoroughness which was part of his character, had determined to see the end of the episode.

text: Time went on. Wildeve grew as eager as Christian himself. When he had first begun the game, he had meant no more than a malicious practical jest at Mrs. Yeobright’s expense. His plan had been, by fair means or foul, to win the money and fling it contemptuously to Thomasin before her aunt’s eyes. But men are drawn away from their purposes even while pursuing them, and by the time the twentieth guinea had been reached it was very doubtful whether Wildeve was aware of any object beyond winning for his own advantage. Besides, he was no longer staking his wife’s money, but Yeobright’s; though Christian, in his anxiety, did not tell him so until afterward.
source_text: Time passed on. Wildeve began to be as excited as Christian himself. When commencing the game his intention had been nothing further than a bitter practical joke on Mrs. Yeobright. To win the money, fairly or otherwise, and to hand it contemptuously to Thomasin in her aunt’s presence, had been the dim outline of his purpose. But men are drawn from their intentions even in the course of carrying them out, and it was extremely doubtful, by the time the twentieth guinea had been reached, whether Wildeve was conscious of any other intention than that of winning for his own personal benefit. Moreover, he was now no longer gambling for his wife’s money, but for Yeobright’s; though of this fact Christian, in his apprehensiveness, did not inform him till afterwards.

text: “And the sovereign rose to greet her, and bent low before her, and returned to his seat upon the throne; and he bade a place be brought for the queen-mother, and she took her station at his right hand. Then she spake, saying, I have but one little request to make of thee; I beseech thee, deny me not. And the king answered her, Speak, my mother, what thou wilt: for I shall not deny thee.”
source_text: “‘And the king rose up to meet her, and bowed himself unto her, and sat down on his throne, and caused a seat to be set for the king’s mother; and she sat on his right hand. Then she said, I desire one small petition of thee; I pray thee say me not nay. And the king said unto her, Ask, on, my mother: for I will not say thee nay.’”

text: Thomasin seized the child and fled. When she reached the covered cart, the horse, though newly out of the stable, stood motionless, as if it too were aware of the disaster. For the first time she recognized whose vehicle it was. She nearly swooned, and would have been unable to take another step had not the urgent need to protect the little girl steeled her into extraordinary self-command. In this torment of suspense she entered the house, placed the baby somewhere safe, roused the boy and the maid, and then hurried out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.
source_text: Thomasin took the child and ran. When she came to the covered car the horse, though fresh from the stable, was standing perfectly still, as if conscious of misfortune. She saw for the first time whose it was. She nearly fainted, and would have been unable to proceed another step but that the necessity of preserving the little girl from harm nerved her to an amazing self-control. In this agony of suspense she entered the house, put the baby in a place of safety, woke the lad and the female domestic, and ran out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.

text: “I said harsh things to her, and she went away from my house. I did not call her back until it was too late. I ought to have drowned myself. It would have been a mercy to the living if the river had swept me away and saved her. But I cannot die. Those who should have lived are dead, and here I remain, alive!”
source_text: “I spoke cruel words to her, and she left my house. I did not invite her back till it was too late. It is I who ought to have drowned myself. It would have been a charity to the living had the river overwhelmed me and borne her up. But I cannot die. Those who ought to have lived lie dead; and here am I alive!”

text: Yet, in spite of everything, Thomasin did not regret setting out. To her there were no demons in the air, as there were to Eustacia, no hidden malice in every bush and branch. The drops that struck her face were not scorpions, but only ordinary rain; Egdon as a whole was no monster at all, merely bare, impersonal ground. Her dread of the place was sensible, and her aversion to its harshest moods quite understandable. At present, in her eyes, it was a windy, drenched expanse where one might suffer considerable discomfort, lose the track if not careful, and perhaps catch cold.
source_text: Yet in spite of all this Thomasin was not sorry that she had started. To her there were not, as to Eustacia, demons in the air, and malice in every bush and bough. The drops which lashed her face were not scorpions, but prosy rain; Egdon in the mass was no monster whatever, but impersonal open ground. Her fears of the place were rational, her dislikes of its worst moods reasonable. At this time it was in her view a windy, wet place, in which a person might experience much discomfort, lose the path without care, and possibly catch cold.

text: To do things thoughtfully, and little by little, seemed in truth almost a duty in the Egdon valleys at this shifting hour, for there was something in the very state of the heath itself that suggested drawn-out, hesitant uncertainty. Such was the kind of stillness the scene possessed. It was not the stillness of complete stagnation, but the seeming stillness of an almost unimaginable slowness. A condition of vigorous life that so nearly imitates the torpor of death is, in its way, a remarkable thing; to display the lifelessness of the desert while at the same time exercising powers like those of the meadow, and even the forest, stirred in any observer the kind of attention usually called forth by reticence and restraint.
source_text: To do things musingly, and by small degrees, seemed, indeed, to be a duty in the Egdon valleys at this transitional hour, for there was that in the condition of the heath itself which resembled protracted and halting dubiousness. It was the quality of the repose appertaining to the scene. This was not the repose of actual stagnation, but the apparent repose of incredible slowness. A condition of healthy life so nearly resembling the torpor of death is a noticeable thing of its sort; to exhibit the inertness of the desert, and at the same time to be exercising powers akin to those of the meadow, and even of the forest, awakened in those who thought of it the attentiveness usually engendered by understatement and reserve.

text: Even the most uncompromising ascetic might feel that he had an inborn right to roam Egdon—he would still be keeping within the bounds of proper indulgence if he exposed himself to influences like these. Such subdued colors and beauties, at the very least, belonged to everyone by birth. Only on summer days at the height of its splendor did the heath’s spirit rise to something like gaiety. More often, it achieved its force through solemnity rather than brilliance, and that kind of intensity was commonly reached in winter darkness, in storms, and in mist. Then Egdon seemed to awaken in answer; the storm was its lover and the wind its friend. Then it became the dwelling place of uncanny apparitions, and proved to be the long-unrecognized original of those vast dim regions that we feel, without clearly seeing them, to surround us in midnight dreams of escape and ruin, and which are never remembered after the dream until scenes like this call them back to life.
source_text: The most thoroughgoing ascetic could feel that he had a natural right to wander on Egdon—he was keeping within the line of legitimate indulgence when he laid himself open to influences such as these. Colours and beauties so far subdued were, at least, the birthright of all. Only in summer days of highest feather did its mood touch the level of gaiety. Intensity was more usually reached by way of the solemn than by way of the brilliant, and such a sort of intensity was often arrived at during winter darkness, tempests, and mists. Then Egdon was aroused to reciprocity; for the storm was its lover, and the wind its friend. Then it became the home of strange phantoms; and it was found to be the hitherto unrecognized original of those wild regions of obscurity which are vaguely felt to be compassing us about in midnight dreams of flight and disaster, and are never thought of after the dream till revived by scenes like this.

text: They were the mummified heathbells of the past summer, once soft and purple, now bleached of color by the Michaelmas rains and dried into dead husks by the October sun. The note from any one of them was so faint that only the faintest merging of hundreds could barely rise out of silence, and the countless bells across the whole slope reached the woman’s ear only as a withered, broken chant. Yet hardly a single note among the many drifting tonight could have so strongly made a listener think of the place it came from. One could inwardly see the endlessness of those joined multitudes, and understand that each of the little trumpets was taken hold of, swept through, and left behind by the wind as completely as if it were as large as a crater.
source_text: They were the mummied heathbells of the past summer, originally tender and purple, now washed colourless by Michaelmas rains, and dried to dead skins by October suns. So low was an individual sound from these that a combination of hundreds only just emerged from silence, and the myriads of the whole declivity reached the woman’s ear but as a shrivelled and intermittent recitative. Yet scarcely a single accent among the many afloat tonight could have such power to impress a listener with thoughts of its origin. One inwardly saw the infinity of those combined multitudes; and perceived that each of the tiny trumpets was seized on entered, scoured and emerged from by the wind as thoroughly as if it were as vast as a crater.

text: At four o’clock there came a soft tap at the door. It was from Ned, who had been sent by Captain Waye to learn whether any news had reached them of Selina. The maid who admitted him looked up into his face as though at a loss for what reply to make, and ushered him in to where Durnford was seated, saying to the younger man, “Will you tell him, please?”
source_text: At four o’clock there was a gentle knock at the door. It was from Charley, who had been sent by Captain Vye to inquire if anything had been heard of Eustacia. The girl who admitted him looked in his face as if she did not know what answer to return, and showed him in to where Venn was seated, saying to the reddleman, “Will you tell him, please?”

text: Some fifty yards away stood the angle where the two banks met, with the fire burning there; inside the bank, raising to the flames one stick at a time, just as before, was the little child. She watched him languidly as, from time to time, he clambered up into the recess of the bank and stood beside the embers. The wind drove the smoke, the child’s hair, and the edge of his pinafore all one way; then the breeze fell, and the pinafore and hair hung motionless, while the smoke rose straight upward.
source_text: Fifty yards off rose the corner of the two converging banks, with the fire upon it; within the bank, lifting up to the fire one stick at a time, just as before, the figure of the little child. She idly watched him as he occasionally climbed up in the nook of the bank and stood beside the brands. The wind blew the smoke, and the child’s hair, and the corner of his pinafore, all in the same direction; the breeze died, and the pinafore and hair lay still, and the smoke went up straight.

text: Eustacia rose and went with him in the direction he indicated, making her way through the damp heath and fern, with the sounds of the revelers still following behind them as they kept up their dance. The moon had now grown bright and silvery, yet the heath resisted that light, and the result was a striking sight: a stretch of country dark and without a gleam beneath an atmosphere filled, from the very zenith to its farthest edges, with the whitest light. To an eye looking down from above, their two faces would have seemed to float in that expanse like two pearls upon a table of ebony.
source_text: Eustacia arose, and walked beside him in the direction signified, brushing her way over the damping heath and fern, and followed by the strains of the merrymakers, who still kept up the dance. The moon had now waxed bright and silvery, but the heath was proof against such illumination, and there was to be observed the striking scene of a dark, rayless tract of country under an atmosphere charged from its zenith to its extremities with whitest light. To an eye above them their two faces would have appeared amid the expanse like two pearls on a table of ebony.

text: “What is the purport of this absurd tale that folk are spreading about Clara and Mr. Venn? I should deem such a scandal degrading if there were the slightest likelihood of its being true. How could so rank a falsehood have come into being? They say one must travel far to learn the news of one’s own village, and it seems I have found it so. Naturally I deny the story wherever it is mentioned; yet it is most irritating, and I cannot imagine how it began. It is far too ridiculous that a girl like Clara should so shame us by being cast off on the very day of her marriage. What has she done?”
source_text: “What is the meaning of this silly story that people are circulating about Thomasin and Mr. Wildeve? I should call such a scandal humiliating if there was the least chance of its being true. How could such a gross falsehood have arisen? It is said that one should go abroad to hear news of home, and I appear to have done it. Of course I contradict the tale everywhere; but it is very vexing, and I wonder how it could have originated. It is too ridiculous that such a girl as Thomasin could so mortify us as to get jilted on the wedding day. What has she done?”

text: Men turned aside by refusal wander as instinctively as bees that have lost their hive, and the calling to which he had half-bitterly given himself was, in several respects, suited to Venn’s nature. Yet his straying, under the pressure of ancient feeling, had often taken the road toward Egdon, though he never thrust himself upon the woman who drew him thither. To be in Thomasin’s heath, and close to her, yet hidden from her sight, was the last small lamb of delight remaining to him.
source_text: Rejected suitors take to roaming as naturally as unhived bees; and the business to which he had cynically devoted himself was in many ways congenial to Venn. But his wanderings, by mere stress of old emotions, had frequently taken an Egdon direction, though he never intruded upon her who attracted him thither. To be in Thomasin’s heath, and near her, yet unseen, was the one ewe-lamb of pleasure left to him.

text: So perfect, so subtle, and so essential was the figure’s effect on the dark cluster of hills that it seemed the only plain reason for their shape. Without it, there was a dome lacking its lantern; with it, the whole architectural need of the mass was fulfilled. The scene was remarkably unified, for the valley, the upland, the barrow, and the figure standing above it together made only a single whole. To look at one part or another of the grouping was not to see something complete, but merely a fragment of one.
source_text: Such a perfect, delicate, and necessary finish did the figure give to the dark pile of hills that it seemed to be the only obvious justification of their outline. Without it, there was the dome without the lantern; with it the architectural demands of the mass were satisfied. The scene was strangely homogeneous, in that the vale, the upland, the barrow, and the figure above it amounted only to unity. Looking at this or that member of the group was not observing a complete thing, but a fraction of a thing.

text: To be forsaken by the two women—he who had once been cherished by both—was an irony too sharp to be borne. He could find a decent refuge only in Thomasin; and once he had made her his wife, he imagined that Eustacia’s remorse would begin, and endure long and cruelly. It was scarcely strange that Prynne, unaware of the new man lurking behind the curtain of events, should have taken Eustacia for an actress. To suppose that the note was anything more than a passing caprice, to conclude that she truly surrendered him to Thomasin, would have demanded a prior knowledge of the change wrought in her by that man’s power. Who could have guessed that she had become liberal through the avarice of a fresh passion, that in desiring one cousin she was indulgent toward the other, that in her hunger to possess she was already yielding?
source_text: To lose the two women—he who had been the well-beloved of both—was too ironical an issue to be endured. He could only decently save himself by Thomasin; and once he became her husband, Eustacia’s repentance, he thought, would set in for a long and bitter term. It was no wonder that Wildeve, ignorant of the new man at the back of the scene, should have supposed Eustacia to be playing a part. To believe that the letter was not the result of some momentary pique, to infer that she really gave him up to Thomasin, would have required previous knowledge of her transfiguration by that man’s influence. Who was to know that she had grown generous in the greediness of a new passion, that in coveting one cousin she was dealing liberally with another, that in her eagerness to appropriate she gave way?

text: “Ah, yes, there is much wisdom, no doubt, in viewing all matters from the cold standpoint of advantage,” said Lenora, with a fire of feeling kept down beneath her words. “But what reason have you for supposing there is aught between Mr. Vale and me? I, too, have a spirit, and no less than you. I am affronted; and any woman in my place would be. It was a gracious surrender on my part to become Adrian’s wife, and not a stratagem, let me remind you; and therefore I will not submit to being regarded as a plotter, merely tolerated because she has slipped herself into the household.”
source_text: “Ah, yes, it is very well to see things in that business point of view,” murmured Eustacia with a smothered fire of feeling. “But why should you think there is anything between me and Mr. Wildeve? I have a spirit as well as you. I am indignant; and so would any woman be. It was a condescension in me to be Clym’s wife, and not a manœuvre, let me remind you; and therefore I will not be treated as a schemer whom it becomes necessary to bear with because she has crept into the family.”

text: Wildeve was a nervous, excitable man, and the game was beginning to wear upon his temper. He writhed, fumed, and shifted in his seat, while the beating of his heart was almost audible. Venn sat with his lips firmly closed and his eyes narrowed to a couple of insignificant twinkles; he scarcely seemed to breathe. He might have been an Arab, or an automaton; he would have been like a statue of red sandstone but for the movement of his arm as he handled the dice-box.
source_text: Wildeve was a nervous and excitable man, and the game was beginning to tell upon his temper. He writhed, fumed, shifted his seat, and the beating of his heart was almost audible. Venn sat with lips impassively closed and eyes reduced to a pair of unimportant twinkles; he scarcely appeared to breathe. He might have been an Arab, or an automaton; he would have been like a red sandstone statue but for the motion of his arm with the dice-box.

text: “No,” he said at last. “All things considered, I don’t blame you. Only think how abrupt it has seemed to me. There I was, knowing nothing whatever, and then all at once I am told that Tamsin has gone to be married. Well, I dare say there was nothing else to be done. Do you know, Mother,” he went on after a little, with a sudden interest in his own former history, “I once used to think of Tamsin as a sweetheart. Yes, I did. How strange boys are! And when I came home and saw her this time, she seemed so much kinder and more affectionate than usual that it brought all that old time back to me, especially on the night of the supper, when she was ill. And yet we kept the supper on just the same—wasn’t that rather hard on her?”
source_text: “No,” he said slowly. “Upon the whole I don’t blame you. But just consider how sudden it seems to me. Here was I, knowing nothing; and then I am told all at once that Tamsie is gone to be married. Well, I suppose there was nothing better to do. Do you know, Mother,” he continued after a moment or two, looking suddenly interested in his own past history, “I once thought of Tamsin as a sweetheart? Yes, I did. How odd boys are! And when I came home and saw her this time she seemed so much more affectionate than usual, that I was quite reminded of those days, particularly on the night of the party, when she was unwell. We had the party just the same—was not that rather cruel to her?”

text: As he was still musing, footsteps came down the stairs. It was the nurse, carrying in her hand a rolled bundle of wet paper. She was so absorbed in what she was doing that she scarcely noticed Venn. She took some lengths of twine from a cupboard, stretched them across the fireplace, and tied each end to the firedog, which had already been drawn forward for that purpose. Then, unrolling the damp sheets, she began pinning them up one by one on the strings, like clothes on a line.
source_text: While he ruminated a footstep descended the stairs. It was the nurse, who brought in her hand a rolled mass of wet paper. The woman was so engrossed with her occupation that she hardly saw Venn. She took from a cupboard some pieces of twine, which she strained across the fireplace, tying the end of each piece to the firedog, previously pulled forward for the purpose, and, unrolling the wet papers, she began pinning them one by one to the strings in a manner of clothes on a line.

text: Had some watcher been stationed close beside the mound, he would have discovered that these figures were lads and men from the surrounding hamlets. Each, in mounting the barrow, had come burdened with bundles of furze, borne on the shoulder by means of a long pole sharpened at either end, so that the faggots might be speared upon it with ease—two before and two behind. They had come from a stretch of the heath some quarter of a mile away, to the rear, where furze was almost the sole growth of the ground.
source_text: Had a looker-on been posted in the immediate vicinity of the barrow, he would have learned that these persons were boys and men of the neighbouring hamlets. Each, as he ascended the barrow, had been heavily laden with furze faggots, carried upon the shoulder by means of a long stake sharpened at each end for impaling them easily—two in front and two behind. They came from a part of the heath a quarter of a mile to the rear, where furze almost exclusively prevailed as a product.

text: Because of this comparatively advanced position, Yeobright might have been considered unlucky. The rural world was not ready for him. A man ought to be only partly ahead of his age; to be entirely in the vanguard of aspiration is fatal to renown. If Philip’s martial son had been intellectually so far in advance that he had tried to achieve civilization without shedding blood, he would have been twice the godlike hero he appeared to be, but no one would ever have heard of an Alexander.
source_text: In consequence of this relatively advanced position, Yeobright might have been called unfortunate. The rural world was not ripe for him. A man should be only partially before his time—to be completely to the vanward in aspirations is fatal to fame. Had Philip’s warlike son been intellectually so far ahead as to have attempted civilization without bloodshed, he would have been twice the godlike hero that he seemed, but nobody would have heard of an Alexander.

text: The only clear meaning in this sky-backed pantomime of shadows was that the woman had no connection with the figures that had replaced her, was carefully keeping out of their way, and had come there for some purpose other than theirs. The observer’s imagination clung by preference to that vanished, solitary form, as though it were something more interesting, more important, more likely to have a history worth knowing than these newcomers, and it unconsciously treated them as intruders. But they stayed, and settled in; and the lonely figure who had until then reigned over the solitude did not seem, for the moment, likely to return.
source_text: The only intelligible meaning in this sky-backed pantomime of silhouettes was that the woman had no relation to the forms who had taken her place, was sedulously avoiding these, and had come thither for another object than theirs. The imagination of the observer clung by preference to that vanished, solitary figure, as to something more interesting, more important, more likely to have a history worth knowing than these newcomers, and unconsciously regarded them as intruders. But they remained, and established themselves; and the lonely person who hitherto had been queen of the solitude did not at present seem likely to return.

text: “Well, wait until he renews his offer. I think he may do so now that he knows—something I told him. For a moment I don’t question that it is the most proper thing for you to marry him. Much as I used to object to him in former days, I am in agreement with you now, you may be sure. It is the only way out of a false position, and a very humiliating one.”
source_text: “Well, wait till he repeats his offer. I think he may do it, now that he knows—something I told him. I don’t for a moment dispute that it is the most proper thing for you to marry him. Much as I have objected to him in bygone days, I agree with you now, you may be sure. It is the only way out of a false position, and a very galling one.”

text: “Of course I will. Isn’t it rather foolish of us to keep spending the little money we have when I can reduce our expenses by taking honest work? Being outdoors will do me good, and who knows whether, in a few months, I may be able to take up my studies again?”
source_text: “Surely I shall. Is it not very unwise in us to go on spending the little money we’ve got when I can keep down expenditures by an honest occupation? The outdoor exercise will do me good, and who knows but that in a few months I shall be able to go on with my reading again?”

text: The road already alluded to crossed the lower reaches of the heath, running from one rim of the sky to the other. For a long stretch it followed the line of an ancient byway, which had once diverged close beside the great Roman Western road, the Via Iceniana, or Icknield Street. On the evening in question it might have been observed that, although the twilight had deepened enough to blur the lesser features of the heath, the pale track of the road still shone almost as distinctly as before.
source_text: The above-mentioned highway traversed the lower levels of the heath, from one horizon to another. In many portions of its course it overlaid an old vicinal way, which branched from the great Western road of the Romans, the Via Iceniana, or Ikenild Street, hard by. On the evening under consideration it would have been noticed that, though the gloom had increased sufficiently to confuse the minor features of the heath, the white surface of the road remained almost as clear as ever.

text: She strained to make them out, but could not. Yet her attention was so intense that it seemed as though her ears were doing the work not only of hearing but of seeing as well. At such moments one can almost believe in this enlargement of faculty. The deaf Dr. Kitto was probably under a similar illusion when he said that, through long effort, his body had become so sensitive to vibrations that he had learned to perceive by it as by ears.
source_text: She strained her eyes to see them, but was unable. Such was her intentness, however, that it seemed as if her ears were performing the functions of seeing as well as hearing. This extension of power can almost be believed in at such moments. The deaf Dr. Kitto was probably under the influence of a parallel fancy when he described his body as having become, by long endeavour, so sensitive to vibrations that he had gained the power of perceiving by it as by ears.

text: They stood in silence, gazing at Eustacia, who, lying there in death, surpassed all her living forms. Her pallor did not fully describe the complexion she had worn; it seemed something beyond mere whiteness, almost luminous. The expression of her delicately shaped mouth was gentle, as though some inward dignity had only just persuaded her to stop speaking. A timeless stillness had arrested it in a fleeting passage between ardour and surrender. Her black hair, looser than either of them had ever seen it, spread about her brow like a forest. The proud bearing of her face, which had once seemed almost too pronounced for life in a country home, had now at last found a background fit for it, and one strangely and artistically beautiful.
source_text: They stood silently looking upon Eustacia, who, as she lay there still in death, eclipsed all her living phases. Pallor did not include all the quality of her complexion, which seemed more than whiteness; it was almost light. The expression of her finely carved mouth was pleasant, as if a sense of dignity had just compelled her to leave off speaking. Eternal rigidity had seized upon it in a momentary transition between fervour and resignation. Her black hair was looser now than either of them had ever seen it before, and surrounded her brow like a forest. The stateliness of look which had been almost too marked for a dweller in a country domicile had at last found an artistically happy background.

text: “Stop!” he said commandingly, laying his hand on Mrs. Yeobright’s arm. “We’re positively besieged. There must be fifty of them outside, if there’s one. You stay here with Thomasin; I’ll go out and face them. You must remain now, for my sake, until they’re gone, so that everything may appear right. Come, Tamsie, dear, don’t be making a scene—we’ve got to be married after this; you can see that as well as I can. Just sit still, that’s all—and don’t talk too much. I’ll deal with them. Blundering fools!”
source_text: “Stop!” he said imperiously, putting his hand upon Mrs. Yeobright’s arm. “We are regularly besieged. There are fifty of them out there if there’s one. You stay in this room with Thomasin; I’ll go out and face them. You must stay now, for my sake, till they are gone, so that it may seem as if all was right. Come, Tamsie dear, don’t go making a scene—we must marry after this; that you can see as well as I. Sit still, that’s all—and don’t speak much. I’ll manage them. Blundering fools!”

text: “Stay a little longer, and I’ll give you a bent sixpence,” said Eustacia, in a softer tone. “Feed in one bit of wood every two or three minutes, but don’t put too much on at once. I’m going to walk along the ridge a little longer, though I’ll keep coming back to you. And if you hear a frog leap into the pond with a splash like a stone being thrown in, make sure you come and tell me, because it means rain is on the way.”
source_text: “Stay a little longer and I will give you a crooked six-pence,” said Eustacia, more gently. “Put in one piece of wood every two or three minutes, but not too much at once. I am going to walk along the ridge a little longer, but I shall keep on coming to you. And if you hear a frog jump into the pond with a flounce like a stone thrown in, be sure you run and tell me, because it is a sign of rain.”

text: There could be no question that he himself had caused the gun to go off; and he sprang into the patch of hollies, lashing the undergrowth violently with his stick, but there was no one there. This second assault was a graver affair than the first, and it was some time before Wildeve regained his composure. A new and extremely disagreeable system of threatening had begun, and the purpose of it seemed to be to inflict serious bodily injury upon him. Wildeve had regarded Venn’s first effort as a kind of rough jest, one the reddleman had allowed himself through mere ignorance of better conduct; but now the line had been crossed that separates what is merely vexing from what is dangerous.
source_text: There was no doubt that he himself was the cause of that gun’s discharge; and he rushed into the clump of hollies, beating the bushes furiously with his stick; but nobody was there. This attack was a more serious matter than the last, and it was some time before Wildeve recovered his equanimity. A new and most unpleasant system of menace had begun, and the intent appeared to be to do him grievous bodily harm. Wildeve had looked upon Venn’s first attempt as a species of horseplay, which the reddleman had indulged in for want of knowing better; but now the boundary line was passed which divides the annoying from the perilous.

text: “Yes—he is awake—he will go to the door,” she said, letting out a breath of relief. “Come this way. I have an ill name with her, and you must not be seen. So I am forced to act in secret—not because I do wrong, but because others choose to say that I do.”
source_text: “Yes—he is awake—he will go to the door,” she said, with a breath of relief. “Come this way. I have a bad name with her, and you must not be seen. Thus I am obliged to act by stealth, not because I do ill, but because others are pleased to say so.”

text: From her raised place the weary woman could make out the roof of the cottage below, together with the garden and the whole little enclosure of the home. At that very moment, as she was about to set out, she noticed a second man coming toward the gate. There was something uncertain in his bearing; he did not look like one arriving on business or by invitation. He examined the house with interest, then walked around it and studied the outer boundary of the garden, as though he were visiting the birthplace of Shakespeare, the prison of Mary Stuart, or the Château of Hougomont. After circling the place and returning once more to the gate, he entered. Mrs. Yeobright was annoyed, for she had counted on finding her son and his wife alone; yet after a moment she saw that the presence of an acquaintance would relieve the awkwardness of her first appearance there, by keeping the conversation to general things until she had grown more at ease with them. She descended the hill to the gate and looked into the sweltering garden.
source_text: From her elevated position the exhausted woman could perceive the roof of the house below, and the garden and the whole enclosure of the little domicile. And now, at the moment of rising, she saw a second man approaching the gate. His manner was peculiar, hesitating, and not that of a person come on business or by invitation. He surveyed the house with interest, and then walked round and scanned the outer boundary of the garden, as one might have done had it been the birthplace of Shakespeare, the prison of Mary Stuart, or the Château of Hougomont. After passing round and again reaching the gate he went in. Mrs. Yeobright was vexed at this, having reckoned on finding her son and his wife by themselves; but a moment’s thought showed her that the presence of an acquaintance would take off the awkwardness of her first appearance in the house, by confining the talk to general matters until she had begun to feel comfortable with them. She came down the hill to the gate, and looked into the hot garden.

text: It might well have been supposed that she was listening to the wind, which had freshened somewhat as the night drew on and claimed the attention. Indeed, the wind seemed made for the place, just as the place seemed made for the hour. A note of it was entirely peculiar; what was heard there could be heard nowhere else. Gust after gust came sweeping from the northwest, and as each raced by, the sound of its passage seemed to split into three. There were treble, tenor, and bass notes in it. The broad rebound of the whole over hollows and rises gave the deepest tone to the chime. Then came the baritone humming of a holly tree. Beneath these in force, though above them in pitch, a diminished voice struggled with a rough little air, and this was the special local sound already hinted at. Thinner and less at once accountable than the other two, it was far more impressive than either. In it lay, so to speak, the heath’s own language; and since it could be heard nowhere on earth except on a heath, it gave some slight explanation for the woman’s strained stillness, which remained as unbroken as ever.
source_text: It might reasonably have been supposed that she was listening to the wind, which rose somewhat as the night advanced, and laid hold of the attention. The wind, indeed, seemed made for the scene, as the scene seemed made for the hour. Part of its tone was quite special; what was heard there could be heard nowhere else. Gusts in innumerable series followed each other from the northwest, and when each one of them raced past the sound of its progress resolved into three. Treble, tenor, and bass notes were to be found therein. The general ricochet of the whole over pits and prominences had the gravest pitch of the chime. Next there could be heard the baritone buzz of a holly tree. Below these in force, above them in pitch, a dwindled voice strove hard at a husky tune, which was the peculiar local sound alluded to. Thinner and less immediately traceable than the other two, it was far more impressive than either. In it lay what may be called the linguistic peculiarity of the heath; and being audible nowhere on earth off a heath, it afforded a shadow of reason for the woman’s tenseness, which continued as unbroken as ever.

text: “It was only that I did not know you in this light, ma’am; and being a man of the most melancholy cast, I was somewhat frightened, that was all. Many a time, if you could see how grievously cast down I grow in my spirits, it would set you to trembling, for fear I might come to take my own life.”
source_text: “’Twas that I didn’t know you in this light, mis’ess; and being a man of the mournfullest make, I was scared a little, that’s all. Oftentimes if you could see how terrible down I get in my mind, ’twould make ’ee quite nervous for fear I should die by my hand.”

text: Accordingly, when keeping watch on the night after the feast, the reddleman saw him come up by the little footpath, lean over the front gate of Clym’s garden, heave a sigh, and turn away again. It was plain enough that Dorn’s dalliance was more in imagination than in deed. The reddleman withdrew before him down the slope to a place where the path was nothing but a deep trench among the heather; there he stooped darkly over the earth for a few moments, and then slipped away. When Dorn reached that spot, his ankle was caught by some hidden thing, and he went down headlong.
source_text: Accordingly, when watching on the night after the festival, the reddleman saw him ascend by the little path, lean over the front gate of Clym’s garden, sigh, and turn to go back again. It was plain that Wildeve’s intrigue was rather ideal than real. Venn retreated before him down the hill to a place where the path was merely a deep groove between the heather; here he mysteriously bent over the ground for a few minutes, and retired. When Wildeve came on to that spot his ankle was caught by something, and he fell headlong.

text: “I am in too miserable a condition to comprehend what you are dwelling upon,” said the unhappy man. “By day and by night the cry is in my ears: ‘You have helped to kill her.’ Yet in my self-disgust I may, I confess, be doing you an injustice, my poor wife. Forgive me for that, Helena, for I hardly know what I am saying or doing.”
source_text: “I am in too sorry a state to understand what you are refining on,” said the wretched man. “Day and night shout at me, ‘You have helped to kill her.’ But in loathing myself I may, I own, be unjust to you, my poor wife. Forgive me for it, Eustacia, for I scarcely know what I do.”

text: “Ever since her aunt changed her mind and said she might have the man after all,” replied Humphrey, keeping his eyes fixed on the fire. He was a rather grave young fellow, and still wore the hook and leather gloves of a furze-cutter; his legs, by virtue of that trade, were encased in swelling leggings as rigid as the Philistine’s greaves of brass. “That’s why they went off to be married, I reckon. You see, after raising such a fuss and stopping the banns, it would have made Mrs. Yeobright look foolish to have a grand wedding in the same parish, as if she had never objected at all.”
source_text: “Ever since her aunt altered her mind, and said she might have the man after all,” replied Humphrey, without removing his eyes from the fire. He was a somewhat solemn young fellow, and carried the hook and leather gloves of a furze-cutter, his legs, by reason of that occupation, being sheathed in bulging leggings as stiff as the Philistine’s greaves of brass. “That’s why they went away to be married, I count. You see, after kicking up such a nunny-watch and forbidding the banns ’twould have made Mis’ess Yeobright seem foolish-like to have a banging wedding in the same parish all as if she’d never gainsaid it.”

text: “But you misunderstand me,” said Adrian softly. “All this was indeed disheartening; yet not so disheartening as what I afterwards came to see—that the trade I had chosen was the emptiest, most frivolous, most womanish pursuit to which a man could ever be yoked. That settled it with me. I determined to abandon it, and to seek some sober employment among the people I knew best, and where I might be of the greatest service. So I have returned home; and this is the way I mean to put my purpose into practice. I shall keep a school as near to Blackmoor as I can, so that I may come across here of an evening and hold a night-school in my mother’s cottage. But first I must spend a little time in study, in order to be properly fitted for it. Now, neighbours, I must be going.”
source_text: “But you mistake me,” pleaded Clym. “All this was very depressing. But not so depressing as something I next perceived—that my business was the idlest, vainest, most effeminate business that ever a man could be put to. That decided me—I would give it up and try to follow some rational occupation among the people I knew best, and to whom I could be of most use. I have come home; and this is how I mean to carry out my plan. I shall keep a school as near to Egdon as possible, so as to be able to walk over here and have a night-school in my mother’s house. But I must study a little at first, to get properly qualified. Now, neighbours, I must go.”

text: “I never had the courage to face gunfire,” Christian said hesitantly. “As for marrying, I confess I’ve made the offer here and there, though it has brought me little enough success. Yes, there are one or two households that might have had a man for a master—such as I am—yet now are governed by a woman alone. Still, it may have been awkward had I succeeded; for, you see, neighbors, there would have been no one left at home to keep Father’s spirits down to the proper level that suits an old man.”
source_text: “I never had the nerve to stand fire,” faltered Christian. “But as to marrying, I own I’ve asked here and there, though without much fruit from it. Yes, there’s some house or other that might have had a man for a master—such as he is—that’s now ruled by a woman alone. Still it might have been awkward if I had found her; for, d’ye see, neighbours, there’d have been nobody left at home to keep down Father’s spirits to the decent pitch that becomes a old man.”

text: Eustacia went back to the house. Supper over, she withdrew early and sat in her bedroom, waiting for the hours to pass. The night was dark and ominous, and Captain Vye, instead of wandering out to chat in some cottage or dropping in at the inn, as he sometimes did on these long autumn evenings, remained indoors and sat below alone, sipping his grog. About ten o’clock a knock sounded at the door. When the servant opened it, the candlelight fell on Fairway’s figure.
source_text: Eustacia returned to the house. Supper having been got over she retired early, and sat in her bedroom waiting for the time to go by. The night being dark and threatening, Captain Vye had not strolled out to gossip in any cottage or to call at the inn, as was sometimes his custom on these long autumn nights; and he sat sipping grog alone downstairs. About ten o’clock there was a knock at the door. When the servant opened it the rays of the candle fell upon the form of Fairway.

text: But when he reached his cousin’s house, he discovered that only Thomasin was at home, Wildeve being at that moment on his way toward the bonfire, innocently kindled by Charley at Mistover. Thomasin, as always, was pleased to see Clym, and led him to look at the sleeping baby, shielding the candlelight carefully from the child’s eyes with her hand.
source_text: But on reaching his cousin’s house he found that only Thomasin was at home, Wildeve being at that time on his way towards the bonfire innocently lit by Charley at Mistover. Thomasin then, as always, was glad to see Clym, and took him to inspect the sleeping baby, carefully screening the candlelight from the infant’s eyes with her hand.

text: As she went back over the particulars of his looks and words—things she had then hardly noticed—it grew clear to her how much they had been governed by his awareness of this new development. “If he had been the sort of man to nurse resentment against a woman who had refused him, he would have boasted of his good fortune in triumphant tones; instead of that, he said nothing at all, out of consideration for my unhappiness, and only suggested that he still loved me, as someone above him.”
source_text: In recalling the details of his glances and words—at the time scarcely regarded—it became plain to her how greatly they had been dictated by his knowledge of this new event. “Had he been a man to bear a jilt ill-will he would have told me of his good fortune in crowing tones; instead of doing that he mentioned not a word, in deference to my misfortunes, and merely implied that he loved me still, as one superior to him.”

text: “Bess struck her so shrewdly that the poor maid swooned clean away; and, fearing there might be a stir among us, I slipped behind the great violone and saw no more. But they bore her into the open air, so they say; yet when they turned to look for Bess, she was nowhere to be found. Ah, what a shriek that girl let out, poor soul! There was the parson in his surplice, lifting his hand and crying, ‘Be seated, my good friends, be seated!’ But not a soul would be seated, not for the devil himself. And, oh, what do you think I discovered, Mrs. Everdene? The parson has a complete suit beneath his surplice!—I could spy his black sleeves whenever he raised his arm.”
source_text: “Sue pricked her that deep that the maid fainted away; and as I was afeard there might be some tumult among us, I got behind the bass viol and didn’t see no more. But they carried her out into the air, ’tis said; but when they looked round for Sue she was gone. What a scream that girl gied, poor thing! There were the pa’son in his surplice holding up his hand and saying, ‘Sit down, my good people, sit down!’ But the deuce a bit would they sit down. O, and what d’ye think I found out, Mrs. Yeobright? The pa’son wears a suit of clothes under his surplice!—I could see his black sleeves when he held up his arm.”

text: It became instantly plain that the dance was going on immediately behind the surface of the door, with no room intervening at all. The rustle of skirts and the brushing of elbows, and now and then the thud of shoulders, could be heard close against the panels. Eustacia, though she lived within two miles of the place, had never once seen the inside of this quaint old house. Between Captain Vye and the Yeobrights there had never been much acquaintance, the former having arrived as a stranger and bought the long-deserted house at Mistover Knap not long before Mrs. Yeobright’s husband died; and with that event, and her son’s departure, whatever friendship had been beginning to form was quite severed.
source_text: It became at once evident that the dance was proceeding immediately within the surface of the door, no apartment intervening. The brushing of skirts and elbows, sometimes the bumping of shoulders, could be heard against the very panels. Eustacia, though living within two miles of the place, had never seen the interior of this quaint old habitation. Between Captain Vye and the Yeobrights there had never existed much acquaintance, the former having come as a stranger and purchased the long-empty house at Mistover Knap not long before the death of Mrs. Yeobright’s husband; and with that event and the departure of her son such friendship as had grown up became quite broken off.

text: “I ought never to have sought you out, or, once I had, I ought to have held on to you. But of course I have no right to say such a thing now. I will only ask this—can I do anything for you? Is there anything in the world a man can do to make you happier than you are now? If there is, I will do it. You may command me, Eustacia, to the full extent of my power; and don’t forget that I’m richer now. Surely something can be done to save you from this! It pains me to see such a rare flower in such a savage place. Is there anything you want bought? Do you want to go somewhere? Do you want to get away from the place altogether? Just say so, and I’ll do anything to stop those tears, which would never have fallen if it weren’t for me.”
source_text: “I ought never to have hunted you out, or, having done it, I ought to have persisted in retaining you. But of course I have no right to talk of that now. I will only ask this—can I do anything for you? Is there anything on the face of the earth that a man can do to make you happier than you are at present? If there is, I will do it. You may command me, Eustacia, to the limit of my influence; and don’t forget that I am richer now. Surely something can be done to save you from this! Such a rare plant in such a wild place it grieves me to see. Do you want anything bought? Do you want to go anywhere? Do you want to escape the place altogether? Only say it, and I’ll do anything to put an end to those tears, which but for me would never have been at all.”

text: He was surrounded by a nest of bright green. The fern-like growth all about him, though dense enough, was entirely alike—a grove of vegetation that seemed machine-made, a world of green triangles with serrated edges, and not a single flower anywhere. The air held a steamy warmth, and the silence had never been broken. Lizards, grasshoppers, and ants were the only signs of life to be seen. The place might have belonged to the ancient carboniferous age, when plant forms were few and fern-shaped; when there was neither bud nor bloom, only an unvarying spread of leaves, and no bird gave voice among them.
source_text: He was in a nest of vivid green. The ferny vegetation round him, though so abundant, was quite uniform—it was a grove of machine-made foliage, a world of green triangles with saw-edges, and not a single flower. The air was warm with a vaporous warmth, and the stillness was unbroken. Lizards, grasshoppers, and ants were the only living things to be beheld. The scene seemed to belong to the ancient world of the carboniferous period, when the forms of plants were few, and of the fern kind; when there was neither bud nor blossom, nothing but a monotonous extent of leafage, amid which no bird sang.

text: At this point the conversation ended; Eustacia’s moral discipline had never subjected her to anything harsher than an exchange of this kind, which, if it ever proved useful in producing good deeds, would hardly have been worth the cost. Yet her thoughts soon drifted far beyond her own person; and, filled with a fervent and unaccountable concern for one to whom she was not even known by name, she set out into the broad expanse of the sunburned heath around her, restless as the Jew Ahasuerus. She had gone perhaps half a mile from her home when she noticed a threatening red glow rising from a ravine a short distance ahead—dim and sullen, like a flame in daylight—and she took it to mean Diggory Venn.
source_text: Here the conversation ceased, Eustacia’s moral training never exceeding in severity a dialogue of this sort, which, if it ever became profitable to good works, would be a result not dear at the price. But her thoughts soon strayed far from her own personality; and, full of a passionate and indescribable solicitude for one to whom she was not even a name, she went forth into the amplitude of tanned wild around her, restless as Ahasuerus the Jew. She was about half a mile from her residence when she beheld a sinister redness arising from a ravine a little way in advance—dull and lurid like a flame in sunlight and she guessed it to signify Diggory Venn.

text: “No—don’t speak of it if it isn’t agreeable to you! It will make my skin creep when I’m lying alone in bed and think of it.
