But you will—ah, yes, I know you will, Timothy; and I shall dream of it all night long! A very strange one? What kind of spirit did you mean when you said, a very strange one, Timothy?—no, no—don’t tell me.”
source_text: “No—don’t talk about it if ’tis agreeable of ye not to! ’Twill make my skin crawl when I think of it in bed alone. But you will—ah, you will, I know, Timothy; and I shall dream all night o’t! A very strange one? What sort of a spirit did ye mean when ye said, a very strange one, Timothy?—no, no—don’t tell me.”

text: Throughout the day he had burned with concern to know how Elswyth fared, yet he had not dared to force himself across a doorway where he was scarcely known, and least of all at so dismal a time. Instead, he had busied himself in shifting, with his ponies and their burden, to another point upon the heath, a little east of his former halt; and there he chose a recess with a wary regard for shelter from wind and drizzle, as though he meant to remain there for some while. Afterward he went back on foot over part of the road he had travelled; and, night now having fallen, he turned aside to the left until he found himself behind a holly bush at the lip of a pit not twenty yards from the barrow.
source_text: During the day he had been exceedingly anxious to learn the condition of Thomasin, but he did not venture to intrude upon a threshold to which he was a stranger, particularly at such an unpleasant moment as this. He had occupied his time in moving with his ponies and load to a new point in the heath, eastward to his previous station; and here he selected a nook with a careful eye to shelter from wind and rain, which seemed to mean that his stay there was to be a comparatively extended one. After this he returned on foot some part of the way that he had come; and, it being now dark, he diverged to the left till he stood behind a holly bush on the edge of a pit not twenty yards from Rainbarrow.

text: At last she abandoned her furtive watch, shut the telescope, and turned back to the dying embers. From them no real light now shone out, except when a sharper-than-usual gust skimmed across their surface and stirred a wavering glow that appeared and vanished like a girl’s blush. She bent over the quiet ring of ashes, and choosing from the sticks a piece that carried the largest live coal at its tip, carried it back to the spot where she had been standing before.
source_text: At last she gave up her spying attitude, closed the telescope, and turned to the decaying embers. From these no appreciable beams now radiated, except when a more than usually smart gust brushed over their faces and raised a fitful glow which came and went like the blush of a girl. She stooped over the silent circle, and selecting from the brands a piece of stick which bore the largest live coal at its end, brought it to where she had been standing before.

text: “This, then, is the delight I have gained for all my pains,” she murmured with a sharp bitterness. “Why did I ever try to summon you back? Adrian, there are times when a strange battle rises in my mind. When I grow quiet after your cruelties, I ask myself, ‘Have I only clasped a mere cloud of ordinary mist at last?’ You are a chameleon, and now you wear your ugliest hue. Go home, or I shall come to hate you!”
source_text: “This is the pleasure I have won by my trouble,” she whispered bitterly. “Why did I try to recall you? Damon, a strange warring takes place in my mind occasionally. I think when I become calm after you woundings, ‘Do I embrace a cloud of common fog after all?’ You are a chameleon, and now you are at your worst colour. Go home, or I shall hate you!”

text: Before long he was breathing plainly; and again and again she strove, by the same means, to restore her husband to life, yet no response came from Bede. There was too grave occasion to believe that he and Elspeth alike had passed for ever beyond the influence of such reviving scents. Their efforts did not cease until the physician arrived, when, one by one, the unconscious trio were borne upstairs and laid in warm beds.
source_text: He soon breathed distinctly, and again and again did she attempt to revive her husband by the same means; but Wildeve gave no sign. There was too much reason to think that he and Eustacia both were for ever beyond the reach of stimulating perfumes. Their exertions did not relax till the doctor arrived, when one by one, the senseless three were taken upstairs and put into warm beds.

text: “I did mean to tell you,” said Wildeve. “But I—well, I’ll speak plainly—I did not care to bring it up when I saw, Eustacia, that your star was in the ascendant. To see a man lying there exhausted from hard labor, as your husband was, made me feel that boasting of my own good luck to you would be very inappropriate. And yet, as you stood there beside him, I could not help feeling, too, that in many ways he was a wealthier man than I.”
source_text: “I did mean to tell you,” said Wildeve. “But I—well, I will speak frankly—I did not like to mention it when I saw, Eustacia, that your star was not high. The sight of a man lying wearied out with hard work, as your husband lay, made me feel that to brag of my own fortune to you would be greatly out of place. Yet, as you stood there beside him, I could not help feeling too that in many respects he was a richer man than I.”

text: “Yes, Venner, that may well be so; yet you are not aware of all that lies behind it. Had it pleased God to have done with me altogether, it would have been for the best in every way. But I am learning to endure the horror of my own life. They say that a time comes when a man grows able to laugh at misery from long familiarity with it. Surely that hour cannot be far off for me!”
source_text: “Yes, Venn, that is very true; but you don’t know all the circumstances. If it had pleased God to put an end to me it would have been a good thing for all. But I am getting used to the horror of my existence. They say that a time comes when men laugh at misery through long acquaintance with it. Surely that time will soon come to me!”

text: “You are blinded, Clym,” she said with warmth. “The day you first saw her was an unlucky one for you. And your plan is nothing but a castle in the air, contrived only to excuse this folly that has taken hold of you, and to soothe your conscience about the irrational position you have got yourself into.”
source_text: “You are blinded, Clym,” she said warmly. “It was a bad day for you when you first set eyes on her. And your scheme is merely a castle in the air built on purpose to justify this folly which has seized you, and to salve your conscience on the irrational situation you are in.”

text: “Then there was a London man, who had once been no more than a waiter at White’s clubhouse. He began by playing for half-crown stakes, and then for higher and higher sums, until at last he grew very wealthy, obtained a post in India, and rose to become Governor of Madras. His daughter married a member of Parliament, and the Bishop of Carlisle stood godfather to one of the children.”
source_text: “Then there was a man of London, who was only a waiter at White’s clubhouse. He began playing first half-crown stakes, and then higher and higher, till he became very rich, got an appointment in India, and rose to be Governor of Madras. His daughter married a member of Parliament, and the Bishop of Carlisle stood godfather to one of the children.”

text: The brilliant lights and sooty shadows that contended on the skin and clothing of the people gathered round gave their features and general outlines a force and boldness almost Dürer-like. Yet the lasting moral expression of each face could not be made out; for as the lively flames rose, dipped, and swayed through the air around them, the patches of darkness and flecks of light across the group’s countenances kept changing shape and place without end. Everything was uncertain, leaf-like in its tremor, lightning-like in its briefness. Eye-sockets of shadow, as hollow as a skull’s, would suddenly become pools of brightness; a lantern jaw would be cavernous one moment and shining the next; wrinkles were sharpened into gullies, or wiped away altogether by a shift in the beam. Nostrils were black wells; the sinews in old necks were gilded moldings; rough things without any special sheen were glazed; bright objects, such as the tip of a furze-hook one of the men carried, shone like glass; and eyeballs gleamed like tiny lamps. Those whom Nature had made merely odd became grotesque, and the grotesque itself turned uncanny; for everything was driven to an extreme.
source_text: The brilliant lights and sooty shades which struggled upon the skin and clothes of the persons standing round caused their lineaments and general contours to be drawn with Dureresque vigour and dash. Yet the permanent moral expression of each face it was impossible to discover, for as the nimble flames towered, nodded, and swooped through the surrounding air, the blots of shade and flakes of light upon the countenances of the group changed shape and position endlessly. All was unstable; quivering as leaves, evanescent as lightning. Shadowy eye-sockets, deep as those of a death’s head, suddenly turned into pits of lustre: a lantern-jaw was cavernous, then it was shining; wrinkles were emphasized to ravines, or obliterated entirely by a changed ray. Nostrils were dark wells; sinews in old necks were gilt mouldings; things with no particular polish on them were glazed; bright objects, such as the tip of a furze-hook one of the men carried, were as glass; eyeballs glowed like little lanterns. Those whom Nature had depicted as merely quaint became grotesque, the grotesque became preternatural; for all was in extremity.

text: Thursday, the thirty-first of August, was one of a succession of days on which snug houses felt close and airless, and a cool draft was a luxury; when the clayey gardens split with cracks that anxious children named “earthquakes”; when loose spokes were found in the wheels of carts and carriages; and when stinging insects infested the air, the ground, and every drop of water that could be found.
source_text: Thursday, the thirty-first of August, was one of a series of days during which snug houses were stifling, and when cool draughts were treats; when cracks appeared in clayey gardens, and were called “earthquakes” by apprehensive children; when loose spokes were discovered in the wheels of carts and carriages; and when stinging insects haunted the air, the earth, and every drop of water that was to be found.

text: “No. Will you, dear Cousin Clym, will you go and try to persuade him not to? He pays no heed to what I say, and dismisses me with tales of his setting out on a journey, and of being back tomorrow, and the like; but I do not believe it. I think you could have some influence with him.”
source_text: “No. Will you, dear Cousin Clym, go and try to persuade him not to go? He takes no notice of what I say, and puts me off with the story of his going on a journey, and will be home tomorrow, and all that; but I don’t believe it. I think you could influence him.”

text: The next morning, at an hour when the sun, seen from any point upon the heath, seemed of little consequence beside the towering bulk of Rainbarrow, and when all the lesser rises in the lower ground resembled an archipelago in a fog-born Aegean, the reddleman emerged from the brambly hollow he had chosen for his lodging and mounted the slopes of Mistover Knap.
source_text: The next morning, at the time when the height of the sun appeared very insignificant from any part of the heath as compared with the altitude of Rainbarrow, and when all the little hills in the lower levels were like an archipelago in a fog-formed Ægean, the reddleman came from the brambled nook which he had adopted as his quarters and ascended the slopes of Mistover Knap.

text: She at once dismissed the wretched Christian from her service for the way he had behaved in the matter; but, finding herself quite helpless and unable to manage without him, she later told him he might remain a little longer if he wished. Then she hurried off to Eustacia, her feeling toward her daughter-in-law now far less favorable than it had been half an hour before, when she first planned the visit. Then her purpose had been to ask kindly whether anything had been accidentally lost; now it was to demand outright whether Wildeve had secretly given her money that had been meant as a sacred gift to Clym.
source_text: She instantly dismissed the wretched Christian from her employ for his conduct in the affair; but, feeling quite helpless and unable to do without him, told him afterwards that he might stay a little longer if he chose. Then she hastened off to Eustacia, moved by a much less promising emotion towards her daughter-in-law than she had felt half an hour earlier, when planning her journey. At that time it was to inquire in a friendly spirit if there had been any accidental loss; now it was to ask plainly if Wildeve had privately given her money which had been intended as a sacred gift to Clym.

text: At half-past eleven, when she was satisfied that the house lay utterly still, Selena had lit her candle, drawn on some warm outer garments, taken her satchel in her hand, and, blowing out the flame again, gone down the stairs. Once she stepped into the open air, she found that rain had already begun to fall, and as she lingered for a moment in the doorway it gathered strength, with every sign of turning heavy. Yet having once committed herself to this course, she could not draw back on account of the weather. Even the arrival of Adrian’s letter would not have stayed her now. The darkness of the night was sepulchral; all the earth seemed draped in mourning. The sharp tops of the firs behind the house stood up against the sky like the towers and pinnacles of an abbey. Nothing beneath the horizon could be seen except a light still shining in the cottage of Martha Bawden.
source_text: At half-past eleven, finding that the house was silent, Eustacia had lighted her candle, put on some warm outer wrappings, taken her bag in her hand, and, extinguishing the light again, descended the staircase. When she got into the outer air she found that it had begun to rain, and as she stood pausing at the door it increased, threatening to come on heavily. But having committed herself to this line of action there was no retreating for bad weather. Even the receipt of Clym’s letter would not have stopped her now. The gloom of the night was funereal; all nature seemed clothed in crape. The spiky points of the fir trees behind the house rose into the sky like the turrets and pinnacles of an abbey. Nothing below the horizon was visible save a light which was still burning in the cottage of Susan Nunsuch.

text: The truth was that Yeobright’s reputation had spread far too widely before he ever left home. “It is a bad thing when your fame runs ahead of your means,” observed the Spanish Jesuit Gracian. At six, he had posed a Scripture riddle: “Who was the first man known to wear breeches?” and applause had rolled in from the far edge of the heath. At seven he had painted the Battle of Waterloo with tiger-lily pollen and blackcurrant juice, there being no watercolors to hand. By the time he was twelve, he had in this way become known as both artist and scholar for at least two miles in every direction. A person whose renown travels three or four thousand yards in the time it takes others in like circumstances to get six or eight hundred must surely have something in him. Perhaps Clym’s fame, like Homer’s, owed something to the accidents of his position; still, famous he was.
source_text: The fact was that Yeobright’s fame had spread to an awkward extent before he left home. “It is bad when your fame outruns your means,” said the Spanish Jesuit Gracian. At the age of six he had asked a Scripture riddle: “Who was the first man known to wear breeches?” and applause had resounded from the very verge of the heath. At seven he painted the Battle of Waterloo with tiger-lily pollen and black-currant juice, in the absence of water-colours. By the time he reached twelve he had in this manner been heard of as artist and scholar for at least two miles round. An individual whose fame spreads three or four thousand yards in the time taken by the fame of others similarly situated to travel six or eight hundred, must of necessity have something in him. Possibly Clym’s fame, like Homer’s, owed something to the accidents of his situation; nevertheless famous he was.

text: It was not until that instant that Julian remembered her as the woman who had treated Elise with such savagery. This in part accounted for the curt and hostile manner in which she had received him. Besides, the child had been ill again; and Mrs. Vale now, as she had ever done since the night when he had been pressed into Elise’s attendance at the bonfire, laid his ailments at Elise’s door, believing her to have some witch-like power over the boy. It was one of those feelings that lie hidden like moles beneath the apparent smoothness of conduct, and it may well have been nourished by Elise’s appeal to the captain, when he had meant to prosecute Mrs. Vale for the pricking in church, to let the whole affair go by; which he had, in fact, consented to do.
source_text: It was not till this moment that Clym recollected her to be the person who had behaved so barbarously to Eustacia. It partly explained the insuavity with which the woman greeted him. Moreover, the boy had been ailing again; and Susan now, as ever since the night when he had been pressed into Eustacia’s service at the bonfire, attributed his indispositions to Eustacia’s influence as a witch. It was one of those sentiments which lurk like moles underneath the visible surface of manners, and may have been kept alive by Eustacia’s entreaty to the captain, at the time that he had intended to prosecute Susan for the pricking in church, to let the matter drop; which he accordingly had done.

text: A lonely place near Rainbarrow, she knew, was often Venn’s chosen stopping-place when he was in this district; and she guessed at once that she had come upon this secret retreat of his. The question immediately came into her mind whether she ought to ask him to show her the way home. Eager, in her distress, to get back, she decided that she would call on him, odd as it was to appear before him at that hour and in that spot. But when Thomasin, carrying out this resolve, reached the van and peered inside, she found it empty, though there could be no doubt that it belonged to the reddleman. The fire was still alight in the stove, and the lantern was hanging from its hook. About the doorway the ground was only lightly sprinkled with rain, not soaked through, which showed her that the door had not been open long.
source_text: A certain secluded spot near Rainbarrow was, she knew, often Venn’s chosen centre when staying in this neighbourhood; and she guessed at once that she had stumbled upon this mysterious retreat. The question arose in her mind whether or not she should ask him to guide her into the path. In her anxiety to reach home she decided that she would appeal to him, notwithstanding the strangeness of appearing before his eyes at this place and season. But when, in pursuance of this resolve, Thomasin reached the van and looked in she found it to be untenanted; though there was no doubt that it was the reddleman’s. The fire was burning in the stove, the lantern hung from the nail. Round the doorway the floor was merely sprinkled with rain, and not saturated, which told her that the door had not long been opened.

text: How close she stood to Arlen! The thought of it was dreadful. She could feel the rise and fall of his breath, and, no doubt, he could feel hers as well. How cruelly she had dealt with him! and yet there they were, keeping the same time together. The spell of the dance astonished her. A distinct boundary, clear as a visible rail, separated her life within this whirling maze from her life beyond it. To begin dancing had been like passing into another climate; outside, she had been held in a frost as sharp as any polar cold, compared with the warm, exotic pulse that lived here. She had entered the dance out of the fretful hours of her later days as one might step, after a night’s walking in the woods, into a chamber lit with splendor. Arlen alone would have meant only excitement; Arlen, joined to the dance, the moonlight, and the hush of secrecy, became a delight. Whether it was chiefly his nature that made up this sweet confusion, or whether the dance and the scene bore the greater share in it, was a subtle question on which even she was wholly at sea.
source_text: How near she was to Wildeve! it was terrible to think of. She could feel his breathing, and he, of course, could feel hers. How badly she had treated him! yet, here they were treading one measure. The enchantment of the dance surprised her. A clear line of difference divided like a tangible fence her experience within this maze of motion from her experience without it. Her beginning to dance had been like a change of atmosphere; outside, she had been steeped in arctic frigidity by comparison with the tropical sensations here. She had entered the dance from the troubled hours of her late life as one might enter a brilliant chamber after a night walk in a wood. Wildeve by himself would have been merely an agitation; Wildeve added to the dance, and the moonlight, and the secrecy, began to be a delight. Whether his personality supplied the greater part of this sweetly compounded feeling, or whether the dance and the scene weighed the more therein, was a nice point upon which Eustacia herself was entirely in a cloud.

text: The moment he could breathe again he sat up and listened. In the darkness beyond there was no sound at all, except for the faint, listless movement of the summer wind. Reaching out to find the thing that had thrown him down, he discovered that two clumps of heath had been tied together across the path, making a loop that would be sure to trip any passerby. Wildeve tore away the cord that held them, and went on at a fair pace. When he reached home, he saw that the cord was red. It was exactly what he had expected.
source_text: As soon as he had recovered the power of respiration he sat up and listened. There was not a sound in the gloom beyond the spiritless stir of the summer wind. Feeling about for the obstacle which had flung him down, he discovered that two tufts of heath had been tied together across the path, forming a loop, which to a traveller was certain overthrow. Wildeve pulled off the string that bound them, and went on with tolerable quickness. On reaching home he found the cord to be of a reddish colour. It was just what he had expected.

text: “If there was one thing to leave me utterly bewildered, it was this incomprehensible matter!... Diggory, if we who are still alive were only permitted to speak with the dead—just once, for a single minute, even through a screen of iron bars, as though they were prisoners—what we might discover! How many who now go riding along with smiles would then be forced to hang their heads! And this mystery—I should know its secret at once. But the grave has sealed her in forever; and how is it to be discovered now?”
source_text: “If there was one thing wanting to bewilder me it was this incomprehensible thing!... Diggory, if we, who remain alive, were only allowed to hold conversation with the dead—just once, a bare minute, even through a screen of iron bars, as with persons in prison—what we might learn! How many who now ride smiling would hide their heads! And this mystery—I should then be at the bottom of it at once. But the grave has forever shut her in; and how shall it be found out now?”

text: “Yes, but there is a difference between then and now, ma’am. She is upset now, and I have thought that if you were to speak to her about me, and hold a good opinion of me yourself, there might be some chance of bringing her over and making her quite free of this Wildeve’s coming and going, and his never knowing whether he will have her or not.”
source_text: “Yes. But there is a difference between then and now, ma’am. She is distressed now, and I have thought that if you were to talk to her about me, and think favourably of me yourself, there might be a chance of winning her round, and getting her quite independent of this Wildeve’s backward and forward play, and his not knowing whether he’ll have her or no.”

text: “Ah, you cannot know. You have seen farther than I have, and have been into towns and among persons of whom I have only ever heard, and have lived more years than I; yet in this one thing I am older than you. There was another man whom I loved once, and now it is you that I love.”
source_text: “Ah, you don’t know. You have seen more than I, and have been into cities and among people that I have only heard of, and have lived more years than I; but yet I am older at this than you. I loved another man once, and now I love you.”

text: “It is hard for you to test me with that remark. A woman may have her reasons even when she is not without feeling, and if I felt ‘worse luck,’ it was no shameful feeling—it was only too natural. There, you see that at any rate I make no attempt at falsehood. Do you remember how, before we were married, I warned you that I did not possess the virtues of a good wife?”
source_text: “It is hard in you to probe me with that remark. A woman may have reason, though she is not without heart, and if I felt ‘worse luck,’ it was no ignoble feeling—it was only too natural. There, you see that at any rate I do not attempt untruths. Do you remember how, before we were married, I warned you that I had not good wifely qualities?”

text: Seven o’clock, the hour appointed for the rehearsal, arrived, and before long Eustacia could hear voices in the fuelhouse. To relieve, in some small degree, her settled sense of the darkness of human existence, she went to the “linhay,” or lean-to shed, which formed the root-store of their dwelling and adjoined the fuelhouse. In the mud wall there was a little rough opening, originally meant for pigeons, through which the interior of the next shed could be seen. A light was shining there now; and Eustacia mounted a stool to peer in upon the scene.
source_text: Seven o’clock, the hour of the rehearsal, came round, and in a short time Eustacia could hear voices in the fuelhouse. To dissipate in some trifling measure her abiding sense of the murkiness of human life she went to the “linhay” or lean-to shed, which formed the root-store of their dwelling and abutted on the fuelhouse. Here was a small rough hole in the mud wall, originally made for pigeons, through which the interior of the next shed could be viewed. A light came from it now; and Eustacia stepped upon a stool to look in upon the scene.

text: “’Tis cleft wood, that’s what it is,” said Timothy Fairway. “Nothing would burn like that except sound timber. And it’s on the knap afore the old captain’s house at Mistover. Such a queer mortal as that man is! To keep a little fire inside his own bank and ditch, so nobody else may enjoy it or come near it! And what a mad old chap he must be, to light a bonfire when there’s no youngsters to delight.”
source_text: “’Tis cleft-wood, that’s what ’tis,” said Timothy Fairway. “Nothing would burn like that except clean timber. And ’tis on the knap afore the old captain’s house at Mistover. Such a queer mortal as that man is! To have a little fire inside your own bank and ditch, that nobody else may enjoy it or come anigh it! And what a zany an old chap must be, to light a bonfire when there’s no youngsters to please.”

text: The loft was lit by a semicircular opening, through which the pigeons made their way to their roosts in those same high quarters of the building; and from this opening the sun cast a bright yellow patch upon the girl’s figure as she knelt there, plunging her bare arms into the soft brown fern that, in such abundance, was used on Egdon for packing away stores of every kind. The pigeons wheeled about her head with complete indifference, and her aunt’s face was just visible above the loft floor, touched by a few wandering specks of light, as she stood halfway up the ladder, gazing toward a place she was not bold enough to climb to.
source_text: The loft was lighted by a semicircular hole, through which the pigeons crept to their lodgings in the same high quarters of the premises; and from this hole the sun shone in a bright yellow patch upon the figure of the maiden as she knelt and plunged her naked arms into the soft brown fern, which, from its abundance, was used on Egdon in packing away stores of all kinds. The pigeons were flying about her head with the greatest unconcern, and the face of her aunt was just visible above the floor of the loft, lit by a few stray motes of light, as she stood halfway up the ladder, looking at a spot into which she was not climber enough to venture.

text: “Yes, you ought to try harder,” echoed Grandfer with great insistence, as though the idea had been his own in the first place. “In plain conscience, every man ought either to marry or to go as a soldier. It’s a disgrace to the nation to do neither the one nor the other. I did both, thank God! Neither to bring men into the world nor to put ’em down—that’s the mark of a poor, idle sort of fellow indeed.”
source_text: “Yes, you should try more,” echoed the Grandfer with insistence, as if he had been the first to make the suggestion. “In common conscience every man ought either to marry or go for a soldier. ’Tis a scandal to the nation to do neither one nor t’other. I did both, thank God! Neither to raise men nor to lay ’em low—that shows a poor do-nothing spirit indeed.”

text: Thomasin followed him as he turned this way and that to skirt the larger bushes, pausing now and then to shade the lantern while he glanced back over his shoulder to judge where Rainbarrow lay above them, since it was essential to keep it straight behind them if they were to hold the proper course.
source_text: Thomasin followed him as he wound right and left to avoid the larger bushes, stopping occasionally and covering the lantern, while he looked over his shoulder to gain some idea of the position of Rainbarrow above them, which it was necessary to keep directly behind their backs to preserve a proper course.

text: The following day was gloomy enough at Blooms-End. Yeobright stayed in his study, bent over the open books; but the work accomplished in those hours was miserably little. Determined that nothing in his behavior toward his mother should suggest sulkiness, he had from time to time spoken to her on ordinary matters, and had ignored the brevity of her replies. With the same resolve to maintain the appearance of conversation, he said, about seven o’clock in the evening, “There’s going to be an eclipse of the moon tonight. I’m going out to watch it.” Then, putting on his overcoat, he left her.
source_text: The next day was gloomy enough at Blooms-End. Yeobright remained in his study, sitting over the open books; but the work of those hours was miserably scant. Determined that there should be nothing in his conduct towards his mother resembling sullenness, he had occasionally spoken to her on passing matters, and would take no notice of the brevity of her replies. With the same resolve to keep up a show of conversation he said, about seven o’clock in the evening, “There’s an eclipse of the moon tonight. I am going out to see it.” And, putting on his overcoat, he left her.

text: “Nonsense, Edmund. Brace yourself like a proper man! Pru, my dear, you and I shall have a reel—eh, my sweet?—before the light fails so completely that no one can judge how comely you remain, though many summers have gone by since your husband, a witch’s brat, plucked you away from me.”
source_text: “Nonsense, Christian. Lift up your spirits like a man! Susy, dear, you and I will have a jig—hey, my honey?—before ’tis quite too dark to see how well-favoured you be still, though so many summers have passed since your husband, a son of a witch, snapped you up from me.”

text: “Well, I can’t make out why a quiet, ladylike little creature like Tamsin Yeobright would wish to be married in such a shabby fashion,” said Susan Nunsuch, the broad woman, who liked to keep to her first topic. “It’s worse than what the poorest folk do. And I shouldn’t have cared for the man, though some may call him good-looking.”
source_text: “Well, I can’t understand a quiet ladylike little body like Tamsin Yeobright caring to be married in such a mean way,” said Susan Nunsuch, the wide woman, who preferred the original subject. “’Tis worse than the poorest do. And I shouldn’t have cared about the man, though some may say he’s good-looking.”

text: In half an hour he had gained the summit. The sky was clear from edge to edge, and the moon scattered her beams across the whole heath, though they scarcely lighted it, save where paths and watercourses had exposed the white flints and shining quartz sand, leaving pale streaks upon the general darkness. After standing there for a while he bent down and touched the heather. It was dry, and he threw himself upon the barrow, his face turned toward the moon, which formed in each of his eyes a little image of herself.
source_text: In half an hour he stood at the top. The sky was clear from verge to verge, and the moon flung her rays over the whole heath, but without sensibly lighting it, except where paths and water-courses had laid bare the white flints and glistening quartz sand, which made streaks upon the general shade. After standing awhile he stooped and felt the heather. It was dry, and he flung himself down upon the barrow, his face towards the moon, which depicted a small image of herself in each of his eyes.

text: “Oh yes. I once heard of an Italian who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis— that is, a foreign coin— in his pocket. He kept playing for twenty-four hours and won ten thousand pounds, cleaning out the bank he was playing against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds and went to the broker’s the next day to sell stock so he could pay the debt. The man he owed the money to went with him in a hackney coach, and to while away the time they tossed up to see who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to go on with the game, so they kept playing all the way. When the coachman stopped, he was told to drive back home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who had gone to sell.”
source_text: “O yes. I once heard of an Italian, who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis, (that’s a foreign sovereign), in his pocket. He played on for twenty-four hours, and won ten thousand pounds, stripping the bank he had played against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds, and went to the broker’s next day to sell stock, that he might pay the debt. The man to whom he owed the money went with him in a hackney-coach; and to pass the time they tossed who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to continue the game, and they played all the way. When the coachman stopped he was told to drive home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who was going to sell.”

text: As has already been said, the mummers were seated on a bench, the end of which ran into the little inner room, or pantry, because there was no space in the larger room. Eustacia, partly from shyness, had taken the middle place, and so could look both into the pantry and into the room where the guests were gathered. When Clym went along the pantry, her eyes followed him through the dimness there. At the far end stood a door which, just as he was reaching for it, was opened from inside by someone; and a stream of light poured out.
source_text: The mummers, as has been stated, were seated on a bench, one end of which extended into the small apartment, or pantry, for want of space in the outer room. Eustacia, partly from shyness, had chosen the midmost seat, which thus commanded a view of the interior of the pantry as well as the room containing the guests. When Clym passed down the pantry her eyes followed him in the gloom which prevailed there. At the remote end was a door which, just as he was about to open it for himself, was opened by somebody within; and light streamed forth.

text: It is true that there was a limit to this drive toward sameness. The Leech, or Doctor, kept his distinct appearance intact—his dark clothes, his odd hat, and the bottle of medicine carried beneath his arm could never be confused with anyone else. The same could be said of the familiar figure of Father Christmas, the older man who went with the group as their general protector on long night journeys from parish to parish, carrying a gigantic club and serving as the keeper of the purse.
source_text: There was, it is true, a limit to this tendency to uniformity. The Leech or Doctor preserved his character intact—his darker habiliments, peculiar hat, and the bottle of physic slung under his arm, could never be mistaken. And the same might be said of the conventional figure of Father Christmas, with his gigantic club, an older man, who accompanied the band as general protector in long night journeys from parish to parish, and was bearer of the purse.

text: “Thomasin, don’t tell me what I ought to feel—I can’t bear it. What strikes so hard is the part that goes beyond what we were led to expect, and that may not be greater for them than for me—they may have already braced themselves for the worst.... I was made wrong, Thomasin,” she went on, with a sorrowful smile. “Some widows can protect themselves from the hurt their children cause by giving their hearts to another husband and starting over. But I was always a poor, frail creature with only one thought—I never had the breadth of heart or the spirit for that. Just as desolate and dazed as I was when my husband’s spirit slipped away, I have remained ever since—never trying in the least to put things right. I was still comparatively young then, and by now I might have had another family, and found comfort in them for the loss of this one son.”
source_text: “Thomasin, don’t lecture me—I can’t have it. It is the excess above what we expect that makes the force of the blow, and that may not be greater in their case than in mine—they may have foreseen the worst.... I am wrongly made, Thomasin,” she added, with a mournful smile. “Some widows can guard against the wounds their children give them by turning their hearts to another husband and beginning life again. But I always was a poor, weak, one-idea’d creature—I had not the compass of heart nor the enterprise for that. Just as forlorn and stupefied as I was when my husband’s spirit flew away I have sat ever since—never attempting to mend matters at all. I was comparatively a young woman then, and I might have had another family by this time, and have been comforted by them for the failure of this one son.”

text: For maskers and masking Helena entertained the most utter disdain. The maskers themselves were not troubled by any such sentiment concerning their calling, though neither were they animated by any special ardour for it. No contrast marks more plainly the difference between an old custom and a mere revival than this: in the revival there is heat, eagerness, and a kind of exaltation, whereas a custom that has truly survived goes on in a grave, almost heedless stillness, so that one is left to wonder why an observance performed with so little spirit should be maintained at all. Like Balaam and the rest of the reluctant seers, the actors seem driven by some inward necessity to speak and act the parts allotted to them, whether they choose or not. It is this unconscious quality of performance that, in an age of restorations and imitations, gives the authentic relic its unmistakable note and distinguishes a genuine survival from a counterfeit reproduction.
source_text: For mummers and mumming Eustacia had the greatest contempt. The mummers themselves were not afflicted with any such feeling for their art, though at the same time they were not enthusiastic. A traditional pastime is to be distinguished from a mere revival in no more striking feature than in this, that while in the revival all is excitement and fervour, the survival is carried on with a stolidity and absence of stir which sets one wondering why a thing that is done so perfunctorily should be kept up at all. Like Balaam and other unwilling prophets, the agents seem moved by an inner compulsion to say and do their allotted parts whether they will or no. This unweeting manner of performance is the true ring by which, in this refurbishing age, a fossilized survival may be known from a spurious reproduction.

text: Clym returned to his lodging under the roof much relieved, and spent the afternoon jotting down the outline of a sermon, by which he meant to begin all that now appeared genuinely feasible of the scheme that had first brought him there, and which he had so long kept before him in one altered form or another, through praise and blame alike. He had examined and reexamined his convictions many times, and saw no cause to change them, though he had greatly scaled back his plan. His sight, strengthened by long indulgence in his native air, had improved, but not enough to justify his undertaking so broad an educational venture. Yet he did not complain—there was still, in a modest way, more than enough to call for every effort and fill all his time.
source_text: Clym retired to his lodging at the housetop much relieved, and occupied himself during the afternoon in noting down the heads of a sermon, with which he intended to initiate all that really seemed practicable of the scheme that had originally brought him hither, and that he had so long kept in view under various modifications, and through evil and good report. He had tested and weighed his convictions again and again, and saw no reason to alter them, though he had considerably lessened his plan. His eyesight, by long humouring in his native air, had grown stronger, but not sufficiently strong to warrant his attempting his extensive educational project. Yet he did not repine—there was still more than enough of an unambitious sort to tax all his energies and occupy all his hours.

text: This was the young man who had first recognized Eustacia; and when she now, in the role of the Turk, answered with proper defiance and immediately began the fight, he took particular care to wield his sword as gently as he could. Wounded, the Knight sank to one knee, as the stage direction required. The Doctor then came forward and revived the Knight by giving him a draught from the bottle he carried, after which the struggle began again, the Turk gradually weakening until he was completely overcome—dying as stubbornly in this venerable drama as, it is said, he does even now.
source_text: This was the lad who had first recognized Eustacia; and when she now, as the Turk, replied with suitable defiance, and at once began the combat, the young fellow took especial care to use his sword as gently as possible. Being wounded, the Knight fell upon one knee, according to the direction. The Doctor now entered, restored the Knight by giving him a draught from the bottle which he carried, and the fight was again resumed, the Turk sinking by degrees until quite overcome—dying as hard in this venerable drama as he is said to do at the present day.

text: Meanwhile, Eustacia, left to herself in her cottage at Alderwood, had fallen greatly into dejection at the turn matters had taken. The consequences that might follow from Clym’s learning that his mother had been sent away from his door that day were apt to be unpleasant; and that was a feature in affairs she detested scarcely less than the terrible.
source_text: In the meantime Eustacia, left alone in her cottage at Alderworth, had become considerably depressed by the posture of affairs. The consequences which might result from Clym’s discovery that his mother had been turned from his door that day were likely to be disagreeable, and this was a quality in events which she hated as much as the dreadful.

text: Had Wildeve but known how completely Venn was in earnest, he might have been yet more alarmed. The reddleman had been almost maddened by seeing Wildeve outside Clym’s house, and he was ready to go to any lengths short of actually shooting him in order to frighten the young innkeeper out of his stubborn designs. The questionable legality of such rough compulsion troubled Venn not at all. It troubles few minds of that sort in such circumstances, and this is sometimes no great loss. From the impeachment of Strafford to Farmer Lynch’s summary dealings with the rogues of Virginia, there have been many victories of justice that are but mockeries of law.
source_text: Had Wildeve known how thoroughly in earnest Venn had become he might have been still more alarmed. The reddleman had been almost exasperated by the sight of Wildeve outside Clym’s house, and he was prepared to go to any lengths short of absolutely shooting him, to terrify the young innkeeper out of his recalcitrant impulses. The doubtful legitimacy of such rough coercion did not disturb the mind of Venn. It troubles few such minds in such cases, and sometimes this is not to be regretted. From the impeachment of Strafford to Farmer Lynch’s short way with the scamps of Virginia there have been many triumphs of justice which are mockeries of law.

text: She had not gone far back the way she had come when sounds ahead of her gave notice of people approaching along the same path in conversation. Presently their heads showed against the sky. They were moving slowly; and though it was too dark to make much of their features, their manner of walking made it plain that they were not laborers on the heath. Eustacia drew a little aside from the narrow track to let them go by. There were two women and a man; and the women’s voices were those of Mrs. Yeobright and Thomasin.
source_text: She had not far retraced her steps when sounds in front of her betokened the approach of persons in conversation along the same path. Soon their heads became visible against the sky. They were walking slowly; and though it was too dark for much discovery of character from aspect, the gait of them showed that they were not workers on the heath. Eustacia stepped a little out of the foot-track to let them pass. They were two women and a man; and the voices of the women were those of Mrs. Yeobright and Thomasin.

text: “By taking a slight turn, I can make my way home by the same road as yours. I should be very glad to accompany you as far as Throope Corner.” When he saw that Clara still remained seated in doubt, he went on, “Perhaps you think it imprudent to be seen upon the same road with me after last summer’s business?”
source_text: “By diverging a little I can make my way home the same as yours. I shall be glad to keep you company as far as Throope Corner.” Seeing that Eustacia sat on in hesitation he added, “Perhaps you think it unwise to be seen in the same road with me after the events of last summer?”

text: Wildeve took a pace or two among the heather without answering. The silence was filled by the murmur of a pollard thorn a little to windward, the breezes sifting through its stubborn twigs as through a sieve. It was as though the night were chanting its dirges through closed teeth.
source_text: Wildeve walked a pace or two among the heather without replying. The pause was filled up by the intonation of a pollard thorn a little way to windward, the breezes filtering through its unyielding twigs as through a strainer. It was as if the night sang dirges with clenched teeth.

text: Before the reddleman’s gaze stretched a slow succession of rises from the level road back into the very heart of the heath. Hillocks, hollows, ridges, and slopes followed one another until all ended in a lofty hill that stood out against the still-lit sky. For a while the traveller’s eye wandered over these features, and at last fixed on one striking object upon the summit. It was a barrow. This swollen mound of earth, lifted above the natural line of the ground, stood on the highest point of the heath’s loneliest height. From the valley it looked no more than a wart upon an Atlantean brow, yet in truth it was of large dimensions. It served as the pole and axis of that heathen world.
source_text: The scene before the reddleman’s eyes was a gradual series of ascents from the level of the road backward into the heart of the heath. It embraced hillocks, pits, ridges, acclivities, one behind the other, till all was finished by a high hill cutting against the still light sky. The traveller’s eye hovered about these things for a time, and finally settled upon one noteworthy object up there. It was a barrow. This bossy projection of earth above its natural level occupied the loftiest ground of the loneliest height that the heath contained. Although from the vale it appeared but as a wart on an Atlantean brow, its actual bulk was great. It formed the pole and axis of this heathery world.

text: While these hurried proceedings were going on here, Venn and Thomasin had been making their way across the lower edge of the heath toward the light. They were not close enough to the river to hear the splash, but they saw the carriage lamp taken away and followed its movement out into the meadow. The moment they reached the cart and horse, Venn understood that some fresh mischance had occurred, and he hurried off in the direction the moving light had taken. He walked more quickly than Thomasin and arrived at the weir by himself.
source_text: While these hasty actions were in progress here, Venn and Thomasin had been toiling through the lower corner of the heath in the direction of the light. They had not been near enough to the river to hear the plunge, but they saw the removal of the carriage lamp, and watched its motion into the mead. As soon as they reached the car and horse Venn guessed that something new was amiss, and hastened to follow in the course of the moving light. Venn walked faster than Thomasin, and came to the weir alone.

text: On Egdon it was customary to begin the preface to a story before one had actually stepped inside the house, so that by the time host and visitor came face to face, one might already be well launched into the main part of the tale. Christian had been saying to them, as the door was still opening on its latch, “To think that I, who leave home only now and then, and scarcely even then, should have been there this morning!”
source_text: It was the custom on Egdon to begin the preface to a story before absolutely entering the house, so as to be well in for the body of the narrative by the time visitor and visited stood face to face. Christian had been saying to them while the door was leaving its latch, “To think that I, who go from home but once in a while, and hardly then, should have been there this morning!”

text: “I know of a place in my thoughts. If you could guide me as far as Wessex Quay, I could manage everything that follows. There are steamers that cross the Channel from there, and by that means I should reach Paris, which is where I long to be. Yes,” she implored with all her heart, “if only you will help me to reach Wessex harbour without my grandfather’s or my husband’s knowledge, I can manage the rest for myself.”
source_text: “I have a place in my mind. If you could help me as far as Budmouth I can do all the rest. Steamers sail from there across the Channel, and so I can get to Paris, where I want to be. Yes,” she pleaded earnestly, “help me to get to Budmouth harbour without my grandfather’s or my husband’s knowledge, and I can do all the rest.”

text: The faded officer gradually drew level with his fellow traveller and bade him good evening. The reddleman turned his head and answered in tones that were both sorrowful and preoccupied. He was young, and his face, though not precisely handsome, came so close to being so that no one would have objected to saying it really was, had it not been for the stain that masked its natural colour. His eyes, which shone so oddly through that coating, were in themselves engaging—sharp as a bird of prey’s, and blue as autumn haze. He wore neither whiskers nor moustache, and so the soft lines of the lower part of his face were plainly visible. His lips were thin, and though they seemed held in by thought, there was from time to time a pleasant movement at their corners. He was dressed from head to foot in a close-fitting corduroy suit, of good quality, scarcely worn, and well suited to its purpose, though its original hue had been taken from it by his trade. It set off the fine shape of his figure to advantage. There was something prosperous in his bearing that implied he was not poor for a man of his station. Any onlooker would naturally have asked why so promising a person should have hidden his attractive appearance by taking up such a strange calling.
source_text: The decayed officer, by degrees, came up alongside his fellow-wayfarer, and wished him good evening. The reddleman turned his head, and replied in sad and occupied tones. He was young, and his face, if not exactly handsome, approached so near to handsome that nobody would have contradicted an assertion that it really was so in its natural colour. His eye, which glared so strangely through his stain, was in itself attractive—keen as that of a bird of prey, and blue as autumn mist. He had neither whisker nor moustache, which allowed the soft curves of the lower part of his face to be apparent. His lips were thin, and though, as it seemed, compressed by thought, there was a pleasant twitch at their corners now and then. He was clothed throughout in a tight-fitting suit of corduroy, excellent in quality, not much worn, and well-chosen for its purpose, but deprived of its original colour by his trade. It showed to advantage the good shape of his figure. A certain well-to-do air about the man suggested that he was not poor for his degree. The natural query of an observer would have been, Why should such a promising being as this have hidden his prepossessing exterior by adopting that singular occupation?

text: “Not exactly here; but in this same heath. But ’tisn’t a thing to talk about. What do ye say,” went on Fairway, in a quicker voice, turning to them as though the notion had never come from Grandfer Cantle—“what do you say to giving the new man and wife a little song tonight before we go to bed, seeing it’s their wedding-day? When folks are just married, it’s as well to look cheerful, since looking sorrowful won’t part ’em. I’m no drinker, as we know; but when the women and children have gone home we can step across to the Quiet Woman and start up a dance in front of the married folk’s door. ’Twill pleasure the young wife, and that’s the main thing I should like, for many a skinful I’ve had from her hands when she was living with her aunt at Blooms-End.”
source_text: “Not exactly here; but in this same heth. But ’tisn’t a thing to talk about. What do ye say,” continued Fairway in brisker tones, and turning upon them as if the idea had not been Grandfer Cantle’s—“what do you say to giving the new man and wife a bit of a song tonight afore we go to bed—being their wedding-day? When folks are just married ’tis as well to look glad o’t, since looking sorry won’t unjoin ’em. I am no drinker, as we know, but when the womenfolk and youngsters have gone home we can drop down across to the Quiet Woman, and strike up a ballet in front of the married folks’ door. ’Twill please the young wife, and that’s what I should like to do, for many’s the skinful I’ve had at her hands when she lived with her aunt at Blooms-End.”

text: “It is too late for such words now. Come with me. I am going to the inn to see whether he has come back. Of course I shall get to the bottom of this matter at once. Mr. Berrisford must not think he can play his games with me, or with anyone of my household.”
source_text: “It is too late to speak so. Come with me. I am going to the inn to see if he has returned. Of course I shall get to the bottom of this story at once. Mr. Wildeve must not suppose he can play tricks upon me, or any belonging to me.”

text: “No,” said Elinor, gazing abstractedly through the window toward the blaze, which at that instant so entirely possessed her thoughts that she felt no sting from her grandfather’s blunt judgment. She could discern Tobias’s figure on the bank, tossing and prodding the fire; and there rose before her mind another shape which that very fire might summon forth.
source_text: “No,” said Eustacia, looking vaguely through the window at the fire, which just then so much engaged her mind that she did not resent her grandfather’s blunt opinion. She could see Charley’s form on the bank, shovelling and stirring the fire; and there flashed upon her imagination some other form which that fire might call up.

text: “Well, you’re as ready for it as you’re ever going to be,” he said. “Though that sort of masquerading wouldn’t have got by in our day. Harry, as the Saracen, ought to be a little more grand in his strutting, and John needn’t bellow as if he were turning himself inside out. Other than that, perhaps you’ll manage. Have you got all your clothes ready?”
source_text: “Well, ye be as well up to it as ever ye will be,” he said. “Not that such mumming would have passed in our time. Harry as the Saracen should strut a bit more, and John needn’t holler his inside out. Beyond that perhaps you’ll do. Have you got all your clothes ready?”

text: To Clym’s regret, as evening wore on the weather turned wet and boisterous. The wind worried the corners of the house with a rasping sound, and beat the eavesdroppings against the panes like peas. He moved restlessly through the empty rooms, checking odd noises at windows and doors by forcing bits of wood into the casements and crevices, and pressing the loosened leadwork of the panes back against the glass. It was one of those nights when fissures in the walls of ancient churches seem to widen, when old stains on the ceilings of ruined manor houses are revived and spread from a man’s hand’s breadth to many feet across. The little gate in the palings before his dwelling kept swinging open and shutting with a click, yet whenever he looked out in hope, no one was there; it was as though unseen forms of the dead were passing in to call upon him.
source_text: To Clym’s regret it began to rain and blow hard as the evening advanced. The wind rasped and scraped at the corners of the house, and filliped the eavesdroppings like peas against the panes. He walked restlessly about the untenanted rooms, stopping strange noises in windows and doors by jamming splinters of wood into the casements and crevices, and pressing together the leadwork of the quarries where it had become loosened from the glass. It was one of those nights when cracks in the walls of old churches widen, when ancient stains on the ceilings of decayed manor houses are renewed and enlarged from the size of a man’s hand to an area of many feet. The little gate in the palings before his dwelling continually opened and clicked together again, but when he looked out eagerly nobody was there; it was as if invisible shapes of the dead were passing in on their way to visit him.

text: At times his state was one of complete remorse, not eased by so much as one tear of genuine grief; and then he would writhe where he lay, burning more from his thoughts than from any bodily pain. “If I could only be sure that she did not die believing I was angered with her,” he said one day in such a fit, “it would be better to dwell on than even a hope of heaven. But that I cannot know.”
source_text: Sometimes his condition had been one of utter remorse, unsoftened by a single tear of pure sorrow: and then he writhed as he lay, fevered far more by thought than by physical ills. “If I could only get one assurance that she did not die in a belief that I was resentful,” he said one day when in this mood, “it would be better to think of than a hope of heaven. But that I cannot do.”

text: The matter had now brought itself to its own close, and the next inevitable step was that they should separate. Clym understood this clearly enough, and Eustacia made as though to end the interview; yet he looked at her with the air of one who had still a single word to speak. Had he never sojourned in Paris, perhaps that word would have remained unspoken forever.
source_text: The situation had now worked itself out, and the next natural thing was for them to part. Clym knew this well enough, and Eustacia made a move of conclusion; yet he looked at her as if he had one word more to say. Perhaps if he had not lived in Paris it would never have been uttered.

text: A traveller who might pause and watch any one of these strangers as Venn now watched them would feel himself in immediate converse with regions beyond human knowledge. Here before him stood a wild mallard, newly come from the home of the north wind. The bird seemed to carry within it a vast northern wisdom. Glacial disasters, snowstorm adventures, flashing auroral splendours, Polaris overhead, Franklin beneath one’s feet—the range of its familiar things was astonishing. Yet the bird, like many another philosopher, appeared, as it regarded the reddleman, to hold that one present hour of ease and reality was worth ten years of recollection.
source_text: A traveller who should walk and observe any of these visitants as Venn observed them now could feel himself to be in direct communication with regions unknown to man. Here in front of him was a wild mallard—just arrived from the home of the north wind. The creature brought within him an amplitude of Northern knowledge. Glacial catastrophes, snowstorm episodes, glittering auroral effects, Polaris in the zenith, Franklin underfoot—the category of his commonplaces was wonderful. But the bird, like many other philosophers, seemed as he looked at the reddleman to think that a present moment of comfortable reality was worth a decade of memories.

text: “But personally, I do not greatly mind if it should never come to pass,” she added with a small air of dignity; “no, I can do without you. It is Aunt whom I am thinking of. She is so proud, and sets such store by the respectability of the family, that she would be cruelly humiliated if this tale were to get abroad before—it is settled. My cousin Adrian, too, would be deeply hurt.”
source_text: “But I don’t care personally if it never takes place,” she added with a little dignity; “no, I can live without you. It is Aunt I think of. She is so proud, and thinks so much of her family respectability, that she will be cut down with mortification if this story should get abroad before—it is done. My cousin Clym, too, will be much wounded.”

text: Thus it was possible that the countenance of an elderly man, who, like the rest, had been drawn up to the heights by the climbing blaze, was not merely the nose and chin it first seemed, but a respectable expanse of human features. He stood there, well pleased, warming himself in the fire’s glow. With a poker, or some long stick, he thrust the stray fragments of wood into the roaring heap, staring into the heart of the pyre, and now and then lifting his eyes to judge the stature of the flame, or to watch the great sparks that rose with it and drifted off into the dark. The bright spectacle and the keen heat appeared to gather cheerfulness within him, until it grew into actual joy. With his cane in hand, he began to caper a private minuet, while a cluster of brass seals beneath his waistcoat glittered and swayed like a clock pendulum; and he began as well to sing, in the thin voice of a bee caught in a chimney—
source_text: Hence it may be that the face of an old man, who had like others been called to the heights by the rising flames, was not really the mere nose and chin that it appeared to be, but an appreciable quantity of human countenance. He stood complacently sunning himself in the heat. With a speaker, or stake, he tossed the outlying scraps of fuel into the conflagration, looking at the midst of the pile, occasionally lifting his eyes to measure the height of the flame, or to follow the great sparks which rose with it and sailed away into darkness. The beaming sight, and the penetrating warmth, seemed to breed in him a cumulative cheerfulness, which soon amounted to delight. With his stick in his hand he began to jig a private minuet, a bunch of copper seals shining and swinging like a pendulum from under his waistcoat: he also began to sing, in the voice of a bee up a flue—

text: She regarded him with a wistful sorrow in her eyes while he spoke, and there was that in her face which made plain that more than one soul in the room might have cause to lament such delicacy of feeling. Perceiving at once that she was truly hurt, he appeared uneasy, and went on: “It is only a passing notion, you understand. I have not the slightest intention of refusing to go through with the marriage, my dearest Tamsin—I could not endure that.”
source_text: She looked wistfully at him with her sorrowful eyes as he said those words, and her aspect showed that more than one person in the room could deplore the possession of sensitiveness. Seeing that she was really suffering he seemed disturbed and added, “This is merely a reflection you know. I have not the least intention to refuse to complete the marriage, Tamsie mine—I could not bear it.”

text: Each person was so hidden by the burden of furze he carried in his own way that he seemed a bush on legs until he had dropped it. The group had come along in a straggling line, like a flock of sheep on the move; that is, the strongest went in front, and the weak and the young followed behind.
source_text: Every individual was so involved in furze by his method of carrying the faggots that he appeared like a bush on legs till he had thrown them down. The party had marched in trail, like a travelling flock of sheep; that is to say, the strongest first, the weak and young behind.

text: It was a still, warm, foggy night, thick with the heavy scents of fresh growth not yet parched by the hot sun, and among them especially the smell of fern. The lantern hanging from Christian’s hand brushed the soft fronds as he went by, stirring up moths and other winged insects, which fluttered out and settled on its glass panes.
source_text: It was a stagnant, warm, and misty night, full of all the heavy perfumes of new vegetation not yet dried by hot sun, and among these particularly the scent of the fern. The lantern, dangling from Christian’s hand, brushed the feathery fronds in passing by, disturbing moths and other winged insects, which flew out and alighted upon its horny panes.

text: “I was obliged to go to Lower Mistover tonight,” he said, “and Mr. Yeobright asked me to leave this here on my way; but, by my faith, I tucked it into the lining of my hat and thought no more of it until I got home and was fastening my gate before going to bed. So I have run back with it at once.”
source_text: “I was a-forced to go to Lower Mistover tonight,” he said, “and Mr. Yeobright asked me to leave this here on my way; but, faith, I put it in the lining of my hat, and thought no more about it till I got back and was hasping my gate before going to bed. So I have run back with it at once.”

text: Her cousin looked her full in the face. Thomasin, no doubt, knew nothing of her husband’s having had any part in the events of that tragic afternoon; yet her expression seemed to suggest that she harbored some suspicion, or at least some awareness, of the much-talked-of intimacy that had once existed between Wildeve and Eustacia.
source_text: Her cousin looked her in the face. No doubt Thomasin was ignorant that her husband had any share in the events of that tragic afternoon; but her countenance seemed to signify that she concealed some suspicion or thought of the reputed tender relations between Wildeve and Eustacia in days gone by.

text: Charley had always thought of Eustacia as Eustacia had once thought of Clym when she first saw him—as something romantic and sweet, hardly flesh and blood. So far had the dignity of her bearing and the pride in her speech kept her from him, except for that single blessed moment when he had been permitted to hold her hand, that he had scarcely imagined her as a woman of ordinary life, wingless and earthly, subject to the accidents of a household and the frictions of domestic strife. The private facts of her existence he had only guessed at. She had been a beautiful marvel, appointed to move in a sphere in which his own life was no more than a speck; and now to see her bent against the wild, dripping bank like some helpless, despairing creature filled him with astonished dread. He could stay where he was no longer. Springing across, he went up to her, touched her lightly with his finger, and said tenderly, “You are poorly, ma’am. What can I do?”
source_text: Charley had always regarded Eustacia as Eustacia had regarded Clym when she first beheld him—as a romantic and sweet vision, scarcely incarnate. He had been so shut off from her by the dignity of her look and the pride of her speech, except at that one blissful interval when he was allowed to hold her hand, that he had hardly deemed her a woman, wingless and earthly, subject to household conditions and domestic jars. The inner details of her life he had only conjectured. She had been a lovely wonder, predestined to an orbit in which the whole of his own was but a point; and this sight of her leaning like a helpless, despairing creature against a wild wet bank filled him with an amazed horror. He could no longer remain where he was. Leaping over, he came up, touched her with his finger, and said tenderly, “You are poorly, ma’am. What can I do?”

text: They drew it up with the utmost care until the damp bucket came into view some two yards below them, like a dead friend returned to earth. Three or four hands reached out for it, then the rope gave a sharp tug, the wheel whirred, the two men in front were thrown backward, the thud of a body falling was heard as it dropped away down the sides of the well, and a deep, booming clamor rose from the bottom. The bucket had vanished once more.
source_text: They pulled with the greatest gentleness, till the wet bucket appeared about two yards below them, like a dead friend come to earth again. Three or four hands were stretched out, then jerk went the rope, whizz went the wheel, the two foremost haulers fell backward, the beating of a falling body was heard, receding down the sides of the well, and a thunderous uproar arose at the bottom. The bucket was gone again.

text: On those most nearly concerned the effect was somewhat different. To strangers who had heard of many such cases, this was only one more; but where the blow falls at once, no amount of forewarning can truly prepare the heart for it. The suddenness of her bereavement dulled Thomasin’s feelings to a certain degree; yet, irrational as it was, the awareness that the husband she had lost ought to have been a better man did nothing to lessen her grief. On the contrary, at first this very thought seemed to give the dead husband a kind of heightened significance in his young wife’s eyes, and to supply the necessary shadow to the rainbow.
source_text: On those most nearly concerned the effect was somewhat different. Strangers who had heard of many such cases now merely heard of one more; but immediately where a blow falls no previous imaginings amount to appreciable preparation for it. The very suddenness of her bereavement dulled, to some extent, Thomasin’s feelings; yet irrationally enough, a consciousness that the husband she had lost ought to have been a better man did not lessen her mourning at all. On the contrary, this fact seemed at first to set off the dead husband in his young wife’s eyes, and to be the necessary cloud to the rainbow.

text: It was sometimes rumored that such men were offenders whose wrongs were paid for, in part, by others—that though they had slipped beyond the reach of the law, they had not escaped the judgment of their own minds, and had embraced their strange calling as a lifelong expiation. Otherwise, why should any man have chosen it? In the present instance the inquiry was singularly apt. The reddleman who had come into Wessex that afternoon seemed to be an example of a handsome figure squandered in the service of the unusual, when an ill-favoured one would have answered quite as well. The only thing in him that was repellent was his tint. Strip that away, and he would have passed for as comely a piece of country manhood as one was likely to meet. A shrewd onlooker might have supposed—which was, in truth, in part the case—that he had forsaken his rightful place in life merely because it offered him no hold on the imagination. Beyond this, a glance at him would have led one to guess that his disposition was built upon kindliness, and upon an intelligence so quick as to be almost cunning, though never quite crossing that line.
source_text: It was sometimes suggested that reddlemen were criminals for whose misdeeds other men wrongfully suffered—that in escaping the law they had not escaped their own consciences, and had taken to the trade as a lifelong penance. Else why should they have chosen it? In the present case such a question would have been particularly apposite. The reddleman who had entered Egdon that afternoon was an instance of the pleasing being wasted to form the ground-work of the singular, when an ugly foundation would have done just as well for that purpose. The one point that was forbidding about this reddleman was his colour. Freed from that he would have been as agreeable a specimen of rustic manhood as one would often see. A keen observer might have been inclined to think—which was, indeed, partly the truth—that he had relinquished his proper station in life for want of interest in it. Moreover, after looking at him one would have hazarded the guess that good nature, and an acuteness as extreme as it could be without verging on craft, formed the framework of his character.

text: The reddleman kept hidden his knowledge of that fair lady, and likewise the fact that Eustacia had been there because he had gone to bring her, in fulfilment of a promise made the instant he heard that the marriage was to be celebrated. He said no more than this, and went on with the tale——
source_text: The reddleman kept to himself his acquaintance with that fair personage, and also that Eustacia was there because he went to fetch her, in accordance with a promise he had given as soon as he learnt that the marriage was to take place. He merely said, in continuation of the story——

text: “Thomasin is not her daughter; and even if she were, that would not excuse me. But that is beside the matter. I have resolved to go to her, and all I ask is whether you will do your best to help me—that is, to put the past behind you; and if she shows herself willing to be reconciled, to meet her halfway, either by receiving her in our house or by accepting an invitation to hers?”
source_text: “Thomasin is not her daughter; and if she were that would not excuse me. But this is beside the point. I have made up my mind to go to her, and all I wish to ask you is whether you will do your best to help me—that is, forget the past; and if she shows her willingness to be reconciled, meet her halfway by welcoming her to our house, or by accepting a welcome to hers?”

text: Charley obeyed at once, and she struck the light, showing herself altered in sex, radiant with colors, and armed from head to foot. Perhaps she shrank a little beneath Charley’s keen gaze, though whether any trace of embarrassment at her masculine dress crossed her face could not be told, since it was hidden by the bands of ribbon once used to cover the face in mumming costumes, imitating the barred visor of a medieval helmet.
source_text: Charley did as commanded, and she struck the light revealing herself to be changed in sex, brilliant in colours, and armed from top to toe. Perhaps she quailed a little under Charley’s vigorous gaze, but whether any shyness at her male attire appeared upon her countenance could not be seen by reason of the strips of ribbon which used to cover the face in mumming costumes, representing the barred visor of the mediæval helmet.

text: “Oh, that is of no consequence,” said Adrian, with a smile that was more sorrowful than gay. Then the little party set off, and was soon swallowed by the darkness; and Valen crossed the threshold and went in. The house was met only by the measured ticking of the clock, for no living creature remained within. Mercer, who served him as cook, manservant, and gardener, had gone to sleep at his father’s house. Valen sank into one of the empty chairs and sat there long, lost in thought. Opposite him stood his mother’s old chair; that very evening others had occupied it, and scarcely remembered that it had once belonged to her. Yet to Valen she was almost a visible presence in that room, as she had been always. Whatever place she held in other men’s recollection, in his mind she was the lofty saint whose brightness even his love for Lena could not quite eclipse. Still his heart was oppressed because his mother had not crowned him on the day of his marriage, on the day that should have been the gladness of his soul. And all that had happened since had justified her discernment and shown the depth of her devotion. He ought to have listened to her, for Lena’s sake no less than his own. “It was all my doing,” he murmured. “Oh, my mother, my mother! if only I might live my life once more, and bear for you what you bore for me!”
source_text: “O, that’s no inconvenience,” said Clym, smiling rather sadly. And then the party drove off and vanished in the night shades, and Yeobright entered the house. The ticking of the clock was the only sound that greeted him, for not a soul remained; Christian, who acted as cook, valet, and gardener to Clym, sleeping at his father’s house. Yeobright sat down in one of the vacant chairs, and remained in thought a long time. His mother’s old chair was opposite; it had been sat in that evening by those who had scarcely remembered that it ever was hers. But to Clym she was almost a presence there, now as always. Whatever she was in other people’s memories, in his she was the sublime saint whose radiance even his tenderness for Eustacia could not obscure. But his heart was heavy, that Mother had _not_ crowned him in the day of his espousals and in the day of the gladness of his heart. And events had borne out the accuracy of her judgment, and proved the devotedness of her care. He should have heeded her for Eustacia’s sake even more than for his own. “It was all my fault,” he whispered. “O, my mother, my mother! would to God that I could live my life again, and endure for you what you endured for me!”

text: The sudden burst of tears surprised Elsinore so deeply that she could not at once command herself, and, with a kind of shame, she turned away from him, though the movement concealed nothing from his sight.
