She continued to sob convulsively for a little longer; then the violence of it slackened, and she grew calmer. Merivale had fought down the impulse to draw her to him, and stood silent beside her.
source_text: This outbreak of weeping took Eustacia herself so much by surprise that she could not leave off, and she turned aside from him in some shame, though turning hid nothing from him. She sobbed on desperately; then the outpour lessened, and she became quieter. Wildeve had resisted the impulse to clasp her, and stood without speaking.

text: “Here come I, Saint George, the gallant knight, With blade unsheathed and spear held tight, Who met the dragon in the field and sent him to his doom, And so won bright Sabra, Egypt’s king’s own daughter, for my room; What living man would dare to face My sword uplifted in this place?”
source_text: “Here come I, Saint George, the valiant man, With naked sword and spear in hand, Who fought the dragon and brought him to the slaughter, And by this won fair Sabra, the King of Egypt’s daughter; What mortal man would dare to stand Before me with my sword in hand?”

text: Through the space of five-and-twenty couples they moved in their dizzy course, and a fresh vitality entered into her being. The pale light of evening gave a strange charm to the scene. There is a certain degree and quality of light which unsettles the balance of the senses and dangerously encourages the gentler moods; joined with movement, it drives the emotions to excess, while reason grows drowsy and blind in exact proportion. That light now fell upon the pair from the moon’s disc. All the dancing girls were affected by it, but Eustacia most of all. The grass beneath their feet had been worn away, and the hard, trampled turf, seen slantwise in the moonlight, gleamed like a polished table. The air grew perfectly still; the flag over the wagon that housed the musicians hung limp against the pole, and the players showed only as dark outlines against the sky, except when the round bells of the trombone, ophicleide, and French horn flashed out like great eyes from the shadow of their bodies. The young women’s pretty gowns lost the finer colors of day and appeared, to one degree or another, as a soft misty white. Eustacia drifted round and round upon Wildeve’s arm, her face exalted and marble-like; her soul had departed from her features and forgotten them, leaving them blank and passive, as features always are when feeling has risen beyond their power to tell it.
source_text: Through the length of five-and-twenty couples they threaded their giddy way, and a new vitality entered her form. The pale ray of evening lent a fascination to the experience. There is a certain degree and tone of light which tends to disturb the equilibrium of the senses, and to promote dangerously the tenderer moods; added to movement, it drives the emotions to rankness, the reason becoming sleepy and unperceiving in inverse proportion; and this light fell now upon these two from the disc of the moon. All the dancing girls felt the symptoms, but Eustacia most of all. The grass under their feet became trodden away, and the hard, beaten surface of the sod, when viewed aslant towards the moonlight, shone like a polished table. The air became quite still, the flag above the wagon which held the musicians clung to the pole, and the players appeared only in outline against the sky; except when the circular mouths of the trombone, ophicleide, and French horn gleamed out like huge eyes from the shade of their figures. The pretty dresses of the maids lost their subtler day colours and showed more or less of a misty white. Eustacia floated round and round on Wildeve’s arm, her face rapt and statuesque; her soul had passed away from and forgotten her features, which were left empty and quiescent, as they always are when feeling goes beyond their register.

text: The choice of Captain Vye’s fuelhouse for the rehearsal was determined by the fact that his dwelling stood almost in the very centre of the heath. The fuelhouse itself was as spacious as a barn, and for such a purpose it was an excellent place. The lads who made up the little company of players lived at various scattered points all around, and by gathering here the distances each had to cover would be nearly equal for all who came.
source_text: The choice of Captain Vye’s fuelhouse as the scene of rehearsal was dictated by the fact that his dwelling was nearly in the centre of the heath. The fuelhouse was as roomy as a barn, and was a most desirable place for such a purpose. The lads who formed the company of players lived at different scattered points around, and by meeting in this spot the distances to be traversed by all the comers would be about equally proportioned.

text: One summer day Clym was in the garden, just outside the parlour window, which, as usual, stood open. He was looking at the potted flowers on the sill; Thomasin had revived them and brought them back to the condition in which his mother had left them. From inside the room he heard a little cry from Thomasin, who was sitting there.
source_text: One summer day Clym was in the garden, immediately outside the parlour window, which was as usual open. He was looking at the pot-flowers on the sill; they had been revived and restored by Thomasin to the state in which his mother had left them. He heard a slight scream from Thomasin, who was sitting inside the room.

text: “I was rather surprised to hear that he had taken a cottage. Like everyone else, I had assumed he would have whisked you away to a home in Paris as soon as you were married. ‘What a cheerful, brilliant future lies ahead of her!’ I thought. I suppose he will go back there with you if his eyesight improves?”
source_text: “I was quite surprised to hear that he had taken a cottage. I thought, in common with other people, that he would have taken you off to a home in Paris immediately after you had married him. ‘What a gay, bright future she has before her!’ I thought. He will, I suppose, return there with you, if his sight gets strong again?”

text: He went on his way, not with haste or resolve, but with the slow gait of a man roused from a heavy, stupefying sleep. It was early afternoon when he came into the valley. The look of the place, the quality of the hour, were exactly those of many such moments long before; and these earlier resemblances encouraged the fancy that she, though gone from it now, would presently come forth to meet him. The garden gate was locked, and the shutters were closed, as he had left them himself on the evening after the funeral. He unlocked the gate, and saw that a spider had already spun a great web, fastening the door to the lintel in the belief that it was never again to be opened. After he had entered the house and thrown back the shutters, he applied himself to searching the cupboards and closets, burning papers, and thinking how best to set the place in order for Eustacia’s arrival, until such time as he might be able to bring his long-postponed plan to pass, if that time should ever come.
source_text: He journeyed onward, not quickly or decisively, but in the slow walk of one who has been awakened from a stupefying sleep. It was early afternoon when he reached the valley. The expression of the place, the tone of the hour, were precisely those of many such occasions in days gone by; and these antecedent similarities fostered the illusion that she, who was there no longer, would come out to welcome him. The garden gate was locked and the shutters were closed, just as he himself had left them on the evening after the funeral. He unlocked the gate, and found that a spider had already constructed a large web, tying the door to the lintel, on the supposition that it was never to be opened again. When he had entered the house and flung back the shutters he set about his task of overhauling the cupboards and closets, burning papers, and considering how best to arrange the place for Eustacia’s reception, until such time as he might be in a position to carry out his long-delayed scheme, should that time ever arrive.

text: She set her bonnet upon her head and, quitting the cottage, went down the slope on the side that led toward Hallows-End, where she followed the valley at an unhurried pace for nearly a mile and a half. In time she came to a place where the green floor of the hollow began to spread more broadly, and the furze-clumps drew farther and farther back from the track on either hand, until, where the ground grew richer, they were reduced to a stray bush now and again. Beyond the uneven spread of turf stood a line of white palings, marking in that quarter the limit of the heath. Against the somber landscape they shone with the sharpness of white embroidery upon dark silk. Behind the palings lay a small garden; beyond the garden, an old, broken-roofed, thatched dwelling, looking out upon the heath and taking in the whole length of the valley. Such was the secluded and little-noticed place to which a man was soon to return, after having passed the latter years of his life in the French metropolis, that whirlpool and heart of the elegant world.
source_text: She put on her bonnet, and, leaving the house, descended the hill on the side towards Blooms-End, where she walked slowly along the valley for a distance of a mile and a half. This brought her to a spot in which the green bottom of the dale began to widen, the furze bushes to recede yet further from the path on each side, till they were diminished to an isolated one here and there by the increasing fertility of the soil. Beyond the irregular carpet of grass was a row of white palings, which marked the verge of the heath in this latitude. They showed upon the dusky scene that they bordered as distinctly as white lace on velvet. Behind the white palings was a little garden; behind the garden an old, irregular, thatched house, facing the heath, and commanding a full view of the valley. This was the obscure, removed spot to which was about to return a man whose latter life had been passed in the French capital—the centre and vortex of the fashionable world.

text: When Clym had gone, Thomasin stole upstairs in the darkness, and, after pausing by the cot to make sure the child slept, she went to the window, softly lifted the edge of the white curtain, and looked out. Venn was still there. She watched the pale brightness gather in the sky above the eastern hill until, at last, the moon’s rim rose and poured light over the valley. Diggory’s figure was now plain upon the grass; he was moving with bent head, plainly searching the turf for the precious lost thing, pacing in zigzags to right and left until he should have covered every foot of ground.
source_text: When Clym was gone Thomasin crept upstairs in the dark, and, just listening by the cot, to assure herself that the child was asleep, she went to the window, gently lifted the corner of the white curtain, and looked out. Venn was still there. She watched the growth of the faint radiance appearing in the sky by the eastern hill, till presently the edge of the moon burst upwards and flooded the valley with light. Diggory’s form was now distinct on the green; he was moving about in a bowed attitude, evidently scanning the grass for the precious missing article, walking in zigzags right and left till he should have passed over every foot of the ground.

text: “Strange notions, has he?” said the old man. “Ah, there’s far too much of this sending children to school nowadays! It does nothing but harm. Every gatepost and barn door you come to is sure to have some foul word or other chalked on it by the young rascals—a woman can scarcely pass without shame at times. If they had never been taught to write, they would never have been able to scrawl such wickedness. Their fathers could not do it, and the country was all the better for that.”
source_text: “Strange notions, has he?” said the old man. “Ah, there’s too much of that sending to school in these days! It only does harm. Every gatepost and barn’s door you come to is sure to have some bad word or other chalked upon it by the young rascals—a woman can hardly pass for shame sometimes. If they’d never been taught how to write they wouldn’t have been able to scribble such villainy. Their fathers couldn’t do it, and the country was all the better for it.”

text: One of the men went to the little footbridge and wrenched away the rail. Then the reddleman and the other two descended together into the water, as they had done before, and, with their strength joined, felt their way forward through the pool to the place where it fell away toward its deepest hollow. Venn had not been wrong in thinking that any person who had sunk for the last time would be carried down to this very spot; for when they had searched to about the middle of the water, their thrust was suddenly checked by some obstruction.
source_text: One of the men went to the footbridge and tore off the handrail. The reddleman and the two others then entered the water together from below as before, and with their united force probed the pool forwards to where it sloped down to its central depth. Venn was not mistaken in supposing that any person who had sunk for the last time would be washed down to this point, for when they had examined to about halfway across something impeded their thrust.

text: Wildeve forgot the loss of the money at the sight of his lost love, whose value in his eyes grew in a kind of geometrical progression with each fresh circumstance that reminded him of their hopeless separation. Full to overflowing with that refined misery of which he was capable, he turned and took the opposite path toward the inn.
source_text: Wildeve forgot the loss of the money at the sight of his lost love, whose preciousness in his eyes was increasing in geometrical progression with each new incident that reminded him of their hopeless division. Brimming with the subtilized misery that he was capable of feeling, he followed the opposite way towards the inn.

text: The three voices moved on, faded, and died away in her ear. So much had been granted her, and all else denied. No event could have been more thrilling. For most of the afternoon she had been delighting herself with the thought of the charm that must surround a man arriving straight from beautiful Paris, steeped in its atmosphere and at home in its allure. And this was the man who had spoken to her.
source_text: The three voices passed on, and decayed and died out upon her ear. Thus much had been granted her; and all besides withheld. No event could have been more exciting. During the greater part of the afternoon she had been entrancing herself by imagining the fascination which must attend a man come direct from beautiful Paris—laden with its atmosphere, familiar with its charms. And this man had greeted her.

text: The sameness of his calling had a quieting effect on him, and was in itself a kind of pleasure. This compulsory narrowing of endeavour gave a plain excuse for humble ways to a man of no ambition, whose scruples would scarcely have permitted him to abide so deeply in obscurity had his faculties been left free. Thus Adrian sometimes broke into song for his own diversion; and when he was required to go with Silas in search of brambles for fagot-ties, he would beguile his companion with little pictures of Parisian manners and people, and so make the hours pass.
source_text: The monotony of his occupation soothed him, and was in itself a pleasure. A forced limitation of effort offered a justification of homely courses to an unambitious man, whose conscience would hardly have allowed him to remain in such obscurity while his powers were unimpeded. Hence Yeobright sometimes sang to himself, and when obliged to accompany Humphrey in search of brambles for faggot-bonds he would amuse his companion with sketches of Parisian life and character, and so while away the time.

text: Though Stephen was annoyed by what appeared to him an incomprehensible preference, he was in some degree comforted by discovering that, for the present at least, the question of marriage as it concerned himself was laid aside. During the next few days he caught sight of her now and then from the window of his chamber, wandering forlornly and listlessly about the garden. At moments he was half resentful toward her for having chosen Gabriel; at others he was saddened by the thought that he himself had stood in the path of Gabriel’s happiness, for the man, after all, was as upright and steadfast a young fellow as any on the heath, since he had begun a new life. In truth, Stephen did not know what course to take.
source_text: Clym, though vexed at what seemed her unaccountable taste, was in a measure relieved to find that at any rate the marriage question in relation to himself was shelved. Through several succeeding days he saw her at different times from the window of his room moping disconsolately about the garden. He was half angry with her for choosing Venn; then he was grieved at having put himself in the way of Venn’s happiness, who was, after all, as honest and persevering a young fellow as any on Egdon, since he had turned over a new leaf. In short, Clym did not know what to do.

text: “Why, I should never so much as have thought of such a thing myself,” said Alder in a voice of injured honesty; “but since you’ve spoken of it, Master Holcombe, I’ll confess we were talking of you. We were marvelling what could keep you lurking here and dawdling away your time, when you’ve made so fine and far-spread a name for yourself in the trinket line—that’s the plain truth of it.”
source_text: “Now, it is a thing I shouldn’t have dreamed of doing, otherwise,” said Fairway in a tone of integrity; “but since you have named it, Master Yeobright, I’ll own that we was talking about ’ee. We were wondering what could keep you home here mollyhorning about when you have made such a world-wide name for yourself in the nick-nack trade—now, that’s the truth o’t.”

text: His chamber looked out upon the front of the dwelling and over the hollow of the heath beyond. The last slanting beams of the winter sun cast the house’s shadow across the palings, over the grassy verge of the heath, and far up the valley, where the chimneys and the tops of the encircling trees projected themselves in long black spikes. After sitting at his labour through the whole day, he determined to take a walk upon the hills before darkness fell; and, setting out at once, he crossed the heath in the direction of Mistover.
source_text: His room overlooked the front of the premises and the valley of the heath beyond. The lowest beams of the winter sun threw the shadow of the house over the palings, across the grass margin of the heath, and far up the vale, where the chimney outlines and those of the surrounding tree-tops stretched forth in long dark prongs. Having been seated at work all day, he decided to take a turn upon the hills before it got dark; and, going out forthwith, he struck across the heath towards Mistover.

text: The scarf that had veiled her head was now drawn a little backward, her face lifted somewhat. Her profile stood out against the flat, leaden cloud beyond; and it was as if the side-shadows of a tragic poetess and a great actress had risen together from the grave to fashion an image like neither, yet faintly akin to both. But this was only the surface of the matter. As to character, a countenance may yield certain half-confessions by its outline; it confesses fully only in its alterations. Indeed, the changing play of the features often tells us more of a man or woman than all the other parts of the body’s patient labor combined. The night, then, disclosed little of the woman whose shape it enfolded, since the moving portions of her face were hidden from view.
source_text: The handkerchief which had hooded her head was now a little thrown back, her face being somewhat elevated. A profile was visible against the dull monochrome of cloud around her; and it was as though side shadows from the features of Sappho and Mrs. Siddons had converged upwards from the tomb to form an image like neither but suggesting both. This, however, was mere superficiality. In respect of character a face may make certain admissions by its outline; but it fully confesses only in its changes. So much is this the case that what is called the play of the features often helps more in understanding a man or woman than the earnest labours of all the other members together. Thus the night revealed little of her whose form it was embracing, for the mobile parts of her countenance could not be seen.

text: “Yes, it frightened me. I had not been to church in a long while. And now I shall not go again for a very long time—perhaps never. I cannot bear to meet their eyes after this. Don’t you think it is terribly humiliating? For hours afterward I wished I were dead, but I do not mind now.”
source_text: “Yes, it frightened me. I had not been to church for a long time. And now I shall not go again for ever so long—perhaps never. I cannot face their eyes after this. Don’t you think it dreadfully humiliating? I wished I was dead for hours after, but I don’t mind now.”

text: Elara could find no quiet indoors, now that there was nothing further to occupy her, and she paced aimlessly to and fro upon the hill, not far from the house she was so soon to quit. In these purposeless wanderings she came by the dwelling of Agnes Nuttall, set a little below her grandfather’s. The door stood partly open, and a band of red firelight spilled across the ground without. As Elara crossed that luminous strip she showed for one fleeting instant as clearly as a shape in a phantasmagoria—a being of radiance ringed about by darkness; then the moment was gone, and night received her again.
source_text: Eustacia could not rest indoors, having nothing more to do, and she wandered to and fro on the hill, not far from the house she was soon to leave. In these desultory ramblings she passed the cottage of Susan Nunsuch, a little lower down than her grandfather’s. The door was ajar, and a riband of bright firelight fell over the ground without. As Eustacia crossed the firebeams she appeared for an instant as distinct as a figure in a phantasmagoria—a creature of light surrounded by an area of darkness; the moment passed, and she was absorbed in night again.

text: “Well, it is strange indeed that just as I had made up my mind to go myself, you should choose to do what I suggested long ago. If I wait for you until tomorrow, another day will be gone, and I know I should be unable to rest through another night without having been there. I want this business brought to an end, and I will have it so. You must call on her afterward—it will amount to the same thing.”
source_text: “Well, it is very odd that just when I had decided to go myself you should want to do what I proposed long ago. If I wait for you to go tomorrow another day will be lost; and I know I shall be unable to rest another night without having been. I want to get this settled, and will. You must visit her afterwards—it will be all the same.”

text: “A good many of us came to know it just as well as he did before long,” said the captain with a chuckle. “I spent seven years under water because of it when I was a boy—in that cursed surgery on the Triumph, watching men carried down to the cockpit with their arms and legs blown to bits.... So the young fellow has settled in Paris. He’s manager to a diamond merchant, or something of the sort, isn’t he?”
source_text: “A good many of us knew as well as He before long,” said the captain, chuckling. “I lived seven years under water on account of it in my boyhood—in that damned surgery of the Triumph, seeing men brought down to the cockpit with their legs and arms blown to Jericho.... And so the young man has settled in Paris. Manager to a diamond merchant, or some such thing, is he not?”

text: “I left off selling reddle last Christmas,” said Venn. “It was a paying business, and by then I saw that I had scraped together enough to lease the dairy of fifty cows, such as my father kept in his day. I had always hoped, if ever I altered my way of life, to come back to that point, and now I have reached it.”
source_text: “I gave up dealing in reddle last Christmas,” said Venn. “It was a profitable trade, and I found that by that time I had made enough to take the dairy of fifty cows that my father had in his lifetime. I always thought of getting to that place again if I changed at all, and now I am there.”

text: In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found the walk to Alderworth no trouble at all, but this fierce spell of heat made the journey a serious effort for a woman well past middle age; and by the end of the third mile she wished she had engaged Fairway to drive her at least part of the way. Yet from where she now stood, Clym’s house was no farther to reach than her own home. So she kept on, while the air about her throbbed in stillness and burdened the earth with a weary heaviness. She glanced up at the sky and saw that the sapphire tint which the zenith wore in spring and early summer had given way to a metallic violet.
source_text: In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found no inconvenience in walking to Alderworth, but the present torrid attack made the journey a heavy undertaking for a woman past middle age; and at the end of the third mile she wished that she had hired Fairway to drive her a portion at least of the distance. But from the point at which she had arrived it was as easy to reach Clym’s house as to get home again. So she went on, the air around her pulsating silently, and oppressing the earth with lassitude. She looked at the sky overhead, and saw that the sapphirine hue of the zenith in spring and early summer had been replaced by a metallic violet.

text: “Well, of course I don’t understand such fine distinctions,” said Sam, backing away from what might have become an unpleasant argument; “and what she really is, we’ll have to leave time to show us. The thing I’ve actually come about is this: to borrow the longest and strongest rope you’ve got. The captain’s bucket has fallen into the well, and they’re short of water; and since all the men are at home today, we think we can get it out for him. We already have three cart-ropes, but they’re not long enough to reach the bottom.”
source_text: “Well, of course I don’t understand such niceties,” said Sam, withdrawing from a possibly unpleasant argument; “and what she is we must wait for time to tell us. The business that I have really called about is this, to borrow the longest and strongest rope you have. The captain’s bucket has dropped into the well, and they are in want of water; and as all the chaps are at home today we think we can get it out for him. We have three cart-ropes already, but they won’t reach to the bottom.”

text: “As the only lady on the heath, I think you might,” said Venn with quiet indirectness. “The matter stands thus. Mr. Wildeve would marry Thomasin at once, and set everything straight, were it not for another woman in the affair. This other woman is some person he has fallen in with and, I believe, meets occasionally on the heath. He will never marry her, and yet because of her he may never marry the woman who loves him truly. Now if you, miss, who have such influence over us menfolk, were to insist that he should deal honorably with your young neighbor Tamsin and forsake the other woman, he might perhaps do it, and spare her a great deal of misery.”
source_text: “As the only lady on the heath I think you might,” said Venn with subtle indirectness. “This is how the case stands. Mr. Wildeve would marry Thomasin at once, and make all matters smooth, if so be there were not another woman in the case. This other woman is some person he has picked up with, and meets on the heath occasionally, I believe. He will never marry her, and yet through her he may never marry the woman who loves him dearly. Now, if you, miss, who have so much sway over us menfolk, were to insist that he should treat your young neighbour Tamsin with honourable kindness and give up the other woman, he would perhaps do it, and save her a good deal of misery.”

text: “Then I saw him at Throope Corner, bringing one home,” said Venn dryly. “A beauty, with a white face and a mane as black as night. He’ll be here soon enough, no doubt.” He rose and, for a moment, looked at Thomasin’s pure, gentle face, over which a shadow of sadness had fallen since he had last seen her. Then he ventured to add, “Mr. Wildeve seems to be away often at this time.”
source_text: “Then I saw him at Throope Corner, leading one home,” said Venn drily. “A beauty, with a white face and a mane as black as night. He will soon be here, no doubt.” Rising and looking for a moment at the pure, sweet face of Thomasin, over which a shadow of sadness had passed since the time when he had last seen her, he ventured to add, “Mr. Wildeve seems to be often away at this time.”

text: Each morning he rose with the dawn, drew on his leggings, and set out to meet Humphrey at the appointed place. It was his habit to labor from four in the morning until midday; then, when the day’s heat stood at its fiercest, he would return home and sleep an hour or two; after which he came forth again and worked on until nightfall at nine.
source_text: Day after day he rose with the sun, buckled on his leggings, and went off to the rendezvous with Humphrey. His custom was to work from four o’clock in the morning till noon; then, when the heat of the day was at its highest, to go home and sleep for an hour or two; afterwards coming out again and working till dusk at nine.

text: The reddleman had been gone only a few minutes when another figure came near the half-rekindled bonfire. It was a widow well known and much respected throughout the neighbourhood, a woman whose position could only be described as genteel. Her face, set off by the darkness of the heath withdrawing around her, shone out in full whiteness, without any softened shadow, like a cameo.
source_text: The reddleman had not been gone more than a few minutes when another person approached the partially revived bonfire. It proved to be a well-known and respected widow of the neighbourhood, of a standing which can only be expressed by the word genteel. Her face, encompassed by the blackness of the receding heath, showed whitely, and without half-lights, like a cameo.

text: “‘I’ll be hanged if there isn’t Mistress Yeo standing up,’ I said,” the narrator repeated, uttering the forbidden phrase with the same cold gravity of countenance as before, which showed plainly enough that compulsion, and not any relish, had driven the repetition. “And the next sound I caught was, ‘I object to the banns,’ from her. ‘I’ll speak with you after the service,’ said the parson, in a most neighborly fashion—yes, he turned all at once into an ordinary man, no holier than yourself or me. Ah, but her face was white! If you can remember that monument in Mellstock church—the cross-legged trooper whose arm the village boys have chipped off? Well, he would have made a tolerable likeness to that woman’s look when she said, ‘I object to the banns.’”
source_text: “‘Be damned if there isn’t Mis’ess Yeobright a-standing up,’ I said,” the narrator repeated, giving out the bad word with the same passionless severity of face as before, which proved how entirely necessity and not gusto had to do with the iteration. “And the next thing I heard was, ‘I forbid the banns,’ from her. ‘I’ll speak to you after the service,’ said the parson, in quite a homely way—yes, turning all at once into a common man no holier than you or I. Ah, her face was pale! Maybe you can call to mind that monument in Weatherbury church—the cross-legged soldier that have had his arm knocked away by the schoolchildren? Well, he would about have matched that woman’s face, when she said, ‘I forbid the banns.’”

text: One evening, some three weeks after the burial of Mrs. Rendel, while the moon’s silver countenance cast a sheaf of beams straight across the floor of Clym’s cottage at Alderbrook, a woman came out from within. She leaned upon the garden gate, as though pausing there for a little ease. The faint glimmer that can invest even a withered crone with unexpected grace gave a kind of divinity to this face, which was already fair.
source_text: One evening, about three weeks after the funeral of Mrs. Yeobright, when the silver face of the moon sent a bundle of beams directly upon the floor of Clym’s house at Alderworth, a woman came forth from within. She reclined over the garden gate as if to refresh herself awhile. The pale lunar touches which make beauties of hags lent divinity to this face, already beautiful.

text: Eustacia’s wandering at first was as uncertain in its course as thistledown drifting on the wind. She knew not what to do. She wished it had been night instead of morning, so that she might at least have endured her misery without the risk of being seen. Mile after mile she went on between the fading ferns and the damp, white spiders’ webs, until at last she turned her steps toward her grandfather’s house. The front door was shut and locked. Instinctively she went round to the end where the stable stood, and, peering in at the stable door, she saw Charley inside.
source_text: Eustacia’s journey was at first as vague in direction as that of thistledown on the wind. She did not know what to do. She wished it had been night instead of morning, that she might at least have borne her misery without the possibility of being seen. Tracing mile after mile along between the dying ferns and the wet white spiders’ webs, she at length turned her steps towards her grandfather’s house. She found the front door closed and locked. Mechanically she went round to the end where the stable was, and on looking in at the stable door she saw Charley standing within.

text: “Phew—I won’t kill you,” he said with a sneer, as though a fresh resolve had come upon him all at once. “I did mean to; but I won’t. That would only turn you into a martyr, and send you where she is; and I’d keep you from her until the end of all things, if I had the power.”
source_text: “Phew—I shall not kill you,” he said contemptuously, as if under a sudden change of purpose. “I did think of it; but—I shall not. That would be making a martyr of you, and sending you to where she is; and I would keep you away from her till the universe come to an end, if I could.”

text: The fifth excursion was made in the afternoon; the weather was fair, and she stayed abroad a long while, walking as far as the uppermost reach of the valley in which Whiteacre lay. At a distance of half a mile she could discern the pale white fence; yet he did not come into view. She returned home with something very near to sickness of heart, and with a humiliating sense of her own weakness. She determined to seek the Parisian no more.
source_text: The fifth sally was in the afternoon; it was fine, and she remained out long, walking to the very top of the valley in which Blooms-End lay. She saw the white paling about half a mile off; but he did not appear. It was almost with heart-sickness that she came home and with a sense of shame at her weakness. She resolved to look for the man from Paris no more.

text: An aged man emerged from the farther glow of the fire, coming up from the direction of the farmhouse. It was he who had met the reddleman on the road that afternoon. He raised his eyes with a wistful air to the crest of the bank where the woman was standing, and the teeth revealed between his parted lips were sound and white as parian.
source_text: An old man came into the remoter light of the fire from the direction of the homestead. He was the same who had overtaken the reddleman on the road that afternoon. He looked wistfully to the top of the bank at the woman who stood there, and his teeth, which were quite unimpaired, showed like parian from his parted lips.

text: Eustacia had heard the passage spoken before, now and then. When the boy finished, she began, in exactly the same words, and went on without pause or departure from the text until she, too, came to the end. It was the same thing, and yet how unlike. In structure alike, it had the added grace and polish of a Raphael after Perugino, which, while keeping the original subject perfectly, utterly surpasses the earlier art.
source_text: Eustacia had occasionally heard the part recited before. When the lad ended she began, precisely in the same words, and ranted on without hitch or divergence till she too reached the end. It was the same thing, yet how different. Like in form, it had the added softness and finish of a Raffaelle after Perugino, which, while faithfully reproducing the original subject, entirely distances the original art.

text: The night was now fairly far spent; and Venn went farther into the heath until he reached a hollow where his van was standing—a place no more than two hundred yards from the scene of the gaming match. He stepped into this traveling home of his, lighted the lantern, and, before fastening the door for the night, paused to think over the events of the past few hours. As he stood there, the first gray of dawn began to show in the northeastern sky, which, the clouds having cleared away, was shining with a gentle radiance in this midsummer season, though it was only between one and two in the morning. Venn, utterly tired out, then closed his door and threw himself down to sleep.
source_text: The night was now somewhat advanced; and Venn plunged deeper into the heath, till he came to a ravine where his van was standing—a spot not more than two hundred yards from the site of the gambling bout. He entered this movable home of his, lit his lantern, and, before closing his door for the night, stood reflecting on the circumstances of the preceding hours. While he stood the dawn grew visible in the northeast quarter of the heavens, which, the clouds having cleared off, was bright with a soft sheen at this midsummer time, though it was only between one and two o’clock. Venn, thoroughly weary, then shut his door and flung himself down to sleep.

text: He would not permit himself to linger long over these conjectures, maxims, and hopes; and at twenty minutes before twelve he once more went quietly to the stable, harnessed the horse, and lighted the lamps. Then, taking the horse by the head, he led him, with the covered carriage, out of the yard to a place by the roadside, some quarter of a mile below the inn.
source_text: He would not allow himself to dwell long upon these conjectures, maxims, and hopes, and at twenty minutes to twelve he again went softly to the stable, harnessed the horse, and lit the lamps; whence, taking the horse by the head, he led him with the covered car out of the yard to a spot by the roadside some quarter of a mile below the inn.

text: Eustacia had always dreamed that, once she was married to Clym, she would gain the power to persuade him to go back to Paris. He had carefully given no promise that he would do so; but could he resist her pleading and her arguments? She had counted so firmly on succeeding that she had told her grandfather Paris, not Budmouth, was most likely to be their future home. All her hopes were tied up in that vision. In the quiet days after their marriage, while Yeobright bent over her lips, her eyes, and the lines of her face, she kept thinking and thinking about it, even as she met his gaze; and now the sight of the books, showing a future hostile to her dream, gave her a sharp, almost painful shock. She was looking forward to the time when, as the wife of some pleasant little household, however modest, near a Paris boulevard, she might spend her days on the edge, at least, of the gay world, catching stray breaths of the city pleasures she was so well suited to enjoy. Yet Yeobright remained as fixed in the opposite purpose as though marriage were meant to deepen the fancies of youthful philanthropy rather than sweep them away.
source_text: Now, Eustacia’s dream had always been that, once married to Clym, she would have the power of inducing him to return to Paris. He had carefully withheld all promise to do so; but would he be proof against her coaxing and argument? She had calculated to such a degree on the probability of success that she had represented Paris, and not Budmouth, to her grandfather as in all likelihood their future home. Her hopes were bound up in this dream. In the quiet days since their marriage, when Yeobright had been poring over her lips, her eyes, and the lines of her face, she had mused and mused on the subject, even while in the act of returning his gaze; and now the sight of the books, indicating a future which was antagonistic to her dream, struck her with a positively painful jar. She was hoping for the time when, as the mistress of some pretty establishment, however small, near a Parisian Boulevard, she would be passing her days on the skirts at least of the gay world, and catching stray wafts from those town pleasures she was so well fitted to enjoy. Yet Yeobright was as firm in the contrary intention as if the tendency of marriage were rather to develop the fantasies of young philanthropy than to sweep them away.

text: Moreover, to light a fire is man’s instinctive, defiant response when, at winter’s threshold, curfew sounds across all Nature. It is a spontaneous, Promethean act of rebellion against the decree that this returning season must bring foul weather, cold darkness, misery, and death. Black chaos descends, and the earth’s chained gods declare, Let there be light.
source_text: Moreover to light a fire is the instinctive and resistant act of man when, at the winter ingress, the curfew is sounded throughout Nature. It indicates a spontaneous, Promethean rebelliousness against that fiat that this recurrent season shall bring foul times, cold darkness, misery and death. Black chaos comes, and the fettered gods of the earth say, Let there be light.

She, together with the others not yet upon the stage, had thus far lingered in the moonlight that poured beneath the porch. With no visible hesitation or constraint she entered, and began—
source_text: and so on. This speech concluded with a challenge to the infidel, at the end of which it was Eustacia’s duty to enter as the Turkish Knight. She, with the rest who were not yet on, had hitherto remained in the moonlight which streamed under the porch. With no apparent effort or backwardness she came in, beginning—

text: “You’ll be in good time to meet the reddleman I spoke of,” said Hatherley. “He has only turned back for his van. We had word that your niece and her husband meant to come home at once after the wedding, and we shall be going down there presently to give them a welcoming song.”
source_text: “Why, you’ll be safe to meet the reddleman that I told ye of,” said Fairway. “He’s only gone back to get his van. We heard that your niece and her husband were coming straight home as soon as they were married, and we are going down there shortly, to give ’em a song o’ welcome.”

text: He sat in his van and thought it over. From Thomasin’s words and manner he had clearly gathered that Wildeve was neglecting her. And if not for Eustacia, for whom else could he be neglecting her? Yet it was hardly believable that matters had gone so far as to suggest that Eustacia was deliberately encouraging him. Venn resolved to make a careful reconnaissance of the lonely road that ran along the valley from Wildeve’s house to Clym’s at Alderworth.
source_text: He sat in his van and considered. From Thomasin’s words and manner he had plainly gathered that Wildeve neglected her. For whom could he neglect her if not for Eustacia? Yet it was scarcely credible that things had come to such a head as to indicate that Eustacia systematically encouraged him. Venn resolved to reconnoitre somewhat carefully the lonely road which led along the vale from Wildeve’s dwelling to Clym’s house at Alderworth.

text: The dim figure that Venn had seen separate from Wildeve in the porch and hasten back into the house was Thomasin. As she came in, she cast off the cloak that had been loosely thrown about her and stepped into the light, where Mrs. Yeobright was seated at her worktable, tucked within the settle so that part of it jutted into the chimney-corner.
source_text: The shadowy form seen by Venn to part from Wildeve in the porch, and quickly withdraw into the house, was Thomasin’s. On entering she threw down a cloak which had been carelessly wrapped round her, and came forward to the light, where Mrs. Yeobright sat at her work-table, drawn up within the settle, so that part of it projected into the chimney-corner.

text: The figure was so much a natural part of the whole motionless structure that to see it stir would have struck the mind as a curious phenomenon. Since stillness was the defining quality of the entire mass of which the person was a part, any break in that stillness seemed to imply disorder.
source_text: The form was so much like an organic part of the entire motionless structure that to see it move would have impressed the mind as a strange phenomenon. Immobility being the chief characteristic of that whole which the person formed portion of, the discontinuance of immobility in any quarter suggested confusion.

text: “True enough, and it would,” said the reveller, pulling himself up with a repentant air. “I’ve such a poor memory, Mrs. Yeobright, that I forget how much the others look up to me. You’d say my spirits must be splendid? But they aren’t always. It weighs on a man to be regarded as a leader, and I often feel the burden of it.”
source_text: “Faith, and so it would,” said the reveller checking himself repentantly. “I’ve such a bad memory, Mis’ess Yeobright, that I forget how I’m looked up to by the rest of ’em. My spirits must be wonderful good, you’ll say? But not always. ’Tis a weight upon a man to be looked up to as commander, and I often feel it.”

text: Yeobright went on through the stillness with a hope that all would soon be well. Three miles farther he reached a place where a gentle perfume drifted across his path, and he paused for a moment to breathe in the familiar scent. It was the very spot where, four hours before, his mother had sunk down in weariness upon the knoll thick with shepherd’s-thyme. As he stood there, a sound half like breathing and half like a moan suddenly came to his ears.
source_text: Yeobright walked on amid this quiet scene with a hope that all would soon be well. Three miles on he came to a spot where a soft perfume was wafted across his path, and he stood still for a moment to inhale the familiar scent. It was the place at which, four hours earlier, his mother had sat down exhausted on the knoll covered with shepherd’s-thyme. While he stood a sound between a breathing and a moan suddenly reached his ears.

text: Because something was evidently expected of him, he had not been home many days before a strong curiosity began to spread through the heath about why he was staying so long. The normal length of a holiday had already gone by, yet he still remained. On the Sunday morning after the week of Thomasin’s wedding, the matter was being discussed at a haircutting in front of Fairway’s house. At that hour and on that day, the local barbering was always carried out here, to be followed at noon by the great Sunday washing of the inhabitants, and then an hour later by the great Sunday dressing. On Egdon Heath, Sunday in the full sense did not begin until dinner-time, and even then it was only a rather battered version of the day.
source_text: Something being expected of him, he had not been at home many days before a great curiosity as to why he stayed on so long began to arise in the heath. The natural term of a holiday had passed, yet he still remained. On the Sunday morning following the week of Thomasin’s marriage a discussion on this subject was in progress at a hair-cutting before Fairway’s house. Here the local barbering was always done at this hour on this day, to be followed by the great Sunday wash of the inhabitants at noon, which in its turn was followed by the great Sunday dressing an hour later. On Egdon Heath Sunday proper did not begin till dinner-time, and even then it was a somewhat battered specimen of the day.

text: The woman whom Thomasin had roused from sleep had hurriedly dressed and kindled a fire, while the other servant was left to continue snoring peacefully at the back of the house. The unconscious forms of Eustacia, Clym, and Wildeve were then carried in and laid upon the carpet with their feet toward the fire, and every restorative measure that could be thought of was immediately set in motion, the stableman being sent at once for a doctor. Yet there appeared to be not the faintest spark of life in any of the three. Then Thomasin, whose numb sorrow had for a moment been driven aside by her frantic exertions, applied a bottle of hartshorn to Clym’s nostrils, after trying it in vain on the other two. He sighed.
source_text: The woman who had been shaken out of her sleep by Thomasin had hastily dressed herself and lighted a fire, the other servant being left to snore on in peace at the back of the house. The insensible forms of Eustacia, Clym, and Wildeve were then brought in and laid on the carpet, with their feet to the fire, when such restorative processes as could be thought of were adopted at once, the stableman being in the meantime sent for a doctor. But there seemed to be not a whiff of life in either of the bodies. Then Thomasin, whose stupor of grief had been thrust off awhile by frantic action, applied a bottle of hartshorn to Clym’s nostrils, having tried it in vain upon the other two. He sighed.

text: When they entered the inn’s large common room, they found some ten men already gathered there from the neighboring district, and the arrival of the new party brought the company to twice that number. Most of them were seated around the room in chairs separated by wooden arms, like the rough stalls of a cathedral, their backs and sides cut with the initials of many a famous drunkard of old who had spent his days and nights between them and now lay, an alcoholic cinder, in the nearest churchyard. Among the cups on the long table before them lay an open parcel of light drapery—the “gown-piece,” as it was called—which was to be raffled. Wildeve stood with his back to the fireplace, smoking a cigar; and the man promoting the raffle, a pedlar from a distant town, was enlarging on the value of the material as the making of a summer dress.
source_text: When they entered the large common room of the inn they found assembled there about ten men from among the neighbouring population, and the group was increased by the new contingent to double that number. Most of them were sitting round the room in seats divided by wooden elbows like those of crude cathedral stalls, which were carved with the initials of many an illustrious drunkard of former times who had passed his days and his nights between them, and now lay as an alcoholic cinder in the nearest churchyard. Among the cups on the long table before the sitters lay an open parcel of light drapery—the gown-piece, as it was called—which was to be raffled for. Wildeve was standing with his back to the fireplace smoking a cigar; and the promoter of the raffle, a packman from a distant town, was expatiating upon the value of the fabric as material for a summer dress.

text: The little slave went on tending the fire as before. He seemed little more than a machine, set in motion and given speech by the capricious will of Eustacia. He might have been the brass statue which Albertus Magnus is said to have animated only enough to make it talk, move, and serve him.
source_text: The little slave went on feeding the fire as before. He seemed a mere automaton, galvanized into moving and speaking by the wayward Eustacia’s will. He might have been the brass statue which Albertus Magnus is said to have animated just so far as to make it chatter, and move, and be his servant.

text: Her sovereign heroes were William the Norman Conqueror, Strafford, and Napoleon Bonaparte, as they stood forth in the Lady’s History from which she had been taught at the institution where her education had been carried on. Had she ever become a mother, she would have bestowed on her sons such names as Saul or Sisera rather than Jacob or David, neither of whom won her regard. While at school, she had often taken the part of the Philistines in divers battles, and had speculated whether Pontius Pilate were indeed as handsome as he was open and candid.
source_text: Her high gods were William the Conqueror, Strafford, and Napoleon Buonaparte, as they had appeared in the Lady’s History used at the establishment in which she was educated. Had she been a mother she would have christened her boys such names as Saul or Sisera in preference to Jacob or David, neither of whom she admired. At school she had used to side with the Philistines in several battles, and had wondered if Pontius Pilate were as handsome as he was frank and fair.

text: Of all the conjectures that crossed his mind as to the stranger’s identity, not once did it enter Everard Wyld’s thoughts that the sufferer might be one of his own blood. At such seasons it was no unheard-of thing for gorse-cutters to lie out beneath the sky, sparing themselves the long tramp home and the weary return at daybreak; yet when Everard caught the low moan he bent nearer, and then perceived that the prostrate figure was that of a woman; and a chill of dread passed through him like the breath of a cavern. Still he was not wholly assured that the woman was his mother until he stooped lower and saw her face, white and deathlike, with the eyes shut.
source_text: Among the different possibilities as to the person’s individuality there did not for a moment occur to Yeobright that it might be one of his own family. Sometimes furze-cutters had been known to sleep out of doors at these times, to save a long journey homeward and back again; but Clym remembered the moan and looked closer, and saw that the form was feminine; and a distress came over him like cold air from a cave. But he was not absolutely certain that the woman was his mother till he stooped and beheld her face, pallid, and with closed eyes.

text: Thomasin emerged from the tree, shaking from her hair and dress the loose berries that had lodged there, and went down the hill with her aunt, each woman carrying half the gathered branches. It was now nearly four o’clock, and the sunlight was withdrawing from the valleys. As the west turned red, the two relations came once more out from the house and plunged into the heath in a different direction from before, toward a point on the distant highway by which the expected man was to return.
source_text: Thomasin came out of the tree, shook from her hair and dress the loose berries which had fallen thereon, and went down the hill with her aunt, each woman bearing half the gathered boughs. It was now nearly four o’clock, and the sunlight was leaving the vales. When the west grew red the two relatives came again from the house and plunged into the heath in a different direction from the first, towards a point in the distant highway along which the expected man was to return.

text: At last Christian put down his shilling, the raffle commenced, and the dice began to go round. When Christian’s turn arrived, he took up the box with a trembling hand, shook it in terror, and cast a pair-royal. Three of the others had thrown only common low pairs, and all the rest had made nothing but points.
source_text: Ultimately Christian laid down his shilling, the raffle began, and the dice went round. When it came to Christian’s turn he took the box with a trembling hand, shook it fearfully, and threw a pair-royal. Three of the others had thrown common low pairs, and all the rest mere points.

text: Ten minutes slipped by. Then a great death’s-head moth emerged from the dim outer air, circled the lantern twice, darted straight upon the candle, and quenched it with the impact of its blow. Wildeve had just cast his lot, but had not yet lifted the box to discover what he had thrown; and now it was impossible.
source_text: Ten minutes passed away. Then a large death’s head moth advanced from the obscure outer air, wheeled twice round the lantern, flew straight at the candle, and extinguished it by the force of the blow. Wildeve had just thrown, but had not lifted the box to see what he had cast; and now it was impossible.

text: “You are exactly like all women. You are always willing to build your life on whatever chance position happens to present itself, while men would rather shape the whole world to fit them. Listen to me, Eustacia. There is something I have resolved not to delay any longer. Your view of the wisdom of _carpe diem_ does not move me today. Our present way of living must soon be brought to an end.”
source_text: “You are just like all women. They are ever content to build their lives on any incidental position that offers itself; whilst men would fain make a globe to suit them. Listen to this, Eustacia. There is a subject I have determined to put off no longer. Your sentiment on the wisdom of _Carpe diem_ does not impress me today. Our present mode of life must shortly be brought to an end.”

text: She could hardly bring herself to accept this strange reality. She had been told that Clym was accustomed to cutting furze, but she had imagined that he did the work only now and then, as a kind of useful diversion; yet here she saw him as nothing but a furze-cutter, dressed in the customary clothes of the trade and, from his movements, seemingly thinking the customary thoughts. As she hurried along, her heart beating hard and forming a dozen rapid plans for rescuing both him and Eustacia from such a life, she watched him go in through his own door.
source_text: She was scarcely able to familiarize herself with this strange reality. She had been told that Clym was in the habit of cutting furze, but she had supposed that he occupied himself with the labour only at odd times, by way of useful pastime; yet she now beheld him as a furze-cutter and nothing more—wearing the regulation dress of the craft, and thinking the regulation thoughts, to judge by his motions. Planning a dozen hasty schemes for at once preserving him and Eustacia from this mode of life, she throbbingly followed the way, and saw him enter his own door.

text: Meanwhile Diggory Venn had gone back to his van, where he stood gazing thoughtfully into the stove. A new prospect had opened before him. But however favorable Mrs. Yeobright’s opinion of him might be as a suitor for her niece’s hand, one condition was absolutely necessary if he was to win Thomasin herself, and that was the giving up of his present wild way of life. He saw little difficulty in that.
source_text: Meanwhile Diggory Venn had returned to his van, where he stood looking thoughtfully into the stove. A new vista was opened up to him. But, however promising Mrs. Yeobright’s views of him might be as a candidate for her niece’s hand, one condition was indispensable to the favour of Thomasin herself, and that was a renunciation of his present wild mode of life. In this he saw little difficulty.

text: Indeed, at that very turning of the night’s descent into darkness, the chief and singular majesty of the Moorland waste first declared itself, and no one could properly be said to know the heath who had not stood there at such an hour. It was most deeply apprehended when it could least distinctly be seen, for its full meaning and effect lay in that moment and in the hours that followed before the next dawn; then, and only then, did it speak its true language. The place was, in truth, akin to night by blood and temper, and when night made its appearance, an evident impulse to draw together could be discerned in its shadows and in the landscape. The melancholy expanse of rises and dips seemed to lift itself and lean toward the evening dusk in utter sympathy, the heath breathing out blackness as swiftly as the sky poured it down. Thus the darkness in the air and the darkness in the earth came together in a deepening alliance, each advancing halfway to meet the other.
source_text: In fact, precisely at this transitional point of its nightly roll into darkness the great and particular glory of the Egdon waste began, and nobody could be said to understand the heath who had not been there at such a time. It could best be felt when it could not clearly be seen, its complete effect and explanation lying in this and the succeeding hours before the next dawn; then, and only then, did it tell its true tale. The spot was, indeed, a near relation of night, and when night showed itself an apparent tendency to gravitate together could be perceived in its shades and the scene. The sombre stretch of rounds and hollows seemed to rise and meet the evening gloom in pure sympathy, the heath exhaling darkness as rapidly as the heavens precipitated it. And so the obscurity in the air and the obscurity in the land closed together in a black fraternization towards which each advanced halfway.

text: “I must speak, Miss Vye, though I know it will pain you. What I have to urge is this. However it has come about—whether it is her fault or yours—her position is certainly the worse. Your relinquishing Mr. Wildeve would really be a gain to you, for how could you marry him? But she cannot escape so lightly; if she loses him, everyone will lay the blame on her. So I ask you—not because her claim is the stronger, but because her plight is the harder—to yield him to her.”
source_text: “I must speak, Miss Vye, in spite of paining you. What I would put before you is this. However it may come about—whether she is to blame, or you—her case is without doubt worse than yours. Your giving up Mr. Wildeve will be a real advantage to you, for how could you marry him? Now she cannot get off so easily—everybody will blame her if she loses him. Then I ask you—not because her right is best, but because her situation is worst—to give him up to her.”

text: In moving from a rural existence to an intellectual one, there are usually at least two intermediate stages, and often many more; and one of these is almost certain to be marked by worldly advancement. It is difficult to picture bucolic calm awakening to intellectual ambitions without imagining social ambition as the bridge between them. Yeobright’s local singularity was that, while he labored after high thinking, he still clung to plain living—indeed, in many respects to a rough and meager life—and to a brotherly fellowship with rustics.
source_text: In passing from the bucolic to the intellectual life the intermediate stages are usually two at least, frequently many more; and one of those stages is almost sure to be worldly advanced. We can hardly imagine bucolic placidity quickening to intellectual aims without imagining social aims as the transitional phase. Yeobright’s local peculiarity was that in striving at high thinking he still cleaved to plain living—nay, wild and meagre living in many respects, and brotherliness with clowns.

text: The trees under which she rested were singularly rough, battered, and savage, and for a few moments Mrs. Ashdown forgot her own storm-worn weariness to regard theirs. Not a branch in the little company of nine but had been splintered, lopped, or twisted awry by the violence of the weather, which, whenever it rose, held the spot in its cruel grasp. Some were scorched and riven as though by lightning, with dark stains running down their trunks like fire-marks, while the earth below was littered with dead pine-needles and drifts of cones flung down by the gales of former years. The place was known as the Devil’s Bellowing, and one had only to visit it on a March or November night to understand the strength of the name. Yet on the present sultry afternoon, when no breeze was perceptible, the trees maintained a continual lament that could scarcely be thought the mere voice of the air.
source_text: The trees beneath which she sat were singularly battered, rude, and wild, and for a few minutes Mrs. Yeobright dismissed thoughts of her own storm-broken and exhausted state to contemplate theirs. Not a bough in the nine trees which composed the group but was splintered, lopped, and distorted by the fierce weather that there held them at its mercy whenever it prevailed. Some were blasted and split as if by lightning, black stains as from fire marking their sides, while the ground at their feet was strewn with dead fir-needles and heaps of cones blown down in the gales of past years. The place was called the Devil’s Bellows, and it was only necessary to come there on a March or November night to discover the forcible reasons for that name. On the present heated afternoon, when no perceptible wind was blowing, the trees kept up a perpetual moan which one could hardly believe to be caused by the air.

text: Still, he resolved to ask her and leave the choice to her own judgment. Indeed, it was with a kind of pleasant feeling that he was doing his duty that one evening he went down to her for that purpose, when the sun was casting across the valley the same long shadow from the house roof that he had seen there countless times while his mother was alive.
source_text: Yet he resolved to ask her, and let her decide for herself. It was even with a pleasant sense of doing his duty that he went downstairs to her one evening for this purpose, when the sun was printing on the valley the same long shadow of the housetop that he had seen lying there times out of number while his mother lived.

text: Venn was reluctant to go, for everything on earth that stirred his interest was under that roof. Since no one in the house would sleep again that night, save the two who slept forever, there was no reason he should not stay. So he withdrew to the niche in the fireplace where he had once sat, and remained there, watching the steam rise from the double row of banknotes as they swayed to and fro in the chimney draft, until their limp softness had given way entirely to a dry, crisp stiffness. Then the woman came to unpin them, and, gathering them together, carried the little stack upstairs. Soon after, the doctor came down from above with the air of a man who had done all he could, and, drawing on his gloves, left the house, the quick sound of his horse’s trotting soon fading along the road.
source_text: Venn was loth to depart, for all on earth that interested him lay under this roof. As nobody in the house had any more sleep that night, except the two who slept for ever, there was no reason why he should not remain. So he retired into the niche of the fireplace where he had used to sit, and there he continued, watching the steam from the double row of banknotes as they waved backwards and forwards in the draught of the chimney till their flaccidity was changed to dry crispness throughout. Then the woman came and unpinned them, and, folding them together, carried the handful upstairs. Presently the doctor appeared from above with the look of a man who could do no more, and, pulling on his gloves, went out of the house, the trotting of his horse soon dying away upon the road.

text: Gambling, after all, is a form of amusement that is much easier to begin when one’s pockets are full than to stop while they remain so; and though Wildeve, in a calmer mood, might have sensibly turned down this invitation, the excitement of his recent success swept him entirely off his feet. He laid one of the guineas on a slab beside the reddleman’s sovereign. “Mine is a guinea,” he said.
source_text: Now, gambling is a species of amusement which is much more easily begun with full pockets than left off with the same; and though Wildeve in a cooler temper might have prudently declined this invitation, the excitement of his recent success carried him completely away. He placed one of the guineas on a slab beside the reddleman’s sovereign. “Mine is a guinea,” he said.

text: At that instant, did some hint reach Clym of the sex of the being concealed within that whimsical disguise, of the breadth of her powers both in suffering and in making others suffer, and of how widely her orbit surpassed that of the companions around her? When the veiled Queen of Love stood before Aeneas, a strange perfume went with her and revealed her nature. If ever such an unseen breath was cast by a woman’s emotion upon the person it sought, it must have announced Elfrida’s presence to Clym now. He gazed at her with a wistful air, then seemed to sink into musing, as though he were losing sight of what lay before him. The brief encounter was over; he moved on, and Elfrida drank her wine without tasting it. The man upon whom she had already resolved to bestow her affection went into the little chamber and crossed it to the far end.
source_text: Did anything at this moment suggest to Yeobright the sex of the creature whom that fantastic guise inclosed, how extended was her scope both in feeling and in making others feel, and how far her compass transcended that of her companions in the band? When the disguised Queen of Love appeared before Æneas a preternatural perfume accompanied her presence and betrayed her quality. If such a mysterious emanation ever was projected by the emotions of an earthly woman upon their object, it must have signified Eustacia’s presence to Yeobright now. He looked at her wistfully, then seemed to fall into a reverie, as if he were forgetting what he observed. The momentary situation ended, he passed on, and Eustacia sipped her wine without knowing what she drank. The man for whom she had pre-determined to nourish a passion went into the small room, and across it to the further extremity.

text: March came in, and the heath began to give the first faint signs of rousing itself from winter’s sleep. The stirring was almost feline in its furtive quiet. The pool beside the bank near Celia’s cottage, which to any one moving and making a disturbance seemed as lifeless and forlorn as ever, would, if watched in silence for a little while, slowly reveal a scene of lively activity. A shy little kingdom of creatures had awakened with the season. Tiny tadpoles and newts rose in bubbles through the water and darted beneath it; toads uttered sounds like those of very young ducks and came forward to the edge in groups of two and three; above, humblebees drifted to and fro in the deepening light, their hum appearing and vanishing like the note of a gong.
source_text: The month of March arrived, and the heath showed its first signs of awakening from winter trance. The awakening was almost feline in its stealthiness. The pool outside the bank by Eustacia’s dwelling, which seemed as dead and desolate as ever to an observer who moved and made noises in his observation, would gradually disclose a state of great animation when silently watched awhile. A timid animal world had come to life for the season. Little tadpoles and efts began to bubble up through the water, and to race along beneath it; toads made noises like very young ducks, and advanced to the margin in twos and threes; overhead, bumblebees flew hither and thither in the thickening light, their drone coming and going like the sound of a gong.

text: “Or perhaps try Thomasin again. Damon, how strange it is that you could have married either her or me without preference, and that you came to me only because I am—cheapest! Yes, yes, it is true. There was a time when I would have cried out against a man like that, and been quite beside myself; but all that is over now.”
source_text: “Or try Thomasin again. Damon, how strange it seems that you could have married her or me indifferently, and only have come to me because I am—cheapest! Yes, yes—it is true. There was a time when I should have exclaimed against a man of that sort, and been quite wild; but it is all past now.”

text: They sat down again and resumed the game with single-guinea stakes, and the play went briskly forward. But Fortune had plainly taken a fancy to the reddleman that night. He won on and on, until fourteen more gold pieces had passed into his ownership. Of the hundred guineas, seventy-nine were now his, while Wildeve had but twenty-one left. The appearance of the two men had become curious. Apart from their movements, a whole little panorama of the game’s changing fortunes seemed to be reflected in their eyes. A tiny candle-flame shone in each pupil, and one might have distinguished there, even in the reddleman, the turns of hope and surrender, though his face gave no sign whatever. Wildeve went on playing with the recklessness of despair.
source_text: Down they sat again, and recommenced with single guinea stakes; and the play went on smartly. But Fortune had unmistakably fallen in love with the reddleman tonight. He won steadily, till he was the owner of fourteen more of the gold pieces. Seventy-nine of the hundred guineas were his, Wildeve possessing only twenty-one. The aspect of the two opponents was now singular. Apart from motions, a complete diorama of the fluctuations of the game went on in their eyes. A diminutive candle-flame was mirrored in each pupil, and it would have been possible to distinguish therein between the moods of hope and the moods of abandonment, even as regards the reddleman, though his facial muscles betrayed nothing at all. Wildeve played on with the recklessness of despair.

text: So, after a week or two had gone by, Mrs. Yeobright began to wonder why she had still heard nothing from her son acknowledging the gift; and to deepen her uneasiness, there arose the thought that resentment might be the reason for his silence. She could scarcely credit it, yet why did he not write? She questioned Christian, and the bewilderment in his replies would at once have made her suspect that something was amiss, had not Thomasin’s note confirmed part of what he said.
source_text: Therefore, when a week or two had passed away, Mrs. Yeobright began to wonder why she never heard from her son of the receipt of the present; and to add gloom to her perplexity came the possibility that resentment might be the cause of his silence. She could hardly believe as much, but why did he not write? She questioned Christian, and the confusion in his answers would at once have led her to believe that something was wrong, had not one-half of his story been corroborated by Thomasin’s note.

text: “No, I’ve got used to it. I should be as white as you if I were to leave off the trade—that is, I should turn white in time—perhaps in six months; not at first, because it has grown into my skin and won’t wash out. Now you’ll never be afraid of a reddleman again, will you?”
source_text: “No, I took to it. I should be as white as you if I were to give up the trade—that is, I should be white in time—perhaps six months; not at first, because ’tis grow’d into my skin and won’t wash out. Now, you’ll never be afraid of a reddleman again, will ye?”

text: They moved on to the turf-shed, and as they drew near, the firelight and the lantern within made plain the shape of a woman lying on a couch of fern, with a circle of heath men and women standing about her. Eustacia did not know that the reclining figure was Mrs. Yeobright, nor did she see Clym among the bystanders until she had come close up. Then she quickly laid her hand upon Wildeve’s arm and motioned him to withdraw from the open side of the shed into the darkness.
source_text: They advanced to the turf-shed, and when they got near it the firelight and the lantern inside showed distinctly enough the form of a woman reclining on a bed of fern, a group of heath men and women standing around her. Eustacia did not recognize Mrs. Yeobright in the reclining figure, nor Clym as one of the standers-by till she came close. Then she quickly pressed her hand up on Wildeve’s arm and signified to him to come back from the open side of the shed into the shadow.

text: Inside, the sight was not exactly the usual one. Gathered about the room was the small group of men who made up the main body of the Egdon company: Fairway himself, Grandfer Cantle, Humphrey, Christian, and one or two of the turf-cutters. It was a warm day, and as a rule they were all in their shirt-sleeves, except Christian, who had always been strangely anxious not to part with so much as a shred of clothing when he was in anyone’s house but his own. Spread across the solid oak table in the center of the room was a bundle of striped linen, held down on one side by Grandfer Cantle and on the other by Humphrey, while Fairway worked over its surface with a yellow block, his face moist and wrinkled with the strain of the task.
source_text: The scene within was not quite the customary one. Standing about the room was the little knot of men who formed the chief part of the Egdon coterie, there being present Fairway himself, Grandfer Cantle, Humphrey, Christian, and one or two turf-cutters. It was a warm day, and the men were as a matter of course in their shirtsleeves, except Christian, who had always a nervous fear of parting with a scrap of his clothing when in anybody’s house but his own. Across the stout oak table in the middle of the room was thrown a mass of striped linen, which Grandfer Cantle held down on one side, and Humphrey on the other, while Fairway rubbed its surface with a yellow lump, his face being damp and creased with the effort of the labour.

text: Though these shaggy hills seemed so lonely, on a winter morning like this several sharp, bright eyes were always ready to turn toward any passer-by. Birds of all sorts lived here in concealment that would have caused surprise anywhere else. A bustard frequented the place, and not many years before, twenty-five of them might have been seen together on Egdon. Marsh harriers rose from the valley near Wildeve’s. A cream-colored courser used to visit this hill, a bird so rare that no more than a dozen have ever been seen in England; but a barbarian would not rest, by day or night, until he had shot the wandering African visitor, and after that, cream-colored coursers seemed to decide that Egdon was no longer worth entering.
source_text: Though these shaggy hills were apparently so solitary, several keen round eyes were always ready on such a wintry morning as this to converge upon a passer-by. Feathered species sojourned here in hiding which would have created wonder if found elsewhere. A bustard haunted the spot, and not many years before this five and twenty might have been seen in Egdon at one time. Marsh-harriers looked up from the valley by Wildeve’s. A cream-coloured courser had used to visit this hill, a bird so rare that not more than a dozen have ever been seen in England; but a barbarian rested neither night nor day till he had shot the African truant, and after that event cream-coloured coursers thought fit to enter Egdon no more.

text: He was a John the Baptist who preached not repentance but elevation of soul. In mind he belonged to a provincial future, which is to say that in many matters he had already come abreast of the leading thinkers in the capital of his day. Much of this growth he may have owed to the years of study he had spent in Paris, where he had made himself familiar with the moral philosophies then in vogue.
