source_text: He was a John the Baptist who took ennoblement rather than repentance for his text. Mentally he was in a provincial future, that is, he was in many points abreast with the central town thinkers of his date. Much of this development he may have owed to his studious life in Paris, where he had become acquainted with ethical systems popular at the time.

text: He accompanied the lad into the heath on his way home, and, returning alone to the house a quarter of an hour later, found Venn and Thomasin ready to depart, all the guests having gone in his absence. The married pair took their places in the four-wheeled dogcart which Venn’s head milker and handyman had driven over from Stickleford to bring them in; little Eustacia and the nurse were safely bestowed upon the open back seat behind; and the milker, mounted on an ancient overstepping pony whose shoes rang like cymbals at every step, followed in the rear, after the fashion of a body-servant from the last century.
source_text: He accompanied the lad into the heath on his way home, and, returning alone to the house a quarter of an hour later, found Venn and Thomasin ready to start, all the guests having departed in his absence. The wedded pair took their seats in the four-wheeled dogcart which Venn’s head milker and handy man had driven from Stickleford to fetch them in; little Eustacia and the nurse were packed securely upon the open flap behind; and the milker, on an ancient overstepping pony, whose shoes clashed like cymbals at every tread, rode in the rear, in the manner of a body-servant of the last century.

text: He had often before gone up there without telling his mother why; but never yet had he seemed so open about his errand while in truth he was hiding it. Three months earlier, he could scarcely have believed himself capable of such a moral posture. When he came back to work in that remote place, he had hoped to find release from the abrasion of social demands; and yet here, too, they had followed him. More than ever he yearned for some realm in which private ambition was not the sole accepted measure of advancement—perhaps such a world as might once have been reflected in the argent sphere shining down upon him then. His gaze roved across the breadth and length of that far-off country: the Bay of Rainbows, the dark Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the immense Walled Plains, and the marvelous Ring Mountains—until he almost seemed to be travelling there in body, standing upon its hollow hills, crossing its deserts, descending its valleys and ancient sea floors, or climbing to the lips of its craters.
source_text: He had often come up here without stating his purpose to his mother; but this was the first time that he had been ostensibly frank as to his purpose while really concealing it. It was a moral situation which, three months earlier, he could hardly have credited of himself. In returning to labour in this sequestered spot he had anticipated an escape from the chafing of social necessities; yet behold they were here also. More than ever he longed to be in some world where personal ambition was not the only recognized form of progress—such, perhaps, as might have been the case at some time or other in the silvery globe then shining upon him. His eye travelled over the length and breadth of that distant country—over the Bay of Rainbows, the sombre Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the vast Walled Plains, and the wondrous Ring Mountains—till he almost felt himself to be voyaging bodily through its wild scenes, standing on its hollow hills, traversing its deserts, descending its vales and old sea bottoms, or mounting to the edges of its craters.

text: “And you’ll find that no small business, my lad,” said Old Jarrow briskly. “Would God the fear of failing powers were not so strong upon me! I’d be off at sunrise tomorrow to look upon the world anew. But seventy-one, though it counts for little in one’s own fireside, is a large reckoning for a wanderer.... Ay, seventy-one, come last Candlemas. Faith, I’d rather have it told in guineas than in years!” And the old fellow gave a sigh.
source_text: “And you’ve your work cut out to do that, my son,” said Grandfer Cantle smartly. “I wish that the dread of infirmities was not so strong in me!—I’d start the very first thing tomorrow to see the world over again! But seventy-one, though nothing at home, is a high figure for a rover.... Ay, seventy-one, last Candlemasday. Gad, I’d sooner have it in guineas than in years!” And the old man sighed.

text: Eustacia, kindled by an inward heat, could not wait for her companions after that. She drew the ribbons back from her face, opened the gate, and immediately set out across the heath. She did not hurry. Her grandfather was abed at that hour, and since she so often wandered over the hills on moonlit nights, he paid no heed to her comings and goings, and, content in his own fashion, left her to be content in hers. But a matter of greater moment than getting indoors now occupied her thoughts. Yeobright, if he possessed the least curiosity, would certainly discover her name. And then? At first she felt some triumph in the way the adventure had ended, though now and again, between those pulses of exultation, she was conscious of embarrassment and a burning blush. Yet another thought returned to chill her: of what use had been her daring? She was still, in truth, a complete stranger to the Yeobright family. The unwarranted glamour of romance with which she had invested that man might yet become her undoing. How had she let herself grow so enamoured of a stranger? And to deepen her distress, there was Thomasin, living day after day in dangerous nearness to him; for she had just learned that, contrary to her first supposition, he meant to remain at home for a long while.
source_text: Eustacia, warmed with an inner fire, could not wait for her companions after this. She flung back the ribbons from her face, opened the gate, and at once struck into the heath. She did not hasten along. Her grandfather was in bed at this hour, for she so frequently walked upon the hills on moonlight nights that he took no notice of her comings and goings, and, enjoying himself in his own way, left her to do likewise. A more important subject than that of getting indoors now engrossed her. Yeobright, if he had the least curiosity, would infallibly discover her name. What then? She first felt a sort of exultation at the way in which the adventure had terminated, even though at moments between her exultations she was abashed and blushful. Then this consideration recurred to chill her: What was the use of her exploit? She was at present a total stranger to the Yeobright family. The unreasonable nimbus of romance with which she had encircled that man might be her misery. How could she allow herself to become so infatuated with a stranger? And to fill the cup of her sorrow there would be Thomasin, living day after day in inflammable proximity to him; for she had just learnt that, contrary to her first belief, he was going to stay at home some considerable time.

text: The minutes went by, and the constable did not appear. Wildeve cooled from his first burst of anger into a troubled dissatisfaction with himself, with the situation, with the constable’s wife, and with all the conditions that had brought him there. He got up and left the house. As a whole, the events of that evening had produced a sobering, if not a chilling, effect on his misdirected affection, and Wildeve was in no mood to wander again after dark toward Alderworth in hopes of catching some chance glimpse of Eustacia.
source_text: The minutes ticked on, and the constable did not arrive. Wildeve cooled down from his state of high indignation to a restless dissatisfaction with himself, the scene, the constable’s wife, and the whole set of circumstances. He arose and left the house. Altogether, the experience of that evening had had a cooling, not to say a chilling, effect on misdirected tenderness, and Wildeve was in no mood to ramble again to Alderworth after nightfall in hope of a stray glance from Eustacia.

text: “Ah, you may be thinking, ‘That frail girl—how is she ever going to get a man to marry her when she decides to?’ But let me tell you this, Aunt: Mr. Wildeve is no profligate, any more than I am an improper woman. He has an unlucky way about him, and he does not go out of his way to win people over if they are not disposed to like him of their own free will.”
source_text: “Ah, you think, ‘That weak girl—how is she going to get a man to marry her when she chooses?’ But let me tell you one thing, Aunt: Mr. Wildeve is not a profligate man, any more than I am an improper woman. He has an unfortunate manner, and doesn’t try to make people like him if they don’t wish to do it of their own accord.”

text: “Chance—there’s no chance in it,” she said with pride. “What could a poor man like you possibly offer me? I am going indoors. I have nothing more to say. Don’t your horses need feeding, or your saddle-bags mended, or don’t you need to find buyers for your wares, that you linger here idly in this way?”
source_text: “Chance—’tis no chance,” she said proudly. “What can a poor man like you offer me, indeed?—I am going indoors. I have nothing more to say. Don’t your horses want feeding, or your reddlebags want mending, or don’t you want to find buyers for your goods, that you stay idling here like this?”

text: Ned and the brandy were soon brought, and it was given beneath the lantern’s wavering light; after which she recovered enough of herself to make it known, by signs, that something ailed her foot. At last Jory Davenant caught her meaning and looked closely at the limb she had indicated. It was swollen and flushed. While they watched, the flush deepened into a more livid hue, and in its midst there showed a bright scarlet point, no bigger than a pea, which proved to be a drop of blood, standing above the smooth skin of her ankle like a tiny dome.
source_text: Sam and the brandy soon arrived, and it was administered by the light of the lantern; after which she became sufficiently conscious to signify by signs that something was wrong with her foot. Olly Dowden at length understood her meaning, and examined the foot indicated. It was swollen and red. Even as they watched the red began to assume a more livid colour, in the midst of which appeared a scarlet speck, smaller than a pea, and it was found to consist of a drop of blood, which rose above the smooth flesh of her ankle in a hemisphere.

text: But this was not the whole of it. Long years before, there had stirred in his mother’s mind a bright notion concerning Thalia and himself. It had never quite hardened into a command, yet it had remained her dearest daydream: that, in due season, they should become husband and wife, so long as the contentment of neither was thereby imperilled. And what other path, then, was open to a son who held his mother’s remembrance sacred as Ellery did? It is a melancholy truth that some private fancy of parents, which a half-hour’s candid talk in their lifetime might have blown away, is, once they are dead, transmuted into a decree of the most unquestionable sort, with consequences to scrupulous children such as the parents themselves, had they lived, would have been the first to condemn.
source_text: But this was not all. Years ago there had been in his mother’s mind a great fancy about Thomasin and himself. It had not positively amounted to a desire, but it had always been a favourite dream. That they should be man and wife in good time, if the happiness of neither were endangered thereby, was the fancy in question. So that what course save one was there now left for any son who reverenced his mother’s memory as Yeobright did? It is an unfortunate fact that any particular whim of parents, which might have been dispersed by half an hour’s conversation during their lives, becomes sublimated by their deaths into a fiat the most absolute, with such results to conscientious children as those parents, had they lived, would have been the first to decry.

text: Then all withdrew, with reiterated blessings on their host, wishing him long days and contentment in his new estate, in phrases that prolonged the parting not a little. Ransome followed them to the threshold, beyond which the heather-swept slope rose before them in a dark, profound ascent, a vast dominion of shadow spreading from their feet almost to the vault overhead, where the first clear outline emerged in the brooding brow of Eaglebarrow. Plunging into that thick obscurity in a file led by Tom the sodman, they made their unmarked way back across the heath.
source_text: All then took their leave, wishing their entertainer long life and happiness as a married man, with recapitulations which occupied some time. Wildeve attended them to the door, beyond which the deep-dyed upward stretch of heath stood awaiting them, an amplitude of darkness reigning from their feet almost to the zenith, where a definite form first became visible in the lowering forehead of Rainbarrow. Diving into the dense obscurity in a line headed by Sam the turf-cutter, they pursued their trackless way home.

text: “Why, I do hope there won’t be any trouble over it,” said Maria, with sudden alarm. “I was a little uneasy about giving it to so young a man as you, and I did think a little about giving the preference to Father Cobb, but I didn’t quite like the idea of letting it go out of the village, nor of taking away your chance; and they all assured me that you were clever. But if you’re feeling nervous, perhaps we had better have him after all; he’s always ready.”
source_text: “Why, I hope there won't be any trouble about it,” said Maria, in sudden consternation. “I was a little afraid to give it out to so young a man as you, and I thought some of giving the preference to Father Cobb, but I did n't quite like to have it go out of the village, nor to deprive you of the opportunity; and they all assured me that you was smart. But if you 're feeling nervous, perhaps we 'd better have him still; he 's always ready.”

text: “But turn now to the realm of Nature, and consider the vast spectacles that have moved before those now tranquil eyes. In our companion’s boyhood there existed no practical means for the swift conveyance of tidings. In moments of grave alarm, it is true, some patriotic hand might kindle the beacon-fire upon a high tower; yet news traveled languidly across our hand-span nation, and months often elapsed after a presidential election before its issue was commonly known. He lived to behold the telegraph darting its messages around the globe, seeming to abolish time and distance and drawing the scattered peoples of the earth into closer fellowship.
source_text: “But turn to the world of Nature, and think of the panoramic scenes that have passed before those now impassive eyes. In our friend's boyhood there was no practical mode of swift communication of news. In great emergencies, to be sure, some patriot hand might flash the beacon-light from a lofty tower; but news crept slowly over our hand-breath nation, and it was months after a presidential election before the result was generally known. He lived to see the telegraph flashing swiftly about the globe, annihilating time and space and bringing the scattered nations into greater unity.

text: “Well, then, there’s photography,” said Captain Silas. “It does seem astonishing, as the minister said: you sit down, and square yourself up, and smooth your hair, and stare steady into a little funnel, and a man ducks his head under a cloth, and pop! there you are, as natural as life—if not more so. And when Uncle Hiram was a young man, there was nothing but portraits and miniatures, and those black-paper-and-scissors likenesses—what do they call them? Yes, sir, all that came within his observation.”
source_text: “Well, then, there 's photographing,” said Captain Abel. “It doos seem amazing, as the minister said: you set down, and square yourself, and slick your hair, and stare stiddy into a funnel, and a man ducks his head under a covering, and pop! there you be, as natural as life,--if not more so. And when Uncle Capen was a young man, there wasn't nothing but portraits and minnytures, and these black-paper-and-scissors portraits,--what do they call 'em? Yes, sir, all that come in under his observation.”

text: “Well,” said Apollos, “it is always so; we never begin to see the worth of a thing until we have lost it. Now that we sort of stand off and look at Uncle Capen from a fair distance, as it were, he rises in the mind. If only he had not kept so quiet, always, about them there wonders. A man really ought, in justice to himself, to blow his own horn—just a little. But that was a grand discourse, wasn’t it, now?”
source_text: “Well,” said Apollos, “'tis always so; we don't begin to realize the value of a thing tell we lose it. Now that we sort o' stand and gaze at Uncle Capen at a fair distance, as it were, he looms. Ef he only hed n't kep' so quiet, always, about them 'ere wonders. A man really ought, in justice to himself, to blow his own horn--jest a little. But that was a grand discourse, wa'n't it, now?”

text: “When we recall how many hard and trying things can spring up within a single day, let us properly value the steady goodness of a man who has lived a blameless life for a hundred years. When we recall how much harm, how much sin, may be compressed into a single instant, let us properly value the principle that kept him so near the Golden Rule, not for a day, not for a decade or a generation, but for a hundred years.
source_text: “When we remember what hard, trying things often arise within a single day, let us rightly estimate the patient well-doing of a man who has lived a blameless life for a hundred years. When we remember what harm, what sin, can be crowded into a single moment, let us rightly estimate the principle that kept him so close to the Golden Rule, not for a day, not for a decade or a generation, but for a hundred years.

text: “Since neither of you seems inclined to listen,” he said, as they rose, “I shall make no apology for breaking in upon you. Mr. Vale is gathering particulars concerning Uncle Bracken for the sermon at his funeral, and it occurred to me that my excellent wife could aid him better than anyone. So I shall leave him to her.”
source_text: “Since neither of you appears to be listening,” he said, as they started up, “I shall not apologize for interrupting. Mr. Holt is collecting facts about Uncle Capen for his funeral sermon, and I thought that my good wife could help him out, if anybody could. So I will leave him.”

text: “I must tell you,” she said, “one of Uncle Capen’s sayings. It was a long time ago, when I was first married and came here. I had a Bible class for young men in Sunday-school, and Uncle Capen joined it. He always wore a cap, and he sat with the boys at the meetings. Well, one Sunday our lesson included that verse—you know—the one that says that if all these things were written down, the world itself could not contain the books that should be written; and there Uncle Capen interrupted me and said, ‘I suppose that means the world as known to the ancients?’”
source_text: “I must just tell you,” she said, “one of Uncle Capen's sayings. It was long ago, at the time I was married and first came here. I had a young men's Bible-class in Sunday-school, and Uncle Capen came into it. He always wore a cap, and sat at meetings with the boys. So, one Sunday, we had in the lesson that verse,--you know,--that if all these things should be written, even the world itself could not contain the books that should be written; and there Uncle Capen stopped me, and said he, 'I suppose that means the world as known to the ancients?'”

text: “Again, my friends, when our companion had reached the later years of life, there was still no better way to journey between far places than the slow, jolting stage-coach; many among you can well recall its delays and hardships. He witnessed the earliest uncertain attempts of that great force, steam, to carry men more swiftly. He saw the first railroad laid in the country; he lived to behold the land spread over with its iron network.
source_text: “Again, my hearers, when our friend was well advanced in life, there was still no better mode of travel between distant points than the slow, rumbling stage-coach; many who are here remember well its delays and discomforts. He saw the first tentative efforts of that mighty factor steam to transport more swiftly. He saw the first railroad built in the country; he lived to see the land covered with the iron net-work.

text: One thing was certain now: when he recovered, he would not go out hunting for a job. He had more stories in his mind as good as “The Whirlpool,” and at forty dollars apiece he could make far more than any wage or salary he might earn in employment. Just when he had believed the struggle lost, it had been won. He had made good his career. The road before him was open. Starting with the _White Mouse_, he would keep adding magazine after magazine to the lengthening list of those who bought his work. Hack-writing could be left behind. Indeed, it had been wasted effort, since it had brought him not a cent. He would give himself to work, real work, and pour out the best that was in him. He wished Ruth were there to share his happiness, and as he sorted through the letters lying on his bed, he found one from her. It was gently reproachful, asking what could have kept him away so long. He read it again and again in adoration, lingering over her handwriting, loving every stroke of her pen, and at last kissing her signature.
source_text: Well, there was one thing certain: when he got well, he would not go out looking for a job. There were more stories in his head as good as “The Whirlpool,” and at forty dollars apiece he could earn far more than in any job or position. Just when he thought the battle lost, it was won. He had proved for his career. The way was clear. Beginning with the _White Mouse_ he would add magazine after magazine to his growing list of patrons. Hack-work could be put aside. For that matter, it had been wasted time, for it had not brought him a dollar. He would devote himself to work, good work, and he would pour out the best that was in him. He wished Ruth was there to share in his joy, and when he went over the letters left lying on his bed, he found one from her. It was sweetly reproachful, wondering what had kept him away for so dreadful a length of time. He reread the letter adoringly, dwelling over her handwriting, loving each stroke of her pen, and in the end kissing her signature.

text: Then the “$8.00” began to smolder again beneath his eyelids, and he brought himself back to his servitude. But he became sly. There was no reason to roam through his thoughts. He had been a fool. He pulled a lever and set his mind spinning around him, a monstrous wheel of fortune, a merry-go-round of memory, a revolving globe of wisdom. Faster and faster it turned, until its vortex drew him in and he was hurled, spinning, through black chaos.
source_text: Then the “$8.00” began to smoulder under his lids again, and he returned himself to servitude. But he grew cunning. There was no need for him to wander through his mind. He had been a fool. He pulled a lever and made his mind revolve about him, a monstrous wheel of fortune, a merry-go-round of memory, a revolving sphere of wisdom. Faster and faster it revolved, until its vortex sucked him in and he was flung whirling through black chaos.

text: And the next day he had gone there, rushing straight from school so as to arrive first, and beating Cheese-Face by two minutes. The other boys said he was all right, and they offered him advice, pointing out where he was weak as a fighter and promising him a win if he followed their instructions. Those same boys had given Cheese-Face advice as well. How they had loved the fight! He broke off in his memories just long enough to envy them the show he and Cheese-Face had put on. Then the fight began, and it went on, with no rounds, for thirty minutes, until the press-room door was opened.
source_text: And he had come there the next day, hurrying from school to be there first, and beating Cheese-Face by two minutes. The other boys said he was all right, and gave him advice, pointing out his faults as a scrapper and promising him victory if he carried out their instructions. The same boys gave Cheese-Face advice, too. How they had enjoyed the fight! He paused in his recollections long enough to envy them the spectacle he and Cheese-Face had put up. Then the fight was on, and it went on, without rounds, for thirty minutes, until the press-room door was opened.

text: “That’s more like it,” came the softened answer. “You make an attempt at writing, but the attempt comes to nothing. I honor and admire your failure. I know the sort of thing you write; I can recognize it with one glance, and there is one element in it that bars it from the magazines. It has nerve, and magazines have no appetite for that particular thing. What they prefer is drivel and sentimentality, and heaven knows they get plenty of that—but not from you.”
source_text: “That’s better,” was the mollified rejoinder. “You try to write, but you don’t succeed. I respect and admire your failure. I know what you write. I can see it with half an eye, and there’s one ingredient in it that shuts it out of the magazines. It’s guts, and magazines have no use for that particular commodity. What they want is wish-wash and slush, and God knows they get it, but not from you.”

text: And so he went on, wavering between despondency and exaltation as he gazed at the shelves crammed full of knowledge. In one unlabeled corner he came upon a “Stanton’s Abridgment.” He turned its pages with almost religious care. Somehow it seemed to answer him in a familiar tongue. He and it were both children of the sea. Then he discovered a “Bowker,” and volumes by Halsey and Meredith. There it was; he would teach himself seamanship. He would leave off drinking, better himself, and become a master. Clara seemed very close to him at that instant. As a master, he might marry her, if she would consent. And if she would not, well—he would still live well among men, because of Her, and in any case he would leave off drinking. Then he recalled the insurers and the shipowners, the two powers a captain must answer to, either of which could ruin him and whose demands were utterly at odds. He looked about the room and shut his eyes on a vision of ten thousand books. No; no more of the sea for him. There was strength in all that accumulated learning, and if he was to accomplish anything great, he must do it on land. Besides, captains were not permitted to bring their wives with them to sea.
source_text: And so he wandered on, alternating between depression and elation as he stared at the shelves packed with wisdom. In one miscellaneous section he came upon a “Norrie’s Epitome.” He turned the pages reverently. In a way, it spoke a kindred speech. Both he and it were of the sea. Then he found a “Bowditch” and books by Lecky and Marshall. There it was; he would teach himself navigation. He would quit drinking, work up, and become a captain. Ruth seemed very near to him in that moment. As a captain, he could marry her (if she would have him). And if she wouldn’t, well—he would live a good life among men, because of Her, and he would quit drinking anyway. Then he remembered the underwriters and the owners, the two masters a captain must serve, either of which could and would break him and whose interests were diametrically opposed. He cast his eyes about the room and closed the lids down on a vision of ten thousand books. No; no more of the sea for him. There was power in all that wealth of books, and if he would do great things, he must do them on the land. Besides, captains were not allowed to take their wives to sea with them.

text: “You see,” she said candidly, “writing has to be a profession, just like any other. Not that I know anything about it, naturally. I’m only using plain common sense. You couldn’t expect to become a blacksmith without spending three years learning the trade—or was it five? Well, writers are paid so much better than blacksmiths that there must be a great many more men who would like to write, who—try to write.”
source_text: “You see,” she said frankly, “writing must be a trade, like anything else. Not that I know anything about it, of course. I only bring common judgment to bear. You couldn’t hope to be a blacksmith without spending three years at learning the trade—or is it five years! Now writers are so much better paid than blacksmiths that there must be ever so many more men who would like to write, who—try to write.”

text: In the days that came after, Joe was too occupied with getting the laundry arranged and under control to give him much thought; and it was not until the day before departure that the newspapers announced he had booked passage on the _Mariposa_. Once, when the instinct for self-preservation stirred in him, he went to a doctor and submitted to a thorough physical examination. Nothing could be discovered wrong with him. His heart and lungs were declared splendid. Every organ, so far as the doctor could determine, was normal and functioning normally.
source_text: In the days that followed Joe was too busy organizing and taking hold of the laundry to bother him much; and it was not until the day before sailing that the newspapers made the announcement that he had taken passage on the _Mariposa_. Once, when the instinct of preservation fluttered, he went to a doctor and underwent a searching physical examination. Nothing could be found the matter with him. His heart and lungs were pronounced magnificent. Every organ, so far as the doctor could know, was normal and was working normally.

text: “I have finished—set the lute aside. Song and singing pass away quickly, like the airy shadows that drift among the purple clover. I have finished—set the lute aside. Once I sang like the early thrushes singing in the dewy bushes; now I am silent. I am like an exhausted linnet, for there is no song left in my throat; my moment for singing has come and gone. I have finished. Set the lute aside.”
source_text: “‘I have done— Put by the lute. Song and singing soon are over As the airy shades that hover In among the purple clover. I have done— Put by the lute. Once I sang as early thrushes Sing among the dewy bushes; Now I’m mute. I am like a weary linnet, For my throat has no song in it; I have had my singing minute. I have done. Put by the lute.’”

text:
At last he had met the woman—the woman he had scarcely thought about, since he was not given to thinking about women, though in some distant, half-conscious way he had expected that one day he would meet her. He had sat beside her at dinner. He had felt her hand in his, had looked into her eyes, and had glimpsed a lovely spirit there; yet it was no more beautiful than the eyes that revealed it, nor than the flesh that clothed it and gave it shape. He did not think of her flesh as flesh, and this was new to him, for the women he had known were only ever flesh to him. Hers was somehow otherwise. He did not see her body as a body liable to the ailments and weakness of bodies. It was more than a covering for her spirit. It seemed an outflow of that spirit, a pure and gracious crystallization of her divine being.

The thought of the divine startled him. It jolted him out of his dreaming into clear, sober thought. No word, no clue, no suggestion of the divine had ever come his way before. He had never believed in it. He had always been irreligious, laughing good-humoredly at the sky-pilots and their soul immortality. There was no life after this, he had maintained; there was only here and now, and then the endless dark. Yet what he had seen in her eyes was soul—an immortal soul that could never die. No man he had known, and no woman either, had ever brought him this message of immortality. But she had. She had breathed it to him the very first instant she looked at him.

Her face drifted before his eyes as he walked on—pale and grave, sweet and delicate, smiling with pity and tenderness as only a spirit could smile, and pure beyond anything he had ever imagined purity to be. Her purity struck him like a blow. It amazed him. He had known good and evil, but purity, as a quality of being, had never before entered his mind. And now, in her, he understood purity as the highest excellence of goodness and cleanness, the total of which was eternal life.
source_text: He had met the woman at last—the woman that he had thought little about, not being given to thinking about women, but whom he had expected, in a remote way, he would sometime meet. He had sat next to her at table. He had felt her hand in his, he had looked into her eyes and caught a vision of a beautiful spirit;—but no more beautiful than the eyes through which it shone, nor than the flesh that gave it expression and form. He did not think of her flesh as flesh,—which was new to him; for of the women he had known that was the only way he thought. Her flesh was somehow different. He did not conceive of her body as a body, subject to the ills and frailties of bodies. Her body was more than the garb of her spirit. It was an emanation of her spirit, a pure and gracious crystallization of her divine essence. This feeling of the divine startled him. It shocked him from his dreams to sober thought. No word, no clew, no hint, of the divine had ever reached him before. He had never believed in the divine. He had always been irreligious, scoffing good-naturedly at the sky-pilots and their immortality of the soul. There was no life beyond, he had contended; it was here and now, then darkness everlasting. But what he had seen in her eyes was soul—immortal soul that could never die. No man he had known, nor any woman, had given him the message of immortality. But she had. She had whispered it to him the first moment she looked at him. Her face shimmered before his eyes as he walked along,—pale and serious, sweet and sensitive, smiling with pity and tenderness as only a spirit could smile, and pure as he had never dreamed purity could be. Her purity smote him like a blow. It startled him. He had known good and bad; but purity, as an attribute of existence, had never entered his mind. And now, in her, he conceived purity to be the superlative of goodness and of cleanness, the sum of which constituted eternal life.

text: Yet it seemed to him that something in the story struck harshly against his sense of beauty and of life. He could find no sufficient motive in Mr. Butler’s existence of stint and deprivation. Had he done it for the sake of a woman, or in pursuit of beauty, Martin could have understood. God’s own mad lover might do anything for the kiss, but not for thirty thousand dollars a year. He was unsatisfied with Mr. Butler’s career. There was something, after all, mean and diminished about it. Thirty thousand a year was very well, but dyspepsia and an inability to be humanly happy stripped so princely an income of all its worth.
source_text: But it seemed to him there was something in the recital that jarred upon his sense of beauty and life. He could not find an adequate motive in Mr. Butler’s life of pinching and privation. Had he done it for love of a woman, or for attainment of beauty, Martin would have understood. God’s own mad lover should do anything for the kiss, but not for thirty thousand dollars a year. He was dissatisfied with Mr. Butler’s career. There was something paltry about it, after all. Thirty thousand a year was all right, but dyspepsia and inability to be humanly happy robbed such princely income of all its value.

text: Martin’s sunburn had been washed out by his labor in the laundry and by the life he now led indoors, while hunger and illness had drained his face still farther toward a pallid white; and over that pallor came a slow wash of color. He had begun to open his mouth to answer, but Ruth cut him short.
source_text: Martin’s sunburn had been bleached by his work in the laundry and by the indoor life he was living, while the hunger and the sickness had made his face even pale; and across this pallor flowed the slow wave of a blush. He was opening his mouth to speak, but Ruth shut him off.

text: Yet the episode had drawn him closer after all. Its recollection remained with him, and in the hours when his spirits were lowest, he turned to it hungrily. The chasm was never so vast again. He had traversed a distance far beyond any degree in the arts, beyond a score of degrees. She was spotless, it was true, in a way he had never even imagined spotlessness; and yet the juice of berries had stained her mouth. She bowed, as he did, beneath the laws of the world. She must eat in order to live, and if her shoes were soaked, she took cold. But that was not the matter. If she could know hunger and thirst, heat and chill, then surely she could know love—and love for a man. Well, he was a man. And why should he not be that man? “It rests with me to prove myself,” he would whisper with ardor. “I shall be the man. I shall make myself the man. I shall prove myself.”
source_text: But still the incident had brought him nearer. The memory of it persisted, and in the moments when he was most cast down, he dwelt upon it eagerly. The gulf was never again so wide. He had accomplished a distance vastly greater than a bachelorship of arts, or a dozen bachelorships. She was pure, it was true, as he had never dreamed of purity; but cherries stained her lips. She was subject to the laws of the universe just as inexorably as he was. She had to eat to live, and when she got her feet wet, she caught cold. But that was not the point. If she could feel hunger and thirst, and heat and cold, then could she feel love—and love for a man. Well, he was a man. And why could he not be the man? “It’s up to me to make good,” he would murmur fervently. “I will be _the_ man. I will make myself _the_ man. I will make good.”

text: “I’ve listened to him deliver a stump speech. It was so artfully foolish and so utterly commonplace, and yet so persuasive, that the party chiefs can scarcely do otherwise than think him dependable and sound, while his empty phrases are so close to the empty phrases of the ordinary voter that—well, you know how any man is pleased when you take his own notions, put them in finer dress, and hand them back to him.”
source_text: “I’ve heard him make a campaign speech. It was so cleverly stupid and unoriginal, and also so convincing, that the leaders cannot help but regard him as safe and sure, while his platitudes are so much like the platitudes of the average voter that—oh, well, you know you flatter any man by dressing up his own thoughts for him and presenting them to him.”

text: “No, I’m not fool enough for that, at any rate,” Martin replied. “But I’ll tell you what I will do. You gave me the finest night of my life. You gave me something money can’t buy. Now I’ve got money, and it means nothing to me. I’d like to hand over to you a thousand dollars of what I don’t care about in exchange for what you gave me that night, which was beyond any price. You need the money. I’ve got more than I need. You want it. You came here for it. There’s no point in trying to wrest it out of me. Take it.”
source_text: “No, I’m not chump enough for that, at any rate,” Martin answered. “But I’ll tell you what I will do. You gave me the greatest night of my life. You gave me what money cannot buy. Now I’ve got money, and it means nothing to me. I’d like to turn over to you a thousand dollars of what I don’t value for what you gave me that night and which was beyond price. You need the money. I’ve got more than I need. You want it. You came for it. There’s no use scheming it out of me. Take it.”

text: He gave a slight nod and watched her with a reflective air as she gathered up the torn scraps of the manuscript and slipped them into the pocket of her jacket—visible proof, as it were, that her errand had succeeded. She put him in mind of Nora Daly, though there was less flame and splendid, unashamed vitality in her than in that other working girl whom he had seen but twice. Yet in dress and bearing the two were much of a kind, and he smiled inwardly at the whim that could imagine either of them in Mrs. Harrow’s drawing-room. The smile soon passed, and a profound loneliness settled over him. That sister of his and the Harrow drawing-room were milestones on the road he had already travelled. He had left them behind. He looked round him with a kind of affection at his handful of books. They alone remained his companions.
source_text: He nodded his head, regarding her thoughtfully as she gathered the torn pieces of manuscript and tucked them into the pocket of her jacket—ocular evidence of the success of her mission. She reminded him of Lizzie Connolly, though there was less of fire and gorgeous flaunting life in her than in that other girl of the working class whom he had seen twice. But they were on a par, the pair of them, in dress and carriage, and he smiled with inward amusement at the caprice of his fancy which suggested the appearance of either of them in Mrs. Morse’s drawing-room. The amusement faded, and he was aware of a great loneliness. This sister of his and the Morse drawing-room were milestones of the road he had travelled. And he had left them behind. He glanced affectionately about him at his few books. They were all the comrades left to him.

text: Martin drew a long breath of relief as the laundryman went out and the door shut after him. He was growing unsociable. Each day he found it harder to keep himself civil with people. Their very presence unsettled him, and the labor of conversation wore on his nerves. They made him fidgety, so that scarcely was he in their company before he was searching for some excuse to be rid of them.
source_text: Martin heaved a sigh of relief when the door closed behind the laundryman. He was becoming anti-social. Daily he found it a severer strain to be decent with people. Their presence perturbed him, and the effort of conversation irritated him. They made him restless, and no sooner was he in contact with them than he was casting about for excuses to get rid of them.

text: She went on speaking in her heated, offended way, but he was not listening to her. He smiled to himself as he looked up into her maidenly face, so innocent, so almost piercingly innocent, that its purity seemed always to enter into him, casting out all that was base and cleansing him in some unearthly radiance, cool and soft and velvety as starlight. _We know there are nasty things in the world_! He cherished the idea of her knowing it, and laughed over it as though it were a lover’s jest. Then, in a sudden vision rich with detail, he saw the whole vast sea of life’s filth that he had known and sailed through, and he forgave her for not understanding the tale. It was no fault of hers that she could not. He thanked God that she had been born and guarded in such innocence. But he knew life, its corruption as well as its beauty, its greatness in spite of the slime that clung to it, and by God he meant to speak of it to the world. Saints in heaven—how could they be anything but pure and fair? No credit to them. But saints in the mire—ah, that was the endless marvel! That was what made life worth while. To see moral greatness rising from cesspools of evil; to lift himself and first catch sight of beauty, faint and distant, through eyes dripping with mud; to see strength, and truth, and high spiritual gift arising out of weakness, frailty, viciousness, and all the depths of brutishness—
source_text: She talked on in her indignant strain, but he was not following her. He was smiling to himself as he looked up into her virginal face, so innocent, so penetratingly innocent, that its purity seemed always to enter into him, driving out of him all dross and bathing him in some ethereal effulgence that was as cool and soft and velvety as starshine. _We know there are nasty things in the world_! He cuddled to him the notion of her knowing, and chuckled over it as a love joke. The next moment, in a flashing vision of multitudinous detail, he sighted the whole sea of life’s nastiness that he had known and voyaged over and through, and he forgave her for not understanding the story. It was through no fault of hers that she could not understand. He thanked God that she had been born and sheltered to such innocence. But he knew life, its foulness as well as its fairness, its greatness in spite of the slime that infested it, and by God he was going to have his say on it to the world. Saints in heaven—how could they be anything but fair and pure? No praise to them. But saints in slime—ah, that was the everlasting wonder! That was what made life worth while. To see moral grandeur rising out of cesspools of iniquity; to rise himself and first glimpse beauty, faint and far, through mud-dripping eyes; to see out of weakness, and frailty, and viciousness, and all abysmal brutishness, arising strength, and truth, and high spiritual endowment—

text: He was gone. Martin watched his tall figure descend the outside steps, and as he turned back to shut the gate, he felt a pang at the sight of the shoulders that had once been broad now hunched over the collapsed wreck of a chest. Martin brought out two tumblers and set himself to reading the book of poems, Henry Vaughn Marlow’s latest collection.
source_text: He was off and away. Martin watched his long figure go down the outside steps, and, on turning to close the gate, noted with a pang the shoulders, which had once been broad, drawn in now over the collapsed ruin of the chest. Martin got two tumblers, and fell to reading the book of verse, Henry Vaughn Marlow’s latest collection.

text: “That is a cheap judgment,” he said quietly. “I first heard it in City Hall Park, from the mouth of a workingman who should have known better. I have heard it many times since, and each time the empty clap-trap of it sickens me. You ought to be ashamed of yourself. To hear the name of that great and noble man on your lips is like finding a dewdrop in a cesspool. You are disgusting.”
source_text: “A cheap judgment,” he remarked quietly. “I heard it first in the City Hall Park, on the lips of a workingman who ought to have known better. I have heard it often since, and each time the clap-trap of it nauseates me. You ought to be ashamed of yourself. To hear that great and noble man’s name upon your lips is like finding a dew-drop in a cesspool. You are disgusting.”

text: Once he made an effort to shake off his torpor and went forward into the forecastle among the sailors. But the breed of sailors seemed changed since the days when he had lived in the forecastle himself. He could find no fellowship with these stolid, ox-eyed, brutish men. Despair seized him. Above, no one had wanted Martin Eden for his own sake; and he could not return to the people of his own class who had once wanted him. He did not want them. He could no more endure them than he could endure the foolish first-cabin passengers and the boisterous young people.
source_text: Once, he tried to arouse himself from his lethargy, and went forward into the forecastle with the sailors. But the breed of sailors seemed to have changed since the days he had lived in the forecastle. He could find no kinship with these stolid-faced, ox-minded bestial creatures. He was in despair. Up above nobody had wanted Martin Eden for his own sake, and he could not go back to those of his own class who had wanted him in the past. He did not want them. He could not stand them any more than he could stand the stupid first-cabin passengers and the riotous young people.

text: “Certainly—just as spies move among enemy camps. How else are you to find out what the other side is doing? Besides, I enjoy their meetings. They’re sturdy fighters, and whether they’re right or wrong, they’ve done their reading. Any one of them knows far more about sociology and all the other -ologies than the average captain of industry. Yes, I’ve attended half a dozen of their meetings, but that doesn’t make me a socialist any more than listening to Charley Hapgood harangue made me a Republican.”
source_text: “Certainly, just as spies frequent hostile camps. How else are you to learn about the enemy? Besides, I enjoy myself at their meetings. They are good fighters, and, right or wrong, they have read the books. Any one of them knows far more about sociology and all the other ologies than the average captain of industry. Yes, I have been to half a dozen of their meetings, but that doesn’t make me a socialist any more than hearing Charley Hapgood orate made me a Republican.”

text: The Eighth Avenue Viaduct, spanning a narrow reach of the San Miguel Inlet, stretched for the length of three city blocks. Halfway across, and again at either end, electric lamps burned. No policeman could cross those end-lights without being seen. It was the chosen ground for the fight that stirred once more beneath Martin’s eyelids. He saw the two gangs, hostile and grim, holding themselves rigidly apart and cheering their own champions; and he saw himself and Scar-Jaw undressing. A little way off, sentries were posted to watch the illuminated ends of the bridge. One of the Boo boys held Martin’s coat, shirt, and cap, ready to sprint with them to safety if the police came. Martin watched himself step into the center, facing Scar-Jaw, and he heard himself say, with his hand lifted in warning:-
source_text: The Eighth Street Bridge, crossing an arm of San Antonio Estuary, was the length of three city blocks. In the middle of the bridge, and at each end, were electric lights. No policeman could pass those end-lights unseen. It was the safe place for the battle that revived itself under Martin’s eyelids. He saw the two gangs, aggressive and sullen, rigidly keeping apart from each other and backing their respective champions; and he saw himself and Cheese-Face stripping. A short distance away lookouts were set, their task being to watch the lighted ends of the bridge. A member of the Boo Gang held Martin’s coat, and shirt, and cap, ready to race with them into safety in case the police interfered. Martin watched himself go into the centre, facing Cheese-Face, and he heard himself say, as he held up his hand warningly:-

text: “It came upon me all at once.” She spoke very slowly, her eyes warm and unsteady and luminous, a faint color rising in her cheeks and lingering there. “I never knew it until this moment—when you took me in your arms. And I never thought I should marry you, Adrian, not until this very instant. How did you contrive to make me love you?”
source_text: “It came to me suddenly.” She was speaking very slowly, her eyes warm and fluttery and melting, a soft flush on her cheeks that did not go away. “I never knew until just now when—you put your arms around me. And I never expected to marry you, Martin, not until just now. How did you make me love you?”

text: The road to North Berkeley had been a long one, but it was not until he climbed the stairs and let himself into his room that he was aware he had traversed it. He found himself seated on the edge of the bed, gazing about him with the blank wonder of a sleepwalker suddenly roused. On the table he saw “Past Due,” and he drew his chair close and took up his pen. There was in him a strict, almost mathematical need for wholeness. Something remained unfinished. It had been put aside until another matter should be brought to an end. Now that other thing was done, and he would give himself to this remaining labor until it too was done. What he should do afterward he did not know. Only this he knew: that a turning point in his life had been reached. A sentence had ended, and he was setting the final stop upon it with careful, workmanlike hands. He felt no curiosity concerning the future. In due time he would learn what awaited him there. Whatever it might be, it no longer seemed to signify. Nothing seemed to signify.
source_text: It was a long walk to North Oakland, but it was not until he went up the steps and entered his room that he knew he had walked it. He found himself sitting on the edge of the bed and staring about him like an awakened somnambulist. He noticed “Overdue” lying on the table and drew up his chair and reached for his pen. There was in his nature a logical compulsion toward completeness. Here was something undone. It had been deferred against the completion of something else. Now that something else had been finished, and he would apply himself to this task until it was finished. What he would do next he did not know. All that he did know was that a climacteric in his life had been attained. A period had been reached, and he was rounding it off in workman-like fashion. He was not curious about the future. He would soon enough find out what it held in store for him. Whatever it was, it did not matter. Nothing seemed to matter.

text: Surely, he told himself, my fine clothes are advertisement enough of my want. Again and again, whenever a chance presented itself, he had dropped a hint as to the object of his errand. Yet the ears of those who admired him remained obstinately shut. They lavished praise upon him, recounted what they had first made of his tale, what they came afterward to think of it, what their wives and children thought as well; but never once did they so much as breathe a suggestion that they meant to pay him for it.
source_text: Surely, he thought to himself, my glad rags in themselves are eloquent advertisement of my need. Time and again, whenever opportunity offered, he hinted about the purpose of his business. But his admirers’ ears were deaf. They sang his praises, told him what they had thought of his story at first sight, what they subsequently thought, what their wives and families thought; but not one hint did they breathe of intention to pay him for it.

text: He had never been so at a loss for words in his life. Honestly, he could not think of a single idea. He felt clumsy and foolish, yet he could not, for the life of him, come up with anything to say. It would have been easier if the interruption had been the Shelly Hot Springs laundry. Then he could have rolled up his sleeves and set to work.
source_text: He had never been so tongue-tied in his life. Positively he did not have an idea in his head. He felt stupid and awkward, but for the life of him he could think of nothing to say. It would have been easier had the intrusion been the Shelly Hot Springs laundry. He could have rolled up his sleeves and gone to work.

text: “But you overlook,” she broke in, her nimble mind catching at a resemblance. “There have been odd inventors who let their families go hungry while they chased such fantasies as perpetual motion. No doubt their wives adored them, and endured with them and for them, not because of, but despite, their obsession with perpetual motion.”
source_text: “But you forget,” she interrupted, the quick surface of her mind glimpsing a parallel. “There have been eccentric inventors, starving their families while they sought such chimeras as perpetual motion. Doubtless their wives loved them, and suffered with them and for them, not because of but in spite of their infatuation for perpetual motion.”

text: In one of the leading juvenile magazines he discovered whole columns of anecdote and incident. Here, surely, was his opening. Yet his paragraphs were sent back, and though he tried again and again, he never managed to get a single one accepted. Much later, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors padded their salaries by writing those very paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies rejected his jokes and comic verse, and the light society verse he contributed to the larger magazines found no foothold anywhere. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew he could write better ones than the ones in print. After obtaining the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he showered them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place even one, he gave up. And yet day after day he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies—scores upon scores of them—not one of which could bear comparison with his. In his dejection, he came to believe that he possessed no judgment at all, that he was mesmerized by his own writing, and that he was merely a self-deceived pretender.
source_text: In one of the great juvenile periodicals he noted whole columns of incident and anecdote. Here was a chance. His paragraphs were returned, and though he tried repeatedly he never succeeded in placing one. Later on, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors augmented their salaries by supplying those paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies returned his jokes and humorous verse, and the light society verse he wrote for the large magazines found no abiding-place. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew that he could write better ones than were published. Managing to obtain the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he deluged them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place one of them, he ceased. And yet, from day to day, he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies, scores and scores of storiettes, not one of which would compare with his. In his despondency, he concluded that he had no judgment whatever, that he was hypnotized by what he wrote, and that he was a self-deluded pretender.

text: So Martin thought, and so he said when Brissenden urged him to give them hell. He obeyed the summons, stepping up to the platform, as the custom was, and addressing the chairman. He began in a low voice, with some hesitation, setting in order the thoughts that had risen in his mind while the Jew was speaking. At such meetings each speaker was allowed five minutes; but when Martin’s five minutes had expired, he was in full stride, and his attack on their doctrines was only half finished. He had seized their attention, and the audience called on the chairman by acclamation to extend Martin’s time. They respected him as an opponent worthy of their minds, and they listened closely, catching every word. He spoke with fire and conviction, sparing no one in his assault on the slaves and their morality and methods, and openly referring to his hearers as the slaves in question. He quoted Spencer and Malthus, and stated the biological law of development.
source_text: So Martin thought, and so he spoke when Brissenden urged him to give them hell. He obeyed the mandate, walking up to the platform, as was the custom, and addressing the chairman. He began in a low voice, haltingly, forming into order the ideas which had surged in his brain while the Jew was speaking. In such meetings five minutes was the time allotted to each speaker; but when Martin’s five minutes were up, he was in full stride, his attack upon their doctrines but half completed. He had caught their interest, and the audience urged the chairman by acclamation to extend Martin’s time. They appreciated him as a foeman worthy of their intellect, and they listened intently, following every word. He spoke with fire and conviction, mincing no words in his attack upon the slaves and their morality and tactics and frankly alluding to his hearers as the slaves in question. He quoted Spencer and Malthus, and enunciated the biological law of development.

text: During those several weeks he saw Ruth half a dozen times, and each meeting brought a fresh exaltation. She helped him with his English, corrected his pronunciation, and set him to work at arithmetic. Yet their talks were not given wholly to the simplest lessons. He had known too much of life, and his mind had ripened too far, to be satisfied entirely with fractions, cube root, parsing, and analysis; and now and then their conversation wandered to other matters—the latest poetry he had read, the newest poet she had studied. And when she read aloud to him her favorite passages, he seemed lifted to the very pinnacle of delight. Never among all the women he had ever heard speak had he heard a voice like hers. The faintest cadence of it stirred his love, and he quivered and throbbed with every word she spoke. It was the tone of it, the calm, the musical inflection—the soft, rich, indefinable blending of culture and a gentle spirit. While he listened, there sounded in the memory of his ears the rough cries of barbarian women and hags, and, in milder degrees of harshness, the shrill voices of working women and the girls of his own class. Then the alchemy of sight would begin its work, and they would pass in review through his mind, each one, by contrast, heightening Ruth’s splendor. His happiness was further deepened by the knowledge that her mind was taking in all she read and trembling with delight at the beauty of the written thought. She read much to him from “The Princess,” and often he saw her eyes filled with tears, so delicately strung was her aesthetic feeling. At such times her own emotion lifted him until he felt himself like a god, and, as he watched and listened, it seemed to him that he was gazing upon the face of life and reading its innermost secrets. And then, becoming conscious of the heights of exquisite sensitiveness to which he had risen, he told himself that this was love, and that love was the greatest thing in the world. And then there would come back, along the galleries of memory, all the former thrills and fires he had known—the intoxication of wine, the embraces of women, the rough sport and give-and-take of bodily conflict—and they seemed petty and base beside the sublime ardor he now possessed.
source_text: During those several weeks he saw Ruth half a dozen times, and each time was an added inspiration. She helped him with his English, corrected his pronunciation, and started him on arithmetic. But their intercourse was not all devoted to elementary study. He had seen too much of life, and his mind was too matured, to be wholly content with fractions, cube root, parsing, and analysis; and there were times when their conversation turned on other themes—the last poetry he had read, the latest poet she had studied. And when she read aloud to him her favorite passages, he ascended to the topmost heaven of delight. Never, in all the women he had heard speak, had he heard a voice like hers. The least sound of it was a stimulus to his love, and he thrilled and throbbed with every word she uttered. It was the quality of it, the repose, and the musical modulation—the soft, rich, indefinable product of culture and a gentle soul. As he listened to her, there rang in the ears of his memory the harsh cries of barbarian women and of hags, and, in lesser degrees of harshness, the strident voices of working women and of the girls of his own class. Then the chemistry of vision would begin to work, and they would troop in review across his mind, each, by contrast, multiplying Ruth’s glories. Then, too, his bliss was heightened by the knowledge that her mind was comprehending what she read and was quivering with appreciation of the beauty of the written thought. She read to him much from “The Princess,” and often he saw her eyes swimming with tears, so finely was her aesthetic nature strung. At such moments her own emotions elevated him till he was as a god, and, as he gazed at her and listened, he seemed gazing on the face of life and reading its deepest secrets. And then, becoming aware of the heights of exquisite sensibility he attained, he decided that this was love and that love was the greatest thing in the world. And in review would pass along the corridors of memory all previous thrills and burnings he had known,—the drunkenness of wine, the caresses of women, the rough play and give and take of physical contests,—and they seemed trivial and mean compared with this sublime ardor he now enjoyed.

text: Nevertheless, by his skillful work and the improvements he made to her kitchen-laundry, he sank enormously in her estimation. The romantic glamour with which her imagination had clothed him faded in the hard light of the fact that he had once been a laundryman. All his books, and all his distinguished friends who came to see him in carriages or bearing innumerable bottles of whiskey, counted for nothing. He was, after all, only a workingman, of her own class and caste. He was more human, more accessible, but he was no longer a mystery.
source_text: Nevertheless, by his masterly operation and improvement of her kitchen-laundry he fell an immense distance in her regard. The glamour of romance with which her imagination had invested him faded away in the cold light of fact that he was an ex-laundryman. All his books, and his grand friends who visited him in carriages or with countless bottles of whiskey, went for naught. He was, after all, a mere workingman, a member of her own class and caste. He was more human and approachable, but, he was no longer mystery.

text: For a moment it crossed his mind that there was intent in the slight. He had felt the strain of her hostility, and he tried to read the meaning her fingers were uttering on the keyboard. Then he put the notion away as absurd and impossible, and surrendered himself the more wholly to the strain of the music. The old enchantment began again to steal over him. His feet were no longer of clay, and his body seemed turned to spirit; before his eyes and behind them there shone a vast radiance; and then the room dissolved, and he was gone, drifting upon the world that was to him so dear. The familiar and the strange mingled in the dream-procession that crowded his sight. He came into far harbors of sun-drenched coasts, and wandered through bazars among savage peoples no man had ever beheld. The odor of the spice islands filled his nostrils as it had on hot, breathless nights at sea, or he drove into the southeast trades through long tropical days, leaving behind him palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea and raising palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea before him. The visions flashed by with the speed of thought. In one instant he was on a mustang, riding hard through the radiant country of the Painted Desert; in the next he peered through wavering heat into the chalk-white tomb of Death Valley, or rowed across a frozen ocean where towering ice islands shone and sparkled in the sun. He stretched on a coral strand where the coco palms leaned down to the soft, murmuring surf. The hulk of some ancient wreck flamed with sapphire fire, and in that glow the hula girls moved to the wild love-songs of the chanters, who sang to the bright plink of ukuleles and the deep boom of tom-toms. It was a lush, tropical night. Behind them a volcano’s crater stood black against the stars. Above, a slender moon drifted pale through the heavens, and the Southern Cross burned faintly low in the sky.
source_text: Once, it entered his mind that there was a deliberate rebuff in all this. He caught her spirit of antagonism and strove to divine the message that her hands pronounced upon the keys. Then he dismissed the thought as unworthy and impossible, and yielded himself more freely to the music. The old delightful condition began to be induced. His feet were no longer clay, and his flesh became spirit; before his eyes and behind his eyes shone a great glory; and then the scene before him vanished and he was away, rocking over the world that was to him a very dear world. The known and the unknown were commingled in the dream-pageant that thronged his vision. He entered strange ports of sun-washed lands, and trod market-places among barbaric peoples that no man had ever seen. The scent of the spice islands was in his nostrils as he had known it on warm, breathless nights at sea, or he beat up against the southeast trades through long tropic days, sinking palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea behind and lifting palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea ahead. Swift as thought the pictures came and went. One instant he was astride a broncho and flying through the fairy-colored Painted Desert country; the next instant he was gazing down through shimmering heat into the whited sepulchre of Death Valley, or pulling an oar on a freezing ocean where great ice islands towered and glistened in the sun. He lay on a coral beach where the cocoanuts grew down to the mellow-sounding surf. The hulk of an ancient wreck burned with blue fires, in the light of which danced the _hula_ dancers to the barbaric love-calls of the singers, who chanted to tinkling _ukuleles_ and rumbling tom-toms. It was a sensuous, tropic night. In the background a volcano crater was silhouetted against the stars. Overhead drifted a pale crescent moon, and the Southern Cross burned low in the sky.

text: “Certainly not. What fool would expect it? From rhyme, yes. There’s Bruce, and Virginia Spring, and Sedgwick. They do very nicely. But poetry—do you know how Vaughn Marlow earns his living? Teaching at a boys’ cramming school down in Pennsylvania, and of all private little hells, such a post is the limit. I wouldn’t swap places with him if he had fifty years ahead of him. And yet his work rises above the mass of contemporary verse-makers like a balas ruby among carrots. And the reviews he receives! Damn them, every one of them, the dull manikins!”
source_text: “Certainly not. What fool expects to? Out of rhyming, yes. There’s Bruce, and Virginia Spring, and Sedgwick. They do very nicely. But poetry—do you know how Vaughn Marlow makes his living?—teaching in a boys’ cramming-joint down in Pennsylvania, and of all private little hells such a billet is the limit. I wouldn’t trade places with him if he had fifty years of life before him. And yet his work stands out from the ruck of the contemporary versifiers as a balas ruby among carrots. And the reviews he gets! Damn them, all of them, the crass manikins!”

text: “But I haven’t come to the point yet. Here it is. I want to work my way into the sort of life you’ve got in this house. There’s more to life than drink, and hard labor, and drifting about. Now, how do I get it? Where do I begin, and where do I put my hands on it? I’m willing to earn my keep, you know, and when it comes to hard work I can make most men feel ill. Once I get started, I’ll work night and day. Maybe you think it’s a joke, me coming to you with all this. I know you’re the last person in the world I ought to ask, but I don’t know anyone else I could ask—unless it’s Arthur. Maybe I ought to ask him. If I was—”
source_text: “But I ain’t got to the point yet. Here it is. I want to make my way to the kind of life you have in this house. There’s more in life than booze, an’ hard work, an’ knockin’ about. Now, how am I goin’ to get it? Where do I take hold an’ begin? I’m willin’ to work my passage, you know, an’ I can make most men sick when it comes to hard work. Once I get started, I’ll work night an’ day. Mebbe you think it’s funny, me askin’ you about all this. I know you’re the last person in the world I ought to ask, but I don’t know anybody else I could ask—unless it’s Arthur. Mebbe I ought to ask him. If I was—”

text: “I’d like to hand it over to you. There must be something you’d want—to go to school, or to business college. You might like to study and become a stenographer. I could arrange it for you. Or perhaps your father and mother are still alive—I could set them up in a grocery store or something like that. Anything you want, just say the word, and I can arrange it for you.”
source_text: “I’d like to turn it over to you. There must be something you want—to go to school or business college. You might like to study and be a stenographer. I could fix it for you. Or maybe your father and mother are living—I could set them up in a grocery store or something. Anything you want, just name it, and I can fix it for you.”

text: Then he understood what it meant, and his heart began to hammer, taunting him to play the lover to this woman, who was no spirit from some other world but only a woman, with lips that a cherry might have stained. He shuddered at the boldness of the thought; yet his whole soul was singing, and reason, in a victorious hymn, told him he was right. Some part of this change in him must have reached her, for she stopped reading, lifted her eyes to him, and smiled. His gaze fell from her blue eyes to her lips, and the sight of that stain drove him half wild. His arms seemed almost to spring out and close about her, in the fashion of his old, heedless life. She appeared to incline toward him, to wait, and all his will struggled to keep him back.
source_text: Then he realized the significance of it, and his heart began pounding and challenging him to play the lover with this woman who was not a spirit from other worlds but a mere woman with lips a cherry could stain. He trembled at the audacity of his thought; but all his soul was singing, and reason, in a triumphant paean, assured him he was right. Something of this change in him must have reached her, for she paused from her reading, looked up at him, and smiled. His eyes dropped from her blue eyes to her lips, and the sight of the stain maddened him. His arms all but flashed out to her and around her, in the way of his old careless life. She seemed to lean toward him, to wait, and all his will fought to hold him back.

text: So Bernard did not singe that parcel, and let the strain in his muscles slacken a little, though the strain in his nerves rose higher than ever; and he listened with a fellow-feeling ear to the other man’s curses as he labored and groaned over the exquisite things women wear when they need not wash their own things. “Dressed starch” was Bernard’s terror, and it was also Tom’s. It was “dressed starch” that stole the few precious minutes they had won. They worked at it from dawn till dusk. At seven in the evening they broke away to run the hotel linen through the mangle. At ten o’clock, while the guests of the hotel slept, the two launders sweated over “dressed starch” until midnight, until one, until two. At half-past two they left off.
source_text: So Martin did not scorch that, and eased down on his muscular tension, though nervous tension rose higher than ever, and he listened sympathetically to the other’s blasphemies as he toiled and suffered over the beautiful things that women wear when they do not have to do their own laundrying. “Fancy starch” was Martin’s nightmare, and it was Joe’s, too. It was “fancy starch” that robbed them of their hard-won minutes. They toiled at it all day. At seven in the evening they broke off to run the hotel linen through the mangle. At ten o’clock, while the hotel guests slept, the two laundrymen sweated on at “fancy starch” till midnight, till one, till two. At half-past two they knocked off.

text: Even as the thought flashed through his mind, he took a heavy blow on the side of the head that almost sent him to the ground. It was a man’s fist, swung by someone so furious and so eager that he had missed the jaw he was aiming for. Martin turned as he reeled and saw the fist coming back at him in a frantic arc. Instinctively he ducked, and the blow went harmlessly past, spinning the man who had thrown it. Martin came back with a left hook, driving his whole weight into the punch as it landed on the off-balance man. The fellow toppled sideways, sprang up at once, and charged wildly. Martin caught sight of his rage-twisted face and wondered what could have stirred the man’s anger so. But even as he wondered, he sent in a straight left, throwing his body behind it. The man pitched backward and collapsed in a twisted heap. Jimmy and the others from the gang were already running toward them.
source_text: Even as the thought flashed through his brain he received a heavy blow on the side of his head that nearly knocked him down. It was a man’s fist, directed by a man so angry and in such haste that the fist had missed the jaw for which it was aimed. Martin turned as he staggered, and saw the fist coming at him in a wild swing. Quite as a matter of course he ducked, and the fist flew harmlessly past, pivoting the man who had driven it. Martin hooked with his left, landing on the pivoting man with the weight of his body behind the blow. The man went to the ground sidewise, leaped to his feet, and made a mad rush. Martin saw his passion-distorted face and wondered what could be the cause of the fellow’s anger. But while he wondered, he shot in a straight left, the weight of his body behind the blow. The man went over backward and fell in a crumpled heap. Jimmy and others of the gang were running toward them.

text: “Just so, faint-hearted creatures, chirping out the tiny morals that have been chirped into them, and afraid to live at all. They will love you, Adrian, but they will love their little doctrines more. What you need is the splendid surrender of life, the great unfettered spirits, the flaming butterflies and not the small gray moths. Ah, you will tire of them too, of all the womanish things, if you are unlucky enough to keep on living. But you will not keep on living. You will not go back to your ships and the sea; so you will linger in these plague-struck cities until your bones go rotted, and then you will die.”
source_text: “Just so, pusillanimous; prattling out little moralities that have been prattled into them, and afraid to live life. They will love you, Martin, but they will love their little moralities more. What you want is the magnificent abandon of life, the great free souls, the blazing butterflies and not the little gray moths. Oh, you will grow tired of them, too, of all the female things, if you are unlucky enough to live. But you won’t live. You won’t go back to your ships and sea; therefore, you’ll hang around these pest-holes of cities until your bones are rotten, and then you’ll die.”

text: They kept fighting past exhaustion and beyond it, into an exhaustion so vast it could scarcely be imagined, until the pack of brutes, its thirst for blood at last satisfied and horrified by what it witnessed, begged them equally to stop. And Cheese-Face, ready either to collapse and die or to remain upright and die, a ghastly creature from whose features every trace of Cheese-Face had been beaten away, faltered and hung back; but Martin leaped in and struck him again and again.
source_text: They fought on, through exhaustion and beyond, to exhaustion immeasurable and inconceivable, until the crowd of brutes, its blood-lust sated, terrified by what it saw, begged them impartially to cease. And Cheese-Face, ready to drop and die, or to stay on his legs and die, a grisly monster out of whose features all likeness to Cheese-Face had been beaten, wavered and hesitated; but Martin sprang in and smashed him again and again.

text: And all the while, though keenly following the talk, Adrian had felt in it a certain vexation as well. It was all of studies and recitations, of the first steps in knowledge, and the schoolroom note of it jarred against the immense things stirring within him—against the hold life had already begun to take upon him, bending his fingers like an eagle’s claws, against the cosmic exaltations that made him smart, and against that shapeless awareness of a mastery somehow his own.
