He thought of himself as a poet wrecked upon the shore of an alien country, laden with beauty’s strange power, faltering and stammering as he tried in vain to sing in the crude, barbarous speech of the people among whom he had been cast. So it was with him. He was alive, painfully alive, to the vast universal matters, and yet he must still busy himself with schoolboy concerns and argue whether he ought to take up Latin.
source_text: And all the while, interested in the discussion, Martin had been aware of an irk in it as well. It was about studies and lessons, dealing with the rudiments of knowledge, and the schoolboyish tone of it conflicted with the big things that were stirring in him—with the grip upon life that was even then crooking his fingers like eagle’s talons, with the cosmic thrills that made him ache, and with the inchoate consciousness of mastery of it all. He likened himself to a poet, wrecked on the shores of a strange land, filled with power of beauty, stumbling and stammering and vainly trying to sing in the rough, barbaric tongue of his brethren in the new land. And so with him. He was alive, painfully alive, to the great universal things, and yet he was compelled to potter and grope among schoolboy topics and debate whether or not he should study Latin.

text: “Oh, no, no!” he cried quickly. “Nothing mean or ordinary. What I mean is that he seems to me a man who has sounded the depths of things, and has been so alarmed by what he found there that he persuades himself he never found it at all. Perhaps that is not the clearest way to put it. Let me try again. A man who has discovered the road to a hidden shrine, yet has not taken it; who may even have glimpsed the shrine itself, and afterward labored to convince himself that it was only a trick of leaves and light. Or again. A man who might have accomplished great things, but set no price upon accomplishment, and who all the while, in the most secret chamber of his heart, regrets that he has not done them; who has laughed in secret at the prizes attached to action, and yet, more secretly still, has longed for those prizes, and for the delight of acting.”
source_text: “Oh, no, no!” he hastened to cry. “Nothing paltry nor vulgar. What I mean is that he strikes me as a man who has gone to the bottom of things, and is so afraid of what he saw that he makes believe to himself that he never saw it. Perhaps that’s not the clearest way to express it. Here’s another way. A man who has found the path to the hidden temple but has not followed it; who has, perhaps, caught glimpses of the temple and striven afterward to convince himself that it was only a mirage of foliage. Yet another way. A man who could have done things but who placed no value on the doing, and who, all the time, in his innermost heart, is regretting that he has not done them; who has secretly laughed at the rewards for doing, and yet, still more secretly, has yearned for the rewards and for the joy of doing.”

text: “But to return. I have shown that no society of slaves can endure, because such a society, by its very nature, must cancel the law of development. The moment a slave society is established, decline begins. It is easy enough for you to speak of setting aside the law of development, but where is the new law of development that will preserve your strength? Put it into words. Has it already been put into words? Then speak it.”
source_text: “But to return. I have shown that no society of slaves can endure, because, in its very nature, such society must annul the law of development. No sooner can a slave society be organized than deterioration sets in. It is easy for you to talk of annulling the law of development, but where is the new law of development that will maintain your strength? Formulate it. Is it already formulated? Then state it.”

text: She rose from her chair and came toward him. For an instant she laid her hand on his shoulder, breathing fast, and then she slipped into his arms. And in his broad, easy manner, unwilling to wound her, knowing that to thrust aside this offering of herself would be to inflict upon a woman the deepest hurt possible, he put his arms about her and held her close. Yet there was no warmth in the embrace, no tenderness in the touch. She had come into his arms, and he held her—that was all. She nestled against him, and then, shifting her position, let her hands creep upward to rest about his neck. But beneath those hands his body was no fire, and he felt clumsy and ill at ease.
source_text: She came forward, out of her chair and over to him. She rested her hand on his shoulder a moment, breathing quickly, and then slipped into his arms. And in his large, easy way, desirous of not inflicting hurt, knowing that to repulse this proffer of herself was to inflict the most grievous hurt a woman could receive, he folded his arms around her and held her close. But there was no warmth in the embrace, no caress in the contact. She had come into his arms, and he held her, that was all. She nestled against him, and then, with a change of position, her hands crept up and rested upon his neck. But his flesh was not fire beneath those hands, and he felt awkward and uncomfortable.

text: But Martin cared nothing for appearances. He had quickly recognized the other man’s disciplined intellect and valued his breadth of knowledge. Besides, Professor Caldwell had no idea what Martin imagined an average English professor to be. Martin wanted him to talk shop, and though he seemed reluctant at first, he managed to draw him into it. For Martin could see no reason why a man should not talk shop.
source_text: But Martin was not concerned with appearances! He had been swift to note the other’s trained mind and to appreciate his command of knowledge. Furthermore, Professor Caldwell did not realize Martin’s concept of the average English professor. Martin wanted him to talk shop, and, though he seemed averse at first, succeeded in making him do it. For Martin did not see why a man should not talk shop.

text: Martin understood the vast distance between himself and this man—the distance the books had created—but he had no trouble crossing back over it. All his life he had belonged to the world of labor, and the fellowship of work came naturally to him. He found a way around the problem of travel, which was too much for the other man’s throbbing head. He would send his trunk to Shelly Hot Springs on Joe’s ticket. As for himself, he had his bicycle. It was seventy miles, and he could ride it on Sunday and still be ready for work Monday morning. In the meantime, he would go home and pack. There was no one to say farewell to. Ruth and all her family were spending the long summer in the Sierras, at Lake Tahoe.
source_text: Martin knew of the enormous gulf between him and this man—the gulf the books had made; but he found no difficulty in crossing back over that gulf. He had lived all his life in the working-class world, and the _camaraderie_ of labor was second nature with him. He solved the difficulty of transportation that was too much for the other’s aching head. He would send his trunk up to Shelly Hot Springs on Joe’s ticket. As for himself, there was his wheel. It was seventy miles, and he could ride it on Sunday and be ready for work Monday morning. In the meantime he would go home and pack up. There was no one to say good-by to. Ruth and her whole family were spending the long summer in the Sierras, at Lake Tahoe.

text: That was the whole of the incident, but he heard Ruth stifle a dry sob in her throat and saw her turn her face away to look out the window. When she turned back to him, she was composed, and there was no trace of the storm in her eyes.
source_text: That was all of the incident, but he heard Ruth muffle a dry sob in her throat, and noticed that she turned her face away to gaze out of the window. When she turned it back to him, it was composed, and there was no hint of the gale in her eyes.

text: “He fritters away his hours over his writing, seeking to achieve what men of genius and those rare fellows with a university training sometimes contrive. A man who has marriage in view ought to be making himself ready for it. But not he. As I have said, and I know you will agree with me, he is not to be relied upon. And why should he be? Such is the sailor’s way. He has never learned thrift or moderation. The lavish years have left their mark upon him. It is not his fault, certainly, but that does not change the grain of him. And have you considered the years of dissipation he must have led? Have you considered that, my dear? You know what marriage requires.”
source_text: “He wastes his time over his writing, trying to accomplish what geniuses and rare men with college educations sometimes accomplish. A man thinking of marriage should be preparing for marriage. But not he. As I have said, and I know you agree with me, he is irresponsible. And why should he not be? It is the way of sailors. He has never learned to be economical or temperate. The spendthrift years have marked him. It is not his fault, of course, but that does not alter his nature. And have you thought of the years of licentiousness he inevitably has lived? Have you thought of that, daughter? You know what marriage means.”

text:
Yet Julian could excuse the smile, for he had already begun to perceive that Valence was not in love with Clara. Later still, he was astonished to gather, from a score of trifling incidents, not merely that Valence cared nothing for Clara, but that he actively disliked her. Julian could make nothing of it. It was a fragment of behavior he could not fit into the larger pattern of the world. And yet he was sorry for the young man, sorry for the emptiness in him that shut him out from any true sense of Clara’s delicacy and grace.

Several Sundays they rode into the hills on their bicycles, and Julian had abundant chance to watch the armed neutrality that held between Clara and Valence. The latter attached himself to Norbert, leaving Adrian and Julian in Clara’s company, a arrangement for which Julian was properly thankful.
source_text: But Martin forgave him the sneer, for he had begun to discover that Olney was not in love with Ruth. Later, he was dumfounded to learn from various little happenings not only that Olney did not care for Ruth, but that he had a positive dislike for her. Martin could not understand this. It was a bit of phenomena that he could not correlate with all the rest of the phenomena in the universe. But nevertheless he felt sorry for the young fellow because of the great lack in his nature that prevented him from a proper appreciation of Ruth’s fineness and beauty. They rode out into the hills several Sundays on their wheels, and Martin had ample opportunity to observe the armed truce that existed between Ruth and Olney. The latter chummed with Norman, throwing Arthur and Martin into company with Ruth, for which Martin was duly grateful.

text: “That was the chief motif,” he explained quickly, “the great underlying motif, the cosmic and universal thing. I tried to make it move in step with the story itself, which after all was only superficial. I was on the right track, but I suppose I handled it badly. I did not succeed in suggesting what I was aiming at. But I’ll learn with time.”
source_text: “That was the major _motif_,” he hurriedly explained, “the big underrunning _motif_, the cosmic and universal thing. I tried to make it keep time with the story itself, which was only superficial after all. I was on the right scent, but I guess I did it badly. I did not succeed in suggesting what I was driving at. But I’ll learn in time.”

text: Mr. Ford, wearing on his expressive face an air of mild and cheerful compliance, began to reach for his pocket, then suddenly turned to Mr. Ends and said that he had left his money at home. Mr. Ends plainly resented this, and Martin saw his arm twitch as though to guard his trouser pocket. Martin knew the money was there.
source_text: Mr. Ford, with an expression on his mobile features of mediate and happy acquiescence, started to reach for his pocket, then turned suddenly to Mr. Ends, and said that he had left his money home. That Mr. Ends resented this, was patent; and Martin saw the twitch of his arm as if to protect his trousers pocket. Martin knew that the money was there.

text: “Steady now, Percy, lad,” he said, striving to cloak his uneasiness beneath a jesting manner. “This is a bit too sudden for your humble servant. Give me a moment to gather my wits. You know I had no wish to come, and I reckon your people are no more eager to set eyes on me than I was to be here.”
source_text: “Hold on, Arthur, my boy,” he said, attempting to mask his anxiety with facetious utterance. “This is too much all at once for yours truly. Give me a chance to get my nerve. You know I didn’t want to come, an’ I guess your fam’ly ain’t hankerin’ to see me neither.”

text: He cast a glance toward his companion, who was bent over the letter, and then toward the books lying on the table. At once there sprang into his eyes a wistfulness, a hunger of desire, as swiftly as such longing leaps into a famished man’s gaze at the sight of food. With an impulsive step, his shoulders swaying a little right and left, he crossed to the table and began to finger the volumes with almost tender care. He looked at the titles and the names of the writers, caught at scattered lines, and let his hands and eyes linger over the books as though they were beloved things; once he recognized a book he had already read. The others were unfamiliar books by unfamiliar authors. Presently he came upon a volume of Swinburne and fell to reading it steadily, forgetting all else, his face warming. Twice he shut the book upon his forefinger in order to stare again at the author’s name. Swinburne! He would remember that name. The man had eyes, and surely he had known color and the blaze of light. But who was Swinburne? Had he been dead for a hundred years, like most poets? Or was he still alive and writing? He turned to the title-page . . . yes, there were other books of his; well, he would go to the free library first thing in the morning and try to get hold of some more of Swinburne’s writings. Then he returned to the text and was lost in it. He did not observe that a young woman had come into the room. The first he knew of her was when he heard Julian’s voice saying:-
source_text: He glanced around at his friend reading the letter and saw the books on the table. Into his eyes leaped a wistfulness and a yearning as promptly as the yearning leaps into the eyes of a starving man at sight of food. An impulsive stride, with one lurch to right and left of the shoulders, brought him to the table, where he began affectionately handling the books. He glanced at the titles and the authors’ names, read fragments of text, caressing the volumes with his eyes and hands, and, once, recognized a book he had read. For the rest, they were strange books and strange authors. He chanced upon a volume of Swinburne and began reading steadily, forgetful of where he was, his face glowing. Twice he closed the book on his forefinger to look at the name of the author. Swinburne! he would remember that name. That fellow had eyes, and he had certainly seen color and flashing light. But who was Swinburne? Was he dead a hundred years or so, like most of the poets? Or was he alive still, and writing? He turned to the title-page . . . yes, he had written other books; well, he would go to the free library the first thing in the morning and try to get hold of some of Swinburne’s stuff. He went back to the text and lost himself. He did not notice that a young woman had entered the room. The first he knew was when he heard Arthur’s voice saying:-

text: “He is four years younger than you,” she said. “He has no place in the world. He has neither position nor salary. He is impractical. If he loves you, then, in the name of common sense, he ought to be doing something that would earn him the right to marry, instead of dallying over those stories of his and those childish dreams. I’m afraid Martin Eden will never grow up. He does not turn naturally to responsibility and to a man’s work in the world, as your father did, or as all our friends do—Mr. Butler, for example. I’m afraid Martin Eden will never be a money-maker. And the world is so arranged that money is necessary to happiness—oh, not those swollen fortunes, but enough to allow for ordinary comfort and decency. He—he has never spoken?”
source_text: “He is four years younger than you,” she said. “He has no place in the world. He has neither position nor salary. He is impractical. Loving you, he should, in the name of common sense, be doing something that would give him the right to marry, instead of paltering around with those stories of his and with childish dreams. Martin Eden, I am afraid, will never grow up. He does not take to responsibility and a man’s work in the world like your father did, or like all our friends, Mr. Butler for one. Martin Eden, I am afraid, will never be a money-earner. And this world is so ordered that money is necessary to happiness—oh, no, not these swollen fortunes, but enough of money to permit of common comfort and decency. He—he has never spoken?”

text: By the time they reached the sidewalk, he had decided there was no harm in trying it. He slipped in behind Ruth and took the outer side. Then the next difficulty came up. Should he offer her his arm? He had never offered anyone his arm in his life. The girls he had known never took a fellow’s arm. For the first few times they walked together with no more than their shoulders nearly touching, and after that it was arms about the waist, and heads resting against the fellows’ shoulders on dark streets. But this was different. She wasn’t that sort of girl. He had to do something.
source_text: It wouldn’t do any harm to try it, he decided, by the time they had reached the sidewalk; and he swung behind Ruth and took up his station on the outside. Then the other problem presented itself. Should he offer her his arm? He had never offered anybody his arm in his life. The girls he had known never took the fellows’ arms. For the first several times they walked freely, side by side, and after that it was arms around the waists, and heads against the fellows’ shoulders where the streets were unlighted. But this was different. She wasn’t that kind of a girl. He must do something.

text: It was not because of Olney, but in spite of Ruth, and his love for Ruth, that he finally resolved not to take up Latin. His money meant time. There was so much else that mattered more than Latin, so many studies calling to him with urgent voices. And he must write. He must earn money. He had had no acceptances. Forty manuscripts were making the endless circuit of the magazines. How did the others manage it? He spent long hours in the free reading-room, reading over what others had written, studying their work eagerly and critically, comparing it with his own, and wondering, wondering, about the secret trick they had found that enabled them to sell their work.
source_text: It was not because of Olney, but in spite of Ruth, and his love for Ruth, that he finally decided not to take up Latin. His money meant time. There was so much that was more important than Latin, so many studies that clamored with imperious voices. And he must write. He must earn money. He had had no acceptances. Twoscore of manuscripts were travelling the endless round of the magazines. How did the others do it? He spent long hours in the free reading-room, going over what others had written, studying their work eagerly and critically, comparing it with his own, and wondering, wondering, about the secret trick they had discovered which enabled them to sell their work.

text: “Wiki-Wiki,” which appeared in _Warren’s Monthly_, became an immediate success. It was issued at once in a handsome volume with broad margins and lavish decoration, aimed at the holiday market, and it sold with astonishing speed. Critics were unanimous in thinking that it would rank alongside those two classics by two great writers, “The Bottle Imp” and “The Magic Skin.”
source_text: “Wiki-Wiki,” published in _Warren’s Monthly_, was an instantaneous success. It was brought out forward in a wide-margined, beautifully decorated volume that struck the holiday trade and sold like wildfire. The critics were unanimous in the belief that it would take its place with those two classics by two great writers, “The Bottle Imp” and “The Magic Skin.”

text: During the opening course of dinner, while he was still struggling to settle on a manner, he remained almost unnaturally silent. He did not know that his silence was contradicting Julian’s boast of the day before, when that brother of hers had announced that he meant to bring home a savage for dinner and begged them not to be startled, since they would discover him to be an engaging savage. Felix Ward, just then, could not have believed that her brother capable of such perfidy—most of all when he had been instrumental in getting that very brother out of an ugly scrape. So he sat at the table, troubled by his own awkwardness and yet enchanted by all that surrounded him. For the first time he understood that eating was something more than a merely practical act. He scarcely knew what he ate; it was only sustenance. Here, however, he was feeding his love of beauty at a table where eating itself had become an art. It was an affair of the mind as well. His imagination was awake. He heard phrases that meant nothing to him, and other words he had encountered only in books—words no man or woman he had ever known possessed the breadth of spirit to utter. When such expressions fell so casually from the lips of the members of that extraordinary household—her household—he felt a secret rapture. The romance, the loveliness, the splendid vitality of books were becoming actual. He was in that rare, ecstatic condition in which a man watches his own dreams emerge from the hidden places of fancy and harden into reality.
source_text: It was during the first part of the dinner, struggling to decide upon his attitude, that he was very quiet. He did not know that his quietness was giving the lie to Arthur’s words of the day before, when that brother of hers had announced that he was going to bring a wild man home to dinner and for them not to be alarmed, because they would find him an interesting wild man. Martin Eden could not have found it in him, just then, to believe that her brother could be guilty of such treachery—especially when he had been the means of getting this particular brother out of an unpleasant row. So he sat at table, perturbed by his own unfitness and at the same time charmed by all that went on about him. For the first time he realized that eating was something more than a utilitarian function. He was unaware of what he ate. It was merely food. He was feasting his love of beauty at this table where eating was an aesthetic function. It was an intellectual function, too. His mind was stirred. He heard words spoken that were meaningless to him, and other words that he had seen only in books and that no man or woman he had known was of large enough mental caliber to pronounce. When he heard such words dropping carelessly from the lips of the members of this marvellous family, her family, he thrilled with delight. The romance, and beauty, and high vigor of the books were coming true. He was in that rare and blissful state wherein a man sees his dreams stalk out from the crannies of fantasy and become fact.

text: “Upon receipt of the signed agreement, we shall be glad to extend to you an advance against royalties in the amount of five thousand dollars. You perceive, I trust, that we have confidence in your work, and intend to stake our hopes on this venture in a considerable way. We should also like, at some future time, to confer with you regarding the preparation of a contract for a term of years—say ten—during which we should retain the sole right to publish, in book form, everything you may write. But let us speak of that later.”
source_text: “On receipt of signed contract we shall be pleased to make you an advance on royalties of five thousand dollars. You see, we have faith in you, and we are going in on this thing big. We should like, also, to discuss with you the drawing up of a contract for a term of years, say ten, during which we shall have the exclusive right of publishing in book-form all that you produce. But more of this anon.”

text: Another week went by, another hard-fought contest carried on each night beneath the electric lights and ending on Saturday afternoon at three, when Joe enjoyed his brief, faded victory and then slipped down to the village to lose himself. Martin’s Sunday was just as it had been before. He slept in the shade of the trees, idly wrestled with the newspaper, and spent long stretches lying on his back, doing nothing and thinking nothing. He was too stunned to think, though he knew he disliked himself. He seemed repelled by his own being, as if he had been corrupted somehow, or were inherently tainted. Everything godlike in him had been wiped away. The drive of ambition had been dulled; he had no life left to feel its sting. He was dead. His soul appeared dead. He was a beast, a laboring beast. He found no beauty in the sunlight filtering through the green leaves, and the blue dome of the sky no longer murmured to him as it once had, suggesting cosmic immensity and mysteries on the verge of being revealed. Life was unbearably dull and stupid, and it left a foul taste in his mouth. A black curtain had been drawn across the mirror of his inner sight, and imagination lay in a darkened sickroom where no shaft of light could enter. He envied Joe, down in the village, running wild, wrenching the slats from the bar, his mind swarming with maggots, gloating in a sodden way over sodden things, fantastically and magnificently drunk and oblivious of Monday morning and the week of numbing toil ahead.
source_text: Another week passed, a great battle that continued under the electric lights each night and that culminated on Saturday afternoon at three o’clock, when Joe tasted his moment of wilted triumph and then drifted down to the village to forget. Martin’s Sunday was the same as before. He slept in the shade of the trees, toiled aimlessly through the newspaper, and spent long hours lying on his back, doing nothing, thinking nothing. He was too dazed to think, though he was aware that he did not like himself. He was self-repelled, as though he had undergone some degradation or was intrinsically foul. All that was god-like in him was blotted out. The spur of ambition was blunted; he had no vitality with which to feel the prod of it. He was dead. His soul seemed dead. He was a beast, a work-beast. He saw no beauty in the sunshine sifting down through the green leaves, nor did the azure vault of the sky whisper as of old and hint of cosmic vastness and secrets trembling to disclosure. Life was intolerably dull and stupid, and its taste was bad in his mouth. A black screen was drawn across his mirror of inner vision, and fancy lay in a darkened sick-room where entered no ray of light. He envied Joe, down in the village, rampant, tearing the slats off the bar, his brain gnawing with maggots, exulting in maudlin ways over maudlin things, fantastically and gloriously drunk and forgetful of Monday morning and the week of deadening toil to come.

text: “He says so, and he ought to know,” came the reply, with a contemptuous wave of the manuscript and a look of disgust. “And he says you must tear it up. He says he’ll not have his wife with such things written about her for anybody to read. He says it’s a disgrace, and he won’t put up with it.”
source_text: “He says so, and he ought to know,” was the answer, with a wave aside of the manuscript, accompanied by a look of loathing. “And he says you’ve got to tear it up. He says he won’t have no wife of his with such things written about her which anybody can read. He says it’s a disgrace, an’ he won’t stand for it.”

text: Martin Eden returned from the sea, drawn back toward California with the longing of a lover. His money gone, he had signed on before the mast aboard the schooner that had gone treasure hunting; and after eight months in the Solomon Islands brought no treasure and only failure, the expedition fell apart. The crew was paid off in Australia, and Martin at once took a berth on a deep-sea ship bound for San Francisco. Those eight months had earned him not only enough to live ashore for many weeks, but also time for a great deal of reading and study.
source_text: Back from sea Martin Eden came, homing for California with a lover’s desire. His store of money exhausted, he had shipped before the mast on the treasure-hunting schooner; and the Solomon Islands, after eight months of failure to find treasure, had witnessed the breaking up of the expedition. The men had been paid off in Australia, and Martin had immediately shipped on a deep-water vessel for San Francisco. Not alone had those eight months earned him enough money to stay on land for many weeks, but they had enabled him to do a great deal of studying and reading.

text: He tried to rouse himself and find some interest to occupy him. He went into the petty officers’ mess, but was glad enough to leave it. He spoke with a quartermaster who was off duty, an intelligent fellow who at once assailed him with socialist propaganda and pressed a handful of leaflets and pamphlets into his hands. He listened while the man laid out the doctrine of slave morality, and, as he did, he half-heartedly thought of his own Nietzschean ideas. But what were they worth, after all? He recalled one of Nietzsche’s wild pronouncements, in which that same madman had cast doubt on truth. And who could say? Perhaps Nietzsche had been right. Perhaps there was no truth in anything, no truth even in truth—no such thing as truth at all. But his mind tired soon enough, and he was content to return to his chair and drowse.
source_text: He strove to stir himself and find something to interest him. He ventured the petty officers’ mess, and was glad to get away. He talked with a quartermaster off duty, an intelligent man who promptly prodded him with the socialist propaganda and forced into his hands a bunch of leaflets and pamphlets. He listened to the man expounding the slave-morality, and as he listened, he thought languidly of his own Nietzsche philosophy. But what was it worth, after all? He remembered one of Nietzsche’s mad utterances wherein that madman had doubted truth. And who was to say? Perhaps Nietzsche had been right. Perhaps there was no truth in anything, no truth in truth—no such thing as truth. But his mind wearied quickly, and he was content to go back to his chair and doze.

text: “I suppose I ought to. I started in the Contra Costa in Oakland when I was eleven, shaking things out for the mangle. That was eighteen years ago, and I’ve never done a thing else. But this is the hardest job I’ve ever had. There ought to be at least one more man on it. We’re working tomorrow night. We always run the mangle on Wednesday nights—collars and cuffs.”
source_text: “I ought to. Began in the Contra Costa in Oakland when I was eleven, shakin’ out for the mangle. That was eighteen years ago, an’ I’ve never done a tap of anything else. But this job is the fiercest I ever had. Ought to be one more man on it at least. We work to-morrow night. Always run the mangle Wednesday nights—collars an’ cuffs.”

text: The matter was hidden from Clara. She had never known the intimacies of the heart. Her only schooling in such things had come from books, where the plain happenings of common days were touched by fancy and transformed into a fairy country of unreality; and she had little suspicion that this blunt seaman was already stealing into her heart and gathering there hidden forces that would one day break loose and rush through her like waves of flame. She did not know love in its living fire. Her understanding of it was altogether theoretical, and she imagined it as a soft-glowing light, tender as dew falling at dawn or the faint shimmer of still water, and cool as the dark velvet of summer nights. To her, love meant rather a tranquil devotion, one that ministered gently to its beloved in an atmosphere hushed, flower-scented, and dim with ethereal peace. She never dreamed of love’s volcanic upheavals, its searing heat and the barren tracts of its parched ashes. She knew neither her own depths nor the world’s hidden powers; and the abysses of life were, to her, only seas of illusion. The wedded tenderness of her father and mother stood as her model of true affinity, and she expected one day to pass, without shock or strain, into that same quiet sweetness of life beside the man she loved.
source_text: The situation was obscured to Ruth. She had never had any experiences of the heart. Her only experiences in such matters were of the books, where the facts of ordinary day were translated by fancy into a fairy realm of unreality; and she little knew that this rough sailor was creeping into her heart and storing there pent forces that would some day burst forth and surge through her in waves of fire. She did not know the actual fire of love. Her knowledge of love was purely theoretical, and she conceived of it as lambent flame, gentle as the fall of dew or the ripple of quiet water, and cool as the velvet-dark of summer nights. Her idea of love was more that of placid affection, serving the loved one softly in an atmosphere, flower-scented and dim-lighted, of ethereal calm. She did not dream of the volcanic convulsions of love, its scorching heat and sterile wastes of parched ashes. She knew neither her own potencies, nor the potencies of the world; and the deeps of life were to her seas of illusion. The conjugal affection of her father and mother constituted her ideal of love-affinity, and she looked forward some day to emerging, without shock or friction, into that same quiet sweetness of existence with a loved one.

text: He broke off awkwardly. He was embarrassed, painfully aware of his own inability to put things into words. He had sensed the vastness and radiance of life in what he had read, but his speech failed him. He could not give expression to what he felt, and in his own mind he compared himself to a sailor on an unfamiliar ship, stumbling in the dark, feeling his way among strange and tangled ropes. Well, he decided, it was up to him to learn his bearings in this new world. He had never encountered anything he could not master when he truly set himself to it, and it was high time he learned to speak the things hidden within him so that she might understand. She loomed large on his horizon.
source_text: He broke off lamely. He was confused, painfully conscious of his inarticulateness. He had felt the bigness and glow of life in what he had read, but his speech was inadequate. He could not express what he felt, and to himself he likened himself to a sailor, in a strange ship, on a dark night, groping about in the unfamiliar running rigging. Well, he decided, it was up to him to get acquainted in this new world. He had never seen anything that he couldn’t get the hang of when he wanted to and it was about time for him to want to learn to talk the things that were inside of him so that she could understand. _She_ was bulking large on his horizon.

text: Driven by his desire to see Ruth, and finding that the long trudge from north Oakland to her house and back again wasted too much time, he left his black suit in pawn instead of his bicycle. The bicycle gave him exercise, saved him hours for work, and still allowed him to see Ruth. A pair of duck knee trousers and an old sweater made a respectable riding outfit, so that he could accompany Ruth on afternoon rides. Moreover, he no longer had much chance to see her at her own home, where Mrs. Morse was vigorously carrying on her campaign of social entertainment. The lofty people he met there, and whom he had once looked up to only a short while before, now bored him. They were no longer lofty. He was nervous and irritable from hard times, disappointment, and the close grind of work, and the talk of such people drove him nearly mad. It was not that he was needlessly vain. He judged the narrowness of their minds by the minds of the thinkers in the books he read. At Ruth’s house he never met a truly broad mind, except Professor Caldwell, and he had encountered Caldwell there only once. The others were fools, simpletons, shallow, dogmatic, and ignorant. It was their ignorance that amazed him. What was wrong with them? What had become of their educations? They had had access to the same books he had. How was it that they had gained nothing from them?
source_text: Driven to shifts in order to see Ruth, and deciding that the long walk from north Oakland to her house and back again consumed too much time, he kept his black suit in pawn in place of his bicycle. The latter gave him exercise, saved him hours of time for work, and enabled him to see Ruth just the same. A pair of knee duck trousers and an old sweater made him a presentable wheel costume, so that he could go with Ruth on afternoon rides. Besides, he no longer had opportunity to see much of her in her own home, where Mrs. Morse was thoroughly prosecuting her campaign of entertainment. The exalted beings he met there, and to whom he had looked up but a short time before, now bored him. They were no longer exalted. He was nervous and irritable, what of his hard times, disappointments, and close application to work, and the conversation of such people was maddening. He was not unduly egotistic. He measured the narrowness of their minds by the minds of the thinkers in the books he read. At Ruth’s home he never met a large mind, with the exception of Professor Caldwell, and Caldwell he had met there only once. As for the rest, they were numskulls, ninnies, superficial, dogmatic, and ignorant. It was their ignorance that astounded him. What was the matter with them? What had they done with their educations? They had had access to the same books he had. How did it happen that they had drawn nothing from them?

text: They disagreed about love, and about magazines, and about many other things, but they were fond of each other, and on Martin’s side it was nothing less than a deep fondness. Day after day they were together, if only for the hour Brissenden spent in Martin’s hot, airless room. Brissenden never came without his quart of whiskey, and when they ate together downtown, he drank Scotch and soda through the whole meal. He always paid for them both, and it was through him that Martin learned the finer points of food, tasted champagne for the first time, and became acquainted with Rhenish wines.
source_text: They disagreed about love, and the magazines, and many things, but they liked each other, and on Martin’s part it was no less than a profound liking. Day after day they were together, if for no more than the hour Brissenden spent in Martin’s stuffy room. Brissenden never arrived without his quart of whiskey, and when they dined together down-town, he drank Scotch and soda throughout the meal. He invariably paid the way for both, and it was through him that Martin learned the refinements of food, drank his first champagne, and made acquaintance with Rhenish wines.

text: Gavin rode downtown on a streetcar, and as he looked out at the houses and intersecting avenues sliding past, he felt a certain regret that he was not more exultant over his friend’s triumph and his own conspicuous success. The lone critic in the country had spoken favorably of the poem, and his own belief that good work could make its way into the magazines had been proved right. Yet exhilaration had gone out of him, and he discovered that he was more eager to see Elwood than to bring him the good tidings. The acceptance of _The Colossus_ had reminded him that through the five days he had given to “Delayed” he had neither heard from Elwood nor once thought of him. Then, for the first time, Gavin perceived the stupor he had been living in, and he was ashamed that he had forgotten his friend. But even the shame did not strike very deep. He was dulled to every feeling except the artistic passions involved in the making of “Delayed.” In all other matters he had been walking in a trance. Indeed, he still was. The whole stretch of life through which the streetcar clattered seemed distant and insubstantial, and he would have felt little interest and less alarm if the great stone spire of the church he passed had suddenly crumbled into powder over his head.
source_text: Martin rode down town on an electric car, and as he watched the houses and cross-streets slipping by he was aware of a regret that he was not more elated over his friend’s success and over his own signal victory. The one critic in the United States had pronounced favorably on the poem, while his own contention that good stuff could find its way into the magazines had proved correct. But enthusiasm had lost its spring in him, and he found that he was more anxious to see Brissenden than he was to carry the good news. The acceptance of _The Parthenon_ had recalled to him that during his five days’ devotion to “Overdue” he had not heard from Brissenden nor even thought about him. For the first time Martin realized the daze he had been in, and he felt shame for having forgotten his friend. But even the shame did not burn very sharply. He was numb to emotions of any sort save the artistic ones concerned in the writing of “Overdue.” So far as other affairs were concerned, he had been in a trance. For that matter, he was still in a trance. All this life through which the electric car whirred seemed remote and unreal, and he would have experienced little interest and less shock if the great stone steeple of the church he passed had suddenly crumbled to mortar-dust upon his head.

text: But Martin gave the blonde back to Jimmy, and the three of them, with half a dozen friends, watched the whirling couples and laughed and bantered among themselves. Everyone was pleased to see Martin again. No book of his had yet been published; in their eyes he held no fictional worth. They liked him for his own sake. He felt like a prince come home from exile, and his lonely heart opened and blossomed in the warmth that enfolded him. He made a wild day of it and was at his very best. And he had money in his pockets too, and, as in the old days when he came back from sea with his wages, he let the money go freely.
source_text: But Martin restored the blonde to Jimmy, and the three of them, with half a dozen friends, watched the revolving couples and laughed and joked with one another. Everybody was glad to see Martin back. No book of his been published; he carried no fictitious value in their eyes. They liked him for himself. He felt like a prince returned from excile, and his lonely heart burgeoned in the geniality in which it bathed. He made a mad day of it, and was at his best. Also, he had money in his pockets, and, as in the old days when he returned from sea with a pay-day, he made the money fly.

text: “Exactly that. I’d give anything to hear them together, and I’d give even a little more not to hear them once the orchestra begins. I’m afraid I’m a hopeless realist. Great singers are not great actors. To hear Barillo sing a love passage with an angel’s voice, and Tetralani answer like another angel, while it all is borne up by a perfect riot of radiant, shimmering music—that is enchanting, profoundly enchanting. I do not merely allow it; I insist upon it. But the spell is broken the moment I look at them—at Tetralani, five feet ten in her stockings and weighing a hundred and ninety pounds, and at Barillo, scarcely five feet four, with a greasy face and the chest of a squat, stunted blacksmith—and at the two of them, posturing, pressing hands to their hearts, throwing their arms skyward like lunatics in an asylum. And then I am expected to take all this as the true illusion of a love scene between a slim, beautiful princess and a handsome, romantic young prince—why, I simply cannot. It is nonsense; it is ridiculous; it is false. That is the trouble with it. It is not real. Don’t tell me anyone in this world ever made love like that. Why, if I had made love to you in such a manner, you would have boxed my ears.
source_text: “Precisely that. I’d give anything to hear them in concert, and I’d give even a bit more not to hear them when the orchestra is playing. I’m afraid I am a hopeless realist. Great singers are not great actors. To hear Barillo sing a love passage with the voice of an angel, and to hear Tetralani reply like another angel, and to hear it all accompanied by a perfect orgy of glowing and colorful music—is ravishing, most ravishing. I do not admit it. I assert it. But the whole effect is spoiled when I look at them—at Tetralani, five feet ten in her stocking feet and weighing a hundred and ninety pounds, and at Barillo, a scant five feet four, greasy-featured, with the chest of a squat, undersized blacksmith, and at the pair of them, attitudinizing, clasping their breasts, flinging their arms in the air like demented creatures in an asylum; and when I am expected to accept all this as the faithful illusion of a love-scene between a slender and beautiful princess and a handsome, romantic, young prince—why, I can’t accept it, that’s all. It’s rot; it’s absurd; it’s unreal. That’s what’s the matter with it. It’s not real. Don’t tell me that anybody in this world ever made love that way. Why, if I’d made love to you in such fashion, you’d have boxed my ears.”

text: But he was disappointed. Her criticism was fair. He admitted as much, yet he had the sense that he had not shown her his work for the sake of classroom correction. The particulars did not matter. They could look after themselves. He could fix them; he could learn to fix them. From life he had seized something large and tried to confine it within the story. It was that great thing taken from life that he had read aloud to her, not matters of sentence construction and semicolons. He wanted her to feel with him this large thing that belonged to him, that he had seen with his own eyes, wrestled with in his own mind, and set down on the page with his own hands in printed words. Well, he had failed, he told himself in secret. Perhaps the editors were right. He had felt the great thing, but he had not managed to transform it. He hid his disappointment and so readily joined her in criticizing it that she did not know that, deep inside him, a strong current of disagreement was flowing.
source_text: But he was disappointed. Her criticism was just. He acknowledged that, but he had a feeling that he was not sharing his work with her for the purpose of schoolroom correction. The details did not matter. They could take care of themselves. He could mend them, he could learn to mend them. Out of life he had captured something big and attempted to imprison it in the story. It was the big thing out of life he had read to her, not sentence-structure and semicolons. He wanted her to feel with him this big thing that was his, that he had seen with his own eyes, grappled with his own brain, and placed there on the page with his own hands in printed words. Well, he had failed, was his secret decision. Perhaps the editors were right. He had felt the big thing, but he had failed to transmute it. He concealed his disappointment, and joined so easily with her in her criticism that she did not realize that deep down in him was running a strong undercurrent of disagreement.

text: He labored on, wretched and almost without hope. It was beginning to seem to him that the second struggle was lost, and that he must turn to work at last. In doing so he would please everyone—the grocer, his sister, Clara, and even Mrs. Varga, to whom he owed a month’s rent. He was two months in arrears for his typewriter, and the agency was demanding either payment or the machine’s return. In his despair, and almost ready to yield, to make peace with fate until he could begin again, he sat for the civil service examination for the Railway Mail. To his surprise, he passed at the head of the list. The position was secured, though no one knew when the summons would come for him to enter upon his duties.
source_text: He toiled on, miserable and well-nigh hopeless. It began to appear to him that the second battle was lost and that he would have to go to work. In doing this he would satisfy everybody—the grocer, his sister, Ruth, and even Maria, to whom he owed a month’s room rent. He was two months behind with his type-writer, and the agency was clamoring for payment or for the return of the machine. In desperation, all but ready to surrender, to make a truce with fate until he could get a fresh start, he took the civil service examinations for the Railway Mail. To his surprise, he passed first. The job was assured, though when the call would come to enter upon his duties nobody knew.

text: She was wounded by his words into seeing the childishness of what she had done, and yet she felt that he had blown it out of all proportion, and so she resented him. They sat in silence for a long while, she thinking with desperate intensity, and he brooding over a love that had already slipped away from him. He knew now that he had never truly loved her. It had been an idealized Ruth he had loved, some ethereal being of his own making, the bright, radiant spirit that lived in his love poems. The real bourgeois Ruth, with all the faults of her class and the incurable crampedness of bourgeois thought in her mind, he had never loved.
source_text: She was stung by his words into realization of the puerility of her act, and yet she felt that he had magnified it unduly and was consequently resentful. They sat in silence for a long time, she thinking desperately and he pondering upon his love which had departed. He knew, now, that he had not really loved her. It was an idealized Ruth he had loved, an ethereal creature of his own creating, the bright and luminous spirit of his love-poems. The real bourgeois Ruth, with all the bourgeois failings and with the hopeless cramp of the bourgeois psychology in her mind, he had never loved.

text: The day was over, and most of the revelers had already gone. But when Martin and Lizzie came out of the trees, they found the gang waiting for them. Martin understood at once what it meant. Trouble was in the air. The gang was there to protect him. They moved out through the park gates with a second gang straggling behind—the friends Lizzie’s young man had gathered to avenge the loss of his sweetheart. Several constables and special officers, expecting trouble, followed at a distance to stop it, and they shepherded the two gangs onto the train to San Francisco, keeping them apart. Martin told Jimmy that he would get off at Sixteenth Street Station and take the electric car to Oakland. Lizzie was very quiet and seemed unconcerned with what was about to happen. The train drew into Sixteenth Street Station, and the waiting electric car was visible, its conductor ringing the gong impatiently.
source_text: The day was done, and the merrymakers had nearly all departed. But as Martin and Lizzie emerged from the trees they found the gang waiting for them. Martin knew immediately the meaning of it. Trouble was brewing. The gang was his body-guard. They passed out through the gates of the park with, straggling in the rear, a second gang, the friends that Lizzie’s young man had collected to avenge the loss of his lady. Several constables and special police officers, anticipating trouble, trailed along to prevent it, and herded the two gangs separately aboard the train for San Francisco. Martin told Jimmy that he would get off at Sixteenth Street Station and catch the electric car into Oakland. Lizzie was very quiet and without interest in what was impending. The train pulled in to Sixteenth Street Station, and the waiting electric car could be seen, the conductor of which was impatiently clanging the gong.

text: He turned at the top of the steps to meet the mailman on his afternoon round. The same recurring fever of expectation seized Martin as he took the bundle of long envelopes. One of them was not long. It was short and thin, and on the outside was printed the address of The New York Outview. He stopped as he began to tear it open. It could not be an acceptance. He had no manuscripts with that magazine. Perhaps—his heart almost ceased beating at the wild thought—perhaps they were commissioning an article from him; but an instant later he dismissed the idea as utterly impossible.
source_text: He turned at the head of the steps to meet the letter-carrier on his afternoon round. The ever recurrent fever of expectancy assailed Martin as he took the bundle of long envelopes. One was not long. It was short and thin, and outside was printed the address of _The New York Outview_. He paused in the act of tearing the envelope open. It could not be an acceptance. He had no manuscripts with that publication. Perhaps—his heart almost stood still at the—wild thought—perhaps they were ordering an article from him; but the next instant he dismissed the surmise as hopelessly impossible.

text: There was no particular stress in his repetition of Martin’s word. Yet Martin read in that ascetic face the unmistakable sign that it feared nothing at all. The eyes had grown narrow until they were eagle-like, and Martin almost drew in his breath as he observed the eagle’s beak, with its flared nostrils, bold, insistent, hostile. Magnificent, he said to himself, his blood quickening at the sight. Out loud, he repeated:-
source_text: There was no special emphasis of his repetition of Martin’s word. But Martin saw in that ascetic face the advertisement that there was nothing of which it was afraid. The eyes had narrowed till they were eagle-like, and Martin almost caught his breath as he noted the eagle beak with its dilated nostrils, defiant, assertive, aggressive. Magnificent, was what he commented to himself, his blood thrilling at the sight. Aloud, he quoted:-

text: He rose from his seat before the curtain fell on the last act, determined to see Her as she came out. There were always many men standing on the sidewalk outside, and he could draw his cap low over his eyes and hide himself behind someone’s shoulder, so that she should not recognize him. He came out of the theatre with the first rush of the crowd; but scarcely had he reached the edge of the sidewalk when the two girls appeared. He knew they were looking for him, and for a moment he could have sworn at that something in him that attracted women. Their casual drifting across the sidewalk toward the curb, as they drew nearer, told him he had been discovered. They slackened their pace and were deep in the throng as they came up beside him. One of them brushed against him and, apparently for the first time, became aware of him. She was a slender dark girl, with black, fearless eyes. But they smiled at him, and he smiled back.
source_text: He left his seat before the curtain went down on the last act, intent on seeing Her as she passed out. There were always numbers of men who stood on the sidewalk outside, and he could pull his cap down over his eyes and screen himself behind some one’s shoulder so that she should not see him. He emerged from the theatre with the first of the crowd; but scarcely had he taken his position on the edge of the sidewalk when the two girls appeared. They were looking for him, he knew; and for the moment he could have cursed that in him which drew women. Their casual edging across the sidewalk to the curb, as they drew near, apprised him of discovery. They slowed down, and were in the thick of the crowd as they came up with him. One of them brushed against him and apparently for the first time noticed him. She was a slender, dark girl, with black, defiant eyes. But they smiled at him, and he smiled back.

text: He was leafing through the magazine when he suddenly came to a halt. “Ephemera” had been given a prominent place, adorned with a splendid headpiece and Beardsley-like borders. On one side of the headpiece was Brissenden’s photograph; on the other was Sir John Value, the British Ambassador. A brief editorial introduction quoted Sir John Value as declaring that there were no poets in America, and that the appearance of “Ephemera” was The Parthenon’s reply. “There, take that, Sir John Value!” Cartwright Bruce was described as the greatest critic in America, and he was quoted as calling “Ephemera” the greatest poem ever written in America. And at last the editor’s foreword closed with these words: “We have not yet made up our minds entirely as to the merits of ‘Ephemera’; perhaps we shall never be able to do so. But we have read it often, wondering at the words and their arrangement, wondering where Mr. Brissenden got them, and how he could fasten them together.” Then the poem itself followed.
source_text: He was turning the pages of the magazine, when suddenly he stopped. “Ephemera” had been featured, with gorgeous head-piece and Beardsley-like margin decorations. On one side of the head-piece was Brissenden’s photograph, on the other side was the photograph of Sir John Value, the British Ambassador. A preliminary editorial note quoted Sir John Value as saying that there were no poets in America, and the publication of “Ephemera” was _The Parthenon’s_. “There, take that, Sir John Value!” Cartwright Bruce was described as the greatest critic in America, and he was quoted as saying that “Ephemera” was the greatest poem ever written in America. And finally, the editor’s foreword ended with: “We have not yet made up our minds entirely as to the merits of “Ephemera”; perhaps we shall never be able to do so. But we have read it often, wondering at the words and their arrangement, wondering where Mr. Brissenden got them, and how he could fasten them together.” Then followed the poem.

text: “Then let’s get at it,” said Harlan, lifting a hand to his brow for an instant. “Lord, what a whopper. I can scarcely make things out. I went over the whole business last night—every bit of it, every bit. Here’s how it stands. The pay for two is a hundred and board. I’ve been drawing sixty; the second man gets forty. But he understood the trade. You’re green yet. If I’m to break you in, I’ll be doing a good deal of your work at the start. Say you begin at thirty and work up to the forty. I’ll deal square with you. The minute you can carry your share, you’ll get the forty.”
source_text: “Then let’s get to it.” Joe held his hand to his head for a moment. “Gee, but it’s a stem-winder. Can hardly see. I went down the line last night—everything—everything. Here’s the frame-up. The wages for two is a hundred and board. I’ve ben drawin’ down sixty, the second man forty. But he knew the biz. You’re green. If I break you in, I’ll be doing plenty of your work at first. Suppose you begin at thirty, an’ work up to the forty. I’ll play fair. Just as soon as you can do your share you get the forty.”

text: “No more of the road for me, thank you kindly. Tramping’s all right, except for one thing—the women. I can’t help it, but I’m a ladies’ man. I can’t get along without them, and when a fellow’s on the road he has to do without. The times I’ve gone by houses where there were dances and parties under way, and heard the women laughing, and caught sight of their white dresses and smiling faces through the windows—Lord! I’m telling you, those minutes were plain hell. I like dancing, and picnics, and moonlight walks, and all the rest of it too much. Give me the laundry, and a decent front, with heavy silver dollars clinking in my pockets. I saw a girl already, only yesterday, and, do you know, I’m feeling as if I’d just as soon marry her as not. I’ve been whistling all day at the thought of it. She’s a beauty, with the gentlest eyes and the sweetest voice you ever heard. I’m for her, you may lay to that. Say, why don’t you get married with all this money to toss about? You could take the finest girl in the country.”
source_text: “No more road in mine, thank you kindly. Hoboin’s all right, exceptin’ for one thing—the girls. I can’t help it, but I’m a ladies’ man. I can’t get along without ’em, and you’ve got to get along without ’em when you’re hoboin’. The times I’ve passed by houses where dances an’ parties was goin’ on, an’ heard the women laugh, an’ saw their white dresses and smiling faces through the windows—Gee! I tell you them moments was plain hell. I like dancin’ an’ picnics, an’ walking in the moonlight, an’ all the rest too well. Me for the laundry, and a good front, with big iron dollars clinkin’ in my jeans. I seen a girl already, just yesterday, and, d’ye know, I’m feelin’ already I’d just as soon marry her as not. I’ve ben whistlin’ all day at the thought of it. She’s a beaut, with the kindest eyes and softest voice you ever heard. Me for her, you can stack on that. Say, why don’t you get married with all this money to burn? You could get the finest girl in the land.”

text: For the first time Ruth looked upon poverty in its sordid reality. Starving lovers had always seemed romantic to her, but she had never understood how starving lovers lived. She had never imagined it could be like this. Again and again her eyes moved from the room to Martin and back. The hot, stale odor of dirty clothes, which had come in with her from the kitchen, turned her sick. Martin, she thought, must be soaked with it if that dreadful woman washed often. Such was the contagion of degradation. When she looked at Martin, it was as though she could see the stain his surroundings had left upon him. She had never seen him unshaven before, and the three days’ growth of beard on his face revolted her. It not only gave him the same dark, murky look as the Silva house, within and without, but seemed to bring out that animal strength in him which she hated. And here he was, his madness confirmed by the two acceptances of which he had spoken with such pride to her. A little longer, and he would have given in and gone to work. Now he would go on in that terrible house, writing and starving for a few months more.
source_text: For the first time Ruth gazed upon the sordid face of poverty. Starving lovers had always seemed romantic to her,—but she had had no idea how starving lovers lived. She had never dreamed it could be like this. Ever her gaze shifted from the room to him and back again. The steamy smell of dirty clothes, which had entered with her from the kitchen, was sickening. Martin must be soaked with it, Ruth concluded, if that awful woman washed frequently. Such was the contagiousness of degradation. When she looked at Martin, she seemed to see the smirch left upon him by his surroundings. She had never seen him unshaven, and the three days’ growth of beard on his face was repulsive to her. Not alone did it give him the same dark and murky aspect of the Silva house, inside and out, but it seemed to emphasize that animal-like strength of his which she detested. And here he was, being confirmed in his madness by the two acceptances he took such pride in telling her about. A little longer and he would have surrendered and gone to work. Now he would continue on in this horrible house, writing and starving for a few more months.

text: He lifted his eyes from his work and saw Joe standing in front of him, tossing flat irons, starched shirts, and manuscripts as if they were toys. From time to time Joe would reach out and fling in a bundle of checks, which joined the whirling jumble as it swept up through the roof and vanished from sight in a great circle. Martin made a strike at him, but Joe caught the axe and sent that spinning too. Then he snatched Martin himself and added him to the whirl. Martin shot up through the roof, clutching at manuscripts, so that when he came back down he had gathered a large armful. But scarcely had he landed than he was hurled up again, and a second time and a third, and then again and again beyond counting, circling endlessly. From somewhere far away he could hear a thin childish voice singing, “Waltz me around again, Willie, around, around, around.”
source_text: He looked up from his task and saw Joe standing before him juggling flat-irons, starched shirts, and manuscripts. Now and again he reached out and added a bundle of checks to the flying miscellany that soared through the roof and out of sight in a tremendous circle. Martin struck at him, but he seized the axe and added it to the flying circle. Then he plucked Martin and added him. Martin went up through the roof, clutching at manuscripts, so that by the time he came down he had a large armful. But no sooner down than up again, and a second and a third time and countless times he flew around the circle. From far off he could hear a childish treble singing: “Waltz me around again, Willie, around, around, around.”

text: She let out a sharp cry. “Oh, Martin, don’t be cruel. You haven’t kissed me even once. You’re as unresponsive as a stone. And think of what I’ve dared to do.” She glanced around with a shiver, though part of the glance was curiosity. “Just think where I am.”
source_text: She gave a sharp cry. “Oh, Martin, don’t be cruel. You have not kissed me once. You are as unresponsive as a stone. And think what I have dared to do.” She looked about her with a shiver, though half the look was curiosity. “Just think of where I am.”

text: “Why, Mother, you’re a woman too!” she exclaimed joyfully. They had risen, and she seized her mother’s hands and stood straight before her in the twilight, aware of a strange, sweet equality between them. “I never would have thought of you like that if we hadn’t had this conversation. I had to learn that I was a woman before I could know that you were one too.”
source_text: “Why, mother, you are a woman, too!” she cried exultantly, as they stood up, catching her mother’s hands and standing erect, facing her in the twilight, conscious of a strangely sweet equality between them. “I should never have thought of you in that way if we had not had this talk. I had to learn that I was a woman to know that you were one, too.”

text: “Then, if you’ve no objection, we’ll fix it this way.” Martin glanced at Gertrude. “You may keep the principal for yourself, if you’ll spend the thirty-five dollars a month on cooking and washing and scrubbing. The seven thousand is yours if you’ll see to it that Gertrude does no more drudgery. Is it a go?”
source_text: “Then, if you’ve no objection, we’ll arrange it this way.” Martin glanced at Gertrude. “You can have the principal to keep for yourself, if you’ll use the thirty-five dollars a month for cooking and washing and scrubbing. The seven thousand is yours if you’ll guarantee that Gertrude does no more drudgery. Is it a go?”

text: The three dollars he had received for the triolets had served to keep him alive until the arrival of the _White Mouse_ check. He had cashed the first check with the sly Portuguese grocer, settling a dollar on account and apportioning the remaining two dollars between the baker and the fruiterer. Adrian was still far from wealthy enough to think of meat, and when the _White Mouse_ check at last came in, he was living on a meager allowance. He hesitated over the question of cashing it. In all his life he had never set foot in a bank, much less entered one on business, and there was in him a foolish, boyish longing to stroll into one of the great banks in Oakland and lay down his endorsed check for forty dollars. Yet common sense, cool and practical, argued that he ought to cash it with his grocer and so create an impression that would afterward bring him larger credit. With reluctance Adrian gave way to the grocer’s claims, paid the bill in full, and received back a pocketful of bright, ringing coins. He also paid the other shopkeepers in full, redeemed his suit and his bicycle, settled one month’s rent on the typewriter, and paid Lucia the month he owed for his room and another month in advance. After all this, there remained in his pocket, for emergencies, a balance of nearly three dollars.
source_text: The three dollars he received for the triolets he used to eke out a precarious existence against the arrival of the _White Mouse_ check. He cashed the first check with the suspicious Portuguese grocer, paying a dollar on account and dividing the remaining two dollars between the baker and the fruit store. Martin was not yet rich enough to afford meat, and he was on slim allowance when the _White Mouse_ check arrived. He was divided on the cashing of it. He had never been in a bank in his life, much less been in one on business, and he had a naive and childlike desire to walk into one of the big banks down in Oakland and fling down his indorsed check for forty dollars. On the other hand, practical common sense ruled that he should cash it with his grocer and thereby make an impression that would later result in an increase of credit. Reluctantly Martin yielded to the claims of the grocer, paying his bill with him in full, and receiving in change a pocketful of jingling coin. Also, he paid the other tradesmen in full, redeemed his suit and his bicycle, paid one month’s rent on the type-writer, and paid Maria the overdue month for his room and a month in advance. This left him in his pocket, for emergencies, a balance of nearly three dollars.

text: After Martin had helped her into the car, he hurried to the post office and spent three of the five dollars on stamps; and later in the day, on his way to the Morse house, when he stopped at the post office to weigh a large number of long, bulky envelopes, he put stamps on all of them except for three of the two-cent ones.
source_text: After Martin had helped her on the car, he hurried to the post-office and invested three of the five dollars in stamps; and when, later in the day, on the way to the Morse home, he stopped in at the post-office to weigh a large number of long, bulky envelopes, he affixed to them all the stamps save three of the two-cent denomination.

text: Once more Ruth judged his mind by outward appearances and by her faith in what was established. Who was he to be right and all the cultivated world wrong? His words and opinions left no mark on her. She was too securely planted in the established order to feel any sympathy for revolutionary notions. She had always known music, and she had loved opera since childhood, and everyone in her circle had loved it as well. So by what right did Martin Eden, so lately risen from ragtime and working-class songs, presume to sit in judgment on the music of the world? She was annoyed with him, and as she walked beside him she felt a dim sense of affront. At best, in her most generous mood, she took his declaration of opinion to be a whim, a random and unwarranted prank. But when he gathered her into his arms at the door and kissed her good night with the tenderness of a lover, she forgot all of it in the rush of her own love for him. And later, lying awake on her pillow, she wondered, as she had often wondered lately, how it was that she loved such an odd man, and loved him in spite of her family’s disapproval.
source_text: Again Ruth measured his thoughts by comparison of externals and in accordance with her belief in the established. Who was he that he should be right and all the cultured world wrong? His words and thoughts made no impression upon her. She was too firmly intrenched in the established to have any sympathy with revolutionary ideas. She had always been used to music, and she had enjoyed opera ever since she was a child, and all her world had enjoyed it, too. Then by what right did Martin Eden emerge, as he had so recently emerged, from his rag-time and working-class songs, and pass judgment on the world’s music? She was vexed with him, and as she walked beside him she had a vague feeling of outrage. At the best, in her most charitable frame of mind, she considered the statement of his views to be a caprice, an erratic and uncalled-for prank. But when he took her in his arms at the door and kissed her good night in tender lover-fashion, she forgot everything in the outrush of her own love to him. And later, on a sleepless pillow, she puzzled, as she had often puzzled of late, as to how it was that she loved so strange a man, and loved him despite the disapproval of her people.

text: In his own little room Martin lived, slept, studied, wrote, and managed his housekeeping. Before the single window, which looked out upon the narrow front porch, stood the kitchen table that served him as desk, library, and typewriting stand. The bed, drawn up against the back wall, took up two-thirds of the room’s entire area. On one side of the table stood a flashy bureau, made for profit rather than use, its thin veneer flaking away day by day. It sat in one corner, and in the opposite corner, on the other side of the table, was the kitchen arrangement—the oil stove perched on a dry-goods box, with dishes and cooking tools inside it, a shelf on the wall for provisions, and a bucket of water on the floor. Martin had to bring his water from the kitchen sink, since there was no tap in his room. On days when his cooking gave off much steam, the yield of veneer from the bureau was especially rich. Above the bed, raised to the ceiling by a tackle, hung his bicycle. At first he had tried to keep it in the basement; but the Silva clan, loosening the bearings and puncturing the tires, had driven him from that refuge. Afterward he tried the little front porch, until a roaring southeaster soaked the wheel through in a single night. Then he gave way and brought it into his room, where he hoisted it overhead.
source_text: In his own small room Martin lived, slept, studied, wrote, and kept house. Before the one window, looking out on the tiny front porch, was the kitchen table that served as desk, library, and type-writing stand. The bed, against the rear wall, occupied two-thirds of the total space of the room. The table was flanked on one side by a gaudy bureau, manufactured for profit and not for service, the thin veneer of which was shed day by day. This bureau stood in the corner, and in the opposite corner, on the table’s other flank, was the kitchen—the oil-stove on a dry-goods box, inside of which were dishes and cooking utensils, a shelf on the wall for provisions, and a bucket of water on the floor. Martin had to carry his water from the kitchen sink, there being no tap in his room. On days when there was much steam to his cooking, the harvest of veneer from the bureau was unusually generous. Over the bed, hoisted by a tackle to the ceiling, was his bicycle. At first he had tried to keep it in the basement; but the tribe of Silva, loosening the bearings and puncturing the tires, had driven him out. Next he attempted the tiny front porch, until a howling southeaster drenched the wheel a night-long. Then he had retreated with it to his room and slung it aloft.

text: He had never imagined that the store of human knowledge was so vast. He was afraid. How could his mind ever take it all in? Later, he remembered that there were other men, many men, who had mastered it; and he drew a deep oath, passionately, under his breath, swearing that his brain could do what theirs had done.
source_text: He had never dreamed that the fund of human knowledge bulked so big. He was frightened. How could his brain ever master it all? Later, he remembered that there were other men, many men, who had mastered it; and he breathed a great oath, passionately, under his breath, swearing that his brain could do what theirs had done.

text: As he looked at her and listened, his thoughts grew bold. He recalled all the wild rapture of her hand pressed in his at the door, and longed for that touch again. His eyes kept drifting to her lips, and he hungered for them with an almost fierce desire. Yet there was nothing coarse or earthly in it. It was pure delight to watch the movement and subtle play of those lips as she shaped the words she spoke; and yet they were no common lips such as belong to all men and women. Their substance was not mere flesh. They were lips of spirit, and his longing for them seemed wholly unlike the desire that had drawn him to other women’s mouths. He might kiss her lips, lay his own bodily lips upon them, but it would be with the grave and exalted ardor with which one might kiss the garment of God. He was unaware of this change in the measure of things that had come over him, and did not know that the light in his eyes as he looked at her was the same light that shines in every man’s eyes when love’s desire is upon him. He did not suspect how hot and manly his gaze was, or that its warm flame was working some alchemy in her spirit. Her keen virginity ennobled and disguised his own feeling, lifting his thoughts into a starry chastity, and he would have been astonished to learn that there shone from his eyes, like warm waves, something that passed into her and awakened an answering heat. She was faintly troubled by it, and more than once, though she knew not why, it broke in upon her thought with its sweet intrusion and forced her to search for the rest of ideas only half spoken. Speech was always easy to her, and such interruptions would have bewildered her had she not decided that it was because he was an unusual sort of man. She was highly susceptible to impressions, and it was not strange, after all, that the aura of a traveler from another world should move her so.
source_text: As he gazed at her and listened, his thoughts grew daring. He reviewed all the wild delight of the pressure of her hand in his at the door, and longed for it again. His gaze wandered often toward her lips, and he yearned for them hungrily. But there was nothing gross or earthly about this yearning. It gave him exquisite delight to watch every movement and play of those lips as they enunciated the words she spoke; yet they were not ordinary lips such as all men and women had. Their substance was not mere human clay. They were lips of pure spirit, and his desire for them seemed absolutely different from the desire that had led him to other women’s lips. He could kiss her lips, rest his own physical lips upon them, but it would be with the lofty and awful fervor with which one would kiss the robe of God. He was not conscious of this transvaluation of values that had taken place in him, and was unaware that the light that shone in his eyes when he looked at her was quite the same light that shines in all men’s eyes when the desire of love is upon them. He did not dream how ardent and masculine his gaze was, nor that the warm flame of it was affecting the alchemy of her spirit. Her penetrative virginity exalted and disguised his own emotions, elevating his thoughts to a star-cool chastity, and he would have been startled to learn that there was that shining out of his eyes, like warm waves, that flowed through her and kindled a kindred warmth. She was subtly perturbed by it, and more than once, though she knew not why, it disrupted her train of thought with its delicious intrusion and compelled her to grope for the remainder of ideas partly uttered. Speech was always easy with her, and these interruptions would have puzzled her had she not decided that it was because he was a remarkable type.
