She was very sensitive to impressions, and it was not strange, after all, that this aura of a traveller from another world should so affect her.

text: Summer’s undoing was close upon them. Yet it still held back, lingering on in a last dim radiance among the hills, darkening the valleys to a deeper purple, weaving a veil of haze from spent strength and overfull ecstasies, and dying with the tranquil satisfaction of one who has lived, and lived well. And there, among the hills, upon their beloved rise, Julian and Clara sat shoulder to shoulder, their heads bowed together over the same pages, he reading aloud from the love sonnets of the woman who had loved Browning as few men are ever privileged to be loved.
source_text: The erasure of summer was at hand. Yet summer lingered, fading and fainting among her hills, deepening the purple of her valleys, spinning a shroud of haze from waning powers and sated raptures, dying with the calm content of having lived and lived well. And among the hills, on their favorite knoll, Martin and Ruth sat side by side, their heads bent over the same pages, he reading aloud from the love-sonnets of the woman who had loved Browning as it is given to few men to be loved.

text: Then there was Ruth. She had liked him for himself, that was beyond question. And yet, for all her liking of him, she had valued the bourgeois scale of judgment more highly. She had been against his writing, and chiefly, it seemed to him, because it brought in no money. That had been her objection to his “Love-cycle.” She, too, had pressed him to get a job. It was true, she made it more elegant by calling it a “position,” but it amounted to the same thing, and in his own mind the old word remained. He had read her everything he wrote—poems, stories, essays—“Wiki-Wiki,” “The Shame of the Sun,” all of it. And she had always, with stubborn consistency, urged him to get a job, to go to work—good God!—as though he had not already been working, stealing from sleep, wearing out his life, simply to make himself worthy of her.
source_text: Then there was Ruth. She had liked him for himself, that was indisputable. And yet, much as she had liked him she had liked the bourgeois standard of valuation more. She had opposed his writing, and principally, it seemed to him, because it did not earn money. That had been her criticism of his “Love-cycle.” She, too, had urged him to get a job. It was true, she refined it to “position,” but it meant the same thing, and in his own mind the old nomenclature stuck. He had read her all that he wrote—poems, stories, essays—“Wiki-Wiki,” “The Shame of the Sun,” everything. And she had always and consistently urged him to get a job, to go to work—good God!—as if he hadn’t been working, robbing sleep, exhausting life, in order to be worthy of her.

text: “Please find enclosed a contract in duplicate for your next book, which we have taken the liberty of sending on to you. You will note that we have raised your royalties to twenty per cent, which is about as high as a conservative publishing house dares venture. If our offer is acceptable to you, please fill in the appropriate blank with the title of your book. We make no stipulations as to its nature. Any book on any subject. If you already have one written, so much the better. Now is the time to strike. The iron could not be hotter.”
source_text: “Please find herewith a contract in duplicate for your next book which we have taken the liberty of forwarding to you. You will please note that we have increased your royalties to twenty per cent, which is about as high as a conservative publishing house dares go. If our offer is agreeable to you, please fill in the proper blank space with the title of your book. We make no stipulations concerning its nature. Any book on any subject. If you have one already written, so much the better. Now is the time to strike. The iron could not be hotter.”

text:
He sent the manuscript off in a flat envelope, addressing it to the editor of the *San Francisco Examiner*. He had the notion that once a newspaper accepted something, it printed it at once, and since he had mailed the manuscript on Friday, he expected it to appear the following Sunday. It seemed to him a fine plan to let Ruth learn of his return in that way. Then, on Sunday afternoon, he would go and see her.

For the moment, though, his attention was taken up by another idea, one he took some pride in as especially sensible, cautious, and modest. He would write an adventure story for boys and sell it to *The Youth’s Companion*. He went to the free reading room and examined the back numbers of *The Youth’s Companion*. He found that serial stories in that weekly usually appeared in five installments of about three thousand words each. He came across a few serials that extended to seven installments, and he decided to write one of that length.
source_text: He mailed the manuscript in a flat envelope, and addressed it to the editor of the _San Francisco Examiner_. He had an idea that anything accepted by a paper was published immediately, and as he had sent the manuscript in on Friday he expected it to come out on the following Sunday. He conceived that it would be fine to let that event apprise Ruth of his return. Then, Sunday afternoon, he would call and see her. In the meantime he was occupied by another idea, which he prided himself upon as being a particularly sane, careful, and modest idea. He would write an adventure story for boys and sell it to _The Youth’s Companion_. He went to the free reading-room and looked through the files of _The Youth’s Companion_. Serial stories, he found, were usually published in that weekly in five instalments of about three thousand words each. He discovered several serials that ran to seven instalments, and decided to write one of that length.

text: A third week passed, and Martin despised himself, and despised life. The feeling of failure weighed on him heavily. The editors had reason for turning down his work. He could see that now, and could laugh bitterly at himself and the dreams he had once dreamed. Ruth sent back his “Sea Lyrics” by post. He read her letter without interest. She tried hard to say how much she admired them and how lovely they were. But she could not lie, and she could not hide the truth from herself. She knew they were failures, and he felt her disapproval in every dutiful, half-hearted line. And she was right. He was convinced of it as he read the poems again. Beauty and wonder had gone out of him, and as he read he found himself wondering what he had meant when he wrote them. His bold turns of phrase seemed grotesque, his finest expressions monstrous, and everything appeared absurd, unreal, impossible. He would have burned the “Sea Lyrics” then and there, if only he had had the strength of will to strike a match. The engine-room was there, but the effort of carrying them to the furnace was not worth the trouble. All his energy went into washing other people’s clothes. He had none left for his own affairs.
source_text: A third week went by, and Martin loathed himself, and loathed life. He was oppressed by a sense of failure. There was reason for the editors refusing his stuff. He could see that clearly now, and laugh at himself and the dreams he had dreamed. Ruth returned his “Sea Lyrics” by mail. He read her letter apathetically. She did her best to say how much she liked them and that they were beautiful. But she could not lie, and she could not disguise the truth from herself. She knew they were failures, and he read her disapproval in every perfunctory and unenthusiastic line of her letter. And she was right. He was firmly convinced of it as he read the poems over. Beauty and wonder had departed from him, and as he read the poems he caught himself puzzling as to what he had had in mind when he wrote them. His audacities of phrase struck him as grotesque, his felicities of expression were monstrosities, and everything was absurd, unreal, and impossible. He would have burned the “Sea Lyrics” on the spot, had his will been strong enough to set them aflame. There was the engine-room, but the exertion of carrying them to the furnace was not worth while. All his exertion was used in washing other persons’ clothes. He did not have any left for private affairs.

text: But while he told her about the two acceptances, about the despair he had felt when the one from the _Transcontinental_ arrived, and the matching delight with which he had received the one from the _White Mouse_, she did not truly follow him. She heard the words he spoke and understood their plain meaning, but she was not with him in his despair or his delight. She could not step outside herself. She had no interest in selling stories to magazines. What mattered to her was marriage. Yet she was not conscious of this, any more than she was conscious that her wish for Martin to accept a position was the instinctive, preparatory urge of motherhood. She would have blushed had anyone put it to her so bluntly, and then perhaps she would have been angered and insisted that her only concern was the man she loved and her wish that he should make the best of himself. So while Martin poured out his heart to her, exultant over the first success his chosen work in the world had won, she attended only to his actual words, glancing now and then around the room, startled by what she saw.
source_text: But while he told her of his two acceptances, of his despair when he received the one from the _Transcontinental_, and of the corresponding delight with which he received the one from the _White Mouse_, she did not follow him. She heard the words he uttered and understood their literal import, but she was not with him in his despair and his delight. She could not get out of herself. She was not interested in selling stories to magazines. What was important to her was matrimony. She was not aware of it, however, any more than she was aware that her desire that Martin take a position was the instinctive and preparative impulse of motherhood. She would have blushed had she been told as much in plain, set terms, and next, she might have grown indignant and asserted that her sole interest lay in the man she loved and her desire for him to make the best of himself. So, while Martin poured out his heart to her, elated with the first success his chosen work in the world had received, she paid heed to his bare words only, gazing now and again about the room, shocked by what she saw.

text: With tears standing in her eyes and her arms about his neck, Clara, as they were parting, told Adrian how dearly she loved him, and how she had always loved him. It was true that her declaration faltered plainly in the middle, but she smoothed over the break with more tears and kisses and broken murmurs, and Adrian understood it as her plea that he forgive her for the time when she had not believed in him and had pressed him to find work.
source_text: With tears in her eyes and her arms around his neck, Marian, at parting, told Martin how much she loved him and always had loved him. It was true, there was a perceptible halt midway in her assertion, which she glossed over with more tears and kisses and incoherent stammerings, and which Martin inferred to be her appeal for forgiveness for the time she had lacked faith in him and insisted on his getting a job.

text: “Of course it is all worthless, nothing but tedious and sordid drudgery; but it is no more tedious and sordid than keeping books for sixty dollars a month, adding up endless columns of meaningless figures until one drops dead. And besides, the hack work keeps me connected with literary things and gives me time to attempt larger work.”
source_text: “Of course it’s all valueless, just so much dull and sordid plodding; but it is no more dull and sordid than keeping books at sixty dollars a month, adding up endless columns of meaningless figures until one dies. And furthermore, the hack-work keeps me in touch with things literary and gives me time to try bigger things.”

text: “Yes, yes,” he cut off her attempted protest. “You would have ruined my writing and my career. Realism is essential to my nature, and the bourgeois spirit despises realism. The bourgeoisie is cowardly. It is afraid of life. And all you tried to do was make me afraid of life. You would have molded me into something formal. You would have squeezed me into a two-by-four pigeonhole of existence, where everything life values becomes unreal, false, and vulgar.” He felt her shift in protest. “Vulgarity—a robust vulgarity, I’ll admit—is the foundation of bourgeois refinement and culture. As I say, you wanted to formalize me, to remake me into one of your own class, with your class ideals, class values, and class prejudices.” He shook his head sadly. “And even now you do not understand what I am saying. My words do not mean to you what I am trying to make them mean. What I say is mere fantasy to you. Yet to me it is living reality. At best, you are only a little puzzled and amused that this raw boy, crawling up out of the mire of the abyss, should sit in judgment on your class and call it vulgar.”
source_text: “Yes, yes,” he shut off her attempted objection. “You would have destroyed my writing and my career. Realism is imperative to my nature, and the bourgeois spirit hates realism. The bourgeoisie is cowardly. It is afraid of life. And all your effort was to make me afraid of life. You would have formalized me. You would have compressed me into a two-by-four pigeonhole of life, where all life’s values are unreal, and false, and vulgar.” He felt her stir protestingly. “Vulgarity—a hearty vulgarity, I’ll admit—is the basis of bourgeois refinement and culture. As I say, you wanted to formalize me, to make me over into one of your own class, with your class-ideals, class-values, and class-prejudices.” He shook his head sadly. “And you do not understand, even now, what I am saying. My words do not mean to you what I endeavor to make them mean. What I say is so much fantasy to you. Yet to me it is vital reality. At the best you are a trifle puzzled and amused that this raw boy, crawling up out of the mire of the abyss, should pass judgment upon your class and call it vulgar.”

text: Ruth and her family were back home again, and Martin, back in Oakland, saw a great deal of her. Now that she had earned her degree, she was no longer studying; and he, having drained every ounce of vigor from mind and body, was no longer writing. This left them more time for one another than they had ever had before, and their closeness deepened quickly.
source_text: Ruth and her family were home again, and Martin, returned to Oakland, saw much of her. Having gained her degree, she was doing no more studying; and he, having worked all vitality out of his mind and body, was doing no writing. This gave them time for each other that they had never had before, and their intimacy ripened fast.

text: “And now, what do you truly know of matter, if you speak only from your own exact science? You know it merely through its manifestations, through what it seems to present to you. You are conscious only of its alterations, or of those alterations in it that produce alterations in your own consciousness. Positive science concerns itself solely with phenomena, and yet you are rash enough to aspire to be ontologists and to traffic in noumena. By the very definition of positive science, however, science has to do only with appearances. As another has observed, knowledge of the phenomenal order can never pass beyond phenomena.
source_text: “And now, what do you know of matter, according to your own positive science? You know it only by its phenomena, its appearances. You are aware only of its changes, or of such changes in it as cause changes in your consciousness. Positive science deals only with phenomena, yet you are foolish enough to strive to be ontologists and to deal with noumena. Yet, by the very definition of positive science, science is concerned only with appearances. As somebody has said, phenomenal knowledge cannot transcend phenomena.

text: Edgar’s estrangement from his family went on unabated. After Mr. Kettlewell’s gratuitous assault, Mr. Otto von Riedel revealed his true colors. The lucky disposal of a handful of short sketches, a few comic verses, and several jests brought Edgar a brief spell of affluence. Not only was he able to pay off part of his obligations, but he had enough remaining to reclaim his black suit and bicycle. The latter, because of a bent crank hanger, needed mending, and, as a gesture of cordiality toward his prospective brother-in-law, he sent it to von Riedel’s establishment.
source_text: Martin’s alienation from his family continued. Following upon Mr. Higginbotham’s unprovoked attack, Mr. Hermann von Schmidt showed his hand. The fortunate sale of several storiettes, some humorous verse, and a few jokes gave Martin a temporary splurge of prosperity. Not only did he partially pay up his bills, but he had sufficient balance left to redeem his black suit and wheel. The latter, by virtue of a twisted crank-hanger, required repairing, and, as a matter of friendliness with his future brother-in-law, he sent it to Von Schmidt’s shop.

text: “Rot, and you know it,” Olney said. “Martin is after a career, not culture. It just so happens that, in his case, culture is only a by-product of career. If he wanted to be a chemist, culture wouldn’t be needed. Martin wants to write, but he’s afraid to admit it because it would make you look bad.”
source_text: “Rot, and you know it,” was Olney’s comment. “Martin’s after career, not culture. It just happens that culture, in his case, is incidental to career. If he wanted to be a chemist, culture would be unnecessary. Martin wants to write, but he’s afraid to say so because it will put you in the wrong.”

text: The masses of cloud on the western horizon caught the sinking sun, and the whole ring of sky flushed rose, while the zenith glimmered with the same warm hue. That rosy light encompassed them, bathing them as she sang, “Good-by, Sweet Day.” She sang in a low voice, resting in the cradle of his arm, her hands clasped in his, their hearts held in one another’s hands.
source_text: The cloud-masses on the western horizon received the descending sun, and the circle of the sky turned to rose, while the zenith glowed with the same warm color. The rosy light was all about them, flooding over them, as she sang, “Good-by, Sweet Day.” She sang softly, leaning in the cradle of his arm, her hands in his, their hearts in each other’s hands.

text: Then she laughed at him, taking pleasure in his confusion, and as he looked into her open eyes and knew that she had divined nothing of what he felt, he grew ashamed. In thought he had indeed ventured too far. Of all the women he had known, there was none who would not have guessed—except her. And she had not guessed. There lay the difference. She was unlike the others. He was horrified by his own coarseness, humbled by her clear innocence, and he looked at her once more across the gulf. The bridge had fallen in.
source_text: Then she laughed at him, delighting in his confusion, and as he looked into her frank eyes and knew that she had divined nothing of what he felt, he became abashed. He had indeed in thought dared too far. Of all the women he had known there was no woman who would not have guessed—save her. And she had not guessed. There was the difference. She was different. He was appalled by his own grossness, awed by her clear innocence, and he gazed again at her across the gulf. The bridge had broken down.

text: Mrs. Leland passed Julian one afternoon in the street, smiled, and dipped her head. He answered with a smile and a lift of his hat. It meant nothing to him. A month earlier it might have repelled him, or at least stirred his curiosity and sent him wondering what went on behind her eyes at that instant. Now it provoked no second reflection. The next moment it was gone from his mind. He forgot it as he might have forgotten the granite post office or the courthouse after walking by them. And yet his mind was in unnatural motion. His thoughts wheeled and circled without rest. At the center of that circle was “work accomplished”; it gnawed at his brain like an immortal worm. He woke to it in the morning. It haunted his sleep at night. Everything in life that came to him through the senses was instantly referred to “work accomplished.” He followed the pitiless chain of logic to its end and found himself reduced to no one, to nothing. Jules Eden, the rough, and Jules Eden, the seaman, had been real, had been himself; but Julian Eden, the celebrated author, did not exist. Julian Eden, the celebrated author, was a mist rising in the public mind and by that public mind imposed upon the flesh of Jules Eden, rough and sailor. But it could not deceive him. He was not that sun-born idol to whom the crowd prayed and for whom it sacrificed its dinners. He knew better.
source_text: Mrs. Morse drove by Martin on the street one day, and smiled and nodded. He smiled back and lifted his hat. The episode did not affect him. A month before it might have disgusted him, or made him curious and set him to speculating about her state of consciousness at that moment. But now it was not provocative of a second thought. He forgot about it the next moment. He forgot about it as he would have forgotten the Central Bank Building or the City Hall after having walked past them. Yet his mind was preternaturally active. His thoughts went ever around and around in a circle. The centre of that circle was “work performed”; it ate at his brain like a deathless maggot. He awoke to it in the morning. It tormented his dreams at night. Every affair of life around him that penetrated through his senses immediately related itself to “work performed.” He drove along the path of relentless logic to the conclusion that he was nobody, nothing. Mart Eden, the hoodlum, and Mart Eden, the sailor, had been real, had been he; but Martin Eden! the famous writer, did not exist. Martin Eden, the famous writer, was a vapor that had arisen in the mob-mind and by the mob-mind had been thrust into the corporeal being of Mart Eden, the hoodlum and sailor. But it couldn’t fool him. He was not that sun-myth that the mob was worshipping and sacrificing dinners to. He knew better.

text: “No more than the average Republican,” came the reply, “or the average Democrat, for that matter. They’re all fools when they’re not cunning, and very few of them are cunning. The only sensible Republicans are the millionaires and their willing agents. They know which side their bread is buttered on, and they know the reason why.”
source_text: “No more than the average Republican,” was the retort, “or average Democrat, either. They are all stupid when they are not crafty, and very few of them are crafty. The only wise Republicans are the millionnaires and their conscious henchmen. They know which side their bread is buttered on, and they know why.”

text: His legs shook beneath him; a sickness of weakness came over him, and he lurched back to the bed and sank upon its edge. The past still held him in its grip. He gazed round the room, bewildered, frightened, unable to tell where he was, until his eyes fell upon the heap of manuscripts in the corner. Then memory lurched forward across four years, and he became conscious of the present, of the books he had unsealed and the world he had wrested from their pages, of his hopes and undertakings, and of his love for a wan shadow of a girl, delicate and guarded and unearthly, who would have died of terror could she have seen but a single instant of what he had just endured—one instant of all the mire of life through which he had splashed.
source_text: His knees were trembling under him, he felt faint, and he staggered back to the bed, sinking down and sitting on the edge of it. He was still in the clutch of the past. He looked about the room, perplexed, alarmed, wondering where he was, until he caught sight of the pile of manuscripts in the corner. Then the wheels of memory slipped ahead through four years of time, and he was aware of the present, of the books he had opened and the universe he had won from their pages, of his dreams and ambitions, and of his love for a pale wraith of a girl, sensitive and sheltered and ethereal, who would die of horror did she witness but one moment of what he had just lived through—one moment of all the muck of life through which he had waded.

text: “Wiki-Wiki,” his Hawaiian short story, was bought by _Warren’s Monthly_ for two hundred and fifty dollars. _The Northern Review_ accepted his essay, “The Cradle of Beauty,” and _Mackintosh’s Magazine_ took “The Palmist,” the poem he had written for Marian. The editors and readers had returned from their summer vacations, and manuscripts were being processed with unusual speed. Yet Martin could not understand what odd impulse had led them to accept, all at once, the very pieces they had been turning down for two years. None of his work had ever been published. Outside of Oakland, he was unknown, and in Oakland, among the few who thought they knew him, he had the reputation of a red-shirt and a socialist. There was no accounting for this sudden marketability of his work. It was pure sleight of fate.
source_text: “Wiki-Wiki,” his Hawaiian short story, was bought by _Warren’s Monthly_ for two hundred and fifty dollars. _The Northern Review_ took his essay, “The Cradle of Beauty,” and _Mackintosh’s Magazine_ took “The Palmist”—the poem he had written to Marian. The editors and readers were back from their summer vacations, and manuscripts were being handled quickly. But Martin could not puzzle out what strange whim animated them to this general acceptance of the things they had persistently rejected for two years. Nothing of his had been published. He was not known anywhere outside of Oakland, and in Oakland, with the few who thought they knew him, he was notorious as a red-shirt and a socialist. So there was no explaining this sudden acceptability of his wares. It was sheer jugglery of fate.

text: “The creed of lunacy,” came the answer. “I have told myself as much in my own hashish trances. Still, take care. These merchant cities will be the death of you. Look at that nest of turncoats where I first encountered you. To call it dry rot is to be too mild. No man can preserve his reason in such air. It is debasing. Not one among them is not debased, male and female alike, all of them animated bellies steered by the lofty intellectual and artistic impulses of oysters—”
source_text: “The philosophy of madness,” was the retort. “So have I assured myself when wandering in hasheesh dreams. But beware. These bourgeois cities will kill you. Look at that den of traitors where I met you. Dry rot is no name for it. One can’t keep his sanity in such an atmosphere. It’s degrading. There’s not one of them who is not degrading, man and woman, all of them animated stomachs guided by the high intellectual and artistic impulses of clams—”

text: “He’ll be all right after a while,” she told him, though she was wondering what kind of trouble Martin had got himself into. “But you’d better get a job first and settle down. Bernard does like to see a man at honest work. That stuff in the newspapers upset him badly. I never saw him so angry before.”
source_text: “He’ll be all right after a time,” she assured him, while she wondered what the trouble was that Martin was in. “But you’d best get a job first an’ steady down. Bernard does like to see a man at honest work. That stuff in the newspapers broke ’m all up. I never saw ’m so mad before.”

text: His greatest difficulty, however, was that he knew no editors and no writers. In fact, he knew no one at all who had ever tried to write. There was no one to instruct him, to suggest a thing, to offer even the faintest piece of advice. He began to wonder whether editors were even real people. They seemed more like parts of a mechanism. And that was what it was: a mechanism. He poured his heart into stories, articles, and poems, and committed them to the machine. He folded them exactly right, enclosed the proper stamps inside the long envelope with the manuscript, sealed it, added more stamps on the outside, and dropped it into the mailbox. It crossed the continent, and after a certain time the mailman brought the manuscript back in yet another long envelope, with the very stamps he had enclosed pasted on the outside. There had been no human editor at the far end, only a clever contrivance of gears that transferred the manuscript from one envelope to another and affixed the stamps. It was like those slot machines into which one dropped pennies and, with a clatter of metal machinery, received either a stick of chewing gum or a tablet of chocolate. Which one came out depended on which slot the penny went into. And so it was with the editorial machine. One slot produced checks, the other rejection slips. Up to now, he had discovered only the latter slot.
source_text: But his chief trouble was that he did not know any editors or writers. And not merely did he not know any writers, but he did not know anybody who had ever attempted to write. There was nobody to tell him, to hint to him, to give him the least word of advice. He began to doubt that editors were real men. They seemed cogs in a machine. That was what it was, a machine. He poured his soul into stories, articles, and poems, and intrusted them to the machine. He folded them just so, put the proper stamps inside the long envelope along with the manuscript, sealed the envelope, put more stamps outside, and dropped it into the mail-box. It travelled across the continent, and after a certain lapse of time the postman returned him the manuscript in another long envelope, on the outside of which were the stamps he had enclosed. There was no human editor at the other end, but a mere cunning arrangement of cogs that changed the manuscript from one envelope to another and stuck on the stamps. It was like the slot machines wherein one dropped pennies, and, with a metallic whirl of machinery had delivered to him a stick of chewing-gum or a tablet of chocolate. It depended upon which slot one dropped the penny in, whether he got chocolate or gum. And so with the editorial machine. One slot brought checks and the other brought rejection slips. So far he had found only the latter slot.

text: To do all this, he would have to stay in California until the sack was full of money. And already the money was starting to come in. If one of the books became a hit, it might let him sell off the whole pile of manuscripts. He could also gather the stories and poems into books, and secure the valley and the bay and the schooner. He would never write again. On that he was determined. But for the present, while he waited for the books to be published, he had to do more than drift through life dazed and stupid, in the kind of heedless stupor into which he had sunk.
source_text: To do all this he must wait in California to fill the sack with money. Already it was beginning to flow in. If one of the books made a strike, it might enable him to sell the whole heap of manuscripts. Also he could collect the stories and the poems into books, and make sure of the valley and the bay and the schooner. He would never write again. Upon that he was resolved. But in the meantime, awaiting the publication of the books, he must do something more than live dazed and stupid in the sort of uncaring trance into which he had fallen.

text: Martin shook his head, but offered no explanation. How could he? He was horrified by the vast intellectual gulf that yawned between himself and his people. He could never bridge it and make them understand his position—the Nietzschean position—toward socialism. There were not enough words in English, or in any language, to render his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest notion of right conduct, as it applied to him, was that he should get a job. That was their first word and their last. It made up the whole vocabulary of their thought. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, foolish slaves, he thought, as his sister went on talking. No wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were forever preoccupied with their own slavery. To them a job was a golden idol before which they knelt and worshipped.
source_text: Martin shook his head, but gave no explanations. How could he explain? He was appalled at the awful intellectual chasm that yawned between him and his people. He could never cross it and explain to them his position,—the Nietzschean position, in regard to socialism. There were not words enough in the English language, nor in any language, to make his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest concept of right conduct, in his case, was to get a job. That was their first word and their last. It constituted their whole lexicon of ideas. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, stupid slaves, he thought, while his sister talked. Small wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were obsessed by their own slavery. A job was to them a golden fetich before which they fell down and worshipped.

text: A fifth week went by, and then a sixth, during which he lived and labored like a machine, with only a faint remnant of something higher in him, a little glimmer of soul, that drove him, at each week’s end, to burn up the hundred and forty miles. But this was no rest. It was beyond mechanical, and it served only to stamp out the last flicker of soul that remained to him from his former life. At the close of the seventh week, without meaning to, too weak to resist, he drifted down to the village with Joe and drowned life and found life until Monday morning.
source_text: A fifth week passed, and a sixth, during which he lived and toiled as a machine, with just a spark of something more in him, just a glimmering bit of soul, that compelled him, at each week-end, to scorch off the hundred and forty miles. But this was not rest. It was super-machinelike, and it helped to crush out the glimmering bit of soul that was all that was left him from former life. At the end of the seventh week, without intending it, too weak to resist, he drifted down to the village with Joe and drowned life and found life until Monday morning.

text:
By the close of the week it seemed to him that he had lived through ages, so remote had the former life and its habits of thought become. Yet he was at a loss for want of groundwork. He tried to tackle volumes that demanded years of preparation before they could be understood. One morning he would bend over a treatise of outworn speculation, and the next over a book of the newest thought, until his mind reeled under the clash and contradiction of the doctrines. It was the same with political economy. On a single shelf at the public library he discovered Marx, Ricardo, Adam Smith, and Stuart Mill, and the intricate formulas of one gave no hint that another’s ideas had been superseded. He was puzzled, and still he was hungry to know. In a single day he had grown curious about economics, labor, and government.

Once, crossing Franklin Square, he had seen a knot of men, and at its heart a half-dozen flushed, loud-voiced figures carrying on a heated debate. He drifted in among the listeners and heard a strange, unfamiliar speech in the mouths of the people’s thinkers. One proved to be a vagabond, another a labor organizer, a third a law student, and the rest voluble wage-earners. For the first time he heard the names of socialism, anarchism, and the single tax, and learned that men were divided by contending social creeds. He caught hundreds of technical terms that were new to him, words belonging to regions of thought his scant reading had never reached. Thus he could not follow the reasoning closely, and could only infer and conjecture the meanings hidden in those odd phrases. Then there was a dark-eyed café waiter who believed in theosophy, a union baker who called himself an unbeliever, an ancient fellow who confounded them all with the curious maxim that what is is right, and another old man who went on endlessly about the cosmos and the father-atom and the mother-atom.
source_text: It seemed to him, by the end of the week, that he had lived centuries, so far behind were the old life and outlook. But he was baffled by lack of preparation. He attempted to read books that required years of preliminary specialization. One day he would read a book of antiquated philosophy, and the next day one that was ultra-modern, so that his head would be whirling with the conflict and contradiction of ideas. It was the same with the economists. On the one shelf at the library he found Karl Marx, Ricardo, Adam Smith, and Mill, and the abstruse formulas of the one gave no clew that the ideas of another were obsolete. He was bewildered, and yet he wanted to know. He had become interested, in a day, in economics, industry, and politics. Passing through the City Hall Park, he had noticed a group of men, in the centre of which were half a dozen, with flushed faces and raised voices, earnestly carrying on a discussion. He joined the listeners, and heard a new, alien tongue in the mouths of the philosophers of the people. One was a tramp, another was a labor agitator, a third was a law-school student, and the remainder was composed of wordy workingmen. For the first time he heard of socialism, anarchism, and single tax, and learned that there were warring social philosophies. He heard hundreds of technical words that were new to him, belonging to fields of thought that his meagre reading had never touched upon. Because of this he could not follow the arguments closely, and he could only guess at and surmise the ideas wrapped up in such strange expressions. Then there was a black-eyed restaurant waiter who was a theosophist, a union baker who was an agnostic, an old man who baffled all of them with the strange philosophy that _what is is right_, and another old man who discoursed interminably about the cosmos and the father-atom and the mother-atom.

text: “What I said wasn’t what I meant,” Martin confessed hesitantly, as he resolved to put himself entirely at the other man’s mercy. “I’m only a rough sort of chap, and I’ve never known anything about society. This girl is everything I’m not, and I’m nothing she is. You don’t think I’m making a fool of myself, do you?” he asked suddenly.
source_text: “What I said ain’t what I meant,” Martin confessed falteringly, while he made up his mind to throw himself wholly upon the other’s mercy. “I’m just a rough sort of a fellow, an’ I ain’t never seen anything of society. This girl is all that I ain’t, an’ I ain’t anything that she is. You don’t think I’m playin’ the fool, do you?” he demanded abruptly.

text: “I must have missed them,” he said. “What I read was the genuine article. It was all lit up and shining, and it shone right into me and lit me up inside, like the sun or a searchlight. That’s how it struck me, but I guess I’m not much up on poetry, miss.”
source_text: “I must ’a’ missed ’em,” he announced. “What I read was the real goods. It was all lighted up an’ shining, an’ it shun right into me an’ lighted me up inside, like the sun or a searchlight. That’s the way it landed on me, but I guess I ain’t up much on poetry, miss.”

text: It was not long before Adrian abandoned his custom of dining in the Japanese restaurants. The instant he gave up the struggle, fortune began to turn; yet it turned too late. He slit open a thick envelope from _The Horizon_ with no stirring of the heart, and found inside a check for three hundred dollars, the acceptance payment for “Voyage.” All the money he owed in the world, even at the pawn-broker’s with its grinding interest, came to less than a hundred dollars. And after he had settled every claim, and taken up the hundred-dollar bill from the attorney of Brierly, he still had more than a hundred in his pocket. He ordered a new suit from his tailor and took his meals in the best cafés in the city. He still slept in his small room at Teresa’s, but his fresh clothes were enough to make the children of the quarter stop shouting “tramp” and “loafer” at him from shed roofs and over the back fences.
source_text: The time was not long when Martin ceased patronizing the Japanese restaurants. At the very moment when he had abandoned the fight, the tide turned. But it had turned too late. Without a thrill he opened a thick envelope from _The Millennium_, scanned the face of a check that represented three hundred dollars, and noted that it was the payment on acceptance for “Adventure.” Every debt he owed in the world, including the pawnshop, with its usurious interest, amounted to less than a hundred dollars. And when he had paid everything, and lifted the hundred-dollar note with Brissenden’s lawyer, he still had over a hundred dollars in pocket. He ordered a suit of clothes from the tailor and ate his meals in the best cafés in town. He still slept in his little room at Maria’s, but the sight of his new clothes caused the neighborhood children to cease from calling him “hobo” and “tramp” from the roofs of woodsheds and over back fences.

text: “Exactly so,” he said. “And I count myself lucky that I was not taken in hand while I was still young. Had it been otherwise, I should be shedding sentimental tears this evening, and the absurd caperings of that admirable duo would only have served to heighten the loveliness of their singing and the loveliness of the orchestra beneath them. You are right enough. It is largely a matter of training. But I am too old for that now. I must have the thing itself, or nothing at all. An illusion that fails to persuade is a plain falsehood, and that is what grand opera becomes to me when little Marcelli goes into a passion, seizes the great Tervani in his arms—she too in a passion—and pours out to her the fervor of his adoration.”
source_text: “The very thing,” he agreed. “And I consider I am fortunate in not having been caught when I was young. If I had, I could have wept sentimental tears to-night, and the clownish antics of that precious pair would have but enhanced the beauty of their voices and the beauty of the accompanying orchestra. You are right. It’s mostly a matter of training. And I am too old, now. I must have the real or nothing. An illusion that won’t convince is a palpable lie, and that’s what grand opera is to me when little Barillo throws a fit, clutches mighty Tetralani in his arms (also in a fit), and tells her how passionately he adores her.”

text: She did not know that she desired him; but for him it was different. He knew that he loved her, and he wanted her as he had never wanted anything in all his life. He had once loved poetry for beauty’s sake; but after meeting her, the gates into the wide realm of love poetry had been flung open. She had taught him understanding more than Bulfinch or Gayley ever had. There was a line that, a week earlier, he would have passed over without a second thought—“God’s own mad lover dying on a kiss”—but now it echoed constantly in his mind. He wondered at its beauty and its truth; and as he looked at her he knew he could gladly die upon a kiss. He felt himself to be God’s own mad lover, and no knighthood could have given him greater pride. And at last he understood the meaning of life and why he had been born.
source_text: She did not know she desired him; but with him it was different. He knew that he loved her, and he desired her as he had never before desired anything in his life. He had loved poetry for beauty’s sake; but since he met her the gates to the vast field of love-poetry had been opened wide. She had given him understanding even more than Bulfinch and Gayley. There was a line that a week before he would not have favored with a second thought—“God’s own mad lover dying on a kiss”; but now it was ever insistent in his mind. He marvelled at the wonder of it and the truth; and as he gazed upon her he knew that he could die gladly upon a kiss. He felt himself God’s own mad lover, and no accolade of knighthood could have given him greater pride. And at last he knew the meaning of life and why he had been born.

text: Editors and publishers added to the daily pile of letters, the former almost on their knees begging for his manuscripts, the latter on their knees for his books—his poor, rejected manuscripts that had kept everything he owned in pawn for so many bleak months merely to pay the postage on them. There were unexpected checks for English serial rights and for advance payments on foreign translations. His English agent reported the sale of German translation rights in three of his books, and told him that Swedish editions, from which he could hope for nothing because Sweden was not a signatory to the Berne Convention, were already appearing on the market. Then came a merely formal request for his permission to issue a Russian translation, since that country, too, lay outside the Berne Convention.
source_text: Editors and publishers contributed to the daily heap of letters, the former on their knees for his manuscripts, the latter on their knees for his books—his poor disdained manuscripts that had kept all he possessed in pawn for so many dreary months in order to fund them in postage. There were unexpected checks for English serial rights and for advance payments on foreign translations. His English agent announced the sale of German translation rights in three of his books, and informed him that Swedish editions, from which he could expect nothing because Sweden was not a party to the Berne Convention, were already on the market. Then there was a nominal request for his permission for a Russian translation, that country being likewise outside the Berne Convention.

text: And she, glancing at him over her shoulder, caught in his face something of all this. It was a transformed face, its great eyes shining as they looked beyond the veil of sound and saw, behind it, the leap and throbbing of life and the vast phantoms of the spirit. She was taken aback. The rough, clumsy fellow had vanished. The ill-cut clothes, the battered hands, the sunburned face were still there; but now they seemed only the bars of a prison through which she beheld a great soul gazing out, voiceless and dumb because those feeble lips could not give it speech. Only for a flashing instant did she see it, and then the lout was there again, and she laughed at the caprice of her imagination. Yet the effect of that brief vision remained, and when the time came for him to beat his awkward retreat and go, she lent him a copy of Swinburne and another of Browning—she was reading Browning in one of her English classes. He looked so young as he stood blushing and stammering his thanks that a tide of pity, almost maternal in its impulse, rose in her. She thought no more of the lout, nor of the captive soul, nor of the man who had looked at her with such masculine force and both delighted and alarmed her. Before her she saw only a boy, shaking her hand with one so rough and hardened that it felt like a nutmeg-grater against her skin, and saying brokenly:-
source_text: And she, glancing at him across her shoulder, saw something of all this in his face. It was a transfigured face, with great shining eyes that gazed beyond the veil of sound and saw behind it the leap and pulse of life and the gigantic phantoms of the spirit. She was startled. The raw, stumbling lout was gone. The ill-fitting clothes, battered hands, and sunburned face remained; but these seemed the prison-bars through which she saw a great soul looking forth, inarticulate and dumb because of those feeble lips that would not give it speech. Only for a flashing moment did she see this, then she saw the lout returned, and she laughed at the whim of her fancy. But the impression of that fleeting glimpse lingered, and when the time came for him to beat a stumbling retreat and go, she lent him the volume of Swinburne, and another of Browning—she was studying Browning in one of her English courses. He seemed such a boy, as he stood blushing and stammering his thanks, that a wave of pity, maternal in its prompting, welled up in her. She did not remember the lout, nor the imprisoned soul, nor the man who had stared at her in all masculineness and delighted and frightened her. She saw before her only a boy, who was shaking her hand with a hand so calloused that it felt like a nutmeg-grater and rasped her skin, and who was saying jerkily:-

text: Then he came upon a cramped lane, hemmed in by weather-beaten wooden sheds. At its far end stood a low brick plant, from which rolled the measured roar of the presses turning out the first issue of the _Herald_. He was eleven then, and Freckle-Jaw was thirteen, and both of them delivered the _Herald_. That was why they were there, waiting for their bundles. And naturally, Freckle-Jaw had set on him once more, and another scuffle had begun, one that never quite came to anything, because at a quarter before four the press-room door was flung wide and the boys’ gang surged inside to fold their sheets.
source_text: Next, he saw a narrow alley, between ramshackle frame buildings. The end of the alley was blocked by a one-story brick building, out of which issued the rhythmic thunder of the presses, running off the first edition of the _Enquirer_. He was eleven, and Cheese-Face was thirteen, and they both carried the _Enquirer_. That was why they were there, waiting for their papers. And, of course, Cheese-Face had picked on him again, and there was another fight that was indeterminate, because at quarter to four the door of the press-room was thrown open and the gang of boys crowded in to fold their papers.

text: Day after day he kept at his labor, and day after day the mail carrier brought him back his manuscripts, each one refused. He had not a cent for postage, so the rejected pages piled up in a drift beneath the table. Then came a day when he had gone forty hours without food. He could not look for a meal at Clara’s, for she had gone to San Rafael for a fortnight’s stay; and, out of sheer shame, he could not bring himself to seek out his sister. As if to sharpen his bad luck, the mailman, on his afternoon round, delivered five more returned manuscripts. It was then that Adrian put on his overcoat, walked it down into Alameda, and returned without it, though with five dollars singing in his pocket. He paid a dollar apiece on account to the four merchants, and in his kitchen he fried a steak with onions, brewed coffee, and set a big pot of prunes to simmering. Then, having eaten, he went to his table-desk and, before midnight, finished an essay he called “The Majesty of Interest.” When he had typed it, he cast it beneath the table, for not a penny of the five dollars remained to buy stamps.
source_text: Day by day he worked on, and day by day the postman delivered to him rejected manuscripts. He had no money for stamps, so the manuscripts accumulated in a heap under the table. Came a day when for forty hours he had not tasted food. He could not hope for a meal at Ruth’s, for she was away to San Rafael on a two weeks’ visit; and for very shame’s sake he could not go to his sister’s. To cap misfortune, the postman, in his afternoon round, brought him five returned manuscripts. Then it was that Martin wore his overcoat down into Oakland, and came back without it, but with five dollars tinkling in his pocket. He paid a dollar each on account to the four tradesmen, and in his kitchen fried steak and onions, made coffee, and stewed a large pot of prunes. And having dined, he sat down at his table-desk and completed before midnight an essay which he entitled “The Dignity of Usury.” Having typed it out, he flung it under the table, for there had been nothing left from the five dollars with which to buy stamps.

text: Who are you, Adrian Vale? he challenged himself in the looking-glass that night when he came back to his room. He stared at his own face for a long while, with a strange, searching wonder. Who are you? What are you? Where do you belong? By every right you belong to girls like Nellie Ferris. You belong with the armies of labor, with all that is mean, and coarse, and ugly. You belong with the cattle and the drudges, in foul places among reek and corruption. There are the wilted cabbages now. Those onions are going bad. Smell them, curse you, smell them. And yet you dare to open books, to listen to noble music, to learn to love fine paintings, to speak the king’s English, to think thoughts no one of your own sort ever thinks, to wrench yourself away from the cattle and the Nellie Ferrises and to love a pale, starry woman who is a million miles beyond you and who seems to live among the constellations! Who are you? and what are you, curse you? And are you going to come through?
source_text: Who are you, Martin Eden? he demanded of himself in the looking-glass, that night when he got back to his room. He gazed at himself long and curiously. Who are you? What are you? Where do you belong? You belong by rights to girls like Lizzie Connolly. You belong with the legions of toil, with all that is low, and vulgar, and unbeautiful. You belong with the oxen and the drudges, in dirty surroundings among smells and stenches. There are the stale vegetables now. Those potatoes are rotting. Smell them, damn you, smell them. And yet you dare to open the books, to listen to beautiful music, to learn to love beautiful paintings, to speak good English, to think thoughts that none of your own kind thinks, to tear yourself away from the oxen and the Lizzie Connollys and to love a pale spirit of a woman who is a million miles beyond you and who lives in the stars! Who are you? and what are you? damn you! And are you going to make good?

text: He wondered whether there was soul in those steel-gray eyes, eyes that were often a clear blue and were made keen by the briny airs of the sunlit deep. He wondered, too, what his own eyes looked like to her. He tried to picture himself as she must see him, looking into those eyes of his, but he could not manage the trick. He could place himself easily enough within other men’s minds, but only men whose lives he knew. He did not know her life. She was mystery and wonder, and how could he guess a single thought of hers? Still, they were honest eyes, he decided, with neither littleness nor cruelty in them. The brown burn of the sun on his face astonished him. He had never imagined himself so dark. He rolled back his shirt-sleeve and compared the white inner side of his arm with his face. Yes, after all, he was a white man. But even his arms were browned by the sun. He turned his arm, lifted the biceps with his other hand, and looked beneath at the place least touched by sunlight. It was very white. At the sight of his bronzed face in the glass, and the thought that it had once been as pale as the underside of his arm, he laughed; nor did he imagine that in all the world there were few fair women whose skins could claim to be softer or whiter than his own, fairer in those parts where the sun had not yet done its work.
source_text: He wondered if there was soul in those steel-gray eyes that were often quite blue of color and that were strong with the briny airs of the sun-washed deep. He wondered, also, how his eyes looked to her. He tried to imagine himself she, gazing into those eyes of his, but failed in the jugglery. He could successfully put himself inside other men’s minds, but they had to be men whose ways of life he knew. He did not know her way of life. She was wonder and mystery, and how could he guess one thought of hers? Well, they were honest eyes, he concluded, and in them was neither smallness nor meanness. The brown sunburn of his face surprised him. He had not dreamed he was so black. He rolled up his shirt-sleeve and compared the white underside of the arm with his face. Yes, he was a white man, after all. But the arms were sunburned, too. He twisted his arm, rolled the biceps over with his other hand, and gazed underneath where he was least touched by the sun. It was very white. He laughed at his bronzed face in the glass at the thought that it was once as white as the underside of his arm; nor did he dream that in the world there were few pale spirits of women who could boast fairer or smoother skins than he—fairer than where he had escaped the ravages of the sun.

text: The last thing he had bought from the fruit store was a sack of potatoes, and for a week he lived on potatoes and nothing else, three times a day. Now and then a dinner at Ruth’s helped keep strength in his body, though the sight of so much food before him was torment enough to make him refuse a second helping when his appetite was at its fiercest. More than once, though gripped by a private shame, he went to his sister’s at mealtime and ate as much as he dared—more, indeed, than he ever dared at the Morse table.
source_text: The last purchase from the fruit store had been a sack of potatoes, and for a week he had potatoes, and nothing but potatoes, three times a day. An occasional dinner at Ruth’s helped to keep strength in his body, though he found it tantalizing enough to refuse further helping when his appetite was raging at sight of so much food spread before it. Now and again, though afflicted with secret shame, he dropped in at his sister’s at meal-time and ate as much as he dared—more than he dared at the Morse table.

text: Her face was pale and drawn. She stood just within the doorway, one hand braced against it for support, the other pressed to her side. She stretched both hands toward him imploringly, and came a step forward to meet him. As he took her hands and guided her to the Morris chair, he was struck by their chill. He pulled up another chair and sat down on its broad arm. He was too bewildered to say anything. In his own mind his affair with Nora was finished and shut away. He felt much as he would have felt if the Silver Creek Laundry had suddenly descended upon the Grand Imperial Hotel with a week’s worth of washing for him to deal with at once. More than once he was on the point of speaking, and each time he held back.
source_text: Her face was white and strained. She stood just inside the door, one hand against it for support, the other pressed to her side. She extended both hands toward him piteously, and started forward to meet him. As he caught her hands and led her to the Morris chair he noticed how cold they were. He drew up another chair and sat down on the broad arm of it. He was too confused to speak. In his own mind his affair with Ruth was closed and sealed. He felt much in the same way that he would have felt had the Shelly Hot Springs Laundry suddenly invaded the Hotel Metropole with a whole week’s washing ready for him to pitch into. Several times he was about to speak, and each time he hesitated.

text: “Now I’m coming to what I mean. I’d never been in a house like this before. When I came a week ago and saw all this, and you, and your mother, and your brothers, and everything else—well, I took to it. I’d heard about places like this and read about them in books, and when I looked around your house, the books seemed to come alive. But what I’m after is that I liked it. I wanted it. I want it still. I want to breathe the kind of air you breathe in this house—air full of books and pictures and beautiful things, where people speak softly and are clean, and their thoughts are clean too. The air I’ve always breathed was mixed up with food and rent and fighting and drinking, and that’s all they talked about as well. Why, when you crossed the room to kiss your mother, I thought it was the loveliest thing I’d ever seen. I’ve seen a great deal of life, and somehow I’ve seen more of it than most of the people who were with me. I like to see, and I want to see more, and I want to see it differently.
source_text: “Now, to come to what I’m drivin’ at. I was never inside a house like this. When I come a week ago, an’ saw all this, an’ you, an’ your mother, an’ brothers, an’ everything—well, I liked it. I’d heard about such things an’ read about such things in some of the books, an’ when I looked around at your house, why, the books come true. But the thing I’m after is I liked it. I wanted it. I want it now. I want to breathe air like you get in this house—air that is filled with books, and pictures, and beautiful things, where people talk in low voices an’ are clean, an’ their thoughts are clean. The air I always breathed was mixed up with grub an’ house-rent an’ scrappin’ an booze an’ that’s all they talked about, too. Why, when you was crossin’ the room to kiss your mother, I thought it was the most beautiful thing I ever seen. I’ve seen a whole lot of life, an’ somehow I’ve seen a whole lot more of it than most of them that was with me. I like to see, an’ I want to see more, an’ I want to see it different.

text: He was still repeating it, again and again—insisting, pleading, threatening, demanding to know whether it wanted any more—when he felt the members of his gang seize him, slap him on the back, and try to get his coat onto him. Then a sudden surge of blackness swept over him, and he slipped into oblivion.
source_text: He was still saying it, over and over,—demanding, entreating, threatening, to know if it wanted any more,—when he felt the fellows of his gang laying hands on him, patting him on the back and trying to put his coat on him. And then came a sudden rush of blackness and oblivion.

text: “And precisely for that reason,” he said softly, “I’m not going to. You are so broad and magnanimous that you put me to shame and make me try to rise to your generosity. I’m not going to marry, and I’m not going to—well, love without marriage, though I’ve had my own part of that sort of thing in the past. I’m sorry I came here today and found you. But it can’t be helped now, and I never imagined it would end like this.
source_text: “And it’s just because of that that I’m not going to,” he said gently. “You are so big and generous that you challenge me to equal generousness. I’m not marrying, and I’m not—well, loving without marrying, though I’ve done my share of that in the past. I’m sorry I came here to-day and met you. But it can’t be helped now, and I never expected it would turn out this way.

text: “But it isn’t necessary for us to have one. We benefit from the work of specialists. That is what they are there for. When I came in, I saw the chimney sweeps hard at work. They are specialists, and once they have finished, you will have clean chimneys without needing to know anything about how chimneys are built.”
source_text: “But it is unnecessary to have it. We profit from the work of the specialists. That’s what they are for. When I came in, I noticed the chimney-sweeps at work. They’re specialists, and when they get done, you will enjoy clean chimneys without knowing anything about the construction of chimneys.”

text: “I can never make out why you, of all people, are a socialist,” Martin said reflectively. “You dislike the crowd so much. Surely there is nothing in the rabble to commend it to your aesthetic soul.” He leveled an accusing finger at the whiskey glass the other man was filling again. “Socialism doesn’t seem to be doing much to reform you.”
source_text: “I never can puzzle out why you, of all men, are a socialist,” Martin pondered. “You detest the crowd so. Surely there is nothing in the canaille to recommend it to your aesthetic soul.” He pointed an accusing finger at the whiskey glass which the other was refilling. “Socialism doesn’t seem to save you.”

text: It was then, at the very bottom of the trough, that the smooth-working editorial machine finally failed. Some gear must have slipped, or an oil cup must have gone dry, for one morning the postman handed him a short, narrow envelope. Martin looked at the upper left corner and saw the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart leaped violently, and at once he felt faint, that dropping sensation accompanied by an odd trembling in his knees. He made his way unsteadily to his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that instant he understood how people can suddenly die on receiving news that is extraordinarily good.
source_text: It was at this time, at the lowest ebb, that the smooth-running editorial machine broke down. A cog must have slipped or an oil-cup run dry, for the postman brought him one morning a short, thin envelope. Martin glanced at the upper left-hand corner and read the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart gave a great leap, and he suddenly felt faint, the sinking feeling accompanied by a strange trembling of the knees. He staggered into his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that moment came understanding to him how people suddenly fall dead upon receipt of extraordinarily good news.

text: But Martin did not cry out. The idea kept gnawing at his mind, a constant torment, while he kept smiling and managed, somehow, to stay polite. When he fell silent, Bernard Higginbotham took over the reins and the conversation. He was a success himself, and he knew it. He had made himself. No one had given him a hand. He owed nothing to anybody. He was doing his duty as a citizen and raising a large family. And then there was Higginbotham’s Cash Store, that proof of his own hard work and ability. He loved Higginbotham’s Cash Store the way some men loved their wives. He unburdened himself to Martin, showing with what care and what vast foresight he had built up the store. And he had plans for it, big plans. The neighborhood was growing quickly. The store was really too cramped. If only he had more space, he could install a dozen labor-saving and money-saving improvements. And he would do it yet. He was putting all his energy into the day when he could buy the lot next door and put up another two-story frame building. He could rent the upper floor, and the entire ground floor of both buildings would become Higginbotham’s Cash Store. His eyes shone as he talked about the new sign that would run all the way across both buildings.
source_text: But Martin did not shout out. The thought gnawed in his brain, an unceasing torment, while he smiled and succeeded in being tolerant. As he grew silent, Bernard Higginbotham got the reins and did the talking. He was a success himself, and proud of it. He was self-made. No one had helped him. He owed no man. He was fulfilling his duty as a citizen and bringing up a large family. And there was Higginbotham’s Cash Store, that monument of his own industry and ability. He loved Higginbotham’s Cash Store as some men loved their wives. He opened up his heart to Martin, showed with what keenness and with what enormous planning he had made the store. And he had plans for it, ambitious plans. The neighborhood was growing up fast. The store was really too small. If he had more room, he would be able to put in a score of labor-saving and money-saving improvements. And he would do it yet. He was straining every effort for the day when he could buy the adjoining lot and put up another two-story frame building. The upstairs he could rent, and the whole ground-floor of both buildings would be Higginbotham’s Cash Store. His eyes glistened when he spoke of the new sign that would stretch clear across both buildings.

text: Back in his rooms, he sank into a Morris chair and sat gazing straight ahead. He did not fall asleep, nor did he think. His mind was empty, except for the moments when unbidden images gathered shape and color and brightness beneath his eyelids. He saw them, yet was hardly aware of them—no more than if they had come in dreams. Still, he was not sleeping. Once he stirred and looked at his watch. It was only eight o’clock. He had nothing to occupy him, and it was far too soon to go to bed. Then his mind emptied again, and the images began to bloom and fade beneath his lids. There was nothing particular in them. They were always thickets of leaves and bushy branches pierced by a fierce, warm sunlight.
source_text: Once in his rooms, he dropped into a Morris chair and sat staring straight before him. He did not doze. Nor did he think. His mind was a blank, save for the intervals when unsummoned memory pictures took form and color and radiance just under his eyelids. He saw these pictures, but he was scarcely conscious of them—no more so than if they had been dreams. Yet he was not asleep. Once, he roused himself and glanced at his watch. It was just eight o’clock. He had nothing to do, and it was too early for bed. Then his mind went blank again, and the pictures began to form and vanish under his eyelids. There was nothing distinctive about the pictures. They were always masses of leaves and shrub-like branches shot through with hot sunshine.

text: “Give me a little time, dearest,” he begged. “This drudgery is only a stopgap, and I don’t mean to make much of it. Give me two years. By then I shall have made my mark, and the editors will be eager to take what I write. I know what I’m saying; I have confidence in myself. I know what is in me; I know, now, what literature really is; I know the common rubbish turned out by all sorts of small men; and I know that in two years’ time I shall be well on the road to success. As for business, I shall never do anything with it. I have no feeling for it. It seems to me flat, foolish, grasping, and underhand. At any rate, I am not made for it. I should never rise beyond a desk job, and how could you and I be happy on a clerk’s miserable wages? I want the finest things in the world for you, and the only time I shall not want them is when something finer still exists. And I mean to get them—every one. The earnings of a successful writer put Mr. Harlan to shame. A best-selling book brings in from fifty to a hundred thousand dollars—sometimes more, sometimes less; but, as a rule, pretty nearly that.”
source_text: “Give me time, dear,” he pleaded. “The hack-work is only makeshift, and I don’t take it seriously. Give me two years. I shall succeed in that time, and the editors will be glad to buy my good work. I know what I am saying; I have faith in myself. I know what I have in me; I know what literature is, now; I know the average rot that is poured out by a lot of little men; and I know that at the end of two years I shall be on the highroad to success. As for business, I shall never succeed at it. I am not in sympathy with it. It strikes me as dull, and stupid, and mercenary, and tricky. Anyway I am not adapted for it. I’d never get beyond a clerkship, and how could you and I be happy on the paltry earnings of a clerk? I want the best of everything in the world for you, and the only time when I won’t want it will be when there is something better. And I’m going to get it, going to get all of it. The income of a successful author makes Mr. Butler look cheap. A ‘best-seller’ will earn anywhere between fifty and a hundred thousand dollars—sometimes more and sometimes less; but, as a rule, pretty close to those figures.”

text: It was second nature to him; under like conditions of first encounters, he had said the same thing many times before. And in truth he could have done nothing else. There was in him such breadth of tolerance and such warmth of fellow-feeling that he was scarcely capable of doing otherwise. The dark-eyed girl smiled with pleasure and recognition, and made as though to pause, while her friend, their arms linked together, broke into a giggle and likewise seemed about to stop. He thought swiftly. It would never do for Her to come out and find him standing there in talk with them. So, as if it were the most natural thing in the world, he slipped in beside the girl with the dark eyes and walked on with her. He felt no embarrassment, no paralysis of speech. He was entirely at ease here, and he held his own magnificently in the banter—full of slang and quick thrusts—that always came first in these rapid acquaintances. When they reached the corner where the chief current of people swept onward, he began to edge into the side street. But the girl with the dark eyes seized his arm, came after him, dragging her companion along, and called out:
source_text: It was automatic; he had said it so often before under similar circumstances of first meetings. Besides, he could do no less. There was that large tolerance and sympathy in his nature that would permit him to do no less. The black-eyed girl smiled gratification and greeting, and showed signs of stopping, while her companion, arm linked in arm, giggled and likewise showed signs of halting. He thought quickly. It would never do for Her to come out and see him talking there with them. Quite naturally, as a matter of course, he swung in along-side the dark-eyed one and walked with her. There was no awkwardness on his part, no numb tongue. He was at home here, and he held his own royally in the badinage, bristling with slang and sharpness, that was always the preliminary to getting acquainted in these swift-moving affairs. At the corner where the main stream of people flowed onward, he started to edge out into the cross street. But the girl with the black eyes caught his arm, following him and dragging her companion after her, as she cried:

text: “Undoubtedly you are both excellent doctors,” he said, “but if you have any regard for the patient’s opinion, allow him to tell you that you are poor diagnosticians. In truth, both of you are afflicted with the very disease you believe you have discovered in me. As for myself, I am beyond it. The socialist philosophy that seethes half-formed in your blood has left me untouched.”
source_text: “Undoubtedly you are both excellent doctors,” he said; “but if you care a whit for the opinion of the patient, let him tell you that you are poor diagnosticians. In fact, you are both suffering from the disease you think you find in me. As for me, I am immune. The socialist philosophy that riots half-baked in your veins has passed me by.”

text: “We would not seek to sway your decision in any manner; but you are our daughter, and we could not endure to see you make such a marriage. He has nothing to offer you but roughness and coarseness, in exchange for all that is refined and delicate in yourself. In every respect he is beneath you. He could never support you. We have no extravagant notions about riches, but comfort is another thing altogether, and our daughter ought at least to marry a man who can provide her with that—not a penniless adventurer, a sailor, a cowboy, a smuggler, and Heaven knows what else, who, to crown it all, is flighty and irresponsible.”
source_text: “We would not influence your choice in any way; but you are our daughter, and we could not bear to see you make a marriage such as this. He has nothing but roughness and coarseness to offer you in exchange for all that is refined and delicate in you. He is no match for you in any way. He could not support you. We have no foolish ideas about wealth, but comfort is another matter, and our daughter should at least marry a man who can give her that—and not a penniless adventurer, a sailor, a cowboy, a smuggler, and Heaven knows what else, who, in addition to everything, is hare-brained and irresponsible.”

text: Edgar collected a sheaf of carbon copies of his short tales, paused for a breath, and then slipped in his “Tide Songs.” In the waning light of a June evening they took to their bicycles and rode away toward the hills. It was the second time he had been alone with her, and as they moved on through the soft warmth, tempered now by the sea air into a pleasant coolness, he was deeply moved by the sense that the world was a fair and orderly place, and that to live and to love were good. They left their bicycles by the roadside and climbed to the brown crest of an open knoll, where the sun-parched grass seemed to exhale a dry, ripe sweetness and a kind of quiet content.
source_text: Martin gathered together a number of carbon copies of his short stories, hesitated a moment, then added his “Sea Lyrics.” They mounted their wheels on a late June afternoon and rode for the hills. It was the second time he had been out with her alone, and as they rode along through the balmy warmth, just chilled by she sea-breeze to refreshing coolness, he was profoundly impressed by the fact that it was a very beautiful and well-ordered world and that it was good to be alive and to love. They left their wheels by the roadside and climbed to the brown top of an open knoll where the sunburnt grass breathed a harvest breath of dry sweetness and content.

text: Elias was steadily losing ground in his struggle. Economize as he might, the money from his hackwork would not meet his expenses. By Thanksgiving his black suit had gone to pawn, and he was forced to decline the Garlands’ invitation to dinner. Clara was by no means pleased with the explanation he gave for staying away, and the effect of her disappointment upon him was one of utter desperation. He told her then that he would come after all; that he would go across to San Francisco, to the _Transcontinental_ office, draw the five dollars owing to him, and with that sum redeem his suit of clothes.
source_text: Martin was steadily losing his battle. Economize as he would, the earnings from hack-work did not balance expenses. Thanksgiving found him with his black suit in pawn and unable to accept the Morses’ invitation to dinner. Ruth was not made happy by his reason for not coming, and the corresponding effect on him was one of desperation. He told her that he would come, after all; that he would go over to San Francisco, to the _Transcontinental_ office, collect the five dollars due him, and with it redeem his suit of clothes.

text: What stirred Adrian most deeply, in a way he could scarcely have foretold, was the relatedness of knowledge—of all knowledge together. He had always been eager to learn, and whatever he learned he set aside in his mind in tidy, separate chambers. On the matter of ships and the sea he possessed a vast hoard. On the matter of women he had a respectable store. Yet these two provinces had lain apart from one another. Between those inner compartments there had been no bridge. It would once have seemed absurd, impossible, that there could be any real link between a woman in hysteria and a schooner with a weather helm, or a vessel lying-to in a gale. But Emersonian philosophy had taught him not merely that such a link was not absurd, but that no thing could exist without relation to every other. Everything was bound to everything else, from the remotest star in the wilderness of space to the countless atoms in the grain of sand beneath a man’s shoe. This revelation filled Adrian with continual wonder, and he found himself forever seeking out the hidden correspondences between all things beneath the sun and beyond it. He made lists of the most mismatched objects and states, and was dissatisfied until he had discovered a blood-relationship among them all—among love, verse, earthquakes, conflagrations, rattlesnakes, rainbows, jewels, deformity, sunsets, the bellowing of lions, gaslight, cannibal feasts, loveliness, murder, lovers, levers, and tobacco.
