In this way he brought the universe into unity and lifted it before his gaze, or moved through its lanes and recesses and savage reaches not as a frightened wanderer lost in mystery and seeking some unseen destination, but as one who observed, measured, and grew intimate with all that could be known. And the more he learned, the more ardently he admired the universe, and life, and his own life moving within the midst of both.
source_text: What, in a way, most profoundly impressed Martin, was the correlation of knowledge—of all knowledge. He had been curious to know things, and whatever he acquired he had filed away in separate memory compartments in his brain. Thus, on the subject of sailing he had an immense store. On the subject of woman he had a fairly large store. But these two subjects had been unrelated. Between the two memory compartments there had been no connection. That, in the fabric of knowledge, there should be any connection whatever between a woman with hysterics and a schooner carrying a weather-helm or heaving to in a gale, would have struck him as ridiculous and impossible. But Herbert Spencer had shown him not only that it was not ridiculous, but that it was impossible for there to be no connection. All things were related to all other things from the farthermost star in the wastes of space to the myriads of atoms in the grain of sand under one’s foot. This new concept was a perpetual amazement to Martin, and he found himself engaged continually in tracing the relationship between all things under the sun and on the other side of the sun. He drew up lists of the most incongruous things and was unhappy until he succeeded in establishing kinship between them all—kinship between love, poetry, earthquake, fire, rattlesnakes, rainbows, precious gems, monstrosities, sunsets, the roaring of lions, illuminating gas, cannibalism, beauty, murder, lovers, fulcrums, and tobacco. Thus, he unified the universe and held it up and looked at it, or wandered through its byways and alleys and jungles, not as a terrified traveller in the thick of mysteries seeking an unknown goal, but observing and charting and becoming familiar with all there was to know. And the more he knew, the more passionately he admired the universe, and life, and his own life in the midst of it all.

text: “Oh, but it’s mental training. It’s discipline for the mind. It’s what produces disciplined minds.” Ruth looked at Martin hopefully, as though she expected him to revise his opinion. “You know, football players have to train before the big game. That’s what Latin does for the thinker. It trains.”
source_text: “Oh, but it’s mental training. It’s mind discipline. It’s what makes disciplined minds.” Ruth looked expectantly at Martin, as if waiting for him to change his judgment. “You know, the foot-ball players have to train before the big game. And that is what Latin does for the thinker. It trains.”

text: And Julian knew that Clara’s diagnosis had been right. In body, he was sound enough. It was his thinking-gear that had broken down, and there was no remedy for that save escape to some island in the South Pacific. The difficulty was that now, with departure almost upon him, he had not the least wish to go. The South Pacific no longer delighted him any more than respectable society did. There was no exhilaration in the prospect of leaving, while the very act of leaving filled him with a bodily fatigue and loathing. He would have felt easier in himself if he were already aboard and beyond it.
source_text: And Martin knew that Lizzie’s diagnosis had been correct. Physically he was all right. It was his “think-machine” that had gone wrong, and there was no cure for that except to get away to the South Seas. The trouble was that now, on the verge of departure, he had no desire to go. The South Seas charmed him no more than did bourgeois civilization. There was no zest in the thought of departure, while the act of departure appalled him as a weariness of the flesh. He would have felt better if he were already on board and gone.

text: Maria could endure it no longer, and went quickly to the stove, where she filled a quart bowl with soup, taking for it the lion’s share of the chopped meat and vegetables her ladle scraped up from the bottom of the pot. Martin stirred himself, sat up, and began to eat, reassuring Maria between spoonfuls that he had not been talking in his sleep and that he had no fever.
source_text: Maria could stand it no longer, and hurried away to the stove, where she filled a quart-bowl with soup, putting into it the lion’s share of chopped meat and vegetables which her ladle scraped from the bottom of the pot. Martin roused himself and sat up and began to eat, between spoonfuls reassuring Maria that he had not been talking in his sleep and that he did not have any fever.

text: “You fool!” he exclaimed at his reflection in the mirror. “You wanted to write, and you tried to write, and there was nothing in you to write about. What did you have in you?—a few childish ideas, some half-formed feelings, a heap of beauty you had never digested, a great dark bulk of ignorance, a heart bursting with love, and an ambition as vast as your love and as useless as your ignorance. And you wanted to write! Why, you are only just reaching the point where you are beginning to have something to write about. You wanted to make beauty, but how could you, when you knew nothing of what beauty is? You wanted to write about life when you knew nothing of life’s fundamental nature. You wanted to write about the world and the order of existence when the world was a Chinese puzzle to you, and all you could ever have written was what you did not know about that order of existence. But cheer up, Martin, my boy. You will write yet. You know a little, only a very little, and now you are on the right road to knowing more. Some day, if luck is with you, you may come very near to knowing all that can be known. Then you will write.”
source_text: “You fool!” he cried at his image in the looking-glass. “You wanted to write, and you tried to write, and you had nothing in you to write about. What did you have in you?—some childish notions, a few half-baked sentiments, a lot of undigested beauty, a great black mass of ignorance, a heart filled to bursting with love, and an ambition as big as your love and as futile as your ignorance. And you wanted to write! Why, you’re just on the edge of beginning to get something in you to write about. You wanted to create beauty, but how could you when you knew nothing about the nature of beauty? You wanted to write about life when you knew nothing of the essential characteristics of life. You wanted to write about the world and the scheme of existence when the world was a Chinese puzzle to you and all that you could have written would have been about what you did not know of the scheme of existence. But cheer up, Martin, my boy. You’ll write yet. You know a little, a very little, and you’re on the right road now to know more. Some day, if you’re lucky, you may come pretty close to knowing all that may be known. Then you will write.”

text: “We’re fortunate that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come along and meet them. I hear Stevens isn’t around. I’m going to see if I can get them started on monism. Just wait until they’ve had a few jolts and you’ll see them begin to thaw.”
source_text: “We’re lucky that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come on and meet them. Stevens isn’t around, I hear. I’m going to get them started on monism if I can. Wait till they get a few jolts in them and they’ll warm up.”

text: After his bath, Julian discovered that the chief launderman had vanished. Most likely he had strolled down for a glass of ale, Julian thought, but the half-mile trudge to the village to make certain seemed a journey of formidable length. He stretched out on his bed with his shoes off, trying to settle the matter. He did not reach for a book. He was too spent to be sleepy, and he lay with scarcely a thought in a half-conscious stupor of fatigue until supper time came. Owen did not show up for that meal, and when Julian overheard the gardener say that he was probably wrenching the slats from the tavern, Julian understood at once. He went to bed straight afterward, and by morning felt himself immensely refreshed. With Owen still away, Julian bought a Sunday newspaper and lay down in a cool patch of shade beneath the trees. How the morning slipped by he could not have said. He neither slept nor was disturbed by anyone, and he did not get to the end of the paper. He returned to it after dinner in the afternoon and dozed off with it in his hands.
source_text: Martin took a bath, after which he found that the head laundryman had disappeared. Most likely he had gone for a glass of beer Martin decided, but the half-mile walk down to the village to find out seemed a long journey to him. He lay on his bed with his shoes off, trying to make up his mind. He did not reach out for a book. He was too tired to feel sleepy, and he lay, scarcely thinking, in a semi-stupor of weariness, until it was time for supper. Joe did not appear for that function, and when Martin heard the gardener remark that most likely he was ripping the slats off the bar, Martin understood. He went to bed immediately afterward, and in the morning decided that he was greatly rested. Joe being still absent, Martin procured a Sunday paper and lay down in a shady nook under the trees. The morning passed, he knew not how. He did not sleep, nobody disturbed him, and he did not finish the paper. He came back to it in the afternoon, after dinner, and fell asleep over it.

text: As Ruth’s front door shut behind them and he descended the steps beside her, he felt himself deeply unsettled. Taking her to the lecture was not the pure delight he had imagined. He did not know what was proper for him to do. On the streets, among people of her class, he had observed that women took men by the arm. Yet he had also seen them when they did not, and he wondered whether such arm-taking was done only in the evening, or only among husbands and wives and relations.
source_text: As Ruth’s front door closed behind them and he came down the steps with her, he found himself greatly perturbed. It was not unalloyed bliss, taking her to the lecture. He did not know what he ought to do. He had seen, on the streets, with persons of her class, that the women took the men’s arms. But then, again, he had seen them when they didn’t; and he wondered if it was only in the evening that arms were taken, or only between husbands and wives and relatives.

text: The door flew open, and the man swept past Adrian with a stormy face, stalking away down the corridor while he muttered oaths beneath his breath and worked his hands into fists. Adrian judged it wiser not to go in at once, and loitered in the passageways for a quarter of an hour. Then he thrust the door open and stepped inside. It was a fresh experience, his first time within an editorial office. Visiting cards, it seemed, had no place there; the office boy carried the message into an inner room that a gentleman was asking for Mr. Hale. When he returned, he signaled to Adrian from halfway across the room and conducted him to the private office, the inner shrine of the editors. Adrian’s first impression was of the room’s disorder and crowded confusion. After that he observed a youngish man with a beard, seated at a roll-top desk, who looked at him with a keen, questioning air. Adrian was struck by the tranquil composure of the man’s expression. Clearly the quarrel with the printer had not disturbed his calm.
source_text: The door jerked open, and the man flung past Martin, with an angry countenance and went down the corridor, muttering curses and clenching his fists. Martin decided not to enter immediately, and lingered in the hallways for a quarter of an hour. Then he shoved the door open and walked in. It was a new experience, the first time he had been inside an editorial office. Cards evidently were not necessary in that office, for the boy carried word to an inner room that there was a man who wanted to see Mr. Ford. Returning, the boy beckoned him from halfway across the room and led him to the private office, the editorial sanctum. Martin’s first impression was of the disorder and cluttered confusion of the room. Next he noticed a bewhiskered, youthful-looking man, sitting at a roll-top desk, who regarded him curiously. Martin marvelled at the calm repose of his face. It was evident that the squabble with the printer had not affected his equanimity.

text: “I don’t care whether you’re honest with me or not. You could do anything you wanted with me. You could throw me in the dirt and walk over me. And you’re the only man in the world who can,” she added with a defiant flash. “I haven’t looked out for myself since I was a child for nothing.”
source_text: “I don’t care whether you’re straight with me or not. You could do anything with me. You could throw me in the dirt an’ walk on me. An’ you’re the only man in the world that can,” she added with a defiant flash. “I ain’t taken care of myself ever since I was a kid for nothin’.”

text: “It was nothing at all,” he said. “Any fellow would have done as much for another. That pack of thugs was hunting for trouble, and Arthur wasn’t troubling them any. They shoved their noses into his business, and then I shoved mine into theirs and landed a few good punches. That’s where some of the skin came off my hands, along with a few of the gang’s teeth. I wouldn’t have missed it for the world. When I saw—”
source_text: “It wasn’t nothin’ at all,” he said. “Any guy ’ud do it for another. That bunch of hoodlums was lookin’ for trouble, an’ Arthur wasn’t botherin’ ’em none. They butted in on ’m, an’ then I butted in on them an’ poked a few. That’s where some of the skin off my hands went, along with some of the teeth of the gang. I wouldn’t ’a’ missed it for anything. When I seen—”

text: “No, he didn’t say anything,” Ruth said. “He only loved me, that was all. I was as astonished as you are. Not a word came from him. He only slipped his arm about me. And—and I wasn’t myself. Then he kissed me, and I kissed him back. I couldn’t have stopped it. I simply had to. And after that I knew I loved him.”
source_text: “No, he didn’t speak,” Ruth explained. “He just loved me, that was all. I was as surprised as you are. He didn’t say a word. He just put his arm around me. And—and I was not myself. And he kissed me, and I kissed him. I couldn’t help it. I just had to. And then I knew I loved him.”

text: “You are a magnificent and noble woman,” he said. “And I’m the one who ought to be proud to know you. And I am—I truly am. You are a beam of light to me in this very dark world, and I have to be honest with you, as honest as you have been with me.”
source_text: “You are a great and noble woman,” he said. “And it is I who should be proud to know you. And I am, I am. You are a ray of light to me in a very dark world, and I’ve got to be straight with you, just as straight as you have been.”

text: Martin was not angry. He did not even feel himself affronted. He bore with Mr. Morse, all the while wondering what it must be like to swallow such a bitter dose of humiliation. He did not refuse the invitation. Instead, he sidestepped it with vague, indefinite replies and asked after the family, especially Mrs. Morse and Ruth. He said Ruth’s name without a pause, easily, though he was privately astonished to find no inner tremor, no old familiar quickening of the pulse or rush of warmth in his blood.
source_text: Martin was not angry. He was not even on his dignity. He tolerated Mr. Morse, wondering the while how it felt to eat such humble pie. He did not decline the invitation. Instead, he put it off with vagueness and indefiniteness and inquired after the family, particularly after Mrs. Morse and Ruth. He spoke her name without hesitancy, naturally, though secretly surprised that he had had no inward quiver, no old, familiar increase of pulse and warm surge of blood.

text: Maria, with eyes full of admiration that nonetheless missed nothing of the weave of Ruth’s clothes or the shape of their tailoring—an unfamiliar cut that somehow gave off a strangely lovely effect—escorted her to the carriage. The cluster of disappointed urchins kept staring until the carriage vanished, and then turned their gaze on Maria, who had suddenly become the most important person in the street. But one of her own children ruined Maria’s standing by announcing that the distinguished visitors had come for her lodger. After that, Maria slipped back into her former anonymity, and Martin began to notice the respectful way the neighborhood’s small fry treated him. As for Maria, Martin’s value in her eyes doubled at once, and if the Portuguese grocer had seen that afternoon’s carriage visit, he would have extended Martin another three dollars and eighty-five cents’ worth of credit.
source_text: Maria, with worshipful eyes that none the less were keen to note the texture of Ruth’s garments and the cut of them (a cut unknown that produced an effect mysteriously beautiful), saw her to the carriage. The crowd of disappointed urchins stared till the carriage disappeared from view, then transferred their stare to Maria, who had abruptly become the most important person on the street. But it was one of her progeny who blasted Maria’s reputation by announcing that the grand visitors had been for her lodger. After that Maria dropped back into her old obscurity and Martin began to notice the respectful manner in which he was regarded by the small fry of the neighborhood. As for Maria, Martin rose in her estimation a full hundred per cent, and had the Portuguese grocer witnessed that afternoon carriage-call he would have allowed Martin an additional three-dollars-and-eighty-five-cents’ worth of credit.

text: “I was hoping I might ask you for a bit of counsel,” he said, and the prompt, gracious assent that followed set his heart leaping. “You remember the last time I was here I told you I couldn’t talk about books and such, because I didn’t know how? Well, I’ve been doing a mighty lot of thinking since then. I’ve spent a deal of time in the library, but most of what I’ve tried to read has been beyond me. Maybe I ought to start at the start. I never had any advantages. I’ve worked hard ever since I was a boy, and since I began going to the library, looking at books with fresh eyes—an’ at new books too—I’ve pretty much come to the conclusion I haven’t been reading the right sort. You know the kind of reading you come across in cow-camps and forecastles isn’t the same as what you’ve got in this house, for example. Well, that’s the kind of stuff I’ve been used to. And yet—an’ I’m not saying this to boast—I’ve always been different from the people I’ve run with. Not that I’m any better than the sailors and cow-punchers I’ve travelled with—I did a spell of cow-punching myself, you know—but I’ve always liked books, read everything I could get my hands on, and, well, I reckon I think differently from most of them.”
source_text: “I wonder if I can get some advice from you,” he began, and received an acquiescence of willingness that made his heart bound. “You remember the other time I was here I said I couldn’t talk about books an’ things because I didn’t know how? Well, I’ve ben doin’ a lot of thinkin’ ever since. I’ve ben to the library a whole lot, but most of the books I’ve tackled have ben over my head. Mebbe I’d better begin at the beginnin’. I ain’t never had no advantages. I’ve worked pretty hard ever since I was a kid, an’ since I’ve ben to the library, lookin’ with new eyes at books—an’ lookin’ at new books, too—I’ve just about concluded that I ain’t ben reading the right kind. You know the books you find in cattle-camps an’ fo’c’s’ls ain’t the same you’ve got in this house, for instance. Well, that’s the sort of readin’ matter I’ve ben accustomed to. And yet—an’ I ain’t just makin’ a brag of it—I’ve ben different from the people I’ve herded with. Not that I’m any better than the sailors an’ cow-punchers I travelled with,—I was cow-punchin’ for a short time, you know,—but I always liked books, read everything I could lay hands on, an’—well, I guess I think differently from most of ’em.

text: “It was the only way into the hiding place. Three hours after I picked up the trail, I was there, in a small mountain valley, a hollow tucked among lava peaks. The whole place had been terraced for taro patches, fruit trees grew there, and there were eight or ten grass huts. But the moment I saw the inhabitants, I knew what I had come upon. One glance at them was enough.”
source_text: “It was the only way in to the hiding-place. Three hours after I found the trail I was there, in a little mountain valley, a pocket in the midst of lava peaks. The whole place was terraced for taro-patches, fruit trees grew there, and there were eight or ten grass huts. But as soon as I saw the inhabitants I knew what I’d struck. One sight of them was enough.”

text: Graham thought of his five hours of sleep each night and smiled. His room was above the laundry and in the same building as the engine that pumped the water, generated the electricity, and drove the laundry machinery. The engineer, who lived next door, came in to see the newcomer and helped Graham rig an electric bulb on an extension wire, so that it could run along a taut cord from above the table to the bed.
source_text: Martin thought of his five hours’ sleep a night, and smiled. The room was situated over the laundry and was in the same building with the engine that pumped water, made electricity, and ran the laundry machinery. The engineer, who occupied the adjoining room, dropped in to meet the new hand and helped Martin rig up an electric bulb, on an extension wire, so that it travelled along a stretched cord from over the table to the bed.

text: As Adrian Vale descended the stairs, his hand slipped into his coat pocket. It emerged with a sheet of brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which he quickly and skillfully rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first breath of smoke deep into his lungs and let it go in one long, slow exhalation. “By God!” he cried aloud, in a tone of awe and amazement. “By God!” he said again. And once more he muttered, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he tore from the shirt and thrust into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he left his head bare beneath it and unfastened his vest, striding onward in magnificent unconcern. He was only vaguely conscious that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming and remaking in his mind the scenes of a moment before.
source_text: As Martin Eden went down the steps, his hand dropped into his coat pocket. It came out with a brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which were deftly rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first whiff of smoke deep into his lungs and expelled it in a long and lingering exhalation. “By God!” he said aloud, in a voice of awe and wonder. “By God!” he repeated. And yet again he murmured, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he ripped out of the shirt and stuffed into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he bared his head to it and unbuttoned his vest, swinging along in splendid unconcern. He was only dimly aware that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming dreams and reconstructing the scenes just past.

text: Yet only at scattered instants was Adrian able to think at all. The house of thought stood shut, its panes nailed over, and he served there as a dim and unpaid keeper. He himself was but a shade. Jonah had been right. They were both shades, and this was labor’s endless limbo. Or was it a dream? Now and again, in the hissing, suffocating heat, as he sent the heavy irons sweeping to and fro over the pale linen, it seemed to him that it was a dream. In another little while, or perhaps not for a thousand years, he would wake in his narrow room, with its ink-stained desk, and resume the writing he had broken off the day before. Or perhaps that too was the dream, and waking would come with the changing of the watch, when he would swing down from his bunk in the pitching forecastle and climb to the deck beneath the tropical stars, to take the helm and feel the cool trade wind moving through his body.
source_text: But it was only at rare moments that Martin was able to think. The house of thought was closed, its windows boarded up, and he was its shadowy caretaker. He was a shadow. Joe was right. They were both shadows, and this was the unending limbo of toil. Or was it a dream? Sometimes, in the steaming, sizzling heat, as he swung the heavy irons back and forth over the white garments, it came to him that it was a dream. In a short while, or maybe after a thousand years or so, he would awake, in his little room with the ink-stained table, and take up his writing where he had left off the day before. Or maybe that was a dream, too, and the awakening would be the changing of the watches, when he would drop down out of his bunk in the lurching forecastle and go up on deck, under the tropic stars, and take the wheel and feel the cool tradewind blowing through his flesh.

text: And as it had been with the birds, so it had been with all else. His foolish, ill-armed ventures into philosophy had come to nothing. The scholastic metaphysics of Kant had unlocked nothing for him; they had served only to make him mistrust his own powers of thought. In much the same way, his attempt to study evolution had been reduced to a desperately technical treatise by a writer named Romanes. He had understood not a word, and the one impression he carried away was that evolution was a dry, dusty doctrine, held by a swarm of little men with immense and incomprehensible vocabularies. And now he discovered that evolution was no mere theory at all, but an accepted process of growth; that scientists no longer quarrelled over its existence, but only over the manner of its working.
source_text: And as it was with birds, so had it been with everything. His ignorant and unprepared attempts at philosophy had been fruitless. The medieval metaphysics of Kant had given him the key to nothing, and had served the sole purpose of making him doubt his own intellectual powers. In similar manner his attempt to study evolution had been confined to a hopelessly technical volume by Romanes. He had understood nothing, and the only idea he had gathered was that evolution was a dry-as-dust theory, of a lot of little men possessed of huge and unintelligible vocabularies. And now he learned that evolution was no mere theory but an accepted process of development; that scientists no longer disagreed about it, their only differences being over the method of evolution.

text: “And there is another thing,” he went on. “You love me. But why do you love me? The very thing in me that drives me to write is the same thing that wins your love. You love me because I am in some way unlike the men you have known and might have loved. I was not made for the desk and the counting-house, for small business wrangles and legal bickerings. Force me to do such things, force me to become like those other men, to do the work they do, breathe the air they breathe, and acquire the point of view they have acquired, and you destroy the difference, destroy me, destroy the thing you love. My urge to write is the most living thing in me. Had I been a mere lump of clay, I should never have wanted to write, nor would you have wanted me for a husband.”
source_text: “And another thing,” he swept on. “You love me. But why do you love me? The thing in me that compels me to write is the very thing that draws your love. You love me because I am somehow different from the men you have known and might have loved. I was not made for the desk and counting-house, for petty business squabbling, and legal jangling. Make me do such things, make me like those other men, doing the work they do, breathing the air they breathe, developing the point of view they have developed, and you have destroyed the difference, destroyed me, destroyed the thing you love. My desire to write is the most vital thing in me. Had I been a mere clod, neither would I have desired to write, nor would you have desired me for a husband.”

text: It was the knot of loquacious socialists and working-class philosophers who held forth in City Hall Park on warm afternoons that led to the great discovery. Once or twice a month, as he rode through the park on his way to the library, Martin would dismount from his wheel and listen to their arguments, and each time he found it hard to tear himself away. The level of discussion was far below what he had known at Mr. Morse’s table. The men were neither grave nor dignified. They lost their tempers readily and called one another names, and oaths and obscene allusions came often to their lips. More than once he had seen them come to blows. And yet, for reasons he could not have explained, there was something vital in the substance of these men’s thoughts. Their logomachy quickened his mind far more than the restrained, quiet dogmatism of Mr. Morse. These men, who butchered English, flung their arms about like madmen, and battled over one another’s ideas with primitive fury, somehow seemed more alive than Mr. Morse and his crony, Mr. Butler.
source_text: It was the knot of wordy socialists and working-class philosophers that held forth in the City Hall Park on warm afternoons that was responsible for the great discovery. Once or twice in the month, while riding through the park on his way to the library, Martin dismounted from his wheel and listened to the arguments, and each time he tore himself away reluctantly. The tone of discussion was much lower than at Mr. Morse’s table. The men were not grave and dignified. They lost their tempers easily and called one another names, while oaths and obscene allusions were frequent on their lips. Once or twice he had seen them come to blows. And yet, he knew not why, there seemed something vital about the stuff of these men’s thoughts. Their logomachy was far more stimulating to his intellect than the reserved and quiet dogmatism of Mr. Morse. These men, who slaughtered English, gesticulated like lunatics, and fought one another’s ideas with primitive anger, seemed somehow to be more alive than Mr. Morse and his crony, Mr. Butler.

text: “I really don’t object to platitudes,” he told Ruth afterward; “what unnerves me is the pompous, self-satisfied, superior certainty with which they’re delivered, and the time it takes to deliver them. Why, I could have given that man the whole history of the Reformation in the time he spent telling me the Union-Labor Party had fused with the Democrats. Do you know, he strips his words the way a professional poker player strips the cards dealt to him. Some day I’ll show you just what I mean.”
source_text: “I really don’t object to platitudes,” he told Ruth later; “but what worries me into nervousness is the pompous, smugly complacent, superior certitude with which they are uttered and the time taken to do it. Why, I could give that man the whole history of the Reformation in the time he took to tell me that the Union-Labor Party had fused with the Democrats. Do you know, he skins his words as a professional poker-player skins the cards that are dealt out to him. Some day I’ll show you what I mean.”

text: He looked about the table. Across from him sat Arthur and Arthur’s brother, Norman. They were her brothers, he reminded himself, and at once his heart grew warm toward them. How dearly this family loved one another! In his mind rose the image of her mother, the welcoming kiss, and the two of them coming toward him with their arms linked. In his own world, no such open affection between parents and children was ever shown. Here, in this world above his own, it seemed a revelation of life’s highest possibilities. Of all he had yet seen in this brief glimpse, it was the finest. He was profoundly stirred by it, and his heart softened with a kind of answering tenderness. All his life he had gone hungry for love. His nature longed for it; it was an inborn need. Yet he had lived without it and, in doing so, had grown hardened. He had never known that he needed love. He did not know it now. He only saw it at work, felt its thrill, judged it beautiful and lofty and magnificent.
source_text: He glanced around the table. Opposite him was Arthur, and Arthur’s brother, Norman. They were her brothers, he reminded himself, and his heart warmed toward them. How they loved each other, the members of this family! There flashed into his mind the picture of her mother, of the kiss of greeting, and of the pair of them walking toward him with arms entwined. Not in his world were such displays of affection between parents and children made. It was a revelation of the heights of existence that were attained in the world above. It was the finest thing yet that he had seen in this small glimpse of that world. He was moved deeply by appreciation of it, and his heart was melting with sympathetic tenderness. He had starved for love all his life. His nature craved love. It was an organic demand of his being. Yet he had gone without, and hardened himself in the process. He had not known that he needed love. Nor did he know it now. He merely saw it in operation, and thrilled to it, and thought it fine, and high, and splendid.

text: “Very well, you disciples of Vogt, I may argue like a village conjurer, but tell me, how do you argue? You have no ground beneath your feet, you dogmatic unbelievers, with your ever-ready positive science, forever hauling it into regions where it has no business to go. Long before the creed of materialistic monism came into fashion, the earth had already been swept away, so that no footing remained. It was Locke who did it—John Locke. Two centuries ago—more than two centuries, in his ‘Essay Concerning Human Understanding,’ he demonstrated the absence of innate ideas. The amusing part is that this is exactly what you maintain. Tonight, over and over again, you have insisted on the absence of innate ideas.”
source_text: “All right, you Haeckelites, I may reason like a medicine man, but, pray, how do you reason? You have nothing to stand on, you unscientific dogmatists with your positive science which you are always lugging about into places it has no right to be. Long before the school of materialistic monism arose, the ground was removed so that there could be no foundation. Locke was the man, John Locke. Two hundred years ago—more than that, even in his ‘Essay concerning the Human Understanding,’ he proved the non-existence of innate ideas. The best of it is that that is precisely what you claim. To-night, again and again, you have asserted the non-existence of innate ideas.

text: “I’d give anything to see you get over it,” she burst out impulsively. “You ought to care when women look at you that way, a man like you. It isn’t natural. It may be all right for sissy-boys, but you weren’t made that way. So help me, I’d be willing and glad if the right woman ever came along and made you care.”
source_text: “I’d give anything to see you get over it,” she broke out impulsively. “You ought to care when women look at you that way, a man like you. It’s not natural. It’s all right enough for sissy-boys. But you ain’t made that way. So help me, I’d be willing an’ glad if the right woman came along an’ made you care.”

text: He put on his hat and went out, pausing at a gun shop, where he spent the rest of the morning buying automatic rifles, ammunition, and fishing tackle. Trading fashions altered, and he knew he would have to wait until he reached Tahiti before ordering his trade goods. They could always come up from Australia, in any case. The decision gave him pleasure. He had managed to avoid doing something, and any action at the moment was distasteful. He returned to the hotel cheerfully, comforted by the thought that the Morris chair awaited him; then, on entering his room, he gave an inward groan at the sight of Joe in that very chair.
source_text: He put on his hat and went out, stopping in at a gun-store, where he spent the remainder of the morning buying automatic rifles, ammunition, and fishing tackle. Fashions changed in trading, and he knew he would have to wait till he reached Tahiti before ordering his trade-goods. They could come up from Australia, anyway. This solution was a source of pleasure. He had avoided doing something, and the doing of anything just now was unpleasant. He went back to the hotel gladly, with a feeling of satisfaction in that the comfortable Morris chair was waiting for him; and he groaned inwardly, on entering his room, at sight of Joe in the Morris chair.

text: He mailed the heavy manuscript to _The Youth’s Companion_, and on Saturday afternoon, after laying plans for an article on pearl-diving, he went to see Ruth. He had telephoned ahead, and she came herself to the door to meet him. The old, familiar vigor of health came streaming out of him and struck her like a blow. It seemed to pass into her own body and run through her veins in a warm, liquid brightness, setting her to trembling with its borrowed strength. He colored warmly as he took her hand and met her blue eyes, but the fresh bronze of eight months in the sun concealed the blush, though it did not spare the neck from the raw irritation of the stiff collar. She noticed the red line of it with a smile that soon faded when she looked at his clothes. They actually fit him now—it was the first suit he had ever had made to order—and he seemed leaner and more finely shaped. Beside that, his cloth cap had been replaced by a soft hat, which she ordered him to put on and then praised him for his looks. She could not remember ever feeling so happy. This change in him was her doing, and she was proud of it, eager with the desire to help him still further.
source_text: He mailed the bulky manuscript to _The Youth’s Companion_, and on Saturday afternoon, after having planned an article on pearl-diving, he went to see Ruth. He had telephoned, and she went herself to greet him at the door. The old familiar blaze of health rushed out from him and struck her like a blow. It seemed to enter into her body and course through her veins in a liquid glow, and to set her quivering with its imparted strength. He flushed warmly as he took her hand and looked into her blue eyes, but the fresh bronze of eight months of sun hid the flush, though it did not protect the neck from the gnawing chafe of the stiff collar. She noted the red line of it with amusement which quickly vanished as she glanced at his clothes. They really fitted him,—it was his first made-to-order suit,—and he seemed slimmer and better modelled. In addition, his cloth cap had been replaced by a soft hat, which she commanded him to put on and then complimented him on his appearance. She did not remember when she had felt so happy. This change in him was her handiwork, and she was proud of it and fired with ambition further to help him.

text: “Well—” She knitted her brows and pinched her lips together as she thought, while he watched her and concluded that her expression was utterly charming. “‘It don’t do to be hasty.’ If you change ‘don’t’ to ‘do not,’ it comes out as ‘It do not do to be hasty,’ which is completely nonsensical.”
source_text: “Well—” She puckered her brows and pursed up her mouth as she thought, while he looked on and decided that her expression was most adorable. “‘It don’t do to be hasty.’ Change ‘don’t’ to ‘do not,’ and it reads, ‘It do not do to be hasty,’ which is perfectly absurd.”

text: “Not in my case,” he said. “I don’t get carried away, though there’s nothing to keep me from it except that I don’t want to. I’ve been drunk only once since I saw you last, and that was by surprise, on an empty stomach. When I work like a beast, I drink like a beast. When I live like a man, I drink like a man—a shot now and then when I feel like it, and that’s all.”
source_text: “Not in mine,” he announced. “No gettin’ oryide for me, though there ain’t nothin’ to stop me except I don’t want to. I’ve ben drunk once since I seen you last, an’ then it was unexpected, bein’ on an empty stomach. When I work like a beast, I drink like a beast. When I live like a man, I drink like a man—a jolt now an’ again when I feel like it, an’ that’s all.”

text: The sweat ran from Adrian in streams. He drank prodigious quantities of water, yet such was the heat of the day and the fury of his labor that the water seemed to pass through the very grain of his body and escape at every pore. At sea, save for brief intervals, the work he had done had always left him time enough to be alone with his own mind. The captain of the vessel had ruled Adrian’s hours; but here the proprietor of the boardinghouse ruled his thoughts as well. He had no thoughts beyond the exhausting, spirit-cracking toil that drained the life from him. Beyond that, to think was impossible. He did not know that he loved Clara. She did not even exist for him, so completely had his driven soul forgotten her. Only when he dragged himself to bed at night, or to breakfast in the morning, did she return to him in faint, momentary flashes of memory.
source_text: The sweat poured from Martin. He drank enormous quantities of water, but so great was the heat of the day and of his exertions, that the water sluiced through the interstices of his flesh and out at all his pores. Always, at sea, except at rare intervals, the work he performed had given him ample opportunity to commune with himself. The master of the ship had been lord of Martin’s time; but here the manager of the hotel was lord of Martin’s thoughts as well. He had no thoughts save for the nerve-racking, body-destroying toil. Outside of that it was impossible to think. He did not know that he loved Ruth. She did not even exist, for his driven soul had no time to remember her. It was only when he crawled to bed at night, or to breakfast in the morning, that she asserted herself to him in fleeting memories.

text: “On the contrary—” Brissenden stopped and let his eyes travel with open insolence over Martin’s plain poverty, from the threadbare tie and the frayed collar to the polished sleeves of the coat and the slight wear at one cuff, ending with a long look at Martin’s hollow cheeks. “On the contrary, hack work is beneath you—so far beneath you that you can never even hope to reach it. Why, man, I could offend you by asking you to eat something.”
source_text: “On the contrary—” Brissenden paused and ran an insolent eye over Martin’s objective poverty, passing from the well-worn tie and the saw-edged collar to the shiny sleeves of the coat and on to the slight fray of one cuff, winding up and dwelling upon Martin’s sunken cheeks. “On the contrary, hack-work is above you, so far above you that you can never hope to rise to it. Why, man, I could insult you by asking you to have something to eat.”

text: The will to live, he thought with scorn, making a useless effort not to draw the air into his bursting lungs. Well, then, he would have to try another way. He filled his lungs with air, packed them to the full. This supply would carry him far below. He turned over and went down headfirst, swimming with all the strength and all the resolve he possessed. Deeper and deeper he went. His eyes were open, and he watched the spectral, phosphorescent streaks of the swift bonita. As he swam, he hoped they would not dart at him, for that might have broken the strain of his will. But they did not strike, and he had time to be grateful for this final mercy of life.
source_text: The will to live, he thought disdainfully, vainly endeavoring not to breathe the air into his bursting lungs. Well, he would have to try a new way. He filled his lungs with air, filled them full. This supply would take him far down. He turned over and went down head first, swimming with all his strength and all his will. Deeper and deeper he went. His eyes were open, and he watched the ghostly, phosphorescent trails of the darting bonita. As he swam, he hoped that they would not strike at him, for it might snap the tension of his will. But they did not strike, and he found time to be grateful for this last kindness of life.

text: He read, and as he read he watched her. At last, he thought, he had reached her. She sat motionless, her eyes fixed unwaveringly on him, scarcely drawing breath, lifted out of herself, he thought, by the spell of the thing he had made. He had called the story “Adventure,” and it was the very pinnacle of adventure—not the adventure of storybooks, but real adventure, that savage taskmaster, terrible in punishment and terrible in reward, faithless and capricious, demanding endless patience and days and nights of bitter labor, offering at the end of thirst and famine or the long pull and monstrous delirium of wasting fever a blazing sunlight glory or a dark death; through blood and sweat and biting insects it led, by long chains of petty and ignoble contacts, to royal climaxes and princely achievements.
source_text: He read, and as he read he watched her. At last he had reached her, he thought. She sat without movement, her eyes steadfast upon him, scarcely breathing, caught up and out of herself, he thought, by the witchery of the thing he had created. He had entitled the story “Adventure,” and it was the apotheosis of adventure—not of the adventure of the storybooks, but of real adventure, the savage taskmaster, awful of punishment and awful of reward, faithless and whimsical, demanding terrible patience and heartbreaking days and nights of toil, offering the blazing sunlight glory or dark death at the end of thirst and famine or of the long drag and monstrous delirium of rotting fever, through blood and sweat and stinging insects leading up by long chains of petty and ignoble contacts to royal culminations and lordly achievements.

text: Martin remembered his blank-verse tragedy and sent that instead. Before mailing it, he read it over, and was struck especially by its awkward, boyish amateurishness and its all-around lack of merit. Nevertheless, he sent it off; and it was published, much to the editor’s lasting regret. The public responded with anger and disbelief. It was too great a departure from Martin Eden’s lofty reputation to be credited to that solemn nonsense. People said he had never written it at all, that the magazine had forged it in a very clumsy way, or that Martin Eden, like the elder Dumas, was so successful he had others do his writing for him. But when he explained that the tragedy had been an early work from his literary childhood, and that the magazine had not been content until it had secured it, there was a loud laugh at the magazine’s expense and a change in editors followed. The tragedy was never issued in book form, though Martin kept the advance royalties that had already been paid.
source_text: Martin recollected his blank-verse tragedy, and sent it instead. He read it over before mailing, and was particularly impressed by its sophomoric amateurishness and general worthlessness. But he sent it; and it was published, to the everlasting regret of the editor. The public was indignant and incredulous. It was too far a cry from Martin Eden’s high standard to that serious bosh. It was asserted that he had never written it, that the magazine had faked it very clumsily, or that Martin Eden was emulating the elder Dumas and at the height of success was hiring his writing done for him. But when he explained that the tragedy was an early effort of his literary childhood, and that the magazine had refused to be happy unless it got it, a great laugh went up at the magazine’s expense and a change in the editorship followed. The tragedy was never brought out in book-form, though Martin pocketed the advance royalties that had been paid.

text: His words trailed off. His carefully framed purpose had come to a standstill on the brink of the dreadful likelihood that he had really spoken to Adrian, and so made a fool of himself. Clara answered nothing at once. She was too intent on trying to bring into harmony the halting, awkward speech and its plainness of thought with what she had read in his countenance. Never had she looked into eyes of greater force. A man who could accomplish anything, those were the words she saw there; and they sat oddly beside the feebleness of his spoken idea. Besides, so intricate and swift was her own mind that she had little true feeling for simplicity. And yet she had sensed power even in the very fumbling of that mind. It had seemed to her like a giant struggling and twisting under the fetters that bound him. When at last she spoke, her face was full of pity.
source_text: His voice died away. His firmly planned intention had come to a halt on the verge of the horrible probability that he should have asked Arthur and that he had made a fool of himself. Ruth did not speak immediately. She was too absorbed in striving to reconcile the stumbling, uncouth speech and its simplicity of thought with what she saw in his face. She had never looked in eyes that expressed greater power. Here was a man who could do anything, was the message she read there, and it accorded ill with the weakness of his spoken thought. And for that matter so complex and quick was her own mind that she did not have a just appreciation of simplicity. And yet she had caught an impression of power in the very groping of this mind. It had seemed to her like a giant writhing and straining at the bonds that held him down. Her face was all sympathy when she did speak.

text: “I beg your pardon,” said Mr. Vale, “but I settled with the printer less than an hour ago, and he took all the cash I had on hand. It was thoughtless of me to be so ill provided; yet the account was not due, and his asking, as a favor, for an immediate payment was wholly unforeseen.”
source_text: “I am sorry,” said Mr. Ends, “but I paid the printer not an hour ago, and he took my ready change. It was careless of me to be so short; but the bill was not yet due, and the printer’s request, as a favor, to make an immediate advance, was quite unexpected.”

text: Well, he would keep fighting his way onward and upward. And he would take Ruth with him. He loved her dearly, and he felt sure she would succeed anywhere. Just as he knew he had been held back by the surroundings of his youth, so he now saw that she had been held back too. She had never been given room to grow. The books on her father’s shelves, the paintings on the walls, the music on the piano—all of it was merely showy pretension. As for true literature, true painting, true music, the Morses and people like them were dead to such things. And greater than such things was life itself, of which they were profoundly and hopelessly ignorant. Despite their Unitarian leanings and their pose of conservative broadmindedness, they were two generations behind interpretive science: their minds were medieval, while their thinking about the final realities of existence and the universe seemed to him to be the same metaphysical habit of thought that was as young as the youngest race and as old as the cave man, and older still—the same impulse that drove the first Pleistocene ape-man to fear the darkness; that led the first impulsive Hebrew savage to embody Eve from Adam’s rib; that prompted Descartes to construct an idealist universe from the projections of his own meager ego; and that inspired the celebrated British churchman to rail against evolution in a satire so biting that it earned instant applause and left his name a notorious scribble on the page of history.
source_text: Well, he would fight his way on and up higher. And he would take Ruth with him. Her he dearly loved, and he was confident that she would shine anywhere. As it was clear to him that he had been handicapped by his early environment, so now he perceived that she was similarly handicapped. She had not had a chance to expand. The books on her father’s shelves, the paintings on the walls, the music on the piano—all was just so much meretricious display. To real literature, real painting, real music, the Morses and their kind, were dead. And bigger than such things was life, of which they were densely, hopelessly ignorant. In spite of their Unitarian proclivities and their masks of conservative broadmindedness, they were two generations behind interpretative science: their mental processes were mediaeval, while their thinking on the ultimate data of existence and of the universe struck him as the same metaphysical method that was as young as the youngest race, as old as the cave-man, and older—the same that moved the first Pleistocene ape-man to fear the dark; that moved the first hasty Hebrew savage to incarnate Eve from Adam’s rib; that moved Descartes to build an idealistic system of the universe out of the projections of his own puny ego; and that moved the famous British ecclesiastic to denounce evolution in satire so scathing as to win immediate applause and leave his name a notorious scrawl on the page of history.

text: Martin went over to the telephone in the corner of the room, and a warm rush went through him the moment he heard Ruth’s voice. In his struggle with the sonnet he had quite forgotten she existed, and now, at the sound of her speaking, his love for her struck him like a blow. And what a voice it was—soft and sweet, like music heard at a distance, faint and far away, or better still, like a silver bell, flawless in tone, clear as crystal. No ordinary woman had a voice like that. It seemed almost heavenly, as though it came from some other world. He could hardly make out what she was saying, so enchanted was he, though he kept his face under control, knowing Mr. Higginbotham’s ferret-like eyes were on him.
source_text: Martin went to the telephone in the corner of the room, and felt a wave of warmth rush through him as he heard Ruth’s voice. In his battle with the sonnet he had forgotten her existence, and at the sound of her voice his love for her smote him like a sudden blow. And such a voice!—delicate and sweet, like a strain of music heard far off and faint, or, better, like a bell of silver, a perfect tone, crystal-pure. No mere woman had a voice like that. There was something celestial about it, and it came from other worlds. He could scarcely hear what it said, so ravished was he, though he controlled his face, for he knew that Mr. Higginbotham’s ferret eyes were fixed upon him.

text: “Its work is done,” Martin said, as they settled down, she on his coat and he stretched out close to the warm earth. He drew in the sweet scent of the tawny grass, and it seemed to enter his mind and send his thoughts racing from the particular to the universal. “It has fulfilled the purpose of its existence,” he went on, patting the dry grass with affection. “It sprang to life with ambition beneath last winter’s dreary downpour, struggled through the fierce early spring, blossomed, and enticed the insects and the bees, cast off its seeds, settled its account with duty and the world, and—”
source_text: “Its work is done,” Martin said, as they seated themselves, she upon his coat, and he sprawling close to the warm earth. He sniffed the sweetness of the tawny grass, which entered his brain and set his thoughts whirling on from the particular to the universal. “It has achieved its reason for existence,” he went on, patting the dry grass affectionately. “It quickened with ambition under the dreary downpour of last winter, fought the violent early spring, flowered, and lured the insects and the bees, scattered its seeds, squared itself with its duty and the world, and—”

text: Having mastered these precautions, Martin consulted “The Duchess” on questions of tone and then set to work, mixing according to the formula. The formula had three parts: first, a pair of lovers are torn apart; second, by some act or event, they are brought together again; third, marriage bells. The third element was fixed and never altered, but the first two could be varied in an endless number of ways. Thus the lovers might be separated by mistaken motives, by a twist of fate, by jealous rivals, by angry parents, by cunning guardians, by plotting relatives, and on and on; and they might be reunited by the man’s brave deed, by a like deed on the woman’s part, by a change of heart in either lover, by the forced confession of a cunning guardian, plotting relative, or jealous rival, by the voluntary confession of the same, by the discovery of some unsuspected secret, by the lover’s storming of the girl’s heart, by the lover’s long and noble sacrifice, and so endlessly. It was especially engaging to have the girl herself propose in the midst of the reconciliation, and, little by little, Martin uncovered other distinctly piquant and attractive devices. But the marriage bells at the end were the one thing with which he could not tamper; though the heavens should roll up like a scroll and the stars fall from the sky, the wedding bells must keep on ringing all the same. As to length, the formula called for a minimum dose of twelve hundred words and a maximum of fifteen hundred.
source_text: Having learned such precautions, Martin consulted “The Duchess” for tone, and proceeded to mix according to formula. The formula consists of three parts: (1) a pair of lovers are jarred apart; (2) by some deed or event they are reunited; (3) marriage bells. The third part was an unvarying quantity, but the first and second parts could be varied an infinite number of times. Thus, the pair of lovers could be jarred apart by misunderstood motives, by accident of fate, by jealous rivals, by irate parents, by crafty guardians, by scheming relatives, and so forth and so forth; they could be reunited by a brave deed of the man lover, by a similar deed of the woman lover, by change of heart in one lover or the other, by forced confession of crafty guardian, scheming relative, or jealous rival, by voluntary confession of same, by discovery of some unguessed secret, by lover storming girl’s heart, by lover making long and noble self-sacrifice, and so on, endlessly. It was very fetching to make the girl propose in the course of being reunited, and Martin discovered, bit by bit, other decidedly piquant and fetching ruses. But marriage bells at the end was the one thing he could take no liberties with; though the heavens rolled up as a scroll and the stars fell, the wedding bells must go on ringing just the same. In quantity, the formula prescribed twelve hundred words minimum dose, fifteen hundred words maximum dose.

text: He remained on deck after dinner longer than usual, but it did him no good, for when he went below he could not sleep. This respite from life had failed him; it was more than he could bear. He switched on the electric light and tried to read. Among the books was a copy of Swinburne. Lying in bed, he turned its pages idly at first, until all at once he realized he was reading with real interest. He finished the stanza, tried to go on, then came back to it again. He laid the book face down on his chest and sank into thought. So that was it. The very thing. How strange it had never occurred to him before. That was the meaning of everything; he had been drifting toward it all along, and now Swinburne had shown him the happy way out. He longed for rest, and here it was, waiting for him. He looked toward the open porthole. Yes, it was big enough. For the first time in weeks he felt happy. At last he had found the cure for his malady. He took up the book and read the stanza slowly aloud:-
source_text: He stayed late on deck, after dinner, but that did not help him, for when he went below, he could not sleep. This surcease from life had failed him. It was too much. He turned on the electric light and tried to read. One of the volumes was a Swinburne. He lay in bed, glancing through its pages, until suddenly he became aware that he was reading with interest. He finished the stanza, attempted to read on, then came back to it. He rested the book face downward on his breast and fell to thinking. That was it. The very thing. Strange that it had never come to him before. That was the meaning of it all; he had been drifting that way all the time, and now Swinburne showed him that it was the happy way out. He wanted rest, and here was rest awaiting him. He glanced at the open port-hole. Yes, it was large enough. For the first time in weeks he felt happy. At last he had discovered the cure of his ill. He picked up the book and read the stanza slowly aloud:-

text: So it was that, as he listened to Professor Caldwell’s easy, fluent talk—the discourse of a man of breeding and intellect—Martin kept seeing the whole sweep of his past before him. He saw himself in the days when he had been nothing more than a hoodlum, wearing a stiff-rimmed Stetson and a square-cut, double-breasted coat, carrying his shoulders with a certain swagger and holding fast to the ideal of being as hard as the police would allow. He made no attempt to soften it for himself or excuse it in any way. There had been a time when he was simply a common tough, the leader of a gang that harassed the police and kept honest working-class families in fear. But his ideals had altered. He looked around at the well-bred, well-dressed men and women, and drew into his lungs the air of culture and refinement, while at the same instant the ghost of his former youth, in stiff-rim and square-cut coat, swaggering and hard, crossed the room before him. That corner hoodlum, he saw, melted into himself, sitting there and conversing with an actual university professor.
source_text: So it was, as he listened to Professor Caldwell’s easy flow of speech—the conversation of a clever, cultured man—that Martin kept seeing himself down all his past. He saw himself when he had been quite the hoodlum, wearing a “stiff-rim” Stetson hat and a square-cut, double-breasted coat, with a certain swagger to the shoulders and possessing the ideal of being as tough as the police permitted. He did not disguise it to himself, nor attempt to palliate it. At one time in his life he had been just a common hoodlum, the leader of a gang that worried the police and terrorized honest, working-class householders. But his ideals had changed. He glanced about him at the well-bred, well-dressed men and women, and breathed into his lungs the atmosphere of culture and refinement, and at the same moment the ghost of his early youth, in stiff-rim and square-cut, with swagger and toughness, stalked across the room. This figure, of the corner hoodlum, he saw merge into himself, sitting and talking with an actual university professor.

text: Again she looked. In her eyes were all the centuries of womanhood since sex first began to stir. And he measured her with a kind of careless confidence, knowing now, boldly, that as he advanced she would start to retreat, shyly and delicately, always ready to turn the game about again should he grow weak. Yet he was human too, and could not help feeling her attraction, while his vanity could not but relish the tribute of her kindness. Oh, he understood it all, knew them thoroughly from A to Z. Decent, as decency went among people of their sort; laboring hard for small wages and scorning to sell themselves for easier gain; anxiously hungry for some little scrap of happiness in the desert of life, and staring toward a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of endless drudgery and the black abyss of a more dreadful misery, the road to which was shorter, though better paid.
source_text: Again she looked. All the centuries of woman since sex began were eloquent in her eyes. And he measured her in a careless way, and knew, bold now, that she would begin to retreat, coyly and delicately, as he pursued, ever ready to reverse the game should he turn fainthearted. And, too, he was human, and could feel the draw of her, while his ego could not but appreciate the flattery of her kindness. Oh, he knew it all, and knew them well, from A to Z. Good, as goodness might be measured in their particular class, hard-working for meagre wages and scorning the sale of self for easier ways, nervously desirous for some small pinch of happiness in the desert of existence, and facing a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of unending toil and the black pit of more terrible wretchedness, the way whereto being briefer though better paid.

text: It proved a memorable night for Martin, for after dinner he met Russ Brissenden. How he had come there, whose friend he was, or by what acquaintance he had been introduced, Martin did not know. Nor did he care enough to ask Ruth about him. In brief, Brissenden seemed to Martin pale and foolish, and was at once dismissed from his thoughts. An hour later he decided that Brissenden was also a boor, from the way he drifted from room to room, peering at the pictures and thrusting his nose into the books and magazines he lifted from the table or took down from the shelves. Although a stranger in the house, he finally withdrew from the rest of the company and settled himself in the middle of it all, crouched in a roomy Morris chair, reading steadily from a slender book he had taken from his pocket. As he read, he idly passed his fingers through his hair with a tender, absent motion. Martin noticed him no more that evening, save once when he saw him joking with what seemed great success with several of the young women.
source_text: It proved a momentous night for Martin, for after dinner he met Russ Brissenden. How he chanced to come there, whose friend he was or what acquaintance brought him, Martin did not know. Nor had he the curiosity to inquire about him of Ruth. In short, Brissenden struck Martin as anaemic and feather-brained, and was promptly dismissed from his mind. An hour later he decided that Brissenden was a boor as well, what of the way he prowled about from one room to another, staring at the pictures or poking his nose into books and magazines he picked up from the table or drew from the shelves. Though a stranger in the house he finally isolated himself in the midst of the company, huddling into a capacious Morris chair and reading steadily from a thin volume he had drawn from his pocket. As he read, he abstractedly ran his fingers, with a caressing movement, through his hair. Martin noticed him no more that evening, except once when he observed him chaffing with great apparent success with several of the young women.

text: “Yes, yes,” he said. “With you, though, you told me to find a position. The plain word job, like much of what I have written, repels you. It is harsh. Yet I can assure you it was no less harsh to me when everyone I knew urged it on me as though they were advising a depraved person to behave decently. But to go on. The issuing of what I had written, and the attention I received from the public, brought about a change in the very substance of your love. Martin Eden, once his work was finished, you would not marry. Your love for him was not strong enough to let you marry him. But now your love is strong enough, and I cannot escape the conclusion that its strength comes from publication and public attention. I do not even speak of royalties in your case, though I am sure they account for the change in your mother and father. Naturally, none of this flatters me. But worse than that, it makes me doubt love itself, sacred love. Is love really such a coarse thing that it has to feed on publication and public attention? It appears so. I have sat and thought about it until my head spun.”
source_text: “Yes, yes,” he said; “except in your case you told me to get a position. The homely word _job_, like much that I have written, offends you. It is brutal. But I assure you it was no less brutal to me when everybody I knew recommended it to me as they would recommend right conduct to an immoral creature. But to return. The publication of what I had written, and the public notice I received, wrought a change in the fibre of your love. Martin Eden, with his work all performed, you would not marry. Your love for him was not strong enough to enable you to marry him. But your love is now strong enough, and I cannot avoid the conclusion that its strength arises from the publication and the public notice. In your case I do not mention royalties, though I am certain that they apply to the change wrought in your mother and father. Of course, all this is not flattering to me. But worst of all, it makes me question love, sacred love. Is love so gross a thing that it must feed upon publication and public notice? It would seem so. I have sat and thought upon it till my head went around.”

text: “There was nothing for him to do. Their chief was a kindly old man, well advanced in years, but he held sway like a monarch. He had come upon the little valley and established the settlement—every bit of it in defiance of the law. Yet he had rifles, an abundance of cartridges, and those Kanakas, accustomed to hunting wild cattle and wild pigs, were excellent marksmen. No, there was no escape for Adrian Vale. He remained—for three months.”
source_text: “Nothing for me to do. Their leader was a kind old fellow, pretty far gone, but he ruled like a king. He had discovered the little valley and founded the settlement—all of which was against the law. But he had guns, plenty of ammunition, and those Kanakas, trained to the shooting of wild cattle and wild pig, were dead shots. No, there wasn’t any running away for Martin Eden. He stayed—for three months.”

text: “That’s plain enough,” he said. “It never occurred to me before. But that doesn’t mean they _had_ to have helped anybody, does it? It seems to me that ‘never helped a soul’ simply leaves the matter open as to whether they helped someone or not. I never gave it a thought before, and I’ll never say it again.”
source_text: “That’s pretty clear,” he said. “I never thought of it before. But it don’t mean they _must_ have helped somebody, does it? Seems to me that ‘never helped nobody’ just naturally fails to say whether or not they helped somebody. I never thought of it before, and I’ll never say it again.”

text: “You ought to have been up at Riley’s barn last night,” Jim said abruptly. “A bunch of the boys put on the gloves. There was a peach from West Oakland. They called him ‘The Rat.’ Slick as silk. Nobody could lay a hand on him. We were all wishing you’d been there. Where were you, anyway?”
source_text: “You should ’a’ ben up at Riley’s barn last night,” Jim announced inconsequently. “A lot of the fellers put on the gloves. There was a peach from West Oakland. They called ’m ‘The Rat.’ Slick as silk. No one could touch ’m. We was all wishin’ you was there. Where was you anyway?”

text: They drew up with a jolt at the entrance to the Grand Pavilion. The cab doors sprang wide. The passenger stepped at once to the ground. Instantly she was entangled in a spell of exquisite music and blinded by a pageant of lights and colour. Someone pressed a small square card into her hand, on which was printed a number—34. She glanced about and saw her cab twenty yards off already falling into line among the waiting throng of carriages, cabs, and motor cars. Then a man who seemed nothing but a starched shirt-front fluttered backward before her; and the next moment she was seated at a small table beside a railing climbed over by a jasmine vine.
source_text: They reined up with a plunge at the Casino entrance. The cab doors flew open. The fare stepped directly upon the floor. At once she was caught in a web of ravishing music and dazzled by a panorama of lights and colours. Some one slipped a little square card into her hand on which was printed a number—34. She looked around and saw her cab twenty yards away already lining up in its place among the waiting mass of carriages, cabs and motor cars. And then a man who seemed to be all shirt-front danced backward before her; and next she was seated at a little table by a railing over which climbed a jessamine vine.

text: We agreed that a dreadful fate seemed to hover over her. Few things could have been less unlikely. Milly, like some immense untouched tract of pine forest, was made to draw the lumberman’s eye. And we knew all too well the habits of the Badgers whenever fortune happened to favor them. They go straight to New York and lay their offerings at the feet of the girl who serves them beans in a beanery. Indeed, the alphabet itself seems to be in on it. The Sunday paper’s headline writer has his work laid out for him.
source_text: We agreed that the awful fate seemed to menace her. Few things were less improbable. Milly, like some vast virgin stretch of pine woods, was made to catch the lumberman’s eye. And well we knew the habits of the Badgers, once fortune smiled upon them. Straight to New York they hie, and lay their goods at the feet of the girl who serves them beans in a beanery. Why, the alphabet itself connives. The Sunday newspaper’s headliner’s work is cut for him.

text: “Listen,” says Tobin. “You’ve no ear for the gift of prophecy or the miracles of the inspired. What did the palmist lady tell you from my hand? It’s coming true before your eyes. ‘Watch out,’ says she, ‘for a dark man and a light woman; they’ll bring you trouble.’ Have you forgotten the black man, though he got some of it back from my fist? Can you show me a fairer woman than the blonde lady who caused my hat to fall into the water? And where is the dollar sixty-five I had in my vest when we left the shooting gallery?”
source_text: “Listen,” says Tobin. “Ye’ve no ear for the gift of prophecy or the miracles of the inspired. What did the palmist lady tell ye out of me hand? ’Tis coming true before your eyes. ‘Look out,’ says she, ‘for a dark man and a light woman; they’ll bring ye trouble.’ Have ye forgot the nigger man, though he got some of it back from me fist? Can ye show me a lighter woman than the blonde lady that was the cause of me hat falling in the water? And where’s the dollar sixty-five I had in me vest when we left the shooting gallery?”

text: Among the customers at Bogle’s was a young man named Seeders, who worked in a laundry office. Mr. Seeders was slight and fair-haired, and looked as though he had lately been rough-dried and starched. He was too shy to hope for Aileen’s attention, and so he generally sat at one of Tildy’s tables, where he applied himself to silence and boiled weakfish.
source_text: Among the customers at Bogle’s was a young man named Seeders, who worked in a laundry office. Mr. Seeders was thin and had light hair, and appeared to have been recently rough-dried and starched. He was too diffident to aspire to Aileen’s notice; so he usually sat at one of Tildy’s tables, where he devoted himself to silence and boiled weakfish.

text: Abe lodged and took his morning meal at Mrs. Doolin’s, two blocks away. Mrs. Doolin had a daughter called Lottie. The roundabout hint has been thrown away—you have guessed it already—Abe worshiped Lottie. She colored every one of his thoughts; she was the concentrated essence of all that was wholesome and medicable—the apothecary’s books held nothing comparable to her. Yet Abe was shy, and his hopes stayed undissolved in the solvent of his diffidence and dread. Behind his counter he was a man apart, serenely aware of peculiar knowledge and merit; beyond it he was a weak-kneed, near-sighted, trolley-cursed wanderer, in garments that hung ill upon him, stained with drugs and breathing of socotrine aloes and valerianate of ammonia.
source_text: Ikey roomed and breakfasted at Mrs. Riddle’s two squares away. Mrs. Riddle had a daughter named Rosy. The circumlocution has been in vain—you must have guessed it—Ikey adored Rosy. She tinctured all his thoughts; she was the compound extract of all that was chemically pure and officinal—the dispensatory contained nothing equal to her. But Ikey was timid, and his hopes remained insoluble in the menstruum of his backwardness and fears. Behind his counter he was a superior being, calmly conscious of special knowledge and worth; outside he was a weak-kneed, purblind, motorman-cursed rambler, with ill-fitting clothes stained with chemicals and smelling of socotrine aloes and valerianate of ammonia.

text: It was our devotion to Nature’s Infallible Artistic Adjustment that moved us. We could not surrender her to a lumberman, doubly damned by wealth and provincialism. We shuddered to imagine Milly, her voice softened and her elbows covered, pouring tea in the marble teepee of a tree murderer. No! In Cypher’s she belonged—in the bacon smoke, the perfume of cabbage, the grand Wagnerian chorus of hurled ironstone china and clattering casters.
source_text: It was our love of the Unerring Artistic Adjustment of Nature that inspired us. We could not give her over to a lumberman, doubly accursed by wealth and provincialism. We shuddered to think of Milly, with her voice modulated and her elbows covered, pouring tea in the marble teepee of a tree murderer. No! In Cypher’s she belonged—in the bacon smoke, the cabbage perfume, the grand, Wagnerian chorus of hurled ironstone china and rattling casters.

text: Twice the long hands of the clocks made their circuit, and the royalties dwindled on their _al fresco_ thrones, then buzzed and clattered away in their state carriages. The music was packed back into its wooden cases and leather and baize bags. The waiters drew away the cloths with marked deliberation near the plain figure seated almost alone.
source_text: Twice the long hands of the clocks went round, Royalties thinned from their _al fresco_ thrones, and buzzed or clattered away in their vehicles of state. The music retired into cases of wood and bags of leather and baize. Waiters removed cloths pointedly near the plain figure sitting almost alone.

text: “Nine o’clock,” said Mr. Mallory. “Dinner’s at seven. At eight, Lottie takes herself off to bed with a headache. At nine, old Vannetti lets me through into his back yard, where there’s a loose plank in Ransom’s fence, next door. I go under her window and help her down the fire-escape. We’ve got to make it early, on account of the preacher. It’s all dead simple if Lottie doesn’t balk when the curtain goes up. Can you fix me one of them powders, Ike?”
source_text: “Nine o’clock,” said Mr. McGowan. “Supper’s at seven. At eight Rosy goes to bed with a headache. At nine old Parvenzano lets me through to his back yard, where there’s a board off Riddle’s fence, next door. I go under her window and help her down the fire-escape. We’ve got to make it early on the preacher’s account. It’s all dead easy if Rosy don’t balk when the flag drops. Can you fix me one of them powders, Ikey?”

text: Jerry closed the trap and lashed his oat-fed horse. The beat of the hoofs rose and faltered, yet it could not quite smother the roar of his swearing. He hurled his curses upward at the stars, hoarse and choking, with a kind of gurgling fury; he slashed savagely with his whip at the vehicles he passed; he flung a stream of fierce, shifting oaths and imprecations down the streets, until a late truck driver, creeping homeward, heard him and was put to shame. But Jerry knew where to turn, and he made for it at a gallop.
