source_text: Jerry shut down the trap and slashed his oat-fed horse. The clatter of hoofs strangled but could not drown the sound of his profanity. He shouted choking and gurgling curses at the starry heavens; he cut viciously with his whip at passing vehicles; he scattered fierce and ever-changing oaths and imprecations along the streets, so that a late truck driver, crawling homeward, heard and was abashed. But he knew his recourse, and made for it at a gallop.

text: The young man followed her upstairs. A pale light from no discernible source softened the darkness of the corridors. They moved without a sound over a stair carpet that its own maker would have disowned. It seemed to have gone vegetable, to have turned, in that heavy, sunless air, into thick lichen or spreading moss, patching the stairs and yielding underfoot like something alive. At each landing, empty niches opened in the wall. Perhaps plants had once been placed there. If so, they had perished in that foul, tainted atmosphere. Maybe saints’ statues had stood there, though it was easy enough to imagine imps and devils dragging them out in the dark and hauling them down to the unholy depths of some furnished pit below.
source_text: The young man followed her up the stairs. A faint light from no particular source mitigated the shadows of the halls. They trod noiselessly upon a stair carpet that its own loom would have forsworn. It seemed to have become vegetable; to have degenerated in that rank, sunless air to lush lichen or spreading moss that grew in patches to the staircase and was viscid under the foot like organic matter. At each turn of the stairs were vacant niches in the wall. Perhaps plants had once been set within them. If so they had died in that foul and tainted air. It may be that statues of the saints had stood there, but it was not difficult to conceive that imps and devils had dragged them forth in the darkness and down to the unholy depths of some furnished pit below.

text: Soapy rose from his bench and sauntered out of the square, crossing the flat black expanse of pavement where Broadway and Fifth Avenue merge and run together. He turned up Broadway and stopped before a shining café, where each night the finest fruits of the vine, the silkworm, and protoplasm assemble.
source_text: Soapy left his bench and strolled out of the square and across the level sea of asphalt, where Broadway and Fifth Avenue flow together. Up Broadway he turned, and halted at a glittering café, where are gathered together nightly the choicest products of the grape, the silkworm and the protoplasm.

text: For a moment Dulcie forgot everything else except that she was beautiful, and that life was about to raise a corner of its mysterious veil so she might glimpse its marvels. No gentleman had ever asked her out before. Now she was stepping, for a brief little while, into the glittering, exalted world.
source_text: Dulcie forgot everything else for a moment except that she was beautiful, and that life was about to lift a corner of its mysterious veil for her to observe its wonders. No gentleman had ever asked her out before. Now she was going for a brief moment into the glitter and exalted show.

text: A withered leaf drifted down into Soapy’s lap. It was Jack Frost’s calling card. He is considerate to the settled folk of Madison Square, and gives them due notice of his yearly visit. At the crossing of four streets he passes his card to the North Wind, footman of the great house of All Outdoors, that those dwelling there may prepare themselves.
source_text: A dead leaf fell in Soapy’s lap. That was Jack Frost’s card. Jack is kind to the regular denizens of Madison Square, and gives fair warning of his annual call. At the corners of four streets he hands his pasteboard to the North Wind, footman of the mansion of All Outdoors, so that the inhabitants thereof may make ready.

text: After he had gone a few yards farther, he glanced at the card without interest. Then, startled, he turned it over and studied it again more closely. One side was blank; on the other, in ink, were written three words: “The Green Door.” Then Rudolf saw, a few paces ahead of him, a man drop the card the negro had handed him as he went by. Rudolf picked it up. It was printed with the dentist’s name and address, along with the usual list of “plate work,” “bridge work,” and “crowns,” and the misleading promises of “painless” treatment.
source_text: When he had travelled a few yards further he glanced at the card indifferently. Surprised, he turned it over and looked again with interest. One side of the card was blank; on the other was written in ink three words, “The Green Door.” And then Rudolf saw, three steps in front of him, a man throw down the card the negro had given him as he passed. Rudolf picked it up. It was printed with the dentist’s name and address and the usual schedule of “plate work” and “bridge work” and “crowns,” and specious promises of “painless” operations.

text: I beg you, let the action pause while Chorus steps down to the footlights and sheds a dirge-like tear over the ample girth of Mr. Hoover. Let the pipes be tuned to the tragedy of tallow, to the curse of heaviness, the disaster of corpulence. Trimmed down, Falstaff might have lent more romance to the ton than Romeo’s frail ribs could ever yield to the pound. A lover may sigh, but he must not wheeze. The fat men are consigned to the retinue of Momus. In vain does the most faithful heart beat above a fifty-two-inch belt. Begone, Hoover! Hoover, at forty-five, flushed and foolish, might have borne Helen herself away; Hoover, forty-five, flushed, foolish, and fat is food for damnation. You never had a chance, Hoover.
source_text: I pray you let the drama halt while Chorus stalks to the footlights and drops an epicedian tear upon the fatness of Mr. Hoover. Tune the pipes to the tragedy of tallow, the bane of bulk, the calamity of corpulence. Tried out, Falstaff might have rendered more romance to the ton than would have Romeo’s rickety ribs to the ounce. A lover may sigh, but he must not puff. To the train of Momus are the fat men remanded. In vain beats the faithfullest heart above a 52-inch belt. Avaunt, Hoover! Hoover, forty-five, flush and foolish, might carry off Helen herself; Hoover, forty-five, flush, foolish and fat is meat for perdition. There was never a chance for you, Hoover.

text: It was Kraft who first put into words the fear that, in some hidden corner, each of us must already have been carrying. The subject arose, naturally enough, in the course of certain questions of art on which we were all at work. One of us drew a comparison between the harmony of a Haydn symphony and pistachio ice cream and the exquisite fitness of Milly and Cypher’s.
source_text: It was Kraft who first voiced the fear that each of us must have held latently. It came up apropos, of course, of certain questions of art at which we were hammering. One of us compared the harmony existing between a Haydn symphony and pistache ice cream to the exquisite congruity between Milly and Cypher’s.

text: On a bench beside the rail sat a young woman attired as though for scarlet motorcars, her hair the color of an unburned meerschaum. As Tobin passed her, he struck her foot without meaning to, and, being courteous to ladies when he had been drinking, he made a clumsy effort to touch his hat while muttering an apology. Instead he sent the hat flying from his head, and the wind bore it away over the side.
source_text: On a seat against the railing was a young woman dressed suitable for red automobiles, with hair the colour of an unsmoked meerschaum. In passing by, Tobin kicks her foot without intentions, and, being polite to ladies when in drink, he tries to give his hat a twist while apologising. But he knocks it off, and the wind carries it overboard.

text: We went into the enchanted chicken coop, which had been dressed up in a mysterious way with red cloth and pictures of hands marked by lines crossing them like the center of a railroad. The sign over the door said it was Madame Zozo, the Egyptian Palmist. Inside there was a fat woman in a red jumper, embroidered all over with pothooks and little beasts. Tobin gave her ten cents and held out one of his hands. She lifted Tobin’s hand, which was as much like a drayhorse’s hoof as anything, and studied it to see whether he had come for a stone in the frog or a cast shoe.
source_text: We went into the enchanted chicken coop, which was fixed mysterious with red cloth and pictures of hands with lines crossing ’em like a railroad centre. The sign over the door says it is Madame Zozo the Egyptian Palmist. There was a fat woman inside in a red jumper with pothooks and beasties embroidered upon it. Tobin gives her ten cents and extends one of his hands. She lifts Tobin’s hand, which is own brother to the hoof of a drayhorse, and examines it to see whether ’tis a stone in the frog or a cast shoe he has come for.

text: “I want you to take a message to that young lady on the bench there. Tell her I’m on my way to the station, bound for San Francisco, where I’m to join that Alaska moose-hunting expedition. Tell her that, since she has ordered me neither to speak to her nor to write to her, I am using this means of making one final appeal to her sense of justice, for the sake of what has been. Tell her that to condemn and cast off a man who has not earned such treatment, without giving him her reasons or even a chance to answer, is unlike the nature I have always believed hers to be. Tell her that in doing this I have, to some extent, disobeyed her commands, in the hope that she may still be willing to see justice done. Go, and tell her that.”
source_text: “I want you to take a message to that young lady on that bench. Tell her I am on my way to the station, to leave for San Francisco, where I shall join that Alaska moose-hunting expedition. Tell her that, since she has commanded me neither to speak nor to write to her, I take this means of making one last appeal to her sense of justice, for the sake of what has been. Tell her that to condemn and discard one who has not deserved such treatment, without giving him her reasons or a chance to explain is contrary to her nature as I believe it to be. Tell her that I have thus, to a certain degree, disobeyed her injunctions, in the hope that she may yet be inclined to see justice done. Go, and tell her that.”

text: “He doesn’t look much from down here,” said Miss Ainsley. “You ought to see him from my room. You know you can see the stars in the daytime if you’re at the bottom of a well. At night my room is like the drift of a coal mine, and it makes Billy Harrow seem like the great diamond brooch with which Night clasps her kimono.”
source_text: “He doesn’t show up very well from down here,” said Miss Leeson. “You ought to see him from my room. You know you can see stars even in the daytime from the bottom of a well. At night my room is like the shaft of a coal mine, and it makes Billy Jackson look like the big diamond pin that Night fastens her kimono with.”

text: But I beg you to notice Mrs. James Williams—Hattie Chalmers, as she was once called—the former belle of Cloverdale. Pale blue, if the bride chooses, belongs to the bride; and this shade she had done it honor. The moss rosebud had gladly lent its pink to her cheeks—and as for the violet!—well, her eyes would serve admirably just as they were, thank you. A useless length of white chaf—oh no, he was steering the auto car—of white chiffon, or perhaps grenadine or tulle, was tied beneath her chin, pretending to keep her bonnet in place. But you and I know very well that the hatpins did the real work.
source_text: But I beg you to observe Mrs. James Williams—Hattie Chalmers that was—once the belle of Cloverdale. Pale-blue is the bride’s, if she will; and this colour she had honoured. Willingly had the moss rosebud loaned to her cheeks of its pink—and as for the violet!—her eyes will do very well as they are, thank you. A useless strip of white chaf—oh, no, he was guiding the auto car—of white chiffon—or perhaps it was grenadine or tulle—was tied beneath her chin, pretending to hold her bonnet in place. But you know as well as I do that the hatpins did the work.

text: “Elias, my dear, you are being foolish. You must go on with your lessons. It is not as though I had given up my piano and taken up some other trade. In teaching I am still learning; I am always among my music. And we can live as merrily as princes on fifteen dollars a week. You must not think of leaving Mr. Varden.”
source_text: “Joe, dear, you are silly. You must keep on at your studies. It is not as if I had quit my music and gone to work at something else. While I teach I learn. I am always with my music. And we can live as happily as millionaires on $15 a week. You mustn’t think of leaving Mr. Magister.”

text: They were very happy while their money held out. So is everyone—but I will not be cynical. Their ambitions were perfectly plain. Joe was to be able very soon to produce pictures that old gentlemen with thin side-whiskers and fat pocketbooks would nearly knock each other down in his studio for the chance to buy. Delia was to grow acquainted with Music, and then grow contemptuous of it, so that when she found the orchestra seats and boxes unsold, she could nurse a sore throat over lobster in a private dining room and refuse to go on stage.
source_text: They were mighty happy as long as their money lasted. So is every—but I will not be cynical. Their aims were very clear and defined. Joe was to become capable very soon of turning out pictures that old gentlemen with thin side-whiskers and thick pocketbooks would sandbag one another in his studio for the privilege of buying. Delia was to become familiar and then contemptuous with Music, so that when she saw the orchestra seats and boxes unsold she could have sore throat and lobster in a private dining-room and refuse to go on the stage.

text: Marlowe smoothed the missionary’s ready-made necktie, tugged his diminishing cuffs into view, tilted his hat to a murderous angle, and drifted toward the young lady. He rolled his eyes at her, was seized with little fits of coughing and “hemms,” grinned, simpered, and ran unchecked through the shameless, insulting ritual of the boulevard flirt. With one eye Marlowe noted that the policeman was staring at him steadily. The young woman stepped away a pace or two and once more devoted her grave attention to the row of shaving mugs. Marlowe went after her, thrust himself boldly to her side, lifted his hat, and said:
source_text: Soapy straightened the lady missionary’s ready-made tie, dragged his shrinking cuffs into the open, set his hat at a killing cant and sidled toward the young woman. He made eyes at her, was taken with sudden coughs and “hems,” smiled, smirked and went brazenly through the impudent and contemptible litany of the “masher.” With half an eye Soapy saw that the policeman was watching him fixedly. The young woman moved away a few steps, and again bestowed her absorbed attention upon the shaving mugs. Soapy followed, boldly stepping to her side, raised his hat and said:

text: On the first landing she met him returning with the astronomer cradled in his arms. He paused and set free the practised scalpel of his tongue, though not in any loud fashion. Little by little, Mrs. Vale collapsed like a starched garment slipping from a hook. Long afterward, folds remained in her mind and in her body. Now and then her inquisitive lodgers would ask what the doctor had said to her.
source_text: On the first landing she met him coming back bearing the astronomer in his arms. He stopped and let loose the practised scalpel of his tongue, not loudly. Gradually Mrs. Parker crumpled as a stiff garment that slips down from a nail. Ever afterward there remained crumples in her mind and body. Sometimes her curious roomers would ask her what the doctor said to her.

text: Gabriel had played his trump card, and those of us who could not match it were called up for scrutiny. Off to one side I noticed a group of professional sureties in sober black, with collars fastened at the back; but there seemed to be some difficulty over their land titles, and they did not seem to be succeeding in getting any of us released.
source_text: Gabriel had played his trump; and those of us who could not follow suit were arraigned for examination. I noticed at one side a gathering of professional bondsmen in solemn black and collars that buttoned behind; but it seemed there was some trouble about their real estate titles; and they did not appear to be getting any of us out.

text: Expression on these matters was drawn from E. Rushmore Calthorpe by the third person at our table. While Calthorpe was describing to me the landscape along the Siberian Railway, the orchestra slipped into a medley. The final strain was “Dixie,” and as the stirring notes broke out they were nearly drowned by a loud burst of applause from nearly every table.
source_text: Expression on these subjects was precipitated from E. Rushmore Coglan by the third corner to our table. While Coglan was describing to me the topography along the Siberian Railway the orchestra glided into a medley. The concluding air was “Dixie,” and as the exhilarating notes tumbled forth they were almost overpowered by a great clapping of hands from almost every table.

text: “Well, yes, for a while we kept up a correspondence,” said the other. “But after a year or two we lost sight of one another. You understand, the West is a pretty broad field, and I was pushing about it pretty briskly myself. But I know Ned will come here to meet me if he’s still alive, for he was always the most faithful, steadfast old fellow in the world. He’ll never forget. I came a thousand miles to stand in this doorway tonight, and it’s worth it if my old comrade shows up.”
source_text: “Well, yes, for a time we corresponded,” said the other. “But after a year or two we lost track of each other. You see, the West is a pretty big proposition, and I kept hustling around over it pretty lively. But I know Jimmy will meet me here if he’s alive, for he always was the truest, stanchest old chap in the world. He’ll never forget. I came a thousand miles to stand in this door to-night, and it’s worth it if my old partner turns up.”

text: Unquiet, mutable, and as evanescent as time itself is a certain great multitude of the inhabitants of the red-brick quarter of the lower West Side. Homeless, yet lodged in a hundred dwellings, they pass from furnished room to furnished room, eternal wanderers—wanderers in their roofs, wanderers in their hearts and thoughts. They croon “Home, Sweet Home” to a syncopated tune; they bear their _lares et penates_ in a hatbox; their vine winds itself about a feathered hat; a rubber plant serves them for a fig tree.
source_text: Restless, shifting, fugacious as time itself is a certain vast bulk of the population of the red brick district of the lower West Side. Homeless, they have a hundred homes. They flit from furnished room to furnished room, transients forever—transients in abode, transients in heart and mind. They sing “Home, Sweet Home” in ragtime; they carry their _lares et penates_ in a bandbox; their vine is entwined about a picture hat; a rubber plant is their fig tree.

text: “The way you talk,” says Tobin, blowing through his moustache and thumping the table with his fist, “is a sore trial to my patience. Good fortune was meant to come from the bend of your nose, yet you bring forth fruit only like the strike of a drum. With all your talk of books, you are like the wind whistling through a chink. Indeed now, I’d think the palm of my hand had lied if the nigger man and the fair-haired lady had not come true and—”
source_text: “The talk of ye,” says Tobin, blowing through his moustache and pounding the table with his fist, “is an eyesore to me patience. There was good luck promised out of the crook of your nose, but ye bear fruit like the bang of a drum. Ye resemble, with your noise of books, the wind blowing through a crack. Sure, now, I would be thinking the palm of me hand lied but for the coming true of the nigger man and the blonde lady and—”

text: “Beg pardon,” said he, “but that’s one question I can never abide. What difference does it make where a fellow was born? Is it just to measure a man by the name on his mailing address? I’ve come across Bluegrass men who shunned liquor, Old Dominioners who traced no line to Pocahontas, Hoosiers who had never penned a book, Mexicans who wore no velvet breeches with silver pieces stitched along the seams, queer Englishmen, prodigal Yankees, hard-hearted Southerners, small-minded Westerners, and city men from the great metropolis who were too pressed to halt for an hour in the street and watch a one-armed grocery boy wrap cranberries in brown paper sacks. Let a man stand on his own feet, and don’t burden him with the badge of any region.”
source_text: “Excuse me,” said he, “but that’s a question I never like to hear asked. What does it matter where a man is from? Is it fair to judge a man by his post-office address? Why, I’ve seen Kentuckians who hated whiskey, Virginians who weren’t descended from Pocahontas, Indianians who hadn’t written a novel, Mexicans who didn’t wear velvet trousers with silver dollars sewed along the seams, funny Englishmen, spendthrift Yankees, cold-blooded Southerners, narrow-minded Westerners, and New Yorkers who were too busy to stop for an hour on the street to watch a one-armed grocer’s clerk do up cranberries in paper bags. Let a man be a man and don’t handicap him with the label of any section.”

text: The patrons at Marden’s were her captives. She could attend to six tables at a time. Those who were pressed for time held back their impatience for the pleasure of merely watching her quick, graceful figure glide among them. Those who had finished their meals ordered more, so they might linger longer in the warmth of her smiles. Every man in the room—and men made up the greater part of the company—endeavored to leave some mark upon her.
source_text: The customers at Bogle’s were her slaves. Six tables full she could wait upon at once. They who were in a hurry restrained their impatience for the joy of merely gazing upon her swiftly moving, graceful figure. They who had finished eating ate more that they might continue in the light of her smiles. Every man there—and they were mostly men—tried to make his impression upon her.

text: “Take a seat,” said the Prince with perfect composure. “I cannot accept your amendment. Women are the born adversaries of clocks and, by that very fact, the allies of all who would win their freedom from these monsters that tally our follies and curtail our pleasures. If you will trust me thus far, I should ask you to tell me your story.”
source_text: “Sit down,” said the Prince calmly. “I do not accept your addition. Women are the natural enemies of clocks, and, therefore, the allies of those who would seek liberation from these monsters that measure our follies and limit our pleasures. If you will so far confide in me I would ask you to relate to me your story.”

text: “Excuse me,” I said, “but my curiosity was not entirely idle. I know the South, and when the band strikes up ‘Dixie,’ I like to watch the crowd. I’ve come to believe that the man who applauds that tune with unusual fervor and an obvious show of sectional loyalty is almost always a native of either Secaucus, New Jersey, or that stretch of this city between the Murray Hill Lyceum and the Harlem River. I was just about to test that theory by asking this gentleman when you broke in with your own — I have to admit, broader theory.”
source_text: “Pardon me,” I said, “but my curiosity was not altogether an idle one. I know the South, and when the band plays ‘Dixie’ I like to observe. I have formed the belief that the man who applauds that air with special violence and ostensible sectional loyalty is invariably a native of either Secaucus, N.J., or the district between Murray Hill Lyceum and the Harlem River, this city. I was about to put my opinion to the test by inquiring of this gentleman when you interrupted with your own—larger theory, I must confess.”

text: This behavior on the part of the Clover Leaf members calls for a word of explanation. Behind the association hall was a smaller room rented by the club. There, personal disputes that began out on the ballroom floor were resolved man to man, with nature’s own weapons, under the board’s supervision. For several years, no lady could have said she had seen a fight at a Clover Leaf dance. The club’s gentlemen members made sure of that.
source_text: This movement on the part of the Clover Leaf members requires a word of elucidation. Back of the association hall was a smaller room rented by the club. In this room personal difficulties that arose on the ballroom floor were settled, man to man, with the weapons of nature, under the supervision of the board. No lady could say that she had witnessed a fight at a Clover Leaf hop in several years. Its gentlemen members guaranteed that.

text: Prince Michael of the Electorate of Valleluna sat upon his favorite bench in the park. The cool September night stirred his blood like some rare and strengthening wine. The benches were far from full, for idlers in parks, with their torpid blood, are quick to sense the first sharpness of autumn and to hurry home from it. The moon had just risen above the roofs of the row of houses that enclosed the quadrangle on the east. Children laughed and played around the delicately spraying fountain. In the darker corners fauns and hamadryads made love, heedless of the mortal eyes that looked on. A hand organ—Philomel by the favor of our stage carpenter, Fancy—warbled and droned in a neighboring street. Beyond the enchanted limits of the little park, street cars hissed and mewed, and the lumbering trains roared like tigers and lions seeking an opening. And above the trees gleamed the great, round, radiant face of an illuminated clock in the tower of an ancient public building.
source_text: Prince Michael, of the Electorate of Valleluna, sat on his favourite bench in the park. The coolness of the September night quickened the life in him like a rare, tonic wine. The benches were not filled; for park loungers, with their stagnant blood, are prompt to detect and fly home from the crispness of early autumn. The moon was just clearing the roofs of the range of dwellings that bounded the quadrangle on the east. Children laughed and played about the fine-sprayed fountain. In the shadowed spots fauns and hamadryads wooed, unconscious of the gaze of mortal eyes. A hand organ—Philomel by the grace of our stage carpenter, Fancy—fluted and droned in a side street. Around the enchanted boundaries of the little park street cars spat and mewed and the stilted trains roared like tigers and lions prowling for a place to enter. And above the trees shone the great, round, shining face of an illuminated clock in the tower of an antique public building.

text: Mr. McGowan was also trying to catch the bright smiles Rosy was flinging around. But he was no outfielder like Ikey; he caught them straight off the bat. Even so, he was Ikey’s friend and customer, and he often stopped by the Blue Light Drug Store to have a bruise dabbed with iodine or a cut sealed up with rubber plaster after a pleasant evening spent on the Bowery.
source_text: Mr. McGowan was also striving to catch the bright smiles tossed about by Rosy. But he was no outfielder as Ikey was; he picked them off the bat. At the same time he was Ikey’s friend and customer, and often dropped in at the Blue Light Drug Store to have a bruise painted with iodine or get a cut rubber-plastered after a pleasant evening spent along the Bowery.

text: “Wait,” he said gravely, “until the clock strikes. I have wealth, power, and learning beyond most men, but when the clock strikes I am afraid. Stay with me until then. This woman will be yours. You have the promise of the hereditary Prince of Valleluna. On the day you are married I will give you $100,000 and a palace on the Hudson. But there must be no clocks in that palace—they measure our follies and put bounds on our pleasures. Do you agree?”
source_text: “Wait,” he said solemnly, “till the clock strikes. I have wealth and power and knowledge above most men, but when the clock strikes I am afraid. Stay by me until then. This woman shall be yours. You have the word of the hereditary Prince of Valleluna. On the day of your marriage I will give you $100,000 and a palace on the Hudson. But there must be no clocks in that palace—they measure our follies and limit our pleasures. Do you agree to that?”

text: My reverie was broken by a tremendous uproar and struggle in another part of the café. Above the heads of the seated patrons, I could see E. Rushmore Coglan locked in fierce combat with a man I did not know. They battled among the tables like Titans; glasses shattered, men snatched up their hats and were knocked down, a brunette screamed, and a blonde began to sing “Teasing.”
source_text: My meditations were interrupted by a tremendous noise and conflict in another part of the café. I saw above the heads of the seated patrons E. Rushmore Coglan and a stranger to me engaged in terrific battle. They fought between the tables like Titans, and glasses crashed, and men caught their hats up and were knocked down, and a brunette screamed, and a blonde began to sing “Teasing.”

text: So she had her point; but for Terry O’Sullivan the evening’s honours came one after another in quick succession. He tossed back his curls, smiled, and moved with effortless ease through the seven exercises for gaining grace in your own room before an open window, ten minutes a day. He danced like a faun; he brought in elegance and style and atmosphere; the words danced lightly on his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in a row with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan had brought.
source_text: Thus she scored; but to Terry O’Sullivan the honours of the evening fell thick and fast. He shook his curls; he smiled and went easily through the seven motions for acquiring grace in your own room before an open window ten minutes each day. He danced like a faun; he introduced manner and style and atmosphere; his words came trippingly upon his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in succession with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan brought.

text: “By the two signs,” Tobin answers, trying to make himself clear, “which you are showing according to the Egyptian palmist’s reading of the sole of my hand, you’ve been selected to balance out with good luck the lines of trouble leading to the Negro man and the blonde lady with her feet crossed in the boat, along with the financial loss of a dollar sixty-five, all of it so far coming true according to Hoyle.”
source_text: “By the two signs,” answers Tobin, trying to explain, “which ye display according to the reading of the Egyptian palmist from the sole of me hand, ye’ve been nominated to offset with good luck the lines of trouble leading to the nigger man and the blonde lady with her feet crossed in the boat, besides the financial loss of a dollar sixty-five, all so far fulfilled according to Hoyle.”

text: “You don’t understand the social machine, father. She belongs to the current that keeps it moving. Every hour and every minute of her life is booked days ahead. I’ve got to have that girl, father, or this town will be nothing but a blackjack swamp from now on. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”
source_text: “You don’t know the social mill, dad. She’s part of the stream that turns it. Every hour and minute of her time is arranged for days in advance. I must have that girl, dad, or this town is a blackjack swamp forevermore. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

text: “Some new fellow,” said Marjorie. “Last night, when I was coming home at Twenty-third and Sixth. He came drifting up, nice and smooth, and made a pass at me. I cut him off sharp, and he slunk away; but he dogged me clear down to Eighteenth and tried his nonsense again. Well, I boxed his ears a beauty, right across the cheek. Then he gave me this look. Is it terribly bad, Till? I’d hate for Mr. Hargrove to notice it when he comes in for his tea and toast at ten.”
source_text: “Fresh guy,” explained Aileen, “last night as I was going home at Twenty-third and Sixth. Sashayed up, so he did, and made a break. I turned him down, cold, and he made a sneak; but followed me down to Eighteenth, and tried his hot air again. Gee! but I slapped him a good one, side of the face. Then he give me that eye. Does it look real awful, Til? I should hate that Mr. Nicholson should see it when he comes in for his tea and toast at ten.”

text: “And that’s what I was coming to,” said the old man, more quietly. “That’s why I asked you in. Something’s wrong with you, boy. I’ve been seeing it for two weeks. Out with it. I reckon I could put my hands on eleven million within twenty-four hours, not counting the real estate. If it’s your liver, there’s the _Rambler_ down in the bay, coaled and ready to steam for the Bahamas in two days.”
source_text: “And that’s what I was coming to,” said the old man, less boisterously. “That’s why I asked you to come in. There’s something going wrong with you, boy. I’ve been noticing it for two weeks. Out with it. I guess I could lay my hands on eleven millions within twenty-four hours, besides the real estate. If it’s your liver, there’s the _Rambler_ down in the bay, coaled, and ready to steam down to the Bahamas in two days.”

text: “Bank drafts for two million,” he concluded, “and a thousand a day accumulating from my claims. And now I want some beef stew and canned peaches. I haven’t stepped off the train since I pulled out of Seattle, and I’m hungry. The swill they feed you on Pullmans doesn’t count. You gentlemen order whatever you like.”
source_text: “Bank drafts for two millions,” was his summing up, “and a thousand a day piling up from my claims. And now I want some beef stew and canned peaches. I never got off the train since I mushed out of Seattle, and I’m hungry. The stuff the niggers feed you on Pullmans don’t count. You gentlemen order what you want.”

text: A footman informed her that this was her cab check, and that she must hand it to the man at the doorway. The man took it from her and summoned the number. Only three hansoms were waiting in rank. The driver of one of them went off and roused Ned, who was dozing in his cab. He swore bitterly, mounted the captain’s perch, and brought his vehicle round to the pier. His fare got in, and the cab swept away into the cool depths of the park by the shortest homeward turns.
source_text: A waiter told her it was her cab check, and that she should give it to the man at the entrance. This man took it, and called the number. Only three hansoms stood in line. The driver of one of them went and routed out Jerry asleep in his cab. He swore deeply, climbed to the captain’s bridge and steered his craft to the pier. His fare entered, and the cab whirled into the cool fastnesses of the park along the shortest homeward cuts.

text: At the young woman’s right hand sat a man of some twenty-four years. He was stoutly made, brisk in his movements, square-jawed, and wore an air of easy good humor. Yet if his portrait recalls that of Philip Wentworth, strip from it every trace of gentility and suburban grace. This fellow was of the rough pavements and the cruel angles of the town. His eyes ranged alertly over the room, and he seemed almost to resent the very cobbles beneath the feet of those upon whom he gazed from his seat above them.
source_text: At this girl’s right hand sat a young man about twenty-four. He was well-built, active, strong-jawed and good-natured. But if his description seems to follow that of James Williams, divest it of anything Cloverdalian. This man belonged to hard streets and sharp corners. He looked keenly about him, seeming to begrudge the asphalt under the feet of those upon whom he looked down from his perch.

text: “I heard ye,” came the verbal crockery. “Ye can make apologies to the trash in the street for planting yer clumsy feet on the hems of their frocks, but ye’d stride across the throat of yer own wife the length of a washing-line and never say, ‘Kiss me fut,’ and I’m certain it’s no great distance from her standin’ at the windy every day rubbin’ it clean for ye while the victuals go cold—such as there is money to buy after ye’ve drunk up yer wages at Hooligan’s every Saturday night, with the gas man callin’ here twice already to-day for his.”
source_text: “I heard ye,” came the oral substitutes for kitchenware. “Ye can apollygise to riff-raff of the streets for settin’ yer unhandy feet on the tails of their frocks, but ye’d walk on the neck of yer wife the length of a clothes-line without so much as a ‘Kiss me fut,’ and I’m sure it’s that long from rubberin’ out the windy for ye and the victuals cold such as there’s money to buy after drinkin’ up yer wages at Gallegher’s every Saturday evenin’, and the gas man here twice to-day for his.”

text: I’ll tell you about that apartment. The building was the usual sort in New York, with its vestibule laid in Parian marble and cobblestones above the first floor. Our rooms were three—well, not exactly flights—to climb up. My lady had taken it unfurnished and then supplied the customary furnishings herself: an 1903 antique parlour set with no upholstery, an oil chromo of geishas in a Harlem tea room, a rubber plant, and a husband.
source_text: I’ll tell you about that flat. The house was the ordinary thing in New York, paved with Parian marble in the entrance hall and cobblestones above the first floor. Our flat was three—well, not flights—climbs up. My mistress rented it unfurnished, and put in the regular things—1903 antique unholstered parlour set, oil chromo of geishas in a Harlem tea house, rubber plant and husband.

text: Before the puckered mirror were set portraits of Lord Kildare, Patrick Dempsey, the Countess of Ravenswood, and Donatello. On one wall hung a plaster cast of a MacNamara in a Roman casque. Beside it glared a lurid chromolithograph of a saffron child attacking an incendiary butterfly. This was Maisie’s last word in art; yet it had never been challenged. Her peace had never been broken by rumors of pilfered vestments; no connoisseur had lifted his brows at her childish naturalist.
source_text: Against the wrinkly mirror stood pictures of General Kitchener, William Muldoon, the Duchess of Marlborough, and Benvenuto Cellini. Against one wall was a plaster of Paris plaque of an O’Callahan in a Roman helmet. Near it was a violent oleograph of a lemon-coloured child assaulting an inflammatory butterfly. This was Dulcie’s final judgment in art; but it had never been upset. Her rest had never been disturbed by whispers of stolen copes; no critic had elevated his eyebrows at her infantile entomologist.

text: The girl in the fruitful hat swiftly caught her companion by the arm and whispered in his ear. The young man promptly showed that he was capable of quick action. Stooping low, he slipped over the edge of the car, hung for an instant with apparent ease, and then vanished. Half a dozen of the top-riders watched the feat with amazement, but said nothing, judging it wise not to show surprise at what might well be the customary way of getting down in this bewildering city. The runaway passenger darted past a hansom and then drifted along like a leaf on a stream, threading his way between a furniture van and a florist’s delivery wagon.
source_text: The girl in the fruitful hat quickly seized her companion by the arm and whispered in his ear. That young man exhibited proof of ability to act promptly. Crouching low, he slid over the edge of the car, hung lightly for an instant, and then disappeared. Half a dozen of the top-riders observed his feat, wonderingly, but made no comment, deeming it prudent not to express surprise at what might be the conventional manner of alighting in this bewildering city. The truant passenger dodged a hansom and then floated past, like a leaf on a stream between a furniture van and a florist’s delivery wagon.

text: Soon after, a young man in evening dress came and took a seat on the third bench from the Prince. For half an hour he smoked cigars in agitated haste, and then he began to watch the face of the clock shining above the trees. His uneasiness was plain to see, and the Prince observed with sorrow that it was somehow bound up with the clock’s slowly advancing hands.
source_text: By and by came a young man in evening clothes and sat upon the third bench from the Prince. For half an hour he smoked cigars with nervous haste, and then he fell to watching the face of the illuminated clock above the trees. His perturbation was evident, and the Prince noted, in sorrow, that its cause was connected, in some manner, with the slowly moving hands of the timepiece.

text: Madam, I challenge you to keep from smiling until you have endured this ordeal: let the Marechal Niel roses Percy brought you on the night you gave him your heart be set before you as a salad, dressed with French dressing, at a Schulenberg table d’hôte. Had Juliet watched her love tokens so dishonored, she would have sought the good apothecary’s lethean herbs all the sooner.
source_text: Madam, I dare you to smile until you suffer this test: Let the Marechal Niel roses that Percy brought you on the night you gave him your heart be served as a salad with French dressing before your eyes at a Schulenberg _table d’hôte_. Had Juliet so seen her love tokens dishonoured the sooner would she have sought the lethean herbs of the good apothecary.

text: Prince Michael sank into a deep sleep. His battered hat slipped from the bench to the ground. The young man picked it up, set it over the disheveled face, and shifted one of the grotesquely limp limbs into a more comfortable position. “Poor devil!” he said, drawing the ragged clothes more snugly about the Prince’s breast.
source_text: Prince Michael sank into deep slumber. His battered hat rolled from the bench to the ground. The young man lifted it, placed it over the frowsy face and moved one of the grotesquely relaxed limbs into a more comfortable position. “Poor devil!” he said, as he drew the tattered clothes closer about the Prince’s breast.

text: As his hope ebbed away, so too did his faith. He sat staring at the yellow, humming gaslight. Then he rose, went to the bed, and began ripping the sheets into strips. Using the blade of his knife, he forced them firmly into every crack around the windows and the door. When everything was sealed tight and stretched taut, he extinguished the light, turned the gas on full once more, and lay down upon the bed with gratitude.
source_text: The ebbing of his hope drained his faith. He sat staring at the yellow, singing gaslight. Soon he walked to the bed and began to tear the sheets into strips. With the blade of his knife he drove them tightly into every crevice around windows and door. When all was snug and taut he turned out the light, turned the gas full on again and laid himself gratefully upon the bed.

text: By Sirius! there was a two-legged creature I felt genuinely sorry for. He was a small man with sandy hair and whiskers much like my own. Henpecked?—why, toucans, flamingos, and pelicans had all taken a turn pecking at him. He washed the dishes and listened while my mistress told him about the shabby, cheap things the lady in the squirrel-skin coat on the second floor had hung on her clothesline to dry. And every evening, while she was making supper, she made him take me out for a walk at the end of a string.
source_text: By Sirius! there was a biped I felt sorry for. He was a little man with sandy hair and whiskers a good deal like mine. Henpecked?—well, toucans and flamingoes and pelicans all had their bills in him. He wiped the dishes and listened to my mistress tell about the cheap, ragged things the lady with the squirrel-skin coat on the second floor hung out on her line to dry. And every evening while she was getting supper she made him take me out on the end of a string for a walk.

text: “Go on,” says Tobin, leaning against his sleeve. “Do be off home. And I’ll sit at the door of it till ye come out in the morning. For it rests with ye to avert the curse of the nigger man and the blonde lady and the financial loss of the one-sixty-five.”
source_text: “Do,” says Tobin, leaning against his sleeve. “Do be off to your home. And I will sit at the door of it till ye come out in the morning. For the dependence is upon ye to obviate the curse of the nigger man and the blonde lady and the financial loss of the one-sixty-five.”

text: “Then come, Miss Marian,” said the young architect, cheerfully, yet with perfect politeness; “you will not have far to go. There is a very respectable, excellent restaurant in the next block. You will need to lean on my arm—so—and walk a little slowly. It is lonely to dine entirely by oneself. I am just a little glad that you slipped on the ice.”
source_text: “Come then, Miss Marian,” said the young architect, gaily, but with perfect courtesy; “you will not have far to walk. There is a very respectable and good restaurant in the next block. You will have to lean on my arm—so—and walk slowly. It is lonely dining all by one’s self. I’m just a little bit glad that you slipped on the ice.”

text: The evening had reached that hour when reserve begins to melt away. One of us ordered three Würzburgers from the waiter; the dark-haired young man signaled with a smile and a nod that he too was included in the request. I quickly put a question to him, eager to test a theory I had formed.
source_text: The evening was at the period when reserve is thawed. One of us mentioned three Würzburgers to the waiter; the dark-haired young man acknowledged his inclusion in the order by a smile and a nod. I hastened to ask him a question because I wanted to try out a theory I had.

text: The furnished room greeted its latest guest with an initial glow of false hospitality, a feverish, worn, and dutiful welcome, like the sham smile of a demirep. Its deceptive comfort shone back in scattered glints from the shabby furniture, from the torn brocade covering of a couch and two chairs, from a cheap foot-wide pier glass set between the two windows, from one or two gilt picture frames, and from a brass bedstead in the corner.
source_text: The furnished room received its latest guest with a first glow of pseudo-hospitality, a hectic, haggard, perfunctory welcome like the specious smile of a demirep. The sophistical comfort came in reflected gleams from the decayed furniture, the ragged brocade upholstery of a couch and two chairs, a foot-wide cheap pier glass between the two windows, from one or two gilt picture frames and a brass bedstead in a corner.

text: As we squeezed through the gates, a Black man thrust his lit cigar against Tobin’s ear, and trouble broke out. Tobin struck him hard in the neck, the women squealed, and, thinking quickly, I pulled the little fellow out of the way before the police arrived. Tobin is always at his worst when he is having a good time.
source_text: As we squeezed through the gates a nigger man sticks his lighted segar against Tobin’s ear, and there is trouble. Tobin hammers his neck, and the women squeal, and by presence of mind I drag the little man out of the way before the police comes. Tobin is always in an ugly mood when enjoying himself.

text: “Doggie,” he said at last, “we don’t live much beyond a dozen lives on this earth, and precious few of us make it to three hundred. If I ever see that flat again, then I’m flat; and if you do, you’re flatter still—and that’s no flattery. I’m laying sixty to one that Westward Ho wins by a dachshund’s length.”
source_text: “Doggie,” says he, finally, “we don’t live more than a dozen lives on this earth, and very few of us live to be more than 300. If I ever see that flat any more I’m a flat, and if you do you’re flatter; and that’s no flattery. I’m offering 60 to 1 that Westward Ho wins out by the length of a dachshund.”

text: And Tildy was willing to remain the unwooed drudge if Aileen could have the flattery and the homage. The blunt nose was loyal to the little Greek. She was Aileen’s friend, and glad to watch her command hearts and draw men’s attention away from smoking pot-pie and lemon meringue. Yet deep beneath our freckles and hay-colored hair, the least handsome of us all dream of a prince or a princess—not secondhand, but coming to us alone.
source_text: And Tildy was content to be the unwooed drudge if Aileen could receive the flattery and the homage. The blunt nose was loyal to the short Grecian. She was Aileen’s friend; and she was glad to see her rule hearts and wean the attention of men from smoking pot-pie and lemon meringue. But deep below our freckles and hay-coloured hair the unhandsomest of us dream of a prince or a princess, not vicarious, but coming to us alone.

text: Pitcher, the confidential clerk in the office of Harvey Maxwell, broker, let a slight expression of interest and surprise cross his ordinarily impassive face when his employer came in briskly at half past nine, accompanied by his young stenographer. With a sharp “Good morning, Pitcher,” Maxwell rushed to his desk as if he meant to vault over it, and then threw himself into the large pile of letters and telegrams awaiting him.
source_text: Pitcher, confidential clerk in the office of Harvey Maxwell, broker, allowed a look of mild interest and surprise to visit his usually expressionless countenance when his employer briskly entered at half past nine in company with his young lady stenographer. With a snappy “Good-morning, Pitcher,” Maxwell dashed at his desk as though he were intending to leap over it, and then plunged into the great heap of letters and telegrams waiting there for him.

text: “New York is a pretty big place,” said Walter Franklin. “I came in a week ago to your old address and learned that you left on a Thursday. That was something of a comfort; it ruled out the possibility of any Friday bad luck. But it didn’t keep me from hunting for you with the police and in every other way ever since!”
source_text: “New York is a pretty large town,” said Walter Franklin. “I came in a week ago to your old address. I found that you went away on a Thursday. That consoled some; it eliminated the possible Friday bad luck. But it didn’t prevent my hunting for you with police and otherwise ever since!

text: Prince Adrian’s shoes were ruined beyond the repair of the most meticulous cobbler. The ragpicker would have refused all discussion of his garments. The fortnight’s stubble on his face was grey and brown and rusty red and a sickly yellow, as though it had been assembled from separate offerings by the chorus of a comic opera. No man alive possessed sufficient means to wear a hat as dreadful as his.
source_text: Prince Michael’s shoes were wrecked far beyond the skill of the carefullest cobbler. The ragman would have declined any negotiations concerning his clothes. The two weeks’ stubble on his face was grey and brown and red and greenish yellow—as if it had been made up from individual contributions from the chorus of a musical comedy. No man existed who had money enough to wear so bad a hat as his.

text: Aileen could hold her own in banter with a dozen people at once. And every smile she gave flew out like shot from a scattergun, striking a dozen hearts in the same instant. All the while, she was carrying off astonishing feats with orders of pork and beans, pot roasts, ham-and, sausage-and-the-wheats, and any number of things on the griddle and in the pan, served straight up and on the side. With all that eating and flirting and lively exchange of wit, Bogle’s came very close to being a salon, with Aileen as its Madame Récamier.
source_text: Aileen could successfully exchange repartee against a dozen at once. And every smile that she sent forth lodged, like pellets from a scatter-gun, in as many hearts. And all this while she would be performing astounding feats with orders of pork and beans, pot roasts, ham-and, sausage-and-the-wheats, and any quantity of things on the iron and in the pan and straight up and on the side. With all this feasting and flirting and merry exchange of wit Bogle’s came mighty near being a salon, with Aileen for its Madame Récamier.

text: In the hot, humming, cabbage-perfumed rooms of Harker’s there had nearly been a tragedy of the heart. Mabel, with her blunt little nose, her straw-colored hair, her freckled skin, and her flour-sack figure, had never known the tribute of an admirer. No man followed her with his eyes as she passed to and fro among the tables, except now and then in that fierce, animal way hunger has when the food is slow in coming. None of them rallied her lightly into bright exchanges of wit. None of them called out to her of a morning, as they did to Nora, making merry at her expense and hinting, when the eggs were tardy, that she had been keeping late company with fortunate swains. No one had ever pressed a turquoise ring upon her finger or asked her to sail away to some far and enchanted “Parsifal.”
source_text: In steaming, chattering, cabbage-scented Bogle’s there was almost a heart tragedy. Tildy with the blunt nose, the hay-coloured hair, the freckled skin, the bag-o’-meal figure, had never had an admirer. Not a man followed her with his eyes when she went to and fro in the restaurant save now and then when they glared with the beast-hunger for food. None of them bantered her gaily to coquettish interchanges of wit. None of them loudly “jollied” her of mornings as they did Aileen, accusing her, when the eggs were slow in coming, of late hours in the company of envied swains. No one had ever given her a turquoise ring or invited her upon a voyage to mysterious, distant “Parsifal.”

text: Within it stood an iron cot, a washstand, and a chair. A shelf served as the dresser. The four naked walls appeared to lean inward upon one, like the sides of a coffin. Your hand stole to your throat; you drew in your breath, you lifted your eyes as though from the bottom of a well, and then you breathed again. Through the glass of the small skylight there showed a square of blue eternity.
source_text: In it was an iron cot, a washstand and a chair. A shelf was the dresser. Its four bare walls seemed to close in upon you like the sides of a coffin. Your hand crept to your throat, you gasped, you looked up as from a well—and breathed once more. Through the glass of the little skylight you saw a square of blue infinity.

text: The girl lay motionless, as though in a swoon. Emil cast an eager glance around the room for a barrel. People who had—no, no; that was for those drawn from water. He began to waft her with his hat. This proved effective, for he tapped her nose with the edge of his derby and she opened her eyes. Then the young man perceived that it was indeed her face, the one absent from the gallery of his heart’s most secret portraits. The open grey eyes, the small nose with its saucy outward turn; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the proper conclusion and reward of all his marvelous adventures. Yet the face was terribly thin and pale.
source_text: The girl lay still, as if in a faint. Rudolf looked around the room excitedly for a barrel. People must be rolled upon a barrel who—no, no; that was for drowned persons. He began to fan her with his hat. That was successful, for he struck her nose with the brim of his derby and she opened her eyes. And then the young man saw that hers, indeed, was the one missing face from his heart’s gallery of intimate portraits. The frank, grey eyes, the little nose, turning pertly outward; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the right end and reward of all his wonderful adventures. But the face was wofully thin and pale.

text: Never, never begin a story in this fashion when you set one down. No opening could well be worse. It is dull, colorless, meager, and liable to amount to nothing but empty air. Yet in this case it may be permitted. For the next paragraph, which ought to have introduced the narrative, is far too wildly extravagant and absurd to be thrust at the reader without some warning.
source_text: Never, never begin a story this way when you write one. No opening could possibly be worse. It is unimaginative, flat, dry and likely to consist of mere wind. But in this instance it is allowable. For the following paragraph, which should have inaugurated the narrative, is too wildly extravagant and preposterous to be flaunted in the face of the reader without preparation.

text: I knew I had him then. He was taking Hot Scotches, seated at a table, and for a full hour he kept the Campbells coming. I sat at his elbow, thumping with my tail for the waiter, and making free with the lunch, such as mamma in her flat never matched, even with all her homemade truck, bought at the delicatessen store eight minutes before papa came home.
source_text: I knew I had him. Hot Scotches he took, sitting at a table. For an hour he kept the Campbells coming. I sat by his side rapping for the waiter with my tail, and eating free lunch such as mamma in her flat never equalled with her homemade truck bought at a delicatessen store eight minutes before papa comes home.

text: Then a strong voice rang out in the hall below, and Sarah sprang for her door, dropping the book on the floor and losing the first round outright to the bear. You have guessed it. She reached the top of the stairs just as her farmer came up three steps at a time and gathered her in, with nothing left behind for the gleaners.
source_text: And then a strong voice was heard in the hall below, and Sarah jumped for her door, leaving the book on the floor and the first round easily the bear’s. You have guessed it. She reached the top of the stairs just as her farmer came up, three at a jump, and reaped and garnered her, with nothing left for the gleaners.

text: “Miss says she knows all about it—that a girl’s easy enough when some chap comes spinning ghost tales and trying to sweeten things up, and that’s why she won’t swallow any of your honey talk. She says she caught you red-handed, clasping a load of calico in the glasshouse. She’d just stepped in to gather a few blooms, and there you were, squeezing the other girl to beat the band. Says it may have looked mighty pretty, all right enough, but it turned her stomach. Says you’d best stir yourself and make a run for the train.”
source_text: “De lady says dat she’s on to de fact dat gals is dead easy when a feller comes spielin’ ghost stories and tryin’ to make up, and dat’s why she won’t listen to no soft-soap. She says she caught yer dead to rights, huggin’ a bunch o’ calico in de hot-house. She side-stepped in to pull some posies and yer was squeezin’ de oder gal to beat de band. She says it looked cute, all right all right, but it made her sick. She says yer better git busy, and make a sneak for de train.”

text: Tildy was a good waitress, and the men put up with her. Those who took seats at her tables spoke to her only in brief phrases lifted from the menu, and then turned their voices sweeter or differently tinted as they addressed the fair Aileen. They twisted in their chairs to look past and around Tildy’s looming figure, so that Aileen’s beauty might lend relish and even a kind of ambrosia to their bacon and eggs.
source_text: Tildy was a good waitress, and the men tolerated her. They who sat at her tables spoke to her briefly with quotations from the bill of fare; and then raised their voices in honeyed and otherwise-flavoured accents, eloquently addressed to the fair Aileen. They writhed in their chairs to gaze around and over the impending form of Tildy, that Aileen’s pulchritude might season and make ambrosia of their bacon and eggs.

text: “It comes to this,” said Harren, tapping with a rigid forefinger at a little pool of spilled coffee. “Caesar had his Brutus—the cotton plant has its bollworm, the chorus girl has her Pittsburg man, the summer boarder has his poison ivy, the hero has his Carnegie medal, art has its Morgan, the rose has its—”
source_text: “It is this,” said Kraft, punctuating in a puddle of spilled coffee with a stiff forefinger. “Caesar had his Brutus—the cotton has its bollworm, the chorus girl has her Pittsburger, the summer boarder has his poison ivy, the hero has his Carnegie medal, art has its Morgan, the rose has its—”

text: There were, in truth, two cherished things belonging to the Darlings of Maple Street in which they both placed a great and tender pride. One was Martin’s gold timepiece, handed down from his father and his father before him. The other was Clara’s hair. Had the Queen of Sheba lived in the rooms opposite, across the air shaft, Clara would have let her tresses spill from the window on some bright afternoon merely to diminish the splendor of Her Majesty’s jewels and gifts. And had King Solomon himself served as the building’s janitor, with all his riches heaped in the cellar below, Martin would have drawn out his watch each time he went by, only to behold the monarch tugging at his beard in envy.
source_text: Now, there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which they both took a mighty pride. One was Jim’s gold watch that had been his father’s and his grandfather’s. The other was Della’s hair. Had the Queen of Sheba lived in the flat across the airshaft, Della would have let her hair hang out the window some day to dry just to depreciate Her Majesty’s jewels and gifts. Had King Solomon been the janitor, with all his treasures piled up in the basement, Jim would have pulled out his watch every time he passed, just to see him pluck at his beard from envy.

text: Rudolf Steiner was a genuine adventurer. There were few evenings on which he did not set out from his hall bedroom in pursuit of whatever was unexpected or outrageous. To him, the most fascinating thing in life was what might be waiting just around the next corner. At times, his eagerness to tempt fate led him down curious roads. Twice he had passed the night in a police station; more than once he had found himself the victim of clever, money-minded swindlers; on one occasion, he had paid for a seductive invitation with both his watch and his cash. Yet he met every challenge flung at him in the gay arena of adventure with undiminished enthusiasm.
source_text: Rudolf Steiner was a true adventurer. Few were the evenings on which he did not go forth from his hall bedchamber in search of the unexpected and the egregious. The most interesting thing in life seemed to him to be what might lie just around the next corner. Sometimes his willingness to tempt fate led him into strange paths. Twice he had spent the night in a station-house; again and again he had found himself the dupe of ingenious and mercenary tricksters; his watch and money had been the price of one flattering allurement. But with undiminished ardour he picked up every glove cast before him into the merry lists of adventure.

text: I was convinced that I had finally found the one genuine cosmopolitan since Adam, and I listened to his talk about the world with the fear that I might catch in it the provincial accent of the mere globe-trotter. But his opinions never wavered or sagged; he was as indifferent to cities, nations, and continents as the winds or gravity.
source_text: I was sure that I had found at last the one true cosmopolite since Adam, and I listened to his worldwide discourse fearful lest I should discover in it the local note of the mere globe-trotter. But his opinions never fluttered or drooped; he was as impartial to cities, countries and continents as the winds or gravitation.

text: Near the curb stood Jerry O’Donovan’s cab. Night-hawk they called Jerry; yet no hansom ever more gleamed or was kept more spotless than his, when it shut its doors on point lace and November violets. And Jerry’s horse! I speak within bounds when I say he was stuffed with oats so full that one of those old ladies who leave their dishes unwashed at home and go about having expressmen arrested would have smiled—yes, smiled—to see him.
source_text: Close to the curb stood Jerry O’Donovan’s cab. Night-hawk was Jerry called; but no more lustrous or cleaner hansom than his ever closed its doors upon point lace and November violets. And Jerry’s horse! I am within bounds when I tell you that he was stuffed with oats until one of those old ladies who leave their dishes unwashed at home and go about having expressmen arrested, would have smiled—yes, smiled—to have seen him.

text: On the boat ride back, when the man called out, “Who wants the good-looking waiter?” Tobin was ready to plead guilty, feeling an urge to blow the foam off a crock of suds, but when he reached into his pocket he found himself dismissed for want of evidence. Someone had jostled his change in the confusion. So we sat there dry on our stools, listening to the dagoes fiddling on deck. If anything, Tobin was in even worse spirits, and less able to take his troubles cheerfully, than he had been when we set out.
source_text: On the boat going back, when the man calls “Who wants the good-looking waiter?” Tobin tried to plead guilty, feeling the desire to blow the foam off a crock of suds, but when he felt in his pocket he found himself discharged for lack of evidence. Somebody had disturbed his change during the commotion. So we sat, dry, upon the stools, listening to the Dagoes fiddling on deck. If anything, Tobin was lower in spirits and less congenial with his misfortunes than when we started.

text: “This is ridiculous,” said Rudolf, in a burst of bluster, “to go without eating. You must stop making election bets of this sort. Supper is ready.” He drew her to a chair at the table and asked, “Is there a cup for the tea?” “On the shelf by the window,” she replied. When he turned back with it, he saw her, her eyes shining with rapture, already beginning on a huge dill pickle she had fished from the paper bags with a woman’s unerring instinct. He took it from her with a laugh and filled the cup to the brim with milk. “Drink that first,” he commanded, “and then you shall have tea, and then a chicken wing. If you are very good, you shall have a pickle tomorrow. And now, if you’ll allow me to be your guest, we’ll have supper.”
source_text: “This is ridiculous,” said Rudolf, blusteringly, “to go without eating. You must quit making election bets of this kind. Supper is ready.” He helped her to a chair at the table and asked: “Is there a cup for the tea?” “On the shelf by the window,” she answered. When he turned again with the cup he saw her, with eyes shining rapturously, beginning upon a huge Dill pickle that she had rooted out from the paper bags with a woman’s unerring instinct. He took it from her, laughingly, and poured the cup full of milk. “Drink that first” he ordered, “and then you shall have some tea, and then a chicken wing. If you are very good you shall have a pickle to-morrow. And now, if you’ll allow me to be your guest we’ll have supper.”

text: “Go down the steps,” says the man with the crooked nose, “and I’ll come in through the door above and let you in. I’ll ask the new girl we have in the kitchen,” says he, “to make you a pot of coffee before you go. It’s fine coffee Katie Mahorner makes for a girl who’s only been landed three months. Step inside,” says the man, “and I’ll send her down to you.”
source_text: “Step down the steps,” says the man with the crooked nose, “and I will enter by the door above and let ye in. I will ask the new girl we have in the kitchen,” says he, “to make ye a pot of coffee to drink before ye go. ’Tis fine coffee Katie Mahorner makes for a green girl just landed three months. Step in,” says the man, “and I’ll send her down to ye.”

text: “That is,” he went on, “if she keeps to the idea until the time comes. We’ve been laying our plans for the getaway for two weeks. One day she says she’ll do it; the same evening she says no way. We’ve settled on tonight, and Rosy’s held to the yes this time for two whole days. But there are still five hours to go, and I’m afraid she’ll leave me in the lurch when the moment of truth arrives.”
source_text: “That is,” he continued, “if she keeps in the notion until the time comes. We’ve been layin’ pipes for the getaway for two weeks. One day she says she will; the same evenin’ she says nixy. We’ve agreed on to-night, and Rosy’s stuck to the affirmative this time for two whole days. But it’s five hours yet till the time, and I’m afraid she’ll stand me up when it comes to the scratch.”

text: Usually at the weekly hops, Maggie kept one patch of wall warm with her back. She felt and displayed such overflowing gratitude whenever some self-sacrificing partner asked her to dance that his own pleasure was cheapened and lessened by it. She had even grown accustomed to seeing Anna nudge the unwilling Jimmy with her elbow, the signal for him to invite her chum to tread over his feet in a two-step.
source_text: Usually at the weekly hops Maggie kept a spot on the wall warm with her back. She felt and showed so much gratitude whenever a self-sacrificing partner invited her to dance that his pleasure was cheapened and diminished. She had even grown used to noticing Anna joggle the reluctant Jimmy with her elbow as a signal for him to invite her chum to walk over his feet through a two-step.

text: Mr. McCaskey was no novice in the art of repartee. He understood what ought to come after the entrée. On the table lay a roast sirloin of pork, decorated with shamrocks. He answered with that, and received in return the proper response: a bread pudding served in an earthen dish. A lump of Swiss cheese, flung with accuracy by her husband, struck Mrs. McCaskey beneath one eye. When she replied with a well-aimed coffee-pot filled with a hot, black, faintly fragrant liquid, the battle, by all the rules of the courses, ought to have been over.
source_text: Mr. McCaskey was no novice at repartee. He knew what should follow the entrée. On the table was a roast sirloin of pork, garnished with shamrocks. He retorted with this, and drew the appropriate return of a bread pudding in an earthen dish. A hunk of Swiss cheese accurately thrown by her husband struck Mrs. McCaskey below one eye. When she replied with a well-aimed coffee-pot full of a hot, black, semi-fragrant liquid the battle, according to courses, should have ended.

text: Anyone who has never been granted the sight of a harried Manhattan broker in the heat of a trading rush is poorly equipped for the study of anthropology. The poet may celebrate the “crowded hour of glorious life.” But the broker’s hour is not merely crowded; its minutes and seconds cling to every strap, and, packed tight, they overflow both the front and rear platforms.
source_text: He who has been denied the spectacle of a busy Manhattan broker during a rush of business is handicapped for the profession of anthropology. The poet sings of the “crowded hour of glorious life.” The broker’s hour is not only crowded, but the minutes and seconds are hanging to all the straps and packing both front and rear platforms.

text: There was great delight among the gentleman lodgers whenever Miss Harrow found an hour to spend upon the stoop. But Miss Vale, the tall fair-haired one who taught at a public school and answered everything with, “Well, really!” would sit on the upper step and sniff. And Miss Dorr, who went every Sunday to Coney to fire at the moving ducks and worked in a department store, would take the lowest step and sniff as well. Miss Harrow sat in the middle, and the men would promptly gather about her.
source_text: There was rejoicing among the gentlemen roomers whenever Miss Leeson had time to sit on the steps for an hour or two. But Miss Longnecker, the tall blonde who taught in a public school and said, “Well, really!” to everything you said, sat on the top step and sniffed. And Miss Dorn, who shot at the moving ducks at Coney every Sunday and worked in a department store, sat on the bottom step and sniffed. Miss Leeson sat on the middle step and the men would quickly group around her.

text: At the next corner he slipped away from his companion and took to his heels. He stopped in the quarter where, by night, the lightest streets, hearts, vows, and librettos are to be found. Women in furs and men in greatcoats moved cheerfully through the sharp winter air. A sudden terror took hold of Soapy that some awful spell had made him immune to arrest. The thought stirred a little panic in him, and when he came upon another policeman, lounging with lordly ease before a brilliant theatre, he seized upon the nearest chance in “disorderly conduct.”
source_text: At the next corner he shook off his companion and ran. He halted in the district where by night are found the lightest streets, hearts, vows and librettos. Women in furs and men in greatcoats moved gaily in the wintry air. A sudden fear seized Soapy that some dreadful enchantment had rendered him immune to arrest. The thought brought a little of panic upon it, and when he came upon another policeman lounging grandly in front of a transplendent theatre he caught at the immediate straw of “disorderly conduct.”

text: The diners took the news in different ways—some with disbelief, some with congratulations; others turned on her the same flow of banter that had until then been aimed at Aileen alone. And Tildy’s heart swelled within her, for at last she saw the towers of Romance rising above the horizon of the grey plain through which she had so long been journeying.
source_text: The diners accepted the revelation in various ways—some incredulously, some with congratulations; others turned upon her the stream of badinage that had hitherto been directed at Aileen alone. And Tildy’s heart swelled in her bosom, for she saw at last the towers of Romance rise above the horizon of the grey plain in which she had for so long travelled.

text: Bennett stood beside his desk, his hands crowded with telegrams and memoranda, a fountain pen tucked over his right ear, his hair falling in untidy strands across his brow. The window stood open, for the dear janitress of spring had released a little warmth through the earth’s awakening registers.
source_text: Maxwell stood by his desk with his hands full of telegrams and memoranda, with a fountain pen over his right ear and his hair hanging in disorderly strings over his forehead. His window was open, for the beloved janitress Spring had turned on a little warmth through the waking registers of the earth.

text: “Oh, yes, doctor,” sniffed Mrs. Parker, as if her own distress at there being trouble in the house were the greater. “I can’t think what is the matter with her. Nothing we could do would bring her round. It’s a young woman, a Miss Elsie—yes, Miss Elsie Leeson. Never before in my house—”
source_text: “Oh, yes, doctor,” sniffed Mrs. Parker, as though her trouble that there should be trouble in the house was the greater. “I can’t think what can be the matter with her. Nothing we could do would bring her to. It’s a young woman, a Miss Elsie—yes, a Miss Elsie Leeson. Never before in my house—”

text: At nine o’clock, Dulcie drew from her trunk a small tin box of biscuits and a little jar of raspberry jam, and made herself a feast. She offered Lord Kitchener a cracker spread with jam; but he only regarded her with the same look the sphinx might have given a butterfly—if, indeed, butterflies are to be found in the desert.
source_text: At nine o’clock Dulcie took a tin box of crackers and a little pot of raspberry jam out of her trunk, and had a feast. She offered General Kitchener some jam on a cracker; but he only looked at her as the sphinx would have looked at a butterfly—if there are butterflies in the desert.

text: Spring’s truest heralds are too delicate for sight and hearing. Some require the blooming crocus, the dogwood starred with blossom, the bluebird’s voice—even so plain a sign as the departing handshake of buckwheat and oyster before they can greet the Lady in Green to their heavy hearts. But to old earth’s favored children come direct and gentle tidings from his newest bride, telling them they need be no stepchildren unless they wish it.
source_text: Spring’s real harbingers are too subtle for the eye and ear. Some must have the flowering crocus, the wood-starring dogwood, the voice of bluebird—even so gross a reminder as the farewell handshake of the retiring buckwheat and oyster before they can welcome the Lady in Green to their dull bosoms. But to old earth’s choicest kin there come straight, sweet messages from his newest bride, telling them they shall be no stepchildren unless they choose to be.

text: At the turning of a street, beneath a gas lamp and gazing across the elevated track toward the moon, stood a man. He was a lank fellow, respectably dressed, with a cigar between his teeth, and I observed that his nose bent in two crooked turns from bridge to tip, like the twisting of a snake. Tobias saw it at the same moment, and I heard him draw a hard breath, as a horse does when the saddle is lifted from its back. He walked at once up to the man, and I followed with him.
source_text: On a street corner, standing under a gas-light and looking over the elevated road at the moon, was a man. A long man he was, dressed decent, with a segar between his teeth, and I saw that his nose made two twists from bridge to end, like the wriggle of a snake. Tobin saw it at the same time, and I heard him breathe hard like a horse when you take the saddle off. He went straight up to the man, and I went with him.

text: But behind the convenient screen, Tildy had flung herself face-down on a table among the pats of butter and the coffee cups, and was sobbing as if her heart would break—out and back again into that gray plain where they journey with blunt noses and hay-colored hair. She had ripped the red hair ribbon from her knot and thrown it to the floor. She despised Seeders utterly; she had accepted his kiss only as one given by a pioneer and a prophetic prince who might have set the clocks in motion and made the pages run in fairyland. But the kiss had been maudlin and unintentional; the court had not moved at the false alarm; she would forever remain the Sleeping Beauty.
source_text: But behind the convenient screen Tildy had thrown herself flat upon a table among the butter chips and the coffee cups, and was sobbing her heart out—out and back again to the grey plain wherein travel they with blunt noses and hay-coloured hair. From her knot she had torn the red hair-bow and cast it upon the floor. Seeders she despised utterly; she had but taken his kiss as that of a pioneer and prophetic prince who might have set the clocks going and the pages to running in fairyland. But the kiss had been maudlin and unmeant; the court had not stirred at the false alarm; she must forevermore remain the Sleeping Beauty.

text: I give it up. I hear of marvelous bargains in fabrics, and of wonders wrought with needle and thread; yet I remain uncertain. I hold my pen suspended in vain whenever I would add to Dulcie’s life some of those pleasures that belong to woman by right of all the unwritten, sacred, natural, unspoken ordinances of heaven’s equity.
