Twice she had been to Coney Island and had ridden the hobby-horses. It is a weary thing to measure one’s pleasures by summers rather than by hours.
source_text: I give it up. I hear of wonderful bargains in fabrics, and of miracles performed with needle and thread; but I am in doubt. I hold my pen poised in vain when I would add to Dulcie’s life some of those joys that belong to woman by virtue of all the unwritten, sacred, natural, inactive ordinances of the equity of heaven. Twice she had been to Coney Island and had ridden the hobby-horses. ’Tis a weary thing to count your pleasures by summers instead of by hours.

text: As the days went by, I grew more tranquil; sorrow still kept its place within my heart, yet I no longer spoke of my own offences in that broken and distracted way. The bare knowledge of them was punishment enough. By the severest effort I restrained the fierce cry of misery, which at times seemed eager to proclaim itself before the whole world, and my outward bearing became quieter and more controlled than it had been at any time since my voyage to the frozen sea.
source_text: As time passed away I became more calm; misery had her dwelling in my heart, but I no longer talked in the same incoherent manner of my own crimes; sufficient for me was the consciousness of them. By the utmost self-violence I curbed the imperious voice of wretchedness, which sometimes desired to declare itself to the whole world, and my manners were calmer and more composed than they had ever been since my journey to the sea of ice.

text: When he had at last subdued the storm of his emotions, he seemed almost to scorn himself for having so yielded to passion; and, mastering the sullen despotism of despair, he brought our discourse once more back to my own person. He bade me recount the story of my earlier years. The account was soon given, yet it stirred in him a multitude of thoughts. I spoke of my longing to discover a friend, of my hunger for a closer fellowship with another mind than had ever been granted me, and declared my belief that no man could claim much happiness who was denied so precious a boon.
source_text: Having conquered the violence of his feelings, he appeared to despise himself for being the slave of passion; and quelling the dark tyranny of despair, he led me again to converse concerning myself personally. He asked me the history of my earlier years. The tale was quickly told, but it awakened various trains of reflection. I spoke of my desire of finding a friend, of my thirst for a more intimate sympathy with a fellow mind than had ever fallen to my lot, and expressed my conviction that a man could boast of little happiness who did not enjoy this blessing.

text: “I have copies of these letters, for during my stay in the hut I contrived to obtain the tools needed for writing, and the letters were often in the hands of Felix or Agatha. Before I leave, I will give them to you; they will confirm the truth of my story. But for now, since the sun is already sinking low, I shall only have time to repeat their substance to you.
source_text: “I have copies of these letters, for I found means, during my residence in the hovel, to procure the implements of writing; and the letters were often in the hands of Felix or Agatha. Before I depart I will give them to you; they will prove the truth of my tale; but at present, as the sun is already far declined, I shall only have time to repeat the substance of them to you.

text: “Do as you please; I shall not. You may abandon your design, but mine has been laid upon me by Heaven, and I dare not forsake it. I am frail enough, yet the unseen powers that aid my revenge will surely grant me strength sufficient for the task.” As he spoke, he tried to rise from the bed, but the effort surpassed his failing powers; he sank back upon the pillow and swooned away.
source_text: “Do so, if you will; but I will not. You may give up your purpose, but mine is assigned to me by Heaven, and I dare not. I am weak, but surely the spirits who assist my vengeance will endow me with sufficient strength.” Saying this, he endeavoured to spring from the bed, but the exertion was too great for him; he fell back and fainted.

text: May had already begun, and I was expecting each day the letter that would set the date of my departure, when Henry suggested a walk through the countryside around Ingolstadt, so that I might take a personal leave of the land I had lived in so long. I gladly agreed to the plan. I was fond of walking, and Clerval had always been my favorite companion in such rambles as I had made through the landscapes of my native country.
source_text: The month of May had already commenced, and I expected the letter daily which was to fix the date of my departure, when Henry proposed a pedestrian tour in the environs of Ingolstadt, that I might bid a personal farewell to the country I had so long inhabited. I acceded with pleasure to this proposition: I was fond of exercise, and Clerval had always been my favourite companion in the ramble of this nature that I had taken among the scenes of my native country.

text: Six years had gone by, years that seemed like a dream save for one lasting and unforgotten mark, and I found myself in the very spot where I had last held my father in my arms before setting out for Ingolstadt. Dear and revered parent! He was still alive to me there. My eyes rested on the portrait of my mother that hung above the mantelpiece. It was a historical scene, painted at my father’s request, and showed Caroline Beaufort in the depth of despair, kneeling beside the coffin of her dead father. Her dress was that of a peasant, and her face was pale; yet such dignity and beauty shone through her that pity was scarcely the feeling it inspired. Beneath this painting hung a miniature of William, and tears came to my eyes as I looked at it. While I remained thus absorbed, Ernest came in; he had learned of my return and hurried to greet me. “Welcome, my dearest Victor,” he said. “Ah, I wish you had come three months earlier, and then you would have found us all happy and rejoicing. You come now to share in a sorrow that nothing can soften; yet I hope your presence will restore our father, who seems to be sinking beneath his grief, and your counsel will persuade poor Elizabeth to give up those vain and cruel reproaches against herself.—Poor William! he was our idol and our pride!”
source_text: Six years had elapsed, passed in a dream but for one indelible trace, and I stood in the same place where I had last embraced my father before my departure for Ingolstadt. Beloved and venerable parent! He still remained to me. I gazed on the picture of my mother, which stood over the mantel-piece. It was an historical subject, painted at my father’s desire, and represented Caroline Beaufort in an agony of despair, kneeling by the coffin of her dead father. Her garb was rustic, and her cheek pale; but there was an air of dignity and beauty, that hardly permitted the sentiment of pity. Below this picture was a miniature of William; and my tears flowed when I looked upon it. While I was thus engaged, Ernest entered: he had heard me arrive, and hastened to welcome me: “Welcome, my dearest Victor,” said he. “Ah! I wish you had come three months ago, and then you would have found us all joyous and delighted. You come to us now to share a misery which nothing can alleviate; yet your presence will, I hope, revive our father, who seems sinking under his misfortune; and your persuasions will induce poor Elizabeth to cease her vain and tormenting self-accusations.—Poor William! he was our darling and our pride!”

text: “They had taken refuge in a wretched cottage in Germany, where I came upon them. Felix soon learned that the treacherous Turk, for whom he and his family had suffered such unimaginable oppression, had discovered that his benefactor was thus reduced to poverty and ruin, and had then betrayed every decent feeling and every sense of honour. He had left Italy with his daughter, and had the insolence to send Felix a small sum of money, which, he said, was intended to help him in some scheme for future support.
source_text: “They found a miserable asylum in the cottage in Germany, where I discovered them. Felix soon learned that the treacherous Turk, for whom he and his family endured such unheard-of oppression, on discovering that his deliverer was thus reduced to poverty and ruin, became a traitor to good feeling and honour and had quitted Italy with his daughter, insultingly sending Felix a pittance of money to aid him, as he said, in some plan of future maintenance.

text: “I commonly sought rest by day, and moved on only when night had sheltered me from the sight of mankind. One morning, however, finding my road led through a deep wood, I ventured to pursue my journey after sunrise; the day, one of the earliest in spring, brightened even me with the beauty of its sunshine and the softness of the air. Feelings of tenderness and delight, long seeming dead within me, began once more to stir. Half astonished by the strangeness of such sensations, I yielded myself to their influence, and, forgetting my solitude and my deformity, dared to be happy. Gentle tears again moistened my cheeks, and I even lifted my dewy eyes in gratitude toward the blessed sun, which had granted me such bliss.
source_text: “I generally rested during the day and travelled only when I was secured by night from the view of man. One morning, however, finding that my path lay through a deep wood, I ventured to continue my journey after the sun had risen; the day, which was one of the first of spring, cheered even me by the loveliness of its sunshine and the balminess of the air. I felt emotions of gentleness and pleasure, that had long appeared dead, revive within me. Half surprised by the novelty of these sensations, I allowed myself to be borne away by them, and forgetting my solitude and deformity, dared to be happy. Soft tears again bedewed my cheeks, and I even raised my humid eyes with thankfulness towards the blessed sun, which bestowed such joy upon me.

text: It was dark when I woke; I was cold too, and half frightened, as if by instinct, finding myself in such utter desolation. Before I had left your chamber, feeling chilled, I had wrapped myself in some clothes, but they were not enough to shield me from the night dews. I was a poor, helpless, miserable creature; I knew and could distinguish nothing; yet, as pain seemed to assail me from every side, I sat down and wept.
source_text: “It was dark when I awoke; I felt cold also, and half frightened, as it were, instinctively, finding myself so desolate. Before I had quitted your apartment, on a sensation of cold, I had covered myself with some clothes, but these were insufficient to secure me from the dews of night. I was a poor, helpless, miserable wretch; I knew, and could distinguish, nothing; but feeling pain invade me on all sides, I sat down and wept.

text: When my guest had somewhat recovered, I had much ado to keep the men away, for they were eager to plague him with a thousand questions; but I would not suffer him to be tormented by their idle curiosity while his body and mind were in a condition whose recovery plainly depended upon complete repose. Once, however, the lieutenant asked why he had ventured so far upon the ice in so singular a conveyance.
source_text: When my guest was a little recovered I had great trouble to keep off the men, who wished to ask him a thousand questions; but I would not allow him to be tormented by their idle curiosity, in a state of body and mind whose restoration evidently depended upon entire repose. Once, however, the lieutenant asked why he had come so far upon the ice in so strange a vehicle.

text: “Oh, Justine! Forgive me for the one instant in which I doubted you. Why did you confess? But do not grieve, dear girl. Do not be afraid. I will declare, I will establish, your innocence. I will soften the hard hearts of your enemies with my tears and my prayers. You shall not die! You, my playmate, my companion, my sister, die on the scaffold? No! No! I could never endure such a dreadful calamity.”
source_text: “Oh, Justine! Forgive me for having for one moment distrusted you. Why did you confess? But do not mourn, dear girl. Do not fear. I will proclaim, I will prove your innocence. I will melt the stony hearts of your enemies by my tears and prayers. You shall not die! You, my playfellow, my companion, my sister, perish on the scaffold! No! No! I never could survive so horrible a misfortune.”

text: The words compelled me to turn inward upon myself. I came to understand that the gifts most prized among my fellow beings were noble, untainted birth joined to wealth. A man might win regard with one of these blessings alone, yet without either he was deemed, save in the rarest cases, a wanderer and a bondman, fated to spend his strength for the advantage of the favored few. And what was I? Of my own making and my maker I knew nothing at all, but this I knew: I possessed no money, no companions, no property of any kind. More than this, I was shaped in a form grotesquely misshapen and repellent; I was not even of the same order as humankind. I was swifter than they and could sustain myself on rougher fare; I endured heat and frost with less damage to my body; in size I surpassed them all. Yet when I searched the world about me, I saw and heard of none like myself. Was I, then, a monster, an offense upon the earth, from whom all men recoiled and whom all men rejected?
source_text: “The words induced me to turn towards myself. I learned that the possessions most esteemed by your fellow creatures were high and unsullied descent united with riches. A man might be respected with only one of these advantages, but without either he was considered, except in very rare instances, as a vagabond and a slave, doomed to waste his powers for the profits of the chosen few! And what was I? Of my creation and creator I was absolutely ignorant, but I knew that I possessed no money, no friends, no kind of property. I was, besides, endued with a figure hideously deformed and loathsome; I was not even of the same nature as man. I was more agile than they and could subsist upon coarser diet; I bore the extremes of heat and cold with less injury to my frame; my stature far exceeded theirs. When I looked around I saw and heard of none like me. Was I, then, a monster, a blot upon the earth, from which all men fled and whom all men disowned?

text: “How am I to soften you? Will no supplication draw a kinder look from you, you that I beg for pity and mercy? Believe me, Valerian, I was once gentle; my heart burned with love and charity; yet am I not solitary, wretchedly solitary? You, my maker, loathe me; what hope then have I from your kind, to whom I owe nothing and who owe me nothing in return? They recoil from me and curse me. The barren crags and melancholy glaciers are my only shelter. Many days have I strayed among them; the frozen caverns, which alone I do not dread, are a home to me, and the sole home man has not denied me. These stern heavens I welcome, for they are more gracious than your race. Were the multitude of men to learn that I exist, they would do as you have done, and arm themselves for my ruin. Why, then, should I not hate those who hate me? I will make no peace with my foes. I am miserable, and they shall taste my misery. Yet still it is in your power to requite me, and to shield them from an ill which only waits for you to render so vast that not only you and your household, but multitudes beyond counting, will be engulfed in the tempest of its fury. Be moved, then, by pity, and do not reject me. Hear my history; after you have heard it, cast me off or pity me, as your judgment shall decide I merit. But hear me first. Even the condemned, under the harshest laws of men, are granted the right to speak in their own defense before sentence falls. Listen to me, Valerian. You charge me with blood, and yet you would, with a tranquil conscience, destroy the being your own hands have formed. Ah, admirable justice of mankind! Still I do not beg you to spare me; only hear me, and then, if you can, if you choose, destroy your creature.”
source_text: “How can I move thee? Will no entreaties cause thee to turn a favourable eye upon thy creature, who implores thy goodness and compassion? Believe me, Frankenstein, I was benevolent; my soul glowed with love and humanity; but am I not alone, miserably alone? You, my creator, abhor me; what hope can I gather from your fellow creatures, who owe me nothing? They spurn and hate me. The desert mountains and dreary glaciers are my refuge. I have wandered here many days; the caves of ice, which I only do not fear, are a dwelling to me, and the only one which man does not grudge. These bleak skies I hail, for they are kinder to me than your fellow beings. If the multitude of mankind knew of my existence, they would do as you do, and arm themselves for my destruction. Shall I not then hate them who abhor me? I will keep no terms with my enemies. I am miserable, and they shall share my wretchedness. Yet it is in your power to recompense me, and deliver them from an evil which it only remains for you to make so great, that not only you and your family, but thousands of others, shall be swallowed up in the whirlwinds of its rage. Let your compassion be moved, and do not disdain me. Listen to my tale; when you have heard that, abandon or commiserate me, as you shall judge that I deserve. But hear me. The guilty are allowed, by human laws, bloody as they are, to speak in their own defence before they are condemned. Listen to me, Frankenstein. You accuse me of murder, and yet you would, with a satisfied conscience, destroy your own creature. Oh, praise the eternal justice of man! Yet I ask you not to spare me; listen to me, and then, if you can, and if you will, destroy the work of your hands.”

text: While I still bent over her in the torment of my grief, I happened to raise my eyes. The room had earlier been darkened, and I was seized by a sort of terror when I saw the pale yellow moonlight streaming into the chamber. The shutters had been flung open, and with an horror beyond all telling, I beheld at the open window the most loathsome and hateful figure. The monster wore a grin; he seemed to mock me, and with his diabolical finger he pointed toward the body of my wife. I sprang to the window and, drawing a pistol from my breast, fired; but he escaped me, leapt from his place, and, running with the speed of lightning, plunged into the lake.
source_text: While I still hung over her in the agony of despair, I happened to look up. The windows of the room had before been darkened, and I felt a kind of panic on seeing the pale yellow light of the moon illuminate the chamber. The shutters had been thrown back, and with a sensation of horror not to be described, I saw at the open window a figure the most hideous and abhorred. A grin was on the face of the monster; he seemed to jeer, as with his fiendish finger he pointed towards the corpse of my wife. I rushed towards the window, and drawing a pistol from my bosom, fired; but he eluded me, leaped from his station, and running with the swiftness of lightning, plunged into the lake.

text: The sound roused an aged woman who had been dozing in a chair beside me. She was a hired nurse, the wife of one of the jailers, and her face bore all those ill features so often seen in such women. The hard, coarse lines of her countenance were those of one long accustomed to look on suffering without pity. Her manner showed her complete indifference; she spoke to me in English, and the voice seemed one I had already heard in the midst of my own misery.
source_text: This sound disturbed an old woman who was sleeping in a chair beside me. She was a hired nurse, the wife of one of the turnkeys, and her countenance expressed all those bad qualities which often characterise that class. The lines of her face were hard and rude, like that of persons accustomed to see without sympathising in sights of misery. Her tone expressed her entire indifference; she addressed me in English, and the voice struck me as one that I had heard during my sufferings.

text: Nothing can wound the human spirit more deeply than, when the senses have been sharpened by one swift event after another, the cold stillness of certainty and inaction that follows, stripping the soul alike of hope and of dread. Elise was dead, and at last she lay at peace, while I still breathed. The blood ran warm through my veins, yet upon my heart there sat a burden of grief and self-reproach so heavy that nothing could lift it. Sleep deserted my eyes; I wandered like some accursed being, for I had done deeds of a darkness too dreadful to be named, and, far worse, I believed that still more misery remained before me. And yet my heart had once been full of tenderness and devotion to what was good. I had entered life with charitable designs and longed for the hour when I might put them into action and prove myself of service to my fellow creatures. Now all was ruined. Instead of that calm inward peace which permits a man to look back upon his past with contentment and draw from it confidence for the future, I was seized by remorse and the consciousness of guilt, and they drove me onward into a hell of torment so fierce that no words can ever fully express it.
source_text: Nothing is more painful to the human mind than, after the feelings have been worked up by a quick succession of events, the dead calmness of inaction and certainty which follows and deprives the soul both of hope and fear. Justine died, she rested, and I was alive. The blood flowed freely in my veins, but a weight of despair and remorse pressed on my heart which nothing could remove. Sleep fled from my eyes; I wandered like an evil spirit, for I had committed deeds of mischief beyond description horrible, and more, much more (I persuaded myself) was yet behind. Yet my heart overflowed with kindness and the love of virtue. I had begun life with benevolent intentions and thirsted for the moment when I should put them in practice and make myself useful to my fellow beings. Now all was blasted; instead of that serenity of conscience which allowed me to look back upon the past with self-satisfaction, and from thence to gather promise of new hopes, I was seized by remorse and the sense of guilt, which hurried me away to a hell of intense tortures such as no language can describe.

text: “Oh, no—not thus—not thus,” the being cried. “And yet what I do must seem to you to bear that meaning, as if my actions confessed it. But I do not ask for pity in my misery. Sympathy I shall never know. When I first desired it, it was the love of virtue, the sense of happiness and tenderness that filled my whole being, which I longed to share. Now virtue has become to me but a shadow, and happiness and affection have withered into bitterness and loathing despair; what, then, should I ask sympathy for? I am content to suffer alone so long as suffering lasts; when I die, I am equally prepared that hatred and disgrace should cling to my memory. Once my imagination was comforted by dreams of virtue, of renown, and of delight. Once I foolishly hoped to find beings who, forgiving my outward shape, would love the noble qualities I was capable of revealing. I was sustained by lofty thoughts of honour and devotion. But now crime has sunk me below the lowest beast. No guilt, no wickedness, no malice, no misery can equal mine. When I review the terrible list of my sins, I cannot believe I am still the same creature whose mind was once filled with sublime and exalted visions of goodness’s beauty and majesty. Yet it is so; the fallen angel turns into a malignant devil. But even that foe of God and man had companions and friends in his ruin; I am alone.”
source_text: “Oh, it is not thus—not thus,” interrupted the being. “Yet such must be the impression conveyed to you by what appears to be the purport of my actions. Yet I seek not a fellow feeling in my misery. No sympathy may I ever find. When I first sought it, it was the love of virtue, the feelings of happiness and affection with which my whole being overflowed, that I wished to be participated. But now that virtue has become to me a shadow, and that happiness and affection are turned into bitter and loathing despair, in what should I seek for sympathy? I am content to suffer alone while my sufferings shall endure; when I die, I am well satisfied that abhorrence and opprobrium should load my memory. Once my fancy was soothed with dreams of virtue, of fame, and of enjoyment. Once I falsely hoped to meet with beings who, pardoning my outward form, would love me for the excellent qualities which I was capable of unfolding. I was nourished with high thoughts of honour and devotion. But now crime has degraded me beneath the meanest animal. No guilt, no mischief, no malignity, no misery, can be found comparable to mine. When I run over the frightful catalogue of my sins, I cannot believe that I am the same creature whose thoughts were once filled with sublime and transcendent visions of the beauty and the majesty of goodness. But it is even so; the fallen angel becomes a malignant devil. Yet even that enemy of God and man had friends and associates in his desolation; I am alone.

text: I passed the winter in this way. The cottagers’ gentle bearing and beauty greatly won my affection; when sorrow weighed upon them, I felt my own spirits sink, and when they were glad, I shared in their gladness. I saw few other human beings besides them, and whenever anyone else entered the cottage, their rough manners and uncouth gait only made the superior grace of my friends seem greater still. I could see that the old man often tried to hearten his children, as I sometimes heard him call them, and to drive away their sadness. He would speak in a cheerful voice, with such a look of kindness that it gave pleasure even to me. Agatha listened with reverence, her eyes at times brimming with tears, which she tried to brush away unseen; yet I usually found her face and manner brighter after she had heard her father’s words of encouragement. It was not so with Felix. He remained the saddest of them all, and even to my inexpert judgment, he seemed to have endured more deeply than the others. Yet if his face was more mournful, his voice was brighter than his sister’s, especially when he spoke to the old man.
source_text: “I spent the winter in this manner. The gentle manners and beauty of the cottagers greatly endeared them to me; when they were unhappy, I felt depressed; when they rejoiced, I sympathised in their joys. I saw few human beings besides them, and if any other happened to enter the cottage, their harsh manners and rude gait only enhanced to me the superior accomplishments of my friends. The old man, I could perceive, often endeavoured to encourage his children, as sometimes I found that he called them, to cast off their melancholy. He would talk in a cheerful accent, with an expression of goodness that bestowed pleasure even upon me. Agatha listened with respect, her eyes sometimes filled with tears, which she endeavoured to wipe away unperceived; but I generally found that her countenance and tone were more cheerful after having listened to the exhortations of her father. It was not thus with Felix. He was always the saddest of the group, and even to my unpractised senses, he appeared to have suffered more deeply than his friends. But if his countenance was more sorrowful, his voice was more cheerful than that of his sister, especially when he addressed the old man.

text: I am already far to the north of London, and as I wander the streets of Petersburgh, I feel a keen northern breeze brushing my cheeks, bracing my spirits and filling me with pleasure. Do you know this sensation? That wind, having come from the very regions toward which I am traveling, seems to offer me an early taste of those icy realms. Quickened by this breath of promise, my reveries grow more lively and intense. I strive in vain to persuade myself that the pole is only a place of frost and desolation; in my mind it always appears as a land of beauty and delight. There, Margaret, the sun is perpetually in view, its wide orb only just touching the horizon and shedding an unending radiance. There—for with your permission, my sister, I will place some confidence in the accounts of earlier navigators—there snow and frost are banished; and, sailing upon a calm sea, we may be carried to a country that surpasses every region yet discovered on the habitable globe in wonder and loveliness. Its forms and productions may be unlike anything known before, just as the phenomena of the heavens unquestionably are in those uncharted wastes. What wonders may not be expected in a land of endless light? There I may discover the marvelous power that draws the needle, and perhaps settle a thousand celestial observations that only this voyage can make forever consistent despite their seeming irregularities. I shall satisfy my eager curiosity by beholding a portion of the world never before seen, and may set foot on ground never before pressed by man. These are my inducements, and they are strong enough to overcome every fear of danger or death, and to make me begin this arduous journey with the gladness of a child who sets out in a small boat with his holiday companions on an exploring expedition up his native river. But even if all these hopes should prove unfounded, you cannot deny the priceless service I shall render to all mankind, even to the latest generation, by finding a passage near the pole to those lands that now require so many months to reach; or by uncovering the secret of the magnet, which, if it can be discovered at all, can only be achieved by an enterprise such as mine.
source_text: I am already far north of London, and as I walk in the streets of Petersburgh, I feel a cold northern breeze play upon my cheeks, which braces my nerves and fills me with delight. Do you understand this feeling? This breeze, which has travelled from the regions towards which I am advancing, gives me a foretaste of those icy climes. Inspirited by this wind of promise, my daydreams become more fervent and vivid. I try in vain to be persuaded that the pole is the seat of frost and desolation; it ever presents itself to my imagination as the region of beauty and delight. There, Margaret, the sun is for ever visible, its broad disk just skirting the horizon and diffusing a perpetual splendour. There—for with your leave, my sister, I will put some trust in preceding navigators—there snow and frost are banished; and, sailing over a calm sea, we may be wafted to a land surpassing in wonders and in beauty every region hitherto discovered on the habitable globe. Its productions and features may be without example, as the phenomena of the heavenly bodies undoubtedly are in those undiscovered solitudes. What may not be expected in a country of eternal light? I may there discover the wondrous power which attracts the needle and may regulate a thousand celestial observations that require only this voyage to render their seeming eccentricities consistent for ever. I shall satiate my ardent curiosity with the sight of a part of the world never before visited, and may tread a land never before imprinted by the foot of man. These are my enticements, and they are sufficient to conquer all fear of danger or death and to induce me to commence this laborious voyage with the joy a child feels when he embarks in a little boat, with his holiday mates, on an expedition of discovery up his native river. But supposing all these conjectures to be false, you cannot contest the inestimable benefit which I shall confer on all mankind, to the last generation, by discovering a passage near the pole to those countries, to reach which at present so many months are requisite; or by ascertaining the secret of the magnet, which, if at all possible, can only be effected by an undertaking such as mine.

text: “That I should live to become the means of evil troubles me; in every other respect, this hour, when I expect my release at any moment, is the only happy one I have known for several years. The shapes of the loved dead pass before me, and I hasten to join them. Farewell, Walton! Seek happiness in peace and shun ambition, even in its seemingly innocent form of seeking renown through science and discovery. And yet why do I say this? I have myself been ruined in these hopes, though another may yet succeed.”
source_text: “That he should live to be an instrument of mischief disturbs me; in other respects, this hour, when I momentarily expect my release, is the only happy one which I have enjoyed for several years. The forms of the beloved dead flit before me, and I hasten to their arms. Farewell, Walton! Seek happiness in tranquillity and avoid ambition, even if it be only the apparently innocent one of distinguishing yourself in science and discoveries. Yet why do I say this? I have myself been blasted in these hopes, yet another may succeed.”

text: In this way many dreadful hours went by; several of my dogs perished, and I myself was on the point of giving way beneath the weight of my sufferings when I saw your ship lying at anchor and offering me a hope of rescue and survival. I had never imagined that vessels ventured so far north, and I was stunned at the sight. I at once broke apart part of my sledge to make oars, and by this means was able, though with endless labor, to move my ice raft toward your ship. I had resolved that, if you were sailing south, I would still commit myself to the mercy of the sea rather than give up my design. I meant to persuade you to let me have a boat, so that I might continue in pursuit of my enemy. But you were heading north. You took me aboard when my strength was nearly gone, and I should soon have succumbed under the burden of all my hardships to a death I still fear, because my task remains unfinished.
source_text: In this manner many appalling hours passed; several of my dogs died, and I myself was about to sink under the accumulation of distress when I saw your vessel riding at anchor and holding forth to me hopes of succour and life. I had no conception that vessels ever came so far north and was astounded at the sight. I quickly destroyed part of my sledge to construct oars, and by these means was enabled, with infinite fatigue, to move my ice raft in the direction of your ship. I had determined, if you were going southwards, still to trust myself to the mercy of the seas rather than abandon my purpose. I hoped to induce you to grant me a boat with which I could pursue my enemy. But your direction was northwards. You took me on board when my vigour was exhausted, and I should soon have sunk under my multiplied hardships into a death which I still dread, for my task is unfulfilled.

text: I was exceedingly astonished to receive so rude a reply from a stranger, and I was no less discomposed when I observed the dark, angry looks of those who were with him. “Why do you answer me in such an uncivil manner?” I said. “Surely it is not the custom of Englishmen to treat strangers with such inhospitable rudeness.”
source_text: I was exceedingly surprised on receiving so rude an answer from a stranger, and I was also disconcerted on perceiving the frowning and angry countenances of his companions. “Why do you answer me so roughly?” I replied. “Surely it is not the custom of Englishmen to receive strangers so inhospitably.”

text: “Get up, my poor girl,” said Elizabeth. “Why are you kneeling if you are innocent? I am not one of your enemies. I believed you innocent, despite all the evidence, until I heard that you had yourself confessed to the crime. You say that report is false; and be certain, dear Justine, that nothing can shake my trust in you for an instant except your own confession.”
source_text: “Rise, my poor girl,” said Elizabeth; “why do you kneel, if you are innocent? I am not one of your enemies, I believed you guiltless, notwithstanding every evidence, until I heard that you had yourself declared your guilt. That report, you say, is false; and be assured, dear Justine, that nothing can shake my confidence in you for a moment, but your own confession.”

text: I stayed in Lausanne for two days, burdened by this painful state of mind. I looked out over the lake: the water was still, everything around me was quiet, and the snowy mountains, “the palaces of nature,” remained unchanged. Little by little, that serene and heavenly scene soothed me, and I went on with my journey toward Geneva.
source_text: I remained two days at Lausanne, in this painful state of mind. I contemplated the lake: the waters were placid; all around was calm; and the snowy mountains, “the palaces of nature,” were not changed. By degrees the calm and heavenly scene restored me, and I continued my journey towards Geneva.

text: “Alas, my father,” I said, “how little you know me. Human beings, and all their feelings and passions, would indeed be debased if a creature like me could feel pride. Poor, unhappy Justine was as innocent as I, and yet the same accusation fell upon her; she died because of it, and I am the cause of all this—I murdered her. William, Justine, and Henry—they all died by my hands.”
source_text: “Alas! My father,” said I, “how little do you know me. Human beings, their feelings and passions, would indeed be degraded if such a wretch as I felt pride. Justine, poor unhappy Justine, was as innocent as I, and she suffered the same charge; she died for it; and I am the cause of this—I murdered her. William, Justine, and Henry—they all died by my hands.”

text: About half a dozen men stepped forward, and when one was chosen by the magistrate, he stated under oath that he had been out fishing the previous night with his son and his brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they noticed a hard northerly wind beginning to rise, and so turned in for shore. It was a very dark night, the moon not yet having risen; they did not go in at the harbour, but, as they were accustomed to do, at a creek some two miles below it. He went on ahead, carrying part of the fishing gear, while the others followed at some distance behind. As he was making his way along the sands, his foot struck against something and he fell full length to the ground. His companions hurried up to help him, and by the light of their lantern they discovered that he had fallen upon the body of a man, who seemed to all appearances dead. At first they supposed it to be the corpse of someone drowned and washed ashore by the waves, but on closer inspection they found that the clothing was not wet, and that the body was not even cold. They immediately carried it to the cottage of an old woman nearby and tried, though without success, to bring it back to life. It seemed to be that of a handsome young man, about twenty-five years old. He had evidently been strangled, for there was no mark of injury save the black imprint of fingers around his neck.
source_text: About half a dozen men came forward; and, one being selected by the magistrate, he deposed that he had been out fishing the night before with his son and brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they observed a strong northerly blast rising, and they accordingly put in for port. It was a very dark night, as the moon had not yet risen; they did not land at the harbour, but, as they had been accustomed, at a creek about two miles below. He walked on first, carrying a part of the fishing tackle, and his companions followed him at some distance. As he was proceeding along the sands, he struck his foot against something and fell at his length on the ground. His companions came up to assist him, and by the light of their lantern they found that he had fallen on the body of a man, who was to all appearance dead. Their first supposition was that it was the corpse of some person who had been drowned and was thrown on shore by the waves, but on examination they found that the clothes were not wet and even that the body was not then cold. They instantly carried it to the cottage of an old woman near the spot and endeavoured, but in vain, to restore it to life. It appeared to be a handsome young man, about five and twenty years of age. He had apparently been strangled, for there was no sign of any violence except the black mark of fingers on his neck.

text: From that place we made our way to Oxenford. As we came within sight of the town, our thoughts were occupied by the recollection of those occurrences that had been enacted there more than a hundred and fifty years before. It was here that King Charles the First had once drawn together his adherents. The city had stood true to him, when the greater part of the kingdom had abandoned his side, and gone over to the banner of Parliament and freedom. The remembrance of that ill-fated monarch and his followers, the graceful Falkner, the haughty Marston, with his queen and his son, invested every quarter of the place, which they might be imagined to have frequented, with a singular attraction. The temper of former ages seemed still to linger there, and we took pleasure in tracing its relics. Yet had these emotions wanted the charm of fancy, the town itself possessed beauty enough to command our admiration. The colleges are venerable and full of character; the streets have an almost stately air; and the fair Isen, flowing along its side through meadows of the softest green, widens into a tranquil sheet of water, which mirrors the noble cluster of towers, pinnacles, and cupolas, half concealed amid the shade of ancient trees.
source_text: From thence we proceeded to Oxford. As we entered this city, our minds were filled with the remembrance of the events that had been transacted there more than a century and a half before. It was here that Charles I. had collected his forces. This city had remained faithful to him, after the whole nation had forsaken his cause to join the standard of Parliament and liberty. The memory of that unfortunate king and his companions, the amiable Falkland, the insolent Goring, his queen, and son, gave a peculiar interest to every part of the city which they might be supposed to have inhabited. The spirit of elder days found a dwelling here, and we delighted to trace its footsteps. If these feelings had not found an imaginary gratification, the appearance of the city had yet in itself sufficient beauty to obtain our admiration. The colleges are ancient and picturesque; the streets are almost magnificent; and the lovely Isis, which flows beside it through meadows of exquisite verdure, is spread forth into a placid expanse of waters, which reflects its majestic assemblage of towers, and spires, and domes, embosomed among aged trees.

text: “My dear Victor, do not talk so. Great misfortunes have come upon us, but let us only hold more closely to what still remains and turn the love we bore to those we have lost toward those who are still with us. Our circle will be small, but it will be knit closely together by affection and by the common burden of sorrow. And when time has eased your despair, new and beloved beings for your care will be born to take the place of those from whom we have been so cruelly taken.”
source_text: “My dear Victor, do not speak thus. Heavy misfortunes have befallen us, but let us only cling closer to what remains and transfer our love for those whom we have lost to those who yet live. Our circle will be small but bound close by the ties of affection and mutual misfortune. And when time shall have softened your despair, new and dear objects of care will be born to replace those of whom we have been so cruelly deprived.”

text: The night wore away, and the sun rose out of the sea. My feelings grew quieter, if quietness it can be called when the fury of rage sinks down into the depths of despair. I left the house, the dreadful scene of the previous night’s conflict, and wandered along the shore, which I almost thought of as an impassable wall between myself and my fellow beings; indeed, the wish that this might truly be so crossed my mind. I longed to spend my life upon that barren rock, lonely, it is true, but free from any sudden blow of misery. If I returned, it would be to be offered up, or to watch those I loved most die in the grasp of a demon whom I myself had made.
source_text: The night passed away, and the sun rose from the ocean; my feelings became calmer, if it may be called calmness when the violence of rage sinks into the depths of despair. I left the house, the horrid scene of the last night’s contention, and walked on the beach of the sea, which I almost regarded as an insuperable barrier between me and my fellow creatures; nay, a wish that such should prove the fact stole across me. I desired that I might pass my life on that barren rock, wearily, it is true, but uninterrupted by any sudden shock of misery. If I returned, it was to be sacrificed or to see those whom I most loved die under the grasp of a dæmon whom I had myself created.

text: “These wonderful accounts stirred strange feelings in me. Was man really, all at once, so powerful, so noble and splendid, and yet so wicked and contemptible? At one moment he seemed nothing more than a shoot of the evil principle; at another, he stood for everything one could imagine as lofty and godlike. To be a great and virtuous man seemed the highest honour that could ever befall a sensitive being; to be base and corrupt, as so many have been in history, seemed the deepest humiliation, a state more wretched than that of the blind mole or the harmless worm. For a long time I could not understand how one man could go out and murder another, or even why laws and governments existed at all; but when I was told of acts of vice and bloodshed, my astonishment faded, and I turned away in disgust and loathing.
source_text: “These wonderful narrations inspired me with strange feelings. Was man, indeed, at once so powerful, so virtuous and magnificent, yet so vicious and base? He appeared at one time a mere scion of the evil principle and at another as all that can be conceived of noble and godlike. To be a great and virtuous man appeared the highest honour that can befall a sensitive being; to be base and vicious, as many on record have been, appeared the lowest degradation, a condition more abject than that of the blind mole or harmless worm. For a long time I could not conceive how one man could go forth to murder his fellow, or even why there were laws and governments; but when I heard details of vice and bloodshed, my wonder ceased and I turned away with disgust and loathing.

text: At first I was moved by the signs of his distress; yet when I remembered what Frankenstein had told me of his gift for eloquence and persuasion, and when I looked once more upon the lifeless body of my friend, my anger flared again. “Wretch!” I cried. “It is fitting that you should come here to whimper over the ruin you have made. You cast a torch into a heap of buildings, and when they are burned down, you sit among the wreckage and mourn their destruction. Hypocritical fiend! If the one you now lament were still alive, he would still be the target, still once more the victim, of your cursed vengeance. What you feel is not pity; you grieve only because the prey of your malice has been taken beyond your reach.”
source_text: I was at first touched by the expressions of his misery; yet, when I called to mind what Frankenstein had said of his powers of eloquence and persuasion, and when I again cast my eyes on the lifeless form of my friend, indignation was rekindled within me. “Wretch!” I said. “It is well that you come here to whine over the desolation that you have made. You throw a torch into a pile of buildings, and when they are consumed, you sit among the ruins and lament the fall. Hypocritical fiend! If he whom you mourn still lived, still would he be the object, again would he become the prey, of your accursed vengeance. It is not pity that you feel; you lament only because the victim of your malignity is withdrawn from your power.”

text: I listened to my father in silence and for some time could not answer him at all. My mind turned swiftly through a crowd of thoughts as I tried to come to some decision. Alas! To me, the prospect of an immediate marriage with my Elizabeth was one of dread and anguish. I was held by a solemn promise I had not yet kept and dared not violate; and if I did, what countless calamities might not fall upon me and my devoted family! Could I join in a feast with this deadly burden still hung about my neck, weighing me down to the earth? I must first fulfill my pledge and allow the monster and his mate to depart before I permitted myself the happiness of a union from which I hoped for peace.
source_text: I listened to my father in silence and remained for some time incapable of offering any reply. I revolved rapidly in my mind a multitude of thoughts and endeavoured to arrive at some conclusion. Alas! To me the idea of an immediate union with my Elizabeth was one of horror and dismay. I was bound by a solemn promise which I had not yet fulfilled and dared not break, or if I did, what manifold miseries might not impend over me and my devoted family! Could I enter into a festival with this deadly weight yet hanging round my neck and bowing me to the ground? I must perform my engagement and let the monster depart with his mate before I allowed myself to enjoy the delight of a union from which I expected peace.

text: In this way I arranged my tasks when I first arrived; but as I went on with my work, it grew more and more dreadful and distasteful to me each day. At times I could scarcely force myself to enter my laboratory for several days together, while at others I laboured day and night in an effort to finish what I had begun. It was, in truth, a loathsome business in which I was occupied. In my first experiment, a sort of ardent frenzy had blinded me to the horror of what I was doing; my thoughts were wholly fixed on the achievement of my task, and I closed my eyes to the terror of my own actions. But now I approached it in full consciousness, and my heart often grew faint at the work of my own hands.
source_text: In this manner I distributed my occupations when I first arrived, but as I proceeded in my labour, it became every day more horrible and irksome to me. Sometimes I could not prevail on myself to enter my laboratory for several days, and at other times I toiled day and night in order to complete my work. It was, indeed, a filthy process in which I was engaged. During my first experiment, a kind of enthusiastic frenzy had blinded me to the horror of my employment; my mind was intently fixed on the consummation of my labour, and my eyes were shut to the horror of my proceedings. But now I went to it in cold blood, and my heart often sickened at the work of my hands.

text: Every conversation of the cottagers now revealed fresh marvels to me. As I listened to the lessons Felix imparted to the Arabian, the strange order of human society was gradually made known to me. I heard of the division of property, of vast riches and miserable poverty, of rank, ancestry, and noble blood.
source_text: “Every conversation of the cottagers now opened new wonders to me. While I listened to the instructions which Felix bestowed upon the Arabian, the strange system of human society was explained to me. I heard of the division of property, of immense wealth and squalid poverty, of rank, descent, and noble blood.

text: “I remember that the first time I did this, the young woman, upon opening the door in the morning, seemed greatly astonished to find a large heap of wood standing outside. She cried out in a loud voice, and the young man came to her side, likewise showing surprise. I noticed with satisfaction that he did not go into the forest that day, but remained to mend the cottage and tend the garden.
source_text: “I remember, the first time that I did this, the young woman, when she opened the door in the morning, appeared greatly astonished on seeing a great pile of wood on the outside. She uttered some words in a loud voice, and the youth joined her, who also expressed surprise. I observed, with pleasure, that he did not go to the forest that day, but spent it in repairing the cottage and cultivating the garden.

text: Thus neither the solace of friendship, nor the loveliness of earth, nor the splendor of heaven, could ransom my soul from misery; even the tones of affection were powerless to avail. I was wrapped in a darkness through which no kindly influence could pierce. The stricken deer, halting with failing limbs toward some untraced thicket, there to stare upon the shaft that had transfixed it, and to perish, was but an emblem of myself.
source_text: Thus not the tenderness of friendship, nor the beauty of earth, nor of heaven, could redeem my soul from woe; the very accents of love were ineffectual. I was encompassed by a cloud which no beneficial influence could penetrate. The wounded deer dragging its fainting limbs to some untrodden brake, there to gaze upon the arrow which had pierced it, and to die, was but a type of me.

text: All was once more hushed, yet his accents still echoed in my mind. My blood burned with a furious desire to pursue the destroyer of my peace and cast him headlong into the sea. I paced my chamber in restless agitation, while my fancy called up a thousand images fitted to harass and wound me. Why had I not followed him, and joined him in a struggle unto death? But I had suffered him to depart, and he had turned his course toward the shore. I trembled to consider what unfortunate being might next be offered up to his ravenous vengeance. Then his other words returned to me: “I shall be with you on your bridal night.” Thus, then, the season was appointed for the accomplishment of my doom. In that hour I was to perish, and his hatred would be at once glutted and extinguished. The thought awakened no terror; yet when I turned to my dear Margaret, and imagined the tears and unending grief that would be hers when she learned that her beloved had been so cruelly torn from her, tears—the first I had shed for many months—gushed from my eyes, and I vowed that I would not yield to my foe without a desperate contest.
source_text: All was again silent, but his words rang in my ears. I burned with rage to pursue the murderer of my peace and precipitate him into the ocean. I walked up and down my room hastily and perturbed, while my imagination conjured up a thousand images to torment and sting me. Why had I not followed him and closed with him in mortal strife? But I had suffered him to depart, and he had directed his course towards the mainland. I shuddered to think who might be the next victim sacrificed to his insatiate revenge. And then I thought again of his words—“_I will be with you on your wedding-night._” That, then, was the period fixed for the fulfilment of my destiny. In that hour I should die and at once satisfy and extinguish his malice. The prospect did not move me to fear; yet when I thought of my beloved Elizabeth, of her tears and endless sorrow, when she should find her lover so barbarously snatched from her, tears, the first I had shed for many months, streamed from my eyes, and I resolved not to fall before my enemy without a bitter struggle.

text: An explanation! You may well ask, what, indeed, has Clara to explain? If this is truly your thought, then my inquiries are at an end, and all my misgivings are laid to rest. Yet you are far removed from me, and it may be that you would shrink from, while still desiring, such an explanation; and as this seems not impossible, I can no longer venture to delay setting down what, in your absence, I have many times longed to say to you, but never found the heart to begin.
source_text: Explanation! You may possibly say, What can Elizabeth have to explain? If you really say this, my questions are answered and all my doubts satisfied. But you are distant from me, and it is possible that you may dread and yet be pleased with this explanation; and in a probability of this being the case, I dare not any longer postpone writing what, during your absence, I have often wished to express to you but have never had the courage to begin.

text: He is now much recovered from his illness and is constantly upon the deck, as though keeping watch for the sledge that had gone before his own. Yet, miserable as he is, his grief does not so wholly engross him that he cannot take a warm interest in the designs of others. He has often talked with me of mine, which I have laid before him without concealment. He entered with close attention into all my reasons for hoping at last to succeed, and into every slight particular of the arrangements I had made to ensure it. His sympathy easily drew from me the language of my own heart; I gave voice to the consuming ardour of my soul and declared, with all the passion that inspired me, how willingly I would surrender my fortune, my very life, every hope I possessed, to the advancement of my undertaking. The life of one man, or his death, seemed to me a small enough price to pay for the knowledge I pursued, for the power I should win and bequeath over the hostile elements that war against our race. As I spoke, a deep shadow came over my listener’s face. At first I saw him struggling to master his feelings; he covered his eyes with his hands, and my own voice trembled and broke as I watched tears fall rapidly between his fingers, while a groan escaped his labouring chest. I stopped; after a little he answered in broken tones: “Unhappy man! Do you, too, share my madness? Have you also tasted the maddening draught? Listen to me; let me tell you my story, and you will fling the cup from your mouth!”
source_text: He is now much recovered from his illness and is continually on the deck, apparently watching for the sledge that preceded his own. Yet, although unhappy, he is not so utterly occupied by his own misery but that he interests himself deeply in the projects of others. He has frequently conversed with me on mine, which I have communicated to him without disguise. He entered attentively into all my arguments in favour of my eventual success and into every minute detail of the measures I had taken to secure it. I was easily led by the sympathy which he evinced to use the language of my heart, to give utterance to the burning ardour of my soul and to say, with all the fervour that warmed me, how gladly I would sacrifice my fortune, my existence, my every hope, to the furtherance of my enterprise. One man’s life or death were but a small price to pay for the acquirement of the knowledge which I sought, for the dominion I should acquire and transmit over the elemental foes of our race. As I spoke, a dark gloom spread over my listener’s countenance. At first I perceived that he tried to suppress his emotion; he placed his hands before his eyes, and my voice quivered and failed me as I beheld tears trickle fast from between his fingers; a groan burst from his heaving breast. I paused; at length he spoke, in broken accents: “Unhappy man! Do you share my madness? Have you drunk also of the intoxicating draught? Hear me; let me reveal my tale, and you will dash the cup from your lips!”

text: The peasant woman, seeing that my mother had turned eyes of wonder and admiration upon the lovely girl, hastened to tell her story. She was not her own child, but the daughter of a nobleman of Milan. Her mother had been German and had died in bringing her into the world. The infant had been entrusted to these kind people to be nursed; at that time they were in better circumstances. They had only lately been married, and their first child had just been born. The girl’s father was one of those Italians who had been reared on memories of ancient Italy’s glory—one of the _schiavi ognor frementi,_ who labored for the freedom of their country. He had fallen a victim to its weakness. Whether he had died, or still lay wasting away in the prisons of Austria, no one knew. His estate had been seized; his child was left an orphan and a beggar. She remained with her foster parents and grew up in their humble dwelling, fairer than a garden rose among dark-leaved brambles.
source_text: The peasant woman, perceiving that my mother fixed eyes of wonder and admiration on this lovely girl, eagerly communicated her history. She was not her child, but the daughter of a Milanese nobleman. Her mother was a German and had died on giving her birth. The infant had been placed with these good people to nurse: they were better off then. They had not been long married, and their eldest child was but just born. The father of their charge was one of those Italians nursed in the memory of the antique glory of Italy—one among the _schiavi ognor frementi,_ who exerted himself to obtain the liberty of his country. He became the victim of its weakness. Whether he had died or still lingered in the dungeons of Austria was not known. His property was confiscated; his child became an orphan and a beggar. She continued with her foster parents and bloomed in their rude abode, fairer than a garden rose among dark-leaved brambles.

text: I had remained composed throughout the day, but as soon as night blotted out the shapes of things, a thousand fears sprang up in my mind. I was anxious and alert, while my right hand clutched a pistol concealed in my breast; every sound filled me with terror, yet I was resolved to sell my life dearly and not flinch from the struggle until either my own life or that of my enemy was ended.
source_text: I had been calm during the day, but so soon as night obscured the shapes of objects, a thousand fears arose in my mind. I was anxious and watchful, while my right hand grasped a pistol which was hidden in my bosom; every sound terrified me, but I resolved that I would sell my life dearly and not shrink from the conflict until my own life or that of my adversary was extinguished.

text: His story was told clearly and with every sign of plain honesty; yet I confess that the letters of Felix and Safie, which he had shown me, together with the sight of the monster from our ship, convinced me of the truth of his account far more than all his earnest, coherent protestations. So such a monster truly exists! I cannot doubt it, though I remain overwhelmed with astonishment and wonder. At times I tried to draw from Frankenstein the details of how his creature had been made, but on this matter he was completely inscrutable.
source_text: His tale is connected and told with an appearance of the simplest truth, yet I own to you that the letters of Felix and Safie, which he showed me, and the apparition of the monster seen from our ship, brought to me a greater conviction of the truth of his narrative than his asseverations, however earnest and connected. Such a monster has, then, really existence! I cannot doubt it, yet I am lost in surprise and admiration. Sometimes I endeavoured to gain from Frankenstein the particulars of his creature’s formation, but on this point he was impenetrable.

text: These meditations have already allayed the disorder with which I opened my letter, and I now feel my spirit warmed by an ardour that seems to lift me nearer to heaven; for nothing so surely calms the mind as a settled aim, an object upon which the soul may fasten its inward gaze. This journey has been the cherished vision of my boyhood. With eager zeal I have perused the narratives of the many expeditions undertaken in hopes of reaching the North Pacific by way of the seas that encircle the pole. You will recall, perhaps, that a complete chronicle of all voyages made in the cause of discovery formed the whole of our kind Uncle Edward’s library. My schooling was much neglected, yet I was ever consumed by a love of reading. Those books were my companions by day and by night, and the more intimately I knew them, the deeper grew that sorrow I had felt as a child when I learned that my father’s last command had forbidden my uncle to permit me to go to sea.
source_text: These reflections have dispelled the agitation with which I began my letter, and I feel my heart glow with an enthusiasm which elevates me to heaven, for nothing contributes so much to tranquillise the mind as a steady purpose—a point on which the soul may fix its intellectual eye. This expedition has been the favourite dream of my early years. I have read with ardour the accounts of the various voyages which have been made in the prospect of arriving at the North Pacific Ocean through the seas which surround the pole. You may remember that a history of all the voyages made for purposes of discovery composed the whole of our good Uncle Thomas’ library. My education was neglected, yet I was passionately fond of reading. These volumes were my study day and night, and my familiarity with them increased that regret which I had felt, as a child, on learning that my father’s dying injunction had forbidden my uncle to allow me to embark in a seafaring life.

text: When I discovered so prodigious a power lodged at last in my own grasp, I remained for a long while irresolute as to the use I should make of it. Though I could impart the spark of life, to fashion a body fit to receive that spark, with all the elaborate threads of sinew, muscle, and blood, was still a task of almost unimaginable toil and complexity. At first I was uncertain whether I ought to attempt a creature resembling myself, or one of humbler structure; yet my fancy, lifted beyond all measure by that earliest triumph, would not suffer me to question my ability to animate a being as intricate and marvelous as man. The materials then at my disposal seemed scarcely equal to such a formidable enterprise, but I never doubted that, in the end, I should prevail. I was prepared for repeated disappointments; my labors might be thwarted again and again, and my finished work might prove defective after all. Still, when I reflected upon the continual advances made each day in natural philosophy and the mechanical arts, I took comfort in the hope that my present endeavors would at least prepare the way for success in the future. Nor did the vast scale and subtlety of my design appear to me any proof that it could not be accomplished. With these sentiments, I set myself to the creation of a human being. The extreme delicacy of the parts greatly retarded my progress, and so I resolved, contrary to my original purpose, to construct the creature on a gigantic scale—some eight feet in height, and with limbs proportioned accordingly. Having come to this determination, and after spending several months in the fruitful collection and ordering of my materials, I at length commenced my work.
source_text: When I found so astonishing a power placed within my hands, I hesitated a long time concerning the manner in which I should employ it. Although I possessed the capacity of bestowing animation, yet to prepare a frame for the reception of it, with all its intricacies of fibres, muscles, and veins, still remained a work of inconceivable difficulty and labour. I doubted at first whether I should attempt the creation of a being like myself, or one of simpler organization; but my imagination was too much exalted by my first success to permit me to doubt of my ability to give life to an animal as complex and wonderful as man. The materials at present within my command hardly appeared adequate to so arduous an undertaking, but I doubted not that I should ultimately succeed. I prepared myself for a multitude of reverses; my operations might be incessantly baffled, and at last my work be imperfect, yet when I considered the improvement which every day takes place in science and mechanics, I was encouraged to hope my present attempts would at least lay the foundations of future success. Nor could I consider the magnitude and complexity of my plan as any argument of its impracticability. It was with these feelings that I began the creation of a human being. As the minuteness of the parts formed a great hindrance to my speed, I resolved, contrary to my first intention, to make the being of a gigantic stature, that is to say, about eight feet in height, and proportionably large. After having formed this determination and having spent some months in successfully collecting and arranging my materials, I began.

text: When I returned home, my first concern was to obtain the complete works of this writer, and then those of Paracelsus and Albertus Magnus. I read and pored over the extravagant speculations of these men with delight; they seemed to me like treasures scarcely known to anyone but myself. I have said of myself that I was always possessed by an ardent desire to uncover nature’s secrets. Yet despite the severe toil and remarkable discoveries of modern philosophers, I always emerged from my studies dissatisfied and unfulfilled. Sir Isaac Newton is said to have confessed that he felt like a child gathering shells along the shore of the vast and unexplored ocean of truth. The successors of his in every branch of natural philosophy whom I knew seemed, to my boyish understanding, no more than novices occupied in the same quest.
source_text: When I returned home my first care was to procure the whole works of this author, and afterwards of Paracelsus and Albertus Magnus. I read and studied the wild fancies of these writers with delight; they appeared to me treasures known to few besides myself. I have described myself as always having been imbued with a fervent longing to penetrate the secrets of nature. In spite of the intense labour and wonderful discoveries of modern philosophers, I always came from my studies discontented and unsatisfied. Sir Isaac Newton is said to have avowed that he felt like a child picking up shells beside the great and unexplored ocean of truth. Those of his successors in each branch of natural philosophy with whom I was acquainted appeared even to my boy’s apprehensions as tyros engaged in the same pursuit.

text: Oh! When shall my guiding spirit, in leading me toward the dæmon, grant me the peace I long for; or must I die while he still lives? If I do die, swear to me, Walton, that he shall not escape, that you will hunt him down and satisfy my vengeance in his death. And do I dare ask you to undertake my own pilgrimage, to endure the sufferings I have endured? No; I am not so selfish. Yet when I am dead, if he should appear, if the ministers of retribution should bring him before you, swear that he shall not live—swear that he shall not triumph over my gathered sorrows and survive to add fresh crimes to his dark record. He is eloquent and persuasive, and once his speech even held sway over my heart; but trust him not. His soul is as monstrous as his body, steeped in deceit and demonic cruelty. Do not listen to him; summon the names of William, Justine, Clerval, Elizabeth, my father, and the miserable Victor, and drive your sword into his heart. I shall remain near and guide the blade to its mark.
source_text: Oh! When will my guiding spirit, in conducting me to the dæmon, allow me the rest I so much desire; or must I die, and he yet live? If I do, swear to me, Walton, that he shall not escape, that you will seek him and satisfy my vengeance in his death. And do I dare to ask of you to undertake my pilgrimage, to endure the hardships that I have undergone? No; I am not so selfish. Yet, when I am dead, if he should appear, if the ministers of vengeance should conduct him to you, swear that he shall not live—swear that he shall not triumph over my accumulated woes and survive to add to the list of his dark crimes. He is eloquent and persuasive, and once his words had even power over my heart; but trust him not. His soul is as hellish as his form, full of treachery and fiend-like malice. Hear him not; call on the names of William, Justine, Clerval, Elizabeth, my father, and of the wretched Victor, and thrust your sword into his heart. I will hover near and direct the steel aright.

text: “Yet I cannot require you to forsake your native land and your friends in order to accomplish this charge; and now that you are about to return to England, you will have little opportunity of encountering him. But the weighing of these matters, and the careful adjustment of what you may judge to be your duties, I must leave to yourself; my own reason and sentiments are already unsettled by the close approach of death. I dare not entreat you to do what I believe to be right, for even now I may still be deceived by feeling.
source_text: “Yet I cannot ask you to renounce your country and friends to fulfil this task; and now that you are returning to England, you will have little chance of meeting with him. But the consideration of these points, and the well balancing of what you may esteem your duties, I leave to you; my judgment and ideas are already disturbed by the near approach of death. I dare not ask you to do what I think right, for I may still be misled by passion.

text: My journey was long, and the hardships I suffered were severe. It was late autumn when I left the region where I had lived so long. I traveled only by night, terrified of coming upon the face of a human being. The world around me was falling into decay; the sun had lost its heat; rain and snow beat down upon me; great rivers lay frozen over; the ground was hard and cold and bare, and I found no refuge. O earth, how often I called down curses on the power that had brought me into existence! The gentleness of my nature had vanished, and everything within me had turned to gall and bitterness. The closer I came to your dwelling, the more fiercely the spirit of revenge burned in my heart. Snow fell and the waters froze, yet I did not rest. Now and then a few incidents guided me, and I had a map of the country; still I often strayed far from my route. The torment of my emotions gave me no peace; nothing happened from which my rage and misery could not draw nourishment. Yet one event, when I reached the borders of Switzerland, when the sun had regained its warmth and the earth had begun once more to show green, confirmed in a singular way the bitterness and horror of my feelings.
source_text: “My travels were long and the sufferings I endured intense. It was late in autumn when I quitted the district where I had so long resided. I travelled only at night, fearful of encountering the visage of a human being. Nature decayed around me, and the sun became heatless; rain and snow poured around me; mighty rivers were frozen; the surface of the earth was hard and chill, and bare, and I found no shelter. Oh, earth! How often did I imprecate curses on the cause of my being! The mildness of my nature had fled, and all within me was turned to gall and bitterness. The nearer I approached to your habitation, the more deeply did I feel the spirit of revenge enkindled in my heart. Snow fell, and the waters were hardened, but I rested not. A few incidents now and then directed me, and I possessed a map of the country; but I often wandered wide from my path. The agony of my feelings allowed me no respite; no incident occurred from which my rage and misery could not extract its food; but a circumstance that happened when I arrived on the confines of Switzerland, when the sun had recovered its warmth and the earth again began to look green, confirmed in an especial manner the bitterness and horror of my feelings.

text: And could not words like these from her, whom I had cherished above every other gift of fortune, be enough to驱 the fiend lurking in my heart? As she spoke, I moved toward her, almost in fear, lest at that very instant the destroyer should have been close at hand to rob me of her.
source_text: And could not such words from her whom I fondly prized before every other gift of fortune suffice to chase away the fiend that lurked in my heart? Even as she spoke I drew near to her, as if in terror, lest at that very moment the destroyer had been near to rob me of her.

text: From the anguish of my own heart, I turned to regard the deep, voiceless sorrow of my Elizabeth. This, too, was my doing! And my father’s grief, and the ruin of that home so lately full of sunshine—all this was wrought by my thrice-accursed hands! You weep, unhappy ones, but these are not your last tears. Once more you shall lift the funeral cry, and the notes of your lamentation shall be heard again and again. Frankenstein, your son, your kinsman, your early and beloved friend; he who would pour out every drop of his life-blood for your sakes, who knows no thought and feels no joy unless it is reflected in your dear faces, who would fill the air with blessings and spend his life in your service—he implores you to weep, to shed tears without number; and he would count himself happier than he had ever hoped, if only relentless fate might be appeased, and if destruction would halt before the peace of the grave had come to end your cruel sufferings.
source_text: From the tortures of my own heart, I turned to contemplate the deep and voiceless grief of my Elizabeth. This also was my doing! And my father’s woe, and the desolation of that late so smiling home all was the work of my thrice-accursed hands! Ye weep, unhappy ones, but these are not your last tears! Again shall you raise the funeral wail, and the sound of your lamentations shall again and again be heard! Frankenstein, your son, your kinsman, your early, much-loved friend; he who would spend each vital drop of blood for your sakes, who has no thought nor sense of joy except as it is mirrored also in your dear countenances, who would fill the air with blessings and spend his life in serving you—he bids you weep, to shed countless tears; happy beyond his hopes, if thus inexorable fate be satisfied, and if the destruction pause before the peace of the grave have succeeded to your sad torments!

text: We are still hemmed in by towering masses of ice, still in immediate peril of being crushed in their strife. The cold is extreme, and many of my unhappy companions have already found their graves in this waste of desolation. Frankenstein’s health has daily declined; a feverish fire yet burns in his eyes, but he is worn out, and when suddenly summoned to effort, he quickly sinks again into seeming lifelessness.
source_text: We are still surrounded by mountains of ice, still in imminent danger of being crushed in their conflict. The cold is excessive, and many of my unfortunate comrades have already found a grave amidst this scene of desolation. Frankenstein has daily declined in health; a feverish fire still glimmers in his eyes, but he is exhausted, and when suddenly roused to any exertion, he speedily sinks again into apparent lifelessness.

text: “And now, with the world spread out before me, where was I to turn my steps? I had determined to flee as far as possible from the scene of my miseries; yet to me, abhorred and loathed, every land must be alike dreadful. At length the thought of you came into my mind. From your papers I had learned that you were my father, my creator; and to whom should I more properly appeal than to him who had bestowed life upon me? Among the instructions Felix had given Safie, geography had not been forgotten; from these I had learned the relative positions of the various countries of the earth.
