You had named Geneva as your native town, and toward that place I resolved to direct my course.
source_text: “And now, with the world before me, whither should I bend my steps? I resolved to fly far from the scene of my misfortunes; but to me, hated and despised, every country must be equally horrible. At length the thought of you crossed my mind. I learned from your papers that you were my father, my creator; and to whom could I apply with more fitness than to him who had given me life? Among the lessons that Felix had bestowed upon Safie, geography had not been omitted; I had learned from these the relative situations of the different countries of the earth. You had mentioned Geneva as the name of your native town, and towards this place I resolved to proceed.

text: “That, too, is my victim!” he cried. “In his slaughter my offenses are completed; the wretched course of my existence is drawn to its end! O, Mercer! noble and self-sacrificing soul! What boots it that I now implore your forgiveness? I, who have beyond recall undone you by destroying all you held dear. Alas! He is cold; he can no longer answer me.”
source_text: “That is also my victim!” he exclaimed. “In his murder my crimes are consummated; the miserable series of my being is wound to its close! Oh, Frankenstein! Generous and self-devoted being! What does it avail that I now ask thee to pardon me? I, who irretrievably destroyed thee by destroying all thou lovedst. Alas! He is cold, he cannot answer me.”

text: Morning came before I reached the village of Chamounix; I had taken no rest, but turned at once back toward Geneva. I could not even shape my feelings into words within my own breast—they pressed on me with the burden of a mountain, and their very intensity crushed my sorrow beneath them. So I returned home, and, entering the house, presented myself before the family. My gaunt, wild look filled them with the deepest alarm, yet I gave no answer to their questions, and scarcely uttered a word. I felt as though a curse were laid upon me—as though I had no claim upon their pity—as though I could never again share companionship with them. And yet I loved them with adoration; and, to preserve them, I resolved to devote myself to the task I most loathed. The thought of such an employment caused all else in life to drift before me like a dream, and that one purpose alone possessed for me the substance of reality.
source_text: Morning dawned before I arrived at the village of Chamounix; I took no rest, but returned immediately to Geneva. Even in my own heart I could give no expression to my sensations—they weighed on me with a mountain’s weight and their excess destroyed my agony beneath them. Thus I returned home, and entering the house, presented myself to the family. My haggard and wild appearance awoke intense alarm, but I answered no question, scarcely did I speak. I felt as if I were placed under a ban—as if I had no right to claim their sympathies—as if never more might I enjoy companionship with them. Yet even thus I loved them to adoration; and to save them, I resolved to dedicate myself to my most abhorred task. The prospect of such an occupation made every other circumstance of existence pass before me like a dream, and that thought only had to me the reality of life.

text: I pursued him, and for many months this has been my labor. Led by a slender clue, I traced the windings of the Rhône, but all in vain. At last the blue Mediterranean came into view, and by a strange chance I saw the fiend, by night, steal aboard and conceal himself in a vessel bound for the Black Sea. I secured my passage in the same ship, but he escaped me, I know not how.
source_text: I pursued him, and for many months this has been my task. Guided by a slight clue, I followed the windings of the Rhone, but vainly. The blue Mediterranean appeared, and by a strange chance, I saw the fiend enter by night and hide himself in a vessel bound for the Black Sea. I took my passage in the same ship, but he escaped, I know not how.

text: One day, as I was slowly mending, I sat in a chair with my eyes half closed and my cheeks as pale as those of the dead. I was sunk in despair and misery, and I often thought it would be better to seek death than to continue in a world that seemed to me filled only with wretchedness. At times I even wondered whether I ought to declare myself guilty and undergo the punishment of the law, being less innocent than poor Justine had been. I was brooding over such thoughts when the door of my chamber was opened and Mr. Kirwin came in. His face showed sympathy and pity; he drew a chair near to mine and spoke to me in French,
source_text: One day, while I was gradually recovering, I was seated in a chair, my eyes half open and my cheeks livid like those in death. I was overcome by gloom and misery and often reflected I had better seek death than desire to remain in a world which to me was replete with wretchedness. At one time I considered whether I should not declare myself guilty and suffer the penalty of the law, less innocent than poor Justine had been. Such were my thoughts when the door of my apartment was opened and Mr. Kirwin entered. His countenance expressed sympathy and compassion; he drew a chair close to mine and addressed me in French,

text: We departed from Oxford with reluctance, and made our way to Matlock, where we were next to repose. The surrounding country bore, in a stronger degree, the likeness of Swiss scenery; yet all was upon a humbler scale, and the soft green heights lacked that diadem of far-off snowy Alps which ever rises above the pine-clad mountains of my own land. We examined the marvelous cavern and the small cabinets of natural history, where the objects of curiosity are arranged in the same fashion as in the collections of Servoz and Chamouny. The latter name caused me to shudder when Henry uttered it, and I was eager to quit Matlock, so closely had that dreadful scene become united with it.
source_text: We left Oxford with regret and proceeded to Matlock, which was our next place of rest. The country in the neighbourhood of this village resembled, to a greater degree, the scenery of Switzerland; but everything is on a lower scale, and the green hills want the crown of distant white Alps which always attend on the piny mountains of my native country. We visited the wondrous cave and the little cabinets of natural history, where the curiosities are disposed in the same manner as in the collections at Servox and Chamounix. The latter name made me tremble when pronounced by Henry, and I hastened to quit Matlock, with which that terrible scene was thus associated.

text: Even if they were to quit Europe and dwell in the deserts of the new world, the first fruits of those affections for which the dæmon pined would still be children; and thus a race of devils would be spread over the earth, making the very existence of mankind precarious and terrible. Had I the right, for my own advantage, to lay this curse upon unborn generations? I had already been shaken by the sophistries of the being I had fashioned; I had been stunned by his infernal threats; but now, for the first time, the wickedness of my promise rose clearly before me. I shuddered at the thought that future ages might denounce me as their plague, one whose selfishness had not hesitated to purchase its own peace at the possible cost of the whole human race.
source_text: Even if they were to leave Europe and inhabit the deserts of the new world, yet one of the first results of those sympathies for which the dæmon thirsted would be children, and a race of devils would be propagated upon the earth who might make the very existence of the species of man a condition precarious and full of terror. Had I right, for my own benefit, to inflict this curse upon everlasting generations? I had before been moved by the sophisms of the being I had created; I had been struck senseless by his fiendish threats; but now, for the first time, the wickedness of my promise burst upon me; I shuddered to think that future ages might curse me as their pest, whose selfishness had not hesitated to buy its own peace at the price, perhaps, of the existence of the whole human race.

text: As I looked at him, his face showed the full measure of malice and treachery. My mind went half mad as I remembered my promise to make another being like him, and, trembling with fury, I ripped apart the work I had been engaged upon. The wretch saw me destroy the creature on whose future existence his own happiness depended, and with a howl of fiendish despair and vengeance, he turned away and left.
source_text: As I looked on him, his countenance expressed the utmost extent of malice and treachery. I thought with a sensation of madness on my promise of creating another like to him, and trembling with passion, tore to pieces the thing on which I was engaged. The wretch saw me destroy the creature on whose future existence he depended for happiness, and with a howl of devilish despair and revenge, withdrew.

text: While they spoke, I had withdrawn to the farthest shadow of the prison chamber, that I might hide the dreadful torment which mastered me. Despair! Who had courage to pronounce the word? The unhappy creature, who on the morrow was to cross the fearful threshold that divides the living from the dead, knew not, as I did, that same dark and sharpened misery. I set my teeth, grinding them together, and a groan, drawn from the very depth of my being, escaped me. Elara started. When she perceived who stood before her, she came nearer and said, “My dear sir, you are very kind to come to me; you, I trust, do not think me guilty?”
source_text: During this conversation I had retired to a corner of the prison room, where I could conceal the horrid anguish that possessed me. Despair! Who dared talk of that? The poor victim, who on the morrow was to pass the awful boundary between life and death, felt not, as I did, such deep and bitter agony. I gnashed my teeth and ground them together, uttering a groan that came from my inmost soul. Justine started. When she saw who it was, she approached me and said, “Dear sir, you are very kind to visit me; you, I hope, do not believe that I am guilty?”

text: “You have travelled; you have spent several years of your life at Ingolstadt; and I confess to you, my friend, that when I saw you last autumn so wretched, fleeing into solitude from the company of every living being, I could not help imagining that you might regret our attachment and think yourself bound in honour to fulfil your parents’ wishes, though they stood against your own inclinations. But this is mistaken reasoning. I confess to you, my friend, that I love you, and that in my tender dreams of the future you have ever been my constant companion and friend. Yet it is your happiness, as much as my own, that I seek when I tell you that our marriage would make me forever miserable unless it were the free dictate of your own heart. Even now I weep to think that, weighed down as you are by the most cruel misfortunes, you may suppress, under the sacred name of honour, all hope of that love and bliss which alone could restore you to yourself. I, who bear you so unselfish an affection, may only multiply your sorrows tenfold by standing in the way of your desires. Ah, Victor, be certain that your cousin and playmate loves you too truly not to suffer deeply from this thought. Be happy, my friend; and if you grant me this one request, be assured that nothing on earth shall ever disturb my peace.
source_text: “You have travelled; you have spent several years of your life at Ingolstadt; and I confess to you, my friend, that when I saw you last autumn so unhappy, flying to solitude from the society of every creature, I could not help supposing that you might regret our connection and believe yourself bound in honour to fulfil the wishes of your parents, although they opposed themselves to your inclinations. But this is false reasoning. I confess to you, my friend, that I love you and that in my airy dreams of futurity you have been my constant friend and companion. But it is your happiness I desire as well as my own when I declare to you that our marriage would render me eternally miserable unless it were the dictate of your own free choice. Even now I weep to think that, borne down as you are by the cruellest misfortunes, you may stifle, by the word _honour_, all hope of that love and happiness which would alone restore you to yourself. I, who have so disinterested an affection for you, may increase your miseries tenfold by being an obstacle to your wishes. Ah! Victor, be assured that your cousin and playmate has too sincere a love for you not to be made miserable by this supposition. Be happy, my friend; and if you obey me in this one request, remain satisfied that nothing on earth will have the power to interrupt my tranquillity.

text: While I was still swayed by these emotions, I left the place where I had committed the murder, and, in search of a more hidden refuge, entered a barn that had seemed to me deserted. There, upon a bed of straw, a woman was sleeping. She was young, not indeed so lovely as the one whose portrait I held, yet pleasing in appearance and radiant with the freshness of youth and health. Here, I thought, was one of those to whom those joyful smiles are granted that are denied to me. Then I bent over her and murmured, “Awake, fairest; thy lover is at hand—he who would give his life to win but one glance of affection from thine eyes. My beloved, awake!”
source_text: “While I was overcome by these feelings, I left the spot where I had committed the murder, and seeking a more secluded hiding-place, I entered a barn which had appeared to me to be empty. A woman was sleeping on some straw; she was young, not indeed so beautiful as her whose portrait I held, but of an agreeable aspect and blooming in the loveliness of youth and health. Here, I thought, is one of those whose joy-imparting smiles are bestowed on all but me. And then I bent over her and whispered, ‘Awake, fairest, thy lover is near—he who would give his life but to obtain one look of affection from thine eyes; my beloved, awake!’

text: “I had admired the perfect features of my cottagers—their grace, their beauty, and their delicate complexions; but how horrified I was when I looked upon myself in a clear pool! At first I recoiled, unable to persuade myself that the figure reflected there was truly my own; and when I was at last fully convinced that I was indeed the monster I have become, I was overwhelmed with the most bitter feelings of despair and shame. Alas! I did not yet fully understand the fatal consequences of this wretched deformity.
source_text: “I had admired the perfect forms of my cottagers—their grace, beauty, and delicate complexions; but how was I terrified when I viewed myself in a transparent pool! At first I started back, unable to believe that it was indeed I who was reflected in the mirror; and when I became fully convinced that I was in reality the monster that I am, I was filled with the bitterest sensations of despondence and mortification. Alas! I did not yet entirely know the fatal effects of this miserable deformity.

text: We were not permitted to talk for any considerable time, since the uncertain condition of my health made every precaution needful that might preserve my calm. Mr. Kirwin entered and insisted that I must not wear myself out by too much effort. Yet my father’s appearance was to me like that of my guardian angel, and little by little I recovered my strength.
source_text: We were not allowed to converse for any length of time, for the precarious state of my health rendered every precaution necessary that could ensure tranquillity. Mr. Kirwin came in and insisted that my strength should not be exhausted by too much exertion. But the appearance of my father was to me like that of my good angel, and I gradually recovered my health.

text: “But soon,” he exclaimed with mournful, earnest fervor, “I shall die, and what I feel now will be felt no more. These blazing torments will soon be ended. I shall mount my funeral pyre in triumph and rejoice in the anguish of the consuming flames. The glow of that blaze will die away; the wind will carry my ashes out to sea. My spirit will rest in peace, or if it does think, it will not think in this way. Farewell.”
source_text: “But soon,” he cried with sad and solemn enthusiasm, “I shall die, and what I now feel be no longer felt. Soon these burning miseries will be extinct. I shall ascend my funeral pile triumphantly and exult in the agony of the torturing flames. The light of that conflagration will fade away; my ashes will be swept into the sea by the winds. My spirit will sleep in peace, or if it thinks, it will not surely think thus. Farewell.”

text: In this solitude I gave the hours of morning to labour; but in the evening, when the air was mild, I wandered along the rugged margin of the sea, to hearken to the billows as they broke in foam and thundered at my very feet. It was a scene at once uniform and forever altering. My thoughts returned to Helvetia; how unlike it was to this bleak and terrible prospect. There the hills are mantled with vines, and the little dwellings lie thickly scattered over the plains. The bright lakes mirror a soft blue sky; and when the winds disturb them, their commotion is but as the sportive outcry of an infant, if compared with the deep clamours of the vast ocean.
source_text: In this retreat I devoted the morning to labour; but in the evening, when the weather permitted, I walked on the stony beach of the sea to listen to the waves as they roared and dashed at my feet. It was a monotonous yet ever-changing scene. I thought of Switzerland; it was far different from this desolate and appalling landscape. Its hills are covered with vines, and its cottages are scattered thickly in the plains. Its fair lakes reflect a blue and gentle sky, and when troubled by the winds, their tumult is but as the play of a lively infant when compared to the roarings of the giant ocean.

text: By birth I am a Genevese, and my family ranks among the most distinguished in that republic. For many years my ancestors had served as counsellors and syndics, and my father had occupied several public offices with honor and distinction. He was esteemed by all who knew him for his integrity and his unwearied devotion to public affairs. The earlier part of his life was spent almost entirely in the service of his country; various circumstances had prevented him from marrying in his youth, and it was not until the decline of life that he became a husband and the father of a family.
source_text: I am by birth a Genevese, and my family is one of the most distinguished of that republic. My ancestors had been for many years counsellors and syndics, and my father had filled several public situations with honour and reputation. He was respected by all who knew him for his integrity and indefatigable attention to public business. He passed his younger days perpetually occupied by the affairs of his country; a variety of circumstances had prevented his marrying early, nor was it until the decline of life that he became a husband and the father of a family.

text: I derive a singular delight from lingering over the memories of childhood, before calamity had darkened my spirit and exchanged its once radiant dreams of broad usefulness for sad and contracted meditations upon myself. Moreover, in tracing the lineaments of my earliest years, I also preserve those occurrences which, by scarcely perceptible degrees, conducted me to the wretched history of my later life; for when I seek to explain to myself the birth of that passion which afterwards governed my fate, I find it springing, like a mountain stream, from mean and nearly forgotten beginnings, yet, enlarging as it went, becoming at last the flood that in its furious passage has swept away all my hopes and joys.
source_text: I feel exquisite pleasure in dwelling on the recollections of childhood, before misfortune had tainted my mind and changed its bright visions of extensive usefulness into gloomy and narrow reflections upon self. Besides, in drawing the picture of my early days, I also record those events which led, by insensible steps, to my after tale of misery, for when I would account to myself for the birth of that passion which afterwards ruled my destiny I find it arise, like a mountain river, from ignoble and almost forgotten sources; but, swelling as it proceeded, it became the torrent which, in its course, has swept away all my hopes and joys.

text: What then became of me? I cannot say; I lost all feeling, and nothing but chains and darkness seemed to bear upon me. At times, indeed, I dreamed that I roamed through flowery meadows and pleasant valleys with the friends of my youth, yet I awoke to find myself in a dungeon. A melancholy mood came over me, but little by little I recovered a clear sense of my sufferings and my condition, and was then released from my prison. They had, it seems, declared me mad, and for many months, as I afterward learned, a solitary cell had been my dwelling.
source_text: What then became of me? I know not; I lost sensation, and chains and darkness were the only objects that pressed upon me. Sometimes, indeed, I dreamt that I wandered in flowery meadows and pleasant vales with the friends of my youth, but I awoke and found myself in a dungeon. Melancholy followed, but by degrees I gained a clear conception of my miseries and situation and was then released from my prison. For they had called me mad, and during many months, as I understood, a solitary cell had been my habitation.

text: She did indeed conceal her sorrow and tried to be the one who comforted us all. She met life with a steady gaze and took up its duties with courage and ardor. She gave herself to those she had been taught to call her uncle and cousins. Never had she been more captivating than now, when she brought back the sunshine of her smiles and bestowed it on us. In her efforts to make us forget, she even forgot her own sorrow.
source_text: She indeed veiled her grief and strove to act the comforter to us all. She looked steadily on life and assumed its duties with courage and zeal. She devoted herself to those whom she had been taught to call her uncle and cousins. Never was she so enchanting as at this time, when she recalled the sunshine of her smiles and spent them upon us. She forgot even her own regret in her endeavours to make us forget.

text: His story had taken up the entire day, and by the time he left, the sun was already close to the horizon. I knew I should make haste down into the valley, since darkness would soon close around me; yet my heart was heavy, and my pace was slow. The effort of finding my way along the narrow mountain paths and setting my feet securely as I went wore on me, all the more because my mind was occupied by the feelings stirred up by the day’s events. It was well into night when I reached the resting place halfway down and sat beside the fountain. The stars came and went as clouds drifted across them; dark pines stood before me, and here and there a shattered tree lay upon the ground. It was a scene of deep solemnity, and it awakened strange thoughts in me. I wept uncontrollably, and wringing my hands in torment, I cried, “Oh stars, and clouds, and winds, you are all about to taunt me; if you truly have pity on me, destroy feeling and memory, let me be as nothing; but if you do not, then go, go, and leave me in darkness.”
source_text: His tale had occupied the whole day, and the sun was upon the verge of the horizon when he departed. I knew that I ought to hasten my descent towards the valley, as I should soon be encompassed in darkness; but my heart was heavy, and my steps slow. The labour of winding among the little paths of the mountain and fixing my feet firmly as I advanced perplexed me, occupied as I was by the emotions which the occurrences of the day had produced. Night was far advanced when I came to the halfway resting-place and seated myself beside the fountain. The stars shone at intervals as the clouds passed from over them; the dark pines rose before me, and every here and there a broken tree lay on the ground; it was a scene of wonderful solemnity and stirred strange thoughts within me. I wept bitterly, and clasping my hands in agony, I exclaimed, “Oh! stars and clouds and winds, ye are all about to mock me; if ye really pity me, crush sensation and memory; let me become as nought; but if not, depart, depart, and leave me in darkness.”

text: The astonishment I first felt at this discovery soon yielded to delight and ecstasy. After so long spent in painful toil, to reach at once the summit of my wishes was the most welcome crown of my efforts. Yet the discovery was so vast and overpowering that it erased from view all the steps by which I had gradually been brought to it, leaving me conscious only of the result. What had been the pursuit and longing of the wisest men since the beginning of the world now lay within my reach. Not that it revealed itself to me all at once, like some enchanted spectacle: the knowledge I had gained was better suited to guide my efforts once they were directed toward the object of my search than to present that object as already achieved. I was like the Arabian who, buried among the dead, discovered a path back to life with nothing to help him but one faint and apparently useless light.
source_text: The astonishment which I had at first experienced on this discovery soon gave place to delight and rapture. After so much time spent in painful labour, to arrive at once at the summit of my desires was the most gratifying consummation of my toils. But this discovery was so great and overwhelming that all the steps by which I had been progressively led to it were obliterated, and I beheld only the result. What had been the study and desire of the wisest men since the creation of the world was now within my grasp. Not that, like a magic scene, it all opened upon me at once: the information I had obtained was of a nature rather to direct my endeavours so soon as I should point them towards the object of my search than to exhibit that object already accomplished. I was like the Arabian who had been buried with the dead and found a passage to life, aided only by one glimmering and seemingly ineffectual light.

text: “You would have me,” I replied, “flee the dwellings of men and live in those wildernesses where the beasts of the field alone would keep me company. How could you, who crave human affection and sympathy, endure such banishment? You would come back and seek their kindness once more, only to find their hatred; your evil passions would be stirred up again, and then you would have a companion to help you in your work of ruin. This must not be. Say no more of it, for I cannot agree.”
source_text: “You propose,” replied I, “to fly from the habitations of man, to dwell in those wilds where the beasts of the field will be your only companions. How can you, who long for the love and sympathy of man, persevere in this exile? You will return and again seek their kindness, and you will meet with their detestation; your evil passions will be renewed, and you will then have a companion to aid you in the task of destruction. This may not be; cease to argue the point, for I cannot consent.”

text: To England, then, I was destined; and it was agreed that my marriage with Elizabeth should be celebrated as soon as I returned. My father’s years made him exceedingly unwilling to suffer any delay. As for myself, there was but one recompense I allowed myself to hope for from my hated labors—one solace for my unequaled sufferings: the prospect of that day when, freed from my wretched bondage, I might claim Elizabeth, and, in my union with her, lose the memory of all that had gone before.
source_text: To England, therefore, I was bound, and it was understood that my union with Elizabeth should take place immediately on my return. My father’s age rendered him extremely averse to delay. For myself, there was one reward I promised myself from my detested toils—one consolation for my unparalleled sufferings; it was the prospect of that day when, enfranchised from my miserable slavery, I might claim Elizabeth and forget the past in my union with her.

text: Several witnesses were summoned, men and women who had known her for many years, and they testified favorably on her behalf; yet fear and abhorrence of the crime they believed her to have committed made them hesitant and unwilling to speak openly. Elizabeth saw even this final hope, her admirable character and blameless behavior, on the point of failing the prisoner, when, though deeply agitated, she asked leave to address the court.
source_text: Several witnesses were called who had known her for many years, and they spoke well of her; but fear and hatred of the crime of which they supposed her guilty rendered them timorous and unwilling to come forward. Elizabeth saw even this last resource, her excellent dispositions and irreproachable conduct, about to fail the accused, when, although violently agitated, she desired permission to address the court.

text: When a second son was born to my parents, a brother seven years younger than myself, they abandoned their roaming habits altogether and settled once more in their own country. We had a house in Geneva and a _campagne_ at Belrive, on the eastern bank of the lake, a little more than a league from the city. We lived chiefly at the latter place, and my parents’ existence was marked by a deep retirement. My own disposition was to shun the multitude and to bind myself warmly to a chosen few. I was therefore careless of my school-fellows in general; yet I formed with one of them the closest and most devoted friendship. Henry Clerval was the son of a Geneva merchant. He was a boy of uncommon gifts and imagination. He delighted in enterprise, in hardship, and even in peril, for their own sake. He was profoundly versed in books of chivalry and romance. He wrote heroic verses and began more than one tale of enchantment and knightly adventure. He would try to make us perform plays and join in masquerades, drawing his characters from the heroes of Roncesvalles, the knights of King Arthur’s Round Table, and that gallant band who shed their blood to win back the holy sepulchre from the infidels.
source_text: On the birth of a second son, my junior by seven years, my parents gave up entirely their wandering life and fixed themselves in their native country. We possessed a house in Geneva, and a _campagne_ on Belrive, the eastern shore of the lake, at the distance of rather more than a league from the city. We resided principally in the latter, and the lives of my parents were passed in considerable seclusion. It was my temper to avoid a crowd and to attach myself fervently to a few. I was indifferent, therefore, to my school-fellows in general; but I united myself in the bonds of the closest friendship to one among them. Henry Clerval was the son of a merchant of Geneva. He was a boy of singular talent and fancy. He loved enterprise, hardship, and even danger for its own sake. He was deeply read in books of chivalry and romance. He composed heroic songs and began to write many a tale of enchantment and knightly adventure. He tried to make us act plays and to enter into masquerades, in which the characters were drawn from the heroes of Roncesvalles, of the Round Table of King Arthur, and the chivalrous train who shed their blood to redeem the holy sepulchre from the hands of the infidels.

text: My father tried to soothe me with assurances that they were well, and, by lingering on those subjects so dear to my heart, sought to lift my sinking spirits; but he soon perceived that a prison can never be a dwelling place of cheerfulness. “What a place this is that you inhabit, my son!” he said, casting a sorrowful glance at the barred windows and the miserable condition of the room. “You set out in search of happiness, yet some fatal misfortune appears to follow you. And poor Clerval—”
source_text: My father calmed me with assurances of their welfare and endeavoured, by dwelling on these subjects so interesting to my heart, to raise my desponding spirits; but he soon felt that a prison cannot be the abode of cheerfulness. “What a place is this that you inhabit, my son!” said he, looking mournfully at the barred windows and wretched appearance of the room. “You travelled to seek happiness, but a fatality seems to pursue you. And poor Clerval—”

text: We grew up together, with less than a year between our ages. I need hardly say that we knew nothing of estrangement or quarrels. Harmony was the very heart of our friendship, and the differences in our natures only drew us closer. Elizabeth was calmer and more self-possessed; for all my eagerness, I was capable of a more sustained application and was more keenly driven by a hunger for knowledge. She occupied herself with pursuing the airy visions of poets, and in the grand and marvelous scenery that encircled our Swiss home—the sublime forms of the mountains, the shifting seasons, storm and stillness, the hush of winter, and the vitality and turmoil of our Alpine summers—she found abundant cause for wonder and pleasure. While my companion regarded the splendid spectacle of the world with a grave and contented mind, I took delight in probing its causes. The world was, to me, a mystery I longed to unravel. Curiosity, an earnest desire to discover nature’s hidden laws, and a joy bordering on ecstasy as they were revealed to me, are among the earliest feelings I can recall.
source_text: We were brought up together; there was not quite a year difference in our ages. I need not say that we were strangers to any species of disunion or dispute. Harmony was the soul of our companionship, and the diversity and contrast that subsisted in our characters drew us nearer together. Elizabeth was of a calmer and more concentrated disposition; but, with all my ardour, I was capable of a more intense application and was more deeply smitten with the thirst for knowledge. She busied herself with following the aerial creations of the poets; and in the majestic and wondrous scenes which surrounded our Swiss home —the sublime shapes of the mountains, the changes of the seasons, tempest and calm, the silence of winter, and the life and turbulence of our Alpine summers—she found ample scope for admiration and delight. While my companion contemplated with a serious and satisfied spirit the magnificent appearances of things, I delighted in investigating their causes. The world was to me a secret which I desired to divine. Curiosity, earnest research to learn the hidden laws of nature, gladness akin to rapture, as they were unfolded to me, are among the earliest sensations I can remember.

text: Yet before I could leave, one duty remained, and the thought of it made me shudder: I had to pack away my chemical instruments, and for that I must go back into the room that had witnessed my detestable labor, and touch the very tools whose sight now revolted me. The next morning, at daybreak, I gathered what courage I could and opened the door of my laboratory. On the floor lay the scattered remains of the half-finished creature I had destroyed, and I felt almost as though I had torn apart the living flesh of a human being. I stopped for a moment to steady myself, then went into the chamber. With a shaking hand I carried the instruments out. Yet I thought I ought not to leave the fragments of my work behind to awaken horror and suspicion in the peasants; so I placed them in a basket with a large quantity of stones, and, having stored it away, I resolved to cast it into the sea that very night. In the meantime I sat on the shore, occupied with cleaning and arranging my chemical apparatus.
source_text: Yet, before I departed, there was a task to perform, on which I shuddered to reflect; I must pack up my chemical instruments, and for that purpose I must enter the room which had been the scene of my odious work, and I must handle those utensils the sight of which was sickening to me. The next morning, at daybreak, I summoned sufficient courage and unlocked the door of my laboratory. The remains of the half-finished creature, whom I had destroyed, lay scattered on the floor, and I almost felt as if I had mangled the living flesh of a human being. I paused to collect myself and then entered the chamber. With trembling hand I conveyed the instruments out of the room, but I reflected that I ought not to leave the relics of my work to excite the horror and suspicion of the peasants; and I accordingly put them into a basket, with a great quantity of stones, and laying them up, determined to throw them into the sea that very night; and in the meantime I sat upon the beach, employed in cleaning and arranging my chemical apparatus.

text: Since my convalescence from the fever, I had made it my nightly practice to take a small portion of laudanum; by that means alone could I secure the repose indispensable to the continuance of life. Now, weighed down by the memory of my manifold calamities, I drank twice my customary dose and was soon sunk in a deep sleep. Yet slumber brought no relief from anguish or reflection; my dreams were thronged with a thousand terrors. Near daybreak I was seized by a sort of nightmare. I seemed to feel the demon’s hand fastened upon my throat and could not wrench myself free; groans and shrieks sounded in my ears. My father, who was keeping watch beside me, observing my agitation, roused me. The waves were beating around us, the murky heavens stretched above, and the fiend was nowhere to be seen. A sense of safety, and the belief that a truce had been made between the passing hour and the inevitable, ruinous future, bestowed upon me a species of peaceful oblivion to which the human mind, by its very constitution, is strangely prone.
source_text: Ever since my recovery from the fever, I had been in the custom of taking every night a small quantity of laudanum, for it was by means of this drug only that I was enabled to gain the rest necessary for the preservation of life. Oppressed by the recollection of my various misfortunes, I now swallowed double my usual quantity and soon slept profoundly. But sleep did not afford me respite from thought and misery; my dreams presented a thousand objects that scared me. Towards morning I was possessed by a kind of nightmare; I felt the fiend’s grasp in my neck and could not free myself from it; groans and cries rang in my ears. My father, who was watching over me, perceiving my restlessness, awoke me; the dashing waves were around, the cloudy sky above, the fiend was not here: a sense of security, a feeling that a truce was established between the present hour and the irresistible, disastrous future imparted to me a kind of calm forgetfulness, of which the human mind is by its structure peculiarly susceptible.

text: I passed the night in misery. At times my pulse throbbed so fast and feebly that I could feel the beating of every artery; at others, I seemed almost to sink to the ground from languor and utter weakness. Along with this terror, I suffered the sharp sting of disappointment. Dreams that had long been my sustenance and my pleasant repose had now become a hell to me; and so swift was the transformation, so total the ruin!
source_text: I passed the night wretchedly. Sometimes my pulse beat so quickly and hardly that I felt the palpitation of every artery; at others, I nearly sank to the ground through languor and extreme weakness. Mingled with this horror, I felt the bitterness of disappointment; dreams that had been my food and pleasant rest for so long a space were now become a hell to me; and the change was so rapid, the overthrow so complete!

text: “What do you mean? What do you require of your captain? Are you, then, so quickly swerved from your purpose? Did you not declare this a glorious expedition? And why was it glorious? Not because the path was smooth and calm as a southern sea, but because it was crowded with peril and dread, because at every fresh event your courage was to be summoned and your resolution shown, because danger and death lay all about it, and these you were to defy and conquer. For this, it was a glorious undertaking; for this, an honourable one. You were to be hailed afterward as benefactors of your race, your names cherished as those of brave men who met death for honour and for the good of mankind. And now, see, at the first thought of danger—or, if you please, the first great and terrible test of your courage—you draw back, and are content to be remembered as men who had not the strength to endure cold and hardship; and so, poor creatures, they were chilled and went home to their warm hearths. Why, such a thing needed no expedition like this; you need not have come so far and dragged your captain to the humiliation of defeat merely to prove yourselves cowards. Oh, be men, or something more than men. Stand fast to your aims and be firm as stone. This ice is not made of the same material as your hearts may be; it is changeable, and cannot resist you if you declare that it shall not. Do not go back to your families with disgrace stamped upon your foreheads. Return as heroes who have fought and won, and who do not know what it is to turn their backs upon the enemy.”
source_text: “What do you mean? What do you demand of your captain? Are you, then, so easily turned from your design? Did you not call this a glorious expedition? “And wherefore was it glorious? Not because the way was smooth and placid as a southern sea, but because it was full of dangers and terror, because at every new incident your fortitude was to be called forth and your courage exhibited, because danger and death surrounded it, and these you were to brave and overcome. For this was it a glorious, for this was it an honourable undertaking. You were hereafter to be hailed as the benefactors of your species, your names adored as belonging to brave men who encountered death for honour and the benefit of mankind. And now, behold, with the first imagination of danger, or, if you will, the first mighty and terrific trial of your courage, you shrink away and are content to be handed down as men who had not strength enough to endure cold and peril; and so, poor souls, they were chilly and returned to their warm firesides. Why, that requires not this preparation; ye need not have come thus far and dragged your captain to the shame of a defeat merely to prove yourselves cowards. Oh! Be men, or be more than men. Be steady to your purposes and firm as a rock. This ice is not made of such stuff as your hearts may be; it is mutable and cannot withstand you if you say that it shall not. Do not return to your families with the stigma of disgrace marked on your brows. Return as heroes who have fought and conquered and who know not what it is to turn their backs on the foe.”

text: His voice sounded choked, and my first impulses, which had made me feel it my duty to obey my dying friend’s request and destroy his enemy, were now checked by a mingling of curiosity and pity. I drew near to this awe-inspiring being; I did not dare lift my eyes to his face again, so terrifying and unearthly was its ugliness. I tried to speak, but the words vanished on my lips. The monster went on pouring out wild and incoherent reproaches against himself. At last, during a brief lull in the storm of his anguish, I found the courage to address him.
source_text: His voice seemed suffocated, and my first impulses, which had suggested to me the duty of obeying the dying request of my friend in destroying his enemy, were now suspended by a mixture of curiosity and compassion. I approached this tremendous being; I dared not again raise my eyes to his face, there was something so scaring and unearthly in his ugliness. I attempted to speak, but the words died away on my lips. The monster continued to utter wild and incoherent self-reproaches. At length I gathered resolution to address him in a pause of the tempest of his passion.

text: Only Clara had power to recall me from those paroxysms; her soft voice would calm me when passion had driven me to frenzy, and awaken in me some remnant of human feeling when I had sunk into a dull stupor. She wept with me and for me. When reason came back, she would gently reprove me and strive to teach me resignation. Ah! it is well for the wretched to be resigned; but for the guilty, there is no peace. The pangs of remorse taint the mournful sweetness that is sometimes found in surrendering oneself to the full indulgence of grief.
source_text: Elizabeth alone had the power to draw me from these fits; her gentle voice would soothe me when transported by passion and inspire me with human feelings when sunk in torpor. She wept with me and for me. When reason returned, she would remonstrate and endeavour to inspire me with resignation. Ah! It is well for the unfortunate to be resigned, but for the guilty there is no peace. The agonies of remorse poison the luxury there is otherwise sometimes found in indulging the excess of grief.

text: “It gave me the greatest pleasure to receive a letter from my uncle, dated in Paris; you are no longer so dreadfully far away, and I may hope to see you in less than a fortnight. My poor cousin, how much you must have suffered! I expect to find you looking even more unwell than when you left Geneva. This winter has been passed in the most miserable distress, tormented as I have been by anxious suspense; yet I hope to see calm in your face and to discover that your heart is not entirely without comfort and peace.
source_text: “It gave me the greatest pleasure to receive a letter from my uncle dated at Paris; you are no longer at a formidable distance, and I may hope to see you in less than a fortnight. My poor cousin, how much you must have suffered! I expect to see you looking even more ill than when you quitted Geneva. This winter has been passed most miserably, tortured as I have been by anxious suspense; yet I hope to see peace in your countenance and to find that your heart is not totally void of comfort and tranquillity.

text: These visions faded when I first read those poets whose inspired outpourings enchanted my soul and lifted it toward heaven. I, too, became a poet, and for a year I lived in a paradise of my own making; I fancied that I, too, might win a place in the temple where the names of Homer and Shakespeare are enshrined. You know well how I failed, and how deeply I felt that disappointment. But at just that time I came into the fortune of my cousin, and my thoughts were redirected into the channel of their earlier inclination.
source_text: These visions faded when I perused, for the first time, those poets whose effusions entranced my soul and lifted it to heaven. I also became a poet and for one year lived in a paradise of my own creation; I imagined that I also might obtain a niche in the temple where the names of Homer and Shakespeare are consecrated. You are well acquainted with my failure and how heavily I bore the disappointment. But just at that time I inherited the fortune of my cousin, and my thoughts were turned into the channel of their earlier bent.

text: “Another event deepened and confirmed these emotions. Not long after I reached the hut, I found some papers in the pocket of the garment I had taken from your laboratory. At first I paid them no attention; but once I could read the characters in which they were written, I set myself to examine them carefully. They proved to be your journal for the four months before my creation. In these pages you recorded in the smallest detail each stage of your work; mixed with this were notes of domestic events. You surely remember these papers. They are here. In them is everything that concerns my cursed beginning; the entire account of that revolting chain of circumstances that brought it about is laid bare; and the most exact description of my vile, detestable form is given in language that reflected your own terror and made mine unchangeable. I grew faint as I read. ‘Miserable day when I was given life!’ I cried in torment. ‘Accursed maker! Why did you fashion a monster so monstrous that even you recoiled from me in horror? God, in mercy, made man fair and attractive, in his own likeness; but my shape is a foul copy of yours, made more dreadful by the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, his fellow demons, to admire him and cheer him on, but I am alone and loathed.’
source_text: “Another circumstance strengthened and confirmed these feelings. Soon after my arrival in the hovel I discovered some papers in the pocket of the dress which I had taken from your laboratory. At first I had neglected them, but now that I was able to decipher the characters in which they were written, I began to study them with diligence. It was your journal of the four months that preceded my creation. You minutely described in these papers every step you took in the progress of your work; this history was mingled with accounts of domestic occurrences. You doubtless recollect these papers. Here they are. Everything is related in them which bears reference to my accursed origin; the whole detail of that series of disgusting circumstances which produced it is set in view; the minutest description of my odious and loathsome person is given, in language which painted your own horrors and rendered mine indelible. I sickened as I read. ‘Hateful day when I received life!’ I exclaimed in agony. ‘Accursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even _you_ turned from me in disgust? God, in pity, made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image; but my form is a filthy type of yours, more horrid even from the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, fellow devils, to admire and encourage him, but I am solitary and abhorred.’

text: The voyage at last ended. We came ashore and made our way to Paris. Before long I discovered that I had strained my strength beyond what I could bear, and that I must rest before I could go on. My father’s concern and kindness were tireless, yet he knew nothing of the source of my misery and tried in vain to cure an ailment beyond all cure. He urged me to seek diversion in society. I loathed the sight of human faces. No, not loathed them! They were my brothers and sisters, my fellow creatures, and I felt drawn even to the most hideous among them, as though they were beings of an angelic order and heavenly contrivance. Yet I felt I had no right to mingle with them. I had loosed among them an enemy whose delight was to spill their blood and feast on their cries. How they would all, every one of them, abhor me and drive me from the earth, if they knew my unholy deeds and the crimes that sprang from me!
source_text: The voyage came to an end. We landed, and proceeded to Paris. I soon found that I had overtaxed my strength and that I must repose before I could continue my journey. My father’s care and attentions were indefatigable, but he did not know the origin of my sufferings and sought erroneous methods to remedy the incurable ill. He wished me to seek amusement in society. I abhorred the face of man. Oh, not abhorred! They were my brethren, my fellow beings, and I felt attracted even to the most repulsive among them, as to creatures of an angelic nature and celestial mechanism. But I felt that I had no right to share their intercourse. I had unchained an enemy among them whose joy it was to shed their blood and to revel in their groans. How they would, each and all, abhor me and hunt me from the world, did they know my unhallowed acts and the crimes which had their source in me!

text: “I refuse,” I answered; “and no torment shall ever wring consent from me. You may make me the most wretched of men, but you shall never make me contemptible in my own sight. Shall I bring forth another being like yourself, whose combined wickedness might lay waste the world? Begone! I have given you my answer; you may torture me, but I will never agree.”
source_text: “I do refuse it,” I replied; “and no torture shall ever extort a consent from me. You may render me the most miserable of men, but you shall never make me base in my own eyes. Shall I create another like yourself, whose joint wickedness might desolate the world. Begone! I have answered you; you may torture me, but I will never consent.”

text: It was a long time before he came round, and I often believed that life had left him altogether. At last he opened his eyes; his breathing was labored, and he could not speak. The surgeon gave him a soothing draught and told us to let him rest undisturbed. In the meantime, he assured me that my friend had, beyond question, only a few hours left to live.
source_text: It was long before he was restored, and I often thought that life was entirely extinct. At length he opened his eyes; he breathed with difficulty and was unable to speak. The surgeon gave him a composing draught and ordered us to leave him undisturbed. In the meantime he told me that my friend had certainly not many hours to live.

text: These reflections lifted my spirits and sent me back with renewed zeal to the study of language. My vocal organs were indeed rough, yet flexible; and though my voice bore little resemblance to the tender music of theirs, I could still utter the words I understood with tolerable ease. It was like the ass and the lap-dog; yet surely the gentle ass, whose feelings were kindly though his behavior was clumsy, deserved kinder treatment than kicks and curses.
source_text: “These thoughts exhilarated me and led me to apply with fresh ardour to the acquiring the art of language. My organs were indeed harsh, but supple; and although my voice was very unlike the soft music of their tones, yet I pronounced such words as I understood with tolerable ease. It was as the ass and the lap-dog; yet surely the gentle ass whose intentions were affectionate, although his manners were rude, deserved better treatment than blows and execration.

text: My present condition was such that every act of deliberate thought was engulfed and lost. I was driven onward by fury; only revenge gave me strength and steadiness. It shaped my emotions, and permitted me to remain calculating and calm at moments when, without it, frenzy or death would have been my fate.
source_text: My present situation was one in which all voluntary thought was swallowed up and lost. I was hurried away by fury; revenge alone endowed me with strength and composure; it moulded my feelings and allowed me to be calculating and calm at periods when otherwise delirium or death would have been my portion.

text: “But how was I to find my way? I knew that I must bend my steps toward the south-west if I would reach my destination, yet the sun was my only compass. I knew not the names of the towns through which I must pass, nor could I inquire of a single soul; and yet I did not despair. From you alone could I hope for aid, though toward you I cherished nothing but hatred. Cruel, insensible maker! You had bestowed upon me sensibility and feeling, and then hurled me forth an object of derision and dread to all mankind. And yet from you alone could I demand compassion and reparation; from you I resolved to seek that justice which I had vainly endeavored to obtain from every other being in human shape.
source_text: “But how was I to direct myself? I knew that I must travel in a southwesterly direction to reach my destination, but the sun was my only guide. I did not know the names of the towns that I was to pass through, nor could I ask information from a single human being; but I did not despair. From you only could I hope for succour, although towards you I felt no sentiment but that of hatred. Unfeeling, heartless creator! You had endowed me with perceptions and passions and then cast me abroad an object for the scorn and horror of mankind. But on you only had I any claim for pity and redress, and from you I determined to seek that justice which I vainly attempted to gain from any other being that wore the human form.

text: Meanwhile Clerval busied himself, so to speak, with the moral relations of things. The bustling scene of life, the virtues of heroes, and the deeds of men were his chosen subjects; and his hope, his dream, was to become one of those whose names are preserved in history as the brave and adventurous benefactors of our race. The saintly spirit of Elizabeth shone in our tranquil home like a lamp kept burning before a shrine. Her sympathy was ours; her smile, her gentle voice, the sweet look of her heavenly eyes, were always there to comfort and enliven us. She was the living spirit of love, meant to soften and draw us near; I might have grown moody in my studies, and harsh through the ardour of my nature, had she not been there to temper me into some likeness of her own sweetness. And Clerval—could anything evil gain a hold upon the noble soul of Clerval? Yet perhaps he would not have been so wholly humane, so considerate in his liberality, so rich in kindness and tenderness even in his passion for daring enterprise, had she not revealed to him the true beauty of beneficence and made the doing of good the end and purpose of his exalted ambition.
source_text: Meanwhile Clerval occupied himself, so to speak, with the moral relations of things. The busy stage of life, the virtues of heroes, and the actions of men were his theme; and his hope and his dream was to become one among those whose names are recorded in story as the gallant and adventurous benefactors of our species. The saintly soul of Elizabeth shone like a shrine-dedicated lamp in our peaceful home. Her sympathy was ours; her smile, her soft voice, the sweet glance of her celestial eyes, were ever there to bless and animate us. She was the living spirit of love to soften and attract; I might have become sullen in my study, rough through the ardour of my nature, but that she was there to subdue me to a semblance of her own gentleness. And Clerval—could aught ill entrench on the noble spirit of Clerval? Yet he might not have been so perfectly humane, so thoughtful in his generosity, so full of kindness and tenderness amidst his passion for adventurous exploit, had she not unfolded to him the real loveliness of beneficence and made the doing good the end and aim of his soaring ambition.

text: Yet in Adrian I beheld the likeness of my earlier self; he was eager, questioning, and intent on gathering knowledge from every quarter. The variety of customs he encountered was to him an endless spring of instruction and delight. He too was bent on an object long cherished in his mind. He meant to travel to India, persuaded that his acquaintance with its several tongues, and the observations he had made of its society, might enable him to render material service to the advance of European settlement and commerce. It was only in England that he could pursue the final execution of his project. He was perpetually occupied, and the sole shadow upon his happiness was my own melancholy and dejection. I strove to hide it as far as I could, that I might not deprive him of the pleasures belonging to one who had just entered upon a fresh course of life, untroubled by sorrow or painful recollection. I frequently declined to go with him, pleading some other engagement, that I might be left in solitude. At the same time I began to gather the materials required for my new work, and the task was to me like the torment of a single drop of water falling without ceasing upon the head. Every thought I gave to it became a source of exquisite anguish, and every word I uttered in speaking of it made my lips tremble and my heart throb violently.
source_text: But in Clerval I saw the image of my former self; he was inquisitive and anxious to gain experience and instruction. The difference of manners which he observed was to him an inexhaustible source of instruction and amusement. He was also pursuing an object he had long had in view. His design was to visit India, in the belief that he had in his knowledge of its various languages, and in the views he had taken of its society, the means of materially assisting the progress of European colonization and trade. In Britain only could he further the execution of his plan. He was for ever busy, and the only check to his enjoyments was my sorrowful and dejected mind. I tried to conceal this as much as possible, that I might not debar him from the pleasures natural to one who was entering on a new scene of life, undisturbed by any care or bitter recollection. I often refused to accompany him, alleging another engagement, that I might remain alone. I now also began to collect the materials necessary for my new creation, and this was to me like the torture of single drops of water continually falling on the head. Every thought that was devoted to it was an extreme anguish, and every word that I spoke in allusion to it caused my lips to quiver, and my heart to palpitate.

text: The monster perceived my resolve in my face and ground his teeth in helpless rage. “Must every man,” he exclaimed, “find a wife to cherish, and every beast its mate, while I remain alone? I once felt affection, and for that I was met with loathing and contempt. Man, you may hate me, but take care! Your days shall be passed in terror and misery, and soon the blow will come that must forever tear your happiness from you. Are you to be blessed while I crawl in the depth of my wretchedness? You may destroy all my other passions, but revenge remains—revenge, from this moment dearer to me than light or sustenance! I may die, but first you, my tyrant and persecutor, shall curse the sun that shines upon your misery. Beware, for I am fearless and therefore strong. I will keep watch with the craft of a serpent, that I may strike with its poison. Man, you shall rue the injuries you have done me.”
source_text: The monster saw my determination in my face and gnashed his teeth in the impotence of anger. “Shall each man,” cried he, “find a wife for his bosom, and each beast have his mate, and I be alone? I had feelings of affection, and they were requited by detestation and scorn. Man! You may hate, but beware! Your hours will pass in dread and misery, and soon the bolt will fall which must ravish from you your happiness for ever. Are you to be happy while I grovel in the intensity of my wretchedness? You can blast my other passions, but revenge remains—revenge, henceforth dearer than light or food! I may die, but first you, my tyrant and tormentor, shall curse the sun that gazes on your misery. Beware, for I am fearless and therefore powerful. I will watch with the wiliness of a snake, that I may sting with its venom. Man, you shall repent of the injuries you inflict.”

text: What emotions possessed the being I hunted, I could never discern. At times, however, he left behind messages scored upon the bark of trees or graven in stone, and these both directed my course and inflamed my rage. “My dominion is not yet ended”—so ran one of these inscriptions—“you yet survive, and my strength remains entire. Pursue me; I am bound for the eternal snows of the north, where you shall learn the torment of cold and frost, to which I am indifferent. If you lag not too far behind, you will find near this spot a dead hare; partake of it and gather strength. Come, then, my foe; we are still to contend for our lives, though many bitter and wretched hours must you first endure before that hour arrives.”
source_text: What his feelings were whom I pursued I cannot know. Sometimes, indeed, he left marks in writing on the barks of the trees or cut in stone that guided me and instigated my fury. “My reign is not yet over”—these words were legible in one of these inscriptions—“you live, and my power is complete. Follow me; I seek the everlasting ices of the north, where you will feel the misery of cold and frost, to which I am impassive. You will find near this place, if you follow not too tardily, a dead hare; eat and be refreshed. Come on, my enemy; we have yet to wrestle for our lives, but many hard and miserable hours must you endure until that period shall arrive.”

text: The length of my absence was left to my own decision; it was to be no more than a few months, or at the most a year. He had also, with fatherly solicitude, taken one kind precaution to secure me a companion. Without consulting me beforehand, he had, together with Elizabeth, arranged that Clerval should meet me in Strasburgh. This did not suit the solitude I desired for the pursuit of my work; yet at the beginning of my journey my friend’s presence could scarcely hinder me, and indeed I was glad to be spared many hours of lonely, tormenting thought. Moreover, Henry might stand as a barrier between me and the intrusion of my enemy. If I were alone, would he not sometimes thrust his hated presence upon me, to remind me of my task or to behold its progress?
source_text: The duration of my absence was left to my own choice; a few months, or at most a year, was the period contemplated. One paternal kind precaution he had taken to ensure my having a companion. Without previously communicating with me, he had, in concert with Elizabeth, arranged that Clerval should join me at Strasburgh. This interfered with the solitude I coveted for the prosecution of my task; yet at the commencement of my journey the presence of my friend could in no way be an impediment, and truly I rejoiced that thus I should be saved many hours of lonely, maddening reflection. Nay, Henry might stand between me and the intrusion of my foe. If I were alone, would he not at times force his abhorred presence on me to remind me of my task or to contemplate its progress?

text: Thus supplied, I resolved to stay in the hut until something happened that might change my mind. Compared with the barren forest where I had been living, the rain-dripping boughs, and the damp ground, it was truly a paradise. I ate my breakfast with enjoyment, and was just about to lift a plank to get a little water for myself, when I heard footsteps. Looking through a narrow crack, I saw a young creature with a pail balanced on her head passing by my hut. She was young and of a gentle manner, unlike what I have since discovered cottagers and farm servants to be. Still, her clothing was poor: a rough blue petticoat and a linen jacket were all she wore; her fair hair was braided, though without any ornament, and she seemed patient, yet sorrowful. I lost sight of her, and about a quarter of an hour later she returned, carrying the pail, now partly filled with milk. As she went along, evidently made uneasy by its weight, she was met by a young man whose face showed an even deeper sadness. After speaking a few murmured words with a mournful air, he took the pail from her head and carried it to the cottage himself. She went after him, and both disappeared. Soon afterward I saw the young man again, tools in his hand, crossing the field behind the cottage; and the girl, too, was occupied, sometimes in the house and sometimes in the yard.
source_text: “Being thus provided, I resolved to reside in this hovel until something should occur which might alter my determination. It was indeed a paradise compared to the bleak forest, my former residence, the rain-dropping branches, and dank earth. I ate my breakfast with pleasure and was about to remove a plank to procure myself a little water when I heard a step, and looking through a small chink, I beheld a young creature, with a pail on her head, passing before my hovel. The girl was young and of gentle demeanour, unlike what I have since found cottagers and farmhouse servants to be. Yet she was meanly dressed, a coarse blue petticoat and a linen jacket being her only garb; her fair hair was plaited but not adorned: she looked patient yet sad. I lost sight of her, and in about a quarter of an hour she returned bearing the pail, which was now partly filled with milk. As she walked along, seemingly incommoded by the burden, a young man met her, whose countenance expressed a deeper despondence. Uttering a few sounds with an air of melancholy, he took the pail from her head and bore it to the cottage himself. She followed, and they disappeared. Presently I saw the young man again, with some tools in his hand, cross the field behind the cottage; and the girl was also busied, sometimes in the house and sometimes in the yard.

text: The ascent is steep in the extreme, yet the track is broken into a succession of short windings, which allow one to conquer the mountain’s sheer face. It is a scene of terrible desolation. In a thousand places the signs of winter avalanches are visible, where trees lie snapped and scattered on the ground—some utterly ruined, others bent and leaning against the projecting rocks of the mountain or across the trunks of neighboring trees. As the path climbs higher, it is crossed by ravines filled with snow, from which stones are continually dislodged above; one in particular is perilous, since the least disturbance, even a loud voice, stirs the air enough to bring ruin upon the speaker’s head. The pines are neither lofty nor luxuriant, but they are dark and grave, lending the whole view a harsher character. I gazed down upon the valley below; immense mists were rising from the rivers that wound through it and curling in thick folds about the opposite mountains, whose peaks were lost in a uniform cover of cloud, while rain fell steadily from the black sky and deepened the melancholy impression made upon me by all around. Alas! Why should man pride himself on sensibilities higher than those of the brute? They only make him a more needy creature. If our impulses were limited to hunger, thirst, and desire, we might be almost free; but now every breeze that stirs, every casual word or sight it may suggest, moves us in turn.
source_text: The ascent is precipitous, but the path is cut into continual and short windings, which enable you to surmount the perpendicularity of the mountain. It is a scene terrifically desolate. In a thousand spots the traces of the winter avalanche may be perceived, where trees lie broken and strewed on the ground, some entirely destroyed, others bent, leaning upon the jutting rocks of the mountain or transversely upon other trees. The path, as you ascend higher, is intersected by ravines of snow, down which stones continually roll from above; one of them is particularly dangerous, as the slightest sound, such as even speaking in a loud voice, produces a concussion of air sufficient to draw destruction upon the head of the speaker. The pines are not tall or luxuriant, but they are sombre and add an air of severity to the scene. I looked on the valley beneath; vast mists were rising from the rivers which ran through it and curling in thick wreaths around the opposite mountains, whose summits were hid in the uniform clouds, while rain poured from the dark sky and added to the melancholy impression I received from the objects around me. Alas! Why does man boast of sensibilities superior to those apparent in the brute; it only renders them more necessary beings. If our impulses were confined to hunger, thirst, and desire, we might be nearly free; but now we are moved by every wind that blows and a chance word or scene that that word may convey to us.

text: “I am on the point of attempting that task, and it is because of that that I am so oppressed by terrors. I cherish these friends dearly; for many months I have, without their knowing it, practiced daily acts of kindness toward them. Yet they are persuaded that I mean them harm, and it is that prejudice I am striving to dispel.”
source_text: “‘I am about to undertake that task; and it is on that account that I feel so many overwhelming terrors. I tenderly love these friends; I have, unknown to them, been for many months in the habits of daily kindness towards them; but they believe that I wish to injure them, and it is that prejudice which I wish to overcome.’

text: Several other men were questioned about my landing, and they were unanimous in saying that, with the fierce north wind which had sprung up in the night, it was highly likely I had been driven to and fro for many hours and forced at last to make my way back almost to the very point from which I had set out. They further remarked that it seemed plain I had brought the body from elsewhere, and that, since I appeared unfamiliar with the coast, I might well have entered the harbour without any knowledge of how far the town of —— lay from the spot where I had left the corpse.
source_text: Several other men were examined concerning my landing, and they agreed that, with the strong north wind that had arisen during the night, it was very probable that I had beaten about for many hours and had been obliged to return nearly to the same spot from which I had departed. Besides, they observed that it appeared that I had brought the body from another place, and it was likely that as I did not appear to know the shore, I might have put into the harbour ignorant of the distance of the town of —— from the place where I had deposited the corpse.

text: I could recount a multitude of trifling incidents which, though small in themselves, revealed the temper of these gentle peasants. In the midst of penury and hardship, Adrian would carry with delight to his sister the first little blossom that dared to show itself through the snow. In the early hours, before she had stirred, he would sweep away the drift that barred her way to the dairy, draw water from the well, and fetch in the firewood from the shed, where, to his constant wonder, his supply was ever restored by an unseen hand. By day, I think, he sometimes laboured for a farmer in the neighbourhood, for he often set out and did not come back until dinner-time, though he brought no wood with him. At other times he busied himself in the garden; but as there was little to occupy him in that frozen season, he would read aloud to the old man and to Clara.
source_text: “I could mention innumerable instances which, although slight, marked the dispositions of these amiable cottagers. In the midst of poverty and want, Felix carried with pleasure to his sister the first little white flower that peeped out from beneath the snowy ground. Early in the morning, before she had risen, he cleared away the snow that obstructed her path to the milk-house, drew water from the well, and brought the wood from the outhouse, where, to his perpetual astonishment, he found his store always replenished by an invisible hand. In the day, I believe, he worked sometimes for a neighbouring farmer, because he often went forth and did not return until dinner, yet brought no wood with him. At other times he worked in the garden, but as there was little to do in the frosty season, he read to the old man and Agatha.

text: Many hours went by, and I still stood by my casement, looking out upon the sea; it lay nearly still, the winds having fallen silent, while all creation seemed to rest beneath the watchful calm of the moon. Only a few fishing boats dotted the water, and from time to time the soft air carried the sound of voices as the men hailed one another across the dark. I was aware of the hush, though scarcely of how deep it truly was, until my ear was abruptly caught by the stroke of oars near the shore, and someone came ashore not far from my dwelling.
source_text: Several hours passed, and I remained near my window gazing on the sea; it was almost motionless, for the winds were hushed, and all nature reposed under the eye of the quiet moon. A few fishing vessels alone specked the water, and now and then the gentle breeze wafted the sound of voices as the fishermen called to one another. I felt the silence, although I was hardly conscious of its extreme profundity, until my ear was suddenly arrested by the paddling of oars near the shore, and a person landed close to my house.

text: On examining my dwelling, I discovered that one of the cottage windows had once occupied part of the wall, though its panes had since been filled in with wood. In one of these boards there was a tiny, nearly invisible crack, through which the eye could just make its way. Through this opening I could see a little room, neatly whitewashed and clean, though almost destitute of furniture. In one corner, beside a small fire, sat an old man, his head bowed upon his hands in a melancholy posture. The young girl was engaged in tidying the cottage; but after a while she took something from a drawer, which kept her occupied, and then sat down beside the old man. He picked up an instrument and began to play, drawing from it sounds sweeter than the song of the thrush or the nightingale. It was a beautiful scene, even for me, a wretch who had never before looked upon anything lovely. The old cottager’s silver hair and kind face filled me with reverence, while the gentle bearing of the girl awakened my affection. He played a soft, mournful tune, and I saw tears gather in the eyes of his gracious companion, though the old man paid no heed until her sobbing became audible. Then he uttered a few words, and the lovely creature, abandoning her work, knelt at his feet. He lifted her up and smiled with such tenderness and love that I was seized by feelings of a strange and overwhelming kind; they were a blend of pain and delight, unlike anything I had ever known, whether from hunger or cold, warmth or nourishment. I withdrew from the window, unable to endure the force of these emotions.
source_text: “On examining my dwelling, I found that one of the windows of the cottage had formerly occupied a part of it, but the panes had been filled up with wood. In one of these was a small and almost imperceptible chink through which the eye could just penetrate. Through this crevice a small room was visible, whitewashed and clean but very bare of furniture. In one corner, near a small fire, sat an old man, leaning his head on his hands in a disconsolate attitude. The young girl was occupied in arranging the cottage; but presently she took something out of a drawer, which employed her hands, and she sat down beside the old man, who, taking up an instrument, began to play and to produce sounds sweeter than the voice of the thrush or the nightingale. It was a lovely sight, even to me, poor wretch who had never beheld aught beautiful before. The silver hair and benevolent countenance of the aged cottager won my reverence, while the gentle manners of the girl enticed my love. He played a sweet mournful air which I perceived drew tears from the eyes of his amiable companion, of which the old man took no notice, until she sobbed audibly; he then pronounced a few sounds, and the fair creature, leaving her work, knelt at his feet. He raised her and smiled with such kindness and affection that I felt sensations of a peculiar and overpowering nature; they were a mixture of pain and pleasure, such as I had never before experienced, either from hunger or cold, warmth or food; and I withdrew from the window, unable to bear these emotions.

text: When my illness had at last departed, I became possessed by a heavy and funereal melancholy from which nothing could deliver me. The figure of Hartwell was ever before my eyes, pale, distorted, and slain. More than once the turmoil into which such meditations drove me caused my companions to fear a fatal relapse. Alas!
