Why had they spared so wretched and loathed a life? Surely only that I might complete the fate appointed me, which now seemed near its end. Soon, ah, very soon, death would extinguish these palpitations and release me from the vast burden of sorrow that pressed me to the earth; and, while the sentence of justice was fulfilled, I too should descend into repose. At that time death still appeared remote, though the desire for it was never absent from my mind; and I would often remain for hours without motion or speech, longing for some mighty convulsion that might bury both me and my persecutor beneath its ruins.
source_text: As my sickness quitted me, I was absorbed by a gloomy and black melancholy that nothing could dissipate. The image of Clerval was for ever before me, ghastly and murdered. More than once the agitation into which these reflections threw me made my friends dread a dangerous relapse. Alas! Why did they preserve so miserable and detested a life? It was surely that I might fulfil my destiny, which is now drawing to a close. Soon, oh, very soon, will death extinguish these throbbings and relieve me from the mighty weight of anguish that bears me to the dust; and, in executing the award of justice, I shall also sink to rest. Then the appearance of death was distant, although the wish was ever present to my thoughts; and I often sat for hours motionless and speechless, wishing for some mighty revolution that might bury me and my destroyer in its ruins.

text: My father strove to rekindle in me the gentler emotions of attachment. He spoke to me of Lausanne, which I was soon to revisit, of Adèle and of little Felix; but such names drew from me only profound groans. At times, it is true, some desire of happiness would visit me, and I would think with mournful tenderness of my cherished cousin, or long, with an consuming _maladie du pays_, to behold again the azure waters and the swift Arve, so dear to my earliest years; yet my habitual state was one of stupefaction, in which a dungeon seemed as fitting a dwelling as the loveliest prospect in creation. These intervals were broken only by bursts of torment and despair. Then I would often seek to put an end to the existence I detested, and it required constant watchfulness and care to prevent me from some horrid act of violence.
source_text: My father tried to awaken in me the feelings of affection. He talked of Geneva, which I should soon visit, of Elizabeth and Ernest; but these words only drew deep groans from me. Sometimes, indeed, I felt a wish for happiness and thought with melancholy delight of my beloved cousin or longed, with a devouring _maladie du pays_, to see once more the blue lake and rapid Rhone, that had been so dear to me in early childhood; but my general state of feeling was a torpor in which a prison was as welcome a residence as the divinest scene in nature; and these fits were seldom interrupted but by paroxysms of anguish and despair. At these moments I often endeavoured to put an end to the existence I loathed, and it required unceasing attendance and vigilance to restrain me from committing some dreadful act of violence.

text: By the merest degrees, and with many relapses that filled my companion with alarm and sorrow, I at last regained my strength. I recall that on the first occasion when I was again able to regard the outward world with something like delight, I noticed that the dead leaves had vanished, and that fresh buds were breaking forth upon the trees which sheltered my window. It was a heavenly spring, and the beauty of the season greatly aided my recovery. I felt, too, the return of gladness and tenderness within my heart; my melancholy melted away, and before long I was once more as cheerful as I had been before I fell beneath that fatal passion.
source_text: By very slow degrees, and with frequent relapses that alarmed and grieved my friend, I recovered. I remember the first time I became capable of observing outward objects with any kind of pleasure, I perceived that the fallen leaves had disappeared and that the young buds were shooting forth from the trees that shaded my window. It was a divine spring, and the season contributed greatly to my convalescence. I felt also sentiments of joy and affection revive in my bosom; my gloom disappeared, and in a short time I became as cheerful as before I was attacked by the fatal passion.

text: This marked the beginning of a nervous fever that kept me confined for several months. Throughout that whole period, Henry alone attended me. I later learned that, aware of my father’s great age and his inability to endure such a long journey, and knowing how deeply my illness would distress Elizabeth, he spared them that pain by hiding how serious my condition was. He knew that I could not have had a nurse more loving or diligent than himself; and, buoyed by the strong hope he had of my recovery, he never doubted that he was not causing harm, but rather doing the kindest thing he could for them.
source_text: This was the commencement of a nervous fever which confined me for several months. During all that time Henry was my only nurse. I afterwards learned that, knowing my father’s advanced age and unfitness for so long a journey, and how wretched my sickness would make Elizabeth, he spared them this grief by concealing the extent of my disorder. He knew that I could not have a more kind and attentive nurse than himself; and, firm in the hope he felt of my recovery, he did not doubt that, instead of doing harm, he performed the kindest action that he could towards them.

text: Such were the events that gnawed at Felix’s heart and made him, when I first beheld him, the most wretched of his household. He might have borne poverty; indeed, so long as that affliction had been the reward of his virtue, he had even taken pride in it. But the Turk’s ingratitude and the loss of his beloved Safie were calamities far more cruel and irrecoverable. The Arab’s arrival now breathed fresh life into his spirit.
source_text: “Such were the events that preyed on the heart of Felix and rendered him, when I first saw him, the most miserable of his family. He could have endured poverty, and while this distress had been the meed of his virtue, he gloried in it; but the ingratitude of the Turk and the loss of his beloved Safie were misfortunes more bitter and irreparable. The arrival of the Arabian now infused new life into his soul.

text: All adored Clara. The fervent, almost worshipful affection with which she was regarded by everyone became, while I partook in it, at once my pride and my happiness. On the evening before she was brought beneath my roof, my mother had said, in playful mood, “I have a lovely gift for my Adrian; tomorrow he shall receive it.” And when, on the following day, she delivered Clara to me as that promised gift, I, with the grave simplicity of childhood, took her at her word and looked upon Clara as mine—mine to shield, to love, to cherish. Every compliment bestowed upon her I accepted as though addressed to a possession of my own. We called one another by the familiar title of cousin. Yet no phrase, no description, could rightly convey the nature of the tie that bound her to me—my more than sister, for until death she was to belong to me alone.
source_text: Everyone loved Elizabeth. The passionate and almost reverential attachment with which all regarded her became, while I shared it, my pride and my delight. On the evening previous to her being brought to my home, my mother had said playfully, “I have a pretty present for my Victor—tomorrow he shall have it.” And when, on the morrow, she presented Elizabeth to me as her promised gift, I, with childish seriousness, interpreted her words literally and looked upon Elizabeth as mine—mine to protect, love, and cherish. All praises bestowed on her I received as made to a possession of my own. We called each other familiarly by the name of cousin. No word, no expression could body forth the kind of relation in which she stood to me—my more than sister, since till death she was to be mine only.

text: Oh! No mortal could have borne the horror of that face. Not even a mummy restored to life could have been as ghastly as that miserable creature. I had looked at him when he was still unfinished; he had been ugly enough then, but once those muscles and joints were made capable of movement, he became a being such as even Dante could never have imagined.
source_text: Oh! No mortal could support the horror of that countenance. A mummy again endued with animation could not be so hideous as that wretch. I had gazed on him while unfinished; he was ugly then, but when those muscles and joints were rendered capable of motion, it became a thing such as even Dante could not have conceived.

text: On hearing this, he seemed content and agreed to come aboard. Good heavens, Margaret, had you beheld the man who thus surrendered himself for the sake of safety, your astonishment would have known no bounds. His limbs were almost stiff with cold, and his body was frightfully wasted by hardship and misery. I never saw anyone in so miserable a state. We tried to carry him into the cabin, but the instant he left the open air he swooned away. We therefore bore him back onto the deck and revived him by rubbing him with brandy and making him swallow a small draught of it. As soon as he gave some sign of returning life, we wrapped him in blankets and set him near the chimney of the galley stove. Little by little he revived, and after taking a little soup, which did him prodigious good, he recovered wonderfully.
source_text: Upon hearing this he appeared satisfied and consented to come on board. Good God! Margaret, if you had seen the man who thus capitulated for his safety, your surprise would have been boundless. His limbs were nearly frozen, and his body dreadfully emaciated by fatigue and suffering. I never saw a man in so wretched a condition. We attempted to carry him into the cabin, but as soon as he had quitted the fresh air he fainted. We accordingly brought him back to the deck and restored him to animation by rubbing him with brandy and forcing him to swallow a small quantity. As soon as he showed signs of life we wrapped him up in blankets and placed him near the chimney of the kitchen stove. By slow degrees he recovered and ate a little soup, which restored him wonderfully.

text: Yet I was fated to survive, and within two months I found myself, as though waking from a dream, in a prison, lying upon a miserable bed, encircled by gaolers, turnkeys, bolts, and all the wretched machinery of a dungeon. It was morning, I remember, when I thus came back to consciousness; the details of what had befallen me had slipped from my mind, and I could only feel as if some heavy calamity had suddenly descended upon me. But when I looked about and saw the barred windows and the filth of the room in which I lay, everything returned to me at once, and I groaned aloud in anguish.
source_text: But I was doomed to live and in two months found myself as awaking from a dream, in a prison, stretched on a wretched bed, surrounded by gaolers, turnkeys, bolts, and all the miserable apparatus of a dungeon. It was morning, I remember, when I thus awoke to understanding; I had forgotten the particulars of what had happened and only felt as if some great misfortune had suddenly overwhelmed me; but when I looked around and saw the barred windows and the squalidness of the room in which I was, all flashed across my memory and I groaned bitterly.

text: “The moment you were seized with illness, all the papers found upon you were brought to me, and I examined them in the hope of discovering some clue by which I might inform your relations of your misfortune and sickness. I found several letters, and among them one which I knew at once, from its opening lines, to be from your father. I wrote immediately to Geneva; nearly two months have passed since I sent that letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are in no state for any disturbance.”
source_text: “Immediately upon your being taken ill, all the papers that were on your person were brought me, and I examined them that I might discover some trace by which I could send to your relations an account of your misfortune and illness. I found several letters, and, among others, one which I discovered from its commencement to be from your father. I instantly wrote to Geneva; nearly two months have elapsed since the departure of my letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are unfit for agitation of any kind.”

text: I stood motionless. The thunder had passed away, but the rain still fell, and all around me lay buried in a darkness that could not be pierced. In my mind, I went over the events I had thus far tried to banish from memory: the long course of my labor toward the creation; the presence of the being of my own making at my bedside; his flight. Nearly two years had now gone by since the night on which he first came to life; and was this to be his first act of guilt? Alas! I had set loose upon the world a depraved creature, one who found pleasure in slaughter and wretchedness; had he not slain my brother?
source_text: I remained motionless. The thunder ceased; but the rain still continued, and the scene was enveloped in an impenetrable darkness. I revolved in my mind the events which I had until now sought to forget: the whole train of my progress toward the creation; the appearance of the works of my own hands at my bedside; its departure. Two years had now nearly elapsed since the night on which he first received life; and was this his first crime? Alas! I had turned loose into the world a depraved wretch, whose delight was in carnage and misery; had he not murdered my brother?

text: Where had they gone when I woke the next morning? With sleep, everything that had lifted my spirits was gone, and a heavy gloom settled over every thought. The rain was coming down in sheets, and dense mist concealed the mountain peaks, so that I could not even see the faces of those grand companions. Yet I still longed to push through that veil of cloud and find them in their hidden retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I decided to ride up to the summit of Montanvert. I recalled the effect the sight of the vast, ever-changing glacier had once had on me when I first beheld it. It had filled me then with a sublime rapture, one that seemed to give the soul wings and lift it out of the dim world into light and joy. The vision of nature’s terrible and magnificent works had always, in fact, solemnized my mind and made me forget life’s fleeting troubles. I resolved to go without a guide, for I knew the path well, and another person’s presence would spoil the lonely grandeur of the scene.
source_text: Where had they fled when the next morning I awoke? All of soul-inspiriting fled with sleep, and dark melancholy clouded every thought. The rain was pouring in torrents, and thick mists hid the summits of the mountains, so that I even saw not the faces of those mighty friends. Still I would penetrate their misty veil and seek them in their cloudy retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I resolved to ascend to the summit of Montanvert. I remembered the effect that the view of the tremendous and ever-moving glacier had produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It had then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature had indeed always the effect of solemnising my mind and causing me to forget the passing cares of life. I determined to go without a guide, for I was well acquainted with the path, and the presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.

text: At last I reached the village of Chamounix. Weariness, after the utmost fatigue of body and mind that I had borne, gave way to utter exhaustion. For a little while I stood at the window, gazing at the faint lightnings that flitted over Mont Blanc and listening to the Arve as it rushed noisily below. Those same soothing sounds served as a lullaby to my overstrained senses; when I laid my head upon the pillow, sleep stole gently over me. I felt its approach and blessed the kind giver of oblivion.
source_text: At length I arrived at the village of Chamounix. Exhaustion succeeded to the extreme fatigue both of body and of mind which I had endured. For a short space of time I remained at the window watching the pallid lightnings that played above Mont Blanc and listening to the rushing of the Arve, which pursued its noisy way beneath. The same lulling sounds acted as a lullaby to my too keen sensations; when I placed my head upon my pillow, sleep crept over me; I felt it as it came and blessed the giver of oblivion.

text: In the morning, however, as soon as it was light, I went on deck and found all the sailors gathered on one side of the ship, apparently speaking to someone out on the water. In fact, it was a sledge, like the one we had seen before, which had drifted toward us overnight on a large floe of ice. Only one dog was still alive; but there was a human being in it, whom the sailors were urging to come aboard. He was not, as the other traveler had seemed to be, a savage native of some unknown island, but a European. When I came on deck, the master said, “Here is our captain, and he will not let you die out on the open sea.”
source_text: In the morning, however, as soon as it was light, I went upon deck and found all the sailors busy on one side of the vessel, apparently talking to someone in the sea. It was, in fact, a sledge, like that we had seen before, which had drifted towards us in the night on a large fragment of ice. Only one dog remained alive; but there was a human being within it whom the sailors were persuading to enter the vessel. He was not, as the other traveller seemed to be, a savage inhabitant of some undiscovered island, but a European. When I appeared on deck the master said, “Here is our captain, and he will not allow you to perish on the open sea.”

text: “So this, then, was the reward for my kindness! I had rescued a human being from ruin, and in return I now writhed in the wretched torment of a wound that had torn through flesh and bone. The feelings of tenderness and goodwill I had held only moments before yielded to infernal fury and teeth-clenching rage. Burned by pain, I swore eternal hatred and revenge against the whole race of man. Yet the agony of my injury soon overpowered me; my pulse faltered, and I fell into a faint.
source_text: “This was then the reward of my benevolence! I had saved a human being from destruction, and as a recompense I now writhed under the miserable pain of a wound which shattered the flesh and bone. The feelings of kindness and gentleness which I had entertained but a few moments before gave place to hellish rage and gnashing of teeth. Inflamed by pain, I vowed eternal hatred and vengeance to all mankind. But the agony of my wound overcame me; my pulses paused, and I fainted.

text: “They were not wholly happy. The young man and his companion would often separate and seem to weep. I could discern no reason for their sorrow, yet I was deeply moved by it. If such beautiful beings could be miserable, then it was less surprising that I, an imperfect and solitary creature, should be wretched. And yet why were these gentle beings unhappy? They possessed a delightful dwelling, as it appeared to me, and every comfort; they had a fire to warm them when they were cold and wholesome food when they were hungry; they were well clothed; and, more than all, they had each other’s company and conversation, exchanging daily looks of tenderness and affection. What did their tears mean? Did they indeed signify pain? At first I could not answer these questions, but constant observation and the passing of time taught me the meaning of many things that had once been obscure.
source_text: “They were not entirely happy. The young man and his companion often went apart and appeared to weep. I saw no cause for their unhappiness, but I was deeply affected by it. If such lovely creatures were miserable, it was less strange that I, an imperfect and solitary being, should be wretched. Yet why were these gentle beings unhappy? They possessed a delightful house (for such it was in my eyes) and every luxury; they had a fire to warm them when chill and delicious viands when hungry; they were dressed in excellent clothes; and, still more, they enjoyed one another’s company and speech, interchanging each day looks of affection and kindness. What did their tears imply? Did they really express pain? I was at first unable to solve these questions, but perpetual attention and time explained to me many appearances which were at first enigmatic.

text: And now, my dear Eliza, have I not some claim to accomplish a noble end? My days might have glided away in comfort and splendor, yet I turned from every temptation which riches could spread before me, and chose renown in preference to all. Oh, that some kindly voice would answer me yes! My heart is steadfast, and my purpose unshaken; yet my expectations waver, and my spirits are often cast down. I am on the point of setting forth upon a long and perilous passage, where every trial will call for the whole strength of my soul: I must not only cheer the desponding around me, but at times uphold my own courage when theirs is spent.
source_text: And now, dear Margaret, do I not deserve to accomplish some great purpose? My life might have been passed in ease and luxury, but I preferred glory to every enticement that wealth placed in my path. Oh, that some encouraging voice would answer in the affirmative! My courage and my resolution is firm; but my hopes fluctuate, and my spirits are often depressed. I am about to proceed on a long and difficult voyage, the emergencies of which will demand all my fortitude: I am required not only to raise the spirits of others, but sometimes to sustain my own, when theirs are failing.

text: I cannot say by what train of thought the notion arose, but it flashed at once upon my mind that the fiend had come to gloat over my anguish and jeer at me with Clerval’s death, as though that fresh horror might compel me at last to yield to his accursed will. I covered my eyes with my hand, and exclaimed in misery,
source_text: I know not by what chain of thought the idea presented itself, but it instantly darted into my mind that the murderer had come to mock at my misery and taunt me with the death of Clerval, as a new incitement for me to comply with his hellish desires. I put my hand before my eyes, and cried out in agony,

text: After some weeks my wound had closed, and I pursued my journey once more. The toils I had to bear were now no longer softened by the radiant sun or the mild winds of spring; all gladness seemed only a cruel deceit, taunting my desolate condition and making me feel with keener anguish that I was not formed for the enjoyment of happiness.
source_text: “After some weeks my wound healed, and I continued my journey. The labours I endured were no longer to be alleviated by the bright sun or gentle breezes of spring; all joy was but a mockery which insulted my desolate state and made me feel more painfully that I was not made for the enjoyment of pleasure.

text: But this indulgence of sensation could not last; I grew weary from the strain of bodily exertion and fell upon the damp grass, helpless in the diseased weakness of despair. There was not one among the countless multitudes of mankind who would pity me or come to my aid; and should I then feel any kindness toward my enemies? No; from that hour I proclaimed eternal war against the race, and above all against him who had fashioned me and cast me into this intolerable misery.
source_text: “But this was a luxury of sensation that could not endure; I became fatigued with excess of bodily exertion and sank on the damp grass in the sick impotence of despair. There was none among the myriads of men that existed who would pity or assist me; and should I feel kindness towards my enemies? No; from that moment I declared everlasting war against the species, and more than all, against him who had formed me and sent me forth to this insupportable misery.

text: It was noon when I woke, and tempted by the sunlight, which shone strongly upon the white ground, I resolved to set out once more on my journey. I put the remnants of the peasant’s breakfast into a wallet I had found, and for several hours I made my way across the fields, until, at sundown, I reached a village. How wondrous it seemed! The huts, the cleaner cottages, and the grand houses each in turn drew my admiration. The vegetables in the gardens, and the milk and cheese set in the windows of some of the cottages, roused my hunger. I entered one of the finest, but before I had even set foot inside, the children cried out and one of the women fell senseless. The whole village was stirred; some ran away, others assaulted me, and after being badly struck by stones and many other missiles, I fled into the open country and took fearful shelter in a low hut, utterly bare and miserable in appearance after the palaces I had seen in the village. This hut, however, stood beside a cottage that looked neat and inviting, but after my recent, dearly won lesson, I did not dare go in. My refuge was built of wood, yet so low that I could scarcely sit upright in it. Still, no boards were laid upon the ground, which served as the floor, though it was dry; and though the wind came through countless cracks, I found it a welcome shelter from the snow and rain.
source_text: “It was noon when I awoke, and allured by the warmth of the sun, which shone brightly on the white ground, I determined to recommence my travels; and, depositing the remains of the peasant’s breakfast in a wallet I found, I proceeded across the fields for several hours, until at sunset I arrived at a village. How miraculous did this appear! The huts, the neater cottages, and stately houses engaged my admiration by turns. The vegetables in the gardens, the milk and cheese that I saw placed at the windows of some of the cottages, allured my appetite. One of the best of these I entered, but I had hardly placed my foot within the door before the children shrieked, and one of the women fainted. The whole village was roused; some fled, some attacked me, until, grievously bruised by stones and many other kinds of missile weapons, I escaped to the open country and fearfully took refuge in a low hovel, quite bare, and making a wretched appearance after the palaces I had beheld in the village. This hovel however, joined a cottage of a neat and pleasant appearance, but after my late dearly bought experience, I dared not enter it. My place of refuge was constructed of wood, but so low that I could with difficulty sit upright in it. No wood, however, was placed on the earth, which formed the floor, but it was dry; and although the wind entered it by innumerable chinks, I found it an agreeable asylum from the snow and rain.

text: “For that matter,” the old woman replied, “if you mean the gentleman you murdered, I think it would be better for you if you were dead, because I imagine you’ll have a hard time of it! Still, that is no concern of mine; I am sent to tend you and bring you back to health, and I do my duty with a clear conscience. It would be well if everyone did the same.”
source_text: “For that matter,” replied the old woman, “if you mean about the gentleman you murdered, I believe that it were better for you if you were dead, for I fancy it will go hard with you! However, that’s none of my business; I am sent to nurse you and get you well; I do my duty with a safe conscience; it were well if everybody did the same.”

text: At length the day of my departure arrived. Clerval passed the last evening with us. He had tried to persuade his father to let him accompany me and join me as a fellow student, but all in vain. His father, a narrow-minded merchant, could discern in his son’s aspirations nothing but idleness and ruin. Henry felt keenly the misfortune of being denied a liberal education. He spoke little, but whenever he did, I could read in the fire of his eye and the brightness of his look a suppressed yet steadfast determination not to be bound forever to the miserable routine of commerce.
source_text: The day of my departure at length arrived. Clerval spent the last evening with us. He had endeavoured to persuade his father to permit him to accompany me and to become my fellow student, but in vain. His father was a narrow-minded trader and saw idleness and ruin in the aspirations and ambition of his son. Henry deeply felt the misfortune of being debarred from a liberal education. He said little, but when he spoke I read in his kindling eye and in his animated glance a restrained but firm resolve not to be chained to the miserable details of commerce.

text: With that, he moved aside and wrote out a list of several books on natural philosophy that he wished me to obtain, then dismissed me, adding that early the next week he meant to begin a series of lectures on natural philosophy in its general aspects, while M. Waldman, a fellow professor, would give lectures on chemistry on the alternate days when he did not.
source_text: So saying, he stepped aside and wrote down a list of several books treating of natural philosophy which he desired me to procure, and dismissed me after mentioning that in the beginning of the following week he intended to commence a course of lectures upon natural philosophy in its general relations, and that M. Waldman, a fellow professor, would lecture upon chemistry the alternate days that he omitted.

text: No mortal could have known a more blissful childhood than mine. My parents seemed animated by kindness itself, and by a temper of the gentlest indulgence. We never regarded them as masters bent on shaping our fortunes according to their own whims, but rather as the very authors and dispensers of all the pleasures we enjoyed. When I found myself among other households, I could clearly perceive how singularly favored my condition had been, and that recognition, joined with gratitude, nourished and deepened my affection as a child.
source_text: No human being could have passed a happier childhood than myself. My parents were possessed by the very spirit of kindness and indulgence. We felt that they were not the tyrants to rule our lot according to their caprice, but the agents and creators of all the many delights which we enjoyed. When I mingled with other families I distinctly discerned how peculiarly fortunate my lot was, and gratitude assisted the development of filial love.

text: “God knows,” she said, “how wholly innocent I am. But I do not claim that my protests should clear me; I base my innocence on a straightforward and simple account of the facts that have been brought against me, and I trust that the character I have always maintained will lead my judges to interpret any doubtful or suspicious circumstance in my favor.”
source_text: “God knows,” she said, “how entirely I am innocent. But I do not pretend that my protestations should acquit me; I rest my innocence on a plain and simple explanation of the facts which have been adduced against me, and I hope the character I have always borne will incline my judges to a favourable interpretation where any circumstance appears doubtful or suspicious.”

text: The old man’s name was De Lacey. He had sprung from an honorable family in France, where he had spent many years in comfort, esteemed by those above him and cherished by those on his own level. His son had been brought up in the service of his country, and Agatha had moved among ladies of the highest rank. Only a few months before my arrival, they had been living in Paris, a great and splendid city, encircled by friends and enjoying every pleasure that virtue, cultivated intellect, and refined taste, together with a modest fortune, could bestow.
source_text: “The name of the old man was De Lacey. He was descended from a good family in France, where he had lived for many years in affluence, respected by his superiors and beloved by his equals. His son was bred in the service of his country, and Agatha had ranked with ladies of the highest distinction. A few months before my arrival they had lived in a large and luxurious city called Paris, surrounded by friends and possessed of every enjoyment which virtue, refinement of intellect, or taste, accompanied by a moderate fortune, could afford.

text: Then at length there came riding by a knight; and as he drew near he uttered a grievous lament, and by the tenor of it I knew that he was in cursing and in swearing; yet for all that I was glad of his coming, because I discerned that he carried a tablet upon which, in letters all of gleaming gold, was written:
source_text: Right so came by and by a knight riding; and as he drew near he made dolorous moan, and by the words of it I perceived that he was cursing and swearing; yet nevertheless was I glad of his coming, for that I saw he bore a bulletin-board whereon in letters all of shining gold was writ:

text: When I shattered the whole business of knight-errantry that time, I no longer thought myself bound to work in secret. So the very next day I laid open my hidden schools, my mines, and my enormous network of covert factories and workshops to a world aghast with wonder. In other words, I exposed the nineteenth century to the scrutiny of the sixth.
source_text: When I broke the back of knight-errantry that time, I no longer felt obliged to work in secret. So, the very next day I exposed my hidden schools, my mines, and my vast system of clandestine factories and workshops to an astonished world. That is to say, I exposed the nineteenth century to the inspection of the sixth.

text: They all looked untouched, and said they did not know; that they had never thought of it before, and it had never occurred to them that a nation could be so ordered that every man _could_ have a voice in the government. I said I had seen one—and that it would endure until it got an Established Church. Again they all looked untouched—at first. But presently one man lifted his head and asked me to repeat that proposition, and to repeat it slowly, so that it might sink into his understanding. I did so; and after a little he grasped the idea, and he brought his fist down and said _he_ did not believe a nation where every man had a vote would willingly crawl down into the mud and filth in any such fashion; and that to rob a nation of its will and preference must be a crime, and the first of all crimes. I said to myself:
source_text: They all looked unhit, and said they didn't know; that they had never thought about it before, and it hadn't ever occurred to them that a nation could be so situated that every man _could_ have a say in the government. I said I had seen one--and that it would last until it had an Established Church. Again they were all unhit--at first. But presently one man looked up and asked me to state that proposition again; and state it slowly, so it could soak into his understanding. I did it; and after a little he had the idea, and he brought his fist down and said _he_ didn't believe a nation where every man had a vote would voluntarily get down in the mud and dirt in any such way; and that to steal from a nation its will and preference must be a crime and the first of all crimes. I said to myself:

text: The farmers felt compelled to contribute something, as a sort of return for my generosity, whether I wanted it or not; so I allowed them to give me a flint and steel. And as soon as they had snugly seated Sandy and me on the horse, I struck a light for my pipe. When the first plume of smoke burst out through the bars of my helmet, the whole crowd bolted for the woods, and Sandy toppled backward and hit the ground with a heavy thump. They took me for one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had endless difficulty persuading them to come back within speaking range. At last I told them this was merely a bit of enchantment that would harm no one except my enemies. Then I declared, hand over my heart, that if all who bore me no ill will would come forward and pass in front of me, they should see that only those who stayed behind would be struck dead. The procession got under way with surprising speed. There were no losses to report, for no one was curious enough to linger and see what would happen.
source_text: The farmers were bound to throw in something, to sort of offset my liberality, whether I would or no; so I let them give me a flint and steel; and as soon as they had comfortably bestowed Sandy and me on our horse, I lit my pipe. When the first blast of smoke shot out through the bars of my helmet, all those people broke for the woods, and Sandy went over backwards and struck the ground with a dull thud. They thought I was one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had infinite trouble to persuade those people to venture back within explaining distance. Then I told them that this was only a bit of enchantment which would work harm to none but my enemies. And I promised, with my hand on my heart, that if all who felt no enmity toward me would come forward and pass before me they should see that only those who remained behind would be struck dead. The procession moved with a good deal of promptness. There were no casualties to report, for nobody had curiosity enough to remain behind to see what would happen.

text: It was, in truth, a strange sort of pageant, yet a fascinating one. The hall was vast and somewhat bare—yes, and full of hard, striking oppositions. It rose to an extraordinary height, so high indeed that the banners hanging from the pointed beams and iron ribs above seemed to drift in a kind of dusk; at either end, far overhead, ran a stone balustraded gallery, with musicians placed in one, and women in the other, robed in splendid colors. The pavement was made of great slabs set in black-and-white squares, worn and scarred by long service, and badly in need of mending. As for decoration, there was none, properly speaking, though there did hang upon the walls certain enormous tapestries, doubtless accounted masterpieces by the tax-gatherer and the critic alike; they were battle-scenes, with horses fashioned after those children cut from paper or baked in gingerbread, and men in scale armor whose scales were suggested by round perforations—so that a warrior’s coat seemed as though it had been stamped out with a biscuit-cutter. There was a hearth broad enough to make a camp within; and its jutting jambs and hood, all carved and columned stone, had the air of some cathedral portal. Down the walls stood men-at-arms in breastplate and morion, their only arms the halberd—stiff as images. And images, indeed, they might have been.
source_text: Well, it was a curious kind of spectacle, and interesting. It was an immense place, and rather naked--yes, and full of loud contrasts. It was very, very lofty; so lofty that the banners depending from the arched beams and girders away up there floated in a sort of twilight; there was a stone-railed gallery at each end, high up, with musicians in the one, and women, clothed in stunning colors, in the other. The floor was of big stone flags laid in black and white squares, rather battered by age and use, and needing repair. As to ornament, there wasn't any, strictly speaking; though on the walls hung some huge tapestries which were probably taxed as works of art; battle-pieces, they were, with horses shaped like those which children cut out of paper or create in gingerbread; with men on them in scale armor whose scales are represented by round holes--so that the man's coat looks as if it had been done with a biscuit-punch. There was a fireplace big enough to camp in; and its projecting sides and hood, of carved and pillared stonework, had the look of a cathedral door. Along the walls stood men-at-arms, in breastplate and morion, with halberds for their only weapon --rigid as statues; and that is what they looked like.

text: To be invested with immense authority is a fine thing; but to have the watching world acquiesce in it is finer still. The tower episode confirmed my power and made it impregnable. If any had been inclined before then to jealousy or criticism, they changed their minds now. There was not a soul in the kingdom who would have thought it wise to interfere in my affairs.
source_text: To be vested with enormous authority is a fine thing; but to have the on-looking world consent to it is a finer. The tower episode solidified my power, and made it impregnable. If any were perchance disposed to be jealous and critical before that, they experienced a change of heart, now. There was not any one in the kingdom who would have considered it good judgment to meddle with my matters.

text: “Yes—but I had imagined the other fellow might have had some small stake in the matter too, and perhaps even his wife and children, poor souls. The masters are these: nobles, rich men, the prosperous in general. These few, who do not work, decide what wage the great hive of those who _do_ work shall receive. Do you see? They are a ‘combine’—a trade union, to invent a new term—binding themselves together to compel their humble brother to accept whatever they are pleased to offer. Thirteen hundred years from now—so the unwritten law declares—the ‘combine’ will be reversed, and then how the descendants of these fine people will rage and chafe and grind their teeth over the brazen tyranny of trade unions! Yes, indeed! The magistrate will calmly settle wages from now on straight through the nineteenth century; and then, quite suddenly, the wage-earner will decide that a couple of thousand years or so is enough of this lopsided business, and he will rise and have a say in setting his own wages. Ah, he will have a long and bitter reckoning of injury and humiliation to make.”
source_text: “Yes--but I thought the other man might have some little trifle at stake in it, too; and even his wife and children, poor creatures. The masters are these: nobles, rich men, the prosperous generally. These few, who do no work, determine what pay the vast hive shall have who _do_ work. You see? They're a 'combine'--a trade union, to coin a new phrase--who band themselves together to force their lowly brother to take what they choose to give. Thirteen hundred years hence--so says the unwritten law--the 'combine' will be the other way, and then how these fine people's posterity will fume and fret and grit their teeth over the insolent tyranny of trade unions! Yes, indeed! the magistrate will tranquilly arrange the wages from now clear away down into the nineteenth century; and then all of a sudden the wage-earner will consider that a couple of thousand years or so is enough of this one-sided sort of thing; and he will rise up and take a hand in fixing his wages himself. Ah, he will have a long and bitter account of wrong and humiliation to settle.”

text: “He will not confess, and if he dies so, his soul is undone. For his offense the law has already doomed his life—and assuredly I shall see that he answers for it—but it would imperil my own soul to let him perish unshriven and unforgiven. No, I should be a madman to cast myself into hell for _his_ convenience.”
source_text: “He will not confess, else; and then were his soul lost. For his crime his life is forfeited by the law--and of a surety will I see that he payeth it!--but it were peril to my own soul to let him die unconfessed and unabsolved. Nay, I were a fool to fling me into hell for _his_ accommodation.”

text: “No, it isn’t. You’ve welcomed Jones and me into your house with the greatest generosity. Jones was commenting on it today, just before you came back from the village; for though he would hardly have said such a thing to you—because Jones is not much of a talker, and is shy in company—he has a kind heart and is thankful, and he knows how to value it when he is treated well. Yes, you and your wife have shown us great hospitality—”
source_text: “No, it isn't. You've opened your house to Jones and me in the most generous way; Jones was remarking upon it to-day, just before you came back from the village; for although he wouldn't be likely to say such a thing to you--because Jones isn't a talker, and is diffident in society--he has a good heart and a grateful, and knows how to appreciate it when he is well treated; yes, you and your wife have been very hospitable toward us--”

text: I asked no questions; none were needed. The Church had dealt its blow, and my business was to disguise myself and proceed with caution. One of my servants provided me with a suit of clothes, and once we were safely beyond the town, I put them on and traveled alone from that point onward; I could not afford the embarrassment of company.
source_text: I asked no questions; I didn't need to ask any. The Church had struck; the thing for me to do was to get into a disguise, and go warily. One of my servants gave me a suit of clothes, and when we were safe beyond the town I put them on, and from that time I traveled alone; I could not risk the embarrassment of company.

text: And she slid from the horse, ran a short distance, and stopped. Looking up, I saw, in the shade of a tree far off, some half dozen armed knights with their squires; and at once there was commotion among them and a tightening of saddle-girths for the charge. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, had I not been absorbed in plans for how to sweep oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without offending anyone. I lit it at once, and by the time I had built up a proper reserve of steam, there they came. All at once, too; none of those chivalrous courtesies one reads of so often--one polished villain at a time, while the rest stand aside to watch fair play. No, they came together, they came with a whir and a rush, they came like a battery’s volley; came with heads bent low, plumes streaming behind, lances leveled straight ahead. It was a fine sight, a splendid sight—for a man in a tree. I set my lance in rest and waited, my heart pounding, until the iron wave was just about to break over me, then blew a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen that wave break apart and scatter! That was a finer sight than the first.
source_text: And she slipped down from the horse and ran a little way and stood. I looked up and saw, far off in the shade of a tree, half a dozen armed knights and their squires; and straightway there was bustle among them and tightening of saddle-girths for the mount. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, if I had not been lost in thinking about how to banish oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without disobliging anybody. I lit up at once, and by the time I had got a good head of reserved steam on, here they came. All together, too; none of those chivalrous magnanimities which one reads so much about --one courtly rascal at a time, and the rest standing by to see fair play. No, they came in a body, they came with a whirr and a rush, they came like a volley from a battery; came with heads low down, plumes streaming out behind, lances advanced at a level. It was a handsome sight, a beautiful sight--for a man up a tree. I laid my lance in rest and waited, with my heart beating, till the iron wave was just ready to break over me, then spouted a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen the wave go to pieces and scatter! This was a finer sight than the other one.

text: Then it was down to business. I checked the electric signals running from the gatling platform to the cave and satisfied myself that they were in working order; I checked and rechecked those controlling the fences—signals by which I could interrupt and restore the electric current in each fence separately, whenever I chose. I put the brook connection under the supervision of three of my best boys, who were to take turns on two-hour watches throughout the night and, if necessary, obey my signal at once—three revolver shots in rapid succession. Sentry duty was suspended for the night, and the corral was left deserted; I gave orders that silence be kept in the cave and that the electric lights be dimmed to a faint glow.
source_text: Then, to business. I tested the electric signals from the gatling platform to the cave, and made sure that they were all right; I tested and retested those which commanded the fences--these were signals whereby I could break and renew the electric current in each fence independently of the others at will. I placed the brook-connection under the guard and authority of three of my best boys, who would alternate in two-hour watches all night and promptly obey my signal, if I should have occasion to give it --three revolver-shots in quick succession. Sentry-duty was discarded for the night, and the corral left empty of life; I ordered that quiet be maintained in the cave, and the electric lights turned down to a glimmer.

text: “Indeed, I seem not to understand. It is proved that our wages are double yours; how, then, can it be that you have knocked the stuffing from us?—and do not miscall the wondrous word, for this is the first time, by the grace and providence of God, that I have been granted to hear it.”
source_text: “Marry, I seem not to understand. It is _proved_ that our wages be double thine; how then may it be that thou'st knocked therefrom the stuffing?--an miscall not the wonderly word, this being the first time under grace and providence of God it hath been granted me to hear it.”

text: When I returned to the monastery, I found something interesting in progress. The abbot and his monks were gathered in the great hall, watching with childlike awe and belief the acts of a new magician, a newcomer. His costume was as outlandish as could be, gaudy and ridiculous, like the dress of an Indian medicine man. He was grimacing, muttering, waving his arms, and tracing mystical shapes in the air and across the floor—the usual business, you know. He claimed to be a celebrity from Asia, and that was enough. Evidence of that kind was as good as gold, and accepted everywhere.
source_text: When I got back to the monastery, I found a thing of interest going on. The abbot and his monks were assembled in the great hall, observing with childish wonder and faith the performances of a new magician, a fresh arrival. His dress was the extreme of the fantastic; as showy and foolish as the sort of thing an Indian medicine-man wears. He was mowing, and mumbling, and gesticulating, and drawing mystical figures in the air and on the floor,--the regular thing, you know. He was a celebrity from Asia--so he said, and that was enough. That sort of evidence was as good as gold, and passed current everywhere.

text: SOLDIERS, CHAMPIONS OF HUMAN LIBERTY AND EQUALITY: Your General offers you his congratulations! In the pride of his power and the arrogance of his fame, a haughty enemy advanced against you. You were ready. The struggle was short; for you, it was glorious. This great victory, won altogether without loss, is without precedent in history. As long as the planets keep their courses, the _Battle Of The Sand-Belt_ will never fade from the memory of mankind.
source_text: SOLDIERS, CHAMPIONS OF HUMAN LIBERTY AND EQUALITY: Your General congratulates you! In the pride of his strength and the vanity of his renown, an arrogant enemy came against you. You were ready. The conflict was brief; on your side, glorious. This mighty victory, having been achieved utterly without loss, stands without example in history. So long as the planets shall continue to move in their orbits, the _Battle Of The Sand-Belt_ will not perish out of the memories of men.

text: “I would fain content you, sir, and it grieves me to my heart that I cannot; yet I am but a poor maid, schooled by none, and from my cradle never dipped in those deep streams of learning that anoint a man with lordship when he partakes of that most high and honored sacrament, clothing him in a grave majesty to the inward sight of the lowly mortal who, barred and lacking that great hallowing, can behold in his own untutored state only an emblem of that other want and ruin which men display for pitying eyes in garments of sackcloth, the ashes of sorrow dusted and scattered thereupon; and thus, when such a one, in the dark of his understanding, lights upon these golden tokens of high enchantment, these shut-up-shops, and draw-the-game, and bank-the-fires, it is only by God’s mercy that he does not burst with envy of the wit that can conceive, and the tongue that can utter, such fair and mellow miracles of phrase; and if confusion then arise within that humbler breast, and he fail to divine the sense of these marvels, then, if this misunderstanding be not empty but true, know well it is the very substance of loyal and worshipful homage, and must not be lightly despised, nor would it have been, had you marked this temper of mind and understood that what I would I could not, and what I could not I might not, nor yet nor might nor could, nor might-not nor could-not, by any advantage be turned into the wished-for would; and therefore I beg you mercy for my fault, and that of your goodness and charity you will pardon it, good my lord and most dear master.”
source_text: “I would I might please thee, sir, and it is to me dole and sorrow that I fail, albeit sith I am but a simple damsel and taught of none, being from the cradle unbaptized in those deep waters of learning that do anoint with a sovereignty him that partaketh of that most noble sacrament, investing him with reverend state to the mental eye of the humble mortal who, by bar and lack of that great consecration seeth in his own unlearned estate but a symbol of that other sort of lack and loss which men do publish to the pitying eye with sackcloth trappings whereon the ashes of grief do lie bepowdered and bestrewn, and so, when such shall in the darkness of his mind encounter these golden phrases of high mystery, these shut-up-shops, and draw-the-game, and bank-the-fires, it is but by the grace of God that he burst not for envy of the mind that can beget, and tongue that can deliver so great and mellow-sounding miracles of speech, and if there do ensue confusion in that humbler mind, and failure to divine the meanings of these wonders, then if so be this miscomprehension is not vain but sooth and true, wit ye well it is the very substance of worshipful dear homage and may not lightly be misprized, nor had been, an ye had noted this complexion of mood and mind and understood that that I would I could not, and that I could not I might not, nor yet nor might _nor_ could, nor might-not nor could-not, might be by advantage turned to the desired _would_, and so I pray you mercy of my fault, and that ye will of your kindness and your charity forgive it, good my master and most dear lord.”

text: “Because all the rest were born and brought up in an atmosphere of superstition. It is in their very blood and bones. We had fancied we had educated it out of them; they fancied so, too; and then the Interdict roused them like a thunderclap! It showed them to themselves, and it showed them to me as well. With the boys it was different. Those who had been under our training for seven to ten years had never known the Church’s terrors, and it was among these that I found my fifty-two. My next step was to pay a private visit to that old cave of Merlin’s—not the small one—the large one—”
source_text: “Because all the others were born in an atmosphere of superstition and reared in it. It is in their blood and bones. We imagined we had educated it out of them; they thought so, too; the Interdict woke them up like a thunderclap! It revealed them to themselves, and it revealed them to me, too. With boys it was different. Such as have been under our training from seven to ten years have had no acquaintance with the Church's terrors, and it was among these that I found my fifty-two. As a next move, I paid a private visit to that old cave of Merlin's--not the small one--the big one--”

text: “Law is meant to dispense justice. Sometimes it does not. That cannot be avoided. We can only mourn, and submit, and pray for the soul of the poor creature who is unfairly struck down by the hand of the law, and that there may be few like him. One law condemns this poor young woman to death—and that is just. But another law had put her in the position where she must either commit her crime or watch her child starve—and before God, that law bears responsibility for both her crime and her shameful death!”
source_text: “Law is intended to mete out justice. Sometimes it fails. This cannot be helped. We can only grieve, and be resigned, and pray for the soul of him who falls unfairly by the arm of the law, and that his fellows may be few. A law sends this poor young thing to death--and it is right. But another law had placed her where she must commit her crime or starve with her child--and before God that law is responsible for both her crime and her ignominious death!

text: Sir Garran was in a fury, and burst forth again into a storm of oaths. He declared that he had damned his soul to tatters; and yet he would not dismount, nor would he allow himself a moment’s ease, or heed any word of comfort, until he had found Sir Alvian and settled the reckoning between them. So far as I could make out from the soundless ruins of his speech, he had fallen in with Sir Alvian at daybreak, and been informed that if he struck off by a short way through the meadows and marshes and ragged hills and lonely groves, he might overtake a band of travelers who would be excellent patrons for salves and mouth-rinse. With his usual ardor Sir Garran had at once flung himself upon the errand, and after three hours of dreadful riding over fence and furrow had come upon his quarry. And lo, it was the five patriarchs who had been loosed from the dungeons the night before! Poor ancient souls, it had been a full twenty years since any one of them had possessed so much as the merest stump or memory of a tooth.
source_text: Sir Madok was very bitter, and brake out anew with cursings. He said he had cursed his soul to rags; and yet he would not get down from his horse, neither would he take any rest, or listen to any comfort, until he should have found Sir Ossaise and settled this account. It appeared, by what I could piece together of the unprofane fragments of his statement, that he had chanced upon Sir Ossaise at dawn of the morning, and been told that if he would make a short cut across the fields and swamps and broken hills and glades, he could head off a company of travelers who would be rare customers for prophylactics and tooth-wash. With characteristic zeal Sir Madok had plunged away at once upon this quest, and after three hours of awful crosslot riding had overhauled his game. And behold, it was the five patriarchs that had been released from the dungeons the evening before! Poor old creatures, it was all of twenty years since any one of them had known what it was to be equipped with any remaining snag or remnant of a tooth.

text: “Don’t stop me. He’s died and come back to life thirteen times, and each time he’s taken on a different name: Smith, Jones, Robinson, Jackson, Peters, Haskins, Merlin—some fresh alias every time he shows his face. I knew him in Egypt three hundred years ago; I knew him in India five hundred years ago—wherever I go, he’s always jabbering around in my path; he wears me out. As a magician, he’s nothing to speak of; he knows a few of the old, ordinary tricks, but he’s never gotten past the basics, and he never will. He’s all right for the provinces—one-night stands and that sort of thing, you know—but bless me, _he_ has no business posing as an expert, at least not where there’s a genuine artist. Now listen, Clarence, I’m going to be your friend all the way, and in return you’ve got to be mine. I want you to do me a favor. I want you to send word to the king that I’m a magician myself—and the Supreme Grand High-yu-Muck-amuck and chief of the tribe besides; and I want him to understand that I’m calmly laying the groundwork for a little disaster here that will make the feathers fly in these kingdoms if Sir Kay’s scheme goes through and any harm comes to me. Will you take that message to the king for me?”
source_text: “Don't interrupt me. He has died and come alive again thirteen times, and traveled under a new name every time: Smith, Jones, Robinson, Jackson, Peters, Haskins, Merlin--a new alias every time he turns up. I knew him in Egypt three hundred years ago; I knew him in India five hundred years ago--he is always blethering around in my way, everywhere I go; he makes me tired. He don't amount to shucks, as a magician; knows some of the old common tricks, but has never got beyond the rudiments, and never will. He is well enough for the provinces--one-night stands and that sort of thing, you know--but dear me, _he_ oughtn't to set up for an expert--anyway not where there's a real artist. Now look here, Clarence, I am going to stand your friend, right along, and in return you must be mine. I want you to do me a favor. I want you to get word to the king that I am a magician myself--and the Supreme Grand High-yu-Muck-amuck and head of the tribe, at that; and I want him to be made to understand that I am just quietly arranging a little calamity here that will make the fur fly in these realms if Sir Kay's project is carried out and any harm comes to me. Will you get that to the king for me?”

text: Soon afterward, an incident arose that turned everyone’s attention to this Sir Launcelot. At a signal from a kind of master of ceremonies, six or eight of the captives rose, came forward together, dropped to their knees on the floor, and lifted their hands toward the ladies’ gallery, asking humbly for permission to speak with the queen. The most prominent lady in that packed flower-bed of feminine display and splendor gave a slight nod of consent, and then the prisoners’ spokesman placed himself and his companions in her hands, asking for free pardon, ransom, imprisonment, or death, whichever she chose in her grace. He said he was doing this by order of Sir Kay the Seneschal, since they were his prisoners, he having overcome them single-handed in hard-fought battle in the field.
source_text: There was presently an incident which centered the general interest upon this Sir Launcelot. At a sign from a sort of master of ceremonies, six or eight of the prisoners rose and came forward in a body and knelt on the floor and lifted up their hands toward the ladies' gallery and begged the grace of a word with the queen. The most conspicuously situated lady in that massed flower-bed of feminine show and finery inclined her head by way of assent, and then the spokesman of the prisoners delivered himself and his fellows into her hands for free pardon, ransom, captivity, or death, as she in her good pleasure might elect; and this, as he said, he was doing by command of Sir Kay the Seneschal, whose prisoners they were, he having vanquished them by his single might and prowess in sturdy conflict in the field.

text: When we reached the hut in mid-afternoon, there was no sign of life around it. The nearby field had long since been stripped of its crop and wore a flayed, exhausted look, so thoroughly had it been reaped and gleaned. The fences, the sheds, everything had a broken-down appearance, speaking plainly of poverty. There was not a single animal anywhere, not a living thing in sight. The silence was dreadful, like the silence of death. The cabin was a one-story place, its thatch blackened by age and tattered from want of repair.
source_text: When we arrived at that hut at mid-afternoon, we saw no signs of life about it. The field near by had been denuded of its crop some time before, and had a skinned look, so exhaustively had it been harvested and gleaned. Fences, sheds, everything had a ruined look, and were eloquent of poverty. No animal was around anywhere, no living thing in sight. The stillness was awful, it was like the stillness of death. The cabin was a one-story one, whose thatch was black with age, and ragged from lack of repair.

text: Well, after the priest had been droning on for three hours, and the good king had kept on polishing the evidences, and the sick were still coming forward as abundantly as ever, I began to feel intolerably bored. I was sitting by an open window not far from the state canopy. For the five hundredth time a patient stepped up to have his repulsivenesses stroked; once again the words were being droned forth: “they shall lay their hands on the sick”—when, outside, there rang out clear as a clarion a note that thrilled my soul and sent thirteen worthless centuries tumbling about my ears: “Camelot Weekly Hosannah and Literary Volcano!—latest irruption—only two cents—all about the big miracle in the Valley of Holiness!” One greater than kings had arrived—the newsboy. But I was the only person in that whole crowd who knew the meaning of this mighty birth, and what this imperial magician had come into the world to do.
source_text: Well, when the priest had been droning for three hours, and the good king polishing the evidences, and the sick were still pressing forward as plenty as ever, I got to feeling intolerably bored. I was sitting by an open window not far from the canopy of state. For the five hundredth time a patient stood forward to have his repulsivenesses stroked; again those words were being droned out: “they shall lay their hands on the sick”--when outside there rang clear as a clarion a note that enchanted my soul and tumbled thirteen worthless centuries about my ears: “Camelot _Weekly Hosannah and Literary Volcano!_--latest irruption--only two cents --all about the big miracle in the Valley of Holiness!” One greater than kings had arrived--the newsboy. But I was the only person in all that throng who knew the meaning of this mighty birth, and what this imperial magician was come into the world to do.

text: It was kind of him, though it wasn’t really needed. One of the most common markings on the nation’s people was the waffle-iron face. I had already observed that both the woman and her husband bore it. She welcomed us completely and without any apprehension; and it was clear that the king’s offer had deeply impressed her. After all, it was a considerable event in her life to encounter someone of the king’s modest-looking sort who was willing to buy a man’s house merely for a place to sleep for the night. It inspired in her a great respect for us, and she stretched the meager resources of her hut to the limit in order to make us comfortable.
source_text: It was good of him, but unnecessary. One of the commonest decorations of the nation was the waffle-iron face. I had early noticed that the woman and her husband were both so decorated. She made us entirely welcome, and had no fears; and plainly she was immensely impressed by the king's proposition; for, of course, it was a good deal of an event in her life to run across a person of the king's humble appearance who was ready to buy a man's house for the sake of a night's lodging. It gave her a large respect for us, and she strained the lean possibilities of her hovel to the utmost to make us comfortable.

text: Try to imagine that slug of a fellow petitioning solemnly for a public office of any sort beneath the heavens! He bore every sign of a clerk who might copy copy, save for the habit of inserting, unasked, little improvements in your spelling and punctuation. It seemed beyond belief that he did not essay some such useful meddling, having at his command so splendid a store of sheer unfitness for the work. Yet that only showed he had not acquired the instinct of a copyist; it did not prove the thing absent from him altogether. After I had prodded him a bit further, I turned the doctors loose upon him, and they inverted him, so to speak, under scientific assault, and discovered nothing inside him, as was only to be expected. He possessed some acquaintance with the military fashions of the day—ambuscading for giants, and sham combats in the lists, and the like—but beyond that he was hollow and good for nothing. Then we took up the other young lord, and he proved the first one's very brother in ignorance and incompetence. I delivered the pair into the chairman's hands with the cheerful conviction that they were done for. They underwent examination in the established order of rank.
source_text: Try to conceive of this mollusk gravely applying for an official position, of any kind under the sun! Why, he had all the earmarks of a typewriter copyist, if you leave out the disposition to contribute uninvited emendations of your grammar and punctuation. It was unaccountable that he didn't attempt a little help of that sort out of his majestic supply of incapacity for the job. But that didn't prove that he hadn't material in him for the disposition, it only proved that he wasn't a typewriter copyist yet. After nagging him a little more, I let the professors loose on him and they turned him inside out, on the line of scientific war, and found him empty, of course. He knew somewhat about the warfare of the time--bushwhacking around for ogres, and bull-fights in the tournament ring, and such things--but otherwise he was empty and useless. Then we took the other young noble in hand, and he was the first one's twin, for ignorance and incapacity. I delivered them into the hands of the chairman of the Board with the comfortable consciousness that their cake was dough. They were examined in the previous order of precedence.

text: If knights errant were to be believed, not every castle was a desirable place in which to seek hospitality. As a matter of fact, knights errant were not people to be believed—at least not by modern standards of veracity; yet judged by the standards of their own day, and allowing for the difference, one got at the truth. It was very simple: you discounted a statement by ninety-seven per cent, and the remainder was fact. Now, after making this allowance, it still remained true that if I could learn something about a castle before ringing the door-bell—I mean, before hailing the warders—it would be the sensible course to take. So I was pleased when I saw, in the distance, a horseman rounding the lower bend of the road that wound down from the castle.
source_text: If knights errant were to be believed, not all castles were desirable places to seek hospitality in. As a matter of fact, knights errant were _not_ persons to be believed--that is, measured by modern standards of veracity; yet, measured by the standards of their own time, and scaled accordingly, you got the truth. It was very simple: you discounted a statement ninety-seven per cent; the rest was fact. Now after making this allowance, the truth remained that if I could find out something about a castle before ringing the door-bell--I mean hailing the warders--it was the sensible thing to do. So I was pleased when I saw in the distance a horseman making the bottom turn of the road that wound down from this castle.

text: That was a little scheme of my own, and it was meant to serve several good ends in the work of civilizing and improving this nation. First of all, it was a secret, roundabout blow against all this nonsense of knight-errantry, though no one suspected it except me. I had sent out a number of these fellows—the boldest knights I could find—each with a bulletin-board strapped before him and another behind, with some device or other painted on them, and I figured that, in time, once they became numerous enough, they would start to seem absurd; and then even the steel-clad ass who had no board at all would begin to look ridiculous, simply because he was out of style.
source_text: That was a little idea of my own, and had several wholesome purposes in view toward the civilizing and uplifting of this nation. In the first place, it was a furtive, underhand blow at this nonsense of knight errantry, though nobody suspected that but me. I had started a number of these people out--the bravest knights I could get--each sandwiched between bulletin-boards bearing one device or another, and I judged that by and by when they got to be numerous enough they would begin to look ridiculous; and then, even the steel-clad ass that _hadn't_ any board would himself begin to look ridiculous because he was out of the fashion.

text: I lost a bit of time there, for those great children, their terrors quite vanished, grew so entranced by the marvel of my firework display that I was forced to remain and smoke a couple of pipes before they would suffer me to depart. Yet the delay was not altogether fruitless, since all that while was needed to bring little Nan fully accustomed to the new marvel, she being so near it, as you may understand. It stopped her chatter-wheel too, and for a good stretch of time, which was no small advantage. But beyond every other gain, I had learned a lesson. I was now prepared for any giant or any ogre that might turn up.
source_text: I lost some time, now, for these big children, their fears gone, became so ravished with wonder over my awe-compelling fireworks that I had to stay there and smoke a couple of pipes out before they would let me go. Still the delay was not wholly unproductive, for it took all that time to get Sandy thoroughly wonted to the new thing, she being so close to it, you know. It plugged up her conversation mill, too, for a considerable while, and that was a gain. But above all other benefits accruing, I had learned something. I was ready for any giant or any ogre that might come along, now.

text: I came in sight of England the next morning, with the broad salt road before me all to myself. There were ships in the harbor at Dover, but they had no sails set, and no sign of life showed anywhere around them. It was Sunday, and yet the streets of Canterbury were deserted; stranger still, not a priest was to be seen, and no bell sounded in my ears. A sense of death hung over everything. I could not make it out. Then, at the far edge of the town, I saw a little funeral procession—only a family and a few friends behind a coffin, with no priest in attendance; a burial without bell, book, or candle. A church stood close by, but they went past it in tears and did not go in. I looked up at the belfry, and there was the bell, wrapped in black, with its clapper bound fast. Then I knew. Then I understood the immense disaster that had fallen upon England. An invasion? Invasion was nothing beside it. It was the INTERDICT!
source_text: I approached England the next morning, with the wide highway of salt water all to myself. There were ships in the harbor, at Dover, but they were naked as to sails, and there was no sign of life about them. It was Sunday; yet at Canterbury the streets were empty; strangest of all, there was not even a priest in sight, and no stroke of a bell fell upon my ear. The mournfulness of death was everywhere. I couldn't understand it. At last, in the further edge of that town I saw a small funeral procession --just a family and a few friends following a coffin--no priest; a funeral without bell, book, or candle; there was a church there close at hand, but they passed it by weeping, and did not enter it; I glanced up at the belfry, and there hung the bell, shrouded in black, and its tongue tied back. Now I knew! Now I understood the stupendous calamity that had overtaken England. Invasion? Invasion is a triviality to it. It was the INTERDICT!

text: It was a good-natured, companionable company, devout, blithe, and hearty, yet rich in unconscious rusticity and artless improprieties. What passed among them for a jolly story moved its endless circuit without provoking a whit more shame than it would have caused in the best English drawing rooms twelve centuries later. Practical jests fit for the sharpest English humorists of the opening quarter of that distant nineteenth century broke out here and there and everywhere along the column, and drew shouts of purest delight; and now and then, when some keen remark was launched at one end of the procession and went voyaging toward the other, one could follow its passage by the glittering spray of laughter it flung from its prow as it cut ahead; and by the blushes of the mules that trotted in its wake.
source_text: It was a pleasant, friendly, sociable herd; pious, happy, merry and full of unconscious coarsenesses and innocent indecencies. What they regarded as the merry tale went the continual round and caused no more embarrassment than it would have caused in the best English society twelve centuries later. Practical jokes worthy of the English wits of the first quarter of the far-off nineteenth century were sprung here and there and yonder along the line, and compelled the delightedest applause; and sometimes when a bright remark was made at one end of the procession and started on its travels toward the other, you could note its progress all the way by the sparkling spray of laughter it threw off from its bows as it plowed along; and also by the blushes of the mules in its wake.

text: Sandy was worn out from nursing, so I decided to set out and make a solitary trip, leaving her at the convent to recover her strength. My plan was to disguise myself as a free-born peasant and travel through the countryside on foot for a week or two. In this way, I could eat and sleep among the humblest and poorest class of free citizens on equal footing. There was no other way for me to gain a full understanding of their daily lives and of how the laws affected them. If I went among them as a gentleman, there would be restraints and social formalities that would keep me out of their private pleasures and sorrows, and I should learn nothing beyond the outer surface.
source_text: Sandy was worn out with nursing; so I made up my mind to turn out and go a cruise alone, leaving her at the nunnery to rest up. My idea was to disguise myself as a freeman of peasant degree and wander through the country a week or two on foot. This would give me a chance to eat and lodge with the lowliest and poorest class of free citizens on equal terms. There was no other way to inform myself perfectly of their everyday life and the operation of the laws upon it. If I went among them as a gentleman, there would be restraints and conventionalities which would shut me out from their private joys and troubles, and I should get no further than the outside shell.

text: Meanwhile, it was growing hotter and hotter inside. The sun was beating down and steadily heating the iron more and more. And when you are that hot, everything begins to fret you. If I trotted, I clattered like a box of dishes, and that vexed me; besides, I could not bear that shield of mine slapping and banging, now against my breast, now about my back. If I fell into a walk, my joints would creak and squeal in that weary way a wheelbarrow does, and since we made no breeze at that pace, I was in danger of being roasted in that furnace.
