Worse still, the more quietly you went, the more the iron seemed to settle on you, and the more tons you appeared to weigh with every passing minute. And all the while you had to keep shifting your hands and changing your spear from one foot to the other, for it became so tiresome to hold it in one hand very long.
source_text: Meantime, it was getting hotter and hotter in there. You see, the sun was beating down and warming up the iron more and more all the time. Well, when you are hot, that way, every little thing irritates you. When I trotted, I rattled like a crate of dishes, and that annoyed me; and moreover I couldn't seem to stand that shield slatting and banging, now about my breast, now around my back; and if I dropped into a walk my joints creaked and screeched in that wearisome way that a wheelbarrow does, and as we didn't create any breeze at that gait, I was like to get fried in that stove; and besides, the quieter you went the heavier the iron settled down on you and the more and more tons you seemed to weigh every minute. And you had to be always changing hands, and passing your spear over to the other foot, it got so irksome for one hand to hold it long at a time.

text: I gave three pennies for my breakfast, and it was a truly ruinous price, too, considering that a dozen people might have breakfasted on that sum; but by then I was in good spirits, and besides, I had always had a rather prodigal streak. Moreover, these people had meant to give me the meal for nothing, meager as their stores were, and so it gave me a pleasant satisfaction to show my gratitude and real thankfulness with a generous financial gesture, where the money would be of far greater use than it was in my helmet, since those pennies were made of iron and not sparing in weight, and my fifty cents amounted to quite a load. It is true that in those days I spent money a little too recklessly; but part of the reason was that I had not yet fully corrected my sense of scale, even after such a long stay in Britain—I had not yet reached the point where I could fully feel that a penny in Arthur’s kingdom and a couple of dollars in Connecticut were, in purchasing power, much the same thing, twins, so to speak. If my departure from Camelot had been put off by only a few days, I could have paid these people in bright new coins from our own mint, and that would have delighted me, as it would have them no less. I had taken on American standards completely. Within a week or two, cents, nickels, dimes, quarters, and half-dollars, along with a little gold, would be trickling in thin but steady streams through the commercial arteries of the kingdom, and I expected to see this new blood quicken the whole life of the land.
source_text: I paid three pennies for my breakfast, and a most extravagant price it was, too, seeing that one could have breakfasted a dozen persons for that money; but I was feeling good by this time, and I had always been a kind of spendthrift anyway; and then these people had wanted to give me the food for nothing, scant as their provision was, and so it was a grateful pleasure to emphasize my appreciation and sincere thankfulness with a good big financial lift where the money would do so much more good than it would in my helmet, where, these pennies being made of iron and not stinted in weight, my half-dollar's worth was a good deal of a burden to me. I spent money rather too freely in those days, it is true; but one reason for it was that I hadn't got the proportions of things entirely adjusted, even yet, after so long a sojourn in Britain--hadn't got along to where I was able to absolutely realize that a penny in Arthur's land and a couple of dollars in Connecticut were about one and the same thing: just twins, as you may say, in purchasing power. If my start from Camelot could have been delayed a very few days I could have paid these people in beautiful new coins from our own mint, and that would have pleased me; and them, too, not less. I had adopted the American values exclusively. In a week or two now, cents, nickels, dimes, quarters, and half-dollars, and also a trifle of gold, would be trickling in thin but steady streams all through the commercial veins of the kingdom, and I looked to see this new blood freshen up its life.

text: The moment we were properly inside the castle gates, we were bidden to appear before her. King Uriens was there, a gentle old man with a quiet air; and with him was his son, Sir Uwaine le Blanchemains, in whom I naturally took an interest, partly because of the tale that he had once fought thirty knights, and partly because of his journey with Sir Gawaine and Sir Marhaus, a story Sandy had been keeping me supplied with. But Morgan was the true center of attention, the dominating spirit of the place; it was clear enough that she was the mistress of that household. She made us sit down, and then, with every sort of charming grace and courtesy, she began to question me. Good heavens, it was like listening to a bird, or a flute, or something of the kind. I was convinced that this woman must have been misjudged, lied about. She went on and on in that clear bright note, and before long a striking young page, dressed in rainbow colors and moving with the easy, flowing grace of a wave, came carrying something on a golden tray. Kneeling to present it to her, he affected his manners a little too much, lost his balance, and brushed lightly against her knee. She thrust a dagger into him as casually as another person might have stuck a knife into a rat!
source_text: As soon as we were fairly within the castle gates we were ordered into her presence. King Uriens was there, a kind-faced old man with a subdued look; and also the son, Sir Uwaine le Blanchemains, in whom I was, of course, interested on account of the tradition that he had once done battle with thirty knights, and also on account of his trip with Sir Gawaine and Sir Marhaus, which Sandy had been aging me with. But Morgan was the main attraction, the conspicuous personality here; she was head chief of this household, that was plain. She caused us to be seated, and then she began, with all manner of pretty graces and graciousnesses, to ask me questions. Dear me, it was like a bird or a flute, or something, talking. I felt persuaded that this woman must have been misrepresented, lied about. She trilled along, and trilled along, and presently a handsome young page, clothed like the rainbow, and as easy and undulatory of movement as a wave, came with something on a golden salver, and, kneeling to present it to her, overdid his graces and lost his balance, and so fell lightly against her knee. She slipped a dirk into him in as matter-of-course a way as another person would have harpooned a rat!

text: “It may not be so, Father. No doubt, as you say, where authority is absolute, a man may do as he pleases and come to no harm; but we poor conjurers are not placed in such fortunate circumstances. Aldric is a very respectable wizard in his modest way, and enjoys a tidy local reputation. He is making his way as best he can, and it would scarcely be good manners for me to seize his post before he has chosen to relinquish it himself.”
source_text: “It may not be, Father. No doubt, as you say, where power is supreme, one can do as one likes and suffer no injury; but we poor magicians are not so situated. Merlin is a very good magician in a small way, and has quite a neat provincial reputation. He is struggling along, doing the best he can, and it would not be etiquette for me to take his job until he himself abandons it.”

text: By this time the tale of the well’s calamity had spread far and wide; and for two or three days now a steady flood of people had been pouring down into the valley. The lower end of it had turned into one vast encampment; there would be no lack of a good house, of that there was no doubt. In the early evening the heralds went from place to place and announced the attempt that was to be made, setting every heart beating at fever pitch. They gave notice that the abbot and his appointed retinue would come in ceremonial procession and take their seats upon the platform at half past ten, and until that hour all the ground under my prohibition must be kept clear; then the bells would fall silent, and that silence would be the sign for the throng to press forward and claim their places.
source_text: The news of the disaster to the well had traveled far by this time; and now for two or three days a steady avalanche of people had been pouring into the valley. The lower end of the valley was become one huge camp; we should have a good house, no question about that. Criers went the rounds early in the evening and announced the coming attempt, which put every pulse up to fever heat. They gave notice that the abbot and his official suite would move in state and occupy the platform at 10:30, up to which time all the region which was under my ban must be clear; the bells would then cease from tolling, and this sign should be permission to the multitudes to close in and take their places.

text: The first thing we came upon that day was a procession of pilgrims. It was not moving in our direction, but we joined it all the same; for it was becoming clearer to me every hour that, if I was to govern this country well, I needed to know the details of its life, and not from others, but through my own direct observation and careful examination.
source_text: The first thing we struck that day was a procession of pilgrims. It was not going our way, but we joined it, nevertheless; for it was hourly being borne in upon me now, that if I would govern this country wisely, I must be posted in the details of its life, and not at second hand, but by personal observation and scrutiny.

text: We had, by this time, a solemn wait upon the stage of about twenty minutes—a delay I had counted on for its effect; it is always wise to give an audience a chance to build its expectancy. At last, out of the silence there rose a noble Latin chant—men’s voices—which broke and swelled and drifted away into the night, a majestic flood of melody. I had arranged that as well, and it was one of the finest effects I ever devised. When it ended I rose upon the platform and spread my hands wide for two minutes, my face turned upward—that always brings on a deathlike hush—and then I slowly uttered this dreadful word with an awful manner that made hundreds tremble and many women faint:
source_text: We had a solemn stage-wait, now, for about twenty minutes--a thing I had counted on for effect; it is always good to let your audience have a chance to work up its expectancy. At length, out of the silence a noble Latin chant--men's voices--broke and swelled up and rolled away into the night, a majestic tide of melody. I had put that up, too, and it was one of the best effects I ever invented. When it was finished I stood up on the platform and extended my hands abroad, for two minutes, with my face uplifted--that always produces a dead hush--and then slowly pronounced this ghastly word with a kind of awfulness which caused hundreds to tremble, and many women to faint:

text: “If you do not know your country’s laws, then it is time you learned them. You are strangers to us; you will not deny that. You may be free men, and we do not deny it; but you may also be slaves. The law is plain: it does not ask the claimant to prove that you are slaves; it asks you to prove that you are not.”
source_text: “An ye do not know your country's laws, it were time ye learned them. Ye are strangers to us; ye will not deny that. Ye may be freemen, we do not deny that; but also ye may be slaves. The law is clear: it doth not require the claimant to prove ye are slaves, it requireth you to prove ye are not.”

text: We knocked out the head of an empty hogshead and hauled it up to the chapel’s flat roof, where we fastened it securely, then poured in gunpowder until it lay an inch deep, loose, at the bottom. After that we set rockets upright in the hogshead as closely as they could stand without support, every sort of rocket there is; and they made, I assure you, a stout and impressive bundle. We ran the wire from a pocket electric battery into that powder, and at each corner of the roof we set a whole store of Greek fire—blue at one corner, green at another, red at another, and purple at the last—and ran a wire into each one.
source_text: We knocked the head out of an empty hogshead and hoisted this hogshead to the flat roof of the chapel, where we clamped it down fast, poured in gunpowder till it lay loosely an inch deep on the bottom, then we stood up rockets in the hogshead as thick as they could loosely stand, all the different breeds of rockets there are; and they made a portly and imposing sheaf, I can tell you. We grounded the wire of a pocket electrical battery in that powder, we placed a whole magazine of Greek fire on each corner of the roof--blue on one corner, green on another, red on another, and purple on the last--and grounded a wire in each.

text: Well, a man like that is bound to be full of fight—that goes without saying. When you have a couple thousand rough men under you, you get more than enough of that kind of entertainment. I certainly did. At last I met my equal, and I got what was coming to me. It happened in the middle of a disagreement carried on with crowbars with a fellow we used to call Hercules. He flattened me with a blow alongside the head that set everything rattling, seemed to wrench every joint in my skull loose and make them overlap their neighbors. Then the world vanished into darkness, and after that I felt nothing and knew nothing at all—at least for a while.
source_text: Well, a man like that is a man that is full of fight--that goes without saying. With a couple of thousand rough men under one, one has plenty of that sort of amusement. I had, anyway. At last I met my match, and I got my dose. It was during a misunderstanding conducted with crowbars with a fellow we used to call Hercules. He laid me out with a crusher alongside the head that made everything crack, and seemed to spring every joint in my skull and made it overlap its neighbor. Then the world went out in darkness, and I didn't feel anything more, and didn't know anything at all --at least for a while.

text: “And still there is another particular that must be set right. He shall bring nothing from without; we will go within—among the filth, and perhaps other loathsome things—and eat the meal with the household, in the manner of the house, and upon terms of full equality, unless the fellow be of the bond class; and lastly, there shall be neither basin nor towel, whether he be bond or free. Pray, walk once more, my lord. There—better now—it is the best thus far; though not yet perfect. Those shoulders have borne no meaner load than iron harness, and they will not bend.”
source_text: “And there is even yet a detail that needs correcting. He must bring nothing outside; we will go in--in among the dirt, and possibly other repulsive things,--and take the food with the household, and after the fashion of the house, and all on equal terms, except the man be of the serf class; and finally, there will be no ewer and no napkin, whether he be serf or free. Please walk again, my liege. There--it is better--it is the best yet; but not perfect. The shoulders have known no ignobler burden than iron mail, and they will not stoop.”

text: Then such a fit of foolish laughter seized him that he lurched about like a drunkard and soon came up against one of our wires. His mouth is still open; he seems to be laughing still. I imagine the face will keep that frozen grin until the body crumbles to dust.
source_text: Then such a delirium of silly laughter overtook him that he reeled about like a drunken man, and presently fetched up against one of our wires. His mouth is spread open yet; apparently he is still laughing. I suppose the face will retain that petrified laugh until the corpse turns to dust.

text: The crowd settled meekly back into their seats, just as I had expected they would. Merlin paused for a moment or two, and during that brief interval I was all nerves and unease. Then he sat down, and I drew a long breath, for I knew the situation was now in my hands. The king said:
source_text: The multitude sank meekly into their seats, and I was just expecting they would. Merlin hesitated a moment or two, and I was on pins and needles during that little while. Then he sat down, and I took a good breath; for I knew I was master of the situation now. The king said:

text: There followed one of the dreariest quarter-hours I have ever been forced to endure. We waited in a lonely stillness, shut in by our rings of wire and by a farther ring of dense smoke. We could not look beyond that wall of smoke, nor could we pierce it with our eyes. But at last it began to tear itself apart, drifting off sluggishly; and by the end of another quarter-hour the ground was once more visible, and our curiosity was at last satisfied. Not a living thing was to be seen! We now discovered that our defenses had been strengthened. The dynamite had gouged out a trench more than a hundred feet wide all around us, and thrown up on either side of it an embankment some twenty-five feet high. As for the destruction of life, it was astonishing. Indeed, it was beyond reckoning. Naturally, we could not count the dead, since they no longer existed as separate beings, but only as indistinguishable protoplasm, mixed with bits of iron and buttons.
source_text: Now ensued one of the dullest quarter-hours I had ever endured. We waited in a silent solitude enclosed by our circles of wire, and by a circle of heavy smoke outside of these. We couldn't see over the wall of smoke, and we couldn't see through it. But at last it began to shred away lazily, and by the end of another quarter-hour the land was clear and our curiosity was enabled to satisfy itself. No living creature was in sight! We now perceived that additions had been made to our defenses. The dynamite had dug a ditch more than a hundred feet wide, all around us, and cast up an embankment some twenty-five feet high on both borders of it. As to destruction of life, it was amazing. Moreover, it was beyond estimate. Of course, we could not _count_ the dead, because they did not exist as individuals, but merely as homogeneous protoplasm, with alloys of iron and buttons.

text: I was not the only prisoner there. There were twenty or more. Poor wretches, many of them were horribly maimed, hacked, and gashed, and their hair, their faces, and their clothes were caked with dark, dried blood. They were in obvious bodily agony, of course; and they must have been worn out, hungry, and thirsty as well; and clearly no one had even given them the comfort of a wash, or the small mercy of a lotion for their wounds. Yet you never heard one of them moan or groan, nor saw any sign of restlessness or any inclination to complain. The thought came to me irresistibly: “The rascals—they have done this sort of thing to others in their time; now that the turn has come to them, they were not expecting any kinder treatment than this. So their calm, philosophical manner is not the result of discipline, intellect, or reasoning; it is only animal training; they are white Indians.”
source_text: I was not the only prisoner present. There were twenty or more. Poor devils, many of them were maimed, hacked, carved, in a frightful way; and their hair, their faces, their clothing, were caked with black and stiffened drenchings of blood. They were suffering sharp physical pain, of course; and weariness, and hunger and thirst, no doubt; and at least none had given them the comfort of a wash, or even the poor charity of a lotion for their wounds; yet you never heard them utter a moan or a groan, or saw them show any sign of restlessness, or any disposition to complain. The thought was forced upon me: “The rascals--_they_ have served other people so in their day; it being their own turn, now, they were not expecting any better treatment than this; so their philosophical bearing is not an outcome of mental training, intellectual fortitude, reasoning; it is mere animal training; they are white Indians.”

text: I sat down beside my fire and looked over my treasure. The first and largest portion of it was parchment, yellowed by age. I examined one leaf more closely and found that it was a palimpsest. Beneath the faint, weather-worn writing of the Yankee historian there could still be traced an even older and dimmer hand—Latin words and phrases, evidently fragments of ancient monastic legends. I turned to the spot marked by my stranger and began to read, as follows:
source_text: I sat down by my fire and examined my treasure. The first part of it--the great bulk of it--was parchment, and yellow with age. I scanned a leaf particularly and saw that it was a palimpsest. Under the old dim writing of the Yankee historian appeared traces of a penmanship which was older and dimmer still--Latin words and sentences: fragments from old monkish legends, evidently. I turned to the place indicated by my stranger and began to read --as follows:

text: No stir of life met the eye; yet there had needs been wounded in the hinder ranks, borne away from the field beneath the shelter of that rolling curtain of smoke, and among the rest there would be sickness — there is always sickness after such an affair. But no succour could come up; this was the final stand of England’s mailed nobility, all that remained of the Order after the late wars of extermination. I could therefore feel myself quite secure in the belief that the greatest power which, in time to come, could be brought against us would be but a petty one — of knights, that is. So I addressed to my army a proclamation of congratulation in these words:
source_text: No life was in sight, but necessarily there must have been some wounded in the rear ranks, who were carried off the field under cover of the wall of smoke; there would be sickness among the others--there always is, after an episode like that. But there would be no reinforcements; this was the last stand of the chivalry of England; it was all that was left of the order, after the recent annihilating wars. So I felt quite safe in believing that the utmost force that could for the future be brought against us would be but small; that is, of knights. I therefore issued a congratulatory proclamation to my army in these words:

text: —“Never yet did a knight arrive but that he found some uncouth adventure, by my troth. In very sooth it is even so, my gracious lord, though indeed it be exceeding hard to declare; and yet, for all that, the thing may not linger, but rather prosper the more through custom. So they rode on toward the maidens, and each gave the other greeting; and the eldest wore a circlet of gold upon her brow, and she was of threescore winters and more—”
source_text: --“came never knight but he found strange adventures, be jabers. Of a truth it doth indeed, fair lord, albeit 'tis passing hard to say, though peradventure that will not tarry but better speed with usage. And then they rode to the damsels, and either saluted other, and the eldest had a garland of gold about her head, and she was threescore winter of age or more--”

text: Then this thought came to me: how careless I had been! When the boy grew quiet again, he would surely marvel why a mighty conjurer such as I should have stooped to beg a lad like him to aid my escape from this place; he would set this and that beside each other, and would discover that I was nothing but a fraud.
source_text: Presently this thought occurred to me: how heedless I have been! When the boy gets calm, he will wonder why a great magician like me should have begged a boy like him to help me get out of this place; he will put this and that together, and will see that I am a humbug.

text: “Mayhap; yet it was their first transgression, and for long they had lived in perfect innocence, differing in nothing from the angels. Prayers, tears, and the mortifying of the flesh availed nothing to persuade that water to run once more. Processions themselves; burnt offerings themselves; even waxen tapers set before the Virgin, each in its turn proved vain; and all the land stood in wonder.”
source_text: “Belike; but it was their first sin; and they had been of perfect life for long, and differing in naught from the angels. Prayers, tears, torturings of the flesh, all was vain to beguile that water to flow again. Even processions; even burnt-offerings; even votive candles to the Virgin, did fail every each of them; and all in the land did marvel.”

text: “Now you know what manner of man I am, brother Jones,” said the smith, with a noble and friendly condescension. “And no doubt you would expect to find me one who is jealous of the respect due him, and cautious in his dealings with strangers until their rank and worth are made plain. But trouble yourself no further on that score; be assured that you shall find me a man who cares nothing for such things, and who is willing to accept any man as his fellow and equal, so long as he carries a true heart within him, however modest his worldly estate may be. And to show it, here is my hand; and I say with my own mouth that we are equals—equals.” And he smiled about at the company with the self-satisfied air of a god performing a gracious and generous act, and fully conscious of it.
source_text: “Now ye know what manner of man I am, brother Jones,” said the smith, with a fine and friendly condescension, “and doubtless ye would look to find me a man jealous of his due of respect and but sparing of outgo to strangers till their rating and quality be assured, but trouble yourself not, as concerning that; wit ye well ye shall find me a man that regardeth not these matters but is willing to receive any he as his fellow and equal that carrieth a right heart in his body, be his worldly estate howsoever modest. And in token of it, here is my hand; and I say with my own mouth we are equals--equals”--and he smiled around on the company with the satisfaction of a god who is doing the handsome and gracious thing and is quite well aware of it.

text: “Though you may be a spy, and though your speech be but a snare laid for my ruin, yet it brings me such comfort that to hear it once more, and words akin to it after, would send me to the gallows with gladness, as one who has at last supped well in an otherwise famished life. And now I shall speak my piece, and you may carry it where you choose, if such be your will. I hanged my neighbors because it was perilous to my own life to show any want of fervor in the lord’s service; the rest did it for no better cause. To-day all make merry that he is gone, yet all go abroad with faces of grief and the crocodile tear, for in that is safety. I have spoken it, I have spoken it! the only words that ever sat sweet upon my tongue, and that sweetness is reward enough. Lead me on, if you please, even to the scaffold, for I am prepared.”
source_text: “Even though you be a spy, and your words a trap for my undoing, yet are they such refreshment that to hear them again and others like to them, I would go to the gallows happy, as having had one good feast at least in a starved life. And I will say my say now, and ye may report it if ye be so minded. I helped to hang my neighbors for that it were peril to my own life to show lack of zeal in the master's cause; the others helped for none other reason. All rejoice to-day that he is dead, but all do go about seemingly sorrowing, and shedding the hypocrite's tear, for in that lies safety. I have said the words, I have said the words! the only ones that have ever tasted good in my mouth, and the reward of that taste is sufficient. Lead on, an ye will, be it even to the scaffold, for I am ready.”

text: Sleep? It was out of the question. In that foul den of a jail, among the shaggy rabble of tippling, brawling, and song-chanting vagabonds, slumber could hardly have found me in any case. Yet what made rest more utterly impossible was the feverish impatience that consumed me to be gone from the place and learn the full extent of whatever might have befallen over yonder in the quarters, as a consequence of that intolerable blunder of mine.
source_text: Sleep? It was impossible. It would naturally have been impossible in that noisome cavern of a jail, with its mangy crowd of drunken, quarrelsome, and song-singing rapscallions. But the thing that made sleep all the more a thing not to be dreamed of, was my racking impatience to get out of this place and find out the whole size of what might have happened yonder in the slave-quarters in consequence of that intolerable miscarriage of mine.

text: “And Marco, there’s something else you have to allow—out of kindness to Jones—because you wouldn’t want to hurt his feelings. He was very eager to show his gratitude in some way, but he is so shy that he couldn’t bring himself to do it directly, and so he asked me to buy a few little things and give them to you and Dame Phyllis, and let him pay for them without your ever knowing they came from him—you know how sensitive a person feels about that sort of thing—and so I said I would, and we’d keep quiet. Well, what he had in mind was a new set of clothes for both of you—”
source_text: “And Marco, there's another thing which you must permit--out of kindness for Jones--because you wouldn't want to offend him. He was very anxious to testify his appreciation in some way, but he is so diffident he couldn't venture it himself, and so he begged me to buy some little things and give them to you and Dame Phyllis and let him pay for them without your ever knowing they came from him--you know how a delicate person feels about that sort of thing --and so I said I would, and we would keep mum. Well, his idea was, a new outfit of clothes for you both--”

text: “It was through me that the change was brought about! And I worked hard indeed to make it happen. But when I told them of the disaster awaiting them, and saw how great their fear became, I knew then that this was the moment to act! So I carefully made it seem to this person and that and the other that your power over the sun would not reach its full strength until tomorrow; and therefore, if anyone wished to save the sun and the world, you must be killed today, while your spells were still only being woven and had no force. Zounds, it was a poor lie, a very ordinary invention, but you should have seen how they snatched at it and swallowed it whole, in the madness of their terror, as though it were heaven’s own rescue sent down to them; and all the while I was laughing inwardly one moment to see them so easily fooled, and praising God the next, that He was willing to use the least of His creatures as the means to save your life. Ah, how well things have gone! You will not need to do the sun any _real_ harm—ah, do not forget that, on your soul do not forget it! Only make a little darkness—only the tiniest little darkness, mind, and stop there. That will be enough. They will see that I lied, being ignorant, as they will suppose—and with the first shadow of that darkness falling, you shall see them go frantic with fear; and they will set you free and make you great! Go now to your triumph! But remember—ah, good friend, I beg you, remember my plea and do no harm to the blessed sun. For _my_ sake, your true friend.”
source_text: “’Tis through _me_ the change was wrought! And main hard have I worked to do it, too. But when I revealed to them the calamity in store, and saw how mighty was the terror it did engender, then saw I also that this was the time to strike! Wherefore I diligently pretended, unto this and that and the other one, that your power against the sun could not reach its full until the morrow; and so if any would save the sun and the world, you must be slain to-day, while your enchantments are but in the weaving and lack potency. Odsbodikins, it was but a dull lie, a most indifferent invention, but you should have seen them seize it and swallow it, in the frenzy of their fright, as it were salvation sent from heaven; and all the while was I laughing in my sleeve the one moment, to see them so cheaply deceived, and glorifying God the next, that He was content to let the meanest of His creatures be His instrument to the saving of thy life. Ah how happy has the matter sped! You will not need to do the sun a _real_ hurt--ah, forget not that, on your soul forget it not! Only make a little darkness--only the littlest little darkness, mind, and cease with that. It will be sufficient. They will see that I spoke falsely,--being ignorant, as they will fancy --and with the falling of the first shadow of that darkness you shall see them go mad with fear; and they will set you free and make you great! Go to thy triumph, now! But remember--ah, good friend, I implore thee remember my supplication, and do the blessed sun no hurt. For _my_ sake, thy true friend.”

text: I could not follow Alisande’s further account of who our captured knights were now—I mean, if she ever came to the telling of it. My interest had faded, and my thoughts were far away and sorrowful. Through broken flashes of the wandering tale, caught here and there and now and then, I only dimly gathered that each of these three knights had lifted one of the three damsels up behind him on his horse, and that one had ridden north, another east, and the third south, all in search of adventures, to meet again and to lie, after year and day. Year and day—and no baggage. It was entirely in keeping with the country’s general simplicity.
source_text: I could not follow Alisande's further explanation of who our captured knights were, now--I mean in case she should ever get to explaining who they were. My interest was gone, my thoughts were far away, and sad. By fitful glimpses of the drifting tale, caught here and there and now and then, I merely noted in a vague way that each of these three knights took one of these three damsels up behind him on his horse, and one rode north, another east, the other south, to seek adventures, and meet again and lie, after year and day. Year and day--and without baggage. It was of a piece with the general simplicity of the country.

text: “Remove his bonds and set him free! Bow before him, great and small, rich and poor alike, for he has become the king’s right hand, robed in power and authority, and his place is on the highest step of the throne! Now let this creeping night be swept away, and bring back light and cheer, that the whole world may bless you.”
source_text: “Away with his bonds, and set him free! and do him homage, high and low, rich and poor, for he is become the king's right hand, is clothed with power and authority, and his seat is upon the highest step of the throne! Now sweep away this creeping night, and bring the light and cheer again, that all the world may bless thee.”

text: Out from his pavilion rode the mighty Sir Almeric, a lofty pillar of steel, grave and unbending, with his long lance set upright in its rest and held fast in his powerful hand; his great horse, too, had its face and chest armored in steel, while its body was hung with rich trappings that nearly swept the earth—truly a splendid sight. A loud acclaim arose then, full of greeting and wonder.
source_text: Out from his tent rode great Sir Sagramor, an imposing tower of iron, stately and rigid, his huge spear standing upright in its socket and grasped in his strong hand, his grand horse's face and breast cased in steel, his body clothed in rich trappings that almost dragged the ground--oh, a most noble picture. A great shout went up, of welcome and admiration.

text: “An ancient hauberk, from the sixth century, the age of King Arthur and the Round Table; said to have once belonged to the knight Sir Sagramor le Desirous; note the round hole in the chain mail over the left breast; its origin cannot be explained; it is thought to have been made by a bullet after the invention of firearms—perhaps, maliciously, by Cromwell’s soldiers.”
source_text: “Ancient hauberk, date of the sixth century, time of King Arthur and the Round Table; said to have belonged to the knight Sir Sagramor le Desirous; observe the round hole through the chain-mail in the left breast; can't be accounted for; supposed to have been done with a bullet since invention of firearms--perhaps maliciously by Cromwell's soldiers.”

text: Thus the world imagined that some great question hung upon this affair, and the world was not mistaken, though it erred in the thing it supposed. No; upon the throwing of that die rested a matter greater still: the life of chivalric adventure. I was indeed a champion, but not of those foolish black arts; I was the defender of plain, unsentimental common sense and reason. Into the lists I rode, resolved either to bring knighthood’s wandering creed to ruin, or perish by its hand.
source_text: So the world thought there was a vast matter at stake here, and the world was right, but it was not the one they had in their minds. No, a far vaster one was upon the cast of this die: _the life of knight-errantry_. I was a champion, it was true, but not the champion of the frivolous black arts, I was the champion of hard unsentimental common-sense and reason. I was entering the lists to either destroy knight-errantry or be its victim.

text: I had expected him to say as much. For a moment, the man and his wife seemed keenly interested in the news and eager to hurry off and pass it on; then, quite suddenly, something else showed in their expressions, and they began to question me. I answered the questions myself and watched closely for the effect they had. Before long I was satisfied that learning who these three prisoners were had somehow altered the whole mood; our hosts’ seeming eagerness to go and spread the story was now only a pretense. The king did not notice the change, and I was glad of it. I steered the talk toward other matters connected with the night’s events, and could see that these people were relieved to have it turn in that direction.
source_text: I was just expecting he would come out with that. For a moment the man and his wife showed an eager interest in this news and an impatience to go out and spread it; then a sudden something else betrayed itself in their faces, and they began to ask questions. I answered the questions myself, and narrowly watched the effects produced. I was soon satisfied that the knowledge of who these three prisoners were had somehow changed the atmosphere; that our hosts' continued eagerness to go and spread the news was now only pretended and not real. The king did not notice the change, and I was glad of that. I worked the conversation around toward other details of the night's proceedings, and noted that these people were relieved to have it take that direction.

text: From that point on, the madam went on piling surprise upon surprise with such speed that the general amazement mounted to a hundred and fifty in the shade, while the power to show it seemed reduced to nothing but breathless “Oh’s” and “Ah’s,” and silent lifts of hands and eyes. She brought in crockery—new crockery, and enough of it; new wooden goblets and other tableware; and beer, fish, chicken, a goose, eggs, roast beef, roast mutton, a ham, a small roast pig, and a generous supply of real white wheaten bread. All in all, that table outshone anything those people had ever seen before by a wide margin. And while they sat there utterly dazed with wonder and awe, I gave a little casual wave of my hand, and the storekeeper’s son appeared out of nowhere and said he had come to collect.
source_text: From this out, the madam piled up the surprises with a rush that fired the general astonishment up to a hundred and fifty in the shade, and at the same time paralyzed expression of it down to gasped “Oh's” and “Ah's,” and mute upliftings of hands and eyes. She fetched crockery--new, and plenty of it; new wooden goblets and other table furniture; and beer, fish, chicken, a goose, eggs, roast beef, roast mutton, a ham, a small roast pig, and a wealth of genuine white wheaten bread. Take it by and large, that spread laid everything far and away in the shade that ever that crowd had seen before. And while they sat there just simply stupefied with wonder and awe, I sort of waved my hand as if by accident, and the storekeeper's son emerged from space and said he had come to collect.

text: “For I once saw him tested at a tournament where many knights were assembled, and at that time no man could withstand him. Ah,” said Sir Gawaine, “damsels, I think you are in the wrong, for it is likely that he who hung that shield there will not remain there long, and then those knights may meet him on horseback, and that would be greater honor for you than this; for I will stay no longer to see a knight’s shield shamed.” And with that Sir Uwaine and Sir Gawaine moved a little away from them, and then they saw Sir Marhaus riding toward them on a great horse. When the twelve damsels saw Sir Marhaus, they fled into the turret as though they were crazed, so that some of them fell in the way. Then one of the knights of the tower raised his shield and cried aloud, “Sir Marhaus, defend thee.” And so they charged together, and the knight broke his spear upon Marhaus, and Sir Marhaus struck him so fiercely that he broke his neck and the horse’s back--
source_text: “--for I saw him once proved at a justs where many knights were gathered, and that time there might no man withstand him. Ah, said Sir Gawaine, damsels, methinketh ye are to blame, for it is to suppose he that hung that shield there will not be long therefrom, and then may those knights match him on horseback, and that is more your worship than thus; for I will abide no longer to see a knight's shield dishonored. And therewith Sir Uwaine and Sir Gawaine departed a little from them, and then were they ware where Sir Marhaus came riding on a great horse straight toward them. And when the twelve damsels saw Sir Marhaus they fled into the turret as they were wild, so that some of them fell by the way. Then the one of the knights of the tower dressed his shield, and said on high, Sir Marhaus defend thee. And so they ran together that the knight brake his spear on Marhaus, and Sir Marhaus smote him so hard that he brake his neck and the horse's back--”

text: “Ho-ho! With us they’re allowed a hundred! With us, any decent mechanic gets a cent a day! I leave the tailor out of it, but not the rest—they’re all allowed a cent a day, and in busy times they get more—yes, as much as a hundred and ten, and even fifteen milrays a day. I’ve paid a hundred and fifteen myself, within the week. Hurrah for protection—to Sheol with free trade!”
source_text: “Ho-ho! With us they are allowed a hundred! With us any good mechanic is allowed a cent a day! I count out the tailor, but not the others--they are all allowed a cent a day, and in driving times they get more--yes, up to a hundred and ten and even fifteen milrays a day. I've paid a hundred and fifteen myself, within the week. 'Rah for protection--to Sheol with free-trade!”

text: Oh, I’m telling you, it was a stunner! You should have seen them fall apart, the whole lot of them. I had eased up on poor, smiling, self-satisfied Dowley so smoothly and quietly that he never guessed anything was coming until the blow came smashing down and tore him to shreds.
source_text: Oh, I tell _you_ it was a smasher! You ought to have seen them to go to pieces, the whole gang. I had just slipped up on poor smiling and complacent Dowley so nice and easy and softly, that he never suspected anything was going to happen till the blow came crashing down and knocked him all to rags.

text: Then I lit the barrel of rockets, and a great fountain of blinding fire-spears shot upward with a hissing roar, rising toward the sky before bursting overhead into a shower of glittering gems! A single vast cry of fear went up from the packed crowd—then all at once it broke into a wild shout of praise, for there, clear and unmistakable in that strange blaze, they saw the liberated water leaping out! The old abbot could not get a word past the tears and the choking in his throat; without saying a thing, he took me in his arms and crushed me. It said more than words ever could. And it was harder to recover from, too, in a country where there were hardly any doctors worth a bent nickel.
source_text: Then I touched off the hogshead of rockets, and a vast fountain of dazzling lances of fire vomited itself toward the zenith with a hissing rush, and burst in mid-sky into a storm of flashing jewels! One mighty groan of terror started up from the massed people --then suddenly broke into a wild hosannah of joy--for there, fair and plain in the uncanny glare, they saw the freed water leaping forth! The old abbot could not speak a word, for tears and the chokings in his throat; without utterance of any sort, he folded me in his arms and mashed me. It was more eloquent than speech. And harder to get over, too, in a country where there were really no doctors that were worth a damaged nickel.

text: From a business standpoint, it was wise to spread the idea that the undertaking was a difficult one. Many a small matter has been magnified by the proper sort of advertising. That monk was steeped in the difficulty of the enterprise; he would steep the others in it as well. In two days, concern would be in full cry.
source_text: As a matter of business it was a good idea to get the notion around that the thing was difficult. Many a small thing has been made large by the right kind of advertising. That monk was filled up with the difficulty of this enterprise; he would fill up the others. In two days the solicitude would be booming.

text: The iron tower and the splendid bedquilt came whirling down the lists, awkwardly yet with a certain grace, and we stepped lightly forward to meet them. We stopped; the tower gave its salute, and I returned it; then we turned and rode side by side to the grand stand, where we faced our king and queen and bowed before them. The queen cried out:
source_text: The iron tower and the gorgeous bedquilt came cumbrously but gracefully pirouetting down the lists, and we tripped lightly up to meet them. We halted; the tower saluted, I responded; then we wheeled and rode side by side to the grand-stand and faced our king and queen, to whom we made obeisance. The queen exclaimed:

text: But we didn’t; which was just as well, because we would have been interrupted. Before the stones had been crashing through the leaves and rebounding from the branches for fifteen minutes, we began to catch a smell. A couple of sniffs told the whole story—it was smoke! At last, the game was over for us. We knew it. When smoke calls, you have to answer. They kept piling their dry brush and damp weeds higher and higher, and when they saw the thick cloud start to billow up and suffocate the tree, they burst into a storm of triumphant cries. I managed to get enough breath to say:
source_text: But we didn't; which was just as well, for we should have been interrupted. Before the stones had been raging through the leaves and bouncing from the boughs fifteen minutes, we began to notice a smell. A couple of sniffs of it was enough of an explanation --it was smoke! Our game was up at last. We recognized that. When smoke invites you, you have to come. They raised their pile of dry brush and damp weeds higher and higher, and when they saw the thick cloud begin to roll up and smother the tree, they broke out in a storm of joy-clamors. I got enough breath to say:

text: Naturally, I was furious, and declared that I had only just stepped ashore after a long passage, and all that kind of nonsense—merely to find out, you understand, whether I might fool that fellow. But it was useless. He recognized me at once. Then I accused him of having given me away. He seemed more astonished than wounded. He opened his eyes very wide, and said:
source_text: Of course, I was indignant, and swore I had just come ashore from a long voyage, and all that sort of thing--just to see, you know, if it would deceive that slave. But it didn't. He knew me. Then I reproached him for betraying me. He was more surprised than hurt. He stretched his eyes wide, and said:

text: We were put near the back, among the servants. We made good speed, and at last came to a halt some time after dark at a wayside inn, perhaps ten or twelve miles from the place where our troubles had begun. My master went at once to his chamber, after ordering supper, and we saw nothing of him again. At daybreak we took our breakfast and prepared to set out.
source_text: We were placed toward the rear, among the servants. We traveled pretty fast, and finally drew rein some time after dark at a roadside inn some ten or twelve miles from the scene of our troubles. My lord went immediately to his room, after ordering his supper, and we saw no more of him. At dawn in the morning we breakfasted and made ready to start.

text: “Well, then, let me have my say. I have no fear of your repeating it. I believe the devil’s work was done last night upon those innocent, wretched people. That old baron got no more than he deserved. If it were left to me, all his kind would meet the same fate.”
source_text: “Well, then, let me say my say. I have no fears of your repeating it. I think devil's work has been done last night upon those innocent poor people. That old baron got only what he deserved. If I had my way, all his kind should have the same luck.”

text: This venture was cricket in all but name. To give the business a fashionable start, and to set it beyond the sneers of fault-finders, I selected my elevens by degree, not by skill. There was not a single warrior on either side who was not a crowned ruler in his own right. As for such stock, Arthur’s court was forever overflowing with it. One might have hurled a stone in any direction and risked disabling a monarch. Naturally, I could not persuade these gentlemen to lay aside their armor; they would not even do it for bathing. They did consent to make the armor distinguishable, so that one side could be told from the other, but that was the farthest they would go. Thus one party played in chain-mail ulsters, while the other appeared in plate made from my new Bessemer steel. Their exercises on the field were the strangest spectacle I had ever witnessed. Being proof against balls, they never dodged aside, but stood their ground and took the blow; when a Bessemer man was at bat and the ball struck him, it would sometimes spring away a hundred and fifty yards. And when a fellow was running, and flung himself flat to slide into his base, the effect was like a warship nosing into harbor. At first I set men of no standing to serve as umpires, but I was forced to abandon that plan. These people were no more accommodating than any ordinary side. The umpire’s first ruling was commonly his last; they split him open with a bat, and his companions carried him off on a door. Once it was seen that no umpire ever came out of a match alive, the office became distinctly unfashionable. So I had to appoint some person whose rank and high place in the government might shield him.
source_text: This experiment was baseball. In order to give the thing vogue from the start, and place it out of the reach of criticism, I chose my nines by rank, not capacity. There wasn't a knight in either team who wasn't a sceptered sovereign. As for material of this sort, there was a glut of it always around Arthur. You couldn't throw a brick in any direction and not cripple a king. Of course, I couldn't get these people to leave off their armor; they wouldn't do that when they bathed. They consented to differentiate the armor so that a body could tell one team from the other, but that was the most they would do. So, one of the teams wore chain-mail ulsters, and the other wore plate-armor made of my new Bessemer steel. Their practice in the field was the most fantastic thing I ever saw. Being ball-proof, they never skipped out of the way, but stood still and took the result; when a Bessemer was at the bat and a ball hit him, it would bound a hundred and fifty yards sometimes. And when a man was running, and threw himself on his stomach to slide to his base, it was like an iron-clad coming into port. At first I appointed men of no rank to act as umpires, but I had to discontinue that. These people were no easier to please than other nines. The umpire's first decision was usually his last; they broke him in two with a bat, and his friends toted him home on a shutter. When it was noticed that no umpire ever survived a game, umpiring got to be unpopular. So I was obliged to appoint somebody whose rank and lofty position under the government would protect him.

text: We carried The Boss to the cave and tended his wound as well as we could, though it was not at all serious. In this task Merlin helped us, though we did not realize it at the time. He had disguised himself as a woman and looked like nothing more than an ordinary old country wife. In that disguise, with his face stained brown and his beard clean-shaven, he had shown up a few days after The Boss was injured and offered to do our cooking, saying that her own people had gone off to join certain new camps the enemy was setting up, and that she was starving. The Boss had been coming along very well and had entertained himself by putting the finishing touches on his record.
source_text: We carried The Boss to the cave and gave his wound, which was not very serious, the best care we could. In this service we had the help of Merlin, though we did not know it. He was disguised as a woman, and appeared to be a simple old peasant goodwife. In this disguise, with brown-stained face and smooth shaven, he had appeared a few days after The Boss was hurt and offered to cook for us, saying her people had gone off to join certain new camps which the enemy were forming, and that she was starving. The Boss had been getting along very well, and had amused himself with finishing up his record.

text: It was a gentle, hushed summer scene, lovely as a vision and lonely as a Sabbath morning. The air was laden with the fragrance of blossoms, the hum of insects, and the light chirping of birds; yet there were no people, no carts, no movement of life at all, nothing stirring anywhere. The road was little more than a meandering track marked by hoofprints, with here and there a dim wheel-rut showing in the grass on either side—ruts made by wheels that seemed to wear tires as broad as a hand.
source_text: It was a soft, reposeful summer landscape, as lovely as a dream, and as lonesome as Sunday. The air was full of the smell of flowers, and the buzzing of insects, and the twittering of birds, and there were no people, no wagons, there was no stir of life, nothing going on. The road was mainly a winding path with hoof-prints in it, and now and then a faint trace of wheels on either side in the grass--wheels that apparently had a tire as broad as one's hand.

text: “Indeed, we shall hear it again,” the boy sighed. “That same old, tiresome story he has told a thousand times in exactly the same words, and will go on telling until the day he dies, every time he gets his barrel filled and feels his exaggeration machine set to work. God grant I had died, or that I had never lived to see this day!”
source_text: “Marry, we shall have it again,” sighed the boy; “that same old weary tale that he hath told a thousand times in the same words, and that he _will_ tell till he dieth, every time he hath gotten his barrel full and feeleth his exaggeration-mill a-working. Would God I had died or I saw this day!”

text: When the king traveled for a change of air, or went on progress, or paid a visit to some distant noble he meant to ruin by the expense of entertaining him, a portion of the government went with him. It was the custom of the day. The Commission responsible for examining candidates for posts in the army accompanied the king to the Valley, though it could just as well have conducted its affairs at home. And although this journey was, for the king, nothing more than a holiday outing, he continued certain official duties all the same. He laid hands on the sick, as always; he held court in the gate at sunrise and heard cases, for he was himself Chief Justice of the King’s Bench.
source_text: When the king traveled for change of air, or made a progress, or visited a distant noble whom he wished to bankrupt with the cost of his keep, part of the administration moved with him. It was a fashion of the time. The Commission charged with the examination of candidates for posts in the army came with the king to the Valley, whereas they could have transacted their business just as well at home. And although this expedition was strictly a holiday excursion for the king, he kept some of his business functions going just the same. He touched for the evil, as usual; he held court in the gate at sunrise and tried cases, for he was himself Chief Justice of the King's Bench.

text: Our new coinage was not merely passing hands in fine style, but its vocabulary had already slipped quite readily into common speech; that is to say, the old denominations had been forgotten, and people now talked of things as costing so many dollars, or cents, or mills, or milrays. It was highly gratifying. We were advancing, there could be no doubt of that.
source_text: Our new money was not only handsomely circulating, but its language was already glibly in use; that is to say, people had dropped the names of the former moneys, and spoke of things as being worth so many dollars or cents or mills or milrays now. It was very gratifying. We were progressing, that was sure.

text: London, to a slave, was an interesting enough place. It was only a very large village, after all, mostly mud and thatch. The streets were muddy, winding, and without paving. The people were a constant surging and wandering crowd, all rags and finery together, with nodding feathers and gleaming armor. The king had a palace there; I saw it from the outside. That sight made me sigh, and yes, swear a little too, in the feeble, boyish way of the sixth century. We saw knights and nobles we knew, though they did not know us in our rags and grime and fresh welts and bruises, and would not have recognized us even if we had called out to them, nor stopped to answer, since it was forbidden to speak with slaves in chains. Sandy passed within ten yards of me on a mule, and I took him to be searching for me. But the thing that almost broke my heart was something that happened in front of our old barracks in a square, while we had to watch a man being boiled alive in oil for counterfeiting pennies. It was the sight of a newsboy—and I could not reach him! Still, I had one comfort: it was proof that Clarence was still alive and hard at work. I meant to be with him before long, and the thought cheered me greatly.
source_text: London--to a slave--was a sufficiently interesting place. It was merely a great big village; and mainly mud and thatch. The streets were muddy, crooked, unpaved. The populace was an ever flocking and drifting swarm of rags, and splendors, of nodding plumes and shining armor. The king had a palace there; he saw the outside of it. It made him sigh; yes, and swear a little, in a poor juvenile sixth century way. We saw knights and grandees whom we knew, but they didn't know us in our rags and dirt and raw welts and bruises, and wouldn't have recognized us if we had hailed them, nor stopped to answer, either, it being unlawful to speak with slaves on a chain. Sandy passed within ten yards of me on a mule--hunting for me, I imagined. But the thing which clean broke my heart was something which happened in front of our old barrack in a square, while we were enduring the spectacle of a man being boiled to death in oil for counterfeiting pennies. It was the sight of a newsboy--and I couldn't get at him! Still, I had one comfort--here was proof that Clarence was still alive and banging away. I meant to be with him before long; the thought was full of cheer.

text: My two experts came in the evening, and pretty well worn out, for they had traveled double tides. They had pack-mules with them, and had brought all that I needed—tools, pump, lead pipe, Greek fire, bundles of big rockets, roman candles, colored fire sprays, electric apparatus, and a variety of other matters—everything required for the most imposing sort of miracle. They had their supper and a little sleep, and about midnight we set out through a solitude so utterly empty and complete that it quite surpassed the necessary conditions. We took possession of the well and all about it. My men were masters of every kind of work, from stoning up a well to making a mathematical instrument. An hour before sunrise we had the leak repaired in excellent shape, and the water began to rise. Then we stored our fireworks in the chapel, locked the place, and went home to bed.
source_text: My two experts arrived in the evening, and pretty well fagged, for they had traveled double tides. They had pack-mules along, and had brought everything I needed--tools, pump, lead pipe, Greek fire, sheaves of big rockets, roman candles, colored fire sprays, electric apparatus, and a lot of sundries--everything necessary for the stateliest kind of a miracle. They got their supper and a nap, and about midnight we sallied out through a solitude so wholly vacant and complete that it quite overpassed the required conditions. We took possession of the well and its surroundings. My boys were experts in all sorts of things, from the stoning up of a well to the constructing of a mathematical instrument. An hour before sunrise we had that leak mended in ship-shape fashion, and the water began to rise. Then we stowed our fireworks in the chapel, locked up the place, and went home to bed.

text: “Ah, brave soul, now I understand it! The cruel statute seizes the condemned man’s goods and leaves his widow and little ones destitute. They might have racked you to death, but without a verdict or a confession they could not have stripped your wife and child. You stood fast beside them like a man; and you—faithful wife, and woman indeed—would have purchased his freedom from torment at the price of your own slow hunger and death. Well, it brings a body low to reckon what your sex can do in the way of self-sacrifice. I’ll enter you both for my settlement; you’ll be suited to it there. It is a Factory where I mean to turn fumbling, scrambling automata into men.”
source_text: “Oh, heart of gold, now I see it! The bitter law takes the convicted man's estate and beggars his widow and his orphans. They could torture you to death, but without conviction or confession they could not rob your wife and baby. You stood by them like a man; and _you_--true wife and the woman that you are--you would have bought him release from torture at cost to yourself of slow starvation and death--well, it humbles a body to think what your sex can do when it comes to self-sacrifice. I'll book you both for my colony; you'll like it there; it's a Factory where I'm going to turn groping and grubbing automata into _men_.”

text: You see, I understood my mistake at once. I had started her going; it was my own doing. It would take her thirty days to work her way down to those facts. And she usually began without any preface and ended without any result. If you interrupted her, she would either keep right on without seeming to notice, or else answer in a couple of words, then go back and repeat the sentence from the beginning. So interruptions only made things worse; and yet I had to interrupt, and pretty often too, if I meant to save my life. A person would die if he let her monotony keep dripping over him all day long.
source_text: You see, I recognized my mistake at once. I had set her works a-going; it was my own fault; she would be thirty days getting down to those facts. And she generally began without a preface and finished without a result. If you interrupted her she would either go right along without noticing, or answer with a couple of words, and go back and say the sentence over again. So, interruptions only did harm; and yet I had to interrupt, and interrupt pretty frequently, too, in order to save my life; a person would die if he let her monotony drip on him right along all day.

text: Oh, what a dear face rose before me! The boy was eager to learn everything about this tender matter. I bound him to secrecy, and then I whispered her name—“Puss Flanagan.” He seemed disappointed and said he did not remember the countess. How natural it was for the little courtier to bestow a rank on her. He asked me where she lived.
source_text: My, the dear face that rose before me! The boy was eager to know all about this tender matter. I swore him to secrecy and then whispered her name--“Puss Flanagan.” He looked disappointed, and said he didn't remember the countess. How natural it was for the little courtier to give her a rank. He asked me where she lived.

text: “Our navy had vanished all at once, and for no reason anyone could discover! Then, just as abruptly and just as strangely, the railways, the telegraph, and the telephone service all broke down; the men deserted in a body, the poles were cut to pieces, and the Church placed an embargo on electric light! I had to act at once—had to be up and doing without a moment’s delay. Your life was safe: no one in these realms but Merlin would dare lay a hand on such a magician as you without ten thousand men at his back. I had only one thing to consider—how to get everything ready in the best possible shape for your arrival. As for myself, I felt secure enough; no one would be eager to interfere with a favorite of yours. So this was what I did. From our different works I chose all the men—boys, I should say—whose loyalty I could answer for under any pressure whatever, and I brought them together in secret and gave them their orders. There are fifty-two of them, none younger than fourteen and none older than seventeen.”
source_text: “Our navy had suddenly and mysteriously disappeared! Also, as suddenly and as mysteriously, the railway and telegraph and telephone service ceased, the men all deserted, poles were cut down, the Church laid a ban upon the electric light! I had to be up and doing--and straight off. Your life was safe--nobody in these kingdoms but Merlin would venture to touch such a magician as you without ten thousand men at his back--I had nothing to think of but how to put preparations in the best trim against your coming. I felt safe myself--nobody would be anxious to touch a pet of yours. So this is what I did. From our various works I selected all the men--boys I mean--whose faithfulness under whatsoever pressure I could swear to, and I called them together secretly and gave them their instructions. There are fifty-two of them; none younger than fourteen, and none above seventeen years old.”

text: The cordial thanks of the Hosannah office are due, from editor down to devil, to the ever courteous and thoughtful Lord High Steward of the Palace’s Third Assistant Vat for several sauces of ice cream, a quality calculated to make the eyes of the recipients humid with gratitude; and it did. When this administration wants to chalk up a desirable name for early promotion, the Hosannah would like a chance to suggest.

text: At Cardolan they were forever holding splendid jousts; and a very stirring and picturesque and absurd species of human bull-baiting they were too, though to a practical man they grew a trifle tiresome. Still, I was commonly present—for two reasons. A man must not stand apart from the matters his friends and his country hold dear if he expects to be liked, especially when he is in politics; and both as man of affairs and as statesman I wanted to study the joust and see whether I could not contrive some improvement in it. That reminds me, by the way, to mention that the very first public act of my reign—and on the very first day of it, too—was to establish a patent bureau; for I knew a kingdom without a patent bureau and sound patent laws was only a crab, and could move in no direction except sideways or backward.
source_text: They were always having grand tournaments there at Camelot; and very stirring and picturesque and ridiculous human bull-fights they were, too, but just a little wearisome to the practical mind. However, I was generally on hand--for two reasons: a man must not hold himself aloof from the things which his friends and his community have at heart if he would be liked--especially as a statesman; and both as business man and statesman I wanted to study the tournament and see if I couldn't invent an improvement on it. That reminds me to remark, in passing, that the very first official thing I did, in my administration--and it was on the very first day of it, too--was to start a patent office; for I knew that a country without a patent office and good patent laws was just a crab, and couldn't travel any way but sideways or backways.

text: “Alas, sir, they are not of that kind! Sir Launcelot will do battle with dragons, and stand his ground against them, and attack them again and yet again, and still again, until he has conquered and destroyed them; and so too will Sir Pellinore and Sir Aglovale and Sir Carados, and perhaps others besides, but there are none else who will dare it, no matter what idle men may say. And indeed, as for those low ruffians yonder, do you think they have not had enough already, but still crave more?”
source_text: “Lack-a-day, sir, they be not of that breed! Sir Launcelot will give battle to dragons, and will abide by them, and will assail them again, and yet again, and still again, until he do conquer and destroy them; and so likewise will Sir Pellinore and Sir Aglovale and Sir Carados, and mayhap others, but there be none else that will venture it, let the idle say what the idle will. And, la, as to yonder base rufflers, think ye they have not their fill, but yet desire more?”

text: “No, it isn’t your head, Sandy. Your head’s all right, so far as it goes, but you don’t understand business; that’s where the trouble lies. It puts you out of court for arguing about business, and you’re wrong to keep on trying. Still, setting that aside, it was a good haul after all, and it will bring forth a handsome crop of reputation in Arthur’s court. And speaking of the cowboys, what a strange country this is for women and men who never grow old. There’s Morgan le Fay, fresh and young as a Vassar pullet to all appearances, and here is this old duke of the South Marches still hacking away with sword and lance at his age, after bringing up such a family as he has. As I hear it, Sir Gawaine killed seven of his sons, and he still had six left for Sir Marhaus and me to take into camp. And then there was that damsel of sixty winters still tripping about in her frosty bloom—how old are you, Sandy?”
source_text: “No, it's not your head, Sandy. Your head's all right, as far as it goes, but you don't know business; that's where the trouble is. It unfits you to argue about business, and you're wrong to be always trying. However, that aside, it was a good haul, anyway, and will breed a handsome crop of reputation in Arthur's court. And speaking of the cowboys, what a curious country this is for women and men that never get old. Now there's Morgan le Fay, as fresh and young as a Vassar pullet, to all appearances, and here is this old duke of the South Marches still slashing away with sword and lance at his time of life, after raising such a family as he has raised. As I understand it, Sir Gawaine killed seven of his sons, and still he had six left for Sir Marhaus and me to take into camp. And then there was that damsel of sixty winter of age still excursioning around in her frosty bloom--How old are you, Sandy?”

text: “Boys, there are a good many curious things about law, and custom, and usage, and all that sort of business, when you come to study them; yes, and about the drift and progress of human opinion and action, too. There are written laws—they die; but there are also unwritten laws—they endure forever. Take the unwritten law of wages: it declares that wages must rise, little by little, through the centuries. And see how it operates. We know what wages are now, here and there, everywhere; we strike an average and call that the wages of today. We know what wages were a hundred years ago, and what they were two hundred years ago; that is as far back as we can go, but it is enough to give us the law of progress, the rate and measure of the recurring increase; and so, without a single document to guide us, we can get pretty close to knowing what wages were three hundred, four hundred, and five hundred years ago. Very well, so far. Do we stop there? No. We turn from the past and apply the law to the future. My friends, I can tell you what men’s wages are going to be at any date in the future you care to name, for hundreds and hundreds of years.”
source_text: “Boys, there's a good many curious things about law, and custom, and usage, and all that sort of thing, when you come to look at it; yes, and about the drift and progress of human opinion and movement, too. There are written laws--they perish; but there are also unwritten laws--_they_ are eternal. Take the unwritten law of wages: it says they've got to advance, little by little, straight through the centuries. And notice how it works. We know what wages are now, here and there and yonder; we strike an average, and say that's the wages of to-day. We know what the wages were a hundred years ago, and what they were two hundred years ago; that's as far back as we can get, but it suffices to give us the law of progress, the measure and rate of the periodical augmentation; and so, without a document to help us, we can come pretty close to determining what the wages were three and four and five hundred years ago. Good, so far. Do we stop there? No. We stop looking backward; we face around and apply the law to the future. My friends, I can tell you what people's wages are going to be at any date in the future you want to know, for hundreds and hundreds of years.”

text: I made a note of that prison’s address for future use, then wandered away. At the first second-hand clothes shop I came across, in a side street, I bought a shabby outfit fit for an ordinary sailor about to set out on a cold voyage, and wrapped my face in a generous bandage, claiming I had a toothache. That covered my worst bruises. It was quite a change. I no longer looked anything like myself. Then I set off in search of that wire, found it, and traced it to its lair. It was a small room above a butcher’s shop, which suggested business in the telegraph line was not exactly booming. The young man in charge was dozing at his desk. I locked the door and tucked the huge key into my breast. This startled the young fellow, and he was about to cry out; but I said:
source_text: I took the address of that prison for future reference and then sauntered off. At the first second-hand clothing shop I came to, up a back street, I got a rough rig suitable for a common seaman who might be going on a cold voyage, and bound up my face with a liberal bandage, saying I had a toothache. This concealed my worst bruises. It was a transformation. I no longer resembled my former self. Then I struck out for that wire, found it and followed it to its den. It was a little room over a butcher's shop--which meant that business wasn't very brisk in the telegraphic line. The young chap in charge was drowsing at his table. I locked the door and put the vast key in my bosom. This alarmed the young fellow, and he was going to make a noise; but I said:

text: “Bondmen!” The word had taken on a dreadful new meaning, and how unspeakably dreadful it was! The king flung up his fetters and dashed them downward with murderous violence; but my lord had stepped aside before the blow came. A score of the villain’s attendants rushed in, and before a breath had passed we were powerless, our hands pinioned behind us. We protested so loudly and so fervently that we were free men that we won the eager notice of that liberty-preaching speaker and his patriotic throng; they closed about us and took on a look of grim resolve. The orator said:
source_text: _Slaves!_ The word had a new sound--and how unspeakably awful! The king lifted his manacles and brought them down with a deadly force; but my lord was out of the way when they arrived. A dozen of the rascal's servants sprang forward, and in a moment we were helpless, with our hands bound behind us. We so loudly and so earnestly proclaimed ourselves freemen, that we got the interested attention of that liberty-mouthing orator and his patriotic crowd, and they gathered about us and assumed a very determined attitude. The orator said:

text: That was some comfort. She was not dead yet by a long way. I sent at once for the sulphur to be made ready, and I dragged out the croup-kettle myself; for I do not fold my hands and wait upon physicians when Jamie or the baby falls ill. I knew how to tend them both, having had experience enough. The little creature had spent a good share of its brief life in my arms, and more than once I could allay its miseries and coax a laugh from it through the wet of tears on its lashes when even its mother failed.
source_text: That was a comfort. She was far from dead yet. I sent for preparations of sulphur, I rousted out the croup-kettle myself; for I don't sit down and wait for doctors when Sandy or the child is sick. I knew how to nurse both of them, and had had experience. This little chap had lived in my arms a good part of its small life, and often I could soothe away its troubles and get it to laugh through the tear-dews on its eye-lashes when even its mother couldn't.

text: “Do not be sad, fair knight, for this is no defeat. You and I have intelligence, and for those who possess intelligence there are no defeats, only victories. See how we shall turn this apparent disaster into publicity—publicity for our soap, and the greatest such publicity ever devised, one that will attract attention beyond anything yet imagined; publicity that will convert that Mount Washington defeat into a Matterhorn triumph. We shall place on your bulletin board, ‘_Patronized by the elect_.’ How do you like that?”
source_text: “Forbear to grieve, fair knight, for this is not a defeat. We have brains, you and I; and for such as have brains there are no defeats, but only victories. Observe how we will turn this seeming disaster into an advertisement; an advertisement for our soap; and the biggest one, to draw, that was ever thought of; an advertisement that will transform that Mount Washington defeat into a Matterhorn victory. We will put on your bulletin-board, '_Patronized by the elect_.' How does that strike you?”

text: Now and then we had an adventure. One night we were caught in a snowstorm while still a mile from the village toward which we were heading. In an instant we were shut in as though by a fog, so thick was the wind-driven snow. You could see nothing, and before long we were lost. The slave-driver flogged us in desperation, for he saw ruin ahead of him; but his blows only made things worse, driving us farther from the road and farther from any chance of help.
