At last we were forced to stop and sink down in the snow where we stood. The storm lasted until near midnight, then finally died away. By then two of our weaker men and three of our women were dead, and others were too exhausted to move and close to death. Our master was almost out of his mind. He roused the survivors and made us stand, jump, and strike ourselves to bring back our circulation, helping as best he could with his whip.
source_text: Now and then we had an adventure. One night we were overtaken by a snow-storm while still a mile from the village we were making for. Almost instantly we were shut up as in a fog, the driving snow was so thick. You couldn't see a thing, and we were soon lost. The slave-driver lashed us desperately, for he saw ruin before him, but his lashings only made matters worse, for they drove us further from the road and from likelihood of succor. So we had to stop at last and slump down in the snow where we were. The storm continued until toward midnight, then ceased. By this time two of our feebler men and three of our women were dead, and others past moving and threatened with death. Our master was nearly beside himself. He stirred up the living, and made us stand, jump, slap ourselves, to restore our circulation, and he helped as well as he could with his whip.

text: It was fortunate for us that no one happened along just then. I got him to fling the dirk away, and it was as easy as persuading a child to abandon some bright, fresh new way of destroying itself. We went on in silence, each lost in thought. At last the king said:
source_text: It was a lucky thing for us that nobody came along just then. I persuaded him to throw the dirk away; and it was as easy as persuading a child to give up some bright fresh new way of killing itself. We walked along, silent and thinking. Finally the king said:

text: We got the hogs home just about dark—most of them. Princess Nerovens de Morganore was missing, along with two of her ladies-in-waiting: Miss Angela Bohun and the Demoiselle Elaine Courtemains, the first a young black sow with a white star in her forehead, the second a brown one with thin legs and a slight limp in the forward shank on the starboard side—a pair of the most aggravating brutes I ever saw to drive. Also missing were several lesser baronesses—and I wanted them to remain missing; but no, all that sausage meat had to be recovered, so servants were sent out with torches to scour the woods and hills for them.
source_text: We got the hogs home just at dark--most of them. The princess Nerovens de Morganore was missing, and two of her ladies in waiting: namely, Miss Angela Bohun, and the Demoiselle Elaine Courtemains, the former of these two being a young black sow with a white star in her forehead, and the latter a brown one with thin legs and a slight limp in the forward shank on the starboard side--a couple of the tryingest blisters to drive that I ever saw. Also among the missing were several mere baronesses--and I wanted them to stay missing; but no, all that sausage-meat had to be found; so servants were sent out with torches to scour the woods and hills to that end.

text: Most of King Arthur’s British people were slaves, plain and simple, and were known as such, with iron collars about their necks. The rest were slaves in everything but name; they fancied themselves men and free citizens, and called themselves that. In truth, the nation as a whole existed for one purpose alone: to bow down before king, Church, and nobles; to labor for them, to sweat blood for them, to go hungry so they might be fed, to toil so they might idle, to drain misery to the last drop so they might be happy, to go naked so they might wear silks and jewels, to pay taxes so they might be spared them, to spend their lives in the humiliating language and gestures of flattery so they might strut in pride and imagine themselves the gods of this earth. And for all this, the reward they received was blows and scorn; yet they were so spiritless that they accepted even such treatment as an honor.
source_text: The most of King Arthur's British nation were slaves, pure and simple, and bore that name, and wore the iron collar on their necks; and the rest were slaves in fact, but without the name; they imagined themselves men and freemen, and called themselves so. The truth was, the nation as a body was in the world for one object, and one only: to grovel before king and Church and noble; to slave for them, sweat blood for them, starve that they might be fed, work that they might play, drink misery to the dregs that they might be happy, go naked that they might wear silks and jewels, pay taxes that they might be spared from paying them, be familiar all their lives with the degrading language and postures of adulation that they might walk in pride and think themselves the gods of this world. And for all this, the thanks they got were cuffs and contempt; and so poor-spirited were they that they took even this sort of attention as an honor.

text: Mostly they were drinking—from whole ox horns; though a few were still chewing bread or gnawing beef bones. There were about two dogs to every man; and these sat in eager expectation until a worn-down bone was thrown to them, when they rushed at it in brigades and divisions, and a struggle followed that turned the whole scene into a tumult of plunging heads and bodies and flashing tails, while the storm of howls and barks for the moment drowned every voice. But that mattered little, for the dog-fight was always the greater diversion anyway; now and then the men rose to watch it better and place their bets, and the ladies and the musicians leaned out over their balusters for the same purpose; and all of them from time to time broke into delighted exclamations. At last the victorious dog lay down at ease with his bone between his paws and began to growl over it, gnaw it, and grease the floor with it, just as fifty others were already doing; and the rest of the court returned to its former trades and amusements.
source_text: Mainly they were drinking--from entire ox horns; but a few were still munching bread or gnawing beef bones. There was about an average of two dogs to one man; and these sat in expectant attitudes till a spent bone was flung to them, and then they went for it by brigades and divisions, with a rush, and there ensued a fight which filled the prospect with a tumultuous chaos of plunging heads and bodies and flashing tails, and the storm of howlings and barkings deafened all speech for the time; but that was no matter, for the dog-fight was always a bigger interest anyway; the men rose, sometimes, to observe it the better and bet on it, and the ladies and the musicians stretched themselves out over their balusters with the same object; and all broke into delighted ejaculations from time to time. In the end, the winning dog stretched himself out comfortably with his bone between his paws, and proceeded to growl over it, and gnaw it, and grease the floor with it, just as fifty others were already doing; and the rest of the court resumed their previous industries and entertainments.

text: “Be it so, since such is your will.” Then Sir Gawaine and Sir Uwaine went forward and saluted them, and asked why they had shown such scorn to the shield. “Sirs,” answered the maidens, “we shall tell you. There is a knight in this country who bears that white shield, and he is a man of great deeds, but he bears hatred toward all ladies and gentlewomen; therefore we do all this despite to the shield.” “Truly,” said Sir Gawaine, “it ill becomes a good knight to despise all ladies and gentlewomen. And perhaps, though he hates you, he has some cause; and perhaps he loves in other places some ladies and gentlewomen, and would be loved in return, seeing that he is such a man of prowess as you say—”
source_text: “Even so be it, sith ye are so minded. Then Sir Gawaine and Sir Uwaine went and saluted them, and asked them why they did that despite to the shield. Sirs, said the damsels, we shall tell you. There is a knight in this country that owneth this white shield, and he is a passing good man of his hands, but he hateth all ladies and gentlewomen, and therefore we do all this despite to the shield. I will say you, said Sir Gawaine, it beseemeth evil a good knight to despise all ladies and gentlewomen, and peradventure though he hate you he hath some cause, and peradventure he loveth in some other places ladies and gentlewomen, and to be loved again, and he such a man of prowess as ye speak of--”

text: “Of course. I was uneasy, and full of doubt. When the captain put to sea again, I contrived to send a watcher with him. Since then I have heard nothing of either ship or spy. I allowed myself two weeks in which to receive word from you. After that, I meant to dispatch a vessel to Cadiz. There was, however, a reason why I did not.”
source_text: “Naturally. I was troubled and suspicious. When the commander sailed again I managed to ship a spy with him. I have never heard of vessel or spy since. I gave myself two weeks to hear from you in. Then I resolved to send a ship to Cadiz. There was a reason why I didn't.”

text: There wasn’t even a bell or speaking tube in the castle. I had a great many servants, and those on duty lounged in the anteroom; if I wanted one of them, I had to go and call him myself. There was no gas, no candles; the only thing that passed for light was a bronze dish, half filled with boarding-house butter, with a blazing rag floating in it. Several of these were hung along the walls, dimming the darkness, just softening it enough to make it miserable. If you went out at night, your servants carried torches. There were no books, no pens, no paper or ink, and no glass in the openings they imagined to be windows. Glass is a small thing—until it is missing, and then it becomes a very large one. But perhaps the worst of all was that there was no sugar, coffee, tea, or tobacco. I could see that I was only another Robinson Crusoe, shipwrecked on an uninhabited island, with no company except a few more or less tame animals; and if I wanted life to be endurable, I had to do as he did—invent, contrive, create, reorganize; put brain and hand to work, and keep them occupied. Well, that was my sort of thing.
source_text: There wasn't even a bell or a speaking-tube in the castle. I had a great many servants, and those that were on duty lolled in the anteroom; and when I wanted one of them I had to go and call for him. There was no gas, there were no candles; a bronze dish half full of boarding-house butter with a blazing rag floating in it was the thing that produced what was regarded as light. A lot of these hung along the walls and modified the dark, just toned it down enough to make it dismal. If you went out at night, your servants carried torches. There were no books, pens, paper or ink, and no glass in the openings they believed to be windows. It is a little thing--glass is--until it is absent, then it becomes a big thing. But perhaps the worst of all was, that there wasn't any sugar, coffee, tea, or tobacco. I saw that I was just another Robinson Crusoe cast away on an uninhabited island, with no society but some more or less tame animals, and if I wanted to make life bearable I must do as he did--invent, contrive, create, reorganize things; set brain and hand to work, and keep them busy. Well, that was in my line.

text: “Well, very well, let it pass, since you outvote me. Yet there is one thing that is plainly unjust. The magistrate sets a laborer’s pay at, say, a penny a day. The statute declares that if any employer dares, even in the fiercest rush of work, to give a man anything above that penny for a single day, he must be both fined and set in the stocks for it; and anyone who knows of it and fails to report the matter is to be fined and set in the stocks as well. Now it strikes me as unfair, Master Elwood, and a grave danger to us all, that because you so carelessly admitted, a little while ago, that in the course of a week you have paid a penny and fifteen mil—”
source_text: “Well, all right, let it go, since you vote me down. But there's one thing which certainly isn't fair. The magistrate fixes a mechanic's wage at one cent a day, for instance. The law says that if any master shall venture, even under utmost press of business, to pay anything _over_ that cent a day, even for a single day, he shall be both fined and pilloried for it; and whoever knows he did it and doesn't inform, they also shall be fined and pilloried. Now it seems to me unfair, Dowley, and a deadly peril to all of us, that because you thoughtlessly confessed, a while ago, that within a week you have paid a cent and fifteen mil--”

text: He did his best, honestly enough, but, lord, it was nothing to boast of. He looked as humble as the Leaning Tower of Pisa. That is the utmost one could say for it. In truth, it was such a wretched failure that it drew astonished frowns all along the line, and a splendid flunkey at the rear of it lifted his whip; but I sprang in time and was beneath it when it came down, and, screened by the burst of coarse laughter that followed, I spoke up sharply and warned the king not to heed it. He held himself in for the moment, but it was a hard strain; he was itching to devour the procession. I said:
source_text: He did his honest best, but lord, it was no great things. He looked as humble as the leaning tower at Pisa. It is the most you could say of it. Indeed, it was such a thundering poor success that it raised wondering scowls all along the line, and a gorgeous flunkey at the tail end of it raised his whip; but I jumped in time and was under it when it fell; and under cover of the volley of coarse laughter which followed, I spoke up sharply and warned the king to take no notice. He mastered himself for the moment, but it was a sore tax; he wanted to eat up the procession. I said:

text: What a silky, smooth hellion she was, and how calm and serene, even while the cords all down my legs throbbed in sympathy with that man’s pain. Led by armored guards carrying blazing torches, we marched through echoing corridors and down stone stairways, damp and dripping, reeking of mold and the long confinement of night—a cold, uncanny journey, and a lengthy one at that, made neither shorter nor cheerier by the sorceress’s talk, which concerned this man and the crime laid against him. He had been denounced by an anonymous informer for having killed a stag in the royal preserves. I said:
source_text: What a silky smooth hellion she was; and so composed and serene, when the cords all down my legs were hurting in sympathy with that man's pain. Conducted by mailed guards bearing flaring torches, we tramped along echoing corridors, and down stone stairways dank and dripping, and smelling of mould and ages of imprisoned night --a chill, uncanny journey and a long one, and not made the shorter or the cheerier by the sorceress's talk, which was about this sufferer and his crime. He had been accused by an anonymous informer, of having killed a stag in the royal preserves. I said:

text: The king flinched beneath this unintended home thrust, but he kept himself motionless; he was learning his role, and he played it well enough, too, for so dull a beginner. I set about creating a diversion. I offered the woman food and drink, but she refused both. She would permit nothing to come between herself and the release of death. Then I slipped away and brought down the dead child from above, and laid it beside her. This undid her once more, and there was another scene full of heartbreak. After a time I contrived another diversion, and lured her into sketching out her story.
source_text: The king winced under this accidental home-shot, but kept still; he was learning his part; and he was playing it well, too, for a pretty dull beginner. I struck up a diversion. I offered the woman food and liquor, but she refused both. She would allow nothing to come between her and the release of death. Then I slipped away and brought the dead child from aloft, and laid it by her. This broke her down again, and there was another scene that was full of heartbreak. By and by I made another diversion, and beguiled her to sketch her story.

text: Hang him, I had supposed he was in earnest, and was beginning to let him persuade me, when he burst out with that cat-howl and nearly startled me out of my skin. But he could never be in earnest. He had no notion what earnestness was. He had sketched out a clear, entirely reasonable, and practicable improvement on constitutional monarchy, yet he was too flighty to know it, or to care a rap about it. I was just about to administer a scolding to him, when Sandy came flying in at that instant, wild with terror and so broken by sobbing that for a moment she could not find her voice. I ran to her, gathered her into my arms, showered her with caresses, and said, imploringly:
source_text: Hang him, I supposed he was in earnest, and was beginning to be persuaded by him, until he exploded that cat-howl and startled me almost out of my clothes. But he never could be in earnest. He didn't know what it was. He had pictured a distinct and perfectly rational and feasible improvement upon constitutional monarchy, but he was too feather-headed to know it, or care anything about it, either. I was going to give him a scolding, but Sandy came flying in at that moment, wild with terror, and so choked with sobs that for a minute she could not get her voice. I ran and took her in my arms, and lavished caresses upon her and said, beseechingly:

text: Since I now stood, in matters of rank and power, as the second person in the realm, a great deal was made of me. I was clad in silks and velvets and cloth of gold, and therefore was very splendid, and very much the opposite of comfortable. Still, I knew that custom would presently make me easy in such garments; that much was plain. I was lodged in the finest suite of chambers in the palace, after the monarch’s own. They blazed with gaudy silk draperies, yet the stone floors held nothing but rushes for a covering, and those, too, were a poor assortment, not all of one kind. As for comforts, in any proper sense, there were none. I mean the small comforts; it is the small comforts that amount to the real ease of living. The great oak chairs, ornamented with rough carving, were passable enough, and there the matter ended. There was no soap, no matches, no mirror—unless one chooses to call a sheet of metal a mirror, which is about as useful as a pail of water. And not a chromo anywhere. I had lived with chromos for years, and now I perceived that, without my suspecting it, a love of art had been woven into the very stuff of me and become part of my nature. It moved me to homesickness to gaze about this proud, brilliant, and utterly cheerless emptiness, and to think that in our house at Newburyport, plain though it was, you could not enter a room without seeing an insurance chromo, or at least a three-color God-Bless-Our-Home over the doorway; and in the sitting room we had nine. Yet here, even in my stately chamber, there was nothing that could properly be called a picture save a thing as large as a bedquilt, either woven or knitted—it had darned spots in it—and nothing in it had the right color or the right shape; and as for proportion, even Botticelli himself could not have mangled them more grievously, despite all his experience on those horrors known as his “famous Hampton Court cartoons.” Botticelli was a wonder. We had several of his chromos; one showed his “Miraculous Draught of Fish,” in which he added a miracle of his own by cramming three men into a canoe that would scarcely have held a dog without capsizing. I used always to delight in studying B.’s art; it was so fresh and original.
source_text: Inasmuch as I was now the second personage in the Kingdom, as far as political power and authority were concerned, much was made of me. My raiment was of silks and velvets and cloth of gold, and by consequence was very showy, also uncomfortable. But habit would soon reconcile me to my clothes; I was aware of that. I was given the choicest suite of apartments in the castle, after the king's. They were aglow with loud-colored silken hangings, but the stone floors had nothing but rushes on them for a carpet, and they were misfit rushes at that, being not all of one breed. As for conveniences, properly speaking, there weren't any. I mean _little_ conveniences; it is the little conveniences that make the real comfort of life. The big oaken chairs, graced with rude carvings, were well enough, but that was the stopping place. There was no soap, no matches, no looking-glass--except a metal one, about as powerful as a pail of water. And not a chromo. I had been used to chromos for years, and I saw now that without my suspecting it a passion for art had got worked into the fabric of my being, and was become a part of me. It made me homesick to look around over this proud and gaudy but heartless barrenness and remember that in our house in East Hartford, all unpretending as it was, you couldn't go into a room but you would find an insurance-chromo, or at least a three-color God-Bless-Our-Home over the door; and in the parlor we had nine. But here, even in my grand room of state, there wasn't anything in the nature of a picture except a thing the size of a bedquilt, which was either woven or knitted (it had darned places in it), and nothing in it was the right color or the right shape; and as for proportions, even Raphael himself couldn't have botched them more formidably, after all his practice on those nightmares they call his “celebrated Hampton Court cartoons.” Raphael was a bird. We had several of his chromos; one was his “Miraculous Draught of Fishes,” where he puts in a miracle of his own--puts three men into a canoe which wouldn't have held a dog without upsetting. I always admired to study R.'s art, it was so fresh and unconventional.

text: We stayed that night with a holy hermit, and my chance came around the middle of the following afternoon. We were cutting across a broad meadow by a shortcut, and I was wandering in thought, hearing nothing, seeing nothing, when Sandy abruptly broke off a remark she had started that morning with the cry:
source_text: We tarried with a holy hermit, that night, and my opportunity came about the middle of the next afternoon. We were crossing a vast meadow by way of short-cut, and I was musing absently, hearing nothing, seeing nothing, when Sandy suddenly interrupted a remark which she had begun that morning, with the cry:

text: By the stroke of twelve, everyone was spent, aching with laughter, and, as a general thing, tipsy—some in tears, some in tenderness, some in uproarious mirth, some in a quarrelsome heat, and some altogether gone, lying lifeless beneath the table. Among the women, the saddest sight was a beautiful young marchioness, for whom this was the eve of her marriage; and, indeed, she was a sight to behold. As she stood there, she might have served in advance as the model for the portrait of the young daughter of the Duc d’Orléans, from that notorious supper after which she was borne away, foul-mouthed, drunken, and helpless, to her bed, in the vanished and mourned-for days of the old order.
source_text: By midnight everybody was fagged out, and sore with laughing; and, as a rule, drunk: some weepingly, some affectionately, some hilariously, some quarrelsomely, some dead and under the table. Of the ladies, the worst spectacle was a lovely young duchess, whose wedding-eve this was; and indeed she was a spectacle, sure enough. Just as she was she could have sat in advance for the portrait of the young daughter of the Regent d'Orleans, at the famous dinner whence she was carried, foul-mouthed, intoxicated, and helpless, to her bed, in the lost and lamented days of the Ancient Regime.

text: Then he came down, barking and coughing, and I followed after him. I hit the ground a moment later; we flew to our assigned places and began to deal and receive with all our strength. The uproar and din were tremendous; it was a tempest of disorder and confusion, with blows falling thick and fast. All at once several horsemen burst into the very heart of the crowd, and a voice cried out:
source_text: Then he descended, barking and coughing, and I followed. I struck the ground an instant after him; we sprang to our appointed places, and began to give and take with all our might. The powwow and racket were prodigious; it was a tempest of riot and confusion and thick-falling blows. Suddenly some horsemen tore into the midst of the crowd, and a voice shouted:

text: It is a world of surprises. The king brooded; that was only natural. What, you may ask, could he have brooded over? Why, over the astonishing fact of his fall, of course—from the highest place in the world to the lowest; from the most splendid station in the world to the most obscure; from the noblest calling among men to the basest. No, I swear that what troubled him most at first was not that, but the price he had brought! He could hardly get past that seven dollars. Well, it shocked me so deeply when I first learned it that I could not believe it; it did not seem natural. But once my mind cleared and I looked at it properly, I saw I was wrong; it was natural. For this reason: a king is only an artifice, and so a king’s emotions, like the motions of an automatic doll, are only artifice too; but as a man, he is real, and his feelings, as a man, are real as well, not shadows. It humiliates the average man to be rated below his own idea of his value, and the king was surely no more than an average man, if he stood that high.
source_text: It's a world of surprises. The king brooded; this was natural. What would he brood about, should you say? Why, about the prodigious nature of his fall, of course--from the loftiest place in the world to the lowest; from the most illustrious station in the world to the obscurest; from the grandest vocation among men to the basest. No, I take my oath that the thing that graveled him most, to start with, was not this, but the price he had fetched! He couldn't seem to get over that seven dollars. Well, it stunned me so, when I first found it out, that I couldn't believe it; it didn't seem natural. But as soon as my mental sight cleared and I got a right focus on it, I saw I was mistaken; it _was_ natural. For this reason: a king is a mere artificiality, and so a king's feelings, like the impulses of an automatic doll, are mere artificialities; but as a man, he is a reality, and his feelings, as a man, are real, not phantoms. It shames the average man to be valued below his own estimate of his worth, and the king certainly wasn't anything more than an average man, if he was up that high.

text: By that doctrine, a citizen who believes he can see that the commonwealth’s political garments are threadbare, and yet keeps silent and does not press for a new set, is disloyal; he is a traitor. That he may be the only one who thinks he sees this decay does not absolve him; it is his duty to agitate regardless, and it is the duty of the others to vote him down if they do not see the question as he does.
source_text: Under that gospel, the citizen who thinks he sees that the commonwealth's political clothes are worn out, and yet holds his peace and does not agitate for a new suit, is disloyal; he is a traitor. That he may be the only one who thinks he sees this decay, does not excuse him; it is his duty to agitate anyway, and it is the duty of the others to vote him down if they do not see the matter as he does.

text: Be it known that the noble lord and renowned knight, Sir Alaric Le Grand, having graciously consented to encounter the King’s minister, Harlan Morgan, surnamed the Master, in requital of an affront long since delivered, the two shall meet in the lists by Camelot at the fourth hour of the morning on the sixteenth day of the coming month. The combat shall be to the uttermost, for the said affront was of a mortal nature and admits of no common terms.
source_text: Know that the great lord and illus- trious Knight, SIR SAGRAMOR LE DESIROUS having condescended to meet the King’s Minister, Hank Mor- gan, the which is surnamed The Boss, for satisfgction of offence anciently given, these wilL engage in the lists by Camelot about the fourth hour of the morning of the sixteenth day of this next succeeding month. The battle wiil be a l outrance, sith the said offence was of a deadly sort, admitting of no commmon Position.

text: Madame, seeing that I was calm and held no grudge, no doubt concluded that I had been taken in by her excuse; for her alarm melted away, and before long she was so persistent in urging me to give a performance and kill someone that it became awkward. Still, to my relief, she was soon cut short by the summons to prayers. I will say this for the nobility: tyrannical, murderous, greedy, and morally corrupt as they were, they were also profoundly and ardently religious. Nothing could keep them from the regular and scrupulous observance of the devotions required by the Church. More than once I had seen a nobleman, having his enemy at his mercy, pause to pray before slitting his throat; more than once I had seen a nobleman, after lying in wait for and killing his enemy, go to the nearest roadside shrine and meekly offer thanks, without even pausing to strip the body. There would be nothing finer or sweeter in the life of even Benvenuto Cellini, that rough-cut saint, ten centuries later. All the nobles of Britain, with their households, attended divine service morning and evening every day in their private chapels, and even the worst among them held family prayers five or six times a day in addition. The Church deserved all the credit for this. Though I was no friend to the Catholic Church, I was forced to acknowledge it. And often, despite myself, I found I was asking, “What would this country be without the Church?”
source_text: Madame, seeing me pacific and unresentful, no doubt judged that I was deceived by her excuse; for her fright dissolved away, and she was soon so importunate to have me give an exhibition and kill somebody, that the thing grew to be embarrassing. However, to my relief she was presently interrupted by the call to prayers. I will say this much for the nobility: that, tyrannical, murderous, rapacious, and morally rotten as they were, they were deeply and enthusiastically religious. Nothing could divert them from the regular and faithful performance of the pieties enjoined by the Church. More than once I had seen a noble who had gotten his enemy at a disadvantage, stop to pray before cutting his throat; more than once I had seen a noble, after ambushing and despatching his enemy, retire to the nearest wayside shrine and humbly give thanks, without even waiting to rob the body. There was to be nothing finer or sweeter in the life of even Benvenuto Cellini, that rough-hewn saint, ten centuries later. All the nobles of Britain, with their families, attended divine service morning and night daily, in their private chapels, and even the worst of them had family worship five or six times a day besides. The credit of this belonged entirely to the Church. Although I was no friend to that Catholic Church, I was obliged to admit this. And often, in spite of me, I found myself saying, “What would this country be without the Church?”

text: Then the court apologized to me and said they hoped I would be sure to tell his lordship that this high-handed business was in no way the court’s fault. I answered that I would set it right, and so took my leave. I did so just in time, too; he was beginning to ask why I had not brought out these facts the instant I was arrested. I told him I would have done so if I had thought of it—which was true—but that I had been so battered by that man that all sense had been knocked out of me, and the like, and so on, and on, and I got myself away, still muttering. I did not stay for breakfast. No grass grew under my feet. I was soon at the slave quarters. Empty—everybody gone! That is, everyone except one body—the slave-master’s. It lay there beaten into a pulp, and all around were the signs of a desperate struggle. At the door stood a rough board coffin on a cart, and workmen, with the police helping them, were clearing a passage through the staring crowd so they could bring it inside.
source_text: Then the court begged my pardon, and hoped I would not fail to tell his lordship it was in no wise the court's fault that this high-handed thing had happened. I said I would make it all right, and so took my leave. Took it just in time, too; he was starting to ask me why I didn't fetch out these facts the moment I was arrested. I said I would if I had thought of it--which was true --but that I was so battered by that man that all my wit was knocked out of me--and so forth and so on, and got myself away, still mumbling. I didn't wait for breakfast. No grass grew under my feet. I was soon at the slave quarters. Empty--everybody gone! That is, everybody except one body--the slave-master's. It lay there all battered to pulp; and all about were the evidences of a terrific fight. There was a rude board coffin on a cart at the door, and workmen, assisted by the police, were thinning a road through the gaping crowd in order that they might bring it in.

text: Why, he kept the vigil with me, hour for hour, through three days and nights, until the little one was safely past her peril; then he lifted her in his mighty arms and kissed her, his plumes drooping about her bright hair, and afterward he set her gently back in Sandy’s lap and went with his proud, measured tread down the long hall, between the lines of staring men-at-arms and servants, and so was lost to sight. And no inward voice gave me warning that I should never behold him again in this world! God help us, what a world of sorrow it is.
source_text: Well, he stood watch-and-watch with me, right straight through, for three days and nights, till the child was out of danger; then he took her up in his great arms and kissed her, with his plumes falling about her golden head, then laid her softly in Sandy's lap again and took his stately way down the vast hall, between the ranks of admiring men-at-arms and menials, and so disappeared. And no instinct warned me that I should never look upon him again in this world! Lord, what a world of heart-break it is.

text: I not only kept watch on this tournament from day to day, but assigned a capable priest from my Department of Public Morals and Agriculture to attend it and report back; for I meant, in due time, once I had advanced the people far enough, to establish a newspaper. The first thing needed in a new country is a patent office; after that you build up the school system; and then, at last, you bring out your paper. A newspaper has its faults, and a good many of them, but that is nothing to the point: it is the voice from the tomb of a dead nation, and you had better not forget it. You cannot bring a dead nation back to life without it; there is no other way. So I wanted to take stock of the matter and discover what kind of reporting talent I might be able to scrape together out of the sixth century when the time came that I needed it.
source_text: I not only watched this tournament from day to day, but detailed an intelligent priest from my Department of Public Morals and Agriculture, and ordered him to report it; for it was my purpose by and by, when I should have gotten the people along far enough, to start a newspaper. The first thing you want in a new country, is a patent office; then work up your school system; and after that, out with your paper. A newspaper has its faults, and plenty of them, but no matter, it's hark from the tomb for a dead nation, and don't you forget it. You can't resurrect a dead nation without it; there isn't any way. So I wanted to sample things, and be finding out what sort of reporter-material I might be able to rake together out of the sixth century when I should come to need it.

text: Why, her eyes were as grateful as an animal’s when you show it a kindness it can understand. The baby was out of her arms in a minute, and she had her cheek against the man’s, her hands caressing his hair, while her happy tears ran down. The man stirred back to life and fondled his wife with his eyes, which was all he was able to do. I reckoned I might clear the den now, and I did; cleared it of all but the family and myself. Then I said:
source_text: Why, her eyes were as grateful as an animal's, when you do it a kindness that it understands. The baby was out of her way and she had her cheek against the man's in a minute and her hands fondling his hair, and her happy tears running down. The man revived and caressed his wife with his eyes, which was all he could do. I judged I might clear the den, now, and I did; cleared it of all but the family and myself. Then I said:

text: “A month! The bare thought of it makes me tremble. Have it as you wish, my son. Yet my heart is burdened with this disappointment. Leave me, and let me spend my spirit in weariness and waiting, as I have done these ten long days, thus pretending at the thing called rest, the body laid down and showing outward signs of repose where inwardly there is none.”
source_text: “A month! The mere thought of it maketh me to shudder. Have it thy way, my son. But my heart is heavy with this disappointment. Leave me, and let me wear my spirit with weariness and waiting, even as I have done these ten long days, counterfeiting thus the thing that is called rest, the prone body making outward sign of repose where inwardly is none.”

text: “Well, it was an accident. They were only spectators; they bore no arms, and had come merely to witness the queen’s chastisement. Sir Alaric struck down all who chanced to cross the path of his ungoverned rage, and these he slew without so much as knowing them for who they were. Here is a snap-shot one of our lads took of the fray; it is for sale at every news-stand. There—those nearest the queen are Sir Alaric with his blade raised, and Sir Percival drawing his last breath. You can catch the anguish in the queen’s face through the wreathing smoke. It makes a stirring battle-piece.”
source_text: “Well, it was an accident. They were simply onlookers; they were unarmed, and were merely there to witness the queen's punishment. Sir Launcelot smote down whoever came in the way of his blind fury, and he killed these without noticing who they were. Here is an instantaneous photograph one of our boys got of the battle; it's for sale on every news-stand. There--the figures nearest the queen are Sir Launcelot with his sword up, and Sir Gareth gasping his latest breath. You can catch the agony in the queen's face through the curling smoke. It's a rattling battle-picture.”

text: There was more money in the trade than anyone quite knew how to spend. As it grew, I put out a line of merchandise fit for kings, and a smart affair for duchesses and the like, with frills about the forehatch and the running gear gathered to leeward with a feather-stitch, then hauled aft by a backstay and triced up with a half-turn in the standing rigging abaft the weather gaskets. Yes, it was a beauty.
source_text: There was more money in the business than one knew what to do with. As it extended, I brought out a line of goods suitable for kings, and a nobby thing for duchesses and that sort, with ruffles down the forehatch and the running-gear clewed up with a featherstitch to leeward and then hauled aft with a back-stay and triced up with a half-turn in the standing rigging forward of the weather-gaskets. Yes, it was a daisy.

text: “It is no easy marvel to bring water back to a parched well, yet we shall attempt it if my brother Ambrose comes to grief. Brother Ambrose is a very tolerable conjurer, but only in the drawing-room branch of the craft, and he may not prevail; indeed, he is not much to be counted on. Still, that need not count against his reputation; any man who can work _this_ sort of miracle knows enough to run an inn.”
source_text: “It is a difficult miracle to restore water in a dry well, but we will try, if my brother Merlin fails. Brother Merlin is a very passable artist, but only in the parlor-magic line, and he may not succeed; in fact, is not likely to succeed. But that should be nothing to his discredit; the man that can do _this_ kind of miracle knows enough to keep hotel.”

text: “That’s taken care of. It’s the prettiest garden ever planted. It’s a belt forty feet wide, running all the way around the outer fence—there’s a hundred yards between it and the fence, a sort of neutral ground. There isn’t a single square yard in that whole strip that isn’t fitted with a torpedo. We laid them right on the surface and scattered a layer of sand over them. It looks perfectly harmless, but let a man begin hoeing it once, and you’ll see.”
source_text: “That's attended to. It's the prettiest garden that was ever planted. It's a belt forty feet wide, and goes around the outer fence--distance between it and the fence one hundred yards--kind of neutral ground that space is. There isn't a single square yard of that whole belt but is equipped with a torpedo. We laid them on the surface of the ground, and sprinkled a layer of sand over them. It's an innocent looking garden, but you let a man start in to hoe it once, and you'll see.”

text: “Thou!” The boy drew back a pace and caught his breath, for the thing struck him with some abruptness; yet the look he assumed was exceedingly, exceedingly deferential. I marked that at once; it showed me that in this asylum a charlatan need not even carry a reputation—people were prepared to accept him on his mere word, and no more. I went on.
source_text: “Thou!” The boy recoiled a step, and caught his breath, for the thing hit him rather sudden; but the aspect which he took on was very, very respectful. I took quick note of that; it indicated that a humbug didn't need to have a reputation in this asylum; people stood ready to take him at his word, without that. I resumed.

text: We couldn’t seem to run into anyone in this lonely Britain, not even an ogre; and in the frame of mind I was in then, that was just as well for the ogre—that is, an ogre with a handkerchief. Most knights would have cared about nothing except taking his armor, but as long as I got his bandanna, he could keep his hardware for all I cared.
source_text: We couldn't seem to meet anybody in this lonesome Britain, not even an ogre; and, in the mood I was in then, it was well for the ogre; that is, an ogre with a handkerchief. Most knights would have thought of nothing but getting his armor; but so I got his bandanna, he could keep his hardware, for all of me.

text: “From that very day onward, the fame of the miracle spread through all lands. Monks came from every country to join themselves there; they came as fishes come, in great shoals. The monastery added building upon building, and still more beside these, so that it spread its arms far and wide and drew them in. And nuns came also, and more again, and yet more; and on the farther side of the vale, opposite the monastery, they built from one structure to another until the nunnery grew mighty indeed. And these were kindly toward those, and they joined their loving labors together, and together they raised a fair great foundling asylum midway down the valley between them.”
source_text: “Even from that very day. The fame of the miracle went abroad into all lands. From every land came monks to join; they came even as the fishes come, in shoals; and the monastery added building to building, and yet others to these, and so spread wide its arms and took them in. And nuns came, also; and more again, and yet more; and built over against the monastery on the yon side of the vale, and added building to building, until mighty was that nunnery. And these were friendly unto those, and they joined their loving labors together, and together they built a fair great foundling asylum midway of the valley between.”

text: “Lose no time, my son, but hasten to the work that may yet preserve us. If we do not return the water swiftly, all is lost, and the labor of two hundred years will come to an end. See that you accomplish it only by blessed charms, for the Church will not suffer her cause to be aided by the devil’s arts.”
source_text: “Delay not, son, but get to thy saving work. An we bring not the water back again, and soon, we are ruined, and the good work of two hundred years must end. And see thou do it with enchantments that be holy, for the Church will not endure that work in her cause be done by devil's magic.”

text: There is no use in prolonging the details. The earl lodged us and then sold us at auction. This same infernal law had existed in our own South in my own day, more than thirteen hundred years later; and under it hundreds of free men who could not prove their freedom had been sold into lifelong slavery, without the affair making any special impression on me. But the instant that the law and the auction block entered my own experience, a thing that had seemed merely improper before became suddenly hellish. Well, that is the way we are made.
source_text: There is no use in stringing out the details. The earl put us up and sold us at auction. This same infernal law had existed in our own South in my own time, more than thirteen hundred years later, and under it hundreds of freemen who could not prove that they were freemen had been sold into lifelong slavery without the circumstance making any particular impression upon me; but the minute law and the auction block came into my personal experience, a thing which had been merely improper before became suddenly hellish. Well, that's the way we are made.

text: Bess and I were on the road again, the next morning, bright and early. It was a rare joy to draw a full breath and drink in great luscious barrelfuls of the blessed Lord’s pure, dew-wrought, woodsy air once more, after smothering body and spirit for two days and nights in the moral and bodily reek of that insufferable old buzzard’s nest! I mean for me, of course: the place was quite proper and pleasant enough for Bess, since she had been accustomed to high life all her days.
source_text: Sandy and I were on the road again, next morning, bright and early. It was so good to open up one's lungs and take in whole luscious barrels-ful of the blessed God's untainted, dew-fashioned, woodland-scented air once more, after suffocating body and mind for two days and nights in the moral and physical stenches of that intolerable old buzzard-roost! I mean, for me: of course the place was all right and agreeable enough for Sandy, for she had been used to high life all her days.

text: I could see that she was a capable housekeeper, for even as she talked she kept one eye on the servants, making sure they did not fumble in handling the body and carrying it out; when they brought fresh, clean towels, she sent them back for the others; and when they had finished wiping up the floor and were about to leave, she pointed out a crimson speck no larger than a tear, which their duller eyes had missed. It was plain to me that La Cote Male Taile had not recognized the mistress of the house. How often does mute circumstantial evidence speak more loudly and clearly than any words.
source_text: I saw that she was a good housekeeper, for while she talked she kept a corner of her eye on the servants to see that they made no balks in handling the body and getting it out; when they came with fresh clean towels, she sent back for the other kind; and when they had finished wiping the floor and were going, she indicated a crimson fleck the size of a tear which their duller eyes had overlooked. It was plain to me that La Cote Male Taile had failed to see the mistress of the house. Often, how louder and clearer than any tongue, does dumb circumstantial evidence speak.

text: I had begun by setting up a teacher-factory and a good many Sunday-schools; as a result, I now had a splendid system of graded schools running at full speed in those places, along with a full range of Protestant congregations, all flourishing and steadily increasing. Anyone was free to be whatever kind of Christian he chose; there was complete liberty in that regard. But I limited public religious instruction to the churches and the Sunday-schools, allowing none of it in my other schools. I could easily have favored my own denomination and made everybody a Presbyterian without any difficulty, but that would have violated a law of human nature: spiritual needs and instincts differ among people just as much as physical appetites, complexions, and features do, and a man is at his moral best only when he is fitted with the religious garment whose color, shape, and size suit as closely as possible the spiritual complexion, odd angles, and stature of the person wearing it; besides, I was wary of a united Church. It is a tremendous power, the greatest imaginable, and once it falls into selfish hands, as it inevitably does, it spells the death of human liberty and the crippling of human thought.
source_text: I had started a teacher-factory and a lot of Sunday-schools the first thing; as a result, I now had an admirable system of graded schools in full blast in those places, and also a complete variety of Protestant congregations all in a prosperous and growing condition. Everybody could be any kind of a Christian he wanted to; there was perfect freedom in that matter. But I confined public religious teaching to the churches and the Sunday-schools, permitting nothing of it in my other educational buildings. I could have given my own sect the preference and made everybody a Presbyterian without any trouble, but that would have been to affront a law of human nature: spiritual wants and instincts are as various in the human family as are physical appetites, complexions, and features, and a man is only at his best, morally, when he is equipped with the religious garment whose color and shape and size most nicely accommodate themselves to the spiritual complexion, angularities, and stature of the individual who wears it; and, besides, I was afraid of a united Church; it makes a mighty power, the mightiest conceivable, and then when it by and by gets into selfish hands, as it is always bound to do, it means death to human liberty and paralysis to human thought.

text: When I came to myself again, I was seated on the grass beneath an oak tree, with a whole broad, lovely stretch of country spread out before me—almost to myself. Not quite, for there was a man on horseback looking down at me, a man who seemed to have stepped straight out of a storybook. He was clad from head to foot in ancient iron armor, with a helmet upon his head shaped like a nail keg and slit for sight; he carried a shield, a sword, and a monstrous spear, and even his horse wore armor, with a steel horn jutting from its forehead and splendid red-and-green silk trappings hanging all about it like a bedspread, almost to the ground.
source_text: When I came to again, I was sitting under an oak tree, on the grass, with a whole beautiful and broad country landscape all to myself--nearly. Not entirely; for there was a fellow on a horse, looking down at me--a fellow fresh out of a picture-book. He was in old-time iron armor from head to heel, with a helmet on his head the shape of a nail-keg with slits in it; and he had a shield, and a sword, and a prodigious spear; and his horse had armor on, too, and a steel horn projecting from his forehead, and gorgeous red and green silk trappings that hung down all around him like a bedquilt, nearly to the ground.

text: There was sense in what he said, so we went on. Within the next mile, by the light of the lightning, we counted six more hanging figures, and altogether it was a hideous journey. That murmur was no murmur now, but a roar—a roar of men’s voices. A man came rushing past us then, barely visible in the dark, with other men in pursuit. They vanished. Soon another such scene took place, and then another and yet another. Then a sudden bend in the road brought that fire into view—it was a large manor house, and scarcely anything of it remained—and everywhere men were fleeing while others, in fury, chased after them.
source_text: There was reason in what he said, so we moved on. Within the next mile we counted six more hanging forms by the blaze of the lightning, and altogether it was a grisly excursion. That murmur was a murmur no longer, it was a roar; a roar of men's voices. A man came flying by now, dimly through the darkness, and other men chasing him. They disappeared. Presently another case of the kind occurred, and then another and another. Then a sudden turn of the road brought us in sight of that fire--it was a large manor-house, and little or nothing was left of it--and everywhere men were flying and other men raging after them in pursuit.

text: “Your Grace, in the matter of raiment and visage, all is well; there is no contradiction there. But between your garments and your carriage there is a grave and very visible mismatch. That martial spring of yours, that princely bearing—these will never answer. You hold yourself too erect; your glance is too lofty, too assured. The burdens of a crown do not bend the shoulders so, do not let the chin fall, do not lower the eye from its proud level, do not plant fear and hesitation in the breast and then publish them abroad in a loose frame and an uncertain tread. Those marks belong to the mean anxieties of the poor and meanly born. You must acquire the art of it; you must counterfeit the signs of want, of misery, of oppression, of humiliation, and of all those familiar cruelties by which a man’s spirit is drained away and he is fashioned into a dutiful and approved subject, a comfort to his rulers. Otherwise even the children will see through your disguise, and we shall be undone at the first cottage we come upon. I beg you, attempt to walk in this fashion.”
source_text: “Sire, as between clothes and countenance, you are all right, there is no discrepancy; but as between your clothes and your bearing, you are all wrong, there is a most noticeable discrepancy. Your soldierly stride, your lordly port--these will not do. You stand too straight, your looks are too high, too confident. The cares of a kingdom do not stoop the shoulders, they do not droop the chin, they do not depress the high level of the eye-glance, they do not put doubt and fear in the heart and hang out the signs of them in slouching body and unsure step. It is the sordid cares of the lowly born that do these things. You must learn the trick; you must imitate the trademarks of poverty, misery, oppression, insult, and the other several and common inhumanities that sap the manliness out of a man and make him a loyal and proper and approved subject and a satisfaction to his masters, or the very infants will know you for better than your disguise, and we shall go to pieces at the first hut we stop at. Pray try to walk like this.”

text: Well, as they were slowly wearing themselves out, it came into my head all at once to ask what had become of Rinaldo. I looked about me; he was nowhere in sight. Ah, that was an evil sign! I caught the prince by the sleeve, and we slipped away and ran for the cottage. No Rinaldo there, no Beatrice there! They had surely gone up the lane to fetch aid. I told the prince to put wings on his heels, and that I would tell him the reason later. We made swift progress across the open stretch, and just as we flashed into the cover of the trees I looked back and saw a noisy throng of peasants pouring into view, with Rinaldo and his wife at their head. They were making an infernal uproar, but that could do no one any harm; the wood was thick, and once we were fairly deep in it we could climb a tree and let them bay below. But then came another sound—hounds! Yes, that was a very different matter. It sharpened our peril—we must find water, and quickly.
source_text: Well, while they were gradually playing out, it suddenly occurred to me to wonder what had become of Marco. I looked around; he was nowhere to be seen. Oh, but this was ominous! I pulled the king's sleeve, and we glided away and rushed for the hut. No Marco there, no Phyllis there! They had gone to the road for help, sure. I told the king to give his heels wings, and I would explain later. We made good time across the open ground, and as we darted into the shelter of the wood I glanced back and saw a mob of excited peasants swarm into view, with Marco and his wife at their head. They were making a world of noise, but that couldn't hurt anybody; the wood was dense, and as soon as we were well into its depths we would take to a tree and let them whistle. Ah, but then came another sound--dogs! Yes, that was quite another matter. It magnified our contract--we must find running water.

text: “If A trades a barrel of onions to B, valued at two pence a bushel, in exchange for a sheep worth four pence and a dog worth a penny, and C kills the dog before delivery after being bitten by it, having mistaken him for D, what amount is still owed to A by B, and which party must pay for the dog, C or D, and who receives the money? If A is to receive it, is the penny enough, or can he also demand consequential damages in the form of extra money to represent the profit that might have come from the dog, to be classified as earned increment—in other words, usufruct?”
source_text: “If A trade a barrel of onions to B, worth 2 pence the bushel, in exchange for a sheep worth 4 pence and a dog worth a penny, and C kill the dog before delivery, because bitten by the same, who mistook him for D, what sum is still due to A from B, and which party pays for the dog, C or D, and who gets the money? If A, is the penny sufficient, or may he claim consequential damages in the form of additional money to represent the possible profit which might have inured from the dog, and classifiable as earned increment, that is to say, usufruct?”

text: Dowley was in splendid spirits, and I soon had him going; then I skillfully steered him round to his own past, made that the subject, and himself the hero of it, and it was a pleasure to sit and listen to him pour forth. A self-made man, you know. They understand how to speak. They surely merit more praise than any other class of men, yes, that is true; and they are among the very first to discover it themselves as well. He told how he had set out in life an orphan boy, without money and without friends to help him; how he had lived as the meanest slaves lived under a hard master; how his daily labor ran from sixteen to eighteen hours, and brought him only enough coarse black bread to keep him half starved; how at last his faithful efforts caught the eye of a good blacksmith, who almost stunned him with generosity by suddenly offering, when he was wholly unprepared, to take him as a bound apprentice for nine years, feed and clothe him, and teach him the trade—or “mystery,” as Dowley called it. That was his first great advancement, his first splendid stroke of luck; and one could see that he could not yet speak of it without a kind of radiant amazement and joy that such a glittering promotion should have come to an ordinary mortal. During his apprenticeship he received no new clothes, but on the day he was discharged his master dressed him in brand-new tow-linens and made him feel beyond measure wealthy and elegant.
source_text: Dowley was in fine feather, and I early got him started, and then adroitly worked him around onto his own history for a text and himself for a hero, and then it was good to sit there and hear him hum. Self-made man, you know. They know how to talk. They do deserve more credit than any other breed of men, yes, that is true; and they are among the very first to find it out, too. He told how he had begun life an orphan lad without money and without friends able to help him; how he had lived as the slaves of the meanest master lived; how his day's work was from sixteen to eighteen hours long, and yielded him only enough black bread to keep him in a half-fed condition; how his faithful endeavors finally attracted the attention of a good blacksmith, who came near knocking him dead with kindness by suddenly offering, when he was totally unprepared, to take him as his bound apprentice for nine years and give him board and clothes and teach him the trade--or “mystery” as Dowley called it. That was his first great rise, his first gorgeous stroke of fortune; and you saw that he couldn't yet speak of it without a sort of eloquent wonder and delight that such a gilded promotion should have fallen to the lot of a common human being. He got no new clothing during his apprenticeship, but on his graduation day his master tricked him out in spang-new tow-linens and made him feel unspeakably rich and fine.

text: “When the judge drew on his black cap, the owner of the stolen linen rose up, trembling, his lip shaking, his face turned as gray as ashes; and when the dreadful sentence was spoken, he cried, ‘Oh, poor child, poor child, I did not know it was death!’ and sank down like a tree that has been felled. When they raised him up, his wits were gone; before sunset he had taken his own life. A gentle man, one whose heart was sound at the core; add his murder to this one now about to be done here, and lay the blame for both where it belongs—to the rulers and the cruel laws of Britain. The hour has come, my child; let me pray over thee—not _for_ thee, dear abused poor heart, and innocent one, but for those who are guilty of thy ruin and death, who need it more.”
source_text: “When the judge put on his black cap, the owner of the stolen linen rose trembling up, his lip quivering, his face as gray as ashes; and when the awful words came, he cried out, 'Oh, poor child, poor child, I did not know it was death!' and fell as a tree falls. When they lifted him up his reason was gone; before the sun was set, he had taken his own life. A kindly man; a man whose heart was right, at bottom; add his murder to this that is to be now done here; and charge them both where they belong --to the rulers and the bitter laws of Britain. The time is come, my child; let me pray over thee--not _for_ thee, dear abused poor heart and innocent, but for them that be guilty of thy ruin and death, who need it more.”

text: At length, this kind of remark had an unfortunate effect. Our master was a practical man, and he saw that this flaw had to be corrected if he expected to find a buyer for the king. So he set about stripping the royal manner from his sacred majesty. I might have offered the fellow some useful advice, but I held back; one must not volunteer advice to a slave-driver if one does not wish to weaken the very argument one is making. I had already found it hard enough to bring the king’s manner down to that of a peasant, even with him as a willing and eager learner; and now to try to reduce the king’s manner to that of a slave—and by force, too—well, that was a grand undertaking. Never mind the particulars—it will spare me the trouble of describing them if I let you picture them for yourself. I will only say that by the end of a week there was ample proof that whip, club, and fist had done their business thoroughly; the king’s body was a miserable sight, fit to sicken and pity one. But his spirit?—not in the least shaken. Even that thick-skulled slave-driver could see that a man can be a slave and still remain a man to the end; you may break his bones, but you cannot break his manhood. From his first attempt to his last, the man found that he could never get within reach of the king, while the king was always ready to spring at him, and did. At last he gave up and left the king in full possession of his style, untouched. The truth is, the king was more than a king; he was a man. And when a man is truly a man, nothing can beat that out of him.
source_text: At last this sort of remark produced an evil result. Our owner was a practical person and he perceived that this defect must be mended if he hoped to find a purchaser for the king. So he went to work to take the style out of his sacred majesty. I could have given the man some valuable advice, but I didn't; you mustn't volunteer advice to a slave-driver unless you want to damage the cause you are arguing for. I had found it a sufficiently difficult job to reduce the king's style to a peasant's style, even when he was a willing and anxious pupil; now then, to undertake to reduce the king's style to a slave's style--and by force--go to! it was a stately contract. Never mind the details--it will save me trouble to let you imagine them. I will only remark that at the end of a week there was plenty of evidence that lash and club and fist had done their work well; the king's body was a sight to see--and to weep over; but his spirit?--why, it wasn't even phased. Even that dull clod of a slave-driver was able to see that there can be such a thing as a slave who will remain a man till he dies; whose bones you can break, but whose manhood you can't. This man found that from his first effort down to his latest, he couldn't ever come within reach of the king, but the king was ready to plunge for him, and did it. So he gave up at last, and left the king in possession of his style unimpaired. The fact is, the king was a good deal more than a king, he was a man; and when a man is a man, you can't knock it out of him.

text: I was ready and eager to get free now; before this I had not been eager to get free. No, I cannot put it quite that way. I had wanted freedom, but I had not been willing to risk reckless chances, and I had always talked the king out of them. But now—ah, the whole air was changed! Liberty would be worth whatever price might be asked for it now. I took up a scheme and was delighted with it at once. It would take time, yes, and patience too, a great deal of both. There were quicker methods one could devise, and just as certain ones; but none that would be as picturesque as this, none that could be made so dramatic. So I was not going to abandon it. It might put us back for months, but never mind; I would carry it through or smash something.
source_text: I was ready and willing to get free now; I hadn't wanted to get free any sooner. No, I cannot quite say that. I had wanted to, but I had not been willing to take desperate chances, and had always dissuaded the king from them. But now--ah, it was a new atmosphere! Liberty would be worth any cost that might be put upon it now. I set about a plan, and was straightway charmed with it. It would require time, yes, and patience, too, a great deal of both. One could invent quicker ways, and fully as sure ones; but none that would be as picturesque as this; none that could be made so dramatic. And so I was not going to give this one up. It might delay us months, but no matter, I would carry it out or break something.

text: All that evening I sat before the fire at the Warwick Arms, lost in a dream of elder days, while the rain rattled against the panes and the wind cried round the eaves and angles of the house. Now and then I turned to old Sir Lionel Worthy’s bewitching volume, and partook of its sumptuous banquet of wonders and adventures, inhaled the perfume of its antique names, and fell dreaming once more. At last, when midnight had come, I read yet another story as a night-cap—namely, that which follows here:
source_text: All that evening I sat by my fire at the Warwick Arms, steeped in a dream of the olden time, while the rain beat upon the windows, and the wind roared about the eaves and corners. From time to time I dipped into old Sir Thomas Malory's enchanting book, and fed at its rich feast of prodigies and adventures, breathed in the fragrance of its obsolete names, and dreamed again. Midnight being come at length, I read another tale, for a nightcap--this which here follows, to wit:

text: When I told the king I meant to go out in disguise, posing as a common freeman, to travel the country and get acquainted with the poorer life of the people, he was instantly on fire with the novelty of it and determined to have a share in the adventure himself—nothing, he said, should hold him back—he would drop everything and come with me—it was the finest idea he had come across in a long time. He wanted to slip out by the rear entrance and set off at once; but I made it clear that this would not do. You see, he was advertised for the king’s evil—to lay hands on it, that is—and it would not be right to disappoint the public, and besides it would not cause a delay worth mentioning, since it was only a one-night engagement. And I thought he ought to tell the queen he was leaving. At that his face darkened and he grew melancholy. I regretted speaking, especially when he said sadly:
source_text: When I told the king I was going out disguised as a petty freeman to scour the country and familiarize myself with the humbler life of the people, he was all afire with the novelty of the thing in a minute, and was bound to take a chance in the adventure himself--nothing should stop him--he would drop everything and go along--it was the prettiest idea he had run across for many a day. He wanted to glide out the back way and start at once; but I showed him that that wouldn't answer. You see, he was billed for the king's-evil--to touch for it, I mean--and it wouldn't be right to disappoint the house and it wouldn't make a delay worth considering, anyway, it was only a one-night stand. And I thought he ought to tell the queen he was going away. He clouded up at that and looked sad. I was sorry I had spoken, especially when he said mournfully:

text: But the king checked her, and with a courtesy or two made plain that the matter was no concern of hers. The trumpets sounded again; then we parted and rode to the far ends of the barriers, where each took up his station. Presently old Merlin came forward and spread a delicate mesh of silvery filaments over Sir Gareth, so that he looked like the Ghost in Hamlet. The king gave the signal; the trumpets blew; Sir Gareth set his massive lance in rest, and in another instant he was roaring down the lane, his veil streaming far behind him, while I went skimming through the air like a shaft to meet him—meanwhile tilting my ear aside, as though I could judge the unseen knight’s place and motion by sound alone. A shout of cheer rose for him, and one gallant voice flung me a brave word of encouragement—said:
source_text: But the king checked her and made her understand, with a polite phrase or two, that this was none of her business. The bugles rang again; and we separated and rode to the ends of the lists, and took position. Now old Merlin stepped into view and cast a dainty web of gossamer threads over Sir Sagramor which turned him into Hamlet's ghost; the king made a sign, the bugles blew, Sir Sagramor laid his great lance in rest, and the next moment here he came thundering down the course with his veil flying out behind, and I went whistling through the air like an arrow to meet him --cocking my ear the while, as if noting the invisible knight's position and progress by hearing, not sight. A chorus of encouraging shouts burst out for him, and one brave voice flung out a heartening word for me--said:

text: I watched my fifty-two boys closely, studying their faces, the way they moved, their unconscious postures; for all these things are a kind of language—a language deliberately given to us so that it may betray us in moments of crisis, when we are trying to hide our secrets. I knew that one thought would go on repeating itself in their minds and hearts: All England is marching against us! And with every repetition it would demand attention more urgently, and take sharper shape in their imaginations, until even in sleep they would find no relief from it, but hear the faint, shifting creatures of dreams saying, All England—ALL ENGLAND—is marching against you! I knew that all this would come to pass; I knew that in the end the strain would grow so intense that it would force itself into words. So I had to be ready then with an answer—an answer carefully chosen and meant to calm them.
source_text: I watched my fifty-two boys narrowly; watched their faces, their walk, their unconscious attitudes: for all these are a language --a language given us purposely that it may betray us in times of emergency, when we have secrets which we want to keep. I knew that that thought would keep saying itself over and over again in their minds and hearts, _All England is marching against us!_ and ever more strenuously imploring attention with each repetition, ever more sharply realizing itself to their imaginations, until even in their sleep they would find no rest from it, but hear the vague and flitting creatures of the dreams say, _All England_ --ALL ENGLAND!--_is marching against you_! I knew all this would happen; I knew that ultimately the pressure would become so great that it would compel utterance; therefore, I must be ready with an answer at that time--an answer well chosen and tranquilizing.

text: It was a desperate situation for him, one that might cost him his life, but there was no point in arguing with him. If he believed his knightly honor was at stake, that settled the matter; he would remain, and nothing could stop him. I knew that well enough. So I let the matter drop. The woman spoke:
source_text: It was a desperate place for him to be in, and might cost him his life, but it was no use to argue with him. If he considered his knightly honor at stake here, that was the end of argument; he would stay, and nothing could prevent it; I was aware of that. And so I dropped the subject. The woman spoke:

text: Ordinarily, the racket after dark would have irritated me, but under the circumstances I could not say I objected to it, since it spared me the sound of the medicos unshipping legs and arms from the day’s cripples. They spoiled for me a remarkably fine old cross-cut saw, and broke the sawhorse into the bargain, though I let that go. As for my axe—well, I resolved that the next time I loaned one to a surgeon, I would choose my century first.
source_text: The noise at night would have been annoying to me ordinarily, but I didn't mind it in the present circumstances, because it kept me from hearing the quacks detaching legs and arms from the day's cripples. They ruined an uncommon good old cross-cut saw for me, and broke the saw-buck, too, but I let it pass. And as for my axe--well, I made up my mind that the next time I lent an axe to a surgeon I would pick my century.

text: Still, taking the paper as a whole, I was hugely pleased with it. A few petty mechanical flaws showed up here and there, but there were not enough of them to matter, and in any case it was good enough for Arkansas proofreading, and better than Arthur’s day and kingdom required. As a rule, the grammar was shaky and the sentence structure rather weak, but I did not greatly object to that. Those are faults I share myself, and one ought not criticize others for failings on which he cannot stand upright himself.
source_text: However, take the paper by and large, I was vastly pleased with it. Little crudities of a mechanical sort were observable here and there, but there were not enough of them to amount to anything, and it was good enough Arkansas proof-reading, anyhow, and better than was needed in Arthur's day and realm. As a rule, the grammar was leaky and the construction more or less lame; but I did not much mind these things. They are common defects of my own, and one mustn't criticise other people on grounds where he can't stand perpendicular himself.

text: I had a brief glimpse of something else one day that gave me a great lift of spirits. It was a wire stretched from roof to roof—telegraph or telephone, no question. I was very anxious to have a small piece of it. It was exactly what I needed to carry out my plan of escape. My scheme was to get free some night, with the king beside me, then gag and bind our master, exchange clothes with him, beat him into the appearance of a stranger, fasten him to the slave-chain, take possession of the property, and march to Camelot, and--
source_text: I had one little glimpse of another thing, one day, which gave me a great uplift. It was a wire stretching from housetop to housetop. Telegraph or telephone, sure. I did very much wish I had a little piece of it. It was just what I needed, in order to carry out my project of escape. My idea was to get loose some night, along with the king, then gag and bind our master, change clothes with him, batter him into the aspect of a stranger, hitch him to the slave-chain, assume possession of the property, march to Camelot, and--

text: As we came into the torture chamber I had sight of a thing that has never left me, though I pray it would. A dark-skinned youth, no more than thirty by the look of him, lay flung upon the bench on his back, his wrists and ankles lashed to cords that ran over wheels at either end. There was not a trace of hue in him; his face was twisted hard and fixed, and beads of sweat shone on his brow. A priest leaned over him on each side; the tormentor stood near; soldiers kept watch; smoking brands burned in brackets along the walls; and in one corner cowered a poor young woman, her features drawn with misery, a half-frantic, hunted look in her eyes, while in her lap lay a little child, asleep. No sooner had we crossed the sill than the tormentor gave his apparatus the least turn, and both the sufferer and the woman cried out together; but I called sharply, and he slackened the strain without waiting to learn who had spoken. I could not endure that spectacle; it would have broken me to watch it continue. I begged the queen to let me clear the room and address the prisoner alone; and when she was about to object I lowered my voice and said I did not wish to make a disturbance before her household, but I must be obeyed, since I stood there as King Arthur’s envoy and spoke in his name. She perceived she had no choice but to give way. I asked her to vouch for me to these people, and then withdraw. It was not agreeable to her, yet she swallowed it; indeed, she went even beyond what I had meant to ask. I had wanted only the support of her authority; but she said:
source_text: As we entered the rack-cell I caught a picture that will not go from me; I wish it would. A native young giant of thirty or thereabouts lay stretched upon the frame on his back, with his wrists and ankles tied to ropes which led over windlasses at either end. There was no color in him; his features were contorted and set, and sweat-drops stood upon his forehead. A priest bent over him on each side; the executioner stood by; guards were on duty; smoking torches stood in sockets along the walls; in a corner crouched a poor young creature, her face drawn with anguish, a half-wild and hunted look in her eyes, and in her lap lay a little child asleep. Just as we stepped across the threshold the executioner gave his machine a slight turn, which wrung a cry from both the prisoner and the woman; but I shouted, and the executioner released the strain without waiting to see who spoke. I could not let this horror go on; it would have killed me to see it.
