I asked the queen to let me clear the place and speak to the prisoner privately; and when she was going to object I spoke in a low voice and said I did not want to make a scene before her servants, but I must have my way; for I was King Arthur's representative, and was speaking in his name. She saw she had to yield. I asked her to indorse me to these people, and then leave me. It was not pleasant for her, but she took the pill; and even went further than I was meaning to require. I only wanted the backing of her own authority; but she said:

text: What does the fellow do now but drop back a couple of hundred yards and then come charging at me for all he was worth, his powder-keg stooped down almost to his horse’s neck and his long lance leveled straight before him. I could see he was in earnest, so I was up the tree by the time he got there.
source_text: Now what does this man do but fall back a couple of hundred yards and then come rushing at me as hard as he could tear, with his nail-keg bent down nearly to his horse's neck and his long spear pointed straight ahead. I saw he meant business, so I was up the tree when he arrived.

text: As the guards helped me across the square, the stillness was so deep that, had my eyes been covered, I should have fancied myself alone, and not hemmed in by ten thousand souls. No motion could be detected among that sea of people; they stood as rigid as carved figures, and as wan, while terror lay upon every face. The silence held while they fastened me to the post; it held yet again while the bundles of dry wood were set, with solemn deliberation, about my ankles, my knees, my thighs, my body. Then came a pause, and, if anything, an even heavier silence; a man bent at my feet with a lighted brand, the crowd leaned forward, all of them gazing and edging from their places without knowing it, and the friar lifted his hands above my head, raised his eyes to the blue heavens, and began to mutter in Latin. In that posture he droned for a little time, and then fell still. I waited two or three heartbeats, then glanced upward; he stood there as if turned to stone. By a single common movement the whole multitude rose slowly and looked to the sky. I followed their glance, and, as certain as fate, there was my eclipse beginning! The blood leaped hotly through my veins; I was a different creature. The black edge crept little by little across the sun’s bright face, my pulse climbed faster and faster, and yet the crowd and the priest remained fixed, staring upward without a stir. I knew that their eyes would come to me next. When they did, I was prepared. I had struck one of the proudest attitudes of my life, my arm lifted and my finger pointed at the sun. It made a splendid effect. You could see the tremor run through the multitude like a wave. Then there rose two cries, one after the other:
source_text: As the soldiers assisted me across the court the stillness was so profound that if I had been blindfold I should have supposed I was in a solitude instead of walled in by four thousand people. There was not a movement perceptible in those masses of humanity; they were as rigid as stone images, and as pale; and dread sat upon every countenance. This hush continued while I was being chained to the stake; it still continued while the fagots were carefully and tediously piled about my ankles, my knees, my thighs, my body. Then there was a pause, and a deeper hush, if possible, and a man knelt down at my feet with a blazing torch; the multitude strained forward, gazing, and parting slightly from their seats without knowing it; the monk raised his hands above my head, and his eyes toward the blue sky, and began some words in Latin; in this attitude he droned on and on, a little while, and then stopped. I waited two or three moments; then looked up; he was standing there petrified. With a common impulse the multitude rose slowly up and stared into the sky. I followed their eyes, as sure as guns, there was my eclipse beginning! The life went boiling through my veins; I was a new man! The rim of black spread slowly into the sun's disk, my heart beat higher and higher, and still the assemblage and the priest stared into the sky, motionless. I knew that this gaze would be turned upon me, next. When it was, I was ready. I was in one of the most grand attitudes I ever struck, with my arm stretched up pointing to the sun. It was a noble effect. You could _see_ the shudder sweep the mass like a wave. Two shouts rang out, one close upon the heels of the other:

text: We were pleased to have the woman with us, for we were short of hands. We were caught in a trap, as you may see—a trap of our own making. If we remained where we were, our dead would destroy us; if we withdrew from our defenses, we would cease to be invincible. We had won, and in our turn we had been defeated. The Boss knew it; we all knew it. If only we could make our way to one of those new camps and somehow arrange terms with the enemy—yes, but The Boss could not go, and neither could I, for I was among the first to be struck down by the poisonous air born of those thousands of dead. Then others fell, and still more. Tomorrow—
source_text: We were glad to have this woman, for we were short handed. We were in a trap, you see--a trap of our own making. If we stayed where we were, our dead would kill us; if we moved out of our defenses, we should no longer be invincible. We had conquered; in turn we were conquered. The Boss recognized this; we all recognized it. If we could go to one of those new camps and patch up some kind of terms with the enemy--yes, but The Boss could not go, and neither could I, for I was among the first that were made sick by the poisonous air bred by those dead thousands. Others were taken down, and still others. To-morrow--

text: In Merlin’s Cave—Clarence and I, along with fifty-two fresh, lively, well-educated, clean-minded young British boys. At daybreak I sent word to the factories and to all our great works to shut down and move every living thing to a safe distance, since everything was to be destroyed by hidden mines, “and there is no saying at what instant—therefore, leave at once.” These people knew me and trusted my word. They would have cleared out without taking time to part their hair, and I could afford to choose my own moment for dating the explosion. You couldn’t have hired one of them to go back, not even for a century, if the explosion were still hanging over them.
source_text: In Merlin's Cave--Clarence and I and fifty-two fresh, bright, well-educated, clean-minded young British boys. At dawn I sent an order to the factories and to all our great works to stop operations and remove all life to a safe distance, as everything was going to be blown up by secret mines, “_and no telling at what moment--therefore, vacate at once_.” These people knew me, and had confidence in my word. They would clear out without waiting to part their hair, and I could take my own time about dating the explosion. You couldn't hire one of them to go back during the century, if the explosion was still impending.

text: We made good time, and a couple of hours before sunset we reached the high borders of the Valley of Holiness, where we let our eyes travel from end to end and took in its character. That is, its broad and general character. The chief features were the three clusters of buildings. From afar, standing apart in that lonely waste that seemed a desert—and was one—they looked like temporal things reduced to toy-like structures. A sight like that is always sad: the stillness is so solemn, and everything seems soaked in death. Yet there was a sound here that broke the silence only by deepening its sadness; it was the faint, distant ringing of bells, drifting to us now and then on the wind, so weak and so gentle that we scarcely knew whether we heard it with our ears or with our souls.
source_text: We made good time; and a couple of hours before sunset we stood upon the high confines of the Valley of Holiness, and our eyes swept it from end to end and noted its features. That is, its large features. These were the three masses of buildings. They were distant and isolated temporalities shrunken to toy constructions in the lonely waste of what seemed a desert--and was. Such a scene is always mournful, it is so impressively still, and looks so steeped in death. But there was a sound here which interrupted the stillness only to add to its mournfulness; this was the faint far sound of tolling bells which floated fitfully to us on the passing breeze, and so faintly, so softly, that we hardly knew whether we heard it with our ears or with our spirits.

text: “The Church holds power now. The Interdict places you and Mordred alike under its ban; it will not be lifted while you still live. The clans are assembling. The Church has mustered every knight still left alive, and the moment you are found, we shall have work enough on our hands.”
source_text: “The Church is master now. The Interdict included you with Mordred; it is not to be removed while you remain alive. The clans are gathering. The Church has gathered all the knights that are left alive, and as soon as you are discovered we shall have business on our hands.”

text:
Then all three cried out, “Sir Knight, we surrender to you as a man unmatched in might.”  
“As for that,” said Sir Launcelot, “I will not accept your surrender to me unless you first yield yourselves to Sir Kay the seneschal. On that condition I will spare your lives, and not otherwise.”  
“Fair knight,” they replied, “we would be loath to do that, for we chased Sir Kay hither, and had we not been hindered by you we should have overcome him; therefore it would be unreasonable to yield ourselves to him.”  
“Well,” said Sir Launcelot, “consider it well, for you must choose whether you will live or die. If you surrender, it must be to Sir Kay.”  
“Fair knight,” they said then, “rather than lose our lives, we will do as you command.”  
“Then,” said Sir Launcelot, “on the next Whitsunday you shall go to King Arthur’s court, and there surrender yourselves to Queen Guinevere. Put yourselves wholly into her grace and mercy, and say that Sir Kay sent you there to be her prisoners.”  

Next morning Sir Launcelot rose early and left Sir Kay still asleep. He took Sir Kay’s armor and shield, armed himself with them, and went to the stable for the horse. Then he took leave of his host and rode away.  

Not long after, Sir Kay awoke and found Sir Launcelot gone. Then he saw that his armor and horse were also missing. “Now, by my faith,” said he, “I know well that he will cause some trouble at King Arthur’s court; for the knights will be bold toward the man they take me for, and that will serve to deceive them. And with his armor and shield I am sure I shall ride in peace.” And shortly afterward Sir Kay departed, thanking his host.
source_text: And then they all three cried, Sir Knight, we yield us unto you as man of might matchless. As to that, said Sir Launcelot, I will not take your yielding unto me, but so that ye yield you unto Sir Kay the seneschal, on that covenant I will save your lives and else not. Fair knight, said they, that were we loath to do; for as for Sir Kay we chased him hither, and had overcome him had ye not been; therefore, to yield us unto him it were no reason. Well, as to that, said Sir Launcelot, advise you well, for ye may choose whether ye will die or live, for an ye be yielden, it shall be unto Sir Kay. Fair knight, then they said, in saving our lives we will do as thou commandest us. Then shall ye, said Sir Launcelot, on Whitsunday next coming go unto the court of King Arthur, and there shall ye yield you unto Queen Guenever, and put you all three in her grace and mercy, and say that Sir Kay sent you thither to be her prisoners. On the morn Sir Launcelot arose early, and left Sir Kay sleeping; and Sir Launcelot took Sir Kay's armor and his shield and armed him, and so he went to the stable and took his horse, and took his leave of his host, and so he departed. Then soon after arose Sir Kay and missed Sir Launcelot; and then he espied that he had his armor and his horse. Now by my faith I know well that he will grieve some of the court of King Arthur; for on him knights will be bold, and deem that it is I, and that will beguile them; and because of his armor and shield I am sure I shall ride in peace. And then soon after departed Sir Kay, and thanked his host.

text: But it is one of nature’s happy arrangements that in moments like these, once a man’s mercury has fallen to a certain level, a reversal sets in and he takes heart again. Hope rises, cheerfulness comes with it, and then he is fit to do something for himself, if anything can be done. When my own recovery came, it came all at once. I told myself that my eclipse would certainly save me and, beyond that, make me the greatest man in the kingdom; and immediately my mercury shot to the top of the tube, while all my anxieties disappeared. I was as contented a man as ever lived. I was even eager for tomorrow to arrive, so strongly did I long to claim that splendid triumph and stand at the center of the nation’s amazement and reverence. And besides, from a practical point of view, it would make my fortune; I knew that.
source_text: But it is a blessed provision of nature that at times like these, as soon as a man's mercury has got down to a certain point there comes a revulsion, and he rallies. Hope springs up, and cheerfulness along with it, and then he is in good shape to do something for himself, if anything can be done. When my rally came, it came with a bound. I said to myself that my eclipse would be sure to save me, and make me the greatest man in the kingdom besides; and straightway my mercury went up to the top of the tube, and my solicitudes all vanished. I was as happy a man as there was in the world. I was even impatient for to-morrow to come, I so wanted to gather in that great triumph and be the center of all the nation's wonder and reverence. Besides, in a business way it would be the making of me; I knew that.

text: “Ah, I know that look: you’ve got a wife at home, poor soul, and you and she have gone to bed hungry many a time so the little ones could have your crust. You know what poverty is, and the daily insults from your social betters, and the heavy hand of the Church and the king.”
source_text: “Ah, I know that sign: thou'st a wife at home, poor soul, and you and she have gone hungry to bed, many's the time, that the little ones might have your crust; you know what poverty is, and the daily insults of your betters, and the heavy hand of the Church and the king.”

text: When Mr. Audley, after a laborious morning in the company of lofty principles, came down to luncheon, he discovered that Mr. Lionel Carfax had ridden over to share that meal with him. Mr. Carfax was standing in the hall, his feet planted very wide apart, reading _The Times_ for the fourth time. “I can do no work,” he said, turning about. “I can’t collect my thoughts. I suppose we are going to war. I had grown so accustomed to the war with Germany that I never imagined it would really come. Heaven help us! What a nuisance it will be.... And Havelock and all those alarmists crowing and saying, ‘I told you so.’ Curse these Germans!”
source_text: When Mr. Britling, after a strenuous morning among high ideals, descended for lunch, he found Mr. Lawrence Carmine had come over to join him at that meal. Mr. Carmine was standing in the hall with his legs very wide apart reading _The Times_ for the fourth time. "I can do no work," he said, turning round. "I can't fix my mind. I suppose we are going to war. I'd got so used to the war with Germany that I never imagined it would happen. Gods! what a bore it will be.... And Maxse and all those scaremongers cock-a-hoop and 'I told you so.' Damn these Germans!"

text: Somehow the last spell in the fire trench has greatly unsettled my mind. I had begun to grow accustomed to the war before, but now I am back to my original astonishment at the whole affair. I keep asking myself what on earth we are really doing, why the war started, why we were drawn into this astonishing routine. It appears orderly, methodical, deliberate. Our officers issue orders and receive orders, and the men farther back receive theirs. Everyone is receiving orders. I suppose it all leads back to Lord Kitchener. For weeks it goes on with the air of being perfectly rational, carefully planned, and entirely proper, and then all at once it strikes one: “But this—this is completely mad!” This going there and back and back and there again; this endless monotony, broken now and then by violence—violence that never achieves anything—is exactly the sort of life a lunatic would lead. Melancholy and mania.... It is nothing but a shared obsession—war. The world is truly insane. I happen to have a brief flash of sanity, and it will not last once I finish this letter. I suppose when a man goes mad and climbs out of a window because he believes the door has been made magically inaccessible, and runs about in the street without his trousers, prodding passers-by with a toasting-fork, he feels the same grim sense of necessary compulsion that we all do when we set off with our packs for the trenches....
source_text: "Somehow the last spell in the fire trench has shaken up my mind a lot. I was getting used to the war before, but now I've got back to my original amazement at the whole business. I find myself wondering what we are really up to, why the war began, why we were caught into this amazing routine. It looks, it feels orderly, methodical, purposeful. Our officers give us orders and get their orders, and the men back there get their orders. Everybody is getting orders. Back, I suppose, to Lord Kitchener. It goes on for weeks with the effect of being quite sane and intended and the right thing, and then, then suddenly it comes whacking into one's head, 'But this--this is utterly _mad_!' This going to and fro and to and fro and to and fro; this monotony which breaks ever and again into violence--violence that never gets anywhere--is exactly the life that a lunatic leads. Melancholia and mania.... It's just a collective obsession--by war. The world is really quite mad. I happen to be having just one gleam of sanity, that won't last after I have finished this letter. I suppose when an individual man goes mad and gets out of the window because he imagines the door is magically impossible, and dances about in the street without his trousers jabbing at passers-by with a toasting-fork, he has just the same sombre sense of unavoidable necessity that we have, all of us, when we go off with our packs into the trenches....

text: If Albion still had a longer reckoning to settle, the French had already paid almost to the edge of endurance. They must have lost well beyond a million of their people, and yet they went on bleeding, and bleeding. Russia also, in the East, had paid far more than man for man in this immense barter of lives. Before long no censorship would be able to stifle the voice of the peoples. There would be no more talk of honour and annexations, of hegemonies and trade routes, only Europe mourning for her dead....
source_text: If England had still the longer account to pay, the French had paid already nearly to the limits of endurance. They must have lost well over a million of their mankind, and still they bled and bled. Russia too in the East had paid far more than man for man in this vast swapping off of lives. In a little while no Censorship would hold the voice of the peoples. There would be no more talk of honour and annexations, hegemonies and trade routes, but only Europe lamenting for her dead....

text: It seemed to him as though a mine had suddenly exploded beneath a great ship at sea, while men shouted and women cried and crouched in fear, and flares moved across the rain-dimpled black waves; then the scene shifted and became a battle on land, with searchlights flickering through the rain and shells flashing ghastly through it, and men, seen only as dark silhouettes against the red flames, ran with fixed bayonets and slipped and struggled through the mud, until at last, their cries thin against the wind, they leapt down into the enemy trenches....
source_text: It seemed to him that suddenly a mine burst under a great ship at sea, that men shouted and women sobbed and cowered, and flares played upon the rain-pitted black waves; and then the picture changed and showed a battle upon land, and searchlights were flickering through the rain and shells flashed luridly, and men darkly seen in silhouette against red flames ran with fixed bayonets and slipped and floundered over the mud, and at last, shouting thinly through the wind, leapt down into the enemy trenches....

text: He was torn by feelings as strange and inexplicable as those of his youth. The same sensation possessed him now that had come over him in childhood whenever some disagreeable confession had to be made to his parents. He knew he could not yet endure an encounter with her, could not bear the burden of telling her, of being watched while he did so. Suddenly he veered to the left. He went off as though he had not noticed her, across the lawn toward the small summer-house on the rise that looked down upon the main road. She called after him, but he made no reply....
source_text: He was wrenched by emotions as odd and unaccountable as the emotions of adolescence. He had exactly the same feeling now that he had had when in his boyhood some unpleasant admission had to be made to his parents. He felt he could not go through a scene with her yet, that he could not endure the task of telling her, of being observed. He turned abruptly to his left. He walked away as if he had not seen her, across his lawn towards the little summer-house upon a knoll that commanded the high road. She called to him, but he did not answer....

text: For an instant, Mr. Vale saw, as in a flash, red flannel petticoats ripped up and hastily turned, in some extemporized frenzy of soldiery, into means of resisting a sudden descent. Washerwomen, startled on their way, seized and put to service at once. Yet one must not allow oneself to be derided out of a decent purpose merely because of absurd trappings. The notion, at any rate, was a right one....
source_text: A momentary vision danced before Mr. Britling's eyes of red flannel petticoats being torn up in a rapid improvisation of soldiers to resist a sudden invasion. Passing washerwomen suddenly requisitioned. But one must not let oneself be laughed out of good intentions because of ridiculous accessories. The idea at any rate was the sound one....

text: Through the darkness he went, and all at once his son seemed to be everywhere around him—at play, scrambling up the cedars, wheeling in impossible circles across the lawn on a bicycle, speaking with solemn gravity of the life that lay before him, stretched out on the grass, breathing in great gasps, sketching outrageous caricatures. Again they were pacing together, back and forth—right across this same place—talking earnestly, yet with a certain bashfulness....
source_text: Across the dark he went, and suddenly his boy was all about him, playing, climbing the cedars, twisting miraculously about the lawn on a bicycle, discoursing gravely upon his future, lying on the grass, breathing very hard and drawing preposterous caricatures. Once again they walked side by side up and down--it was athwart this very spot--talking gravely but rather shyly....

text: “Look at that agreeable fellow. There he stands—_Echt Deutsch_—if ever anything was. Look at my son there! Do you see the two of them locked in mortal combat? The whole thing is absurd. The world is growing sane. They may still be fighting in the Balkans; in many respects the Balkan States are still far behind civilisation. But to imagine decent countries like this, or Germany, sinking back into bloodshed again! No.... When I see Rendezvous at it and still at it, I begin to understand how hard pressed Germany must be to keep at it. I begin to realise how wearied Germany must be of the high road, the dust and heat, the endless drill and discipline.... My sympathies go out to the South Germans. Old Manning here always makes me think of Austria. Imagine Germany coming up like Rendezvous on a Sunday morning and peering stiffly over Austria’s fence. ‘Come out for a brisk walk, man. Keep fit....’”
source_text: "Look at that pleasant person. There he is--_Echt Deutsch_--if anything ever was. Look at my son there! Do you see the two of them engaged in mortal combat? The thing's too ridiculous. The world grows sane. They may fight in the Balkans still; in many ways the Balkan States are in the very rear of civilisation; but to imagine decent countries like this or Germany going back to bloodshed! No.... When I see Rendezvous keeping it up and keeping it up, I begin to see just how poor Germany must be keeping it up. I begin to realise how sick Germany must be getting of the high road and the dust and heat and the everlasting drill and restraint.... My heart goes out to the South Germans. Old Manning here always reminds me of Austria. Think of Germany coming like Rendezvous on a Sunday morning, and looking stiffly over Austria's fence. 'Come for a good hard walk, man. Keep Fit....'"

text: It was as though, in Hugh’s slim, well-proportioned figure, dressed in khaki, he were seeing for the first time the loveliness of youth. There was an exquisite frailty in his clear skin, his candid eyes; in the finely drawn eyebrow that was so unmistakably like his mother’s. And this creature of radiance and courage spoke with the same gravity and wisdom as any weather-beaten, shop-worn old man....
source_text: It was as if he perceived the beauty of youth for the first time in Hugh's slender, well balanced, khaki-clad body. There was infinite delicacy in his clear complexion, his clear eyes; the delicately pencilled eyebrow that was so exactly like his mother's. And this thing of brightness and bravery talked as gravely and as wisely as any weather-worn, shop-soiled, old fellow....

text: Mr. Britling was probably far more candid and clear-sighted with himself and with the world than many intelligent people, and yet there were a good many features of his relations with his wife, with the people around him, with his country, with God, and with the very nature of things, to which he persistently turned his back. But a back too determinedly turned may mean as much as a finger pointed, and in this backward fashion, and in a tacit way so far as even his formal thoughts and unspoken remarks were concerned, Mr. Britling knew that he loved his son because he had poured into him the greatest amount of hope and imagination, because he was the one living continuation of that dear life with Mary, so lovingly stormy then, so beautiful now in memory, which had truly taken possession of Mr. Britling’s whole heart. The boy had been the delight and wonder of the young parents; they could scarcely believe there had ever been a being so fragile and sweet, and they brought a great deal of imagination and humour to the close study of his tiny personality and to the anticipation of his future. Mr. Britling’s mind was full of splendid plans for his education. All that mental expansion no doubt helped greatly to shape Mr. Britling’s singular affection, and woven through it were even gentler and more delicate strands, for the boy had many of Mary’s qualities. But there were other matters that were ignored even more conspicuously. One quiet element in the slow widening of the rift between Edith and Mr. Britling was her cool opinion of her stepson. She was consistently kind to this tousle-haired, untidy little dreamer; he was very well looked after in her care, she liked him and was amused by him—it is hard to see what more Mr. Britling could have wanted—but it was as clear as day that she felt this was not a child she would have cared to bear herself. It was quite absurd, and perfectly natural, that this should seem to Mr. Britling an injustice to Hugh.
source_text: Mr. Britling was probably much franker and more open-eyed with himself and the universe than a great number of intelligent people, and yet there were quite a number of aspects of his relations with his wife, with people about him, with his country and God and the nature of things, upon which he turned his back with an attentive persistence. But a back too resolutely turned may be as indicative as a pointing finger, and in this retrogressive way, and tacitly even so far as his formal thoughts, his unspoken comments, went, Mr. Britling knew that he loved his son because he had lavished the most hope and the most imagination upon him, because he was the one living continuation of that dear life with Mary, so lovingly stormy at the time, so fine now in memory, that had really possessed the whole heart of Mr. Britling. The boy had been the joy and marvel of the young parents; it was incredible to them that there had ever been a creature so delicate and sweet, and they brought considerable imagination and humour to the detailed study of his minute personality and to the forecasting of his future. Mr. Britling's mind blossomed with wonderful schemes for his education. All that mental growth no doubt contributed greatly to Mr. Britling's peculiar affection, and with it there interwove still tenderer and subtler elements, for the boy had a score of Mary's traits. But there were other things still more conspicuously ignored. One silent factor in the slow widening of the breach between Edith and Mr. Britling was her cool estimate of her stepson. She was steadfastly kind to this shock-headed, untidy little dreamer, he was extremely well cared for in her hands, she liked him and she was amused by him--it is difficult to imagine what more Mr. Britling could have expected--but it was as plain as daylight that she felt that this was not the child she would have cared to have borne. It was quite preposterous and perfectly natural that this should seem to Mr. Britling to be unfair to Hugh.

text: “It isn’t the Leinster men who are asking them over now, at any rate,” said Mr. Havelock, setting his little trap. “Only the other morning one of your so-called ‘faithfuls,’ Mr. Ainslie, was in the _Evening Herald_ declaring that he would sooner have the country conquered by Germany than governed under self-rule; Mr. Carr has been playing the same tune; and Major Kearney—the fellow who was running German Mausers last April—was bragging that he would swear loyalty to the German Emperor before he would see O’Donnell in office.”
source_text: "It isn't the Redmondites who invite them now, anyhow," said Mr. Britling, springing his mine. "The other day one of your 'loyalists,' Andrews, was talking in the _Morning Post_ of preferring conquest by Germany to Home Rule; Craig has been at the same game; Major Crawford, the man who ran the German Mausers last April, boasted that he would transfer his allegiance to the German Emperor rather than see Redmond in power."

text: There was some friendly banter between the good man and Mr. Britling over bringing Mr. Direck to church on Sunday morning. “He’s terribly lax,” Mr. Dimple said to Mr. Direck, beaming. “Terribly lax. But then these days everybody is so lax. And he’s very good to my Coal Club; I don’t know what we should do without him. So I just keep after him a little. And if he doesn’t go to church, well, at any rate he doesn’t go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but at least he isn’t a dissenter....”
source_text: There was some amiable sparring between the worthy man and Mr. Britling about bringing Mr. Direck to church on Sunday morning. "He's terribly Lax," said Mr. Dimple to Mr. Direck, smiling radiantly. "Terribly Lax. But then nowadays Everybody _is_ so Lax. And he's very Good to my Coal Club; I don't know what we should do without him. So I just admonish him. And if he doesn't go to church, well, anyhow he doesn't go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but anyhow he's not a dissenter...."

text: And yet this was only forty-two miles from London. Surely that ought to place it within the suburban orbit. If Matching’s Easy were in America, commuters would be living there. But in making that assumption, Mr. Direck betrayed a lack of knowledge of a matter of the first importance to anyone who wishes to understand England. There is a gap in the suburbs of London. The suburbs spread west and south, and even west by north, but to the north-east there are no suburbs at all; there is Essex instead. Essex is no suburban county; it is a county of its own, distinct and lovable, and it conquers the heart. Between dear Essex and the very centre of affairs lie two immense barriers, the East End of London and Epping Forest. Before a train could carry a load of season-ticket holders to any district of villas, it would have to swing around this saved woodland and cover twenty useless miles; and so, once one is beyond the main Great Eastern lines, Essex still lives in the quiet of the eighteenth century, while London, the modern Babylon, is, like the stars, only a light in the night sky. In Matching’s Easy, as Mr. Britling soon explained to Mr. Direck, there are half a dozen old people who have never looked upon London in their lives—and do not wish to.
source_text: And yet this was only forty-two miles from London. Surely it brought things within the suburban range. If Matching's Easy were in America, commuters would live there. But in supposing that, Mr. Direck displayed his ignorance of a fact of the greatest importance to all who would understand England. There is a gap in the suburbs of London. The suburbs of London stretch west and south and even west by north, but to the north-eastward there are no suburbs; instead there is Essex. Essex is not a suburban county; it is a characteristic and individualised county which wins the heart. Between dear Essex and the centre of things lie two great barriers, the East End of London and Epping Forest. Before a train could get to any villadom with a cargo of season-ticket holders it would have to circle about this rescued woodland and travel for twenty unprofitable miles, and so once you are away from the main Great Eastern lines Essex still lives in the peace of the eighteenth century, and London, the modern Babylon, is, like the stars, just a light in the nocturnal sky. In Matching's Easy, as Mr. Britling presently explained to Mr. Direck, there are half-a-dozen old people who have never set eyes on London in their lives--and do not want to.

text: “And that is where our English habit brings its troubles. The young Prussian honors and fears his father; he honors authority, attends, obeys—and his father has a grip on him. But I said to myself from the first, ‘No, whatever comes, I will not seize God’s place. I will not be the priest-patriarch to my children. They shall grow, and I will grow with them, aiding them, but not binding them down or casting a shadow over them.’ They grow the more. But they stumble the more. Life ceases to be a discipline and becomes an experiment....”
source_text: "And that's where our English way makes for distresses. Mr. Prussian respects and fears his father; respects authorities, attends, obeys and--_his father has a hold upon him_. But I said to myself at the outset, 'No, whatever happens, I will not usurp the place of God. I will not be the Priest-Patriarch of my children. They shall grow and I will grow beside them, helping but not cramping or overshadowing.' They grow more. But they blunder more. Life ceases to be a discipline and becomes an experiment...."

text: She glanced back and saw Mr. Vallance coming along the path by the meadow. He appeared pale, worn, and heavy with fatigue; even his stiff hair and clipped moustache seemed to mirror his dejection. He wore an old tweed knickerbocker suit and carried a large atlas and several sheets of paper. There was something uncertain in the way he came on, as though he wished to speak with her yet feared the welcome she might give him.
source_text: She looked over her shoulder and discovered Mr. Britling approaching by the field path. He looked white and tired and listless, even his bristling hair and moustache conveyed his depression; he was dressed in an old tweed knickerbocker suit and carrying a big atlas and some papers. He had an effect of hesitation in his approach. It was as if he wanted to talk to her and doubted her reception for him.

text: “Liege was only a scuff on the varnish,” said Mr. Vardon. “A few thousand killed, a few score thousand killed, what does it signify? Not one red cent to them. There’s a fellow at the Savoy who saw them go tramping into Brussels. He lunched with me yesterday at the same table. All day long it kept up, a huge unbroken stream of men in grey. Wagons without end, guns without end, the whole male strength of a nation and all its gear, moving on....
source_text: "Liège was just a scratch on the paint," said Mr. Direck. "A few thousand dead, a few score thousand dead, doesn't matter--not a red cent to them. There's a man arrived at the Cecil who saw them marching into Brussels. He sat at table with me at lunch yesterday. All day it went on, a vast unending river of men in grey. Endless waggons, endless guns, the whole manhood of a nation and all its stuff, marching....

text: While Mr. Britling was making his rounds along insignificant roads and amusing Mr. Van der Pant with discourses on how near victory already seemed, and on the delicate merits of everything in English life that was idle, wasteful, and evasive, the war was moving out of its first swift phase into something slower and harsher. The German retreat came to an end on the Aisne, and the long outflanking movements of both armies toward the Channel began. The English efforts in October to help Belgium arrived too late to save Antwerp, and after a prolonged and intricate struggle in Flanders the British failed to turn the German right flank, lost Ghent, Menin, and the Belgian coast, but held Ypres and repelled every attempt of the enemy to reach Dunkirk and Calais. At the same time the smaller German colonies and islands were falling before the navy; the Australian battleship _Sydney_ destroyed the _Emden_ at Cocos Island, and the British naval reverse at Coronel was erased by the battle of the Falklands. The Russians were successful on their left and captured Lemberg, and after some reverses advanced to Przemysl, occupying most of Galicia; but the defeat at Tannenberg had shattered their advance in East Prussia, and the Germans were pushing toward Warsaw. Turkey had entered the war and was suffering enormous losses in the Caucasus. The Dardanelles had been bombarded for the first time, and the British were at Basra on the Euphrates.
source_text: While Mr. Britling was patrolling unimportant roads and entertaining Mr. Van der Pant with discourses upon the nearness of victory and the subtle estimableness of all that was indolent, wasteful and evasive in English life, the war was passing from its first swift phases into a slower, grimmer struggle. The German retreat ended at the Aisne, and the long outflanking manoeuvres of both hosts towards the Channel began. The English attempts to assist Belgium in October came too late for the preservation of Antwerp, and after a long and complicated struggle in Flanders the British failed to outflank the German right, lost Ghent, Menin and the Belgian coast, but held Ypres and beat back every attempt of the enemy to reach Dunkirk and Calais. Meanwhile the smaller German colonies and islands were falling to the navy, the Australian battleship _Sydney_ smashed the _Emden_ at Cocos Island, and the British naval disaster of Coronel was wiped out by the battle of the Falklands. The Russians were victorious upon their left and took Lemberg, and after some vicissitudes of fortune advanced to Przemysl, occupying the larger part of Galicia; but the disaster of Tannenberg had broken their progress in East Prussia, and the Germans were pressing towards Warsaw. Turkey had joined the war, and suffered enormous losses in the Caucasus. The Dardanelles had been shelled for the first time, and the British were at Basra on the Euphrates.

text: And then came a waltz, and Herr Heinrich bowed to the visitor’s wife, led her round three times, and returned her with exact punctuality to the very spot from which he had taken her; while the Indian young gentleman, who must not be called “coloured,” waltzed very well with Cecily. Mr. Direck tried to assume a tolerant European attitude toward this brown-and-white partnership. But he rescued her from the Asiatic entanglement as soon as he could, and danced with her once more, and then again.
source_text: And then there was a waltz, and Herr Heinrich bowed to and took the visitor wife round three times, and returned her very punctually and exactly to the point whence he had taken her, and the Indian young gentleman (who must not be called "coloured") waltzed very well with Cecily. Mr. Direck tried to take a tolerant European view of this brown and white combination. But he secured her as soon as possible from this Asiatic entanglement, and danced with her again, and then he danced with her again.

text: At first he would not admit the full savagery of this war. He could not bring himself to believe that the assault on Britain and on Western Europe at large sprang from the gathered feeling of an entire people. To him the Allies were contending with a machine, not with a national temper. He resisted the notion that the mass of a highly civilised nation could be moved by a true and enduring hatred. Ill will was something foreign to his nature; he would not concede that most men are more easily turned to enmity than to friendship. He tried as hard as he could to believe—and in his “When War is Over” he tried just as hard to persuade others—that this conflict was the distorted venture of a small circle, of narrow yet potent forces, an insult to the prevailing good humour of mankind. The cruelty, waste, and absurdity of war were so plain to him that he almost begged pardon for insisting on them. He was convinced that once war had actually begun and disclosed its character among the Western peoples, they would unite at once to prevent its ever recurring. They would cry out, “But we cannot do such things to one another!” He imagined German aggressiveness being judged even by Germans themselves; a reckoning, a breakdown, a conference of liberal statesmen from the world’s powers, and then a general return to good will on a firmer foundation of safety. He believed—and many in England believed with him—that a large part of the German people would greet a victorious Allied army as deliverers from unbearable political delusions.
source_text: Notably he would not recognise at first the extreme bitterness of this war. He would not believe that the attack upon Britain and Western Europe generally expressed the concentrated emotion of a whole nation. He thought that the Allies were in conflict with a system and not with a national will. He fought against the persuasion that the whole mass of a great civilised nation could be inspired by a genuine and sustained hatred. Hostility was an uncongenial thing to him; he would not recognise that the greater proportion of human beings are more readily hostile than friendly. He did his best to believe--in his "And Now War Ends" he did his best to make other people believe--that this war was the perverse exploit of a small group of people, of limited but powerful influences, an outrage upon the general geniality of mankind. The cruelty, mischief, and futility of war were so obvious to him that he was almost apologetic in asserting them. He believed that war had but to begin and demonstrate its quality among the Western nations in order to unify them all against its repetition. They would exclaim: "But we can't do things like this to one another!" He saw the aggressive imperialism of Germany called to account even by its own people; a struggle, a collapse, a liberal-minded conference of world powers, and a universal resumption of amiability upon a more assured basis of security. He believed--and many people in England believed with him--that a great section of the Germans would welcome triumphant Allies as their liberators from intolerable political obsessions.

text: People come into the house without any ceremony. There is a great deal of kindness, but no politeness. Mr. Britling will go away for three or four days, and when he comes back and I step forward to greet him and bow, he will walk straight past me, or he will say simply, “How do, Heinrich?”
source_text: "People walk into the house without ceremony. There is much kindness but no politeness. Mr. Britling will go away for three or four days, and when he returns and I come forward to greet him and bow, he will walk right past me, or he will say just like this, 'How do, Heinrich?'"

text: “I’m content now in my mind—in a way. He wasn’t there at all, really—unless he was dead. But if I saw Teddy coming over that hedge toward me—it would seem quite natural.... No, don’t stare like that. I know he is dead, truly. And it is a comfort. It brings peace.... All those thoughts of him being horribly crushed or mutilated or lying there screaming—or things like that—they’ve gone now. He’s out of that spoiled body. He’s my Teddy again, whole and untouched.... Somewhere out of sight.... Whole.... Sleeping.”
source_text: "I'm content now in my mind--in a way. He wasn't anyhow there--unless he was dead. But if I saw Teddy coming over the hedge there to me--It would be just natural.... No, don't stare at me. I know really he is dead. And it is a comfort. It is peace.... All the thoughts of him being crushed dreadfully or being mutilated or lying and screaming--or things like that--they've gone. He's out of his spoilt body. He's my unbroken Teddy again.... Out of sight somewhere.... Unbroken.... Sleeping."

text: He was no longer writing to the particular parents of a single boy, but to that vast burden of suffering, regret, bitterness, and weariness that lay hidden behind the veil of the “front.” Slowly, relentlessly, the manhood of Germany was being worn away. As he sat in the silence, he could think that at least two million men of the Central Powers were dead, and an equal number were maimed and disabled. In comparison with that, our British losses, immense and widespread though they were by the measure of any earlier experience, were still small; our greater armies had yet to endure, and we had lost irrevocably not much more than a quarter of a million. Yet the tragedy was accumulating against us. We knew enough already to understand what the German homes must really be like, homes to which those dead men would never return....
source_text: He was no longer writing to the particular parents of one particular boy, but to all that mass of suffering, regret, bitterness and fatigue that lay behind the veil of the "front." Slowly, steadily, the manhood of Germany was being wiped out. As he sat there in the stillness he could think that at least two million men of the Central Powers were dead, and an equal number maimed and disabled. Compared with that our British losses, immense and universal as they were by the standard of any previous experience, were still slight; our larger armies had still to suffer, and we had lost irrevocably not very much more than a quarter of a million. But the tragedy gathered against us. We knew enough already to know what must be the reality of the German homes to which those dead men would nevermore return....

text: The guests were gathering in the paneled hall, where, against the dark background, the acetylene candles threw them into a dazzling display. Mr. Britling, in a black velvet cloak and black silk tights, made a darker shape among the shadows; the brightest figures were Miss Corner and her sister, glittering in peacock green and silver, their sleek bodies given a serpent-like grace by their shimmering clothes. They were talking with the German tutor, who had turned himself into a kind of cotton Cossack—a spectacled one, in buff and bright green. Mrs. Britling looked dignified and lovely in a purple djibbah, while her stepson had become a striking, motionless figure in black and crimson. Teddy had devised something elaborate and effective in the Egyptian manner, with a fish-basket and a cuirass made from that thin matting one finds behind washstands; the little boys were brigands, with hugely baggy breeches and cummerbunds into which they had tucked an assortment of paper-knives, toy pistols, and similar weapons. Mr. Carmine and his young companion had arrived in genuine Indian dress; everyone felt that Mr. Carmine looked like a mullah. The aunt-like lady with the noble nose stood apart from all this light-heartedness in a black silk gown and a gold chain. She seemed unwilling to make a fool of herself, though she encouraged the others in their extravagance with nods and cryptic smiles. Even so, she had put pink ribbons in her cap. A family consisting of a father, a fair-haired mother, and two young daughters, suitably dressed for the occasion, had just come in and were shedding their outer wraps with the help of host and hostess.
source_text: The company was assembling in the panelled hall, and made a brilliant show in the light of the acetylene candles against the dark background. Mr. Britling in a black velvet cloak and black silk tights was a deeper shade among the shadows; the high lights were Miss Corner and her sister, in glittering garments of peacock green and silver that gave a snake-like quality to their lithe bodies. They were talking to the German tutor, who had become a sort of cotton Cossack, a spectacled Cossack in buff and bright green. Mrs. Britling was dignified and beautiful in a purple djibbah, and her stepson had become a handsome still figure of black and crimson. Teddy had contrived something elaborate and effective in the Egyptian style, with a fish-basket and a cuirass of that thin matting one finds behind washstands; the small boys were brigands, with immensely baggy breeches and cummerbunds in which they had stuck a selection of paper-knives and toy pistols and similar weapons. Mr. Carmine and his young man had come provided with real Indian costumes; the feeling of the company was that Mr. Carmine was a mullah. The aunt-like lady with the noble nose stood out amidst these levities in a black silk costume with a gold chain. She refused, it seemed, to make herself absurd, though she encouraged the others to extravagance by nods and enigmatical smiles. Nevertheless she had put pink ribbons in her cap. A family of father, golden-haired mother, and two young daughters, sympathetically attired, had just arrived, and were discarding their outer wrappings with the assistance of host and hostess.

text: When he was gone, Mr. Britling felt an overwhelming urge to take a personal part in the country’s physical efforts. He wanted, as he put it, to “get his skin into it.” He had already come to think the volunteer movement a hopeless affair. After resisting any volunteer movement at all for a good while, the War Office had decided to acknowledge it in a way that made it absurd. The volunteers were to have no officers and no uniforms that could even faintly be mistaken for those of the regular army, so that if the Germans ever invaded, they would be able to see exactly what they were facing from miles off. Wilkins saw his idea of an entire nation enrolled, registered, and badged according to ability, his vision of everyone falling into place in one great voluntary national effort, dismissed as the foolish dream of that most ignorant of human creatures, a “novelist.” _Punch_ made gentle fun of him; he was shown wearing a ridiculous cocked hat of his own invention, and designing cocked hats for everyone else. Wilkins was told to “shut up” in a flood of anonymous letters, and both publicly and privately told to “leave things to Kitchener.” To shout in clear, loud tones, “leave things to Kitchener,” and then go off to the theatre or the river or an automobile excursion, was widely felt at the time to be the proper behavior for a patriot. There was a very widespread belief that to become a volunteer when one should be quietly doing nothing at all was, in some obscure way, a form of disloyalty....
source_text: When he had gone, the craving for a personal share in the nation's physical exertions became overpowering in Mr. Britling. He wanted, he felt, to "get his skin into it." He had decided that the volunteer movement was a hopeless one. The War Office, after a stout resistance to any volunteer movement at all, decided to recognise it in such a manner as to make it ridiculous. The volunteers were to have no officers and no uniforms that could be remotely mistaken for those of the regulars, so that in the event of an invasion the Germans would be able to tell what they had to deal with miles away. Wilkins found his conception of a whole nation, all enrolled, all listed and badged according to capacity, his dream of every one falling into place in one great voluntary national effort, treated as the childish dreaming of that most ignorant of all human types, a "novelist." _Punch_ was delicately funny about him; he was represented as wearing a preposterous cocked hat of his own design, designing cocked hats for every one. Wilkins was told to "shut up" in a multitude of anonymous letters, and publicly and privately to "leave things to Kitchener." To bellow in loud clear tones "leave things to Kitchener," and to depart for the theatre or the river or an automobile tour, was felt very generally at that time to be the proper conduct for a patriot. There was a very general persuasion that to become a volunteer when one ought to be just modestly doing nothing at all, was in some obscure way a form of disloyalty....

text: “Campanella,” said Hugh, without showing the least interest in Miss Corner. “Nothing ever changes in England, because the people who want to change things always change their minds before they change anything else. I’ve spent the last six months in London talking. They call it studying art. Before that I was a science student, and I want to be one again. Don’t you think, sir, there’s something about science? It’s steadier than anything else in the world.”
source_text: "Campanella," said Hugh, without betraying the slightest interest in Miss Corner. "Nothing changes in England, because the people who want to change things change their minds before they change anything else. I've been in London talking for the last half-year. Studying art they call it. Before that I was a science student, and I want to be one again. Don't you think, Sir, there's something about science--it's steadier than anything else in the world?"

text: That had always been his unmistakable manner: a little stiff, a little comical, and invariably neat, pink, and methodical. The boys had taken to him without hesitation, Mrs. Britling had liked him, everyone had found him a pleasant fellow. He never complained of anything except picnics. But picnics he did object to; the sudden exodus of the household into the open country for the eating of cold meals, with neither knife nor fork, in the grave middle hours of the day. He remonstrated with Mr. Britling, respectfully but with great firmness. It was, he maintained, part of their agreement that he should have a cooked meal at midday. Otherwise his Magen became confused and upset. In the evening he could not eat with any proper seriousness or enjoyment....
source_text: That had been his constant effect; a little stiff, a little absurd, and always clean and pink and methodical. The boys had liked him without reserve, Mrs. Britling had liked him; everybody had found him a likeable creature. He never complained of anything except picnics. But he did object to picnics; to the sudden departure of the family to wild surroundings for the consumption of cold, knifeless and forkless meals in the serious middle hours of the day. He protested to Mr. Britling, respectfully but very firmly. It was, he held, implicit in their understanding that he should have a cooked meal in the middle of the day. Otherwise his Magen was perplexed and disordered. In the evening he could not eat with any gravity or profit....

text: The inveterate cheerfulness of his sunny disposition had once more lifted Mr. Ainsworth clean out of the pit of pessimism. He had already stood on the edge of the phrase as he strayed across the sedgy fields toward Saffron Market; now it returned to him again, like a rightful sovereign come home from banishment.
source_text: The invincible hopefulness of his sanguine temperament had now got Mr. Britling well out of the pessimistic pit again. Already he had been on the verge of his phrase while wandering across the rushy fields towards Market Saffron; now it came to him again like a legitimate monarch returning from exile.

text: Everyone appeared singularly merry and bright of eye. Mr. Darren found a curious lift of spirit in all this uncommon brilliance of dress and in the easy give-and-take of service; it made everyone seem more open, more plainspoken, more direct. Even Mr. Hale had shown a solid sort of good looks that Mr. Darren had not before suspected, and young Hale now left no uncertainty at all as to his own handsome face. Mr. Darren quite forgot his errand and his rank, and indeed forgot matters in general, in a foolish contentment that his golden pheasants matched the gleam of the warm, smiling girl beside him. So he sat down at her side—“Anywhere you like,” said Mrs. Hale—with far less embarrassment than he would have felt in his ordinary clothes at so unmistakable a confession of preference. And there was something in her eyes, impossible to define and yet deeply gratifying, that told him that now, when he had slipped free of the stern, square commands of his patriotic tailor in New York, she had made some discovery of him.
source_text: Everybody seemed unusually gay and bright-eyed. Mr. Direck found something exhilarating and oddly exciting in all this unusual bright costume and in this easy mutual service; it made everybody seem franker and simpler. Even Mr. Britling had revealed a sturdy handsomeness that had not been apparent to Mr. Direck before, and young Britling left no doubts now about his good looks. Mr. Direck forgot his mission and his position, and indeed things generally, in an irrational satisfaction that his golden pheasants harmonised with the glitter of the warm and smiling girl beside him. And he sat down beside her--"You sit anywhere," said Mrs. Britling--with far less compunction than in his ordinary costume he would have felt for so direct a confession of preference. And there was something in her eyes, it was quite indefinable and yet very satisfying, that told him that now he escaped from the stern square imperatives of his patriotic tailor in New York she had made a discovery of him.

text: October gave way to November, and day by day Mr. Britling was compelled to confront new aspects of the war, to think it through and think it through again, to see his first judgments tested, bent out of shape, and displaced. His mind ranged farther and wider, and sank more deeply, until all his earlier writing seemed to him painfully superficial, no more than an automatic string of obvious remarks prompted by the war’s startling surprise. As his ideas grew subtler and more profound, they became harder to put into words; he spoke less, and at table he grew absent-minded and irritable. To two people in particular Mr. Britling found his real thoughts impossible to express: Mr. Direck and Mr. Van der Pant.
source_text: October passed into November, and day by day Mr. Britling was forced to apprehend new aspects of the war, to think and rethink the war, to have his first conclusions checked and tested, twisted askew, replaced. His thoughts went far and wide and deeper--until all his earlier writing seemed painfully shallow to him, seemed a mere automatic response of obvious comments to the stimulus of the war's surprise. As his ideas became subtler and profounder, they became more difficult to express; he talked less; he became abstracted and irritable at table. To two people in particular Mr. Britling found his real ideas inexpressible, to Mr. Direck and to Mr. Van der Pant.

text: "You can't imagine how things contract when one is right up against them. One doesn’t know what is going on even a few miles away from us until the newspapers arrive. Then, by reading a little between the lines and making some daring guesses, we manage to fit our own small fragment of experience into some larger pattern. Of course I have thought at times about Teddy. But oddly enough, I have never really thought of him as being out here. It is strange, I know, but I haven’t. I can’t imagine why....
source_text: "You can't imagine how things narrow down when one is close up against them. One does not know what is happening even within a few miles of us, until we get the newspapers. Then, with a little reading between the lines and some bold guessing, we fit our little bit of experience with a general shape. Of course I've wondered at times about Teddy. But oddly enough I've never thought of him very much as being out here. It's queer, I know, but I haven't. I can't imagine why....

text: And one afternoon, while Mr. Britling was taking his customary walk, a neglected-looking pheasant with a white collar set a train of reverie moving in his mind. The world of Matching’s Easy was becoming crowded now with such elderly birds. Would that ever come again after the war? He pictured his son Hugh as an old man, telling the little ones about parks and preserves and game laws, and footmen and butlers, and the wonderful game of golf, and how, all at once, Mars came striding through the land in khaki, and all these things dimmed and disappeared, so that in the end it was found they were gone....
source_text: And one afternoon as Mr. Britling took his constitutional walk, a reverie was set going in his mind by the sight of a neglected-looking pheasant with a white collar. The world of Matching's Easy was getting full now of such elderly birds. Would _that_ go on again after the war? He imagined his son Hugh as a grandfather, telling the little ones about parks and preserves and game laws, and footmen and butlers and the marvellous game of golf, and how, suddenly, Mars came tramping through the land in khaki and all these things faded and vanished, so that presently it was discovered they were gone....

text: Mr. Direck’s mind was caught in a confusion only too familiar in that year of tragedy. He was held fast by a contradiction; like most Americans then, his feelings were plainly pro-Ally, and like so many of them he was firmly convinced that it would be both wrong and impossible for the United States to enter the war. His sympathies were warmly with the Dower House and the little dependent cottage; he would have been moved to tears at the sight of the Stars and Stripes woven together with the three other great red, white, and blue banners that were leading the world against German imperialism and militarism, but even so his mind refused to follow that impulse. Set against all such emotions was something very deep in Mr. Direck’s makeup, an idea of America that had settled almost without his noticing it: America as a state apart from the Old World order, a fresh beginning for mankind, something far too precious and too noble to be dragged into even the most righteous of European wars. America was to be the start of humanity’s blending, neither German nor British nor French nor national in any old sense. She was meant to be the great experiment in peace and common sense. She must keep civilisation and social order outside this conflict, serve as a refuge for all those finer things that perish under strain and disorder; it was her duty to preserve the standards of life and the demands of humanity in conquered provinces and prison camps, to be the healer and judge, the voice of protest and not the striking hand. Surely there were already enough striking hands.
source_text: Mr. Direck's mind was in a perplexity only too common during the stresses of that tragic year. He was entangled in a paradox; like a large majority of Americans at that time his feelings were quite definitely pro-Ally, and like so many in that majority he had a very clear conviction that it would be wrong and impossible for the United States to take part in the war. His sympathies were intensely with the Dower House and its dependent cottage; he would have wept with generous emotion to see the Stars and Stripes interwoven with the three other great banners of red, white and blue that led the world against German imperialism and militarism, but for all that his mind would not march to that tune. Against all these impulses fought something very fundamental in Mr. Direck's composition, a preconception of America that had grown almost insensibly in his mind, the idea of America as a polity aloof from the Old World system, as a fresh start for humanity, as something altogether too fine and precious to be dragged into even the noblest of European conflicts. America was to be the beginning of the fusion of mankind, neither German nor British nor French nor in any way national. She was to be the great experiment in peace and reasonableness. She had to hold civilisation and social order out of this fray, to be a refuge for all those finer things that die under stress and turmoil; it was her task to maintain the standards of life and the claims of humanitarianism in the conquered province and the prisoners' compound, she had to be the healer and arbitrator, the remonstrance and not the smiting hand. Surely there were enough smiting hands.

text: It was not that he loved her. He knew it almost as plainly as if he had said it to himself: he did not. And yet, just as clearly, though more quietly and in a way both of them ignored, it was equally true that she was scarcely in love with him either. The whole arrangement was satisfying precisely because it lacked love and any real feeling, and because it was friendly in its emotional emptiness. It left their minds free to handle all the phrases and gestures of love without pain. She could call up tears and ecstasies as easily as one calls for servants, and he could play the lover without ever feeling that he had surrendered control. They filled a need each had long had in the other’s life—if only she could restrain that strange gift she had for jealousy and for the impulse to torment. There, he thought, she violated the understanding between them and introduced actual realities, and it was this small crack that had grown into the serious break it now was. He knew that whenever she was reasonable she feared that break and wanted to mend it as much as he did. But the deep, simple force of the instincts they had tacitly agreed to leave uncrossed washed away the very supports of their reconciliation.
source_text: It wasn't that he was in love with her. He knew almost as clearly as though he had told himself as much that he was not. But then, on the other hand, it was equally manifest in its subdued and ignored way that as a matter of fact she was hardly more in love with him. What constituted the satisfactoriness of the whole affair was its essential unlovingness and friendly want of emotion. It left their minds free to play with all the terms and methods of love without distress. She could summon tears and delights as one summons servants, and he could act his part as lover with no sense of lost control. They supplied in each other's lives a long-felt want--if only, that is, she could control her curious aptitude for jealousy and the sexual impulse to vex. There, he felt, she broke the convention of their relations and brought in serious realities, and this little rift it was that had widened to a now considerable breach. He knew that in every sane moment she dreaded and wished to heal that breach as much as he did. But the deep simplicities of the instincts they had tacitly agreed to bridge over washed the piers of their reconciliation away.

text: The park had a cultivated wildness, like nature in some old Italian painting; dappled fallow deer stood close by and regarded the two men without fear; the path descended through oak trees and a few dwarfed fronds of bracken to a small, lingering stream, which paused now and then to make a game of ponds and waterfalls and to carry along a little fleet of water-lily leaves; and then their course bent in an idle curve toward the cedars and shrubberies of the great house. To an American eye the house seemed low and sprawling, and its red-brick chimneys rose in a long line like infantry drawn up in the open. As they turned the corner to the front of the house by a path cut through the laurel bushes, there was, to the right, a glimpse of a garden bright with flowers and of terraces beyond.
source_text: The park had a trim wildness like nature in an old Italian picture; dappled fallow deer grouped close at hand and looked at the two men fearlessly; the path dropped through oak trees and some stunted bracken to a little loitering stream, that paused ever and again to play at ponds and waterfalls and bear a fleet of water-lily leaves; and then their way curved round in an indolent sweep towards the cedars and shrubberies of the great house. The house looked low and extensive to an American eye, and its red-brick chimneys rose like infantry in open order along its extended line. There was a glimpse of flower-bright garden and terraces to the right as they came round the corner to the front of the house through a path cut in the laurel bushes.

text: What struck him about the streets he walked through was their deep unease. There were plainly fewer omnibuses and less traffic of every kind, but there was an extraordinary number of people wandering aimlessly. The flow on the pavements had become annoyingly slow. More people were standing around, and fewer were moving about their affairs. This was especially true of the women he noticed. Many of them seemed to have come in from the suburbs and the edges of London, carried along by some undefined expectation, unable to remain at home.
source_text: His impression of the streets through which he passed was an impression of great unrest. There were noticeably fewer omnibuses and less road traffic generally, but there was a quite unusual number of drifting pedestrians. The current on the pavements was irritatingly sluggish. There were more people standing about, and fewer going upon their business. This was particularly the case with the women he saw. Many of them seemed to have drifted in from the suburbs and outskirts of London in a state of vague expectation, unable to stay in their homes.

text: There had been a time when he could rouse himself to such a basic sense of duty and pass through periods of the strictest self-discipline. Yet, always, in the end, he would be caught off guard from some unexpected direction. At last he had stopped hoping for any victory so absolute. He had been willing to think that in recent years age, and a growing habit of wisdom, had somewhat slowed the headlong force of his will; that if he had not conquered it, he had at least reduced it to some extent. But this latest folly was surely the worst of all. To hurtle through this patient world with—how much did the car weigh? A ton, certainly, and perhaps more—heedless of every danger. Not only to himself, but to others as well. At that thought, he gripped the steering wheel again. Once more he saw the cyclist’s bowed back, once more he sensed rather than saw the hot, furious advance of the motor bicycle, and then, for a long instant, he drove helplessly at the wall....
source_text: There had been a time when he could exhort himself to such fundamental charge and go through phases of the severest discipline. Always at last to be taken by surprise from some unexpected quarter. At last he had ceased to hope for any triumph so radical. He had been content to believe that in recent years age and a gathering habit of wisdom had somewhat slowed his leaping purpose. That if he hadn't overcome he had at least to a certain extent minimised it. But this last folly was surely the worst. To charge through this patient world with--how much did the car weigh? A ton certainly and perhaps more--reckless of every risk. Not only to himself but others. At this thought, he clutched the steering wheel again. Once more he saw the bent back of the endangered cyclist, once more he felt rather than saw the seething approach of the motor bicycle, and then through a long instant he drove helplessly at the wall....

text: “This contrast between Heinrich’s caution and Teddy’s easy-going temper, when one comes to consider it, is, I suppose, one of the most fundamental in the world. It runs through everything. It is bound up with education, with statesmanship, with morals. Will you create, or will you accept? Those are the two extremes in all such matters. I suppose wisdom’s answer to that is, as with all wise answers, a compromise. I suppose one must accept, and then make as much of it as one can.... Have you spoken at all to my eldest son?”
source_text: "This contrast between Heinrich's carefulness and Teddy's easy-goingness, come to look at it, is I suppose one of the most fundamental in the world. It reaches to everything. It mixes up with education, statecraft, morals. Will you make or will you take? Those are the two extreme courses in all such things. I suppose the answer of wisdom to that is, like all wise answers, a compromise. I suppose one must accept and then make all one can of it.... Have you talked at all to my eldest son?"

text: It lessened Mr. Direck’s pleasure in this stream of information a little that it was carrying them away from the rest of the company. He wanted to see more of his newly discovered cousin, and to find out what the baby and the Bengali gentleman—whom plainly one must not call “colored”—and the lady with the large nose and all the other enigmas were likely to do. But instead, Mr. Britling was conducting him off by himself, as though giving him a tour of the house, and talking so quickly and on so many different subjects that there was no opening to put in a question, let alone to bring up the business Mr. Direck had come over to discuss.
source_text: It detracted a little from Mr. Direck's appreciation of this flow of information that it was taking them away from the rest of the company. He wanted to see more of his new-found cousin, and what the baby and the Bengali gentleman--whom manifestly one mustn't call "coloured"--and the large-nosed lady and all the other inexplicables would get up to. Instead of which Mr. Britling was leading him off alone with an air of showing him round the premises, and talking too rapidly and variously for a question to be got in edgeways, much less any broaching of the matter that Mr. Direck had come over to settle.

text: So she moved about among several houses, all of which at least were always glad to receive her. In the first days of each stay she would work wonders of tact and keep a strict watch on her tongue. But soon the demons of controversy and pride would gain the upper hand. She would begin, softly but resolutely, to correct; she would begin to object to the manner and quality of the treatment she was receiving. It was quite usual for her visits to end in silent fury on both sides, both host and guest alike. The striking thing was that this mute anger never lasted. However much she could irritate, she could never truly give offense. After an interval, during which her name was never spoken, people would begin to ask how Cousin Jane was getting on.... A cautious exchange of letters would then begin, slowly leading to a new invitation.
source_text: So she circulated about a number of houses which at any rate always welcomed her coming. In the opening days of each visit she performed marvels of tact, and set a watch upon her lips. Then the demons of controversy and dignity would get the better of her. She would begin to correct, quietly but firmly, she would begin to disapprove of the tone and quality of her treatment. It was quite common for her visit to terminate in speechless rage both on the side of host and of visitor. The remarkable thing was that this speechless rage never endured. Though she could exasperate she could never offend. Always after an interval during which she was never mentioned, people began to wonder how Cousin Jane was getting on.... A tentative correspondence would begin, leading slowly up to a fresh invitation.

text: "I wanted to write everything about Jewell to his mother, or his sweetheart, or someone; I wanted to revel in praising him, to say all the things I now truly see in him that I never fully realized before, but I haven’t even had that small consolation. He was a Poor Law child; he was brought up in one of those dreadful places between Sutton and Banstead in Surrey. I’ve told you about all his sweethearts. *Soldiers Three* was his Bible; he was always bursting into 'Tipperary,' and he never quite managed the tune, nor did he ever learn more than three lines of it. He spiced all his speech with 'b----y'; it was his jewel, his ruby. But he had the courage of a robin or a squirrel; I never knew him afraid, or anything but good-humored. Hardships and humiliations only made him more talkative. And he would have gone hungry just to have something to give away.
source_text: "I wanted to write all about Jewell to his mother or his sweetheart or some one; I wanted to wallow in his praises, to say all the things I really find now that I thought about him, but I haven't even had that satisfaction. He was a Poor Law child; he was raised in one of those awful places between Sutton and Banstead in Surrey. I've told you of all the sweethearting he had. 'Soldiers Three' was his Bible; he was always singing 'Tipperary,' and he never got the tune right nor learnt more than three lines of it. He laced all his talk with 'b----y'; it was his jewel, his ruby. But he had the pluck of a robin or a squirrel; I never knew him scared or anything but cheerful. Misfortunes, humiliations, only made him chatty. And he'd starve to have something to give away.

text: And this double refraction of his mind, by which the intensified and particular Britling merely revealed a larger, impersonal Britling underneath, brought with it a duplication of conscience and of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was accountable for his private honour, his debts, the Dower House he had created, and the rest; but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. From that point of view the world was his egg. He had a subconscious illusion that he himself had laid it. He had a subconscious fear that he had let it grow cold and that it was spoiled. He felt an urgent need to sit upon it and warm it. The richness and variety of his conversation were largely due to this conviction; it was a continual effort to spread his mental feathers over the work before him....
