source_text: And this double refraction of his mind by which a concentrated and individualised Britling did but present a larger impersonal Britling beneath, carried with it a duplication of his conscience and sense of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was answerable for his private honour and his debts and the Dower House he had made and so on, but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. The world from the latter point of view was his egg. He had a subconscious delusion that he had laid it. He had a subconscious suspicion that he had let it cool and that it was addled. He had an urgency to incubate it. The variety and interest of his talk was largely due to that persuasion, it was a perpetual attempt to spread his mental feathers over the task before him....

text: It was over the chessboard that they first began to sense how widely their sympathies diverged. Mr. Alderton played with a dazzling surface, much liberality, and utter instability; he was always ready the instant his opponent had moved, and only afterward considered the position. His thoughts were generally sounder than his play. Mrs. Alderton seemed, in some strange way, his natural counterweight: where he was restless, she was composed. She never hurried a move, and never showed the least inclination to yield. Patiently, firmly, by prudence and reflection, without brilliance, without a slip, she had beaten him so steadily at chess that at last it provoked such appalling storms of temper in him that he was forced to abandon the game. After each of these occasions he took great pains to assure her that he had been merely angry with himself. Yet he felt, and would not quite allow himself to think—though she gathered it from stray bursts of heat—that this was not the whole truth of his outbreak.
source_text: It was over the chess-board that they first began to discover their extensive difficulties of sympathy. Mr. Britling's play was characterised by a superficial brilliance, much generosity and extreme unsoundness; he always moved directly his opponent had done so--and then reflected on the situation. His reflection was commonly much wiser than his moves. Mrs. Britling was, as it were, a natural antagonist to her husband; she was as calm as he was irritable. She was never in a hurry to move, and never disposed to make a concession. Quietly, steadfastly, by caution and deliberation, without splendour, without error, she had beaten him at chess until it led to such dreadful fits of anger that he had to renounce the game altogether. After every such occasion he would be at great pains to explain that he had merely been angry with himself. Nevertheless he felt, and would not let himself think (while she concluded from incidental heated phrases), that that was not the complete truth about the outbreak.

text: In those days he had no kindly creed of compromise. He had bent the knee to no “domesticated God,” but spoke of “pitiless truth”; he had accepted no comfortable, pseudo-aristocratic social order, but dreamed of a democracy “mewing its mighty youth” such as the world had never seen. He believed that his mind would help build this grand national _imago_, winged and divine, out of the awkward, crawling, snobbish, comfort-loving caterpillar that was Victorian England. His life had begun with such dreams, and perhaps their radiance had helped bring him quick success. Then his wife died, and he married again and somehow grew more concerned with his income; then the rather costly first of the eight experiences drained away so much of his imaginative force, and the second drained away so much more, and there were quarrels and rancors, and he lost his bearings, and the years went by. He had not failed. In fact, among his generation he was counted a success. Only he, in the watches of the night, could judge what sort of success it was. He was known far and wide, and known respectably; he prospered. Much had come, oh, by some strange luck, but everything was ruined by his unbreakable flaws. Under that empty, enviable surface there was the pain of waste. Waste, waste, waste—his heart, his imagination, his wife, his son, his country, his car....
source_text: In those days he had had no amiable doctrine of compromise. He had truckled to no "domesticated God," but talked of the "pitiless truth"; he had tolerated no easy-going pseudo-aristocratic social system, but dreamt of such a democracy "mewing its mighty youth" as the world had never seen. He had thought that his brains were to do their share in building up this great national _imago_, winged, divine, out of the clumsy, crawling, snobbish, comfort-loving caterpillar of Victorian England. With such dreams his life had started, and the light of them, perhaps, had helped him to his rapid success. And then his wife had died, and he had married again and become somehow more interested in his income, and then the rather expensive first of the eight experiences had drained off so much of his imaginative energy, and the second had drained off so much, and there had been quarrels and feuds, and the way had been lost, and the days had passed. He hadn't failed. Indeed he counted as a success among his generation. He alone, in the night watches, could gauge the quality of that success. He was widely known, reputably known; he prospered. Much had come, oh! by a mysterious luck, but everything was doomed by his invincible defects. Beneath that hollow, enviable show there ached waste. Waste, waste, waste--his heart, his imagination, his wife, his son, his country--his automobile....

text: How we had squandered Ireland! The riches that lay there, the gallantry and talent, the chance for friendship—all of it was being left to the Ulster politicians and the Tory women to taint and ruin, just as we had left India to the habits of the garrulous women of the army and the repressive instincts of our mandarins. We were too lazy, too careless. We spent our idle days leaving everything to someone else. Was this the incurable British quality, just as it was the incurable Britling quality?
source_text: How we had wasted Ireland! The rich values that lay in Ireland, the gallantry and gifts, the possible friendliness, all these things were being left to the Ulster politicians and the Tory women to poison and spoil, just as we left India to the traditions of the chattering army women and the repressive instincts of our mandarins. We were too lazy, we were too negligent. We passed our indolent days leaving everything to somebody else. Was this the incurable British, just as it was the incurable Britling, quality?

text: His aim in the book he was preparing to write was to think through the possible forms of government that might bring the world a steadier and more rational direction. He still believed in democracy, yet he was coming more and more to see that democracy had not yet found its own method. It must seize upon men’s consciences; it must arm itself with institutions not yet fully shaped. Endless years of patient thought, of experiment, of debate stretched ahead of mankind before that grand conception could be made actual, and before justice, the established and proven right, could truly govern the earth.
source_text: His purpose in the book he was beginning to write was to reason out the possible methods of government that would give a stabler, saner control to the world. He believed still in democracy, but he was realising more and more that democracy had yet to discover its method. It had to take hold of the consciences of men, it had to equip itself with still unformed organisations. Endless years of patient thinking, of experimenting, of discussion lay before mankind ere this great idea could become reality, and right, the proven right thing, could rule the earth.

text: It doesn't matter whether we actually turn out a fighting force or not," said Wilkins. "What matters is that everyone is enthusiastic now—serious, too. Everyone is ready to put on some sort of uniform and accept some sort of orders. The thing is to seize them while they are still willing. This is the moment to get the country arranged and organised, so it will be ready for the internal strains that are bound to come later. But there is no sign at all of any desire to welcome this general offering. It's as if this war were some special entertainment to which only the War Office's most select friends were invited. And I don't accept that the national volunteers would be useless—even from a military standpoint. There are plenty of fit men of our age, and men of the right age who are better kept at home—armament workers, for instance—and there are all the boys below the age. They may not be below the age before it's all over....
source_text: "It doesn't matter whether we really produce a fighting force or not," said Wilkins. "Everybody now is enthusiastic--and serious. Everybody is willing to put on some kind of uniform and submit to some sort of orders. And the thing to do is to catch them in the willing stage. Now is the time to get the country lined up and organised, ready to meet the internal stresses that are bound to come later. But there's no disposition whatever to welcome this universal offering. It's just as though this war was a treat to which only the very select friends of the War Office were to be admitted. And I don't admit that the national volunteers would be ineffective--even from a military point of view. There are plenty of fit men of our age, and men of proper age who are better employed at home--armament workers for example, and there are all the boys under the age. They may not be under the age before things are over...."

text: There was a brief pause. Father and son had each gone over this scene many times in their heads, and now they discovered that several sentences, which had seemed especially apt in rehearsal, were no longer needed. Mr. Britling scratched his cheek with the end of his pen. “I’m glad you want to go, Hugh,” he said.
source_text: There was a little pause. Both father and son had rehearsed this scene in their minds several times, and now they found that they had no use for a number of sentences that had been most effective in these rehearsals. Mr. Britling scratched his cheek with the end of his pen. "I'm glad you want to go, Hugh," he said.

text: That was the outward state of affairs. Is it any surprise that, in German eyes, the hour appeared to have struck for the triumphant declaration of German supremacy in the world? A day or two before the shooting in Dublin, the murder at Sarajevo had been thrust once more to the front of world events by an Austrian ultimatum to Serbia of the most extreme severity. From the moment that ultimatum was issued, the road to Armageddon lay open and inescapable before Europe’s feet. After the Dublin clash, there was no turning back. For a week, Europe was taken up with what was little more than the recitation of a formula. Austria could not retract her unconditional threats without confessing error and defeat; Russia could not abandon Serbia without disgrace; Germany stood behind Austria; France was tied to Russia by a long alliance of mutual support; and England could not bear to see France destroyed or a clamorous and menacing rival grow still stronger. Perhaps Germany believed Russia would yield to her, perhaps she relied on England’s uncertainty and weak perplexity; both were in the calculation, but above all she relied on war. She relied on war, and since no nation on earth had ever been so thoroughly equipped for war as she was, she also relied on victory.
source_text: Such were the appearances of things. Can it be wondered if it seemed to the German mind that the moment for the triumphant assertion of the German predominance in the world had come? A day or so before the Dublin shooting, the murder of Sarajevo had been dragged again into the foreground of the world's affairs by an ultimatum from Austria to Serbia of the extremest violence. From the hour when the ultimatum was discharged the way to Armageddon lay wide and unavoidable before the feet of Europe. After the Dublin conflict there was no turning back. For a week Europe was occupied by proceedings that were little more than the recital of a formula. Austria could not withdraw her unqualified threats without admitting error and defeat, Russia could not desert Serbia without disgrace, Germany stood behind Austria, France was bound to Russia by a long confederacy of mutual support, and it was impossible for England to witness the destruction of France or the further strengthening of a loud and threatening rival. It may be that Germany counted on Russia giving way to her, it may be she counted on the indecisions and feeble perplexities of England, both these possibilities were in the reckoning, but chiefly she counted on war. She counted on war, and since no nation in all the world had ever been so fully prepared in every way for war as she was, she also counted on victory.

text: Then, after a pause, he said, “The Treaty of Bucharest was a wicked treaty. It must be undone. Whatever this German King of Bulgaria does, that treaty must be undone, and the Bulgarians must be brought together again as one people. They must have to answer for themselves, whatever punishment they deserve; they must have nothing more, whatever reward they earn.”
source_text: Then, after a pause, "The Treaty of Bucharest was an evil treaty. It must be undone. Whatever this German King of Bulgaria does, that treaty must be undone and the Bulgarians united again into one people. They must have themselves, whatever punishment they deserve, they must have nothing more, whatever reward they win."

text: For Cora, at any rate, the war had never ceased to have its true meaning. She remained as certain as ever that German militarism, and the German assault upon Belgium and France, was the war’s central fact. All lesser questions she set aside with impatience. She went on asking why America did not go to battle. “We are fighting for Belgium. Will you not fight for the Dutch and Norwegian ships? Will you not even fight for your own ships that the Germans are sending to the bottom?”
source_text: For Cissie, at any rate, had not lost touch with the essential issues of the war. She was as clear as ever that German militarism and the German attack on Belgium and France was the primary subject of the war. And she dismissed all secondary issues. She continued to demand why America did not fight. "We fight for Belgium. Won't you fight for the Dutch and Norwegian ships? Won't you even fight for your own ships that the Germans are sinking?"

text: “Take my word for it,” said Aunt Wetherby in her most solemn manner. “The man is mad. It will be shown in the end. He will finish his life in an asylum—as a lunatic. I have known it in my bones for years, and I have said as much in confidence.... I was aware of things.... To those few friends I could depend on not to take me amiss.... At last, at any rate, I am free to speak plainly.”
source_text: "Mark my words," said Aunt Wilshire in her most impressive tones. "He is insane. It will be proved to be so. He will end his days in an asylum--as a lunatic. I have felt it myself for years and said so in private.... Knowing what I did.... To such friends as I could trust not to misunderstand me.... Now at least I can speak out.

text: Mr. Renshaw now had a clear purpose in his mind, and he pressed on more quickly, though still with scrupulous care and certainty. He was already within a mile or so of Market Saffron before he remembered that he had in effect made an appointment with himself at Alderley Grange. He found himself confronted by two opposing intentions. He weighed various possibilities.
source_text: Mr. Britling had a definite purpose now in his mind, and he drove faster, but still very carefully and surely. He was already within a mile or so of Market Saffron before he remembered that he had made a kind of appointment with himself at Pyecrafts. He stared at two conflicting purposes. He turned over certain possibilities.

text: It was as they rushed past the picturesque walls beneath the great old trees that ring Brandismead Park. It was only a slight error in judging distance. Ahead of them, and well to the left, a postman was riding a bicycle; coming toward them, with that strange effect of relentless anger peculiar to motor-cycles, was a motor cyclist. At first Mr. Britling thought he would not try to squeeze between the two; then he decided he would hasten and do it; then he went back to his first decision; and then it seemed to him that he was moving so fast he must inevitably run over the postman. His instinct to avoid that sent the car sharply across the motor cyclist’s path. “Oh, my God!” cried Mr. Britling. “My God!” He twisted the wheel over and, in a frenzy, flung his feet among his levers.
source_text: It was as they swept by the picturesque walls under the big old trees that encircle Brandismead Park. It was nothing but a slight miscalculation of distances. Ahead of them and well to the left, rode a postman on a bicycle; towards them, with that curious effect of implacable fury peculiar to motor cycles, came a motor cyclist. First Mr. Britling thought that he would not pass between these two, then he decided that he would hurry up and do so, then he reverted to his former decision, and then it seemed to him that he was going so fast that he must inevitably run down the postman. His instinct not to do that pulled the car sharply across the path of the motor cyclist. "Oh, my God!" cried Mr. Britling. "My God!" twisted his wheel over and distributed his feet among his levers dementedly.

text: Now I ask you to be patient with me and to listen to the end. There was a time, in the war’s earlier days, when patience was nearly impossible, because over us there hung the fear of ruin and defeat. That fear need haunt us no longer. What we dreaded has already come to pass. Now that we sit together in spirit beside the broken bodies of our dead, surely we may be as patient as the hills.
source_text: _Now I want you to be patient with me and hear me out. There was a time in the earlier part of this war when it was hard to be patient because there hung over us the dread of losses and disaster. Now we need dread no longer. The dreaded thing has happened. Sitting together as we do in spirit beside the mangled bodies of our dead, surely we can be as patient as the hills._

text: She was saying, “It should not have been known that I was here. There are spies everywhere. Everywhere. There is even one now—or something very like a spy. They pretend it is a hot-water bottle. A pretext.... Oh, yes, I admit it is absurd. But I have been hunted by spies. Unending spies. Endless, endless spies. Their tricks are almost unbelievable.... He has never forgiven me....”
source_text: She was saying: "It should not have been known I was here. There are spies everywhere. Everywhere. There is a spy now--or a lump very like a spy. They pretend it is a hot-water bottle. Pretext.... Oh, yes! I admit--absurd. But I have been pursued by spies. Endless spies. Endless, endless spies. Their devices are almost incredible.... He has never forgiven me....

text: Perhaps Mr. Britling had said that Germans were, after all, human beings, or that if England had paid attention to Matthew Arnold in the eighties, its officers might by now have combined efficiency with courage and good temper. Perhaps he himself had let a little corrosive acid into what he said. At once the hatred blazed up again. “Who are _you_, sir? What are _you_, sir? What right have _you_, sir? What claim have _you_, sir?”...
source_text: Perhaps Mr. Britling had remarked that Germans were after all human beings, or that if England had listened to Matthew Arnold in the 'eighties our officers by this time might have added efficiency to their courage and good temper. Perhaps he had himself put a touch of irritant acid into his comment. Back flared the hate. "Who are _you_, Sir? What are _you_, Sir? What right have _you_, Sir? What claim have _you_, Sir?"...

text: "There I am puzzled again. His work is known even in Germany. His articles are reprinted in German and Austrian journals. One would expect him to have a certain authoritative manner. One would expect some discussion at table of philosophy and aesthetics.... Yet it is not so. When I ask him questions, they are often not seriously answered. At times it is as though he dislikes the questions I put to him. Yesterday I asked him whether he agreed or disagreed with Mr. Bernard Shaw. He merely said—I wrote it down in my memoranda—he said, ‘Oh! Mixt Pickles.’ What is one to make of that?—Mixt Pickles!"...
source_text: "There again I am puzzled. His work is known even in Germany. His articles are reprinted in German and Austrian reviews. You would expect him to have a certain authority of manner. You would expect there to be discussion at the table upon questions of philosophy and aesthetics.... It is not so. When I ask him questions it is often that they are not seriously answered. Sometimes it is as if he did not like the questions I askt of him. Yesterday I askt of him did he agree or did he not agree with Mr. Bernard Shaw. He just said--I wrote it down in my memoranda--he said: 'Oh! Mixt Pickles.' What can one understand of that?--Mixt Pickles!"...

text: For the moment Mr. Direck could think of no further excuse. Yet it was quite plain in his mind that something must be done; he wished he knew of someone in London who could send him a telegram of recall, to save him from committing himself once more to the casual fortunes of Mr. Britling’s car. And then another interest rose to the forefront of his thoughts.
source_text: For the moment Mr. Direck couldn't think of any further excuse. But it was very clear in his mind that something must happen; he wished he knew of somebody who could send a recall telegram from London, to prevent him committing himself to the casual destinies of Mr. Britling's car again. And then another interest became uppermost in his mind.

text: Letty’s eyes were bright and ardent, but her voice remained soft and restrained. After a pause, she continued in the same matter-of-fact tone. “You see now, Cissie, why I hold so tightly to the belief that Teddy is alive. If Teddy is alive, then even if he’s wounded, there will still be some happiness in it—and all this won’t be just rot. But if he’s dead, then everything is so horribly foolish and cruel from beginning to end—”
source_text: Letty's eyes were bright and intense, but her voice was soft and subdued. She went on after a pause in the same casual voice. "You see now, Cissie, why I cling to the idea that Teddy is alive. If Teddy is alive, then even if he is wounded, he will get some happiness out of it--and all this won't be--just rot. If he is dead then everything is so desperately silly and cruel from top to bottom--"

text: You can’t stand aside from things of that sort," said Lady Frensham with the greatest fervor, "when the country is on the very brink of civil war.... You people who try to make out there isn’t a serious crisis when there plainly is one will be held more to account than anyone else—when the civil war does come. It won’t spare you. Remember that!
source_text: "You can't keep out of things like that," said Lady Frensham with the utmost gusto, "when the country's on the very verge of civil war.... You people who try to pretend there isn't a grave crisis when there is one, will be more accountable than any one--when the civil war does come. It won't spare you. Mark my words!"

text: And having uttered this judgment, Mr. Britling, a little astonished at the swift force of his own anger once it had been set free, suddenly broke off, made a renouncing gesture with his arms, and, as though seized by an idea, hurried out of her room and out of the house to where Gladys was waiting. He climbed into her and set her going, and after some difficulty with the gears, caused by the violence of his emotion, he turned her round and drove away with her—crushing the corner of a little bed of snapdragon as he swung—bearing him back with sullen, painstaking persistence to the Dower House and its newspapers and correspondence and vexations, and to that gnawing, unreasonable sense of the world’s emptiness and aimlessness which he had hoped Mrs. Harrowdean might soothe. And the farther he got from Mrs. Harrowdean, the harsher and more unjust it seemed to him that he had been to her.
source_text: And having delivered himself of this judgment Mr. Britling, a little surprised at the rapid vigour of his anger, once he had let it loose, came suddenly to an end of his words, made a renunciatory gesture with his arms, and as if struck with the idea, rushed out of her room and out of the house to where Gladys stood waiting. He got into her and started her up, and after some trouble with the gear due to the violence of his emotion, he turned her round and departed with her--crushing the corner of a small bed of snapdragon as he turned--and dove her with a sulky sedulousness back to the Dower House and newspapers and correspondence and irritations, and that gnawing and irrational sense of a hollow and aimless quality in the world that he had hoped Mrs. Harrowdean would assuage. And the further he went from Mrs. Harrowdean the harsher and unjuster it seemed to him that he had been to her.

text: It was rare that he truly appeared to be seeing the war; few people ever did. To most of the world it arrived only as an endless mass of disjointed, noisy, bewildering impressions. But all the while he was at least straining to see it, to make it simpler, to draw out its essential meaning until it might be grasped as something epic and intelligible, as an issue that could be clearly stated....
source_text: It was rare that he really seemed to be seeing the war; few people saw it; for most of the world it came as an illimitable multitude of incoherent, loud, and confusing impressions. But all the time he was at least doing his utmost to see the war, to simplify it and extract the essence of it until it could be apprehended as something epic and explicable, as a stateable issue....

text: Each of these gentlemen arrived bearing, with him, some hint or shadow of criticism directed against England. It was one thing for Mr. Britling himself to be severe upon England; that was an Englishman’s right and prerogative. But to catch Mr. Van der Pant casting doubts upon British efficiency, or to fancy in Mr. Direck those pale, elevated American airs of superiority toward war, was scarcely less trying than to hear Mrs. Harrowdean uttering unfriendly things about Edith. It stirred an even sharper instinct of protection.
source_text: Each of these gentlemen brought with him the implication or the intimation of a critical attitude towards England. It was all very well for Mr. Britling himself to be critical of England; that is an Englishman's privilege. To hear Mr. Van der Pant questioning British efficiency or to suspect Mr. Direck of high, thin American superiorities to war, was almost worse than to hear Mrs. Harrowdean saying hostile things about Edith. It roused an even acuter protective emotion.

text: Our guns behind us were searching for the German guns. It was the worst din imaginable, like a pair of giant madmen battering away among enormous pots and pans. They fired all kinds of shells, including stink shells and Jack Johnsons, and though our corner did not get much of the stink shells, there was a faint bite of chlorine in the air all afternoon. Most of the stink shells fell short. We had no masks, but we improvised a kind of protection with our handkerchiefs. It did not help much. It was rather like the chemistry room after Heinrich and the boys had been mixing up things. For the most part I was occupied helping the wounded men. Then, all at once, there was a lull. After that someone said the Germans were coming, and I caught sight of them.
source_text: "Our guns behind felt for the German guns. It was the damnest racket. Like giant lunatics smashing about amidst colossal pots and pans. They fired different sorts of shells; stink shells as well as Jack Johnsons, and though we didn't get much of that at our corner there was a sting of chlorine in the air all through the afternoon. Most of the stink shells fell short. We hadn't masks, but we rigged up a sort of protection with our handkerchiefs. And it didn't amount to very much. It was rather like the chemistry room after Heinrich and the kids had been mixing things. Most of the time I was busy helping with the men who had got hurt. Suddenly there came a lull. Then some one said the Germans were coming, and I had a glimpse of them.

text: She must go into the cottage and kiss Nell, and tuck the parcel away somewhere out of sight until she could discover some poor fellow in uniform to whom she might send it. In her sorrow she had been merciless to Nell. In the selfishness of grief she had handled Nell as carelessly as though she were a chair or a table, forgetting that Nell too might be tired, might still be dreaming of happiness ahead. Nell still had the lover’s part to play, and her man was already in khaki. There could be no year for her such as Elsie had known in those days before the war laid its shadow over the earth. Before Nell was married peace must come, and peace was still very far off. And Derry too would have to take his chance....
source_text: She must go into the cottage and kiss Cissie, and put away that parcel out of sight until she could find some poor soldier to whom she could send it. She had been pitiless towards Cissie in her grief. She had, in the egotism of her sorrow, treated Cissie as she might have treated a chair or a table, with no thought that Cissie might be weary, might dream of happiness still to come. Cissie had still to play the lover, and her man was already in khaki. There would be no such year as Letty had had in the days before the war darkened the world. Before Cissie's marrying the peace must come, and the peace was still far away. And Direck too would have to take his chances....

text: His thoughts wandered back again to those ancient heresies of the Gnostics and the Manichaeans, which regarded the God of the world as wholly evil and sought escape from the black corruption of existence only through the severest abstinence, avoidance, and distortion. For a time his soul descended into the inhospitable darkness of those desperate creeds. “I who have loved life,” he murmured, and for a moment might have believed that he would have preferred never to have had a son....
source_text: His mind drifted back once more to those ancient heresies of the Gnostics and the Manichæans which saw the God of the World as altogether evil, which sought only to escape by the utmost abstinences and evasions and perversions from the black wickedness of being. For a while his soul sank down into the uncongenial darknesses of these creeds of despair. "I who have loved life," he murmured, and could have believed for a time that he wished he had never had a son....

text: The two sides took up their positions, and at once a sort of pattern became visible to Mr. Vale. In the middle stood Mr. Arden and the captain of the other side, with the ball lying between them. They were about to bully off and begin the match. On either hand of each centre were ranged four more forwards. They all had the eager, capable air of young people full of spirit, and Mr. Vale regretted that he had not had more exercise to give some warrant to his own brisk look. Just behind each centre forward hovered one of the Arden boys. Farther back, on either side, came a dimmer line of three backs, people of milder temper or greater age. Among them was Mr. Fenwick, who was thought to possess excellent natural gifts for hockey, though little practice. Mr. Fenwick stood behind Mr. Vale. Mrs. Arden occupied the position of centre back. Then, in one corner of Mr. Vale’s side, there was a little girl of six or seven, and in the half-circle before the goal a lady in a motoring dust-coat and a very small man whom Mr. Vale had not noticed before. Mr. Lionel Carfax, stripped down to his braces, which were handsomely worked with Oriental embroidery, kept goal for our side.
source_text: The sides took their places for the game, and a kind of order became apparent to Mr. Direck. In the centre stood Mr. Britling and the opposing captain, and the ball lay between them. They were preparing to "bully off" and start the game. In a line with each of them were four other forwards. They all looked spirited and intent young people, and Mr. Direck wished he had had more exercise to justify his own alert appearance. Behind each centre forward hovered one of the Britling boys. Then on each side came a vaguer row of three backs, persons of gentler disposition or maturer years. They included Mr. Raeburn, who was considered to have great natural abilities for hockey but little experience. Mr. Raeburn was behind Mr. Direck. Mrs. Britling was the centre back. Then in a corner of Mr. Direck's side was a small girl of six or seven, and in the half-circle about the goal a lady in a motoring dust coat and a very short little man whom Mr. Direck had not previously remarked. Mr. Lawrence Carmine, stripped to the braces, which were richly ornamented with Oriental embroidery, kept goal for our team.

text: He liked to come into some quiet village and observe the knots of sturdy, khaki-clad youngsters moving about their work, the tethered horses, the subdued bustle, the occasional flashes of guns and ammunition wagons. Everywhere one went now there were soldiers, and still more soldiers. Men were streaming steadily into Flanders, and soon into Gallipoli as well, yet it seemed to make no difference to the vast numbers still training at home. He felt a pleasant thrill at the obvious increase in military traffic on the railways; he liked the promise and air of mystery in the long trains of flatcars carrying tarpaulin-covered wagons, carts, and guns that he would meet on his way to Liverpool Street station. In the silence of the night he could imagine defeat, but when he saw the men, when he traveled through the country, it was impossible to believe in any ending except victory....
source_text: He liked to come into some quiet village and note the clusters of sturdy khaki-clad youngsters going about their business, the tethered horses, the air of subdued bustle, the occasional glimpses of guns and ammunition trains. Wherever one went now there were soldiers and still more soldiers. There was a steady flow of men into Flanders, and presently to Gallipoli, but it seemed to have no effect upon the multitude in training at home. He was pleasantly excited by the evident increase in the proportion of military material upon the railways; he liked the promise and mystery of the long lines of trucks bearing tarpaulin-covered wagons and carts and guns that he would pass on his way to Liverpool Street station. He could apprehend defeat in the silence of the night, but when he saw the men, when he went about the land, then it was impossible to believe in any end but victory....

text: “That’s exactly how I feel about this whole war business. There isn’t any real death over here. Everything is arranged and packed away neatly. Even accidents are cushioned on all sides. If a man were thrown from a horse here, he’d be lying in bed, comfortable, before long.... And there—it’s like some other planet. It’s outside.... I’m going outside.... Instead of there being no death anywhere, death is everywhere out there, outside. We’ll have to use all our ingenuity to kill one another. It’s the opposite of this world.”
source_text: "That's just how I feel about this war business. There's no real death over here. It's laid out and boxed up. And accidents are all padded about. If one got a toss from a horse here, you'd be in bed and comfortable in no time.... And there; it's like another planet. It's outside.... I'm going outside.... Instead of there being no death anywhere, it is death everywhere, outside there. We shall be using our utmost wits to kill each other. A kind of reverse to this world."

text: The night was raw and desolate, yet thick with stars. By now he knew the lay of the town so well that he could tell exactly where every bomb in the barrage had come down. There was the corner where walls had been scorched black and beams charred bare, and where three wounded horses had been burned to death in a barn; there the line of houses, some shattered, some scarcely touched, where a maimed child had screamed for two hours before anyone could reach her beneath the wreckage that held her fast and carry her off to hospital. In the wan glow of the hooded street lamps he saw everywhere the dark cavities and jagged breaks of broken windows, sometimes in plenty, sometimes only as isolated and uncanny wounds among the otherwise untouched houses. He had already gone to see many of the sufferers in the small cottage hospital where Aunt Bertram had only just died. She was the eleventh to die. In all, fifty-seven people had been killed or wounded in this brilliant German exploit. They were all civilians, and only twelve of them were men.
source_text: The night was cold and bleak, but full of stars. He had already mastered the local topography, and he knew now exactly where all the bombs that had been showered upon the place had fallen. Here was the corner of blackened walls and roasted beams where three wounded horses had been burnt alive in a barn, here the row of houses, some smashed, some almost intact, where a mutilated child had screamed for two hours before she could be rescued from the debris that had pinned her down, and taken to the hospital. Everywhere by the dim light of the shaded street lamps he could see the black holes and gaps of broken windows; sometimes abundant, sometimes rare and exceptional, among otherwise uninjured dwellings. Many of the victims he had visited in the little cottage hospital where Aunt Wilshire had just died. She was the eleventh dead. Altogether fifty-seven people had been killed or injured in this brilliant German action. They were all civilians, and only twelve were men.

text: His lamp still burned, though for some time he had no longer been writing by its light. Imperceptibly, day had arrived and done away with his need for that private ring of yellow glow. Colour had returned to the world—clean, pearly colour, clear and distinct as a child’s glance or a girl’s voice—and a golden shred of cloud hung in the sky above the church tower. A mist lay over the pond, a soft grey mist no more than a yard high. A covey of partridges ran and paused and ran again through the dewy grass beyond his garden railings. There were very many partridges this year, because there had been so little shooting. Further off in the meadow a hare sat upright, motionless as stone. A horse neighed.... Wave after wave of warmth and light swept before the sunrise across the world of Matching’s Easy. It was as though there were nothing in the world but morning and sunrise.
source_text: His lamp was still burning, but for some time he had not been writing by the light of his lamp. Insensibly the day had come and abolished his need for that individual circle of yellow light. Colour had returned to the world, clean pearly colour, clear and definite like the glance of a child or the voice of a girl, and a golden wisp of cloud hung in the sky over the tower of the church. There was a mist upon the pond, a soft grey mist not a yard high. A covey of partridges ran and halted and ran again in the dewy grass outside his garden railings. The partridges were very numerous this year because there had been so little shooting. Beyond in the meadow a hare sat up as still as a stone. A horse neighed.... Wave after wave of warmth and light came sweeping before the sunrise across the world of Matching's Easy. It was as if there was nothing but morning and sunrise in the world.

text: “What makes this visit even more interesting to me, if that is possible,” he said to Mrs. Britling, “than it would otherwise be, is that this Essex country is the country where my maternal grandmother was brought up, and also, going back a long way, where my mother’s father’s people came from. My mother’s father’s people were very early New England people indeed.... Well, no. If I said _Mayflower_ that wouldn’t be true. But it would be near enough. They were Essex Hinkinsons. That’s who they were. I must be, at the very least, a good third Essex myself. My grandmother was an Essex Corner, and I must admit I’ve had some notion—”
source_text: "What makes this visit even more interesting if possible to me," he said to Mrs. Britling, "than it would otherwise be, is that this Essex country is the country in which my maternal grandmother was raised, and also long way back my mother's father's people. My mother's father's people were very early New England people indeed.... Well, no. If I said _Mayflower_ it wouldn't be true. But it would approximate. They were Essex Hinkinsons. That's what they were. I must be a good third of me at least Essex. My grandmother was an Essex Corner, I must confess I've had some thought--"

text: It was a village swollen with hospitality. Not just the inn, but all the houses around the linden tree—the shoemaker’s, the postmistress’s, the white house farther on, every house in fact except the pastor’s—were filled with Adam Meyer’s summer guests. And over and among them moved Adam Meyer himself, making sure they ate well, making sure they rested well, making sure they had music and did not go without moonlight—a host who lost sight of profit in his hospitality, an innkeeper with an artist’s passion for his inn.
source_text: It was a village distended with hospitalities. Not only the inn but all the houses about the place of the linden tree, the shoe-maker's, the post-mistress's, the white house beyond, every house indeed except the pastor's house, were full of Adam Meyer's summer guests. And about it and over it went and soared Adam Meyer, seeing they ate well, seeing they rested well, seeing they had music and did not miss the moonlight--a host who forgot profit in hospitality, an inn-keeper with the passion of an artist for his inn.

text: Then he found himself launched into an explanation of certain matters to Mr. Britling. Safe in his bed, he could fancy that he was speaking very slowly and with great care while Mr. Britling listened; already he was more than halfway into dreamland, or he could not have imagined anything so unbelievable.
source_text: Then he found himself under way with an exposition of certain points to Mr. Britling. In the security of his bed he could imagine that he was talking very slowly and carefully while Mr. Britling listened; already he was more than half way to dreamland or he could not have supposed anything so incredible.

text: "Do you really think our catastrophe will spare the Atlantic for your sake? Do you imagine there can be any freedom of the seas beyond the freedom we ourselves uphold, except the freedom to strike at you? For forty years the British fleet has shielded all of America from attack out of Europe. Your Monroe Doctrine now hides in its shadow....
source_text: "Do you think that our disaster will leave the Atlantic for you? Do you fancy there is any Freedom of the Seas possible beyond such freedom as we maintain, except the freedom to attack you? For forty years the British fleet has guarded all America from European attack. Your Monroe doctrine skulks behind it now....

text: “There is only one way to learn a business like driving an automobile,” said Mr. Harbury outside his own front door, as he settled himself in the driver’s seat. “And that is to make up your mind from the very first that you will run no risks. Go slowly if you please. Pull up and consider whenever you are uncertain. But do nothing in haste, and allow yourself no blunders.”
source_text: "There is only one way to master such a thing as driving an automobile," said Mr. Britling outside his front door, as he took his place in the driver's seat, "and that is to resolve that from the first you will take no risks. Be slow if you like. Stop and think when you are in doubt. But do nothing rashly, permit no mistakes."

text: “Almost two-thirds of the papers read before our Massachusetts association during my time with it have concerned that very point,” said Mr. Vandeleer at last. “One of our most influential men was able to engage a remarkably capable and well-trained young lady from the University of Michigan to prepare a digest of all those representative utterances. We are having it issued in a most tasteful manner as the club volume for our autumn season. The drift of her conclusions is that religion is not the same thing as religions. That most religions are old, while religion itself is forever new.... Well, to put it simply, religion is the continual rediscovery of that Great Thing Beyond.... What that Great Thing may be is called by many names, but if you know it is there and if you keep in mind that it is there, then you have religion.... That is about the way she worked it out.... I shall send you the book as soon as a copy reaches me.... I should not pretend to state it as clearly as she does. She has a genuinely analytical mind. But it is one of the most suggestive little books I have ever seen. It grips you and makes you think.”
source_text: "Very nearly two-thirds of the papers read before our Massachusetts society since my connection with it, have dealt with that very question," Mr. Direck began. "And one of our most influential members was able to secure the services of a very able and highly trained young woman from Michigan University, to make a digest of all these representative utterances. We are having it printed in a thoroughly artistic mariner, as the club book for our autumn season. The drift of her results is that religion isn't the same thing as religions. That most religions are old and that religion is always new.... Well, putting it simply, religion is the perpetual rediscovery of that Great Thing Out There.... What the Great Thing is goes by all sorts of names, but if you know it's there and if you remember it's there, you've got religion.... That's about how she figured it out.... I shall send you the book as soon as a copy comes over to me.... I can't profess to put it as clearly as she puts it. She's got a real analytical mind. But it's one of the most suggestive lil' books I've ever seen. It just takes hold of you and _makes_ you think."

text: The realities were bad enough without any rumours added. At one dinner table after another Mr. Britling heard direct accounts of cruelty and spite. One story that stayed with him was of British prisoners being carried into Germany and then, at a station, separated from their French fellow sufferers and forced to “run the gauntlet” back to their train through lines of German soldiers, fists and bayonets raised against them. There were also credible reports of the same men having their overcoats taken from them in freezing weather, being set upon by dogs, split away from their own countrymen, and shoved in among Russians and Poles with whom they could not even speak. In this way Lissauer’s Song of Hate bore fruit in a thousand acts of cruelty toward wounded, helpless men. The English had cheated great Germany out of another easy victory like that of ’71. They must be made to pay. That, too clearly, was the psychological process. At one German station a woman had stepped from a train, crossed the platform, and spat in the face of a wounded Englishman.... And such things were by no means the monopoly of one side or the other. At a journalistic gathering Mr. Britling met a small, pale, determined woman who had lately been nursing in the north of France. She told of wounded men lying among the coal in coal-sheds, of nurses being far too few and supplies lacking of every kind, of an outright refusal to allow any help in such matters to reach the German “swine.” ... “Why have they come here? Let our own boys have it first. Why couldn’t they stay in their own country? Let the filth die.”
source_text: The realities were evil enough without any rumours. Over various dinner-tables Mr. Britling heard this and that first-hand testimony of harshness and spite. One story that stuck in his memory was of British prisoners on the journey into Germany being put apart at a station from their French companions in misfortune, and forced to "run the gauntlet" back to their train between the fists and bayonets of files of German soldiers. And there were convincing stories of the same prisoners robbed of overcoats in bitter weather, baited with dogs, separated from their countrymen, and thrust among Russians and Poles with whom they could hold no speech. So Lissauer's Hate Song bore its fruit in a thousand cruelties to wounded and defenceless men. The English had cheated great Germany of another easy victory like that of '71. They had to be punished. That was all too plainly the psychological process. At one German station a woman had got out of a train and crossed a platform to spit on the face of a wounded Englishman.... And there was no monopoly of such things on either side. At some journalistic gathering Mr. Britling met a little white-faced, resolute lady who had recently been nursing in the north of France. She told of wounded men lying among the coal of coal-sheds, of a shortage of nurses and every sort of material, of an absolute refusal to permit any share in such things to reach the German "swine." ... "Why have they come here? Let our own boys have it first. Why couldn't they stay in their own country? Let the filth die."

text: Massacres of boys—that is the real heart of modern war. The slaughter of the young. It is the ruin of mankind’s inheritance, the squandering of all the life and all the resources of the future on the hate and greed of the present. Fools and rogues, politicians, swindlers, and those who profit from men’s suspicions and their unthinking, generous rage, bring wars about; the passivity and meekness of most men allow them to do it. Are you and I to endure such things until the entire structure of our civilization, so slowly and so painfully built, is utterly brought down?
source_text: _Massacres of boys! That indeed is the essence of modern war. The killing off of the young. It is the destruction of the human inheritance, it is the spending of all the life and material of the future upon present-day hate and greed. Fools and knaves, politicians, tricksters, and those who trade on the suspicions and thoughtless, generous angers of men, make wars; the indolence and modesty of the mass of men permit them. Are you and I to suffer such things until the whole fabric of our civilisation, that has been so slowly and so laboriously built up, is altogether destroyed?_

text: Then his hour arrived. The small, impressively upholstered lady who had supped at the Dower House the previous night launched herself bravely upon Mrs. Teddy. From the confusion of that encounter the ball came spinning within Mr. Durrant’s reach. Where, and in what fashion, was he to strike? A brief pause for thought was only natural.
source_text: Then came his moment. The little formidably padded lady who had dined at the Dower House overnight, made a gallant attack upon Mrs. Teddy. Out of the confusion of this clash the ball spun into Mr. Direck's radius. Where should he smite and how? A moment of reflection was natural.

text: Names were called out. “I’ll take Cecily!” Mr. Direck heard Mr. Britling say quite early. As the opposing sides were chosen, they split into two groups. There appeared to be some difficulty over certain names. Mr. Britling, indicating the more robust-looking of the Indian gentlemen, said, “You, Sir.”
source_text: Names were shouted. "I'll take Cecily!" Mr. Direck heard Mr. Britling say quite early. The opposing sides as they were picked fell into two groups. There seemed to be difficulties about some of the names. Mr. Britling, pointing to the more powerful looking of the Indian gentlemen, said, "_You_, Sir."

text: “That diary-hunting business is exactly the kind of thing that makes this war so interesting to think about. Everything that might possibly help bring victory is being worked out and tested. The Germans are much more interested in psychology than we are, just as they are more interested in war itself, though they don’t seem especially clever at it. So they try to make all their men understand the war, while our fellows are singing ‘Tipperary.’ But what the men actually write down isn’t the lovely material it ought to be; most of them just make lists of their meals, and some of the diaries are nothing but inventories of what was eaten, while many have set down the most damaging accounts of outrages and looting. The French are translating and publishing them. The Germans would give anything now to have those foolish diaries back. And now they’ve issued an order that no one is to go into battle carrying any written papers at all.... Our people became so eager about keeping records and about the value of accidental writings that one of the main jobs after a German attack had failed was to hook in the documentary dead and see what they were carrying.... It’s a strange sport, this body fishing. You have a kind of triple hook on a rope, and you cast it and drag it along. They do the same. The other day a body near Hooghe was hooked by both sides, and they ended up in a tug-of-war, with a sharpshooter or two taking shots whenever our men got too worked up. Several men were hit. The Irish—it was an Irish regiment—got him, or at least they got the larger part of him....”
source_text: "That diary-hunting strategy is just the sort of thing that makes this war intellectually fascinating. Everything is being thought out and then tried over that can possibly make victory. The Germans go in for psychology much more than we do, just as they go in for war more than we do, but they don't seem to be really clever about it. So they set out to make all their men understand the war, while our chaps are singing 'Tipperary.' But what the men put down aren't the beautiful things they ought to put down; most of them shove down lists of their meals, some of the diaries are all just lists of things eaten, and a lot of them have written the most damning stuff about outrages and looting. Which the French are translating and publishing. The Germans would give anything now to get back these silly diaries. And now they have made an order that no one shall go into battle with any written papers at all.... Our people got so keen on documenting and the value of chance writings that one of the principal things to do after a German attack had failed had been to hook in the documentary dead, and find out what they had on them.... It's a curious sport, this body fishing. You have a sort of triple hook on a rope, and you throw it and drag. They do the same. The other day one body near Hooghe was hooked by both sides, and they had a tug-of-war. With a sharpshooter or so cutting in whenever our men got too excited. Several men were hit. The Irish--it was an Irish regiment--got him--or at least they got the better part of him....

text: “They won’t see that inefficiency is a kind of dishonesty. If a man does not steal sixpence, they think it a small matter if he wastes half a crown. There is wisdom for you! From the standpoint of a nation at war, sixpence is merely a fifth part of half a crown....
source_text: "They won't see that inefficiency is a sort of dishonesty. If a man doesn't steal sixpence, they think it a light matter if he wastes half a crown. Here follows wisdom! _From the point of view of a nation at war, sixpence is just a fifth part of half a crown_....

text: “It is only with an effort that I can remember what life felt like in the spring of 1914. Do you remember Heinrich and his attempt to make a table chart of the roses, so that we could sit outside the barn and read the names of all the roses in the barn court? Like the mountain charts they have laid out on tables in Switzerland. What an unimaginable thing that seems now! For all I know, I shot Heinrich the other night. For all I know, he is one of the lumps we counted after the attack had fallen back.
source_text: "It's only by an effort that I can recall how life felt in the spring of 1914. Do you remember Heinrich and his attempt to make a table chart of the roses, so that we could sit outside the barn and read the names of all the roses in the barn court? Like the mountain charts they have on tables in Switzerland. What an inconceivable thing that is now! For all I know I shot Heinrich the other night. For all I know he is one of the lumps that we counted after the attack went back.

text: “But now just see what a good little bruddykins I am. I’ve seen a fight, a proper thrilling fight, and I’ve saved it till last to tell you about.... It was a fight in the air. And the British won. It started with a German machine appearing, very tiny and high up, gliding toward our lines a long way off to the left. We could tell it was German because of the black cross; they put a black Iron Cross on the wings and tail of every aeroplane, and our officer could make it out through his glasses. (He let me have a look.) Then suddenly, whack, whack, whack, a row of little smoke puffs came behind it, and then one in front of it, which meant our anti-aircraft guns were having a shot at it. Then, just as suddenly, Archibald broke off, and we could see the British machine buzzing across the German’s path. It was exactly like two birds wheeling in the sky. Or wasps. They buzzed like wasps. There was a faint crackling sound—like brushing your hair in frosty weather. They were firing at one another. Then our lieutenant cried out, ‘Hit, by Jove!’ and gave the glasses to Park, then at once wanted them back. He says he saw pieces flying off the machine.”
source_text: "But now see what a good bruddykins I am. I have seen a fight, a real exciting fight, and I have kept it to the last to tell you about.... It was a fight in the air. And the British won. It began with a German machine appearing, very minute and high, sailing towards our lines a long way to the left. We could tell it was a German because of the black cross; they decorate every aeroplane with a black Iron Cross on its wings and tail; that our officer could see with his glasses. (He let me look.) Suddenly whack, whack, whack, came a line of little puffs of smoke behind it, and then one in front of it, which meant that our anti-aircraft guns were having a go at it. Then, as suddenly, Archibald stopped, and we could see the British machine buzzing across the path of the German. It was just like two birds circling in the air. Or wasps. They buzzed like wasps. There was a little crackling--like brushing your hair in frosty weather. They were shooting at each other. Then our lieutenant called out, 'Hit, by Jove!' and handed the glasses to Park and instantly wanted them back. He says he saw bits of the machine flying off.

text: It is astonishing how murderous a man can feel in a bayonet rush. We are made to yell; indeed, we are urged to yell. The result is to numb the more civilized parts of the mind. One stops asking questions—about anything at all. One turns into a bayonet man. As I spring forward, I picture soft, well-fed fellows waiting ahead of me, and I am possessed by a wish to finish them off cleanly. This sort of business—
source_text: "It is surprising how bloodthirsty one feels in a bayonet charge. We have to shout; we are encouraged to shout. The effect is to paralyse one's higher centres. One ceases to question--anything. One becomes a 'bayoneteer.' As I go bounding forward I imagine fat men, succulent men ahead, and I am filled with the desire to do them in neatly. This sort of thing--"

text: “They’ve had such a wonderful time. And Teddy is the most darling husband. And they have that pretty little house and the most entertaining baby. And every Sunday they play hockey. And Teddy does his work. And every week is just like the one before it. It is all simply heavenly. Always the same heaven. Each Sunday a new week of heaven begins again. And this, you see, isn’t heaven; it’s earth. They don’t realize it, but they are growing bored. I’ve been watching them, and they are getting terribly bored. It’s heartbreaking to see, because they’re almost the dearest people to me. Teddy used to keep making endless jokes about the house and the baby and his work and Letty, and now—he’s exhausted every possible joke. Only now and then does he come up with a new one. It’s like spring flowers and then—summer. And Letty sits around and doesn’t sing. They want something new to happen.... And then there are Mr. and Mrs. Britling. They love each other. Far more than Mrs. Britling imagines, or Mr. Britling either, for that matter. Once, I suppose, things were heavenly for them too. Until quite suddenly it began to happen to them that nothing new ever happened....”
source_text: "They've had a lovely time. And Teddy is the dearest husband. And they have a sweet little house and a most amusing baby. And they play hockey every Sunday. And Teddy does his work. And every week is like every other week. It is just heavenly. Just always the same heavenly. Every Sunday there is a fresh week of heavenly beginning. And this, you see, isn't heaven; it is earth. And they don't know it but they are getting bored. I have been watching them, and they are getting dreadfully bored. It's heart-breaking to watch, because they are almost my dearest people. Teddy used to be making perpetual jokes about the house and the baby and his work and Letty, and now--he's made all the possible jokes. It's only now and then he gets a fresh one. It's like spring flowers and then--summer. And Letty sits about and doesn't sing. They want something new to happen.... And there's Mr. and Mrs. Britling. They love each other. Much more than Mrs. Britling dreams, or Mr. Britling for the matter of that. Once upon a time things were heavenly for them too, I suppose. Until suddenly it began to happen to them that nothing new ever happened...."

text: Day after day Mr. Britling followed the shifting fortunes of the war, impatient, bewildered, yet never losing faith in Britain’s eventual victory. The country was still crowded with soldiers and still basking in summer sunshine. A second haymaking made up for the poor first crop, the wheat harvest was magnificent, and the great fig tree at the corner of the Dower House had never yielded so generously, nor produced figs so plump and juicy....
source_text: Day after day Mr. Britling traced the swaying fortunes of the conflict, with impatience, with perplexity, but with no loss of confidence in the ultimate success of Britain. The country was still swarming with troops, and still under summer sunshine. A second hay harvest redeemed the scantiness of the first, the wheat crops were wonderful, and the great fig tree at the corner of the Dower House had never borne so bountifully nor such excellent juicy figs....

text: “My motoring is not above criticism,” said Mr. Alden with a candor that seemed almost judicial. “But I have only lately come into this machine for myself—after a good many years of hiring cars, that indolent contrivance by which people provide car, chauffeur, fuel, tires, insurance, and Heaven knows what else for a fixed sum each month. It used to weary me beyond expression. I cannot now understand how I endured it so long. They would send me one after another of these trim, disdainful lads, who raced when I wished to crawl and crawled when I wished to race, who took every curve on the wrong hand at full tilt, and pursued dogs and poultry as if it were the finest amusement in the world, and all the rest of it. They would not even suffer me to choose my own roads. I should have bought a car years ago and driven it myself, if it had not been for that abominable business of the crank one had to turn when the engine failed. But here, as you see, is a tolerably inexpensive motor with an electric starter—American, I need hardly tell you. And here I am, at last, proceeding at my own tempo.”
source_text: "My driving leaves something to be desired," said Mr. Britling with an air of frank impartiality. "But I have only just got this car for myself--after some years of hired cars--the sort of lazy arrangement where people supply car, driver, petrol, tyres, insurance and everything at so much a month. It bored me abominably. I can't imagine now how I stood it for so long. They sent me down a succession of compact, scornful boys who used to go fast when I wanted to go slow, and slow when I wanted to go fast, and who used to take every corner on the wrong side at top speed, and charge dogs and hens for the sport of it, and all sorts of things like that. They would not even let me choose my roads. I should have got myself a car long ago, and driven it, if it wasn't for that infernal business with a handle one had to do when the engine stopped. But here, you see, is a reasonably cheap car with an electric starter--American, I need scarcely say. And here I am--going at my own pace."

text: “That’s strange.... But perhaps a son is different. Now that I think of it—I’ve never spoken of Mary.... Not to anyone, ever. I’d never realized that before. But I haven’t. I couldn’t. No. Losing a lover is something private, something for oneself. I’ve been through that, you see. But a son is more separate from you. Completely. And more your own doing. It isn’t losing something inside you; it’s losing a hope and a pride.... Once, when I was a little boy, I made a drawing very carefully. It took me a long time.... Then a big boy tore it up. For no reason at all. Just because he was cruel.... That—that was exactly like losing Hugh....”
source_text: "That's queer.... But perhaps--a son is different. Now I come to think of it--I've never talked of Mary.... Not to any one ever. I've never thought of that before. But I haven't. I couldn't. No. Losing a lover, that's a thing for oneself. I've been through that, you see. But a son's more outside you. Altogether. And more your own making. It's not losing a thing _in_ you; it's losing a hope and a pride.... Once when I was a little boy I did a drawing very carefully. It took me a long time.... And a big boy tore it up. For no particular reason. Just out of cruelty.... That--that was exactly like losing Hugh...."

text: It was no kind of learned or formal dancing. It was a spontaneous answer to the leaping American music that Mr. Britling played out with his feet. You kept the rhythm, and beyond that you did as your own impulses dictated. If you had a partner, you joined hands; you fluttered toward and away from one another; you moved together down the long floor; you entangled yourselves in romantic attractions and repulsions with other couples. There was no objection to dancing alone. Teddy, for instance, danced by himself in order to work out certain Egyptian gestures that were beginning to sprout in his mind. Nor was there any objection to your joining hands in a cheerful serpent....
source_text: It wasn't any sort of taught formal dancing. It was a spontaneous retort to the leaping American music that Mr. Britling footed out. You kept time, and for the rest you did as your nature prompted. If you had a partner you joined hands, you fluttered to and from one another, you paced down the long floor together, you involved yourselves in romantic pursuits and repulsions with other couples. There was no objection to your dancing alone. Teddy, for example, danced alone in order to develop certain Egyptian gestures that were germinating in his brain. There was no objection to your joining hands in a cheerful serpent....

text: “But all the time, your mind is on the Germans. Here, they are about a quarter of a mile off, or nearly that. When you catch sight of them, they look like a low wall of variegated stone—except that the stones are sandbags. The Germans have theirs in black and white, so you can’t make out which are loopholes and which are the black bags. Our men haven’t been nearly so ingenious, and the War Office’s fondness for uniformity has left us with nothing but white bags. It certainly looks tidier, no doubt. But it also makes our loopholes obvious. For a while, black sandbags weren’t allowed. The Germans did some sniping, but not much. Only one of our men was hit, by a stray bullet that passed through the sandbag at the top of the parapet. He only got a cut in the neck, and it didn’t stop him walking back. They shelled the trenches half a mile to our left, though, and that seemed pretty lively. The sandbags were flying about. But the men keep low, and it looks worse than it really is. The weather was fine and pleasant, as General French always says. And after three days and nights of cramped living and petty tasks, one in the front trench and two a little farther back, and then two days in support, we came back—and here we are again, waiting for our second go.
source_text: "But all the time you want to know about the Germans. They are a quarter of a mile away at this part, or nearly a quarter of a mile. When you snatch a peep at them it is like a low parti-coloured stone wall--only the stones are sandbags. The Germans have them black and white, so that you cannot tell which are loopholes and which are black bags. Our people haven't been so clever--and the War Office love of uniformity has given us only white bags. No doubt it looks neater. But it makes our loopholes plain. For a time black sandbags were refused. The Germans sniped at us, but not very much. Only one of our lot was hit, by a chance shot that came through the sandbag at the top of the parapet. He just had a cut in the neck which didn't prevent his walking back. They shelled the trenches half a mile to the left of us though, and it looked pretty hot. The sandbags flew about. But the men lie low, and it looks worse than it is. The weather was fine and pleasant, as General French always says. And after three days and nights of cramped existence and petty chores, one in the foremost trench and two a little way back, and then two days in support, we came back--and here we are again waiting for our second Go.

text: She had been lying there in a condition of passive surrender, her swollen, wet eyes open and indifferent to all things. But those sudden movements called her back to attention. She began to watch the robin, and to observe how it cast a sidelong look at her and seemed to be considering a closer approach. She made the slightest possible movement, and at once the little creature was in a projecting spray of hawthorn berries above her head.
source_text: She had been lying in a state of passive abandonment, her swollen wet eyes open, regardless of everything. But those quick movements caught her back to attention. She began to watch the robin, and to note how it glanced sidelong at her and appeared to meditate further approaches. She made an almost imperceptible movement, and straightway the little creature was in a projecting spray of berried hawthorn overhead.

text: What pleasant folk the Germans had been—sun-loving, leaf-loving people, to whom “Frisch Auf” had seemed the most natural of national watchwords. Mr. Britling found himself thinking of the particular Germans who had composed that company: the men with their comically militant little hats of green and blue, each with an inevitable feather stuck smartly into the band behind; the good-natured roundnesses beneath their upturned moustaches; the fair, settled women, so sensible about the comforts of living and so gentle with the children; their sincere delight in scenery and Art and Great Writers; their habitual inclination to burst into song; their pliancy beneath the guiding hands of Herr Alois Mayer. He thought of the soft South German country, broad and beautiful as it unrolled away, of the small tidy towns with their red roofs, the ancient castles, the large flourishing farms, the clearly signposted footpaths, the welcoming inns, and the innocent profusion of Aussichtsthurms....
source_text: Right pleasant people those Germans had been, sun and green-leaf lovers, for whom "Frisch Auf" seemed the most natural of national cries. Mr. Britling thought of the individual Germans who had made up the assembly, of the men's amusingly fierce little hats of green and blue with an inevitable feather thrust perkily into the hatband behind, of the kindly plumpnesses behind their turned-up moustaches, of the blonde, sedentary women, very wise about the comforts of life and very kind to the children, of their earnest pleasure in landscape and Art and Great Writers, of their general frequent desire to sing, of their plasticity under the directing hands of Adam Meyer. He thought of the mellow south German landscape, rolling away broad and fair, of the little clean red-roofed townships, the old castles, the big prosperous farms, the neatly marked pedestrian routes, the hospitable inns, and the artless abundant Aussichtthurms....

text: The full account of Mr. Britling’s two-day search for some simple, convenient ladder by which he might enter the service of his threatened country would make a lengthy story. It would open with the sight of a neatly brushed patriot, his intelligent face set in a look of purpose, sitting in the train toward London, reading the war reports—the first reassuring reports for many days—and trying not to seem like a man whose life had been torn up by the roots and whose whole being burned with devotion; and it would end, after forty-eight hours of bustle, questioning, talking, waiting, and telephoning, with the same gentleman, a little wearied and with a sort of tired indifference in his eyes, taking the short cut from the station across Claverings park to resume his relation with the roots he had left behind. The true meaning of that interval had been the correction of Mr. Britling’s brief illusion that the government of the British Empire was in any sense intelligent, informed, or wise.
source_text: The detailed story of Mr. Britling's two days' search for some easy and convenient ladder into the service of his threatened country would be a voluminous one. It would begin with the figure of a neatly brushed patriot, with an intent expression upon his intelligent face, seated in the Londonward train, reading the war news--the first comforting war news for many days--and trying not to look as though his life was torn up by the roots and all his being aflame with devotion; and it would conclude after forty-eight hours of fuss, inquiry, talk, waiting, telephoning, with the same gentleman, a little fagged and with a kind of weary apathy in his eyes, returning by the short cut from the station across Claverings park to resume his connection with his abandoned roots. The essential process of the interval had been the correction of Mr. Britling's temporary delusion that the government of the British Empire is either intelligent, instructed, or wise.

text: It might well have appeared to the fiery Prussian zealot that all her likely enemies were bewildered, split against themselves, and brought to the edge of helpless confusion. The British Isles seemed to be sliding unmistakably toward civil strife. Threat answered threat, and one reckless fool jostled another for the world’s applause; the National Volunteers armed themselves against the Ulster men; and all things moved with a kind of dreadful mechanism from drill and demonstration toward the fatal landing of guns at Howth and the first blood spilled in Dublin’s streets. That miserable clash, more than almost anything else, must have hardened the Reich in the path it had already chosen. There can be no doubt of it: Ireland’s fomenters gave the last seal to the European war. In England itself there was a midsummer ague of strikes; Liverpool was strangled by a dockers’ stoppage, the East Anglian farm labourers were in rebellion, and the building trades across the kingdom stood on the brink of a lockout. Russia seemed to be in the throes of social revolution. From Baku to Petrograd there were rising outbreaks in the towns, and on the 23rd—the very day of the Austrian ultimatum—Cossacks were assaulting barbed-wire barriers in the streets of the capital. The London Stock Exchange had fallen into panic and confusion because of some immense and enigmatic selling of securities from abroad. And France—France, it seemed, was surrendered to nothing else but the absorbing spectacle of the Caillaux murder trial, the case of the former premier’s wife charged with killing a blackmailing journalist. It was a matter steeped in the grossest sexual violence. Before so spicy a display, France appeared to have neither leisure nor mind for M. Humbert’s revelation, the Reporter of the Army Committee, declaring that the artillery lacked ammunition, that the infantry wore boots “thirty years old,” and not even enough of those...
source_text: It may well have seemed to the belligerent German patriot that all her possible foes were confused, divided within themselves, at an extremity of distraction and impotence. The British Isles seemed slipping steadily into civil war. Threat was met by counter-threat, violent fool competed with violent fool for the admiration of the world, the National Volunteers armed against the Ulster men; everything moved on with a kind of mechanical precision from parade and meeting towards the fatal gun-running of Howth and the first bloodshed in Dublin streets. That wretched affray, far more than any other single thing, must have stiffened Germany in the course she had chosen. There can be no doubt of it; the mischief makers of Ireland set the final confirmation upon the European war. In England itself there was a summer fever of strikes; Liverpool was choked by a dockers' strike, the East Anglian agricultural labourers were in revolt, and the building trade throughout the country was on the verge of a lockout. Russia seemed to be in the crisis of a social revolution. From Baku to St. Petersburg there were insurrectionary movements in the towns, and on the 23rd--the very day of the Austrian ultimatum--Cossacks were storming barbed wire entanglements in the streets of the capital. The London Stock Exchange was in a state of panic disorganisation because of a vast mysterious selling of securities from abroad. And France, France it seemed was lost to all other consideration in the enthralling confrontations and denunciations of the Caillaux murder trial, the trial of the wife of her ex-prime Minister for the murder of a blackmailing journalist. It was a case full of the vulgarest sexual violence. Before so piquant a spectacle France it seemed could have no time nor attention for the revelation of M. Humbert, the Reporter of the Army Committee, proclaiming that the artillery was short of ammunition, that her infantry had boots "thirty years old" and not enough of those....

text: Thus—a vivid reality known, as yet, to only a few hundred minds—the immense disaster of the Great War was massing behind the scattered, distracted, and casual spectacle of an unconcerned world, much as the storms and rains of late September gather behind the brilliance and languor of August, and with scarcely more deliberate human purpose.
