For most of mankind, the European crisis was, at best, just something in the newspapers, no more significant than the political unrest in South Africa, where the Herzogites were oddly restless, or the possibility of trouble between Turkey and Greece. What truly occupied people in England in those last months of peace were boxing and the summer sales. A dazzling young Frenchman, Carpentier, who had knocked out Bombardier Wells, came back to defeat Gunboat Smith as well, to the boundless joy of France and the entire Latin world, amid the hearty cheers of Anglo-Saxondom. There was also a British victory over the Americans at polo, and a lively, cultivated newspaper debate over the proper motto for the London County Council’s coat of arms. The trial of Madame Caillaux filled the papers with energetic reports and striking images; Gregori Rasputin was stabbed and became the center of much spirited gossip about the Russian court; and Ulivi, the Italian fraud who claimed he could detonate mines by means of an “ultra-red” ray, was unmasked and fled away with a lady, very amusingly. For a few days all work at Woolwich Arsenal was brought to a halt because a certain Mr. Entwhistle, after refusing to erect a machine on a concrete foundation laid by non-union men, was rather rudely dismissed, and the Irish trouble trudged on in its tedious, troublesome fashion. People divided their attention among these various matters and carried on with their own affairs.
source_text: Thus--a vivid fact as yet only in a few hundred skulls or so--the vast catastrophe of the Great War gathered behind the idle, dispersed and confused spectacle of an indifferent world, very much as the storms and rains of late September gathered behind the glow and lassitudes of August, and with scarcely more of set human intention. For the greater part of mankind the European international situation was at most something in the papers, no more important than the political disturbances in South Africa, where the Herzogites were curiously uneasy, or the possible trouble between Turkey and Greece. The things that really interested people in England during the last months of peace were boxing and the summer sales. A brilliant young Frenchman, Carpentier, who had knocked out Bombardier Wells, came over again to defeat Gunboat Smith, and did so to the infinite delight of France and the whole Latin world, amidst the generous applause of Anglo-Saxondom. And there was also a British triumph over the Americans at polo, and a lively and cultured newspaper discussion about a proper motto for the arms of the London County Council. The trial of Madame Caillaux filled the papers with animated reports and vivid pictures; Gregori Rasputin was stabbed and became the subject of much lively gossip about the Russian Court; and Ulivi, the Italian impostor who claimed he could explode mines by means of an "ultra-red" ray, was exposed and fled with a lady, very amusingly. For a few days all the work at Woolwich Arsenal was held up because a certain Mr. Entwhistle, having refused to erect a machine on a concrete bed laid down by non-unionists, was rather uncivilly dismissed, and the Irish trouble pounded along its tiresome mischievous way. People gave a divided attention to these various topics, and went about their individual businesses.

text: Now that I am a sergeant, Park speaks to me again about all these matters, and we have a first lieutenant who is too curious to resist such technical details. They are only technical details. You have to regard them as such. One does not think of the dead body as a man only lately dead, who may have had a wife and business ties and a fondness for oysters or pale brandy. Nor as something that laughed and cried and disliked being hurt. That would ruin everything. One thinks of him simply as a uniform bearing marks that will tell us what sort of material we are up against, and perhaps with papers that may give us some clue as to how far he and his men are growing tired of the whole business....
source_text: "Now that I am a sergeant, Park talks to me again about all these things, and we have a first lieutenant too keen to resist such technical details. They are purely technical details. You must take them as that. One does not think of the dead body as a man recently deceased, who had perhaps a wife and business connections and a weakness for oysters or pale brandy. Or as something that laughed and cried and didn't like getting hurt. That would spoil everything. One thinks of him merely as a uniform with marks upon it that will tell us what kind of stuff we have against us, and possibly with papers that will give us a hint of how far he and his lot are getting sick of the whole affair....

text: “This is not the kind of war,” he said, “that can be decided by tallying guns and rifles. Something that has weighed on all of us has grown unbearable and must be brought to an end. And it will be brought to an end. I don’t know what soldiers and politicians make of our chances, but I do know what plain, sensible men think of the matter. I know that all we millions of reasonable, civilized spectators are ready to spend our last shillings and give our very lives rather than see Germany left undefeated. I know the same feeling exists in America, and that if America is given even half an opportunity—if she is given just one more insolent shake of that foolish mailed fist in her face—she too will enter this war at our side. Italy will come in. She is bound to come in. France will fight as one man. I am quite ready to believe that the Germans have endless rifles and guns; that they possess the most perfect maps, spies, and plans one could imagine. I am quite ready to hear that they have a thousand terrifying surprises in equipment hidden up their sleeves. I am quite ready for them to win sweeping victories and for us to suffer appalling defeats. Those are the first facts. What I do know is that the Germans understand nothing of the human spirit; that they have not for a moment dreamed of the demon of resentment this war will awaken. Did we not all trust them not to fire their guns? Was not that the very heart of our liberal and peaceful faith? And there they are, in the middle of Europe, firing their guns.”
source_text: "This isn't the sort of war," he said, "that is settled by counting guns and rifles. Something that has oppressed us all has become intolerable and has to be ended. And it will be ended. I don't know what soldiers and politicians think of our prospects, but I do know what ordinary reasonable men think of the business. I know that all we millions of reasonable civilised onlookers are prepared to spend our last shillings and give all our lives now, rather than see Germany unbeaten. I know that the same thing is felt in America, and that given half a chance, given just one extra shake of that foolish mailed fist in the face of America, and America also will be in this war by our side. Italy will come in. She is bound to come in. France will fight like one man. I'm quite prepared to believe that the Germans have countless rifles and guns; have got the most perfect maps, spies, plans you can imagine. I'm quite prepared to hear that they have got a thousand tremendous surprises in equipment up their sleeves. I'm quite prepared for sweeping victories for them and appalling disasters for us. Those are the first things. What I do know is that the Germans understand nothing of the spirit of man; that they do not dream for a moment of the devil of resentment this war will arouse. Didn't we all trust them not to let off their guns? Wasn't that the essence of our liberal and pacific faith? And here they are in the heart of Europe letting off their guns?"

text: The evening was almost as bright as day, the whole world washed in cool moonlight. The road shone silver, turning pink where Mr. Britling’s headlamp swept over it; the dark grass by the roadside and the bushes on the bank flashed for an instant into an almost harsh green as the beam moved on. The full moon was rising higher in the sky, so brilliant that scarcely a star could be seen in the blue-grey heavens. Far off, a mile away, the houses glimmered white, and now and then a moth would flicker into the lamp light, hover there for a moment, and then disappear once more into the dark.
source_text: The evening seemed as light as day, a cool moonshine filled the world. The road was silver that flushed to pink at the approach of Mr. Britling's headlight, the dark turf at the wayside and the bushes on the bank became for a moment an acid green as the glare passed. The full moon was climbing up the sky, and so bright that scarcely a star was visible in the blue grey of the heavens. Houses gleamed white a mile away, and ever and again a moth would flutter and hang in the light of the lamps, and then vanish again in the night.

text: He had the ridiculous notion that this should be a sixpenny telegram. The matter plagued him. He wanted to hand the little wretch sixpence, and yet all he possessed was a threepenny bit and a shilling; he could not see what was to be done, and his mind refused to work. It would be a dreadful thing to give her a shilling, and somehow he could not offer mere coppers for so grave a business as Rupert’s death. Then, all at once, the difficulty dropped away, and he put the shilling into the child’s hand. She looked up at him, questioning, disbelieving. “Is there an answer, sir, please?”
source_text: He had an absurd conviction that this ought to be a sixpenny telegram. The thing worried him. He wanted to give the brat sixpence, and he had only threepence and a shilling, and he didn't know what to do and his brain couldn't think. It would be a shocking thing to give her a shilling, and he couldn't somehow give just coppers for so important a thing as Hugh's death. Then all this problem vanished and he handed the child the shilling. She stared at him, inquiring, incredulous. "Is there a reply, Sir, please?"

text: I want to say this as plainly and directly as I can: I believe Germany, which has been the chief power at the center of this war, bears the greatest blame for it. Writing to you as an Englishman to a German, with the war still going on, we must be absolutely clear about that between us. I am convinced that in the ten years leading up to your defeat of France in 1871, Germany turned toward evil, and that her refusal to deal generously with France or to make friends with any other major power in the world is the fundamental cause of this war. Germany won—and she crushed the defeated. She inflicted unbearable humiliations. She set out to prepare for fresh acts of aggression; long before this slaughter began she was already waging war on land and sea, launching battleships, building strategic railways, creating a vast machinery for war supplies, issuing threats, forcing the whole world to strain and hurry just to keep up with her.... In the end, every European nation had only two choices left: submission to German will, or war. And it was not a will that any honorable man could submit to. It came as a narrow, harsh, and ungenerous will. It was the will of Zabern. It is not as though you had resolved to become an imperial people and gather and unite the world. You did not want to unite the world. You wanted to place the foot of an intensely national Germany, a sentimental and illiberal Germany, a Germany that cherished portraits of your absurd Kaiser and his brood of sons, a Germany in uniform, reading black-letter type, and despising every kultur except her own, upon the neck of a divided and humiliated humanity. It was an unbearable vision. I would rather the whole world perished.
source_text: _I want to tell you quite plainly and simply that I think that Germany which is chief and central in this war is most to blame for this war. Writing to you as an Englishman to a German and with war still being waged, there must be no mistake between us upon this point. I am persuaded that in the decade that ended with your overthrow of France in 1871, Germany turned her face towards evil, and that her refusal to treat France generously and to make friends with any other great power in the world, is the essential cause of this war. Germany triumphed--and she trampled on the loser. She inflicted intolerable indignities. She set herself to prepare for further aggressions; long before this killing began she was making war upon land and sea, launching warships, building strategic railways, setting up a vast establishment of war material, threatening, straining all the world to keep pace with her threats.... At last there was no choice before any European nation but submission to the German will, or war. And it was no will to which righteous men could possibly submit. It came as an illiberal and ungracious will. It was the will of Zabern. It is not as if you had set yourselves to be an imperial people and embrace and unify the world. You did not want to unify the world. You wanted to set the foot of an intensely national Germany, a sentimental and illiberal Germany, a Germany that treasured the portraits of your ridiculous Kaiser and his litter of sons, a Germany wearing uniform, reading black letter, and despising every kultur but her own, upon the neck of a divided and humiliated mankind. It was an intolerable prospect. I had rather the whole world died._

text: Lunch was once more spread as an open-air feast. Three visitors had come in; they had driven down from London, packed together on a motor-cycle and side-car, a brother and two sisters, apparently, and they seemed to have made hilarity into a creed. The rumours of the hockey match to come, which had been drifting at the edge of Mr. Direck’s awareness ever since his arrival, grew denser and more numerous.... It dawned on him that he was expected to play....
source_text: Lunch was an open-air feast again. Three visitors had dropped in; they had motored down from London piled up on a motor-cycle and a side-car; a brother and two sisters they seemed to be, and they had apparently reduced hilariousness to a principle. The rumours of coming hockey that had been floating on the outskirts of Mr. Direck's consciousness ever since his arrival, thickened and multiplied.... It crept into his mind that he was expected to play....

text: Then, when I began to feel certain that we had the trench fast in our hands, I became aware, all at once, of my own weariness and of how high the sun had climbed. I looked around me and saw that most of the others were laboring as hard as I had been. “We’ve done it,” I said; and it was the first thing I had spoken since I had bidden my two prisoners come out, and set a man with a hurt leg to keep watch over them. “It’s a bit of all right,” said Merritt, leaving off too, and striking a light to a half-smoked cigarette. I think he had kept it tucked behind his ear ever since the rush. He had stuck close to my side all the while, I now understood. Then old Hargreaves came up, looking uncommonly cheerful despite a slight bayonet prick in his forearm, and asking me to bind it up again. It was a comfort to see him nearly sound as well.
source_text: "Then when I began to feel sure of having got hold of the trench for good, I began to realise just how tired I was and how high the sun had got. I began to look about me, and found most of the other men working just as hard as I had been doing. 'We've done it!' I said, and that was the first word I'd spoken since I told my two Germans to come out of it, and stuck a man with a wounded leg to watch them. 'It's a bit of All Right,' said Ortheris, knocking off also, and lighting a half-consumed cigarette. He had been wearing it behind his ear, I believe, ever since the charge. Against this occasion. He'd kept close up to me all the time, I realised. And then old Park turned up very cheerful with a weak bayonet jab in his forearm that he wanted me to rebandage. It was good to see him practically all right too.

text: “It’s like another world,” said Mr. Britling, looking over the top of his paper. “Assassination as a political weapon. Can you imagine anything like that happening these days west of the Adriatic? Imagine someone assassinating the American Vice-President, and the bombs immediately being traced to the arsenal at Toronto!... We in the West take our politics more seriously.... Won’t you have another egg, Direck?”
source_text: "It's like another world," said Mr. Britling, over his paper. "Assassination as a political method. Can you imagine anything of the sort happening nowadays west of the Adriatic? Imagine some one assassinating the American Vice-President, and the bombs being at once ascribed to the arsenal at Toronto!... We take our politics more sadly in the West.... Won't you have another egg, Direck?"

text: He found himself, in a most extraordinary way, severed from every one of his possessions. His house in Antwerp had been left to the conqueror; his valuables and those treasured trifles dear to him had been deftly buried in the garden; and beyond the clothes in his rucksack he had no change of linen at all. The boots upon his feet were the only shoes he possessed. None of the slippers in the house would do for him, being all far too small, until at last Mrs. Larch remembered young Herr Otto’s pair, still lying unpacked upstairs. She fetched them down, and they fitted him perfectly. It seemed only a sort of poetic retribution, a prelude to wider national redress, to assign them to Belgium on the instant....
source_text: He was astonishingly cut off from all his belongings. His house in Antwerp was abandoned to the invader; valuables and cherished objects very skilfully buried in the garden; he had no change of clothing except what the rucksack held. His only footwear were the boots he came in. He could not get on any of the slippers in the house, they were all too small for him, until suddenly Mrs. Britling bethought herself of Herr Heinrich's pair, still left unpacked upstairs. She produced them, and they fitted exactly. It seemed only poetical justice, a foretaste of national compensations, to annex them to Belgium forthwith....

text: “You imagine John Bull is dead, and that some unfamiliar breed has taken over his coat and boots,” said Mr. Britling. “But I suspect you’ll discover before long that it is the old John Bull after all. Not perhaps the John Bull of Mrs. Humphry Ward, nor the John Bull of Mrs. Henry Wood, but in the essential matter still faithful to Shakespeare, to Fielding, to Dickens, to Meredith....”
source_text: "You think that John Bull is dead and a strange generation is wearing his clothes," said Mr. Britling. "I think you'll find very soon it's the old John Bull. Perhaps not Mrs. Humphry Ward's John Bull, or Mrs. Henry Wood's John Bull but true essentially to Shakespeare, Fielding, Dickens, Meredith...."

text: The long, narrow table beneath the great sycamores, set between the house and the converted barn that Mr. Direck had learned was used for “dancing and all that sort of thing,” was laid with a blue linen diaper cloth, and that, too, took him by surprise. This was his first meal in a private English household, and for reasons he could not clearly have explained, he had expected something very formal and severe, with “spotless napery.” He had also imagined a rigid, efficient service from unyielding parlourmaids, and the whole affair, indeed, to be intensely genteel. Instead, two smiling women servants emerged from what was presumably the kitchen side, pushing a strange wicker contrivance, which his little guide told him was called Aunt Clatter—an apt name, plainly—and which carried the meal upon its shelves. And while the maids at this travelling sideboard carved the meat, opened bottles, and did the like, the small boy and a slightly older brother, with some help from two young men of no very clear standing or connection, served the guests. Mrs. Britling sat at the head of the table and spoke with Mr. Direck in the manner of a hostess asking questions and receiving answers only half accepted, all the while keeping a vigilant eye on the proceedings.
source_text: The long narrow table under the big sycamores between the house and the adapted barn that Mr. Direck learnt was used for "dancing and all that sort of thing," was covered with a blue linen diaper cloth, and that too surprised him. This was his first meal in a private household in England, and for obscure reasons he had expected something very stiff and formal with "spotless napery." He had also expected a very stiff and capable service by implacable parlourmaids, and the whole thing indeed highly genteel. But two cheerful women servants appeared from what was presumably the kitchen direction, wheeling a curious wicker erection, which his small guide informed him was called Aunt Clatter--manifestly deservedly--and which bore on its shelves the substance of the meal. And while the maids at this migratory sideboard carved and opened bottles and so forth, the small boy and a slightly larger brother, assisted a little by two young men of no very defined position and relationship, served the company. Mrs. Britling sat at the head of the table, and conversed with Mr. Direck by means of hostess questions and imperfectly accepted answers while she kept a watchful eye on the proceedings.

text: Presently Hugh came fluttering out of the house to learn what had happened. “One can’t seem to work with all these big things going on,” he said in apology. He took possession of the _Daily News_, while his father and mother read _The Times_. The voices of the younger boys floated from the shadow of the trees; they had carried all their toy soldiers outside and were making entrenched camps in the garden.
source_text: Presently Hugh came flitting out of the house to hear what had happened. "One can't work somehow, with all these big things going on," he apologised. He secured the _Daily News_ while his father and mother read _The Times_. The voices of the younger boys came from the shade of the trees; they had brought all their toy soldiers out of doors, and were making entrenched camps in the garden.

text: He felt a small disappointment on discovering that the servants wore no livery. In the American magazines, in the American moving pictures of English tales, and in the mansions of the very wealthy Americans who lived in England, they invariably did. Even the Mansion House had misled him; he had once met a fellow countryman who had lately dined there and who had spoken of “flunkeys” powdered and clad in cloth of gold—like Thackeray’s Jeames Yellowplush. But here there were only two slender, unobtrusive, attentive young men in black coats, wearing about them a mild devotion rather than any air of state. He was disappointed too by a certain absence of magnificence in the company. The ladies struck him as badly dressed; none had that crisp challenge, that “There! and what have you to say to it?” of the well-dressed American woman, and the men, too, seemed less tailored than simply, almost correctly, clothed.
source_text: He was a little disappointed to find that the servants didn't wear livery. In American magazine pictures and in American cinematograph films of English stories and in the houses of very rich Americans living in England, they do so. And the Mansion House is misleading; he had met a compatriot who had recently dined at the Mansion House, and who had described "flunkeys" in hair-powder and cloth of gold--like Thackeray's Jeames Yellowplush. But here the only servants were two slim, discreet and attentive young gentlemen in black coats with a gentle piety in their manner instead of pride. And he was a little disappointed too by a certain lack of splendour in the company. The ladies affected him as being ill-dressed; there was none of the hard snap, the "_There!_ and what do you say to it?" about them of the well-dressed American woman, and the men too were not so much tailored as unobtrusively and yet grammatically clothed.

text: She herself was in confusion. She had come to care very deeply for Mr. Direck, and to have complete trust and confidence in him, and yet her affection seemed to find no outlet except a continual reproaching of him and of America for shunning war. She had fallen in love with him when he was in fancy dress; she was a young woman with a keener appetite for body and color than she realized, and what she actually resented in him, though she did not know it, was that he seemed never inclined to bring the spirit of fancy dress into ordinary life. At first he had appealed to both her imagination and her senses, and she wanted him to keep on doing so. Instead, he seemed to be sinking more and more into repeated declarations of devotion and the dull, efficient performance of humanitarian obligations. Lately he was always trying to convince her that what he was doing was the proper and honorable thing for him to do; what he did not understand, what she herself did not understand, was how his propriety and good sense irritated her. And when he saw that he irritated her, he made a very sincere effort to be still more proper and sensible, and more plainly and unmistakably proper and sensible than before.
source_text: She herself was in a tangle. She had grown to be very fond of Mr. Direck, and to have a profound trust and confidence in him, and her fondness seemed able to find no expression at all except a constant girding at his and America's avoidance of war. She had fallen in love with him when he was wearing fancy dress; she was a young woman with a stronger taste for body and colour than she supposed; what indeed she resented about him, though she did not know it, was that he seemed never disposed to carry the spirit of fancy dress into everyday life. To begin with he had touched both her imagination and senses, and she wanted him to go on doing that. Instead of which he seemed lapsing more and more into reiterated assurances of devotion and the flat competent discharge of humanitarian duties. Always nowadays he was trying to persuade her that what he was doing was the right and honourable thing for him to do; what he did not realise, what indeed she did not realise, was the exasperation his rightness and reasonableness produced in her. When he saw he exasperated her he sought very earnestly to be righter and reasonabler and more plainly and demonstrably right and reasonable than ever.

text: Mrs. Haviland moved through the village in a bright patterned frock, carrying herself with a bold, almost triumphant air. By then Stephen had been entered on the lists as “missing, reported killed,” and she had received two letters from his comrades. They said that Stephen had been left behind in the shattered shell of a farmhouse with one or two other wounded men, and that when the Canadians took the position back these wounded had been discovered slaughtered. Not one had been found alive. Afterward the Canadians had been compelled to withdraw. Mr. Loring had taken immense pains to trace wounded men from Stephen’s company, and also any likely Canadians, both in the base hospital in France and in London, and to draw from them whatever information he could. He had made the matter a service to Elsie. Only one of his witnesses was entirely certain about Stephen, but, unhappily, he was certain in the worst way. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and plainly that must have been Stephen. “He had been stabbed in half a dozen places. His head was almost cut off his body.”
source_text: Mrs. Teddy went about the village in a coloured dress bearing herself confidently. Teddy had been listed now as "missing, since reported killed," and she had had two letters from his comrades. They said Teddy had been left behind in the ruins of a farm with one or two other wounded, and that when the Canadians retook the place these wounded had all been found butchered. None had been found alive. Afterwards the Canadians had had to fall back. Mr. Direck had been at great pains to hunt up wounded men from Teddy's company, and also any likely Canadians both at the base hospital in France and in London, and to get what he could from them. He had made it a service to Cissie. Only one of his witnesses was quite clear about Teddy, but he, alas! was dreadfully clear. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and obviously that must have been Teddy. "He had been prodded in half-a-dozen places. His head was nearly severed from his body."

text: Mr. Alderton did all he could not to credit such things. They ran counter to the ordinary order of his thoughts. They set a cruel tension upon his mind. Perhaps, he told himself, they had been garbled in the telling, made to appear more savage or less defensible than they truly were. Perhaps they were the work of isolated ruffians, altogether separate from the regular conduct of the war. Now and then some feeble-brained officer might have tried to make himself dreadful.... And as for the shelling of cathedrals and the outrage of Louvain, why, Mr. Alderton was ready enough to maintain that Gothic stone is not holy ground when military need drives straight through it.... It was only after the war had dragged on for several months that Mr. Alderton’s hesitant, recoiling mind was forced, by official statements and an outcry of witnesses, into a fixed conviction of the grim fact of organized rape and murder, of devastation, filth, and monstrous coercions, that stained the first rush of the Prussians into Belgium and Champagne....
source_text: Mr. Britling did his utmost not to believe these things. They contradicted his habitual world. They produced horrible strains in his mind. They might, he hoped, be misreported so as to seem more violent or less justifiable than they were. They might be the acts of stray criminals, and quite disconnected from the normal operations of the war. Here and there some weak-minded officer may have sought to make himself terrible.... And as for the bombardment of cathedrals and the crime of Louvain, well, Mr. Britling was prepared to argue that Gothic architecture is not sacrosanct if military necessity cuts through it.... It was only after the war had been going on some months that Mr. Britling's fluttering, unwilling mind was pinned down by official reports and a cloud of witnesses to a definite belief in the grim reality of systematic rape and murder, destruction, dirtiness and abominable compulsions that blackened the first rush of the Prussians into Belgium and Champagne....

text: “You don’t look steadily at anything while the guns are firing. When a large gun goes off or a shell explodes close by, you seem for a moment neither to see nor to hear. You go on being stunned at intervals. One catches sight of things only in a kind of flicker between the impacts....
source_text: "You don't look at anything steadily while the guns are going. When a big gun goes off or a shell bursts anywhere near you, you seem neither to see nor hear for a moment. You keep on being intermittently stunned. One sees in a kind of flicker in between the impacts....

text: Well, there they were at last. This time I saw them plain. They came out in a rush, advanced a little way, and then went down, while our shell was bursting among them and behind them. A good deal of it was sailing too far. I watched what our own fellows were doing, emptied a heap of cartridges that lay ready to my hand, and began to blaze away. Half the German assault never came out of their trench at all. If they truly meant to press matters against us, which I rather doubt, they went about it in a half-hearted fashion. They showed themselves for no more than five minutes, and left two or three score men lying on the ground. Whenever we saw a man stirring we were ordered to fire at him; he might be an unwounded one trying to crawl back. For a while our guns gave them a bad time. Then it was practically finished, but toward sunset their guns answered us again, and the artillery duel went on until moonlight. The men in our third company got it pretty heavily, and after that our guns seemed to find the range and gain the upper hand....
source_text: "Well, there they were. This time I saw them. They were coming out and running a little way and dropping, and our shell was bursting among them and behind them. A lot of it was going too far. I watched what our men were doing, and poured out a lot of cartridges ready to my hand and began to blaze away. Half the German attack never came out of their trench. If they really intended business against us, which I doubt, they were half-hearted in carrying it out. They didn't show for five minutes, and they left two or three score men on the ground. Whenever we saw a man wriggle we were told to fire at him; it might be an unwounded man trying to crawl back. For a time our guns gave them beans. Then it was practically over, but about sunset their guns got back at us again, and the artillery fight went on until it was moonlight. The chaps in our third company caught it rather badly, and then our guns seemed to find something and get the upper hand....

text: “I’m weary of this high, brittle talk of yours about the war.... You are a nation of ungenerous spectators—watching us choke or be choked. You wager on our victory. And we shall win; we shall win. And you will profit. And when we have won a triumph only a shade less dreadful than defeat, then you imagine you will step in and meddle with our peace. Bleed us a little more to satisfy your hyphenated patriots....”
source_text: "I'm sick of this high thin talk of yours about the war.... You are a nation of ungenerous onlookers--watching us throttle or be throttled. You gamble on our winning. And we shall win; we shall win. And you will profit. And when we have won a victory only one shade less terrible than defeat, then you think you will come in and tinker with our peace. Bleed us a little more to please your hyphenated patriots...."

text: The two images would not remain steadily together in his mind. When he thought of the broken pledge that had let those slaughtering hosts pour into the quiet decency of Belgium, that had shattered her cities, burned her villages, and filled the lovely gorges of the Ardennes with blood and smoke and terror, he was overwhelmed by a flood of self-righteous anger, a self-righteous anger that was indeed wholly Teutonic in character, one that for a while silenced his old friendship and every kindly feeling toward Germany, filled him with destructive impulses, and obsessed him with a wish to hear of death and more death and still more death in every German town and home....
source_text: The two pictures would not stay steadily in his mind together. When he thought of the broken faith that had poured those slaughtering hosts into the decent peace of Belgium, that had smashed her cities, burnt her villages and filled the pretty gorges of the Ardennes with blood and smoke and terror, he was flooded with self-righteous indignation, a self-righteous indignation that was indeed entirely Teutonic in its quality, that for a time drowned out his former friendship and every kindly disposition towards Germany, that inspired him with destructive impulses, and obsessed him with a desire to hear of death and more death and yet death in every German town and home....

text: “Something,” she said, and out of that vague demand a number of matters came into view. She declared herself a Socialist, and there still lingered in Mr. Direck’s mind an old-fashioned American prejudice against the term. To him Socialists were linked with Anarchists and deported foreigners. It was plain, too, that she knew Mr. Britling’s essays and discussions thoroughly. She believed that everyone, man or woman alike, should be chiefly occupied in doing some definite service for the world. (“There’s my secretaryship of the Massachusetts Modern Thought Society, anyhow,” said Mr. Direck.) As for herself, she wanted to be doing something; it was only because she did not know what she ought to do that she read so widely and so hungrily. She wanted, as it were, to lose herself in some undertaking. Deep in Mr. Direck’s nature was the conviction that what she ought to be doing was making love in a transport of egotistical delight, and relishing every fragment of her own lovely self and her own charming vitality—while she had them—but for the sake of their talk he did not care to put it more plainly than to say that he thought one owed it to oneself to cultivate one’s personality. At this she took issue with him hotly.
source_text: "_Something_," she said, and in the expansion of this vague demand they touched on a number of things. She said that she was a Socialist, and there was still in Mr. Direck's composition a streak of the old-fashioned American prejudice against the word. He associated Socialists with Anarchists and deported aliens. It was manifest too that she was deeply read in the essays and dissertations of Mr. Britling. She thought everybody, man or woman, ought to be chiefly engaged in doing something definite for the world at large. ("There's my secretaryship of the Massachusetts Modern Thought Society, anyhow," said Mr. Direck.) And she herself wanted to be doing something--it was just because she did not know what it was she ought to be doing that she was reading so extensively and voraciously. She wanted to lose herself in something. Deep in the being of Mr. Direck was the conviction that what she ought to be doing was making love in a rapturously egotistical manner, and enjoying every scrap of her own delightful self and her own delightful vitality--while she had it, but for the purposes of their conversation he did not care to put it any more definitely than to say that he thought we owed it to ourselves to develop our personalities. Upon which she joined issue with great vigour.

text: He was still only in the broken beginnings of conversation when everything was thrown into confusion by the momentous arrival of Lady Frensham, and there was a general shifting about of seats. Lady Frensham had come from London by motorcar; she appeared wrapped in veils and layers and an enormous dust coat, with some kind of nephew in attendance who had driven the car. She was plainly a woman born to prevail. The arrival of this vigorous presence swept away the languor of the afternoon. Mr. Philbert took off the wrappings and passed them to the footman.
source_text: He was still only in the fragmentary stage of conversation when everything was thrown into commotion by the important arrival of Lady Frensham, and there was a general reshuffling of places. Lady Frensham had arrived from London by automobile; she appeared in veils and swathings and a tremendous dust cloak, with a sort of nephew in her train who had driven the car. She was manifestly a constitutionally triumphant woman. A certain afternoon lassitude vanished in the swirl of her arrival. Mr. Philbert removed wrappings and handed them to the manservant.

text: It was all right. She had always known, deep down, that it was all right. (Keep close to him.) Only for a little while had she thought otherwise. But that had been folly. Hadn’t she always known he was alive? And there he stood, alive indeed! (Keep close to him.) Yet it was so sweet to be certain at last—after all that agony—to clasp him, to cling about him, to feel the firm body of him, kissing her, and crying too, crying with her. “Tom, my darling!”
source_text: It was all right. She had always known it was all right. (Hold close to him.) Except just for a little while. But that had been foolishness. Hadn't she always known he was alive? And here he was alive! (Hold close to him.) Only it was so good to be sure--after all her torment; to hold him, to hang about him, to feel the solid man, kissing her, weeping too, weeping together with her. "Teddy my love!"

text: We are not a thorough people. Whenever we consider one thing, we consider its opposite as well. When we embrace an opinion, we also admit a provisional notion that it is probably almost as wrong as it is right. We are atmospheric. They are concrete.... All this filthy, vile, unjust, and cruel business is honest, genuine war. We pretend that war does not hurt. They know better.... The Germans are a simple, honest people. That is their virtue. Perhaps it is their only virtue....
source_text: "We're not a thorough people. When we think of anything, we also think of its opposite. When we adopt an opinion we also take in a provisional idea that it is probably nearly as wrong as it is right. We are--atmospheric. They are concrete.... All this filthy, vile, unjust and cruel stuff is honest genuine war. We pretend war does not hurt. They know better.... The Germans are a simple honest people. It is their virtue. Possibly it is their only virtue...."

text: Zinc was indispensable for cartridges; it had to be refined zinc, and of an exceptional purity, or else the shooting would go badly awry. The trouble was, we had allowed the refining trade to slip from England into Belgium and Germany. Only one or two British firms were left now.... Unless we bestirred ourselves tremendously, we should find ourselves short of cartridges.... At any rate, short of cartridges made in such a way as to give sound shooting. “And there you are!” said the keen-faced man.
source_text: Zinc was necessary for cartridges; it had to be refined zinc and very pure, or the shooting went wrong. Well, we had let the refining business drift away from England to Belgium and Germany. There were just one or two British firms still left.... Unless we bucked up tremendously we should get caught short of cartridges.... At any rate of cartridges so made as to ensure good shooting. "And there you are!" said the sharp-featured man.

text: But how twisted she had been beneath that show of pride! For four years she had allowed him to believe that he was the only man who truly mattered in the world, while all the time she had plainly and deliberately deceived him. She had made a fool of him, and perhaps of the others too, simply to keep her little retinue and play queen in her own world. And now at last there was humiliation, bitter humiliation, with Mamie, chin lifted and smiling brightly in triumph, looking down on him.
source_text: But how tortuous she had been behind that outward pride of hers! For four years she had let him think he was the only man who really mattered in the world, and all the time quite clearly and definitely she had deceived him. She had made a fool of him and she had made a fool of the others perhaps--just to have her retinue and play the queen in her world. And at last humiliation, bitter humiliation, and Mamie with her chin in the air and her bright triumphant smile looking down on him.

text: “You think I spend my time staring into space,” she remarked.... “Not at all! I’ve been planning all kinds of things.... I’ve been thinking about how I might get to Germany.... Or perhaps one could catch them in Switzerland.... I’ve had all sorts of schemes. They can’t stay guarded forever....”
source_text: "You think I go about staring at nothing," she remarked.... "Not a bit of it! I have been planning all sorts of things.... I have been thinking how I could get to Germany.... Or one might catch them in Switzerland.... I've had all sorts of plans. They can't go guarded for ever....

text: “Ah, your real troubles have scarcely begun. Wait until you have a child of your own. That is what goes to the quick. You are given the most fragile thing in existence, a young mind of your own blood. You know you are answerable for it, you feel the burden of that responsibility—and yet you lose hold of it. You cannot reach it. We have, nowadays, let slip the old notion of fatherhood by divine right, and nothing has come to take its place. I have tried not to be a narrowing master, a taskmaker, a household despot to that boy—only it has come to mean his taking his own path.... I do not impose myself. I had hoped to counsel. But you see, he values my regard and approval. Too much. When all goes well, I hear of it. When the sky lowers for him, he keeps his sorrow to himself. Just when I would most eagerly have gone into it with him.... Something is wrong now, something serious perhaps. I can feel that he wants to speak. And yet there it is—it seems I am the very last person before whom he could bring himself to confess folly, or frailty.... Something I ought only to laugh over and say, ‘That is in all our blood, my dear little replica. Let us see what can be done about it.’...”
source_text: "Ah! your troubles in life haven't begun yet. Wait till you're a father. That cuts to the bone. You have the most delicate thing in the world in hand, a young kindred mind. You feel responsible for it, you know you are responsible for it; and you lose touch with it. You can't get at it. Nowadays we've lost the old tradition of fatherhood by divine right--and we haven't got a new one. I've tried not to be a cramping ruler, a director, a domestic tyrant to that lad--and in effect it's meant his going his own way.... I don't dominate. I hoped to advise. But you see he loves my respect and good opinion. Too much. When things go well I know of them. When the world goes dark for him, then he keeps his trouble from me. Just when I would so eagerly go into it with him.... There's something the matter now, something--it may be grave. I feel he wants to tell me. And there it is!--it seems I am the last person to whom he can humiliate himself by a confession of blundering, or weakness.... Something I should just laugh at and say, 'That's in the blood of all of us, dear Spit of myself. Let's see what's to be done.'..."

text: That was a peculiarity of Mr. Harrowby’s mind. It had the habit of widening out from his own person into matters of a more general cast. Many minds, indeed, are so disposed in these days. He was not so sharply individualized as people are commonly assumed to be—by our laws, in our tales, in our moral reckonings. There was in him a vicarious strain. He could glide from fixed self-accusation into the warmest penitence for himself as the Motorist in the abstract, or for himself as the nation, or for himself as Mankind. From sorrow for having smashed his guest and his car he could move, by a transition almost too slight for him to notice, into sorrow for every mishap ever caused by a motorist’s blunder since motorcars first came into being. That whole long line of offenders became Mr. Harrowby. Or rather, Mr. Harrowby became that immense line of offenders.
source_text: That was a trick of Mr. Britling's mind. It had this tendency to spread outward from himself to generalised issues. Many minds are like that nowadays. He was not so completely individualised as people are supposed to be individualised--in our law, in our stories, in our moral judgments. He had a vicarious factor. He could slip from concentrated reproaches to the liveliest remorse for himself as The Automobilist in General, or for himself as England, or for himself as Man. From remorse for smashing his guest and his automobile he could pass by what was for him the most imperceptible of transitions to remorse for every accident that has ever happened through the error of an automobilist since automobiles began. All that long succession of blunderers became Mr. Britling. Or rather Mr. Britling became all that vast succession of blunderers.

text: “Mr. Direck!” her voice rang out, confident and clear. (Such are the moments that make a heroic life.) The ball sped past the racing Teddy. Mr. Direck stopped it with his foot, a move he had only just learned from the eldest Britling son. He was neither slow nor hurried. He was inside the half-circle, and the way to goal was blocked only by the lady in the dust-cloak and Mr. Lawrence Carmine. He feinted a shot to Mr. Carmine’s left and then, quick as a snake striking, drove the ball to the right.
source_text: "Mr. Direck!" came her voice, full of confidence. (Of such moments is the heroic life.) The ball shot behind the hurtling Teddy. Mr. Direck stopped it with his foot, a trick he had just learnt from the eldest Britling son. He was neither slow nor hasty. He was in the half-circle, and the way to the goal was barred only by the dust-cloak lady and Mr. Lawrence Carmine. He made as if to shoot to Mr. Carmine's left and then smacked the ball, with the swiftness of a serpent's stroke, to his right.

text: “I dislike his going,” he said. “I trust everything will turn out well for him.... Teddy has grown so solemn lately. I know it is a shabby sentiment; it has nothing of the Roman spirit in it, but I am grateful that this cannot befall Hugh—” He made a calculation. “Not for a year and three months yet, even if they push him into it on his very birthday....”
source_text: "I don't like his going," he said. "I hope it will be all right with him.... Teddy's so grave nowadays. It's a mean thing, I know, it has none of the Roman touch, but I am glad that this can't happen with Hugh--" He computed. "Not for a year and three months, even if they march him into it upon his very birthday....

text: The Christmas of 1914 found Britain, whose countryside had until then been almost as tranquil and soldier-free as Connecticut, already well advanced upon the road to becoming a land of uniforms, shell works, and military stores. The soldiers came first, in accordance with the familiar and much-admired British maxim of catching the hare before laying the kitchen fire. Christmas had always been a season of merry-making at the Manor House, with dressing up and capering and quick-stepping in abundance; but this year the times were too unsettled to permit the assembling of a company. Julian was granted leave for the day after Christmas, but Robin was held fast; and Nan and Bess went away with the little boy to take rooms near him. The de Vrieses had been looking forward to an English Christmas at Ashcombe Hall, but within three weeks of Christmas Day Mr. de Vries had found work he could do in Sheffield, and carried his household off with him. The two small boys brightened their spirits with paper chains and colored stars, but the Christmas tree was denounced as too German, and it was suddenly agreed that Santa Claus had reverted to Father Christmas after all. They discovered also that the price of tin soldiers had risen beyond their reach, and they had to go without the electric lanterns upon which they had set their hearts. A Christmas entertainment had been planned at Langford, but at the eleventh hour Lady Marston was summoned away to an orphaned nephew gravely wounded near Ypres, and the lights of Langford were extinguished.
source_text: The Christmas of 1914 found England, whose landscape had hitherto been almost as peaceful and soldierless as Massachusetts, already far gone along the path of transformation into a country full of soldiers and munition makers and military supplies. The soldiers came first, on the well-known and greatly admired British principle of "first catch your hare" and then build your kitchen. Always before, Christmas had been a time of much gaiety and dressing up and prancing and two-stepping at the Dower House, but this year everything was too uncertain to allow of any gathering of guests. Hugh got leave for the day after Christmas, but Teddy was tied; and Cissie and Letty went off with the small boy to take lodgings near him. The Van der Pants had hoped to see an English Christmas at Matching's Easy, but within three weeks of Christmas Day Mr. Van der Pant found a job that he could do in Nottingham, and carried off his family. The two small boys cheered their hearts with paper decorations, but the Christmas Tree was condemned as too German, and it was discovered that Santa Claus had suddenly become Old Father Christmas again. The small boys discovered that the price of lead soldiers had risen, and were unable to buy electric torches, on which they had set their hearts. There was to have been a Christmas party at Claverings, but at the last moment Lady Homartyn had to hurry off to an orphan nephew who had been seriously wounded near Ypres, and the light of Claverings was darkened.

text: He set out, in some detail, how he was to shape his dissertation and in what manner it would win approval, then spoke of his military service and of the several steps by which he would afterward rise through that regular professorial career that lay before him. He admitted that philology did not greatly stir him, but, as he put it, “it is what I must do.” And so he would do it for the rest of his life. For his own part, his sympathies lay with ideas of universal citizenship, with Esperanto and Ido and all the schemes for a common tongue and the like assaults upon the barriers between one human being and another. But the authorities at home had no liking for such cosmopolitan notions, and so he was giving them up. “Here,” he said with a faint note of envy, “it is as though there were no authorities at all.”
source_text: He explained how he would prepare his thesis and how it would be accepted, and the nature of his army service and the various stages by which he would subsequently ascend in the orderly professorial life to which he was destined. He confessed a certain lack of interest in philology, but, he said, "it is what I have to do." And so he was going to do it all his life through. For his own part he was interested in ideas of universal citizenship, in Esperanto and Ido and universal languages and such-like attacks upon the barriers between man and man. But the authorities at home did not favour cosmopolitan ideas, and so he was relinquishing them. "Here, it is as if there were no authorities," he said with a touch of envy.

text: Two Zeppelins had approached from out over the sea and had been engaged by an anti-aircraft gun that came by car from Ipswich. The town’s first warning of the raid had been the crash of that gun. Many people had hurried outside to see what was going on. It was doubtful whether anyone had actually caught sight of the Zeppelins, though everyone swore they had heard the beat of their engines. Then, all at once, the bombs began to rain down. Only six struck houses or people; the rest fell in destructive clusters very near one another on the local golf course, and at least half failed to explode at all and did not appear to have been fitted to do so.
source_text: Two Zeppelins had come in from over the sea, and had been fired at by an anti-aircraft gun coming on an automobile from Ipswich. The first intimation the people of the town had had of the raid was the report of this gun. Many had run out to see what was happening. It was doubtful if any one had really seen the Zeppelins, though every one testified to the sound of their engines. Then suddenly the bombs had come streaming down. Only six had made hits upon houses or people; the rest had fallen ruinously and very close together on the local golf links, and at least half had not exploded at all and did not seem to have been released to explode.

text: The door stood flung wide, just as he had left it, heedless of all the small jealousies and private claims of living and possession. Even the windows were thrown open, as though he had been in need of air; he, who had always so carefully kept them shut ever since he first came to England. Across the empty hearth lay the great oak branch he had brought in for Billy, but its leaves and twigs had now withered, and many had broken away to scatter on the floor. Billy's cage stood empty on a little table in the corner. Rather than packing, the young man had plainly paced the room in some mood of heightening emotion; the bed was in disorder, as if he had flung himself upon it more than once, and his books had been scattered about in despair. He had made a few feeble beginnings of packing in a borrowed cardboard box. The violin lay on the chest of drawers as though laid out in state; the drawers were all partly open; and in the middle of the floor there sprawled a wretched blue shirt, dropped there, the most crushed and broken-hearted of garments. In the fireplace was an abandoned pencil sketch of a girl's face, torn in two....
source_text: The door stood wide open, as he had left it open, careless of all the little jealousies and privacies of occupation and ownership. Even the windows were wide open as though he had needed air; he who had always so sedulously shut his windows since first he came to England. Across the empty fireplace stretched the great bough of oak he had brought in for Billy, but now its twigs and leaves had wilted, and many had broken off and fallen on the floor. Billy's cage stood empty upon a little table in the corner of the room. Instead of packing, the young man had evidently paced up and down in a state of emotional elaboration; the bed was disordered as though he had several times flung himself upon it, and his books had been thrown about the room despairfully. He had made some little commencements of packing in a borrowed cardboard box. The violin lay as if it lay in state upon the chest of drawers, the drawers were all partially open, and in the middle of the floor sprawled a pitiful shirt of blue, dropped there, the most flattened and broken-hearted of garments. The fireplace contained an unsuccessful pencil sketch of a girl's face, torn across....

text: He drifted off on a tangent, describing the men in his platoon. “The best man in our lot is an ex-grocer’s assistant, but, to spare us empty generalizations, it happens that the worst man—a moon-faced fellow, almost unable to lace his boots without help and a good scolding—is also an ex-grocer’s assistant. Our most objectionable member is a little cad with a snub nose, who has read Kipling and fancies himself the closest thing that has ever existed to Private Ortheris. He goes about hunting for the other two of the Soldiers Three; it is rather like an unpopular politician trying to put together a ministry. And he is conscientiously foul-mouthed. He feels the loss of an opportunity to say ‘bloody’ as keenly as a snob feels the dropping of an H. Sometimes he goes back and repeats a sentence, fitting the ‘bloody’ into it. I used to swear a little, beyond the range of your parental ear, but Ortheris has cured me of that. When he is near, I am prim and careful in my speech. I now see that cursing is a way of chewing up one’s own dirt. In a platoon there is no room for indifference; one must either love or hate. I have a feeling that my first taste of battle will not be with Germans, but with Private Ortheris....”
source_text: He went off at a tangent to describe the men in his platoon. "The best man in our lot is an ex-grocer's assistant, but in order to save us from vain generalisations it happens that the worst man--a moon-faced creature, almost incapable of lacing up his boots without help and objurgation--is also an ex-grocer's assistant. Our most offensive member is a little cad with a snub nose, who has read Kipling and imagines he is the nearest thing that ever has been to Private Ortheris. He goes about looking for the other two of the Soldiers Three; it is rather like an unpopular politician trying to form a ministry. And he is conscientiously foul-mouthed. He feels losing a chance of saying 'bloody' as acutely as a snob feels dropping an H. He goes back sometimes and says the sentence over again and puts the 'bloody' in. I used to swear a little out of the range of your parental ear, but Ortheris has cured me. When he is about I am mincing in my speech. I perceive now that cursing is a way of chewing one's own dirt. In a platoon there is no elbow-room for indifference; you must either love or hate. I have a feeling that my first taste of battle will not be with Germans, but with Private Ortheris...."

text: His thoughts became steadier and more lucid; they sank deeper and spread farther. His early, merely superficial judgments were confirmed and enriched, or else supplanted by others. He thought as he walked the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he rehearsed his ideas in essays and leading articles, then revised and corrected them. At moments there was ease and weariness, but never any real escape. The war loomed above him like a watchful schoolmaster, day after day, week after week, unmoved by fatigue or impatience, a rod in its hand.
source_text: His thoughts grew firmer and clearer; they went deeper and wider. His first superficial judgments were endorsed and deepened or replaced by others. He thought along the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he tried over his thoughts in essays and leading articles and reviewed them and corrected them. Now and then came relaxation and lassitude, but never release. The war towered over him like a vigilant teacher, day after day, week after week, regardless of fatigue and impatience, holding a rod in its hand.

text: On Saturday Sir Edward Carson was still occupying the center of attention; Russia had stepped in and asked for more time for Serbia, and the _Daily Chronicle_ pronounced the day a critical one for Europe. By Monday, Dublin, with bayonet charges and bullets, had driven Serbia into a corner. No shots had yet been fired in the East, and the turmoil in Ireland on which Germany had counted was already far advanced. Sir Edward Grey was said to be laboring earnestly for peace.
source_text: On Saturday Sir Edward Carson was still in the central limelight, Russia had intervened and demanded more time for Serbia, and the _Daily Chronicle_ declared the day a critical one for Europe. Dublin with bayonet charges and bullets thrust Serbia into a corner on Monday. No shots had yet been fired in the East, and the mischief in Ireland that Germany had counted on was well ahead. Sir Edward Grey was said to be working hard for peace.

text: “Well,” said Mr. Direck, and paused for a moment. It was all so charming that one could not keep on being merely discreet. The atmosphere was easy and friendly. His tone put any offence out of countenance. And he bestowed upon the young lady the full benefit of a very expressive eye. “I’m very glad to meet you, Cousin Corner. How are the old folks at home?”
source_text: "Well," said Mr. Direck, and hesitated for a moment. It was so delightful that one couldn't go on being just discreet. The atmosphere was free and friendly. His intonation disarmed offence. And he gave the young lady the full benefit of a quite expressive eye. "I'm very pleased to meet you, Cousin Corner. How are the old folks at home?"

text: Field hockey as it was played at Ashcombe Manor in the days before the war was a pastime in which peril, bodily exertion, and benevolence were blended in very generous measure. With the exception of the baby in the pram and the apparently placid but inwardly indignant aunt who pushed it to and fro in a spot of maximum hazard just behind the goal without a net, everyone took part. Those in full possession of their limbs and some acquaintance with the rules went forward; the less informed played in a more retiring manner behind the front line; and the elderly, the frail, and the overweight were employed chiefly as impediments in front of goal. A number of the players wore padded shin-guards, and all the rest were taken to possess them and expected to conduct themselves on that understanding.
source_text: Hockey as it was played at the Dower House at Matching's Easy before the war, was a game combining danger, physical exercise and kindliness in a very high degree. Except for the infant in the perambulator and the outwardly calm but inwardly resentful aunt, who wheeled the child up and down in a position of maximum danger just behind the unnetted goal, every one was involved. Quite able-bodied people acquainted with the game played forward, the less well-informed played a defensive game behind the forward line, elderly, infirm, and bulky persons were used chiefly as obstacles in goal. Several players wore padded leg-guards, and all players were assumed to have them and expected to behave accordingly.

text: He began going back over the list of his adventures, and the details of his automobile mishaps mingled with the story of his sentimental steering. There was, for instance, that colossal Siddons business. He had been taking a bend in a girlish friendship and had taken it altogether too fast. What a hideous tangle that had been! Once one is off the road, anything can happen, from a bent mudguard to the car landing on top of you. And then there had been his forty-miles-an-hour burst with the brilliant and gifted Delphine Marquise—for whom he was meant to write a play and become a perfect Annunzio. Until Willersley turned up—very like the motorcyclist—whirring toward him from the other direction. And after that came rage, humiliation....
source_text: He began to recapitulate items in the catalogue of his escapades, and the details of his automobile misadventures mixed themselves up with the story of his heart steering. For example there was that tremendous Siddons affair. He had been taking the corner of a girlish friendship and he had taken it altogether too far. What a frightful mess that had been! When once one is off the road anything may happen, from a crumpled mud-guard to the car on the top of you. And there was his forty miles an hour spurt with the great and gifted Delphine Marquise--for whom he was to have written a play and been a perfect Annunzio. Until Willersley appeared--very like the motor-cyclist--buzzing in the opposite direction. And then had ensued angers, humiliations....

text: He leaned back from his desk and let his thoughts run over these matters. His imagination made a feeble effort to picture a world in which credit had disappeared and money itself had become doubtful in worth. He imagined that a great many people would continue buying and selling at, or close to, the old prices, simply out of habit.
source_text: He sat back from his desk contemplating these things. His imagination made a weak attempt to picture a world in which credit has vanished and money is of doubtful value. He supposed a large number of people would just go on buying and selling at or near the old prices by force of habit.

text: In those miserable weeks when she refused to accept death, Letty’s face altered. Her pretty colour faded; she turned pale, and her mouth set into hardness, while her eyes took on a fixed, hard shine. She never cried, never showed any sign of grief, and she kept talking about Teddy in a dry, casual tone. All the time she spoke of his eventual return. “Teddy,” she would say, “will be surprised at this,” or, “Teddy will feel cheated when he sees how I’ve changed that.”
source_text: Letty's face changed in those pitiful weeks when she was denying death. She lost her pretty colour, she became white; her mouth grew hard and her eyes had a hard brightness. She never wept, she never gave a sign of sorrow, and she insisted upon talking about Teddy, in a dry offhand voice. Constantly she referred to his final return. "Teddy," she said, "will be surprised at this," or "Teddy will feel sold when he sees how I have altered that."

text: “Many a time we have spoken together, oh yes, many a time,” he said to Mrs. Harwood. “And now it all must cease. She is fond of flowers, she is fond of birds. She is very gentle and pure. I have taught her a good many words in German, and more than once I have tried to sketch her in pencil, and now I must depart and never look upon her again.”
source_text: "Often we have spoken together, oh yes, often," he assured Mrs. Britling. "And now it must all end. She loves flowers, she loves birds. She is most sweet and innocent. I have taught her many words in German and several times I have tried to draw her in pencil, and now I must go away and never see her any more."

text: “Night, perhaps, is a little more nerve-racking than day. You raise your head and look around, and there is the flat, dim country with its ruined houses, its lumps of stuff that are dead bodies, and its long blurred lines of sandbags, while the searchlights sweep about like white windmill arms, and now and then a flare or a star shell flashes up. And you have that ugly feeling of people creeping and creeping all night long between the trenches....
source_text: "The night time is perhaps a little more nervy than the day. You get your head up and look about, and see the flat dim country with its ruined houses and its lumps of stuff that are dead bodies and its long vague lines of sandbags, and the searchlights going like white windmill arms and an occasional flare or star shell. And you have a nasty feeling of people creeping and creeping all night between the trenches....

text: He endeavored to persuade himself that he had not heard what he had heard; but Mrs. Vale possessed a lively pen and an even livelier faculty for afterthought, and once her mind had fastened upon the matter she carried her attack forward in half a dozen dazzling letters.... Yet, on the other hand, she avowed an ever-growing devotion to Mr. Letham. And to avow devotion to Mr. Letham was to place him beneath a deep sense of obligation—since, in truth, he was a modest man. Thus he discovered himself in a very perplexing state of feeling.
source_text: He tried to imagine he hadn't heard all that he had heard, but Mrs. Harrowdean had a nimble pen and nimbler afterthoughts, and once her mind had got to work upon the topic she developed her offensive in half-a-dozen brilliant letters.... On the other hand she professed a steadily increasing passion for Mr. Britling. And to profess passion for Mr. Britling was to put him under a sense of profound obligation--because indeed he was a modest man. He found himself in an emotional quandary.

text: Running hard to lend help to Mr. Raeburn came the youngest Britling boy, a delightful contrast. He was like a puffball propping and assisting a conger eel. Before Mr. Direck, the little stout man was full of alertness. Teddy was backing the attack near the middle of the field, shouting “Centre!” while Mr. Britling, very round and determined, bounced straight toward the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as fast as her sister, stood between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man’s stick struck Cecily’s. A tangle of sticks and feet followed, and the little short man seemed to be trying to bring Cecily down as one fells a tree. She attempted to pass the ball to her centre forward—too late—and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was darting back toward Mr. Britling’s goal in a rush that Mr. Direck saw it was his duty to join.
source_text: Running hard to the help of Mr. Raeburn was the youngest Britling boy, a beautiful contrast. It was like a puff ball supporting and assisting a conger eel. In front of Mr. Direck the little stout man was being alert. Teddy was supporting the attack near the middle of the field, crying "Centre!" while Mr. Britling, very round and resolute, was bouncing straight towards the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as swiftly as her sister, was between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man's stick had clashed with Cecily's. Confused things happened with sticks and feet, and the little short man appeared to be trying to cut down Cecily as one cuts down a tree, she tried to pass the ball to her centre forward--too late, and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was flickering back towards Mr. Britling's goal in a rush in which Mr. Direck perceived it was his duty to join.

text: “But it is your schoolmasters and catechisms that have turned you from God.... They would have it that He owns the whole of Nature. And all manner of foolish pretensions. Like those medieval heralds who were forever insisting that Christ must surely have been a fine gentleman, and therefore entitled to bear arms. But God lies within Nature and necessity. Necessity is a thing beyond God—beyond right and wrong, beyond space and time, a riddle forever sealed. God is nearer than that. Necessity is the outermost thing, but God is the inmost thing. Closer is He than breathing and nearer than hands and feet. He is the Other Thing beyond this world. Greater than Nature or Necessity, for He is spirit and they are blind, though not yet governing them.... Not yet....”
source_text: "But it's your teachers and catechisms have set you against God.... They want to make out He owns all Nature. And all sorts of silly claims. Like the heralds in the Middle Ages who insisted that Christ was certainly a great gentleman entitled to bear arms. But God is within Nature and necessity. Necessity is a thing beyond God--beyond good and ill, beyond space and time, a mystery everlastingly impenetrable. God is nearer than that. Necessity is the uttermost thing, but God is the innermost thing. Closer He is than breathing and nearer than hands and feet. He is the Other Thing than this world. Greater than Nature or Necessity, for he is a spirit and they are blind, but not controlling them.... Not yet...."

text: The seventh entanglement had been deeply unsettling. He had made an ass of himself with a girl still very much in her youth, young enough to make him redden at the recollection; it had not advanced far, yet it had so soured his nights with uncomfortable meditations that he had—certainly not for the first time—resolved, once and for all, to abandon these foolish romantic fantasies. Then, when Mrs. Vale drifted into his orbit, she appeared to be precisely the safeguard required to keep his imagination from going astray. She arrived laden with adoration so intense it bordered on worship. She was the most brilliant and quick-witted of young widows. She wrote criticism, and very well too, for the Scrutinizer and the Partisan, and now and again verse for the Right Revue—whenever the mood took her. She possessed an occasional streak of high spirits that was almost better than wit, and which quickly made her a favourite with nearly everyone she encountered; besides, she was only twenty miles off, in her charming little house and her absurd, cheerful park.
source_text: The seventh affair had been very disconcerting. He had made a fool of himself with quite a young girl; he blushed to think how young; it hadn't gone very far, but it had made his nocturnal reflections so disagreeable that he had--by no means for the first time--definitely and forever given up these foolish dreams of love. And when Mrs. Harrowdean swam into his circle, she seemed just exactly what was wanted to keep his imagination out of mischief. She came bearing flattery to the pitch of adoration. She was the brightest and cleverest of young widows. She wrote quite admirably criticism in the _Scrutator_ and the _Sectarian_, and occasionally poetry in the _Right Review_--when she felt disposed to do so. She had an intermittent vein of high spirits that was almost better than humour and made her quickly popular with most of the people she met, and she was only twenty miles away in her pretty house and her absurd little jolly park.

text: In the hall he came upon a tall man in khaki, standing there and reading the copy of _The Times_ that ordinarily reposed upon the hall table. At Mr. Ashwell’s entrance the man turned, and disclosed the keen, birdlike face of Mr. Adrian Vane. It was as though his friend had forestalled him by a step.
source_text: In the hall he found a tall figure in khaki standing and reading _The Times_ that usually lay upon the hall table. The figure turned at Mr. Britling's entry, and revealed the aquiline features of Mr. Lawrence Carmine. It was as if his friend had stolen a march on him.

text: “It’s only a place of cruel things. It’s strewn with knives. It’s full of disease and accidents. As for God—either there is no God, or he’s an idiot. A drooling idiot. He’s like some fool who tears the wings off flies.”
source_text: "It is just a place of cruel things. It is all set with knives. It is full of diseases and accidents. As for God--either there is no God or he is an idiot. He is a slobbering idiot. He is like some idiot who pulls off the wings of flies."

text: Edith’s home was better off than Mary’s; she brought her own money into it, and the rearing of her children was conducted with much greater efficiency than Mary’s instinctive ways. Hugh had nearly died in his first year; some summer sickness had seized upon him, and that had bound him to his father’s heart with a knot of fear. But no illness had ever come near Edith’s nursery. It was Hugh whom Mr. Britling had once seen, small and green-faced and miserable under an anaesthetic for a necessary little operation on his adenoids. His younger children had never pierced Mr. Britling’s heart with any such pity; they were not as delicate as their elder brother, not so exposed to the petty hostilities of dust and germs. From things like this there grew a vague, formless cloud of championship for Hugh. Jealousies and suspicions lie hidden in every human relation. We move through life magnificently pretending they are not there, pretending to the generosity we would like to possess. And in all step-relationships jealousy and suspicion are not merely hidden; they are alive.
source_text: Edith's home was more prosperous than Mary's; she brought her own money to it; the bringing up of her children was a far more efficient business than Mary's instinctive proceedings. Hugh had very nearly died in his first year of life; some summer infection had snatched at him; that had tied him to his father's heart by a knot of fear; but no infection had ever come near Edith's own nursery. And it was Hugh that Mr. Britling had seen, small and green-faced and pitiful under an anaesthetic for some necessary small operation to his adenoids. His younger children had never stabbed to Mr. Britling's heart with any such pitifulness; they were not so thin-skinned as their elder brother, not so assailable by the little animosities of dust and germ. And out of such things as this evolved a shapeless cloud of championship for Hugh. Jealousies and suspicions are latent in every human relationship. We go about the affairs of life pretending magnificently that they are not so, pretending to the generosities we desire. And in all step-relationships jealousy and suspicion are not merely latent, they stir.

text: So Mr. Britling followed the course of his eighth digression, rather overtaxed in the business of flowers and the choosing of little trinkets, and pursued by the unseen and tireless Oliver, driven into an unwelcome diligence of attentions—attentions in the style of the professional lover in French novels—by the recollection and the anticipation of tearful scenes. “Then you don’t love me! And it’s all ruined. I’ve risked gossip and my reputation.... I was a fool ever to dream of making love beautifully....”
source_text: So Mr. Britling trod the path of his eighth digression, rather overworked in the matter of flowers and the selection of small jewellery, stalked by the invisible and indefatigable Oliver, haunted into an unwilling industry of attentions--attentions on the model of the professional lover of the French novels--by the memory and expectation of tearful scenes. "Then you don't love me! And it's all spoilt. I've risked talk and my reputation.... I was a fool ever to dream of making love beautifully...."

text: Only the most shallow-minded person would now insist that man is a rational being.
