Man is an irrational being, and it was wholly irrational, and entirely human, for Mr. Britling, in the course of his nightly self-reproaches, to confuse his private resentment over his infatuation for Mrs. Harrowdean with his ill-judged assault on the wall of Brandismead Park. He should never have bought that car; he should never have been so eager to meet Mrs. Harrowdean more than halfway.
source_text: Only the most superficial mind would assert nowadays that man is a reasonable creature. Man is an unreasonable creature, and it was entirely unreasonable and human for Mr. Britling during his nocturnal self-reproaches to mix up his secret resentment at his infatuation for Mrs. Harrowdean with his ill-advised attack upon the wall of Brandismead Park. He ought never to have bought that car; he ought never to have been so ready to meet Mrs. Harrowdean more than halfway.

text: For a few moments, the old sting of jealousy started to fester again. He remembered the fierce rivalry that had ended in his defeat, the contest of gifts and treats.... It had been so public that everyone in Carrierville knew about it, talked about it, and chose sides.... And above it all, Mamie, with her brilliant smile, had glided on like a goddess in procession....
source_text: For some moments the old sting of jealousy rankled again. He recalled the flaring rivalry that had ended in his defeat, the competition of gifts and treats.... A thing so open that all Carrierville knew of it, discussed it, took sides.... And over it all Mamie with her flashing smile had sailed like a processional goddess....

text: They came upon the German tutor in a small courtyard, playing badminton with the two younger boys. He was a stout young fellow with spectacles and neat, economical movements; the game proceeded largely by missed strokes, and the tally was kept in German. He won in a deliberate way, owing chiefly to the younger boy’s zeal, for his ardent returns were forever going wide. At once the children assailed Mrs. Darrell with a united burst of eagerness. “Mummy! Is it going to be fancy-dress supper?”
source_text: They found the German tutor in a little court playing Badminton with the two younger boys. He was a plump young man with glasses and compact gestures; the game progressed chiefly by misses and the score was counted in German. He won thoughtfully and chiefly through the ardour of the younger brother, whose enthusiastic returns invariably went out. Instantly the boys attacked Mrs. Britling with a concerted enthusiasm. "Mummy! Is it to be dressing-up supper?"

text: There was a great deal of rose garden, and it made the air delicious. It was crowded with huge, tumbling rose bushes and with neglected standard roses, and it had a long pergola of creepers and trailers, and a great arbour; while beneath, over the beds everywhere and in defiance of all rules, the blossoms of a multitude of pansies and stock and little trailing plants surged and packed and scuffled and marched and launched great massed attacks. Then Mr. Britling led them around a red-walled vegetable garden, abundant with fruit trees, and through a door into a terraced square that had once been a farmyard outside the converted barn. The barn doors had been replaced by a window pierced with glass doors, and in the middle of the square a deep tank had been built, filled with rainwater, in which Mr. Britling remarked casually that “everybody” bathed when the weather was hot. Thyme and rosemary and other sweet-smelling plants grew on the terrace about the tank, and ten clipped little arbor vitae trees stood like sentries. Mr. Direck was tantalisingly aware that beyond some lilac bushes his newly found cousin and the related young woman in blue were playing tennis with the Indian and another young man, while whenever it was needed the large-nosed lady crossed the scene and brooded placidly over the perambulator. And Mr. Britling, choosing a seat from which Mr. Direck could not quite manage to see comfortably through the green branches those fleeting glimpses of pink and blue and white and brown, went on talking about England and America in their relation to one another and to everything else under the sun.
source_text: There was quite a lot of rose garden, it made the air delicious, and it was full of great tumbling bushes of roses and of neglected standards, and it had a long pergola of creepers and trailers and a great arbour, and underneath over the beds everywhere, contrary to all the rules, the blossom of a multitude of pansies and stock and little trailing plants swarmed and crowded and scrimmaged and drilled and fought great massed attacks. And then Mr. Britling talked their way round a red-walled vegetable garden with an abundance of fruit trees, and through a door into a terraced square that had once been a farmyard, outside the converted barn. The barn doors had been replaced by a door-pierced window of glass, and in the middle of the square space a deep tank had been made, full of rainwater, in which Mr. Britling remarked casually that "everybody" bathed when the weather was hot. Thyme and rosemary and suchlike sweet-scented things grew on the terrace about the tank, and ten trimmed little trees of _Arbor vitae_ stood sentinel. Mr. Direck was tantalisingly aware that beyond some lilac bushes were his new-found cousin and the kindred young woman in blue playing tennis with the Indian and another young man, while whenever it was necessary the large-nosed lady crossed the stage and brooded soothingly over the perambulator. And Mr. Britling, choosing a seat from which Mr. Direck just couldn't look comfortably through the green branches at the flying glimpses of pink and blue and white and brown, continued to talk about England and America in relation to each other and everything else under the sun.

text: The curious thing," said Mr. Britling, continuing his examination of the matter, "is that when one looks at such things now, as one looks at them as perfectly possible, they are not nearly so terrifying and overwhelming to the mind as they would have seemed—last week. I believe I should pack you all into Gladys and set off westward with a sort of exhilaration....
source_text: "The curious thing," said Mr. Britling, pursuing his examination of the matter, "is that, looking at these things as one does now, as things quite possible, they are not nearly so terrifying and devastating to the mind as they would have seemed--last week. I believe I should load you all into Gladys and start off westward with a kind of exhilaration...."

text: The hands of fate did not pause for Herr Heinrich’s embarrassments and miseries. He fretted from the room below to his own again, he set out on mysterious and useless errands toward the village inn, he wandered uneasily about the garden. His head and face grew pinker and pinker; his eyes were flushed and troubled. Everyone tried to say and do kind and reassuring things for him.
source_text: The hands of fate paused not for Herr Heinrich's embarrassments and distresses. He fretted from his room downstairs and back to his room, he went out upon mysterious and futile errands towards the village inn, he prowled about the garden. His head and face grew pinker and pinker; his eyes were flushed and distressed. Everybody sought to say and do kind and reassuring things to him.

text: “War is a heavy black drape drawn over all the hopes and gentleness of humankind. And yet, somehow, it has always let a few shafts of light slip through; now—no, I am not dreaming—it is wearing thin, and here and there, at a thousand places, the brightness is beginning to pierce it. We owe it to all these beloved young men—”
source_text: _"War is a curtain of dense black fabric across all the hopes and kindliness of mankind. Yet always it has let through some gleams of light, and now--I am not dreaming--it grows threadbare, and here and there and at a thousand points the light is breaking through. We owe it to all these dear youths--"_

text: “One speaks,” he said, “and only weeks and months afterward does one begin to grasp the sense of one’s own words. A month ago I was saying that this was the greatest event in history. I said it was the highest summons ever made to the will and strength of Britain. I said there was not a single life throughout our empire that would not be profoundly altered by this war. I uttered all those things; they passed through my lips; I suppose some dim notion was behind them.... But only now do I truly understand what it is I was saying. Now—let me repeat it as though I had never spoken it before; this _is_ the greatest thing in history, that we _are_ all required to do our utmost to oppose this gigantic assault on the peace and liberty of the world. Well, to do our utmost does not mean idling in agreeable gardens and waiting for the papers.... It means renouncing comfort and safety....
source_text: "One talks," he said, "and then weeks and months later one learns the meaning of the things one has been saying. I was saying a month ago that this is the biggest thing that has happened in history. I said that this was the supreme call upon the will and resources of England. I said there was not a life in all our empire that would not be vitally changed by this war. I said all these things; they came through my mouth; I suppose there was a sort of thought behind them.... Only at this moment do I understand what it is that I said. Now--let me say it over as if I had never said it before; this _is_ the biggest thing in history, that we _are_ all called upon to do our utmost to resist this tremendous attack upon the peace and freedom of the world. Well, doing our utmost does not mean standing about in pleasant gardens waiting for the newspaper.... It means the abandonment of ease and security....

text: But when he reached the hospital, he found her badly injured indeed. She had been crushed in some intricate way that had left the upper part of her body unharmed, propped at an angle among the pillows. Beneath the sheets and counterpane, the horror of bandaged, shattered limbs and tortured flesh was hidden from view. Morphia, he understood, had been given to spare her pain, though before long she might have to endure it. She lay there in bed with a look almost of being enthroned, very pale and motionless, her strong profile, prominent nose, untidy hair, and a certain dignity making her seem less like an old woman than some immensely important, very ancient man, an aged pope perhaps. After they had lifted her, dressed her, and put her to bed, she had said nothing except, “Send for Hughie Britling, The Dower House, Matching’s Easy. He is the best of the bunch.” She had gone on repeating the address and that praise steadily, as if in capital letters, even after they had told her a telegram had already been sent.
source_text: But he found her in the hospital very much hurt indeed. She had been smashed in some complicated manner that left the upper part of her body intact, and lying slantingly upon pillows. Over the horror of bandaged broken limbs and tormented flesh below sheets and a counterpane were drawn. Morphia had been injected, he understood, to save her from pain, but presently it might be necessary for her to suffer. She lay up in her bed with an effect of being enthroned, very white and still, her strong profile with its big nose and her straggling hair and a certain dignity gave her the appearance of some very important, very old man, of an aged pope for instance, rather than of an old woman. She had made no remark after they had set her and dressed her and put her to bed except "send for Hughie Britling, The Dower House, Matching's Easy. He is the best of the bunch." She had repeated the address and this commendation firmly over and over again, in large print as it were, even after they had assured her that a telegram had been despatched.

text: You may suppose all this sounds like nothing but grumbling, but the thing I am trying to say—I set it down now, in a decent round hand, to ease my temper—is that the whole affair could be managed far better and far more cheaply if it were not left in the hands of these wholly untrained and excessively select military gentlemen. They fancy they are guiding England and setting an example to us all; whereas in truth they are only holding us back. What on earth are they doing with everything? Not one of them, when he is at home, is permitted to order his own dinner, or thrust his nose into the kitchen, or examine the household accounts.... The average British colonel is a helpless old gentleman; he ought to have a nurse.... This is no mere petty complaint from an affronted stomach; it is a serious question for the nation. Before long the nation may need the food that is being squandered on all these antics. Taken together, it must come to the daily destruction of tons upon tons of every sort of provision. Tons.... And if the war lasts longer than we have imagined!
source_text: "You will think all this is awful grousing, but the point I want to make--I hereby to ease my feelings make it now in a fair round hand--is that all this business could be done far better and far cheaper if it wasn't left to these absolutely inexperienced and extremely exclusive military gentlemen. They think they are leading England and showing us all how; instead of which they are just keeping us back. Why in thunder are they doing everything? Not one of them, when he is at home, is allowed to order the dinner or poke his nose into his own kitchen or check the household books.... The ordinary British colonel is a helpless old gentleman; he ought to have a nurse.... This is not merely the trivial grievance of my insulted stomach, it is a serious matter for the country. Sooner or later the country may want the food that is being wasted in all these capers. In the aggregate it must amount to a daily destruction of tons of stuff of all sorts. Tons.... Suppose the war lasts longer than we reckon!"

text: He engaged the gears and set off, presently gliding in a subdued run, his motor scarcely more than a murmur, along the straight road toward Alderwick. The noises behind him diminished and dwindled, until they were lost altogether, leaving beneath the moon an immense, untroubled hush. There appeared to be no movement save his own, no music but the neat, restrained, mechanical beat before his feet. Soon he emerged onto the high road, and, disregarding the byway that branched off toward Brackenfold, went on evenly eastward in the direction of the sea. He had never driven at night before. He had expected a clumsy, wearisome passage; instead he found himself in an unimagined, silvered magnificence of motion. It was as though the car itself were traveling on moonlight that night.... Brackenfold could wait. Indeed, the later he reached Brackenfold, the more affecting and romantic the small farce of reconciliation would be. And he was in no haste for that farce. He felt he needed to take in this vast summer stillness around him, the one thing among all the day’s things that seemed to carry any hint of the grand tragedy unfolding for mankind. Moving through that motionless vigil, one could for the first time imagine the millions out there, the broad river valleys, villages, towns, mountain chains, harbors, and seas, all at their inaudible labor.
source_text: He slipped in his gear to starting, and was presently running quietly with his engine purring almost inaudibly along the level road to Hartleytree. The sounds behind him grew smaller and smaller, and died away leaving an immense unruffled quiet under the moon. There seemed no motion but his own, no sound but the neat, subdued, mechanical rhythm in front of his feet. Presently he ran out into the main road, and heedless of the lane that turned away towards Pyecrafts, drove on smoothly towards the east and the sea. Never before had he driven by night. He had expected a fumbling and tedious journey; he found he had come into an undreamt-of silvery splendour of motion. For it seemed as though even the automobile was running on moonlight that night.... Pyecrafts could wait. Indeed the later he got to Pyecrafts the more moving and romantic the little comedy of reconciliation would be. And he was in no hurry for that comedy. He felt he wanted to apprehend this vast summer calm about him, that alone of all the things of the day seemed to convey anything whatever of the majestic tragedy that was happening to mankind. As one slipped through this still vigil one could imagine for the first time the millions away there marching, the wide river valleys, villages, cities, mountain-ranges, ports and seas inaudibly busy.

text: And Cecily made everything far more difficult. She showed him no mercy. She held him at a distance. “How can I let you make love to me,” she said, “when all our English men are going off to the war, when Teddy is a prisoner and Hugh is in the trenches? If I were a man—!”
source_text: And Cecily made things much more difficult. She was pitiless with him. She kept him aloof. "How can I let you make love to me," she said, "when our English men are all going to the war, when Teddy is a prisoner and Hugh is in the trenches. If I were a man--!"

text: “Quite true,” said Mr. Darnel, to whom it appeared the proper moment had come for speech. “That is perhaps America’s difficulty even more than England’s. Though I have not had the paternal experience you have gone through.... I can see very plainly that a son is a matter of the gravest consequence.”
source_text: "That's very true," said Mr. Direck, to whom it seemed the time was ripe to say something. "This is the problem of America perhaps even more than of England. Though I have not had the parental experience you have undergone.... I can see very clearly that a son is a very serious proposition."

text: He walked the terrace with her and talked. He said that even now very few people understood how frail was the credit structure on which the modern world depended. It was only a vast tissue of confidence, resting in great part upon the incurious laziness of—everybody. So long as men, taken together, continued to believe in it, it held; but let faith weaken enough, and it might break under any shock. It might disappear altogether—as credit disappeared when Italy was broken up by the Goths—and leave us with nothing but solid things, real estate, possession being nine points of the law, and all that kind of thing. Did she remember that late novel of Gissing’s?—Veranilda, it was called. It showed the world at a time when wealth meant nothing except what one could carry hidden on one’s person or keep guarded beside one. That sort of change had come slowly to the Roman world, through the length of generations; but now we lived faster, and our institutions were more brittle. No one knew how strong credit really was, or how weak; no one knew whether even this present strain might not bring it crashing to pieces.... And then all the small life we had known would go drifting away....
source_text: He paced the lawn with her and talked. He said that even now very few people realised the flimsiness of the credit system by which the modern world was sustained. It was a huge growth of confidence, due very largely to the uninquiring indolence of--everybody. It was sound so long as mankind did, on the whole, believe in it; give only a sufficient loss of faith and it might suffer any sort of collapse. It might vanish altogether--as the credit system vanished at the breaking up of Italy by the Goths--and leave us nothing but tangible things, real property, possession nine points of the law, and that sort of thing. Did she remember that last novel of Gissing's?--"Veranilda," it was called. It was a picture of the world when there was no wealth at all except what one could carry hidden or guarded about with one. That sort of thing came to the Roman Empire slowly, in the course of lifetimes, but nowadays we lived in a rapider world--with flimsier institutions. Nobody knew the strength or the weakness of credit; nobody knew whether even the present shock might not send it smashing down.... And then all the little life we had lived so far would roll away....

text: Her mind was crushed to the ground by this disaster, one she had never permitted herself to imagine until this very moment. She had never allowed herself to think of it. Her husband’s figure, like some miserable animal, hurt and bleeding, filled her thoughts. She scarcely thought of Hugh. “Oh, what can I do for him?” she asked herself, sinking down before the unlit fire in her bedroom.... “What can I say or do?”
source_text: Her mind was beaten to the ground by this catastrophe, of which to this moment she had never allowed herself to think. She had never allowed herself to think of it. The figure of her husband, like some pitiful beast, wounded and bleeding, filled her mind. She gave scarcely a thought to Hugh. "Oh, what can I _do_ for him?" she asked herself, sitting down before her unlit bedroom fire.... "What can I say or do?"

text: “It’s an odd thing, Father, but I feel almost a kind of _mutinous_ stir in writing like this. One begins to suspect that it is somehow wrong to think at all. It comes of discipline—of belonging to a machine. And yet I am not sure one ought to think. If one really follows things out in this spirit, where is it all to end? Merrick—his proper name, by the way, is Martin Keene—has none of these scruples. ‘The bloody Germans pushed into Belgium,’ he says. ‘We’ve got to shove ’em out again. That’s all there is to it. Leastways, that’s all _I_ know.... I don’t know nothing about Serbia, I don’t know nothing about anything, except that the Germans have got to be stopped from this sort of game for ever and ever, amen.’...”
source_text: "It's a queer thing, Daddy, but I have a sort of _seditious_ feeling in writing things like this. One gets to feel that it is wrong to think. It's the effect of discipline. Of being part of a machine. Still, I doubt if I ought to think. If one really looks into things in this spirit, where is it going to take us? Ortheris--his real name by the by is Arthur Jewell--hasn't any of these troubles. 'The b----y Germans butted into Belgium,' he says. 'We've got to 'oof 'em out again. That's all abart it. Leastways it's all _I_ know.... I don't know nothing about Serbia, I don't know nothing about anything, except that the Germans got to stop this sort of gime for Everlasting, Amen.'...

text: This was only the newest example of an old infirmity. Would there ever be a kind of man whose mind moved swiftly while his deeds came on at a measured pace? Such a creature, indeed, would be formidable. Mr. Harrow’s mind was swift and hopeful, and his actions were swifter still, more impatient than the thoughts that begot them. Even in youth he had seen his acts thrust themselves rudely through the press of his intentions and fling him into sudden mortifications. Before his reasons had time to assemble themselves, his resolutions were already in being. He had made a thousand earnest protests to himself; he had tried to mend the flaws of his nature by means of maxims pinned above his bed and memoranda tucked into his watch case. “Be calm!” one of them ran. “Never lose sight of the goal.” And, “Advance steadily, in order, without pause; only so shall you reach the place you seek.” In mistrusting every impulse, in setting a jealous watch upon every fancy, he believed there lay his only chance of escape from the ambush of innumerable disasters. Otherwise he seemed to himself to be dancing among powder kegs and tangled wire.
source_text: This was just the latest instance of a life-long trouble. Will there ever be a sort of man whose thoughts are quick and his acts slow? Then indeed we shall have a formidable being. Mr. Britling's thoughts were quick and sanguine and his actions even more eager than his thoughts. Already while he was a young man Mr. Britling had found his acts elbow their way through the hurry of his ideas and precipitate humiliations. Long before his reasons were marshalled, his resolutions were formed. He had attempted a thousand remonstrances with himself; he had sought to remedy the defects in his own character by written inscriptions in his bedroom and memoranda inside his watch case. "Keep steady!" was one of them. "Keep the End in View." And, "Go steadfastly, coherently, continuously; only so can you go where you will." In distrusting all impulse, scrutinising all imagination, he was persuaded lay his one prospect of escape from the surprise of countless miseries. Otherwise he danced among glass bombs and barbed wire.

text: Indeed, this was far from the first occasion on which his mind had set up its whirring torment within his skull. Earlier encounters had taught him to make the most careful provision for just such assaults. Hanging over the foot of the bed was a light, warmly fleeced pyjama suit of llama wool, and beside it stood two high boots of the same stuff, fastening at mid-calf. Thus equipped, Mr. Vale set about making tea. A Primus stove waited in the fender of the fireplace, and upon it stood a bright brass kettle, highly polished and full of water; a little table held a tea-caddy, a teapot, a lemon, and a glass. Mr. Vale lit the stove and then wandered to his desk. He meant to write certain “Plain Words on Ireland.” He switched on his study lamp and sat brooding beside it until the sound of the water coming to the boil summoned him to his tea-making.
source_text: Indeed this was by no means the first time that his brain had become a whirring torment in his skull. Previous experiences had led to the most careful provision for exactly such states. Over the end of the bed hung a light, warm pyjama suit of llama-wool, and at the feet of it were two tall boots of the same material that buckled to the middle of his calf. So protected, Mr. Britling proceeded to make himself tea. A Primus stove stood ready inside the fender of his fireplace, and on it was a brightly polished brass kettle filled with water; a little table carried a tea-caddy, a tea-pot, a lemon and a glass. Mr. Britling lit the stove and then strolled to his desk. He was going to write certain "Plain Words about Ireland." He lit his study lamp and meditated beside it until a sound of water boiling called him to his tea-making.

text: Miss Corner was drawn into the sunset party. She spoke with Mr. Carmine and, Mr. Direck thought, showed a remarkable originality of mind. She said that “The City of the Sun” was like the cities the boys sometimes built on the playroom floor. She called it the dearest little city and went on to give some entertaining details. She described the painted walls, which made the circuit of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. Then she asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian or Chinese Utopias.
source_text: Miss Corner was drawn into the sunset party. She talked to Mr. Carmine and displayed, Mr. Direck thought, great originality of mind. She said "The City of the Sun" was like the cities the boys sometimes made on the playroom floor. She said it was the dearest little city, and gave some amusing particulars. She described the painted walls that made the tour of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. She asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian nor Chinese Utopias.

text: “But I daresay any number of people could contrive an Irish pamphlet. Only you could have written ‘The Still Places.’ Oh, why don’t you bring that great, lovely thing to its end, and leave all this world of fact and journals, all these coarse, noisy, contentious, discordant affairs, to other people? You have the enchanted faculty; you might be a poet. You can carry us away from all these dreadful realities to Beauty-land, and instead you are content to be merely a critic and a quarreller. It is your company about you. It is your wretched realities. It is that Practicality always at your elbow. You ought never to catch sight of a newspaper. You ought never to let an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on dishes of milk sipped in valleys of asphodel.”
source_text: "But I suppose all sorts of people could write an Irish pamphlet. Nobody but you could write 'The Silent Places.' Oh, _why_ don't you finish that great beautiful thing, and leave all this world of reality and newspapers, all these Crude, Vulgar, Quarrelsome, Jarring things to other people? You have the magic gift, you might be a poet, you can take us out of all these horrid things that are, away to Beautyland, and you are just content to be a critic and a disputer. It's your surroundings. It's your sordid realities. It's that Practicality at your elbow. You ought never to see a newspaper. You ought never to have an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on bowls of milk drunk in valleys of asphodel."

text: He had made up his mind that he would not play. Yet he confided this resolution to several people in turn. He told Mr. Harbury, and Mr. Harbury said, “We shall put you at full-back, where you’ll have a stroke now and then and not much else to trouble you. The only thing to remember is to strike with the flat of your stick and never lift it above your shoulders.” He told Ned, and Ned replied, “My best advice is that you dress as lightly as decency will allow, and put your collar and tie in your pocket before the game starts. Hockey is, by rights, a winter game.” He mentioned it to the elderly lady with the sharp nose and the air of a maiden aunt, and she said, almost with envy, “Everybody here is asked except me. I mind the perambulator. One mustn’t be jealous, I suppose. I don’t know why I shouldn’t play. I’m not nearly as old as all that.” He told Philip, and Philip cautioned him to be very sure he did not get hold of one of the springed sticks. He began to wonder whether it would not be wiser to retire to his own room and lock the door, or else wander away for a walk in Cedarings Park. But then he would miss Miss Ware, who, it seemed certain, was coming up for hockey. Yet if he did not miss her, he might only expose himself to ridicule in her eyes, and wipe out the effect of the green silk gown with the golden pheasants.
source_text: He decided he would not play. He took various people into his confidence. He told Mr. Britling, and Mr. Britling said, "We'll make you full back, where you'll get a hit now and then and not have very much to do. All you have to remember is to hit with the flat side of your stick and not raise it above your shoulders." He told Teddy, and Teddy said, "I strongly advise you to dress as thinly as you can consistently with decency, and put your collar and tie in your pocket before the game begins. Hockey is properly a winter game." He told the maiden aunt-like lady with the prominent nose, and she said almost enviously, "Every one here is asked to play except me. I assuage the perambulator. I suppose one mustn't be envious. I don't see why I shouldn't play. I'm not so old as all that." He told Hugh, and Hugh warned him to be careful not to get hold of one of the sprung sticks. He considered whether it wouldn't be wiser to go to his own room and lock himself in, or stroll off for a walk through Claverings Park. But then he would miss Miss Corner, who was certain, it seemed, to come up for hockey. On the other hand, if he did not miss her he might make himself ridiculous in her eyes, and efface the effect of the green silk stuff with the golden pheasants.

text: Mr. Direck had already begun, on the liner, to accustom himself to the jumping inconsecutiveness of English conversation. He now made what he felt was a very fair hop, and lowered his voice to a confidential undertone. (It was probably Adam, in his first talk with Eve, who discovered how agreeable it was to slip into a confidential undertone beside a pretty ear, with a pretty wave of hair above it.)
source_text: Mr. Direck had already begun on the liner to adapt himself to the hopping inconsecutiveness of English conversation. He made now what he felt was quite a good hop, and he dropped his voice to a confidential undertone. (It was probably Adam in his first conversation with Eve, who discovered the pleasantness of dropping into a confidential undertone beside a pretty ear with a pretty wave of hair above it.)

text: Not all of Hugh’s letters were taken up with those grim technical matters. News and gossip did not always come his way; it was uncommon for a young man with a commission to descend to shop talk for the benefit of two young men who had none; newspapers were scarce and maps scarcer still, and even in France, with the guns audible, Hugh could for the time being find war almost as dull as it had sometimes seemed in England. Yet his complaints about military methods gradually fell silent. “Things are done better out here,” he observed, and, “We’re nearer reality here. I’m beginning to respect my Captain, who is acquiring a sense of locality. Happily for our prospects.” And elsewhere he wondered, in a peculiarly his own way, whether he was simply growing accustomed to army ways, whether he was beginning to perceive their reason, or whether it was really that army ways drew closer and closer to efficiency as they drew nearer the enemy. “And here one doesn’t have the haunting feeling that war is after all an hallucination. It’s already common sense and the business of life....”
source_text: Not all Hugh's letters were concerned with these grim technicalities. It was not always that news and gossip came along; it was rare that a young man with a commission would condescend to talk shop to two young men without one; there were few newspapers and fewer maps, and even in France and within sound of guns, Hugh could presently find warfare almost as much a bore as it had been at times in England. But his criticism of military methods died away. "Things are done better out here," he remarked, and "We're nearer reality here. I begin to respect my Captain. Who is developing a sense of locality. Happily for our prospects." And in another place he speculated in an oddly characteristic manner whether he was getting used to the army way, whether he was beginning to see the sense of the army way, or whether it really was that the army way braced up nearer and nearer to efficiency as it got nearer to the enemy. "And here one hasn't the haunting feeling that war is after all an hallucination. It's already common sense and the business of life....

text: Never had the twin refraction of his consciousness shown itself with such sharpness. On the one side, the Alton of the impartial mind watched the ordinary peace of the world go out like daylight when a prison shutter drops across the window; and on the other, the Alton of private existence saw all the easy comfort of his dealings with Mrs. Vale slipping away in a baffling and unreasonable quarrel. He did not wish to lose Mrs. Vale; he regarded the rupture with a deep and deeply selfish consternation. It seemed to him the senseless ending of an arrangement whose rare and inimitable felicity he was only just beginning to recognize.
source_text: Never had the double refraction of his mind been more clearly defined. On the one hand the Britling of the disinterested intelligence saw the habitual peace of the world vanish as the daylight vanishes when a shutter falls over the window of a cell; and on the other the Britling of the private life saw all the pleasant comfort of his relations with Mrs. Harrowdean disappearing in a perplexing irrational quarrel. He did not want to lose Mrs. Harrowdean; he contemplated their breach with a profound and profoundly selfish dismay. It seemed the wanton termination of an arrangement of which he was only beginning to perceive the extreme and irreplaceable satisfactoriness.

text: He came down to lunch in a frayed, spent condition, and was greatly harassed by a volley of questions from Herr Heinrich. For it was an ineradicable trait of Herr Heinrich’s that he asked questions; much of what he said took the form of question and answer, and his appetite for information was as evident as his conviction that German ought not merely to be spoken, but spoken “out loud.” He always introduced his inquiries with the word “Please,” and he persisted in attributing to his employer a universal knowledge which, after a strenuous morning of eager literary labour, was exceedingly tedious to have to disprove. He now seized the pause in the anxiety and congratulations that had followed Mr. Direck’s appearance—and Mr. Direck was so little the worse for his adventure that, with the help of the kind Teddy, he had risen, dressed, and come down to lunch—to broach the matter that had been on his mind all the morning, even to the neglect of the lessons of the Masters Britling.
source_text: He came down to lunch in a frayed, exhausted condition, and was much afflicted by a series of questions from Herr Heinrich. For it was an incurable characteristic of Herr Heinrich that he asked questions; the greater part of his conversation took the form of question and answer, and his thirst for information was as marked as his belief that German should not simply be spoken but spoken "out loud." He invariably prefaced his inquiries with the word "Please," and he insisted upon ascribing an omniscience to his employer that it was extremely irksome to justify after a strenuous morning of enthusiastic literary effort. He now took the opportunity of a lull in the solicitudes and congratulations that had followed Mr. Direck's appearance--and Mr. Direck was so little shattered by his misadventure that with the assistance of the kindly Teddy he had got up and dressed and come down to lunch--to put the matter that had been occupying his mind all the morning, even to the detriment of the lessons of the Masters Britling.

text: “Oh, we should fight—of course we should fight. Could anyone save a born fool imagine we should do anything else? They know very well we should fight. The Germans are not altogether fools. But the thing is preposterous. Why should Germany attack France? It is like saying that Mr. Lanyon here should suddenly seize an axe and fall upon Clara.... It is only the fantasy of their military scribblers. Such idiotic schoolboy nonsense. Isn’t that a lovely rose-coloured pillar? Clara only had it put in last year.... I detest all this talk of wars and rumours of wars.... It has troubled my whole life. And every year it grows worse, and every year more hollow....”
source_text: "Oh!--we should fight. Of course we should fight. Could any one but a congenital idiot suppose we shouldn't fight? They know we should fight. They aren't altogether idiots in Germany. But the thing's absurd. Why _should_ Germany attack France? It's as if Manning here took a hatchet suddenly and assailed Edith.... It's just the dream of their military journalists. It's such schoolboy nonsense. Isn't that a beautiful pillar rose? Edith only put it in last year.... I hate all this talk of wars and rumours of wars.... It's worried all my life. And it gets worse and it gets emptier every year...."

text: On Thursday, the thirtieth of July, Cailloux, Carson, the strikes, and all the ordinary concerns of daily life had been driven clean from the front page of the newspaper; the stock markets were in a frenzy of disorder, and food prices were mounting in wild, absurd leaps. Austria was shelling Belgrade, in defiance of the accepted usages of war; Russia was mobilising; Mr. Aitken declared that he was not slackening his efforts “to do everything possible to circumscribe the area of possible conflict,” and the Vienna Conference of Peace Societies was put off. “I do not see why a conflict between Russia and Austria should involve Western Europe,” said Mr. Berridge. “Our concern is only for Belgium and France.”
source_text: On Thursday, the 30th of July, Caillaux, Carson, strikes, and all the common topics of life had been swept out of the front page of the paper altogether; the stock exchanges were in a state of wild perturbation, and food prices were leaping fantastically. Austria was bombarding Belgrade, contrary to the rules of war hitherto accepted; Russia was mobilising; Mr. Asquith was, he declared, not relaxing his efforts "to do everything possible to circumscribe the area of possible conflict," and the Vienna Conference of Peace Societies was postponed. "I do not see why a conflict between Russia and Austria should involve Western Europe," said Mr. Britling. "Our concern is only for Belgium and France."

text: But the clash of habits and ways that followed the invasion of the English villages by French and Belgian refugees did not always cast the newcomers as Catholics and the hosts as “Neo-European.” In Mr. Dimple’s case, the positions were reversed. He met Mr. Britling in Claverings Park and poured out his troubles to him....
source_text: But the conflict of manners and customs that followed the invasion of the English villages by French and Belgian refugees did not always present the immigrants as Catholics and the hosts as "Neo-European." In the case of Mr. Dimple it was the other way round. He met Mr. Britling in Claverings park and told him his troubles....

text: She had left Mr. Britling to his atlas. He was lying face down beneath the hedge, the book spread out before him. Not long ago, his occupation would have seemed to her the most absurd thing imaginable. He was tracing boundaries on his maps with great care in red ink, using a fountain pen. But now she understood.
source_text: She had left Mr. Britling to his atlas. He lay prone under the hedge with it spread before him. His occupation would have seemed to her only a little while ago the absurdest imaginable. He was drawing boundaries on his maps very carefully in red ink, with a fountain pen. But now she understood.

text: Julian wrote more often than his father had ever dared to expect, and for the most part his messages were scratched out in pencil. It was almost as though some inherited craving for talk were awakening in him, and he found himself somewhat bereft of a sympathetic listener. Ellis, the school friend who had enlisted beside him, was not, it seemed, a man for theories. “Ellis is turning into a martinet,” Julian wrote. “He’s a sergeant now too, which makes a considerable divide between us.” Mr. Vale found it exceedingly hard to answer. There were so many grave and profound things he wished to say, and never did. Instead he filled his replies with overparticular accounts of the little happenings at the Manor House. Now and then, with a half-conscious echo of his son’s manner, he tried his hand at the theological and philosophical rabbits Julian had started running. But the exemplary letters he composed at night, those from a Father to a Son at War, were never set down on paper. So much the better, for there are many things of that kind that are fit to be thought and ill to be spoken....
source_text: Hugh wrote more frequently than his father had dared to hope, pencilled letters for the most part. It was as if he was beginning to feel an inherited need for talk, and was a little at a loss for a sympathetic ear. Park, his schoolmate, who had enlisted with him, wasn't, it seemed, a theoriser. "Park becomes a martinet," Hugh wrote. "Also he is a sergeant now, and this makes rather a gulf between us." Mr. Britling had the greatest difficulty in writing back. There were many grave deep things he wanted to say, and never did. Instead he gave elaborate details of the small affairs of the Dower House. Once or twice, with a half-unconscious imitation of his boy's style, he took a shot at the theological and philosophical hares that Hugh had started. But the exemplary letters that he composed of nights from a Father to a Son at War were never written down. It was just as well, for there are many things of that sort that are good to think and bad to say....

text: So Mr. Britling had grown disenchanted with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn in and entrusted with the insignia of a special constable. The business of a special constable consisted chiefly in not understanding what was happening in the military world, and in doing what he was told, in the matter of keeping watch and guard over points that might conceivably be vulnerable. He was also to be available in the event of civil disturbance. Mr. Britling was supplied with a truncheon and sent out to protect various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-west of Matching’s Easy. It was never quite plain to him what he was supposed to do if he came upon a motor-car full of armed enemies busied in weakening a culvert, or maliciously deepening some strategic ford. He imagined that he would either strike up a conversation with them, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both at once. But since he did not for a moment believe that any human creature was likely to meddle with the telegraphs, telephones, roads, and appliances entrusted to his care, his uncertainty did not much disturb him. He ranged the lonely lanes and paths by night, and came to know better a host of odd little cries and rustlings that rose from the hedges and coverts after dark. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, which seemed more than half disposed to make battle for its prey until he overawed and routed it with the beam of his electric torch....
source_text: So Mr. Britling was out of conceit with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn and entrusted with the badge of a special constable. The duties of a special constable were chiefly not to understand what was going on in the military sphere, and to do what he was told in the way of watching and warding conceivably vulnerable points. He had also to be available in the event of civil disorder. Mr. Britling was provided with a truncheon and sent out to guard various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-westward of Matching's Easy. It was never very clear to him what he would do if he found a motor-car full of armed enemies engaged in undermining a culvert, or treacherously deepening some strategic ford. He supposed he would either engage them in conversation, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both things simultaneously. But as he really did not believe for a moment that any human being was likely to tamper with the telegraphs, telephones, ways and appliances committed to his care, his uncertainty did not trouble him very much. He prowled the lonely lanes and paths in the darkness, and became better acquainted with a multitude of intriguing little cries and noises that came from the hedges and coverts at night. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, who seemed more than half inclined to give him battle for its prey until he cowed and defeated it with the glare of his electric torch....

text: The little lady was glowing, her cheeks flushed and her eyes bright. Her fair hair was tousled, and her hat sat a little awry. She looked as though she were relishing some excitement she had not often known. “All the gold’s being hoarded too,” she said, her voice bubbling with delight. “Faber says our cheques probably won’t be worth _that_ in a few days. He dashed off to London to get gold from his clubs—while he still can. I had to make Hickson take a cheque. ‘Never,’ I said, ‘will I have anything more to do with you—never—unless you do....’ Even then he looked at me as though he half believed he might refuse.”
source_text: The little lady was very flushed and bright-eyed. Her fair hair was disordered, her hat a trifle askew. She had an air of enjoying unwonted excitements. "All the gold's being hoarded too," she said, with a crow of delight in her voice. "Faber says that probably our cheques won't be worth _that_ in a few days. He rushed off to London to get gold at his clubs--while he can. I had to insist on Hickson taking a cheque. 'Never,' I said, 'will I deal with you again--never--unless you do....' Even then he looked at me almost as if he thought he wouldn't.

text: His heart was so bruised and aching that he could not bear the thought of another human being being miserable. He settled himself in his chair, drew her onto his knees, and said all the things he could think of to comfort her, to calm her, to make her feel that she mattered to him. He spoke of every pleasant thing in their lives, of everything, except that he never once named that dear pale youth who was waiting now.... He could wait a little longer....
source_text: His heart was so sore and wounded that he could not endure that another human being should go wretched. He sat down in his chair and drew her upon his knees, and said everything he could think of to console her and reassure her and make her feel that she was of value to him. He spoke of every pleasant aspect of their lives, of every aspect, except that he never named that dear pale youth who waited now.... He could wait a little longer....

text: And one day in early June, when those figs were still only a promise, Teddy came to the Dower House with Letty to say good-bye before going to the front. He was leaving in a draft to make up the various gaps and losses; where he was bound he did not know. Essex was doing well there, though at a bloody cost. Mrs. Britling had tea laid out on the lawn beneath the blue cedar, and Mr. Britling found himself at a loss for any fitting words, so that in his embarrassment he talked almost as though Teddy’s going away meant nothing at all. He was still tormented by that strange sense that Teddy was somehow his responsibility. Teddy was far less exuberant than in his pre-khaki days; there was in him a quiet exaltation rather than any brisk excitement. He knew now what lay before him. He knew now that war was no lark, that for him it was to be the gravest thing he had ever known, or was ever likely to know. There were no more jokes about Letty’s pension, and a general avoidance of talk about high explosives and asphyxiating gas....
source_text: And one day in early June while those figs were still only a hope, Teddy appeared at the Dower House with Letty, to say good-bye before going to the front. He was going out in a draft to fill up various gaps and losses; he did not know where. Essex was doing well but bloodily over there. Mrs. Britling had tea set out upon the lawn under the blue cedar, and Mr. Britling found himself at a loss for appropriate sayings, and talked in his confusion almost as though Teddy's departure was of no significance at all. He was still haunted by that odd sense of responsibility for Teddy. Teddy was not nearly so animated as he had been in his pre-khaki days; there was a quiet exaltation in his manner rather than a lively excitement. He knew now what he was in for. He knew now that war was not a lark, that for him it was to be the gravest experience he had ever had or was likely to have. There were no more jokes about Letty's pension, and a general avoidance of the topics of high explosives and asphyxiating gas....

text: “I don’t want to go,” said Hugh, with his hands thrust deep in his pockets. “I want to go and work with Cardinal. But this is something everyone has to do. Haven’t you been saying that all day? ... It’s like going out to chase down a burglar or destroy a mad dog. It’s a kind of necessary sanitation....”
source_text: "I _don't_ want to go," said Hugh with his hands deep in his pockets. "I want to go and work with Cardinal. But this job has to be done by every one. Haven't you been saying as much all day?... It's like turning out to chase a burglar or suppress a mad dog. It's like necessary sanitation...."

text: She began to turn over the dense impressions left by a thousand recollections. She and her sister were close in years; they had grown up in one another’s confidence, known each other with an intimacy so complete that, and yet that night it came to Nora as if she knew Maris not at all. A year before she would have sworn she knew every corner of her. But the old, familiar Maris—with the clear complexion and the roguish eye, with her defiant schoolgirl conviction that “handsome young fellows” were the finest thing in the world, with her open, ardent devotion to Philip, with her delight in playful deceptions and country walks and all the brightness and laughter of life—this Maris, so satisfying, so entire, so final, had been pushed away as though she were only a discarded mask. Nora no longer knew her sister’s eyes. Maris’s hand had grown thin and strange, a little creased now; her face had sharpened, her mouth thinned; her movements, once so easy to forecast, had become baffling, and Nora found herself driven back upon guesses. In their school days Maris had been of an ardent, excitable nature; tears had come readily to her. But not once had she cried, nor shown the least sign of tears, since Philip’s name had first appeared in the list of casualties.... What was the force of this terrible strain? How far could it carry her? Was Maris truly capable of becoming a Charlotte Corday? Of carrying through some long-pondered act of vengeance, of working her way month by month and year by year, steadily nearer and nearer to revenge?
source_text: She began to weigh the concentrated impressions of a thousand memories. She and her sister were near in age; they knew each other with an extreme intimacy, and yet it seemed to Cissie that night as though she did not know Letty at all. A year ago she would have been certain she knew everything about her. But the old familiar Letty, with the bright complexion, and the wicked eye, with her rebellious schoolgirl insistence upon the beautifulness of "Boof'l young men," and her frank and glowing passion for Teddy, with her delight in humorous mystifications and open-air exercise and all the sunshine and laughter of life, this sister Letty, who had been so satisfactory and complete and final, had been thrust aside like a mask. Cissie no longer knew her sister's eyes. Letty's hand had become thin and unfamiliar and a little wrinkled; she was sharp-featured and thin-lipped; her acts, which had once been predictable, were incomprehensible, and Cissie was thrown back upon speculations. In their schooldays Letty had had a streak of intense sensibility; she had been easily moved to tears. But never once had she wept or given any sign of weeping since Teddy's name had appeared in the casualty list.... What was the strength of this tragic tension? How far would it carry her? Was Letty really capable of becoming a Charlotte Corday? Of carrying out a scheme of far-seeing vengeance, of making her way through long months and years nearer and nearer to revenge?

text: And before long, Mr. Alcott found himself wandering in the rose garden beside Miss Celia. She had the gentlest face for moonlight, and her pale, gleaming gown made her seem altogether a creature wrought of moonshine. If Mr. Alcott had not been in love with her before, he was now in love beyond recall. As for Mamie Kendricks, whose capricious cruelty had pricked him like a venomous thorn ever since they left Massachusetts, she suddenly slipped into the far dim category of Ancient History.
source_text: And presently Mr. Direck found himself strolling through the rose garden with Cecily. She had the sweetest moonlight face, her white shining robe made her a thing of moonlight altogether. If Mr. Direck had not been in love with her before he was now altogether in love. Mamie Nelson, whose freakish unkindness had been rankling like a poisoned thorn in his heart all the way from Massachusetts, suddenly became Ancient History.

text: After so much explanation, we may now return to the errand that had originally brought Mr. Ferris to Marden’s Close—the commission on which the Massachusetts society had dispatched him: the ordering of Mr. Halden’s mental unveiling. Mr. Ferris saw Mr. Halden only by daylight, and even then with an attention more and more diverted toward Miss Celia Lorne. We may discern him a little more clearly in the dark, with no distraction save his own.
source_text: After this much of explanation it is possible to go on to the task which originally brought Mr. Direck to Matching's Easy, the task that Massachusetts society had sent him upon, the task of organising the mental unveiling of Mr. Britling. Mr. Direck saw Mr. Britling only in the daylight, and with an increasing distraction of the attention towards Miss Cecily Corner. We may see him rather _more_ clearly in the darkness, without any distraction except his own.

text: And then she went on saying “poor dear,” saying it after a moment because nothing else had risen in her mind. She longed, above all things, to be his comfort, and before very long she was performing comfort so badly that she became aware of her own defeat. And that deepened her defeat, and that deepened the numbing consciousness of defeat....
source_text: And then she went on saying "poor dear," saying it presently because there was nothing more had come into her mind. She desired supremely to be his comfort, and in a little while she was acting comfort so poorly that she perceived her own failure. And that increased her failure, and that increased her paralysing sense of failure....

text: And then came the Sunday of the _Times_ telegram, which spoke of “a retreating and a broken army.” Mr. Britling did not see it, but Mr. Manning brought the report of it over in a state of deep alarm. Things, he said, seemed as bad as they could possibly be. The English were falling back toward the coast in great confusion. They were “in the air” and already cut off from the Trench. They had come within an inch of “a Sedan” beneath the fortifications of Maubeuge.... Mr. Britling was thunderstruck. He went into his study and stood staring helplessly at the maps. It was as though David had thrown his stone—and missed!
source_text: And then came the Sunday of _The Times_ telegram, which spoke of a "retreating and a broken army." Mr. Britling did not see this, but Mr. Manning brought over the report of it in a state of profound consternation. Things, he said, seemed to be about as bad as they could be. The English were retreating towards the coast and in much disorder. They were "in the air" and already separated from the Trench. They had narrowly escaped "a Sedan" under the fortifications of Maubeuge.... Mr. Britling was stunned. He went to his study and stared helplessly at maps. It was as if David had flung his pebble--and missed!

text: Everyone talked cheerfully, though Mr. Direck would have had difficulty later in remembering what they had been talking about, except that he somehow picked up the useful fact that Miss Corner was named Cecily and her sister Letty. Then—so far as the old Essex custom went—the men were left alone for a few minutes for some imaginary drinks and the lighting of cigars and cigarettes, after which everyone made their way through the mingled moonlight and afterglow to the barn. Mr. Britling sat down at a pianola in the corner and began to play the familiar strains of “Whistling Rufus.”
source_text: Everybody chattered gaily, though Mr. Direck would have found it difficult to recall afterwards what it was they chattered about, except that somehow he acquired the valuable knowledge that Miss Corner was called Cecily, and her sister Letty, and then--so far old Essex custom held--the masculine section was left for a few minutes for some imaginary drinking, and a lighting of cigars and cigarettes, after which everybody went through interwoven moonlight and afterglow to the barn. Mr. Britling sat down to a pianola in the corner and began the familiar cadences of "Whistling Rufus."

text: I don't object to that," said Mr. Britling. "Women here go into politics and into public-houses—I don't see why they shouldn't. If those things are fit for men, they're fit for women too; we don't have your kind of chivalry. But what is so disgraceful is the particular malignant absurdity of this kind of Toryism. It is disgraceful. There's no point pretending otherwise. The people you have heard and seen are by no means an unfair specimen of our governing class—of a certain part of it, at any rate—as it is today. Quite fair, in fact. And you can see how astonishingly they have lost hold of everything. There was a time when they could be politic.... Buried away, they still have political instincts even now.... But this Irish business makes me feel sick. Because, you know, it's true—we are drifting toward civil war there.
source_text: "I don't mind that," said Mr. Britling. "Women over here go into politics and into public-houses--I don't see why they shouldn't. If such things are good enough for men they are good enough for women; we haven't your sort of chivalry. But it's the peculiar malignant silliness of this sort of Toryism that's so discreditable. It's discreditable. There's no good in denying it. Those people you have heard and seen are a not unfair sample of our governing class--of a certain section of our governing class--as it is to-day. Not at all unfair. And you see how amazingly they haven't got hold of anything. There was a time when they could be politic.... Hidden away they have politic instincts even now.... But it makes me sick to think of this Irish business. Because, you know, it's true--we _are_ drifting towards civil war there."

text: "And their complexions," said Mr. Direck, over the pyramid of fruit, plainly meaning it for a compliment. So that was all right.... But the girl on his left was speaking across the table to the German tutor, and did not hear what he had said. So even if it was not exactly elegant, it did not matter....
source_text: "And their complexions," said Mr. Direck, over the pyramid of fruit, quite manifestly intending a compliment. So that was all right.... But the girl on the left of him was speaking across the table to the German tutor, and did not hear what he had said. So that even if it wasn't very neat it didn't matter....

text: Mr. Direck found little cause to alter his dictum in the experiences that followed that afternoon. Indeed, the afternoon and the day after went on steadily proving what an excellent dictum it had been. The landscape was the traditional English landscape, and everyone seemed livelier, more irresponsible, more disordered, than anyone could have expected, and wholly inexplicable by any recognised code of English relations....
source_text: Mr. Direck found little reason to revise his dictum in the subsequent experiences of the afternoon. Indeed the afternoon and the next day were steadily consistent in confirming what a very good dictum it had been. The scenery was the traditional scenery of England, and all the people seemed quicker, more irresponsible, more chaotic, than any one could have anticipated, and entirely inexplicable by any recognised code of English relationships....

text: Imagination beheld a little figure laboring bravely through the slush and snow of the Carpathians; saw it reeling beneath its first taste of shellfire; placed it amid assaults and retreats and exhaustion and hunger, and perhaps a frantic rush in the dark; and guessed at the blow that wounded it. Then came the piteous procession of the prisoners into captivity, captivity in a land laid waste, impoverished, and embittered. There were wounds swathed in filthy rags, pain and idleness, and a poor little Heinrich, bent and broken, sitting apart in a crowded compound and tending a humiliating hurt....
source_text: Imagination glimpsed a little figure toiling manfully through the slush and snow of the Carpathians; saw it staggering under its first experience of shell fire; set it amidst attacks and flights and fatigue and hunger and a rush perhaps in the darkness; guessed at the wounding blow. Then came the pitiful pilgrimage of the prisoners into captivity, captivity in a land desolated, impoverished and embittered. Came wounds wrapped in filthy rags, pain and want of occupation, and a poor little bent and broken Heinrich sitting aloof in a crowded compound nursing a mortifying wound....

text: “You believe you are finished with it for all time. So did we once believe. We were every bit as complacent as you when France collapsed in ’71.... Your case is merely another step in the same insularity. You imagine that your empty detachment is a kind of moral elevation. So did we, so did we....
source_text: "You think you are out of it for good and all. So did we think. We were as smug as you are when France went down in '71.... Yours is only one further degree of insularity. You think this vacuous aloofness of yours is some sort of moral superiority. So did we, so did we....

text: “No, it does not; I assure you it does not. Having imagined there was meaning where there was none, and a praiseworthy ambition where in fact there was no praiseworthy ambition, I am well quit of my error, and no damage has been done. Young women have often committed such follies before, and have often repented of them in poverty and obscurity. In one sense, I am sorry the matter has come to nothing, because it would have been a bad thing for me from a worldly point of view; in another sense, I am glad it has fallen through, because it would also have been a bad thing for me from a worldly point of view—it is scarcely necessary to say I could have gained nothing by it. No harm has been done at all. I have not proposed to the young lady, and, between ourselves, I am by no means sure, on reconsideration, that I should ever have committed myself to that extent. Mr. Lorry, you cannot govern the petty vanities and capriciousness of empty-headed girls; you must not expect to do so, or you will always be disappointed. Now, pray say no more about it. I tell you, I regret it for the sake of others, but I am content for my own. And I am truly very much obliged to you for permitting me to approach you on the subject, and for giving me your counsel; you know the young lady better than I do; you were right, it never would have done.”
source_text: “No it doesn’t; I assure you it doesn’t. Having supposed that there was sense where there is no sense, and a laudable ambition where there is not a laudable ambition, I am well out of my mistake, and no harm is done. Young women have committed similar follies often before, and have repented them in poverty and obscurity often before. In an unselfish aspect, I am sorry that the thing is dropped, because it would have been a bad thing for me in a worldly point of view; in a selfish aspect, I am glad that the thing has dropped, because it would have been a bad thing for me in a worldly point of view--it is hardly necessary to say I could have gained nothing by it. There is no harm at all done. I have not proposed to the young lady, and, between ourselves, I am by no means certain, on reflection, that I ever should have committed myself to that extent. Mr. Lorry, you cannot control the mincing vanities and giddinesses of empty-headed girls; you must not expect to do it, or you will always be disappointed. Now, pray say no more about it. I tell you, I regret it on account of others, but I am satisfied on my own account. And I am really very much obliged to you for allowing me to sound you, and for giving me your advice; you know the young lady better than I do; you were right, it never would have done.”

text: “My meaning,” replied the man of business, “is, of course, friendly and appreciative, and that it reflects the highest credit upon you, and—in short, my meaning is everything you could wish. But—really, you know, Mr. Stryver—” Mr. Lorry broke off and shook his head at him in the strangest way, as though he were forced, despite himself, to add inwardly, “you know, there really is altogether far too much of you!”
source_text: “My meaning,” answered the man of business, “is, of course, friendly and appreciative, and that it does you the greatest credit, and--in short, my meaning is everything you could desire. But--really, you know, Mr. Stryver--” Mr. Lorry paused, and shook his head at him in the oddest manner, as if he were compelled against his will to add, internally, “you know there really is so much too much of you!”

text: The lady thus alluded to, whose deep-seated assurance that she was the equal of any stranger could not be disturbed by hardship or peril, stood there with her arms crossed and remarked in English to the first figure on whom her glance fell, “Well, I’m sure, Mr. Impudence! I hope _you_ are very well!” She likewise emitted a distinctly British cough for Madame Defarge; but neither of the two paid her much attention.
source_text: The lady in question, whose rooted conviction that she was more than a match for any foreigner, was not to be shaken by distress and, danger, appeared with folded arms, and observed in English to The Vengeance, whom her eyes first encountered, “Well, I am sure, Boldface! I hope _you_ are pretty well!” She also bestowed a British cough on Madame Defarge; but, neither of the two took much heed of her.

text: “Well!” said his friend Stryver, “you take it more calmly than I had expected, and are less eager for my advantage than I imagined; though, to be sure, you know well enough by now that your old companion is a man of considerable determination. Yes, Sydney, I’ve had enough of this kind of life, with nothing else to vary it; I feel that it is a pleasant thing for a man to have a home to go to when he feels inclined to go there (and when he doesn’t, he can remain away), and I feel that Miss Manette will make a good figure in any rank, and will always reflect credit on me. So I have settled the matter. And now, Sydney, old fellow, I want to say a word to _you_ about _your_ prospects. You are in a bad state, you know; you really are in a bad state. You do not understand the worth of money, you live too roughly, you will break down before long, and be sick and poor; you really ought to think about a nurse.”
source_text: “Well!” said his friend Stryver, “you take it more easily than I fancied you would, and are less mercenary on my behalf than I thought you would be; though, to be sure, you know well enough by this time that your ancient chum is a man of a pretty strong will. Yes, Sydney, I have had enough of this style of life, with no other as a change from it; I feel that it is a pleasant thing for a man to have a home when he feels inclined to go to it (when he doesn’t, he can stay away), and I feel that Miss Manette will tell well in any station, and will always do me credit. So I have made up my mind. And now, Sydney, old boy, I want to say a word to _you_ about _your_ prospects. You are in a bad way, you know; you really are in a bad way. You don’t know the value of money, you live hard, you’ll knock up one of these days, and be ill and poor; you really ought to think about a nurse.”

text: “Take heart, dear miss, take heart! Business! The worst will be done with in a moment; it is only crossing the room door, and then the worst is over. After that, all the good you bring him, all the comfort, all the happiness you bring him, will begin. Let our good friend here help you on that side. Well done, friend Defarge. Come now. Business, business!”
source_text: “Courage, dear miss! Courage! Business! The worst will be over in a moment; it is but passing the room-door, and the worst is over. Then, all the good you bring to him, all the relief, all the happiness you bring to him, begin. Let our good friend here, assist you on that side. That’s well, friend Defarge. Come, now. Business, business!”

text: The powerful current, so rapid, so deep, and so sure, seemed in the morning hush like a kindly companion. He wandered beside the water, far from the dwellings, and in the sun’s light and warmth he fell asleep upon the bank. When he woke and rose again, he stayed there still a while longer, gazing at a whirlpool that spun and spun without purpose, until the stream took it into itself and bore it onward to the sea.—“Like me.”
source_text: The strong tide, so swift, so deep, and certain, was like a congenial friend, in the morning stillness. He walked by the stream, far from the houses, and in the light and warmth of the sun fell asleep on the bank. When he awoke and was afoot again, he lingered there yet a little longer, watching an eddy that turned and turned purposeless, until the stream absorbed it, and carried it on to the sea.--“Like me.”

text: The sun—the brilliant sun that restores to humankind not merely light, but life renewed, hope, and freshness—broke over the crowded city in clear, radiant splendor. Through tinted panes and patchwork windows, through cathedral dome and crumbling crack, it cast its impartial beam. It illuminated the room where the murdered woman lay. It did. He had tried to keep it out, but it would pour in. If the scene had been hideous enough in the dimness of morning, what was it now, beneath all that dazzling light?
source_text: The sun—the bright sun, that brings back, not light alone, but new life, and hope, and freshness to man—burst upon the crowded city in clear and radiant glory. Through costly-coloured glass and paper-mended window, through cathedral dome and rotten crevice, it shed its equal ray. It lighted up the room where the murdered woman lay. It did. He tried to shut it out, but it would stream in. If the sight had been a ghastly one in the dull morning, what was it, now, in all that brilliant light!

text: “No,” replied the Dodger, “not here, for this ain’t the place for justice; besides, my attorney is breakfasting this morning with the Vice President of the House of Commons; but I shall have something to say elsewhere, and so will he, and so will a very numerous and respectable circle of acquaintances who’ll make those beaks wish they’d never been born, or that they’d had their footmen hang them on their own hat-pegs before they let them come out this morning to try it on me. I’ll—”
source_text: “No,” replied the Dodger, “not here, for this ain’t the shop for justice: besides which, my attorney is a-breakfasting this morning with the Wice President of the House of Commons; but I shall have something to say elsewhere, and so will he, and so will a wery numerous and ’spectable circle of acquaintance as’ll make them beaks wish they’d never been born, or that they’d got their footmen to hang ’em up to their own hat-pegs, afore they let ’em come out this morning to try it on upon me. I’ll—”

text: As Mr. Gamfield did happen to labour under the slight imputation of having bruised three or four boys to death already, it occurred to him that the board had perhaps, by some unaccountable freak, decided that this extraneous circumstance ought to affect their proceedings. It was very unlike their usual manner of conducting business, if they had; but still, as he had no particular desire to revive the rumour, he twisted his cap in his hands and walked slowly away from the table.
source_text: As Mr. Gamfield did happen to labour under the slight imputation of having bruised three or four boys to death already, it occurred to him that the board had, perhaps, in some unaccountable freak, taken it into their heads that this extraneous circumstance ought to influence their proceedings. It was very unlike their general mode of doing business, if they had; but still, as he had no particular wish to revive the rumour, he twisted his cap in his hands, and walked slowly from the table.

text: “The little ones, my dear,” said Raskin, “are the young children their mothers send out on errands, carrying sixpences and shillings; and the trick is simply to take the money from them—they’re always clutching it in their hands—then fling them into the gutter, and stroll away at an easy pace, as though nothing had happened but a child that had tripped and hurt itself. Ha! ha! ha!”
source_text: “The kinchins, my dear,” said Fagin, “is the young children that’s sent on errands by their mothers, with sixpences and shillings; and the lay is just to take their money away—they’ve always got it ready in their hands,—then knock ’em into the kennel, and walk off very slow, as if there were nothing else the matter but a child fallen down and hurt itself. Ha! ha! ha!”

text: This agreeable idea was now constantly in his thoughts. Six years was a long span, yet how much shorter it seemed than never, than the prospect he had so long been forced to endure! Jacob had served twice seven years for Rachel; what were six for a woman like this? He strove to take more pleasure in the thought of waiting for her than in that of winning her at once. Boldwood believed his love to be so deep, so strong, so eternal, that perhaps she had never yet known its full measure, and this patience in delay would give him a welcome chance to prove it sweetly. He would efface the six years of his life as though they were mere minutes—so little did he prize his time on earth in comparison with her love. Through all those six years of airy, immaterial courtship, he would show her how little heed he paid to anything except as it led toward the final attainment.
source_text: This pleasant notion was now continually in his mind. Six years were a long time, but how much shorter than never, the idea he had for so long been obliged to endure! Jacob had served twice seven years for Rachel: what were six for such a woman as this? He tried to like the notion of waiting for her better than that of winning her at once. Boldwood felt his love to be so deep and strong and eternal, that it was possible she had never yet known its full volume, and this patience in delay would afford him an opportunity of giving sweet proof on the point. He would annihilate the six years of his life as if they were minutes—so little did he value his time on earth beside her love. He would let her see, all those six years of intangible ethereal courtship, how little care he had for anything but as it bore upon the consummation.

text: Bathsheba’s feelings were always, to some degree, governed by her caprice, as is true of many other women. Something large rose in her throat and surged up to her eyes, and she thought she would let the tears that were gathering fall, if they chose. They did fall, abundantly, and one dropped upon the stone bench beside her. Once she had begun to cry, though she scarcely knew why, she could not stop because of the crowding thoughts she knew only too well. She would have given anything in the world to be, like those children, heedless of the meaning of their words, too innocent to feel any need for such expression. All the ardent scenes of her brief life seemed to stir again with greater feeling at that instant, and those scenes that had been without feeling when they occurred were emotional now. Yet sorrow came to her more like a luxury than like the old scourge of earlier days.
source_text: Bathsheba’s feeling was always to some extent dependent upon her whim, as is the case with many other women. Something big came into her throat and an uprising to her eyes—and she thought that she would allow the imminent tears to flow if they wished. They did flow and plenteously, and one fell upon the stone bench beside her. Once that she had begun to cry for she hardly knew what, she could not leave off for crowding thoughts she knew too well. She would have given anything in the world to be, as those children were, unconcerned at the meaning of their words, because too innocent to feel the necessity for any such expression.
