All the impassioned scenes of her brief experience seemed to revive with added emotion at that moment, and those scenes which had been without emotion during enactment had emotion then. Yet grief came to her rather as a luxury than as the scourge of former times.

text: Troy spread them upon the grass and, with an unreadable face, fell to work setting them in the ground. The snowdrops he placed in a line along the outer side of the coping, while the rest he arranged within the bounds of the grave. The crocuses and hyacinths were to rise in ordered rows; some of the summer blossoms he laid above her head and feet, the lilies and forget-me-nots above her heart. The rest he scattered through the open spaces between.
source_text: Troy laid these out upon the grass, and with an impassive face set to work to plant them. The snowdrops were arranged in a line on the outside of the coping, the remainder within the enclosure of the grave. The crocuses and hyacinths were to grow in rows; some of the summer flowers he placed over her head and feet, the lilies and forget-me-nots over her heart. The remainder were dispersed in the spaces between these.

text: Compelled in some measure to abandon dreaming if he was to find his way, he came at last to the churchyard and went around it beneath the wall, where several ancient trees were growing. A broad strip of grass ran along this side, and Gabriel’s steps were muffled by its softness, even at this hardening season of the year. When he reached a trunk that seemed the oldest of the old, he became aware of a figure standing behind it. Gabriel did not stop walking, and in the next instant he accidentally struck a loose stone with his foot. The sound was enough to rouse the motionless stranger, who gave a start and took up an indifferent pose.
source_text: Obliged, to some extent, to forgo dreaming in order to find the way, he reached the churchyard, and passed round it under the wall where several ancient trees grew. There was a wide margin of grass along here, and Gabriel’s footsteps were deadened by its softness, even at this indurating period of the year. When abreast of a trunk which appeared to be the oldest of the old, he became aware that a figure was standing behind it. Gabriel did not pause in his walk, and in another moment he accidentally kicked a loose stone. The noise was enough to disturb the motionless stranger, who started and assumed a careless position.

text: She moved a few yards away, and Clym followed her. When she came to the corner of the enclosure, where steps had been made for climbing the boundary bank, she leaped up with a lightness that seemed odd after her sluggish approach to the well. It also happened to reveal that the seeming weariness was not caused by any want of strength.
source_text: She moved away a few yards, and Clym followed. When she reached the corner of the enclosure, where the steps were formed for mounting the boundary bank, she sprang up with a lightness which seemed strange after her listless movement towards the well. It incidentally showed that her apparent languor did not arise from lack of force.

text: “About six months. By then I’ll have finished my reading—yes, we can do this, and this heartache will be over. Of course, we shall live in complete seclusion, and our married life will not begin to show itself to the world until we take the house in Budmouth, about which I have already written a letter. Would your grandfather permit you?”
source_text: “About six months. At the end of that time I shall have finished my reading—yes, we will do it, and this heart-aching will be over. We shall, of course, live in absolute seclusion, and our married life will only begin to outward view when we take the house in Budmouth, where I have already addressed a letter on the matter. Would your grandfather allow you?”

text: “Oh, I thought you did. If you ever feel moved to blame me, remember a certain evening by Rainbarrow, when you promised to meet me and did not come. You sent me a letter, and my heart was pained to read it in a way I hope yours never will be. That was one point at which our paths divided. I then did something in haste.... But she is a good woman, and I will say no more.”
source_text: “Oh, I thought you were. If ever you _should_ be inclined to blame me, think of a certain evening by Rainbarrow, when you promised to meet me and did not. You sent me a letter; and my heart ached to read that as I hope yours never will. That was one point of divergence. I then did something in haste.... But she is a good woman, and I will say no more.”

text: “I’ve something to tell ’ee, Mother,” he cried in a high, thin voice. “That woman asleep there walked with me to-day; and she said I was to say that I had seen her, and that she was a broken-hearted woman, cast off by her son, and then I came on home.”
source_text: “I’ve got something to tell ’ee, Mother,” he cried in a shrill tone. “That woman asleep there walked along with me today; and she said I was to say that I had seed her, and she was a broken-hearted woman and cast off by her son, and then I came on home.”

text: Alden’s face, flushed at first, went white as he read on. It was flawless art. Form had conquered substance, if conquest it could be called where the last imaginable particle of substance had found utterance in so exquisite a structure as to leave Alden’s head whirling with delight, to bring tears of passion to his eyes, and to send a shiver creeping over his spine. It was a long poem of six or seven hundred lines, and it was a strange, astonishing, otherworldly thing. It was dreadful, impossible; and yet there it lay, scratched in black ink across the pages of paper. It treated of man and the gropings of his soul in their final terms, sounding the depths of space for the witness of the farthest suns and the colors of rainbow spectra. It was a wild revel of imagination, carousing in the mind of a dying man who murmured half under his breath and was alive with the feverish flutter of failing heartbeats. The poem moved in grand cadence to the cold uproar of interstellar strife, to the advance of starry legions, to the collision of icy suns and the blazing into life of nebulae in the darkened waste; and through it all, continual and faint, like a silver thread, ran the fragile, piping voice of man, a petulant note amid the shrieking of planets and the shattering of worlds.
source_text: Martin’s face, flushed at first, paled as he read on. It was perfect art. Form triumphed over substance, if triumph it could be called where the last conceivable atom of substance had found expression in so perfect construction as to make Martin’s head swim with delight, to put passionate tears into his eyes, and to send chills creeping up and down his back. It was a long poem of six or seven hundred lines, and it was a fantastic, amazing, unearthly thing. It was terrific, impossible; and yet there it was, scrawled in black ink across the sheets of paper. It dealt with man and his soul-gropings in their ultimate terms, plumbing the abysses of space for the testimony of remotest suns and rainbow spectrums. It was a mad orgy of imagination, wassailing in the skull of a dying man who half sobbed under his breath and was quick with the wild flutter of fading heart-beats. The poem swung in majestic rhythm to the cool tumult of interstellar conflict, to the onset of starry hosts, to the impact of cold suns and the flaming up of nebulae in the darkened void; and through it all, unceasing and faint, like a silver shuttle, ran the frail, piping voice of man, a querulous chirp amid the screaming of planets and the crash of systems.

text: She had come back to his side, and she saw him bend his arm at the elbow, the biceps sliding beneath his shirt sleeve and swelling into a hard, heavy knot of muscle. The sight repelled her. In sentiment, she disliked it. Yet her pulse, her blood, every fiber in her, loved it and longed for it, and in that old, unaccountable way she leaned toward him instead of drawing back. And in the instant that followed, when he crushed her against him, the mind in her, preoccupied with life’s outward show, rebelled; while the heart in her, the woman in her, concerned with life itself, rose in triumph. It was in such moments that she felt to the fullest the vastness of her love for Valen, for it was almost a fainting ecstasy to feel his strong arms around her, holding her fast, hurting her with the pressure of their ardor. At such times she found a defense for her betrayal of her principles, for her trespass against her own lofty ideals, and, above all, for her silent defiance of her mother and father. They did not want her to marry this man. It shocked them that she should love him. It shocked her too, sometimes, when she was away from him, a cool and reasoning being. With him, she loved him—in truth, at times a troubled and anxious love; but love it was, a love stronger than she.
source_text: She had come back to his side, and she saw him double his arm at the elbow, the biceps crawling under his shirt-sleeve and swelling into a knot of muscle, heavy and hard. The sight repelled her. Sentimentally, she disliked it. But her pulse, her blood, every fibre of her, loved it and yearned for it, and, in the old, inexplicable way, she leaned toward him, not away from him. And in the moment that followed, when he crushed her in his arms, the brain of her, concerned with the superficial aspects of life, was in revolt; while the heart of her, the woman of her, concerned with life itself, exulted triumphantly. It was in moments like this that she felt to the uttermost the greatness of her love for Martin, for it was almost a swoon of delight to her to feel his strong arms about her, holding her tightly, hurting her with the grip of their fervor. At such moments she found justification for her treason to her standards, for her violation of her own high ideals, and, most of all, for her tacit disobedience to her mother and father. They did not want her to marry this man. It shocked them that she should love him. It shocked her, too, sometimes, when she was apart from him, a cool and reasoning creature. With him, she loved him—in truth, at times a vexed and worried love; but love it was, a love that was stronger than she.

text: Joe sat on the bed while Martin unpacked. The box had once been a case for breakfast food, and Mr. Higginbotham had charged him fifty cents for it. Two rope handles, which Martin had nailed on himself, had in a technical sense made it a trunk fit for the baggage car. Joe stared with wide, bulging eyes as a few shirts and several changes of underwear came out of the box, followed by books, and still more books.
source_text: Joe sat on the bed while Martin unpacked. The box was a packing-case for breakfast food, and Mr. Higginbotham had charged him half a dollar for it. Two rope handles, nailed on by Martin, had technically transformed it into a trunk eligible for the baggage-car. Joe watched, with bulging eyes, a few shirts and several changes of underclothes come out of the box, followed by books, and more books.

text: “He started with very few advantages, relatively speaking. His father had been a bank cashier, but he lingered on for years, dying of consumption in Arizona, so that when he was gone Mr. Butler—Charles Butler, as he was called—found himself alone in the world. His father had come from Australia, you know, so he had no relatives in California. He went to work in a printing office—I have heard him describe it many times—and at first he earned three dollars a week. Today his income is at least thirty thousand a year. How did he manage it? He was honest, faithful, industrious, and thrifty. He denied himself the pleasures that most boys allow themselves. He made it his rule to set aside a certain amount every week, whatever he had to go without in order to do it. Of course, he soon earned more than three dollars a week, and as his pay went up he saved more and more.
source_text: “He had comparatively no advantages at first. His father had been a bank cashier, but he lingered for years, dying of consumption in Arizona, so that when he was dead, Mr. Butler, Charles Butler he was called, found himself alone in the world. His father had come from Australia, you know, and so he had no relatives in California. He went to work in a printing-office,—I have heard him tell of it many times,—and he got three dollars a week, at first. His income to-day is at least thirty thousand a year. How did he do it? He was honest, and faithful, and industrious, and economical. He denied himself the enjoyments that most boys indulge in. He made it a point to save so much every week, no matter what he had to do without in order to save it. Of course, he was soon earning more than three dollars a week, and as his wages increased he saved more and more.

text: From that evening at Ruth’s, Martin carried away a strange tangle of emotions and contradictions. He was let down by the aim he had set himself and by the people he had climbed in order to be among. Yet at the same time, his success left him heartened. The ascent had been easier than he had expected. He was above the climb, and—he did not, out of false humility, disguise this from himself—he was above the beings among whom he had climbed, with the obvious exception of Professor Caldwell. On life and books he knew more than they did, and he could not help wondering into what corners and crannies they had tucked away their educations. He did not know that he himself had an exceptional vigor of mind; nor did he know that those who were prone to probing depths and thinking ultimate thoughts were not to be found in the drawing rooms of the world’s Morses; nor did he suspect that such people were like lonely eagles, soaring alone in the blue sky far above the earth and its teeming burden of sociable life.
source_text: From the evening at Ruth’s Martin brought away with him strange confusions and conflicting feelings. He was disappointed in his goal, in the persons he had climbed to be with. On the other hand, he was encouraged with his success. The climb had been easier than he expected. He was superior to the climb, and (he did not, with false modesty, hide it from himself) he was superior to the beings among whom he had climbed—with the exception, of course, of Professor Caldwell. About life and the books he knew more than they, and he wondered into what nooks and crannies they had cast aside their educations. He did not know that he was himself possessed of unusual brain vigor; nor did he know that the persons who were given to probing the depths and to thinking ultimate thoughts were not to be found in the drawing rooms of the world’s Morses; nor did he dream that such persons were as lonely eagles sailing solitary in the azure sky far above the earth and its swarming freight of gregarious life.

text: It was Sunday evening, and they discovered the little assembly room crammed with Oakland socialists, most of them from the laboring class. The orator, a shrewd Jew, awakened in Martin admiration and hostility at once. His bent, meager shoulders and pinched chest marked him unmistakably as a true offspring of the crowded ghetto, and upon Martin there fell with peculiar force the ancient contest of the frail, miserable slaves against the haughty few who had dominated them and would continue to dominate them to the end of time. To Martin this shriveled thread of a man was a symbol. He stood there as the representative of that whole lamentable throng of weaklings and incompetents who, by the law of biology, went under on the broken edge of existence. They were the unfit. Despite their crafty doctrines and their antlike habit of working together, Nature cast them off in favor of the exceptional man. From the abundant brood of life she scattered with her fertile hand, she chose only the best. Men, copying her, did the same when they bred race horses and cucumbers. Perhaps a maker of a cosmos might have contrived a wiser plan; yet the beings of this particular cosmos had to endure this particular plan. Certainly they might writhe as they died, as the socialists writhed, as the man on the platform and the sweating audience were writhing even then while they took counsel over some new contrivance by which to lessen the burdens of living and cheat the cosmos.
source_text: It was Sunday night, and they found the small hall packed by the Oakland socialists, chiefly members of the working class. The speaker, a clever Jew, won Martin’s admiration at the same time that he aroused his antagonism. The man’s stooped and narrow shoulders and weazened chest proclaimed him the true child of the crowded ghetto, and strong on Martin was the age-long struggle of the feeble, wretched slaves against the lordly handful of men who had ruled over them and would rule over them to the end of time. To Martin this withered wisp of a creature was a symbol. He was the figure that stood forth representative of the whole miserable mass of weaklings and inefficients who perished according to biological law on the ragged confines of life. They were the unfit. In spite of their cunning philosophy and of their antlike proclivities for coöperation, Nature rejected them for the exceptional man. Out of the plentiful spawn of life she flung from her prolific hand she selected only the best. It was by the same method that men, aping her, bred race-horses and cucumbers. Doubtless, a creator of a Cosmos could have devised a better method; but creatures of this particular Cosmos must put up with this particular method. Of course, they could squirm as they perished, as the socialists squirmed, as the speaker on the platform and the perspiring crowd were squirming even now as they counselled together for some new device with which to minimize the penalties of living and outwit the Cosmos.

text: “That fits neatly with the persistence of the established order,” he replied, his anger toward the editors getting the better of him. “What is, is not only right, but the best that could possibly be. The mere fact that anything exists is proof enough that it is fit to exist—to exist, mind you, as the average person unconsciously believes, not merely under present circumstances, but under all circumstances. Of course it is their ignorance that leads them to believe such nonsense—their ignorance, which is nothing more nor less than the henidical mental process described by Weininger. They imagine they are thinking, and it is such thoughtless creatures who decide the lives of the few who truly do think.”
source_text: “That’s in line with the persistence of the established,” he rejoined, his heat against the editor-folk getting the better of him. “What is, is not only right, but is the best possible. The existence of anything is sufficient vindication of its fitness to exist—to exist, mark you, as the average person unconsciously believes, not merely in present conditions, but in all conditions. It is their ignorance, of course, that makes them believe such rot—their ignorance, which is nothing more nor less than the henidical mental process described by Weininger. They think they think, and such thinkless creatures are the arbiters of the lives of the few who really think.”

text: But the deepest alteration of all, and the one that gave her the keenest pleasure, was the change in his speech. He not only spoke more correctly, but with greater ease, and his vocabulary had grown rich with many unfamiliar words. Yet when excitement or ardor took hold of him, he slipped back into the old drawl and the soft swallowing of final sounds. There was, too, at times an uneasy hesitation as he tried out the new words he had acquired. On the other hand, with this new fluency there came a quickness and playful wit in his thinking that delighted her. It was the same old turn for humor and banter that had once made him a favorite among his own companions, though until now he had been unable to bring it forth in her presence for want of words and training. He was only just beginning to find his bearings and to feel that he was not altogether a stranger. But he proceeded with great caution, almost with punctilious care, letting Clara determine the tempo of gayety and invention, matching her pace, yet never venturing to pass beyond it.
source_text: But the most radical change of all, and the one that pleased her most, was the change in his speech. Not only did he speak more correctly, but he spoke more easily, and there were many new words in his vocabulary. When he grew excited or enthusiastic, however, he dropped back into the old slurring and the dropping of final consonants. Also, there was an awkward hesitancy, at times, as he essayed the new words he had learned. On the other hand, along with his ease of expression, he displayed a lightness and facetiousness of thought that delighted her. It was his old spirit of humor and badinage that had made him a favorite in his own class, but which he had hitherto been unable to use in her presence through lack of words and training. He was just beginning to orientate himself and to feel that he was not wholly an intruder. But he was very tentative, fastidiously so, letting Ruth set the pace of sprightliness and fancy, keeping up with her but never daring to go beyond her.

text: In the days that followed, she was no longer quite herself, but a strange, perplexing being, stubborn in her judgments and contemptuous of self-examination, refusing to look ahead or to consider herself and where she was drifting. She lived in a fever of tingling mystery, now frightened, now delighted, and always bewildered. Yet one thought remained firmly fixed in her mind, and that was her safeguard. She would not allow Martin to declare his love. So long as she prevented that, everything would be all right. In a few days he would be gone to sea. And even if he did speak, everything would still be all right. It could not be otherwise, because she did not love him. Of course, it would be a painful half hour for him and an awkward half hour for her, since it would be her first proposal. She thrilled sweetly at the idea. She was truly a woman, with a man ready to ask her to marry him. It appealed to everything elemental in her sex. The structure of her life, of everything that made her what she was, trembled and quivered. The thought flickered through her mind like a moth drawn to flame. She even imagined Martin proposing, with her own words placed in his mouth, and she practiced her refusal, softening it with kindness and urging him toward true and noble manhood. And above all, he must give up smoking cigarettes. She would make a special point of that. But no, she must not let him speak at all. She could stop him, and she had told her mother she would. Flushed and aflame, she reluctantly let the imagined scene fade. Her first proposal would have to wait for a more favorable time and a more suitable man.
source_text: In the days that swiftly followed she was no longer herself but a strange, puzzling creature, wilful over judgment and scornful of self-analysis, refusing to peer into the future or to think about herself and whither she was drifting. She was in a fever of tingling mystery, alternately frightened and charmed, and in constant bewilderment. She had one idea firmly fixed, however, which insured her security. She would not let Martin speak his love. As long as she did this, all would be well. In a few days he would be off to sea. And even if he did speak, all would be well. It could not be otherwise, for she did not love him. Of course, it would be a painful half hour for him, and an embarrassing half hour for her, because it would be her first proposal. She thrilled deliciously at the thought. She was really a woman, with a man ripe to ask for her in marriage. It was a lure to all that was fundamental in her sex. The fabric of her life, of all that constituted her, quivered and grew tremulous. The thought fluttered in her mind like a flame-attracted moth. She went so far as to imagine Martin proposing, herself putting the words into his mouth; and she rehearsed her refusal, tempering it with kindness and exhorting him to true and noble manhood. And especially he must stop smoking cigarettes. She would make a point of that. But no, she must not let him speak at all. She could stop him, and she had told her mother that she would. All flushed and burning, she regretfully dismissed the conjured situation. Her first proposal would have to be deferred to a more propitious time and a more eligible suitor.

text: “I am glad of that. I should not like to see my daughter, my only daughter, who is so pure and untainted, give her heart to a man like him. There are noble men in the world, men who are clean, true, and manly. Wait for one of them. Some day you will find such a man, and you will love him and be loved in return, and you will be happy with him as your father and I have been happy together. And there is one thing you must always keep in mind—”
source_text: “I am glad of that. I should not care to see my daughter, my one daughter, who is so clean and pure, love a man like him. There are noble men in the world who are clean and true and manly. Wait for them. You will find one some day, and you will love him and be loved by him, and you will be happy with him as your father and I have been happy with each other. And there is one thing you must always carry in mind—”

text: “All right, Richard, my boy,” old Anthony said cheerfully. “You can be off to your club now. I’m glad it isn’t your liver. But don’t forget to light a few punk sticks now and then in the joss house to the great god Mazuma. You say money won’t buy time? Why, of course you can’t have eternity wrapped up and sent to your door for a price, but I’ve seen Father Time get some mighty sore stone bruises on his heels when he’s walked through the gold diggings.”
source_text: “All right, Richard, my boy,” said old Anthony, cheerfully. “You may run along down to your club now. I’m glad it ain’t your liver. But don’t forget to burn a few punk sticks in the joss house to the great god Mazuma from time to time. You say money won’t buy time? Well, of course, you can’t order eternity wrapped up and delivered at your residence for a price, but I’ve seen Father Time get pretty bad stone bruises on his heels when he walked through the gold diggings.”

text: “Ye should understand,” he says, “I’m bringing to fulfillment the salvation foretold by the lines in my palm. I’m searching for the crooked-nosed man who is meant to bring us good fortune. That is all that can save us. Jawn, did you ever in all your days see a pack of hellions with noses any straighter?”
source_text: “Ye should know,” says he, “I’m working out the salvation promised by the lines in me palm. I’m looking for the crooked-nose man that’s to bring the good luck. ’Tis all that will save us. Jawn, did ye ever see a straighter-nosed gang of hellions in the days of your life?”

text: My fondness for my guest grows stronger with each passing day. He inspires both my admiration and my pity to a remarkable degree at the same time. How can I behold so noble a being ruined by misery without experiencing the most acute sorrow? He is so gentle, and yet so wise; his mind is so well cultivated, and when he speaks, though his words seem chosen with the finest care, they still pour forth with such speed and with eloquence unmatched by any other.
source_text: My affection for my guest increases every day. He excites at once my admiration and my pity to an astonishing degree. How can I see so noble a creature destroyed by misery without feeling the most poignant grief? He is so gentle, yet so wise; his mind is so cultivated, and when he speaks, although his words are culled with the choicest art, yet they flow with rapidity and unparalleled eloquence.

text: I sank into the chaise that was to carry me away and gave myself up to the most sorrowful thoughts. I, who had always been surrounded by kind companions, forever occupied in trying to give and receive pleasure—I was now alone. At the university to which I was bound, I would have to make my own friends and serve as my own guardian. My life had so far been unusually quiet and domestic, and this had left me with an unconquerable dislike of unfamiliar faces. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself utterly ill suited to the company of strangers. Such were my thoughts as I began my journey; yet as I went on, my spirits and hopes gradually lifted. I was passionately eager to gain knowledge. At home, I had often felt it was cruel to spend my youth shut up in one place, and I had longed to go out into the world and take my place among other human beings. Now my wishes were being fulfilled, and it would truly have been foolish to regret it.
source_text: I threw myself into the chaise that was to convey me away and indulged in the most melancholy reflections. I, who had ever been surrounded by amiable companions, continually engaged in endeavouring to bestow mutual pleasure—I was now alone. In the university whither I was going I must form my own friends and be my own protector. My life had hitherto been remarkably secluded and domestic, and this had given me invincible repugnance to new countenances. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself totally unfitted for the company of strangers. Such were my reflections as I commenced my journey; but as I proceeded, my spirits and hopes rose. I ardently desired the acquisition of knowledge. I had often, when at home, thought it hard to remain during my youth cooped up in one place and had longed to enter the world and take my station among other human beings. Now my desires were complied with, and it would, indeed, have been folly to repent.

text: There was a wide gap in age between my parents, yet this difference seemed only to draw them closer together in the ties of devoted affection. My father’s upright mind was governed by a strong sense of justice, which made it necessary for him to admire deeply if he was to love deeply. Perhaps, in earlier years, he had suffered from discovering too late the worthlessness of someone he had cherished, and therefore came to place a higher value on proved merit. His devotion to my mother had something of gratitude and reverence in it, utterly unlike the foolish fondness of old age; it sprang from respect for her virtues and from a wish, at least in some measure, to repay her for the sorrows she had borne. This gave an indescribable grace to his conduct toward her. Everything was made to give way to her desires and comfort. He sought to protect her, like a delicate exotic plant sheltered by a gardener, from every harsher gust, and to surround her with whatever might awaken pleasant feelings in her gentle and kindly mind. Her health, and even the calmness of a spirit that had until then remained steady, had been shaken by what she had endured. During the two years before their marriage my father had gradually given up all his public duties; and as soon as they were united they went to the mild climate of Italy, where the change of place and the attractions of travel through that land of marvels were meant to restore her weakened body.
source_text: There was a considerable difference between the ages of my parents, but this circumstance seemed to unite them only closer in bonds of devoted affection. There was a sense of justice in my father’s upright mind which rendered it necessary that he should approve highly to love strongly. Perhaps during former years he had suffered from the late-discovered unworthiness of one beloved and so was disposed to set a greater value on tried worth. There was a show of gratitude and worship in his attachment to my mother, differing wholly from the doting fondness of age, for it was inspired by reverence for her virtues and a desire to be the means of, in some degree, recompensing her for the sorrows she had endured, but which gave inexpressible grace to his behaviour to her. Everything was made to yield to her wishes and her convenience. He strove to shelter her, as a fair exotic is sheltered by the gardener, from every rougher wind and to surround her with all that could tend to excite pleasurable emotion in her soft and benevolent mind. Her health, and even the tranquillity of her hitherto constant spirit, had been shaken by what she had gone through. During the two years that had elapsed previous to their marriage my father had gradually relinquished all his public functions; and immediately after their union they sought the pleasant climate of Italy, and the change of scene and interest attendant on a tour through that land of wonders, as a restorative for her weakened frame.

text: With the storm there came a change in the weather; and the harder the wind drove, and the more furiously the rain beat down, the colder it grew. Before long, all sorts of bugs and ants and worms and the like began to crowd in from the wet and crawl down inside my armor to keep warm; and while some of them behaved tolerably enough, and nestled among my clothes and settled down, the greater part were a restless, uneasy lot, and never kept still, but kept wandering and searching for they knew not what; especially the ants, which went creeping in tiresome files from one end of me to the other for hours on end, and are a sort of creature I never wish to sleep with again. My advice to any person in such a situation would be not to roll about or kick up a fuss, because that only arouses the curiosity of all the various animals and makes every one of them want to come out and see what is happening, which makes matters worse than they were before, and naturally makes you curse the harder, too, if you are able. Yet if a man does not roll about and struggle, he will die; so perhaps it is as well to do either one way or the other, for there is no real choice. Even after I was frozen stiff I could still feel that tickling, just as a corpse does when undergoing electrical treatment. I said I would never wear armor after that trip.
source_text: With the storm came a change of weather; and the stronger the wind blew, and the wilder the rain lashed around, the colder and colder it got. Pretty soon, various kinds of bugs and ants and worms and things began to flock in out of the wet and crawl down inside my armor to get warm; and while some of them behaved well enough, and snuggled up amongst my clothes and got quiet, the majority were of a restless, uncomfortable sort, and never stayed still, but went on prowling and hunting for they did not know what; especially the ants, which went tickling along in wearisome procession from one end of me to the other by the hour, and are a kind of creatures which I never wish to sleep with again. It would be my advice to persons situated in this way, to not roll or thrash around, because this excites the interest of all the different sorts of animals and makes every last one of them want to turn out and see what is going on, and this makes things worse than they were before, and of course makes you objurgate harder, too, if you can. Still, if one did not roll and thrash around he would die; so perhaps it is as well to do one way as the other; there is no real choice. Even after I was frozen solid I could still distinguish that tickling, just as a corpse does when he is taking electric treatment. I said I would never wear armor after this trip.

text: For a moment there was no answer in that deep darkness and that graveyard stillness. But when the sun’s silver edge thrust itself out a moment or two later, the gathering burst into a mighty shout and came surging down like a flood to overwhelm me with blessings and gratitude; and Clarence, to be sure, was not the least of the stream.
source_text: There was no response, for a moment, in that deep darkness and that graveyard hush. But when the silver rim of the sun pushed itself out, a moment or two later, the assemblage broke loose with a vast shout and came pouring down like a deluge to smother me with blessings and gratitude; and Clarence was not the last of the wash, to be sure.

text: At last I ventured a story of my own, and its success was immense. Not immediately, of course, for the native of those islands does not, as a rule, melt under the first touch of a humorous remark; but by the fifth telling they began to show cracks here and there; by the eighth, they were starting to crumble; by the twelfth repetition they were breaking off in pieces; and by the fifteenth they had fallen to fragments, whereupon I fetched a broom and swept them up. This is figurative language. Those islanders—well, at first they are slow to pay back your investment of effort, but in the end they make the returns of all other nations seem poor and small by comparison.
source_text: At last I ventured a story myself; and vast was the success of it. Not right off, of course, for the native of those islands does not, as a rule, dissolve upon the early applications of a humorous thing; but the fifth time I told it, they began to crack in places; the eight time I told it, they began to crumble; at the twelfth repetition they fell apart in chunks; and at the fifteenth they disintegrated, and I got a broom and swept them up. This language is figurative. Those islanders--well, they are slow pay at first, in the matter of return for your investment of effort, but in the end they make the pay of all other nations poor and small by contrast.

text: “Oh, Sandy, you have come at last—how I have yearned for you! Sit here beside me—do not go away—never leave me again, Sandy, never again. Where is your hand? Give it to me, dear; let me keep it—there—now all is right, all is calm, and I am happy once more—we are happy once more, aren’t we, Sandy? You are so faint, so indistinct, you are only a mist, a cloud, but you are here, and that is blessing enough; and I have your hand; do not take it away—it is only for a little while, I shall not need it for long.... Was that the child?... Hello-Central!... she does not answer. Asleep, perhaps? Bring her when she wakes, and let me touch her hands, her face, her hair, and say good-bye to her.... Sandy! Yes, you are there. I lost myself for a moment, and I thought you had gone.... Have I been ill long? It must be so; it feels like months to me. And such dreams! such strange, terrible dreams, Sandy! Dreams that were as real as waking life—delirium, of course, but so real! Why, I thought the king was dead, I thought you were in Gaul and could not come home, I thought there was a revolution; in the wild madness of those dreams, I thought that Clarence and I and a few of my cadets fought and wiped out all the chivalry of England! But even that was not the strangest part. I seemed to be some creature from a far-off unborn age, centuries ahead, and even that felt as real as everything else! Yes, I seemed to have leaped back out of that age into this one of ours, and then forward to it again, and was left there, a stranger and abandoned in that strange England, with an abyss of thirteen centuries opening between me and you! between me and my home and my friends! between me and all that is precious to me, all that could make life worth living! It was dreadful—more dreadful than you can ever imagine, Sandy. Ah, watch over me, Sandy—stay with me every moment—don’t let me lose my mind again; death is nothing, let it come, but not with those dreams, not with the torment of those hideous dreams—I cannot bear that again.... Sandy?...”
source_text: “Oh, Sandy, you are come at last--how I have longed for you! Sit by me--do not leave me--never leave me again, Sandy, never again. Where is your hand?--give it me, dear, let me hold it--there --now all is well, all is peace, and I am happy again--_we_ are happy again, isn't it so, Sandy? You are so dim, so vague, you are but a mist, a cloud, but you are _here_, and that is blessedness sufficient; and I have your hand; don't take it away--it is for only a little while, I shall not require it long.... Was that the child?... Hello-Central!... she doesn't answer. Asleep, perhaps? Bring her when she wakes, and let me touch her hands, her face, her hair, and tell her good-bye.... Sandy! Yes, you are there. I lost myself a moment, and I thought you were gone.... Have I been sick long? It must be so; it seems months to me. And such dreams! such strange and awful dreams, Sandy! Dreams that were as real as reality--delirium, of course, but _so_ real! Why, I thought the king was dead, I thought you were in Gaul and couldn't get home, I thought there was a revolution; in the fantastic frenzy of these dreams, I thought that Clarence and I and a handful of my cadets fought and exterminated the whole chivalry of England! But even that was not the strangest. I seemed to be a creature out of a remote unborn age, centuries hence, and even _that_ was as real as the rest! Yes, I seemed to have flown back out of that age into this of ours, and then forward to it again, and was set down, a stranger and forlorn in that strange England, with an abyss of thirteen centuries yawning between me and you! between me and my home and my friends! between me and all that is dear to me, all that could make life worth the living! It was awful --awfuler than you can ever imagine, Sandy. Ah, watch by me, Sandy --stay by me every moment--_don't_ let me go out of my mind again; death is nothing, let it come, but not with those dreams, not with the torture of those hideous dreams--I cannot endure _that_ again.... Sandy?...”

text: “By my troth, ye must not doubt it!.... Give the beast a hearty feed, lad, and spare it not if thou wouldst keep thy crown; so hie thee straightway to the stable and do as I command.... Sir, ill tidings I bear, and—what, are these pilgrims? Then you can do no better, good people, than draw near and hearken to the story I must tell, since it touches you closely, for you are set to seek what you shall not discover, and to pursue what you shall pursue in vain, my life standing surety for my truth, and my truth and message being this, to wit: that a chance has befallen the like of which has been seen but once in these two hundred years, and that but that single time when that same disaster smote the sacred vale in that manner, by decree of the Most High, for reasons just and causes thereto belonging, wherein the matter—”
source_text: “By the mass ye may not question it!.... Give him good feed, boy, and stint it not, an thou valuest thy crown; so get ye lightly to the stable and do even as I bid.... Sir, it is parlous news I bring, and--be these pilgrims? Then ye may not do better, good folk, than gather and hear the tale I have to tell, sith it concerneth you, forasmuch as ye go to find that ye will not find, and seek that ye will seek in vain, my life being hostage for my word, and my word and message being these, namely: That a hap has happened whereof the like has not been seen no more but once this two hundred years, which was the first and last time that that said misfortune strake the holy valley in that form by commandment of the Most High whereto by reasons just and causes thereunto contributing, wherein the matter--”

text: I kept spies out every night, of course, to bring in news. With each report, the whole business grew more and more imposing. The hosts were assembling, assembling; along every road and track in England the knights were riding, and priests rode beside them to encourage these first Crusaders, since this was the Church’s war. All the nobles, great and small, were making their way there, and all the gentry as well. This was exactly as expected. We would reduce this sort of people to such a degree that the common folk would have no choice but simply to step forward with their republic and--
source_text: I had spies out every night, of course, to get news. Every report made things look more and more impressive. The hosts were gathering, gathering; down all the roads and paths of England the knights were riding, and priests rode with them, to hearten these original Crusaders, this being the Church's war. All the nobilities, big and little, were on their way, and all the gentry. This was all as was expected. We should thin out this sort of folk to such a degree that the people would have nothing to do but just step to the front with their republic and--

text: “Our provisioning here is a fair sample of the whole machine. It is a machine devised not with the least notion of securing the best outcome—that never enters the creed of the War Department—but in order that there may be a rule for every circumstance and no one need argue. The ration of bread and other matter per man is, if anything, somewhat too liberal, and over it there hangs a very savage though not very effective apparatus of weighing and inspection. An allowance a shade too ample is, naturally enough, a plain invitation to waste or pilfering—as anyone except our absurd old fool of a War Department would understand. The inspection concerns quantity, which any idiot can grasp, rather than quality. The proper test of army meat is the nose. If it does not stink, it is good....
source_text: "Our feeding here is typical of the whole system. It is a system invented not with any idea of getting the best results--that does not enter into the War Office philosophy--but to have a rule for everything, and avoid arguments. There is rather too generous an allowance of bread and stuff per man, and there is a very fierce but not very efficient system of weighing and checking. A rather too generous allowance is, of course, a direct incentive to waste or stealing--as any one but our silly old duffer of a War Office would know. The checking is for quantity, which any fool can understand, rather than for quality. The test for the quality of army meat is the smell. If it doesn't smell bad, it is good....

text: “They keep my window open,” he said in complaint to Mr. Direck. “Many a time I have asked them not to. And, of course, he did not understand.” He had gone out by climbing the ivy. Anything might have happened to him—anything. He was not accustomed to going out by himself. He was too young.
source_text: "They leave my window open," he complained to Mr. Direck. "Often I have askit them not to. And of course he did not understand. He has out climbit by the ivy. Anything may have happened to him. Anything. He is not used to going out alone. He is too young.

text: He played hockey with an astonishing fervor and lightness of foot. He always took the field in his frock coat, and the knitted scarf was never once put aside; he regarded the game wholly as a chance for rapid feints and private dexterity. He sprang over the ground like a kitten, whirled abruptly, flung himself upward and landed facing new quarters; his ruddy, sunlit face shone with pleasure, while the coat-tails and scarf streamed and flickered behind him in breathless pursuit of his speed. He never sent the ball on to the others; he never grasped his proper station in the play; he sought only to turn himself into a bounding shield about the ball as he forced it toward the goal. But he would not allow Émile to take part at all, and Madame played as a lady, as a Madonna, as a saint bearing the symbol of her martyrdom. The game and all its ardor moved around her and then away from her; she stayed brave yet indulgent, composed, making her utmost effort to perform the astonishing duties required of her, but in essence a creature of passive graces, living chiefly for them; Lilla, flashing past her, eager and swift, seemed of a wholly different order of being....
source_text: He played hockey with an extraordinary zest and nimbleness. Always he played in the tail coat, and the knitted muffler was never relinquished; he treated the game entirely as an occasion for quick tricks and personal agility; he bounded about the field like a kitten, he pirouetted suddenly, he leapt into the air and came down in new directions; his fresh-coloured face was alive with delight, the coat tails and the muffler trailed and swished about breathlessly behind his agility. He never passed to other players; he never realised his appointed place in the game; he sought simply to make himself a leaping screen about the ball as he drove it towards the goal. But André he would not permit to play at all, and Madame played like a lady, like a Madonna, like a saint carrying the instrument of her martyrdom. The game and its enthusiasms flowed round her and receded from her; she remained quite valiant but tolerant, restrained; doing her best to do the extraordinary things required of her, but essentially a being of passive dignities, living chiefly for them; Letty careering by her, keen and swift, was like a creature of a different species....
